Audio clip of Hamilton Centre Tory candidate sparks political firestorm on eve of election

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/audio-clip-hamilton-centre-tory-candidate-sparks/docview/3171931459/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Audio+clip+of+Hamilton+Centre+Tory+candidate+sparks+political+firestorm+on+eve+of+election&amp;title=The+Hamilton+Spectator+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2025-02-26&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Hamilton+Spectator+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: The provincial Liberals are calling for Hamilton Centre Tory candidate Sarah Bokhari to be suspended and investigated over alleged "influence peddling" in a recorded phone call - a claim the Progressive Conservatives quickly rejected as a "smear" tactic by a jilted acquaintance.
The messy allegations - which include differing calls for police involvement from both the Liberal and PC parties - exploded publicly the day before the Feb. 27 vote.
An audio recording purported to be Bokhari speaking - in a mix of English and Urdu - began circulating online late Tuesday, prompting Liberal Leader Bonnie Crombie to publicly call the next day for a RCMP probe into potential "influence peddling."
A Liberal release included a purported Bokhari quote translated from the recording telling an unnamed individual that "your file will not be moved until I receive my donation ... your file will not move to the city planner until I receive my donation."
The Spectator reached out to Bokhari's campaign to ask for context about the phone call and allegation, but has yet to receive a response from the candidate.
The Progressive Conservative central campaign did not dispute the authenticity of the recording when contacted by The Spectator.
Instead, a spokesperson said in an email statement the recorded comments were made "in jest to someone (Bokhari) believed was a friend."
The statement also suggests the recording was "edited" and circulated as a "smear" attempt by a jilted individual whose "inappropriate" advances were rebuffed by the candidate. The email suggested Bokhari would be contacting police about the matter.
The Spectator has not been able to contact or confirm the identity of the individual who received the phone call or message. It is also unclear what planning matter, if any, was being referenced in the call.
The Tory spokesperson later specified the call happened last summer and was "completely unrelated to the election campaign." It also said Bokhari was not working for the provincial government when the phone conversation occurred.
Bokhari, who has run in Hamilton Centre in past campaigns, worked as a political staffer attached to the parliamentary assistant to the premier's office from 2023 until January 2024, according to her LinkedIn page.
The NDP has also chimed in on the allegation, with Hamilton Centre candidate Robin Lennox sharing a central campaign post on X.
The post says if the recorded phone call did indeed come from the Hamilton Centre candidate, "we need answers for why his team is shaking down Ontarians for quid pro quos and donations."
Bokhari is running in Hamilton Centre, where the race has been largely dominated by a public battle between Lennox for the NDP and former NDPer-turned-Independent incumbent Sarah Jama.
The Liberals have turned to former justice of the peace Eileen Walker in the Hamilton Centre race, while the Greens have Lucia Iannantuono.
Matthew Van Dongen is a reporter at The Hamilton Spectator. mvandongen@thespec.com
CAPTION: Sarah Bokhari is running for the Progressive Conservatives in Hamilton Centre.
CREDIT: Matthew Van Dongen

Subject: Donations

Business indexing term: Subject: Donations

Document 2 of 500

5 things we learned about Mark Carney from the Liberal leadership debates

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/5-things-we-learned-about-mark-carney-liberal/docview/3171483016/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=5+things+we+learned+about+Mark+Carney+from+the+Liberal+leadership+debates&amp;title=The+Hamilton+Spectator+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2025-02-26&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Hamilton+Spectator+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: In the end, it was a friendly affair.
Liberal leadership candidates used their podiums to introduce themselves to party members during these week's debates, but they also shielded Mark Carney, the race's perceived front-runner, from devastating blows that could have helped Pierre Poilievre's Conservatives attack the party during what is expected to be a spring election campaign.
Only Karina Gould, the former Government House leader and cabinet minister, seemed to want to draw Carney into a debate, and even then, it was a pretty tame affair.
Here are a few takeaways from Carney's performance:
Carney's French
It's laborious, painful to listen to, and harder to watch. It may not matter but Monday's French-language performance was a warning that Carney needs to double down on his language studies unless he wants Bloc Québécois Leader Yves-François Blanchet and Poilievre to make mincemeat out of him during a campaign debate.
Freeland is a good friend
Carney, I'm told, was concerned about how Chrystia Freeland would approach him in the debate. The two are long-time friends, and he is the godfather to her youngest child. But he didn't need to worry. It was he who suggested Freeland didn't have the chops for the current task of defending Canada from Donald Trump, telling the audience that while Trump might have been after Canada's market share when Freeland was renegotiating the North American Free Trade Agreement, now the American president is after "our country."
Freeland came to Carney's defence at least three times on Monday. She ensured he could clearly get the words supply management out of his mouth when he told the audience that culture, water and Canada's protection for dairy, eggs, and poultry would never be traded away, an important policy stance for some ridings in Quebec, Ontario and Atlantic Canada.
Later, Freeland re-asked the French moderator's question so Carney could tell the audience he planned to scrap the consumer carbon price.
She also ensured that the audience understood that when he said everyone agreed with Hamas ("Nous sommes d'accord avec Hamas") that he meant to say that everyone on stage agreed on Hamas - that the terrorist group has no place in any discussion about the future of Gaza.
But Freeland wasn't the only candidate to lend Carney a helping hand.
Frank Baylis helped clarify Carney's misspeak when he said Poilievre wanted to cut Radio-Canada - as the Conservative leader has said he wants to defund the CBC, not the French arm of the public broadcaster. Baylis suggested that scrapping the CBC would impact Radio-Canada, as the two share infrastructure (which is true).
Tuesday, there were no lifelines for Carney. But he didn't need them either.
Had Freeland concluded, as many in her own camp have, that she won't win and her best play now is courting a key post in a Carney cabinet?
Carney's key policies
Carney repeated that he's laser focused on the economy, on growing wages faster than inflation, and pledged to be a more hands on manager than Prime Minister Justin Trudeau. Among his promises: a middle-income tax cut, scrapping the GST on the purchase of new homes, eliminating the consumer price on carbon. He said he'll keep the Liberals' child care, dental care, and pharmacare programs. He wants to increase competition in the grocery, broadband, and transportation sectors, and suggested he'd continue with the Liberals' firearms buyback program, and "reinforce" Criminal Code provisions for sexual abuse. He demonstrated a keen interest in artificial intelligence, and sees Canadian investments there as key to finding government efficiencies and combating cybersecurity and information warfare.
Carney is open to new pipelines
Carney also suggested it is "possible" a Liberal government under his watch builds a new pipeline. There could be an Energy East-type project, he said, bringing Alberta's oil to the Maritimes, but he suggested it would have to be built to displace American imports. Trump had changed the calculations, he suggested. But a new pipeline would only get built, if it is in the interest of the entire country and the provinces would get a veto. "Quebec, Ontario, Manitoba, Saskatchewan must be in agreement," he said.
Bill 21
Carney appears to be breaking rank with Trudeau's firm opposition to Bill 21. The Quebec legislation bars wearing religious symbols by people in some public sector jobs, such as teachers and judges - and the current federal government argues the law breaches the Canadian Charter of Rights and Freedoms. When the candidates were asked about that Monday, Carney responded, "There must be reasonable accommodations." Then he said he would "respect absolutely the tradition of laicïté" - often described as the separation between church and state - "here in Quebec." Does that mean a Carney-led government would not intervene in the challenge of the Quebec law at the Supreme Court? When asked, his team sidestepped the question, as well as what he meant specifically when he said he supports "reasonable accommodations."
The term "reasonable accommodation" refers to the majority accommodating religious minorities, namely Muslims, Sikhs and Jews. Back in 2008, Quebec's Bouchard-Taylor Commission recommended a limited ban on wearing religious symbols for people with "coercive" powers of the state, such as police officers, judges and prosecutors. It discussed the accommodating of religious minorities through a new policy of "interculturalism" that would reconcile ethnic diversity with the promotion of francophone identity. Later, the Parti Quebecois and the Coalition Avenir Quebec (CAQ) governments proposed ways to promote the French language while treating religious minorities as second-class citizens, all in the name of reasonable accommodation.
Poilievre's Conservatives have spoken from both sides of their mouths on this issue. During their leadership debates in 2022, Poilievre said he would intervene at the Supreme Court to fight Bill 21. But after he won, his Quebec lieutenant said a Poilievre-led government would not in fact intervene. When the Supreme Court hearing was announced last month, Poilievre's office refused to say where he stood on the issue. Despite weeks of requests for Poilievre's office to clarify his stance on Bill 21, it has refused to respond. Now Carney has some explaining to do, too.
CAPTION: Mark Carney speaks during a Liberal leadership debate in Montreal on Feb. 24, 2025.U.S. President Donald Trump and the Canadian economy dominated the discussion as Liberal leadership candidates faced off in the first of two debates. The French debate will be followed by an English debate. Former finance minister Chrystia Freeland, former central banker Mark Carney, former House leader Karina Gould and Montreal businessman and former MP Frank Baylis are vying to replace Prime Minister Justin Trudeau. (Feb. 25, 2025 / The Canadian Press)Liberal Party of Canada leadership candidate Mark Carney speaks during the English-language Liberal Leadership debate in Montreal on Feb. 25, 2025.
CREDIT: Althia Raj

Sick of unwanted calls and texts? Here's why Canada's Do Not Call List won't stop political parties and these groups from contacting you

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/sick-unwanted-calls-texts-heres-why-canadas-do/docview/3171354688/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Sick+of+unwanted+calls+and+texts%3F+Here%27s+why+Canada%27s+Do+Not+Call+List+won%27t+stop+political+parties+and+these+groups+from+contacting+you&amp;title=The+Hamilton+Spectator+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2025-02-26&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Hamilton+Spectator+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Are you sick of receiving unsolicited phone calls or texts from businesses, charities, political parties or other sources? You could register your cellphone or land line with Canada's National Do Not Call List (DNCL).
Doug Ford text messages
Ontario residents have reported receiving text messages from Premier Doug Ford in recent weeks leading up to the Feb. 27 provincial election, and wondered how he got their phone number. Some even replied "Stop" to the messages, but continued to receive similar messages.
Do Not Call List exemptions
It turns out that registering with the DNCL will not eliminate all unsolicited calls.
The Government of Canada notes several kinds of calls are exempted from the DNCL rules.
The exemptions include unsolicited calls made by, or on behalf of the following:
Canadian registered charities;Political parties, riding associations and candidates;Persons or entities collecting information for a survey; andNewspapers of general circulation, for the purpose of soliciting subscriptions.
Telemarketing calls from persons or organizations with whom you have an existing business relationship are also exempt. If you have purchased, leased or rented a product or service in the last 18 months from the telemarketer, you may still receive calls.
You may also be contacted if you have a written contract with the telemarketer for a service that is still in effect or expired within the last 18 months.
If you've asked a telemarketer about a product or service within the last six months or expressed your consent to be contacted on a written or online form, you may receive calls. The same goes if you have provided verbal permission in an audio recording that's been verified by an independent third party.
Why political 'robocalls' and texts aren't going away
The Canadian Radio-television and Telecommunications Commission (CRTC) confirmed to Metroland that regardless of whether Canadians are registered with the National Do Not Call List, residents may still receive calls and texts from political parties and candidates. The CRTC is an independent quasi-judicial tribunal that regulates the Canadian communications sector in the public interest.
"Similarly, text messages or emails asking for your support for a political party or candidate or requesting your opinion on various issues are not subject to Canada's Anti-Spam Legislation, whether during an election period or not, as they are typically not commercial in nature," noted spokesperson Megan MacLean.
Elections Ontario media relations co-ordinator Dave Pearce said Ontario election laws do not prohibit campaign telephone calls or text messages. This includes individual calls and texts, as well as the practice of "robocalling."
"Robocalling" is considered a legitimate campaign activity, and political entities can communicate with voters using automated phone calls or by hiring call centre agents during the campaign period, Pearce noted.
What about research and polling?
Calls from individuals or organizations made solely for the purpose of market research or surveys are also exempt from the DNCL rules. These calls are not considered telemarketing calls under Canada's unsolicited telecommunications rules.
How to register
Consumers can list their home, mobile, fax or VoIP number to reduce telemarketing calls. Registration is free. Telemarketers have 31 days from the date you register to stop contacting you.
The Government of Canada offers a six-step process to register your number online.
Registrations on the DNCL are indefinite and do not expire on their own.
CAPTION: Registering with Canada's National Do Not Call List will not eliminate all unsolicited calls. The Government of Canada notes several kinds of calls are exempted from the rules.
CREDIT: Mike Pearson

Hamilton-area Today: Winter may have peaked, but mini-cabin move-in is ongoing + 'Fairly invisible' provincial election is nearly over

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/hamilton-area-today-winter-may-have-peaked-mini/docview/3171354649/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Hamilton-area+Today%3A+Winter+may+have+peaked%2C+but+mini-cabin+move-in+is+ongoing+%2B+%27Fairly+invisible%27+provincial+election+is+nearly+over&amp;title=The+Hamilton+Spectator+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2025-02-26&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Hamilton+Spectator+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Good morning! It's Feb. 26, here are the top stories today in the Hamilton area.
Today's traffic and weather
The warmer weather continues - Environment Canada is forecasting a high of 3 C at the airport weather station. A flood watch around local waterways is in effect.
Traffic and transit updates
Find the latest provincial road closures and traffic incidents via Ontario 511. Closures in Hamilton are available on the Hamilton police incident feed.
Taking transit today? Find the latest GO Transit service updates here. The latest information on local bus services are available here: Hamilton, Burlington and Oakville.
Top stories on The SpecExtensive search at landfill for missing woman
A sprawling Hamilton landfill is now the site of a concentrated search for the remains of a missing 40-year-old woman who police believe may have been killed in her downtown apartment and disposed of through a garbage chute.
We don't know how many delayed tiny shelters are occupied
Twelve of 40 mini-cabins for up to 80 people were expected to be occupied by the end of last week, but city officials haven't said how many now live at the site.
'We haven't even started'
Heavy snowfalls and a recent cold snap mean Hamilton-area maple syrup producers are getting a later start to their season than in past years - but they still expect a good harvest.
Will Hamilton residents vote?
Opinion polls suggest Doug Ford's PCs are cruising to victory. Locally, the winter campaign has been "fairly invisible" for a host of reasons. Will locals cast their ballots, or will low voter turnout scar this winter election?
Last chance for RSV vaccine
Hamilton families have one last chance to get their babies immunized against a common virus that can cause serious lung infections before a clinic at McMaster Children's Hospital closes.
Subscribe to our newsletters for the latest local content.
CAPTION: Aerial footage of the Glanbrook landfill shows Hamilton police officers combing through a "target zone" where investigators believe there is evidence of Shalini Singh's remains.
CREDIT: Hamilton Spectator Staff

Document 5 of 500

Will you vote in Ontario's 'invisible' election Thursday?: Campaign competes for attention with blizzards, U.S. tariffs and federal leadership race

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/will-you-vote-ontarios-invisible-election/docview/3171234545/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Will+you+vote+in+Ontario%27s+%27invisible%27+election+Thursday%3F%3A+Campaign+competes+for+attention+with+blizzards%2C+U.S.+tariffs+and+federal+leadership+race&amp;title=The+Spectator&amp;issn=11899417&amp;date=2025-02-26&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.1&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Spectator&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Judith Bishop spent an hour Tuesday trying to convince her fellow Hamiltonians to vote in what some political observers have labelled an "invisible" provincial election.
Bishop, a past school trustee and current member of Concerned Seniors for Sensible Government, said her group is worried about voter turnout Thursday. The snap winter election is already competing with a world in political chaos.
"There is such an enormous amount going on, with Donald Trump and possibly another Canadian election right around the corner," said Bishop.
She staked out a busy Westdale corner to chat up residents about what she considers the "poor record" of the incumbent provincial Tories on education, homelessness and health care. "We wanted to remind people that despite everything going on, it is still important to get out and vote."
They might have their work cut out for them.
If you put much stock in opinion polls, Doug Ford's Progressive Conservative party appears to be coasting toward re-election despite criticism from opponents - including Bishop - the Tory leader "needlessly" triggered a $189-million campaign 15 months early. "There is no reason for an election now ... (and) I hope people remember that," she said.
Locally, the winter campaign has been "fairly invisible" for a host of reasons, suggested Peter Graefe, McMaster University political-science professor, earlier this month. It's a bad sign considering the previous provincial election held in June already featured record-low turnout of around 44 per cent.
This year, about six per cent of eligible voters cast a ballot in a truncated, three-day advance poll period - a lower early-bird turnout than in recent elections. (Locally, advance turnout ranged from 6.12 per cent in Hamilton East-Stoney Creek to as high as 8.15 per cent in Hamilton West-Ancaster-Dundas.)
In Hamilton, the rare February vote has forced some candidates to venture door-to-door in spiked boots for safety and literally drill into frozen ground to erect election signs - only to see them buried in back-to-back snowstorms earlier this month. The snap election also caught some parties "unprepared," said Graefe. He noted some local candidates were only nominated after the writ dropped.
He also agreed there is a lot of competition for the attention of worried would-be voters. This includes a lame duck prime minister and the threat of tariff-driven job losses - which Ford has cited as the reason to seek a new, "strong mandate" nearly a year and a half before an election was due to be called.
Opposition Liberal and NDP leaders have argued Ford used the tariff scare for personal political advantage - and even suggested he triggered the early vote to get ahead of any findings in a police probe of the Greenbelt scandal.
If you're looking to vote based on the issues on Thursday, there is no lack of local concerns and the following are examples highlighted by the Spectator in-depth.
Local issues
Local health-care workers pushed to make voters aware of a troubling lack of family doctors - more than 75,000 city residents don't have one - and continued reliance on "hallway medicine" in hospitals;
The city and non-profit housing groups, meanwhile, are desperate for funding for supportive housing that could help move hundreds of unhoused people living in tents out of parks or off the street;
Critics are also raising the alarm about provincial legislation making it easier for developers to apply for urban boundary expansions that would allow homes on thousands of acres of Hamilton farmland.
Hamilton and area also has its share of intriguing riding races that could boost voter interest.
The city's only open race on Hamilton Mountain is "the one to watch," suggested Vox Pop Labs founder Clifton van der Linden, who has pioneered various data-driven tools for voters and election forecasting. The McMaster professor noted the riding boasts three "well-known, but very different" candidates who have publicly duked it out to replace outgoing NDP representative Monique Taylor.
Those include trustee and Liberal Dawn Danko, city bylaw official and Tory Monica Ciriello and the NDP's Kojo Damptey, an activist and university instructor who narrowly lost a Mountain council race in 2022.
In Hamilton Centre, there is also a fascinating battle between NDP-turned-independent incumbent Sarah Jama and the party's replacement candidate, physician Robin Lennox.
Jama - who was controversially ejected from the NDP due to online posts about the war in Gaza and the resulting furor - has an enthusiastic team at her back that includes disaffected members of the NDP's own riding association. Some traditionally NDP-supporting labour groups have also backed Jama instead.
Still, it is always a "tough slog" for any independent candidate to win an election battle with a party-backed competitor, said van der Linden - especially given the long orange history of a riding once represented by current mayor Andrea Horwath.
Matthew Van Dongen is a
reporter for The Spectator.
mvandongen@thespec.com.
CAPTION: Ontario election day is Thursday.Judith Bishop and a small group of older adults spend part of Tuesday urging west Hamilton residents to vote in the Feb. 27 election.Spectator file photoCathie Coward The Hamilton Spectator

CREDIT: Matthew Van Dongen Reporter Matthew Van Dongen is a reporter for The Spectator. mvandongen@thespec.com.

Document 6 of 500

Will Hamilton residents vote in Ontario's 'invisible' election Feb. 27?

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/will-hamilton-residents-vote-ontarios-invisible/docview/3171232863/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Will+Hamilton+residents+vote+in+Ontario%27s+%27invisible%27+election+Feb.+27%3F&amp;title=The+Hamilton+Spectator+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2025-02-26&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Hamilton+Spectator+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Judith Bishop spent an hour Tuesday trying to convince her fellow Hamiltonians to vote in what some political observers have labelled an "invisible" provincial election.
Bishop, a past school trustee and current member of Concerned Seniors for Sensible Government, said her group is worried about voter turnout Feb. 27. The snap winter election is already competing with a world in political chaos.
"There is such an enormous amount going on, with Donald Trump and possibly another Canadian election right around the corner," said Bishop.
She staked out a busy Westdale corner to chat up residents about what she considers the "poor record" of the incumbent provincial Tories on education, homelessness and health care. "We wanted to remind people that despite everything going on, it is still important to get out and vote."
They might have their work cut out for them.
If you put much stock in opinion polls, Doug Ford's Progressive Conservative party appears to be coasting toward re-election despite criticism from opponents - including Bishop - the Tory leader "needlessly" triggered a $189-million campaign 15 months early. "There is no reason for an election now ... (and) I hope people remember that," she said.
Locally, the winter campaign has been "fairly invisible" for a host of reasons, suggested Peter Graefe, McMaster University political-science professor, earlier this month. It's a bad sign considering the previous provincial election held in June already featured record-low turnout of around 44 per cent.
This year, about six per cent of eligible voters cast a ballot in a truncated, three-day advance poll period - a lower early-bird turnout than in recent elections. (Locally, advance turnout ranged from 6.12 per cent in Hamilton East-Stoney Creek to as high as 8.15 per cent in Hamilton West-Ancaster-Dundas.)
In Hamilton, the rare February vote has forced some candidates to venture door-to-door in spiked boots for safety and literally drill into frozen ground to erect election signs - only to see them buried in back-to-back snowstorms earlier this month. The snap election also caught some parties "unprepared," said Graefe. He noted some local candidates were only nominated after the writ dropped.
He also agreed there is a lot of competition for the attention of worried would-be voters. This includes a lame duck prime minister and the threat of tariff-driven job losses - which Ford has cited as the reason to seek a new, "strong mandate" nearly a year and a half before an election was due to be called.
Opposition Liberal and NDP leaders have argued Ford used the tariff scare for personal political advantage - and even suggested he triggered the early vote to get ahead of any findings in a police probe of the Greenbelt scandal.
If you're looking to vote based on the issues on Feb. 27, there is no lack of local concerns and the following are examples highlighted by the Spectator in-depth.
Local issuesLocal health-care workers pushed to make voters aware of a troubling lack of family doctors - more than 75,000 city residents don't have one - and continued reliance on "hallway medicine" in hospitals;The city and non-profit housing groups, meanwhile, are desperate for funding for supportive housing that could help move 100s of unhoused people living in tents out of parks or off the street;Critics are also raising the alarm about provincial legislation making it easier for developers to apply for urban boundary expansions that would allow homes on 1,000s of acres of Hamilton farmland.
Hamilton and area also has its share of intriguing riding races that could boost voter interest.
The city's only open race on Hamilton Mountain is "the one to watch," suggested Vox Pop Labs founder Clifton van der Linden, who has pioneered various data-driven tools for voters and election forecasting. The McMaster professor noted the riding boasts three "well-known, but very different" candidates who have publicly duked it out to replace outgoing NDP representative Monique Taylor.
Those include trustee and Liberal Dawn Danko, city bylaw official and Tory Monica Ciriello and the NDP's Kojo Damptey, an activist and university instructor who narrowly lost a Mountain council race in 2022.
In Hamilton Centre, there is also a fascinating battle between NDP-turned-independent incumbent Sarah Jama and the party's replacement candidate, physician Robin Lennox.
Jama - who was controversially ejected from the NDP due to online posts about the war in Gaza and the resulting furor - has an enthusiastic team at her back that includes disaffected members of the NDP's own riding association. Some traditionally NDP-supporting labour groups have also backed Jama instead.
Still, it is always a "tough slog" for any independent candidate to win an election battle with a party-backed competitor, said van der Linden - especially given the long orange history of a riding once represented by current mayor Andrea Horwath.
Matthew Van Dongen is a reporter for The Spectator. mvandongen@thespec.com.
CAPTION: Judith Bishop and a small group of older adults spend part of Tuesday urging west Hamilton residents to vote in the Feb. 27 election.Election day is Thursday in Ontario.Judith Bishop and a small group of older adults spend part of Tuesday urging west Hamilton residents to vote in the Feb. 27 election.
CREDIT: Matthew Van Dongen

We can't afford this nuclear fantasy

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/we-cant-afford-this-nuclear-fantasy/docview/3171232827/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=unknown&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=We+can%27t+afford+this+nuclear+fantasy&amp;title=The+Spectator&amp;issn=11899417&amp;date=2025-02-26&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.11&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Spectator&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Doug Ford's proposal to bury Highway 401 lanes from Brampton or Mississauga in the west to Scarborough or Markham in the east, with an estimated price tag of at least $100 billion, has been described as being a "fantasy that would bankrupt" the province.
Although the 401 proposal has drawn the most attention among the Ford government's increasingly grandiose infrastructure proposals, it actually isn't the largest.
That status goes to the government's plans to dramatically expand the province's now aging fleet of nuclear reactors. A 10,000-megawatt (MW) facility proposed just before the election call for Wesleyville, Ont., between Coburg and Kingston, could break the $200-billion mark in capital costs alone.
That estimate is based on the actual costs of the most recently completed nuclear construction project in North American, the Vogtle plant in Georgia. That facility, completed last summer, came in at $50 billion (Canadian) for 2,200 MW capacity. A simple extrapolation of those costs to the Wesleyville project would give a figure of over $200 billion.
But there is even more to the Ford government's nuclear plan.
A proposed new 4,800-MW facility at the Bruce nuclear site would come in around $100 billion on the same basis. New estimates by the U.S. Tennessee Valley Authority on the costs of the type of the four 300-MW reactors proposed for the Darlington site suggests costs in the range of $25 billion.
To this has to be added the costs of the refurbishments of the existing reactors at Bruce, Darlington and potentially, the Pickering B site, with potential costs of between $35 billion and $50 billion.
For context, the scale of Ontario's nuclear proposals, relative to provincial GDP, would be comparable to that of the Muskrat Falls hydro project in Labrador. That project really did push the Province of Newfoundland and Labrador to the brink of bankruptcy, save for a massive federal bailout.
At least the Muskrat Falls project was subject to external economic and environmental reviews. Unfortunately, the warnings about the project's risks were ignored. In contrast, none of Ontario's proposals have been subject to any form of meaningful external review in terms of economic, technological or environmental rationality.
The Ford government's nuclear heavy strategy appears to be premised on an assumption that a massive nuclear expansion program will turn Ontario into an electricity production and export "superpower."
The fundamental problem with that strategy is that Ontario has no comparative advantage in electricity production.
Comparative advantage in energy tends to be a product of accidents of geography. Ontario was the beneficiary of such an accident through the first half of the 20th century, where hydro-electricity, principally from Niagara Falls, provided the foundation of the industrial base that was built through the Golden Horseshoe around the western end of Lake Ontario, from Niagara to Oshawa.
But that advantage was lost from the early 1960s onward when the province ceased to be a hydro-dominated system, turning first to the construction of coal-fired plants, and then a massive nuclear construction program from the 1960s to the 1990s.
Ontario turned out to be no better at building and operating these types of plants than anyone else in North America.
The province therefore lost its comparative advantage in electricity production. The recent experience with attempts at constructing new nuclear facilities in the U.S. and Europe, like the Vogtle project, suggest such advantage cannot be restored through a nuclear expansion program. Renewable energy sources, combined with energy storage offer much more cost-effective, lower-impact and lower-risk options.
Instead, when it comes to energy, any economic strategy for Ontario has to focus on controlling energy costs and improving energy productivity, not energy production. The province is already taking $7.3 billion a year from general revenues, funds that otherwise would be spent, for example, on schools and hospitals, to artificially lower hydro costs for industrial and residential consumers.
The Ford government has given no indication of what its nuclear expansion program will cost or how it will be financed. Past experience tells us it will be Ontario electricity ratepayers and taxpayers who are likely to be ultimately stuck with the bills.
Ontario needs to engage in a serious debate about the future of its energy systems. But it needs to look to pathways to decarbonize the province without risking bankrupting it in the process.
Mark Winfield is a professor
of environmental and urban change at York University
and co-chair of the faculty's Sustainable Energy Initiative.
CAPTION:

CREDIT: Mark Winfield Mark Winfield is a professor of environmental and urban change at York University and co-chair of the faculty's Sustainable Energy Initiative.;

Document 8 of 500

Icy stairs and snow bank signs: Candidates face challenges reaching voters in rare winter election campaign

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/icy-stairs-snow-bank-signs/docview/3171232810/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Icy+stairs+and+snow+bank+signs%3A+Candidates+face+challenges+reaching+voters+in+rare+winter+election+campaign&amp;title=The+Spectator&amp;issn=11899417&amp;date=2025-02-26&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.6&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Spectator&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Falling down stairs, pushing stuck cars out of snow banks and cancelling events are just some of the challenges candidates have faced on the winter campaign trail, but Sol Mamakwa may have them all beat.
This is Ontario's first winter election campaign since 1981 and the incumbent NDP candidate for Kiiwetinoong in northwestern Ontario has rented a plane with skis so he can try to visit some of the 24 fly-in First Nation communities in his riding that spans nearly 300,000 square kilometres.
"(It's) not too bad, but it's frickin' cold right now," he said recently during a break from campaigning in Pickle Lake. "Last week has been minus 40."
He was trying to visit two to three fly-in communities a day with his plane over the last week or so of the campaign. At a cost of $6,000 to $8,000 per day that's about all his campaign can afford, so he is trying to maximize his flying time.
In Kenora-Rainy River, directly south of Kiiwetinoong, the temperatures were similarly bone chilling, and the Progressive Conservative incumbent has been feeling it while out canvassing.
"I look like Kenny from South Park," Greg Rickford said, describing how he bundles himself up from head to toe.
"Once you get past minus 30, if your skin is exposed and you're out there for any amount of time, you're putting yourself at a little bit of risk."
Rickford, who has several provincial and federal campaigns under his belt, said he usually likes to door-knock four to five hours a day, but it's just not possible this winter. He has pivoted to canvassing by phone at some points, largely for the sake of his team.
"These are volunteer folks from the riding and from other parts of Ontario," he said. "I'm extremely grateful for them, but I'm especially grateful because this has been an uncharacteristically very cold, sustained winter so far."
Even down in Toronto's Willowdale riding, incumbent Progressive Conservative candidate Stan Cho has been limiting his team's exposure to the elements, with truncated canvassing shifts. When the volunteers get back to the office to warm up, there are sweet and sour chicken wings, sausages and egg salad sandwiches - all made by his mother to provide the fuel.
And, as in many campaign offices across the province, hand warmers aplenty are on offer.
Back up north, meanwhile, Mamakwa has been outfitted in ski pants, a parka, a homemade hat made with moose hide and beaver fur and sturdy boots and mitts that belonged to his late father.
He has also been making use of the winter roads, often built over frozen lakes and rivers until the spring thaw, with his team also using them to deliver lawn signs. He put one up in the middle of a frozen lake during an ice fishing derby.
Though in this election, whether it's in Kiiwetinoong, Kanata, or King-Vaughan, calling them "lawn" signs is a bit of a misnomer.
"Order your very own decorative orange snowbank sign!" NDP incumbent candidate Jennifer French wrote on social media to supporters in her Oshawa riding.
The NDP's incumbent candidate in Waterloo, Catherine Fife, wrote on social media that she slipped down some stairs, but called it a "strong canvass" nonetheless after she got to meet a cute baby.
While out canvassing in Willowdale, Cho has helped not one, not two but three drivers whose cars were stuck in the snow.
"(For one driver), we had to go into his recycling bin, pick out egg cartons, like the whole 30-egg type of egg cartons, to get some traction under the tires, because he was just spinning out," he said.
"That one took a little longer than most, but generally it's just a little push. Happy to do it for the neighbours."
Though for all the fun winter campaigning war stories the candidates are amassing, Fraser said Ontario should not even be in an election right now.
The Liberals, NDP and Greens all say they don't buy Progressive Conservative Leader Doug Ford's justification of needing a new mandate to deal with U.S. President Donald Trump and tariffs, and suggest he called the snap election to capitalize on good poll numbers.
The Canadian Press
CAPTION: New Democrat MPP Sol Mamakwa, running for re-election in the riding of Kiiwetinoong, has been canvassing using a plane fitting with skis.Sol Mamakwa photoVia The Canadian Press

CREDIT: Allison Jones and Liam Casey The Canadian Press

Document 9 of 500

Letters

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/letters/docview/3171231756/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=unknown&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Letters&amp;title=The+Spectator&amp;issn=11899417&amp;date=2025-02-26&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.11&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Spectator&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Important mail in limbo
Re: Lack of mail delivery unacceptable, Feb. 25
The retirement community where I live has also been experiencing long gaps of no mail delivery, and not just with the latest snowfall. Our roads and sidewalks are properly cleared and my Spectator has been delivered every day without fail. The only mail I have received since Feb. 10 was yesterday. My voting card and one other piece of mail was delivered. I spoke to the substitute mail person about where the rest of my mail was: utility bills, bank statements, credit card statements, the Ontario rebate cheque, insurance renewal documents and income tax documents, all of which should have been received in the last 10 days. I was told the mail was sitting at the work station of the regular delivery person. The casual person was brought in to deliver the voting cards.
To register a complaint, you can call Canada Post at 1-866-607-6301. You can also contact Canada Post online. Have as many details as possible about missing mail and dates/duration of non-delivery.
Judith Pyke, Hamilton
Housing is a complex issue
Re: The housing, homelessness bill keeps growing. Here's how Hamilton spends nearly $200 million, Feb. 24
I just finished reading the excellent article by Teviah Moro. This was the best, most understandable explanation of housing in all different areas and types of the city. As a former resident of Hamilton Housing Authority from many years ago, I thought I had a reasonable understanding of how it all worked. I was sadly mistaken. It is a difficult system for anyone who is underhoused or homeless to navigate. This is a must-read for anyone seeking a place to live in today's housing market. Thank you for putting this complex issue in terms for everyone to understand.
Laurel Thompson, Hamilton
Housing questions still remain
Teviah Moro presented a comprehensive picture of how the city spends money on housing. By my calculation, based on the numbers in his article, of the $186 million spent last year, about $138.5 million went to subsidize folks in homes. As was pointed out, this is a good thing, both financially and, probably equally or more importantly, psychologically. However, the one thing I could not find in the article was for all the money spent, how many people went from being homeless to being "homed?" The only thing I saw in this regard was an agreement signed for $8.2 million toward 800 units of affordable housing by 2027. This works out to $10,250 per unit, so clearly there must be more money coming from elsewhere. It seems that there would be two objectives for spending the $186 million: one, keep people in their homes, and two, move people from not having some kind of permanent roof over their heads to having one.
Richard Ronchka, Carlisle
Get out and vote on Thursday
I would like to remind voters in Ontario about the results of the 2022 provincial election.
Voter turnout was about 43.5 per cent. Here are the parties' vote percentages (of all eligible voters): Progressive Conservative, 18 per cent; Liberal, 10 per cent; NDP, 10 per cent; "other," five per cent; "did not vote," 52 per cent. These results allowed the Progressive Conservatives to get a majority. We cannot have another PC majority. How do we get the 52 per cent who did not vote to vote? Let us remember the many bad decisions of the Progressive Conservatives. Please vote!
Roman Caruk, Hamilton
Attacks are hypocritical
I find it humorous that so many people are quick to point out that Doug Ford is a bully, but don't pass up the chance to troll and bully him and the Progressive Conservative party as a whole on every platform available. None of what I read is constructive criticism, it is all rants and personal attacks. I guess the culture of divisive and rude entitlement is not limited to the south. Canadians have apparently removed the guardrails of acceptable free speech and dialogue.
Troy Smillie, Binbrook
Don't reward PC party
If Doug Ford wins this election, we will send the message that we are OK with an unnecessary snap election, that we don't mind our health-care system remains in crisis and that we support Ford's harsh response to the looming tariff which has increased tensions between the U.S. and Canada and even resulted in booing of each other's national anthems at sporting events.
Instead of holding Ford accountable and choosing a new premier, we will reward him if he is re-elected. Simply put.
Tina Maini, Hamilton

We can't afford Doug Ford's nuclear fantasy

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/we-cant-afford-doug-fords-nuclear-fantasy/docview/3171231634/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=We+can%27t+afford+Doug+Ford%27s+nuclear+fantasy&amp;title=The+Hamilton+Spectator+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2025-02-26&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Hamilton+Spectator+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Doug Ford's proposal to bury Highway 401 lanes from Brampton or Mississauga in the west to Scarborough or Markham in the east, with an estimated price tag of at least $100 billion, has been described as being a "fantasy that would bankrupt" the province.
Although the 401 proposal has drawn the most attention among the Ford government's increasingly grandiose infrastructure proposals, it actually isn't the largest.

That estimate is based on the actual costs of the most recently completed nuclear construction project in North American, the Vogtle plant in Georgia. That facility, completed last summer, came in at $50 billion (Canadian) for 2,200 MW capacity. A simple extrapolation of those costs to the Wesleyville project would give a figure of over $200 billion.

For context, the scale of Ontario's nuclear proposals, relative to provincial GDP, would be comparable to that of the Muskrat Falls hydro project in Labrador. That project really did push the Province of Newfoundland and Labrador to the brink of bankruptcy, save for a massive federal bailout.
At least the Muskrat Falls project was subject to external economic and environmental reviews. Unfortunately, the warnings flowing from those reviews about the project's risks were ignored. In contrast, none of Ontario's proposals have been subject to any form of meaningful external review in terms of their economic, technological or environmental rationality.
The Ford government's nuclear heavy strategy appears to be premised on an assumption that a massive nuclear expansion program will turn Ontario into an electricity production and export "superpower."
The fundamental problem with strategy is that Ontario has no comparative advantage in electricity production.
Comparative advantage in energy tends to be a product of accidents of geography. Ontario was the beneficiary of such an accident through the first half of the 20th century, where hydro-electricity, principally from Niagara Falls, provided the foundation of the industrial base that was built through the Golden Horseshoe around the western end of Lake Ontario, from Niagara to Oshawa.
But that advantage was lost from the early 1960s onward when the province ceased to be a hydro-dominated system, turning first to the construction of coal-fired plants, and then a massive nuclear construction program from the 1960s to the 1990s.
Ontario turned out to be no better at building and operating these types of plants than anyone else in North America.
The province therefore lost its comparative advantage in electricity production. The recent experience with attempts at constructing new nuclear facilities in the U.S. and Europe, like the Vogtle project, suggest such advantage cannot be restored through a nuclear expansion program. Renewable energy sources, combined with energy storage offer much more cost-effective, lower-impact and lower-risk options.
Instead, when it comes to energy, any economic strategy for Ontario has to focus on controlling energy costs and improving energy productivity, not energy production. The province is already taking $7.3 billion a year from general revenues, funds that otherwise would be spent, for example, on schools and hospitals, to artificially lower hydro costs for industrial and residential consumers.
The Ford government has given no indication of what its nuclear expansion program will cost or how it will be financed. Past experience tells us it will be Ontario electricity ratepayers and taxpayers who are likely to be ultimately stuck with the bills.
Ontario needs to engage in a serious debate about the future of its energy systems. But it needs to look to pathways to decarbonize the province without risking bankrupting it in the process.

Subject: Capital costs; Economic development; Energy; Electricity; Coal-fired power plants; Nuclear power plants; Proposals

Location: United States--US

Title: We can't afford Doug Ford's nuclear fantasy

Ford campaign talks up renewed tariff threat

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/ford-campaign-talks-up-renewed-tariff-threat/docview/3171231058/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Ford+campaign+talks+up+renewed+tariff+threat&amp;title=The+Spectator&amp;issn=11899417&amp;date=2025-02-26&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.6&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Spectator&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Progressive Conservative Leader Doug Ford highlighted the renewed threat of U.S. tariffs Tuesday to hammer home the central message of his campaign in the final days of the snap election.
Ford, who has painted himself as the best choice to safeguard Ontario's economy, zeroed in on U.S. President Donald Trump's latest comments on the looming tariffs during a campaign stop in Ottawa, stressing the province needs to be "ready for anything" over the next four years.
Trump said Monday the tariffs, which were pushed back to March 4 after Canada agreed to introduce new security measures at the border, are "going forward on time, on schedule." His executive order calls for 25 per cent tariffs on all Canadian imports, with a lower 10 per cent levy on Canadian energy.
Ford mused Tuesday about making Americans pay more for the electricity Ontario sends to the United States in response to any levies.
"We're reviewing the cost of electricity we're sending down there. And if he puts tariffs on anything in Canada or Ontario, they're getting a tariff on their electricity," Ford said.
"And then we'll go to the next stage and we'll wait to see what happens from there."
He gave no further details on the proposed measure, or how it would fit in with his previously announced plan to cut off energy exports to the U.S. should Trump move forward with sweeping tariffs next week.
Ford said he doesn't want a tariff war, "but if someone comes in and starts attacking our families, attacking our businesses and attacking everything we do in Canada, that's it, I'm full in there.
"I'm in the ring and I'll do whatever it takes to make sure that we hit back and they feel the pain like we feel the pain."
Ford has made economic stability in the face of possible tariffs the centrepiece of his campaign, arguing he needs a stronger mandate to navigate the next four years of a Trump presidency.
But the other party leaders have called the $189-million election unnecessary given that the Progressive Conservatives already held a majority and would have received support for stimulus measures.
Liberal Leader Bonnie Crombie said Tuesday that Ford should have diversified Ontario's economy and worked to eliminate interprovincial trade barriers a long time ago, once again accusing him of calling the election for personal gain.
Asked whether she would, as premier, leverage Ontario's electricity exports in a potential trade war, Crombie said it's important to remember how integrated the province's energy grid is with states across the border.
The Canadian Press
CAPTION:

CREDIT: Paola Loriggio and Sonja Puzic The Canadian Press

Document 12 of 500

Feb. 26: Important mail stuck in limbo, get out and vote on Thursday and other letters to the editor

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/feb-26-important-mail-stuck-limbo-get-out-vote-on/docview/3171230623/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Feb.+26%3A+Important+mail+stuck+in+limbo%2C+get+out+and+vote+on+Thursday+and+other+letters+to+the+editor&amp;title=The+Hamilton+Spectator+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2025-02-26&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Hamilton+Spectator+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Important mail stuck in limbo
Re: Lack of mail delivery unacceptable, Feb. 25
The retirement community where I live has also been experiencing long gaps of no mail delivery, and not just with the latest snowfall. Our neighbourhood has door-to-door delivery. Our roads and sidewalks are properly cleared and my Spectator has been delivered every day without fail.
The only mail I have received since Feb. 10 was Monday. My voting card and one other piece of mail was delivered. I spoke to the substitute mail person about where the rest of my mail was: utility bills, bank statements, credit card statements, the Ontario rebate cheque, insurance renewal documents and income tax documents, all of which should have been received in the last 10 days. I was told that the mail was sitting at the work station of the regular delivery person. The casual person was brought in to deliver the voting cards.
This is the second such incident with non-delivery of mail since mail carriers went back to work in December.
To register a complaint, you can call Canada Post at 1-866-607-6301. You can also contact Canada Post online. Have as many details as possible about missing mail and dates/duration of non-delivery.
Judith Pyke, Hamilton
Housing is a complex issue
Re: The housing, homelessness bill keeps growing. Here's how Hamilton spends nearly $200 million, Feb. 24
I just finished reading the excellent article by Teviah Moro. This was the best, most understandable explanation of housing in all different areas and types of the city. As a former resident of Hamilton Housing Authority from many years ago, I thought I had a reasonable understanding of how it all worked. I was sadly mistaken. It is a difficult system for anyone who is underhoused or homeless to navigate. This a must-read for anyone seeking a place to live in today's housing market. Thank you for putting this complex issue in terms for everyone to understand.
Laurel Thompson, Hamilton
Housing questions still remain
Teviah Moro presented a comprehensive picture of how the city spends money on housing. By my calculation, based on the numbers in his article, of the $186 million spent last year, about $138.5 million went to subsidize folks in homes. As was pointed out, this is a good thing, both financially and, probably equally or more importantly, psychologically. However, the one thing I could not find in the article was for all the money spent, how many people went from being homeless to being "homed"? The only thing I saw in this regard was an agreement signed for $8.2 million toward 800 units of affordable housing by 2027. This works out to $10,250 per unit, so clearly there must be more money coming from elsewhere. It seems that there would be two objectives for spending the $186 million: one, keep people in their homes, and two, move people from not having some kind of permanent roof over their heads to having one.
Richard Ronchka, Carlisle
Get out and vote on Thursday
I would like to remind voters in Ontario about the results of the 2022 provincial election.
Voter turnout was about 43.5 per cent. Here are the parties' vote percentages (of all eligible voters): Progressive Conservative, 18 per cent; Liberal, 10 per cent; NDP, 10 per cent; "other," five per cent; "did not vote," 52 per cent. These results allowed the Progressive Conservatives to get a majority. We cannot have another Conservative majority. How do we get the 52 per cent who did not vote to vote? Let us remember the many bad decisions of the Conservatives. Please vote!
Roman Caruk, Hamilton
Attacks on Ford are hypocritical
I find it humorous that so many people are quick to point out that Doug Ford is a bully, but don't pass up the chance to troll and bully him and the Progressive Conservative Party as a whole on every platform available. None of what I read is constructive criticism, it is all rants and personal attacks. I guess the culture of divisive and rude entitlement is not limited to the south. Canadians have apparently removed the guardrails of acceptable free speech and dialogue.
Troy Smillie, Binbrook
Vote for the environment
The sign on my front lawn says, "Vote for the Environment." Unfortunately, the current Ontario provincial campaigns seem to have all but ignored voter concerns about the environment. The local chapter of Seniors for Climate Action Now hope Ontario voters, but especially seniors, will make thoughtful, pro-environment decisions when they enter the polling booths on Thursday. We hope voters will think of their children, grandchildren and future generations. What will their lives be like suffering through wildfires, heat waves, flooding and other unnatural disasters caused by climate change?
These so-called natural disasters are not natural at all and often ridiculously described as "once-in-a-lifetime events," but there are too many now to consider them exceptional. You would think this would spur governments and individuals to seriously and aggressively attend to our commitments under the Paris climate agreement; pursue and support renewable energy sources; and stop subsidizing the fossil fuel industry to the tune of over $6 billion each year or $214 for every taxpayer in Canada.
Yes, you might argue some governments have done some good in mitigating and adapting to climate change, but the urgency is sadly lacking. Unless, of course, there is a crisis such as the fire in Jasper, Alta., last summer. Once the fire was out with its shocking destruction, the finger pointing between the federal and provincial government was fierce. Both blamed the other rather than taking a serious look at root causes - namely, a warming planet.
Shelley Rempel, Ancaster
We know what Ford will bring
When growing up, whenever I was about to make a bad decision or to exercise poor judgment of Biblical proportions, my parents would say something like, "Well, go ahead, do what you will, but don't come crying to me when you realize your mistake." The same can be said for this provincial election. The only reason for this election is so that Doug Ford can get himself re-elected. The only true campaign issue is whether are we prepared to forgive Ford, and sign on for six more years of his Progressive Conservative government.
As an electorate, we have been warned. We know exactly what we're getting. We have seen the underfunding of health care and education. We have witnessed the erosion of municipal autonomy. We know where this government's priorities lie. So if the Conservatives win Thursday's election, as the polls would indicate, then it's "please don't come crying to me" if you can't find affordable long-term care for an aging parent, when another emergency ward is shuttered, when more farmland is transposed into urban sprawl or the deficit balloons to pay for the "401 Big Dig" and free licence plates.
We know what we're getting and we can do better.
Norman Brown, Hamilton
Don't reward PC Party
If Doug Ford wins this election, we will send the message that we are OK with an unnecessary snap election, that we don't mind our health-care system remains in crisis and that we support Ford's harsh response to the looming tariff which has increased tensions between the U.S. and Canada and even resulted in booing of each other's national anthems at sporting events. Instead of holding Ford accountable and choosing a new premier, we will reward him if he is re-elected. Simply put.
Tina Maini, Hamilton
CAPTION: Readers have been writing in wondering why their Canada Post mail delivery has seemingly come to a halt.

Document 13 of 500

Doug Ford's son-in-law facing Toronto police misconduct allegations of insubordination, sharing confidential information

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/doug-fords-son-law-facing-toronto-police/docview/3171483822/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Doug+Ford%27s+son-in-law+facing+Toronto+police+misconduct+allegations+of+insubordination%2C+sharing+confidential+information&amp;title=The+Hamilton+Spectator+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2025-02-25&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Hamilton+Spectator+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Premier Doug Ford's son-in-law is facing more than a dozen police misconduct allegations including that he forwarded dozens of emails detailing his policing duties - including one regarding a sexual assault investigation - from his work account to his wife.
Staff Sgt. Ernest (Dave) Haynes faces 15 non-criminal charges under the Police Services Act for discreditable conduct, insubordination and breach of confidence, according to notices of hearings provided by the tribunal. None of the allegations have been proven.
His wife, Ford's daughter Krista Ford Haynes, is identified only as "KF" in the documents provided by the tribunal.
The charges relate to Haynes allegedly expressing his opinion on policing matters, unsolicited, over email and social media, making posts on his personal Instagram in his service uniform and sharing confidential matters with his wife.
Haynes' lawyer did not respond to a voicemail or email requesting comment. Haynes did not respond to the Star's request for comment in a direct message to his Instagram account.
Ford Haynes did not respond to an email or direct message request for comment.
In a GoFundMe campaign to raise money for outside legal counsel, Ford Haynes said her husband "has been facing some serious allegations as a result of him trying to be transparent about a safe working environment." She goes on to say her husband was "sent home on unpaid leave" for seven months over the course of four years "for asking for more information about the ethicacy of a vaccine being forced upon it's members (sic)."
Haynes' latest lawyer Bath-Sheba van den Berg, previously represented members of the so-called Freedom Convoy at the Public Order Emergency Commission inquiry into the use of emergency measures during the pandemic.
The Toronto Police Association, which represents officers, typically provide legal counsel to their members at the tribunal, but officers can choose their external counsel at their own cost.
Haynes remains a staff sergeant - a supervisory role within the service - earning over $150,000 in 2023, according to the province's salary disclosure list. Haynes is not suspended and is on approved leave, a Toronto police spokesperson confirmed in an email.
The charges against Haynes are grouped into five separate notices of hearing.
The first relates to an allegation Haynes sent an unsolicited mass email to his colleagues in December 2023.
Haynes is alleged to have sent the email to everyone in central and south Etobicoke's 22 Division, where he was assigned, as well as some members of 31 Division in North York - a total of 246 people.
"In the email, you expressed your personal opinion relating to policing matters; you were critical of some of the Service's initiatives and direction taken in certain areas. This undermined the Chief's and Command's position," the notice of hearing says.
The second incident relates to a sexual assault investigation.
Also in December 2023, Haynes allegedly sent an email to senior officers, copying the sexual assault complainant.
"The email contained confidential police information including previous internal email correspondence relating to the matter," the notice of hearing said.
The nature of the comments was not disclosed.
The same email was forwarded to his wife and also a law student, none of whom, along with the complainant, were authorized to receive it, the notice alleges.
The third set of charges relates to a missing persons case.
Again in December 2023, the service alleges Haynes emailed a sergeant and staff sergeant at the London Police Service after he learned about comments an officer from that service made about a missing person who had been located and found wanted under warrant in London.
The nature of those comments was not disclosed.
It is then alleged Haynes forwarded that email to his wife.
The fourth group of charges relates to Haynes' Instagram account - a space where he recently posted he started to "humanize the badge". He has frequently shared critical views of public officials, COVID-19 mandates and the media while also posting content like workout videos.
The allegations relating to his social media relate to several specific posts.
In one from February 2024, Haynes is alleged to have disclosed police business by discussing a complaint made against him.
In others, it's alleged Haynes can be seen in his police uniform on what is a personal account, which the service says is contrary to its social media procedure.
The final complaint contains allegations Haynes sent 79 emails from his work account to his wife between November 2020 and February 2024, the majority of which "had some direct connection" to his official policing duties.
There is no indication in the tribunal documents that email correspondence was shared with any of Haynes' other family members, including the premier. When asked if the emails were ever shared with Ford, an unidentified spokesperson for the Ontario PC Party simply responded: "No."
Haynes is next scheduled to be before the tribunal in April.
CAPTION: Recent posts on Dave Haynes's Instagram page.Krista Ford Haynes and her husband Dave are raising money for their legal bills through Gofundme.
CREDIT: Jennifer Pagliaro

Document 14 of 500

How to make a difference in an Ontario election that may change nothing: My guide for voters

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/how-make-difference-ontario-election-that-may/docview/3171483814/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=How+to+make+a+difference+in+an+Ontario+election+that+may+change+nothing%3A+My+guide+for+voters&amp;title=The+Hamilton+Spectator+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2025-02-25&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Hamilton+Spectator+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: A vote is a terrible thing to waste.
Which is why today's column is a concise voter's guide to making your vote matter more on election day this Thursday.
Politicians keep telling us that campaigns matter. Judging by the dismal 44 per cent turnout in the last provincial election, most Ontarians disagree - and are disengaged.
In my previous life as a foreign correspondent, I spent 11 years in Asia and the Middle East writing about people who were willing to die for democracy.
So when I hear people here at home complain about local democracy, I feel a kind of post-democratic stress disorder. Giving up on democracy is giving in to apathy, yes, but it is more than that.
It is not just a dereliction of duty, it is a delegation of authority - empowering others while disenfranchising you. Refusing to choose is itself a decision to let others decide for you.
That's not to say choosing is easy. For it is harder than ever to discern the differences among political parties, personalities and policies.
So let me try to explain why campaigns count - and how every vote can count in its own way.
What about comparing policies?
If you listen to NDP Leader Marit Stiles and Liberal Leader Bonnie Crombie, the election is all about hallway health care, doctor's shortages, housing shortfalls, affordability and a scandal-plagued Progressive Conservative government. If you heed PC Leader Doug Ford, it's all about affordability, fending off Donald Trump's tariffs and defending the economy - the better to bankroll health care and education.
The problem for Crombie and Stiles is that health care rarely rises to the level of a vote-determining issue in Canadian politics. Polls consistently show that Ontarians believe Ford's Tories are best-placed to tackle economic issues, including Trump's tariffs - the decisive ballot question for most voters.
Election platforms were once a big deal in Canadian politics, but the days of fully-costed plans are past. The big hole in the latest PC platform is Ford's promise to dig a tunnel under the 401, which is buried deep in the document without a price tag (experts estimate it easily would cost more than $100 billion).
New Democrats have countered on traffic congestion by vowing to remove all tolls from Highway 407 - but won't disclose the multibillion-dollar cost. Liberals have also promised billions of dollars in tax cuts without saying how they'd make up for lost revenues.
And so, in the age of endless social media feeds and short attention spans, every platform looks like a meme with one recurring theme - pocketbook politics. Now, all three major parties are awkwardly aligned on tax cuts, toll cuts and fee cuts while magically boosting government services and supports.
The leaders held back their putative platforms until the campaign's final days, leaving voters less time to read up because none of it adds up. It is the banality and vanity of small differences.
So what's the campaign really about?
This election is less a policy debate and more a personality contest. Ford has grown on Ontarians, partly because he has grown up - he's less bombastic than before and more folksy; less chippy and more chipper.
That said, if you do the math, the surveys show a theoretical majority of voters are opposed to Ford's Tories. So why does he keep winning?
Historically, Ford's opponents couldn't agree on the opposition party best placed to displace him. Centrist and left-leaning voters split their votes among rival "progressive" parties - Liberals, New Democrats and Greens - allowing Tories to come up the middle.
How can votes count more?
Electoral reform won't happen anytime soon in Ford's Ontario. Rival parties won't form a common front or come together because they are too tribal.
The only way to catch the front-runner is for progressive voters to back the horse with the best chance of catching up.
New Democrats keep insisting only they have what it takes, because they won more seats in the last two elections.
All they needed was more Liberal voters swinging behind them.
Yet that's precisely what happened in 2018 and 2022, when voters put the Liberal dynasty in the penalty box after 15 years in power. Voters swung over to the NDP, yet the result was two missed opportunities for the opposition.
Even when the stars were in alignment for New Democrats, they hit a ceiling. They won the consolation prize of "Official Opposition" (with extra legislative funding), while the Liberals languished. But what does the NDP have to show for two terms of supposedly holding the Tories to account?
Today, Ford is coasting to re-election as premier, notwithstanding his blunders on housing, health care and education. Meanwhile, the Liberals have leapfrogged ahead of the NDP as their brand rebounds.
No matter how many times New Democrats introduce Stiles as "the next premier of Ontario," it is a delusion wrapped in an illusion inside an election. Unable to defeat Ford in 2018, 2022 and now 2025, how many more chances does the NDP deserve?
The Liberals, like it or not, have a fighting chance - if not now, then in future. Since becoming leader, Crombie has not exactly caught fire but she is catching up to Ford after running a credible campaign, hovering at 30 per cent in most polls compared to 20 per cent or less for the NDP.
Her party is strongest in the GTA and Ottawa, but weak elsewhere. The Liberal party she took over in late 2023 is starting far behind in the seat count.
And yet the fact that her Liberals are weak in many rural ridings and the southwest is not a reason to abandon them, but all the more reason to shore up their base - so they can rebuild for the future. For unlike the NDP, Liberals are not condemned to the purgatory of perpetual opposition.
In truth, Ontario politics today has more to do with geography than ideology, with each party relying on a regional power base and erratic pockets of support. The NDP that once boasted about being the conscience of parliament acts more like a boutique party that has become a boon for Ford's Tories.
I don't expect diehard New Democrats to defect, even if their cause is doomed. It's their democratic right to elect another right-wing government by default.
But when interested voters ask me who can defeat Ford's Tories, I tell them that every election offers a choice - even if they don't like the options. It's a hard question worth asking, even if you don't like the answer.
This election is not about who will be premier, for that is preordained by Ford's cynically clever timing. It's about the race for second place - and who can do a better job of holding Ford's feet to the fire after the last seven years of lukewarm opposition that laid the groundwork for another four years of PC government.
That's democracy.
CAPTION: An Ontario voter make his way to an advance polling station in Toronto, on Feb. 20, 2025.
CREDIT: Martin Regg Cohn
