Document 15 of 500

Replied 'Stop' but still getting political texts from Doug Ford or others during Ontario election campaign? You're not alone

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/replied-stop-still-getting-political-texts-doug/docview/3170918011/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Replied+%27Stop%27+but+still+getting+political+texts+from+Doug+Ford+or+others+during+Ontario+election+campaign%3F+You%27re+not+alone&amp;title=The+Hamilton+Spectator+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2025-02-25&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Hamilton+Spectator+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Love them or hate them, text messages have become a key part of provincial and federal elections in Canada.
One of the most common messages Ontario voters have discovered comes from the Ontario PC Party.
It reads: "It's Doug Ford. Help me stand up to Donald Trump to protect our jobs and economy."
A Metroland reader said in an email they have received more than a dozen campaign text messages since early February.
The reader said they have replied "stop" but continue to receive the texts.
Metroland reached out to the Elections Ontario, the Canadian Radio-television and Telecommunications Commission (CRTC) and the Information and Privacy Commissioner of Ontario to find out what Ontario residents can do about unwanted political texts.
What Elections Ontario says
Elections Ontario media relations co-ordinator Dave Pearce said Ontario's provincial election laws do not prohibit campaign telephone calls or text messages. This includes individual calls and texts as well as the practice of "robocalling."
"Robocalling" is considered a legitimate campaign activity, and political entities can communicate with voters using automated phone calls or by hiring call centre agents during the campaign period, Pearce noted.
"If the email, text message, or phone call you received violated election law, directed you to the wrong voting location or impersonated a candidate, political party, or Elections Ontario, you may submit a formal complaint to Elections Ontario in writing by mail, fax or email," Pearce explained.
If you are registered on the National Do Not Call List, you may still receive calls from political parties and candidates, Pearce noted.
Pearce added Elections Ontario is a trusted source for accurate election information.
"If you have any questions or come across something you're unsure about, visit us at elections.on.ca for the facts you can trust," he added.
What the CRTC says
The CRTC is an independent quasi-judicial tribunal that regulates the Canadian communications sector in the public interest.
CRTC spokesperson Megan MacLean said political parties are exempt from unsolicited telecommunications rules. As a result, individuals who have registered with the National Do Not Call List may still receive calls from political parties and candidates. Individuals who receive solicitation requests (requesting campaign donations, for example) from a political party or candidate can ask to be added to their internal do-not-call list.
"Similarly, text messages or emails asking for your support for a political party or candidate or requesting your opinion on various issues are not subject to Canada's Anti-Spam Legislation, whether during an election period or not, as they are typically not commercial in nature," MacLean noted.
What Ontario's Information and Privacy Commissioner says
Ontario's information and privacy commissioner (IPC) previously told Metroland that political parties and candidates are not subject to Ontario's access and privacy laws.
The IPC's office noted it has previously recommended any future private sector privacy laws in Ontario include political parties.
"Advances in technology allow for the collection and use of large amounts of personal information, raising privacy and security concerns," the IPC said in a statement. "Ensuring strong privacy protections supports public confidence in how personal information is handled."
CAPTION: Political parties may send unsolicited text messages during the course of a political campaign. You can ignore them, respond to them or report them as spam.
CREDIT: Mike Pearson

Subject: Provincial elections; Do not call lists; Personal information; Election law; Privacy; Candidates; Voters; Elections; Political parties

All four provincial party leaders have now put forth plans to support Ontario through U.S. President Donald Trump's tariff plans along with pledges to improve affordability, build schools and more.

Bonnie Crombie to endorse Mark Carney as federal Liberal leader

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/bonnie-crombie-endorse-mark-carney-as-federal/docview/3170778922/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Bonnie+Crombie+to+endorse+Mark+Carney+as+federal+Liberal+leader&amp;title=The+Hamilton+Spectator+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2025-02-25&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Hamilton+Spectator+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Bonnie Crombie is endorsing Mark Carney to be federal Liberal leader, the Star has learned.
The Ontario Liberal chief will make it official Tuesday in Toronto, sources say.
Carney, the former governor of the Bank of Canada and the Bank of England, is the front-runner to succeed Prime Minister Justin Trudeau in the March 9 leadership contest.
"Bonnie will endorse Mark Carney ... citing their agreement on the need for leaders who have the right priorities at this critical moment," a senior provincial Grit confided Monday.
Last June, Carney held a Bay Street fundraiser for Crombie and the two are to meet before Thursday's Ontario election "to discuss the economy and health care," the source said, speaking confidentially in order to discuss internal deliberations.
At the Star's editorial board on Friday, the provincial Liberal leader, whose own polling had been hindered by Trudeau's unpopularity, said Carney's rise has been "helpful" to her candidates.
"I think the renewal of leadership is also helpful. I mean, people want to engage at the doors. 'Do you think Carney will win on one ballot?' They hear that we're Liberals and they want to engage," said Crombie.
"Before the renewal, they didn't want to engage so much," she admitted.
Her endorsement comes as Carney debates rivals Chrystia Freeland, Karina Gould and Frank Baylis Monday and Tuesday in Montreal.
At the Star's editorial board Monday, provincial NDP Leader Marit Stiles was asked about the travails of the federal New Democrats.
With federal NDP Leader Jagmeet Singh languishing in the national polls, some New Democrats have privately expressed concern that might have an impact on the official opposition party at Queen's Park.
But Stiles said that Crombie's move to the right of former Liberal premier Kathleen Wynne has helped her party because there's still widespread support for left-leaning politics.
"I push back a little on that," she said. "I don't see that at all. A lot of what Crombie and the Liberals have been trying to do is take a lot of our policy and they are running on that."
Stile predicted progressive voters will see through that on Thursday and reject Crombie's move to the centre.
"What I'm finding is that a lot of previous Liberal voters here in Ontario are not interested in that."
While polls have suggested Doug Ford's Progressive Conservatives have benefitted from a halo effect from Pierre Poilievre's federal Conservatives in the past, the provincial Tories say Carney's surge in public opinion surveys has changed that.
Ford's PCs now believe they can win more Ontario ridings Thursday than Poilievre's party will win here in a federal election expected in spring.
CAPTION: Federal Liberal leadership candidate Mark Carney and Ontario Liberal Leader Bonnie Crombie appear at a fundraiser in Toronto in June 2024.
CREDIT: Robert Benzie, Kristin Rushowy

Subject: Leadership; Voters; Public opinion surveys; Elections

Document 18 of 500

For-profit health care isn't worth the cost

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/profit-health-care-isnt-worth-cost/docview/3170778908/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=unknown&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=For-profit+health+care+isn%27t+worth+the+cost&amp;title=The+Spectator&amp;issn=11899417&amp;date=2025-02-25&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.10&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Spectator&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: After a slow start, the major players in the Ontario election have finally released their game plans for improving primary care. As a family doctor, who is regularly asked if I'm taking on new patients (and grudgingly must decline), I was relieved to see that every party has committed to at least some of what is needed.
Partial solutions, however, are not enough. Especially when some of the largest corporations in Canada are starting to turn the primary care crisis into profit.
First, the good news.
We've known for a long time that expanding team-based primary care makes sense, not only to improve access for patients but to ensure higher quality of care, and to provide better conditions for the health-care professionals working in them.
Each of the major parties in Ontario have committed to making this the cornerstone of expansion, including a $1.8-billion commitment by the incumbent Progressive Conservatives, $4.05 billion by the NDP and $3.1 billion promised by the Liberals. The Association of Family Health Teams of Ontario recommends just over $4 billion of investments over five years, over and above any possible changes to family doctor remuneration.
Each party has also promised several other measures, including reducing the nearly 20 hours of paperwork a week family doctors are saddled with and incentives to bring doctors who have left primary care back into the fold.
The scope of the problem and delayed action are reflected by the substantial but necessary commitments required to reimagine care. Many of the building blocks are already in place, including existing models of team-based Family Health Teams and Community Health Centres. Expanding them and ensuring they work in harmony is no small task.
This is also very achievable. In fact, other countries with high-performing primary care have shown us the way. They've made sure care is technologically integrated, socially accountable, place-based much like schools and school boards, and available after hours. In fact, they've been so successful that walk-in clinics are almost non-existent - imagine that! We need to follow.
The urgency for this type of change is only heightened by external threats. Plans to expand "patient first" care are coming up against the increasing privatization of primary care from unexpected sources: grocers and telecom companies like Loblaw and Telus and lesser known private-equity investment firms.
Defenders of corporatizing primary care often say that if care is publicly funded, it doesn't really matter whether or not the profit motive is at play.
Evidence and experience tell us otherwise.
For-profit care, even when publicly funded, leads to higher costs and worse patient outcomes. For-profit clinics are incentivized to prioritize people who are wealthier and healthy, less expensive to treat, and provide larger profit margins. In for-profit settings, patients are more likely to be asked to pay out of pocket for ancillary or "uninsured" services.
Sometimes, that publicly funded care is inappropriately made contingent on paying these out-of-pocket fees.
In fact, in Ontario, we've very recently seen many of these trends play out in both for-profit cataract surgery, as well as long-term care. In its most extreme cases, publicly funded for-profit care can even be enabled by political corruption, as is being investigated in Alberta and has been previously alleged in Ontario.
These differences matter, not only because of the scale and scope of the primary care crisis, but because choices being made today can set the course for the next few decades.
Aspiring premiers must lead with funding commitments conditioned on not-for-profit care, with the necessary support to spread and scale team-based care as quickly as possible.
To ensure the sustainable system of a reimagined system of care, leaders must also lead with Ontario's regulatory power to prevent for-profit primary care from becoming the new normal.
Like any winning strategy, our next government's game plan needs to combine a potent offence, with dependable defence.
Dr. Danyaal Raza is a family physician at St. Michael's
Hospital, Unity Health Toronto and past chair of
Canadian Doctors for Medicare.
CAPTION:

CREDIT: Dr. Danyaal Raza Dr. Danyaal Raza is a family physician at St. Michael's Hospital, Unity Health Toronto and past chair of Canadian Doctors for Medicare.;

Subject: Patients; Primary care; Profit margins; Teams; Medicare; Physicians

Document 19 of 500

Doug Ford has taken a wrecking ball to Hamilton health care

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/doug-ford-has-taken-wrecking-ball-hamilton-health/docview/3170778652/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Doug+Ford+has+taken+a+wrecking+ball+to+Hamilton+health+care&amp;title=The+Hamilton+Spectator+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2025-02-25&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Hamilton+Spectator+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: About 40 years ago, Tommy Douglas warned we must protect public health care from "subtle strangulation."
In Doug Ford's anything-but-subtle strangulation of Ontario's health care, his government has openly "take a wrecking ball" to Hamilton's.
Recently, the Canadian Union of Public Employees revealed Hamilton's largest hospital network, Hamilton Health Sciences, is projecting a deficit of $112 million, with St. Joseph's Healthcare projecting one of $24.3 million, for the fiscal year in which Ford hopes to seize another victory. While designating $3 billion for rebate cheques and tunnels, Ford has not addressed hospital deficits, instead playing "hide-and-seek with hospital funding."
Ontario's public hospital funding, as well as its overall health-care spending, are the lowest per capita of any province. As a result, Hamilton hospitals (and others) face these dramatic shortfalls in funding.
With 85 cents of every health-care dollar spent on staffing, Hamilton Health Sciences is eliminating staff positions and pulling job postings to attempt to reduce this enormous deficit, leaving critical front-line positions vacant. Nearly half of the remaining, exhausted staff are now considering quitting their jobs. Meanwhile, patients suffer on stretchers in hallways in Hamilton hospitals and wait, on average, 21 hours in Hamilton emergency rooms. Ford promised to fix this crisis in 2021, then allowed it to double in severity.
It's not just Hamilton. Some 2.5 million people in this province have no family doctor. After Ford capped wages of nurses and health professionals, thousands left due to overwork and burnout. Ontario now has the fewest nurses per capita of any province.
Your father may be waiting, along with 2,000 others, on that hallway stretcher for days because Ontario has the fewest hospital beds in Canada. Your mother's surgery may be delayed, along with 250,000 on wait lists, because provincial operating rooms are not adequately staffed and therefore under-used. Your grandparent may be one of Ontario's palliative patients who die at home without painkillers.
The starvation of public health care by Ford, is not only widespread but deliberate. Ford has always been supportive of for-profit U.S.-style health care. While disabling the public system he has presented himself as Ontario's rescuer, offering so-called "alternatives" in privatized clinics (carefully described as "independent health facilities"), repeating the fiction that private health care reduces wait times and that will help clear the backlogs he created.
Ford knows this isn't true, which is why he's been so coy. Before the 2022 election, Ford assured voters he would not expand private hospitals. Immediately following that election, he announced the expansion of privatized surgeries and diagnostics. As he publicly promised "Ontarians will always access health care with their OHIP card," patients were extra-billed thousands for privatized surgeries, and hundreds for medically unnecessary tests.
Instead of halting these illegal practices, as promised, Ford increased funding to private clinics by more than 200 per cent. The result has been gross wastage of public dollars (your taxes). Much more is spent for every surgery in privatized clinics, compared to those in public hospitals. Public hospitals have been forced to hire high-cost nursing staff from private agencies for which spending has increased sixfold since 2020.
While positioning himself as a defender of Canada's values, Ford - in the shadow of the 2026 trade negotiations in which the defence of Medicare will be central - is planning more for-profit wasteful health care.
Hamilton hospitals have always punched above their weight in the delivery of superlative health care. Ford has been a disaster for that proud legacy. On Feb. 27, Hamiltonians must heed the warning of Tommy Douglas, and vote as if our lives depend on it.
CAPTION: Ontario's public hospital funding, as well as its overall health-care spending, are the lowest per capita of any province. As a result, hospitals face dramatic shortfalls in funding,Dr. Nancy Olivieri, Michael Hurley and Natalie Mehra write.
CREDIT: Dr. Nancy Olivieri, Michael Hurley and Natalie Mehra

Subject: Hospitals; Public health; Health sciences; Strangulation; Health care policy; Funding; Per capita

Document 20 of 500

Without reform, election is just a game

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/without-reform-election-is-just-game/docview/3170771597/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=unknown&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Without+reform%2C+election+is+just+a+game&amp;title=The+Spectator&amp;issn=11899417&amp;date=2025-02-25&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.11&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Spectator&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Ontario goes to the polls on Thursday. If you're reading this, you're probably well aware of the fact.
I would bet that you like to keep yourself informed not only of the news, but also of opinion as well. It's a sure bet since you're reading the newspaper and this is an opinion column. I only make sure bets. So, barring any kind of unforeseen situation, like another major snowfall, chances are you'll vote in this election. Right?
I'm not making any bets on this election. I never do anymore. Not after the surprise 1990 NDP win under Bob Rae, a shock many still haven't gotten over.
I don't give much credence to polls except to consider them as election interference. The tail wagging the dog sort of thing. I believe people are swayed by them and I question their integrity, like I would of anyone who attempts to predict the future. There is always the unpredictable in human nature. Polls can be wrong.
I'm a firm believer the only poll that matters is the one on election day, where people enter into a private moment between themselves and their conscience to cast their vote for good governance and fair representation. That's where the promise of democracy lies. Up until then, anything is possible.
Voting is more than a civic responsibility. We have an ethical obligation to participate in our own governing and how we, via the state, respond to the needs within our communities, particularly as they relate to food and shelter. Climate change is making that very apparent.
With this election, I feel like we're playing a game, one controlled by publicists and pollsters all brokering promises for power. The people are the pawns they sacrifice in their game of chess, providing relief then taking it away, like the Ford government did with the basic income pilot. That truly hurt the Hamilton community.
I spent the past week watching the Cable 14 debates for all the Hamilton ridings. I want to thank all the candidates who showed up to debate their opponents. Your commitment to a fair democratic process is the foundation on which our freedoms lie.
I go into greater detail on my Substack, but what I want to share here is a general feeling I had of an "Us Versus Them" attitude in Ontario politics and it was very evident in the debates.
The "Us" being the Liberals, NDP and Greens along with the general population via the Hamilton media - that is, The Spec, the CBC and CHCH, who showed up with questions. The "Them" being the Progressive Conservatives, where four out of five candidates skipped their debates with lame excuses. If they can't get it together to have a working email address for the press to contact, or understand the importance of these debates to the community, are they really the right person for the job? It's not a big ask.
However, as I watched, I got annoyed with the Us side. The Us side is all on the same page, with very minor variations. Increase housing, increase social assistance rates, protect the Greenbelt, fund education and fix health care. Yet the Us side is being asked to split itself into three, rather than join forces for a bigger and greater Us side with a collaborative majority that could effect real change.
People are dying because the Us side can't get its act together to share power. It's pathetic.
We need real change in our electoral process. If the Us side joined forces for one election, they could implement proportional representation and then everyone's vote would count. Voters wouldn't have to bet on strategic voting in a gamed system. Regardless of the outcome of this election, democracy will die without reform.
If we can't vote on what's in our hearts and if we have to strategically play a game to ensure we don't get bad governance, then the system is broken.
One more thing: watch the debates, make up your own mind.
Margaret Shkimba is a Hamilton writer.
For more visit margaretshkimba.Substack.com. You can follow me on X (@menrvasofia),
@margaretshkimba.bsky.social on Bluesky.
CAPTION: If the Liberals, NDP and Greens joined forces for one election, they could implement proportional representation and then everyone's vote would count, Margaret Shkimba writes.Giordano Ciampini The Canadian Press

CREDIT: Margaret Shkimba OPINION Margaret Shkimba is a Hamilton writer. For more visit margaretshkimba.Substack.com. You can follow me on X (@menrvasofia), @margaretshkimba.bsky.social on Bluesky.;

Document 21 of 500

Without reform, elections are just a game

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/without-reform-elections-are-just-game/docview/3170771287/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Without+reform%2C+elections+are+just+a+game&amp;title=The+Hamilton+Spectator+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2025-02-25&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Hamilton+Spectator+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Ontario goes to the polls on Thursday. If you're reading this, you're probably well aware of the fact.
I would bet that you like to keep yourself informed not only of the news, but also of opinion as well. It's a sure bet since you're reading the newspaper and this is an opinion column. I only make sure bets. So, barring any kind of unforeseen situation, like another major snowfall, chances are you'll vote in this election. Right?
I'm not making any bets on this election. I never do anymore. Not after the surprise 1990 NDP win under Bob Rae, a shock many still haven't gotten over.
I don't give much credence to polls except to consider them as election interference. The tail wagging the dog sort of thing. I believe people are swayed by them and I question their integrity, like I would of anyone who attempts to predict the future. There is always the unpredictable in human nature. Polls can be wrong.
I'm a firm believer the only poll that matters is the one on election day, where people enter into a private moment between themselves and their conscience to cast their vote for good governance and fair representation. That's where the promise of democracy lies. Up until then, anything is possible.
Voting is more than a civic responsibility. We have an ethical obligation to participate in our own governing and how we, via the state, respond to the needs within our communities, particularly as they relate to food and shelter. Climate change is making that very apparent.
With this election, I feel like we're playing a game, one controlled by publicists and pollsters all brokering promises for power. The people are the pawns they sacrifice in their game of chess, providing relief then taking it away, like the Ford government did with the basic income pilot. That truly hurt the Hamilton community.
I spent the past week watching the Cable 14 debates for all the Hamilton ridings. I want to thank all the candidates who showed up to debate their opponents. Your commitment to a fair democratic process is the foundation on which our freedoms lie.
I go into greater detail on my Substack, but what I want to share here is a general feeling I had of an "Us vs Them" attitude in Ontario politics and it was very evident in the debates.
The "Us" being the Liberals, NDP and Greens along with the general population via the Hamilton media - that is, The Spec, the CBC and CHCH, who showed up with questions. The "Them" being the Conservatives, where four out of five candidates skipped their debates with lame excuses. If they can't get it together to have a working email address for the press to contact, or understand the importance of these debates to the community, are they really the right person for the job? It's not a big ask.
However, as I watched, I got annoyed with the Us side. The Us side is all on the same page, with very minor variations. Increase housing, increase social assistance rates, protect the Greenbelt, fund education and fix health care. Yet the Us side is being asked to split itself into three, rather than join forces for a bigger and greater Us side with a collaborative majority that could effect real change.
People are dying because the Us side can't get its act together to share power. It's pathetic.
We need real change in our electoral process. If the Us side joined forces for one election, they could implement proportional representation and then everyone's vote would count. Voters wouldn't have to bet on strategic voting in a gamed system. Regardless of the outcome of this election, democracy will die without reform.
If we can't vote on what's in our hearts, if we have to strategically play a game to ensure we don't get bad governance then the system is broken.
One more thing: watch the debates, make up your own mind.
CAPTION: If the Liberals, NDP and Greens joined forces for one election, they could implement proportional representation and then everyone's vote would count, Margaret Shkimba writes.
CREDIT: Margaret Shkimba

Document 22 of 500

Voters can now compare promises ahead of election day: All four major political parties have released their platforms

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/voters-can-now-compare-promises-ahead-election/docview/3170771279/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Voters+can+now+compare+promises+ahead+of+election+day%3A+All+four+major+political+parties+have+released+their+platforms&amp;title=The+Spectator&amp;issn=11899417&amp;date=2025-02-25&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.1&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Spectator&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Voting day for Ontario's snap election is fast approaching Thursday - but only now can voters finally compare the full slate of campaign promises from each of Ontario's major political parties.
The Tories were the last party to unveil a formal election platform Monday, following releases by the Liberals and NDP last week and the early-bird Greens in mid-February.
The documents contain many pledges - but not necessarily cost estimates - to deal with issues ranging from affordability to health care to looming tariffs. There are links to the full party platforms below - but also highlighted promises on six issues of particular interest to Hamilton voters.
Homelessness
There are more than 1,600 homeless residents in Hamilton, including 250 known to be living outside - often in contentious tent encampments. About 6,100 households are on a waiting list for social housing.
PC (ontariopc.ca/our-plan)
The Tories have earmarked $75.5 million for programs to help house those living in encampments. The party is also planning legislation to beef up trespass and drug-use enforcement in parks and to add to the Building Ontario Fund, which prioritizes affordable housing projects.
NDP (ontariondp.ca/platform)
The party is vowing to build at least 300,000 "permanently affordable" homes, as well as 60,000 supportive units with wraparound care for the homeless. They've also promised better rent control.
Liberal (ontarioliberal.ca - click 'policy' tab)
The Liberals promise new supportive-housing units, "phased-in" rent control, clearing the Landlord and Tenant Board backlog and establishing a provincial emergency fund to help prevent evictions.
Green (gpo.ca/platform)
The Greens want to work with non-profits to build 250,000 new affordable homes and 60,000 spaces in supportive housing, along with more funding for shelters for women and families.
LRT housing
Hamilton's LRT project is buying and demolishing dozens of buildings along its lower-city route - forcing many tenants to relocate. With that in mind, the city has lobbied the province to hand over any leftover provincial lands along the line for new affordable housing.
PC
Doug Ford's Tories have repeatedly said they're open to discussing possible uses for leftover LRT properties, but have yet to make any specific public commitments.
NDP
The party once headed by Hamilton Mayor Andrea Horwath says it backs the idea of using remnant LRT properties for affordable housing.
Liberal
In an interview, party leader Bonnie Crombie said the province should be using surplus public land to create new affordable housing or long-term-care facilities.
Green
By email, the party said it believes the province should make surplus or underutilized land available to municipalities to build affordable housing, particularly on transit lines.
Long-term care
There were close to 2,000 Hamilton seniors languishing on waiting lists for long-term care in December, with waits stretching as long as five years.
PC
The Tories pledged to continue a $6.4-billion plan to build 58,000 new and upgraded long-term-care beds by 2028. The Spectator has also reported on a Mountain brow sale of provincial land meant to create a new LTC complex - but the deal remains unannounced and its status unclear.
NDP
The party has vowed to "stop privatization" of long-term care, work to ensure people can live longer at home and reverse Bill 7, a 2022 law allowing hospital officials to choose a long-term-care home for a patient without consent.
Liberal
The Liberals say they'll boost funding for home care, repeal Bill 7 and create a tax credit for seniors that allows them to save up to $10,000 in medical expenses a year to help them age at home.
Green
The Greens promise to "phase out" for-profit care, build 48,000 non-profit LTC spaces by 2029 and create smaller, "person-centred" care homes as well as more Indigenous-led, rural and remote LTC spaces.
Transit
The city's off-and-on-again LRT is finally moving forward - but the city has called on upper levels of government to "expedite" the project as a tariff-fighting stimulus project. Hamilton is also seeking funding help to push ahead with a long-term plan to reroute and grow its bus system.
PC
The Tory government announced last year that Hamilton's LRT - which it cancelled, then resurrected - is going to tender. The party is also vowing ongoing GO train improvements through Hamilton to Niagara.
NDP
The party has promised to upload half the cost of transit operating costs for cities. (In Hamilton, the city pays about $108 million a year to run transit.) It will also improve GO services through to Niagara.
Liberal
The party says it will improve GO train service through Hamilton to Niagara and "expedite" major rapid transit projects like Hamilton's LRT.
Green
The party vows to prioritize transit over highways and to upload transit costs once covered by the province, but now paid by local taxpayers.
Urban boundaries
City politicians say their vow to "freeze" Hamilton's urban boundary and grow up, rather than out is again under threat - this time from changes to provincial law and policy that make it easier for developers to apply for urban boundary expansions and harder for the city to say no.
PC
The Tory government famously backtracked in 2023 on an unpopular urban boundary expansion in Hamilton. But it has since introduced new legislation allowing developers to apply for such expansions at any time - and appeal to an unelected tribunal if council says "no." The province has said the changes were meant to beef up housing supply and ensure "balanced" consideration of development applications.
NDP
The party's platform says a NDP government commits to "focusing growth sustainably and cost-effectively within the pre-2022 urban boundaries," which it says will keep infrastructure costs down.
Liberal
The party platform pledges to "protect prime farmland." In an interview, Crombie said the province must be more "consultative" with municipal partners and respect local decision-making powers.
Green
The Green platform pledges to support frozen urban boundaries and reverse provincial policy changes that encourage sprawl. It pledges to incentivize new housing specifically within existing urban footprints.
Supervised
consumption sites
Under new provincial rules, two Hamilton supervised drug-consumption sites will be forced to close at the end of March, which critics argue will lead to a spike in overdose deaths.
PC
The Tories say they will enforce a ban on safe drug-consumption sites near schools and child-care facilities, but also spend $530 million creating new addiction and recovery hubs.
NDP
The NDP platform promises to fund mental-health providers to support people with addictions, and "use evidence-based strategies to address the opioid epidemic." They also vow to fund detox and residential treatment beds.
Liberal
In a release last year, Crombie said supervised drug-consumption sites should not be near schools. The party wants to bring opioid overdose deaths "down to zero in four years" through expanded treatment and rehabilitation.
Green
The Greens promise to reopen safe consumption sites closed by the Tories and add new sites across the province.
Matthew Van Dongen is
a reporter at The Spectator. mvandongen@thespec.com
Kate McCullough is
a reporter at The Spectator.
kmccullough@thespec.com
CAPTION: Tent encampments are a visible reminder of a big issue in the 2025 provincial election: homelessness and the unaffordable cost of housing.City politicians says Hamilton's frozen urban boundary is again under threat due to provincial legislation.Hamilton Spectator file photoThe Hamilton Spectator file photo

CREDIT: Kate McCullough and Matthew Van Dongen Reporters Matthew Van Dongen is a reporter at The Spectator. mvandongen@thespec.com Kate McCullough is a reporter at The Spectator. kmccullough@thespec.com

Subject: Provincial elections; Drug overdose; Legislation; Long term health care; Voters; Affordable housing; Funding; Politics; Homeless people; Cost estimates; Narcotics; Rent control

Document 23 of 500

Is life any better today than in 2018?

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/is-life-any-better-today-than-2018/docview/3170771241/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=unknown&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Is+life+any+better+today+than+in+2018%3F&amp;title=The+Spectator&amp;issn=11899417&amp;date=2025-02-25&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.10&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Spectator&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Set aside the unforgiving winter weather that could affect voter turnout, with some of the hardest-hit regions receiving over 50 centimetres of snow last week.
Set aside the fact that the provincial election was originally scheduled for June 4, 2026. And finally, set aside the fact that the RCMP is conducting a criminal investigation into Doug Ford's Greenbelt scandal.
The one question we, as Ontarians, should ask ourselves is this: Are our lives better today than they were in 2018?
Since taking office almost seven years ago, our health-care system is in shambles. The premier gleefully boasted about spending billions on health care during last week's debate, yet, time and again, horror stories of patients waiting for care in emergency departments - with some not making it out alive - have become far too common across towns and cities. In fact, one in five hospitals with an emergency room experienced unforeseen closures in 2024, making it one of the worst years for ER shutdowns ever.
Don't take my word for it. Look to the government's own findings. Internal documents from last year detailed that 10 per cent of patients admitted to Ontario hospitals in the emergency department had to wait a minimum of two days before finally being assigned a bed.
Furthermore, this issue is compounded by 2.5 million people lacking access to a family doctor, according to the Ontario College of Family Physicians. Instead of regular, proactive care that could prevent emergency visits, many rely on walk-in clinics or require travelling 50 kilometres or more to see a family doctor, if they are lucky enough to have one.
While the health-care system suffers under Ford's leadership, our province is gripped with another pressing provincial crisis: housing.
Contrary to popular belief, this issue is primarily the responsibility of provincial governments, although throughout Canada's history, it has been a shared duty with federal authorities.
Presently, Ontario ranks as the second most expensive province to rent, only behind B.C. Despite a modest decline of around five per cent, the average asking price for apartments is $2,329 as of January 2025, per Rentals.ca.
In the same month, the average price of a single-family home was $942,700. Is it any wonder, then, that eight in 10 Canadians believe home ownership is only for the rich, as revealed by a 2024 Ipsos poll? Hmm, I wonder.
The truth is, Ontarians are hurting. Life's most basic necessities, most notably housing and health care, have only worsened since 2018. Ford has had plenty of time to make our province a decent place to live and work.
He's failed.
We need a seismic shift from the status quo and a serious look at alternative parties to provide the means for that shift. Some voters may be reluctant to consider the NDP and Liberals because of Bob Rae and Kathleen Wynne, but it's important to remember that Marit Stiles isn't Bob Rae and Bonnie Crombie isn't Kathleen Wynne.
And, to be fair, any future leader of the Progressive Conservative Party should not, ideally, be tethered to Ford.
The last provincial election's turnout was abysmal, with only 43.5 per cent of eligible voters exercising their right to vote (a historic record low). Younger generations, particularly Gen Z and millennials, are especially vulnerable to the housing crisis and stand to benefit the most from meaningful change.
The stakes are too high to sit this one out - take it to the voting booth on Thursday, Feb. 27. Vote.
Alan Joseph is a political commentator who offers analysis on a wide range of issues. See more at
substack.com/
@aljocommentary.
CAPTION:

CREDIT: Alan Joseph Alan Joseph is a political commentator who offers analysis on a wide range of issues. See more at substack.com/ @aljocommentary.;

Document 24 of 500

PCs make $40B in promises, including cheaper booze

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/pcs-make-40b-promises-including-cheaper-booze/docview/3170771229/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=PCs+make+%2440B+in+promises%2C+including+cheaper+booze&amp;title=The+Spectator&amp;issn=11899417&amp;date=2025-02-25&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.6&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Spectator&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Ontario's Progressive Conservatives released their platform Monday, with three days until election day, and it contains $40 billion in promises plus a proposal to get rid of the minimum retail price for liquor.
Among the promises are a few previously unannounced measures including a $5-billion Protect Ontario Account to help respond to American tariffs, $705 million to expand STEM and skilled trades training capacity at post-secondary institutions, and $50 million to support modular housing.
But the platform does not include a full costing breakdown of how a re-elected Progressive Conservative government would pay for the promises and contains some pledges with no cost estimates, such as Doug Ford's plan to build a tunnel under Highway 401.
Ford defended the numbers, citing his track record from the past seven years in government.
"We've been very fiscally responsible," he said at a campaign event. "We're prudent fiscal managers with the taxpayers' money. Not only haven't we raised taxes, we reduced the cost of paying on debt from previous governments."
He later suggested that economic growth will help offset some of the spending, but also said if U.S. President Donald Trump's threatened tariffs are implemented, Ontario won't be able to balance its budget.
Ford has made warnings about what will happen if Canada is hit with tariffs a large part of his campaign, and his platform contains billions of dollars in planned spending to respond to them. In addition to the $5-billion Protect Ontario Account, he is proposing $10 billion in support for employers through a tax deferral, up to $40 million for municipalities hard hit by tariffs, and up to $120 million to increase bars' and restaurants' wholesale alcohol discount.
Aside from the tariff fight, Ford also put another alcohol goodie in his platform, proposing to eliminate the floor price for spirits.
Minimum prices for spirits vary based on volume and alcohol content and are indexed to inflation, and the minimum retail price for a 750 millilitre bottle of vodka is currently set to rise this weekend to $31.15.
"Under the Liberals...they would say, 'You have to charge this much, because if you don't charge this much, then people will drink too much,'" Ford said.
Liberal Leader Bonnie Crombie was in Ottawa, highlighting her health-care promises.
"People are telling me that they're afraid to get sick, not afraid that they're going to get great health care when they receive it, but they know that our hospitals are overflowing and that they're underfunded, and the wait times are enormous," she said.
The Canadian Press
CAPTION: Ontario PC Leader Doug Ford makes an announcement in Toronto on Monday.Chris Young The Canadian Press

CREDIT: Allison Jones The Canadian Press

Document 25 of 500

Promises, promises: Hamilton voters can now compare party platforms ahead of Thursday's election

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/promises-hamilton-voters-can-now-compare-party/docview/3170771222/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Promises%2C+promises%3A+Hamilton+voters+can+now+compare+party+platforms+ahead+of+Thursday%27s+election&amp;title=The+Hamilton+Spectator+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2025-02-25&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Hamilton+Spectator+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

The party vows to prioritize transit over highways and to upload transit costs once covered by the province, but now paid by local taxpayers.
Urban boundaries
City politicians say their vow to "freeze" Hamilton's urban boundary and grow up, rather than out is again under threat - this time from changes to provincial law and policy that make it easier for developers to apply for urban boundary expansions and harder for the city to say no.
PC
The Tory government famously backtracked in 2023 on an unpopular urban boundary expansion in Hamilton. But it has since introduced new legislation allowing developers to apply for such expansions at any time - and appeal to an unelected tribunal if council says "no." The province has said the changes were meant to beef up housing supply and ensure "balanced" consideration of development applications.
NDP
The party's platform says a NDP government commits to "focusing growth sustainably and cost-effectively within the pre-2022 urban boundaries," which it says will keep infrastructure costs down.
Liberal
The party platform pledges to "protect prime farmland." In an interview, Crombie said the province must be more "consultative" with municipal partners and respect local decision-making powers.
Green
The Green platform pledges to support frozen urban boundaries and reverse provincial policy changes that encourage sprawl. It pledges to incentivize new housing specifically within existing urban footprints.
Supervised consumption sites
Under new provincial rules, two Hamilton supervised drug-consumption sites will be forced to close at the end of March, which critics argue will lead to a spike in overdose deaths.
PC
The Tories say they will enforce a ban on safe drug-consumption sites near schools and child-care facilities, but also spend $530 million creating new addiction and recovery hubs.
NDP
The NDP platform promises to fund mental-health providers to support people with addictions, and "use evidence-based strategies to address the opioid epidemic." They also vow to fund detox and residential treatment beds.
Liberal
In a release last year, Crombie said supervised drug-consumption sites should not be near schools. The party wants to bring opioid overdose deaths "down to zero in four years" through expanded treatment and rehabilitation.
Green
The Greens promise to reopen safe consumption sites closed by the Tories and add new sites across the province.
Matthew Van Dongen is a reporter at The Spectator. mvandongen@thespec.com
Kate McCullough is a reporter at The Spectator. kmccullough@thespec.com
CAPTION: Tent encampments are a visible reminder of a big issue in the 2025 provincial election: homelessness and the unaffordable cost of housing.Demolition of buildings continues along Hamilton's LRT corridor. The city wants the next provincial government to commit to turning over excess land along the line for new affordable housing.Transit promises from parties in this 2025 election range from an "expedited" Hamilton LRT to the province "uploading" some bus operating costs for the HSR.City politicians says Hamilton's frozen urban boundary is again under threat due to provincial legislation.The pending closure of 10 supervised consumption sites in Ontario, including two in Hamilton, has become an election issue.A provincial law that allows seniors awaiting long-term care to be sent to faraway facilities has left some families worried.
CREDIT: Kate McCullough, Matthew Van Dongen

Subject: Drug overdose; Provincial elections; Legislation; Long term health care; Voters; Affordable housing; Politics; Funding; Homeless people; Cost estimates; Operating costs; Narcotics; Rent control

Business indexing term: Subject: Affordable housing Cost estimates Operating costs Rent control

Document 26 of 500

Public health care is being starved

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/public-health-care-is-being-starved/docview/3170770930/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=unknown&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Public+health+care+is+being+starved&amp;title=The+Spectator&amp;issn=11899417&amp;date=2025-02-25&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.11&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Spectator&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: About 40 years ago, Tommy Douglas warned we must protect public health care from "subtle strangulation."
In Doug Ford's anything-but-subtle strangulation of Ontario's health care, his government has openly "taken a wrecking ball" to Hamilton's.
Recently, the Canadian Union of Public Employees revealed Hamilton's largest hospital network, Hamilton Health Sciences, is projecting a deficit of $112 million, with St. Joseph's Healthcare projecting one of $24.3 million, for the fiscal year in which Ford hopes to seize another victory. While designating $3 billion for rebate cheques and tunnels, Ford has not addressed hospital deficits, instead playing "hide-and-seek with hospital funding."
Ontario's public hospital funding, as well as its overall health-care spending, are the lowest per capita of any province. As a result, Hamilton hospitals (and others) face these dramatic shortfalls in funding.
With 85 cents of every health-care dollar spent on staffing, Hamilton Health Sciences is eliminating staff positions and pulling job postings to attempt to reduce this enormous deficit, leaving critical front-line positions vacant. Nearly half of the remaining, exhausted staff are now considering quitting their jobs. Meanwhile, patients suffer on stretchers in hallways in Hamilton hospitals and wait on average 21 hours in Hamilton emergency rooms. Ford promised to fix this crisis in 2021, then allowed it to double in severity.
It's not just Hamilton. Some 2.5 million people in this province have no family doctor. After Ford capped wages of nurses and health professionals, thousands left due to overwork and burnout. Ontario now has the fewest nurses per capita of any province.
Your father may be waiting, along with 2,000 others, on that hallway stretcher for days because Ontario has the fewest hospital beds in Canada. Your mother's surgery may be delayed, along with 250,000 on wait lists, because provincial operating rooms are not adequately staffed and therefore underused. Your grandparent may be one of Ontario's palliative patients who die at home without painkillers.
The starvation of public health care by Ford is not only widespread but deliberate. Ford has always been supportive of for-profit U.S.-style health care. While disabling the public system he has presented himself as Ontario's rescuer, offering so-called "alternatives" in privatized clinics (carefully described as "independent health facilities"), repeating the fiction that private health care reduces wait times and will help clear the backlogs he created.
Ford knows this isn't true, which is why he's been so coy. Before the 2022 election, Ford assured voters he would not expand private hospitals. Immediately following that election, he announced the expansion of privatized surgeries and diagnostics. As he publicly promised, "Ontarians will always access health care with their OHIP card," patients were extra-billed thousands for privatized surgeries and hundreds for medically unnecessary tests.
Instead of halting these illegal practices, as promised, Ford increased funding to private clinics by more than 200 per cent. The result has been gross wastage of public dollars (your taxes). Much more is spent for every surgery in privatized clinics, compared to those in public hospitals. Public hospitals have been forced to hire high-cost nursing staff from private agencies for which spending has increased sixfold since 2020.
While positioning himself as a defender of Canada's values, Ford - in the shadow of the 2026 trade negotiations in which the defence of Medicare will be central - is planning more for-profit wasteful health care.
Hamilton hospitals have always punched above their weight in the delivery of superlative health care. Ford has been a disaster for that proud legacy. On Feb. 27, Hamiltonians must heed the warning of Tommy Douglas, and vote as if our lives depend on it.
Dr. Nancy Olivieri is a Hamilton-born physician and professor
at the University of Toronto.
Michael Hurley is president,
Ontario Council of Hospital Unions, and a member of CUPE's national executive board. Natalie Mehra is executive director,
Ontario Health Coalition.
CAPTION: Ontario's public hospital funding, as well as its overall health-care spending, are the lowest per capita of any province. As a result, hospitals face dramatic shortfalls in funding, Dr. Nancy Olivieri, Michael Hurley and Natalie Mehra write.Cathie Coward The Hamilton Spectator

CREDIT: Dr. Nancy Olivieri, Michael Hurley and Natalie Mehra Dr. Nancy Olivieri is a Hamilton-born physician and professor at the University of Toronto. Michael Hurley is president, Ontario Council of Hospital Unions, and a member of CUPE's national execu;

Document 27 of 500

Feb. 25: A case of 'buyer beware' with landfill nearby, mail delivery grinds to halt and other letters to the editor

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/feb-25-case-buyer-beware-with-landfill-nearby/docview/3170770896/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Feb.+25%3A+A+case+of+%27buyer+beware%27+with+landfill+nearby%2C+mail+delivery+grinds+to+halt+and+other+letters+to+the+editor&amp;title=The+Hamilton+Spectator+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2025-02-25&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Hamilton+Spectator+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Buyer beware when landfill nearby
Re: Stoney Creek landfill stench holding residents 'hostage' in homes, Feb. 24
The industrial landfill in Upper Stoney Creek has been there for decades. Landfills have an odour. Always have. My question is, why would you want to buy a home and raise a family beside an industrial landfill when you know, or at least should know, that there will be an odour associated with the area and then complain about the stench?
It's like buying a home beside the Hamilton airport and complaining about the noise of all the airplanes make. It's like buying a home beside Highway 401 and complaining about the noise and pollution.
If I buy a house beside a cattle farm that has been there for years and years, and then complain about the smell of manure, do I have the right to demand the farmer stop raising cattle? I don't think so. I'd just look for another place to buy a house.
Stop complaining, the landfill was there long before your house was. It would be a little different if someone wanted to develop a landfill in the middle of a residential area.
Rusty Escott, Dunnville
Snow clearing priorities questioned
With the largest amount of snow accumulation in years blocking roads in the city, we know we all have to be patient. However, I do wonder at the city's priorities.
Aberdeen east of the 403, a heavily travelled and potholed road, still has snowbanks narrowing the outside lane. The same is true for the stretch on Dundurn Street across from Fortinos. The one lane that is not left-turning is dangerous because of snow.
Meanwhile, roads like West 5th on the Mountain, a much wider route, had the snowbanks carted away last week. It doesn't make sense.
Carol Town, Hamilton
Snow hampering mail delivery
Re: Fed up with snowy sidewalks, Feb. 22
In my area, the city did a nice job clearing the sidewalks, sometimes more than once, since the last storm.
However, there is one major street where only one side was done and guess what - the unplowed side is where the community mailboxes are located. So no mail for a week. No voter information cards, no CRA tax forms, nothing. I am perplexed.
Ed Chong, Ancaster
Lack of mail delivery unacceptable
For more than 20 days, those of us who live in a 25-storey building in the Durand have not received mail delivery from Canada Post. This means the more than 300 residents do not know the location of our polling station nor do we have our voting cards. And furthermore, we haven't received the Ontario tax rebate cheques or tax receipts so we can file our tax returns, etc.
Canada Post has an obligation to deliver mail, and while exceptional circumstances may mean a delivery is be delayed, no delivery for more than 20 days is not acceptable.
Jeanne Mayo, Hamilton
Lots of voters are plugged in
Re: As elections go, this one's been a snooze, Feb. 22
Scott Radley's column doesn't jibe with my experience. Canvassing in Hamilton Centre, I met a good number of people who voted in advance or planned to. The lively Cable 14 riding debates and solid media coverage on CHCH, in the Spectator and more were mentioned.
Doug Ford tried to put voters asleep with a mid-winter election and PC candidates avoiding debates and interviews. What a cynical strategy. It's encouraging that more people are engaged in this highly contested campaign than he might think.
Gord McNulty, Hamilton
CAPTION: A Hamilton councillor wants the city to explore legal options to deal with GFL's stink-prone industrial landfill in upper Stoney Creek and potential tax relief for stench-weary area residents. A readers says smells are to be expected when living next to a landfill.

Document 28 of 500

Before you cast your ballot, ask yourself if life is better today than in 2018

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/before-you-cast-your-ballot-ask-yourself-if-life/docview/3170770867/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Before+you+cast+your+ballot%2C+ask+yourself+if+life+is+better+today+than+in+2018&amp;title=The+Hamilton+Spectator+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2025-02-25&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Hamilton+Spectator+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Set aside the unforgiving winter weather that could affect voter turnout, with some of the hardest-hit regions receiving over 50 centimetres of snow last week.
Set aside the fact the provincial election was originally scheduled for June 4, 2026. And finally, set aside the fact the RCMP is conducting a criminal investigation into Doug Ford's Greenbelt scandal.
The one question we, as Ontarians, should ask ourselves is this: Are our lives better today than they were in 2018?
Since taking office almost seven years ago, our health-care system is in shambles. The premier gleefully boasted about spending billions on health care during last week's debate, yet, time and again, horror stories of patients waiting for care in emergency departments - with some not making it out alive - have become far too common across towns and cities. In fact, one in five hospitals with an emergency room experienced unforeseen closures in 2024, making it one of the worst years for ER shutdowns ever.
Don't take my word for it. Look to the government's own findings. Internal documents from last year detailed that 10 per cent of patients admitted to Ontario hospitals in the emergency department had to wait a minimum of two days before finally being assigned a bed.
Furthermore, this issue is compounded by 2.5 million people lacking access to a family doctor, according to the Ontario College of Family Physicians. Instead of regular, proactive care that could prevent emergency visits, many rely on walk-in clinics or require travelling 50 kilometres or more to see a family doctor, if they are lucky enough to have one.
While the health-care system suffers under Ford's leadership, our province is gripped with another pressing provincial crisis: housing. Contrary to popular belief, this issue is primarily the responsibility of provincial governments, although throughout Canada's history, it has been a shared duty with federal authorities.
Presently, Ontario ranks as the second most expensive province to rent, only behind B.C. Despite a modest decline of around five per cent, the average asking price for apartments is $2,329 as of January 2025, per Rentals.ca.
In the same month, the average price of a single-family home was $942,700. Is it any wonder, then, that eight in 10 Canadians believe home ownership is only for the rich, as revealed by a 2024 Ipsos poll? Hmm, I wonder.
The truth is, Ontarians are hurting. Life's most basic necessities, most notably housing and health care, have only worsened since 2018. He's had plenty of time to make our province a decent place to live and work.
He's failed.
We need a seismic shift from the status quo and a serious look at alternative parties to provide the means for that shift. Some voters may be reluctant to consider the Ontario NDP and Liberals because of Bob Rae and Kathleen Wynne, but it's important to remember that Marit Stiles isn't Bob Rae and Bonnie Crombie isn't Kathleen Wynne.
And, to be fair, any future leader of the Progressive Conservative Party should not, ideally, be tethered to Ford.
The last provincial election's turnout was abysmal, with only 43.5 per cent of eligible voters exercising their right to vote (a historic record low). Younger generations, particularly Gen Z and millennials, are especially vulnerable to the housing crisis and stand to benefit the most from meaningful change.
The stakes are too high to sit this one out - take it to the voting booth on Thursday, Feb. 27.
Vote.
CAPTION: Last month the average price of a single-family home in Ontario was $942,700 and the province is the second-most expensive province to rent. Doug Ford has had plenty of opportunities to improve our lives, yet we're worse off, Alan Joseph writes.
CREDIT: Alan Joseph

Subject: Emergency medical care; Scandals; Voters; Single family; Health care; Voter behavior

People: Wynne, Kathleen; Rae, Bob

Document 30 of 500

Doug Ford pledges $40B in new spending to offset Donald Trump's threatened tariffs

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/doug-ford-pledges-40b-new-spending-offset-donald/docview/3171050925/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Doug+Ford+pledges+%2440B+in+new+spending+to+offset+Donald+Trump%27s+threatened+tariffs&amp;title=The+Hamilton+Spectator+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2025-02-24&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Hamilton+Spectator+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Donald Trump's looming tariffs mean Ontario must continue to bleed red ink for the foreseeable future, admits Doug Ford.
As Ford's Progressive Conservatives unveiled their platform - a 32-page manifesto that is not fully costed and pledges $40 billion in new spending - he conceded deficits are here to stay.
"If the tariffs hit us, yes, we won't be able to balance because - just similar to the pandemic - we're going to invest into the people," the PC leader told reporters Monday at a North York painters' union centre.
The U.S. president is threatening 25 per cent tariffs on all Canadian products next month, which would be harmful to Ontario's trade-dependent economy.
Since being elected in 2018, the Tories, who governed during the global COVID-19 pandemic, have run massive budget deficits.
Under Ford, the provincial debt has soared by $116 billion to $429 billion, the largest debt of any subnational jurisdiction in the world.
But asked whether he was disrespecting taxpayers by not releasing a costed platform before Thursday's provincial election, the Tory chief was defensive.
"Not at all. We're pretty clear that if these tariffs come, our investments going to be over $40 billion no matter if it's loans or other investments we have for people and businesses to make sure they're secure here if Trump's tariffs come and hit us," he said.
"We've been very fiscally responsible. We're prudent fiscal managers with the taxpayers' money."
But NDP Leader Marit Stiles said Ford's platform is "not worth the paper it's written on."
"For the third election in a row, Doug Ford's making the same empty promises. People know he's failed to deliver for years - just look at the cost of rent or groceries," said Stiles.
"Let's put the waste, corruption and false promises of the Ford years in the rearview mirror, and let's elect an NDP government on your side."
Liberal Leader Bonnie Crombie said she's "never seen" a platform "spend so much and do so little."
"This is a platform for lobbyists and insiders. What has he done to make life more affordable for people," Crombie in Toronto-St. Paul's, where she was joined by local candidate Stephanie Smyth, a prominent former CP24 anchor.
"He hasn't costed the fantasy tunnel and he doesn't tell us how he's going to pay for it either," she said, referring to Ford's proposed Mississauga-Scarborough tunnel under Highway 401, which could cost $150 billion and is buried on page eight of the PC platform without any price tag.
Despite no feasibility study on the scheme, the Tories have vowed to moved forward on the tunnel.
But it was not included in the "highways and roads" section of the PC election booklet.
Instead, there was 38-word sentence touting the project underneath a longer entry on how the Tories would "return sanity to bike lanes" by removing them from Bloor Street West, Yonge Street and University Avenue, which was finalized last December.
One new item in the manifesto was the elimination of the minimum price for beer, wine and spirits.
That hearkens to Ford's 2018 "buck-a-beer" election pledge that fell flat because brewers could not afford to produce and sell beer for that price.
Currently, wine must sell for no less than $8.10 for a 750 ml bottle, while beer cannot be sold for less than $2.82 per litre - or $1.41 for a 500 ml tallboy can - and spirits, such as vodka and gin, must sell for at least $31.15 a 750 ml bottle.
Ford, who does not drink alcohol, said he was eliminating the floor price to give people a break.
"They don't do it in Alberta. They don't do it in Quebec. So why do we have to have the base? Let's reduce the cost of that. Put more money back into people's pockets again," he said.
But the LCBO, the Crown-owned booze monopoly, announced Monday beer taxes would increase by about 4.4 per cent on schedule on April 1.
Earlier in Ottawa, Crombie was focused on what she sees as the ballot question: finding a family doctor for the 2.5 million Ontarians who don't have one.
"People are telling me that they're afraid to get sick ... they know that our hospitals are overflowing and that they're underfunded and the wait times are enormous," the Liberal leader said, blasting Ford's claim that patients with minor medical problems are swamping emergency rooms instead of going to walk-in clinics.
"I don't know what choice people have," said Crombie, who has pledged to ensure every Ontarian has a family doctor within four years - promises that Ford and Stiles have also made.
"It's not their fault, Doug, it's yours."
At his platform launch, Ford insisted he would improve access to doctors, which is why he hired Dr. Jane Philpott, a former federal Liberal minister, as his health guru.
"It just goes to show, I don't care what political colour you come from, she's well renowned around the entire country, and in four years, we're going to connect ... every single person (with a physician)," he said.
Speaking to the Star editorial board, Stiles said the doctors' shortage "is taking a big toll on Ontarians" and impacting the economy.
"Employers that I talk to - in everything from tech to auto - will tell you that what's making it difficult for them to attract more workers here ... is the fact that they can't ensure that their workers are going to have a home they can afford, that they can't guarantee they're going to get a school for their kids, or a family doctor," she said.
Green Leader Mike Schreiner, meanwhile, was in Bracebridge rallying support for Parry Sound-Muskoka candidate Matt Richter who is trying to unseat Tory Graydon Smith.
"Community members of all political stripes are getting behind Matt because they know that he will be the best advocate for them at Queen's Park," said Schreiner.
"I know from knocking on thousands of doors here that people are ready for change. Electing Matt is the only way to stop another Conservative seat and make that change happen."
CAPTION: Ontario PC Leader Doug Ford makes an announcement in Toronto on Feb. 24, 2025.
CREDIT: Robert Benzie, Rob Ferguson, Kristin Rushowy

Document 31 of 500

Ontario election: What you need to know about voting on election day

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/ontario-election-what-you-need-know-about-voting/docview/3170241088/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Ontario+election%3A+What+you+need+to+know+about+voting+on+election+day&amp;title=The+Hamilton+Spectator+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2025-02-24&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Hamilton+Spectator+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: On Thursday, Ontarians will head to the polls to decide who will lead the country's most populated province.
Though voters have been able to cast their vote early, day-of voting remains a vital and rich tradition for those seeking to make their voices heard at the ballot box.
Here's everything you need to know about voting in-person on election day in Ontario:
When do the polls open and close?
Polls will open at 9 a.m. and close at 9 p.m. on Feb. 27, per Elections Ontario. Between those hours, you can vote in-person at one of the province's more than 7,000 voting locations, which are commonly housed at schools, places of worship and community centres, among others.
Ontario election: Here's the Toronto weather forecast for voting day
Where can I vote?
Elections Ontario has an online tool that allows voters to find their electoral district and election day voting location by postal code.
If, for example, you live at the Scotiabank Arena, you'll be voting at St. Andrew's Centre and electing a representative for Spadina-Fort York.
Those without a permanent address can use the "place where you have returned to most often to eat or sleep in the past five weeks" as an address to vote.
Do I need ID to vote?
Yes. Elections Ontario requires you to show identification at your polling place, but it doesn't necessarily need to include a photo of you and it can be shown on a mobile device.
If you've been sent a voter information card in the mail, your ID can be anything from a birth certificate to a health card to a student card from one of the province's universities, among many others.
Ontario election: Haven't received your voter information card? Here's what to do
If you haven't been issued a voter information card, your piece of ID must have both your full name and your home address. This can include an Ontario driver's license, insurance statement, a child tax benefit statement or a school transcript, per Elections Ontario.
Will I have to wait in line to vote?
Elections Ontario did not say whether they expect to see lines on election night.
But Ontario hasn't had a winter election since the 1920s, so it's possible that some of the inclement weather that has walloped the province this month could contribute to and difficulty parking or getting to the polls.
(In the 1926 election, which took place on Dec. 1, the Star reported that some voters "felt it too cold to brave the near-zero temperatures" to vote; voter turnout that year was still over 64 per cent, however, compared to the 44 per cent and 56 per cent figures in 2022 and 2018, respectively.)
What will the weather be like on election day?
Toronto-area voters, including in Peel, Durham and York should prepare for chilly conditions, with cloudy skies and a 60 per cent chance of snow or rain in the forecast. Temperatures are expected to reach a high of 3 C and a low of -6 C.
As a result, it may be worth dressing warmly and allowing for extra time for travel to ensure a smooth trip to the polls.
When will we know who won the election?
In the last Ontario election, most networks called the election by 9:30 p.m. on election night. When Ontarians went to the polls in 2014 and 2018, networks were likewise able to call the election results before most of the province went to bed.
While it's yet unclear whether this election will be as quick to project, recent polls gave Ford a commanding lead over Bonnie Crombie's Liberals and Marit Stiles' New Democrats.
With files from Anastasia Blosser and Joyce Li.
CAPTION: Voters in Ontario can vote in-person on Feb. 27 from 9 a.m. to 9 p.m. at one of more than 7,000 voting locations across the province.
CREDIT: Hayden Godfrey

Subject: Voters; Voter behavior; Elections
