Document 32 of 500

Showdown will hinge on one thing: confidence

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/showdown-will-hinge-on-one-thing-confidence/docview/3169989948/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=unknown&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Showdown+will+hinge+on+one+thing%3A+confidence&amp;title=The+Spectator&amp;issn=11899417&amp;date=2025-02-24&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.9&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Spectator&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: A few assumptions to start. Mark Carney will handily win the federal Liberal party's nomination, become prime minister, and march - more or less straightaway - into Rideau Hall to ask the governor general to call an election.
During the contest that follows, U.S. President Donald Trump will not suddenly decide to take his foot off the gas. He will continue to bully, intimidate and harass Canada. He will impose tariffs, dangle new threats and heighten economic pressure. In other words, the current crisis will not just persist - it will escalate.
So, make no mistake: this crisis is not the backdrop, it will define the stakes, the strategy and the ultimate outcome of the election. And with it, three fundamental realities will emerge - each shaping the battle ahead.
First, is the exceedingly obvious point that this election will not be about Justin Trudeau's record, nor will it be a referendum on the carbon tax. The Liberal brand, battered and bruised as it may be, may just well escape a judgment of its record as the election becomes a genuine fight over the future of the country.
Second, is a more counterintuitive point: this contest won't be decided on policy.
At his recent "Canada First" rally, Conservative Leader Pierre Poilievre quipped that Carney would simply "plagiarize" all his policies before the election. It is a clever way to salt the earth for Carney's policy agenda in the coming campaign, to be sure, but it was also a tacit admission of something deeper.
The economic challenges facing Canada are so glaring - so long overdue - that a broad policy consensus has already taken shape. One drawn closer by the fact that Carney, Stephen Harper's appointee as governor of the Bank of Canada, carries a distinctly fiscally conservative pedigree.
Yes, Poilievre and Carney will try to outflank each other on the margins. But on the most urgent issues - meeting our NATO commitment, diversifying our trading relationships, eliminating interprovincial trade barriers, tackling productivity stagnation - their objectives will be strikingly similar.
Their execution will of course be wildly different. But the point is this election won't be won or lost on the ideological extremes.
Third, and most importantly, is the fact the ballot question has already been set in stone.
As with Ontario's election, this federal race won't hinge on who is best suited to "negotiate" with Trump. Canadians see through that. No one is "best suited" to deal with an irrational, transactional and unpredictable actor. Instead, the ballot question will be: Who is best able to protect Canadians from the harm that Trump's presidency has already inflicted and will continue to bring?
With no incumbency disadvantage, or advantage, and no radical ideological divide in the eyes of most voters, this race will come down to political intangibles. Who has the leadership qualities to reassure Canadians? Who exudes confidence, strength, and control? Who makes us believe together we can get through this as Canadians? Who convinces voters they can handle Trump's economic aggression without making things worse?
All the while making it clear they are on the side of hard working, everyday Canadians who are finding life more and more difficult.
The stakes are unlike anything in recent history. The political ground is shifting beneath our feet. There is no playbook for what's ahead. But as Canadians narrow their focus on self-preservation in the wake of Trump's return, so too must Carney and Poilievre narrow their strategies around that singular fear. In the end, this election will be a referendum on one thing: who can make Canadians feel safest in a world growing more volatile by the day.
And it will be won by the leader who best embodies that safety and galvanizes Canadians to hold the line against the coming chaos.
Jaime Watt is the executive chair of Navigator Ltd.
and a Conservative strategist.
CAPTION:

CREDIT: Jaime Watt OPINION Jaime Watt is the executive chair of Navigator Ltd. and a Conservative strategist.;

Feb. 24: Snowblower community lives on, fill empty debate seats with cut-outs and other letters to the editor

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/feb-24-snowblower-community-lives-on-fill-empty/docview/3169989141/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Feb.+24%3A+Snowblower+community+lives+on%2C+fill+empty+debate+seats+with+cut-outs+and+other+letters+to+the+editor&amp;title=The+Hamilton+Spectator+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2025-02-24&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Hamilton+Spectator+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Snowblowers always at the ready
Re: Big snowfall brings back a flurry of memories, Feb. 21
Boy did you hit the nail on the head today, Lorraine! I am married to one of those retired snowblower guys and we live next to another retired snowblower guy (a good friends of ours).
I get such a good chuckle out of seeing who will win the "I got started first" contest. They are out all day clearing driveways. First they clear the driveways and then they wait in anticipation of the plow coming through so that they can attack the windrows.
My husband even invested in a heated vest to minimize his warm-up breaks. It's lovely to see that there are still big hearts in our communities. And yes, I too coveted a one-piece snowsuit, but had to wait until my 20s to get one - bright red with black fake fur collar. I loved that suit and I was easy to spot on the slopes!
Catherine Brady, Burlington
Fill empty seat with cutout
The Progressive Conservatives' strategy to avoid debating in the local debates is brilliant.
Since they have no candidate to defend their dismal record, the other candidates attack each other reminding viewers of their past failings and policies.
I'd like to see a cardboard cutout of the missing PC candidate placed at their empty chair and the other candidates take turns addressing it while reminding viewers of Doug Ford's disastrous reign. Maybe then the PCs will send candidates to try to defend Ford's record.
Don Zeller, Ancaster
You get what you vote for
Due to Doug Ford's policies, my vibrant, 65-year-old friend spent more than 24 hours in two different hallways in a Hamilton hospital with a broken hip after falling on snow-covered ice.
As I write this, on day three, there is no date or time for surgery. She is balancing her agony with not being "too demanding" of the overworked nurses.
Go ahead and vote for Ford, but you better pray you do not need emergency surgery in Hamilton.
And please be kind to medical staff if you do. By the way, my 88-year-old mother and I were tortured by hallway medicine in Oshawa last year, so this appears to be the norm in Ontario.
Seniors in pain in hallways with bright lights, loud alarms, staff rushing by, no sleep, needing bedpans and delirium sets in. You get what you vote for my friends.
Adele Barrett, Hamilton
Trump must be embarrassed
With great interest I and many other Canadians watched the Four Nations championship game Thursday night. While it was a pleasure seeing Canada win 3-2, I feel it will come with great consequences.
I understand that Donald Trump will sign an executive order to immediately impose a 100 per cent tariff on any Canadian hockey team entering the U.S. It must be very embarrassing for this U.S. dictator to realize that his wannabe 51st state has beaten his Team USA.
Edward Beitel, Ancaster
What's really being lost?
Re: 'It's going to be felt by everybody': Angst over closure of Hamilton supervised drug-use sites, Feb. 21
The article states the 2023-24 fiscal year saw 3,400 visits for drug consumption with 46 overdose reversals and 6,000 visits for other services. This represents four overdoses reversed per month (on average), while the wraparound services were accessed 500 times a month.
What is being closed is consumption sites adjacent to schools and daycares.
The other components of the CTS program that are being "lost" are supervised drug use, on-site distribution of clean drug use kits (needles, pipes, etc.) and on-site prescribing of "safer supply" drugs (usually 40 tablets of 8 mg Dilaudid daily) dispensed to the patients in bottles.
The clean-use kits and Dilaudid prescriptions will still be available at other locations.
What is really being "lost" versus simply moved?
We have not been told: Whether these sites have been successful in reducing HIV and Hep C transmission in the community, and; whether the prescribing of the Dilaudid tablets as part of the program has contributed to a provincewide increase in Dilaudid addiction, particularly in minors, of whom I saw three last week.
Given that those four overdoses per month may have been mitigated if that person(s) had been in a recovery-based program (do we know if they were?), or managed in the community by peers with naloxone kits on hand, it seems to me that the "special sauce" of the CTS has been the wraparound services, most of which will be available, if delivered as promised, at the HART sites.
Lori Regenstreif, Hamilton
Suggesting a new ballot option
Like many, l am concerned about vote splitting that allows a conservative government to be elected with 36 per cent of the vote when by nature we are a centre-left province.
After watching three of the debates and not having any of the Conservatives show up, l find little difference in the platforms of the Liberals, NDP and Greens. So to not waste your vote, there should be another option on the ballot to the person(s) you do not want elected and your vote would then automatically be directed to the candidate that has accumulated the most votes.
Tom Winning, Hamilton
Please get out and vote
Doug Ford called an unnecessary election in winter for a number of reasons but none of them included needing a mandate to fight tariffs. He already had a majority.
This is what I believe. One, he was ahead in popularity polls and realized that the longer the circus south of the border and the orange clown (who he was glad won) continued, the more his popularity would wane.
Two, he thought the winter weather would suppress the vote, thus, probably favouring him.
Three, he saw that the federal Liberals were gaining in popularity.
If he really wanted to protect Ontario's economy and jobs, he wouldn't have called an election. He might have to actually stand up to the orange clown he admires. If "Captain Canada" really wanted to stand up for Ontario, why is he continuing with his deal with Elon Musk? Why is American liquor still on the shelves in the LCBO?
So, please vote. Please don't let Ford get away with this.
By the way, I have always voted Conservative up until the party decided to pander to the lunatic fringe and emulate the politics to the south. I stopped voting, but not anymore. I will be voting for another party.
Betty Baker, Stoney Creek
Keep different parties in power
Over the last 60 years, I have voted in just about every election whether local, provincial or federal. At the federal and provincial level I prefer that one government is Liberal and the other is Conservative at any given point. So I vote accordingly. I never vote for the socialists having seen the negative impact they have on the economy in Europe.
Richard Holloway, Oakville
CAPTION: A man uses his snow blower on Magnolia Drive on the west mountain in 2022. Responding to a recent column by Lorraine Sommerfeld, a reader says she gets a good chuckle out of seeing who will win the "I got started first" snowblower contest after each snowfall.

Document 34 of 500

What are Ontario's 3 major parties' plans for DEI? We don't know. They didn't respond to our questions

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/what-are-ontarios-3-major-parties-plans-dei-we/docview/3169989136/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=What+are+Ontario%27s+3+major+parties%27+plans+for+DEI%3F+We+don%27t+know.+They+didn%27t+respond+to+our+questions&amp;title=The+Hamilton+Spectator+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2025-02-24&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Hamilton+Spectator+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: It's about trust. Our relationship with our readers is built on transparency, honesty and integrity. As such, we have launched a trust initiative to tell you who we are and how and why we do what we do. This editorial is part of that project.
Important issues of diversity, equity and inclusion appear to be getting more divisive.
U.S. President Donald Trump loudly opposes many measures to address these challenges. His government is actively opposing them. And some companies were already moving away from programs before Trump took office.
Efforts have only escalated since, and Canada is feeling similar pressures.
But abandoning these efforts will not serve any of us well. They break down barriers to making our country more inclusive, equitable and fair, and our businesses and organizations more successful.
This is why Metroland, as part of its ongoing commitment to these principles, continues to champion them. And it is why Metroland editorial employees set up the IDEA (inclusion, diversity, equity and accessibility) committee five years ago to ensure these efforts do not fade from our priorities.
It is also why the committee spearheaded an initiative this month to survey the main provincial parties for their positions on some of these issues.
The Q-and-A focused on accessibility, diversity, equity, inclusion, food insecurity, basic income, housing and homelessness. As we all know, these issues affect different parts of our communities in many ways.
What better time to ask the four major parties for their policies on these matters than during an election?
Alas, only the Greens have responded.
There may be various reasons for this, but the lack of response by the Progressive Conservative, Liberal and New Democratic parties does not look good for them. Is it not a priority for provincial party leaders? Are they more comfortable with other forms of communication?
By declining or simply not getting around to responding to Metroland's questionnaire, the parties have missed an opportunity to speak to millions of Ontarians, at a time when there is heightened focus on these matters and others.
This isn't just opinion. Metroland editors know this through the results of a provincial election survey of readers, which highlighted that housing, homelessness and affordability were among their top concerns. Others included accessibility rights, disability supports, addressing hate culture and antisemitism, among others.
And Metroland journalists know it through regular communication with readers and other members of our communities - voters, volunteers, professionals, business leaders, politicians - and from various organizations we meet with regularly.
Dealing aggressively with these issues is not just the right thing to do, it is the best route forward to a prosperous and peaceful future together.
CAPTION: The party leaders: PC Doug Ford; NDP Marit Stiles; Green Party Mike Schreiner; Liberal Bonnie Crombie.
CREDIT: Metroland Editorial Leaders

Document 35 of 500

Ford vague on paying for campaign promises: 'I believe in growing revenue,' Progressive Conservative leader says

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/ford-vague-on-paying-campaign-promises/docview/3169989100/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Ford+vague+on+paying+for+campaign+promises%3A+%27I+believe+in+growing+revenue%2C%27+Progressive+Conservative+leader+says&amp;title=The+Spectator&amp;issn=11899417&amp;date=2025-02-24&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.6&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Spectator&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Progressive Conservative Leader Doug Ford offered little clarity Sunday on how he would pay for billions of dollars in promises if re-elected, as looming tariffs from the U.S. continue to keep economic stability at the forefront of the election campaign.
Ford was pressed on how he would cover proposals such as building a tunnel under Highway 401 at a campaign stop in Sault Ste. Marie a day before his party is set to release its platform for Thursday's vote.
"I believe in growing revenue," Ford said during an event at the Algoma Steel plant, suggesting this was previously achieved by growing the tax base but offering no specific measures.
Ford has pitched himself as the best option to protect Ontario's economy, and triggered the snap election arguing that he needs a stronger mandate to navigate the next four years of a Donald Trump presidency in the United States.
The other leaders have called the election unnecessary, saying they would have backed stimulus measures proposed by the majority Progressive Conservatives in response to possible U.S. tariffs.
When asked Sunday when he would lay out his plan to deal with the tariffs, Ford said: "Well, you don't have a plan without seeing what they're going to come at us (with.)"
"We have a strong plan to reinvest into the people, into jobs," he said.
Ford previously announced that if re-elected, he would spend $10 billion on support for employers through a six-month deferral of provincially administered taxes on Ontario businesses and $3 billion toward payroll tax and premium relief.
Another $600 million would go toward a fund aimed at attracting investment, as well as $300 million to expand an Ontario manufacturing tax credit.
NDP Leader Marit Stiles and Liberal Leader Bonnie Crombie have also presented their vision to take on the levies.
New Democrats have promised to bring in a federal-provincial income support program in response to tariffs, and direct agencies to procure locally and create new supply chains for trade-exposed industries.
The Liberals have said they will establish a "fight tariff fund" that would give Ontario businesses a lower interest rate, and offer a $150,000 bonus to Canadian doctors and nurses working in the U.S. if they return here to work.
Ford has called northern Ontario the "frontline" against the tariffs, in part because of the mineral-rich Ring of Fire region northeast of Thunder Bay.
Ford's northern tour has also seen him face questions regarding his candidates' lack of participation in local debates in Thunder Bay and Sault Ste. Marie. "I encourage all our candidates ... you don't have to go to these debates. Some do, some don't," he said Sunday, adding he would rather have them "knocking on doors, talking to the people directly."
CAPTION: Signage from Elections Ontario sits in front of a polling station last week in Toronto. Voters will head to the polls on Thursday.Giordano Ciampini The Canadian Press

Mountain candidates spar over fixes for housing, affordability crisis: Conservative candidate misses debate due to scheduling conflict in riding considered wide open

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/mountain-candidates-spar-over-fixes-housing/docview/3169989030/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Mountain+candidates+spar+over+fixes+for+housing%2C+affordability+crisis%3A+Conservative+candidate+misses+debate+due+to+scheduling+conflict+in+riding+considered+wide+open&amp;title=The+Spectator&amp;issn=11899417&amp;date=2025-02-24&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.3&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Spectator&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: How important are developers to housing?
In Hamilton Mountain riding, it depends on who you ask.
"The fact is, we need to work with developers and they're important members of our community," Liberal candidate Dawn Danko said at a debate hosted by Cable 14 Friday night. "If we don't work with them, we are not going to build the way we want."
"They're developers, not business community members ... Nobody is denying they build homes, but we have alternatives," NDP candidate Kojo Damptey replied, pointing to non-profits, land trusts and co-ops.
"Those are the things we want to (use), because we don't want to be influenced by developers."
Green candidate Joshua Czerniga, meanwhile, said his party wants to encourage fourplexes along major transit routes and provide $25,000 loans for people to add affordable rental units to their properties.
Missing from this back-and-forth was Progressive Conservative candidate Monica Ciriello, a top city bylaw official and one of many provincial Tory candidates who appear to have skipped televised debates during the election campaign.
Host Mike Fortune said at the top of the show Ciriello couldn't attend due to a scheduling conflict. In an email, a spokesperson said Ciriello wanted to attend but her campaign did not receive an invitation until a day before the debate.
Cable 14 has said it reached out to all candidates starting in early February. Organizers tried again last week after two local PC campaigns said they were unaware of those efforts.
A quick remark from Danko aside, viewers never heard her absence referenced again.
Instead, candidates focused their criticism on the political parties in the room and, as has been the case during the campaign, at each other.
As Danko touched on Damptey's past support for encampments, the NDP challenger cut her off, bringing up how her husband, Coun. John-Paul Danko, voted in favour of a 2023 protocol that allowed tents in parks subject to certain restrictions (the councillor recently voted to scrap that protocol.)
"I delegated at city council (saying) that response wasn't needed. What is needed is affordable homes," said Damptey, a community activist, university lecturer and former head of the Hamilton Centre for Civic Inclusion.
"As my husband was mentioned, I would like to correct the record that he is not the person running for this election. I am, Dawn Danko, in case you missed that," Danko later said.
Still, Danko took a moment to defend her husband, telling Damptey the protocol was needed in part because the city "was being sued by people that have supported your campaign."
"My husband actually moved the motion to remove that (protocol) because we need to move to shelter spaces, wraparound supports (and) transitional housing," she said.
The candidates differed on solutions to the housing and affordability crisis, which dominated the debate.
Danko and the Liberals want to implement fair and phased-in rent control, scrap punitive taxes, reduce wait times at the Landlord and Tenant Board, and rapidly increase housing starts by removing development red tape.
Damptey, who's running for the open seat left behind by longtime MPP Monique Taylor, said the NDP wants to support families with rebate checks and "make sure there's a full rental cap." He said more housing is needed, but didn't say how the party would get that built, beyond working with alternatives like non-profits, land trusts and co-ops.
For, the Greens' Czerniga, Damptey's comments rung hollow.
"What I find most interesting about the NDP is they've been the government in waiting for many years now and they talk about these things, but nothing ever changes, nothing ever happens."
Czerniga is no stranger to the NDP, or politics.
For more than a decade, he served on the NDP's executive branch, working alongside Taylor as her chief financial officer. In 2021, when he sought the nomination for the riding's federal seat, Taylor endorsed him.
"What I slowly saw was the decay after Marit Stiles took over," Czerniga said in response to question about why he left the NDP last year. "I saw the yelling, the screaming across the floor, the telling me what Doug Ford has done wrong ... but never once presenting solutions."
Sebastian Bron is a reporter
at The Hamilton Spectator. sbron@thespec.com
CAPTION: The provincial Hamilton Mountain debate Friday night included, from left, Liberal candidate Dawn Danko, NDP candidate Kojo Damptey and Green candidate Joshua Czerniga.Liberal hopeful Dawn Danko makes a point during Friday's debate.NDP candidate Kojo DampteyCathie Coward photosThe Hamilton Spectator

Document 37 of 500

Liberal leadership debates: 3 key things to watch for during Monday and Tuesday's showdowns

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/liberal-leadership-debates-3-key-things-watch/docview/3169989007/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Liberal+leadership+debates%3A+3+key+things+to+watch+for+during+Monday+and+Tuesday%27s+showdowns&amp;title=The+Hamilton+Spectator+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2025-02-24&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Hamilton+Spectator+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: OTTAWA-Less than two weeks before federal Liberals choose Canada's next prime minister, four candidates to replace Justin Trudeau are set to square off in a pair of debates that could help decide who leads the governing party into a coming federal election.
It's widely seen as a key moment in a condensed race, 49 days after Trudeau suspended Parliament and announced he will resign on March 9 when his party chooses its next leader.
First, on Monday at 8 p.m. EST, the candidates will gather in a Montreal film studio for a French debate that will test how perceived anglophone frontrunners handle themselves in the language of a province crucial to the party's hopes to remain in power.
Then, on Tuesday at 8 p.m. EST, comes the English debate, when contenders will appear in that same studio to pitch themselves to a likely broader audience of Liberals and curious politicos across the country.
For Greg MacEachern, a veteran Liberal strategist and principal at KAN Strategies, the moment is a chance for the Liberals to showcase their offering for a general election that could come in a matter of weeks. But it also comes with risk.
"It doesn't matter the party, but occasionally leadership debates can really show division, or sow division," MacEachern said.
Here are three things to watch for in the Liberal leadership debates.
Leurs français
In a race dominated by anglophone candidates, Monday's French debate is expected to be a critical test of linguistic ability for a party with a political base and deep history in Québec.
"There will be a lot of rankings and reviews of the candidates' French," said Dan Arnold, a seasoned Liberal campaign adviser who worked as director of research and advertising in Trudeau's Prime Minister's Office (PMO).
Arnold said those rankings will matter, since French-dominated Québec is a "key part of the Liberal winning coalition," so "you want to make sure you connect with Quebecers."
But it also matters to Liberals outside Québec, Arnold said, since they understand that whomever they choose as leader must be attractive to Québec voters.
Arnold recalled how, in the 2006 Liberal leadership contest, a newspaper judged the candidate he worked for - former Ontario Liberal education minister Gerard Kennedy - to have poor French.
"It really just deep-sixed his campaign, because people were like, 'well, he can't speak French well enough, so he can't win in Québec,'" Arnold said.
Vandana Kattar, a Liberal campaign organizer who also worked in Trudeau's PMO, said she'll be watching for "the quality of language ability" from the candidates - whether they are at ease and appear conversational, or get thrown off by a technical word or phrase.
While most of the candidates have already demonstrated varying degrees of French ability, with former Liberal MP Frank Baylis the sole contender from the province of Québec, competence in the official language has come up as an issue. Before the party disqualified him, Ottawa-area MP Chandra Arya rejected the assertion that the prime minister should be proficient in French and English.
Former Paul Martin-era MP Ruby Dhalla twice asked for a "personal translator" for the French debate, but she was also disqualified from the race on Friday.
Fireworks
For Kattar and others, another big thing to watch for is whether - or to what extent - the candidates attack each other during the debates.
"My sense is that everybody's probably just going to pile on (Mark) Carney," she said, referring to the former central banker running for the job.
Carney has emerged as the perceived frontrunner, after his campaign raked in more than $1.75 million in donations by Feb. 9, according to Elections Canada returns - more than all the other candidates' reported donations combined. Scores of MPs and cabinet ministers in the Trudeau government have also endorsed the former Bank of Canada and Bank of England governor.
Arnold said he'll be watching especially for how former finance minister Chrystia Freeland pursues Carney, given that he appears to be outpacing her in the race.
"She kind of needs to knock him off his game," Arnold said. "She has to make the case that she's more electable than Mark Carney. And one way to do that is for him to stumble in the debate."
Arnold added that former House leader Karina Gould might also be "more aggressive than she would otherwise be" - especially if she sees a real path to victory in the contest.
But Liberal strategists are also aware that friction between the candidates could be clipped and used by their political opponents if it is particularly cutting. For example, if one candidate lands a real blow against a rival that wins the contest, that exchange could be fodder for the Conservatives in the coming general election.
That's even more of a concern in a shortened race like this one, with opposition parties signalling they intend to topple the Liberal minority government and trigger an election with Parliament set to return with a new prime minister on March 24, MacEachern said.
"It is very much within your prerogative and your right to pursue a tear-down campaign, but I think Liberals will be looking at that through the lens of: how is this really helping Canada, and how is this helping our cause?" he said.
The final boss
That cause, of prolonging their party's hold in office, will face its true challenge in that coming general election. Polls show their main rival in that contest will be Pierre Poilievre's Conservatives.
That looming match-up means Liberals watching this week's leadership debates will also consider how the candidates might perform as the party's standard-bearer in the next federal election, Kattar said.
"(The Conservatives) are so good on their feet, they're so good at (communications), they're so good at driving message. And if you're knocked off your message, how good are you to snap back?"
Arnold added that he is curious for any "preview" of how the candidates would take on Poilievre, and how they might try to "define him" for Canadian voters.
For MacEachern, the candidates - especially the frontrunners - should treat the debates as a chance to send a message to Liberals, and also to the public in general.
And that means aiming attacks at Conservatives, rather than each other.
"It's a big opportunity not to waste," he said.
CAPTION: Liberal leadership candidates, from top left, clockwise, Chrystia Freeland, Mark Carney, Frank Baylis and Karina Gould.
CREDIT: Alex Ballingall

Hamilton-area Today: How the city spends millions in housing cash + will warm week bring the big thaw?

Full text: Good morning! It's Feb. 24, here are the top stories today in the Hamilton area.
Today's traffic and weather
A warmer week is on the horizon. Environment Canada is forecasting a high of 3 C at the airport weather station. The warmer weather will bring 40 kilometre-per-hour winds winds gusting up to 60 km/h. There are no alerts.
Traffic and transit updates
Find the latest provincial road closures and traffic incidents via Ontario 511. Closures in Hamilton are available on the Hamilton police incident feed.
Taking transit today? Find the latest GO Transit service updates here. The latest information on local bus services are available here: Hamilton, Burlington and Oakville.
Top stories on The SpecThe breakdown: $186 million in housing cash
In 2025, the city plans to spend nearly $200 million on housing, shelters, encampments and tenant-support initiatives. Hamilton's housing boss explained how that spending is broken down.
Warm week won't cure winter blues
With temperatures going up - and chances of rain through the week - some residents may have been hoping for a big thaw to clear sidewalks, parking spots and right-hand lanes. But highs of 1 C to 5 C and little rain is not enough to cause a big thaw, Environment Canada meteorologist Shem Willie predicts.
Last election debate focuses on housing
Hamilton Mountain candidates talked about affordability and housing in the final debate for the open provincial seat left by MPP Monique Taylor. Liberal candidate Dawn Danko and NDP candidate Kojo Damptey argued over the contribution of developers, non-profits and other builders. Green candidate Joshua Czerniga focused on fourplexes. The PC candidate skipped the debate.
"Children can't go outside and play"
A Hamilton councillor wants the city to explore legal options to deal with GFL's stink-prone industrial landfill in upper Stoney Creek and potential tax relief for stench-weary area residents.
Election casts shadow on Greenbelt
The provincial election Feb. 27 is one day before the 20th anniversary of the legislation passed to protect the two-million-acre band of farmland, escarpment, forests and wetlands. As a result, Greenbelt supporters aren't quite sure whether to celebrate the anniversary or prepare for protests.
Also, here are some Hamilton area maple syrup festivals and events.

Document 39 of 500

Ontario election: Don't be fooled by proponents of for-profit health care. It always leads to higher costs and decreased care

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/ontario-election-dont-be-fooled-proponents-profit/docview/3169987593/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Ontario+election%3A+Don%27t+be+fooled+by+proponents+of+for-profit+health+care.+It+always+leads+to+higher+costs+and+decreased+care&amp;title=The+Hamilton+Spectator+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2025-02-24&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Hamilton+Spectator+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: After a slow start, the major players in the Ontario election have finally released their game plans for improving primary care. As a family doctor, who is regularly asked if I'm taking on new patients (and grudgingly must decline), I was relieved to see that every party has committed to at least some of what is needed.
Partial solutions, however, are not enough. Especially when some of the largest corporations in Canada are starting to turn the primary care crisis into profit.
First, the good news.
We've known for a long time that expanding team-based primary care makes sense, not only to improve access for patients but to ensure higher quality of care, and to provide better conditions for the health care professionals working in them. Each of the major parties in Ontario have committed to making this the cornerstone of expansion, including a $1.8 billion commitment by the incumbent Conservatives, $4.05 billion by the NDP and $3.1 billion promised by the Liberals. The Association of Family Health Teams of Ontario recommends just over $4 billion of investments over five year, over-and-above any possible changes to family doctor remuneration.
Each party has also promised several other measures, including reducing the nearly 20 hours of paperwork a week family doctors are saddled with and incentives to bring doctors who have left primary care, back into the fold.
The scope of the problem and delayed action are reflected by the substantial but necessary commitments required to reimagine care. Many of the building blocks already in-place, including existing models of team-based Family Health Teams and Community Health Centers. Expanding them and ensuring they work in harmony is no small task.
This is also very achievable. In fact, other countries with high-performing primary care have shown us the way. They've made sure care is technologically integrated, socially accountable, is place-based much like schools and school boards, and available after-hours. In fact, they've been so successful that walk-in clinics are almost non-existent - imagine that! We need to follow.
The urgency for this type of change is only heighted by external threats. Plans to expand "patient first" care are coming up against the increasing privatization of primary care from unexpected sources: grocers and telecom companies like Loblaw and Telus and lesser known private-equity investment firms.
Defenders of corporatizing primary care often say that if care is publicly funded, it doesn't really matter whether or not the profit-motive is at play.
Evidence and experience tell us otherwise.
For-profit care, even when publicly funded, leads to higher costs and worse patient outcomes. For-profit clinics are incentivized to prioritize people who are wealthier and healthy, less expensive to treat, and provide larger profit-margins. In for-profit settings, patients are more likely to be asked to pay out-of-pocket for ancillary or "uninsured" services.
Sometimes, that publicly funded care is inappropriately made contingent on paying these out-of-pocket fees. In fact, in Ontario, we've very recently seen many of these trends play-out in both for-profit cataract surgery, as well as long-term care. In its most extreme cases, publicly funded for-profit care can even be enabled by political corruption, as is being investigated in Alberta and has been previously alleged in Ontario.
These differences matter, not only because of the scale and scope of the primary care crisis, but because choices being made today can set the course for the next few decades. Aspiring premiers must lead with funding commitments conditioned on not-for-profit care, with the necessary support to spread-and-scale team-based care as quickly as possible.
To ensure the sustainable system of a reimagined system of care, leaders must also lead with Ontario's regulatory power to prevent for-profit primary care from becoming the new normal.
Like any winning strategy, our next government's game plan needs to combine a potent offence, with dependable defence.
CREDIT: Danyaal Raza Contributor

Subject: Primary care; Patients; Teams; Clinical outcomes

A guide to the Ontario election's most dramatic ridings to watch. Is yours one of them?

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/guide-ontario-elections-most-dramatic-ridings/docview/3169987591/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=A+guide+to+the+Ontario+election%27s+most+dramatic+ridings+to+watch.+Is+yours+one+of+them%3F&amp;title=The+Hamilton+Spectator+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2025-02-24&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Hamilton+Spectator+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: There are 124 ridings in Ontario and each is represented by one MPP in the legislature. But some ridings have higher stakes and more drama than others.
Here are 10 to watch across the province when the ballots are tallied in the Feb. 27 Ontario election.
Mississauga East-Cooksville
This is ground zero for Liberal Leader Bonnie Crombie in her bid to return the party to prominence following devastating defeats in the last two elections.
After 15 months of leading her small caucus of nine MPPs from the sidelines, she is counting on voters who elected her three times as mayor of Mississauga to give her a seat in the legislature.
While the sprawling city has been a stronghold for Doug Ford's Progressive Conservatives, the riding has no incumbent after MPP Kaleed Rasheed, a former Tory, decided not to run again.
He was a cabinet minister but was ejected from the PCs to sit as an Independent after giving the integrity commissioner wrong information about a 2020 trip to Las Vegas with associates of Ford and a Greenbelt developer.
Running for the PCs is Silvia Gualtieri, an insurance executive and mother-in-law of Brampton Mayor Patrick Brown, who was Ford's predecessor as party leader. Gualtieri is also the sister of Mississauga-Lakeshore Conservative MPP Rudy Cuzzetto.
Crombie and Brown have had plenty of tangles in Peel politics, upping the ante on this race.
The NDP is running renters' advocate Alex Venuto and the Green candidate is David Zeni, a math and science teacher.
Eglinton-Lawrence
There was plenty of drama in this key PC-held Toronto riding well before election day.
Two-term incumbent Conservative Robin Martin decided not to run again, raising Liberal hopes.
Then, as the nomination deadline closed, Liberal candidate Vince Gasparro got an unexpected boost. New Democrat hopeful Natasha Doyle-Merrick backed out to support him, hoping to avoid an anti-Ford vote split that could keep the area Conservative.
"The race in Eglinton-Lawrence is pivotal," she said.
That move set the stage for a two-way race between Gasparro, a finance executive who served as principal secretary to former Toronto mayor John Tory, and Conservative candidate Michelle Cooper.
She is executive director of the PC Ontario Fund, the party's fundraising arm.
"Even the other party has recognized that this is a Bonnie versus Doug race," Crombie's campaign told the Star, speaking of the NDP.
New Democrat Leader Marit Stiles was less enthusiastic, acknowledging she did not know her candidate was going to drop out and saying she is "disappointed" voters will not have an NDP option.
The Green candidate is Leah Tysoe.
Hamilton Centre
In a bid to retake this downtown riding from Independent MPP Sarah Jama, the New Democrats nominated Dr. Robin Lennox, a family physician.
She's an assistant clinical professor at McMaster University's medical school who also provides care to homeless people and others facing substance use challenges.
Jama, who was ejected from the NDP caucus in October 2023 for insubordination, had tried to get back into the party but her application was rejected, angering some on the riding association's executive.
While Lennox has the party's backing, some in the NDP are frustrated at Jama's ouster. She has a dedicated following as an Independent and has won the endorsements of the city's labour council and a major Steelworkers union local.
The Liberals have nominated Eileen Walker, a former social worker and justice of the peace.
Sarah Bokhari, who ran in the riding in 2022, is the Progressive Conservative candidate and Lucia Iannantuono, an electrical engineer, is running for the Greens.
This riding is important to the NDP. Former party leader Andrea Horwath represented it for years before leaving provincial politics to run successfully for mayor of the steel city in 2022.
Etobicoke-Lakeshore
The Liberals are making a determined effort here after coming within 842 votes of Conservative incumbent Christine Hogarth in 2022.
Liberal candidate Lee Fairclough, a former president of St. Mary's Hospital in Kitchener and until recently a senior vice-president at the Centre for Addiction and Mental Health in Toronto, is making a second run at the riding.
She is campaigning heavily on health-care issues.
The NDP candidate is Rozhen Asrani, a product manager in the health-care technology sector who has served as the president of the Mimico Residents Association. The Green Party candidate is Sean Garcia (McClocklin).
York South-Weston
Michael Ford's decision not to run again for the Progressive Conservatives after one term at Queen's Park has thrown this race wide open.
The New Democrats are hoping former one-term MPP Faisal Hassan can make a comeback in the riding that was Liberal for years until the party's 2018 collapse.
Ford, nephew of the PC leader, wrested the riding from Hassan in the 2022 provincial election and quickly found himself in cabinet, but took a leave of absence for health reasons last fall.
Stepping in to run for the Tories is Mohamed Firin, a former member of the Premier's Council on Equality of Opportunity.
Daniel Di Giorgio, a Toronto Catholic school board trustee, is running for the Liberals. The Green candidate is Lilian Barrera.
Haldimand-Norfolk
Bobbi Ann Brady won this riding as an Independent in 2022 after longtime Tory MPP Toby Barrett retired - and threw his full support to her. This election, he's been putting up signs and knocking on doors once again.
The PCs, who placed second in the last election, have recruited Norfolk County Mayor Amy Martin as their candidate in a bid to regain the riding that includes Port Dover, a Lake Erie fishing and beach town famous for fried perch dinners.
Martin is on an unpaid leave of absence for the campaign.
It's tough for Independent candidates to win in Ontario politics, which makes this an interesting test for both Brady and Ford.
The Liberals are running Vandan Patel, the NDP Erica Englert and the Green Party candidate is Anna Massinen.
Oshawa
Voters have their choice of MPPs - the one who's been at Queen's Park for 11 years, and her PC predecessor.
New Democrat Jennifer French, a teacher, is facing a challenge from former MPP Jerry Ouellette, who held the riding from 1995 until French defeated him by more than 7,000 votes in 2014.
The Conservatives sense an opening in Oshawa after French won by a narrow margin of 757 votes over the Conservatives in 2022 as the governing party made a determined push for labour votes, taking NDP ridings in Hamilton, Windsor and Timmins.
Liberal candidate Viresh Bansal has come under fire for anti-Sikh and homophobic comments, which Crombie has condemned. After several days of controversy, the Liberals parted ways with Bansal on Friday. Nicholas Sirgool is running for the Greens.
Ottawa Centre
Former city councillor Catherine McKenney, who placed a strong second in the city's 2022 mayoralty race, is taking a run at this downtown riding for the New Democrats.
The opening came when NDP incumbent Joel Harden, who won by a huge margin in 2022, left provincial politics to run for the New Democrats in the next federal election, expected soon.
In the mayoralty contest, McKenney outperformed the winner, Mark Sutcliffe, in downtown wards.
She faces Liberal candidate Thomas Simpson, a former public servant and vice-president of the Canadian National Institute for the Blind. Canadian Armed Forces veteran Scott Healey is carrying the PC banner and Simon Beckett is running for the Green Party.
Parkdale-High Park
The departure of New Democrat MPP Bhutila Karpoche for federal politics puts this riding, with its mix of neighbourhoods and incomes, in play.
It has seen close races between the NDP and Liberals in the past.
Alexa Gilmour, a United Church minister and mother of five active in social causes, is running for the New Democrats.
The Liberal candidate is Nadia Guerrera, an educator who ran for the party in neighbouring York South-Weston in the 2022 election.
Queen's Park political staffer Justine Teplycky, a former radio reporter and director of communications to Paul Calandra in the Ministry of Municipal Affairs and Housing, is on the ballot for the Conservatives.
Running for the Greens is Anna Gorka, an architect who specializes in sustainable design and has lived in Parkdale since 1982.
Sault Ste. Marie
With incumbent Conservative Ross Romano leaving electoral politics for personal reasons, this previously Liberal riding has become a political battleground.
One of the biggest local issues has been a doctor shortage after a major medical clinic cut thousands of patients loose last spring because their physicians retired or moved on.
Stiles made a point of launching the NDP's health-care platform here and Crombie has also been in the border city to campaign for the Liberals. Ford had to cancel a planned stop Tuesday after a crash at Pearson International Airport resulted in flight delays and cancellations, but he is planning to visit.
The New Democrats are running city Coun. Lisa Vezeau Allen, while the Liberal candidate is small business owner Gurwinder Dusanjh.
Romano is the campaign manager for Conservative hopeful Chris Scott, a former senior political staffer at the legislature who has previously run Romano's campaigns.
The Green candidate is Jaycob Jacques, a student and the party's youth co-chair.
CAPTION: Liberal candidate Vince Gasparro and Progressive Conservative candidate Michelle Cooper are running in the riding of Eglinton-Lawrence.
CREDIT: Rob Ferguson

Document 41 of 500

Letters

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/letters/docview/3169987588/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=unknown&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Letters&amp;title=The+Spectator&amp;issn=11899417&amp;date=2025-02-24&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.9&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Spectator&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Fill empty seat with cutout
The Progressive Conservatives' strategy to avoid debating in the local debates is brilliant. Since they have no candidate to defend their dismal record, the other candidates attack each other reminding viewers of their past failings and policies.
I'd like to see a cardboard cutout of the missing PC candidate placed at their empty chair and the other candidates take turns addressing it while reminding viewers of Doug Ford's disastrous reign. Maybe then the PCs will send candidates to try to defend Ford's record.
Don Zeller, Ancaster
You get what
you vote for
Due to Doug Ford's policies, my vibrant, 65-year-old friend spent more than 24 hours in two different hallways in a Hamilton hospital with a broken hip after falling on snow-covered ice.
As I write this, on day three, there is no date or time for surgery. She is balancing her agony with not being "too demanding" of the overworked nurses.
Go ahead and vote for Ford, but you better pray you do not need emergency surgery in Hamilton.
And please be kind to medical staff if you do. By the way, my 88-year-old mother and I were tortured by hallway medicine in Oshawa last year, so this appears to be the norm in Ontario.
Seniors in pain in hallways with bright lights, loud alarms, staff rushing by, no sleep, needing bedpans and delirium sets in. You get what you vote for my friends.
Adele Barrett, Hamilton
Trump must
be embarrassed
With great interest I and many other Canadians watched the Four Nations championship game Thursday night. While it was a pleasure seeing Canada win 3-2, I feel it will come with great consequences.
I understand that Donald Trump will sign an executive order to immediately impose a 100 per cent tariff on any Canadian hockey team entering the U.S. It must be very embarrassing for this U.S. dictator to realize that his wannabe 51st state has beaten his Team USA.
Edward Beitel, Ancaster
Snowblowers
at the ready
Re: Big snowfall brings back a flurry of memories, Feb. 21
Boy did you hit the nail on the head today, Lorraine! I am married to one of those retired snowblower guys and we live next to another retired snowblower guy (a good friends of ours).
I get such a good chuckle out of seeing who will win the "I got started first" contest. They are out all day clearing driveways. First they clear the driveways and then they wait in anticipation of the plow coming through so that they can attack the windrows.
My husband even invested in a heated vest to minimize his warm-up breaks. It's lovely to see there are still big hearts in our communities. And yes, I too coveted a one-piece snowsuit, but had to wait until my 20s to get one - bright red with a fake fur collar. I loved that suit and I was easy to spot on the slopes!
Catherine Brady, Burlington
Suggesting a new ballot option
Like many, l am concerned about vote splitting that allows a conservative government to be elected with 36 per cent of the vote when by nature we are a centre-left province.
After watching three of the debates and not having any of the Conservatives show up, l find little difference in the platforms of the Liberals, NDP and Greens. So to not waste your vote, there should be another option on the ballot to the person(s) you do not want elected and your vote would then automatically be directed to the candidate that has accumulated the most votes.
Tom Winning, Hamilton

Document 42 of 500

Trump's tariffs shine a light on our vulnerability and why Ontario needs more to sell, not just more markets

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/trumps-tariffs-shine-light-on-our-vulnerability/docview/3169792199/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Trump%27s+tariffs+shine+a+light+on+our+vulnerability+and+why+Ontario+needs+more+to+sell%2C+not+just+more+markets&amp;title=The+Hamilton+Spectator+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2025-02-23&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Hamilton+Spectator+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: At his campaign kickoff, Progressive Conservative leader Doug Ford made it clear: this election is about the economy.
He's right, of course, but the question is, what kind of economy is Ontario building?
The province cannot afford to rely on outdated strategies that leave it vulnerable to global trade disruptions.
The looming economic turbulence, particularly from U.S. President Donald Trump's tariff threats, demands a new approach, one that prioritizes resilience, sovereignty, and innovation.
The old model of attracting foreign branch plants with government subsidies is not enough.
Ontario needs a serious, long-term economic strategy that puts its own companies and workers first.
For too long, Canada has been dependent on exporting raw materials while relying on foreign multinationals for high-value industries.
This has left the economy exposed to the whims of global markets, resulting in poor terms of trade and limited economic security.
If Ontario wants to be truly competitive, it must shift from being a supplier of low-margin commodities to a producer of high-value goods and services, like intellectual property and digital services.
That means strengthening the province's fastest-growing companies and ensuring that Ontario is not just participating in the global economy but shaping it.
More bluntly, we need to be producing more of the 21st-century products the world needs.
Ontario is already home to world-class innovation.
Our cybersecurity firms protect critical digital infrastructure. Our health-tech companies are improving global health care systems. Our cleantech sector is commercializing research that reduces emissions and creates high-paying jobs.
These are just some of the industries that will define the future, but they need better support to grow, scale, and stay in Canada.
Without a clear strategy to expand these businesses globally, Ontario risks watching them get snapped up by foreign interests and losing out on the economic benefits of their success.
Governments in every major innovation hub play an active role in supporting domestic industry, and Ontario should be no different.
Entrepreneurs need better access to growth capital to ensure that promising businesses have the funding to scale and compete globally.
The provincial government must also take strategic procurement seriously, prioritizing Ontario-made technologies and services instead of relying on foreign firms.
Every dollar spent on local innovation strengthens the economy and keeps public money working within the province.
Education and workforce development are equally critical. Ontario's talent shortage in AI, cybersecurity, and advanced manufacturing is a major barrier to growth.
More investment in education, training, and talent attraction is needed to ensure the province has the skilled workforce required for an economy of the future.
Economic security also means having a real industrial strategy. In an increasingly unpredictable global economy, trade relationships are fragile, and tariffs are expected to rise.
Former Bank of Canada governor Stephen Poloz has warned that protectionism is on the rise worldwide. Ontario cannot afford to be naive.
The province needs a clear plan that ensures its economic resilience and reduces its dependence on foreign multinationals for key technologies and services.
This requires more than vague commitments - it demands a deliberate effort to expand high-value sectors and ensure Ontario companies can win in global markets.
And Ontario's economy does not exist in isolation. When Ontario businesses succeed, all of Canada benefits. That's why eliminating interprovincial trade barriers and creating a more co-ordinated national strategy is important.
In recent weeks, political leaders have spoken about a "Team Canada" approach to economic challenges. It is the right instinct, but words alone are not enough.
What's needed is action - concrete policies that support Canadian companies and allow them to thrive in an increasingly competitive world.
These solutions address old economy challenges, and while they must be tackled, they are not enough on their own.
The bigger question is: what does the world need that we can build and sell? Access to markets is important, but the real focus should be on creating new, high-value products and services that position Ontario as an indispensable player in the global economy.
The challenge isn't just about reducing trade barriers - it's about ensuring we have something unique and competitive to offer.
This election isn't just about navigating the latest trade dispute - it's about ensuring Ontario's economy is built to withstand whatever comes next.
We can't lurch from crisis to crisis, reacting to every shift in global politics. We need a long-term strategy that makes Ontario stronger, more competitive, and less vulnerable to forces beyond our control.
Prosperity doesn't happen by accident. It comes from bold leadership, smart investment, and a commitment to building industries that will define the future.
Ontario has the talent, the innovation, and the ambition to lead. What it needs now is a government willing to back that vision with action.
The question in this election isn't just who will manage the next four years - it's who has a plan to make Ontario strong for the next 40.
CAPTION: Ontario is home to world-class innovation in areas such as cybersecurity, writes Marcella Arthur, Mike Andrade and Skaidra Puodziunas. But they need support to grow, scale, and stay in Canada.
CREDIT: Marcella Arthur, Mike Andrade and Skaidra Puodziunas Contributors

Document 43 of 500

Hamilton Mountain candidates spar over solutions to housing, affordability crisis

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/hamilton-mountain-candidates-spar-over-solutions/docview/3169792190/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Hamilton+Mountain+candidates+spar+over+solutions+to+housing%2C+affordability+crisis&amp;title=The+Hamilton+Spectator+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2025-02-23&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Hamilton+Spectator+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: How important are developers to housing?
In Hamilton Mountain riding, it depends on who you ask.
"The fact is we need to work with developers and they're important members of our community," Liberal candidate Dawn Danko said at a debate hosted by Cable 14 Friday night. "If we don't work with them, we are not going to build the way we want."
"They're developers, not business community members ... Nobody is denying they build homes, but we have alternatives," NDP candidate Kojo Damptey replied, pointing to non-profits, land trusts and co-ops. "Those are the things we want to (use), because we don't want to be influenced by developers."
Green candidate Joshua Czerniga, meanwhile, said his party wants to encourage fourplexes along major transit routes and provide $25,000 loans for people to add affordable rental units to their properties.
Missing from this back-and-forth was Progressive Conservative candidate Monica Ciriello, a top city bylaw official and one of many provincial Tory candidates who appear to have skipped televised debates during the election campaign.
Host Mike Fortune said at the top of the show Ciriello couldn't attend due to a scheduling conflict. In an email, a spokesperson said Ciriello wanted to attend but her campaign did not receive an invitation until a day before the debate.
Cable 14 has said it reached out to all candidates starting in early February. Organizers tried again last week after two local PC campaigns said they were unaware of those efforts.
A quick remark from Danko aside, viewers never heard her absence referenced again.
Instead, candidates focused their criticism on the political parties in the room - and, as has been the case during the campaign, at each other.
As Danko touched on Damptey's past support for encampments, the NDP challenger cut her off, bringing up how her husband, Coun. John-Paul Danko, voted in favour of a 2023 protocol that allowed tents in parks subject to certain restrictions. (The councillor recently voted to scrap that protocol.)
"I delegated at city council (saying) that response wasn't needed. What is needed affordable homes," said Damptey, a community activist, university lecturer and former head of the Hamilton Centre for Civic Inclusion.
"As my husband was mentioned, I would like to correct the record that he is not the person running for this election. I am, Dawn Danko, in case you missed that," Danko later said.
Still, Danko took a moment to defend her husband, telling Damptey the protocol was needed in part because the city "was being sued by people that have supported your campaign."
"My husband actually moved the motion to remove that (protocol) because we need to move to shelter spaces, wraparound supports (and) transitional housing," she said.
The candidates differed on solutions to the housing and affordability crisis, which dominated the debate.
Danko and the Liberals want to implement fair, phased-in rent control, scrap punitive taxes, reduce wait times at the Landlord and Tenant Board and rapidly increase housing starts by removing development red tape.
Damptey, who's running for the open seat left behind by longtime MPP Monique Taylor, said the NDP want to support families with rebate checks and "make sure there's a full rental cap." He said more housing is needed, but didn't say how the party would get that built - beyond working with alternatives like non-profits, land trusts and co-ops.
For, the Greens' Czerniga, Damptey's comments rung hollow.
"What I find most interesting about the NDP is they've been the government in waiting for many years now and they talk about these things, but nothing ever changes, nothing ever happens."
Czerniga is no stranger to the NDP, or politics.
For more than a decade, he served on the NDP executive branch, working alongside Taylor as her chief financial officer. In 2021, when he sought the nomination for the riding's federal seat, Taylor endorsed him.
"What I slowly saw was the decay after Marit Stiles took over," Czerniga said in response to question about why he left the NDP last year. "I saw the yelling, the screaming across the floor, the telling me what Doug Ford has done wrong ... but never once presenting solutions."
Sebastian Bron is a reporter at The Hamilton Spectator. sbron@thespec.com.
CAPTION: Dawn Danko, Liberal candidate for Hamilton Mountain.Joshua Czerniga, Green candidate for Hamilton Mountain.Kojo Damptey, NDP candidate for Hamilton Mountain.The provincial Hamilton Mountain debate Friday night included (from left) Liberal candidate Dawn Danko, NDP candidate Kojo Damptey and Green candidate Joshua Czerniga.
CREDIT: Sebastian Bron

Document 44 of 500

No one cares about the Ontario election. Was that Doug Ford's plan?

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/no-one-cares-about-ontario-election-was-that-doug/docview/3169792181/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=No+one+cares+about+the+Ontario+election.+Was+that+Doug+Ford%27s+plan%3F&amp;title=The+Hamilton+Spectator+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2025-02-23&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Hamilton+Spectator+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: In a recurring feature, Susan Delacourt, a small-l liberal, and Matt Gurney, a small-c conservative, bring their different perspectives - and shared commitment to civil disagreement - to the political debates of the moment.
Susan Delacourt: I don't know if you've noticed, Matt, but we are days away from an Ontario election. While it's dangerous to make predictions in politics these days, you want to take a guess at what we might see when all the votes are tallied Thursday night?
Matt Gurney: You know, I'll get to your question in a moment. But I have to share something first. Readers will have guessed how Susan and I do these. We message back and forth, clean up the text and then send it to the editors. Do you know what happened, Susan, between you sending the first message and me replying with this one? It's very funny.
SD: Given how many things you do in a day, I'm not sure I'd even hazard a guess.
MG: Ha. Well, I'll just tell you. I saw your message and decided I'd rather type on my computer than my phone. So I went to my office. As I was heading there, I checked the mail slot at the side of my house. And therein I found ... a $200 cheque from the government of Ontario! I'd been wondering where my pre-election loot was.
SD: So does that have you feeling all warm about Doug Ford's chances in this election?
MG: It has me slightly closer to a new PS5. My feelings about Ford's chances haven't changed much in a long time. He's cruising to a majority. Perhaps an even larger one than he has. I don't think any poll has suggested anything else in a long time, and the recent announcement by the Liberals that they're pivoting to focus on the NDP is, despite Bonnie's Crombie's valiant effort to deny the blindingly obvious, a sign that the opposition parties know it's a fight for second. What can I say? If I reincarnate, in my next life, I want opponents like the ones Doug has faced over the years.
SD: Forgive me, but what's a PS5?
MG: A PlayStation 5. I find the bribe cheques so odious and craven that I'm insisting on spending the money on something ridiculous and unnecessary. I'll make a charity donation of my own money, but I'm taking what Ford is sending me and wasting it on a frill.
SD: It wasn't so long ago that Chrystia Freeland made a fiery exit from Justin Trudeau's cabinet decrying "costly political gimmicks," and I don't get the sense she's been rewarded for that statement of principle. But that's a story for another day - another contest, also creeping up quickly on us.
Based on what is mainly anecdotal evidence, I do think that at least some Ontario voters are not keen to reward Ford for calling this election. Donald Trump also took some steam out of Ford's Captain Canada campaign when he granted the 30-day reprieve on some tariffs. So I do think Ford has probably had to work harder than he expected in this election to justify it happening.
MG: Honestly, I don't see any sign that Ford is working particularly hard. He's not hesitating to take long breaks from campaigning to jet down to Washington and accomplish absolutely squat while he's there. This has been one of the strangest elections I've ever observed or covered, to the extent that I'm covering it. I don't think I have heard anyone discussing it out there in the real world even once. I know it's a busy news cycle on a lot of fronts, but it seems to me like the opposition parties are trying and failing to get anyone to notice them, Ford is trying to play act as Captain Canada, and another PC majority looms.
SD: I'll tell you why I don't like the trail this has blazed for the federal election. You're right that Ford has mailed a bit of it in, especially when it comes to giving detailed, properly costed platform proposals. He and his candidates haven't been all that great at participating in debates. I can see how all parties, but especially federal Conservatives, would see this as a new standard in what one can get away with when it comes to campaigning.
MG: The last few Ontario elections have been like this, though. I'm not saying there haven't been important issues. The last election was in the immediate aftermath of the pandemic and the provincial government's total abandonment of Ottawa during the convoy. There were lots of important issues. But the electorate seems massively checked out. That's probably something we can't really explore the depths of in this format. But I think it's something we should be aware of and probably be concerned about. What happens if you throw an election and no one shows up?
SD: It's a very good question. I'll be watching those turnout numbers this week. It was less than 44 per cent in the 2022 election - an all-time low. I would not be surprised if this one sets a new apathy record. I do think it's interesting how we're being told to expect a change election federally, while Ontario voters seem pretty content with more of the same. Perhaps all the chaos we're seeing south of the Canada-U.S. border is making Canadians a little more nervous about rampant change. But that's just a theory.
MG: It could be. My own theory is that it's closer to "I don't have the spare energy for Ontario politics. Doug is fine." Whatever it is, seems to be working out as the PC leader intended. Thanks for the PS5.
SD: If the race is for second place, as you suggested, I will also be watching for clues about how Ontarians are feeling about the Liberals and New Democrats, federally. Some of the latest polls suggest that the Liberals, especially under prospective leader Mark Carney, are benefiting from a collapse of the NDP's support. Should the Ontario NDP place third on Thursday, there will be speculation about whether the federal NDP will be in any hurry to have a snap election anytime soon.
MG: Yeah, there'll obviously be some lessons to glean from the provincial result that'll bear on the federal race. Frankly, if the provincial NDP totally tanks and federal polls keep showing Jagmeet Singh heading in the same direction, whether or not we have an election is probably going to stop being anything the federal NDP have a say in. If you're Carney, why wouldn't you pull the trigger?
SD: A spring filled with snap elections - just the thing for a nervous nation. It gives us lots to write about, though.
CAPTION: Progressive Conservative Leader Doug Ford speaks to reporters, accompanied by other Council of the Federation members, at the Mayflower Hotel in Washington, on Feb. 12, 2025. T
CREDIT: Susan Delacourt, Matt Gurney

ONTARIO ELECTION 2025: When is the provincial election, how do I vote? This guide can help

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/ontario-election-2025-when-is-provincial-how-do-i/docview/3169792176/se-2?accountid=15090

To help you navigate the voting process, we've put together this helpful guide to aid in understanding where and when you can vote and what are your rights as a voter.
We won't tell you who to vote for - that's up to you to decide - but hopefully we can make your job as an elector easier.
