Document 46 of 500

She waited 40 hours in the ER, surrounded by sick patients. It was the most dangerous place she could be - and a worsening reality in Ontario

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/she-waited-40-hours-er-surrounded-sick-patients/docview/3169792173/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=She+waited+40+hours+in+the+ER%2C+surrounded+by+sick+patients.+It+was+the+most+dangerous+place+she+could+be+-+and+a+worsening+reality+in+Ontario&amp;title=The+Hamilton+Spectator+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2025-02-23&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Hamilton+Spectator+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Christina Shehata was told she had a life-threatening condition soon after she arrived at Kingston General Hospital's emergency department.
The 51-year-old with metastatic breast cancer had a high fever, and tests revealed she lacked a type of white blood cell that fights infection, putting her at further risk.
Diagnosed with febrile neutropenia, a medical emergency for cancer patients, Christina quickly received IV antibiotics and was told she needed to be admitted.
But no in-patient beds were available. Instead, Christina remained in the emergency department. It would take 40 hours for her to get moved to an inpatient bed.
"I was terrified for her," said her husband, Dr. Adam Shehata, a family physician.
He said Christina received appropriate care in the ED but waiting near other patients put her at high risk for an infection.
"The area she was in was essentially a row of hospital gurneys separated only by curtains with sick patients on either side of her," Adam said. "Her risk of infection was so high and there was nothing I could do to protect her."
Doug Ford promised to end hallway medicine. But Ontario's ER wait times have gotten worse in the past three years, confidential report shows
Across Ontario, patients are waiting hours - sometimes days - in EDs, a problem that has gotten substantially worse over the last three years, according to provincial data obtained by the Star.
In many hospitals, patients waiting in the ED are being treated in hallways, storage areas and other unconventional spaces due to a lack of beds elsewhere in the hospital. This kind of "hallway medicine" frustrates patients but also risks their health and puts further stress on hospital staff struggling to cope in crowded conditions.
Health-care issues, including the family doctor shortage and hallway medicine, have emerged as key issues in the Feb. 27 snap election. During his 2018 campaign, Doug Ford promised to end hallway health care, making the pledge a key part of the Progressive Conservative platform.
Four months after getting elected, Ford stated, "One patient treated in a hallway is one patient too many," and said it was "unacceptable" for patients to wait hours to see a doctor or to get care in hospital hallways.
"Since then, it's gone from bad to worse," said Dr. Raghu Venugopal, an emergency physician in Toronto. "What bad hallway medicine was has only deteriorated to chair- and waiting-room medicine."
Venugopal cites a long list of conditions he had to treat in hospital chairs, including a brain bleed, a broken spine, a bowel obstruction and a heart attack.
Research shows patients who "board" for hours in the ED, particularly older patients, can face a higher risk of death.
"It isn't just that patients get treated in hallways," said Dr. Andrew Petrosoniak, an emergency physician and trauma team leader at St. Michael's Hospital. "Patients who require hospital-level care, their risk of death goes up the longer they're in the emergency department. You can debate the percentages, but there is a not insignificant increase in mortality."
A report by Ontario Health, not previously made public, shows that between April 2022 and October 2024, wait times for patients needing an in-patient bed at the 90th percentile ranged from roughly 32 hours to just over 40 hours. This means 90 per cent of patients stayed in the ED for these lengths of time or less but 10 per cent stayed longer. Pre-pandemic, wait times for an in-patient bed at the 90th percentile ranged roughly from 20 to 30 hours.
Experts say a combination of factors are leading to extended ED waits: an insufficient number of hospital beds to admit new patients; a lack of long-term-care and home-care options, meaning patients who no longer need acute care have to remain in hospital; people relying on EDs due to a shortage of family doctors and difficulties getting diagnostic imaging and specialist care; and ongoing staffing shortages that limit how many patients hospitals can treat quickly.
In recent years, hospitals have seen a rise in the number of very sick patients coming to the ED. These more complex patients, many with a combination of chronic illnesses, require more time to stabilize, assess and treat, which leads to longer wait times for other patients.
"We're seeing more and more frail elderly, and the complexity of the cases that we're seeing is that much greater," said Ottawa-based emergency physician Dr. James Worrall, who noted that Ontario's population of people over 65 has increased dramatically in the past 20 years.
Since 2018, the Ford government has added 3,500 new hospital beds and has said 3,000 more are in the pipeline, tied to 50 new hospital projects. The province has said it has also made significant investments in long-term care. Before the election, the PCs pledged a $1.4 billion investment to connect every Ontarian to primary care by 2029.
In a statement, the Ministry of Health noted the province's "ED landscape and patient demographic have evolved over the years in particular from the pandemic experience," an issue "not unique to Ontario."
Adam, who took Christina to Kingston General Hospital on Feb. 9, said he does not blame the hospital or its staff for her long wait. He said doctors and nurses provided excellent care and understood the couple's worries, but said they couldn't move her due to capacity issues.
During the overnight hours, it got so loud that hospital staff gave Christina earplugs. Earlier that evening, Adam said, nearby patients were ill and coughing and a patient next to Christina had to use a commode.
"The stench was everywhere," said Adam, who was told no isolation rooms were available. "Everyone can understand why they wouldn't want their sick wife, who doesn't have the ability to fight off infection, beside someone who is defecating on one side and coughing on the other."
After more than 24 hours, Adam said Christina was moved out of the area with curtained-off gurneys and into a room with a door that closed. Though still in the ED, Adam said the new space offered more privacy and protection.
In a statement, Kingston Health Sciences Centre (KHSC) said it has experienced a "sustained increase in patient volumes and continues to operate at high capacity" like many hospitals in Canada. It said this is primarily due to "population growth, as well as a growing number of patients with more complex needs than ever before."
KHSC said its teams are "committed to providing timely, high-quality, safe care" and that "rooms and care space allocation" is based on "several factors, including patient acuity and specific medical needs."
Dr. Candace McNaughton, an emergency medicine physician at Sunnybrook hospital, said the challenges Ontario EDs face are more significant now than before COVID. She said individual hospitals and their staff are "doing everything they can with the limited resources they have," but it's not enough.
"Most of my health encounters start with, 'I am so sorry for the wait,' " she said.
Recent research led by McNaughton showed that since 2020, more adults are leaving Ontario EDs without being seen by a physician. The study, published in December, found these patients face a 14 per cent higher risk of death or hospitalization within seven days when compared to patients who left the ED without being seen by a physician before 2020.
McNaughton, a scientist at Sunnybrook Research Institute, said the study raises a "concerning signal" that should prompt system-wide policy interventions.
"There's something wrong with the system; people are not doing as well as they did pre-2020," she said.
After three days, Christina left the hospital following antibiotic treatment and she's now well enough to resume cancer therapy. She said she hopes her experience will highlight longstanding system challenges and push politicians to act.
"It was ridiculous what I experienced," Christina said. "People lining the hallways. People behind these flimsy curtains. People not able to get seen by a doctor or get admitted right away.
"It shouldn't be this way."
CAPTION: Christina Shehata was only separated from other sick patients by curtains during her hours-long ER wait."We're seeing more and more frail elderly and the complexity of the cases that we're seeing is that much greater," said Ottawa-based emergency physician Dr. James Worrall.Christina and Adam Shehata. Christina has metastatic breast cancer and went to Kingston General for a life-threatening condition. She got the appropriate medical care but waited 40 hours in the ER before being admitted to hospital for an inpatient bed.Christina and Adam Shehata. She got the appropriate medical care but waited 40 hours in the ER before being admitted to hospital for an inpatient bed.
CREDIT: Megan Ogilvie, Kenyon Wallace

Document 47 of 500

On the campaign trail with Marit Stiles, enthusiasm is high - but recognition is low. Here's why the NDP leader is struggling to be heard

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/on-campaign-trail-with-marit-stiles-enthusiasm-is/docview/3169866208/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=On+the+campaign+trail+with+Marit+Stiles%2C+enthusiasm+is+high+-+but+recognition+is+low.+Here%27s+why+the+NDP+leader+is+struggling+to+be+heard&amp;title=The+Hamilton+Spectator+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2025-02-22&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Hamilton+Spectator+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Meet Marit Stiles.
If you don't know much about her, then you know this much: Stiles remains a political unknown to many Ontarians.
Up close on the campaign trail, the NDP leader has a winning way - wooing voters and waxing eloquent with volunteers.
But zoom out to the big picture, and her New Democratic Party is falling behind - at least according to the telltale public opinion polls.
Why the disconnect? Why can Stiles connect face-to-face on the ground, while her party is losing ground province-wide?
Welcome to the winter election of 2025, where the public is disengaged - or engaged only on issues that aren't vote-winners for the NDP: Donald Trump, for example, and the economy, where the Tories poll best.
Everyone is talking about Trump's presidential musings, and voters are hearing a lot from Progressive Conservative Leader Doug Ford as he seeks re-election as premier. All of which makes it harder for Stiles to be seen and heard - and recognized.
Blame the provincial New Democratic Party for her predicament. Nobody could be found to run against her for the party leadership two years ago, which is why Stiles won by acclamation - but without the critical acclaim that raises a politician's profile.
Blame the federal party. Nationally, the NDP is falling fast in public opinion polls, draining crossover support from Ontario voters and volunteers in the home-stretch of the province's Feb. 27 election.
All that said - and unseen - you wouldn't know it from watching Stiles on the stump. She is indefatigable and unflappable despite the bad tidings in the polling.
Hopping aboard her campaign bus Friday, I watch her reaching out to party loyalists who are fanning out to "get out the vote" - GOTV in the GTA. Stiles begins her day by casting her own ballot in an advance poll, then heads out to record a podcast - as politicians do in 2025.
Painted in the party's distinctive orange livery, the bus motors through Toronto traffic and delivers us to the Danforth. This is where the NDP is hoping to reclaim the Beaches-East York riding it lost to the rival Liberals in 2022.
Behind the wheel on this whistle-stop tour is Ewen Steele, the trusty driver for hire whom the NDP always turns to for election charters. Over the years, he's kept the campaign on an even keel while ferrying a busload of seemingly unbalanced journalists.
In the election of 2025, however, there are fewer media - and so no media buses laid on by any of the campaigns. Today I am the sole political journalist invited on board, tagging along for a ride on the inside.
Steele steers the bus past fresh snowbanks dotting the Danforth and I slip inside to watch local candidate Kate Dupuis warming up the troops in the winter chill. They are primed for the leader's grand entrance:
"When they come in, we just let out a big cheer for 'The next premier of Ontario,' " Dupuis instructs them.
"Oh! Here she comes!"
On cue, the crowd lets out a lusty cheer and chants, "Marit! Marit! Marit!"
Impressively, if implausibly, Stiles keeps a straight face as she is introduced to supporters yet again as "the next premier of Ontario." After a decade working for ACTRA, the Canadian actors' union, and many more years in politics, she understands performance art.
"Wow! This is awesome!" the leader gushes to the gaggle of supporters, beaming widely. "That momentum is what I'm feeling all across the province."
Rallying the troops, Stiles raises the volume. When she's on a roll, this happy warrior lapses into traces of the Newfoundland accent she left behind when moving to Ontario for university.
Turning more serious, she bemoans the betrayals and bungling of Ford's Tories over their past two terms in power - on the Greenbelt boondoggle, housing shortages and health care underfunding. But it is all about affordability.
Not unlike Ford and Liberal Leader Bonnie Crombie, Stiles never stops talking about pocketbook politics. She rails against grocery price "gouging," prompting party workers to hoist their placards proclaiming, "Fight Rising Costs" (with "Marit Stiles for Premier" printed on the obverse).
The NDP's answer? A monthly grocery rebate for eligible Ontarians depending on income, estimated to cost more than $4 billion a year.
Stiles also vows to remove road tolls for anyone and everyone travelling on Highway 407, whether truckers or commuters - at a multibillion-dollar cost she won't disclose (the party opposes price signals for vehicles, no matter the cost to the environment or the treasury).
Another pocketbook pitch, price to be determined.
And then her grand finale: "I will be the premier that's on the side of every single person in this province."
But she can only be premier if the small crowd of party loyalists in this local campaign office can execute on her exhortation to "flip this seat to (NDP) orange ... here in Beaches-East York." Put another way, the NDP is coveting a seat now held by Liberal MPP Mary-Margaret McMahon, a high profile former city councillor who would be hard to knock off.
Which means that Stiles is once again pitting New Democrats against Liberals in this riding, as in others where Ford's Progressive Conservatives hope to profit from vote-splitting among progressive voters.
Targeting Liberals is a recurring theme for the NDP leader. I watched her make a direct appeal for Liberal votes at her campaign launch three weeks ago, when she caricatured Crombie as a closet Conservative who would fit comfortably in Ford's cabinet.
Back then, she asked traditional Liberal supporters to choose New Democrats as the true progressives who could beat Ford's Tories this time. The problem with that pitch is that the record shows New Democrats couldn't make it happen in the last two elections when the stars were truly in alignment - voters had confined a tired Liberal party to the penalty box after 15 years in power.
Now, the Liberal brand is making a slow comeback in Ontario - notably after Justin Trudeau announced his intention to resign as prime minster - and the NDP is falling further behind. Even from the cocoon of a campaign bus, it's impossible to avoid the data points, because pollsters are connecting the dots.
Most surveys show Ford's Tories holding more than 40 per cent support, Crombie's Liberals closing in on 30 per cent, and Stiles's NDP struggling to stay above 20 per cent. Beyond party preference, however, Stiles is lagging in leader preference.
Awkwardly, the latest Abacus Data poll shows only 16 per cent of voters think she'd make the best premier, well behind Crombie at 23 per cent and Ford at 35 per cent.
In the latest Leger poll, Stiles is strangely an also-ran: fully 31 per cent of Ontarians said they "don't know/do not recognize the name" of the NDP leader (compared to just 13 per cent who had no recognition or opinion of Crombie, and 4 per cent for Ford).
Which means that after two years on the job - where Job One is making a name for yourself and your party as the legislature's official opposition leader - one in three voters don't know or don't recognize her work in the campaign home-stretch. Possibly that's because the NDP caucus was distracted by its own internal battles over the Israel-Gaza conflict a year ago, with Stiles expelling pro-Palestinian MPP Sarah Jama for defying her (and then facing pushback from Toronto-St. Paul's MPP Jill Andrew, who publicly challenged the leader's decision on Jama).
Now, the Liberals have turned the NDP's traditional vote-borrowing strategy against them: Crombie is asking New Democrats to help her close the gap with Ford's Tories and to stop fighting each other.
Sitting in the back of her campaign bus after the rally, Stiles is all smiles until I ask about the Liberal strategy. No matter that it is an echo of the old NDP playbook, she is scathing.
"It sounds desperate and it sounds to me like they've given up," she says in our interview, quickly contrasting the Liberal strategy with her own seemingly clear-eyed objectives:
"Bonnie Crombie has quit the fight. I will not quit," Stiles tells me. "I'm not running to be the opposition leader. I'm running to be the premier and to form a government."
She ridicules the Liberal appeal as risible, arguing that only New Democrats are true to themselves - notwithstanding the internal contradictions that riddle all party platforms, including hers.
"The difference is this: I am speaking to people and their values," she muses. "I'm not just saying, 'Put aside your values, put aside what you believe in and just vote for me.' "
The problem with the pitch is that it's not catching, not yet. Voters can't align with your values if they can't connect with your campaign.
As I leave her whistle-stop tour - next stop Brampton - Stiles insists she's unstoppable: "This last week is absolutely critical - elections matter."
Elections do matter. But only if your message matters to voters before election day, now just days away.

Subject: Public opinion; Political campaigns; Polls & surveys; Voters; Political leadership; Elections; Political parties

Company / organization: Name: Democratic Party; NAICS: 813940

Title: On the campaign trail with Marit Stiles, enthusiasm is high - but recognition is low. Here's why the NDP leader is struggling to be heard

Publication title: The Hamilton Spectator (Online); Hamilton

Publication year: 2025

Publication date: Feb 22, 2025

Section: politics/political-opinion

Publisher: Torstar Syndication Services, a Division of Toronto Star Newspapers Limited

Place of publication: Hamilton

Document 48 of 500

Doug Ford promised to end hallway medicine. But Ontario's ER wait times have gotten worse in the past three years, confidential report shows

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/doug-ford-promised-end-hallway-medicine-ontarios/docview/3169499591/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Doug+Ford+promised+to+end+hallway+medicine.+But+Ontario%27s+ER+wait+times+have+gotten+worse+in+the+past+three+years%2C+confidential+report+shows&amp;title=The+Hamilton+Spectator+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2025-02-22&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Hamilton+Spectator+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: We've all heard the stories: Your neighbour who waited almost 12 hours in agony to see if her appendix needed to be removed. An elderly woman with a suspected brain bleed lying on a stretcher for eight hours. A 99-year-old who waited four hours for an ambulance after collapsing on the floor of her retirement home.
They all got the care they needed, eventually. But the long waits they had to endure are illustrative of a disturbing trend happening in Ontario emergency departments, borne out by data that has never been made public.
According to an internal provincial report obtained by the Star, Ontario patients visiting EDs in the past three years waited longer than they did in the previous 13 years.
The report, prepared by Ontario Health but not made public, provides sweeping, multi-year data for key performance indicators going back to 2008, offering a long-term overview of the health of Ontario's EDs under successive provincial governments.
Health care in Ontario has become a big provincial election issue. Despite Progressive Conservative Leader Doug Ford's 2018 election promise to end hallway medicine, the data shows that wait times for ambulance offloads, initial physician assessments and admissions to in-patient beds became notably worse in the last three years, while patients spent more time overall in EDs.
In a response to the Star, the Ministry of Health said the province's ED landscape, along with patient demographics, have evolved "in particular from the pandemic experience."
The ministry said it invested $134 million in 2023-24 to improve ED services in 165 hospitals, provided $14.7 million for a strategy to train and upskill emergency nurses, and launched a program to support physicians in small and rural hospitals. It added that it is also working to connect all residents with a family doctor through its "Primary Care Action Team," led by former federal health minister Dr. Jane Philpott.
"Wait times are really much more of a symptom than the disease itself, and more reflective of the stresses on our hospital system and our health-care system writ large," said Dr. Michael Herman, an Ottawa-area emergency physician. "We're trying to do the best with what we can, but it's an increasing struggle to try and meet the demands on our system."
The new data paints a picture of EDs across the province still struggling to recover from system-wide cracks exposed by the COVID-19 pandemic, from professional burnout, nurse shortages and increasingly complex patients, to insufficient numbers of in-patient beds, primary care options and long-term-care spaces.
Here we break down some of the most striking data from the confidential report.
One of the clearest trends in the data, presented as measurements of the 90th percentile, is that between 2021-22 and October 2024 (the most recent month captured by the report), patients across Ontario spent more time in EDs compared to the previous 13 years.
The data show that patients at the 90th percentile were spending anywhere from about seven-and-a-half hours to just over 10 hours in EDs between 2008-09 and 2020-21. This means that 90 per cent of patients stayed in the ED for these lengths of time or less. But that also means 10 per cent of patients stayed longer.
During the last three years, the 90th percentile length of stay jumped to a range of about nine-and-a-half hours to a high of nearly 13 hours (January 2024). As of October 2024, length of stay at the 90th percentile was 11.7 hours.
Ottawa-based emergency physician Dr. James Worrall said most long wait times are related to what he calls the "back door" - getting patients out of the ED - rather than the number of people showing up at the front door. The problem, he said, is that when beds are full elsewhere in the hospital, admitted patients have to stay in the ED until space is available. The result? Stretchers in hallways.
"So the knock-on effect is crippling the efficiency of the emergency department, which drives up the wait times for everybody," Worrall said.
Worrall uses the analogy of someone washing the dishes at their kitchen sink who can't put the clean dishes in the drying rack.
"Very quickly your sink fills up with dishes that have already been washed and it's really challenging to wash any new dishes," he said. "That's basically what we're dealing with."

The data shows that in the 13 years prior to 2021-22, most ED patients at the 90th percentile waited between about three and four hours for an initial assessment by a physician. That range rose to between four and just over four-and-a-half hours between 2021-22 and October 2024. Dr. Raghu Venugopal, an emergency physician who works the overnight shift at three busy Toronto EDs, said longer waits for an initial assessment are attributable to several causes: patients who are more elderly and medically complex; patients with lower-acuity problems but with no other option but to visit the ED; and fewer places to see patients due to overcrowding.
"An elderly patient with cognitive impairment who cannot speak for themselves and is found on the ground needs a trauma, cardiac, neurological, metabolic and infectious work-up," Venugopal said. "The share of these patients is increasing and that takes physician time. An ER visit for such a patient is pretty much a compressed admission to hospital. This makes others wait."
He added that he is seeing an increasing number of patients come to the ED with less urgent needs because they have nowhere else to go. This could include patients seeking ODSP food voucher signatures, insulin and hypertension medication and prescription refills.
"Because they have no physical complaint, they will wait."
In 2021-22, the 90th percentile for time to an in-patient bed began around 20 hours. This means that 90 per cent of patients spent 20 hours or less in the ED waiting for an in-patient bed once a decision was made to admit them.
While 20 hours is no doubt a long time to wait for a bed, this time was more or less in line with the trend of the previous 13 years.
But by January 2022, that time had reached 35 hours. For the next two-and-a-half years, wait times for an in-patient bed at the 90th percentile were between about 27 hours and just over 40 hours.
Herman, a member of the Canadian Association of Emergency Physicians' public affairs committee, explains that a certain percentage of beds in hospitals will be occupied by alternate-level-of-care (ALC) patients who are waiting to be discharged to long-term care or home care. But when there aren't enough spaces in the community, these patients remain in hospital, creating backups that trickle down to the ED, which, Herman said, "is the one place in the health-care system where we can't say 'we're full.' "
This has the unfortunate result of patients waiting in ED hallways, alcoves and even in front of nursing stations bustling with activity, said Erin Ariss, a registered nurse and president of the Ontario Nurses' Association.
"You can imagine being a patient in front of a desk where it is chaos. There's phone calls, consultations between nurses and physicians," she said. "There's an inability to have any dignity or privacy in those spaces."
The Health Ministry said it has made "permanent investments" to support the creation of additional beds in regions struggling with this issue, increasing funding to the sector by four per cent in the last two fiscal years. It also says it has introduced "patient-flow initiatives" across the province.
The data shows that ambulance offload times - the amount of time it takes for a patient to be transferred from paramedics to the care of a hospital emergency department - at the 90th percentile began to rise noticeably in 2021-22 from a low of about 40 minutes to a high of nearly 70 minutes.
The following year, offload times rose even higher, reaching almost 110 minutes in October 2022, a far cry from what the province says should take 30 minutes at the 90th percentile. In 2023-24, times dropped slightly but in some months offload times reached higher than 80 minutes.
For the first 10 months of 2024, concerted efforts by paramedic services and hospitals to target delays began to pay off, with offload times falling back to levels seen before the pandemic.
Why is this indicator important? Ambulances waiting to offload patients at hospitals are not able to respond to 911 calls, which can result in what some paramedic services call "Level Zero," when no ambulances are available to respond to calls.
In 2023, the Ottawa Paramedic Service, for example, was at Level Zero 1,672 times. That same year, Ottawa paramedics spent 102,105 hours in offload delay, an increase of 8.6 per cent from 2022.
Ottawa paramedic chief Pierre Poirier notes that offload times in Ottawa have improved significantly since October 2024 as a result of mitigation efforts, such as reducing patient transports and diverting patients away from EDs.
Offload delays remain stubbornly high in some areas. In Toronto, for example, in-hospital wait times for paramedics at the 90th percentile from Jan. 1 to Feb. 12 this year were 111 minutes. During that time, Toronto paramedics spent more than 800 hours in hospital each day on average, up from an average of 750 hours a day during the same time frame in 2024, according to Toronto paramedic chief Bikram Chawla.
"As with other paramedic services across Ontario, in-hospital wait times for paramedics remains a significant challenge negatively impacting ambulance availability," Chawla said.
Worrall said that because EDs are often full with admitted patients, there aren't any resources available for patients who arrive in ambulances, meaning the patients must remain with the paramedics.
"I can go and talk to patients who are still with the paramedics, but I can't do a proper examination and I can't get any tests or treatments started until there is a bed available for the patient to be offloaded," he said. "So that's obviously a real problem."
For its part, the ministry says it has increased funding for a dedicated offload nurse program to allow hospitals to hire nurses and other health professionals whose focus is on transferring patients. This program, along with partnerships between paramedic services and hospitals, has helped contribute to a reduction in offload times by more than 50 per cent since Oct. 2022, the ministry said, with some regions seeing offload times drop by close to 90 per cent.
The number of patients coming to Ontario EDs saw modest increases in the past 17 years, with fluctuations in volume ranging generally between about 400,000 and 500,000 annually.
In October 2024, ED volumes reached 516,295, about six per cent higher than October 2023. The report notes that this increase can be attributed primarily to seriously ill patients who came to hospital but didn't need to be admitted.
While there has been a slight increase in the number of patients visiting EDs in recent years, volumes, on the whole, have not seen massive deviations for over a decade. So why are wait times getting longer?
In addition to an increase in the number of complex elderly patients presenting to the ED, as well as an insufficient number of in-patient beds, many patients arrive with multiple complaints stemming from a lack of access to family doctors, specialists or diagnostic imaging, said Venugopal.
As a result, getting in front of an ED doctor is an opportunity for a patient to have multiple problems addressed.
"People have legitimate problems piled up," Venugopal said. "You can see a patient with three to seven medical problems, easily."
CAPTION: Emergency Department physician Dr. James Worrall in one of the trauma rooms at Ottawa Hospital Civic campus Thursday. He says long waits are often caused by delays getting patients out of emerg.New data paints a picture of EDs across the province still strugglingto recover from system-wide cracks exposed by the pandemic.
CREDIT: Kenyon Wallace, Megan Ogilvie

Subject: Hospitals; Emergency medical care; Primary care; Paramedics; Length of stay; Trends; Complex patients; Nurses; Pandemics; Physicians

Feb. 21: Fed up with snowy sidewalks, PC candidates should attend debates and other letters to the editor

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/feb-21-fed-up-with-snowy-sidewalks-pc-candidates/docview/3169499492/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Feb.+21%3A+Fed+up+with+snowy+sidewalks%2C+PC+candidates+should+attend+debates+and+other+letters+to+the+editor&amp;title=The+Hamilton+Spectator+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2025-02-22&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Hamilton+Spectator+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Fed up with snowy sidewalks
I am extremely frustrated with the lack of bylaw enforcement from the city for properties that do not clear their sidewalks of snow. This has been a continuous issue in our neighbourhood for many years and nothing seems to get done about it. We have a visually impaired family member who uses a guide dog to navigate sidewalks and crosswalks on her daily route, and she must walk on the road on long stretches in our neighbourhood. Even the sidewalk on Upper James that is a commercial stretch has not been cleared.
We have emailed bylaw and our city councillor and after a week, everything is the same. We don't know if the property owners have been contacted or if the city is going to clear the sidewalks. The response has been that bylaw officers are really busy, which I understand after a week of snowstorms, but this is not a new issue. I would like to see some of our 2025 tax increase go to bylaw enforcement for this issue or just have the city clear the sidewalks, because nothing is changing.
Gary Brown, Hamilton
Canada gets it done in OT
Having just watched Connor McDavid's overtime goal for Canada against Team USA, I am now waiting for Donald Trump's executive order overruling the result of the competition as a last desperate effort to make American hockey great again. Yeah right!
Jim Young, Burlington
Want my vote? Show up
Re: No-show Progressive Conservative candidates spur criticism at Hamilton election debates, Feb. 21
Let me get this straight. The political party that called the election, the political party currently under investigation by the RCMP for the Greenbelt scandal, the political party using my tax dollars to build a private spa at Ontario Place and the political party that has ripped apart our education and health-care systems can't even show up to be accountable to the public during election debates? You want my vote? Start by showing up and telling me why.
Ryan McMahon, Hamilton
What do they have to hide?
Why are Conservative candidates not showing up to debates? I think that if they don't have the courage to go on television and debate with other candidates in their riding, and tell us their policies and what they believe in and stand for, then they don't deserve my vote. What do they have to hide?
Charles Monachino, Hamilton
Party that called election should show up
I have been waiting excitedly for the Cable 14 provincial election debates, where the candidates in the local ridings can square off against each other over the issues their respective parties are campaigning on in this unnecessary, ill-timed and plain old stupid election. But what did I learn from the party that called the election in the first place? That, once again, they are not taking part. Once again, it seems it has been decreed by the mighty Doug Ford that PC candidates shall not take part, lest they accidentally divulge how horrible their policies have been and will continue to be.
Ron Tammer, Hamilton
Is arrogance or fear at play?
Conservative candidates do not show up for debates. Is this arrogance or is fear? Doug Ford is Trumpian in his arrogance. His candidates may be afraid to debate the issues because they do not have answers. If your candidate is afraid to talk to the press, how can you expect them to speak for you?
Ford claims he is the only person capable of dealing with Donald Trump; the other leaders have dealt with a bully, Doug Ford, for seven years. In this election, show Ford you won't be bribed or bullied by a party that is arrogant and afraid.
Ian Martin, Hamilton
'Scheduling conflicts' abound
So Sarah Bokhari, the PC candidate for Hamilton Centre, missed Tuesday's debate because of a "scheduling conflict." Well, I can understand that. Maybe she had a dinner date, which is obviously more important than provincial politics. Funny, though, there's a lot of PC candidates who have a lot of dinner dates these days. But we know what should happen when people don't turn up for work.
Richard Harris, Hamilton
Are candidates being muzzled?
The definition of a debate: "a formal discussion on a particular topic or topics in a public meeting or legislative assembly in which opposing arguments are put forward." This allows us to hear from each candidate as to why they should represent us or defend their current record. Unfortunately, the Progressive Conservatives don't seem to be allowed to show up at any of the debates or interviews with local media. In effect, they have been muzzled. This is unacceptable and an insult to all constituents. Please think long and hard if your PC candidate or incumbent refuses to answer any media questions and are not showing up to debates. Is this someone you want to represent you?
Rob Baboth, Ancaster
Coalition should be considered
A "coalition" of the NDP and the Liberal party would actually be a good thing for all the people of Ontario. If Bonnie Crombie's and Marit Stiles' intentions are truly authentic to take down Doug Ford, a powerful combination of the two parties might leave the Tories clinging on the ropes.
As they say, "The enemy of my enemy is my friend." A "red" and "orange" alliance should be considered at any future party conventions of the said parties.
Monty Miller, Hamilton
Hoping to read court outcome
Re: Luxury cars stolen from Hamilton were bound for Ghana, Iraq and UAE, Feb. 20
I would like to commend our Hamilton police for their auto theft bust through Project Polar Bear. Hopefully our justice system follows through with its part of the deal. I trust the excellent crime beat reporters on the Spec staff will keep the public abreast of the progress or lack thereof in this case. The bust makes headlines, but we rarely hear about the conclusion. It would be great to know our police service isn't wasting its time in these cases.
Mike Davy, Hamilton
Flag is about hope, unity, dignity
When we raise our Canadian flag, we raise hope, we raise unity and dignity, respect and love of country and freedom for all. It is not for one person with an agenda, or an angry mob to hide behind.
Norma McArthur, Hamilton
We boo the U.S. but not Soviets?
Re: Booing is directed at Trump, Feb. 20
Canadians are upset with Donald Trump. I get it. But there was no booing when the Soviet Union's national anthem was played during the Summit Series back in 1972. It was at a time when the Cold War was at its height and there was a lot more at stake politically. I guess times have changed.
Chris Asimoudis, Ancaster
Americans would react similarly
I'm not a fan of booing the U.S. anthem, but I think the world and in particular the people of America know why it is being done. As a baby boomer who has been watching sports for more than 65 years, I do have a question, though. If a North Korean team was playing exhibition games of hockey in United States a week after Kim Jong Un threatened to invade the United States and annihilate their country, do you think the fans would give the North Korean anthem a polite applause? My guess is if the North Koreans were playing in New York City, Boston or Philadelphia, they would be booed, jeered, given the raspberry, threatened and be lucky to get out alive.
Walt Fisher, Hamilton
Drop internal barriers now
In such uncertain times, why is our federal government not breaking down the barriers regarding trade between provinces? I'm sure there are products available from other provinces we could trade with, within our own country. One would use Canadian dollars and crossing provincial borders would be hassle-free. I don't pretend that I know all there is to know regarding the economics of this, but I would much rather spend my consumer dollars on "Made in Canada" products than the U.S.
Catherine Castellan, Hannon
Bring trains, not wider highway
Re: Doug Ford announces plan to widen QEW between Burlington and St. Catharines, Feb. 14
There is no need to widen the QEW Niagara. What is needed is reliable, effective and modern trains that can link Fort Erie to Toronto, to Oshawa to Belleville to Kingston to Ottawa. Another arm of rapid transit should go from Toronto to Barrie to North Bay, Sudbury, Sault Ste. Marie, Wawa and Thunder Bay. We could reach north to Timmins. Quebec could link Montreal to Quebec City to the Maritime provinces in the future, as well as west from Kenora to British Columbia. We could position ourselves for the future.
The auto companies expected there to be an auto for every person. Past planning for an efficient public transit has always been sabotaged. We would still need cars in the future, but think of the steel and other raw materials and labour needed to build this proposed endeavour. We need the Niagara peninsula for agricultural purposes.
Joseph Berlingieri, Caledonia
Thankful for reliable carrier
I would like to extend a hearty thank you to my very reliable carrier, Jon, who delivers my paper every day, despite how bad the weather might be, and has been this last while. He has been my carrier for a few years now and never lets me down. Thanks, Jon.
Robert Coxe, Hamilton
Canada not a nation of bullies
Selfishness reigns in the Conservative Party just as it reigns in the Republican Party. Donald Trump's "America First" has been brought north to the very original thinking leader of the Republican North Party, Pierre Poilievre. What Trump has done, Poilievre has mirrored. Will Canadians buy the latest Conservative slogan that takes us back to the extreme nationalism that caused the First World War? Really, Canada above all others? Do we screw other countries like the U.S. is doing or should we try to work together? Conservatives say screw them! What a myopic, immoral view for Canada to follow. I guess Poilievre will go bully Saint Pierre and Miquelon if he gets elected to show how great we are. "Canada Too Can Become a Bully" should be the slogan for Poilievre and the Republican Party of Canada.
Bob Macoritti, Ancaster
Snowblower worth the cost
I am always surprised by people who are no longer young and who, living where we do, still shovel snow. A decent snowblower, gas or electric, can be had for $300 or $400. It's much quicker and better for your health, especially your back!
Richard Holloway, Oakville
Simple gesture brings smiles
You have probably noticed the increasing number of people begging for change on medians at just about major intersections in Hamilton.
I was discussing this with a woman in a customer shuttle for the Mazda dealership on the Mountain on my way home early last summer. Turns out she also volunteers periodically at the Westley Center and indicated the first thing they run out of every day are clean socks. Why socks? If you wear a pair of dirty stock for seven to 10 days, it is very uncomfortable. She now purchases socks at discount stores and hands them out to people begging for money.
I thought this was brilliant and I started carrying packages of socks in my car. I purchase a double pair of thermal socks at the dollar store for $4.50, split them into two pairs and put them in small Ziploc bags with an additional $2 for a coffee. I always keep one or two bags on my passenger seat for easy access.
Every time I hand these out at stoplights, I quickly tell the individuals what is inside the bag and invariably I get a broad smile and a sincere thank you. It is very little to me, but I makes me feel good that I am directly helping someone in need. People go to church weekly and give $20 to $50 each to their church without blinking an eye. If some of that money went directly to homeless or unemployed people, it would make their lives a bit more bearable. Consider it for yourself.
Jim Casey, Hamilton
Donate rebate where it's needed
Thanks, Doug Ford. If only the population of Ontario realized what your $200 bribe could do in the hands of the right people. More than 11 million cheques have been issued, or $2.2 billion. If you were anywhere serious about the affordable housing and rental crisis, that kind of funding could build a lot of good starter homes. Unfortunately, all Ford and his crones know are McMansions and condos at OPEC profit margins.
I have handed my cheque to Habitat for Humanity Hamilton. I volunteer and I have seen the incredible and positive impact they have. I urge everyone to consider accepting this money, but put it toward the very thing we elected him to do but won't. People need homes and education and health care. He must think we are all new or something. Do your job or get out of the way and help those who can.
David Will, Ancaster
CAPTION: I'm not a fan of booing the U.S. anthem, but I think the world and in particular the people of America know why it is being done, a reader says.With sidewalks not shovelled, people have no other option than to walk on the plowed streets. A fed-up reader wants to see some of their 2025 tax increase go to bylaw enforcement for this issue.

Subject: Provincial elections; Political campaigns; Walkways; Candidates; Debates; Elections; Flags; Political parties

Document 50 of 500

Skelly defends government record in Flamborough-Glanbrook debate

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/skelly-defends-government-record-flamborough/docview/3169499481/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Skelly+defends+government+record+in+Flamborough-Glanbrook+debate&amp;title=The+Hamilton+Spectator+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2025-02-22&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Hamilton+Spectator+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Flamborough-Glanbrook PC candidate and incumbent MPP Donna Skelly defended the Doug Ford government's record - and, as her opponents called it, "unnecessary election" call - in a televised Feb. 20 debate.
Skelly, who is seeking her third term at Queen's Park and was re-elected in 2022 with 46 per cent of the vote, faced off against Liberal Joshua Bell, NDP candidate Lilly Noble and Green candidate Janet Errygers at a debate hosted by Cable 14 and moderated by Mike Fortune.
Skelly defended the snap election, saying she believes the election is one of the most important in her lifetime due to the threat of tariffs from U.S. President Donald Trump.
"We are in for a rough ride over the next four years and there is only one leader who is capable of navigating these very turbulent waters, and that man is Doug Ford," she said. "We need a strong mandate to navigate these turbulent times."
However, Bell, the Liberal candidate who works with Hamilton Health Sciences (HHS), said he was running in the "unnecessary election" because he has seen the damage the Ford government has done to communities and families through underfunding the health-care system.
He accused the Ford government of focusing on a proposed tunnel under Highway 401 and the Therme Spa at Ontario Place, rather than hiring doctors and nurses for what he called a "health-care crisis."
But Skelly said the government has spent a "historic" amount of money on health care, spending $85 billion in 2024 and $50 billion on new hospitals.
Skelly, a former Hamilton city councillor, was also pressed on the Progressive Conservative's move to open the Greenbelt and Hamilton's urban boundary, but argued the urban boundary is now the purview of the City of Hamilton and noted Ford has apologized for opening the Greenbelt.
However, the NDP's Noble, who recently worked with Stop Sprawl HamOnt, argued that under Bill 185 developers can appeal an application dealing with the urban boundary or Greenbelt directly to the Ontario Land Tribunal.
"Their government has allowed urban boundary expansion against the wishes of municipalities," she said.
For his part, Bell accused Skelly of lying to the people of Flamborough-Glanbrook in 2022 by saying the government would not open up the Greenbelt or change the city's urban boundary against council's wishes.
"Here she is again, standing in front of all of us here today, saying the same thing again," he said. "She can't be trusted on it, at all."
Noble also accused the PC government of neglecting the education system, alleging every school board in Ontario is running a deficit and does not have enough money. However, Skelly argued the Ford government has provided adequate education funding and school boards have to learn how to manage their books.
On the education file, Skelly said she is proud of securing funding for a new east Waterdown elementary school, two new schools in Binbrook and hundreds of new spaces for students at three other schools across the riding.
"We have invested a record amount of money in education and it is paying off here in Flamborough-Glanbrook," she said. "We're building schools quickly and we want to get them done."
The challengers also focused on the fact that Skelly was the only PC candidate in the city expected to take part in the Cable 14 debates.
"Donna Skelly showed up guys, she's here for her job interview," said the Green party's Errygers. "Colour me surprised."
For her part, Skelly said she is comfortable in the debate format and spent many years in television.
"I felt that I wanted to be here and I'm here."
Noble questioned why no other PC candidate showed up to the debates.
"They're applying for a job and they didn't show up to the job interview," she said.
New Blue Party candidate Kristen Halfpenny is also running in Flamborough-Glanbrook and sent a video statement to the debate.
Mac Christie is a reporter at The Hamilton Spectator. macchristie@thespec.com.

From left, Joshua Bell (Liberal), Lilly Noble (NDP), Janet Errygers (Green) and Donna Skelly (PC).

Document 51 of 500

Remaining Mohawk College staff feel 'overworked' in wake of layoffs, union says

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/remaining-mohawk-college-staff-feel-overworked/docview/3169499471/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Remaining+Mohawk+College+staff+feel+%27overworked%27+in+wake+of+layoffs%2C+union+says&amp;title=The+Hamilton+Spectator+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2025-02-22&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Hamilton+Spectator+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: His life had revolved around Mohawk College for a decade.
Then, last month, Christopher Arndt, who had been a student, teacher and support staff, was notified his job was cut.
"The feeling is that none of it mattered," the former digital-skills technician said. "Like, it's gone. Move on, figure something out."
Ardnt's is one of at least 380 positions across the college axed in recent months as the Hamilton Mountain institution works to curb spending in the face of a projected $50-million deficit.
Meanwhile, employees who remain are shouldering more work in the wake of layoffs.
"People are already talking about how overworked they're feeling," said Sarah Harvie, vice-president of OPSEU 241, which represents support staff. "They're being expected to take on extra work or the duties of laid-off colleagues."
The college has "navigated an unprecedented amount of change in a very short period of time," acknowledging it's in a period of "transition," spokesperson Sean Coffey said in an email.
"It will take time to adjust to the changes that have taken place and the college is committed to supporting employees through this process," he wrote.
The college has said it is "rightsizing" the workforce to match its shrinking enrolment, nearly half of which was once international students.
Faculty, staff and students rallied at the Fennell Avenue West campus on Feb. 19 to "save our colleges," as underfunded institutions across the province face "financial crisis," unions said in a statement.
"Instead of providing bridge funding to help colleges adjust, (Doug) Ford is leaving them to cut programs and lay off hundreds of faculty and support staff provincewide," they wrote.
Unions have a message for voters, who head to the polls on Thursday: "We need a new government in Ontario that will support our public college system."
They're calling on the next provincial government to increase per-student funding and change the formula to allow for greater domestic enrolment, provide "immediate" bridge support and invest in full-time faculty and staff over "administrative bloat."
It's "heartbreaking" to say goodbye to colleagues and dozens of programs, said Heather Giardine-Tuck, president of OPSEU 240, which represents full-time and contract faculty.
Unions fear students will bear the brunt of these changes.
Faculty workloads will be "maximized," leaving less time for development and new course creation, Giardine-Tuck said.
The college increasingly relies on part-time employees, which "hurts the quality of education and reduces student services," unions say.
"The students are ultimately going to be the ones suffering," Harvie said. "The level of support is not going to be there."
Delivering quality education is the college's "top priority," Coffey said, noting recent decisions aren't just about cutting costs, but about being "responsive" to workforce needs.
"With 5,000 fewer students coming to the college, the demand for services and supports will not be the same, nor will the ability for the college to support and maintain staffing at previous levels," he wrote.
Students are already seeing the effects of the changes.
City School, a free program designed to help adults achieve post-secondary education, and the College Student Success Innovation Centre research hub are closing and a multi-sensory therapy lab has closed to the public, but remains open as a classroom.
The college's Makerspace, home to 3D printers, laser engravers and other creative tools, is "closed until further notice," Coffey said. The decision to close it was "based on several factors," including staff reductions, he said. Coffey said the college is considering moving the equipment to another space so students can still use it.
The Digital Creativity Centre where Arndt used to work was gutted, the union said, leaving few staff to support student endeavours in podcasting, design and other creative areas, and provide tech support.
Arndt's job loss comes with "anger and frustration" over an underfunded public system and Mohawk's reliance on international students to balance its budget.
"Our colleges built up everything that my career was connected to on a house of cards," he said. "Suddenly they're clutching their pearls and gasping and saying, 'Oh my God, how could this happen?'"
Kate McCullough is a reporter at The Hamilton Spectator. kmccullough@thespec.com
CAPTION: Christopher Arndt was laid off from his job as a digital-skills technician at Mohawk College last month.At least 380 positions at Mohawk College were cut in recent months as the institution works to curb spending in the face of a projected $50-million deficit.Mohawk College union leaders are calling on the next provincial government to support the Ontario post-secondary sector in "financial crisis." Pictured here, from left, are OPSEU president Susan Lau, vice-president Sarah Harvie, and Heather Giardine-Tuck, president of OPSEU 240.
CREDIT: Kate McCullough

Document 52 of 500

Younger people are bitter about not owning a house. When and how will they roar?

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/younger-people-are-bitter-about-not-owning-house/docview/3169499464/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Younger+people+are+bitter+about+not+owning+a+house.+When+and+how+will+they+roar%3F&amp;title=The+Hamilton+Spectator+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2025-02-22&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Hamilton+Spectator+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: The political landscape is shifting uncertainly as bitterness grows among younger people who can't afford the house they want, experts say.
"The levels of anxiety and mental ill health and insecurity and unhappiness that come from being locked out of home ownership, and then being unable to pay high and rising rents, is just enormously challenging to manage," said Paul Kershaw, a professor at the University of British Columbia.
People in their 20s, 30s and 40s - known as millennials and Generation Z - feel their hard work is not paying off for them as it did for their parents when they bought their homes, pollster Darrell Bricker said.
"They are bitter," he said, citing surveys. He expects they will respond by seeking to remove governments.
It doesn't help to tell people to settle for condos or for other forms of housing they don't want, Bricker said.
"What is the ideal house in Canada? It's really clear. A patch of land with your own four walls, your own driveway and a backyard," said Bricker, chief executive of Ipsos Public Affairs. "Younger people feel they no longer have access to that."
It remains unclear how this disaffection will affect the Feb. 27 provincial election, researchers told a Wilfrid Laurier University housing forum titled "The Housing Crisis and Political Conflict Across Generations."
Partly this is because the risk of a job-threatening trade war between Canada and the U.S. has suddenly overtaken housing as a top concern, Bricker said.
Another factor: Progressive Conservative leader Doug Ford, seeking re-election despite failing to boost housing starts, is a savvy politician facing opposition leaders who seem unknown and untested.
Bricker anticipates election turnout will be low which will further help Ford.
Also, younger people are not responding in a uniform way. Some research shows young men with a variety of grievances are turning to the political right, while young women are becoming more progressive, the forum heard.
"I think bitterness might exist," said Ben Jesseau, 22, president of Laurier's undergraduate student government that sponsored the housing forum.
"I fully expect to be a renter for most of my life. The idea of being able to buy a house in your 20s or 30s feels like a foreign concept that isn't going to happen."
It annoys younger people to hear older people - baby boomers and members of generation X - rejoice over a windfall they receive upon selling a home they bought for little, Jesseau said. Younger people know it's the big windfall that's keeping them out of the housing market.
Also irksome is telling younger people that they could afford a home if they stopped buying $6 coffees.
"Let's be real," Jesseau said. "Being told that's the reason you can't buy a house feels insulting."
Bitterness felt by younger generations seems clear in a 2024 World Happiness Report sponsored in part by the United Nations. It found Canadians older than 60 rank their happiness at No. 8 in the world, between Netherlands and Australia.
Canadians younger than 30 rank their happiness at No. 58, between Malta and Ecuador. That's a big change from 2008 when young Canadians felt the same happiness as older Canadians, researchers found.
The happiness of younger Canadians has fallen steadily since 2013; they are now the least happy age group in the nation. Since 2010 only four countries (Jordan, Venezuela, Lebanon and Afghanistan) have seen a bigger plunge in happiness among their young people.
In 1997 someone aged 25 to 34 earning a typical wage in Kitchener-Waterloo could afford a typical house because it cost just five times their income, Kershaw said. By 2022 a typical house in Kitchener-Waterloo cost 19 times the typical income of someone aged 25 to 34.
Older homeowners simply had to sleep and watch television while the escalating value of their home made them wealthy, he said.
Younger people find their hard work does not put them within reach of the home they want. They must instead find ways to hack the housing market. Often this means being lucky enough to lean on wealthy parents, which adds more unfairness.
"Whoever wins the next provincial election here on Feb. 27, I hope their following budget includes promises to make Ontario work for every generation," said Kershaw, founder of a think tank called Generation Squeeze that advocates for fairness among generations.
"Start thinking about how housing has been a boon for some and a hardship for others, and how do we reconcile the gains and the losses in a way that works for young and old alike."
One option might be to impose a property tax surcharge on homes worth more than $1 million, Kershaw said. The revenue could fund social programs that largely benefit younger people, such as education and child care.
CAPTION: Darrell BrickerPaul Kershaw"I fully expect to be a renter for most of my life. The idea of being able to buy a house in your 20s or 30s feels like a foreign concept that isn't going to happen," said Ben Jesseau, 22, president of Laurier's undergraduate student government that sponsored the housing forum.
CREDIT: Jeff Outhit

Document 53 of 500

Five top takeaways from the campaign trail this week

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/five-top-takeaways-campaign-trail-this-week/docview/3169499457/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Five+top+takeaways+from+the+campaign+trail+this+week&amp;title=The+Hamilton+Spectator+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2025-02-22&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Hamilton+Spectator+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: The leadership candidates began the week with their second - and final - debate and finished it with Progressive Conservative Leader Doug Ford flying to Washington to meet with U.S. governors, and the NDP and Liberals releasing costed platforms. The Greens were the first to release theirs, last Friday, and the PCs are expected to on Monday.
Here are the top takeaways from the campaign trail as the countdown to the Feb. 27 vote nears.
End Highway 407 tolls
The NDP outlined $70 billion in new spending over the next three years, and $37 billion in total savings and new revenue to partly offset that. It also pledged an additional $830 million per year over 10 years for school capital improvements. The party wants to provide families with a grocery rebate, create a school nutrition program, double the Ontario Disability Support Payments, and ensure access to primary care teams - either a family doctor or nurse practitioner, or both - for all Ontarians, as well as more affordable housing. It would also boost capital gains taxes and put a tax on high-end homes. Its plan to remove tolls on Highway 407 is not costed as the amount would have to be negotiated.
A doctor for every Ontarian
The Liberal platform includes $65 billion in new spending over four years, offset by about $28 billion in savings or repurposing existing funding. It is about "getting the basics right for Ontario" with "no new taxes, no tax increases but there are no fantasy tunnels, either," Crombie said Friday, referring to Ford's plan to tunnel under Highway 401 from Mississauga to Scarborough. The plan promises a family doctor for every Ontarian in four years, mental health coverage, no interest on student loans, eliminating the HST on home heating, more teachers and education workers in schools, doubling disability payments, and cutting the teacher education program to one year.
"Say it to my face"
The New Democrats lost one candidate to controversy, and Crombie defended three candidates who have come under fire for questionable social media postings, saying they all had apologized and two of the trio made the comments at least a decade ago. Ford also defended an incumbent who has been accused of workplace misconduct towards employees. The NDP candidate in Elgin-Middlesex-London, Amanda Zavitz, apologized and later stepped down after comments she made last year surfaced in which she said she secretly wants "to be a Black woman."
With regards to the Liberal candidate remarks - which appeared to make light of intimate partner violence - Crombie had said she had been focused on preparing for Monday's debate, and not comments from the past. On Friday, the Liberals and the candidate parted ways. On Wednesday, she also accused the PCs of "wanting to sling mud," adding that "if Doug Ford has something to say to me, he should have the balls to say it to my face."
Highway 401 tunnel "a fantasy"
The 90-minute all-candidates' debate on Family Day Monday, at CBC headquarters in downtown Toronto, touched on everything from health care to housing. NDP Leader Marit Stiles went after Ford for not addressing the housing shortage or health care, saying "the hallway health-care crisis was invented by the previous Liberal government, but Doug Ford and his government have poured gasoline all over it."
Crombie, meanwhile, again made light of Ford's plan to dig under Highway 401, saying "a fantasy tunnel, how dumb is that? After seven years of lies, why should anyone believe you?" Crombie continued her attacks on Ford, on Thursday telling the Canadian Club that since the PCs came to power, "every single metric of economic success has gotten worse in Ontario."
Trump's tariffs
Ford made an announcement with Nova Scotia Premier Tim Houston on Thursday - the day before the duo headed to Washington - both committing to eliminating interprovincial trade barriersto help improve the economy and ease the blow of tariffs threatened by U.S. President Donald Trump. "We're facing the biggest threat to our economic security and independence as a country, we need to rise to the challenge," Ford said in Milton. "We need to match these words with bold actions."
CAPTION: From left, PC Leader Doug Ford, Green Party Leader Mike Schreiner, Liberal Leader Bonnie Crombie and NDP Leader Marit Stiles pose for a photo before the Ontario leaders' debate on Family Day.
CREDIT: Kristin Rushowy

Subject: Political campaigns; Leadership; Candidates; Tariffs

Business indexing term: Subject: Leadership Tariffs
