Document 54 of 500

The Greenbelt saga is 'not over,' warn advocates - and they want you to vote accordingly

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/greenbelt-saga-is-not-over-warn-advocates-they/docview/3169499435/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=The+Greenbelt+saga+is+%27not+over%2C%27+warn+advocates+-+and+they+want+you+to+vote+accordingly&amp;title=The+Hamilton+Spectator+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2025-02-22&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Hamilton+Spectator+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Ontario's Greenbelt is about to celebrate its 20th birthday with a ballot box full of uncertainty.
The provincial snap election is Feb. 27 - one day before the anniversary of legislation passed in 2005 to protect the two-million-acre band of farmland, escarpment, forests and wetlands hugging the Greater Golden Horseshoe and most of Hamilton.
As a result, supporters of the green ribbon of protected land aren't quite sure whether to celebrate the anniversary or gird for protests - but about two-dozen of them gathered at a downtown Hamilton brew pub this week for a slightly nervous party.
"I'm always worried about the Greenbelt," said Environment Hamilton head and party organizer Ian Borsuk. "The (most recent) government promised they wouldn't touch it - and then they famously did."
Partygoers talked to candidates who dropped by, tried the beer and signed cards for the Birthday Belt that featured messages of both celebration and warning. "Green forever!" wrote one well-wisher.
"Hands off Doug!" read another message, a reference to Progressive Conservative leader Doug Ford's flip-flopping on a plan to allow mass Greenbelt development - including on 1,800 acres in Hamilton.
That plan was abandoned in 2023 in the face of resident opposition, scathing watchdog reports about preferential treatment for developers and a police probe that is ongoing. "I made a promise to you that I wouldn't touch the Greenbelt. I broke that promise. And for that I'm very, very sorry," Ford said at the time.
Soon afterwards, the Tory government also walked-back a separate forced urban boundary expansion that would have allowed development on nearly 5,400 acres of Hamilton farm and other rural land.
The twin reversals sparked celebration among agricultural and other grassroots groups - like Stop Sprawl HamOnt and Environment Hamilton - that worked to galvanize opposition to farmland development in and out of the Greenbelt.
The U-turns also spurred dismay for developers with plans already in the works for thousands of new homes - and anguish for families trying to sell Greenbelt land they argued can no longer support farming.
Now fast-forward to 2025: Do voters in this snap election still care about the Greenbelt and urban boundary fiascos?
Opposition parties hope so, with leaders like the Liberals' Bonnie Crombie and NDP's Marit Stiles suggesting the Tories called an early election to get ahead of any public findings from an ongoing RCMP investigation into the Greenbelt land swap. For his part, Ford has said he triggered the campaign because he needs a "strong mandate" to deal with U.S. tariff threats.
Some residents will always care, said political scientist Chris Erl - but short of a dramatic update from the RCMP probe, the Greenbelt controversy may not fire up voters worried about more visible problems like unaffordable housing, lineups for family doctors or tariff-related threat to jobs. "Just reminding people about the (controversial) things Doug Ford has done, I don't think that's going to be enough," he said earlier in the campaign.
But sensational police update or not, voters don't have to look to the past to find threats to the Greenbelt or Hamilton farmland, argued Borsuk.
For example, the next provincial government is expected to oversee a mandatory 10-year review of the Greenbelt, which, in theory, could see new requests to remove parcels for development. Ontario's fast-tracked road-building laws, meanwhile, are paving the way for the controversial new highways through protected land in the Greater Toronto Area.
And since Ford's dramatic reversal of Hamilton's urban boundary expansion, the province has gradually changed planning policies and laws to make it easier for developers to apply for urban boundary expansions - and harder for local councils to say no.
As a result, city planners have already received applications to expand housing into thousands of acres of farmland beyond Hamilton's urban border. Some of those would-be expansions now appear destined to be settled by the Ontario Land Tribunal, rather than by elected governments.
"It's demoralizing to see the government change the rules again and again to benefit land speculators," Borsuk said.
The solution, he argues, is to elect a government willing to maintain, but also grow the Greenbelt. "We need to protect the farmland we have left. The Greenbelt is the most effective way to do that."
Ahead of voting day, here's a reminder of ongoing Greenbelt and urban boundary debates in Hamilton:
Greenbelt encroachment
Doug Ford has famously bounced back and forth on Greenbelt development plans, and admitted as much during a recent televised election debate: "I think I was pretty clear. I said I was and then I wasn't and then I went ahead and did it."
But his 2023 flip-flop came with what sounds like an unequivocal promise: "It was a mistake to open the Greenbelt ... I'll be reversing the changes we made and won't make any changes to the Greenbelt in the future." His election opponents, including Crombie, Stiles and the Green's Mike Schreiner, have all vowed to protect the green ribbon of development-free land.
That sounds like the end of the story, right?
But "it's not over," said Peter Appleton, a local organizer with the Greenbelt Guardians group that helped organize local opposition to the original "Greenbelt grab."
Today, Appleton and other wary advocates are watching a new application filed by a large Toronto-based builder, Mizrahi Developments, seeking an urban boundary expansion that would also extend into the Greenbelt zone in Ancaster. The plan is to build 14 single-detached and 61 townhouses on a forested Sulphur Springs Road property.
The developer has not responded to Spectator questions about the application and, right now, such a build is clearly against the law, city staff have said.
But with a mandated review of the Greenbelt on the horizon, advocates worry it is possible developers will once again pitch targeted removals. Developers also unsuccessfully sought a Greenbelt carve-out for a different property on the edge of Ancaster during the cancelled 2022 land swap.
"Developers know they have a government in place that is willing to use MZOs (minister's zoning orders) to get their way," Appleton said.
Outside Hamilton, the better-known Greenbelt battle is over Highway 413 and the Bradford bypass - key Tory road-building promises aimed at alleviating car commuter frustration.
But those "needless superhighways" threaten the integrity and functions of the Greenbelt - like water quality protection and farmland preservation - at a time when tariff threats are reinforcing the importance of locally grown food, argued former Toronto mayor and Tory politician David Crombie, who wrote a recent election plea in the Toronto Star urging voters not to support any leader who won't protect the Greenbelt.
"Keeping this land protected helps keep food affordable for Ontario families," he wrote in the opinion piece published in the Star.
Backdoor boundary expansion
The average voter might feel a bit of whiplash trying to keep track of urban boundary politics in Ontario.
In 2022, the province shocked Hamilton by forcing it to allow development across 5,400-plus acres of farmland in Stoney Creek and Glanbrook, despite a high-profile local council vote to "freeze" the urban boundary and grow up, not out. But the province reversed the decision the very next year, spurring relief among anti-sprawl advocates and farmers fearful of losing scarce agricultural land.
But it turns out that relief was premature.
Since then, the province has updated its growth policies and introduced new legislation that allows any developer to apply for an urban boundary expansion of any size, at any time. If a local council says "no," then the thwarted developer can appeal to the Ontario Land Tribunal.
That's already happening. With more than 3,000 acres of proposed urban boundary expansions back on the table, mostly in Elfrida, but with projects also pitched along Twenty Road West and in Ancaster. In one case, a development group is also seeking a ministerial order to override city opposition.
The past Tory government told The Spectator the policy changes were meant to bolster housing supply and ensure would-be developments are "considered in a balanced manner."
Earlier this month, Mayor Andrea Horwath and council rejected a developer plea to support the request for a minister's zoning order for a Twenty Road West boundary expansion.
But fuming local councillors have characterized the cascade of rule changes as a "backdoor" effort to achieve the same urban boundary expansion Ontario publicly rolled back just a year ago.
The solution is simple, said Ancaster councillor Craig Cassar. The next provincial government needs to "restore decision-making power" about city growth to cities, he said, rather than using ministerial overrides or arm's-length tribunals.
"We have the local knowledge and experts, we're accountable to local residents," he said. "Let local decision-making be done at the local level."
Matthew Van Dongen is a reporter at The Spectator. mvandongen@thespec.com
CAPTION: Environment Hamilton head Ian Borsuk holds a "birthday card" for Ontario's Greenbelt.Mizrahi Developments is seeking an urban boundary expansion that would also extend into the Greenbelt zone in Ancaster. The plan is to build 14 single-detached and 61 townhouses on a forested Sulphur Springs Road property.Mizrahi Developments is proposing 14 single-detached and 61 townhouse units on Sulphur Springs Road in Ancaster.Hundreds of Hamilton residents protested the province's original Greenbelt carve-outs in 2023 - sometimes in costume.Peter Appleton is worried about a proposed residential townhouse development on Sulphur Springs Road in Ancaster - an area that is in the Greenbelt and beyond Hamilton's urban boundaries.
CREDIT: Matthew Van Dongen

Subject: Agriculture; Agricultural land; Councils; Birthdays; Voters; Decision making; Elections; Roads & highways; Farms; Tariffs

Business indexing term: Subject: Tariffs

Title: The Greenbelt saga is 'not over,' warn advocates - and they want you to vote accordingly

Publication title: The Hamilton Spectator (Online); Hamilton

Document 55 of 500

As elections go, this one's been a snooze

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/as-elections-go-this-ones-been-snooze/docview/3169499378/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=As+elections+go%2C+this+one%27s+been+a+snooze&amp;title=The+Hamilton+Spectator+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2025-02-22&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Hamilton+Spectator+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: It's not like people aren't paying attention to politics these days. That's all many people are talking about, it seems.
You can't go five minutes without hearing someone's opinion about Donald Trump. Five seconds if you're on social media. That even spilled into hockey this week and turned an exhibition series that we wanted to win into an impassioned battle over our way of life that we had to win.
Our federal campaign - the one that hasn't officially started yet but, y'know, has - is everywhere. Discussions about Pierre Poilievre, Mark Carney, Jagmeet Singh and even Justin Trudeau are now heated across the country.
There's even been plenty of chatter about Hamilton city council and the budget deliberations that just wrapped up. Along with a variety of other issues.
But our provincial election?
"This has to be one of the sleepiest campaigns I can remember," says Coun. Brad Clark, who served a term as a Conservative MPP and cabinet minister.
"I'm sensing the same thing," says Coun. Ted McMeekin, who spent 18 years as a Liberal at Queen's Park. "There's not a lot going on."
With less than a week remaining before we head to the polls, this race really does feel like it's been sponsored by a sleeping pill company. One that's struggling to carve out a place in the conversation.
What's going on?
The fact that it was called as a snap election in the middle of a term is probably contributing to that. Some parties were still scrambling to nominate candidates nearly a week into the campaign. That could help explain why some residents have been saying they've received little or no campaign literature.
The time of year and wintery conditions might also be part of the issue. Getting around is more of a challenge for candidates and the blisteringly cold temperatures have made it uncomfortable to stay outside knocking on doors for hours on end.
The fact that there appeared to be far fewer lawn signs than usual, even before last week's storms, didn't help. Now that there are shoulder-high snowbanks all over town, those signs that offer a constant reminder that we're in a campaign and burn the candidates' names into our brains through osmosis aren't there.
Anger and a desire for change often drive people to the voting booth. Since most polls are suggesting Doug Ford is headed for another comfortable win, we can only conclude that while some are eager for something new, many aren't. Without that, some of the fire that stokes vigorous campaigns is missing.
Of course, that could be resolved if any of the challengers to Ford could find the issue that would coalesce the voting public. But that hasn't happened. Not broadly. There are issues at play, of course, but none that have seemed to fire up the masses.
On top of everything else, honestly, whose brilliant idea was it to hold the televised leaders' debate on Family Day?
"Dinner time, too," McMeekin says.
Yeah. Strange. This was the opportunity to really get people engaged. Instead it was done at a time that wasn't exactly ideal. This scheduling made no sense.
It all makes you wonder what turnout is going to look like on Thursday. In 2018, 56.7 per cent of eligible voters cast a ballot. Four years later, it was 44.1 per cent. This time?
"Like 2022," says Kate Harrison, vice-chair of Summa Strategies and a commentator on national and provincial politics. "Or perhaps lower."
That would be unfortunate.
All that said, there are a few days left. There's a chance things could change in the final week of the race. Will they?
Maybe. Stuff happens. A misstep, an issue arising that nobody saw coming, a scandal breaking, whatever. Any of those things might generate some interest.
Can Harrison see anything happening that would turn this from seemingly much ado about not much to something that grabs the public's attention?
"It would take something pretty extraordinary."
CAPTION: Ontario PC Leader Doug Ford, left to right, Ontario Green Party Leader Mike Schreiner, Ontario Liberal Leader Bonnie Crombie and Ontario NDP Leader Marit Stiles chat before the start of the Ontario leaders' debate on Monday.
CREDIT: Scott Radley

Subject: Local elections

Scott Radley is a columnist with the Hamilton Spectator focusing on sports
and politics. Reach him at

No Bond villain is scarier than Bezos

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/no-bond-villain-is-scarier-than-bezos/docview/3169364340/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=No+Bond+villain+is+scarier+than+Bezos&amp;title=The+Spectator&amp;issn=11899417&amp;date=2025-02-22&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=G.4&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Spectator&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: James Bond now gets free shipping on spyware and martini shakers.
There are so many things happening that were once unthinkable. America betrays an ally and sides with a murderous dictator. The City of Toronto is bamboozled by snow. Humans are marrying robots. Vladimir Guerrero Jr. is now counting down the days until he is an ex-Jay.
And now 007 is an Amazon agent.
That news arrived Thursday. Amazon MGM Studios, which owns the rights to 4,000 movies and 17,000 TV shows, has gained "creative control" of James Bond. Independent producers Michael G. Wilson and Barbara Broccoli, who guided and guarded the franchise like junkyard dogs for decades, agreed to a new "joint venture." They will keep an ownership stake and lounge in the fog machine of semi-retirement when it comes to future casting and scripts.
Mike Hopkins, head honcho at Amazon MGM Studios, said in a statement: "Since his theatrical introduction over 60 years ago, James Bond has been one of the most iconic characters in filmed entertainment ... We are honoured to continue this treasured heritage, and look forward to ushering in the next phase of the legendary 007 ... "
Future titles: "From Fulfilment Centre With Love." "License to Shop." "Dr. No Bezos." "No Time to Refund." "Prime Days Never Die." "For Lauren's Eyes Only." "Alexapussy."
The fate of James Bond is now in the hands of Amazon.
The company already held distribution rights. But now it can shape the MI6 agent to better fit with current mores and corporate interests. Say, why is this brooding protagonist, first imagined by author Ian Fleming, slowly poisoning himself with vodka when we can promote a fantastic Whole Foods protein shake?
What's with all the womanizing? I know, in the third act of "The Prime Member Who Loved Me," James starts listening to relationship books on Audible and delivers a teary soliloquy on an Italian veranda as the bad guys close in with directed-energy bazookas.
"I wronged you, Bond Girls. I am sorry, Honey Ryder. Forgive me, Miss Taro. Where have you gone, Pussy Galore? For all the heartache I inflicted, I shall pick up all overnight shipping costs on thousands of wonderful products that will make you fondly remember I am but a dashing and highly disturbed lover of gadgets who does not play well with others."
I don't trust Jeff Bezos with James Bond any more than I'd trust Pornhub with Winnie the Pooh. Oh my God, what is going on? Why is Pooh going to an orgy? Tigger, put down that bottle of baby oil! You're not Diddy. No, Eeyore, that's not thistle - it's a sex toy!
James Bond is a lone wolf. He is not a corporate shill. Yeah, yeah, I know. Amazon MGM Studios has a C-suite full of Hollywood execs who will faithfully nurture the Bond universe for a new generation.
But what happens when Bezos wants a say? The broligarchs are now jockeying for cultural supremacy. They yearn to leave their DNA on everything. You don't think Elon Musk wants to gain control of the X-Men franchise just for the cross-branding opportunities? Mark Zuckerberg fantasizes about owning DC Comics just so he can create a new superhero that has no moral compass and turtles in political headwinds: It's a bird, it's a plane, it's Zuckupman!
Why would anyone trust these craven broligarchs? Bezos looks like a Bond villain. Does anyone at the Washington Post still believe it's fantastic to be owned by one of the world's richest men who promised to stay out of editorial but is now more hands on than a cephalopod?
That masthead slogan - "Democracy Dies in Darkness" - needs a rewrite.
"Journalism Dies in Meddling."
The Wall Street Journal recently reported that soon after Bezos dined with Donald and Melania Trump at Mar-a-Lago in December, Amazon offered $40 million to license the first lady's upcoming documentary about her transition back to the White House. That's like giving $40 million to Doug Ford to write a book about origami. After 27 folds, is it a swan ... or a Greenbelt?
Most other studios, including Netflix and Apple, took a hard pass on Melania's vanity project. But Jeff Bozo was eager to ingratiate himself to his new overlords. He knows this doc won't get more viewers than "The Chevy Chase Show." No matter.
If Barron pitched a new reality show - "Barron Wants a Hot Chick" - Bezos would have opened his chequebook as Melania regaled him with horror stories about how buying off the rack is a crime against humanity. If the president ever asks, James Bond will be wearing a MAGA cap in his next caper: "DEI is DOA."
Agent 007 better shake himself a strong martini or three this evening.
He now takes orders from Amazon. No villain could be more terrifying.
CAPTION: Daniel Craig as James Bond in "No Time to Die." With news that Amazon is taking over the franchise, Agent 007 better shake himself a strong martini or three, writes Vinay Menon.Nicola Dove MGM/tribune news service

CREDIT: Vinay Menon OPINION

Document 58 of 500

Skelly defends Ford's record in Flamborough-Glanbrook debate: Candidates squared off over Greenbelt, health care and education

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/skelly-defends-fords-record-flamborough-glanbrook/docview/3169359869/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Skelly+defends+Ford%27s+record+in+Flamborough-Glanbrook+debate%3A+Candidates+squared+off+over+Greenbelt%2C+health+care+and+education&amp;title=The+Spectator&amp;issn=11899417&amp;date=2025-02-22&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.4&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Spectator&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Flamborough-Glanbrook PC candidate and incumbent MPP Donna Skelly defended the Doug Ford government's record - and, as her opponents called it, "unnecessary election" call - in a televised Feb. 20 debate.
Skelly, who is seeking her third term at Queen's Park and was re-elected in 2022 with 46 per cent of the vote, faced off against Liberal Joshua Bell, NDP candidate Lilly Noble and Green candidate Janet Errygers at a debate hosted by Cable 14 and moderated by Mike Fortune.
Skelly defended the snap election, saying she believes the election is one of the most important in her lifetime due to the threat of tariffs from U.S. President Donald Trump.
"We are in for a rough ride over the next four years and there is only one leader who is capable of navigating these very turbulent waters, and that man is Doug Ford," she said. "We need a strong mandate to navigate these turbulent times."
However, Bell, the Liberal candidate who works with Hamilton Health Sciences (HHS), said he was running in the "unnecessary election" because he has seen the damage the Ford government has done to communities and families through underfunding the health-care system.
He accused the Ford government of focusing on a proposed tunnel under Highway 401 and the Therme Spa at Ontario Place rather than hiring doctors and nurses for what he called a "health-care crisis."
But Skelly said the government has spent a "historic" amount of money on health care, spending $85 billion in 2024 and $50 billion on new hospitals.
Skelly, a former Hamilton city councillor, was also pressed on the Progressive Conservative's move to open the Greenbelt and Hamilton's urban boundary, but argued the urban boundary is now the purview of the City of Hamilton and noted Ford has apologized for opening the Greenbelt.
However, the NDP's Noble, who recently worked with Stop Sprawl HamOnt, argued that under Bill 185 developers can appeal an application dealing with the urban boundary or Greenbelt directly to the Ontario Land Tribunal.
"Their government has allowed urban boundary expansion against the wishes of municipalities," she said.
For his part, Bell accused Skelly of lying to the people of Flamborough-Glanbrook in 2022 by saying the government would not open up the Greenbelt or change the city's urban boundary against council's wishes.
"Here she is again, standing in front of all of us here today, saying the same thing again," he said. "She can't be trusted on it at all."
Noble also accused the PC government of neglecting the education system, alleging every school board in Ontario is running a deficit and does not have enough money. However, Skelly argued the Ford government has provided adequate education funding and school boards have to learn how to manage their books.
On the education file, Skelly said she is proud of securing funding for a new east Waterdown elementary school, two new schools in Binbrook and hundreds of new spaces for students at three other schools across the riding.
"We have invested a record amount of money in education and it is paying off here in Flamborough-Glanbrook," she said. "We're building schools quickly and we want to get them done."
The challengers also focused on the fact that Skelly was the only PC candidate in the city expected to take part in the Cable 14 debates.
"Donna Skelly showed up, guys, she's here for her job interview," said the Green Party's Errygers. "Colour me surprised."
For her part, Skelly said she is comfortable in the debate format and spent many years in television.
"I felt that I wanted to be here and I'm here."
Noble questioned why no other PC candidate showed up to the debates.
"They're applying for a job and they didn't show up to the job interview," she said.
New Blue Party candidate Kristen Halfpenny is also running in Flamborough-Glanbrook and sent a video statement to the debate.
Mac Christie is a reporter at The Hamilton Spectator.
macchristie@thespec.com.
CAPTION: Mike Fortune moderates the Flamborough-Glanbrook provincial election debate at Cable 14 on Feb. 20. From left, Joshua Bell (Liberal), Lilly Noble (NDP), Janet Errygers (Green) and Donna Skelly (PC).Cathie Coward The Hamilton Spectator

CREDIT: Mac Christie

Letters

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/letters/docview/3169359868/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=unknown&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Letters&amp;title=The+Spectator&amp;issn=11899417&amp;date=2025-02-22&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.21&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Spectator&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Fed up with snowy sidewalks
I am extremely frustrated with the lack of bylaw enforcement from the city for properties that do not clear their sidewalks of snow. This has been a continuous issue in our neighbourhood for many years and nothing seems to get done about it. We have a visually impaired family member who uses a guide dog to navigate sidewalks and crosswalks on her daily route, and she must walk on the road on long stretches in our neighbourhood. Even the sidewalk on Upper James that is a commercial stretch has not been cleared.
We have emailed bylaw and our city councillor and after a week, everything is the same. We don't know if the property owners have been contacted or if the city is going to clear the sidewalks. The response has been that bylaw officers are really busy, which I understand after a week of snowstorms, but this is not a new issue. I would like to see some of our 2025 tax increase go to bylaw enforcement for this issue or just have the city clear the sidewalks, because nothing is changing.
Gary Brown, Hamilton
Canada gets
it done in OT
Having just watched Connor McDavid's overtime goal for Canada against Team USA, I am now waiting for Donald Trump's executive order overruling the result of the competition as a last desperate effort to make American hockey great again. Yeah right!
Jim Young, Burlington
Gretzky didn't
belong on the ice
At the start of the game it was announced that Wayne Gretzky was named honorary captain of Team Canada. What a slap in the face. He came out of the USA side bench, gave a thumbs up to the USA players and never wore anything honouring the Canadian team like his USA counterpart wearing an American jersey. I am surprised he wasn't wearing a MAGA hat.
Whomever made that decision to invite him was as tone deaf as they come. Stay home Wayne, Canadians don't need your brand of patriotism!
Pat McLaughlin, Hamilton
Want my vote? Show up
Re: No-show Progressive
Conservative candidates spur criticism at Hamilton election debates, Feb. 21
Let me get this straight. The political party that called the election, the political party currently under investigation by the RCMP for the Greenbelt scandal, the political party using my tax dollars to build a private spa at Ontario Place and the political party that has ripped apart our education and health-care systems can't even show up to be accountable to the public during election debates? You want my vote? Start by showing up and telling me why.
Ryan McMahon, Hamilton
What do they have to hide?
Why are Conservative candidates not showing up to debates? I think that if they don't have the courage to go on television and debate with other candidates in their riding, and tell us their policies and what they believe in and stand for, then they don't deserve my vote. What do they have to hide?
Charles Monachino,
Hamilton
Of any party,
PCs should attend
I have been waiting excitedly for the Cable 14 provincial election debates, where the candidates in the local ridings can square off against each other over the issues their respective parties are campaigning on in this unnecessary, ill-timed and plain old stupid election. But what did I learn from the party that called the election in the first place? That, once again, they are not taking part. Once again, it seems it has been decreed by the mighty Doug Ford that PC candidates shall not take part, lest they accidentally divulge how horrible their policies have been and will continue to be.
Ron Tammer, Hamilton
Is arrogance
or fear at play?
Conservative candidates do not show up for debates. Is this arrogance or fear? Doug Ford is Trumpian in his arrogance. His candidates may be afraid to debate the issues because they do not have answers. If your candidate is afraid to talk to the press, how can you expect them to speak for you?
Ford claims he is the only person capable of dealing with Donald Trump; the other leaders have dealt with a bully, Doug Ford, for seven years. In this election, show Ford you won't be bribed or bullied by a party that is arrogant and afraid.
Ian Martin, Hamilton
'Scheduling
conflicts' abound
So Sarah Bokhari, the PC candidate for Hamilton Centre, missed Tuesday's debate because of a "scheduling conflict." Well, I can understand that. Maybe she had a dinner date, which is obviously more important than provincial politics.
Funny, though, there's a lot of PC candidates who have a lot of dinner dates these days. But we know what should happen when people don't turn up for work.
Richard Harris, Hamilton
Coalition should be considered
A "coalition" of the NDP and the Liberal party would actually be a good thing for all the people of Ontario. If Bonnie Crombie's and Marit Stiles' intentions are truly authentic to take down Doug Ford, a powerful combination of the two parties might leave the Tories clinging on the ropes.
As they say, "The enemy of my enemy is my friend." A "red" and "orange" alliance should be considered at any future party conventions of the said parties.
Monty Miller, Hamilton
Hoping to read court outcome
Re: Luxury cars stolen from Hamilton were bound for
Ghana, Iraq and UAE, Feb. 20
I would like to commend our Hamilton police for their auto theft bust through Project Polar Bear. Hopefully our justice system follows through with its part of the deal. I trust the excellent crime beat reporters on the Spec staff will keep the public abreast of the progress or lack thereof in this case. The bust makes headlines, but we rarely hear about the conclusion. It would be great to know our police service isn't wasting its time in these cases.
Mike Davy, Hamilton
Flag is about
hope and unity
When we raise our Canadian flag, we raise hope, we raise unity and dignity, respect and love of country and freedom for all. It is not for one person with an agenda, or an angry mob to hide behind.
Norma McArthur, Hamilton
We boo the U.S. but not Soviets?
Re: Booing is directed
at Trump, Feb. 20
Canadians are upset with Donald Trump. I get it. But there was no booing when the Soviet Union's national anthem was played during the Summit Series back in 1972. It was at a time when the Cold War was at its height and there was a lot more at stake politically. I guess times have changed.
Chris Asimoudis, Ancaster
Americans would react similarly
I'm not a fan of booing the U.S. anthem, but I think the world and in particular the people of America know why it is being done. As a baby boomer who has been watching sports for more than 65 years, I do have a question, though. If a North Korean team was playing exhibition games of hockey in United States a week after Kim Jong Un threatened to invade the United States and annihilate their country, do you think the fans would give the North Korean anthem a polite applause? My guess is if the North Koreans were playing in New York City, Boston or Philadelphia, they would be booed, jeered, given the raspberry, threatened and be lucky to get out alive.
Walt Fisher, Hamilton
Drop internal
barriers now
In such uncertain times, why is our federal government not breaking down the barriers regarding trade between provinces? I'm sure there are products available from other provinces we could trade with, within our own country. One would use Canadian dollars and crossing provincial borders would be hassle-free. I don't pretend that I know all there is to know regarding the economics of this, but I would much rather spend my consumer dollars on "Made in Canada" products than the U.S.
Catherine Castellan, Hannon
Bring trains, not wider highways
Re: Doug Ford announces
plan to widen QEW between Burlington and St. Catharines, Feb. 14
There is no need to widen the QEW Niagara. What is needed is reliable, effective and modern trains that can link Fort Erie to Toronto, to Oshawa to Belleville to Kingston to Ottawa.
Another arm of rapid transit should go from Toronto to Barrie to North Bay, Sudbury, Sault Ste. Marie, Wawa and Thunder Bay. We could reach north to Timmins. Quebec could link Montreal to Quebec City to the Maritime provinces in the future, as well as west from Kenora to British Columbia.
We could position ourselves for the future.
The auto companies expected there to be an auto for every person. Past planning for an efficient public transit has always been sabotaged. We would still need cars in the future, but think of the steel and other raw materials and labour needed to build this proposed endeavour. We need the Niagara peninsula for agricultural purposes.
Joseph Berlingieri, Caledonia
Canada not a
nation of bullies
Selfishness reigns in the Conservative party just as it reigns in the Republican party. Donald Trump's "America First" has been brought north to the very original thinking leader of the Republican North Party, Pierre Poilievre. What Trump has done, Poilievre has mirrored.
Will Canadians buy the latest Conservative slogan that takes us back to the extreme nationalism that caused the First World War? Really, Canada above all others? Do we screw other countries like the U.S. is doing or should we try to work together? Conservatives say screw them! What a myopic, immoral view for Canada to follow.
I guess Poilievre will go bully Saint Pierre and Miquelon if he gets elected to show how great we are. "Canada Too Can Become a Bully" should be the slogan for Poilievre and the Republican Party of Canada.
Bob Macoritti, Ancaster
Simple gesture brings smiles
You have probably noticed the increasing number of people begging for change on medians at just about major intersections in Hamilton.
I was discussing this with a woman in a customer shuttle for the Mazda dealership on the Mountain on my way home early last summer. Turns out she also volunteers periodically at the Westley Center and indicated the first thing they run out of every day are clean socks. Why socks? If you wear a pair of dirty stock for seven to 10 days, it is very uncomfortable.
She now purchases socks at discount stores and hands them out to people begging for money.
I thought this was brilliant and I started carrying packages of socks in my car. I purchase a double pair of thermal socks at the dollar store for $4.50, split them into two pairs and put them in small Ziploc bags with an additional $2 for a coffee. I always keep one or two bags on my passenger seat for easy access.
Every time I hand these out at stoplights, I quickly tell the individuals what is inside the bag and invariably I get a broad smile and a sincere thank you. It is very little to me, but I makes me feel good that I am directly helping someone in need. People go to church weekly and give $20 to $50 each to their church without blinking an eye.
If some of that money went directly to homeless or unemployed people, it would make their lives a bit more bearable. Consider it for yourself.
Jim Casey, Hamilton
CAPTION: With sidewalks not shovelled, people have no other option than to walk on the plowed streets. A fed-up reader wants to see some of their 2025 tax increase go to bylaw enforcement to deal with impassable sidewalks.Cathie Coward The Hamilton Spectator file photo

Document 60 of 500

NDP, Liberals release full platforms

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/ndp-liberals-release-full-platforms/docview/3169359808/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=NDP%2C+Liberals+release+full+platforms&amp;title=The+Spectator&amp;issn=11899417&amp;date=2025-02-22&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.16&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Spectator&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Ontario's NDP and Liberals are both pledging tens of billions of dollars in new spending to pay for their campaign promises, but are taking different approaches to the other side of the ledger, with the NDP proposing wealth taxes and the Liberals eyeing "efficiencies."
The two parties released their full platforms Friday with less than a week to go until next Thursday's election, and the Progressive Conservatives are set to release theirs on Monday. The Greens put out their platform on Feb. 12.
The NDP platform promises $70 billion in new spending over three years along with $37 billion in new revenue and savings, including tax increases.
The party estimates that new tax brackets for those earning $300,000, $400,000 and $500,000 a year would bring in about $3 billion per year.
It also says increasing the amount of capital gains subject to tax from 50 per cent to 80 per cent would bring in about $3.5 billion per year, and increasing the provincial land transfer tax rate on homes purchased for $3 million or more would see $33 million a year in revenue.
NDP Leader Marit Stiles had already announced the bulk of the party's promises ahead of the platform release, including a monthly grocery rebate, connecting everyone to a family doctor, establishing a public builder for 300,000 affordable homes and creating a universal school food program.
The NDP would put $10.5 billion over three years into public health - including their promise to attach everyone to team-based primary care.
Their home-building plan would cost $7.5 billion over three years and their grocery rebate would cost $11 billion over that time.
Doubling disability support payments and Ontario Works payments would cost nearly $23 billion over three years. Spending on education would increase by $13 billion over three years, not including spending $830 million per year to address a school maintenance backlog.
Ontario's Liberals are promising $65 billion in new spending over four years, along with finding more than $28 billion in efficiencies, in a platform they say contains no new taxes or tax increases.
Crombie also highlighted some previously unannounced promises on education in the platform, including building 90 new schools.
The Liberal platform estimates that their health-care promises, including attaching every Ontarian to a family doctor, would cost $29 billion over four years. A group of promises including home building, responding to tariffs and implementing tax cuts are costed at $26 billion over four years, though a more specific breakdown was not available.
The Canadian Press
CAPTION: Marit Stiles Bonnie Crombie

CREDIT: Allison Jones and Liam Casey The Canadian Press

Document 61 of 500

Voters urged to safeguard Greenbelt, local farmland: Grassroot groups say city's green ribbon of protected land remains under threat as election day looms

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/voters-urged-safeguard-greenbelt-local-farmland/docview/3169359792/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Voters+urged+to+safeguard+Greenbelt%2C+local+farmland%3A+Grassroot+groups+say+city%27s+green+ribbon+of+protected+land+remains+under+threat+as+election+day+looms&amp;title=The+Spectator&amp;issn=11899417&amp;date=2025-02-22&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.1&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Spectator&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Ontario's Greenbelt is about to celebrate its 20th birthday with a ballot box full of uncertainty.
The provincial snap election is Feb. 27 - one day before the anniversary of legislation passed in 2005 to protect the two-million-acre band of farmland, escarpment, forests and wetlands hugging the Greater Golden Horseshoe and most of Hamilton.
As a result, supporters of the green ribbon of protected land aren't quite sure whether to celebrate the anniversary or gird for protests - but about two-dozen of them gathered at a downtown Hamilton brew pub this week for a slightly nervous party.
"I'm always worried about the Greenbelt," said Environment Hamilton head and party organizer Ian Borsuk. "The (most recent) government promised they wouldn't touch it - and then they famously did."
Partygoers talked to candidates who dropped by, tried the beer and signed cards for the Birthday Belt that featured messages of both celebration and warning. "Green forever!" wrote one well-wisher.
"Hands off Doug!" read another message, a reference to Progressive Conservative leader Doug Ford's flip-flopping on a plan to allow mass Greenbelt development - including on 1,800 acres in Hamilton.
That plan was abandoned in 2023 in the face of resident opposition, scathing watchdog reports about preferential treatment for developers and a police probe that is ongoing. "I made a promise to you that I wouldn't touch the Greenbelt. I broke that promise. And for that I'm very, very sorry," Ford said at the time.
Soon afterwards, the Tory government also walked-back a separate forced urban boundary expansion that would have allowed development on nearly 5,400 acres of Hamilton farm and other rural land.
The twin reversals sparked celebration among agricultural and other grassroots groups - like Stop Sprawl HamOnt and Environment Hamilton - that worked to galvanize opposition to farmland development in and out of the Greenbelt.
The U-turns also spurred dismay for developers with plans already in the works for thousands of new homes - and anguish for families trying to sell Greenbelt land they argued can no longer support farming.
Now fast-forward to 2025: Do voters in this snap election still care about the Greenbelt and urban boundary fiascos?
Opposition parties hope so, with leaders like the Liberals' Bonnie Crombie and NDP's Marit Stiles suggesting the Tories called an early election to get ahead of any public findings from an ongoing RCMP investigation into the Greenbelt land swap. For his part, Ford has said he triggered the campaign because he needs a "strong mandate" to deal with U.S. tariff threats.
Some residents will always care, said political scientist Chris Erl - but short of a dramatic update from the RCMP probe, the Greenbelt controversy may not fire up voters worried about more visible problems like unaffordable housing, lineups for family doctors or tariff-related threat to jobs. "Just reminding people about the (controversial) things Doug Ford has done, I don't think that's going to be enough," he said earlier in the campaign.
But sensational police update or not, voters don't have to look to the past to find threats to the Greenbelt or Hamilton farmland, argued Borsuk.
For example, the next provincial government is expected to oversee a mandatory 10-year review of the Greenbelt, which, in theory, could see new requests to remove parcels for development. Ontario's fast-tracked road-building laws, meanwhile, are paving the way for the controversial new highways through protected land in the Greater Toronto Area.
And since Ford's dramatic reversal of Hamilton's urban boundary expansion, the province has gradually changed planning policies and laws to make it easier for developers to apply for urban boundary expansions - and harder for local councils to say no.
As a result, city planners have already received applications to expand housing into thousands of acres of farmland beyond Hamilton's urban border. Some of those would-be expansions now appear destined to be settled by the Ontario Land Tribunal, rather than by elected governments.
"It's demoralizing to see the government change the rules again and again to benefit land speculators," Borsuk said.
The solution, he argues, is to elect a government willing to maintain, but also grow the Greenbelt. "We need to protect the farmland we have left. The Greenbelt is the most effective way to do that."
Ahead of voting day, here's a reminder of ongoing Greenbelt and urban boundary debates in Hamilton:
Greenbelt encroachment
Doug Ford has famously bounced back and forth on Greenbelt development plans, and admitted as much during a recent televised election debate: "I think I was pretty clear. I said I was and then I wasn't and then I went ahead and did it."
But his 2023 flip-flop came with what sounds like an unequivocal promise: "It was a mistake to open the Greenbelt ... I'll be reversing the changes we made and won't make any changes to the Greenbelt in the future." His election opponents, including Crombie, Stiles and the Green's Mike Schreiner, have all vowed to protect the green ribbon of development-free land.
That sounds like the end of the story, right?
But "it's not over," said Peter Appleton, a local organizer with the Greenbelt Guardians group that helped organize local opposition to the original "Greenbelt grab."
Today, Appleton and other wary advocates are watching a new application filed by a large Toronto-based builder, Mizrahi Developments, seeking an urban boundary expansion that would also extend into the Greenbelt zone in Ancaster. The plan is to build 14 single-detached and 61 townhouses on a forested Sulphur Springs Road property.
The developer has not responded to Spectator questions about the application and, right now, such a build is clearly against the law, city staff have said.
But with a mandated review of the Greenbelt on the horizon, advocates worry it is possible developers will once again pitch targeted removals. Developers also unsuccessfully sought a Greenbelt carve-out for a different property on the edge of Ancaster during the cancelled 2022 land swap.
"Developers know they have a government in place that is willing to use MZOs (minister's zoning orders) to get their way," Appleton said.
Backdoor boundary expansion
The average voter might feel a bit of whiplash trying to keep track of urban boundary politics in Ontario.
In 2022, the province shocked Hamilton by forcing it to allow development across 5,400-plus acres of farmland in Stoney Creek and Glanbrook, despite a high-profile local council vote to "freeze" the urban boundary and grow up, not out. But the province reversed the decision the very next year, spurring relief among anti-sprawl advocates and farmers fearful of losing scarce agricultural land.
But it turns out that relief was premature.
Since then, the province has updated its growth policies and introduced new legislation that allows any developer to apply for an urban boundary expansion of any size, at any time. If a local council says "no," then the thwarted developer can appeal to the Ontario Land Tribunal.
That's already happening. With more than 3,000 acres of proposed urban boundary expansions back on the table, mostly in Elfrida, but with projects also pitched along Twenty Road West and in Ancaster. In one case, a development group is also seeking a ministerial order to override city opposition.
The past Tory government told The Spectator the policy changes were meant to bolster housing supply and ensure would-be developments are "considered in a balanced manner."
But fuming local councillors have characterized the cascade of rule changes as a "backdoor" effort to achieve the same urban boundary expansion Ontario publicly rolled back just a year ago.
The solution is simple, said Ancaster councillor Craig Cassar. The next provincial government needs to "restore decision-making power" about city growth to cities, he said, rather than using ministerial overrides or arm's-length tribunals.
"We have the local knowledge and experts, we're accountable to local residents," he said. "Let local decision-making be done at the local level."
Matthew Van Dongen is
a reporter at The Spectator. mvandongen@thespec.com
CAPTION: Peter Appleton is worried about a proposed residential townhouse development on Sulphur Springs Road in Ancaster - an area that is in the Greenbelt and beyond Hamilton's urban boundaries.Environment Hamilton head Ian Borsuk holds a "birthday card" for Ontario's Greenbelt.Mizrahi Developments is seeking an urban boundary expansion that would also extend into the Greenbelt zone in Ancaster. The plan is to build 14 single- detached and 61 townhouses on a forested Sulphur Springs Road property.Cathie Coward The Hamilton SpectatorMatthew Van Dongen The Hamilton SpectatorCathie Coward The Hamilton Spectator

Document 62 of 500

There's plenty of hypocrisy to go around

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/theres-plenty-hypocrisy-go-around/docview/3169359609/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=unknown&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=There%27s+plenty+of+hypocrisy+to+go+around&amp;title=The+Spectator&amp;issn=11899417&amp;date=2025-02-22&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.19&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Spectator&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: On Day 1 of the second term of his presidency, Donald Trump signed an executive order titled "Restoring Freedom of Speech and Ending Federal Censorship." He then doubled down on efforts to do exactly the opposite.
In the weeks since, Trump and his allies have waged a campaign of what can only be described as censorship - muzzling government scientists, public health officials and diplomats; scrubbing words like "diversity," "gender" and "pronouns" from government websites in the name of fighting "wokeness"; and punishing news organizations that don't play along with the president's latest delusion.
That last move stands out for its sheer bloody-mindedness. The White House banned reporters from the Associated Press, the news service that feeds thousands of news organizations around the world, from the Oval Office and Air Force One. All because the AP won't follow Trump's dictat to rename the Gulf of Mexico as the "Gulf of America."
Penalizing a news organization for speech that Trump doesn't like is, as the AP's top editor said, "a plain violation of the First Amendment to the U.S. Constitution." You know, the one that says government should not do anything "abridging the freedom of speech, or of the press."
There's more. On top of all this, Trump and his Oval Office sidekick Elon Musk are using the courts and economic muscle to intimidate much of the U.S. media. Trump is suing several news organizations for coverage he doesn't like; Musk is doing the same. As Andy Craig noted on MSNBC, "no matter how baseless, a lawsuit from Musk can cost millions to defend."
Musk has also had journalists whose coverage he doesn't like thrown off his social media site, X. The result is a media prone to pull its punches, fearful of retribution from the most powerful and the wealthiest men in America.
The first and most obvious thing to note here is the blatant hypocrisy of it all. Trump boasts that "we have saved free speech in America" even as his administration silences critics, bans language itself, and cows the news media. His vice-president, JD Vance, goes to a security summit in Munich to tell European leaders that the biggest threat they face is their own failure to defend free speech, or at least his concept of it. Even as he consorts with the leader of Germany's far-right Alternative für Deutschland party and fails to criticize the likes of Vladimir Putin.
The second notable thing is the silence of American conservatives who spent the past several years loudly complaining about censorship and "cancel culture" and accusing the left of trampling the very concept of free speech. As Rex Huppke writes in USA Today, "those folks are all pretty quiet right now." It turns out they were angry when the other side was riding high and policing their speech; now that their guy is in power and sticking it to their enemies, they don't have much to say.
There's plenty of hypocrisy to go around. Many on the left were indeed happy to dismiss free speech as at best outmoded and at worst racist. They did "cancel" people whose views they didn't like or stood by while the more extreme in their ranks drove supposed enemies off social media or had them fired for transgressing the progressive orthodoxies of two or three years ago.
They ignored warnings from some of us that defending the speech of those whose views you find objectionable is not just right in principle, but in your own best interest as well. It may not seem that way when you have the power to silence your critics. But when the tables are turned - as they inevitably will be - you'll realize the importance of upholding free expression for everyone.
In the United States, those tables have indeed turned with the rise of Trump. It's the progressive left that's now on the defensive and it's learning the hard way that the right to speak freely cannot be taken for granted. It can be easily eroded, even in the land of the First Amendment, by power and money. All the more reason to defend it, especially for those you disagree with.
Andrew Phillips is a staff columnist with Torstar.
CAPTION:

CREDIT: Andrew Phillips OPINION;

Document 63 of 500

Candidates spar over health care, housing: Conservative Amy Martin seeks to unseat Independent Bobbi Ann Brady in Haldimand-Norfolk

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/candidates-spar-over-health-care-housing/docview/3169359572/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Candidates+spar+over+health+care%2C+housing%3A+Conservative+Amy+Martin+seeks+to+unseat+Independent+Bobbi+Ann+Brady+in+Haldimand-Norfolk&amp;title=The+Spectator&amp;issn=11899417&amp;date=2025-02-22&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.7&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Spectator&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Housing and health care appeared to matter more than tariffs to the hundreds of Haldimand-Norfolk voters who packed the Simcoe legion for an election debate on Tuesday.
Progressive Conservative candidate Amy Martin said a re-elected Tory government needs a "strong mandate" to protect residents and local industry from potential economic devastation wrought by U.S. President Donald Trump's threatened tariffs.
Incumbent Independent Bobbi Ann Brady said she advocated for "Team Canada" with Republican lawmakers at a rural leaders summit in Iowa last month - but she also criticized Tory Leader Doug Ford for using the "distraction" of tariffs to justify a snap election.
Liberal candidate Vandan Patel, meanwhile, argued Ontario voters should leave trade policy to Ottawa and focus on what the province directly oversees. "This province needs hospitals. This province needs proper schools. Forget the tariffs," Patel said to cheers.
"We have no control over what Donald Trump does," added Green Party candidate Anna Massinen.
The race in the traditionally conservative riding is widely seen as a battle between Martin and Brady, who was elected as an Independent in 2022 after being denied in her bid to succeed her old boss, longtime Conservative MPP Toby Barrett, as the Tory candidate.
Martin says the riding would be better served by having an MPP in government, while Brady argues she can more forcefully advocate for residents from outside the party system.
The five participating debate candidates - Garry Tanchak of the New Blue Party sent his regrets - sparred over how to help residents of the rural riding find affordable homes and family doctors.
Martin touted the PC's $1.8-billion plan to connect every Ontarian with a physician while expanding access to care through pharmacies and in-home paramedic visits.
"It's nice to hear that after six years, Doug Ford might actually have a plan to fix this," retorted Brady, who wants to fast-track accreditation for foreign-trained doctors and give nurse practitioners the same OHIP billing rights as doctors.
Massinen - an environmental advocate and teacher who lives in Norfolk - criticized Ford's push to privatize health-care services instead of adequately funding the public system.
NDP candidate Erica Englert, a university student from Dunnville, said that a funding shortfall forces rural residents to seek health care outside their communities, while seniors who should be in long-term care languish in badly needed hospital beds.
"We shouldn't have to use Smile Cookie money to fund our hospital," Englert said, referring to an annual Tim Hortons fundraiser to benefit Dunnville's hospital.
Martin, on leave from her job as mayor of Norfolk to run provincially, noted hospitals in regions around Haldimand-Norfolk are expanding while local hospitals are left waiting.
"That's all the more reason why we should have an MPP at the table being part of these discussions before funding is allocated," she said.
Brady countered that the Conservatives have no answers when it comes to hiring more health-care workers. "Without staff who are paid appropriately and treated appropriately, a hospital bed is simply a piece of furniture," she said.
Patel, a Cambridge businessperson, said the health-care system must be strengthened to prepare for the next pandemic.
Push for more housing
The Conservative candidate supports adding accessory dwelling units in urban areas to boost housing stock and rental inventory.
"The answer on affordability is more housing, more infill, more density," Martin said, noting her party cut regulations to speed the planning process and waived fees on not-for-profit housing.
But Brady said giving developers a break on fees has not led to lower prices for first-time buyers or seniors looking to downsize.
"Ontario builds fewer homes per capita than other provinces," Brady said. "And I remind you, Doug Ford removed rent control."
The Greens want to preserve farmland and build within existing urban boundaries, prioritizing intensification over urban sprawl, Massinen said.
"We need a government that cares about the people - all the people - and not just lining the pockets of developers," she said.
Drawing on personal experience, Englert said covering the rising cost of tuition and rent makes saving for a down payment next to impossible.
"If we're building homes that no one can afford, what is the point of building those homes?" she said.
J.P. Antonacci's reporting is funded by the Canadian government through its Local Journalism Initiative. jpantonacci@thespec.com
CAPTION: Five of the six candidates vying to be Haldimand-Norfolk's next MPP spoke to a packed house at the Simcoe legion on Tuesday. Independent incumbent Bobbi Ann Brady, left, debated with Erica Englert of the NDP, PC candidate Amy Martin, Anna Massinen of the Green Party and Liberal candidate Vandan Patel.J.P. Antonacci photos The Hamilton Spectator

Remaining Mohawk staff feel 'overworked,' union says: Next government urged to support colleges in 'crisis'

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/remaining-mohawk-staff-feel-overworked-union-says/docview/3169359556/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Remaining+Mohawk+staff+feel+%27overworked%2C%27+union+says%3A+Next+government+urged+to+support+colleges+in+%27crisis%27&amp;title=The+Spectator&amp;issn=11899417&amp;date=2025-02-22&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.1&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Spectator&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: His life had revolved around Mohawk College for a decade.
Then, last month, Christopher Arndt, who had been a student, teacher and support staff, was notified his job was cut.
"The feeling is that none of it mattered," the former digital-skills technician said. "Like, it's gone. Move on, figure something out."
Ardnt's is one of at least 380 positions across the college axed in recent months as the Hamilton Mountain institution works to curb spending in the face of a projected $50-million deficit.
Meanwhile, employees who remain are shouldering more work in the wake of layoffs.
"People are already talking about how overworked they're feeling," said Sarah Harvie, vice-president of OPSEU 241, which represents support staff. "They're being expected to take on extra work or the duties of laid-off colleagues."
The college has "navigated an unprecedented amount of change in a very short period of time," acknowledging it's in a period of "transition," spokesperson Sean Coffey said in an email.
"It will take time to adjust to the changes that have taken place and the college is committed to supporting employees through this process," he wrote.
The college has said it is "rightsizing" the workforce to match its shrinking enrolment, nearly half of which was once international students.
Faculty, staff and students rallied at the Fennell Avenue West campus on Wednesday to "save our colleges," as underfunded institutions across the province face "financial crisis," unions said in a statement.
"Instead of providing bridge funding to help colleges adjust, (Doug) Ford is leaving them to cut programs and lay off hundreds of faculty and support staff provincewide," they wrote.
Unions have a message for voters, who head to the polls on Thursday: "We need a new government in Ontario that will support our public college system."
They're calling on the next provincial government to increase per-student funding and change the formula to allow for greater domestic enrolment, provide "immediate" bridge support and invest in full-time faculty and staff over "administrative bloat."
It's "heartbreaking" to say goodbye to colleagues and dozens of programs, said Heather Giardine-Tuck, president of OPSEU 240, which represents full-time and contract faculty.
Unions fear students will bear the brunt of these changes.
Faculty workloads will be "maximized," leaving less time for development and new course creation, Giardine-Tuck said.
The college increasingly relies on part-time employees, which "hurts the quality of education and reduces student services," unions say.
"The students are ultimately going to be the ones suffering," Harvie said. "The level of support is not going to be there."
Delivering quality education is the college's "top priority," Coffey said, noting recent decisions aren't just about cutting costs, but about being "responsive" to workforce needs.
"With 5,000 fewer students coming to the college, the demand for services and supports will not be the same, nor will the ability for the college to support and maintain staffing at previous levels," he wrote.
Students are already seeing the effects of the changes.
City School, a free program designed to help adults achieve post-secondary education, and the College Student Success Innovation Centre research hub are closing and a multi-sensory therapy lab has closed to the public, but remains open as a classroom.
The college's Makerspace, home to 3D printers, laser engravers and other creative tools, is "closed until further notice," Coffey said.
The Digital Creativity Centre where Arndt used to work was gutted, the union said, leaving few staff to support student endeavours in podcasting, design and other creative areas, and provide tech support.
Arndt's job loss comes with "anger and frustration" over an underfunded public system and Mohawk's reliance on international students to balance its budget.
"Our colleges built up everything that my career was connected to on a house of cards," he said. "Suddenly they're clutching their pearls and gasping and saying, 'Oh my God, how could this happen?' "
Kate McCullough is a reporter
at The Hamilton Spectator. kmccullough@thespec.com
CAPTION: Mohawk College union leaders are calling on the next provincial government to support the Ontario post-secondary sector in "financial crisis." Pictured here, from left, are OPSEU president Susan Lau, vice-president Sarah Harvie and Heather Giardine-Tuck, president of OPSEU 240.Christopher Arndt was laid off from his job as a digital-skills technician at Mohawk College last month.Kate McCulloughphotosThe Hamilton Spectator

Document 65 of 500

Feb. 21: Refusing to attend debates is undemocratic, thank Trump for our self-discovery and other letters to the editor

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/feb-21-refusing-attend-debates-is-undemocratic/docview/3169163418/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Feb.+21%3A+Refusing+to+attend+debates+is+undemocratic%2C+thank+Trump+for+our+self-discovery+and+other+letters+to+the+editor&amp;title=The+Hamilton+Spectator+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2025-02-21&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Hamilton+Spectator+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: We need to change the system
Re: Vote splitting helps Tories, Feb. 19
Eleanor Blackborow's solution to vote splitting, asking all left-leaning voters to vote Liberal, doesn't make sense. It would work exactly the same if they all voted NDP or all voted Green. A far better solution would be for all left-leaners to vote strategically for the candidate most likely to beat the PCs in their riding. There are websites that you can check for this information.
The essential next step would be for all parties (hopefully including the PCs) to get together and bring in proportional representation so we can stop this nonsense of strategic voting and finally get a government that fairly represents us all.
Heather Yoell, Dundas
Unite behind the Greens
The author is quite right about the problems of a divided political left, but her solution is wrong. Here's a better one. Voters of the left, unite and cast your vote for the Greens, a party with a fully-costed platform, the only viable energy and housing programs, and whose platform is fiscally responsible. They also have the benefit of having no political baggage, while being led by Mike Schreiner, the most respected member of the legislature. If you want to unite the left, there is a very positive Green alternative.
Andy Blackwood, Simcoe
Get out there and vote
For all the people that do not get up and vote, this is the time to get out there and cast a ballot. The last election Doug Ford got in because only 44 per cent of the people voted. He tried to take your farm land. He has watched as hospital emergency rooms close and schools are falling apart. I am not telling you how to vote, but just get out there and vote. If you don't vote than don't complain. You have rights and this is the time to use them.
Paul Elcome, Ancaster
Vote strategically on Feb. 27
Conservatives can still win a riding with a small percentage of the votes if the remaining votes are split poorly among the other parties. Strategic voting helps to ensure that doesn't happen. The non-partisan website votewell.ca explains how this works and makes recommendations for each individual riding. Check it out. Don't waste your vote.
Don Zeller, Ancaster
Why do voters tolerate this?
Of the many wrong-headed Ford government decisions - and there are many - the apparent policy to have PC candidates ditch local debates is a puzzle to me.
It isn't hard to understand the policy itself because PC candidates would be put on the spot to defend policies that have been unpopular and are very expensive, like using taxpayer money to fund projects that are not priorities for most Ontarians. Most say they care about health care, housing and affordability, which have not been the focus for the Ford government.
What is difficult to understand is why PC voters accept this undemocratic behaviour and continue to vote for candidates who are unable or unwilling to answer questions from local constituents. In fact, living in a provincial riding held by a PC MPP, it is impossible to get a response from his office between elections. Why do Progressive Conservative voters put up with this kind of inaccessible governance?
Frances Murray, Brantford
A record they can't defend
You have to wonder why PC candidates don't show up for debates for this election. It must because they can't defend their dismal record on protecting the Greenbelt, housing, a tunnel in Toronto and myriad of other issues they prefer to duck. Who can vote for such a group of chickens?
Larry Sicinski, Hamilton
Protect our drinking water
Re: Burlington shouldn't cater to the minority in Nelson quarry debate, Feb. 18
A quarry does not belong on the Mount Nemo plateau. The Mount Nemo plateau is the point of origin for the headwaters of Grindstone Creek. A drinking water source we need to protect. It is up to us to protect our woodlands and water access for future generations. Never minimize the value of our green space.
Deb Fester, Hamilton
Thank Trump for self-discovery
The culture of the U.S. has been so tacitly woven into our Canadian culture over the decades that we have been oblivious to it. We are now slowly loosening the weave and rediscovering that true north, strong and free tapestry that defines our Canada.
Judith Keczan, Lincoln
What happened to compassion?
The DOGE crew led by Elon Musk has essentially shut down USAID. This agency was responsible for earmarking and distributing funds for humanitarian causes both domestic and abroad. In their eyes, the organization was rampant with fraud and incompetence. Where is their compassion and empathy for their fellow, less fortunate man? The concept of the Good Samaritan has all but been forgotten. Now Musk wants to jail those from "60 Minutes" who aired an episode featuring a USAID insider. Reminds me of Nazi Germany in the 1930s. When will we ever learn?
Sinclair Tyios, Burlington
Ukraine didn't start the war
Re: Trump says Ukraine started the war that's killing its citizens, Feb. 19
The thing that is so confounding is that Donald Trump's "crew of sycophants" is willing to forego integrity, decency and truth to propagate the inconceivable.
We all know Ukraine did not start the war. They were invaded, without provocation, by a greedy, poisonous dictator who has shown his true colours time and time again. Vladimir Putin plays Trump like a cheap violin. A few baseless, flattering sentences and Trump is putty in his hands. Sad.
Yes, I am a proud Canadian, one of the millions who are astounded at the short memory of the current president. And you can forget about grabbing Canada as the 51st state; felons are not our preferred leaders.
Paula Kent Kuchmey, Caledonia
Who should be at the table?
Who should be at the table? In 1938 at the Munich Conference (which resulted in the Munich Agreement), president Edvard Benes of Czechoslovakia was not at the table. If he had been he could have mentioned that Czechoslovakia had some of the strongest fortifications in Europe plus 35 combat divisions, plus the 100 French divisions. At this time Germany had only 17 divisions available. Neville Chamberlain and Edouard Daladier should not have been at the table. Benes should have been as the country that is being invaded should always be at the table. President Volodymyr Zelenskyy of Ukraine should be at the table. Let's not forget the lesson of 1938.
Ken Lowe, Grimsby
Think and act with your wallet
It is normal to want to react when a bully threatens you. However, before doing anything drastic, suppose we take a step back, a deep breath and think carefully about the situation.
Firstly, the convicted felon who is currently running the U.S. either doesn't understand the principles of tariffs or he doesn't care about the consequences to his citizens. It is not Canadians who will be paying the 25 per cent tariff on goods imported into the U.S. It is the citizens of America who will see prices go up and feel it in their wallets.
Secondly, all Canadians (industry, business and citizens) should think and act with our wallets. Stop buying citrus from Florida, bourbon from Kentucky, chocolate from Pennsylvania, using Netflix or Disney Plus, vacationing in the U.S., etc. I am sure there are other ways as well that we can influence the American economy if we put our minds to it!
We can give these measures a few months to have their effect on the "average" American citizen (not the billionaires who are running the show) to see if wiser heads prevail. This will also allow our governments time to formulate a well-thought-out retaliatory plan if one is needed, which can then be implemented whenever it becomes necessary.
Suzanne Cooper, Ancaster
Trump hasn't thought this through
Despite U.S President Donald Trump's claims to be striving for autarky (national self-sufficiency through domestic production of the needs of society) as opposed to globalism, a closer look clearly indicates that he is in fact not securing the future of the American economy, but instead betting on intimidation to impose his will on other nations, as his unpredictable mood of the day dictates. Real autarky requires not merely penalizing imports to protect domestic production, but also having a viable policy to develop industry and agriculture to compensate for the reduced importation of goods.
Trump would also have to create the robotics and job-training programs needed to replace the lost labour of the illegal migrants, who he proposes to deport en mass, in order to sustain the American economy. Lesser options include prisoner and military personnel labour, which carries with it the very real danger of the state having an incentive to falsely accuse citizens of crimes, in order to enslave them as a labour force to replace lost illegal migrant labour.
Autarky may be a desirable aim, but it requires more than whimsical and potentially temporary tariffs to succeed. Taxing out foreign competition is only half of the equation. There also has to be an increase in domestic production and investors need the capital, incentive, and security needed to make it happen.
Trump is instead working toward a return of the robber baron Gilded Age, where corrupt plutocrats can rape the nation and enslave the masses.
Gary Werfhorst, Hamilton
CAPTION: As election day draws near, more readers are urging others to vote strategically or to rally behind one party.
