Document 66 of 500

Liberal and NDP candidates trade fire: Hamilton East- Stoney Creek TV debate missing PC incumbent

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/liberal-ndp-candidates-trade-fire/docview/3169001151/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Liberal+and+NDP+candidates+trade+fire%3A+Hamilton+East-+Stoney+Creek+TV+debate+missing+PC+incumbent&amp;title=The+Spectator&amp;issn=11899417&amp;date=2025-02-21&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.3&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Spectator&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Progressive Conservative incumbent candidate Neil Lumsden was missing in action at the televised Hamilton East-Stoney Creek riding debate Wednesday, which left the Liberal and NDP candidates turning their fire on each other.
When Liberal candidate Heino Doessing spoke of his party's plan to address the housing crisis - in part, he said, by reducing development charges to encourage building homes for low- and middle-income Ontarians - NDP candidate Zaigham Butt jumped in.
"He doesn't understand, the Liberals had 15 years (in power from 2003 to 2018) and they did not deliver for this province," said Butt. "What makes (Liberal Leader) Bonnie (Crombie) different? What makes you different? Enough is enough. People have lost faith in politics because of the provincial Liberals."
"Everyone knows Bonnie Crombie is a new leader, and we have a whole new slate of candidates," Doessing replied. "We believe in what we're going to do and we are going to deliver."
Green Party candidate Pascale Marchand said the two rival candidates were "just raising your voices to speak louder than the other" and "rehashing the past."
Marchand later took a jab at her opponents, suggesting the NDP suffers from too much internal "party politics" and that "Liberal complacency has led to Tory majorities."
Lumsden's PC campaign team told The Spectator he had "committed to other events in our community" by the time he had received an invitation to appear in the Cable 14 debate.
The Spec asked a question to the candidates about how they would strive to balance representing the desires of their riding constituents with sticking to their party's policies.
Doessing answered that his "No. 1 job" is to be "the community's voice."
"The Liberals say one thing and do another," retorted Butt. "With the NDP, what you see is what you get."
Marchand replied that the Greens do not utilize a party whip to ensure MPPs vote in lockstep, "so I have the ability to step up for my community."
Other issues debated included environmental protection, public transit and health care.
Butt spoke of the need to more aggressively tackle Ontario's "affordability crisis."
In this vein, all three candidates pledged to double Ontario Disability Support Program payments. Marchand said the Greens would double Ontario Works payments as well.
In addition, she said the Greens would resurrect "basic income," the guaranteed living income program first tested in Hamilton in 2017, cancelled by the Tories one year later before the pilot program had concluded.
Three additional candidates were featured at the debate presenting pre-recorded video messages, including Communist Drew Garvie, who said Ontario's minimum hourly wage should be increased from $17.20 to $25.
Doessing called for expanding health-care funding in Ontario to cover mental illnesses, and pledged to cut income taxes and reduce hydro bills for middle income families.
"It was the Liberals that sold off Hydro One," said Butt. "We are paying more on hydro because of the Liberals. Remember, this is why they lost (the 2018 election) ... Year after year we hear promises from the Liberals and they don't deliver, and this time will be no different."
"It's kind of rich coming from the NDP, given that we have a (Hamilton) NDP mayor who keeps raising taxes," said Doessing. He said that if TV viewers watching the debate had a drinking game where "every time my friend from the NDP brings up the past, oh my God, you'll be having a good time."
Jon Wells is a Spectator
reporter. Reach him at jwells@thespec.com
CAPTION: Liberal candidate Heino Doessing, left, and NDP candidate Zaigham Butt engaged in some heated exchanges at the Cable 14 live debate Wednesday night. The candidates are running in the Hamilton East-Stoney Creek riding.Cathie Coward The Hamilton Spectator

CREDIT: Jon Wells Reporter

U.S. imports make up 10% of its shelves, Loblaw says: Company expects more customers to 'Buy Canadian'

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/u-s-imports-make-up-10-shelves-loblaw-says/docview/3169000448/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=U.S.+imports+make+up+10%25+of+its+shelves%2C+Loblaw+says%3A+Company+expects+more+customers+to+%27Buy+Canadian%27&amp;title=The+Spectator&amp;issn=11899417&amp;date=2025-02-21&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.12&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Spectator&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Loblaw, Canada's largest food and drug chain, only buys a small fraction of its goods from the U.S., CEO Per Bank said on Thursday, tamping down fears that the looming Trump tariff war could cause a spike in grocery bills.
In a quarterly update, Bank said the company has been lining up alternatives to American suppliers in case U.S. President Donald Trump follows through on his 25 per cent tariff threat that would likely prompt retaliatory tariffs from Ottawa on U.S. goods entering Canada.
According to Bank, U.S. imports make up less than 10 per cent of Loblaw's overall cost of goods.
"And it's mostly in produce," he said during a conference call with financial analysts Thursday morning.
After years of high inflation, Bank said he isn't expecting consumers will fork over an extra 25 per cent for U.S. imports, prompting a major shift to Canadian-made products or imports from other countries.
He said Loblaw is already tracking a significant impact from the "Buy Canadian" movement among shoppers, with a roughly 10 per cent sales lift in Canadian-produced goods during one week earlier this month.
In the household goods and cleaning aisle, for example, Loblaw sources products from about 30 vendors in the U.S., but also sells Canadian-made versions under its private label "control brands" - President's Choice and No Name.
If Ottawa decides to slap tariffs on U.S. cleaning products, those big brands "will not be competitive anymore," Bank said, "and all the sales will go to our control brands."
"That's good for Canada, it's good for customers and it's good for us," he added. "If you have a 25 per cent tariff on Kellogg's, if that should come so far, then of course we'll probably promote our control brands instead of Kellogg's because nobody will buy it if it's 25 per cent more expensive."
Though it's not fully clear which American goods would be subject to tariffs from Canada, Ottawa has already telegraphed what it would target in the first round of a trade war with the U.S.
Before Trump agreed earlier this month to delay tariffs for 30 days to allow for negotiations, the Trudeau government announced a list of 25 per cent counter tariffs on $30-billion worth of American goods - the first phase of a broader, $155-billion tariff plan.
At the time, academics and food executives said the initial list appeared to have been designed to blunt the impact at grocery costs for Canadian shoppers.
During the financial update on Thursday, Loblaw, a network of more than 2,800 stores that includes Shoppers Drug Mart, No Frills, Fortinos and Real Canadian Superstore, reported $14.6 billion in fourth-quarter retail sales, up three per cent compared to the previous year.
But profits at the chain dipped by 14.6 per cent to $462 million, which the company said was due to a re-evaluation of its liability for outstanding loyalty points, since more customers are participating in its PC Optimum program.
CAPTION:

Document 68 of 500

'Forget the tariffs': Candidates in Haldimand-Norfolk spar over health care, housing

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/forget-tariffs-candidates-haldimand-norfolk-spar/docview/3168997143/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=%27Forget+the+tariffs%27%3A+Candidates+in+Haldimand-Norfolk+spar+over+health+care%2C+housing&amp;title=The+Hamilton+Spectator+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2025-02-21&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Hamilton+Spectator+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Housing and health care appeared to matter more than tariffs to the hundreds of Haldimand-Norfolk voters who packed the Simcoe legion for an election debate on Tuesday.
Progressive Conservative candidate Amy Martin said a re-elected Tory government needs a "strong mandate" to protect residents and local industry from potential economic devastation wrought by U.S. President Donald Trump's threatened tariffs.
Incumbent independent Bobbi Ann Brady said she advocated for "Team Canada" with Republican lawmakers at a rural leaders summit in Iowa last month - but she also criticized Tory leader Doug Ford for using the "distraction" of tariffs to justify a snap election.
Liberal candidate Vandan Patel, meanwhile, argued Ontario voters should leave trade policy to Ottawa and focus on what the province directly oversees. "This province needs hospitals. This province needs proper schools. Forget the tariffs," Patel said to cheers.
"We have no control over what Donald Trump does," added Green Party candidate Anna Massinen.
The race in the traditionally conservative riding is widely seen as a battle between Martin and Brady, who was elected as an independent in 2022 after being denied in her bid to succeed her old boss, longtime Conservative MPP Toby Barrett, as the Tory candidate.
Martin says the riding would be better served by having an MPP in government, while Brady argues she can more forcefully advocate for residents from outside the party system.
The five participating debate candidates - Garry Tanchak of the New Blue Party sent his regrets - sparred over how to help residents of the rural riding find affordable homes and family doctors.
Martin touted the PC's $1.8-billion plan to connect every Ontarian with a physician while expanding access to care through pharmacies and in-home paramedic visits.
"It's nice to hear that after six years, Doug Ford might actually have a plan to fix this," retorted Brady, who wants to fast-track accreditation for foreign-trained doctors and give nurse practitioners the same OHIP billing rights as doctors.
Massinen - an environmental advocate and teacher who lives in Norfolk - criticized Ford's push to privatize health-care services instead of adequately funding the public system.
NDP candidate Erica Englert, a university student from Dunnville, said that a funding shortfall forces rural residents to seek health care outside their communities, while seniors who should be in long-term care languish in badly needed hospital beds.
"We shouldn't have to use Smile Cookie money to fund our hospital," Englert said, referring to an annual Tim Hortons fundraiser to benefit Dunnville's hospital.
Martin, on leave from her job as mayor of Norfolk to run provincially, noted hospitals in regions around Haldimand-Norfolk are expanding while local hospitals are left waiting.
"That's all the more reason why we should have an MPP at the table being part of these discussions before funding is allocated," she said.
Brady countered that the Conservatives have no answers when it comes to hiring more health-care workers. "Without staff who are paid appropriately and treated appropriately, a hospital bed is simply a piece of furniture," she said.
Patel, a Cambridge businessperson, said the health-care system must be strengthened to prepare for the next pandemic.
Push for more housing
The Conservative candidate supports adding accessory dwelling units in urban areas to boost housing stock and rental inventory.
"The answer on affordability is more housing, more infill, more density," Martin said, noting her party cut regulations to speed the planning process and waived fees on not-for-profit housing.
But Brady said giving developers a break on fees has not led to lower prices for first-time buyers or seniors looking to downsize.
"Ontario builds fewer homes per capita than other provinces," Brady said. "And I remind you, Doug Ford removed rent control."
The Greens want to preserve farmland and build within existing urban boundaries, prioritizing intensification over urban sprawl, Massinen said.
"We need a government that cares about the people - all the people - and not just lining the pockets of developers," she said.
Drawing on personal experience, Englert said covering the rising cost of tuition and rent makes saving for a down payment next to impossible.
"If we're building homes that no one can afford, what is the point of building those homes?" she said.
J.P. Antonacci's reporting is funded by the Canadian government through its Local Journalism Initiative. jpantonacci@thespec.com

Document 69 of 500

Crombie would expand OHIP with mental-health coverage: Ford vows to break down interprovincial trade barriers and cut red tape

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/crombie-would-expand-ohip-with-mental-health/docview/3168997087/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Crombie+would+expand+OHIP+with+mental-health+coverage%3A+Ford+vows+to+break+down+interprovincial+trade+barriers+and+cut+red+tape&amp;title=The+Spectator&amp;issn=11899417&amp;date=2025-02-21&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.7&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Spectator&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Ontario NDP Leader Marit Stiles cast her ballot in the advance polls that opened Thursday, as her party lost a second election candidate, while the other party leaders talked mental health, interprovincial trade and rental housing policies.
The Progressive Conservative, Liberal and NDP leaders have all said their full platforms will come "soon," with the election now one week away.
Liberal Leader Bonnie Crombie said a Liberal government would cover mental health care under the Ontario Health Insurance Plan. It would cost about $1 billion, she said, and would fill in the gaps of private coverage rather than replacing it.
"There will be more explanation as we roll out the platform," she said after her speech at the Canadian Club in Toronto. "I will say that it will serve those people who don't have coverage today."
Stiles also said her platform release would be "imminent," offering the snap election timing as a defence for not having it out sooner.
"Doug Ford called an election three weeks ago," she said after voting at an advance poll in the morning. "His intention was to catch us all off our game."
Later in the day, the NDP candidate for Elgin-Middlesex-London announced that she is resigning her candidacy. It came after the Progressive Conservatives dug up comments Amanda Zavitz - who is white - made saying that she wishes she was a Black woman and that she wishes she had lived experiences of poverty and addiction.
The Progressive Conservatives have dug up dirt on a series of Liberal candidates over the past week, but have also been turning their attention to NDP candidates as well, branding them as "radical."
PC Leader Doug Ford has focused much of his attention stateside this election, suggesting the ballot question is who will best protect Ontario from a Donald Trump presidency in the U.S. and threats of tariffs. He shifted focus slightly Thursday.
Standing beside Nova Scotia Premier Tim Houston at an announcement in Milton, Ont., he said a re-elected Progressive Conservative government would break down interprovincial trade barriers and cut associated red tape, blaming the federal government for existing logjams.
"The biggest barrier blocking us from getting our immense resources to market, to getting our critical minerals out of the ground, or building the nuclear power plants that will fuel our economic growth for decades to come, it's not in Washington, Moscow or Beijing," he said.
"It's right here in Ottawa. We need the federal government to get out of the way. We need the federal government to cut its layers and layers of bureaucracy and red tape by scrapping impact assessment requirements that raise costs, slow down approvals and stop provinces and territories from building."
It was the first time in 10 days that Ford took questions on the campaign trail, and he fielded a variety of them on Thursday.
On previous comments suggesting he supports the death penalty, he said he was just joking.
On an uncosted promise to build a tunnel under Highway 401 that experts say could cost tens of billions of dollars he said he and his team are "visionaries."
And on the $200 cheques his previous government mailed out, he said he hasn't received his, but he will donate it to a food bank.
Crombie said the country definitely needs to get rid of interprovincial trade barriers, but she questioned why Ford didn't do it during his previous time in government.
"He's had seven years, but now on the eve of a crisis with a trade war threat and tariff threat, now he's moving on it," she said.
The Canadian Press
CAPTION: Advance voting is underway across Ontario with election day set for Feb. 27.Giordano Ciampini The Canadian Press

Document 70 of 500

Auto tariffs will be destructive for both sides

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/auto-tariffs-will-be-destructive-both-sides/docview/3168997045/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=unknown&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Auto+tariffs+will+be+destructive+for+both+sides&amp;title=The+Spectator&amp;issn=11899417&amp;date=2025-02-21&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.10&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Spectator&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: For more than 200 years, the United States and Canada have been more than just allies - we've been family. And like siblings, we may have our petty rivalries in hockey, baseball and basketball.
But when it comes to national security and economic prosperity, we've stood together since the War of 1812 ended.
Our bond is so close that missing a highway exit in Detroit can land you in Windsor - and our economies are just as intertwined. Until recently, trade flowed freely and border crossings were smooth.
As a Michigander, I see the connection between us all the time: my neighbour drives a Canadian-built Toyota RAV4, while Michigan factories rely on Canadian parts. Every day, thousands of workers cross the Detroit-Windsor bridge or tunnel, keeping both economies strong.
But that's changing. U.S. President Donald Trump's tariff war threatens this partnership and I worry about the potential long-term damage - not just to our national economies, but also to hard-working people in Michigan and Ontario.
As an old-fashioned Republican, I believe in free markets and free trade. Tariffs interfere with business, drive up costs and weaken our economies.
Trump's plan to impose auto tariffs on Canada "in the neighbourhood of 25 per cent," as he put it on Tuesday, has broken the trust that once existed between our two countries. And given Trump's ever-changing trade policies, it's no wonder Canadians are nervous about what's next. His proposed steel and aluminum tariffs are already rattling the auto industry, putting every job in the sector - on both sides of the border - at risk. Ford CEO Jim Farley warned that such tariffs could devastate our joint auto industry like never before.
And when the auto industry suffers, so do Michigan and Ontario. Both economies depend on tax dollars from these companies and the workers who power them. Trade isn't just about money, after all - it's about people, families and communities, too. Tariffs mean job losses, higher prices and struggling small businesses.
In 2024, more than $700 billion in goods moved between the U.S. and Canada, and 25 per cent of that went through Detroit-Windsor. Making trade more difficult threatens our shared success. It reminds me of the Prohibition era, when Michigan and Canadian smugglers carried liquor across the frozen Detroit River. Will this generation's outlaws be smuggling car parts rather than liquor? If Canada can't count on the U.S. to honour trade agreements, it'll look elsewhere.
Auto tariffs will disrupt trade and damage the foundations of our U.S.-Canada partnership. Michigan and Ontario would bear the brunt of the fallout, with job losses and business closures, but it wouldn't be just us for long. Michigan has historically led the U.S. by a few years when it comes to recessions. The Great Recession hit Michigan in 2001, for example, while the rest of the country didn't feel it until 2008. If the automotive industry goes dark in Michigan and Ontario, it won't take years for the rest of the North American economy to slow down. It will take months.
The best way forward is clear: Strengthen our partnership. Don't weaken it. So, Justin Trudeau and Doug Ford, it's time to determine how you're going to respond to what Trump does next. The economies of Michigan and Ontario - not to mention the U.S. and Canada - are depending on you. Let's keep our economic bridge (and tunnel) open.
Andrea Bitely is a contributing columnist for the Detroit Free Press and the founder and principal of Bitely Communications.
CAPTION:

CREDIT: Andrea Bitely;

Letters

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/letters/docview/3168996996/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=unknown&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Letters&amp;title=The+Spectator&amp;issn=11899417&amp;date=2025-02-21&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.11&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Spectator&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: We need to change the system
Re: Vote splitting helps Tories, Feb. 19
Eleanor Blackborow's solution to vote splitting, asking all left-leaning voters to vote Liberal, doesn't make sense. It would work exactly the same if they all voted NDP or all voted Green. A far better solution would be for all left-leaners to vote strategically for the candidate most likely to beat the PCs in their riding. There are websites that you can check for this information.
The essential next step would be for all parties (hopefully including the PCs) to get together and bring in proportional representation so we can stop this nonsense of strategic voting and finally get a government that fairly represents us all.
Heather Yoell, Dundas
Unite behind the Greens
The author is quite right about the problems of a divided political left, but her solution is wrong. Here's a better one. Voters of the left, unite and cast your vote for the Greens, a party with a fully costed platform, the only viable energy and housing programs, and whose platform is fiscally responsible. They also have the benefit of having no political baggage, while being led by Mike Schreiner, the most respected member of the legislature. If you want to unite the left, there is a very positive Green alternative.
Andy Blackwood, Simcoe
Get out and cast your ballot
In the last election, Doug Ford got in because only 44 per cent of the people voted. He tried to take your farmland. He has watched as hospital emergency rooms close and schools fall apart. I am not telling you how to vote, but just get out there and cast a ballot. If you don't vote than don't complain. You have rights and this is the time to use them.
Paul Elcome, Ancaster
Vote strategically on Feb. 27
Conservatives can still win a riding with a small percentage of the votes if the remaining votes are split poorly among the other parties. Strategic voting helps to ensure that doesn't happen. The non-partisan website votewell.ca explains how this works and makes recommendations for each individual riding. Check it out. Don't waste your vote.
Don Zeller, Ancaster
Why do voters tolerate this?
Of the many wrong-headed Ford government decisions - and there are many - the apparent policy to have PC candidates ditch local debates is a puzzle to me.
It isn't hard to understand the policy itself because PC candidates would be put on the spot to defend policies that have been unpopular and are very expensive, like using taxpayer money to fund projects that are not priorities for most Ontarians. Most say they care about health care, housing and affordability, which have not been the focus for the Ford government.
What is difficult to understand is why PC voters accept this undemocratic behaviour and continue to vote for candidates who are unable or unwilling to answer questions from local constituents. In fact, living in a provincial riding held by a PC MPP, it is impossible to get a response from his office between elections. Why do Progressive Conservative voters put up with this kind of inaccessible governance?
Frances Murray, Brantford
A record they can't defend
You have to wonder why PC candidates don't show up for debates for this election. It must be because they can't defend their dismal record on protecting the Greenbelt, housing, a tunnel in Toronto and myriad of other issues they prefer to duck. Who can vote for such a group of chickens?
Larry Sicinski, Hamilton
Protect our drinking water
Re: Burlington shouldn't cater to the minority
in Nelson quarry debate, Feb. 18
A quarry does not belong on the Mount Nemo plateau. The Mount Nemo plateau is the point of origin for the headwaters of Grindstone Creek. A drinking water source we need to protect. It is up to us to protect our woodlands and water access for future generations. Never minimize the value of our green space.
Deb Fester, Hamilton
Canada rediscovering itself
The culture of the U.S. has been so tacitly woven into our Canadian culture over the decades that we have been oblivious to it. We are now slowly loosening the weave and rediscovering that true north, strong and free tapestry that defines our Canada.
Judith Keczan, Lincoln

Angst over closure of supervised drug-use sites: Concern expressed during symposium on issues of mental health and addiction

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/angst-over-closure-supervised-drug-use-sites/docview/3168996994/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Angst+over+closure+of+supervised+drug-use+sites%3A+Concern+expressed+during+symposium+on+issues+of+mental+health+and+addiction&amp;title=The+Spectator&amp;issn=11899417&amp;date=2025-02-21&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.1&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Spectator&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: More grief, including a spike in deadly overdoses, will follow the closure of Hamilton's supervised drug-consumption sites this spring, doctors, advocates and users warn.
Under new provincial rules, two services - one operated by Hamilton Urban Core Community Health Centre and the other by YWCA Hamilton - will cease at the end of March.
"It's going to be a big change. It's not going to be a good one for people," said Robert (Randy) Cameron, who uses Urban Core's downtown site.
Consumption and treatment services (CTS) sites provide people who use illicit drugs a place to consume under the supervision of trained staff in an effort to prevent overdose deaths, but it also connects them with other health and social services.
The closure of Urban Core's CTS at St. Paul's Presbyterian Church on James Street South will put more strain on the health-care system, Cameron predicts.
For instance, it provided clean needles. This means fewer people share or reuse needles - practices that can lead to serious infections and stays in hospital, he explained. "Besides me feeling it, it's going to be felt by everybody, especially the taxpayer, I think, in coming years."
The closures will ramp up costs, but the most immediate concern is heightened risk of overdose deaths, said Dr. Tim O'Shea, a member of the Hamilton Social Medicine Response Team. "The evidence would say that when you take away places for people to use drugs safely, they're going to use drugs in unsafe places and in unsafe ways."
O'Shea and Cameron offered insight on the provincial policy shift during a symposium hosted by Urban Core at its Cannon Street East health centre Wednesday.
The forum on mental health and addiction explored a variety of topics. A common thread, from one speaker to the next, was concern about the upcoming closures.
This past summer, the Progressive Conservative government announced the closure of CTS sites in Ontario by March 31 under new rules that bar them within 200 metres of schools and child-care centres.
At the same time, Health Minister Sylvia Jones unveiled a $378-million plan to open 19 homelessness and addiction recovery treatment (HART) hubs across the province.
Jones, who associated CTS sites with spikes in crime, said the HART hubs aspire to "shift addiction toward a model that emphasizes recovery, treatment and community safety."
They will combine a range of services: primary care, addiction and mental-health treatment, social services and supportive housing. "I want to be clear. They will not provide 'safer' supply (of drugs), supervised drug consumption or needle-exchange programs," Jones emphasized.
In Hamilton, opioid-related deaths reached a high of 166 in 2022 but then dropped off to 148 in 2023, the city noted. That's still more than in past years. In 2018, there were 128 deaths, a considerable spike from the previous year of 88. In 2016, the death toll was 52.
The overdose problem, driven by fentanyl and unpredictable mixes with other substances, overlaps with a homelessness crisis. In Hamilton, roughly 1,600 people are experiencing homelessness and about 250 people are known to live outside, the city noted.
Cameron recently lined up housing after a few years spent unhoused. "I know the situation out there. It's a desperate feeling ... They're stigmatized."
Meanwhile, he understands why some are upset about the symptoms of street life, such as discarded needles, Cameron said. "But to shut down (supervised consumption sites) is going to make these people straying more out on the streets and it's going to cause more issues."
Urban Core, which has operated a supervised drug-use service since 2018, is closing its CTS on March 31, but opening Hamilton's sole HART hub on April 1. It won't replace the CTS site, but the hub will serve as a "lifeline for those at risk of fatal overdoses and recurring homelessness," executive director Sandy Ezepue told Wednesday's symposium.
Dr. Robin Lennox, the NDP's candidate in Hamilton Centre in Ontario's Feb. 27 election, told the forum the fundamentals of the hub model are sound, but axing supervised consumption services in the process is wrong-headed.
"It's like installing airbags in your car and removing the seatbelts. It doesn't make sense. We need more of both, not less of either," said Lennox, a family physician who's also medical director of the YWCA safer-use program.
That initiative, which opened in 2022 in partnership with Keeping Six and the Hamilton Social Medicine Response Team, is not a CTS but operates as a temporary urgent public health needs site under the auspices of Health Canada.
At the time of Jones's announcement, the YWCA wasn't immediately clear on how the policy pivot would affect its program but now realizes it must close under the new provincial rules, CEO Medora Uppal told The Spectator. "Not happy about the restrictions on safe consumption because they have been life-saving for everyone who's come and used the program," she said and noted more than 90 overdoses have been reversed during the initiative.
Despite the loss of supervised consumption services, the YWCA aims to redouble its harm-reduction efforts to "reduce the risk to women we're supporting as much as possible," Uppal said.
During the 2023-24 fiscal year, Urban Core's CTS at St. Paul's had 3,400 visits for supervised consumption with 46 overdoses reversed on the premises. But it also saw more than 6,000 visits for other services, which included nursing assessments, wound care, mental-health support and referrals to primary care and social services.
"For many, this site represents hope and a chance of survival when other systems have failed them," Ezepue said. With the CTS closure, the plan is to maintain a drop-in service at St. Paul's and launch a mobile medical clinic, she noted.
When it's open, the HART hub will provide an array of services and include primary care, mental health, addiction care, pain management and life-skills development.
In addition to its Cannon Street East centre, Urban Core is renting a building on Aberdeen Avenue to serve as a residential facility with treatment beds and supportive housing for clients. "The goal is that by the time they leave the 24-7 residential treatment, they're ready to come out and reintegrate into society," Ezepue said.
The plan also involves co-locating services with such partners as social service organization Good Shepherd and Indwell, which operates deeply affordable and supportive housing.
But as Urban Core works toward opening the hub by April 1, Ezepue acknowledges need in the community will outstrip its services.
That has also been the case with the CTS, she said. "One is not enough."
Teviah Moro is a reporter
at The Hamilton Spectator.
tmoro@thespec.com

Document 73 of 500

Ads in America should help Ontario, not Ford

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/ads-america-should-help-ontario-not-ford/docview/3168996987/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=unknown&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Ads+in+America+should+help+Ontario%2C+not+Ford&amp;title=The+Spectator&amp;issn=11899417&amp;date=2025-02-21&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.10&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Spectator&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: For months, the Ontario government has been promoting the narrative that "it's happening here" through a barrage of ads published in newspapers across the province and online.
The ads state that "more people are working than ever before," with "over 700,000 more people working today than in 2018." That's the year Doug Ford's Progressive Conservatives came to power.
Technically, these were non-partisan ads.
They complied with the Government Advertising Act of 2004, which allows for the promotion of Ontario or any part of it as a desirable location for living, working, investing, studying or visiting. Yet the PCs previously took issue with this very approach, when they opposed a decision by Kathleen Wynne's Liberals to relax rules around partisan government advertising.
Once in power, the PCs apparently discovered that government advertising can be an effective tool for fostering positive impressions of the governing party.
When such advertising precedes an election, it supplements a party's paid election advertising.
There would likely be an uproar if the "it's happening here" campaign ran during this snap election, as additional scrutiny is usually applied during an election period to ensure that advertisements funded by the government do not appear to support the governing party. But placing ads in the Wall Street Journal that portray Ontario as America's "secure economic partner in an insecure world" falls under advocacy advertising.
Ontario and Canada should both undertake media campaigns in the U.S. to convey strong messages to American audiences.
The Wall Street Journal ad, for example, advocates effectively for Canada's critical minerals sector: "We have an abundance of the critical minerals needed for both economic growth and the new technologies that keep our countries safe." It further states, "In this time of global instability, let's strengthen the economic bonds we've always shared."
This seems reasonable - until you consider that supporters of U.S. President Donald Trump's "America first" agenda would likely endorse both statements, with the rationale that "strengthening the economic bonds we've always shared" implies erasing the border.
Although the province's ads were no doubt intended to alter American decision-makers' and influencers' perceptions of Ontario and Canada, this is a fluid situation, to say the least. The Ford government is walking a fine line as it attempts to fight back against Trump's threats without antagonizing Trump's team.
One presumes the Ontario government tested these ads via focus group before spending massive amounts of money booking slots in U.S. media - but what about now? Do they still resonate after a month of Trump's executive orders? Do they firmly communicate our position?
Canada is a polite neighbour. We initially laughed off Trump's threats to annex the country and make it the 51st state. But it's time for a different narrative, one that's far less deferential.
Ford, who has styled himself as Ontario's protector and claims to need a stronger mandate for the fight ahead, might want his U.S. ad campaign to be more explicit.
The message should be: Ontario is open for business, but it's not for sale.
For now, these commercials and video clips of Ford's visits to Washington remain a win-win for the PCs, but not for Ontario taxpayers.
Éric Blais is president of Headspace Marketing in Toronto, a marketing
communications firm
helping clients build
their brands in Quebec.
CAPTION:

CREDIT: Éric Blais;

Document 74 of 500

Hamilton Mountain race heating up: Candidates trade 'personal' jabs, bring in well-known faces to campaign

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/hamilton-mountain-race-heating-up/docview/3168996870/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Hamilton+Mountain+race+heating+up%3A+Candidates+trade+%27personal%27+jabs%2C+bring+in+well-known+faces+to+campaign&amp;title=The+Spectator&amp;issn=11899417&amp;date=2025-02-21&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.4&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Spectator&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: For over a decade, provincial elections on the Hamilton Mountain were relatively quiet, with the NDP firmly in control and no real campaign controversies.
Not this year.
As election day nears, Mountain candidates are campaigning with cabinet ministers and federal party heavyweights to court voters in the city's only open provincial seat. Some are also sparring on social media or by news release - and they're not pulling punches.
The departure of longtime New Democrat MPP Monique Taylor - who will run in the next federal election - has made the Hamilton Mountain race one of the city's "most interesting and competitive," local political scientist Chris Erl said.
And with jabs flying among two of the competing parties, "This is certainly a more personal election that you've seen in quite some time."
Orange since 2011, electoral projection website 338Canada suggests the riding could be a toss-up - and parties are throwing high-profile faces into the campaign.
"It's a riding where all the parties have been focusing a lot of their energy and resources, possibly to detriment of other ridings in the city," Erl said.
Liberal challenger Dawn Danko, a trustee and former school board chair, has benefited from more than one visit from Ontario party leader Bonnie Crombie. She has also campaigned with federal MPs Filomena Tassi and Lisa Hepfner and was spotted at a local event for federal leadership candidate Mark Carney earlier this week.
Conservative candidate Monica Ciriello, a top city bylaw official, has also enjoyed party help. She campaigned with cabinet ministers and stood with Doug Ford in a visit with a construction union Thursday.
Efforts to flip the seat have also erupted in a communications and social media war of words between the Liberal candidate and NDP opponent Kojo Damptey, a community activist, university lecturer and former head of the Hamilton Centre for Civic Inclusion.
On social media, Danko has labelled Damptey the "defund the police" candidate and criticized him for his encampment activism.
The NDP replied this week with a media release accusing Danko of being "in the pocket" of would-be Greenbelt developers.
It references $250 donations each from Sergio and Angela Manchia to the college professor's successful campaign for school board trustee in 2022.
When asked about the NDP claims, Danko said in a statement she is firmly opposed to Greenbelt development. She didn't comment on the referenced developer donations.
Sergio Manchia, a developer and longtime former planner, has donated to multiple municipal candidates over the years. He was named in a provincial watchdog report as a developer who stood to benefit from the Ford government's now-cancelled plan to open the Greenbelt. Manchia has argued his lobbying to open certain Greenbelt lands preceded the Ford government, followed the rules and was "no secret."
Danko also took the opportunity to fire another jab at Damptey and claimed he's funded by people associated with "extreme activist organizations" who put abolishing the police and supporting encampments above the concerns of residents.
As a past municipal candidate, Damptey advocated for scrutiny of the police budget and a reallocation of any "excess" funds to other community safety alternatives, which included housing and mental health.
Damptey did not reply directly to questions about the "defund" label lobbed by his opponent. He stressed by email his campaign is focused on connecting with residents and hearing their concerns. He defended the NDP's public questioning of donations to Danko, however, and argued it's fair to ask a candidate about her connection to developers involved in a provincial scandal that's still under police investigation.
Open-seat races often come with a side of political punches and amped-up rhetoric and that's no different in this case, said Erl. The key is why. "I think a lot of it comes down to focusing on a message they can control," he said.
On one hand, Erl said the Liberals might be trying to shy away from Danko's time on Hamilton's public school board, which has been mired in code of conduct probes in recent years.
On the other, he thinks the NDP could be trying to distract from the riding's Green Party candidate, Joshua Czerniga, having served on its executive branch - as well as Damptey's past support of former party member Sarah Jama, who was turfed from caucus and is now running as an Independent in Hamilton Centre.
"Both campaigns are using personal attacks to make sure the message best fits what they want voters to hear and shies away from some of the controversies they have in their backgrounds."
And what about Ciriello? She has been largely absent from the public punches.
Ciriello said by email her campaign is taking "a different approach," devoting more time to door knocking than social-media barbs.
"I think there's probably a segment of the Conservative machinery in the city that thinks if the battle between Kojo and Dawn gets nastier and really splits people, then they might be able to sneak up the middle," Erl said.
A debate among the Mountain candidates is set to air on Cable 14 on Friday at 7 p.m. Ciriello won't attend, but the other candidates are expected to be there.
Sebastian Bron is a reporter
at The Hamilton Spectator. sbron@thespec.com.
CAPTION: Longtime New Democrat MPP Monique Taylor will run in the next federal election. Her departure has made the Hamilton Mountain race one of the city's "most interesting and competitive," local political scientist Chris Erl says. Kojo Damptey, NDP candidate for Hamilton Mountain.Dawn Danko, Liberal candidate for Hamilton MountainMonica Ciriello, PC candidate for Hamilton MountainThe Hamilton Spectator file photoThe Hamilton Spectator file photoThe Hamilton Spectator file photo

CREDIT: Sebastian Bron Reporter

'It's going to be felt by everybody': Angst over closure of Hamilton supervised drug-use sites

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/going-be-felt-everybody-angst-over-closure/docview/3168996845/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=%27It%27s+going+to+be+felt+by+everybody%27%3A+Angst+over+closure+of+Hamilton+supervised+drug-use+sites&amp;title=The+Hamilton+Spectator+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2025-02-21&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Hamilton+Spectator+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Document 76 of 500

Barbs thrown at missing PC candidate: Hamilton West-Ancaster-Dundas hopefuls discuss health care, housing during debate

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/barbs-thrown-at-missing-pc-candidate/docview/3168996792/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Barbs+thrown+at+missing+PC+candidate%3A+Hamilton+West-Ancaster-Dundas+hopefuls+discuss+health+care%2C+housing+during+debate&amp;title=The+Spectator&amp;issn=11899417&amp;date=2025-02-21&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.3&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Spectator&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: A missing PC candidate and his party leader dominated much of the chatter during a provincial candidates debate for Hamilton West-Ancaster-Dundas on Wednesday night.
Plenty of barbs were thrown at Doug Ford and his Progressive Conservative (PC) Party during the debate - and all three attending candidates said they had hoped to pose questions to local Tory candidate John Demik during the live show at Cable 14.
Host Mike Fortune announced at the beginning of the show that Demik was unable to attend "due to a miscommunication" between Cable 14 and his campaign team. Demik, a construction industry veteran, said in a statement his plan is to be "present in our community, at people's doors and listening to concerns about the unprecedented economic uncertainty Ontario is facing."
All but one of the Tory provincial candidates in Hamilton appear to be skipping local televised election debates this year - with the exception being Flamborough-Glanbrook incumbent Donna Skelly, who took on challengers earlier Wednesday in her riding debate.
While Demik and Mountain candidate Monica Ciriello say they did not receive or were unaware of invites to the debates, the others told The Spectator that there had been scheduling conflicts on those dates.
That raised the ire of participating candidates at the HWAD debate Wednesday.
During an open forum, Green candidate Guy Bisson said he had hoped to ask the Tory candidate about his party's plan to deal with the rising costs associated with extreme weather.
"It's ironic that the party that called the election has directed its members not to participate in the democratic process," said Bisson, a retired labour lawyer.
Incumbent NDP candidate Sandy Shaw said it had been "difficult to prepare" for the debate not knowing whether Demik would attend. She had hoped to ask him about the party's handling of health care.
"It's just so shameful," Shaw added.
And Julia Brown, candidate for the Liberals, said she was going to ask how Ford justified calling an early election despite having a majority. Candidates also debated issues of crime, health care, housing, urban boundary expansion and taxation on Wednesday.
Among the highlights:
On health care, Brown shared that she herself doesn't have a family doctor, while touting the Liberals' plan to pay nurses a living wage and ensure all Ontarians have a doctor within the next four years. Shaw fired back at Brown, noting that the crisis in health care didn't start under Ford, but instead when the Liberals were in power from 2003 to 2018. "They had so long to fix it and they didn't do it," said Shaw.
On crime and theft, all three candidates agreed on the need to invest in the justice system, with only Bisson not explicitly advocating for additional funding for police. He noted that the province needs to invest in addressing the root causes of crime, such as the opioid crisis and housing precarity. "We have a crisis of caring in this province," added Bisson.
Brown, who works in financial aid at Mohawk College, accused the NDP of making campaign promises that would require higher taxes amid the affordability crisis. Shaw said tax dollars "would be respected" by the NDP.
Also running in the riding are: Spender Rocchi, representing the None of the Above Direct Democracy Party; Nori Smith, representing the Electoral Reform Party; and Lee Weiss Vassor, representing the New Blue Party.
Fallon Hewitt is a reporter
at The Hamilton Spectator.
fhewitt@torstar.ca
CAPTION: CHCH reporter Eric Vienneau asks a question during a candidate debate for the Hamilton West-Ancaster-Dundas riding Wednesday night. From left, Julia Brown (Liberal), Sandy Shaw (NDP) and Guy Bisson (Green).Cathie Coward The Hamilton Spectator

Document 77 of 500

Hamilton Mountain election race gets 'personal' as parties battle for city's only open seat

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/hamilton-mountain-election-race-gets-personal-as/docview/3168996778/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Hamilton+Mountain+election+race+gets+%27personal%27+as+parties+battle+for+city%27s+only+open+seat&amp;title=The+Hamilton+Spectator+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2025-02-21&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Hamilton+Spectator+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: For over a decade, provincial elections on the Hamilton Mountain were relatively quiet, with the NDP firmly in control and no real campaign controversies.
Not this year.
As election day nears, Mountain candidates are campaigning with cabinet ministers and federal party heavyweights to court voters in the city's only open provincial seat. Some are also sparring on social media or by press release - and they're not pulling punches.
The departure of longtime New Democrat MPP Monique Taylor - who will run in the next federal election - has made the Hamilton Mountain race one of the city's "most interesting and competitive," local political scientist Chris Erl said.
And with jabs flying among two of the competing parties, "This is certainly a more personal election that you've seen in quite some time."
Orange since 2011, electoral projection website 338Canada suggests the riding could be a toss-up - and parties are throwing high-profile faces into the campaign.
"It's a riding where all the parties have been focusing a lot of their energy and resources, possibly to detriment of other ridings in the city," Erl said.
Liberal challenger Dawn Danko, a trustee and former school board chair, has benefited from more than one visit from Ontario party leader Bonnie Crombie. She has also campaigned with federal MPs Filomena Tassi and Lisa Hepfner and was spotted at a local event for federal leadership candidate Mark Carney earlier this week.
Conservative candidate Monica Ciriello, a top city bylaw official, has also enjoyed party help. She campaigned with cabinet ministers and stood with Doug Ford in a visit with a construction union Thursday.
Efforts to flip the seat have also erupted in a communications and social media war of words between the Liberal candidate and NDP opponent Kojo Damptey, a community activist, university lecturer and former head of the Hamilton Centre for Civic Inclusion.
On social media, Danko has labelled Damptey the "defund the police" candidate and criticized him for his encampment activism.
The NDP replied this week with a media release accusing Danko of being "in the pocket" of would-be Greenbelt developers. It references $250 donations each from Sergio and Angela Manchia to the college professor's successful campaign for school board trustee in 2022.
When asked about the NDP claims, Danko said in a statement she is firmly opposed to Greenbelt development. She didn't comment on the referenced developer donations.
Sergio Manchia, a developer and longtime former planner, has donated to multiple municipal candidates over the years. He was named in a provincial watchdog report as a developer who stood to benefit from the Ford government's now-cancelled plan to open the Greenbelt. Manchia has argued his lobbying to open certain Greenbelt lands preceded the Ford government, followed the rules and was "no secret."
Danko also took the opportunity to fire another jab at Damptey and claimed he's funded by people associated with "extreme activist organizations" who put abolishing the police and supporting encampments above the concerns of residents.
As a past municipal candidate, Damptey advocated for scrutiny of the police budget and a reallocation of any "excess" funds to other community safety alternatives, which included housing and mental health.
Damptey did not reply directly to questions about the "defund" label lobbed by his opponent. He stressed by email his campaign is focused on connecting with residents and hearing their concerns. He defended the NDP's public questioning of donations to Danko, however and argued it's fair to ask a candidate about her connection to developers involved in a provincial scandal that's still under police investigation.
Open-seat races often come with a side of political punches and amped-up rhetoric and that's no different in this case, said Erl. The key is why. "I think a lot of it comes down to focusing on a message they can control," he said.
On one hand, Erl said the Liberals might be trying to shy away from Danko's time on Hamilton's public school board, which has been mired in code of conduct probes in recent years.
On the other, he thinks the NDP could be trying to distract from the riding's Green Party candidate, Joshua Czerniga, having served on its executive branch - as well as Damptey's past support of former party member Sarah Jama, who was turfed from caucus and is now running as an independent in Hamilton Centre.
"Both campaigns are using personal attacks to make sure the message best fits what they want voters to hear and shies away from some of the controversies they have in their backgrounds."
And what about Ciriello? She has been largely absent from the public punches.
Ciriello said by email her campaign is taking "a different approach," devoting more time to door knocking than social-media barbs.
"I think there's probably a segment of the Conservative machinery in the city that thinks if the battle between Kojo and Dawn gets nastier and really splits people, then they might be able to sneak up the middle," Erl said.
A debate among the Mountain candidates is set to air on Cable 14 on Friday at 7 p.m. Ciriello won't attend, but the other candidates are expected to be there.
Sebastian Bron is a reporter at The Hamilton Spectator. sbron@thespec.com.
CAPTION: Dawn Danko, Liberal candidate for Hamilton MountainMonica Ciriello, PC candidate for Hamilton MountainKojo Damptey, NDP candidate for Hamilton Mountain.Longtime New Democrat MPP Monique Taylor will run in the next federal election. Her departure has made the Hamilton Mountain race one of the city's "most interesting and competitive," local political scientist Chris Erl says.
CREDIT: Sebastian Bron

Document 78 of 500

Most Tory candidates skipping local debates: Incumbent PC Donna Skelly agreed to face challengers

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/most-tory-candidates-skipping-local-debates/docview/3168996763/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Most+Tory+candidates+skipping+local+debates%3A+Incumbent+PC+Donna+Skelly+agreed+to+face+challengers&amp;title=The+Spectator&amp;issn=11899417&amp;date=2025-02-21&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.1&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Spectator&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: All but one of the Tory provincial candidates in Hamilton appear to be skipping local election debates - an increasingly common, Ontario-wide trend some critics call disrespectful to voters.
Cable 14 has again partnered with local news agencies, including The Spectator, to stage candidate debates in each of the five Hamilton-based provincial ridings.
All Progressive Conservative candidates were invited, local organizers say - but as of debate week, only one had agreed to take part, Flamborough-Glanbrook incumbent Donna Skelly. The former city councillor and TV reporter was also the lone local PC debate representative in 2022.
Worth noting: for this snap election, two local Tory candidates say they did not receive or were unaware of invites to the debates, which continue this week through Friday.
Regardless, candidates for the PC party have faced criticism in Niagara, Burlington, Kitchener and Brantford for ducking locally organized debates in 2025.
Voters "have a right to be upset" if candidates do not show up for local debates, suggested McMaster University political science professor Peter Graefe, who said some residents value the chance to see would-be MPPs publicly defend their party's ideas - or record in government - ahead of election day.
"You'd expect parties that respected the democratic process to make sure their candidates were there," he said.
But Graefe also argued the practical value of debates and associated news coverage has become much less important over time to political parties, which increasingly rely on sophisticated data to track and woo voters.
There may also be "risk mitigation" in play to guard against video-recorded gaffes, he said.
"You are probably going to be more effective driving votes to your party during that two or three hours by knocking on doors rather than (by participating in) a debate."
That reasoning echoes the response offered by the Progressive Conservative central campaign to questions about whether local candidates were being told not to participate in debates.
"Ontario PC candidates will spend every day of the campaign at people's doors, speaking to voters about our plan to protect Ontario," said an emailed statement from a party spokesperson.
Locally, candidates offered different reasons for not taking part in debates.
The first televised local debate on Tuesday went ahead without Tory candidate Sarah Bokhari, who told debate organizers she had a scheduling conflict.
The incumbent Tory and cabinet minister in Hamilton East-Stoney Creek, Neil Lumsden, said via his campaign he had "already committed to other events" by the time he received a debate invite.
In 2022, Lumsden made news when he told The Spectator he was directed to not take part in local debates in that election.
"Listen, I'm fine with it," Lumsden said at the time. "I believe strongly that we do things as a party and I'm one of the people on Doug Ford's team and, as corny as it sounds, if the decision is made, then the decision is made. I believe that, whatever the decision made ... I'm fine with it."
Two other candidates, Monica Ciriello in Hamilton Mountain and John Demik in Hamilton West-Ancaster-Dundas, said via campaign staff they were not aware of, or did not receive, invitations to the debates. Demik's campaign manager later said the invite was sent to an email not used by the campaign.
Cable 14 general manager Jonathan Freedman said organizers reached out more than once through what they thought were the correct channels in early February - and the dates were advertised in The Spectator starting last Saturday.
"They can still show up; I hope they do," he said in advance of the second night of debates Wednesday, adding organizers had once more reached out again to the campaigns.
Demik did not participate and his absence was explained during the debate as due to a "miscommunication." It was not immediately clear if Ciriello was considering the renewed invitation to take part in the Hamilton Mountain debate on Friday.
On Tuesday, some Hamilton Centre candidates criticized the decision by Progressive Conservative candidates to skip debates, calling it a matter of accountability.
Liberal candidate Eileen Walker called the provincewide trend a "planned tactic" and suggested it was meant to prevent candidates from inadvertently saying things they shouldn't.
"I can understand not wanting to have to answer for (Tory Leader Doug) Ford's failures," said NDP candidate Robin Lennox. "(But) if you're going to be the one making policy, you need to be able to defend that policy."
Matthew Van Dongen is
a reporter at The Spectator. mvandongen@thespec.com
CAPTION: PC candidates Monica Ciriello, Neil Lumsden, Sarah Bokhari and John Demik indicated they would not be taking part in Cable 14 election debates. Ciriello and Demik said they did not receive the invitation.The Hamilton Spectator image

CREDIT: Matthew Van Dongen Reporter

The last debate, which will cover the Hamilton Mountain, will take place on Feb. 21 from 7 to 8:30 p.m. Watch at Cable14.com and check TheSpec.com for coverage.

Title: No-show Progressive Conservative candidates spur criticism at Hamilton election debates

Document 81 of 500

Feb. 20: Sing loud and proud tonight, make your voice heard on Feb. 27 and other letters to the editor

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/feb-20-sing-loud-proud-tonight-make-your-voice/docview/3168623383/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Feb.+20%3A+Sing+loud+and+proud+tonight%2C+make+your+voice+heard+on+Feb.+27+and+other+letters+to+the+editor&amp;title=The+Hamilton+Spectator+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2025-02-20&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Hamilton+Spectator+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Sing loud and proud tonight
On Thursday night, when Team Canada takes to the ice in Boston to challenge the Americans for supremacy in hockey, I have a request for all Canadians. From the homes, pubs, arenas and TV rooms from Yellowknife, Vancouver, Windsor, St. John's and all points in between, when our anthem is sung, stand up and join in strong and loud! Be as proud as you can be for Canada on this occasion. Our great nation needs all the patriotism it can muster during these challenging times.
When the American anthem is sung, listen with respect. Our threat is not from American citizens, but from those who continue to demand we become the 51st state. Both nations have proud pasts and have worked together well for many years and hopefully will be good neighbours for years to come.
So sing our anthem with pride and vigour this Thursday so it can be heard all the way to Boston and around the globe! Go Canada go!
Mike LoSchiavo, Stoney Creek
Booing is directed at Trump
Re: Anthem booing not patriotic, Feb. 19
To the letter writer who deemed the "hateful" booing at American hockey players in Montreal as disgraceful, unpatriotic and an embarrassment, let's be clear here. The booing was in no way directed at the hockey players or anything to do with the game or sports in general. It was 100 per cent directed at Donald Trump and his vision of Canada becoming their 51st state.
Rusty Escott, Dunnville
Let Americans know how you feel
For those Canadians who are upset with booing American teams, get with the program! We live in a free country and can express our disdain with the U.S. in the only way we can. Where were these upset people when the "freedom convoy" rode around with upside down flags? I guess that was OK. Or banners that said F-Trudeau? No problem?
We are Canadians, we are not a doormat for the U.S. If you cannot boo at a hockey game, where can you boo? These same people have no problem when gloves get dropped and two players pummel each other.
As far as it being classless? You have to fight fire with fire. We want Americans to know and the powers that be we are not an appendage of the United States nor do we ever intend to be. Boo on!
Robin Magder, Dundas
Hockey by and for Canadians
As a proud Canadian, why are we not moving forward with our own Canadian national hockey league? Montreal, Toronto, Winnipeg, Ottawa, Calgary, Vancouver and Edmonton already exist. Let's just add a team or two like Halifax or Quebec City or Hamilton or any other city who wants and can support a national hockey team? We have the best hockey players in the world. Why are we subsidizing the U.S. with our hockey talent? Do you think current teams in Utah, California or Florida or wherever would survive without our teams and our rich hockey history drawing them to the rink? No way.
Pat Banning, Dundas
City right to fight quarry expansion
Re: Burlington shouldn't cater to the minority in Nelson quarry debate, Feb. 18
Kevin Powers is a public relations consultant hired by Nelson Aggregates to write opinion pieces for his client to promote quarry approval. His commentary published on Feb. 18 spread misinformation, which may mislead the public.
We agree that one neighbour in North Burlington is forcing us to spend millions of our taxpayer dollars and years of our time to expand its profit-driven ambitions for Mount Nemo. That neighbour is Nelson Aggregates.
Unfortunately for citizens, we have to defend the well water, air quality and endangered species on Mount Nemo once again, even after a lengthy provincial hearing denied Nelson's quarry proposal in 2012.
The facts were not, and are still not, on Nelson's side. According to Burlington's own air-quality experts, illegal levels of dangerous fine dust have been released from the site into the neighbourhood. Blasting has caused huge chunks of limestone rock to land on neighbours' front lawns and the adjacent golf course. Thankfully no one has been killed from these fly rock incidents.
Burlington and Halton Region councils and citizens groups have to fight this dangerous idea once again, and they are right to do so. Nelson is exaggerating regarding the legal fees charged to date, and wrong when they say a majority of residents want a new quarry.
Burlington and Ontario residents know that protecting well water, air quality, endangered species and prime farmland, especially within a UNESCO World Biosphere Reserve, is in the long term interest of everyone, just not the pocketbook of one out-of-touch neighbour.
Sarah Harmer and Gord Pinard, Protecting Escarpment Rural Land (PERL) and CORE Burlington
Parties need to work together
I keep reading about the possibility that Doug Ford could win this upcoming election but wonder with his awful track record and swath of destruction who in their right mind would vote for him? Developers and many business types that stand to reap the benefits he might willingly grace them with comes to mind. My advice to the other political parties, lay your egos aside for once and come together as one force that would guarantee the Progressive Conservatives are finally shown the door. We truly deserve better government.
Robert Panchyson, Burlington
Make your voice heard on Feb. 27
There is an election Feb. 27. How should you vote you ask? Watch the TV news, buy a local paper, go online and then pick the candidate who resonates with your views.
Don't vote for the incumbent if you can't recall what he or she has done for you. Don't vote for a candidate who can't make time for a media interview, or an all-candidates meeting. If a candidate can't make time now to engage with you, what makes you think they will suddenly find time to hear your concerns when the paycheque comes, whether they talk to you or not.
Write a letter, make a call or even protest to make your voice heard. Choose a candidate and vote. This election matters, so make sure you feel that what you want from government matters too.
Jean Wise, Hamilton
Limit police budget increases
Re: It's time to cap city spending and tax hikes, councillor says, Feb. 12
In a recent Spectator article, Coun. Brad Clark is quoted as saying that in future budgets all departments should be limited to an increase equal to about the rate of inflation. I like the idea, but have one concern.
Would this limitation also apply to the largest expenditure on the budget, Hamilton Police Services? Clark has never shown any indication he is willing to force the HPS to find savings and continually votes in favour of its massive increases each year.
I, like most Hamiltonians, am not anti-police. Many of us find it difficult, however, to understand how requests for large increases to their annual budget continue to be approved with little or no scrutiny.
So Coun. Clark, is HPS also included in your future motion, or will they be exempt?
Don Holland, Hamilton
Wrong time to raise taxes
Re: Hamilton council settles at 5.6 per cent budget hike, Feb. 13
The economics of private business requires accountable leaders to budget and adjust accordingly, with foresight and constraint (needs vs. wants). It would appear many on city council lack such budgetary skill, or worse, have given way to personal agenda "wants." If, as the mayor has said, "we're growing and aging, and this budget is balanced to meet those needs," then I ask, if aging is a concern, should excessive tax hikes likewise, not be of concern for such homeowners on fixed incomes? With steel tariffs and other levies to follow, does council's budget take into account reciprocal, homeowner impact? Let me further remind of the biggest budgetary unknown to come: LRT and its to-be-determined debt load - a budgetary "want" this council is pushing to expedite! All we ask as taxpayers is for level-headed budgeting and collective accountability of our property tax dollars.
Greg MacDougall, Binbrook
Heartfelt thanks to hospital staff
Recently, my husband was admitted to Hamilton General Hospital and sent to the heart investigation unit (HIU). There he received an angiogram, during the course of which the medical staff discovered an 85 per cent blocked artery and put in a stent. Throughout the day, both of us received nothing but kindness, professionalism and compassion and he received excellent medical care. In this day and age when we hear so much gloom and doom about Canada's health-care system, it was refreshing to see that not only did he receive what might easily be life-saving care, but received it in a timely manner from extremely competent staff. Kudos and heartfelt thanks (pun intended, but deeply felt nonetheless) to the staff at HGH and the HIU in particular.
Cara Blackwood, Simcoe
Spec keeps us informed
If you've missed reading the Spec in the last few days, shame on you. There have been several articles dealing with Doug Ford and the election as well as Canada standing up to Donald Trump and his bull. Two of the better ones that I have read and are worth reading are by Susan Koswan, who talks about Ford and all his transgressions since he has been in power and thinks he deserves another four years. The other is by Donald Abelson and talks about Canada taking a stand on the American craziness coming from there.
Thanks for the Spec printing these good commentaries to inform their readers.
Suzanne Bacik, Stoney Creek
Americans need to stand up to Trump
Recent polls in the U.S., show a strong approval for Donald Trump's actions, although the respondents do not like his behaviour and wish he would be "less rude." You are reminded that the citizens of the U.S. voted him into office and, by a majority of the populace approving of his actions, are complicit in backing him.
My wife and I have many American friends who are totally shocked that his disgraceful actions are allowed to flourish in your country. No one seems to have the intestinal fortitude to take him on. Even though he does not rule Canada, his negative influence has spread in countless homes beyond your borders, even to the hockey arenas.
It's not OK to treat your best friend in such a despicable manner. Trump's behaviours will, in the long run, make Canada a better place to be. When will the citizenry of the U.S. grow a spine and deal with him?
Bob Vardy, Ancaster
CAPTION: Nelson Quarry wants to expand onto land that is now a small golf course and unused fields. Burlington and Halton Region councils and citizens groups have to fight this dangerous idea once again, and they are right to do so, readers write.Fans cheer as Canada warms up prior to Four Nations Face-Off hockey action against the United States in Montreal on Saturday. Readers remain divided on booing the American national anthem.

Full text: Good morning! It's Feb. 20, here are the top stories today in the Hamilton area.
Today's traffic and weather
Take your snowbrush. It isn't much but the Hamilton area could see about two centimetres of snow beginning in the morning Thursday. Environment Canada says winds from the northwest will make the wind chill feel like -16 C. More flurries are expected tonight. There are no alerts.
Traffic and transit updates
Find the latest provincial road closures and traffic incidents via Ontario 511. Closures in Hamilton are available on the Hamilton police incident feed.
Taking transit today? Find the latest GO Transit service updates here. The latest information on local bus services are available here: Hamilton, Burlington and Oakville.

Some 166 investors lost money to Douglas Grozelle. Some of them lost everything.
Measles count rises to 53 in Grand Erie region
Health unit is hosting vaccination clinics on Feb. 20 in Simcoe and Feb. 24 in Vittoria
City seeking tariff input from businesses
The online survey is open until Feb. 28.
Party politics or doing politics differently? NDP, former party incumbent spar at Hamilton Centre debate
Progressive Conservative candidate Sarah Bokhari didn't participate due to 'scheduling conflict'
McMaster University 'silent' on staffing changes at campus sexual violence office
The sexual violence prevention and response office is down to one staff member after the other two were terminated last month; Students say that's not enough
Theatre review | HTI gets it just about right with its rambunctious production of 'The 25th Annual Putnam County Spelling Bee'
Go root for your favourites in a comedy that celebrates correct spelling as if it was the watchword in life, writes Gary Smith.
Subscribe to our newsletters for the latest local content.
