Document 83 of 500

The world needs more Canadian patriotism

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/world-needs-more-canadian-patriotism/docview/3168616949/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=unknown&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=The+world+needs+more+Canadian+patriotism&amp;title=The+Spectator&amp;issn=11899417&amp;date=2025-02-20&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.14&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Spectator&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Doug Ford stated Canada must come first, a sentiment later called into question by an unnecessary $180-million election.
Pierre Poilievre then announced his "Canada First plan" to respond to Donald Trump's economic attacks.
Unfortunately for Poilievre and Ford, their "Canada First" slogan triggers memories of the late 19th century "Canada Firsters." They opposed French Canadian influences, Indigenous Peoples' rights and pushed for a British-centric Canadian identity dedicated to the empire.
Ford's open mic gaff, where he claimed he was "100 per cent happy that Trump won" reveals a deeper problem. These 21st-century Canada Firsters, no doubt, love Canada, but they want to create a Canada in the likeness of Trump's "America First."
Poilievre was quick to make a Canada First announcement reminiscent of Trump's approach and proposed significant cuts to foreign aid. He argued much of this aid is "wasted, stolen and swallowed up by bureaucracies that do not serve our interests." He intends to use the money to fund a new military base in Nunavut.
Like their 19th-century cousins, these Canada First advocates envision a Canada with an America-centric identity - essentially the same idea, just under a different empire.
Courtesy of Trump, there is a resurgent wave of Canadian nationalism. However, do Canadians really believe in a "knock-off" of America First nationalism? I think not.
Many Canadians desire a more compassionate form of ethical patriotism with a national purpose.
The late Canadian theologian Douglas John Hall observed we seek "not nationalism but the care of the nation." Hall introduced the concept of "patriotic engagement," which emphasized a thoughtful and responsible love for Canada and combined appreciation with critical reflection. Instead of self-serving nationalism, he advocated for a patriotism that actively pursues justice, acknowledges both past and present shortcomings and strives for the common good with humility and ethical responsibility.
This patriotic engagement understands Canada is our home on Native land. For John Ralston Saul, Canada is a "Métis nation" because our core identity, values and political culture are deeply shaped by Indigenous rather than purely colonial European traditions. Canadians' history of negotiation, willingness to compromise, support for multiculturalism and social inclusion reflects Indigenous roots.
Patriotic engagement believes Canadians collaborate and work together to ensure everyone has enough and is cared for. Even as our politicians fail us on these issues, universal health care, public education and social services are celebrated as national achievements and symbols of pride and embody our core values of fairness and equality.
Patriotic engagement reflects Canada's commitment to promoting peace and global solidarity to improve the world for all. We take pride in our peacekeeping tradition, despite reduced contributions in recent years. In 2000, more than 600,000 (one in 50) Canadians signed the jubilee petition to cancel the debts of the poorest countries.
Patriotic engagement is not acquiescence; it firmly defends our national interests. Leaders must engage in diplomatic negotiation, not appeasement with Trumpists. No more humiliating trips, like the recent visit of the premiers to the White House. We will offer something only if we receive something respectful in return.
Patriotic engagement makes Canadians reject the imperial lust for power and domination, the "those-who-grab-get" mentality and the "bullying" hubris of the "Firsters' " nationalism of Trump world.
Canadians aren't perfect but we are not Americans. Our patriotic engagement is characterized by a self-deprecating humility, politeness, humour and social responsibility for each other and the environment. We avoid the extremes of nationalism while fostering a strong sense of belonging.
On our good days, I think purposeful patriotic engagement is what the world sees in Canadians and we should look for in ourselves. It is why what U2 singer Bono said years ago is still true today: "The world needs more Canada."
David Pfrimmer is professor emeritus for public ethics
at Martin Luther College at Wilfrid Laurier University.
CAPTION:

CREDIT: David Pfrimmer David Pfrimmer is professor emeritus for public ethics at Martin Luther College at Wilfrid Laurier University.;

Document 84 of 500

Nothing unites Canada like Trump

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/nothing-unites-canada-like-trump/docview/3168616144/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Nothing+unites+Canada+like+Trump&amp;title=The+Spectator&amp;issn=11899417&amp;date=2025-02-20&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=G.4&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Spectator&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: The red hats are always hassling me to say something nice about their hero.
It would be easier for me to do a 180 on cauliflower or extol the humanitarian virtues of dirty bombs. That said, I will offer this olive branch to the red hats today: the U.S. president has done more for Canadian patriotism in one month than all of our prime ministers combined. Really.
Agent Orange has triggered a red-and-white tsunami.
I'm reading about the early fur trade and sipping Canadian vodka and dusting off Blue Rodeo deep cuts. What's that? Nickelback? Let's not get crazy. The point is I have never felt a deeper pride in being Canadian. And I am not alone.
Go into a grocery store and you will now see someone buying bacon in a Canadian flag tuque. Drive down any street and you will see Canadian flags fluttering in blizzard conditions. I was chatting with my neighbour recently and asked about his new porch banner.
He got it on Amazon. And get this: it was initially sold out.
I can't recall another time when Canadian nationalism led to booming sales.
I glanced at Amazon's bestseller list for flags and banners. It was dominated by our maple leaf in vertical tri-band glory. There were a few outliers in the Top 50, including a "F--k Trudeau" at No. 41. Man, those truckers can really hold a grudge.
But as I scrolled the list, my retinas were exposed to more red and white than inside a candy cane factory on Mars.
According to a story in Fortune this weekend, sales at just one Canadian flag maker have nearly doubled in a year. As Matt Skipp, co-owner of Flags Unlimited, told the magazine: "This recent animosity, or negotiation, or Trump-talk has absolutely lit a fire under flag sales. The more Trump talks, the more flags we sell."
The same is true for T-shirts and hats, including the popular "Canada Is Not For Sale" cap Doug Ford modelled. Amazon is selling Canadian flag bed sheets, travel mugs, tattoos, bumper stickers, pins, luggage tags, patches, adult onesies (?), decals and a red-and-white skull neck gaiter, which might provide decent camo should Trump send F-35s and M1A2 Abrams tanks across the border to transform Ottawa into Mar-a-Lago North.
Then Melania will rename ByWard Market as Be Best Eat.
I think Trump's bizarre obsession with making Canada "the 51st state" started after someone told him Boston Pizza and Lone Star Texas Grill are Canadian chains. Or maybe he just wants our natural resources? Dominion over Sunshine Girls?
All I know is the best part of Trump 2.0 so far is how every democratic country is grabbing the sniffing salts and taking a deep whiff of reckoning.
The man has contempt for his allies. This can't be argued. He is dismissive of Denmark and yearns to have slumber parties with Kim Jong Un, where the two will talk golf and paint each other's toenails. Trump wants to be besties with the bad boys. You know why calls between Vladimir Putin and Xi Jinping no longer require translators? Crying laughing is a universal language.
The dictators and oppressors can hereby stop wasting time fighting "American soft power and influence." Trump is now doing it for them with reckless abandon.
Nobody wants to see peace in Ukraine more than I do. But how on earth does America begin "peace talks" with Russia this week in Saudi Arabia and not give Ukraine a seat at the diplomatic table? That's like throwing a birthday party for your spouse and - surprise! - not inviting her.
It's not that Trump wants to betray Ukraine. He just won't hesitate to do so if he thinks he can personally get more out of Russia by way of a Moscow Trump Hotel or even a lifetime supply of borscht he can feed the political prisoners he eventually locks up in Greenland.
Here's that cup of Kvass, Adam Kinzinger. Enjoying the poisoned stroganoff, Jack Smith?
Trump is not the leader of the free world - he's the extortionist-in-chief.
Hey, Ukraine, hand over your rare earth minerals and maybe I'll keep you armed to survive this genocidal invasion. Hey, Canada and Mexico, remember that trade treaty I made you sign during Trump 1.0? I changed my mind. I'm now slapping a 100 per cent tariff on poutine and guacamole. Hey, Panama, all proceeds from your shipping canal will now go into a crypto trust fund for my great grandkids. Don't make me nuke you.
No surprise countries are giving the side-eye as American democracy retreats.
Bring on the flags and tuques! Let us cherish this nation of laws and decency!
Donald Trump has made Canadian patriotism great again.
CAPTION: The maple leaf flies over Nathan Phillips Square on Flag Day last Saturday. Vinay Menoncan't recall another time when Canadian nationalism led to booming sales of the country's standard.Nick Lachance Toronto Star

Document 85 of 500

NDP, past party incumbent spar at Hamilton Centre debate: Candidates' stances on homelessness, tariffs and party politics showcased at Cable TV debate

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/ndp-past-party-incumbent-spar-at-hamilton-centre/docview/3168616102/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=NDP%2C+past+party+incumbent+spar+at+Hamilton+Centre+debate%3A+Candidates%27+stances+on+homelessness%2C+tariffs+and+party+politics+showcased+at+Cable+TV+debate&amp;title=The+Spectator&amp;issn=11899417&amp;date=2025-02-20&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.3&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Spectator&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: It's a question Sarah Jama often addresses when door-knocking: What can she do for Hamilton Centre as an Independent candidate?
Some - like competing NDP candidate Robin Lennox - have suggested the riding's residents would be better off with an MPP that has the support of a party aiming to form government.
In a live debate on Cable 14 on Tuesday, Jama said during her time as an Independent MPP, she put forward 20 motions "drafted by community groups," weighed in on bills and participated in committees "where the hard work goes in."
"I've been able to do a lot of work to make sure that ideas from across the riding are being tabled over and over again," she said.
Jama is running for re-election in the riding after the New Democrats decided against welcoming back the activist ousted from the party over controversial comments on the Israel-Hamas war.
She joined candidates Lucia Iannantuono representing the Ontario Greens, Lennox for the New Democrats and Eileen Walker for the Ontario Liberal Party for the first of several Hamilton-area riding debates Tuesday. (PC candidate Sarah Bokhari, who also ran in the riding for the party in 2022, didn't participate due to a "scheduling conflict," her campaign team said.)
Lennox, a doctor and the new NDP candidate in the historically orange riding, argued her party is the official opposition and has a chance at displacing a "wave of conservatism" spreading across North America.
"While I think it's wonderful to have many voices in the conversation, we also need to be able to form a movement toward progressive governance," she said. "The only way we're going to do that is if we build power within a party."
Jama, who has often voted with the NDP in the legislature, previously said anyone who runs for the party in Hamilton Centre "risks splitting the vote." But when asked about the prospect of vote-splitting with her former party, Jama said Tuesday that the people she had met at the door are "excited about a new way of doing politics, that moves away from the status quo of picking one party or another."
Lennox said she isn't especially concerned that a choice between two candidates who may share views would divide voters, noting she continues to see a "vibrant NDP community" in Hamilton Centre.
Walker, meanwhile, said undecided voters she's encountered are on the fence between the Liberals and the NDP.
"Who is it that (Tory leader) Doug Ford seems to be the most afraid of? Who does he attack all the time? It's always the Liberals," she later said.
Candidates debated issues of homelessness, health care, housing affordability and taxation on Tuesday. Here are some of the highlights:
On homelessness and encampments, Jama said Hamilton needs rent control and "real investments" from the province into nonmarket and supportive housing, while Lennox offered the NDP's plan for 60,000 new supportive-housing units the party says will save money in health care, social service and justice costs. Iannantuono said there should be "fixed-term, low-rate financing" to non-profit builders to expedite development. Walker said the province has been "wholly neglectful" in funding housing, and that it needs to fund and work with municipalities. Candidates agreed safe-consumption sites are "vital," with Walker adding they shouldn't be in neighbourhoods with children or near schools.
Liberal and Green candidates sparred over the Greens' plan to increase taxes for heavy emitters already facing U.S. tariffs. "Are we going to bury them, are we going to increase our unemployment?" Walker asked. Iannantuono said residents' health doesn't need to be put at risk to keep people employed. "Those 9,000 jobs can be secured through investments that will make our industry cleaner. ... We have, hopefully coming through in Hamilton, a transition to real 'green steel.' "
Lennox accused the Ontario Liberals of moving toward the "centre-right" as they rebuild, becoming a "Conservative Party-lite." "Other people would say that we're centre-left, so I'm a little surprised to hear you say that," Walker responded.
Also running in the riding are Mitch Novosad, representing the New Blue Party; and Nathalie Xian Yi Yan, who is running as an Independent.
Kate McCullough is a reporter
at The Hamilton Spectator. kmccullough@thespec.com

Only one full platform out as vote nears: Progressive Conservative Leader Doug Ford has been notably absent from the campaign trail

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/only-one-full-platform-out-as-vote-nears/docview/3168616071/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Only+one+full+platform+out+as+vote+nears%3A+Progressive+Conservative+Leader+Doug+Ford+has+been+notably+absent+from+the+campaign+trail&amp;title=The+Spectator&amp;issn=11899417&amp;date=2025-02-20&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.11&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Spectator&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Advance polls open Thursday in Ontario's election - one week before general voting day - and only the Green Party has released a full platform, while Progressive Conservative Leader Doug Ford has been notably absent from the campaign trail.
Ford has not taken questions on the campaign trail or made public appearances besides the two debates in nine days. He did respond to questions while in Washington, D.C., last week, but he made that trip as premier, alongside the country's other provincial and territorial leaders.
Amid the snap, $189-million election he called, Ford's last public announcement in Ontario was on Feb. 10, when he said he would ban Chinese equity from Ontario government-funded energy projects. There has been no Chinese equity in government-funded energy projects during the Progressive Conservatives' seven years in government.
Ford has made about half a dozen other policy announcements during the campaign, including an additional $22 billion on infrastructure, $1 billion in the Skills Development Fund, uploading the Ottawa LRT, purchasing two police helicopters, taking tolls off Highway 407 East, and re-announcing his uncosted intention to build a tunnel under Highway 401.
A few Progressive Conservative candidates who have served in Ford's cabinet have also made announcements on behalf of the party, including on Ford's plan to help Ontario residents and businesses if tariffs are implemented by the United States.
A spokesperson for Ford said his full platform will be released "in the coming days."
Ford is in hiding, said NDP Leader Marit Stiles and Liberal Leader Bonnie Crombie.
"He called an early, unnecessary election at the cost of $189 million (and) he's been absent for 50 per cent of it," Crombie said Wednesday in Clarence-Rockland, just east of Ottawa.
"OK, a couple of days he went to Washington. Where has he been? Where is he today? He's been absent. And that's taking voters for granted."
Crombie said much of her policy has been released, and her team is working on the costing.
"Of course, this was a snap election, and we have released much of our policy ahead of time anyways," she said.
Crombie's central campaign promise has been to get everyone in Ontario a family doctor within four years, but she has also announced policies such as cutting the middle-income tax rate, eliminating HST on home heating and hydro, eliminating the provincial land transfer tax for some homebuyers, scrapping development charges, introducing phased-in rent control and doubling disability support payments.
Stiles compared Ford's two trips to Washington - he has another one coming up this week - with his relative absence on the campaign trail.
"He is more focused on vying for Donald Trump's attention than talking to the people of Ontario," she wrote in a statement.
The Canadian Press
CAPTION:

CREDIT: Allison Jones The Canadian Press

Document 87 of 500

The world needs more Canadian patriotism

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/world-needs-more-canadian-patriotism/docview/3168615995/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=The+world+needs+more+Canadian+patriotism&amp;title=The+Hamilton+Spectator+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2025-02-20&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Hamilton+Spectator+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Doug Ford stated Canada must come first, a sentiment later called into question by an unnecessary $180-million election.
Pierre Poilievre then announced his "Canada First plan" to respond to Donald Trump's economic attacks.
Unfortunately for Poilievre and Ford, their "Canada First" slogan triggers memories of the late 19th century "Canada Firsters." They opposed French Canadian influences, Indigenous Peoples' rights and pushed for a British-centric Canadian identity dedicated to the Empire.
Ford's open mic gaff, where he claimed he was "100 per cent happy that Trump won" reveals a deeper problem. These 21st-century Canada Firsters, no doubt, love Canada, but they want to create a Canada in the likeness of Trump's "America First." Ford spoke for a lot of Canadians who felt betrayed. Unfortunately, he quickly proposed building a "Fortress Am-Can" for Canada and the U.S. We want in!
Poilievre was quick to make a Canada First announcement reminiscent of Trump's approach and proposed significant cuts to foreign aid. He argued much of this aid is "wasted, stolen and swallowed up by bureaucracies that do not serve our interests." He intends to use the money to fund a new military base in Nunavut.
Like their 19th-century cousins, these Canada First advocates envision a Canada with an America-centric identity - essentially the same idea, just under a different empire.
Courtesy of Trump, there is a resurgent wave of Canadian nationalism. However, do Canadians really believe in a "knock-off" of America First nationalism? I think not.
Many Canadians desire a more compassionate form of ethical patriotism with a national purpose.
The late Canadian theologian Douglas John Hall observed we seek "not nationalism but the care of the nation." Hall introduced the concept of "patriotic engagement," which emphasized a thoughtful and responsible love for Canada and combined appreciation with critical reflection. Instead of self-serving nationalism, he advocated for a patriotism that actively pursues justice, acknowledges both past and present shortcomings and strives for the common good with humility and ethical responsibility.
This patriotic engagement understands Canada is our home on native land. For John Ralston Saul, Canada is a "Métis nation" because our core identity, values and political culture are deeply shaped by Indigenous rather than purely colonial European traditions. Canadians' history of negotiation, willingness to compromise, support for multiculturalism and social inclusion reflects Indigenous roots.
Patriotic engagement believes Canadians collaborate and work together to ensure everyone has enough and is cared for. Even as our politicians fail us on these issues, universal health care, public education and social services are celebrated as national achievements and symbols of pride and embody our core values of fairness and equality.
Patriotic engagement reflects Canada's commitment to promoting peace and global solidarity to improve the world for all. We take pride in our peacekeeping tradition, despite reduced contributions in recent years. In 2000, more than 600,000 (one in 50) Canadians signed the jubilee petition to cancel the debts of the poorest countries.
Patriotic engagement is not acquiescence; it firmly defends our national interests. Leaders must engage in diplomatic negotiation, not appeasement with Trumpists. No more humiliating trips, like the recent visit of the premiers to the White House. We will offer something only if we receive something respectful in return.
Patriotic engagement makes Canadians reject the imperial lust for power and domination, the "those-who-grab-get" mentality and the "bullying" hubris of the "Firsters'" nationalism of Trump world.
Canadians aren't perfect but we are not Americans. Our patriotic engagement is characterized by a self-deprecating humility, politeness, humour and social responsibility for each other and the environment. We avoid the extremes of nationalism while fostering a strong sense of belonging.
On our good days, I think purposeful patriotic engagement is what the world sees in Canadians and we should look for in ourselves. It is why what U2 singer Bono said years ago is still true today: "The world needs more Canada."
CAPTION: There should be no more humiliating trips, like the recent visit of Canada's premiers to the White House. We will offer something only if we receive something respectful in return, David Pfrimmer writes.
CREDIT: David Pfrimmer

Subject: Canadians; Nationalism; Patriotism

Party politics or doing politics differently? NDP, former party incumbent spar at Hamilton Centre debate

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/party-politics-doing-differently-ndp-former/docview/3168830372/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Party+politics+or+doing+politics+differently%3F+NDP%2C+former+party+incumbent+spar+at+Hamilton+Centre+debate&amp;title=The+Hamilton+Spectator+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2025-02-19&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Hamilton+Spectator+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

"Other people would say that we're centre-left, so I'm a little surprised to hear you say that," Walker responded.

Document 89 of 500

Got a text from an Ontario election candidate? Here's what Ontario's privacy watchdog says about unsolicited messages

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/got-text-ontario-election-candidate-heres-what/docview/3168374570/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Got+a+text+from+an+Ontario+election+candidate%3F+Here%27s+what+Ontario%27s+privacy+watchdog+says+about+unsolicited+messages&amp;title=The+Hamilton+Spectator+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2025-02-19&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Hamilton+Spectator+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Have you received unsolicited text messages from candidates running in the Feb. 27 provincial election?
You're not alone.
Political parties use text messaging to help make guesses about who is likely to support them at the polls, noted Peter Graefe, associate professor of political science at McMaster University.
"It's a very effective technique because it doesn't cost much," Graefe said.
If you respond with a simple yes or no, or you choose to write something more colourful, your response is valuable to the party, Graefe added.
Ontarians have reported receiving recent text messages from Ontario PC Leader and incumbent premier Doug Ford.
The Ontario PC Party has also confirmed it sends text messages, but has not indicated how it accesses Ontario residents' cellphone numbers.
That's raised privacy concerns for some voters.
'This is an invasion of my privacy'
Inside Halton reader Marie Webb told us: "I was shocked and annoyed on receiving a text message from Doug Ford. I choose who I want to give my cell number to. This is an invasion of my privacy. Use the landline where I can choose to answer or not."
Reader Yvette Nugent questioned why the PC government can send texts, but won't use an electronic method, like direct deposit, to issue $200 taxpayer rebates.
"It would've been more cost efficient if the government would have done the direct deposit," Nugent said in an email to Metroland.
Readers have also told us they have received unsolicited texts from other political parties.
What Ontario's Information and Privacy Commissioner says
While it's not always clear how political parties and candidates access cellphone data, we asked Ontario's information and privacy commissioner (IPC), to weigh in on the issue.
The IPC serves as the province's transparency watchdog and provides oversight for access and privacy laws which apply to provincial and municipal public institutions such as ministries, municipalities, colleges, hospitals and child and family service providers.
But the IPC confirmed in a statement to Metroland that political parties and candidates are not subject to Ontario's access and privacy laws.
"Regulations related to political advertising are overseen by Elections Ontario, which oversees the Elections Act, and the Election Finances Act," the IPC's statement notes. "If third-party vendors sell personal data, including telephone numbers, to political parties in Ontario, those vendors may be subject to federal privacy laws overseen by the Office of the Privacy Commissioner of Canada.
"Unsolicited commercial electronic messages, such as spam, are subject to federal anti-spam legislation which is enforced by the Canadian Radio-television and Telecommunications Commission."
Future laws should include political parties
The IPC's office noted it has previously recommended any future private sector privacy laws in Ontario include political parties.
"Advances in technology allow for the collection and use of large amounts of personal information, raising privacy and security concerns," the IPC's statement added. "Ensuring strong privacy protections supports public confidence in how personal information is handled."
Do-not-call list exemptions
The Canadian Radio-television and Telecommunications Commission (CRTC) notes that regardless of whether Canadians are registered with the National Do Not Call List, residents may still receive calls and texts from political parties and candidates.
Parties and candidates are exempt from the do-not-call list, but some rules still apply.
Parties and candidates can contact people on the list, provided they maintain and respect their internal do-not-call list and identify themselves and the purpose of the call.
If you reply "stop" to political campaign text messages, you should not receive further communication, according to the intent of the CRTC rules.
How to lodge a complaint
If you've received a misleading call, text or email about the election, you can contact the CRTC to lodge a complaint.
While parties and candidates are exempt from the do-not-call rules, they are not exempt from telemarketing and automatic dialing-announcing device rules, the CRTC notes.
CAPTION: A Reddit user provided a screenshot of a text message gauging support for Progressive Conservative Leader Doug Ford. The provincial election is Feb. 27.Ontario Premier Doug Ford.
CREDIT: Mike Pearson

Subject: Provincial elections; Personal information; Privacy; Candidates; Political advertising; Text messaging; Elections; Political parties

Niagara West candidates meeting a low-key affair

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/niagara-west-candidates-meeting-low-key-affair/docview/3168374028/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Niagara+West+candidates+meeting+a+low-key+affair&amp;title=The+Hamilton+Spectator+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2025-02-19&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Hamilton+Spectator+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Anyone hoping to see the political sparks fly during the Niagara West All-Candidates Debate Night at West Niagara Secondary School on Tuesday must have left the meeting disappointed.
Progressive Conservative incumbent Sam Oosterhoff, along with NDP challenger Dave Augustyn, Liberal party candidate Shauna Boyle and the Green Party's Mark Harrison, politely answered submitted questions from moderator Samuel Miele for about 70 minutes without landing any significant political punches. And there was no back and forth debating between the candidates.
Oosterhoff and Augustyn appeared to have a good grasp of the variety of issues and questions the candidates had 60 seconds to respond to, while Boyle and Harrison appeared unprepared at times.
On the matter of potential tariffs on Canadian exports to the U.S. by the Trump administration, Oosterhoff said the PCs will do whatever is necessary to protect people's livelihoods.
"We will spend, if necessary, billions of dollars to provide income support for businesses and impacted workers, retooling opportunities, retraining opportunities, investing more than we ever have before in infrastructure so we are able to keep jobs here," Oosterhoff said.
Boyle said the Liberals have an eight-part plan to tackle the tariff issue.
"It's going to take a co-ordinated response if we are going to fight back against Trump," Boyle said.
"I think we have to be careful not to be reactionary," said Harrison, who added the tariffs could provide an opportunity for Canadian businesses to "unleash" their potential.
Augustyn used the opportunity to blast the Ford government.
"We would not have Doug Ford negotiate a deal because if we look at the deals he's done with the Therme spa, with the Greenbelt, with the Beer Store boondoggle, with the overpriced Starlink, he doesn't know how to negotiate, he always puts us second," said Augustyn, who added the snap election over the U.S tariff threat "doesn't make sense whatsoever."
On the matter of health care and recruiting more doctors and nurses in the province, Oosterhoff said the Ford government is creating new medical schools, increasing support for nurse training and streamlining the process for international doctors to become licensed in Ontario.
Augustyn promised an NDP government would recruit 3,500 more doctors and reduce the amount of paperwork doctors are required to complete.
Boyle said the Liberals will build two new medical schools, and Harrison suggested doctors use artificial intelligence to support them in their work.
All four candidates agreed the province's Landlord and Tenant Board needs more support to speed up the adjudication process, with Oosterhoff suggesting the case backlog has dropped dramatically.
When asked what their parties would do to ease traffic congestion within Niagara's towns, Boyle said the Liberals are committed to the proposed GO station in Grimsby, Harrison said the Greens will encourage as much public transit as possible, Oosterhoff said the Tories will build more highways and are investing in public transit and Augustyn said the NDP supports shifting more traffic to Highway 407 and expanding the QEW.
Miele told the meeting all eight of the Niagara West candidates were invited to take part in the event.
About 200 people attended the gathering that was sponsored by the three west Niagara chambers of commerce and streamed live on YouTube.
CAPTION: Niagara West candidates Dave Augustyn of the NDP (left) and Liberal Party candidate Shauna Boyle on the stage in the auditorium at West Niagara Secondary School for Tuesday's All-Candidates Debate Night.Ontario Green Party candidate Mark Harrison (left) and Progressive Conservative candidate Sam Oosterhoff were among the four Niagara West candidates that participated in the All-Candidates Debate Night at West Niagara Secondary School on Tuesday.
CREDIT: Mark Newman

Subject: Public transportation; Candidates; Secondary schools; Debates; Elections; Tariffs

ONTARIO ELECTION 2025: We asked the parties about their commitment to diversity, equity and inclusion. Only the Green Party responded: Green Party outlines its stance on inclusion, diversity, equity and accessibility programs

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/ontario-election-2025-we-asked-parties-about/docview/3168224545/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=ONTARIO+ELECTION+2025%3A+We+asked+the+parties+about+their+commitment+to+diversity%2C+equity+and+inclusion.+Only+the+Green+Party+responded%3A+Green+Party+outlines+its+stance+on+inclusion%2C+diversity%2C+equity+and+accessibility+programs&amp;title=The+Hamilton+Spectator+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2025-02-19&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Hamilton+Spectator+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Do you want to know where political parties or their leaders stand on issues related to inclusion, diversity, equity and accessibility (IDEA) in Ontario? We did too.
Spearheaded by Metroland's IDEA Committee - a group of editorial employees committed to inclusive storytelling - we invited the four main political parties to answer a series of questions so you, the voter, could see how they planned to address these issues if elected.
It turns out these issues are also important to many readers, who answered our provincial election survey and listed affordability, accessibility and food insecurity in Ontario as being among their top issues.
The Progressive Conservative, Liberal and New Democratic parties did not provide a response by deadline. The following is the Green Party of Ontario's answers to the questions. This article will be updated should the other parties send in their submissions.
Accessibility
Q: What are your party's plans to further implement and enforce Accessibility for Ontarians with Disabilities Act (AODA) standards?
Living with a disability shouldn't mean living in poverty and facing barriers. Ontario Greens will fulfil all commitments in the Accessible Ontario Pledge by the AODA Alliance.
In order to further support Ontarians living with disabilities the Green Party will:
Immediately double Ontario Disability Support Program (ODSP) rates as a first step to implementing a basic income, and tie future increases to inflation.Improve the Assistive Devices Program to better meet the needs of those requiring assistive tools, including more up-to-date devices, training, and fewer barriers to access, as well as ensuring appropriate standards for repair of devices.Ensure that new affordable housing stock is accessible, and require affordable housing retrofits to meet the same standards.Immediately increase funding for community living agencies across Ontario in line with both cost of living and the needs of developmentally delayed adults,Ensure supports for house cleaning and other needs for people with disabilities.
Housing and homelessness
Q: What are key elements to your party's strategy to ending homelessness in Ontario?
In order to end homelessness in the province, Ontario Greens would:
Immediately support people living in encampments with their housing and support needs until permanent housing solutions are built and resume the homelessness census cancelled by the Ford government to ensure we create sustainable, long-term solutions.Work with non-profits to build 250,000 new affordable non profit and co-op homes and 60,000 permanent supportive homes with guaranteed funding for mental health, addictions and other supports.Utilize a housing first model to ensure that stable, permanent housing solutions are the first priority when helping those in need.Deploy temporary and permanent supportive modular housing projects on provincially-owned land as quickly as possible. Create tax credits and investment funds to help advance these housing technologies.Increase annual funding for women's shelters, as well as safe and accessible transitional and supportive housing options for women and their families. Increase funding for culturally appropriate transitional housing.Affordability
Q: Should Ontario reinstate a version of the basic income pilot? Why or why not?
Ontario Greens believe in treating people with dignity and respect. That's why we will phase in a basic income, with the first step being to at least double Ontario Disability Support Program (ODSP) and Ontario Works (OW) rates, and get rid of aggressive and unfair clawbacks, including clawbacks from the Canada Disability Benefit.
Further, in order to end legislated poverty, we will:
Eliminate any unnecessary red tape, reporting requirements, and other barriers typically faced by those needing financial support.Remove ODSP from the purview of Employment Ontario.Ensure shelter allowances reflect the shelter costs where people live.
Food insecurity
Q: How will your party tackle the high cost of food?
A: Greedy grocery corporations must be stopped and we must support local food and farmers to strengthen our food security. Ontario Greens will:
Ensure the existing federal-provincial-territorial grocery retailer code of conduct is mandatory, enforceable, transparent, and benefits both customers and farmers.Introduce strict anti-gouging and collusion laws to stop grocery corporations from gouging people on their grocery bills.Create an Ontario Foodbelt to permanently protect prime farmland from being lost to non-agricultural uses, such as urban sprawl, highways, and gravel mining.Invest in local food hubs to support local farmers, supply chains and businesses.Increase provincial investment in AgTech to support innovation in the food and farming sector, including research into low-carbon grain drying and greenhouse heating solutions.Support supply management and defend Ontario food and farmers in trade negotiations.Eliminate property tax penalties for farmers with small-scale, value-added production facilities on their farms.IDEA values
Q: What is your party's plan for inclusion, diversity, equity and accessibility programs?
Ontario Greens are committed to building a more accessible and equitable province so no one is left behind. Here's, broadly, how we'll do it:
Improve quality of life for people living with a disability.Take an intersectional lens to addressing gender equity.Fight to eradicate systemic racism.Support and improve rights for 2SLGBTQIA+ communities.Create a more fair justice system in Ontario.Improving equity through language access.
"Our updated election platform is set to be released very soon, where you'll find more details on our plan for inclusion, diversity, equity and accessibility programs," the party noted.
CAPTION: Spearheaded by Metroland's IDEA Committee - a group of editorial employees committed to inclusive storytelling - we invited the four main political parties to answer a series of questions.
CREDIT: Metroland Staff

Document 92 of 500

ONTARIO ELECTION 2025: What is a Guaranteed Basic Annual Income?: Pros and cons of Guaranteed Basic Annual Income merit discussion as battle to beat poverty rolls on

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/ontario-election-2025-what-is-guaranteed-basic/docview/3168224466/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=ONTARIO+ELECTION+2025%3A+What+is+a+Guaranteed+Basic+Annual+Income%3F%3A+Pros+and+cons+of+Guaranteed+Basic+Annual+Income+merit+discussion+as+battle+to+beat+poverty+rolls+on&amp;title=The+Hamilton+Spectator+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2025-02-19&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Hamilton+Spectator+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: The notion of a Guaranteed Basic Annual Income (GBAI) has been around for nearly a century in Canada.
It is essentially a contract between either a provincial or the federal government to provide each adult citizen with a minimum basic annual income above the poverty line. It can have many variations, but usually involves the replacement of a suite of other social programs, like Ontario Works or the Ontario Disability Support Program, in favour of an income that pays more to its recipients.
It wasn't until 2017 that a three-year pilot project was launched in Ontario.
About Ontario's Basic Income Pilot4,000 low-income residents in Hamilton, Lindsay and Thunder Bay between the ages of 18 and 64 were selected to take part in the program, while others who did not receive a basic income as part of the program were selected as participants to compare their experiences and judge the outcome of the pilot.Single basic income program participants received up to $16,989 per year, while couples received $24,027 annually.Individuals with disabilities received a $500 per month top-up.Participants who earned income would see their payments reduced by 50 cents for every dollar they earned.
The goal of the pilot program was to replace Ontario Works and Ontario Disability Support Program payments with guaranteed basic annual income payments and to see how program participants fared.
The project was cancelled in 2018, about a year into the three-year pilot, by Doug Ford's newly-elected Progressive Conservative government. The final payment to program participants was made in March 2019.
Because it didn't run its full course, complete data on the success of the pilot program was difficult to discern and a final report on it was never completed by the new government.
Benefits of a basic incomeIn 2019, former senator Hugh Segal, a longtime fierce proponent of GBAI, said, "I see our transfers to people, not counting education and health, cost about $140 billion a year from our provinces and the federal government, yet the percentage of the people living below the poverty line hasn't improved in a quarter of a century ... we can be doing this more efficiently and more effectively." Segal believed GBAI was a more effective way to defeat poverty than current programs provide.Proponents of GBAI see it as a way to provide a safety net for people facing job loss or economic downturns.Individuals might be more likely to pursue further education or training and find housing they can afford if they have a stable income base.It was seen as an opportunity to roll an array of different social and welfare systems into one system, making GBAI easier to administer.
Concerns related to instituting a basic income programCritics of GBAI worry about its costs. They fear it would expand the Ministry of Children, Community and Social Services budget by billions more than its current 2024 budget of nearly $20 billion. For instance, in 2024, the ministry paid about $9 billion to about 640,000 Ontarians receiving ODSP and Ontario Works. If they were to receive the single-earner amount under the basic income pilot, the total would increase to $15.5 billion annually.Critics fear a basic income might encourage fewer people to work, as opposed to much lower payments offered on Ontario Works.How does a government fund basic income programs?
GBAI could be funded in a phased approach replacing some social program funding, through various pilots, before larger implementation.
Government funding could be applied through a targeted tax increase or through reallocation of existing government tax revenues.
CAPTION: In this file photo, the Raging Grannies perform as 35 people attend a public meeting for a Basic Income Guarantee in Peterborough. In 2017, Ontario ran a basic income pilot in Lindsay, Hamilton and Thunder Bay. The pilot was cancelled in 2018, with final payments made to recipients in March 2019 following a change in government.
CREDIT: Tim Kelly

Subject: Poverty; Pilot projects; Disability; Basic income

Will highway politics drive your vote?: Both the Tories and Liberals have pitched a wider QEW over the years

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/will-highway-politics-drive-your-vote/docview/3168222973/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Will+highway+politics+drive+your+vote%3F%3A+Both+the+Tories+and+Liberals+have+pitched+a+wider+QEW+over+the+years&amp;title=The+Spectator&amp;issn=11899417&amp;date=2025-02-19&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.1&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Spectator&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: It didn't grab headlines like the world's biggest tunnel proposal, but there is also a multibillion-dollar highway promise for the Hamilton area in this snap election: a fast-tracked widening of the Queen Elizabeth Way.
Earlier this month, Progressive Conservative Leader Doug Ford announced plans to spend an extra $15 billion to "speed up key capital projects," including a widening of the Queen Elizabeth Way between St. Catharines and Burlington. (A 2022 provincial transportation plan for the Greater Golden Horseshoe called for capacity improvements on both the QEW and Highway 403, but did not specify a time frame.)
We don't know much about the proposed widening - including cost, when work might start or even where, exactly, new traffic lanes would be added - because the Tory campaign did not respond to Spectator questions about the promise. (Worth noting: there are already four QEW traffic lanes in each direction in Stoney Creek, but only three in parts of Niagara.)
But asphalt promises have been a staple of re-election bids past and present for the Tories, noted University of Toronto professor and transportation expert Matti Siemiatycki, including the latest ambitious plan for a tunnel under Highway 401 and a contentious megahighway through the Greenbelt.
"Getting around by car is a huge political issue and elections are won in the auto-oriented suburbs," he said, pointing to predictable voter frustrations with both congestion and affordability. "The Tories have been laser-focused on this issue for the past two or three elections - but other party leaders are also zeroing in with their own proposals."
The NDP's Marit Stiles, for example, has promised to remove tolls from both the public and privately owned sections of Highway 407. In an interview, she lumped the so-far vague Tory QEW widening promise in with the "fantasy tunnel" pitched for under Highway 401 in the GTA.
"The thing you need to know about Doug Ford is he is going to promise everything (on transportation) ... but his promises are no good," Stiles said during a visit to Hamilton. "He's had seven years to improve transit, plenty of time to negotiate removing tolls on (Highway) 407 ... and he hasn't done one thing."
Green Leader Mike Schreiner noted in an interview that the widening of Highway 401 through Mississauga and Milton "improved my commute to Queen's Park for about three months ... now it's back to gridlock." He argued battling congestion requires better transit, improved regional GO Transit and "building homes near where people work, within the existing urban boundary."
Liberal Leader Bonnie Crombie said there are likely highways in the province that need improvement, and she would be interested in looking at truck-only lanes on Highway 407, "but my priority is always going to be public transit." (Worth noting: both the Liberals and Tories have also promised to upload the costs of a new Ottawa LRT, while everyone seems to agree the GO Train network needs improvement.)
Interestingly, Niagara-to-Hamilton QEW widening was actually pitched by a former Liberal government as far back as 2011 - then, as an environmentally preferable alternative to a Tory-backed mid-peninsula highway that threatened to carve up the escarpment.
That mothballed project, which spurred mass environmental protest, proposed to pave a multi-lane path from the border at Fort Erie through Flamborough to a 400-series connection north of Burlington.
Regardless, the resurrected proposal raises questions about the evolution of pavement politics in Hamilton - and whether we need more-and-wider highways at all.
Once a group of mid-pen highway enthusiasts, Hamilton council gradually shifted political gears and formally asked the province in 2018 to instead widen both the QEW and Highway 403 up the Mountain.
That support, historically, was linked to the city's own road-widening ambitions - specifically, a desire to expand the increasingly busy Linc and Red Hill Valley parkways to six lanes from four.
An environmental assessment study to look at widening started in 2020 - but it has largely been stuck in neutral since, with Indigenous consultation concerns and a slippery Red Hill scandal making parkway changes a sensitive topic.
Now Mayor Andrea Horwath, meanwhile, has suggested a provincial takeover of the parkways - an idea that, so far, has not showed up in any political party's campaign promises.
City transportation staff have warned in the past it makes no sense to widen busy local parkways before Ontario deals with its own traffic "backlogs" where city-owned roads disgorge drivers onto equally full 400-series highways. An updated report on the need for and feasibility of parkway widening is expected this year.
It isn't yet clear if the Tories' unexplained QEW-widening plan would address capacity issues near the Red Hill outlet at all. Past studies have shown widening Highway 403, meanwhile, would be environmentally tricky and space-constrained thanks to Cootes Paradise marsh and the escarpment itself.
The problem with all of the highway widening plans and promises, however, is "that they really haven't worked," said Siemiatycki.
"We have decades of experience now to show building bigger highways has not solved congestion ... it's a short-term fix and relatively quickly, that space gets filled up."
Studies refer to this concept as induced demand, the phenomena of new highway traffic lanes gradually attracting even more car drivers to fill them.
Opinions on the need for road-widening differ on Hamilton's current council, which has not yet taken a collective position on parkway expansion.
"Expanding highways, whether they are provincial or municipal, is unsustainable and the wrong thing to do on many levels," argued Ancaster councillor Craig Cassar. "It's not going to solve congestion. Just look at the 401.
"We all agree congested roads are a problem. But the only way we're going to solve that is by providing people realistic alternatives to exclusively driving in single-occupancy vehicles."
Stoney Creek councillor Brad Clark - who was transportation minister in the early 2000s Tory provincial government when the mid-peninsula corridor was first debated - said he believes some area highway widening or improvements are needed, depending on the proposal.
He noted the city made a strategic decision to divert more truck traffic onto its parkways and surrounding highways when it banned large tractor-trailers from downtown streets in 2023.
"That was the goal ... and it appears to be working," he said, pointing to new city statistics showing more truck traffic on the parkways last year. "But now if we want to keep trade moving, it would be a mistake not to increase the capacity," he said of both the parkways and adjoining provincial highways.
Matthew Van Dongen
is a Spectator reporter. mvandongen@thespec.com
CAPTION: The PC campaign has promised to fast-track the widening ofthe QEW from Niagara to Burlington this election.The QEW through Stoney Creek, with four lanes in each direction, will gum up near the Red Hill Valley Parkway at rush hour.Cathie Coward The Hamilton SpectatorCathie Coward The Hamilton Spectator

CREDIT: Matthew Van Dongen Reporter Matthew Van Dongen is a Spectator reporter. mvandongen@thespec.com

Letters

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/letters/docview/3168222897/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=unknown&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Letters&amp;title=The+Spectator&amp;issn=11899417&amp;date=2025-02-19&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.9&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Spectator&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Anthem booing
not patriotic
Re: Canada's 4 Nations Fight-Off with U.S. showed how much hockey - and this country - means to us, Feb. 16
Disgraceful, hateful booing at American hockey players is not patriotic. What an embarrassment. The final humiliation that night was the 3-1 loss. Canadian fans need to show more class. The Americans may have started the fight on the ice, but it actually started in the stands. And for the writer to say it was "justified booing" is outrageous.
Chris Asimoudis, Ancaster
Column provided food for thought
Re: Take a look around your kitchen to get a sense of Indigenous people's ingenuity, Feb. 18
Call me impressed by Paul Racher's knowledge-filled commentary on the significant contribution of the humble Indigenous people of our world to the growth of farming techniques. I had no idea that many of the vegetables that we enjoy today might not be here if not for their ingenuity - potatoes, corn, tomatoes, beans, squash, peppers, heck, even tapioca (which is toxic if not for their techniques), and many more. Who knew?
Dave Hachey, Dundas
Tariffs nothing
new for Trump
Re: Ford shows rivals how to meet the moment, Feb. 18
In spite of Donald Trump imposing tariffs on steel and aluminum here for over a year in 2018-19, Doug Ford was 100 per cent for Trump being re-elected. That fact alone should be enough to stop voters from electing him again.
Dave Farr, Hamilton
What a vote
for Ford means
As a businessperson/politician, Doug Ford clearly believes unrestricted economic growth is the main criteria for a successful society and this dictates the nature of most of his right-wing agenda. It must be emphasized that a vote for Ford in the upcoming election means:
Ignoring a dismal track record over the last few years for his government;
Agreeing to the unnecessary building of the 413 and an inane 401 tunnel;
Accepting the continued, excessive use of undemocratic ministerial zoning orders;
Buying into unsustainable environmental destruction, substandard health care, and an underfunded education system and;
Saying "no" to improved social welfare spending.
Fortunately, there are alternatives available which we really need to consider for a better quality of life in Ontario.
Jim Warren, Hamilton
Vote splitting
only helps Tories
Am I the only voter in Ontario to notice Liberals, NDP and Green combined have enough supporters to beat the Conservatives? Three left-wing parties are two too many left-wing parties. We love your objectives, but you can never win when the support is so diluted. I am asking Ontario voters on the left to make your vote count. If you are NDP or Green supporters, vote Liberal. Otherwise we will be dealing with Doug Ford all over again.
Eleanor Blackborow,
Flamborough

Document 95 of 500

Trudeau government scrambling to kick-start buyback program for gun owners

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/trudeau-government-scrambling-kick-start-buyback/docview/3168222872/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Trudeau+government+scrambling+to+kick-start+buyback+program+for+gun+owners&amp;title=The+Hamilton+Spectator+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2025-02-19&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Hamilton+Spectator+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: OTTAWA-The Trudeau government is in talks with police forces across the country in an 11th-hour-effort to launch its weapons buyback program for gun owners before a potential spring election.
Although the program is underway for retailers, Ottawa's scramble to kick-start it for individual gun owners has been complicated by a problem: who will collect the guns?
Negotiations with police forces across the country are ongoing, including with the RCMP and the Ontario Provincial Police, as well as provinces and territories, in an effort to sign deals that would get them to run the program, a government official told the Star on the condition they were not named, because they were unauthorized to discuss private details.
The federal government says it expects to launch the program this spring but has not announced any deals, though a nearly $3.5-million offer to Winnipeg police recently received the support of the city's council.
However, concerns from police plus a potential spring election could upend the program, which is a key part of Prime Minister Justin Trudeau's gun-control agenda.
Tom Stamatakis, the president of the Canadian Police Association, which represents around 60,000 police officers, argued police across the country do not have the resources to run the program even with federal funding to do it. He also said there are concerns about how the program would be managed, how it would affect police and community relationships and whether it makes sense to push it out before an election that could result in the program being canned.
"I'm not optimistic at all, I'm worried about it," Stamatakis told the Star. "If I was in a position to advise, I would worry about making a significant investment in something that could change after the election."
The promise of a voluntary nationwide buyback program was part of a sweeping ban of more than 1,500 "assault-style" firearms following the massacre of 22 people in Nova Scotia in 2020, and is a key fixture in what Ottawa says is the strongest gun-control agenda in Canada's history.
The first phase of the program, targeted at retailers only, started in December after several delays, as Ottawa added hundreds more gun variants to its banned list in an effort to shore up support for the program.
Its second phase, for individual gun owners, has faced more challenges around how to collect the weapons. Canada Post is refusing to participate, citing safety concerns for its staff.
That's in part why as a deadline of Oct. 30 to finish the program inches closer and the prospects of an early election rise, the federal government has turned to police forces across the country to help get the process underway.
A spokesperson for Rachel Bendayan, the associate public safety minister who recently took over leading the program, said phase one is "making great progress" and phase two is expected to launch this spring.
But Tracey Wilson, of the Canadian Coalition for Firearm Rights, told the Star the program is "dead in the water, as far as I'm concerned." She expects the federal government to soon reach out to gun owners with a deadline to report the banned guns they have.
A report approved by Winnipeg's city council said if a deal is reached, local police would collect the guns from individual gun owners and temporarily store them before they are destroyed by a third-party designated by the federal government. It says all costs associated with the program would be covered by the federal government. In 2021, the Parliamentary Budget Officer estimated the program could cost as much as $756 million.
The RCMP, which acts as the provincial police force in every province except for Ontario and Quebec, said it was told that any questions regarding the program be sent to Public Safety Canada. The National Police Federation, the union that represents RCMP officers, also declined to comment.
The Trudeau government's gun-control agenda was a massive wedge issue in the 2021 campaign, and the Tories under Pierre Poilievre routinely describe the program as an attack on lawful gun owners, including hunters and sports-shooters.
Liberal Leadership contenders Chrystia Freeland and Karina Gould have committed to continuing with the program, but Mark Carney did not commit one way or another. Asked if he would keep the program if he becomes prime minister on Tuesday, NDP Leader Jagmeet Singh also did not give an answer.
Police unions and experts, meanwhile, have warned it's illegal guns coming from the United States that are most often used by criminals in Canada and should be prioritized - an issue the Trudeau government has raised with Donald Trump's White House.
CAPTION: A restricted gun licence holder holds a AR-15 at his home in Langley, B.C., on May 1, 2020.
CREDIT: Mark Ramzy

Document 96 of 500

Campaigns dig up dirt, sling mud as vote nears

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/campaigns-dig-up-dirt-sling-mud-as-vote-nears/docview/3168222859/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Campaigns+dig+up+dirt%2C+sling+mud+as+vote+nears&amp;title=The+Spectator&amp;issn=11899417&amp;date=2025-02-19&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.5&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Spectator&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Ontario's snap election campaign is heading for its post-debate home stretch, with political parties starting to dig up dirt, sling mud and change course.
The Feb. 27 vote is now less than 10 days away, and while there is nary a costed platform to be found, aside from the Greens', the campaign appears to have entered a different phase.
The Progressive Conservatives are showcasing problematic posts from Liberal candidates with increasing frequency. The Liberals are road testing a new tactic of trying to woo NDP voters, though denigrating the party as acting like "money grows on trees." The NDP is dismissing the Liberals as desperate.
Liberal Leader Bonnie Crombie first tried out her pitch to NDP voters during Monday night's debate, then drove it home Tuesday by going to Hamilton, a city that traditionally shows deep NDP support, to repeat the message.
"I'm reaching out today to NDP voters, and I'm asking them, if you want to change our health-care system, please vote for Ontario's Liberals, and together, we can change the government," Crombie said.
"We have the momentum. We have the wind at our sails, but we can't make that change without your support."
Crombie is pitching herself as the strongest alternative to Progressive Conservative Leader Doug Ford, though polls suggest the Tories have a sizable lead over both the Liberals and NDP.
NDP Leader Marit Stiles said the Liberals' goal is just to try to get official party status.
"That just tells you everything you need to know about that party, where they're at," she said at an announcement in Toronto. "Their path is they're just trying to make party status. Look the truth is, my focus is, and continues to be, flipping blue seats to orange."
Political parties require at least 12 seats in the legislature to get party status, which gives them more resources and more opportunities to ask questions and participate in debates. At dissolution, the Liberals held nine seats.
Ford had been scheduled to fly to Sault Ste. Marie, Ont., for an announcement, but the party said delays at Pearson International Airport prevented him from leaving Toronto.
Ford last took questions in Washington, D.C., when he and other Canadian premiers travelled there to push back against U.S. tariffs, but the last time he took questions in Ontario during the snap provincial election he called for Feb. 27 was one week ago Monday.
Stiles said Ford is "in hiding."
"Last night, after the debate, he wouldn't even stand up in front of the reporters," she said. "He will not defend his government. He will not take responsibility for the state of this province. He is hoping that people are just going to stay home and not vote."
Progressive Conservative staffers have been spending a lot of time going through social media posts of Liberal candidates and have highlighted several that they call homophobic, misogynist and offensive.
Crombie has largely dismissed the Tories' efforts and has not dropped any of the candidates, but she did denounce one post Tuesday from her candidate in Oshawa.
Viresh Bansal posted a response in 2023 to an NDP statement on the killing of Sikh leader Hardeep Singh Nijjar after Prime Minister Justin Trudeau said the government had "credible allegations" linking agents of the Indian government to the fatal shooting in B.C.
"You can thank India for cleaning trash people. Ask your gay friend @JustinTrudeau to do the same," Bansal wrote.
The Canadian Press
CAPTION:

CREDIT: Allison Jones The Canadian Press

Document 97 of 500

Ford survives to battle another day

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/ford-survives-battle-another-day/docview/3168222694/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=unknown&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Ford+survives+to+battle+another+day&amp;title=The+Spectator&amp;issn=11899417&amp;date=2025-02-19&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.9&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Spectator&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Finally, just in time, an election on the issues that matter to voters.
Ontarians were snowed under by an absurdly early mid-winter campaign focused on the phoney issue of Donald Trump's tariffs - and Doug Ford's fatuous claim that only he, as Ontario's Progressive Conservative leader, could force the U.S. president to back down.
Now, thanks to Monday night's widely televised debate among the major party leaders, real issues were talked about - and are worth thinking about. They are also worth voting on.
Ever since he triggered the Feb. 27 election on the pretext of Trump's tariffs, Ford has claimed America was the only provincial issue that mattered - and that he was the only real leader with the means to address it. But with barely 10 days until voting day, Ford isn't setting the agenda anymore.
Facing his NDP, Liberal and Green opponents for the first time on a level playing field, Ford had to answer pointed questions for 90 minutes.
Not on foreign tariffs that fall under federal jurisdiction, but on critical provincial issues: Housing shortages and doctor shortages. Hallway health care and education shortfalls. Traffic congestion and tax cuts. Affordability and credibility.
The questions - from the debate moderator but also hurled at Ford by NDP Leader Marit Stiles, Liberal Leader Bonnie Crombie and the Green Party's Mike Schreiner - put the PC leader on the defensive for the first time in the campaign. Try as Ford did to frame this as a battle of wills between himself and Trump, the debate served as a reminder this election is a four-way fight pitting Ford against his provincial opponents, not a foreign president.
The problem with Ford's pitch is that it rings hollow in a debate where all the other leaders are in complete agreement. They all oppose the sale of Canada and the imposition of tariffs, and will spend whatever it takes to protect the Ontario economy. No debate there. No disagreement anywhere.
Like Canadian politicians of all political stripes, Ontario politicians from rival parties are fully agreed and aligned on how to respond to the stars and stripes. And so, unable to hide behind his opposition to Trump in the White House, Ford had to face his opponents on their - our - home turf, on issues closer to home.
Ford flailed on his Greenbelt boondoggle and broken promises to preserve farmland, repeating his apology for going back on his word so that he could make a virtue of his vice. Ford fessed up that he'd had to hire a longtime Liberal (Dr. Jane Philpott) on the eve of the election to help him solve the doctor shortage, making a virtue of necessity.
All that said, voters aren't looking for perfection from Ford. They're just looking for an alternative to him and in the last two elections they haven't found one.
In Monday's debate, voters had a chance to look again. They got to see two party leaders appearing in a televised debate for the first time: the NDP's Stiles and the Liberals' Crombie. (Schreiner of the Greens, a veteran of election debates, got in his usual good lines, but his party remains on the sidelines of this election with two seats and roughly five per cent voter support.)
Stiles went on the attack against both Ford's Tories and Crombie's Liberals, blaming them both for hallway health care as she usually does. Whenever Ford is talking health care, he's losing votes, according to PC strategists, which is why he kept pivoting to remind viewers that a healthy economy underpins health care.
Crombie took a different tack, going on the attack against Ford but offering an olive branch to New Democrats by seeking their support instead of splitting the opposition vote: "I'm asking those of you who voted NDP in the last election to vote Liberal," Crombie said to the camera.
But it was the jousting between Crombie and Ford that elicited by far the greatest rancour on both sides.
Calling him a son of "privilege" who had a "job handed to you" at his father's printing firm, she called him out of touch as premier, challenging him to say if he even knew the price of a dozen eggs.
Yet, for all their disagreement on provincial issues, the four party leaders spent much of the night fighting over who was Ontario's foremost tax fighter. Each of them promised more giveaways to leave more money in people's pockets - by cutting tolls or taxes; and by cutting rebate cheques (Ford) or rebating grocery bills (a Stiles idea, now paused by B.C.'s NDP).
It was as if the ghost of Donald Trump was wafting across the debate stage - not by raising tariffs but cutting taxes and imprinting a new form of pocketbook populism on the province.
Will Monday night reset the election? The problem with provincial campaign debates is that only the most politically engaged people tune in and only the most patient people watch the clips the next day.
While Crombie and Stiles both made their debate debut and benefited from the exposure, it's fair to say that Ford easily survived the encounter to fight another day (and spend another two days in Washington again this week).
The Tories have enjoyed a massive lead from the first, which is why they called an early election to lock it in. Now that they're in the home stretch, the PCs are counting on voters sleepwalking to the polls while Ford slow walks his victory lap.
Martin Regg Cohn covers
provincial politics for Torstar.
CAPTION: Progressive Conservative Leader Doug Ford, seen waiting for the start of Monday's Ontario leaders' debate, was on the defensive for the first time in the campaign, writes Martin Regg Cohn.The Canadian Press file photo

Feb. 19: Consider what a vote for Ford means, anthem booing not patriotic and other letters to the editor

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/feb-19-consider-what-vote-ford-means-anthem/docview/3168222638/se-2?accountid=15090

Full text: What a vote for Ford means
As a businessperson/politician, Doug Ford clearly believes unrestricted economic growth is the main criteria for a successful society and this dictates the nature of most of his right-wing agenda. It must be emphasized that a vote for Ford in the upcoming election means:
Ignoring a dismal track record over the last few years for his government;Agreeing to the unnecessary building of the 413 and an inane 401 tunnel;Accepting the continued, excessive use of undemocratic ministerial zoning orders;Buying into unsustainable environmental destruction, substandard health care, and an underfunded education system and;Saying "no" to improved social welfare spending.
Fortunately, there are alternatives available which we really need to consider for a better quality of life in Ontario.
Jim Warren, Hamilton
Tariffs nothing new for Trump
Re: Ford shows rivals how to meet the moment, Feb. 18
In spite of Donald Trump imposing tariffs on steel and aluminum here for over a year in 2018-19, Doug Ford was 100 per cent for Trump being re-elected. That fact alone should be enough to stop voters from electing him again.
Dave Farr, Hamilton
Vote splitting helps Tories
Am I the only voter in Ontario to notice Liberals, NDP and Green combined have enough supporters to beat the Conservatives? Three left-wing parties are two too many left-wing parties. We love your objectives, but you can never win when the support is so diluted. I am asking Ontario voters on the left to make your vote count. If you are NDP or Green supporters, vote Liberal. Otherwise we will be dealing with Doug Ford all over again.
Eleanor Blackborow, Flamborough

It warms my heart to read the letter from Elizabeth Accordino. If we stay together, firm and remain focused with our American cousins who share our common values, I am confident we can overcome the difficulties caused by a group of extremists who do not have decency, integrity, honesty or love for fellow human beings.
Robert Lee, Dundas

Re: Take a look around your kitchen to get a sense of Indigenous people's ingenuity, Feb. 18
Call me impressed by Paul Racher's knowledge-filled commentary on the significant contribution of the humble Indigenous people of our world to the growth of farming techniques. I had no idea that many of the vegetables that we enjoy today might not be here if not for their ingenuity - potatoes, corn, tomatoes, beans, squash, peppers, heck, even tapioca (which is toxic if not for their techniques), and many more. Who knew?
Dave Hachey, Dundas
Anthem booing not patriotic
