Disgraceful, hateful booing at American hockey players is not patriotic. What an embarrassment. The final humiliation that night was the 3-1 loss.
Canadian fans need to show more class. The Americans may have started the fight on the ice, but it actually started in the stands. And for the writer to say it was "justified booing" is outrageous.
Chris Asimoudis, Ancaster
It was the best game in years
Tuned in to Hockey Night in Canada to watch Canada play the U.S.A. It was the best game in 10 years. Three fights broke out in the first nine seconds - you read that right, not the first nine minutes.
It reminded me of the famous Roger Dangerfield line: "I went to a fight the other night and a hockey game broke out."
Joe Benedetti, Hamilton
Proud to display the flag
So pleased to display the Canadian flag poster provided in the Hamilton Spectator on Saturday! The flag provides a reminder to both our family and those passing by outside that we must rise to the challenges and threats of the U.S. meddling in our affairs. All Canadians can contribute by taking a strong stand. Now is the time.
Donna Arnold, Hamilton

Document 99 of 500

Tunnels, tolls and a wider QEW: will highway politics drive your vote?

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/tunnels-tolls-wider-qew-will-highway-politics/docview/3168222629/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Tunnels%2C+tolls+and+a+wider+QEW%3A+will+highway+politics+drive+your+vote%3F&amp;title=The+Hamilton+Spectator+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2025-02-19&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Hamilton+Spectator+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: It didn't grab headlines like the world's biggest tunnel proposal, but there is also a multibillion-dollar highway promise for the Hamilton area in this snap election: a fast-tracked widening of the Queen Elizabeth Way.
Earlier this month, Progressive Conservative leader Doug Ford announced plans to spend an extra $15 billion to "speed up key capital projects," including a widening of the Queen Elizabeth Way between St. Catharines and Burlington. (A 2022 provincial transportation plan for the Greater Golden Horseshoe called for capacity improvements on both the QEW and Highway 403, but did not specify a time frame.)
We don't know much about the proposed widening - including cost, when work might start or even where, exactly, new traffic lanes would be added - because the Tory campaign did not respond to Spectator questions about the promise. (Worth noting: there are already four QEW traffic lanes in each direction in Stoney Creek, but only three in parts of Niagara.)
But asphalt promises have been a staple of re-election bids past and present for the Tories, noted University of Toronto professor and transportation expert Matti Siemiatycki, including the latest ambitious plan for a tunnel under Highway 401 and a contentious megahighway through the Greenbelt.
"Getting around by car is a huge political issue and elections are won in the auto-oriented suburbs," he said, pointing to predictable voter frustrations with both congestion and affordability. "The Tories have been laser-focused on this issue for the past two or three elections - but other party leaders are also zeroing in with their own proposals."
The NDP's Marit Stiles, for example, has promised to remove tolls from both the public and privately owned sections of Highway 407. In an interview, she lumped the so-far vague Tory QEW widening promise in with the "fantasy tunnel" pitched for under Highway 401 in the GTA.
"The thing you need to know about Doug Ford is he is going to promise everything (on transportation) ... but his promises are no good," Stiles said during a visit to Hamilton. "He's had seven years to improve transit, plenty of time to negotiate removing tolls on (Highway) 407 ... and he hasn't done one thing."
Green Leader Mike Schreiner noted in an interview that the widening of Highway 401 through Mississauga and Milton "improved my commute to Queen's Park for about three months ... now it's back to gridlock." He argued battling congestion requires better transit, improved regional GO Transit and "building homes near where people work, within the existing urban boundary."
Liberal Leader Bonnie Crombie said there are likely highways in the province that need improvement, and she would be interested in looking at truck-only lanes on Highway 407, "but my priority is always going to be public transit." (Worth noting: both the Liberals and Tories have also promised to upload the costs of a new Ottawa LRT, while everyone seems to agree the GO Train network needs improvement.)
Interestingly, Niagara-to-Hamilton QEW widening was actually pitched by a former Liberal government as far back as 2011 - then, as an environmentally preferable alternative to a Tory-backed mid-peninsula highway that threatened to carve up the escarpment. (That mothballed project, which spurred mass environmental protest, proposed to pave a multi-lane path from the border at Fort Erie through Flamborough to a 400-series connection north of Burlington.)
Regardless, the resurrected proposal raises questions about the evolution of pavement politics in Hamilton - and whether we need more-and-wider highways at all.
Once a group of mid-pen highway enthusiasts, Hamilton council gradually shifted political gears and formally asked the province in 2018 to instead widen both the QEW and Highway 403 up the Mountain.
That support, historically, was linked to the city's own road-widening ambitions - specifically, a desire to expand the increasingly busy Linc and Red Hill Valley parkways to six lanes from four.
An environmental assessment study to look at widening started in 2020 - but it has largely been stuck in neutral since, with Indigenous consultation concerns and a slippery Red Hill scandal making parkway changes a sensitive topic.
Now Mayor Andrea Horwath, meanwhile, has suggested a provincial takeover of the parkways - an idea that, so far, has not showed up in any political party's campaign promises.
City transportation staff have warned in the past it makes no sense to widen busy local parkways before Ontario deals with its own traffic "backlogs" where city-owned roads disgorge drivers onto equally full 400-series highways. An updated report on the need for and feasibility of parkway widening is expected this year.
It isn't yet clear if the Tories' unexplained QEW-widening plan would address capacity issues near the Red Hill outlet at all. Past studies have shown widening Highway 403, meanwhile, would be environmentally tricky and space-constrained thanks to Cootes Paradise marsh and the escarpment itself.
The problem with all of the highway widening plans and promises, however, is "that they really haven't worked," said Siemiatycki.
"We have decades of experience now to show building bigger highways has not solved congestion ... it's a short-term fix and relatively quickly, that space gets filled up."
Studies refer to this concept as induced demand, the phenomena of new highway traffic lanes gradually attracting even more car drivers to fill them.
Opinions on the need for road-widening differ on Hamilton's current council, which has not yet taken a collective position on parkway expansion.
"Expanding highways, whether they are provincial or municipal, is unsustainable and the wrong thing to do on many levels," argued Ancaster councillor Craig Cassar. "It's not going to solve congestion. Just look at the 401.
"We all agree congested roads are a problem. But the only way we're going to solve that is by providing people realistic alternatives to exclusively driving in single-occupancy vehicles."
Stoney Creek councillor Brad Clark - who was transportation minister in the early 2000s Tory provincial government when the mid-peninsula corridor was first debated - said he believes some area highway widening or improvements are needed, depending on the proposal.
He noted the city made a strategic decision to divert more truck traffic onto its parkways and surrounding highways when it banned large tractor-trailers from downtown streets in 2023.
"That was the goal ... and it appears to be working," he said, pointing to new city statistics showing more truck traffic on the parkways last year. "But now if we want to keep trade moving, it would be a mistake not to increase the capacity," he said of both the parkways and adjoining provincial highways.
Matthew Van Dongen is a Spectator reporter. mvandongen@thespec.com
CAPTION: The QEW through Stoney Creek features four vehicle lanes in each direction - but traffic can still gum up near the Red Hill Valley Parkway at rush hour.The PC campaign has promised fast-tracked widening of the QEW from Niagara to Burlington this election.

Crombie, Stiles unable to make headway

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/crombie-stiles-unable-make-headway/docview/3168222626/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=unknown&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Crombie%2C+Stiles+unable+to+make+headway&amp;title=The+Spectator&amp;issn=11899417&amp;date=2025-02-19&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.9&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Spectator&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: In all the years I have been conducting public opinion research, I have never seen the mood of the Ontario electorate as bleak and negative as it is today.
Concerns about affordability that have dominated the public opinion agenda for two years persist, leaving vast swaths of the population feeling like they are treading water or falling behind. Concerns about a deteriorating health system have eroded what was once a source of pride and viewed as a hallmark differentiator between Canada and other nations. Added to these are anxieties about other serious issues, including global conflict and threats from the U.S.
Normally, this type of mood and sentiments are very bad news for a governing party, because it means they are running against the most formidable opponent an incumbent can face in electoral politics - "time for a change."
Virtually all polls published in the second week of the Ontario election campaign would indicate that Doug Ford has - at least for the time being - been able to avoid this confrontation. He and his team have been able to accomplish this by a combination of clever (some might say cynical and opportunist) strategy and luck.
By choosing to call an early election, before the federal parties go to the polls, he has been able to ride the tail winds on a very unpopular Liberal brand - the same one that also puts Pierre Poilievre 20 points ahead in some national polls.
Even more fortuitously, Donald Trump's chaotic behaviour and threats have left Ontarians feeling even more vulnerable to forces beyond their control.
The latter has not only allowed Ford to run against an opponent who is more unpopular and isn't in the race - Trump - it has also let him put masculinity on the ballot.
Indeed, as we have seen in the States and throughout the western world, and as pollsters are finding more and more, a sense of vulnerability and massive uncertainty has produced a voter demand for strongman protection and authoritative solutions - without being too flip about what is clearly a serious and potentially dangerous scenario, voters seem to be looking for "Dad." Whether or not it is warranted, Ford - more than Stiles or Crombie - projects an image of strength to voters.
With a lead of more than 20 points, Ford and his PCs do not have to attract a single new vote to guarantee a massive electoral victory in two weeks - he just needs to make sure they don't lose a meaningful portion of their current base to the Liberals and NDP.
However, a big part of Liberal Leader Bonnie Crombie and NDP Leader Marit Stiles' dilemma is that, before they have any chance of attracting current PC supporters, they have to get Ontarians to know who they are.
With almost one half of the population not having any feelings, one way or another, about Stiles and over one third expressing the same ambivalence toward Crombie, why would Ford raise their profiles by even mentioning them, let alone engaging in any serious debates that also might cast a light on issues where he does not fare as well? Better to put on his "Dad" hat and fight Donald Trump by going to Washington or from his chair as head of the Council of the Federation - and ignore Crombie and Stiles.
And this is where the dilemma for the Liberals and NDP has the potential to enter the realm of moral hazard.
Even if Crombie and Stiles could raise their profiles and approval ratings before the Feb. 27 election day, and even if they could find a way to exploit Ford's vulnerabilities in this short period of time, the fact is, current PC voters aren't particularly inclined to switch their vote to either the Liberals or the NDP.
But Liberal voters are very likely to consider the NDP as their second choice and NDP voters are equally likely to choose the Liberals as their second choice. In other words, it is much easier - and more likely - for both opposition parties to make gains at the other's expense than it is for them to erode the PC base in a way that could change the outcome of the election.
And both opposition camps are acutely aware of this. If there is no movement in the polls in the next week, their focus could very well turn to one another in an attempt to win the second prize of Official Opposition.
Should Ford win this election ... and should Pierre Poilievre repeat in spring or early summer, it would not surprise me at all to see a serious conversation take place between the Liberals and NDP about confronting this reality by merging.
Allan Gregg is an opinion
researcher, a principal at
Earnscliffe Strategies and
the co-host of the Red-Tory podcast.
CAPTION:

Document 101 of 500

Plane crash in Toronto: 18 passengers sent to hospital after Delta flight with 80 on board crashes at Pearson; Cause still under investigation; Delays at airport expected for days

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/plane-crash-toronto-18-passengers-sent-hospital/docview/3168093206/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Plane+crash+in+Toronto%3A+18+passengers+sent+to+hospital+after+Delta+flight+with+80+on+board+crashes+at+Pearson%3B+Cause+still+under+investigation%3B+Delays+at+airport+expected+for+days&amp;title=The+Hamilton+Spectator+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2025-02-18&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Hamilton+Spectator+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: A Delta plane has crashed at Toronto Pearson International Airport with around 80 people on board the flight. Multiple people have been injured and police and paramedics are on the scene. Follow the Star's live updates on Monday.
8:20 p.m. (updated) The cause of the crash is still under investigation and being led by the Transportation Safety Board, Toronto Pearson fire chief Todd Aitken said Monday night.
He would not speculate on the cause.
Eighteen injured passengers have been sent to local hospitals, he said.
"The runway was dry and there was no crosswind conditions," Aitken said.
Pearson and Mississauga Fire sent 18 vehicles combined, said Aitken, who added that Peel paramedics also responded with 18 paramedic units.
7:13 p.m. Pearson airport update
Seventeen passengers were injured as a result of the crash, according to Pearson officials. Three people were taken to hospital. Twenty-two Canadians were on board, the rest were internationals.
Two runways at Pearson are closed and delays are expected for days to come, Pearson officials said in an update.
"We are very grateful that there is no loss of life and relatively minor injuries," Deborah Flint, CEO of the Greater Toronto Airports Authority, said.
"No airport CEO wants to have these types of press conferences, but this is exactly what our emergency, our operations and our first responder partners are all practiced and trained for."
7:11 p.m. After a few hours of people waiting idly for their luggage from their cancelled flights, most bags are now arriving.
Several baggage carousels are now operating through Pearson Airport's earlier-bustling Canada arrivals, gradually clearing out the backlog.
Most travellers are now heading out, with their belongings in tow, others are voicing their frustrations attempting to rebook their vacation plans while some are sitting by, unsure what to do.
7:03 p.m. The media update has started.
6:30 p.m. Uxbridge, Ont. resident Ryan Blanchet, 34, and group of Canadians were gathered at a bar at Pearson watching the Canada-Finland 4 Nations Face-Off hockey game while emergency crews responded to the crash to their left.
As he and others looked out the windows, they could see the flashing lights of emergency crews off in the distance.
"We were watching the game, hoping everyone was OK and then Canada scored, and cheers broke out," he said. "It was really mixed emotions but we really didn't know what was going on."
He says the people scheduled to board his Porter flight to Puerto Vallarta, Mexico, were the closest to the crash site.
"I saw the ORNGE chopper and I was like: Oh that's not good," he said.
6:17 p.m. One Pearson-bound flight from Lisbon, Portugal made it partway across the Atlantic before turning around. Another flight - this one a British Airways plane from London - is doing the same.
Put together, 46 flights were forced to divert while Pearson was closed, according to Flightradar24, an online air traffic tracker. Montreal's Pierre Elliott Trudeau International received the most diversions, with 11. Ottawa received another nine.
6:13 p.m. How are travellers coping?
Heidi Matthews had been trying to fly from Toronto to St. John's since Thursday when she learned her travel plans were once again delayed, this time by the collision.
She said the airport never updated travellers on what had happened but she could see a motorcade of ambulances and buses travel across the tarmac from her seat at the gate.
"I think everyone's disturbed," she said. "Once, we even got a message saying that the airport was closed and that we should leave."
Matthews added that the airport was already a mess and travellers were frustrated before the announcements were made.
6:12 p.m. As Guelph resident Brett Aoyadjian, 50, was heading out onto his Porter flight to Mexico, scheduled to depart Monday, he could see the damage left behind from the Delta plane crash.
"Terrified for the people," he said. "I didn't even realize what I was looking at when I first saw it. And then I realized that's the engine, wheels and then a wing over here and another wing there. I was like: What do you mean?"
His flight is now delayed to Tuesday.
6:11 p.m. "The hearts of the entire global Delta family are with those affected by today's incident at Toronto-Pearson International Airport," said Delta CEO Ed Bastian. "I want to express my thanks to the many Delta and Endeavor team members and the first responders on site. We are working to confirm the details and will share the most current information on http://news.delta.com as soon as it becomes available. In the meantime, please take care and stay safe."
Anyone with loved ones on the flight can contact Delta's passenger inquiry center at 1-866-629-4775 in Canada and 1-800-997-5454 in the United States.
The airline cancelled all its flights to and from Pearson for the rest of the day and has issued a travel waiver.
5:55 p.m. There will be a media briefing on the plane crash at 6:30 p.m., Pearson airport says. About a dozen members of media waiting for the news conference at the Greater Toronto Airports Authority (GTAA) head office near Pearson.
5:45 p.m. Scott Hamilton, an aviation consultant with over 40 years of experience, told the Star it's "extremely rare" for a plane to flip during a landing.
"Over the decades, there have been a number of incidents," Hamilton said. "Not many though, and some have been survivable while some have not."
In videos circulating online, Hamilton said it's clear that at least one of the plane's wings was separated in the crash.
"The fact that the wing separated and the fuel spill did not create a conflagration is, in my opinion, a miracle because that's normally how big fire-driven explosions happen," he said.
5:37 p.m. Toronto weather forecast
A winter weather travel advisory is in effect for Toronto beginning overnight. Local snow amounts of 4 cm to 8 cm are expected, with reduced visibility at times.
5:36 p.m. Mitsubishi CRJ900
Mitsubishi Heavy Industries, who owns the CRJ series aircraft that crashed, told the Star they are "aware that a Delta Air Lines flight CRJ900LR was involved in an incident at Toronto Pearson Airport" and they will "fully cooperate with the ongoing investigation."
5:30 p.m. GTAA arrivals
Nearly three hours after the collision, scheduled departing and arriving flights resumed at Pearson at 5 p.m. Airport authorities were stationed at the arrivals gate to provide support to the families of passengers who were on the flight.
5:29 p.m. Flights resume at Pearson airport
Departures and arrivals have resumed at Toronto Pearson as of 5 p.m. All 76 passengers and four crew from Delta flight 4819 were accounted for. A number of passengers were taken to local hospitals. GTAA staff are supporting families of passengers at arrivals, Pearson said on X.
5:17 p.m. John C. Munro Hamilton International Airport is also accepting diverted aircrafts according to Colleen Ryan, associate director of communications at the airport.
"The airport has robust protocols in place to manage the flow of aircraft while maintaining the safety of incoming and departing flights," she told the Star.
5:15 p.m. Around the time of the collision, Environment Canada recorded west-blowing winds of up to 65 km/h, speeds high enough to "break twigs off trees" and make walking into the wind "almost impossible" according to the weather agency. The most common runway configuration at Pearson directs arriving flights to come in from the east.
5:10 p.m. The last crash at Pearson International happened in May 2019, when an Air Canada Express flight from Pearson bound for Sudbury was turned back due to inclement weather, and struck a fuel car while taxiing for a gate. Five people were injured, according to records from the Aviation Safety Network.
4:58 p.m. Delta says there were 76 passengers and four crew members on board the flight.
4:55 p.m. PC Leader Doug Ford says he's "relieved there are no casualties" after the crash at Pearson airport.
In a post on X, he says provincial officials are in contact with the airport and local authorities and will provide help as needed. - Canadian Press
4:47 p.m. 12 people injured, paramedics say
Lawrence Saindon, a supervisor with Peel Regional Paramedic Services, said there were 15 patients, 12 of whom have injuries ranging from mild to moderate.
He said two were taken by air ambulance to St. Michael's Hospital and another who was transported by ground ambulance to SickKids.
"We have no life threatening injuries to anybody at this time," he said, stressing that there are no fatalities.
"All crew and passengers have been accounted for."
4:45 p.m. "I'm relieved to learn that all passengers and crew are accounted for after today's plane crash at Toronto Pearson," Toronto mayor Olivia Chow posted on X.
"Thank you to the first responders, crew and airport staff for their quick actions and commitment to keeping everyone safe," Chow said.
Here's the latest on the crash of a Delta plane at Toronto's Pearson airport
4:39 p.m. Ann Nguyen, 19, was at Pearson with her mother and sister. The group had planned to fly to Cancun, Mexico, during her reading week but said the flight has been delayed six times since Sunday, due to the weather, and then cancelled on Monday.
"We got here, and then as we were coming in we found out that the plane crashed," said Nguyen, expressing relief that there are no fatalities.
Frustrated over her travel situation, she said she rather receive a refund than have the flight rescheduled again.
4:37 p.m. Kevin Feng, a Toronto resident who was heading on a Delta flight to New York Monday afternoon, was among the passengers whose flights were cancelled. He said the notice he received made no mention of the crash but he learned what happened from a staff member in the airport.
"It's scary," he said, especially knowing it's the same airline he was set to fly with.
4:36 p.m. Delta says it is aware of the reports of an "incident" involving flight 4819 from Minneapolis to Toronto.
"We are working to confirm any details and will share the most current information on news.delta.com as soon as it becomes available," the airline said in a statement.
4:33 p.m. The Transportation Safety Board of Canada is deploying a team of investigators following the aircraft collision to gather information and assess the occurrence, a statement read.
4:31 p.m. Montreal airport taking diversion flights
The airport in Montreal posted to X on Monday afternoon, saying they are taking some diversion flights.
"Due to an accident involving a @Delta Airlines aircraft at @TorontoPearson's airport, #YUL is preparing to receive several diversion flights. Our teams are already in action to welcome them. However, given the large number of passengers at YUL, please note that the situation may cause delays at various points along the passenger process. Thank you for your patience and understanding."
4:30 p.m. Three people with critical injuries
The ambulance service Ornge says it has transported three people with critical injuries to hospital, including a young person.
In an email, spokesperson Joshua NcNamara says one "pediatric patient" is being sent to SickKids hospital with critical injuries.
McNamara says one man in his 60s with critical injuries was sent to St. Michael's Hospital and a woman in her 40s, also with critical injuries, was sent to Sunnybrook Health Sciences Centre.
The number of critically injured people reported by Ornge is more than those previously reported by a spokesperson for the local paramedic service, who had said one person had critical but non-life-threatening injuries, while seven others were said to have mild to moderate injuries. - Canadian Press
4:25 p.m. I'm closely following the serious incident at the Pearson Airport involving Delta Airlines flight 4819 from Minneapolis. All 80 passengers onboard are accounted for. Updates will follow," Anita Anand said on X.
4:23 p.m. "I am relieved to hear there are no casualties following the plane crash at Pearson airport today. Grateful to first responders and airport authorities for their work today, and I'm wishing those injured a swift and full recovery," Bonnie Crombie wrote on X.
4:17 p.m. On air traffic control, a man is heard describing the scene at Pearson.
"Just so you're aware, there are people outside walking around the aircraft there," he says.
"Yeah, we've got it," another man responds. "The aircraft is upside down and burning."
Video of the Delta plane crash
4:15 p.m. Just after 4 p.m., the U.S. Federal Aviation Administration (FAA) confirmed 80 people were onboard the flight at the time of the crash. All passengers were evacuated. Canada's Transportation Safety Board will be in charge of the investigation, the FAA said.
4:10 p.m. According to the U.S. Department of Transportation, there were 80 people onboard at the time of the crash.
4:05 p.m. "This airplane just crashed on 23," an air traffic controller is heard saying around 2:13 p.m., according to archives accessed by the Star. A controller then directs another flight to pull up and go around.
4 p.m. Minutes before the crash, around 2:08 p.m., air traffic control at Pearson directs the Delta flight to contact the air traffic control tower.
Then, about five minutes later, air traffic control tells another flight that its approach clearance has been cancelled.
"Runway 23 is currently closed," a controller says to another plane. "It's gonna be a little while here."
3:57 p.m. 80 people were reportedly on the plane.
3:52 p.m. Two passengers with serious injuries were transported to nearby hospitals, including one taken to SickKids, a Peel police spokesperson said. No fatalities have been reported, they said.
3:51 p.m. Over the intercom in Terminal 3 departures at Pearson airport just now: "All flights have been suspended. More information will be shared as soon as it becomes available. Sorry for the inconvenience and thanks for your patience."
3:47 p.m. The airport has confirmed the incident involves a Delta Air Lines plane arriving from Minneapolis, and images posted on social media show the plane flipped over on the tarmac.
3:43 p.m. All flights are suspended at Pearson airport.
3:42 p.m. According to Flightradar24, the plane - a Delta Airlines flight from Minneapolis - arrived at Pearson at 2:13 p.m. The plane, a Mitsubishi CRJ-900LR, has room for about 75 passengers.
3:31 p.m. One pediatric patient was transported to hospital with critical injuries according to ORNGE. The emergency response service said they do not have information on any other transported patients yet.
ORNGE said they sent three helicopters and two critical care ambulances.
3:20 p.m. Peel police responding to the crash
Police could not comment on any injuries but said that "most of the passengers are out."
A post on X from Toronto Pearson confirmed that "All passengers and crew are accounted for."
The crash happened "upon landing," according to a post from Toronto Pearson, "involving a Delta Airlines plane arriving from Minneapolis."
CAPTION: A Delta Airlines plane flipped upon arrival at Toronto's Pearson Airport and ended up on its roof Monday, injuring 17 people. Feb. 18: Do your part when shopping, let's grow food not grass and other letters to the editor

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/feb-18-do-your-part-when-shopping-lets-grow-food/docview/3168006445/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Feb.+18%3A+Do+your+part+when+shopping%2C+let%27s+grow+food+not+grass+and+other+letters+to+the+editor&amp;title=The+Hamilton+Spectator+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2025-02-18&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Hamilton+Spectator+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Do your part when shopping
The determination to buy Canadian wherever possible in grocery stores and elsewhere is highly commendable. At the same time, let's not forget that there are plenty of excellent products available that, while not Canadian, are not American either.
A quick survey of my kitchen cupboards reveals preserved peaches from Bulgaria, hummus from Lebanon, sesame oil from Taiwan, pasta and olive oil from Italy, jam from France and mincemeat and butter chicken sauce from the U.K.
Our first priority should be to support Canadian producers wherever possible - but a strong second priority should be to buy non-U.S. imported products wherever possible. That will help to send the right message both to the United States and to current and potential trade partners around the world.
Jim Young, Dundas
Grow food, not grass
With American threats prepping to be reality, Canada should or could grow its own citrus fruits and vegetables by way of factory greenhouses. The building of these structures would enhance our ability to feed ourselves on a more diversified level.
I say rid the landscape of "dope greenhouses" and grow real food. It seems like a very good idea to me.
John H. Miller, Hamilton
No tough choices from council
Re: Hamilton council settles at 5.6 per cent budget hike, Feb. 13
Congratulations to Hamilton's city council for passing just a measly 5.62 per cent increase in the budget. Pay no attention to the fact that is more than three times greater than Canada's annualized inflation rate of 1.8 per cent.
Perhaps we can look forward to our taxpayer-supported Poet-in-Place paying lyrical tribute to a leadership that is unafraid of tough choices - unafraid because they just don't make them.
Tony Carlson, Waterdown
Death penalty not the answer
Re: Recording reveals Doug Ford calling for return of death penalty in campaign speech, Feb. 13
Instead of calling for the death penalty, which by the way is state-sanctioned murder, Doug Ford should be lobbying the federal government to change the bail laws and the Youth Criminal Justice Act. As well, build more jails so people aren't released due to overcrowding and build more courts so people aren't released because their case took too long to come before a judge.
There are solutions to crime problems that might take more work and will than just killing people.
Pat Stevens, Grimsby
Not all Americans support Trump
One of my fondest childhood memories is of crossing the Peace Bridge, travelling from Buffalo to Hamilton with my parents to visit our Canadian relatives.
You see, early in the 20th century my grandparents and their siblings emigrated from Italy. My grandparents settled in Buffalo; their siblings settled in Hamilton. Over the years, there were frequent visits back and forth. Food and wine and laughter flowed freely.
As years passed, the visits continued along with weddings and funerals. Crossing the bridge to visit always felt like I was going to my second home. Now grown and married with children of my own, I would mention familiar landmarks as we drove along the Queen E., pointing out Hamilton's "Mountain" when it came into view to let the kids know we were almost there.
I remember my Canadian family with deep affection, especially my second cousin (since passed away) who was like a brother to me. These days, I still get that same special feeling when I cross the bridge to attend the Shaw Festival at Niagara-on-the-Lake, visit the butterfly museum or see a play in Toronto.
The reason I share all this is to tell my Canadian friends that there are millions of Americans like me who do not support our current government's disgraceful actions and are heartbroken to see our Canadian ally being subjected to such shameful treatment.
These actions hurt us all. It is my hope that our friendship remains strong and that you all continue to stand up to America's bully president and his corrupt cronies.
At the age of 81, in light of current events, my only regret is that Grandpa didn't settle in Canada with his brother.
Elizabeth Accordino, Perrysburg, N.Y.
CAPTION: The determination to buy Canadian wherever possible is commendable. At the same time, let's not forget that there are plenty of excellent products available that, while not Canadian, are not American either, a reader writes.

Document 103 of 500

Ford shows rivals how to meet the moment

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/ford-shows-rivals-how-meet-moment/docview/3167861927/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=unknown&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Ford+shows+rivals+how+to+meet+the+moment&amp;title=The+Spectator&amp;issn=11899417&amp;date=2025-02-18&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.10&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Spectator&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Cometh the hour, cometh the man.
Whether or not the above was spoken by or about Winston Churchill is irrelevant. In a battle between truth and legend, print the legend. When Europe was facing its darkest hour as Nazi Germany's armies spread across the continent, it was Churchill who answered the call and kept the Allied powers in the game long enough to plot and then execute victory.
With the western world once again facing the prospect of blisteringly dark times, this time death by its own hand in the form of a U.S. administration busy knocking away the pillars of its own dominance, the time has come for serious leadership.
And so: Enter Doug Ford. Yes, Doug Ford. Cometh the hour, cometh the man.
It's an enduring fiction that elections are decided by policies and a rational working through of outcomes. Most elections are vibe shifts. Tired of the (federal) Liberals after over a decade in power? Elect Stephen Harper (and then Justin Trudeau when Harper wears out his welcome). Tired of a decade-plus of Dalton McGuinty and Kathleen Wynne? Elect Ford.
And so it is in the Year of our Lord 2025. Only this time the vibe shift has happened south of the border. The U.S. is now led by a petty, venal mercantilist who has tried to bully and dominate every room he's ever been in. The answer to the problem of Trump isn't so much hyper-literate policy countermeasures as it is street smarts. And Ford has always considered himself the man from Main Street.
No offence to Bonnie Crombie, Marit Stiles or Mike Schreiner, but this just isn't your fight. In this new world, you're a dial-up modem belching out squeals as you try to connect with your audience. Forget Trump. Ford will not be defeated by a forensic retelling of his failings, whether on the cost of freeing our alcohol or on Greenbelt development.
Ford's response to Trump's tariff threat is exactly what Ontarians most like about him. It might be a bit all over the shop, but it's passionate and pro-The People he's paid to represent, whether in Confederation or around the world. His energy and attitude are what's required at this moment, not business as usual. To pull a Crombie, Stiles or Schreiner out of the drawer right now is to pull the butter knife out at the gun-and-bluster fight.
Take Crombie. A one-time (and wholly unremarkable) federal MP and the former mayor of Mississauga, she says Ontario deserves a premier "who will take this pivotal moment seriously." And why isn't Ford taking "this pivotal moment seriously?" Well, you see, he called an "early election" in the "midst of an economic crisis." Of course, Ford has already won an election in the midst of an economic crisis, in the form of his re-election in 2022 when we were in the depths of market turmoil post-COVID and post-Russia's invasion of Ukraine. It's hard to see why Ontarians would punish him for not waiting until 2026.
Ford's calculation was that, in a country dying for a change of direction at the top, it was better to reconfirm his mandate before that change happened and before Trump could land more haymakers. Ford didn't "quit" on Ontarians, he's putting his faith in them.
As for Stiles, when your record is presiding over the selection of Jagmeet Singh as the president of the federal NDP and your acclamation as provincial leader of the Ontario NDP because nobody else could raise the $55,000 entry fee, convincing voters you're the best option to face a problem like Trump is to choose granola over experience. This is undoubtedly harsh on Stiles, but these are harsh times.
Another enduring fiction is that voters like to balance their provincial votes against their federal preferences. In this case, that would mean swerving left in Ontario with Canada set to swerve right when Parliament is reconvened only to be cast into the coming federal election. But the vibe shift in the U.S. means all of Canada will need to be yoked in the same direction - and quickly. It will not be time to break in new leadership or rookie politicians.
And while a government led by Pierre Poilievre would itself be reasonably amateur, it would benefit from some consistency and experience across its provincial counterparts. One of the artifacts of the post-Meech Lake period that needs to be reversed in the face of Trump is the decline of Canadian federalism. The country needs one song sheet and people who are ready to sing from it. And Doug Ford has always been strong of voice.
Would it be nice if the Ontario PC website had a section called "policy" on it? Yes. Would it be nice if Ford and his team hadn't bloated the provincial budget? Again, yes. Does any of this matter right now? No. Canadians' minds are concentrated at the prospect of a hanging. They want protection.
Which brings us to another reality often forgotten in politics. There are no options called "perfect." Elections are a choice between flawed options, and most voters go with their gut. What's more, they base their choice on the mood that surrounds them. And right now, the current mood music is dire. The province needs a brawler with a bit of unpredictability. All of this points to a Ford win.
Cometh the hour, cometh the (flawed) man.
Andrew MacDougall
is a director at Trafalgar Strategy, a U.K.-based
consultancy and is a former director of communications to former prime minister Stephen Harper.
CAPTION: Doug Ford's response to Trump's tariff threat is exactly what Ontarians most like about him: passionate and pro-The People he's paid to represent.The Associated Press file photo

CREDIT: Andrew MacDougall;

Full text: Do your part when shopping

Not all Americans back Trump
One of my fondest childhood memories is of crossing the Peace Bridge, travelling from Buffalo to Hamilton with my parents to visit our Canadian relatives.
You see, early in the 20th century my grandparents and their siblings emigrated from Italy. My grandparents settled in Buffalo; their siblings settled in Hamilton. Over the years, there were frequent visits back and forth.
As years passed, the visits continued along with weddings and funerals. Crossing the bridge to visit always felt like I was going to my second home. Now grown and married with children of my own, I would mention familiar landmarks as we drove along the Queen E., pointing out Hamilton's "Mountain" when it came into view to let the kids know we were almost there.

These actions hurt us all. It is my hope that our friendship remains strong and that you all continue to stand up to America's bully president and his corrupt cronies.

Elizabeth Accordino, Perrysburg, N.Y.
Ready to 'bare' arms?
Re: Happy to stand up for Canada, Feb. 10
In his letter, Anthony Corsini states he would be "one of the first in line" to bear arms to prevent Canada becoming the 51st state. Well, lots of luck Mr. Corsini! With Justin Trudeau's Liberals' desire to confiscate and destroy all the legally owned firearms in the country, the only arms Mr. Corsini will be bearing are his bare arms.
Greg Baran, Dunnville

Document 105 of 500

Child critically injured in plane crash at Pearson airport

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/child-critically-injured-plane-crash-at-pearson/docview/3167860979/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Child+critically+injured+in+plane+crash+at+Pearson+airport&amp;title=The+Spectator&amp;issn=11899417&amp;date=2025-02-18&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.6&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Spectator&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: A critically injured child is among at least 19 passengers hurt after a Delta Air Lines plane from Minneapolis crashed and flipped over on the tarmac at Toronto's Pearson Airport on Monday afternoon.
Ornge air ambulance said it was transporting one pediatric patient to Toronto's SickKids hospital and two adults with critical injuries to other hospitals in the city. Peel Regional Paramedic Services said they had recorded 19 injuries from the crash by late Monday afternoon, adding that the plane's 80 passengers and crew had all been accounted for.
Images and videos posted on social media showed the plane, a Mitsubishi CRJ-900LR, flipped over on the tarmac and passengers fleeing the jet as emergency crews responded to the scene.
Audio recording from the Pearson airport tower shows Delta Air Lines flight 4819 was cleared to land shortly after 2 p.m. and the tower warned the pilots of a possible air flow "bump" in the glide path from an aircraft in front of it.
There were no further conversations with the Delta flight until the tower confirmed that a plane had crashed, with air controllers quickly redirecting traffic to accommodate the crash scene.
Audio conversations from ground crews at the airport recorded a burst of commotion from workers at about the same time, with someone yelling at another person to "get off the phone," while another crew member described "a huge emergency."
Several minutes later, air traffic control can be heard in the recording directing a medevac helicopter for landing, and noting there are people walking around the aircraft.
"Yeah, we've got it," the medevac responds. "The aircraft ... is upside down and burning."
Delta Air Lines said in a social media post that the flight was operated by its subsidiary Endeavor Air and had departed from St. Paul International Airport in Minneapolis with 76 passengers and four crew on board.
"Our primary focus is taking care of those impacted," it wrote.
The plane had arrived at Pearson amid blowing snow following a winter storm that hit the Toronto region over the weekend.There was no immediate word on the cause of the crash.
John C. Munro Hamilton International Airport announced on social media that it was accepting aircraft that were being diverted from Toronto late Monday afternoon. Airport spokesperson Colleen Ryan told The Hamilton Spectator that the airport had protocols in place in order to "manage the flow of aircraft while maintaining the safety of incoming and departing flights."
The Transportation Safety Board of Canada said it is deploying a team of investigators to Pearson airport and federal Transport Minister Anita Anand said she was closely following the "serious incident."
Her U.S. counterpart, Transportation Secretary Sean Duffy, said inspectors from the Federal Aviation Administration were on their way to Toronto and the Canadian investigators would take the lead.
Ontario Premier Doug Ford said in a social media post that provincial officials are in contact with the airport and local authorities and will provide help as needed.
After the crash, flights in and out of the airport were temporarily suspended.
CAPTION: Delta flight 4819 from Minneapolis lays upside down on the runway covered in foam at Pearson International Airport on Monday afternoon in Toronto. At least 19 people were injured, including three critically, in the incident.Steve Russell Toronto Star

CREDIT: The Canadian Press

Document 106 of 500

Poilievre wasn't prepared for rise of Carney and Trump

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/poilievre-wasnt-prepared-rise-carney-trump/docview/3167860951/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=unknown&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Poilievre+wasn%27t+prepared+for+rise+of+Carney+and+Trump&amp;title=The+Spectator&amp;issn=11899417&amp;date=2025-02-18&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.10&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Spectator&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: "There are unknown unknowns." That's how former U.S. secretary of defence Donald Rumsfeld referred to the risks that come from situations so unexpected they would not normally be considered. Unknown unknowns are far more difficult to anticipate than known unknowns.
This typology of knowns forms the "Rumsfeld Matrix," which is often used in strategic planning in business and, presumably, by political strategists.
What were the "known unknowns" that federal Conservative Leader Pierre Poilievre and his advisers could have anticipated during his meteoric rise in the polls?
This rise began in the spring of 2022, when the Conservatives and Liberals were closely matched and continued until early this year, when they had established a 24-point lead prior to Trudeau's resignation and a subsequent surge in support for the Liberals.
They must have known that, at some point, the relentless attacks against a "wacko" prime minister who "broke Canada" would succeed. That Canadians would grow weary of him. And he'd lose his caucus' support.
They must have known that the carbon tax election they demanded would become irrelevant if the Liberals changed their stance.
They must have known that a potential Donald Trump return to the White House could threaten Canada's economy with a stronger mandate to put America first.
And they must have known that Mark Carney was organizing to replace Prime Minister Justin Trudeau at a moment's notice.
The resignation of Finance Minister and Deputy Prime Minister Chrystia Freeland, followed by the prime minister's, and the Liberals' pivot on carbon pricing were perhaps unknown unknowns. But the potential for a perfect storm triggered by Trump 2.0 must have been foreseen.
In business, these scenarios are usually gamed out with plans to handle various outcomes.
However, planning for a new narrative for the Conservative party seems to be occurring rather late. According to CBC reporting, the party has commissioned surveys to help adapt Poilievre's message to the new reality. This includes testing new lines of attack against the Liberals. Meanwhile, several Conservatives believe much money has been invested in Axe the Tax, with little time left to change course.
In my line of work, this means we have been hit by external factors we don't control and did not plan for. We're conducting research to figure out a new strategy. And there are strong differences of opinion among the C-suite on the go-forward strategy. That is usually quite destabilizing and puts you in a competitively weak position.
Meanwhile, Poilievre has a fresh sign on the lectern about "Canada First." And Carney - not a career politician - would apparently lift the Liberals in the polls to a tie with the Conservatives, thereby erasing the Conservatives' massive lead - if you believe recent polls.
Back in 2022, Maclean's appropriately asked on its cover "Why Is Pierre Poilievre So Angry?" We've learned why over the past three years. And now, as he faces a quickly changing situation, he no doubt isn't any happier than he was when he started. That's a challenge for a man who's been on the attack his entire political life.
Canadians don't want an attack dog. They now want a guard dog.
And that's a space another conservative, Doug Ford, Ontario's self-proclaimed protector, is more than happy to occupy during a snap election.
And, while it's too soon to know for sure, Carney is unlikely to be anyone's lap dog.
This asks the question: can the Conservatives teach their dog new tricks?
Perhaps. But there's little time. Furthermore, one has to wonder if Poilievre would want to, or be able to, walk away from a political style that has served him so well thus far - a political persona the party tried to soften with "meet Pierre the husband and father" ads coinciding with a new look.
He still attacks and opposes much more naturally than he charms. He now needs to prove he can inspire trust and confidence as Canadians face the consequences of a known known: Donald Trump is going to affect our lives for at least four more years.
Éric Blais is president of Headspace Marketing Inc.
CAPTION: Conservative Leader Pierre Poilievre is shifting from "Axe The Tax" slogans to "Canada First" but it may be too late for the Conservative leader to rebrand himself.ETHAN CAIRNS THE CANADIAN PRESS

CREDIT: Éric Blais;

Subject: Conservatism; Strategic planning; Politics; Prime ministers; Tax elections

Ontario election: What to expect at the leaders' debate tonight

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/ontario-election-what-expect-at-leaders-debate/docview/3167716150/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Ontario+election%3A+What+to+expect+at+the+leaders%27+debate+tonight&amp;title=The+Hamilton+Spectator+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2025-02-17&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Hamilton+Spectator+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: It is the main event of the Feb. 27 Ontario election campaign.
The four major party leaders square off on Monday night at CBC's Toronto Broadcast Centre for a 90-minute live televised debate.
It's the second and final time Progressive Conservative Leader Doug Ford, NDP Leader Marit Stiles, Liberal Leader Bonnie Crombie and Green Leader Mike Schreiner will gather on the same stage during this campaign.
The four met Friday in North Bay for a midday debate on northern issues.
Sparks flew on the shores of Lake Nipissing with Crombie accusing Ford of telling "lies" about her record and him saying it was "a joke" when she tried to distance herself from some decisions made by Liberal premiers Dalton McGuinty and Kathleen Wynne between 2003 and 2018.
But that was just a warm-up for Monday's more closely watched debate.
With Ford, whose Tories have been in office since 2018, enjoying a big lead in public opinion polls, Stiles, Crombie and Schreiner will have their sights set on him.
Each leader has spent hours preparing with simulated debates using advisers as role-playing stand-ins in an effort to gird for expected attacks.
Mindful of the target on his back, Ford's team has implored him to "take the high road" and remain calm in the face of opposition barbs.
He wants to keep the conversation focused on his chosen ballot question: which leader is best equipped to "protect Ontario" from the harmful effects of U.S. President Donald Trump's threatened tariffs on Canadian goods.
"Over the last five weeks, we've seen what the next four years will look like under President Trump's White House. He'll shift the goalpost every single day on a whim, creating economic chaos every single time he does it," said Ford, who was caught on a "hot mic" early in the campaign saying he "100 per cent" wanted Trump to win the U.S. election last November and has felt betrayed by him.
As she did in the North Bay debate, Stiles will be asking Ontarians if they feel better off now than they were in 2018.
"After seven years of Doug Ford, we've had a lot of talk but it's never been worse," she said Friday, pointing to the high cost of housing and the homelessness crisis that has seen tent encampments mushroom across Ontario.
Crombie, for her part, will be reminding viewers that 2.5 million Ontarians can't access a family doctor and touting the Liberal plan to cure that problem.
"You can't trust him because he doesn't deliver. Back in 2018, he said he'd end hallway medicine, the wait times have doubled. Back in 2018, he said he'd cut your taxes. He hasn't cut your taxes. He didn't get it done," she said of Ford.
Schreiner, meanwhile, will be underscoring the Tories' record on building housing, and raising the $8.28-billion Greenbelt land swap scandal that's now being investigating by the RCMP.
"Let's look at the record over the last seven years. Housing starts are at an all-time low in Ontario. Why is that most of the government's housing plans have been, 'How do we unlock profits for wealthy land speculators?' instead of how do we unlock affordable homes that people can afford?" he said.
Metro Morning radio host David Common will moderate the 6:30 p.m. to 8 p.m. debate that will be broadcast on CBC, CTV, CP24, CityNews, Global, TVO, CHCH, and CPAC as well radio stations across the province.
Colin D'Mello, Global News Queen's Park bureau chief, and CTV News Queen's Park bureau chief Siobhan Morris will join Common in asking questions of the candidates.
Coincidentally, the debate is being held on Family Day, a statutory holiday that was created in Ontario after being a cornerstone re-election promise of Liberal premier Dalton McGuinty in the 2007 campaign.
CAPTION: Clockwise from top left: Liberal Leader Bonnie Crombie, Progressive Conservative Leader Doug Ford, NDP Leader Marit Stiles and Green Party Leader Mike Schreiner.
CREDIT: Robert Benzie, Rob Ferguson, Kristin Rushowy

Document 108 of 500

How Donald Trump, tariffs and the Liberal leadership are erasing Ontario's election - and playing right into Doug Ford's hands

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/how-donald-trump-tariffs-liberal-leadership-are/docview/3167716063/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=How+Donald+Trump%2C+tariffs+and+the+Liberal+leadership+are+erasing+Ontario%27s+election+-+and+playing+right+into+Doug+Ford%27s+hands&amp;title=The+Hamilton+Spectator+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2025-02-17&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Hamilton+Spectator+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Ontario voters clueing in to the fact they are in the middle of a quick, unexpected election could be forgiven for thinking their choices on the Feb. 27 provincial ballot will be Donald Trump, tariffs or Mark Carney.
Political observers and some door-knocking candidates say that international and national headlines blasting Ontarians like a fire hose, stoking dire financial fears and debate over who best to lead Canada, are almost erasing the provincial election - likely to the benefit of the Progressive Conservatives.
"I think that was what (Conservative Leader) Doug Ford was gambling on when he called an early vote," said Prof. Lydia Miljan, head of political science at the University of Windsor, in an interview. "He's seizing the uncertainty of the moment while (U.S. President) Donald Trump sucks up all the political oxygen with a continual lurching of, 'Here's a tariff, delay the tariff, maybe double the tariff' - it's exhausting.
"If it wasn't for the few Progressive Conservative signs I am seeing around, I would have forgotten that there is a provincial race going on at all because we are talking so much about the federal Liberal leadership race and Trump and all this tariff noise."
John Campbell, a former city councillor running for the Liberals against PC incumbent Kinga Surma in Etobicoke Centre, said voters answering the door during this rare mid-winter campaign seem unhappy about a lot of things, including Ford calling the election 15 months ahead of schedule and his leadership in general. But many also seem apathetic about forcing change.
"I think, by and large, people are just really disappointed with the whole news cycle," said Campbell, who is also a former Toronto District School Board chair.
"You've got Gaza, you've got Trump, you've got Ukraine, you got a mishmash in Ottawa - all of the leadership stuff there. You've got people in power here who nobody really seems to like that much but there's not a whole lot of hope in the air, and people are just kind of throwing their hands up and saying, 'What a mess it is.'"
Campbell said his pitch to shake off voter apathy is that Ford is only pretending to be a fiscal conservative, spending $612 million to get alcohol into corner stores before the election, and $3 billion to send every Ontarian a $200 cheque during the campaign, as the underfunding of health care, colleges and universities, and public schools are degrading Ontarians' quality of life.
"Most people are mixed on Doug Ford or not very happy," Campbell said. "I just hope this election gets some momentum soon."
Ford launched the campaign Jan. 29 saying he needs a "clear mandate" from Ontarians to fight Trump and his threatened 25 per cent tariffs on Canadian-made goods being sold in the U.S. that would severely damage the province's economy. "He wants to undermine us in any way he can," Ford said, days before being caught saying he cheered Trump's win after a campaign in which the Republican leader promised tariffs.
While Liberal Leader Bonnie Crombie and NDP Leader Marit Stiles campaigned in the cold and snow, Ford was in the international spotlight, making headlines along with other premiers and territorial leaders during a mission to Washington to fight tariffs.
They warned U.S. lawmakers that Trump's levies would ravage the economies of both nations in meetings that included a stop at the White House to chat with two Trump officials - but not the president.
Ontario's integrity commissioner approved Ford's midelection trip provided his activities weren't for partisan purposes. Ford's social media accounts posted a highlight reel of the trip that includes his party's logo and campaign slogan of "Protect Ontario," prompting Stiles to say she will ask for an integrity investigation of the trip. If that happens, any finding will come well after the Feb. 27 vote.
Miljan said provincial leaders' debates should give Crombie and Stiles the chance to remind voters who they are and what they stand for, and to put Ford on the hot seat. The debates, however, are unlikely to galvanize voters or drown out competing news events.
"Debates don't really matter that much - they're great for pundits and media to have something to focus on, and will be helpful to introduce Crombie and Stiles to the electorate, but Doug Ford's a known quantity and he's playing the 'Captain Canada' card," she said.
"You've also got federal Liberal leadership debates coming up," she noted, between five candidates including Carney, former governor of the Bank of Canada and then Bank of England, and Chrystia Freeland, MP and former deputy prime minister. Those debates could potentially cause further voter confusion.
In Oshawa, New Democrat Jennifer French takes a break from door-knocking in her bid for a third term in office, knowing she'll soon be heading back out into an evening snowstorm.
"I thought initially it would be too cold for conversations at the door because people are surrounded by political stories at all levels, but they've actually been really engaging at the door, they know there is a provincial election on," French said.
Existential fears over Trump's tariffs is a bit of déjà vu for Oshawa, she said, since the community in 2018 faced news of the coming closure of the local General Motors plant, and the loss of almost 3,000 jobs. A campaign against that closure ended with the production line being reopened and people rehired to make pickup trucks.
"Everyone is looking for strong, capable leadership," French said. "While Canada might not be for sale, this has been a unifying threat from Trump. I don't have to lead people to think about the ways that the Ford government has pushed privatization," she said.
"There are so many layers of politics happening all at once. When I was door-knocking in a building filled with seniors, who are at home and watching the news, there is a lot of anxiety and uncertainty with the volatility in the States, there is anticipation of a possible federal election," French said.
"The seniors are worried about costs going up, about their loved ones, about themselves," she said. "The conversation about provincial health care might not be what they lead with but it's the second point they raise: 'Have you've seen Trump? OK. Well, what are you going to do to help seniors and make sure they stay a priority? It becomes very community-based very quickly."
The Progressive Conservative campaign did not respond to the Star's requests for comment.
CAPTION: Liberal Leader Bonnie Crombie talks to students at a campaign event at George Brown College in Toronto, on Feb. 11, 2025.
CREDIT: David Rider

Ontario election debate: Doug Ford, Bonnie Crombie, Marit Stiles and Mike Schreiner all make pledges about family doctors

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/ontario-election-debate-doug-ford-bonnie-crombie/docview/3167578058/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Ontario+election+debate%3A+Doug+Ford%2C+Bonnie+Crombie%2C+Marit+Stiles+and+Mike+Schreiner+all+make+pledges+about+family+doctors&amp;title=The+Hamilton+Spectator+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2025-02-17&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Hamilton+Spectator+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: The Ontario Leaders' Debate will be held Monday night at 6:30 p.m. ahead of the provincial election on Feb. 27. Read our election promises tracker. See our election poll tracker. Follow our live updates Monday evening with the Star's Queen's Park bureau.
7:02 p.m. Interesting that the debate organizers keep giving extra time to Doug Ford to respond to attacks. It's normal for the incumbent to attract the most criticism; it's unusual for the incumbent to win extra time as a bonus.
- Martin Regg Cohn
7 p.m. Doug Ford blaming the federal Liberals for crime in Ontario while Bonnie Crombie reminds him he has been leading the province for seven years and things are getting worse.
- Robert Benzie
6:56 p.m. Just a matter of time until PC Leader Doug Ford went on the attack against Liberal Leader Bonnie Crombie. We like to say that she lives rent-free in his head.
Interesting how the other party leaders are all posing questions to Doug Ford - giving the moderator a run for his money.
- Martin Regg Cohn
(Updated) 6:52 p.m. Another shot at Ford from Crombie: "You were promised a tax cut but never got it." A reference to Ford's unfulfilled 2018 promise for a middle-class income tax cut. Crombie has promised one.
"You didn't get it done, Doug, you didn't cut our taxes," Crombie says.
"I can't get a word in edgewise here," Ford protests in exchange with Crombie.
- Rob Ferguson
6:49 p.m. Mike Schreiner says "the rich insiders are getting richer" in Doug Ford's Ontario.
Marit Stiles promises rent controls and grocery rebates. Doug Ford touts the $200 cheques the Tories sent out last month just before he called the early election. - Robert Benzie
6:47 p.m. First zinger of the night... Ford touts hiring former federal health minister Dr. Jane Philpott to reform the system. Crombie shoots back "you had to hire a Liberal" to fix it. - Kris Rushowy
6:46 p.m. Inevitable and predictable that NDP Leader Marit Stiles would pin hallway health care on a previous Liberal government, from 7 years ago. - Martin Regg Cohn
6:42 p.m. Ford now mentioning his primary care plan, which is actually $1.8 billion over four years. And for 2 million people, which is different from the OMA number that 2.5 million don't have a family doctor. - Rob Ferguson
6:41 p.m. Tough early round for Doug Ford, asking about health care. Progressive Conservative strategists will tell you privately, when we're talking about health care, we're losing.
Not that they're losing the election, but they'd rather talk about something else - like Donald Trump and the economy. Hence Ford's quick pivot to a healthy economy underpinning health care. - Martin Regg Cohn
(Updated) 6:39 p.m. Family doctor promises
Liberal Leader Bonnie Crombie promises to get everyone in Ontario a family doctor within four years. Right now, about 2.5 million people don't have one, according to the Ontario Medical Association.
Mike Schreiner, the Green Leader, also promises doctors for all within four years.
PC Leader Doug Ford says his government has attracted thousands of doctors and nurses already, but does not mention $2 billion plan to connect all those who do not have one to a doctor within four years.
NDP Leader Marit Stiles cites stats like long waits in ERs and thousands of patients turned loose from a Sault Ste. Marie health clinic last year, saying Ontario cannot afford to go on like this. - Robert Benzie and Rob Ferguson
6:37 p.m.More time to respond to name checks
CBC host David Common tells viewers that if any of the candidates name checks another, that person gets 30 seconds to respond. Idea is to keep candidates speaking about their own priorities and platforms. - Kris Rushowy
6:37 p.m. The leaders are asked about their first jobs.
Crombie: cashier at Woolco
Stiles: babysitting, swimming instructor
Ford:delivering Globe & Mail as a kid, Canada Packers
Schreiner: feeding cattle/driving combine on family farm, mowing lawns - Kris Rushowy
6:34 p.m. Bonnie Crombie says "after seven years 2.5 million people don't have a family doctor" and 1 million are using food banks. She says Ontario isn't recognizable.
Green Leader Mike Schreiner says Ford has "abandoned his job." Schreiner blasts Ford for the Ontario Place spa, the 401 tunnel scheme and ripping up bike lanes. - Robert Benzie
6:32 p.m. The debate is underway
Doug Ford says the election he triggered early is all about who can best "protect Ontario," which is the Progressive Conservatives' campaign slogan.
Marit Stiles says she will be "on your side" fighting rising costs. "On Your Side" is the New Democrats' slogan. And the Liberal slogan ... "More for you." - Robert Benzie and Kris Rushowy
6:31 p.m. I love a debate, it's core to a democratic election, but I always remind my media friends: It's not a boxing match - it's a sales pitch, where politicians need to connect with voters. Stay tuned! - Martin Regg Cohn
6:29 p.m. Hunkered down at home in front of the television, putting myself in the shoes of ordinary voters like me who are watching the debate from their couch - instead of the spin room or rarefied newsroom where there's too much electricity in the air! - Political Columnist Martin Regg Cohn
6:26 p.m. Kris Rushowy from the STar's Queen's Park Bureau is at CBC headquarters, Queen's Park Bureau Chief Robert Benzie is covering from Queen's Park.
6:23 p.m. Ontario party leaders have been given the terms of the debate and are getting ready to begin, posing for photos at CBC headquarters' Front St. studio. All four leaders are expected to scrum when the debate concludes at 8 p.m. - Kris Rushowy
What to expect at the leaders' debate tonight
It is the main event of the Feb. 27 Ontario election campaign.
The four major party leaders square off on Monday night at CBC's Toronto Broadcast Centre for a 90-minute live televised debate.
It's the second and final time Progressive Conservative Leader Doug Ford, NDP Leader Marit Stiles, Liberal Leader Bonnie Crombie and Green Leader Mike Schreiner will gather on the same stage during this campaign.
The four met Friday in North Bay for a midday debate on northern issues.
Read the full story from the Star's Queen's Park bureau
What time is the debate happening and who will participate?
The live debate will be held from 6:30 p.m. to 8 p.m.
The debate will feature Liberal Leader Bonnie Crombie, PC Leader Doug Ford, Green Party Leader Mike Schreiner and NDP Leader Marit Stiles.
Who will host the debate?
The debate will be moderated by CBC host David Common, CTV News Queen's Park Bureau Chief Siobhan Morris and Global Bureau Chief and President of the Queen's Park Press Gallery Colin D'Mello.
The Ontario Leaders' Debate is open to leaders of all parties that currently have an elected MPP sitting in the legislature.
Where can I watch the debate?
It will air on all partner television platforms. It will also be simulcast on various radio stations across the province and streamed online.
Poll suggests race is tightening, but Doug Ford still well ahead
Doug Ford remains well ahead, but the Feb. 27 Ontario election race is tightening somewhat as Bonnie Crombie's Liberals gain ground, a new poll suggests.
The Abacus Data survey for the Star found Ford's Progressive Conservatives at 41 per cent support, ahead of Crombie's Liberals at 28 per cent, Marit Stiles's New Democrats at 21 per cent and Mike Schreiner's Greens at six per cent.
That's a five percentage point drop for the Tories and a four percentage point increase for the Liberals since the last Abacus survey released Feb. 7. The NDP remained the same and the Greens went up one percentage point.
Read the full story from the Star's Robert Benzie
Inside Doug Ford's trip to Washington - and how he was received by Americans
In politics, as in war, symbolism matters.
So with Canada on the brink of a trade war with the United States, it was symbolic that the nation's provincial and territorial leaders secured a meeting in the White House.
Yes, it was at the last minute - and scheduled thanks to an $85,000-a-month lobbying firm retained by the Council of the Federation - and it was only with Jim Blair, one of President Donald Trump's deputy chiefs of staff, and White House personnel director Sergio Gor.
Read the full story from the Star's Robert Benzie
Rivals slam Doug Ford on health care, death penalty as he insists he's the best choice to take on Donald Trump
Bonnie Crombie tried to distance herself from previous Liberal governments in the first leaders' debate of the Feb. 27 election campaign, prompting Doug Ford to growl "what a joke."
In a testy exchange, the Liberal leader said she disagrees with some moves by Liberal premiers Dalton McGuinty and Kathleen Wynne during the 15 years they were in power - a span that ended with a 2018 win by Ford's Progressive Conservatives.
"As you know, I'm a new Liberal leader and I'm not taking responsibility for decisions made previously," Crombie said during a 90-minute debate on northern issues Friday at Canadore College in North Bay.
Read the full story from the Star's Queen's Park bureau
Liberal Leader Bonnie Crombie stands by candidate who made light of intimate partner violence
Liberal Leader Bonnie Crombie is standing by a candidate despite his social media posts making light of intimate partner violence in the wake for former CBC host Jian Ghomeshi's legal troubles.
"These are tweets from over a decade ago, and the candidate has apologized," Liberal campaign spokeswoman Bahoz Dara Aziz said Friday in regard to Brian Hamilton, running in Thunder Bay-Superior North.
Hamilton, a Thunder Bay councillor and coffee shop owner hoping to wrest the riding from the NDP's Lise Vaugeois in the Feb. 27 election, said he is a different person now.
Read the full story from the Star's Rob Ferguson
Ontario NDP candidate in Eglinton-Lawrence folds campaign to help Liberal opponent win
The NDP hopeful in Eglinton-Lawrence says she withdrew her candidacy to avoid a vote-split that could help Ontario's Progressive Conservatives keep the seat.
In a post on X Thursday evening, Natasha Doyle-Merrick said: "I recognize the race in Eglinton-Lawrence is a clear two-party contest between Liberals and Conservatives. To prevent a Conservative win and more years of neglect, I'm stepping aside to avoid a vote split," on the political left.
In an attached statement, the mental health worker said she made the difficult decision after much reflection, adding that she first sought the NDP nomination because she felt Robin Martin, the riding's PC MPP since 2018, had ignored some voices in the community.
Read the full story from the Star's David Rider
Ontario election promise tracker
Ontario is heading to the polls Feb. 27 and we're tracking what each party is promising ahead of the Ontario election.
All four provincial party leaders have now put forth plans to support Ontario through U.S. President Donald Trump's tariff plans along with pledges to improve affordability and more.
Here's what else the Progressive Conservatives, NDP, Liberals and Greens have pledged since the start of the campaign.
