Document 125 of 500

Ford's 401 tunnel costly, unworkable

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/fords-401-tunnel-costly-unworkable/docview/3166970310/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=unknown&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Ford%27s+401+tunnel+costly%2C+unworkable&amp;title=The+Spectator&amp;issn=11899417&amp;date=2025-02-15&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.19&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Spectator&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Doug Ford continues to insist that part of his plan to combat gridlock will include the construction of a tunnel under Highway 401, stretching all the way from Mississauga to Markham.
The Progressive Conservative leader says it would be the world's longest tunnel, but no dollar figure or timeline has been attached, because feasibility studies must be carried out first.
Rather than wasting millions of Ontarians' dollars on that, here is my feasibility study, offered for the very reasonable price of free: no.
If there is another example in recent political history of a leader making a promise this recklessly expensive and completely impracticable, it does not leap to mind. On top of that, a tunnel wouldn't even do much to address the problem it was intended to solve. It's the rare election-pledge trifecta: costly, unworkable, ineffective.
Start with the money. The Scarborough subway extension, which is less than eight kilometres long, has an estimated cost of around $6 billion. Extrapolating from that, a tunnel under the 401 at something like 50 kilometres long would cost in the ballpark of $60 billion. Add in the usual cost escalations on an infrastructure project that could easily take decades, and the final price tag could easily approach $100 billion.
To put that in context, Ford's decision to send out $200 cheques to everyone in the province cost an estimated $3 billion. Even if the 401 tunnel cost $60 billion, that would be the equivalent of having sent out $4,000 cheques instead. Not cheap.
And even if that kind of outlay could be justified, how such a project might actually work is hard to fathom. A tunnel of the length proposed would need multiple entry and exit points, but the stretch of highway that Ford is considering is already covered on the surface with existing roads, homes and businesses. Where would all the tunnels to get in and out of the big tunnel go?
The scale, too, seems wildly impractical. For the Beijing Olympics in 2022, China built a tunnel under the mountains of Zhangjiakou to complete the final stretch of the route from the city to the alpine venue. After early testing, the organizers realized they had to install high-intensity lights through the length of it because drivers were becoming fatigued in the darkened space. It was only nine kilometres long. What kinds of hazards would be created by a 50-kilometre tunnel? This is the stuff of disaster movies.
But for the sake of argument, let's imagine the budgetary and logistical issues could be overcome, and Ford's tunnel plan could somehow become a reality. Would it significantly ease traffic congestion on Highway 401? Well, no. Ask any traffic engineer or planner if adding an additional lane to an existing road frees up traffic, and they will explain that, no, more space just means that more vehicles will cram in to fill the void.
If the tunnel were connected to existing transit routes, it could encourage commuters to take a more efficient mode of transportation across that corridor. But Ford himself talks about the tunnel plan as a boon primarily to drivers, and the transit part is only mentioned as a tacked-on bonus - even as actual transit infrastructure projects in the province are many years and billions of dollars over budget.
It's also worth noting here that the world's big tunnel projects, like the one in Zhangjiakou, are built specifically to go under mountains or bodies of water - where it's a practical necessity that justifies the expense of building underground.
Put another way, there's a simple explanation for why Ford's tunnel would be the world's longest: because no one else would even consider building such a thing.
Scott Stinson is a journalist based in suburban Toronto.
CAPTION:

CREDIT: Scott Stinson Scott Stinson is a journalist based in suburban Toronto.;

Document 127 of 500

Ontario party leaders spar over housing in first debate

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/ontario-party-leaders-spar-over-housing-first/docview/3166970284/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Ontario+party+leaders+spar+over+housing+in+first+debate&amp;title=The+Spectator&amp;issn=11899417&amp;date=2025-02-15&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.16&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Spectator&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: The leaders of Ontario's New Democratic, Liberal and Green parties went on the offensive Friday against Progressive Conservative Leader Doug Ford, who polls suggest is cruising to another majority government, in the first debate of the provincial election campaign.
The debate in North Bay was held specifically to discuss northern issues. The leaders talked about road safety and infrastructure in the north, as well as the addictions crisis that has hit many northern communities hard, but the three party leaders also saw an opportunity to zero in on housing and Ford's record across the province.
"Where are the homes that Doug Ford said he was going to build?" NDP Leader Marit Stiles said.
"Where are the homes, Doug? Because they're not in our communities. They're not in northern communities. They're not anywhere in Ontario right now. He has not done a dang thing about it. That is the truth."
Ontario's housing starts were down 16 per cent in 2024 compared to the previous year, and the province has not met any of its interim targets toward building 1.5 million homes since Ford promised that in the 2022 election.
Last year's target was 125,000 homes, but Canada Mortgage and Housing Corp., data show there were just over 72,000 housing starts in 2024 in Ontario. Government officials previously said that even though interest rates have been coming down, it takes a while for that decrease to have an impact and they still expect to meet the 1.5 million homes target.
Green Party Leader Mike Schreiner said housing starts are low while housing prices are high in part because Ford has the wrong priorities.
"Most of the government's housing plans have been: how do we unlock profits for wealthy land speculators, instead of how do we unlock affordable homes that people can afford," Schreiner said.
When Ford was asked to talk about housing, he said his government had cut red tape and regulations, and removed HST from purpose-built rental housing. But he spent most of his time criticizing the others' housing records and plans.
He slammed Liberal Leader Bonnie Crombie's record as mayor of Mississauga, but she disputed his statements.
"Premier, you know, you talk a good tale and you sound kind of folksy and charming, but none of what you're saying is actually true," she said.
"You told us seven years ago you would end hallway health care. It's gotten twice as bad. You said you'd cut our taxes, but you didn't do that either. You didn't get it done, and you said you'd build 1.5 million homes."
When the moderator turned to Ford to allow him to defend his housing record, he immediately began talking about the fight against possible tariffs from the United States, which he has spoken about frequently on the campaign trail - prompting Stiles to exclaim, "Oh my gosh."
The reference to U.S. President Donald Trump also prompted Stiles to accuse Ford of saying one thing in public and another in private, noting a video that recently caught Ford saying he had hoped Trump would win the U.S. election.
"You have shown us who you are," Stiles said. "You say one thing in front of the public, like this, and you go to say another thing behind closed doors."
Leaders also denounced comments Ford made about the death penalty, as revealed by the Toronto Star. The newspaper reported that at a police gala in London, Ont., on the first day of the campaign Ford called for stronger penalties for home invaders who kill people so the offenders could be sent "right to sparky."
The Canadian Press
CAPTION: Ontario Liberal Leader Bonnie Crombie, left to right, Ontario NDP Leader Marit Stiles, moderator CBC Radio Morning North host Markus Schwabe, Ontario Progressive Conservative Party Leader Doug Ford and Ontario Green Leader Mike Schreiner take part in the Ontario election debate in North Bay on Friday.Gino Donato The Canadian Press

CREDIT: Allison Jones and Liam Casey The Canadian Press

Hamilton East- Stoney Creek

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Hamilton+East-+Stoney+Creek&amp;title=The+Spectator&amp;issn=11899417&amp;date=2025-02-15&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.12&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Spectator&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: The candidates
Zaigham Butt (NDP)
The accountant and union leader with the Professional Institute of the Public Service of Canada is also an Order of Hamilton recipient and ran in the riding for the first time in 2022.
Heather Curnew (Ontario Party)
The candidate is CEO at Myrrh Ministries.
Wieslawa Derlatka (New Blue)
No biographical information was immediately available.
Heino Doessing (Liberal)
The former municipal councillor in Denmark moved to Canada in 2010. He has since worked with an accounting firm for some of Canada's largest pension and private equity funds.
Drew Garvie (Communist)
Garvie previously ran as a federal communist candidate in Toronto.
Neil Lumsden (PC)
The incumbent MPP was Ontario's minister of sport. He was general manager and CEO of the Hamilton Tiger-Cats in 1999 and is in the Canadian Football League's Hall of Fame.
Pascale Marchand (Green)
The constituency assistant for Hamilton's Ward 4 is a community advocate who is nominated for a 2025 YWCA Women of Distinction award.
Background
The riding of Hamilton East-Stoney Creek was created in time for the 2007 provincial election from parts of the previous Hamilton East and Stoney Creek ridings. It was held by Paul Miller with the NDP from 2007 until 2022, and briefly as an Independent, until Progressive Conservative Neil Lumsden won the 2022 election.

Bobbi Ann Brady (Independent)
Brady won the riding in 2022 without party affiliation after the longtime Tory staffer was passed over as the PC candidate.

The Dunnville resident studies criminology at the University of Ottawa.

The current mayor of Norfolk County previously served one term on council representing Port Dover.

Independent MPP Bobbi Ann Brady has represented Haldimand-Norfolk since 2022. She was the longtime executive assistant to Progressive Conservative MPP Toby Barrett, who represented the riding from its formation in 1999. Brady ran as an Independent in the previous election after being passed over as PC candidate in favour of former Haldimand Mayor Ken Hewitt, who lost by just over 2,000 votes.

Ontario provincial leaders debate: Here's how to watch on Family Day, Feb. 17

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/ontario-provincial-leaders-debate-heres-how-watch/docview/3167206498/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Ontario+provincial+leaders+debate%3A+Here%27s+how+to+watch+on+Family+Day%2C+Feb.+17&amp;title=The+Hamilton+Spectator+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2025-02-14&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Hamilton+Spectator+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: The Ontario election is quickly approaching on Feb. 27.
And while many residents make plans to join their loved ones for Family Day on Monday, Feb. 17, the provincial political party leaders will square off against each other during a debate at 6:30 p.m., available to watch on television or through livestream.
Who are the provincial leaders?
Progressive Conservative Leader Doug Ford, NDP Leader Marit Stiles, Liberal Leader Bonnie Crombie and Green Party Leader Mike Schreiner will take part in the debate on CBC Television, starting at 6:30 p.m.
"The CBC's David Common will moderate while Global's Colin D'Mello and CTV's Siobhan Morris will also pose questions to the leaders," the news organization said.
There won't be a live audience in the studio for the debate.
Where to watch the debate?
The discussion is being run by a broadcast partnership consisting of CBC, CTV, Global, TVO, CHCH, CPAC, CityNews, CP24 and OMNI News. Check your local news channel for the broadcast.
A livestream is also available on YouTube.
The online streamed version will also include American Sign Language interpretation and close captioning for those who need the service.
CAPTION: Progressive Conservative Leader Doug Ford, NDP Leader Marit Stiles, Liberal Leader Bonnie Crombie and Green Party Leader Mike Schreiner will take part in the televised debate, which runs from 6:30-8 p.m. ET. The CBC's David Common will moderate while Global's Colin D'Mello and CTV's Siobahn Morris will also pose questions to the leaders.#Ontario #Politics #News #Toronto #ElectionThe Ontario Votes logo.

Document 131 of 500

NDP questioning Ford's U.S. trip: 'This demands transparency and accountability,' Siles says

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/ndp-questioning-fords-u-s-trip/docview/3166472223/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=NDP+questioning+Ford%27s+U.S.+trip%3A+%27This+demands+transparency+and+accountability%2C%27+Siles+says&amp;title=The+Spectator&amp;issn=11899417&amp;date=2025-02-14&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.6&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Spectator&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Ontario's NDP and Liberal party leaders cried foul Thursday over a trip Progressive Conservative Leader Doug Ford took to Washington, D.C., in the middle of a snap election he called, saying he's touting that visit for partisan purposes.
NDP Leader Marit Stiles wrote to the province's integrity commissioner to ask him to look into the trip, including a campaign-style video Ford's team produced using clips from the jaunt.
"In my view, his actions have repeatedly and intentionally blurred the lines between partisan campaigning and official provincial business," Stiles wrote in the complaint. "This demands transparency and accountability."
Ford and the country's other premiers travelled to Washington this week to push back against U.S. President Donald Trump's threats to impose steep tariffs on Canadian goods. The Progressive Conservatives have said the party paid travel expenses for Ford and campaign staff.
Ford made the trip as premier, but he is also in the midst of campaigning in a snap provincial election that he called.
Ahead of the trip, integrity commissioner J. David Wake wrote that it would not violate the caretaker convention - a principle that during an election the only government business that should continue is in a few instances, including routine matters or those that are urgent and in the public interest.
Wake wrote that in the context of current U.S.-Canada political relations, the trip meets the urgent and public interest criteria. However, he said that such work was appropriate "provided that the activities are not then used for partisan purposes."
Upon Ford's return to Ontario, his social media accounts posted a video showing clips of him in Washington that ended with the Progressive Conservative party logo.
It was taken down and reposted without the logo shortly after Stiles said she would raise it with the integrity commissioner. Stiles suggested she still views Ford as using the trip for partisan gain.
"I don't think anyone is fooled by that (removal of the logo), and I think it is a sure sign of guilt as well," she said at a campaign stop in Sudbury.
"I think that he knows, and they know, they did something wrong, and yet that never seems to stop them."
The Canadian Press
CAPTION:

CREDIT: Allison Jones The Canadian Press

Subject: Public interest; Provincial elections

Location: United States--US

Document 132 of 500

Letters

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/letters/docview/3166471912/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=unknown&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Letters&amp;title=The+Spectator&amp;issn=11899417&amp;date=2025-02-14&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.11&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Spectator&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: City must live within its means
Re: It's time to cap city spending and tax hikes, councillor says,
Feb. 12
Good for you Brad Clark! We've been living in Coun. Clark's district for quite some time and have always agreed with his determination to make things better for his constituents. Any person living within a budget knows that sometimes things have to be left behind in order to meet all of one's financial obligations. We totally support Clark's proposal to our city council.
All we need now is for the other councillors to do their job and work for the citizens of Hamilton not for what "city hall" would like to have from its wish list.
Catherine Castellan, Hannon
Our cities need
financial assistance
Coun. Brad Clark wants the city to cap tax increases at 2.5 per cent. I understand the reasoning. Many people already find it difficult to pay their property tax and no doubt the city bureaucracy is not perfectly efficient. But I disagree with Clark. The City of Hamilton, along with other municipalities, is financially overstretched. I get the impression that councillors are working hard to keep increases to a minimum. And it is only too obvious that we need more capital investments; in roads, water and sewer lines, health facilities, and on and on. Capping taxes poses a risk - to vehicle safety and more generally public health.
Let's lobby the province to do more for our municipalities. And let's vote for a party that is more likely to do that.
Richard Harris, Hamilton
This is no time
for tax hikes
Good for Coun. Brad Clark. He is correct in saying they will have a taxpayer revolt on their hands if this next increase happens. Of course, if you ask people what they "need" they will "need" more than they actual do, especially when it does not cost them anything. It is about time to say this is what you are getting - work with it.
This is more important now than ever with the tariffs being put on our steel which will no doubt put a lot of residents out of work. How will they pay their taxes with no income?
Diana MacKenzie, Hamilton
Weather doesn't stop Spec carrier
I was both surprised and pleased as I looked out my front window this morning and saw today's edition of The Hamilton Spectator on our snow-covered driveway. The familiar postman's slogan came into my head. "Neither rain, nor snow, nor sleet, nor hail keep the postman from their appointed rounds." Kudos to our Hamilton Spectator carrier Charlene!
Mary Elizabeth Toth, Ancaster
Ontarians should
be Ford's focus
Ontario will soon elect a new government.
Listening to Doug Ford I wasn't sure if he was aware of that fact. I hear him mention Donald Trump a lot. He also mentioned China. He talks a great deal about highways.
I have not heard him say anything about underfunded education, sustainable development, the approximately $1,100 a month some people live on, housing, hallway medicine, doctor shortages, long wait times for almost any medical test, nurse shortages - in fact I rarely if ever hear him talk about the problems Ontario has.
Ford called this election for three reasons. Trump's fascist government gave him a villain to call out.
He wanted an election before Pierre Poilievre tears apart Canada, as Trump is doing in the U.S. And he wanted an election before the RCMP gave its report on the Greenbelt scandal. All self serving, not much, if anything, to help Ontarians.
I will vote for a leader and party that has its focus on Ontario, not Trump, not China and not highways that will enrich developers and save 20 minutes on a friend's drive to his cottage.
Bill Sullivan, Hamilton
CAPTION:

Subject: Local elections; Roads & highways; Tax increases

Document 133 of 500

Liberals' Crombie has not lived up to the hype

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/liberals-crombie-has-not-lived-up-hype/docview/3166471828/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=unknown&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Liberals%27+Crombie+has+not+lived+up+to+the+hype&amp;title=The+Spectator&amp;issn=11899417&amp;date=2025-02-14&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.10&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Spectator&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Ontario Liberal Leader Bonnie Crombie was expected to breathe new life into the hapless party.
But it appears the Liberal party - which doesn't even have official status in the legislature because of its low numbers - remains on life support.
There were great hopes for Crombie, the former mayor of Mississauga and former Liberal MP, when she won the leadership race in December 2023.
Supporters were gleeful to get someone of her so-called stature and more than satisfied she could take the party from third place to the government benches, and show Premier Doug Ford and his PCs the door.
Well, it appears things haven't gone her way.
As it turns out, she is not known in many parts of the province, I'm told. Strike 1.
Well past the election start, the party was still scrambling to find candidates in each of the 124 ridings, even though Ford has been hinting for the past six months he would call an early election. Strike 2.
Ontario voters go to the polls Feb. 27 after a tight four-week election campaign.
By the way, the Liberals don't even have riding associations in about a third of the ridings. I will call that Strike 3.
While some will disagree, I believe her tactical error was the decision not to seek a seat in the legislature.
Instead, her handlers told her it would make more sense to wander around the province preaching the Liberal gospel. How did that work out for her?
Crombie, who turned 65 last week, was behind the eight ball right off the bat by not being a member of the legislature when she won the nomination.
The fact is there is a host of reasons for the Liberals' inability of make inroads.
Because the Grits don't have party status they don't get the kind of funding necessary from the legislature to hire staff. And it's staff that helps prepare a party for an election.
Even though the Liberals were totally unprepared for this election campaign, they will quite likely finish second in the popular vote. But that will not translate into seats, leaving a diminished New Democratic Party to form the Official Opposition.
Rightly or wrongly, Crombie will shoulder the party's poor performance.
She has stumbled a few times, the most glaring being the allegation in bold letters on the side of her campaign bus that 11,000 people died in Ontario last year while awaiting surgery.
According to data from provincial agency Ontario Health, from January to November, 1,365 adult Ontarians died while on a wait-list for surgery.
Whoops.
That's what happens when you are trying to run a provincial campaign on a shoestring.
It's hard to believe Crombie can't seem to get traction when both health care and education are in deep trouble thanks to Ford and his PC government. And let's not forget Ford's litany of goof-ups and transgressions.
The fact is Crombie has not lived up to the hype, and I'd say her chances of sticking around are slim when the party has its next annual general meeting following the election, at which there is a mandatory leadership review
Frankly, the best thing that could happen to Crombie is that she loses her bid for the riding of Mississauga-East-Cooksville and is allowed to bow out gracefully. The Tories in the last election swept Mississauga, Brampton and both York and Halton regions.
In the meantime, the "van party" will languish in third place until the next "miracle worker" comes along.
Richard J. Brennan
is a retired politics reporter living in Dundas.
His Substack handle
is The Badger.
CAPTION:

CREDIT: Richard J. Brennan;

Subject: Legislatures

Title: Liberals' Crombie has not lived up to the hype

Publication title: The Spectator; Hamilton, Ont.

First page: A.10

Publication year: 2025

Publication date: Feb 14, 2025

Section: Editorial

Publisher: Torstar Syndication Services, a Division of Toronto Star Newspapers Limited

Place of publication: Hamilton, Ont.

Country of publication: Canada

Publication subject: General Interest Periodicals--Canada

ISSN: 11899417

Source type: Newspaper

Language of publication: English

Document type: Editorial

ProQuest document ID: 3166471828

Document URL: https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/liberals-crombie-has-not-lived-up-hype/docview/3166471828/se-2?accountid=15090

Ontario's Liberals stumbled into this election and have yet to find their footing

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/ontarios-liberals-stumbled-into-this-election/docview/3166471318/se-2?accountid=15090

Document 135 of 500

Feb. 14: City must live within its means, this is no time for tax hikes and other letters to the editor

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/feb-14-city-must-live-within-means-this-is-no/docview/3166471128/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Feb.+14%3A+City+must+live+within+its+means%2C+this+is+no+time+for+tax+hikes+and+other+letters+to+the+editor&amp;title=The+Hamilton+Spectator+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2025-02-14&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Hamilton+Spectator+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: City must live within its means
Re: It's time to cap city spending and tax hikes, councillor says, Feb. 12
Good for you Brad Clark! We've been living in Coun. Clark's district for quite some time and have always agreed with his determination to make things better for his constituents. Any person living within a budget knows that sometimes things have to be left behind in order to meet all of one's financial obligations. We totally support Clark's proposal to our city council. All we need now is for the other councillors to do their job and work for the citizens of Hamilton not for what "city hall" would like to have from its wish list.
Catherine Castellan, Hannon
Cities need financial assistance
Coun. Brad Clark wants the city to cap tax increases at 2.5 per cent. I understand the reasoning. Many people already find it difficult to pay their property tax and no doubt the city bureaucracy is not perfectly efficient. But I disagree with Clark. The City of Hamilton, along with other municipalities, is financially overstretched. I get the impression that councillors are working hard to keep increases to a minimum. And it is only too obvious that we need more capital investments; in roads, water and sewer lines, health facilities, and on and on. Capping taxes poses a risk - to vehicle safety and more generally public health.
Let's lobby the province to do more for our municipalities. And let's vote for a party that is more likely to do that.
Richard Harris, Hamilton
This is no time for tax hikes
Good for Coun. Brad Clark. He is correct in saying they will have a taxpayer revolt on their hands if this next increase happens. Of course, if you ask people what they "need" they will "need" more than they actual do, especially when it does not cost them anything. It is about time to say this is what you are getting - work with it. This is more important now than ever with the tariffs being put on our steel which will no doubt put a lot of residents out of work. How will they pay their taxes with no income?
Diana MacKenzie, Hamilton
Weather doesn't stop carrier
I was both surprised and pleased as I looked out my front window this morning and saw today's edition of The Hamilton Spectator on our snow-covered driveway. The familiar postman's slogan came into my head. "Neither rain, nor snow, nor sleet, nor hail keep the postman from their appointed rounds." Kudos to our Hamilton Spectator carrier Charlene!
Mary Elizabeth Toth, Ancaster
A constant in uncertain times
With all the uncertainty and turmoil, angst and worry, there is a constant I count on daily; the delivery of the Spectator. This morning it was there, flush against my door so I had only to reach down, not step out into the snow in my socks. Mute footprints in the deep snow testified that Ryan, as usual, was delivering. I needed that.
Bob Tyrrell, Hamilton
Ontarians should be the focus
Ontario will soon elect a new government. Listening to Doug Ford I wasn't sure if he was aware of that fact. I hear him mention Donald Trump a lot. He also mentioned China. He talks a great deal about highways. I have not heard him say anything about underfunded education, sustainable development, the approximately $1,100 a month some people live on, housing, hallway medicine, doctor shortages, long wait times for almost any medical test, nurse shortages - in fact I rarely if ever hear him talk about the problems Ontario has.
Ford called this election for three reasons. Trump's fascist government gave him a villain to call out. He wanted an election before Pierre Poilievre tears apart Canada, as Trump is doing in the U.S. And he wanted an election before the RCMP gave its report on the Greenbelt scandal. All self serving, not much, if anything, to help Ontarians.
I will vote for a leader and party that has its focus on Ontario, not Trump, not China and not highways that will enrich developers and save 20 minutes on a friend's drive to his cottage.
Bill Sullivan, Hamilton
Stop feeding Trump's appetite for attention
Trump, Trump, Trump, Trump, all I see is Trump, everywhere in the media and elsewhere. He is not God! He thrives on chaos. Cut off his "oxygen" so he cannot spew his vitriol far and wide, throughout the world. We must work diligently to defeat his goals of dominance, but at the same time we should stop putting a microphone to his racist and dangerous outbursts.
John H. Miller, Hamilton
CAPTION: Putting a cap on city spending and tax hikes is more important now than ever with many residents facing tariff-induced unemployment, a reader says.

Subject: Local elections; Roads & highways; Tax increases

Document 136 of 500

Why your vote matters on Feb. 27

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/why-your-vote-matters-on-feb-27/docview/3166471073/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=unknown&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Why+your+vote+matters+on+Feb.+27&amp;title=The+Spectator&amp;issn=11899417&amp;date=2025-02-14&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.10&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Spectator&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: The president of the U.S. is threatening to make Canada the 51st state and has already launched the first volleys in a trade war.
An ascendant Liberal party, bolstered by Trump-induced anger and anxiety, has made the federal horse race more interesting than expected. The tensions - and drama - are high.
No wonder, then, that so few seem to be paying attention to an Ontario election that nobody much wanted, at a time of year not suited to elections, in which an incumbent leader (albeit with an approval rating of 38 per cent) is expected to romp to victory.
Turning out voters was already a struggle in this province. A historically low not-quite 44 per cent of 10.7 million eligible voters cast a ballot in the 2022 general election, and that one was in June, and everyone knew it was coming. The Tories formed a majority government with just 18 per cent of the total potential vote.
That's not good for democracy. And whether you love Doug Ford, tolerate him or detest his politics, it won't be good for democracy if he is allowed to waltz back into the premier's office without a real debate on the issues that matter.
Take family doctors. About two-and-a-half million Ontarians were without one last year, and that figure is projected to increase. Every party is promising to invest in solving the problem, but they differ significantly on how, how urgently and at what cost.
The NDP, for instance, has promised to spend $4 billion to recruit 3,500 doctors over the next four years, while the Progressive Conservatives propose to spend less than half as much on a more modest plan. The Liberals, as one might expect, fall somewhere in the middle.
We may not have wanted this election, but we now face an important choice: Are you willing to pay higher taxes or for the province to incur more debt for a more urgent response to the family-doctor shortage that torments so many families in this province? Or do you prefer a more affordable, but slower and partial solution?
Or take housing, an area of largely provincial jurisdiction in which all three parties are making expensive (yet uncosted) promises. So many of the challenges we face in this province stem from the lack of affordable housing.
And while all parties are vowing to confront the crisis, they differ profoundly on rent controls, on non-market housing and on the controversial question of fourplexes. On the latter, both the Liberals and the NDP want developers to be able to build four-unit buildings without municipal approval, while the PCs oppose such a plan.
Again, Ontarians face a clear - and crucial - choice: Is it more important that fourplexes be constructed, allowing for what housing experts call gentle densification, or that municipalities have the power to reject such plans if they are worried about what it will mean for the infrastructure and character of their neighbourhoods?
And it goes way beyond the doctor shortage or the question of fourplexes. Education and municipalities, property rights and civil rights - these are at least partly provincial issues. In other words, much of the stuff that makes up the day-to-day life of your average Ontarian.
Of course, if you're reading this, you likely already know that provincial elections matter. But like many people in this province, you've got a lot going on right now: groceries are unaffordable, commutes are miserable, Trump is scary.
But this is why we must care, and why we should urge our friends to care, despite the cold and all the competing demands on our attention. On Feb. 27, we'll have a rare opportunity to exert a little influence, to try to preserve what's best in our province and city and fix what's broken.
CAPTION:

Document 137 of 500

Doug Ford broke ethics rules with campaign ad, opponents charge

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/doug-ford-broke-ethics-rules-with-campaign-ad/docview/3166819291/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Doug+Ford+broke+ethics+rules+with+campaign+ad%2C+opponents+charge&amp;title=The+Hamilton+Spectator+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2025-02-13&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Hamilton+Spectator+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Doug Ford violated ethics rules against using government activities for partisan purposes in an online ad about his trip to Washington to lobby against Trump tariffs, opposition parties charge.
The ad on the Progressive Conservative leader's X feed Thursday pictured him walking past the U.S. Capitol. It used the party's "Protect Ontario" logo and slogan from his bid for a third term as premier in a snap election he called for Feb. 27.
The ad was quickly taken off social media and reposted a few hours later with no Conservative logo or slogans.
"If that isn't a sure sign of guilt, I don't know what is," NDP Leader Marit Stiles said in Sudbury, insisting the ad retains partisan overtones because it was posted in the context of the campaign.
"I don't think anyone's fooled," Stiles added, noting her party intends to file a complaint with Ontario's integrity commissioner J. David Wake, an independent officer of the legislature.
Ford's campaign defended the ad.
"Footage of the premier being the premier is routinely used in political social content," spokesperson Ivana Yelich said in a statement to the Star.
"However, out of an abundance of caution, the video was reposted without campaign branding."
Wake's office has ruled that fighting tariffs threatened by U.S. President Donald Trump is "an urgent manner in the public interest" but cautioned that Ford and his cabinet members can take part in related events "provided that the activities are not then used for partisan purposes."
The Conservative party slogan in the ad, in which Ford intones, "Let's focus on restoring the pride of made in the U.S.A and made in Canada," quickly raised concerns once it hit social media.
Liberal Leader Bonnie Crombie said the Conservative campaign knew of the caution issued by the integrity commissioner and chose to ignore it.
"Doug Ford plays fast and loose with the rules," she told reporters in Elmvale, near Barrie.
Ford appeared in the ad with PC candidates Vic Fedeli and Stephen Lecce, walking with the U.S. Capitol towering above them. Ford is pictured speaking to the U.S. Chamber of Commerce and meeting with U.S. officials and in news conferences and scrums with reporters.
As head of the Council of the Federation, he led a delegation of premiers to Washington to argue against tariffs on Canada. The Progressive Conservatives said they paid the travel expenses of Ford and his campaign staff.
The ad is a clear violation of ethics rules, Stiles said.
"They know they did something wrong, and yet that never seems to stop them."
Crombie had previously raised concerns the trip violated the so-called "caretaker convention," which holds that the only government business that can be carried out during an election campaign is routine or urgent matters in the public interest.
CAPTION: Premier Doug Ford leaves after speaking to reporters at the Mayflower Hotel in Washington on Feb. 12, 2025.
CREDIT: Rob Ferguson, Kristin Rushowy

Subject: Public interest; Political campaigns; Logos; Ethics; Political advertising; Social networks; Reporters; Tariffs

Document 138 of 500

Chrystia Freeland proposes cash incentives to bring Canadian doctors home

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/chrystia-freeland-proposes-cash-incentives-bring/docview/3166819283/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Chrystia+Freeland+proposes+cash+incentives+to+bring+Canadian+doctors+home&amp;title=The+Hamilton+Spectator+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2025-02-13&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Hamilton+Spectator+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: OTTAWA-Liberal leadership candidate Chrystia Freeland said she would try to bring Canadian doctors and nurses home, offering them cash incentives to leave jobs in the U.S. and work here.
Freeland unveiled the policy Thursday and said as the U.S. administration attempts economic warfare, Canada should respond forcefully with tariffs, but also work to bring Canadian doctors back home.
"We should also pursue policies that capitalize on this moment to strengthen our wonderful country, including our universal public health-care system," she said in a statement.
Freeland said she would offer doctors a $200,000 signing bonus to work in Canada and pay off up to $300,000 in student loans. Nurses would be offered a $100,000 incentive to move back to Canada and up to $50,000 to help with student loans, she said. She is also pledging to work with provinces and territories to shorten foreign credential approvals for doctors and nurses down to one month.
The incentives would target Canadian citizens who are doctors or nurses working in the U.S.
As of 2022, more than 6.5 million Canadians didn't have a family doctor, according to a research report from the MAP centre for urban health solutions, a Toronto-based research institute.
Freeland said her proposals would help turn that tide.
"My three proposals to bring Canadian doctors and nurses back home and recognize foreign credentials would dramatically boost Canada's health-care workforce and improve Canadians' access to the care they deserve," she said.
With less than a month to go in the Liberal leadership race three of the other contenders in the race also had policy announcements.
Former Bank of Canada governor Mark Carney announced a housing initiative, pledging to reduce barriers to construction and incentivize modular construction to speed home construction while reducing costs.
In a statement, he said his government would do everything it could to confront the housing crisis.
"Strong communities aren't built on long commutes and financial worries. They're made possible when all Canadians can afford to live with dignity," he said. "We need strong leadership and a clear vision to confront the housing crisis head-on, and build the strongest economy in the G7."
Candidate Karina Gould made a commitment to modernize the Employment Insurance system. She said the entire system needs an overhaul to better reflect modern jobs that are often contract or gig positions.
"I would bring forward a plan to update our social safety net to really help Canadians and not just catch them when they fall, but also to enable them to bounce back even stronger than before," she said.
Gould said that would mean improving EI, but also ensuring adequate benefits for seniors and the disabled. Without offering specifics, she said a modernized EI system could put Canada on the path to a basic income.
A basic income program was piloted in Ontario but later cancelled by the Ford government. A basic income program usually means providing payments to individuals as a way to help them turn the corner on poverty, get retrained, or find employment.
Former MP Ruby Dhalla also pledged to provide mental health care to all Canadians and appoint a health czar to find ways to improve the system.
Liberals will select a new leader on March 9.
CAPTION: Liberal leadership candidate Chrystia Freeland speaks to the Toronto Star's editorial board on Feb. 10, 2025.
CREDIT: Ryan Tumilty

Document 139 of 500

Ford's bulldozing of Ontario must be stopped

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/fords-bulldozing-ontario-must-be-stopped/docview/3166092702/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=unknown&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Ford%27s+bulldozing+of+Ontario+must+be+stopped&amp;title=The+Spectator&amp;issn=11899417&amp;date=2025-02-13&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.12&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Spectator&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Difficult as it is to look away from the train wreck in U.S. politics, we must focus on our own in Ontario.
With this snap provincial election on Feb. 27, it's time to end the bulldozing of Ontario by a government that doesn't even pretend to acknowledge the reality of our climate breakdown as it lays waste to environmental protection.
As well as wasting our tax dollars, Doug Ford's team is breaking what was working well, such as The Beer Store. Long held up as an exemplary example of a "zero hero" because of the deposit, return, reuse and recycling of beer bottles, cans and cases, the early cancellation of beer store contracts cost us $1.4 billion.
The government has also misplaced incentives. By both removing rebates for electric vehicles and cancelling vehicle licence fees, it increases our dependence on internal combustion engines, a major source of greenhouse gas emissions. The licence fee cancellation alone meant the loss of $1.1 billion in government revenue.
Ford's assault on our environment started immediately after he was first elected as he cancelled more than 700 clean-energy contracts to the tune of $230 million.
Since then, this government has:
repealed all protective environmental and climate change legislation;
violated the Environmental Bill of Rights multiple times;
excessively used Minister's Zoning Orders to ignore local planning and environmental assessments;
tripled energy from polluting gas-power plants since 2017;
decided to remove bike lanes in Toronto at a cost of $48 million;
began redeveloping Ontario Place, priced to date at $2 billion, with a process that was not "fair, transparent or accountable," wrote the Ontario auditor general. The loss of this well-needed urban, public, waterfront green space is bad enough, but to add insult to environmental injury, more than 800 trees were clear-cut under cover of darkness. This destruction dislocated local wildlife and drove coyotes into nearby neighbourhoods. As well, the plan includes allowing raw sewage overflow directly into Lake Ontario.
decided to close the Ontario Science Centre against the government's own expert advice. "Canadian Architect" wrote the environmental impact of demolishing it means "the destruction of much more of the natural landscape, but also ... the landfilling of an extremely large building that could have endured for decades longer."
and - biggest and baddest of all - is the Greenbelt scandal that threatened to carve up our valuable protected farmland and green space under the guise of addressing our housing crisis. It was averted because our communities fought back, but this scandal is still under RCMP investigation.
On top of all this, Ford was "100 per cent happy" about Trump getting re-elected and is trash-talking how electing Liberal Bonnie Crombie or NDP Marit Stiles as premier would be "an absolute disaster."
Need more?
Check out Environmental Defence protecting Ontario's environment.
If any or all of this resonates with you, take the pledge - both provincial and federal - with Cooperate for Canada to find the Unity Candidate in your riding.
Susan Koswan is a freelance contributing columnist based in Waterloo Region. Reach her at
greyandstillgreen
@gmail.com.
CAPTION:

Document 140 of 500

We need the party leaders to plug into an electricity debate

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/we-need-party-leaders-plug-into-electricity/docview/3166092423/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=We+need+the+party+leaders+to+plug+into+an+electricity+debate&amp;title=The+Hamilton+Spectator+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2025-02-13&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Hamilton+Spectator+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Next Monday is the leaders' debate at the CBC. It will be consequential because of the many problems we face today.
In every single provincial election since 2003, the three major parties have declined to debate the hydro rate problem and its causes. This issue affects all others because of Doug Ford's spending on it.
First, some history. Shortly after being elected, then-premier Mike Harris went fishing in the Northwest Territories with Kenneth Lay, chair of Enron. Harris then had Enron and a who's who of the investment community design Ontario's electricity market which opened in June 2002. Despite an expensive ad campaign promising "lower rates" and promising "nothing will go wrong" by 2007 rates had doubled, by 2010 rates tripled and then by the time Ford is elected in 2018, rates had quadrupled. Since deregulation was implemented in 2002, every single government has had to subsidize rates showing how flawed hydro deregulation is.
Ford has taken hydro subsidies to new heights. Ford loves to say he keeps all his promises. In the 2018 election he promised to lower hydro rates by 12 per cent. Ford has not lowered rates by even one per cent. All Ford has done is hide and protect Harris' failed deregulation scheme with billions in subsidies. According to the Financial Accountability Office, every year Ford has spent up to $7 billion in hydro subsidies and plans to spend $118 billion over the next 20 years, never dealing with the problem.
Ford is very vulnerable on this issue and has shown he is worried about public opinion on hydro rates. In October 2020 Ford offered ratepayers one of the Tories favourite words: "choice." They could choose "time of use" payments or they could choose tiered payments; so much for the first 750 or 1,000 kwh and then so much for the next tier. Doing the math shows it is just a wash which works out to be exactly the same cost. Then in April 2023 Ford introduces ultralow overnight rates 2.4 cents a kwh. from 11 p.m. to 7 a.m.
On Aug. 28, Ford announced the biggest competitive expansion of private power in Ontario history. In his announcement he made the exact same promise as Harris and used the word "affordable" nine times. Ford calling nuclear power "clean and affordable" is like calling lasagna and cheesecake diet foods. History shows the opposite.
The public was so angry at the Liberals in the last two elections for selling Hydro One, they were sent to the political wilderness. To date, Ford has spent $49 billion on hydro subsidies. That's enough to buy back Hydro One five times over. The Liberals would be well advised to admit they made a mistake and promise to buy back Hydro One.
A number of countries around the world have recognized the fact that the transition to green energy and electric vehicles will never happen under a for-profit system and have created public green power companies. We need to look seriously at creating a green public power company ourselves. We have 94 years of proof that public power works.
Currently in Ontario, electricity is a great business to be in. Ford guarantees the profits and taxpayers get the bill.
We cannot let another election pass without our leaders debating this critical problem, the affordability and climate crisis, demand action on this issue.
CAPTION: In the 2018 election Doug Ford promised to lower hydro rates by 12 per cent. Ford has not lowered rates by even one per cent, while pouring billions into subsidies, Paul Kahnert writes.
CREDIT: Paul Kahnert

Subject: Provincial elections; Electric rates; Electricity; Subsidies

Business indexing term: Subject: Subsidies

Document 141 of 500

Ontarians should turn out in droves on Feb. 27

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/ontarians-should-turn-out-droves-on-feb-27/docview/3166092346/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Ontarians+should+turn+out+in+droves+on+Feb.+27&amp;title=The+Hamilton+Spectator+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2025-02-13&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Hamilton+Spectator+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: It's frightening that the U.S, a (former) strong democracy has sunk to an oligarchy, headed by two egotists whose ambitions and egos are sure to collide at some point. Then what?
Closer to home, we have a provincial story to watch, headed by another ego-driven leader and one-time Donald Trump fan, who called an unnecessary costly election when we're all feeling a pinch in our wallets. Because this snap election is so soon, this is my last opportunity to address it.
Doug Ford just gave us all $200 (adding to the highest deficit provincial Tories have run in years). Aren't Tories traditionally the watchdogs of the public purse? I guess Ford missed Finance 101. And Health Care 101. Does anyone think, in this uncertain economy, that this deficit will shrink? What ever happened to mindful PCs? Obviously he doesn't care about voters, calling an election in the dead of winter.
We should turn out in droves for ABF (Anyone but Ford). We can protest his nepotism and U-turns, not to mention cronyism with developers and his disdain for elected councils. Not only did he break the contract with LCBO, costing $612 million according to the Financial Accountability Office, he's now ripped up another contract, with Elon Musk's Starlink; cost unknown. He's campaigning on his mirage of tunnelling under the 401, again uncosted, but estimated at many billions! This tunnel would have to be very high and wide. Visualize subterranean collisions, and the intricacies and costs of extrications, especially in fiery accidents. On our dime!
We have excellent choices in the Burlington riding.
I bet few even know the name of the PC incumbent because she's been mostly invisible, except for photo-ops. The two flyers I received during Natalie Pierre's term didn't even mention Ford's name.
Carrying the Liberal torch is Andrea Grebenc, a former Halton school board chair who led the board through the tough pandemic. Highly respected locally, she even earned a cybersecurity designation.
A bright young chemical engineer, Megan Beauchemin, is the NDP candidate.
As a data and digital organizer for an environmental organization, Kyle Hutton could be a factor for the Green Party, with climate change so key today.
Excellent candidates all.
It was nice to learn that local singer/songwriter Sarah Harmer will receive a 2025 humanitarian award at the JUNO spring ceremony for her environmental work. She has been vocal on protecting Mount Nemo and the Niagara Escarpment (a UNESCO Biosphere Reserve), the habitat of the Jefferson salamander, and in efforts to reform gravel mining. This will be the second time she worked to prevent expansion of the Nelson Quarry. Its previous expansion proposal was denied.
Now our council and residents are opposing a second bid. How many times do residents and councils have to fight the same costly fights? There is another long very costly hearing looming, expected to last several weeks. When I was a councillor in the 1970s and '80s fielding complaints about blasting, Nelson's line was always, "We'll be mined out in a couple of years."
The escarpment commission has consistently opposed mining on the Mount Nemo plateau. Doug Ford, are you listening?
This week Burlington committees had light agendas, but the March sessions look heavy. There was one planning application approved, replacing two homes at 2169 and 2175 Ghent Avenue with two three-storey rental apartment buildings of six units each.
This winter has seemed so long and cold. I'm looking forward to signs of spring, like the annual closing of King Road to protect Jefferson salamanders during their March-April crossings of the road to their breeding ponds, and the annual nesting of Hamilton's peregrine falcons on King Street's Sheraton Hotel ledge.
CAPTION: Progressive Conservative Leader Doug Ford speaks during his campaign launch in Windsor on Jan. 29. He called an unnecessary and costly election when we're all feeling a pinch in our wallets, Joan Little writes.

Document 142 of 500

Premiers launch 'charm offensive': Trump's top advisers tell Canadian delegation to take what the president says 'at face value'

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/premiers-launch-charm-offensive/docview/3166084041/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Premiers+launch+%27charm+offensive%27%3A+Trump%27s+top+advisers+tell+Canadian+delegation+to+take+what+the+president+says+%27at+face+value%27&amp;title=The+Spectator&amp;issn=11899417&amp;date=2025-02-13&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.9&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Spectator&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: A top White House adviser said Canada becoming the 51st state remains a consideration following a meeting with premiers where the Canadian leaders were cautioned to take U.S. President Donald Trump at his word.
The last-minute meeting with James Blair, deputy chief of staff, and Sergio Gor, director of the presidential personnel office, provided an opportunity for premiers to make Canada's case directly to top Trump aides.
"They committed to share our message of willingness to engage and to work on key areas of agreement, and that Canada would never be the 51st state," said British Columbia Premier David Eby as he left the White House on Wednesday.
Blair posted on social media that the meeting with the premiers was pleasant. But, he said, "we never agreed that Canada would not be the 51st state. We only agreed to share Premier Eby's comments."
"Further, we said the best way to understand President Trump's position is to take what he says at face value," Blair said
All 13 premiers descended on the United States capital this week for the first time ever. The diplomatic push reflects rising alarm among Canadians about Trump's unpredictable tariff strategy.
Ontario Premier Doug Ford said Wednesday's meeting was "very constructive." Premiers were advised to continue conversations after key members are sworn in, including the president's nominee to run the Commerce Department, Howard Lutnick, and Trump's pick for top trade negotiator, Jamieson Greer.
Trump returned to the White House less than a month ago with a tariff agenda that could rapidly realign global trade. Canada has been unable to escape the geopolitical upheaval triggered by its closest neighbour and largest trading partner.
Trump signed an order Monday that would impose 25 per cent tariffs on all steel and aluminum imports, including imports from Canada, on March 12.
Those duties could come on top of across-the-board tariffs on all Canadian imports, with a lower 10 per cent levy on Canadian energy, which were delayed until at least March 4.
A White House official confirmed that if both rounds of tariffs are implemented, the duties would stack on top of each other. That would mean a tariff of up to 50 per cent on Canadian steel and aluminum exports to the U.S.
Provincial and territorial leaders remained optimistic Wednesday that diplomatic efforts can still push Trump away from implementing duties.
Manitoba Premier Wab Kinew said it's not a time to be issuing ultimatums and instead called the premiers' mission in Washington a "charm offensive."
The Canadian Press
CAPTION:

CREDIT: Kelly Geraldine Malone The Canadian Press

Document 143 of 500

Ontario Greens pledge to build two million homes and cut taxes: Liberal Crombie promises pay equity for health-care workers

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/ontario-greens-pledge-build-two-million-homes-cut/docview/3166083321/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Ontario+Greens+pledge+to+build+two+million+homes+and+cut+taxes%3A+Liberal+Crombie+promises+pay+equity+for+health-care+workers&amp;title=The+Spectator&amp;issn=11899417&amp;date=2025-02-13&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.9&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Spectator&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: The Green Party of Ontario will build two million homes in a decade, cut taxes for low- and middle-income earners and create a "foodbelt" to protect farmland if it wins the election, leader Mike Schreiner pledged Wednesday as he released the party's platform.
Liberal Leader Bonnie Crombie, meanwhile, promised to end hospital hallway health care in part by paying nurses and personal support workers more and ensuring wage parity across the entire system.
Progressive Conservative Leader Doug Ford remains in Washington, D.C., in his capacity as Ontario premier as he attempts to head off looming U.S. tariffs, while NDP Leader Marit Stiles had no public events Wednesday.
The Greens are the first party to release a fully costed platform. The NDP and Liberals say they will release costed platforms soon while the Progressive Conservatives committed to releasing their plan in the coming days, though it wasn't clear if it will be costed.
Schreiner said he would legalize fourplexes across the province and mid-rise residential buildings six-to-11 storeys tall in larger cities.
He would also cut taxes for those earning less than $65,000 or households earning less than $100,000 and double the rates of both the Ontario Disability Support Program and Ontario Works.
"Our plan addresses the fairness that we need to build affordable homes and protect renters, to bring fairness back to our health care and education systems, to protect the prime farmland that feeds us and lower your energy bills and reduce climate pollution," Schreiner said at a campaign stop in Toronto.
Earlier Wednesday, Crombie said her party would address a long-standing issue in health care: pay parity.
"It shouldn't matter where that nurse or where that PSW is employed," Crombie said. "Whether they are in the community in a long-term-care facility or in the hospital, there should be parity in their wages."
The COVID-19 pandemic laid bare the problem of unequal pay among nurses and personal support workers.
The lack of pay parity played a major role in nursing home staffing shortages during the pandemic, Ontario's Long-Term Care COVID-19 Commission concluded.
"Financial disparity between sectors of the health-care system leads to the needless movement of staff who understandably are looking for greater and more stable income," wrote Justice Frank Marrocco, who led the commission.
The Canadian Press
CAPTION: Ontario Green Leader Mike Schreiner appears at a campaign event as the party launches its 2025 platform in Toronto Wednesday.Chris Young The Canadian Press

CREDIT: Liam Casey and Jordan Omstead The Canadian Press

Subject: Agricultural land; Pandemics; COVID-19

Document 144 of 500

Feb. 13: Fly the Maple Leaf on Saturday, get people into shelters now and other letters to the editor

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/feb-13-fly-maple-leaf-on-saturday-get-people-into/docview/3166082385/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Feb.+13%3A+Fly+the+Maple+Leaf+on+Saturday%2C+get+people+into+shelters+now+and+other+letters+to+the+editor&amp;title=The+Hamilton+Spectator+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2025-02-13&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Hamilton+Spectator+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Fly the Maple Leaf this Saturday
This Saturday marks 60 years since the Maple Leaf was raised on Parliament Hill. Now more than ever, Canadians need to show pride in our country. We are facing the threat of devastating tariffs and hearing threats to our sovereignty from President Donald Trump. Let us show our pride in and the strength of our country. Fly our flag, our symbol of the true north, strong and free.
Margaret Tremblay, Dundas
Get people into shelters now
With bitterly cold temperatures forecast for Monday, this has got to be the week and weekend to move the people living in tents in Bayfront Park into the small shelters 500 metres away.
As a former combat engineer in the Canadian Army, the temporary community looks to me ready to go. Leaving aside the fact that my old unit, 5e Regiment du Genie de Combat, would've had this project completed in about 24 hours, it's imperative that in this situation perfect not be the enemy of good.
David Collier, Hamilton
City needs zero-based budgeting
Re: It's time to cap city spending and tax hikes, councillor says, Feb. 12
It's interesting how Hamilton council is suddenly feeling the pressure of a taxpayer revolt with the projected 5.7 per cent property tax increase. Coun. Matt Francis declared this council has a spending problem. Duh, really? Coun. Brad Clark suggested a cap of 2.5 per cent should be the target for next year. How about zero-based budgeting? No increase that isn't justified as a need to have, not as a want to have. Start taking a look at the out-of-control growth in employment numbers. As a senior on a fixed pension, I object!
Dennis Martin, Binbrook
Poilievre's been taking notes
It appears the leader of the opposition has once again been watching American news channels. He has picked up on Donald Trump's attempt to freeze funding related to foreign aid around the world. Unfortunately Pierre Poilievre seems to be hearing only those voices from the right that scream "look how much money we can save" by ignoring the plight of countries less privileged then ours. Obviously he does not hear, or perhaps hears and chooses to avoid, those voices that talk about the concept of using "soft power" in those countries that benefit from foreign aid. Nor does he hear the voices that talk about fighting disease and starvation along with myriad of other humanitarian issues.
It is also interesting that he puts forward the idea that somehow funding between foreign aid and military spending create a binary choice, which it does not. He has created this binary choice and put it forward to the public as a cover for his desire to follow through with his Trump-lite agenda which is laid bare by this promise.
Don Courtnage, Ancaster
Help struggling Canadians first
I'm not a big fan of Pierre Poilievre but more and more I'm liking what I hear. We spent $15.5 billion in foreign aid in 2022-23! When we have people living on the street, people not able to buy food, hospitals are a mess and costs are soaring, we are giving money away that could be used here to help with these issues. If all were well with Canada and we had surplus I'm all for giving to others, but we need to tighten our belts and that includes the government.
Diana MacKenzie, Hamilton
Consider donating your 'rebate'
I received my $200 cheque from Doug Ford in the mail. I have chosen to donate the $200 to Interval House for Victoria and Friends, Shelter for Women. I hope others who have received a cheque that is not necessary for their personal needs will consider donating the money to a worthy cause.
Hinda Levine, Hamilton
Where's my 'good government?'
How many times do voters have to be told that Premier Doug Ford has failed Ontario financially as well as not making the health system, the education system, the environment crisis and the housing and homeless crises a priority? He offers no solutions. Instead he spends millions of taxpayers' dollars to allow alcohol to be sold in corner stores. His focus has been on building a new highway and now is sending taxpayers a $200 cheque, thinking this will really help us to deal with our bills. The Canadian Constitution reads that we have a right to good government. I have yet to find any necessary implementation of action that is the result of good governing. The facts prove otherwise.
Norma Ducau, Hamilton
Hospital CEOs should speak out
Hospital CEOs have the most secretive and shielded jobs in this province. I bet very few people if any could even name one in Hamilton. When it comes to health care in Ontario we've heard from just about everyone, from people on the street to unions to politicians to media and we all know who everyone is blaming. You would think it would be standard practice to hear from the people who earn those big paycheques and run these hospitals to find out what they're doing to make sure money is spent wisely and what can be done to make things better. What exactly is their job? Someone please tell us.
Red Taylor, Hamilton
Snowbirds should change course
Dear Snowbirds, the time is now to dump your properties in the U.S. It's not safe for Canadians there. We have always known that with their lax gun laws, but the warm weather made us forget. Take your money to Mexico where the country is happy to have you and the president doesn't want to take over your country. Or a Caribbean destination. Anywhere but the U.S. Sorry Elvis, I wont be getting to Graceland this year.
Robin Magder, Dundas
Buying less should be the focus
Re: Patriotic shopping is good, but this is better, Feb. 8
Paul Berton makes several important points in his commentary. One of the key points in my view, is that we shop too much. Ultimately what we need to remember is that shopping and buying less (as well as buying carefully regardless of the country of origin of the product) is what will save the planet. And that should be our focus now: to tread carefully and minimally on this planet that is our forever home, the home for all of us and needs to be lived on in a balanced way. Hapless consumerism and always wanting more is not the way to go. (Read more on careful shopping in Paul Berton's very interesting book on this topic, "Shopomania." I just got it from the library.)
Renate Manthei, Hamilton
An alternative to tariffs
I understand it when tariffs are applied to product being dumped into the U.S. at well below market prices, but across the board tariffs are just the work of a bully flexing his muscles. Maybe it is time that U.S. government officials looked at the reasons out of country product is readily purchased by American companies. Maybe it is their quality or their competitiveness within that particular industry or a combination of both. Once they understand this, they should then invest in improvements within that industry.
Brian McEwan, Hamilton
Cancel U.S. fighter jet contract
Now that we know Donald Trump's tariffs have nothing to do with border security but his wish to acquire us by punishing us economically, it would be in our best interest to strategically target specific American companies rather than general products. A cancellation of our contract with Boeing to purchase 88 F35 fighter jets for $74 billion would definitely resonate with our southern neighbours. I'm also with Jagmeet Singh to target Elon Musk with 100 per cent tariffs on his Teslas.
Tom Winning, Hamilton
Gaza seizure will start new war
How insane must things get before people rise up en masse to decry the reckless stated ambitions coming out of Donald Trump's mouth? There is no restraint, no accountability, seemingly no consideration for the consequences of his words. He has zero respect for Canada's sovereignty, nor Panama's, nor Greenland's and now the Gaza Strip. I can't think of a better way to restart war in the Middle East than to attempt the theft of this territory for development and his ultimate ownership of it. He has coerced Israel into playing along and will attempt to strong-arm the Arab nations to do the same. That likely won't go too well. Anti-Americanism would reach a fevered pitch and, voilà, war! Vladimir Putin and Xi Jinping must be licking their chops.
Chris Carroll, Hamilton
Happy to re-draw the border
I agree we should re-draw the border. Canada will happily welcome New York, Maine, Connecticut, Vermont, Wyoming, Montana, Michigan, North Dakota, Minnesota, Wisconsin, Washington or even California (we hear you are not too happy with what's going on there) to join our wonderful country. The people there would be ecstatic to become part of our beautiful country. There would be lots of amazing benefits to them. So yes, let's re-draw that line further south.
Erin Marof, Hamilton
A one-word response for Trump
If President Donald Trump continues his rant to make Canada his 51st state, our government should issue a single one-word response to Trump. This response comes from an American general during the Second World War.
During the Battle of the Bulge in December 1944, the American 101st Airborne Division defending Bastogne Belgium was soon surrounded by superior German forces. When asked to surrender to German forces or be destroyed, the American commander, Gen. Anthony McAuliffe, issued a one-word response to the German commander, "Nuts."
It took the Germans some time to figure out what his response meant.
Trump would have no problem figuring out our Canadian response of being the 51st state, "Nuts."
Mike LoSchiavo, Stoney Creek
MP's literature disappointing
On Jan. 2, I wrote to my local MP, Leslyn Lewis, to inquire about the CPC's policies on, climate change, affordable housing, unaffordable food prices, energy costs, pharmacare and dental care. To date, I have received no response. However, upon retrieving my mail, I did receive a mail out from my MP stating, "The Liberal government is trying to ban natural health products." Stunned, I did some research. What I found was, that following mislabelling, adverse reactions and deaths from natural products, there was published in 2021, Report 2 of the Commissioner of the Environment and Sustainable Development. Following this report, there was in fact, legislation created to accurately label natural health products to make them safer for consumers. This bill is prudent and aims to protect consumers, something I think most of us favour. Not Ms. Lewis though. When not promoting conspiracy theories and while opposing public safety, you would think she could provide details of her party's policies to her constituents. But no. In light of all of the serious issues impacting ordinary Canadians, the threats to our economy, our sovereignty and our general well-being, her priority is to oppose the labelling of natural health products. What a commentary on the state of our politics.
Gary Beemer, Caledonia
CAPTION: I can't think of a better way to restart war in the Middle East than to attempt the theft of Gaza for development by the United States, a reader says.Canadians are being asked to fly the flag this Saturday to mark its 60th birthday and to send a patriotic message south of the border.
