Document 145 of 500

'This brought them to tears': Province OKs Haldimand-Norfolk's first hospice

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/this-brought-them-tears-province-oks-haldimand/docview/3166082187/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=%27This+brought+them+to+tears%27%3A+Province+OKs+Haldimand-Norfolk%27s+first+hospice&amp;title=The+Hamilton+Spectator+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2025-02-13&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Hamilton+Spectator+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: News that the province will fund four hospice beds in Haldimand-Norfolk was enough to make some members of the local hospice committee cry.
"Everybody is extremely excited and thrilled that we've been allocated these beds," Norfolk Haldimand Community Hospice executive director Andrea Binkle told The Spectator.
"Some of our founding members, this actually brought them to tears. Because this has been a dream for a very long time and to be actually supported to make this happen is just unbelievable."
The citizens' committee has been working since 2019 to build Haldimand-Norfolk's first brick-and-mortar hospice, while also offering free bereavement counselling and support services out of a satellite office in Port Dover.
The vision is a 16,000-square-foot facility in a rural setting that features 10 hospice beds, plus programming space and rooms for caregivers to sleep over.
Responding to a Health Ministry call for proposals for new hospice beds, the committee applied for operating funds for six beds in October. The ministry approved four beds just before the provincial election was called, but Binkle said her group was assured the funding does not hinge on the Ford government getting re-elected.
No money will flow until a new hospice is built, at which point the ministry will provide annual funding to staff and operate the beds.
The hospice will be designed to hold 10 palliative-care beds and the committee will apply for more funding when next able, Binkle said. "We'll start out with the four beds and we might repurpose the other rooms, working with our health-care partners to see what other needs are within the community that these beds could be used for," she said.
The effort to provide "high-quality, compassionate end-of-life care close to home" is backed by both municipalities and MPP Bobbi Ann Brady, in recognition of Haldimand-Norfolk being the only area along the London-Hamilton-Niagara corridor without a hospice.
Residents in need of palliative care currently end up in surrounding communities like Brantford, Hamilton, Niagara and Woodstock.
After years of searching for a suitable future home, the hospice committee was overjoyed when Jarvis businessperson Alan "Willy" Williamson donated a portion of the family farm in memory of his father, Drew Williamson.
Prior to his death in 2023, Williamson - who founded a farm-equipment business and was a longtime Lions Club member - had discussed donating property to the hospice. This led to the gift of 10 acres centrally located between the two rural counties.
Now the committee is busy getting the ownership transferred, working on land severance and rezoning applications and sorting out road access.
The hospice will be designed to connect residents with nature, with pathways through the forested property made wide enough to fit a bed.
Binkle said it will make a big difference for families and caregivers to have their loved ones receive specialized end-of-life care in a "peaceful, supportive environment."
Construction of the new hospice is expected to cost around $14 million. Anyone interested in donating toward the build can visit norfolkhaldimandhospice.ca for more details.
Binkle said the provincial approval was a big morale boost for the hospice committee. "It is public acknowledgment that the government is behind this hospice and has confidence in this hospice to deliver on caring for people in end of life," she said.
"That's what we're here to do and we're happy that the government is willing to work with us on making that happen."
J.P. Antonacci's reporting is funded by the Canadian government through its Local Journalism Initiative. jpantonacci@thespec.com.
CAPTION: Andrea Binkle, an experienced health-care executive and consultant with a background in palliative care, is executive director of the Norfolk Haldimand Community Hospice.From left, Ontario Trillium Foundation (OTF) volunteer Keith Robicheau, Haldimand County Mayor Shelley Ann Bentley, MPP Bobbi Ann Brady, Norfolk County Deputy Mayor Doug Brunton and Haldimand-Norfolk hospice executive director Andrea Binkle.The OTF recently awarded a $125,000 grant to support free grief and bereavement programming for residents, while Ontario's Health Ministry approved funds for four hospice beds once the facility is built.Drew Williamson on his family farm in Jarvis. Williamson co-founded a successful farm-equipment business known as the D & W Group (Doughty & Williamson). Prior to his death in 2023, hediscussed plans to donate part of the family's property for the Norfolk Haldimand Community Hospice. His son, Alan "Willy" Williamson has fulfilled the family's wish by agreeing to sever and donate part of the farm for the hospice.

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/province-oks-haldimand-norfolks-first-hospice/docview/3166082170/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Province+OKs+Haldimand-+Norfolk%27s+first+hospice%3A+Health+ministry+approves+four+palliative+care+beds+for+facility&amp;title=The+Spectator&amp;issn=11899417&amp;date=2025-02-13&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.5&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Spectator&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Document 147 of 500

Letters

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/letters/docview/3166080947/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=unknown&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Letters&amp;title=The+Spectator&amp;issn=11899417&amp;date=2025-02-13&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.13&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Spectator&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Get people into shelters now
With bitterly cold temperatures forecast for Monday, this has got to be the week and weekend to move the people living in tents in Bayfront Park into the small shelters 500 metres away.
As a former combat engineer in the Canadian Army, the temporary community looks to me ready to go. Leaving aside the fact that my old unit, 5e Regiment du Genie de Combat, would've had this project completed in about 24 hours, it's imperative that in this situation perfect not be the enemy of good.
David Collier, Hamilton
City needs zero-based budgeting
Re: It's time to cap city spending and tax hikes, councillor says, Feb. 12
It's interesting how Hamilton council is suddenly feeling the pressure of a taxpayer revolt with the projected 5.7 per cent property tax increase. Coun. Matt Francis declared this council has a spending problem. Duh, really? Coun. Brad Clark suggested a cap of 2.5 per cent should be the target for next year. How about zero-based budgeting? No increase that isn't justified as a need to have, not as a want to have. Start taking a look at the out-of-control growth in employment numbers. As a senior on a fixed pension, I object!
Dennis Martin, Binbrook
Poilievre's been taking notes
It appears the leader of the opposition has once again been watching American news channels. He has picked up on Donald Trump's attempt to freeze funding related to foreign aid around the world. Unfortunately Pierre Poilievre seems to be hearing only those voices from the right that scream "look how much money we can save" by ignoring the plight of countries less privileged then ours. Obviously he does not hear, or perhaps hears and chooses to avoid, those voices that talk about the concept of using "soft power" in those countries that benefit from foreign aid. Nor does he hear the voices that talk about fighting disease and starvation along with myriad of other humanitarian issues.
It is also interesting that he puts forward the idea that somehow funding between foreign aid and military spending create a binary choice, which it does not. He has created this binary choice and put it forward to the public as a cover for his desire to follow through with his Trump-lite agenda which is laid bare by this promise.
Don Courtnage, Ancaster
Help struggling Canadians first
I'm not a big fan of Pierre Poilievre but more and more I'm liking what I hear. We spent $15.5 billion in foreign aid in 2022-23! When we have people living on the street, people not able to buy food, hospitals are a mess and costs are soaring, we are giving money away that could be used here to help with these issues. If all were well with Canada and we had surplus I'm all for giving to others, but we need to tighten our belts and that includes the government.
Diana MacKenzie, Hamilton
Donate your
'rebate' cheque
I received my $200 cheque from Doug Ford in the mail. I have chosen to donate the $200 to Interval House for Victoria and Friends, Shelter for Women. I hope others who have received a cheque that is not necessary for their personal needs will consider donating the money to a worthy cause.
Hinda Levine, Hamilton
Where's my 'good government?'
How many times do voters have to be told that Premier Doug Ford has failed Ontario financially as well as not making the health system, the education system, the environment crisis and the housing and homeless crises a priority? He offers no solutions.
Instead he spends millions of taxpayers' dollars to allow alcohol to be sold in corner stores. His focus has been on building a new highway and now is sending taxpayers a $200 cheque, thinking this will really help us to deal with our bills.
The Canadian Constitution reads that we have a right to good government. I have yet to find any necessary implementation of action that is the result of good governing. The facts prove otherwise.
Norma Ducau, Hamilton
Fly the Maple Leaf this Saturday
This Saturday marks 60 years since the Maple Leaf was raised on Parliament Hill. Now more than ever, Canadians need to show pride in our country. We are facing the threat of devastating tariffs and hearing threats to our sovereignty from President Donald Trump.
Let us show our pride in and the strength of our country. Fly our flag, our symbol of the true north, strong and free.
Margaret Tremblay, Dundas
Snowbirds should change course
Dear Snowbirds, the time is now to dump your properties in the U.S. It's not safe for Canadians there. We have always known that with their lax gun laws, but the warm weather made us forget. Take your money to Mexico where the country is happy to have you and the president doesn't want to take over your country. Or a Caribbean destination. Anywhere but the U.S. Sorry Elvis, I wont be getting to Graceland this year.
Robin Magder, Dundas
Hospital CEOs should speak out
Hospital CEOs have the most secretive and shielded jobs in this province. I bet very few people if any could even name one in Hamilton. When it comes to health care in Ontario we've heard from just about everyone, from people on the street to unions to politicians to media and we all know who everyone is blaming. You would think it would be standard practice to hear from the people who earn those big paycheques and run these hospitals to find out what they're doing to make sure money is spent wisely and what can be done to make things better. What exactly is their job? Someone please tell us.
Red Taylor, Hamilton
Buying less should be focus
Re: Patriotic shopping is good, but this is better, Feb. 8
Paul Berton makes several important points in his commentary. One of the key points in my view, is that we shop too much.
Ultimately what we need to remember is that shopping and buying less (as well as buying carefully regardless of the country of origin of the product) is what will save the planet. And that should be our focus now: to tread carefully and minimally on this planet that is our forever home, the home for all of us and needs to be lived on in a balanced way. Hapless consumerism and always wanting more is not the way to go. (Read more on careful shopping in Paul Berton's very interesting book on this topic, "Shopomania." I just got it from the library.)
Renate Manthei, Hamilton
An alternative
to tariffs
I understand it when tariffs are applied to product being dumped into the U.S. at well below market prices, but across the board tariffs are just the work of a bully flexing his muscles. Maybe it is time that U.S. government officials looked at the reasons out of country product is readily purchased by American companies. Maybe it is their quality or their competitiveness within that particular industry or a combination of both.
Once they understand this, they should then invest in improvements within that industry.
Brian McEwan, Hamilton
Gaza seizure
will start new war
How insane must things get before people rise up en masse to decry the reckless stated ambitions coming out of Donald Trump's mouth? There is no restraint, no accountability, seemingly no consideration for the consequences of his words. He has zero respect for Canada's sovereignty, nor Panama's, nor Greenland's and now the Gaza Strip.
I can't think of a better way to restart war in the Middle East than to attempt the theft of this territory for development and his ultimate ownership of it. He has coerced Israel into playing along and will attempt to strong-arm the Arab nations to do the same. That likely won't go too well.
Anti-Americanism would reach a fevered pitch and, voilà, war! Vladimir Putin and Xi Jinping must be licking their chops.
Chris Carroll, Hamilton
CAPTION: Canadians are being asked to fly the flag this Saturday to mark its 60th birthday and to send a patriotic message south of the border.John Rennison The Hamilton Spectator file photo

Document 148 of 500

Voters should turn out in droves on Feb. 27

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/voters-should-turn-out-droves-on-feb-27/docview/3166080880/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=unknown&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Voters+should+turn+out+in+droves+on+Feb.+27&amp;title=The+Spectator&amp;issn=11899417&amp;date=2025-02-13&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.13&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Spectator&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: It's frightening that the U.S, a (former) strong democracy has sunk to an oligarchy, headed by two egotists whose ambitions and egos are sure to collide at some point. Then what?
Closer to home, we have a provincial story to watch, headed by another ego-driven leader and one-time Donald Trump fan, who called an unnecessary costly election when we're all feeling a pinch in our wallets. Because this snap election is so soon, this is my last opportunity to address it.
Doug Ford just gave us all $200 (adding to the highest deficit provincial Tories have run in years). Aren't Tories traditionally the watchdogs of the public purse? I guess Ford missed Finance 101. And Health Care 101. Does anyone think, in this uncertain economy, that this deficit will shrink? What ever happened to mindful PCs? Obviously he doesn't care about voters, calling an election in the dead of winter.
We should turn out in droves for ABF (Anyone but Ford). We can protest his nepotism and U-turns, not to mention cronyism with developers and his disdain for elected councils. Not only did he break the contract with LCBO, costing $612 million according to the Financial Accountability Office, he's now ripped up another contract, with Elon Musk's Starlink; cost unknown. He's campaigning on his mirage of tunnelling under the 401, again uncosted, but estimated at many billions! This tunnel would have to be very high and wide. Visualize subterranean collisions, and the intricacies and costs of extrications, especially in fiery accidents. On our dime!
We have excellent choices in the Burlington riding.
I bet few even know the name of the PC incumbent because she's been mostly invisible, except for photo-ops. The two flyers I received during Natalie Pierre's term didn't even mention Ford's name.
Carrying the Liberal torch is Andrea Grebenc, a former Halton school board chair who led the board through the tough pandemic. Highly respected locally, she even earned a cybersecurity designation.
A bright young chemical engineer, Megan Beauchemin, is the NDP candidate.
As a data and digital organizer for an environmental organization, Kyle Hutton could be a factor for the Green Party, with climate change so key today.
Excellent candidates all.
It was nice to learn that local singer/songwriter Sarah Harmer will receive a 2025 humanitarian award at the Juno Awards spring ceremony for her environmental work. She has been vocal on protecting Mount Nemo and the Niagara Escarpment (a UNESCO Biosphere Reserve), the habitat of the Jefferson salamander, and in efforts to reform gravel mining. This will be the second time she worked to prevent expansion of the Nelson Quarry. Its previous expansion proposal was denied.
Now our council and residents are opposing a second bid. How many times do residents and councils have to fight the same costly fights? There is another long very costly hearing looming, expected to last several weeks. When I was a councillor in the 1970s and '80s fielding complaints about blasting, Nelson's line was always, "We'll be mined out in a couple of years."
The escarpment commission has consistently opposed mining on the Mount Nemo plateau. Doug Ford, are you listening?
This week Burlington committees had light agendas, but the March sessions look heavy. There was one planning application approved, replacing two homes at 2169 and 2175 Ghent Ave. with two three-storey rental apartment buildings of six units each.
This winter has seemed so long and cold. I'm looking forward to signs of spring, like the annual closing of King Road to protect Jefferson salamanders during their March-April crossings of the road to their breeding ponds, and the annual nesting of Hamilton's peregrine falcons on King Street's Sheraton Hotel ledge.
Freelance columnist
Joan Little is a former
Burlington alderperson
and Halton councillor. Reach her at specjoan@
cogeco.ca
CAPTION:

CREDIT: Joan Little OPINION Freelance columnist Joan Little is a former Burlington alderperson and Halton councillor. Reach her at specjoan@ cogeco.ca;

Subject: Voters; Elections

Document 149 of 500

We need party leaders to plug into electricity debate

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/we-need-party-leaders-plug-into-electricity/docview/3166080878/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=unknown&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=We+need+party+leaders+to+plug+into+electricity+debate&amp;title=The+Spectator&amp;issn=11899417&amp;date=2025-02-13&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.12&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Spectator&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Next Monday is the leaders' debate at the CBC.
It will be consequential because of the many problems we face today.
In every single provincial election since 2003, the three major parties have declined to debate the hydro rate problem and its causes.
This issue affects all others because of Doug Ford's spending on it.
First, some history. Shortly after being elected, then-premier Mike Harris went fishing in the Northwest Territories with Kenneth Lay, chair of Enron. Harris then had Enron and a who's who of the investment community design Ontario's electricity market which opened in June 2002.
Despite an expensive ad campaign promising "lower rates" and promising "nothing will go wrong," by 2007, rates had doubled, by 2010, rates tripled and then by the time Ford is elected in 2018, rates had quadrupled.
Since deregulation was implemented in 2002, every single government has had to subsidize rates showing how flawed hydro deregulation is.
Ford has taken hydro subsidies to new heights. Ford loves to say he keeps all his promises. In the 2018 election, he promised to lower hydro rates by 12 per cent.
Ford has not lowered rates by even one per cent.
All Ford has done is hide and protect Harris' failed deregulation scheme with billions in subsidies.
According to the Financial Accountability Office, every year Ford has spent up to $7 billion in hydro subsidies and plans to spend $118 billion over the next 20 years, never dealing with the problem.
Ford is very vulnerable on this issue and has shown he is worried about public opinion on hydro rates.
In October 2020, Ford offered ratepayers one of the Tories favourite words: "choice."
They could choose "time of use" payments or they could choose tiered payments; so much for the first 750 or 1,000 kwh and then so much for the next tier.
Doing the math shows it is just a wash which works out to be exactly the same cost.
Then in April 2023 Ford introduces ultralow overnight rates 2.4 cents a kwh. from 11 p.m. to 7 a.m.
On Aug. 28, Ford announced the biggest competitive expansion of private power in Ontario history.
In his announcement, he made the exact same promise as Harris and used the word "affordable" nine times.
Ford calling nuclear power "clean and affordable" is like calling lasagna and cheesecake diet foods. History shows the opposite.
The public was so angry at the Liberals in the last two elections for selling Hydro One, they were sent to the political wilderness.
To date, Ford has spent $49 billion on hydro subsidies. That's enough to buy back Hydro One five times over.
The Liberals would be well advised to admit they made a mistake and promise to buy back Hydro One.
A number of countries around the world have recognized the fact that the transition to green energy and electric vehicles will never happen under a for-profit system and have created public green power companies.
We need to look seriously at creating a green public power company ourselves. We have 94 years of proof that public power works.
Currently in Ontario, electricity is a great business to be in. Ford guarantees the profits and taxpayers get the bill.
We cannot let another election pass without our leaders debating this critical problem, the affordability and climate crisis, demand action on this issue.
Paul Kahnert a writer
on social justice issues.
See more at muckrack.com/paul-kahnert/articles.
CAPTION:

CREDIT: Paul Kahnert Paul Kahnert a writer on social justice issues. See more at muckrack.com/paul-kahnert/articles.;

Document 150 of 500

Canada's premiers invited to White House to meet 'senior advisers' to Donald Trump

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/canadas-premiers-invited-white-house-meet-senior/docview/3165834498/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Canada%27s+premiers+invited+to+White+House+to+meet+%27senior+advisers%27+to+Donald+Trump&amp;title=The+Hamilton+Spectator+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2025-02-12&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Hamilton+Spectator+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: WASHINGTON-Canada is on a full-court diplomatic press against U.S. President Donald Trump's tariffs.
For the first time in history all 13 provincial and territorial leaders are in the American capital at the same time to warn U.S. lawmakers that Trump's levies will be bad for the economies of both nations.
The subnational leaders on Tuesday secured an afternoon meeting at the White House with "senior advisers" to Trump.
It was not immediately clear who the premiers would see, but they were not expected to meet the president.
Doug Ford denies his Washington trip is a taxpayer-funded campaign stop
Federal Finance Minister Dominic LeBlanc, here to meet with Howard Lutnick, the president's commerce secretary, and Kevin Hassett, Trump's top economic adviser, insisted diplomacy is working.
"(Lutnick) indicated that this would be a good week to continue our conversation around border security in the fight against fentanyl," LeBlanc said
"We think we've made a lot of progress on that issue. We have absolutely no daylight between ourselves and the American administration in terms of fighting against fentanyl, dealing with precursor chemicals that come from China, transnational organized crime, border integrity," said LeBlanc.
"So I'm not pessimistic. I am optimistic by nature," he said after a breakfast meeting with the premiers.
On a snowy day in Washington, LeBlanc poured cold water on the idea of pre-emptive tariffs to counter Trump's threatened 25 per cent levies on all Canadian imports to the U.S.
"No, we're not looking to go ahead of the United States in some kind of tariffs. We don't think tariffs are the way to proceed," he stressed.
But some of premiers here as part of the Council of the Federation lobbying mission say it is necessary to be ready to retaliate given Trump's 25 per cent tariffs on steel and aluminum that could take effect next month.
Quebec Premier François Legault said "retaliation must be done if and only if there are tariffs that are really charged."
"So far, it won't be done before March 4. But we have to prepare ourselves. We already have an enhanced list of counter tariffs, I think you should consider also putting exportation tariff on products like aluminum, where they really need us," said Legault.
Alberta Premier Danielle Smith appealed for calm.
"I think diplomacy is working. The fact that we got a 30-day reprieve was because we agreed to work on the cross-border problem of fentanyl," noted Smith.
"I was pleased to see the federal government announce the fentanyl czar, and my administration has already been in touch with him," she said.
"What I've seen from this president is that he is prepared to make a deal. We've made a deal on that. I think we can make a deal on steel and aluminum by making very much the same argument that I'm making on energy and critical minerals," added the Albertan.
"We've won the argument that those raw materials are essential to American success. It comes in, it makes value-added products, it creates good American jobs, it creates good American products that we buy back. Same thing for aluminum. They just do not make enough for their defence industry, their auto industry and their critical needs," she said.
"So I think aluminum can make the very same argument and on steel, we buy as much steel product from them as they buy."
Ontario's Doug Ford, here on the first of two diplomatic trips during the Feb. 27 provincial election, said the premiers have to show the Maple Leaf flag.
"What success looks like is zero tariffs. We're their largest trading partner and their number one customer and I'm not too sure that they fully understand the impact on both countries," said Ford, the Progressive Conservative leader whose trip has been criticized by Liberal Leader Bonnie Crombie and NDP Leader Marit Stiles.
"Interest rates will go up. Plants will be closed on both sides of the border. And the more we communicate, the better it is. We're down here to listen to our U.S. colleagues and we're down here to communicate with the U.S. colleagues and business people on both sides of the border, and we have to make sure that we communicate how important it is to be stronger together," he said.
Kirsten Hillman, Canada's ambassador to the U.S., said it useful to have so many Canadian political leaders in D.C. at once.
"It's good that the premiers are all here. This is the first time in history that all 13 provincial and territorial leaders have been in Washington at the same time," said Hillman.
"Our job is to demonstrate in as much granularity as possible - across all regions of this country - what the (Canadian) relationship with the United States means for Americans," she said.
"So what benefits they get from it ... in order ... to change the benefit calculation of the White House and the president with regards to the imposition of those tariffs."
CAPTION: Doug Ford speaks to reporters, accompanied by other Council of the Federation members, at the Mayflower Hotel in Washington on Wednesday.

Letters

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/letters/docview/3165677692/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=unknown&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Letters&amp;title=The+Spectator&amp;issn=11899417&amp;date=2025-02-12&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.11&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Spectator&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: No change if you don't vote
Since the election in 2022, Ontario's debt has soared by more than $86 billion. Recently, we've watched billions wasted on the Ontario Place redevelopment fiasco, the Greenbelt land grab and the contract-cancelling expansion of beer and wine to convenience stores. Meanwhile, millions have been cut from legal aid and health care is underfunded by billions. Two and one-half million Ontarians don't have a family doctor. Highway 413 is being forced through to benefit developers and urban sprawl, not you. And there's an ongoing RCMP criminal investigation about land rezoning. Is that the kind of government and leadership that Ontarians deserve?
In the last provincial election in 2022, only 44 per cent of Ontarians showed up to vote. That was the lowest voter turnout in Ontario history. It also means that only 18 per cent of Ontarians voted for the Progressive Conservatives and gave them a majority. Obviously, the PC party doesn't want you think about any of that. It seems they prefer that you not vote at all. When politicians show you who they are, believe them. Vote on Feb. 27!
James Newman, Hamilton
It's your responsibility to vote
Voters in Ontario are asked to make a very critical decision on which party they want to govern Ontario. Yet at least 40 per cent will not vote, even though they may be critical of Doug Ford and the Progressive Conservatives. Those who don't vote are undermining democracy. It is a privilege and a responsibility to vote.
Those who do vote need to make an educated decision based on the current reality. Ontario voters cannot afford to give the Progressive Conservatives a majority.
Those thousands of people who wait 10 to 20 hours in an emergency room cannot vote for Ford. Those who have students cannot vote for Ford. Those who now understand that our deficit under Ford is greater than the Liberals cannot vote for Ford. Those who have seen Ford cancel millions of dollars for environmental programs cannot vote for Ford. Those millions who do not have a doctor cannot vote for Ford.
Bottom line, every voter needs to vote based on the facts that face us.
Roman Caruk, Hamilton
Don't blame mortgage rates
Re: Hamilton housing market sees 'cautious start' to 2025, Feb. 6
I just read the article in the Spec about the "cautious start" to the housing market to start 2025. One of the reasons given is the lack of improvement on high mortgage rates despite the Bank of Canada's series of lowering interest rates. This actually made me laugh out loud. We bought our first home in 1977 with an 11.75 per cent interest rate. We renewed the mortgage five years later at 19.75 per cent. And now they're calling a five per cent interest rate high. I don't think most people actually know what a "high" interest rate is.
Rusty Escott, Dunnville
Pedestrians need to follow rules
Driving along Hunter Street, I had a green light while passing the TH&B Station. A group was leaving the station and were starting to cross Hunter, against the light. I beeped my horn and the first person halted and didn't look up. When I looked in my rear-view mirror, the group had continued to cross the road against the light. A truck behind me had managed to stop in order to not run over anyone. Most pedestrians are not aware that a flashing red hand or number in a pedestrian signal means do not start to cross the road. I see parents hurrying their children to cross when the signal is saying stop. Why are only drivers expected to follow the rules of the road?
Peter Gauthier, Hamilton
The world needs more Canada
Re: The real play behind Karina Gould's Liberal
leadership bid, Feb. 10
Jaime Watt's opinion piece in Monday's Spectator is a wonderful affirmation of the positive impact Canada can and does make on our world and, in our immediate circumstances, such a contrast to the dangerous behaviour and actions of Donald Trump, especially on our continent.
Can you imagine an executive of a conservative think tank in the U.S. praising the attributes of a candidate for the leadership of the federal Liberal party, namely Karina Gould? That is a clear example of why Canada should never be absorbed into the mess that is the United States. Canadians can support the good qualities of a candidate from any of our political parties and vote accordingly, not blinded by some idiotic assumption that political affiliation is a lifelong and blind commitment.
There are lots of reasons for Canada never sinking to joining or being absorbed by the destructive morass that is currently the United States of America. Watt's column reinforces the world's need for the best of Canada's thinking and influence, unencumbered by the current destructiveness of its undemocratic and arrogant neighbour under its current president.
Bill Millar, Stoney Creek

Premiers make diplomatic push after new tariff threat: 'Let's stick together and please get the message' to Trump, Ford says

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/premiers-make-diplomatic-push-after-new-tariff/docview/3165674354/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Premiers+make+diplomatic+push+after+new+tariff+threat%3A+%27Let%27s+stick+together+and+please+get+the+message%27+to+Trump%2C+Ford+says&amp;title=The+Spectator&amp;issn=11899417&amp;date=2025-02-12&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.7&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Spectator&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: U.S. President Donald Trump's planned 25 per cent tariffs on steel and aluminum imports would be stacked on top of other levies on Canadian goods, says a White House official who confirmed the plan Tuesday on background.
The news comes as Prime Minister Justin Trudeau cautioned U.S. Vice-President JD Vance against Trump's promised steel and aluminum levies, while Canadian premiers picked up the Team Canada mantle in Washington to push against Trump's tariff threats.
Trudeau and Vance are in Paris for a global summit on artificial intelligence. A senior government official said Trudeau spoke with the vice-president about the impact steel tariffs would have in Ohio, which Vance represented previously in the U.S. Senate.
The president signed executive orders Monday to impose 25 per cent tariffs on all steel and aluminum imports into the United States, including Canadian products, starting March 12.
Trump previously threatened 25 per cent across-the-board tariffs on Canadian imports, with a lower 10 per cent levy on Canadian energy, and says those tariffs could still proceed in early March.
Trudeau called the tariffs "unacceptable" Tuesday and said he was working with international partners to deliver a "firm and clear" response.
Tuesday's news from a White House official that these two classes of tariff would stack on top of each other would mean a total 50 per cent tariff on Canadian steel and aluminum, if those planned measures proceed.
On Monday, federal Finance Minister Dominic LeBlanc spoke with Trump's newly confirmed Treasury Secretary Scott Bessent. The "conversation focused on our common objective - building a strong North American economy that benefits citizens and industries on both sides of our shared border," LeBlanc said in a social media post.
Monday's tariff threat marks another development in Trump's larger plans to reshape global trade and American foreign policy through tariffs.
Ontario Premier Doug Ford appealed directly to American business leaders in the United States capital Tuesday, asking them to reach out to Republican lawmakers and the president himself on Canada's behalf.
"Let's stick together and please get the message to President Trump - this is not a good idea for both countries," Ford told members of the U.S. Chamber of Commerce.
Ford said he was disappointed by the latest duties. While premiers will be talking with the federal government about retaliatory measures, Ford said it's the last thing anyone wants.
British Columbia Premier David Eby, who is also in the U.S. capital, has said Canadians are "way too reliant on the decisions of one person in the White House."
Despite months of diplomacy from all levels of Canada's government, it remains unclear what Trump wants from America's northern neighbour. His initial tariff threats were linked to border security and fentanyl, but he has since widened his complaints to include defence spending and trade deficits.
The Canadian Press
CAPTION: Ontario Premier Doug Ford speaks during an event with the U.S. Chamber of Commerce, Tuesday in Washington. Ford said Canada will retaliate with tariffs if forced.Mark Schiefelbein The Associated Press

Where do Brantford-Brant MPP contenders stand on amalgamation?: Voters need to 'know who we can depend on,' says Brant County mayor

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/where-do-brantford-brant-mpp-contenders-stand-on/docview/3165674307/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Where+do+Brantford-Brant+MPP+contenders+stand+on+amalgamation%3F%3A+Voters+need+to+%27know+who+we+can+depend+on%2C%27+says+Brant+County+mayor&amp;title=The+Spectator&amp;issn=11899417&amp;date=2025-02-12&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.4&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Spectator&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Whether they like it or not, would-be MPPs in Brantford-Brant are being pulled into a contentious local debate over amalgamation.
In January, Brantford Mayor Kevin Davis used strong-mayor powers to commission a study exploring the benefits of amalgamating with the County of Brant - despite county Mayor David Bailey declaring the rural municipality is "unequivocally opposed" to becoming one with the city.
Now that voters are headed to the polls Feb. 27 for a snap provincial election, area residents are demanding to know where provincial candidates stand on the hot-button issue.
"The preservation of our community's autonomy is crucial," wrote one Brant County resident who started a petition last month to "halt the proposed amalgamation." Hundreds of people signed, requesting both the MP and MPP to "publicly declare their standpoints on the proposed amalgamation."
Some residents have also taken to social media to say they'd like Brant County to remain a separate municipality.
If they wanted to live in Brantford, they would have moved there, one resident wrote on Facebook. Others pointed to the challenges other municipalities - like nearby Haldimand and Norfolk - faced when amalgamating, the vast difference between the county's rural needs and the urban demands of the city, and Brantford's "misguided" priorities.
Bailey, the county's mayor, also voiced the need for provincial election candidates to give a "very clear" idea of where they stand on the issue, so voters "know who we can depend on," he wrote in a Facebook post in January.
The rural County of Brant completely surrounds the City of Brantford on all borders. But while they share some services like paramedics and have the same health-care system, as well as joint representation at the provincial and federal level, the two remain distinct municipalities.
The most recent Tory provincial government studied alternate governance models - including amalgamation - for a number of Ontario municipalities as far back as 2019, but has not forced any municipal marriages to date. It also backed off a high-profile promise to fully split up Peel Region in 2023.
Here's what provincial candidates in Brantford-Brant have to say about the prospect of amalgamation:

Liberal MPP candidate Ron Fox said he has heard a "loud and clear" message from residents.
His party will defer to municipal and regional councils as "the local leaders who know best" and will "never force" amalgamation on municipalities because "shotgun weddings just don't work," he told The Spectator.
He believes there used to be "great co-operation" between the two municipalities for social services, housing and infrastructure, but "it's gotten worse in the last few years, especially with all the downloading of costs from the province."
He said he plans to remove "the conditions that create competition" to have "two municipalities side by side, working together again."

Green candidate Karleigh Csordas also believes there is "room for improved efficiency" for shared services, but thinks any decision about amalgamation "must be based on clear, transparent consultation with residents to ensure their needs and priorities are fully considered," she told The Spectator.
One of the biggest concerns for both Brantford and Brant County is urban sprawl and associated greenfield development, she said.
"Expanding into farmland is expensive, inefficient and environmentally harmful," said Csordas, arguing both municipalities should focus on prioritizing infill development and sustainable planning.

NDP candidate Harvey Bischof told The Spectator he sees "no value" in pursuing an amalgamation at this time since it would require "enormous collaboration between two consenting parties."
He suggested in the future both municipalities could recognize the collective advantage of "a closer relationship" - but added that is "clearly not the case right now."
Bischof said he's ready to fight for issues of concern to the riding as a whole, including the "drastic need" for a new hospital facility, the push to hire primary care physicians, the "hugely problematic" lack of affordable housing, and support needs in schools.

Progressive Conservative incumbent Will Bouma's office told The Spectator he has "always believed that small, representative local governments are most effective when they work together to strengthen the communities they serve."
In the face of "unprecedented economic uncertainty," his party's local priorities include growing the economy, creating new jobs, protecting livelihoods and keeping Ontario "competitive."
Celeste Percy-Beauregard's reporting is funded by the
Canadian government through its Local Journalism Initiative. cpercybeauregard@torstar.ca.
CAPTION: Brantford-Brant 2025 Ontario election candidates: Harvey Bischof (NDP); Karleigh Csordas (Green); Will Bouma (Progressive Conservative) and Ron Fox (Liberal).

CREDIT: Celeste Percy-Beauregard Local Journalism Initiative Reporter Celeste Percy-Beauregard's reporting is funded the Canadian government through its Local Journalism Initiative. cpercybeauregard@torstar.ca.

Document 154 of 500

Leaders of Liberals, NDP promise to help young people

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/leaders-liberals-ndp-promise-help-young-people/docview/3165674137/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Leaders+of+Liberals%2C+NDP+promise+to+help+young+people&amp;title=The+Spectator&amp;issn=11899417&amp;date=2025-02-12&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.8&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Spectator&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Two Ontario party leaders laid out their plans on the campaign trail Tuesday to help young people deal with the affordability crisis.
Liberal Leader Bonnie Crombie said she would eliminate interest on student loans, create 40,000 paid internships and apprenticeships through tax credits to companies and make student residences more affordable.
"Ontario's Liberals have a plan to smash our unemployment rate in half," Crombie said at a campaign stop in Toronto.
"We're also going to ensure that you have a family doctor, and we're also going to promise you that you won't be saddled with student loans and student debt."
Crombie would extend the amount of time needed to repay Ontario Student Assistance Program loans and increase the income threshold when those repayments begin to $50,000.
She said she would also cap international student enrolment at 10 per cent at all Ontario colleges and universities.
NDP Leader Marit Stiles said her party would introduce rent control, turn student loans into grants and eliminate interest on student loans.
"We need to take the pressure off of students," Stiles said at a campaign stop in Kitchener.
Both Stiles and Crombie say they would keep post-secondary tuition frozen for domestic students and they would increase funding to post-secondary institutions.
Progressive Conservative Leader Doug Ford is in Washington, D.C., to meet with U.S. lawmakers and business leaders in his capacity as Ontario premier.Two Ontario party leaders laid out their plans to help young people deal with the affordability crisis, promising to reduce student debt and youth unemployment.
The pledges outlined by Liberal Leader Bonnie Crombie and NDP Leader Marit Stiles on the campaign trail Tuesday came as Progressive Conservative Leader Doug Ford joined a diplomatic push in Washington, D.C., in his capacity as Ontario premier.
Crombie said she would eliminate interest on student loans, create 40,000 paid internships and apprenticeships through tax credits to companies and make student residences more affordable.
"Ontario's Liberals have a plan to smash our unemployment rate in half," Crombie said at a stop in Toronto. "We're also going to ensure that you have a family doctor, and we're also going to promise you that you won't be saddled with student loans and student debt."
Crombie would extend the amount of time needed to repay Ontario Student Assistance Program loans and increase the income threshold when those repayments begin to $50,000.
She said she would also cap international student enrolment at 10 per cent at all Ontario colleges and universities.
"We've got to open up spaces for our own students, but also we have to fund our colleges and universities properly," Crombie said.
"Because right now, what's happening is they're relying on foreign students to pay the bills, and that is not sustainable," the Liberal leader added.
Stiles said her party would introduce rent control, turn student loans into grants and eliminate interest on student loans.
"We need to take the pressure off of students," Stiles said at a campaign stop in Kitchener.
The Canadian Press
CAPTION: Ontario Liberal Leader Bonnie Crombie promised she would eliminate interest on student loans at a campaign in Toronto on Tuesday.Chris Young The Canadian Press

CREDIT: Liam Casey The Canadian Press

Document 155 of 500

Canada must hit back hard against Trump's economic warfare

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/canada-must-hit-back-hard-against-trumps-economic/docview/3165674007/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Canada+must+hit+back+hard+against+Trump%27s+economic+warfare&amp;title=The+Hamilton+Spectator+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2025-02-12&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Hamilton+Spectator+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Donald Trump's threat of tariffs on Canadian imports looms over the economy like a guillotine.
If the blade drops, the country could be pushed into a deep recession, wiping out thousands of jobs and reversing decades of economic co-operation with the United States.
Even if Trump delays the move again, the message is clear: Canada is at the mercy of the president's whims. Whenever we displease him, the hammer may fall.
This is more than a routine trade dispute. Trump's threats amount to economic warfare, especially given his past statements about annexing Canada as a "51st state" or seizing control of our borders and resources.
There is no room for complacency. Canada must be prepared to fight back - not just with retaliatory tariffs that could do as much harm to Canadian consumers as they do to the U.S. economy, but with a broader strategy to reduce economic dependence on the U.S. and shield Canadian workers from the worst effects of Trump's trade aggression.
One approach is to target Trump's inner circle. Just as Canada imposed sanctions on Russian oligarchs after the invasion of Ukraine, similar measures could be applied to members of Trump's cabinet and family. Billionaires like Elon Musk, whose businesses - including Starlink, Tesla and X (formerly Twitter) - have interests in Canada, could be affected by government policy decisions. Progressive Conservative Leader Doug Ford briefly floated the idea of cancelling the province's contract with Starlink. Though he backed down, the concept remains viable as part of a broader effort to hold powerful business figures accountable.
Another measure would be freezing or seizing American corporate assets in Canada. It cannot be business as usual for U.S. companies operating here while their government actively undermines Canadian sovereignty. Repatriating strategic sectors - such as natural resources - by taking public control of private U.S. holdings would both weaken corporate America and reinforce Canada's ability to control its own economy.
Energy exports offer another point of leverage. The U.S. depends on Canadian oil, gas and electricity, which is why Trump has only threatened a 10 per cent tariff on the sector. The Canadian government could introduce its own 15 per cent export tax, driving up energy costs south of the border while generating more than $20 billion per year in revenue. Instead of absorbing the funds into general revenues, Ottawa could channel them directly to provinces where the resources are produced, supporting worker-transition programs and economic diversification.
Intellectual property rights present yet another opportunity for countermeasures. Canadians pay a premium for brand-name American drugs that could be manufactured domestically as generics. By limiting U.S. pharmaceutical patents, Canada could reduce drug costs, create new business opportunities, and deliver an economic counterpunch to the American sector.
These ideas are just the beginning. Additional options include "Buy Canadian" procurement policies, restrictions on foreign acquisitions of Canadian firms, and greater investment in Canadian cultural industries to reduce U.S. media influence.
With Trump's trade threats escalating, Canada cannot afford to be caught off guard. There is no rational negotiation with a bully. If we don't act now, we may not get another chance.
CAPTION: With Donald Trump's trade threats escalating, Canada cannot afford to be caught off guard, Hadrian Mertins-Kirkwood writes.
CREDIT: Hadrian Mertins-Kirkwood

Subject: International trade; Tariffs

Business indexing term: Subject: International trade Tariffs

Location: United States--US; Canada

People: Trump, Donald J

Title: Canada must hit back hard against Trump's economic warfare

Document 156 of 500

Feb. 12: Nothing will change if you don't vote, mortgage rates aren't that high and other letters to the editor

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/feb-12-nothing-will-change-if-you-dont-vote/docview/3165673965/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Feb.+12%3A+Nothing+will+change+if+you+don%27t+vote%2C+mortgage+rates+aren%27t+that+high+and+other+letters+to+the+editor&amp;title=The+Hamilton+Spectator+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2025-02-12&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Hamilton+Spectator+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: No change if you don't vote
Since the election in 2022, Ontario's debt has soared by more than $86 billion. Recently, we've watched billions wasted on the Ontario Place redevelopment fiasco, the Greenbelt land grab and the contract-cancelling expansion of beer and wine to convenience stores. Meanwhile, millions have been cut from legal aid and health care is underfunded by billions. Two and one-half million Ontarians don't have a family doctor. Highway 413 is being forced through to benefit developers and urban sprawl, not you. And there's an ongoing RCMP criminal investigation about land rezoning. Is that the kind of government and leadership that Ontarians deserve?
In the last provincial election in 2022, only 44 per cent of Ontarians showed up to vote. That was the lowest voter turnout in Ontario history. It also means that only 18 per cent of Ontarians voted for the Progressive Conservatives and gave them a majority. Obviously, the PC Party doesn't want you think about any of that. It seems they prefer that you not vote at all. When politicians show you who they are, believe them. Vote on Feb. 27!
James Newman, Hamilton
It's your responsibility to vote
Voters in Ontario are asked to make a very critical decision on which party they want to govern Ontario. Yet at least 40 per cent will not vote, even though they may be critical of Doug Ford and the Progressive Conservatives. Those who don't vote are undermining democracy. It is a privilege and a responsibility to vote.
Those who do vote need to make an educated decision based on the current reality. Ontario voters cannot afford to give the Progressive Conservatives a majority.
Those thousands of people who wait 10 to 20 hours in an emergency room cannot vote for Ford. Those who have students cannot vote for Ford. Those who now understand that our deficit under Ford is greater than the Liberals cannot vote for Ford. Those who have seen Ford cancel millions of dollars for environmental programs cannot vote for Ford. Those millions who do not have a doctor cannot vote for Ford.
Bottom line, every voter needs to vote based on the facts that face us.
Roman Caruk, Hamilton
Bring real help to Ontarians
Re: Doug Ford talks tough on China ahead of Washington trip to fight Donald Trump's tariffs, Feb. 10
I am so glad that Doug Ford is our man in Washington, keeping those tariffs away from Ontario! Except for the fact that he was utterly powerless in stopping Donald Trump from doing whatever he feels like doing anyway. So, what else have you got, Ford? How about we talk about your successes getting housing for all those people in tents in our parks. Oh, right. Or your great successes with our health care system? Maybe not. At least we have a lot of highways and alcohol in every corner store. Cost billions, but totally worth it.
Martin Sarabura, Ancaster
Don't blame mortgage rates
Re: Hamilton housing market sees 'cautious start' to 2025, Feb. 6
I just read the article in the Spec about the "cautious start" to the housing market to start 2025. One of the reasons given is the lack of improvement on high mortgage rates despite the Bank of Canada's series of lowering interest rates. This actually made me laugh out loud. We bought our first home in 1977 with an 11.75 per cent interest rate. We renewed the mortgage five years later at 19.75 per cent. And now they're calling a five per cent interest rate high. I don't think most people actually know what a "high" interest rate is.
Rusty Escott, Dunnville
Pedestrians playing dangerous game
Driving along Hunter Street, I had a green light while passing the TH&B Station. A group was leaving the station and were starting to cross Hunter, against the light. I beeped my horn and the first person halted and didn't look up. When I looked in my rear-view mirror, the group had continued to cross the road against the light. A truck behind me had managed to stop in order to not run over anyone. Most pedestrians are not aware that a flashing red hand or number in a pedestrian signal means do not start to cross the road. I see parents hurrying their children to cross when the signal is saying stop. Why are only drivers expected to follow the rules of the road?
Peter Gauthier, Hamilton
Bolster our institutions, culture
Watching what is happening to our neighbours down south worries me a great deal about our own country. Now is a time when we should be bolstering our own institutions and building more national self-reliance. I am by no means an isolationist, but if our biggest partner in many areas is tearing itself down from within, we need to build ourselves up.
We need to improve our education systems and become world leaders in basic and applied scientific research. We have world class medical facilities and should be building them up as magnets for the world's brightest.
We should be celebrating our culture and lifting up our creators. It is an inescapable fact that the American cultural milieu is going to have an impact on us - that's a force as sure and strong as gravity. However, we have a legacy in Canada of incredible artists in all fields and we can encourage them through grants, artist housing, and intentional cultural consumption.
I believe (or hope to) that America will come through its current predicament, but in the meantime, we must assert ourselves as a lighthouse for Canadians and the world.
Patrick Hehl, Hamilton
The world needs more of Canada
Re: The real play behind Karina Gould's Liberal leadership bid, Feb. 10
Jaime Watt's opinion piece in Monday's Spectator is a wonderful affirmation of the positive impact Canada can and does make on our world and, in our immediate circumstances, such a contrast to the dangerous behaviour and actions of Donald Trump, especially on our continent.
Can you imagine an executive of a conservative think tank in the U.S. praising the attributes of a candidate for the leadership of the federal Liberal party, namely Karina Gould? That is a clear example of why Canada should never be absorbed into the mess that is currently the United States under a dictator and his cronies. Canadians can support the good qualities of a candidate from any of our political parties and vote accordingly, not hampered or blinded by some idiotic assumption that political affiliation is a lifelong and blind commitment.
There are lots of reasons for Canada never sinking to joining or being absorbed by the destructive morass that is currently the United States of America. Watt's column reinforces the world's need for the best of Canada's thinking and influence, unencumbered by the current destructiveness of its undemocratic and arrogant neighbour under its current president.
Bill Millar, Stoney Creek
Is a rapper-in-residence next?
Re: City Poet survives council search for budget savings, Feb. 9
Poetry is a beautiful art form - at home, I have poetry on my shelf.
But in these tough times, I agree that the poet in place should be cut, though personally I am a fan myself.
True, the poet's salary and expenses are a proverbial "drop in the bucket."
But the symbolism shows that our council will not make the tough choices.
So they're telling us to "suck it'
And turning deaf ears to our collective voices.
There are painful choices to make, that's true.
But I think encampments, homelessness, and keeping our taxes down should take priority, don't you?
I love poetry, don't get me wrong.
But if a poet-in-place is OK, how about a rapper-in-residence, a Hamilton home harpist or a purveyor of ping pong?
A city poet would be wonderful in better times and would make a welcome cultural institution.
But perhaps our schools and libraries can further promote poetry, now there's a solution.
Scott Radley wrote it best a year ago - a bunch of councillors around the table have already collaborated to write the official city verse:
"Roses are red,
Violets are blue,
You're drowning in taxes,
Oh well."
Stan Nowak, Dundas
Beware the Blabberwock
With apologies to Lewis Carroll's "Jabberwocky."
O you who rail against a dime for a poet "we can't afford,"
Have you taken the time, told Ford "Stop withholding municipal gold?"
Have you, noses in the budget papers, sniffed out millions to save?
Or been whipped up by a dog whistle your masters gave?
O you who early were robbed of poetry's treasure
By a soulless system of rate and measure -
Open the rusty hinges of your starved limbic system,
Dust off your amygdala and hippocampus, tell 'em you've missed 'em!
To the poet in place if you're reading this ditty,
Take heart! Your voice is part of our ambitious city!
Mary Love, Hamilton
CAPTION: Those who don't vote are undermining democracy. It is a privilege and a responsibility to vote, a reader says.

Subject: Culture; Poets; Pedestrians; Mortgage rates; Roads & highways; Tariffs; Leadership; Interest rates; Voters; Voter behavior

Business indexing term: Subject: Mortgage rates Tariffs Leadership Interest rates

Canada must hit back hard against Trump

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/canada-must-hit-back-hard-against-trump/docview/3165673962/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=unknown&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Canada+must+hit+back+hard+against+Trump&amp;title=The+Spectator&amp;issn=11899417&amp;date=2025-02-12&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.10&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Spectator&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Hadrian Mertins-Kirkwood
is a senior researcher with the Canadian Centre for Policy Alternatives.

Document 158 of 500

Former student sues Robert Land Academy for $3.5M

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/former-student-sues-robert-land-academy-3-5m/docview/3165928915/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Former+student+sues+Robert+Land+Academy+for+%243.5M&amp;title=The+Hamilton+Spectator+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2025-02-11&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Hamilton+Spectator+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: When he came across a trailer for a Netflix documentary featuring a woman haunted by her boarding school experience, it shook him to his core.
"I had to watch it," the man recalled.
"It was the last thing I wanted to watch, but I couldn't not watch it."
The harrowing stories recounted by former students in 2024's "The Program: Cons, Cults and Kidnapping" mirrored what the British Columbia resident said he experienced during his three years at Robert Land Academy on the outskirts of Wellandport in West Lincoln.
"Watching these people on TV gave me the courage to do what they're doing ... to be a voice," he said.
The former student at the Niagara private school recently launched a $3.5-million lawsuit against the institution for what he says was ongoing physical and sexual abuse that went ignored by administration.
Founded in 1978, Robert Land Academy provides a military-style school program for boys in grades 5 through 12 with an emphasis on academic remediation and university admission.
Tuition and board is $64,000 per 10-month school term.
The complainant, referred to in court documents as B.C. as his identity is protected under a court-ordered publication ban, is seeking general damages for loss of education, opportunity and income related to physical and sexual assault, battery, forcible confinement and mental distress.
B.C. started at Robert Land Academy in the late 1990s.
His parents believed its small class sizes and structured environment would match his learning style.
Instead, according to a statement of claim filed recently in Superior Court of Justice in Welland, he was physically abused by staff, and sexually assaulted by an older student who was his supervisor.
When he reported the sexual abuse to staff, the lawsuit alleges, he was interrogated, gaslit and blamed by school administrators.
According to the statement of claim, the abuse disclosure was not reported to police or his parents, and the student supervisor was not expelled.
"For decades, I have tried to forget the horrible things that happened to me at RLA," B.C. said.
"I felt scared, alone and depressed, and I am hoping to finally get some closure on this traumatic experience."
The complainant's lawyer, Alexi Wood of Toronto's St. Lawrence Barristers PC, said Robert Land Academy needs to be held accountable for failing to protect the students in its care.
"This lawsuit is about ensuring no other child faces abuse," she said.
The claims have not been proven in court, and a statement of defence has yet to be filed.
Wood said 30 lawsuits have been filed against RLA, the majority within the past year. This is the only RLA case her firm is involved with.
In an emailed statement, Robert Land Academy officials said "upon learning of the alleged incident involving one of the school's staff members, we immediately placed the individual on leave."
"Out of respect for the privacy of those involved and a fair and impartial process, we will not provide further comment as the legal investigation remains ongoing," said the statement attributed to headmaster Pete Stock.
"The safety and well-being of our students is our top priority and these alleged incidents that have been reported in the media do not reflect the values of the school, past or present."
Stock said the school is co-operating with an ongoing investigation by Family and Children's Services Niagara.
FACS would not comment on the matter.
"Due to privacy requirements in the Child, Youth and Family Services Act, we are unable to confirm whether FACS Niagara is involved with any specific investigation," said Brett Sweeney, the agency's communications director.
The academy's lawyer, Elizabeth Bowker of Stieber-Berlach LLP in Toronto, said Friday RLA remains steadfast in its commitment to provide a safe environment for its students.
"Robert Land Academy has been serving students and the community for almost half a century and is dedicated to cultivating well-rounded young men through holistic education, with a focus on academic excellence, active living, values-driven principles and positive habits and routines," she said in an emailed statement.
According to RLA's website, programming is geared toward students who struggle in the traditional public school system for a variety of reasons including academics, behaviour, attitude and other problems.
It also provides benefits to students who have been diagnosed with certain learning disorders.
Although not affiliated with the Canadian Armed Forces, the campus and internal structure follow a traditional military model.
The campus itself replicates a 19th-century British fort, that includes military-style barracks where students sleep. Students wear uniforms and are assigned military ranks and titles.
The website said the vast majority of students experience a dramatic improvement in academic performance within the first semester, and 100 per cent of graduates who have applied are accepted into university or college.
Meanwhile, B.C. said he has tried to build a life for himself since his days at the academy, but has struggled with feelings of shame and guilt.
"I just tried to push it down for years with unhealthy ways - issues with drugs and alcohol and depression and suicidal thoughts," he said.
Now, he said, he wants to "slay the dragon."
"I was sick of living in the dark about it and not talking about it."
His relationship with his parents is better these days and he is buoyed by the support he's received from friends and family.
He maintains the lawsuit has "nothing to do with money," rather it's about accountability and raising awareness of childhood trauma.
He hopes by sharing his story he can help others.
"It's time to talk about it ... don't keep it buried inside," he said. "People love you. Get some help if you're struggling."
CAPTION: Robert Land Academy in West Lincoln.

Subject: Students; Privacy; Sex crimes; Sexual abuse; Learning; Litigation

Title: Former student sues Robert Land Academy for $3.5M

Publication title: The Hamilton Spectator (Online); Hamilton

Publication year: 2025

Document 159 of 500

'We will not be bullied': Trump's new steel tariffs could affect 'thousands' of local jobs, says Hamilton mayor

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/we-will-not-be-bullied-trumps-new-steel-tariffs/docview/3165492406/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=%27We+will+not+be+bullied%27%3A+Trump%27s+new+steel+tariffs+could+affect+%27thousands%27+of+local+jobs%2C+says+Hamilton+mayor&amp;title=The+Hamilton+Spectator+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2025-02-11&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Hamilton+Spectator+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Hamilton Mayor Andrea Horwath is condemning newly imposed steel tariffs and warning they could affect "thousands and thousands" of local jobs.
She also called Monday for a continued "Team Canada" approach to mitigate tariff impacts she said would be "quite damaging" to the economy of the nation's most famous steel town.
Late Monday, U.S. President Donald Trump signed an executive order imposing 25 per cent tariffs on all imported steel and aluminum, including from Canada, with no exemptions.
The abrupt move came barely a week after the president offered an apparent month-long reprieve from his original vow to impose tariffs on virtually all products imported from Canada.
Such across-the-board tariffs might have taken a $1-billion bite out of Hamilton's economy, the city said - and created a "doomsday" scenario for Canadian steel producers, according to an industry association.
But even a more targeted tariff war would be "really quite damaging" to Hamilton's economy, Horwath said in an interview.
"At the end of the day, this is a town that still does a lot of steelmaking," she said. "We have thousands and thousands of people employed in primary steelmaking, but also in secondary processing as well."
Horwath said she has spoken to both provincial and federal leaders about the importance of "fighting for us and with us."
"I'm all in for this Team Canada approach," she said, noting the local chamber of commerce is organizing an advocacy mission to Washington and city council is expected to formally direct staff Wednesday to explore the potential for "Buy Canadian" procurement options.
Details of the federal government's response to the new tariffs were not immediately available Monday evening.
But Hamilton Liberal MP Chad Collins, who recently met with officials at ArcelorMittal Dofasco to talk about the threat, noted online that the tariffs violate existing trade agreements and that Canada will "take retaliatory action" to protect workers.
"We will not be bullied," he said.
That long-running threat of tariffs was already hurting local businesses long before Monday's announcement, according to city economic development and steel industry advocates.
Ron Wells, who heads the union representing Stelco workers, said Monday there has been a hiring freeze locally and "slow" sales since shortly after Trump was elected, threatening tariffs against Canada.
"They've told us sales are slow, the order books are low," the president of USW Local 1005 said in an interview Monday. "(A tariff war) is not going to help our business, obviously."
Keanin Loomis, CEO of the Canadian Institute of Steel Construction, previously told The Spectator he was aware of contract cancellations, job losses and project disruptions blamed on the threat of tariffs. City economic development staff have also cited knowledge of delayed business expansions and shrinking orders for some companies.
The city dealt with similar Trumpian tariffs imposed in 2018, which lasted about a year before trade negotiations ended the spat.
Steel exports to the U.S. from Canada fell 38 per cent during the 2018-19 trade war, said McMaster University economics professor Colin Mang. A similar, sustained drop in demand for Canadian steel this time "would be pretty significant" for the industry, he said Monday.
"That would definitely lead to job losses."
The 2018 tariff war hit some businesses harder than others.
In parliamentary hearings that year, family-owned Janco Steel of Stoney Creek reported its business in the U.S. dropped 60 per cent soon after tariffs were imposed, spurring cancelled orders and worries about potential layoffs.
"The success of Janco Steel's business model is predicated upon having an open trade border with the United States," senior manager Stephen Young told a parliamentary committee at that time.
The Spectator has reached out to Janco Steel about the implications of the latest tariffs, but has yet to hear back.
By comparison, Cleveland-Cliffs CEO Lourenco Goncalves - whose U.S.-based company recently took over Stelco - told reporters earlier this month he believes the Hamilton steelmaker will "benefit" from Trump's tariffs, which he supports. He pointed to Stelco's strong 2018 financial results to back up his argument.
Stelco was able to sell more steel domestically and at higher prices during a year-long steel tariff spat that started in 2018, enabling the company to make money and avoid layoffs.
Hamilton's biggest steelmaker, ArcelorMittal Dofasco, has so far declined to comment publicly about tariff impacts.
But the head of Dofasco's global parent company, ArcelorMittal CEO Aditya Mittal, noted in a recent earnings call with analysts that 25 per cent steel tariffs imposed in 2018 cost the company about $100 million per quarter. At that time, those costs were "more than offset by revenue," he added.
The only guarantee in a tariff war is the cost of steel will go up on both sides of the border, suggested David Ogilvie, owner of Waterdown's Service Steel and Manufacturing Inc., which supplies tool-and-die shops and special purpose machinery builders.
"We've been through this before ... And the prices went up - way up. Everyone cranked their prices," he said. "What that means is more of my operating line is going to raw materials."
Ogilvie said he expects "some impact" from a tariff war, if it lasts - but added his small business is flexible enough to survive.
"I wouldn't want to have 50 or 60 (employees). That'd be risky."
The renewed tariff threat blew up on the Ontario election campaign trail Monday.
"This is the next four years. Shifting goalposts and constant chaos, putting our economy at risk," wrote Progressive Conservative leader Doug Ford, who announced plans to go to Washington Tuesday to lobby U.S. politicians. Ford has cited the tariff threat as a reason he called an Ontario election more than a year early.
NDP leader Marit Stiles, meanwhile, made a campaign stop at a Tim Hortons on Ottawa Street Monday with labour leaders, including Wells from the United Steelworkers.
She criticized Ford for not doing more to "tariff-proof" the economy since 2018.
"I will fight like hell for every single job," she said. "I will make sure the jobs here in Hamilton are front and centre."
Liberal leader Bonnie Crombie slammed a "self-serving" Ford for calling an election in the middle of a trade war.
"You don't see another premier who would think of calling an election at a time of crisis," she said during a Toronto campaign stop.
Matthew Van Dongen is a reporter at The Spectator. mvandongen@thespec.com
CAPTION: U.S. President Donald Trump's steel and aluminum tariffs will affect "thousands and thousands" of Hamilton jobs, Mayor Andrea Horwath says. Here, steel rolls sit in ArcelorMittal Dofasco yard.Mayor Andrea Horwath is condemning the latest "irresponsible" threat of steel tariffs from U.S. President Donald Trump that she warned could affect "thousands and thousands" of local jobs.Galvanized steel sits on the mill floor at Stelco.
