What will it take to fix Ontario's broken health-care system? Here's what provincial parties are promising

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/what-will-take-fix-ontarios-broken-health-care/docview/3165403551/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=What+will+it+take+to+fix+Ontario%27s+broken+health-care+system%3F+Here%27s+what+provincial+parties+are+promising&amp;title=The+Hamilton+Spectator+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2025-02-11&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Hamilton+Spectator+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: The next time you get a knock on your door from a candidate running in the Feb. 27 provincial election, ask them how they plan to fix Ontario's health-care crisis.
That's the message the Ontario Medical Association (OMA) hopes to deliver in the final weeks of the election campaign.
Dr. Dominik Nowak, OMA president, said he's pleased that all parties have been talking about the importance of connecting Ontario residents with a family doctor. Without an immediate solution, the OMA says 4.4 million Ontario residents could be without a family doctor by the end of next year.
Last month in the town of Walkerton, Ont., more than 1,000 people lined up in the cold for a chance to connect with a family doctor who was accepting new patients.
Ontario short 2,600 doctors
Citing figures from HealthForceOntario, the OMA says Ontario has 2,600 vacant physician positions across the province, a number that has remained virtually unchanged since last January.
"When someone asks for our vote in this election, we should be asking them how they can rebuild our health-care system and how can they connect every single person in this province to a family doctor," said Nowak, a family doctor at Women's College Hospital in Toronto.
Rebuilding the health-care system will take more than convincing new doctors to set up practices.
What's needed
"It will take building out teams of family doctors, nurses, nurse practitioners, pharmacists, social workers and all sorts of health professionals working together," said Nowak.
Modernizing health-care funding to support family practice startups is also key, Nowak added.
Why new grads are turning away from family practice
After speaking with medical students and new graduates, Nowak said the next generation of health professionals seems reluctant to pursue family medicine.
"To be a family doctor in Ontario is no longer seen as a viable career choice and that's because of the adding up of pressures of running a practice, paying for overhead, nursing team members, administrative team members, technology and an aging population that has more health-care needs," Nowak said.
Nowak said Ontario must modernize its funding models to help doctors care for people as they grow older.
Ontario's doctors also face an average of 19 hours of "unnecessary red tape" that limit their time spent with patients, said Nowak.
"There's a world where medical students feel they want to do family practice," said Nowak. "We know that. It's one of the most important parts of our health-care system. It's the most important thing that a government can do in health care is connect everyone to a family doctor."
No endorsement
While the OMA isn't endorsing any one party in the Feb. 27 election, Nowak said voters should choose a party with an ambitious plan to fix the family doctor shortage.
Voters should also ask for short-term plans on what each party would do over 30-, 60- or 90-day periods.
What the main parties say
Here is what the three main provincial parties have proposed thus far.
Ontario NDP Leader Marit Stiles announced a "family health guarantee" on Feb. 7, an objective proposed to connect every Ontario resident to a family doctor.
The plan includes recruiting and supporting 3,500 new doctors, expanding northern Ontario health care by hiring 350 doctors, cutting red tape in doctors' offices and clearing the path for 13,000 internationally trained doctors to pursue residency positions in Ontario.
On Jan. 27, just two days before a snap provincial election was called, the Progressive Conservative government announced $1.4 billion in new funding and $400 million in previously approved primary care funding to connect every Ontario resident to a family doctor within four years.
The Tories plan to create and expand 305 additional family health teams to connect two million people to primary care. Funding also includes more than $235 million in 2025-26 to establish and expand 80 additional primary care teams provincewide, connecting 300,000 more people to primary care this year.
If elected, Bonnie Crombie and the Ontario Liberal Party have pledged to guarantee a family doctor "close to home" for every Ontario resident by the end of the party's first term in office.
The Liberals have promised $3.1 billion to attract, recruit, retain and integrate 3,100 family doctors by 2029.
The Liberals also intend to create two new medical schools and double the number of medical school spots and residency positions.
The party hopes to incentivize family doctors to serve in rural and northern communities, enhance virtual care and reduce the time medical professionals spend filing paperwork.
CAPTION: Dr. Dominik Nowak, president of the Ontario Medical Association.Hundreds of people lined up outside the Walkerton Legion Hall on a cold and snowy day recently to sign up for a new family doctor in January.

Disability issues are everyone's issues

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/disability-issues-are-everyones/docview/3165403543/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Disability+issues+are+everyone%27s+issues&amp;title=The+Hamilton+Spectator+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2025-02-11&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Hamilton+Spectator+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: There's lots happening to talk about.
I spent Friday morning watching Hamilton councillors go through the budget process. I got enough of the picture to figure out quite quickly what the ask was - amendments that would add to or deduct from the budget bottom line. Why that was so difficult for some councillors is worrisome.
I get the budget document is big and change is hard, but the ask was specific. It's not rocket science.
Kudos to Ward 1 Coun. Maureen Wilson for maintaining order and decorum. Whiner of the week award goes to Ward 5 Coun. Matt Francis for his "I demand to see" temper tantrum regarding motions ruled out of order. That was embarrassing. As was his attempt to quash the $25,000 Poet in Place program. Like that's going to make a difference in our multi-billion-dollar budget. But it just might in the life of some kid. That could help down the road with the police budget. You can see that, right?
But I didn't see the rest of the proceedings, so I won't comment further, except to add my shock and surprise along with everyone else to discover that our underpasses are not targeted for cleaning on a regular basis. Yuck.
What I wanted to focus on, and there's a connection, are the words Doug Ford spoke regarding people on ODSP as healthy and at home watching "The Flintstones" when they should be out looking for a job. This was in response to a question regarding Liberal and NDP game-changing promises to increase social assistance rates.
Let's be very clear for the people who are confused.
People who are on ODSP and many of those on OW who are waiting to get approved for ODSP are not what are considered "healthy" people. They have mental or physical health challenges or restrictions that limit their ability to earn money from paid employment. The province is responsible for their funding. Not Justin Trudeau. Or Pierre Poilievre. But the Progressive Conservative government of Doug Ford.
They are legislated into a poverty you can't imagine, living with a pain you can't fathom.
That the premier of Ontario could make such an ignorant statement is right up there with Donald Trump mocking the disabled reporter during his first assault on America. It also recalls Ford's comments when he was a councillor regarding a home for children with special needs, complaining that he didn't think they'd leave the house. He should have been stopped then.
But then Ford is a fan of Donald Trump. Well, a fair-weather fan. Trump's tariff tantrum has tested his loyalty, Ford says he feels betrayed, while scolding Trudeau to "appoint the fentanyl czar." Every bully has a sidekick, a lesser than who stands behind and echoes the bully's words. This is Ford calling Trump a "customer." And what's the customer? Always right.
Back to Ford's stupidly ableist comments regarding healthy people on ODSP. It's exhausting to have to encounter this attitude of insulting dismissal when there are people with disabilities struggling almost everywhere you look. Well, where I look. I guess some people can hide from it. Hermits, maybe?
People with disabilities aren't represented in their numbers at the voting booth. When daily life is a struggle, extra expectations can be too much to navigate. The system doesn't help, it often hinders.
This election we need to make decisions with those who can't in mind. We who can are responsible for our neighbours who can't in the voting booth and in budget deliberations. Our interests are their interests. The sooner we accept that, the better life can get for everyone.
People need reminding that there but for the grace of god, or fate or karma or whatever you want to call it, go I when they see people struggle in life doing the things most of us do with ease and take for granted. Like voting. Vote for their issues; they are your issues too. And thank your lucky stars you can still do something about them.
CAPTION: At a Feb. 7 campaign event, PC Leader Doug Ford said "healthy young people that are sitting on the couch watching 'The Flintstones,' they should be out there, they should be working" when asked about increasing ODSP rates.

Ontario election 2025 promise tracker: From Trump tariffs to family doctors, here's what every provincial party leader is pledging

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/ontario-election-2025-promise-tracker-trump/docview/3165403538/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Ontario+election+2025+promise+tracker%3A+From+Trump+tariffs+to+family+doctors%2C+here%27s+what+every+provincial+party+leader+is+pledging&amp;title=The+Hamilton+Spectator+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2025-02-11&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Hamilton+Spectator+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

All four provincial party leaders have now put forth plans to support Ontario through U.S. President Donald Trump's tariff threats, which have been postponed for 30 days.
Here's what else the Progressive Conservatives, NDP, Liberals and Greens have pledged since the start of the campaign.

Document 163 of 500

Letters

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/letters/docview/3165305251/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=unknown&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Letters&amp;title=The+Spectator&amp;issn=11899417&amp;date=2025-02-11&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.10&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Spectator&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Trump will never be satisfied
Re: Trump's latest threat of steel tariffs could affect 'thousands and thousands' of local jobs, says Hamilton mayor, Feb. 10
I heard the Tangerine Toddler is imposing 25 per cent tariffs on steel and aluminum on Canada. I just want to make sure Doug Ford and Danielle Smith know that no matter what Canada gives Donald Trump, nothing will be enough.
I have heard both of them say in different ways, strengthen the border, give him what he wants. Yes, strengthen the border to stop the flow of guns and drugs coming in to Canada from the States. Their own border security have figures to show less than one per cent of fentanyl comes from Canada. He wants Canada. That's what we would have to give him. And that probably wouldn't be enough.
Doug and Danielle, stop thinking you can deal with him like any other businessperson. Stop giving him the attention he craves and thrives on. You cannot trust a word he says. Our federal government is lobbying American lawmakers to try and get their support on this. Ultimately the Republican party is the only thing that might rein him in. But I doubt it will stand up to him. They have created a mess and the rest of the world will have to deal with it for the next four years. Hopefully not longer.
Sharon McKibbon, Hamilton
Why weren't snowplows out?
On the drive from Guelph to Burlington Saturday evening during the snow event, highways 6 south and 5 east were unplowed the entire route. Yes, the "killer highway" from 9 p.m. to 10:15 p.m. was snow covered with no lane distinctions visible. Manoeuvring the snow-covered roads was tricky and required extremely cautious driving. Highway 5 through Waterdown (except for three blocks around Main Street) were not cleared. The first and only signs of snowplows were at Brant Street with three plows heading west to help cars unable to get up the hill. This snowfall was predicted for several days. There is no excuse for no plows to be out, especially on such a dangerous and well-used highway as Highway 6. Accident prevention should be foremost in the work of the Ministry of Transportation. Who didn't read the weather report?
Jackie Bajus, Burlington
Some councillors are out of touch
Re: City poet survives council search for budget savings,
Feb. 9
Congratulations to those councillors who understand the financial crisis residents are facing and are making practical suggestions to reduce our municipal budget. Unfortunately the defeat of the proposal to axe the poet in place pilot clearly demonstrates how out of touch some councillors are to the real concerns of their constituents. It is time for council to have the courage to put their pet projects on hold while our city gets its spending in order.
If the current trend continues they will soon be faced with thousands of residents defaulting on their taxes, throwing us into further financial chaos. As Coun. Mike Spadafora thundered, "enough is enough."
David Borsellino, Hamilton
Bicycle barriers not to blame
Re: Bike barriers impede
traffic, Feb. 8
Bike barriers did not impede traffic on Wilson Street during the ice storm. The weather was the issue and drivers were warned to avoid the roads unless absolutely necessary. Those bike barriers also protect cyclists the other 10 months of the year we don't receive snow; they didn't magically appear in the winter to inconvenience this driver. The bike lanes didn't cause the icy roads. Perhaps instead of railing against bike lanes, ask your ward councillor to better prepare in advance next time and start de-icing sooner.
Erin Shacklette, Hamilton
Address animal overpopulation
Re: Stalemate with Hamilton puts SPCA expansion plans 'on hold,' Feb. 8
This is the second expansion that I am aware of over the years. They need to look at the root cause of overcrowding. I would suggest opening a free clinic with money raised to spay or neuter pets. This would limit the number of unwanted cats and dogs so an expansion would not be needed. Vet costs have become so unaffordable for some people. I think this would be a better solution.
Jackie Jones, Hamilton
Seniors want
to adopt pets too
We often hear about the crowded situations at SPCAs and rescue centres.
We lost our 13-year-old golden retriever this past summer. In the fall, we applied at several rescue centres and made two trips to the Hamilton/Burlington SPCA to apply for a dog. We explained that we had a good friend who would adopt the pet if it outlived us.
We are an active pair of 80-year-olds who just wanted another dog. We have a house in the city, a boat and an RV in a park on a lake in northern Ontario; we have much to offer a pet.
We were shocked to find out no one would give us a dog because we were too old. How many other adoptions have been turned down in the overloaded system because seniors are not allowed to adopt? Where is the logic in this situation, with no regard to the particular potential adoptees?
We have since adopted a new puppy. She is a handful, but we are happy with our decision.
Laurel Thompson, Hamilton
What's the holdup at the SPCA?
I would be interested in why the city has been holding up the expansion of the SPCA for the past seven years. If the SPCA owns the building and "pitched three very good options" then they should be allowed to expand to help as many animals as they can. How complex can this be? We, as taxpayers, should know what the issues are. Why is it being kept secret?
This is coming from a council that voted to keep a "poet-in-place" program which costs taxpayers $25,000 a year. It's clear that this council has more issues than we realized.
Diana MacKenzie, Hamilton
Health should
be election focus
Re: 'Demand action': Nurses, hospital workers ask voters to make health-care 'crisis' a ballot box issue, Feb. 10
If this article doesn't make those staunch Conservatives out there question their vote, then we are all in a lot of trouble. This article has cemented my decision where my marker will land on election day.
Catherine Castellan, Hannon
Vote for health, not highways
Well fellow Ontarians, come election day Feb. 27 you will help decide if health care "trumps" a massive highway tunnel. The incumbent has an obsession with booze and highways. I say tunnels are for rodents and the much needed health care billions of dollars is for Ontario's human species. Please vote with your conscience.
Garry Young, Canfield
Some food
for thought
Re: Put a focus on farming,
Feb. 8
I 100 per cent agree with the letter from Martha Howatt. It is time we focus more on farming; subsidizing farmers and encouraging some young folks to get into farming. This will be needed more and more in the future.
Janet Remias, Ancaster
Feeling afraid
for our country
Re: This is how Canadians
are feeling right now, Feb. 8
The column from Carol Greene said it all! The points she made regarding how we are feeling over this whole Donald Trump business was spot on. I definitely feel "scared, enraged, exhausted and trying for hopeful." I never thought in my lifetime that I would have to worry about a possible dictatorship taking over my country.
I grew up hearing the stories of the Second World War from my parents and how devastating and difficult those times were. These days we are living in makes me afraid for our country and for the whole world. Canada is the best country in the world to live in and we must stand together to support our country in any little way we can. In this harsh world let's try to be hopeful and kind and believe better times are ahead.
Judie McKenna, Hamilton
Happy to buy Canadian
My guess is that some of our spending habits will change permanently. My family loves pears. For years, unthinkingly, as the food shopper I bought Bartlett or Bosc varieties. A couple of weeks ago I tried Canadian-grown Anjou pears (probably from B.C.) and found they are not only Canadian but just as good, and cheaper. Maybe something good will come out of all the disrespect we have been getting from Donald Trump.
Richard Harris, Hamilton

Document 164 of 500

Steel, aluminum tariffs 'unjustified': Canada consulting with international partners, for 'clear and calibrated' response: Champagne

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/steel-aluminum-tariffs-unjustified/docview/3165305081/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Steel%2C+aluminum+tariffs+%27unjustified%27%3A+Canada+consulting+with+international+partners%2C+for+%27clear+and+calibrated%27+response%3A+Champagne&amp;title=The+Spectator&amp;issn=11899417&amp;date=2025-02-11&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.6&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Spectator&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Donald Trump has signed executive orders to slap 25 per cent tariffs on all steel and aluminum imports into the United States, including Canadian products, starting March 4 - the same deadline given to Canada to convince the U.S. president to halt his plan for across-the-board duties.
"It's a big deal. This is the beginning of making America rich again," Trump said as he signed the executive orders in the Oval Office.
The orders remove the exceptions and exemptions from Trump's 2018 tariffs on steel, which excluded Canada and other countries from the duties. Monday's orders also increased aluminum tariffs to 25 per cent from 10 per cent, which was what he'd set them at in 2018.
In a post on X Monday evening, Industry Minister François-Philippe Champagne said steel and aluminum tariffs on Canada would be "totally unjustified."
"Canadian steel and aluminum support key industries in the U.S. from defence, shipbuilding, energy to automotive," Champagne said.
The minister said the government is consulting with its international partners, adding that its response will be "clear and calibrated."
"We will continue to stand up for Canada, support our workers, and defend our industries as we have always done and always will," he said.
Monday's move marks another escalation in Trump's rapidly changing geopolitical agenda that's charting America on a new course for foreign policy and trade. The president also suggested he would also announce "reciprocal tariffs" later this week.
It's all happening one week after Trump temporarily paused plans to slap Canada and Mexico with 25 per cent across-the-board tariffs and a lower 10 per cent levy on Canadian energy.
Trump delayed those levies until at least March 4 in response to border security commitments from both countries, saying it would allow time to reach a "final economic deal."
Some experts say Trump's tariffs are likely a first step in his plan to rattle the Canada-U.S.-Mexico Agreement ahead of a mandatory review in 2026.
Trump did not offer much relief on Monday for Canadians hoping the delay would allow for a deal ending the tariff threat for good. Trump said America has been "pummeled by both friend and foe alike" and duties would bring manufacturing back to the United States.
"If we make it in the United States, we don't need it to be made in Canada," Trump said. "We have the jobs. That's why Canada should be the 51st state."
Canadian premiers are travelling to Washington this week on a joint mission to meet with Republicans lawmakers and attempt to sway the president away from the threatened duties.
Ontario Progressive Conservative Leader Doug Ford said he spoke with the CEOs of Stelco and Dofasco, prominent Canadian steel companies, about the looming tariffs Monday morning.
He said Ontario is prepared to react "hard" and "fast."
The Canadian Press
CAPTION:

Document 165 of 500

Steel tariffs could affect 'thousands of jobs,' says mayor: U.S. president announces renewal of 25 per cent charge on steel and aluminum

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/steel-tariffs-could-affect-thousands-jobs-says/docview/3165305065/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Steel+tariffs+could+affect+%27thousands+of+jobs%2C%27+says+mayor%3A+U.S.+president+announces+renewal+of+25+per+cent+charge+on+steel+and+aluminum&amp;title=The+Spectator&amp;issn=11899417&amp;date=2025-02-11&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.1&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Spectator&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Hamilton Mayor Andrea Horwath is condemning newly imposed steel tariffs and warning they could affect "thousands and thousands" of local jobs.
She also called Monday for a continued "Team Canada" approach to mitigate tariff impacts she said would be "quite damaging" to the economy of the nation's most famous steel city.
Late Monday, U.S. President Donald Trump signed an executive order imposing 25 per cent tariffs on all imported steel and aluminum, including from Canada, with no exemptions.
The abrupt move came barely a week after the president offered an apparent month-long reprieve from his original vow to impose tariffs on virtually all products imported from Canada.
Such across-the-board tariffs might have taken a $1-billion bite out of Hamilton's economy, the city said - and created a "doomsday" scenario for Canadian steel producers, according to an industry association.
But even a more targeted tariff war would be "really quite damaging" to Hamilton's economy, Horwath said in an interview.
"At the end of the day, this is a town that still does a lot of steelmaking," she said. "We have thousands and thousands of people employed in primary steelmaking, but also in secondary processing as well."
Horwath said she has spoken to both provincial and federal leaders about the importance of "fighting for us and with us."
"I'm all in for this Team Canada approach," she said, noting the local chamber of commerce is organizing an advocacy mission to Washington and city council is expected to formally direct staff Wednesday to explore the potential for "Buy Canadian" procurement options.
Details of the federal government's response to the new tariffs were not immediately available Monday evening.
But Hamilton Liberal MP Chad Collins, who recently met with officials at ArcelorMittal Dofasco to talk about the threat, noted online that the tariffs violate existing trade agreements and that Canada will "take retaliatory action" to protect workers.
"We will not be bullied," he said.
That long-running threat of tariffs was already hurting local businesses long before Monday's announcement, according to city economic development and steel industry advocates.
Ron Wells, who heads the union representing Stelco workers, said Monday there has been a hiring freeze locally and "slow" sales since shortly after Trump was elected, threatening tariffs against Canada.
"They've told us sales are slow, the order books are low," the president of USW Local 1005 said in an interview Monday. "(A tariff war) is not going to help our business, obviously."
Keanin Loomis, CEO of the Canadian Institute of Steel Construction, previously told The Spectator he was aware of contract cancellations, job losses and project disruptions blamed on the threat of tariffs. City economic development staff have also cited knowledge of delayed business expansions and shrinking orders for some companies.
The city dealt with similar Trumpian tariffs imposed in 2018, which lasted about a year before trade negotiations ended the spat.
Steel exports to the U.S. from Canada fell 38 per cent during the 2018-19 trade war, said McMaster University economics professor Colin Mang. A similar, sustained drop in demand for Canadian steel this time "would be pretty significant" for the industry, he said Monday.
"That would definitely lead to job losses."
The 2018 tariff war hit some businesses harder than others.
In parliamentary hearings that year, family-owned Janco Steel of Stoney Creek reported its business in the U.S. dropped 60 per cent soon after tariffs were imposed, spurring cancelled orders and worries about potential layoffs.
"The success of Janco Steel's business model is predicated upon having an open trade border with the United States," senior manager Stephen Young told a parliamentary committee at that time.
The Spectator has reached out to Janco Steel about the implications of the latest tariffs, but has yet to hear back.
By comparison, Cleveland-Cliffs CEO Lourenco Goncalves - whose U.S.-based company recently took over Stelco - told reporters earlier this month he believes the Hamilton steelmaker will "benefit" from Trump's tariffs, which he supports. He pointed to Stelco's strong 2018 financial results to back up his argument.
Stelco was able to sell more steel domestically and at higher prices during a year-long steel tariff spat that started in 2018, enabling the company to make money and avoid layoffs.
Hamilton's biggest steelmaker, ArcelorMittal Dofasco, has so far declined to comment publicly about tariff impacts.
But the head of Dofasco's global parent company, ArcelorMittal CEO Aditya Mittal, noted in a recent earnings call with analysts that 25 per cent steel tariffs imposed in 2018 cost the company about $100 million per quarter.
At that time, those costs were "more than offset by revenue," he added.
The only guarantee in a tariff war is the cost of steel will go up on both sides of the border, suggested David Ogilvie, owner of Waterdown's Service Steel and Manufacturing Inc., which supplies tool-and-die shops and special purpose machinery builders.
"We've been through this before ... And the prices went up - way up. Everyone cranked their prices," he said. "What that means is more of my operating line is going to raw materials."
Ogilvie said he expects "some impact" from a tariff war, if it lasts - but added his small business is flexible enough to survive.
"I wouldn't want to have 50 or 60 (employees). That'd be risky."
The renewed tariff threat blew up on the Ontario election campaign trail Monday.
"This is the next four years. Shifting goalposts and constant chaos, putting our economy at risk," wrote Progressive Conservative leader Doug Ford, who announced plans to go to Washington Tuesday to lobby U.S. politicians. Ford has cited the tariff threat as a reason he called an Ontario election more than a year early.
NDP Leader Marit Stiles, meanwhile, made a campaign stop at a Tim Hortons on Ottawa Street Monday with labour leaders, including Wells from the United Steelworkers.
She criticized Ford for not doing more to "tariff-proof" the economy since 2018.
"I will fight like hell for every single job," she said. "I will make sure the jobs here in Hamilton are front and centre."
Liberal leader Bonnie Crombie slammed a "self-serving" Ford for calling an election in the middle of a trade war.
"You don't see another premier who would think of calling an election at a time of crisis," she said during a Toronto campaign stop.
Matthew Van Dongen is a reporter at The Spectator. mvandongen@thespec.com

Document 166 of 500

Disability issues are everyone's issues

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/disability-issues-are-everyones/docview/3165304542/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=unknown&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Disability+issues+are+everyone%27s+issues&amp;title=The+Spectator&amp;issn=11899417&amp;date=2025-02-11&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.10&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Spectator&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: There's lots happening to talk about.
I spent Friday morning watching Hamilton councillors go through the budget process. I got enough of the picture to figure out quite quickly what the ask was - amendments that would add to or deduct from the budget bottom line. Why that was so difficult for some councillors is worrisome.
I get the budget document is big and change is hard, but the ask was specific. It's not rocket science.
Kudos to Ward 1 Coun. Maureen Wilson for maintaining order and decorum. Whiner of the week award goes to Ward 5 Coun. Matt Francis for his "I demand to see" temper tantrum regarding motions ruled out of order. That was embarrassing. As was his attempt to quash the $25,000 Poet in Place program. Like that's going to make a difference in our multi-billion-dollar budget. But it just might in the life of some kid. That could help down the road with the police budget. You can see that, right?
But I didn't see the rest of the proceedings, so I won't comment further, except to add my shock and surprise along with everyone else to discover that our underpasses are not targeted for cleaning on a regular basis. Yuck.
What I wanted to focus on, and there's a connection, are the words Doug Ford spoke regarding people on ODSP as healthy and at home watching "The Flintstones" when they should be out looking for a job. This was in response to a question regarding Liberal and NDP game-changing promises to increase social assistance rates.
Let's be very clear for the people who are confused.
People who are on ODSP and many of those on OW who are waiting to get approved for ODSP are not what are considered "healthy" people. They have mental or physical health challenges or restrictions that limit their ability to earn money from paid employment. The province is responsible for their funding. Not Justin Trudeau. Or Pierre Poilievre. But the Progressive Conservative government of Doug Ford.
They are legislated into a poverty you can't imagine, living with a pain you can't fathom.
That the premier of Ontario could make such an ignorant statement is right up there with Donald Trump mocking the disabled reporter during his first assault on America. It also recalls Ford's comments when he was a councillor regarding a home for children with special needs, complaining that he didn't think they'd leave the house. He should have been stopped then.
But then Ford is a fan of Donald Trump. Well, a fair-weather fan. Trump's tariff tantrum has tested his loyalty, Ford says he feels betrayed, while scolding Trudeau to "appoint the fentanyl czar." Every bully has a sidekick, a lesser than who stands behind and echoes the bully's words.
This is Ford calling Trump a "customer." And what's the customer? Always right.
Back to Ford's stupidly ableist comments regarding healthy people on ODSP. It's exhausting to have to encounter this attitude of insulting dismissal when there are people with disabilities struggling almost everywhere you look. Well, where I look. I guess some people can hide from it. Hermits, maybe?
People with disabilities aren't represented in their numbers at the voting booth. When daily life is a struggle, extra expectations can be too much to navigate. The system doesn't help, it often hinders.
This election we need to make decisions with those who can't in mind. We who can are responsible for our neighbours who can't in the voting booth and in budget deliberations. Our interests are their interests. The sooner we accept that, the better life can get for everyone.
People need reminding that there but for the grace of god, or fate or karma or whatever you want to call it, go I when they see people struggle in life doing the things most of us do with ease and take for granted. Like voting. Vote for their issues; they are your issues too. And thank your lucky stars you can still do something about them.
Margaret Shkimba is a
Hamilton writer. For more, visit margaretshkimba.Substack.com. You can also follow her on X (@menrvasofia) or on Bluesky (@margaretshkimba.bsky.social).

Document 167 of 500

Should Brantford amalgamate with Brant County? Voters demand 'very clear' answers from would-be MPPs

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/should-brantford-amalgamate-with-brant-county/docview/3165304492/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Should+Brantford+amalgamate+with+Brant+County%3F+Voters+demand+%27very+clear%27+answers+from+would-be+MPPs&amp;title=The+Hamilton+Spectator+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2025-02-11&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Hamilton+Spectator+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Whether they like it or not, would-be MPPs in Brantford-Brant are being pulled into a contentious local debate over amalgamation.
In January, Brantford Mayor Kevin Davis used strong-mayor powers to commission a study exploring the benefits of amalgamating with the County of Brant - despite county Mayor David Bailey declaring the rural municipality is "unequivocally opposed" to becoming one with the city.
Now that voters are headed to the polls Feb. 27 for a snap provincial election, area residents are demanding to know where provincial candidates stand on the hot-button issue.
"The preservation of our community's autonomy is crucial," wrote one Brant County resident who started a petition last month to "halt the proposed amalgamation." Hundreds of people signed, requesting both the MP and MPP to "publicly declare their standpoints on the proposed amalgamation."
Some residents have also taken to social media to say they'd like Brant County to remain a separate municipality.
If they wanted to live in Brantford, they would have moved there, one resident wrote on Facebook. Others pointed to the challenges other municipalities - like nearby Haldimand and Norfolk - faced when amalgamating, the vast difference between the county's rural needs and the urban demands of the city, and Brantford's "misguided" priorities.
Bailey, the county's mayor, also voiced the need for provincial election candidates to give a "very clear" idea of where they stand on the issue, so voters "know who we can depend on," he wrote in a Facebook post in January.
The rural County of Brant completely surrounds the City of Brantford on all borders. But while they share some services like paramedics and have the same health-care system, as well as joint representation at the provincial and federal level, the two remain distinct municipalities.
The most recent Tory provincial government studied alternate governance models - including amalgamation - for a number of Ontario municipalities as far back as 2019, but has not forced any municipal marriages to date. It also backed off a high-profile promise to fully split up Peel Region in 2023.
Here's what provincial candidates in Brantford-Brant have to say about the prospect of amalgamation:
Liberal MPP candidate Ron Fox said he has heard a "loud and clear" message from residents.
His party will defer to municipal and regional councils as "the local leaders who know best" and will "never force" amalgamation on municipalities, because "shotgun weddings just don't work," he told The Spectator.
He believes there used to be "great co-operation" between the two municipalities for social services, housing and infrastructure but "it's gotten worse in the last few years, especially with all the downloading of costs from the province."
He said he plans to remove "the conditions that create competition" to have "two municipalities side by side, working together again."
Green candidate Karleigh Csordas also believes there is "room for improved efficiency" for shared services, but thinks any decision about amalgamation "must be based on clear, transparent consultation with residents to ensure their needs and priorities are fully considered," she told The Spectator.
One of the biggest concerns for both Brantford and Brant County is urban sprawl and associated greenfield development, she said.
"Expanding into farmland is expensive, inefficient and environmentally harmful," said Csordas, arguing both municipalities should focus on prioritizing infill development and sustainable planning.
NDP candidate Harvey Bischof told The Spectator he sees "no value" in pursuing an amalgamation at this time since it would require "enormous collaboration between two consenting parties."
He suggested in the future both municipalities could recognize the collective advantage of "a closer relationship" - but added that is "clearly not the case right now."
Bischof said he's ready to fight for issues of concern to the riding as a whole, including the "drastic need" for a new hospital facility, the push to hire primary care physicians, the "hugely problematic" lack of affordable housing, and support needs in schools.
Progressive Conservative incumbent Will Bouma's office told The Spectator he has "always believed that small, representative local governments are most effective when they work together to strengthen the communities they serve."
In the face of "unprecedented economic uncertainty" his party's local priorities include growing the economy, creating new jobs, protecting livelihoods and keeping Ontario "competitive."
Celeste Percy-Beauregard's reporting is funded by the Canadian government through its Local Journalism Initiative. The funding allows her to report on stories about Brant County. Reach her at cpercybeauregard@torstar.ca.

Document 168 of 500

Feb. 11: Trump will never be satisfied, what's the holdup at the SPCA and other letters to the editor

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/feb-11-trump-will-never-be-satisfied-whats-holdup/docview/3165304487/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Feb.+11%3A+Trump+will+never+be+satisfied%2C+what%27s+the+holdup+at+the+SPCA+and+other+letters+to+the+editor&amp;title=The+Hamilton+Spectator+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2025-02-11&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Hamilton+Spectator+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Trump will never be satisfied
Re: Trump's latest threat of steel tariffs could affect 'thousands and thousands' of local jobs, says Hamilton mayor, Feb. 10
I heard the Tangerine Toddler is imposing 25 per cent tariffs on steel and aluminum on Canada. I just want to make sure Doug Ford and Danielle Smith know that no matter what Canada gives Donald Trump, nothing will be enough. I have heard both of them say in different ways, strengthen the border, give him what he wants. Yes, strengthen the border to stop the flow of guns and drugs coming in to Canada from the States. Their own border security have figures to show less than one per cent of fentanyl comes from Canada. He wants Canada. That's what we would have to give him. And that probably wouldn't be enough.
Doug and Danielle, stop thinking you can deal with him like any other businessperson. Stop giving him the attention he craves and thrives on. You cannot trust a word he says. Our federal government is lobbying American lawmakers to try and get their support on this. Ultimately the Republican party is the only thing that might rein him in. But I doubt it will stand up to him. They have created a mess and the rest of the world will have to deal with it for the next four years. Hopefully not longer.
Sharon McKibbon, Hamilton
What happened to the 30 days?
President Donald Trump has announced he will impose a 25 per cent tariff on steel and aluminum. So apparently, besides not being able to read, write, spell, pronounce words or open a Bible correctly he can't do math. He doesn't know how much 30 days are either.
Steve Marfil, Hamilton
Why weren't snowplows out?
On the drive from Guelph to Burlington Saturday evening during the snow event, highways 6 south and 5 east were unplowed the entire route. Yes, the "killer highway" from 9 p.m. to 10:15 p.m. was snow covered with no lane distinctions visible. Manoeuvring the snow-covered roads was tricky and required extremely cautious driving. Highway 5 through Waterdown (except for three blocks around Main Street) were not cleared. The first and only signs of snowplows were at Brant Street with three plows heading west to help cars unable to get up the hill. This snowfall was predicted for several days. There is no excuse for no plows to be out, especially on such a dangerous and well-used highway as Highway 6. Accident prevention should be foremost in the work of the Ministry of Transportation. Who didn't read the weather report?
Jackie Bajus, Burlington
Some councillors are out of touch
Re: City poet survives council search for budget savings, Feb. 9
Congratulations to those councillors who understand the financial crisis residents are facing and are making practical suggestions to reduce our municipal budget. Unfortunately the defeat of the proposal to axe the poet in place pilot clearly demonstrates how out of touch some councillors are to the real concerns of their constituents. It is time for council to have the courage to put their pet projects on hold while our city gets its spending in order. If the current trend continues they will soon be faced with thousands of residents defaulting on their taxes, throwing us into further financial chaos. As Coun. Mike Spadafora thundered, "enough is enough."
David Borsellino, Hamilton
Bicycle barriers not to blame
Re: Bike barriers impede traffic, Feb. 8
Bike barriers did not impede traffic on Wilson Street during the ice storm. The weather was the issue and drivers were warned to avoid the roads unless absolutely necessary. Those bike barriers also protect cyclists the other 10 months of the year we don't receive snow; they didn't magically appear in the winter to inconvenience this driver. The bike lanes didn't cause the icy roads. Perhaps instead of railing against bike lanes, ask your ward councillor to better prepare in advance next time and start de-icing sooner.
Erin Shacklette, Hamilton
Some food for thought
Re: Put a focus on farming, Feb. 8
I 100 per cent agree with the letter from Martha Howatt. It is time we focus more on farming; subsidizing farmers and encouraging some young folks to get into farming. This will be needed more and more in the future.
Janet Remias, Ancaster
Address animal overpopulation
Re: Stalemate with Hamilton puts SPCA expansion plans 'on hold,' Feb. 8
This is the second expansion that I am aware of over the years. They need to look at the root cause of overcrowding. I would suggest opening a free clinic with money raised to spay or neuter pets. This would limit the number of unwanted cats and dogs so an expansion would not be needed. Vet costs have become so unaffordable for some people. I think this would be a better solution.
Jackie Jones, Hamilton
Seniors want to adopt pets too
We often hear about the crowded situations at SPCAs and rescue centres.
We lost our 13-year-old golden retriever this past summer. In the fall, we applied at several rescue centres and made two trips to the Hamilton/Burlington SPCA to apply for a dog. We explained that we had a good friend who would adopt the pet if it outlived us.
We are an active pair of 80-year-olds who just wanted another dog. We have a house in the city, a boat and an RV in a park on a lake in northern Ontario; we have much to offer a pet.
We were shocked to find out no one would give us a dog because we were too old. How many other adoptions have been turned down in the overloaded system because seniors are not allowed to adopt? Where is the logic in this situation, with no regard to the particular potential adoptees?
We have since adopted a new puppy. She is a handful, but we are happy with our decision.
Laurel Thompson, Hamilton
What's the SPCA holdup?
I would be interested in why the city has been holding up the expansion of the SPCA for the past seven years. If the SPCA owns the building and "pitched three very good options" then they should be allowed to expand to help as many animals as they can. How complex can this be? We, as taxpayers, should know what the issues are. Why is it being kept secret? This is coming from a council that voted to keep a "poet-in-place" program which costs taxpayers $25,000 a year. It's clear that this council has more issues than we realized.
Diana MacKenzie, Hamilton
Happy to buy Canadian
My guess is that some of our spending habits will change permanently. My family loves pears. For years, unthinkingly, as the food shopper I bought Bartlett or Bosc varieties. A couple of weeks ago I tried Canadian-grown Anjou pears (probably from B.C.) and found they are not only Canadian but just as good, and cheaper. Maybe something good will come out of all the disrespect we have been getting from Donald Trump.
Richard Harris, Hamilton
Health should be election focus
Re: 'Demand action': Nurses, hospital workers ask voters to make health-care 'crisis' a ballot box issue, Feb. 10
If this article doesn't make those staunch Conservatives out there question their vote, then we are all in a lot of trouble. This article has cemented my decision where my marker will land on election day.
Catherine Castellan, Hannon
Vote for health, not highways
Well fellow Ontarians, come election day Feb. 27 you will help decide if health care "trumps" a massive highway tunnel. The incumbent has an obsession with booze and highways. I say tunnels are for rodents and the much needed health care billions of dollars is for Ontario's human species. Please vote with your conscience.
Garry Young, Canfield
Feeling afraid for our country
Re: This is how Canadians are feeling right now, Feb. 8
The column from Carol Greene said it all! The points she made regarding how we are feeling over this whole Donald Trump business was spot on. I definitely feel "scared, enraged, exhausted and trying for hopeful." I never thought in my lifetime that I would have to worry about a possible dictatorship taking over my country. I grew up hearing the stories of the Second World War from my parents and how devastating and difficult those times were. These days we are living in makes me afraid for our country and for the whole world. Canada is the best country in the world to live in and we must stand together to support our country in any little way we can. In this harsh world let's try to be hopeful and kind and believe better times are ahead.
Judie McKenna, Hamilton
Many Americans are reeling too
It was interesting to read Carol Greene's thoughts on how Canadians are feeling now. I just happen to be on a road trip through the U.S. (planned before all the Donald Trump tariff nonsense) and I can say that a lot of Americans are feeling the same way. One woman talked about being frightened of what she was seeing going on. I overheard two educators expressing concern about Trump's plans to force states to be solely responsible for education.
Overall, I get the impression that Americans, like Canadians, are overwhelmed by the scope and lack of forethought for the consequences of myriad policy changes that are being implemented or mused upon. Certainly, no American voted for their country taking ownership of Gaza and remaking it into the "Riviera of the Middle East."
Notwithstanding all this nonsense emanating from Washington, the vast majority of Americans are still nice, decent people to speak and deal with. And we Canadians should not forget that.
Ralph D'Angelo, Hamilton
We can do better for the unhoused
Re: Sparks fly as Hamilton council scraps another homeless mini-cabin location, Feb. 6
Shame on us, Hamilton. HATS (Hamilton Alliance of Tiny Shelters) has shelters ready and available to the City of Hamilton, but they have no place to put them because of neighbourhood pushback. What a disgrace! Think about it. If you were homeless in the middle of the winter, would you prefer living in a tent or in a mini-shelter with heat and a lock on the door? It is a no-brainer.
To the residents of the neighbourhoods which are pushing back: Do not do unto others what you would not have them do unto you.
The mini-shelters are not a permanent solution, but they are something that we can and should offer as an interim solution. It is time for all the unsheltered individuals to find a place out of the cold. Come on Hamilton, we must do better.
Hinda Levine, Hamilton
Kudos to Ian Thomas and Carnegie Gallery
Musician Ian Thomas has still got it! And he generously gave it all, at the recent fundraising concert for Carnegie Gallery, at Ancaster Memorial Art Centre. An intimate memoir of songs and stories, Ian charmed the audience with his music, humour and gratitude for life and love. And the audience loved him right back.
Celebrating 45 years of supporting the arts, the Carnegie Gallery in Dundas is a community gem. With modest beginnings and huge enthusiasm, in the leased Andrew Carnegie Library, the gallery has evolved to proudly own and restore its historic and handsome, modernized facility. Through its robust schedule of exhibitions, classes, lectures, workshops and tours, the gallery continues its original mandate to "help artists make a living from their work." Thanks to Ian Thomas and his musicians, the Carnegie board, artist members, staff, volunteers, and sponsors, that's just what this very special evening did.
Robin McGinlay, Dundas
CAPTION: The local SPCA says it can't advance expansion plans while talks over its shared space with Hamilton Animal Services on Dartnall Road remain stuck in a years-long stalemate.Just before attending the Super Bowl in New Orleans, U.S. President Donald Trump said he intended to levy tariffs on all steel and aluminum imports. "No matter what Canada gives Donald Trump, nothing will be enough," a reader writes.

Liberals and NDP focus on health care

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/liberals-ndp-focus-on-health-care/docview/3165304473/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Liberals+and+NDP+focus+on+health+care&amp;title=The+Spectator&amp;issn=11899417&amp;date=2025-02-11&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.6&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Spectator&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Ontario's NDP and Liberal leaders tried to keep voters focused on health care Monday, even as actions by the U.S. president threatened to once again draw attention elsewhere in the provincial election campaign.
Progressive Conservative Leader Doug Ford has tried to make dealing with President Donald Trump the ballot question in the snap Feb. 27 election he called, and kept his sights set on those external forces Monday.
Trump has said he intends to impose 25 per cent tariffs on all steel and aluminum imports into the United States and Ford said at a campaign stop in Oakville that he spoke in the morning to the heads of Stelco and Dofasco.
"We're going to face this for four years: shifting goalposts constantly and constant chaos, all designed to hurt our economy and undermine our workers," Ford said.
He announced that if re-elected, he would ban Chinese equity from Ontario government-funded energy, critical mineral and infrastructure assets. The Progressive Conservatives said there has been no Chinese equity in government-funded energy projects during their seven years in government.
The other party leaders have expressed concerns about the potential tariffs - NDP Leader Marit Stiles added a last-minute stop to her Monday itinerary to talk to steelworkers in Hamilton - but want to keep talking to voters about health care, which they see as a weak spot for Ford.
Liberal Leader Bonnie Crombie once again highlighted her plan to ensure everyone in Ontario has a family doctor within the next four years.
CAPTION:

Document 170 of 500

Two Trudeau cabinet ministers say they won't seek re-election

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/two-trudeau-cabinet-ministers-say-they-wont-seek/docview/3165492453/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Two+Trudeau+cabinet+ministers+say+they+won%27t+seek+re-election&amp;title=The+Hamilton+Spectator+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2025-02-10&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Hamilton+Spectator+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: OTTAWA - Two members of Justin Trudeau's cabinet, both representing GTA ridings, announced Monday they will not seek re-election in 2025, a year in which a federal election is expected sooner rather than later.
International Trade Minister Mary Ng and Justice Minister Arif Virani each said they had informed Trudeau that they will not be running again.
Ng, who has represented Markham-Thornhill since longtime Liberal MP John McCallum stepped down in 2017, wrote in a statement that the political arena now faces a "critical challenge" that "transcends partisan lines": a deluge of disinformation and "attacks on the character of our leaders," she said, is deterring people from entering politics.
"My faith in Canada's future remains unshakable," wrote Ng. "Though I won't see re-election, I remain committed to building this future - because Canada is a place where anything is possible."
Earlier in the day, Virani wrote on social media that his "fight for Canada" will continue outside the House of Commons after the next election.
"I say this with a heavy heart, and after considerable soul-searching for the past several weeks," Virani wrote in a statement.
The ministers will keep their portfolios and will not leave Trudeau's front bench immediately, particularly as Ng shapes Canada's trade diversification strategy amid U.S. President Donald Trump's tariff threats.
"My first priority has been safeguarding our access to the world's largest economic partnership through CUSMA, our free trade agreement between Canada, the United States, and Mexico," Ng wrote, adding that Canada also has "everything it takes to succeed" as it faces off against its southern neighbour.
Ng became small business minister in 2018 before taking on the international trade file in 2019. She had previously worked in the Ontario government and served as director of public appointments in the Prime Minister's Office under Trudeau.
Virani practised law for 15 years before entering public life nearly a decade ago. He spent eight years as parliamentary secretary to numerous ministers covering a range of portfolios before being named justice minister and attorney general in 2023.
He wrested the Toronto riding of Parkdale-High Park away from the New Democrats in 2015 in a close race that saw the Liberals edge out two-term incumbent MP Peggy Nash by a margin of just over 1,000 votes.
In his statement, Virani said he plans to "continue, doggedly, to fight alongside the Liberal party" and its next leader. The justice minister is one of a handful of Liberal cabinet ministers to back former deputy prime minister Chrystia Freeland's bid to take on that mantle.
Virani's riding, which will go by the name Taiaiako'n - Parkdale-High Park in the next election, is already being challenged by Jagmeet Singh's NDP.
In September, NDP MPP Bhutila Karpoche announced her intention to seek the federal nomination in the riding, which she now represents provincially. Karpoche, who became the party's official candidate late last year, is widely seen as a key part of the federal NDP's plan to capitalize on discontent with the Liberals by winning back a number of long-lost Toronto seats.
Virani cited his family as his primary reason for leaving federal politics, saying his two children and his wife, Suchita, have "only known a life with their husband and dad in politics, frequently out of the city, sometimes out of the country.
"People say that is the price of public service," his statement read. "It is my turn now to give back to the people I love most."
Ng did not provide a reason for her departure, but thanked Trudeau for his leadership and his "commitment to creating seats at the table." She has not publicly endorsed any of his would-be successors.
Ng's riding has only been held by Liberals since its creation in 2015. In the 2021 election, she won 61.5 per cent of the vote.
CAPTION: Justice Minister Arif Virani rises during question period in the House of Commons on Dec. 9, 2024.

Document 171 of 500

Doug Ford talks tough on China ahead of Washington trip to fight Donald Trump's tariffs

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/doug-ford-talks-tough-on-china-ahead-washington/docview/3165492319/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Doug+Ford+talks+tough+on+China+ahead+of+Washington+trip+to+fight+Donald+Trump%27s+tariffs&amp;title=The+Hamilton+Spectator+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2025-02-10&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Hamilton+Spectator+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Ontario's election campaign is taking a detour to Washington, D.C. - thanks to U.S. President Donald Trump's tariff gambit.
Progressive Conservative Leader Doug Ford, who chairs the Council of the Federation, is leading a delegation there Tuesday to lobby American lawmakers and business leaders.
At midday, Ford will give a speech to the United States Chamber of Commerce, the world's largest business organization, before he and a dozen provincial and territorial leaders meet elected officials to share their concerns about Trump's moves.
The chamber has warned the president against imposing tariffs because the levies would hurt businesses and consumers on both sides of the border.
In the evening, Ford will co-host a reception at the Library of Congress for premiers and U.S. lawmakers with South Dakota Republican Sen. Kevin Cramer, a key Trump ally who co-chairs the bipartisan American Canadian economy and security caucus.
The Ontarian will make a second visit to the U.S. capital on Feb. 20 to discuss the trade crisis with delegates to the National Governors' Association winter conference.
His two trips are coming in the middle of a snap Feb. 27 election Ford triggered because he wanted a new four-year mandate to "outlive and outlast" the Trump administration.
Revving up for his unusual stateside push, Ford was taking aim at China on Monday.
The PC leader said he would ban Chinese parts from energy projects and prohibit participation by any Chinese state-owned enterprises in critical mineral or infrastructure projects if elected to a third term.
Trump's latest scheme to place 25 per cent tariffs on steel and aluminum - despite a pledge last week to hold off on any levies until March - shows why Ontario must be flexible, Ford told reporters in Oakville after a fundraising breakfast.
"We're going to face this for four years, shifting goalposts constantly and constant chaos," he said.
While he has focused his campaign on fighting tariffs, he suffered a setback a week ago when he was caught on a "hot mic" saying that he "100 per cent" supported Trump to win the November U.S. election over Kamala Harris.
"We're in a battle for the future of our economy," Ford said Monday, emphasizing it's crucial to convince Americans that U.S.-Canada economic co-operation is the best way to meet trade threats from China and put Trump off his talk of making Canada the 51st state.
"I'm going to tell them exactly what I'm telling you, Ontario is taking action to secure our border and delivering results."
Previously planned moves to take U.S. spirits, wines and beers off LCBO shelves and ban American companies from major contracts remain on hold for now, the PC leader said.
"Let's first see what he's going do to," he added, sending a signal south of the border that Canada as a whole has a plan for a "hard" response.
"We are not going to roll over. There's one thing that President Trump has underestimated - the will of the Canadian people," said Ford.
"We never started a fight we don't want. But when someone you know gives you a pop in the nose, they're going to get that pop twice as hard back."
In Toronto, Liberal Leader Bonnie Crombie said the tough talk from Ford is ironic given the Tory's professed admiration for Trump in last week's headline-grabbing gaffe.
Crombie noted it the president campaigned on placing tariffs on Canada and Mexico during in the U.S. election.
Her Liberals are airing television ads they hope will draw widespread public attention to that and eat into Ford's current lead in public opinion polls.
"We're showing him for who he is, someone who wanted a self-serving election," said Crombie, whose campaign has been mostly about improving health care by guaranteeing every Ontarian access to a family doctor.
"You don't see another premier who would think of calling an election at a time of crisis, when we're responding to a tariff threat, and a president who wants to wage a trade war and our premier supports that president," she said.
New Democrat Leader Marit Stiles, who met with steelworkers in Hamilton on Monday morning after highlighting her push for a higher nurse-to-patient ratio in hospitals to improve care, said Ford is exploiting the trade crisis to divert attention from pressing issues on the home front.
"Doug Ford doesn't want to talk about the fact that people in Ontario are losing their family members in hallways in our hospitals because there aren't enough beds and there aren't enough nurses," Stiles told supporters in Port Colborne before taking a shot at Ford's support for Trump.
"Doug Ford has shown us what he's made of," she added. "I don't think we can trust Doug Ford to stand up for Ontarians and to protect all our jobs and defend our workers and our communities."
Along with Green Leader Mike Schreiner, the party chiefs are gearing up for a Friday debate on northern issues in North Bay and another next Monday night in Toronto at the CBC Broadcast Centre.
CAPTION: Ontario Progressive Conservative Leader Doug Ford appears at a campaign even at BWXT Precision Manufacturing Inc., in Oakville on Feb. 10, 2025.
