Document 181 of 500

Boosting access to doctors focus of campaign trail

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/boosting-access-doctors-focus-campaign-trail/docview/3164583048/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Boosting+access+to+doctors+focus+of+campaign+trail&amp;title=The+Spectator&amp;issn=11899417&amp;date=2025-02-08&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.14&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Spectator&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: The main Ontario party leaders have now laid out their plans on how to get primary care to millions of people without a family doctor.
NDP Leader Marit Stiles promised to connect every Ontarian to a family doctor or nurse practitioner if her party wins the election by focusing on building up the team-based primary care model at a cost of $4 billion.
"It's a major shift that will make it easier and faster to get the care that you need," Stiles said Friday at a campaign stop in Sault Ste. Marie.
If elected, Stiles said her party would add 3,500 doctors to the primary care system over the next four years. She said 350 of those doctors would be in northern Ontario.
The plan would add 380 new primary care teams of doctors, nurse practitioners and other health-care professionals across the province. The NDP said they would also infuse the system with cash in order to recruit and retain doctors.
The Ontario Medical Association and the Ontario College of Family Physicians say 2.5 million Ontarians do not have a family doctor and that number will climb to 4.4 million by next year.
The NDP's plan is similar to the Progressive Conservative plan, but bigger in scope.
In the two days before the writ dropped, then-premier Doug Ford made more than $7 billion in announcements, including $1.4 billion in new money to beef up primary care in addition to $400 million previously committed to that plan.
The conservatives do not agree with primary care data the two physician groups have provided, preferring to cite a Canadian Institute for Health Information report that said 90 per cent of Ontarians have a regular health-care provider.
Under the Tories' plan, two million patients will be connected to primary care by 2029 and everyone in the province will have a family doctor or primary care team.
Ford appointed Jane Philpott, a former federal Liberal health minister, as head of the provincial primary care action team to implement that plan.
"We're sparing no expense," Ford said at a campaign stop in Toronto's east end on Friday. "We're going to make sure we connect every single person in Ontario to a family physician."
Liberal Leader Bonnie Crombie also reiterated her pledge to get everyone a family doctor within four years at a stop in Thunder Bay, Ont.
"Do you know there are 28,000 people here in the Thunder Bay area that don't have access to a family doctor?" Crombie said.
"That's absurd and that has to change."
The Liberals have said they will hire 3,100 doctors to solve the primary care crisis at a cost of $3.1 billion.
The Green Party has yet to announce its health-care plans, but Leader Mike Schreiner said they would hire more nurses, doctors and personal support workers in every region of Ontario. The party would plan to have every Ontarian linked to a primary care provider within three to five years and stop health-care privatization.
In other pledges, Mike Schreiner said if his party forms government, it would cut income taxes for those earning less than $65,000 annually and raise taxes on those in the top tax bracket.
The Canadian Press
CAPTION:

CREDIT: Liam Casey and Allison Jones The Canadian Press

Subject: Primary care

Document 182 of 500

Letters

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/letters/docview/3164583047/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=unknown&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Letters&amp;title=The+Spectator&amp;issn=11899417&amp;date=2025-02-08&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.19&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Spectator&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: A plea for safer sidewalks
It took until Jan. 31 for our winter's first need-to-shovel snowfall. The following week must also have been a week of emergency rooms full of people who were injured falling on the ice that developed from unshovelled walks. I am pleading that before the next predicted snowfall, for everyone to get out your shovels and prepare to use them. I am tired and frustrated of being stuck in the house just because the sidewalks are too dangerous to walk on. My dog and I thank you all.
A.J. Williams, Hamilton
Put mayoral powers to use
I just read the weather and this weekend will feel like -17 C with the wind chill. Thank goodness candidates are taking the time to address the problems in the Middle East and to advise us of their global views (please note the sarcasm). Why not demand the mayor with strong powers open up some buildings so unhoused citizens won't freeze to death? Why not demand action for the hungry and homeless? Citizens are sick of the delays.
Robin Magder, Dundas
Fans should have a seat
Rather than boo the singing of "The Star-Spangled Banner," instead stay seated and remain silent. Standing while the anthem is sung shows respect, but by staying seated shows no respect for the U.S. and its president. It's much more effective to stay seated and silent.
Terry Middlemiss, Hamilton
Let's invite our friends to play
Fans have been booing the U.S. national anthem before NBA and NHL games in Canada. A more proactive approach would be for this country to host a positive event. What we now call the Commonwealth Games originated as the British Empire Games, in Hamilton, in 1930. Maybe it's time for Canada to make plans for the event's 100th anniversary in 2030 and show Americans we've got other BFFs by hosting the games again.
David Collier, Hamilton
Ford has short memory
So Doug Ford wants to be seen as the man to "stand up to Trump." I listened to a Mexican trade representative today on the radio. He talked about the premier of Ontario pointing his finger at Mexico on Day 1. How does that help anybody but Donald Trump? Then Ford dreams up "Fortress AMCAN." Not CANAM! Trump likes to divide and conquer. Way to go, Doug. "Stand up" is not the same as "suck up." Remember that, voters.
Richard Law, Burlington
Don't forget the Greens
A recent article in the Spec headlined, "Main parties discuss encampments, disability payments and highway tolls" neglects any mention of the Green Party of Ontario. It left me wondering who gets to decide what a "main party" is. The GPO is running a full slate of candidates across the province, we have sitting members, a leader who was courted by the Liberals and a comprehensive platform on all those issues and more. It is a disservice to voters not to mention that they have a choice that puts policy before politics.
Steven Toth, Hamilton
Put a focus on farming
I'm busy trying to sort out the various platforms for each of the provincial party leaders. I'm really surprised that I haven't heard much about food security. It seems that this would be the time for Doug Ford to step back from Highway 413 and various other farmland development deals and be offering subsidies to farmers and young people interested in getting into farming. We need to be growing and raising our own food and stop depending on the U.S. to supply our demands.
Martha Howatt, Hamilton
Bike barriers impede traffic
After a recent ice storm, I learned something I can only hope the City of Hamilton also learned. Bike lanes and city roads do not mix. After spending 45 minutes idling on the Wilson Street access, police finally arrived and got us moving and we saw the problem. Buses slid into the bike barriers and became stuck. One bus on each side. Thanks to bike lanes, there wasn't enough room to manoeuvre around vehicles. Those of us lucky enough to get traction couldn't go anywhere thanks to the cement bike barriers. Since our weather is only going to get more unpredictable in the future maybe the City of Hamilton might want to rethink these ridiculous bike barriers that cause nothing but chaos during winter weather. Oh, I'm sorry. Am I being unfair to the - at most - 10 bike riders that use the accesses in the warmer weather? I hope they were in one of the vehicles stuck last Friday and maybe tomorrow also the city sanders could do a better job this week than last week.
Brenda Stricker, Jerseyville

Document 183 of 500

We need to hold on tight to our rage

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/we-need-hold-on-tight-our-rage/docview/3164583026/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=unknown&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=We+need+to+hold+on+tight+to+our+rage&amp;title=The+Spectator&amp;issn=11899417&amp;date=2025-02-08&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.17&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Spectator&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Canadians are mad. Oh boy, are we mad! I feel it; I bet you feel it too. Almost every commentator I read this week is trying to outdo all the others in channelling our collective rage at Donald Trump.
Anger can be good. It can propel you into doing things you might not dare to do otherwise. Especially when it's combined with a dose of humiliation - i.e. how could we have allowed ourselves to get into a position where we can so easily be shoved around?
We should find a way to bottle the feeling we have now. If we're really going to do what needs to be done, we'll need a way to remind ourselves of the sour taste of this week. Because it will fade, inevitably, and as it fades our resolve to escape the trap we're in will weaken.
Right now there's an across-the-board consensus. When it comes to what Canada must do in the face of the Trump threat, you can hardly tell who's talking. Pierre Poilievre, Mark Carney, Chrystia Freeland, the premiers, every business leader and analyst who pops up on the news shows - they're all saying essentially the same thing: We've got to tear down those notorious internal barriers to trade. We've got to diversify our trade. Explore new markets for our energy and critical minerals. Be more competitive. Get serious about national security. And we've got to do it now!
The truth is that apparent consensus is a mile wide and an inch deep. We're at the beginning of a once-in-several-generations national debate on what kind of Canada we want, a debate that should be the focus of the fast-approaching federal election campaign. And the deeper we get into that debate, the more this week's consensus will crumble.
There will no doubt be some easy wins in the next few months. Some of those internal trade barriers will be torn down, finally. Premier Doug Ford promises "full steam ahead" on that front; British Columbia wines will soon be freely available in the LCBO, he says.
Hooray! Other barriers will fall simply because they're silly. Did you know you have to be an Ontario resident to buy and sell bullfrogs in this province? I didn't till I checked. Hopefully we'll soon have free trade in bullfrogs.
But other barriers will be much more contentious. Most fish caught in Newfoundland must be processed there; ending that restriction could cost hundreds of jobs where they're badly needed. Some provinces will pay lip service to national co-operation while blocking it when it matters.
Quebec Premier François Legault was out this week pledging solidarity against Trump while hemming and hawing on whether his province is prepared to drop its opposition to a cross-Canada energy pipeline. There's still "no social acceptability" for that in Quebec, he said.
More broadly, the vision for a tougher, Trump-proof Canada sketched out this week by Poilievre and the leading Liberal leadership candidates skews to the right. They disagree on details, but generally the direction is toward cutting taxes, speeding up big projects like pipelines, boosting energy exports and making the country more attractive to investors.
All of that is highly contentious. The left in all its manifestations will soon mobilize against it on the grounds that opposing Trump should not involve Trumpifying Canada.
One person's pointless red tape or inefficient internal trade barrier is another person's vital health and safety regulation or guarantee of a local job.
That's when this week's consensus will fray and may finally come apart. Governments, especially the new federal government to be chosen this year, will have to bull through some formidable obstacles. And to do that they will need the support of a public fed up with dithering and delay.
That's when we'll need to reach for that bottle of rage and take a big gulp.
Andrew Phillips is a staff columnist with Torstar.
CAPTION:

Doug Ford under attack in Super Bowl ads

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/doug-ford-under-attack-super-bowl-ads/docview/3164582958/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Doug+Ford+under+attack+in+Super+Bowl+ads&amp;title=The+Hamilton+Spectator+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2025-02-08&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Hamilton+Spectator+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Doug Ford's campaign fumble is getting the Super Bowl spotlight.
Liberal Leader Bonnie Crombie will be airing two different 30-second commercials during the NFL championship game Sunday night between the Kansas City Chiefs and the Philadelphia Eagles.
With Ontario voters headed to the polls on Feb. 27, Crombie's team wants viewers of what is expected to be the year's most-watched TV broadcast to see for themselves what Ford said last Monday.
In a "hot mic" gaffe at his Etobicoke campaign office, the Progressive Conservative leader said he wanted Donald Trump to win last November's U.S. presidential election.
"On election day, was I happy this guy won? One hundred per cent I was. Then the guy pulled out the knife and f---ing yanked it in me," said Ford, not realizing a media pool camera was still rolling.
The Liberals have cut a new version of a 60-second ad they released Tuesday on the misstep.
"Doug Ford just said he was happy Donald Trump won the election. Happy? Was he happy Canadians might lose their jobs?" intones a female narrator with footage of Trump boasting "the tariff will be substantial" and that he'd like to see "Canada become our 51st state."
"Or does he just care so little about the people of this province that he saw an attack on Ontario as an opportunity for himself," she continues before a clip of Ford telling reporters in January that he "needs a clear mandate" to deal with Trump, which is why he called a $189-million election 15 months early.
The second Liberal spot airing on Super Bowl Sunday features Crombie talking about Ontario's health-care challenges.
"Leadership is about priorities and for seven years, Doug Ford has had the wrong ones," she says against the backdrop of video of her touring a hospital and chatting with doctors and nurses.
"The result? A health-care system in crisis. People are afraid to get sick. As premier, I'll have one priority: fixing our health care," the Liberal leader continues.
"We'll take the money that Ford wastes on bad deals and invest it where it matters, starting with getting you a family doctor," she continues.
"I'm Bonnie Crombie, the new leader of Ontario's Liberals and I'll get more for you."
A senior Liberal official, speaking confidentially in order to discuss strategy, said the Super Bowl play is a big one for a party that was in office from 2003 until 2018 but finished third in seats in the past two elections behind Ford's Tories and the New Democrats now led by Marit Stiles.
"It's been a long time since we have had the kind of fundraising machine and clear, focused message that makes this kind of advertising possible," said the Grit insider.
"When you talk about what actually matters to people, you'll quickly find that people want to get behind you and support that message."
The Trump blunder is potentially harmful to Ford's re-election bid because he has framed the campaign as a referendum on who could best deal with the U.S. president's threatened 25 per cent tariffs on Canadian goods.
CAPTION: This Ontario Liberal ad will be aired during the Super Bowl and targets recent comments made by Progressive Conservative Leader Doug Ford.This Ontario Liberal ad outlines their plans for health care.Progressive Conservative Leader Doug Ford during a press conference in Etobicoke on Feb. 3, 2025.

Document 185 of 500

These Canadian snowbirds aren't leaving Florida - even though Trump's threats are a slap in the face

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/these-canadian-snowbirds-arent-leaving-florida/docview/3164582848/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=These+Canadian+snowbirds+aren%27t+leaving+Florida+-+even+though+Trump%27s+threats+are+a+slap+in+the+face&amp;title=The+Hamilton+Spectator+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2025-02-08&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Hamilton+Spectator+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: FORT MYERS, Fla.-Afternoons are for tennis at the Palmetto Palms RV Resort, even if you're 82 years old and it's 84 degrees Fahrenheit.
Donald Lamadeleine plays energetically, despite his age, and despite the sun beating down on the court - or maybe because of it.
Lamadeleine is one of a multitude of Canadian snowbirds who have fallen in love with Florida's sunshine and he's not about to give it up because U.S. President Donald Trump has been threatening tariffs and musing about Canada as his country's 51st state.
Lamadeleine and his wife, Suzanne St. Pierre, 79, have been visiting Florida annually for 30 years, 18 of them in the Palmetto Palms, a gated community of 685 RVs and modest homes for people over 55, where they can always find doubles for tennis on the outdoor court. In February.
"The tariffs probably won't go through," said Lamadeleine, a thought echoed by neighbour Barbara Auswald, 82, from the GTA.
"He's already making concessions with Mexico, and giving us a month," reasoned Auswald, who lives in the park with her husband Erich, 83.
Lamadeleine jokingly introduces himself to his American neighbours as a resident of the 51st state - just for laughs - but, like many of his fellow snowbirds, Lamadeleine just doesn't believe Trump will follow through on all his threats, including the tariffs, which would hurt everyone.
Florida elected Trump, twice, and the airwaves in this small city of 97,000 are full of talk-show pundits socking it liberals, mocking the elites who just don't get it - who haven't been getting it for years and haven't been listening to the growing number of voters now implacably opposed to an agenda they see as a direct attack on their traditional values.
There's a number you can't get away from in Florida this week: more than 77 million Americans voted for Trump.
Still, maybe it's age, maybe it's wisdom, maybe it's wishful thinking, but some of the Canadian snowbirds in this proudly red state are unmoved by Trump's threats and proclamations that the U.S. doesn't need Canada.
Couples with dual incomes and no divorces - who worked hard, spent cautiously, stayed married and saved prodigiously to earn the privilege of escaping winter for months at a time - are not the kind of people who run off madly in all directions the first few weeks into a new presidency, no matter how startling they may be.
Lamadeleine's daughter cancelled a holiday in Hawaii to protest Trump's America.
"I told her, are you crazy," said Lamadeleine, a general contractor whose point of view has something to do with age. At 82, each new day is a gift not to be squandered. He took up tennis at 70. He and his wife still cycle to the beach, play pickleball, volleyball, fish.
"Do what you want, it's your life," he said he told his daughter. "I'm not cancelling."
Lamadeleine doesn't support all of Trump's policies, but he supports some of them, like his hard line on China and illegal immigrants - emphasis on illegal.
If prices were to increase due to tariffs, Lamadeleine, who still does some work as a contractor and owns a campground near Ottawa where he and his wife spend the summer, is in a position to handle it.
The Auswalds meanwhile, have decided to sell their land in the RV park because the value of the Canadian dollar is making their annual U.S. excursion increasingly expensive, with no relief in sight. They plan to spend more time visiting Canada.
Canadian Snowbirds make up the single largest group of tourists to Fort Myers, said Mayor Kevin B. Anderson. They visit so routinely and stay for so long that they are part of the social fabric of the city; Anderson counts Canadians among his closest friends.
"I think a majority of people will say we're good neighbours for each other," said Anderson, who approves of Trump's ability to "get things done."
Canadian snowbirds gravitate toward Fort Myers the same way immigrants to Canada tend to cluster by nationality, said Anderson. Germans prefer Cape Coral, about a 30-mile drive away. Greeks congregate in Tarpon Springs to the north.
Restaurants like the ones in the Fort Myers Historical District, an area that was derelict 40 years ago and has been restored to its Art Deco glory, rely on the winter tourist trade.
Summers are slower because the snowbirds have gone home and the locals are taking their summer vacations, Anderson said.
"They count on those three to four months to really carry them through the year."
Losing the winter tourist trade from Canada would have an economic impact, but like the residents of Palmetto Palms RV Park, Anderson doubts Trump will make good on his tariff threat.
"The president - he's a negotiator," said Anderson. "I'm an optimist and I have to think this is all going to work out before it adversely affects Americans as well."
If the tariffs do go through? "Even if prices go up, it's not going to kill everything," Anderson believes.
Snowbirds have learned to be cautious when talking politics with their American friends, especially in the run-up to the election when emotions were running high.
Florida voted for Trump. Twice. The local Dairy Queen plays gospel music: "I remember where we were when you gave your life to Jesus," goes the chorus.
"We almost stopped talking to a couple we've been friends with for years," said Pierre Fortin, one eye on the hockey game at Hertz Arena in Fort Myers, where the Florida Everblades were losing badly to the Greenville Swamp Rabbits. (The final score was 3-0).
"We still don't understand how anyone could vote for him," said Fortin, 69, a former database engineer from Ottawa.
In the next seat over, Canadian John Kraft, 87, said the tariffs announcement felt like more than an economic measure. It felt personal.
"It was like a slap in the face," he said.
On local radio and on television, the news is almost exclusively Trump, with Republicans unabashedly crowing victory. On Fox News, the new tagline is: "Right all along."
In between discussion panels about the latest astonishing Trump initiative, Ontario has placed ads promoting itself as America's partner in energy. They're meant to sound confident and proud, but somehow come off as pleading - pick me.
Fort Myers is also a winter home for Americans who live in states with abundant snow and cold. Some seem personally embarrassed by the performance of their president - a man they refer to as 45 and 47 - to distinguish between his two presidencies.
At the Lucky Screw restaurant in Historic Fort Myers, Tom Smith sighed deeply when tariffs came up.
He and his wife, Jeanne Parsons, flee Minnesota in winter, which they are quick to point out is a blue state.
"Tariffs aren't good for anybody," he said, over $5 tacos and beer.
"It's a circular firing squad - everyone is shooting at each other."
Few people know how tariffs might affect them personally. Smith expects to see it at the gas pump. Parsons points out that Trump supporters probably don't know either.
"I don't think everyone knows where all their goods and services come from," said Parsons, 71.
"Putting tariffs on something - people think it's a one-way street. I think they'll be surprised."
CAPTION: The beach, in early February - that's the draw of Florida in winter.Barbara and Erich Auswald, who have decided to sell their land in the RV park. Not because of Trump - rather, it's the Canadian dollar."I'm an optimist," says Fort Myers Mayor Kevin B Anderson.Fort Myers is a proud American gulf town; thanks to Trump, the maps now say "Gulf of America."Pierre Fortin, right, andJohn Kraft take in aFlorida Everblades hockey game -the home side lost 3-0.Eighty-two year old Donald Lamadeleine, who jokingly introduces himself to his American neighbours as a resident of the 51st state.Donald Lamadeleine, 82, and his wife, Suzanne St. Pierre, 79, say they don't believe Donald Trump's tariffs will ever go through.Historic Fort Myers, a hub for Canadian snowbirds.

Document 186 of 500

The time to tear down internal trade barriers is now

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/time-tear-down-internal-trade-barriers-is-now/docview/3164582836/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=The+time+to+tear+down+internal+trade+barriers+is+now&amp;title=The+Hamilton+Spectator+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2025-02-08&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Hamilton+Spectator+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Canadians and Americans are holding their breath as they wait to learn if they will have to deal with 25 per cent tariffs on the goods they buy every day.
In response to the threat of tariffs, Canadian premiers have stepped up with their own ideas about how to respond to Donald Trump's tariff threats, from Danielle Smith's insistence that a more diplomatic approach is necessary, to Doug Ford's approach of threatening to cut off various sources of American trade in Ontario.
There has been much talk about a Team Canada approach with regards to tariffs. But how can there even be a Team Canada when the provinces can't agree to trade freely with one another?
This tariff debacle should be the sobering wake up call to all provincial premiers that international trade partners should not be seen as unwavering allies, and that diversifying trade is essential to maintaining economic prosperity.
One obvious place to start is looking within our own house, and breaking down the trade barriers that preposterously continue to exist between Canadian provinces.
The refrain has long been that eliminating interprovincial trade barriers is an insurmountable task.
Section 121 of the Constitution states "all Articles of the Growth, Produce, or Manufacture of any one of the Provinces shall, from and after the Union, be admitted free into each of the other Provinces." This is clear language, and should mean that we are a unified nation - one country, with one market.
However, despite this clear language in the Constitution, trade barriers continue to be high.
Former Canadian Chamber of Commerce president Perrin Beatty has stated there are many different levels of barriers to tackle, including mobility barriers and commercial barriers, some of which have existed since confederation.
According to a recent post on X by former policy advisor to Québec's Minister of Finance Jean Philippe Fournier, even though the political will existed at a certain point in Quebec (notably, the province that is most closed off to interprovincial trade), the minister was stopped from dropping barriers by the realization that each province was creating regulations in silos without taking into consideration the actions of other provinces.
These rules ended up creating niche interest groups of companies who adapted to these regulations and would lobby the government not to standardize with other provinces so they could stay in business.
And despite text in Section 121 that reads clearly, the Supreme Court of Canada's restrictive interpretation of it has taken out all its teeth. Section 121 was considered in the Supreme Court case of R. v. Comeau, which involved a man who bought some beer for personal consumption in Quebec and brought it over the border to his home in New Brunswick. He was caught and handed a $300 ticket, and took his case all the way to the Supreme Court with the help of the legal charity the Canadian Constitution Foundation.
While Gerard Comeau had success at the lower court, the Supreme Court found Section 121 does not allow absolute free trade across provinces.
Provinces can adopt laws and regulations that restrict trade if they show the overall aim is for another purpose, like "public health." This has allowed provinces to impose all kinds of trade restrictions under the guise of some other purpose.
This was an unfortunate result that should be seen as inconsistent with the plain reading of the constitution, but could easily be addressed through political action.
Provinces could choose to enact freer trade, which would unite us as a country during this time of deep economic uncertainty. The political will to tackle interprovincial trade appears to be getting stronger. Conservative Leader Pierre Poilievre recently released a video questioning why it's easier to trade with other countries than within Canada itself.
Reports also show the federal government and the premiers are finally having conversations about the positive effects of breaking down these barriers.
Team Canada and "Buy Canadian" are not complete until buying Canadian means having free trade to do exactly that within the borders of our own country.
CAPTION: The current push to "buy Canadian" won't get far when we have rules in place that fine you for carrying beer over a provincial border.
CREDIT: Sabine El-Chidiac and Christine Van Geyn

Document 187 of 500

The time to tear down trade barriers is now

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/time-tear-down-trade-barriers-is-now/docview/3164582743/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=unknown&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=The+time+to+tear+down+trade+barriers+is+now&amp;title=The+Spectator&amp;issn=11899417&amp;date=2025-02-08&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.18&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Spectator&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Canadians and Americans are holding their breath as they wait to learn if they will have to deal with 25 per cent tariffs on the goods they buy every day.
In response to the threat of tariffs, Canadian premiers have stepped up with their own ideas about how to respond to Donald Trump's tariff threats, from Danielle Smith's insistence that a more diplomatic approach is necessary, to Doug Ford's approach of threatening to cut off various sources of American trade in Ontario.
There has been much talk about a Team Canada approach with regards to tariffs. But how can there even be a Team Canada when the provinces can't agree to trade freely with one another?
This tariff debacle should be the sobering wake-up call to all provincial premiers that international trade partners should not be seen as unwavering allies, and that diversifying trade is essential to maintaining economic prosperity.
One obvious place to start is looking within our own house, and breaking down the trade barriers that preposterously continue to exist between Canadian provinces.
The refrain has long been that eliminating interprovincial trade barriers is an insurmountable task.
Section 121 of the Constitution states "all Articles of the Growth, Produce, or Manufacture of any one of the Provinces shall, from and after the Union, be admitted free into each of the other Provinces." This is clear language, and should mean that we are a unified nation - one country, with one market.
However, despite this clear language in the Constitution, trade barriers continue to be high.
Former Canadian Chamber of Commerce president Perrin Beatty has stated there are many different levels of barriers to tackle, including mobility barriers and commercial barriers, some of which have existed since confederation.
According to a recent post on X by former policy advisor to Québec's Minister of Finance Jean Philippe Fournier, even though the political will existed at a certain point in Quebec (notably, the province that is most closed off to interprovincial trade), the minister was stopped from dropping barriers by the realization that each province was creating regulations in silos without taking into consideration the actions of other provinces.
These rules ended up creating niche interest groups of companies who adapted to these regulations and would lobby the government not to standardize with other provinces so they could stay in business.
And despite text in Section 121 that reads clearly, the Supreme Court of Canada's restrictive interpretation of it has taken out all its teeth. Section 121 was considered in the Supreme Court case of R. v. Comeau, which involved a man who bought some beer for personal consumption in Quebec and brought it over the border to his home in New Brunswick. He was caught and handed a $300 ticket, and took his case all the way to the Supreme Court with the help of the legal charity the Canadian Constitution Foundation.
While Gerard Comeau had success at the lower court, the Supreme Court found Section 121 does not allow absolute free trade across provinces.
Provinces can adopt laws and regulations that restrict trade if they show the overall aim is for another purpose, like "public health." This has allowed provinces to impose all kinds of trade restrictions under the guise of some other purpose.
This was an unfortunate result that should be seen as inconsistent with the plain reading of the constitution, but could easily be addressed through political action.
Provinces could choose to enact freer trade, which would unite us as a country during this time of deep economic uncertainty. The political will to tackle interprovincial trade appears to be getting stronger. Conservative Leader Pierre Poilievre recently released a video questioning why it's easier to trade with other countries than within Canada itself.
Reports also show the federal government and the premiers are finally having conversations about the positive effects of breaking down these barriers.
Team Canada and "Buy Canadian" are not complete until buying Canadian means having free trade to do exactly that within the borders of our own country.
Sabine El-Chidiac is the
Canadian Policy Associate
at the Consumer Choice Centre. Christine Van Geyn is the
litigation director at the
Canadian Constitution
Foundation.
CAPTION:

CREDIT: Sabine El-Chidiac and Christine Van Geyn;

$200 'tax rebate' cheques are being sent to dead Ontarians. Here's what to do if one lands in your mailbox

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/200-tax-rebate-cheques-are-being-sent-dead/docview/3164582735/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=%24200+%27tax+rebate%27+cheques+are+being+sent+to+dead+Ontarians.+Here%27s+what+to+do+if+one+lands+in+your+mailbox&amp;title=The+Hamilton+Spectator+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2025-02-08&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Hamilton+Spectator+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Last week, 90-year-old Constance Williams was surprised to receive a "taxpayer rebate" cheque addressed to her deceased sister, Hazel.
"I figured it was a mistake," said Williams. "I did not open (the envelope)."
Instead, she mailed it back to the Ministry of Finance with a note explaining that her sister had died last year on Jan. 1.
Fifteen million Ontarians are slated to receive a $200 cheque from the provincial government, a controversial move expected to cost the treasury $3 billion.
The rollout of the program, which began on Jan. 17, has experienced some challenges, including cheques addressed to names that individuals haven't used in years.
And it appears Williams is not the only person to get a cheque in the mail addressed to the dead.
Commenters on the online forum Reddit have talked about receiving cheques meant for recently deceased relatives.
"My mother got Ford's check today for the $200 thing for my decreased father. We're wondering what we should do about it," wrote one Redditor, who did not respond to requests for comment.
Here's what to do if you get such a cheque in the mail.
What to do about the cheques
Williams filed her sister's taxes on her behalf last April, making her sister eligible for the rebate posthumously. Williams assumed she wouldn't be able to deposit the cheque, given that it was addressed to someone who didn't have a bank account.
When issuing the cheques, the Ministry of Finance used "the most recent tax data available, as is standard practice for provincial and federal tax payments," a spokesperson said in an email.
Cheques can be cashed by the executor of the estate by bringing proof of death to a bank, the email said, noting that different financial institutions may have different processes.
Anyone having issues depositing their cheques should contact their financial institution, the statement added. Otherwise, people can call ServiceOntario at 1-833-351-0409, the ministry said.
A challenging rollout
A scam alert recently appeared on the province's "taxpayer rebate" webpage warning residents receiving the cheques to be on the lookout for scammers.
Some people, meanwhile, have struggled to deposit their cheques because they've been printed with the wrong names, such as maiden names they haven't used in years.
Williams thinks the cheques were a political move meant to buy votes. There are better uses for the money, she said. For one, it could have been used to support the hospital system, added Williams, a retired pediatric nurse and researcher, or toward the education system or food banks.
What to do if you haven't received your cheque yet
Waiting for your cheque can be pretty anxiety-provoking, especially if you're still recovering from holiday spending. The government is working "as quickly as possible" to distribute the cheques, said a government spokesperson in an email.
Your cheque may still be on its way.
You can check your address and rebate status on the ServiceOntario website. You'll just need an account login, the last four digits or your Social Insurance Number, your date of birth, and the postal code of the mailing address you used in your tax return.
You can't get your cheque by direct deposit; the "rebates" are being paid by cheque only. So check your mailbox if you haven't in a while.
For more information visit Ontario.ca/TaxpayerRebate.
CAPTION: Doug Ford's government began sending out taxpayer rebate cheques on Jan. 17, but some residents raised questions after receiving some addressed to deceased loved ones.

Subject: Social insurance numbers; Financial institutions

Document 189 of 500

Another way to shop

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/another-way-shop/docview/3164582646/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=unknown&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Another+way+to+shop&amp;title=The+Spectator&amp;issn=11899417&amp;date=2025-02-08&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.18&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Spectator&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: A looming trade war has everyone stressed - politicians, businesses, consumers. Not only will tariffs hurt our economy, they will also hurt our wallets - or will they?
A trade war is a dark cloud, to be sure, but could there be a silver lining?
Already, Canadians are looking more closely at labels, not just for ingredients, but for the origin of products, and asking: "Do we really want this?"
Who is making it, and from what and where?
Shopping locally and patriotically is all well and good, but is it enough? Should we be asking whether we need it at all?
In a world where so many have so little, do we need so much?
We waste food on an appalling scale. We buy stuff we don't use. We shop for clothes we never wear. We replace things that aren't broken.
It is hard on our wallets, on our psyches, on the planet.
The Earth is bending under the weight of it all. One way or another, we can't afford it.
U.S. President Donald Trump is obsessed by his country's trade deficit, but isn't it rooted in Americans' voracious appetite for stuff, the cheaper the better?
Canadians are much the same. Our homes are bigger than ever, but our living space is constantly shrinking. Our basements, cupboards, closets and garages are overflowing with stuff. Self-storage units are ubiquitous. The dumps are at capacity.
And still we insist governments make it easy and inexpensive for us to throw stuff away.
Is it time for a bigger rethink?
While international trade is admittedly complex, and often counterintuitive, consider this: we import far more bottled water into the Great Lakes region than we export.
That's right - ships, trains and trucks laden with bottled water are passing each other across the oceans, canals, railways and roads. This, remember, in a country with an endless supply of cheap, safe, healthy, delicious free-flowing water from household faucets that make many nations green with envy.
And yet, will any dalliance with patriotic shopping now be overtaken in short order by the usual determinants: esthetics and price?
Will we continue to buy stuff because it looks good (or we've been told looks good) and is inexpensive (or we've been told is inexpensive)?
Indeed, as shoppers - and as Canadians - we tend to do what we're told.
At the moment, we're listening to Premier Doug Ford, Prime Minister Justin Trudeau and the media, who say we should act individually to fight tariffs.
But soon enough, will we default to the marketing gurus, experts at making us do what they want, never mind what it means for tariffs or our credit card balances?
Subarus are great for wilderness driving; let's get one for highway commuting. Interesting people drink Heineken; I'll have one of those.
It's a famous brand; we need to join the crowd.
Jennifer Lopez likes Coach bags; they must be good. Timothée Chalamet uses Bleu, a men's fragrance; maybe that would help my social life. George Clooney drinks Nespresso coffee. Mmm.
Ultimately, are they are more powerful than Ford or Trudeau, and will they trump patriotism or price, to say nothing of quality, healthiness or the environment?
Perhaps the real win will be a more responsible approach to consumerism, one that balances our needs with our wants, and the economy with the planet.
Paul Berton is editor-in-chief
of Metroland Media and the author of "Shopomania: Our Obsession with Possession."
CAPTION: Shopping locally and patriotically is all well and good, but is it enough, Paul Berton asks. Should we be asking whether we need it at all?Julia Nikhinson The Associated Press file photo

CREDIT: Paul Berton The Hamilton Spectator Opinion;

Subject: Trade disputes; Shopping; Bottled water; Tariffs

Document 190 of 500

Donald Trump is not joking about making Canada the 51st state, Justin Trudeau warns in hot mic comments

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/donald-trump-is-not-joking-about-making-canada/docview/3164380241/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Donald+Trump+is+not+joking+about+making+Canada+the+51st+state%2C+Justin+Trudeau+warns+in+hot+mic+comments&amp;title=The+Hamilton+Spectator+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2025-02-07&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Hamilton+Spectator+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: U.S. President Donald Trump's statements about annexing Canada are not a joke, and are driven in part by his desire to see the U.S. benefit from Canada's critical mineral resources, Prime Minister Justin Trudeau has said in a series of candid comments made behind closed doors.
"I suggest that not only does the Trump administration know how many critical minerals we have but that may be even why they keep talking about absorbing us and making us the 51st state," the prime minister told a Toronto gathering of Canadian company executives, industry and union leaders on Friday morning.
"They're very aware of our resources, of what we have and they very much want to be able to benefit from those," Trudeau said in answer to a question from the floor, after media had been ushered out of the room.
"But Mr. Trump has it in mind that one of the easiest ways of doing that is absorbing our country. And it is a real thing."
Trudeau continued to talk about "my conversation with" the president, but his comments - inadvertently broadcast after media were ordered out of the session where he had opened the economic summit - were cut off.
A worker was ordered to pull the plug on the speaker after staff realized the session was audible.
The economic summit opened with Trudeau thanking the group for coming to share information and plan tactical and strategic moves for the coming weeks, months and years.
As he finished, the prime minister acknowledged that not everyone in the room is a fan of his, but he welcomed their input.
In the portion of his remarks that were open to the media, Trudeau won applause for stressing the need to drop interprovincial trade barriers.
"It's about time we had genuine free trade within Canada."
He said there's no lack of political will because all premiers say they support the idea.
But, he said, there are "challenges within bureaucracies to say, oh, but it'll be hard to recognize teaching certificates from one court of the country versus another. It'll be hard to get proper labour mobility. Or there's a reason we have a specific tire requirement on our trucks for our province, or a specific need for a first aid kit in those trucks."
He stressed the need to diversify trade internationally, adding, "Geography means we're always going to both benefit and be challenged by trade with the United States."
Nearly 200 industry and trade association representatives, union leaders, Canada-U.S. advisory council members, and executives from across a number of sectors attended the morning meeting, with a plenary session and Q&A with the prime minister and sectoral workshops after that.
The names of those attending were not released, and media access to them was restricted after Trudeau's opening remarks.
Among the business leaders in attendance were Teck Resources CEO Jonathan Price, Candace Laing, CEO of the Canadian Chamber of Commerce, Daniel Tisch, CEO of the Ontario Chamber of Commerce, and David Ossip, CEO of tech firm Dayforce. Lisa Baiton of the Canadian Association of Petroleum Producers, and Dan Kelly, CEO of the Canadian Federation of Independent Business, were also in attendance. Charlie Angelakos, vice president of global external affairs at McCain Foods, was also on-site.
Also spotted in the audience were executives with Manulife, Power Corp.'s Paul Genest, Hydro Quebec's Michael Sabia, TIFF's Cameron Bailey, Navdeep Bains with Rogers, BCE's president Mirko Bibic, and Rob Prichard, chair of Via Rail's high-speed division.
Progressive Conservative Leader Doug Ford, running for a third term as premier in a snap election he called for Feb. 27, scoffed at the summit as he campaigned Friday at an auto parts plant in Scarborough.
"I don't need to be in a room looking at the people I've talked to 1000 times," Ford told reporters.
"You know what the Trump administration wants? They've been very, very clear. They've been clear to me. They've been clear everywhere in North America. They need to tighten up the border. It's been four days" since the tariff threat was postponed to March 1.
"Let's stop the flow of drugs going back and forth across the border," he added, recommending retired general Rick Hillier as the fentanyl czar.
"Or any general, or any police, former chief, police chief, let's get this done. Listen to the customer. The customer is the Trump administration, our largest trading partner in the world. If he's saying this is a serious crisis, let's show them. Don't talk. I'm sick and tired of politicians talking. Let's get the helicopters in the air, the planes and the boots and the drones and the ships going up and down the water. I'm done with the talking. Let's show some action."
Progressive Conservative Leader Doug Ford, running for a third term as Ontario premier in a snap election he called for Feb. 27, scoffed at the summit as he campaigned Friday at an auto parts plant in Scarborough.
"I don't need to be in a room looking at the people I've talked to 1,000 times," Ford told reporters.
"You know what the Trump administration wants? They've been very, very clear," Ford Said. "They need to tighten up the border. It's been four days" since the tariff threat was postponed to March 1.
"Let's stop the flow of drugs going back and forth across the border," he added, recommending retired general Rick Hillier as the fentanyl czar, or any military "general," police chief or former chief.
"Let's get this done. Listen to the customer. The customer is the Trump administration, our largest trading partner in the world. If he's saying this is a serious crisis, let's show them. Don't talk," he said.
"I'm sick and tired of politicians talking. Let's get the helicopters in the air, the planes and the boots and the drones and the ships going up and down the water. I'm done with the talking. Let's show some action."
Industry Minister François-Philippe Champagne later told the Star that the government is not rethinking its critical minerals strategy which he has pitched as a way to deepen co-operation with the United States, and instead said it gives Canada "leverage" in its talks with Trump.
"They need us more than ever," he said "They understand, particularly the military guys, they are very worried about strategy in terms of being their partner, in terms of critical minerals, yeah, I would say this is a great leverage for Canada, because the military folks understand that they cannot rebuild their industrial base, their defence, military industrial base, without Canada."
Among organized labour heads like Unifor President Lana Payne the concern is that Trump is not kidding about his threats.
"I'm taking everything seriously right now because I have a lot of members whose jobs are at stake in this climate of chaos and instability, and the more we see of that, the worse it gets for us, which is why it's so important that Canada is coming together right now."
Payne said Trump's tariff and annexation threats are scaring away investment, according to many of the business leaders in the room at the summit.
"What was most poignant for me are the folks that were reiterating what we have been seeing on the ground floor in real time, and that is the threat of the tariffs themselves are already having an impact on our economy, on Canadian workplaces and on Canadian workers, and that is something that we are all going to have to grapple with, because the threats are not going to end, and in fact, they may escalate before we get to a place where we get a resolution."
In an interview, Beth Burke, head of the Canadian American Business Council, said most Americans don't believe Trump is serious about his annexation threats.
Burke sees it as "as part of the broader negotiating strategy. It's about leverage and showing your strength over your counterpart that you're negotiating with. And I think he uses it as a way to assert dominance and show that the U.S. has the upper hand. While I know it is wildly offensive to Canadians, I think a lot of Americans think he's joking and see it as a joke and don't fully understand and appreciate the back story of how offensive it is."
Burke said she is optimistic that tariffs will be ultimately avoided.
"I think there's always a deal to be made, I think President Trump is always looking for that deal. So I'm hopeful."
Trudeau's remarks about critical minerals recognize their value across a number of sectors from energy, defence and beyond.
There are plenty of reasons Trump could be interested in Canada's critical minerals, particularly in the high-tech industry.
"Without critical minerals, there would essentially be no tech industry to speak of," said tech analyst and author Carmi Levy. "From batteries to chips to the myriad components that make up smartphones, computers, cars, and every other tech-laden tool of modern life, everything we define as "electronic" requires at least some critical minerals as part of their manufacture."
Canada might not be military superpower, but it's a big player in critical minerals, Levy said.
"Canada is easily a global superpower given its well documented reserves of such minerals," said Levy.
With files from Rob Ferguson, Queen's Park bureau
Correction -- Feb. 7, 2024
This article has been updated. A previous version incorrectly stated that David Ossip is CEO of tech firm Ceridian. He is CEO of Dayforce. Ceridian rebranded and changed its legal name to Dayforce in 2024.
CAPTION: "They're very aware of our resources, of what we have and they very much want to be able to benefit from those," Justin Trudeau said Friday to a gathering ofcompany executives, industry and union leaders.

Document 191 of 500

Here are important dates for eligible voters for the Feb. 27 Ontario election

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/here-are-important-dates-eligible-voters-feb-27/docview/3164379774/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Here+are+important+dates+for+eligible+voters+for+the+Feb.+27+Ontario+election&amp;title=The+Hamilton+Spectator+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2025-02-07&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Hamilton+Spectator+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: The 2025 Ontario election is underway, and the average person may be thinking about how to prepare for the Feb. 27 vote.
When Premier Doug Ford visited Lt.-Gov. Edith Dumont Jan. 28 requesting she formally dissolve the 43rd parliament of Ontario, he officially triggered an early provincial election, estimated to cost about $189 million, said Greg Essensa, Ontario's chief electoral officer. A scheduled provincial election had already been set for June 2026.
This will be the first February provincial election since one was held Feb. 27, 1883.
Ford has repeatedly said he needs a new mandate from Ontario voters to protect the province from potential increased tariffs from U.S. President Donald Trump.
Here are the key dates for the public to know about the 44th general election. All times are Eastern Standard Time:
Jan. 30 - Feb. 26: Eligible voters can submit ballots before election day at their respective election office. They can also request a home visit until 6 p.m. on Feb. 26.
Jan. 29 - Feb. 21: Residents can submit their application to vote by mail until 6 p.m. on Feb. 21. Apply online or downland an application at elections.on.ca. Officials need to receive your voting kit by 6 p.m. on Feb. 27.
Feb. 13: Voters can view the complete list of candidates for their riding at elections.on.ca after nominations close at 2 p.m.
Feb. 17 - 19: Election officials will visit participating hospitals with voting kits to allow patients and staff to vote.
Feb. 17 - 22: Voter information cards will be mailed out to residents. The documents will identify where and when to vote. Eligible voters will need to provide any updates or add any voter information to the registry by Feb. 17.
Feb. 20: Assistive Voting Technology will be installed for use at all local election offices until polling day.
Feb. 20 - 22: Eligible voters can submit a ballot at any advance voting location within their electoral district from 10 a.m. to 8 p.m. Visit voterinformationservice.elections.on.ca for dates, times and locations.
Feb. 26 - 27: Political advertising is prohibited.
Feb. 27: Eligible voters can submit a ballot on election day from 9 a.m. to 9 p.m. Visit voterinformationservice.election.on.ca for assigned locations. If you are a student studying in Ontario, but living away from home, you can vote in the electoral district of your permanent home, or in the electoral district of your address while attending school at on-campus voting locations.
Feb. 27: Election surveys that have not previously been made public may not be published from 12:01 a.m. to 9 p.m.
To be eligible to vote, a person must be 18 years of age or older, a Canadian citizen and a resident of Ontario.
An eligible voter will also need identification. If you are on the register, you will receive a voter information card in the mail that will detail where to vote. A person must bring the voter information card and one piece of identification showing your name to receive a ballot.
You can still vote if you do not receive a voter information card, but your name may not be on the register. You must bring one piece of identification that shows both your name and home address. Photo identification is not required.
Identification accepted includes most documents issued by the federal, provincial or municipal governments. These include:
Ontario driver's licencebirth certificatemarriage certificateOntario health cardCanadian Armed Forces ID cardCanadian passportcertificate of Canadian citizenshipcertificate of Indian Statuscitizenship cardfirearms licenseOld Age Security Identification card
Other identification items include:
blood donor cardcredit/debit cardemployee cardunion cardstudent cardchild tax benefit statementproperty assessment noticeincome tax noticecancelled personalized chequesigned loan or financial agreement with a financial institutionutility bill
CAPTION: Ontarians go to the polls on Feb. 27, the first time a provincial election has been held during the month of February since 1883.

Subject: Provincial elections; Election districts; Local elections; Voters; Identification; Political advertising

ONTARIO ELECTION 2025: With Trump's tariff threats on hold, is Ontario's doctor shortage now the top election issue?

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/ontario-election-2025-with-trumps-tariff-threats/docview/3164379755/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=ONTARIO+ELECTION+2025%3A+With+Trump%27s+tariff+threats+on+hold%2C+is+Ontario%27s+doctor+shortage+now+the+top+election+issue%3F&amp;title=The+Hamilton+Spectator+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2025-02-07&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Hamilton+Spectator+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Now that Donald Trump's tariff threats are on hold, the Ontario Medical Association hopes voters will turn their attention to another crisis, the province's ongoing doctor shortage.
OMA president Dr. Dominik Nowak plans to visit election battleground areas of Mississauga and Brampton on Thursday to meet with local physicians and discuss the shortages.
Long lineups in Walkerton
Nowak, a family doctor at Women's College Hospital in Toronto, pointed to Walkerton, Ont., where more than 1,000 people lined up in the cold last month for a chance to land a family doctor.
"What we saw in Walkerton is a heartbreaking example of how far Ontarians are being pushed to access basic care," Nowak said in a news release. "Health care is something we pride ourselves on as Canadians, yet our system is struggling to deliver even the most fundamental services."
Provincewide doctor shortages
Citing figures from HealthForce Ontario, the OMA said Ontario has 2,600 vacant physician positions across the province, a number that has remained virtually unchanged since last January.
Here are the areas of Ontario with the highest physician vacancies, according to HealthForce Ontario:
Toronto: 699Ottawa: 196Hamilton: 57London: 56Kitchener: 52Thunder Bay: 45St. Catharines: 42Peterborough: 41Sudbury: 30Windsor: 28 Kingston: 27Barrie: 21 Wait times for critical surgeries
With a provincial election on Feb. 27, the organization representing 43,000 physicians and health professionals is calling for immediate action through a campaign called "Stop the Crisis."
The OMA says more than 2.5 million Ontario residents don't have access to a family doctor and the number is projected to climb to 4.4 million by next year.
Patients are also facing long wait times for critical surgeries. The OMA said the average wait for hip replacement surgery is 276 days, 303 days for knee replacements, and 282 days for cataract surgery.
Policy solutions
The governing Progressive Conservatives and opposition parties have offered various solutions to address the issue. The Tories have pledged $1.8 billion to connect two million more Ontarians to publicly funded family doctors or primary care teams within four years.
The Liberals recently announced if the party forms government it would invest $3.1 billion to attract, recruit, retain, and integrate 3,100 family doctors by 2029.
Stop the Crisis campaign
The OMA's Stop the Crisis campaign offers the following objectives for parties contesting the current provincial election:
Ensure every Ontarian has access to a family doctor Address emergency department closures and overcrowding Strengthen northern and rural health care Reduce wait times for specialists and diagnostics
"These solutions are ready to be implemented and would provide direct, meaningful, and lasting improvements for physicians and their patients," said Kimberly Moran, the association's CEO. "There is no time left to wait. Improvements to our health system must start now."
Disclaimer: Reader survey results are not scientific. As the informal findings of a survey presented to the readers of this site, they reflect the opinions of those who have chosen to participate. The survey is available online to anyone interested in taking it. This poll does not restrict the number of votes each person can cast.
CAPTION: Dr. Dominik Nowak, president of the Ontario Medical Association, said 2.5 million Ontarians are without a family doctor. The figure could climb to 4.4 million within a year without urgent action.Hundreds of people lined up outside the Walkerton Legion Hall on a cold and snowy day recently to sign up for a new family doctor.

Document 193 of 500

Ford wants you not to vote. You have to vote

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/ford-wants-you-not-vote-have/docview/3164231005/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Ford+wants+you+not+to+vote.+You+have+to+vote&amp;title=The+Hamilton+Spectator+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2025-02-07&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Hamilton+Spectator+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: There is an asteroid that may hit Earth in 2032. I've discovered recently that I kind of love reading about asteroids aimed at us. I don't think I'm alone.
In 2018, Doug Ford declared he was a Republican and he loved Donald Trump.
He was quoted this week saying he was 100 per cent happy Trump won the recent election. He didn't know his buddy would be a bigger political gift to him than the COVID-19 virus. The onset of a global pandemic let him "aw shucks" his way into having us believe he felt our pain.
Trump aiming a steamroller at Canada and dropping it into drive has allowed Ford to throw himself in front of a raging tiger and assure us he will defend us at all costs.
Neither is true, but why let facts get in the way of a good story?
I am deeply exhausted. I would love to be saved from all of this, but Ford is incapable of making that so, and we need to stop looking for a hero. We are the heroes; not the guy most notable for opaque backroom deals, backtracking, apologizing, or getting sued. So much egg. So much face.
Ford said he called an election because Trump's tariffs were far too great a threat for him to conquer without a new mandate. He has had an iron grip on this province since 2018, with back-to-back majority governments. His silent MPPs are hauling their blue signs out of storage for another round of getting re-elected for doing little.
He has his mandate and doesn't need to burn $189 million more of our dollars to prove it.
The tariffs were a convenient ruse for a man who needed another crisis to wield fear as a cudgel.
The threat was enough to get him to "tear up" a ridiculous contract with Elon Musk's Starlink - $100 million of your bucks. There were Canadian options on the table. As you may be noticing, Musk is currently dismantling the United States of America from the inside out, and ramping up to do far worse, apparently unfettered by law.
When Trump called a timeout on tariffs for a few weeks, Ford decided it was now cool to go forward with the Starlink contract. With a man who operates outside established law. A man who has infiltrated the U.S. government with a bunch of kids, like some terrible movie.
Starlink back on? Ford must no longer sees the tariffs - and Trump and Musk - as a threat.
Then Ford no longer needs a new "mandate" and a bogus election. Doug, it's a threat or it's not a threat. You can't suck and blow at the same time, especially when Ontarians are on the hook for it.
Ford has lurched from crisis to crisis, doubled down before reversing numerous times, ripped up the science centre with zero notice, razed Ontario Place because we need a spa, started paving prime farmland, promised a tunnel under the 401 (I don't even know where to start with that one), and gutted our health care as nurses and doctors are pushed beyond burnout while their private-sector counterparts rake in government money.
Talk to any teacher you know for a glimpse of what is happening in our schools.
Ending rent controls means Ford has fuelled renters' nightmares and destabilized a huge swath of Ontarians.
Musk and Trump are united against people like me and you.
Ford has signalled his words - especially his campaigning words - are hollow. They are now dangerous. You build an alligator-filled moat between your flock and people like this - you don't give them $100 million.
Keep buying Canadian, keep an eye on your friends and neighbours, and know in the heart of every Canadian burns a bright ember that understands how wonderful this country is, and how far we will go to protect it.
Rediscovering this has been Trump's gift to us.
I'm still Team Asteroid.
CAPTION: Doug Ford - who at one time was quite happy to see Donald Trump in office - has lurched from one crisis to the next, Lorraine Sommerfeld says.

Subject: Political campaigns; Tariffs

Business indexing term: Subject: Tariffs

Location: United States--US

People: Trump, Donald J

Title: Ford wants you not to vote. You have to vote

NDP, Green leaders converge on east Hamilton

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/ndp-green-leaders-converge-on-east-hamilton/docview/3164230743/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=NDP%2C+Green+leaders+converge+on+east+Hamilton&amp;title=The+Spectator&amp;issn=11899417&amp;date=2025-02-07&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.4&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Spectator&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: NDP Leader Marit Stiles and Green Leader Mike Schreiner both travelled to Hamilton for the first time in this snap election campaign Thursday to rally supporters.
Stiles visited the campaign headquarters of East Hamilton-Stoney Creek candidate Zaigham Butt to reiterate NDP rent control promises and vow to "flip this seat from blue to orange."
The east city seat was held by the NDP for 15 years under longtime former MPP Paul Miller until he was ejected from the party in 2022 over anti-Muslim allegations, which he denied. Tory candidate Neil Lumsden went on to win the seat in the election later that year.
In the same riding, Schreiner visited the headquarters of candidate Pascale Marchand on Thursday to kick off the campaign for Hamilton-area Green candidates and highlight a promise to build more affordable homes within the city's existing urban boundary.
Hamilton council is trying to maintain its existing urban boundary and has clashed with the most recent Tory-led provincial government, which infamously opened the Greenbelt and thousands of acres of farmland to development before reversing both decisions in 2023.
CAPTION: Above, Ontario NDP Leader Marit Stiles stops by the campaign office of Hamilton East-Stoney Creek candidate Zaigham Butt, right.Right, Ontario Green Party Leader Mike Schreiner meets with supporters in east Hamilton.Cathie Coward photos The Hamilton Spectator

CREDIT: Hamilton Spectator Staff

Ford wants you not to vote. You have to vote: The tariffs were a convenient ruse for a man who needed another crisis to wield fear as a cudgel
