Document 196 of 500

Letters

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/letters/docview/3164229615/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=unknown&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Letters&amp;title=The+Spectator&amp;issn=11899417&amp;date=2025-02-07&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.12&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Spectator&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Inquest delay is ludicrous
Re: Coroner's inquest called into death of woman shot by Hamilton police, Feb. 5
If ever anyone doubted our criminal justice system needs revamping, particularly concerning police Special Investigations Unit probes, the poster child for reform would be the case of Robyn Garlow. I will not speculate on whether police were justified in her shooting, but for more than six years to pass before a coroner calls an inquest is ludicrous. It is theoretically possible to walk around the world six times within that span.
David Shupe, Dundas
Council right to reject expansion
Re: Hamilton councillors reject pitch for fast-tracked urban expansion, Feb. 5
Thank you, Hamilton council, for doing the right thing in turning down developers' expansion of the urban boundary in Glanbrook.
As everyone knows, the housing crisis is affecting folks currently under nylon or canvas, without kitchens and bathrooms, having no vehicle or proper clothing to attend job interviews.
Million-dollar-plus homes in Glanbrook are far from government offices, medical services, discount grocery stores, even bus stops. And we're all aware of situations where "10 per cent of affordable homes" were nowhere to be seen when development is complete.
Empty lots, empty city housing units, empty buildings per work-from-home-now employees, empty schools are waiting to be renovated to homes, affordable to buy or rent. Even homes purportedly confiscated years ago for LRT could certainly be residences for at least the next 10 years, given LRT's current quicksand.
Developers, please resurrect wartime home plans that are still lovely homes and streets today. Get a consult from Pennsylvania Amish on how to get the job done. Please. People are suffering.
Lynda Pierce, Mount Hope
This situation
is unacceptable
Re: Sparks fly as Hamilton council scraps another homeless mini-cabin location, Feb. 6
Here we are just into February, these tiny cabins/mini-houses were supposed to be ready to launch in January and council is still debating where to put them. Are you serious, city council? Please get it together, because this situation is absolutely unacceptable.
Lisa Johnston, Hamilton
HATS should
follow Bulldogs
The effort that the people at HATS (Hamilton Alliance for Tiny Shelters) put into their project is truly amazing. Volunteers building cabins, trying for years to help people. Council and staff waffle and delay and butt heads then do nothing.
If I were associated with HATS, I'd be doing a Bulldogs thing. Head to Brantford and help the homeless people there. Where a council can make decisions that make a difference.
Rob Millen, Hamilton
We need land for food production
The latest episode of Donald Trump's chaotic and irresponsible edicts reinforces our need to ensure food security for our future. A future that includes more climate change and a less than trustworthy food exporter/neighbour like the U.S. We cannot rely on imported food as other countries will have to deal with climate change and will look after their own populations' needs before they export food to us. Keeping farm land from development provides an important resource for the future of the people who live and work in the GTHA. One acre of good farmland can produce 11,500 kilograms (25,400 pounds) of potatoes. Other crops such as lettuce yield less (11,000 pounds or 5,000 kg), but still add a significant amount of food to feed the growing population of the GTHA.
The current need for housing, although a critical situation, should not be used as a reason to remove farmland from production. According to the City of Hamilton, there is sufficient land within our urban boundaries to provide the amount of housing that we need to meet the current and proposed increase in Hamilton's population. Developers and some politicians cannot see beyond their bank balance or the next election. These people will reduce farmland and build single detached homes and in doing so will create a very serious crisis for our children and all the generations to follow - food shortage. We have a chance to elect people and parties that recognize the perils of climate change denial and the critical importance of farmland for food sustainability for future generations.
Bill Sullivan, Hamilton
Put the focus
on fentanyl users
Re: Poilievre would impose life sentences for trafficking more than 40 mg of fentanyl, Feb. 5
I watched Pierre Poilievre's "Breaking Bad" photo op in Vancouver yesterday. Without a doubt, the fentanyl crisis in Canada has resulted in thousands of deaths of our citizens across our country. We need to stop these preventable deaths. The question is how?
Poilievre believes mandatory life sentences for those convicted of trafficking over 40 milligrams of fentanyl is the solution. I disagree.
The focus should be on those individuals who are suffering from an addiction to fentanyl. The Canadian government should increase the necessary resources and support. This would allow our fellow citizens to recover from their addiction to fentanyl, regain their health and once again rejoin society in a productive and prosperous way.
Mary Elizabeth Toth,
Ancaster
Buying Canadian with pride
After we gentle-mannered Canadian neighbours were rhetorically bashed in the head from behind by President Donald Trump with threats of punishing tariffs, I don't take my eyes off of the news for more than say 20 minutes at a time in order to know how best to react and to be prepared alongside of the rest of Canadians with the best logical plan of action to stand up to a man who actually wants to control the world.
My plan is to join other Canadians in trying to buy "Made In Canada" as much as is possible. It's the least I can do and maybe realistically the only thing I can do since I am not wealthy.
So when I was drinking some Canadian apple juice instead of U.S. orange juice yesterday, I felt an overwhelming emotion of pride because that apple juice represented my patriotic heart and reminded me of every single Canadian soldier or anyone involved in protecting democracy around the world and lost their lives, and my eyes filled with tears.
We all know that saying, "an apple a day keeps the doctor away." Well, maybe, just maybe, a Canadian apple a day can keep a dictator away!
Ana Long, Hamilton
Getting the
message out
I received my rebate from Doug Ford today and had a great idea on how to spend it. I decided to have a slogan hat made much like Ford did for himself. Only my hat states, "Ontario voters not for sale."
Mike Langille, Hamilton
Tear down
trade barriers
Tariffs have been used by the Americans against Canada off and on for more than 100 years. And yet, here we are today, unprepared and facing them again. We've learned nothing in 100 years. We snipe at the Americans with moral superiority and blissfully ignore the elephant in the room - interprovincial trade barriers. It's easier to trade north, south than east, west. In R. v. Comeau, the Supreme Court of Canada ruled against a New Brunswick man bringing back more than 12 pints of beer from Quebec, citing the province's right to manage supply and demand of liquor. Really? Only in Canada! We act like 10 squabbling nations. When will Canada pull up its big boy pants and get rid of all interprovincial trade barriers so that we can speak to the Americans with one unified voice?
Donald Zelem, Simcoe
It's a topsy-turvy world out there
Re: Trump says he wants U.S.
to take charge of Gaza Strip
at news conference with
Netanyahu, Feb. 4
When an accused war criminal from Israel and a convicted felon from the United States meet in the Oval Office to sort out world issues, the world seems topsy-turvy. What could seem more bizarre? But there is that feeling that we "ain't" seen nothing yet.
Ray Varey, Dundas

Document 197 of 500

Next premier must protect Greenbelt

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/next-premier-must-protect-greenbelt/docview/3164229320/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=unknown&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Next+premier+must+protect+Greenbelt&amp;title=The+Spectator&amp;issn=11899417&amp;date=2025-02-07&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.11&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Spectator&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: All of the Ontario party leaders and candidates in the Feb. 27 election need to understand that the Greenbelt is not just something nice to have - it's absolutely essential to Ontario's future success and security.
It's not just about green space - it's about our economy, our food security and our water resources. The Greenbelt is already one of the best tools we have for taking care of what matters most. We must be looking at ways to protect and expand it, not chip away at it.
Ontarians understand the importance of the Greenbelt. Polls show consistently that more than eight out of 10 residents want it protected. They understand that the Greenbelt is land that puts food on our tables, water in our glasses, money in people's pockets and protects us from the worst excesses caused by climate change.
The Greenbelt is also an asset that contributes $9.6 billion to Ontario's GDP and supports 177,000 jobs. A protected Greenbelt ensures that our economy remains secure and resilient, regardless of whatever tariffs and trade barriers other countries may impose.
Here's the thing: Ontario relies heavily on food imports. A study in 2014 found that our province imports roughly $10 billion more food than we export.
We get a lot of what we eat from other parts of the world, which means when there's a disruption in global trade - like tariff wars or supply chain issues - we feel it.
The Greenbelt can help offset that reliance by providing local, homegrown food, especially when global supply chains start to buckle. Keeping this land protected helps keep food affordable for Ontario families.
The Greenbelt has demonstrated already how crucial it is for securing our food and water. Right now, there are more than 750,000 acres of farmland protected within it; had this not been protected, it surely would have been gobbled up by urban sprawl.
Outside the Greenbelt, nearly half a million acres of farmland have been lost in Ontario since 1991 and were still losing an average of 320 acres per day. This massive decline puts us at risk of having to rely even more on food imports; the Greenbelt protects against this.
While U.S. President Donald Trump muses about breaking agreements and grabbing Canadian water, the Greenbelt provides clean, fresh water to more than seven million people in Ontario. This is free to us - all it requires is protecting the Greenbelt so it can do its job.
The Greenbelt also helps prevent $224 million in flood damage every year.
With climate change throwing more extreme weather our way, it's clear that the Greenbelt's role in securing clean water and flood prevention for future generations is more important than ever.
The pressure is mounting. Global agricultural conglomerates and developers are pushing harder than ever to erase this treasure for their own benefit.
After public pressure, the premier and his government said it would protect the Greenbelt, but they still vow to ram two needless superhighways right through it, which would cause permanent damage to its farmland, water resources and forests.
The public needs to ask all party leaders who seek to form the next government: Will you protect, strengthen and expand the Greenbelt?
Ontario can't afford to treat its Greenbelt like an afterthought and certainly not as a place that can be easily chopped up and paved over. We need to protect it permanently to ensure that our province remains resilient in the face of economic shocks, climate change, and all the trade and economic challenges the future might throw at us.
David Crombie is former chair of the Greenbelt Council. Margaret Prophet is co-ordinator of the Ontario Greenbelt Alliance.
CAPTION:

CREDIT: David Crombie and Margaret Prophet;

Subject: Agricultural land; Food; Climate change; Water resources

Document 198 of 500

Crombie slams Ford for not curing the health-care woes

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/crombie-slams-ford-not-curing-health-care-woes/docview/3164229165/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Crombie+slams+Ford+for+not+curing+the+health-care+woes&amp;title=The+Spectator&amp;issn=11899417&amp;date=2025-02-07&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.6&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Spectator&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Liberal Leader Bonnie Crombie is rubbing salt on Doug Ford's wounds over the chronic health-care challenges that ail Ontario.
Blasting the Progressive Conservative leader as the Feb. 27 election campaign began its second week, Crombie noted treatment of patients in hospital corridors has doubled and the doctor shortage has worsened since he took office in 2018.
"Doug Ford said he'd end hallway medicine. He didn't get it done. He said he'd cut your taxes. He didn't get it done. He said he'd build 1.5 million homes. He didn't get it done," she said Thursday in Scarborough.
Crombie - whose bright red campaign bus is emblazoned with the slogan "2.5 Million People Have No Family Doctor: This is Doug Ford's Ontario" - has promised to cure the physician shortage through a slew of measures that could cost $3.1 billion.
They include spending $250 million over four years in targeted retention aimed at 2,400 doctors who have left family medicine or are nearing retirement, offering bonuses of $150,000 to repatriate Canadian doctors from abroad to Ontario, and recruiting 650 of the 6,000 family doctors currently working in other disciplines.
As well, she wants to help doctors by slashing "the administrative burden taking up 50 per cent of their time."
"Let's get rid of fax machines at long last and implement a centralized referral system and ensure that they have the right staff (and) offer more evening and weekend care," she said.
In Waterloo, Ford, who is seeking his third term in office, blamed previous Liberal governments for the shortage of physicians.
"If the Liberals didn't cut nurses and they didn't cut medical (school) seats, we wouldn't be in this spot," he told reporters after receiving the endorsement of the 25,000-member Ontario Pipe Trades Council.
"We've hired Dr. Jane Philpott to connect every single Ontarian with a family doctor," Ford said of the former federal Liberal health minister who is now director of the medical school at Queen's University in Kingston.
On the eve of Ford's snap election call, the PCs announced $1.4 billion in new funding to help Philpott expand the province's network of primary health care teams with a goal of getting every Ontarian a family doctor by 2029.
"There's no doubt that people have been waiting a long time, too long, frankly, to get connected to a family care practitioner in their community," MPP Sylvia Jones, serving as Ford's health minister, acknowledged on Jan. 27.
But that announcement raised questions as to whether the $1.4 billion is enough because there's a huge discrepancy between numbers provided by Jones (Dufferin-Caledon) and the Ontario Medical Association.
The OMA says there are currently 2.5 million Ontarians without a family doctor, and predicts that figure will rise to 4.4 million next year as the population grows and doctors retire or leave the province.
But Jones maintained just two million Ontarians will need to be connected to a family doctor in the next four years.
Ford boasted his government approved two new medical schools in Ontario. However, students will not graduate from them for several more years.
NDP Leader Marit Stiles, meanwhile, was in Hamilton to promise "real rent control" that would further protect tenants from higher costs and renovictions.
"After seven years of Doug Ford, rent is higher than ever. It's the biggest expense in your family's budget and it's only getting worse," said Stiles.
Also Thursday, Green Leader Mike Schreiner was in Guelph to tout his party's plan to increase Ontario Disability Support Program and Ontario Works payments and build 310,000 affordable homes.
"Poverty in Ontario is escalating at an alarming rate," said Schreiner, noting the Association of Municipalities of Ontario found 81,000 people were homeless in the province last year.
CAPTION: Ontario Liberal Leader Bonnie Crombie said the treatment of patients in hospital corridors has doubled and the doctor shortage has worsened since Doug Ford took office in 2018.Chris Young The Canadian Press

CREDIT: Robert Benzie, Rob Ferguson and Kristin Rushowy Toronto Star

Document 199 of 500

Wanted: Workers in Hamilton to help oversee Feb. 27 election: Elections Ontario to hire more than 55,000 across province

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/wanted-workers-hamilton-help-oversee-feb-27/docview/3164229163/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Wanted%3A+Workers+in+Hamilton+to+help+oversee+Feb.+27+election%3A+Elections+Ontario+to+hire+more+than+55%2C000+across+province&amp;title=The+Spectator&amp;issn=11899417&amp;date=2025-02-07&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.3&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Spectator&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Doug Ford isn't likely to claim credit for it, but the Progressive Conservative candidate's snap election call equals jobs: temporary Elections Ontario positions, paying $20 to $29.50 per hour, with some available to those as young as 16.
In Hamilton, that means more than 2,000 jobs spread across the city's five riding election offices, to work either election day Feb. 27, or during the three-day advance poll period from Feb. 20 to 22.
Elections Ontario, described on its website as a non-partisan office of the Legislative Assembly of Ontario administering elections, will hire for more than 55,000 positions across the province's 124 electoral districts, accommodating 7,000 voting locations.
Each election office/district will hire between 400 to 600 poll officials, Elections Ontario media co-ordinator Dave Pearce told The Spectator.
There are 14 categories of positions listed on the Elections Ontario website.
To apply online, go to jobs.elections.on.ca/en/apply.
The highest paying positions include area manager, who works election day for up to 13 hours, and also advance poll supervising deputy returning officer, who works up to three consecutive days during the advance poll period, for up to 11 hours per day.
Qualifications listed on the Elections Ontario website to fill these positions include having access to a cellphone and vehicle, availability to attend a training session, and "ability to read and observe voting procedures at the voting location."
Among the duties for the poll clerk position, for example, is "ensuring the voting location is free of any partisan campaign materials."
All of the positions require Canadian citizenship, and most require that you are at least 18 years old, apart from several information assistant jobs, that pay $20 an hour, in which you need to be 16 on election day, and among other skills "have the ability to stand and work for long stretches of time."
Two of the positions - special ballot officer and resource staff - require that you are bilingual, "as determined by the French Language Services Act."
Strength of body and mind seem required for three of the jobs, where in order to be hired you must have the "ability to understand, rationalize and resolve situations efficiently as they arise," and also the "ability to lift up to 60 lbs" in electronic equipment.
Jon Wells is a reporter
at the Hamilton Spectator. jwells@thespec.com
CAPTION: There are 14 categories of positions listed on the Elections Ontario website.The Hamilton Spectator file photo

CREDIT: Jon Wells Reporter

Feb. 7: Shooting inquest delay is ludicrous, HATS should follow the Bulldogs to Brantford and other letters to the editor

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/feb-7-shooting-inquest-delay-is-ludicrous-hats/docview/3164229086/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Feb.+7%3A+Shooting+inquest+delay+is+ludicrous%2C+HATS+should+follow+the+Bulldogs+to+Brantford+and+other+letters+to+the+editor&amp;title=The+Hamilton+Spectator+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2025-02-07&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Hamilton+Spectator+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Inquest delay is ludicrous
Re: Coroner's inquest called into death of woman shot by Hamilton police, Feb. 5
If ever anyone doubted our criminal justice system needs revamping, particularly concerning police Special Investigations Unit probes, the poster child for reform would be the case of Robyn Garlow. I will not speculate on whether police were justified in her shooting, but for more than six years to pass before a coroner calls an inquest is ludicrous. It is theoretically possible to walk around the world six times within that span.
David Shupe, Dundas
This situation is unacceptable
Re: Sparks fly as Hamilton council scraps another homeless mini-cabin location, Feb. 6
Here we are just into February, these tiny cabins/mini-houses were supposed to be ready to launch in January and council is still debating where to put them. Are you serious, city council? Please get it together, because this situation is absolutely unacceptable.
Lisa Johnston, Hamilton
HATS should follow the Bulldogs
The effort that the people at HATS (Hamilton Alliance for Tiny Shelters) put into their project is truly amazing. Volunteers building cabins, trying for years to help people. Council and staff waffle and delay and butt heads then do nothing.
If I were associated with HATS, I'd be doing a Bulldogs thing. Head to Brantford and help the homeless people there. Where a council can make decisions that make a difference.
Rob Millen, Hamilton
Council right to reject expansion
Re: Hamilton councillors reject pitch for fast-tracked urban expansion, Feb. 5
Thank you, Hamilton council, for doing the right thing in turning down developers' expansion of the urban boundary in Glanbrook.
As everyone knows, the housing crisis is affecting folks currently under nylon or canvas, without kitchens and bathrooms, having no vehicle or proper clothing to attend job interviews.
Million-dollar-plus homes in Glanbrook are far from government offices, medical services, discount grocery stores, even bus stops. And we're all aware of situations where "10 per cent of affordable homes" were nowhere to be seen when development is complete.
Empty lots, empty city housing units, empty buildings per work-from-home-now employees, empty schools are waiting to be renovated to homes, affordable to buy or rent. Even homes purportedly confiscated years ago for LRT could certainly be residences for at least the next 10 years, given LRT's current quicksand.
Developers, please resurrect wartime home plans that are still lovely homes and streets today. Get a consult from Pennsylvania Amish on how to get the job done. Please. People are suffering.
Lynda Pierce, Mount Hope
We need land for food production
The latest episode of Donald Trump's chaotic and irresponsible edicts reinforces our need to ensure food security for our future. A future that includes more climate change and a less than trustworthy food exporter/neighbour like the U.S. We cannot rely on imported food as other countries will have to deal with climate change and will look after their own populations' needs before they export food to us. Keeping farm land from development provides an important resource for the future of the people who live and work in the GTHA. One acre of good farmland can produce 11,500 kilograms (25,400 pounds) of potatoes. Other crops such as lettuce yield less (11,000 pounds or 5,000 kg), but still add a significant amount of food to feed the growing population of the GTHA.
The current need for housing, although a critical situation, should not be used as a reason to remove farmland from production. According to the City of Hamilton, there is sufficient land within our urban boundaries to provide the amount of housing that we need to meet the current and proposed increase in Hamilton's population. Developers and some politicians cannot see beyond their bank balance or the next election. These people will reduce farmland and build single detached homes and in doing so will create a very serious crisis for our children and all the generations to follow - food shortage. We have a chance to elect people and parties that recognize the perils of climate change denial and the critical importance of farmland for food sustainability for future generations.
Bill Sullivan, Hamilton
Getting the message out
I received my rebate from Doug Ford today and had a great idea on how to spend it. I decided to have a slogan hat made much like Ford did for himself. Only my hat states, "Ontario voters not for sale."
Mike Langille, Hamilton
Buying Canadian with new-found pride
After we gentle-mannered Canadian neighbours were rhetorically bashed in the head from behind by President Donald Trump with threats of punishing tariffs, I don't take my eyes off of the news for more than say 20 minutes at a time in order to know how best to react and to be prepared alongside of the rest of Canadians with the best logical plan of action to stand up to a man who actually wants to control the world.
My plan is to join other Canadians in trying to buy "Made In Canada" as much as is possible. It's the least I can do and maybe realistically the only thing I can do since I am not wealthy.
So when I was drinking some Canadian apple juice instead of U.S. orange juice yesterday, I felt an overwhelming emotion of pride because that apple juice represented my patriotic heart and reminded me of every single Canadian soldier or anyone involved in protecting democracy around the world and lost their lives, and my eyes filled with tears.
We all know that saying, "an apple a day keeps the doctor away." Well, maybe, just maybe, a Canadian apple a day can keep a dictator away!
Ana Long, Hamilton
Tear down internal trade barriers
Tariffs have been used by the Americans against Canada off and on for more than 100 years. And yet, here we are today, unprepared and facing them again. We've learned nothing in 100 years. We snipe at the Americans with moral superiority and blissfully ignore the elephant in the room - interprovincial trade barriers. It's easier to trade north, south than east, west. In R. v. Comeau, the Supreme Court of Canada ruled against a New Brunswick man bringing back more than 12 pints of beer from Quebec, citing the province's right to manage supply and demand of liquor. Really? Only in Canada! We act like 10 squabbling nations. When will Canada pull up its big boy pants and get rid of all interprovincial trade barriers so that we can speak to the Americans with one unified voice?
Donald Zelem, Simcoe
Put the focus on fentanyl users
Re: Poilievre would impose life sentences for trafficking more than 40 mg of fentanyl, Feb. 5
I watched Pierre Poilievre's "Breaking Bad" photo op in Vancouver yesterday. Without a doubt, the fentanyl crisis in Canada has resulted in thousands of deaths of our citizens across our country. We need to stop these preventable deaths. The question is how?
Poilievre believes mandatory life sentences for those convicted of trafficking over 40 milligrams of fentanyl is the solution. I disagree.
The focus should be on those individuals who are suffering from an addiction to fentanyl. The Canadian government should increase the necessary resources and support. This would allow our fellow citizens to recover from their addiction to fentanyl, regain their health and once again rejoin society in a productive and prosperous way.
Mary Elizabeth Toth, Ancaster
It's a topsy-turvy world
Re: Trump says he wants US to take charge of Gaza Strip at news conference with Netanyahu, Feb. 4
When an accused war criminal from Israel and a convicted felon from the United States meet in the Oval Office to sort out world issues, the world seems topsy-turvy. What could seem more bizarre? But there is that feeling that we "ain't" seen nothing yet.
Ray Varey, Dundas
CAPTION: Robyn Garlow, 30, died in a police shooting in October 2018. Waiting for more than six years to pass before a coroner calls an inquest is ludicrous, a reader writes.

Document 201 of 500

Canadian pride rises due to Trump threats

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/canadian-pride-rises-due-trump-threats/docview/3164228988/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Canadian+pride+rises+due+to+Trump+threats&amp;title=The+Spectator&amp;issn=11899417&amp;date=2025-02-07&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.7&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Spectator&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Two new polls suggest Quebecers are feeling pretty good about their relationship with Canada these days.
Between December and February, the share of Quebecers who said they were "very proud" or "proud" to be Canadian increased 13 points from 45 per cent to 58 per cent, according to an Angus Reid poll conducted online Sunday and Monday. The online poll was conducted among 1,811 respondents. Because it was conducted online, it can't be assigned a margin of error.
It says the percentage of people in Quebec expressing pride in Canada increased faster than it did in the country as a whole.
The poll recorded an average increase in national pride across Canada of nine points, from 58 per cent to 67 per cent.
The Prairies are dragging the average down, with Alberta up three points, Saskatchewan up four points and Manitoba down four points. Reported pride in Canadian identity is up 12 points in B.C., nine points in Ontario and 15 points in Atlantic Canada.
The poll also tracked a spike in the number of Canadians who said they have a "deep emotional attachment to Canada" - from 30 per cent to 45 per cent in Quebec, and from 49 per cent to 59 per cent across Canada.
"The enemies of my enemies are my friends," said Daniel Béland, director of the McGill Institute for the Study of Canada.
With Canada on the brink of a tariff war with the United States, he said, the "common enemy" is now U.S. President Donald Trump.
"There is a sense of unity in adversity" and identification with Canada is being used as "a kind of shield against Trump," Béland said.
Geneviève Tellier, a professor at the University of Ottawa's school of political studies, said that Trump's tariff threat was perceived as "an attack, a betrayal even," and touched "a sensitive chord" provoking a "wave of patriotism."
She said we seldom see polling show that Quebecers see themselves as being "in the same boat" with other Canadians.
"Until now, we thought that in Quebec, it was different than in the rest of Canada, so we were going to vote for the (Bloc Québécois) to represent that difference. But with this crisis, it brought Quebecers back into line with the rest of Canada," she said.
"That's good news for the (federal) Liberals, most certainly. That should worry the Conservatives, but also the Bloc in Quebec."
And while polls of provincial voting intentions put the sovereigntist Parti Québécois in the lead, Frédéric Boily, a professor of political science at the University of Alberta, said a "wind of patriotism" is blowing through the country.
"The time is not right for Quebec nationalism," Boily said.
"The idea that we must form a common front to be able to fight Donald Trump, who is now almost designated as an enemy of Canada, also takes away arguments from Quebec nationalism to say that we could possibly react more effectively than Canada does if we were a nation."
The Canadian Press
CAPTION:

CREDIT: Michel Saba The Canadian Press

NDP, Green leaders converge on east Hamilton

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/ndp-green-leaders-converge-on-east-hamilton/docview/3164464067/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=NDP%2C+Green+leaders+converge+on+east+Hamilton&amp;title=The+Hamilton+Spectator+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2025-02-06&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Hamilton+Spectator+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: NDP Leader Marit Stiles and Green Leader Mike Schreiner both travelled to Hamilton for the first time in this snap election campaign Thursday to rally supporters.
Stiles visited the campaign headquarters of East Hamilton-Stoney Creek candidate Zaigham Butt to reiterate NDP rent control promises and vow to "flip this seat from blue to orange."
The east city seat was held by the NDP for 15 years under longtime former MPP Paul Miller until he was ejected from the party in 2022 over anti-Muslim allegations, which he denied. Tory candidate Neil Lumsden went on to win the seat in the election later that year.
In the same riding, Schreiner visited the headquarters of candidate Pascale Marchand on Thursday to kick off the campaign for Hamilton-area Green candidates and highlight a promise to build more affordable homes within the city's existing urban boundary.
Hamilton council is trying to maintain its existing urban boundary and has clashed with the most recent Tory-led provincial government, which infamously opened the Greenbelt and thousands of acres of farmland to development before reversing both decisions in 2023.
CAPTION: Ontario Green Party Leader Mike Schreiner meets with supporters in east Hamilton.Ontario NDP Leader Marit Stiles stops by the campaign office of Hamilton East-Stoney Creek candidate Zaigham Butt (at right).

Document 203 of 500

Ontario election: Bonnie Crombie slams Doug Ford for not curing the province's health-care woes

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/ontario-election-bonnie-crombie-slams-doug-ford/docview/3163834668/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Ontario+election%3A+Bonnie+Crombie+slams+Doug+Ford+for+not+curing+the+province%27s+health-care+woes&amp;title=The+Hamilton+Spectator+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2025-02-06&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Hamilton+Spectator+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Liberal Leader Bonnie Crombie is rubbing salt on Doug Ford's wounds over the chronic health-care challenges that ail Ontario. woes
Blasting the Progressive Conservative leader as the Feb. 27 election campaign began its second week, Crombie noted treatment of patients in hospital corridors has doubled and the doctor shortage has worsened since he took office in 2018.
"Doug Ford said he'd end hallway medicine. He didn't get it done. He said he'd cut your taxes. He didn't get it done. He said he'd build 1.5 million homes. He didn't get it done," she said Thursday in Scarborough.
Crombie - whose bright red campaign bus is emblazoned with the slogan "2.5 Million People Have No Family Doctor: This is Doug Ford's Ontario" - has promised to treat the physician shortage through a slew of measures that could cost $3.1 billion.
They include spending $250 million over four years in targeted retention aimed at 2,400 doctors who have left family medicine or are nearing retirement, offering bonuses of $150,000 to repatriate Canadian doctors from abroad to Ontario, and recruiting 650 of the 6,000 family doctors currently working in other disciplines.
As well, she wants to help doctors by slashing "the administrative burden taking up 50 per cent of their time."
"Let's get rid of fax machines at long last and implement a centralized referral system and ensure that they have the right staff (and) offer more evening and weekend care," she said.
Ford's Tories argue that they are tackling the doctor shortage in what rival parties said is a move that came too late after almost seven years in power.
On the eve of the snap election call, the PCs pledged $1.4 billion in new funding for expanded primary health care teams and said it would get every Ontarian who does not have one a family doctor by 2029.
"There's no doubt that people have been waiting a long time, too long, frankly, to get connected to a family care practitioner in their community," MPP Sylvia Jones, serving as Ford's health minister, acknowledged on Jan. 27.
But that announcement raised questions as to whether the $1.4 billion is enough because there's a huge discrepancy between numbers provided by Jones (Dufferin-Caledon) and the Ontario Medical Association.
The OMA says there are currently 2.5 million Ontarians without a family doctor, and predicts that figure will rise to 4.4 million next year as the population grows and doctors retire or leave the province.
But Jones maintained just two million Ontarians will need to be connected to a family doctor in the next four years.
The Conservative government also approved two new medical schools in Ontario, but graduates from them will not begin working as doctors for several more years.
Also Thursday, Green Leader Mike Schreiner was in Guelph to tout his party's plan to increase Ontario Disability Support Program and Ontario Works payments and build 310,000 affordable homes.
"Poverty in Ontario is escalating at an alarming rate," said Schreiner, noting the Association of Municipalities of Ontario found 81,000 people were homeless in the province last year.
"Over the past two years, food bank usage in Ontario has increased by 78 per cent," he said.
"Doug Ford isn't just failing to solve the problem - he's making it worse by keeping Ontarians with disabilities locked in legislated poverty."
Later Thursday, NDP Leader Marit Stiles will campaign in Hamilton and in Sault Ste. Marie, where her party hopes to pick up a key northern riding vacated by the retirement of Tory Ross Romano.
Ford will be in Waterloo, where he is to meet staff at a Linamar auto parts plant.
The Tory campaign is confident it has weathered the worst of his Monday gaffe when he was caught on a hot mic saying that he "100 per cent" wanted Donald Trump to win the American presidency last November.
"On election day, was I happy this guy won? One hundred per cent I was. Then the guy pulled out the knife and f---ing yanked it in me," Ford said at his campaign office in Etobicoke.
Since the misstep, the PC leader, who has framed the snap Ontario election as a referendum on who could best deal with Trump's threatened 25 per cent tariffs against Canada, has faced questions about how he could have supported someone who had cut off personal protective equipment imports to Canada during the COVID-19 pandemic.
"There's Trump 1.0 and then there was Trump 2.0," he said Wednesday.
CAPTION: Ontario Liberal Leader Bonnie Crombie appears at a campaign event in Scarborough on Feb. 6, 2025.

Document 204 of 500

A vote for Ford is a vote for Trump-style politics

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/vote-ford-is-trump-style-politics/docview/3163706962/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=A+vote+for+Ford+is+a+vote+for+Trump-style+politics&amp;title=The+Hamilton+Spectator+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2025-02-06&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Hamilton+Spectator+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Doug Ford wants your vote and claims he can take on Donald Trump in a trade war. However, Ford's words, policies and history raise serious questions to those claims and his suitability to remain in office.
For starters, Ford himself has stated that he is "a big Republican" and that his support for Trump is "unwavering."
Despite questioning Trump in the past on protections around trade, Ford, like Trump, has a similar political economic worldview where policies support easy money, debt forgiveness and socialism for the mega-rich, while the rest of us get neo-liberal austerity.
Ford and Trump both favour government deregulation, privatizing public goods and not properly funding public education and health-care systems in order to enable their privatization.
Ford's government has indicated it would like the Ontario education system to follow the model that states like Alabama and Arkansas are pursuing, where students can learn entirely online, often through technology provided by private companies which can collect data on students and workers.
This could pose a threat to unionized public education jobs as well as harm optimal and equitable student learning conditions.
Similarly in health care, Ford seems to favour American style policies of privatization, just like Trump.
However, Ford can be contradictory and vague about these policies. Ford publicly has stated all Ontarians can access health care, but this is happening as emergency rooms are overflowing, private clinics are expanding over public options, and health-care workers are burning out. Many of these workers are women and racialized, which further raises questions about Ford's feelings about workers with certain identities.
Ford, like Trump, has questionable views about the equality of women and uses sexist rhetoric. Ford allegedly called at least one reporter, "a little bitch" whereas Trump's abused women and claimed he can "grab" them wherever he wants. Despite this, Ford has said that Trump has "strong moral fibre."
Ford, like Trump, also scapegoats immigrants. For Ford, it's not about building walls per se, but Ford has falsely blamed immigrants for crimes, urban sprawl and dismantling environmental protections.
Ford has publicly claimed to support Muslim victims of hate crimes, however, when the time came to condemn Islamophobia in the legislature, Ford turned his back on the Muslim community.
Ford, like Trump, also has questionable sympathies to violent far-right extremists. Trump supported and pardoned the Jan. 6 rioters, whereas Ford's troubling inaction during the so-called "freedom convoy" that terrorized residents of Ottawa for over a month, raises questions about his own far-right sympathies.
We might guess that Trump would not have supported the actions on Jan. 6, 2021, if the protesters had been members of Black Lives Matter, antifa, Indigenous, pro-Palestinian, environmental or antiwar protesters.
Likewise, we should all ask, would it have taken over a month to address the "freedom convoy" in Ottawa had those protesters not been white, and not supported an anti-Liberal government, anti-COVID protections and pro-privatization agenda?
Ford, like Trump, is also willing to use secrecy and heavy-handed government power to stifle democracy and take away average working people's rights. As we watch on our screens Trump's use of state power to round up immigrants and build a detention centre at Guantanamo Bay, which will be "bigger than any of history's worst concentration camps," we should remember that Ford, on multiple occasions, has been willing to use the notwithstanding clause to push an anti-democratic agenda in addition to hiding his own government's ministerial mandate letters.
Ford's rhetoric of personal freedom and government waste in a world of super rich billionaires should be questioned by everyone who is not a billionaire. Workers need government regulations and protections from an exploitative billionaire class.
Ford's policies enable freedoms for billionaires, not the working classes.
As much as Ford is now saying he is "ripping up" the deal he has made with Elon Musk for high speed internet, he may still be going ahead with it. Ford's past actions, contradictions and general support for American right-wing politics, suggest any vote for Ford, is probably a vote for Trump-style politics.
CAPTION: U.S. President Donald Trump and Progressive Conservative Leader Doug Ford both favour government deregulation, privatizing public goods, and under funding public education and health care in order to enable their privatization, Andrew Wilkin writes.
CREDIT: Andrew Wilkin

Document 205 of 500

Letters

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/letters/docview/3163706959/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=unknown&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Letters&amp;title=The+Spectator&amp;issn=11899417&amp;date=2025-02-06&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.9&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Spectator&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Trump looks to build empire
So now, after Panama, Greenland and Canada, Donald Trump demands Gaza should also be part of his New American Empire. Under his brilliant rule, of course. That's four countries in just two weeks since his inauguration. With 195 countries recognized by the United Nations, at this rate, Trump could rule the world by the American mid-term elections. A scary new world indeed.
Jim Young, Burlington
Comments raise questions
Doug Ford is caught saying on a video with a group of firefighters, "Was I happy this guy won? One hundred per cent I was. Then the guy pulled out the knife." Let's put this in context. During Donald Trump's election campaign, Trump made numerous negative comments about Canada; slanderous and sexual comments about women; racist remarks about racialized Americans and called undocumented immigrants rapists, murderous and dog eaters. Ford was pleased that Trump was elected president. This says a lot about Doug Ford. He openly supported Trump until "the guy pulled out the knife." Questions should be raised as to whether Ford has the "moral character" to be re-elected premier of Ontario.
Malcolm Buchanan, Hamilton
We're living in strange times
What's a Canadian to do? Donald Trump threatens tariffs while the premier mumbles his support into a hot microphone. The leader of the opposition scrambles for new catchphrases while the man they said was done buys us another 30 days. Sometimes it's best to just follow Glinda's advice to Dorothy: click your heels together three times while saying, "There's no place like home."
Phil Beard, Dundas
An alternative to booing
First, let me say that I totally understand the impulse to show our disgust with actions and comments of the current U.S. administration. However, our argument is not with our American friends. To maintain our dignity, out of consideration for the singer and respect for our historic relationship, let's remain silent during the singing of the U.S. anthem - but during "O Canada," let's all stand and sing loud and proud! Too few athletes and fans exercise this expression of national pride.
Rose Marie Woodley-Robinson
No more anthems at games
My initial response to hearing about this is: why are we even playing our two countries' national anthems at professional sporting events? These games have nothing to do with national pride anymore, it's now all about millionaire athletes and billionaire owners. Have you ever watched the athletes on the field, or on the ice, while the national anthems are being played? They all look to be bored silly and can't wait until it's over. Dispense with the national anthems altogether and get on with the games.
David Ward, Hagersville
Canada needs to stay strong
No one really knows what His Orangeness is thinking or planning. It is safe to assume that virtually everything pouring from his mouth is a lie.
Canada's best position is to remain tough. Donald Trump does entertain, if not respect, those who will stand up to him. Kudos to the Liberal government for standing up for Canada. That alone should earn the respect of the voters, even before considering the content of our response to the proposed tariffs.
Pierre Poilievre's response, to lower taxes (that is, cutting income just as more cash flow is needed to lessen the impact on business and individuals) and to recall Parliament so he can attempt to bring down the government, is not in the national interest. Now is the time for solidarity, not a divisive election campaign (are you listening, Doug?).
It's time to show the world it is wrong to conflate niceness and politeness for weakness. It's the perfect moment to show the world how tough and resilient Canadians are when the moment calls for it. We have done it before, and we will do it again to protect this greatest of nations.
Paul W. Bognar, Hamilton
Fly your flag with pride
Every Canadian who feels we are better off on our own rather than being a 51st state should fly the flag prominently. Go buy a flag if you don't have one. In fact, buy as many as you can! Clearly, we don't want to experience tariff pain, but if we stand proud and resolute and unyielding to President Donald Trump, he will soon give up, especially as the U.S. inflation rate starts to creep up. Bullies only fight easy prey.
Martin Sarabura, Ancaster

Document 206 of 500

Main parties discuss encampments, disability payments and highway tolls: Ongoing tariff threat took a back seat

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/main-parties-discuss-encampments-disability/docview/3163706898/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Main+parties+discuss+encampments%2C+disability+payments+and+highway+tolls%3A+Ongoing+tariff+threat+took+a+back+seat&amp;title=The+Spectator&amp;issn=11899417&amp;date=2025-02-06&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.11&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Spectator&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Ontario's main political parties pivoted to affordability measures Wednesday as tariffs took a back seat on the campaign trail.
The NDP and Liberal leaders focused on housing and affordability issues, with promises to end homeless encampments and provide more financial support for people with disabilities.
The Progressive Conservatives say they will make an ongoing tax cut for gas and diesel permanent and remove tolls from the publicly owned portion of Highway 407 east of Toronto.
The affordability crisis has dogged Progressive Conservative Leader Doug Ford's government over the past several years and was set to be a major campaign issue before U.S. President Donald Trump won the election.
Homeless encampments sprouted up across Ontario during the pandemic and have increased in number since. There were some 1,400 encampments in the province in 2023, the Association of Municipalities of Ontario found.
More than 80,000 people were homeless in Ontario last year, the association said in a groundbreaking report based on provincial data released last month.
NDP Leader Marit Stiles listed several steps she would take to end encampments in communities across the province, calling their presence Ford's "greatest failure." But she would not say how much her plan would cost, stating only "that will all come" later.
The Association of Municipalities of Ontario has said $11 billion over 10 years would be required to end chronic homelessness.
Meanwhile, Liberal Leader Bonnie Crombie vowed to double payments under the Ontario Disability Support Program, which currently maxes out at $1,368 per month for a single person. She said the boost would be permanent, pegged to inflation and phased in over two years.
"It will be costed and part of the platform when we release it," she said during a campaign stop in Hamilton, adding that the pledge goes "hand in hand" with her key priority to find everyone in Ontario a family doctor in four years.
Shortly before Ford called the snap election for Feb. 27, his government announced it was giving municipalities up to $75.5 million to end encampments by creating more emergency shelter spaces and affordable housing units.
People are simply moving from one encampment to another and the last-minute pot of money will not solve the problem, Stiles said in Toronto on Wednesday.
"After seven years of Doug Ford, encampments are the new normal," she said. "Seeing tents in parks is a stark reminder of how utterly Doug Ford has failed. He has failed on housing, he has failed on health care, he has failed on creating good jobs and he has failed to make life affordable."
Stiles said an NDP government would create 60,000 new supportive housing units, have the province pay for shelter costs instead of municipalities and double social assistance rates. She did not indicate how much the promises would cost.
"First of all, that will all come, but I will tell you this, we can't afford not to do this," she said.
With files from Sharif Hassan
The Canadian Press
CAPTION: Progressive Conservative Leader Doug Ford appears at a news conference in Pickering, Ont., on Wednesday.Chris Young The Canadian Press

CREDIT: Allison Jones and Liam Casey With files from Sharif Hassan The Canadian Press

Subject: Homeless encampments; Disability; Homeless people

Title: Main parties discuss encampments, disability payments and highway tolls:   Ongoing tariff threat took a back seat

Publication title: The Spectator; Hamilton, Ont.

Document 207 of 500

Wanted: Workers in Hamilton to help oversee Feb. 27 election

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/wanted-workers-hamilton-help-oversee-feb-27/docview/3163706693/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Wanted%3A+Workers+in+Hamilton+to+help+oversee+Feb.+27+election&amp;title=The+Hamilton+Spectator+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2025-02-06&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Hamilton+Spectator+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Doug Ford isn't likely to claim credit for it, but the Progressive Conservative candidate's snap election call equals jobs: temporary Elections Ontario positions, paying $20 to $29.50 per hour, with some available to those as young as 16.
In Hamilton, that means more than 2,000 jobs spread across the city's five riding election offices, to work either election day Feb. 27, or during the three-day advance poll period from Feb. 20 to 22.
Elections Ontario, described on its website as a non-partisan office of the Legislative Assembly of Ontario administering elections, will hire for more than 55,000 positions across the province's 124 electoral districts, accommodating 7,000 voting locations.
Each election office/district will hire between 400 to 600 poll officials, Elections Ontario media co-ordinator Dave Pearce told The Spectator.
There are 14 categories of positions listed on the Elections Ontario website.
To apply online, go to jobs.elections.on.ca/en/apply.
The highest paying positions include area manager, who works election day for up to 13 hours, and also advance poll supervising deputy returning officer, who works up to three consecutive days during the advance poll period, for up to 11 hours per day.
Qualifications listed on the Elections Ontario website to fill these positions include having access to a cellphone and vehicle, availability to attend a training session, and "ability to read and observe voting procedures at the voting location."
Among the duties for the poll clerk position, for example, is "ensuring the voting location is free of any partisan campaign materials."
All of the positions require Canadian citizenship, and most require that you are at least 18 years old, apart from several information assistant jobs, that pay $20 an hour, in which you need to be 16 on election day, and among other skills "have the ability to stand and work for long stretches of time."
Two of the positions - special ballot officer and resource staff - require that you are bilingual, "as determined by the French Language Services Act."
Strength of body and mind seem required for three of the jobs, where in order to be hired you must have the "ability to understand, rationalize and resolve situations efficiently as they arise," and also the "ability to lift up to 60 lbs" in electronic equipment.
Jon Wells is a reporter at the Hamilton Spectator. jwells@thespec.com
CAPTION: Elections Ontario is hiring to fill more than 55,000 positions across the province to manage and oversee the Feb. 27 election.Elections Ontario is hiring to fill more than 55,000 positions across the province to manage and oversee the Feb. 27 election.

Document 208 of 500

Don't vote for more Trump-style politics

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/dont-vote-more-trump-style-politics/docview/3163706646/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=unknown&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Don%27t+vote+for+more+Trump-style+politics&amp;title=The+Spectator&amp;issn=11899417&amp;date=2025-02-06&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.9&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Spectator&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Doug Ford wants your vote and claims he can take on Donald Trump in a trade war. However, Ford's words, policies and history raise serious questions to those claims and his suitability to remain in office.
For starters, Ford himself has stated that he is "a big Republican" and that his support for Trump is "unwavering."
Despite questioning Trump in the past on protections around trade, Ford, like Trump, has a similar political economic worldview where policies support easy money, debt forgiveness and socialism for the mega-rich, while the rest of us get neo-liberal austerity.
Ford and Trump both favour government deregulation, privatizing public goods and not properly funding public education and health-care systems in order to enable their privatization.
Ford's government has indicated it would like the Ontario education system to follow the model that states like Alabama and Arkansas are pursuing, where students can learn entirely online, often through technology provided by private companies which can collect data on students and workers.
This could pose a threat to unionized public education jobs as well as harm optimal and equitable student learning conditions.
Similarly in health care, Ford seems to favour American style policies of privatization, just like Trump.
However, Ford can be contradictory and vague about these policies. Ford publicly has stated all Ontarians can access health care, but this is happening as emergency rooms are overflowing, private clinics are expanding over public options, and health-care workers are burning out. Many of these workers are women and racialized, which further raises questions about Ford's feelings about workers with certain identities.
Ford, like Trump, has questionable views about the equality of women and uses sexist rhetoric. Ford allegedly called at least one reporter, "a little bitch" whereas Trump's abused women and claimed he can "grab" them wherever he wants. Despite this, Ford has said that Trump has "strong moral fibre."
Ford, like Trump, also scapegoats immigrants. For Ford, it's not about building walls per se, but Ford has falsely blamed immigrants for crimes, urban sprawl and dismantling environmental protections.
Ford has publicly claimed to support Muslim victims of hate crimes, however, when the time came to condemn Islamophobia in the legislature, Ford turned his back on the Muslim community.
Ford, like Trump, also has questionable sympathies to violent far-right extremists. Trump supported and pardoned the Jan. 6 rioters, whereas Ford's troubling inaction during the so-called "freedom convoy" that terrorized residents of Ottawa for over a month, raises questions about his own far-right sympathies.
We might guess that Trump would not have supported the actions on Jan. 6, 2021, if the protesters had been members of Black Lives Matter, antifa, Indigenous, pro-Palestinian, environmental or antiwar protesters.
Likewise, we should all ask, would it have taken over a month to address the "freedom convoy" in Ottawa had those protesters not been white, and not supported an anti-Liberal government, anti-COVID protections and pro-privatization agenda?
Ford, like Trump, is also willing to use secrecy and heavy-handed government power to stifle democracy and take away average working people's rights. As we watch on our screens Trump's use of state power to round up immigrants and build a detention centre at Guantanamo Bay, which will be "bigger than any of history's worst concentration camps," we should remember that Ford, on multiple occasions, has been willing to use the notwithstanding clause to push an anti-democratic agenda in addition to hiding his own government's ministerial mandate letters.
Ford's rhetoric of personal freedom and government waste in a world of super rich billionaires should be questioned by everyone who is not a billionaire. Workers need government regulations and protections from an exploitative billionaire class.
Ford's policies enable freedoms for billionaires, not the working classes.
As much as Ford is now saying he is "ripping up" the deal he has made with Elon Musk for high speed internet, he may still be going ahead with it. Ford's past actions, contradictions and general support for American right-wing politics, suggest any vote for Ford, is probably a vote for Trump-style politics.
Andrew Wilkin lives
in Hamilton. His research
explores precarious teaching and educational labour
conditions in a polycrisis.
He can be found on X at
@teachrprecarity and
Bluesky at
@teachrprecarity.bsky.social.
CAPTION: Donald Trump and Doug Ford both favour government deregulation, privatizing public goods, and under funding public education and health care, Andrew Wilkin writes.Tribune News Services and Torstar

Document 209 of 500

How could Ford be surprised?

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/how-could-ford-be-surprised/docview/3163706612/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=unknown&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=How+could+Ford+be+surprised%3F&amp;title=The+Spectator&amp;issn=11899417&amp;date=2025-02-06&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.8&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Spectator&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Picture this: Inmates in the prison yard getting some fresh air. It's all fun and games until a fight breaks out. There is tussling and shoving. The guards come running and separate the crowd.
As everyone retreats, we see one prisoner on the ground, bleeding on the concrete. Clutching his abdomen, he gasps and chokes out: "On election day, was I happy this guy won? One hundred per cent I was. Then the guy pulled out the knife and f---ing yanked it in me."
This is the story of Doug Ford and President Donald Trump. Now, guess who pulled out the knife and who got yanked?
Let me stop chuckling because this is a ridiculous situation. Not "ha-ha" ridiculous, but "are-you-for-real" ridiculous. As someone who keeps up with politics at home and abroad, I cannot comprehend why Ford would be surprised that Trump, the man who has absolutely no allegiance to anyone, would not have any loyalty to Ontarians or Canadians? I mean, we saw him waving the knife around like a lunatic for the last eight years, threatening any and everyone. And yet, Ford thought a Trump victory was something to celebrate.
Like many Canadians, I wasn't celebrating Trump's win. I still don't understand why anyone would trust that Trump wouldn't shove a shiv in their stomach and happily watch them bleed out. Trump promised to rip up the U.S. Constitution, why would he hesitate to shank us, their friendly neighbour to the north?
It was never a question of if Trump would betray Canada. It was when and how he would do it. Truthfully, the entire tariff crisis feels like manufactured pandemonium. I'm not saying we won't wake up 30 days from now to 25 per cent tariffs slapped on us, but Trump's ultimate goal is to create enough confusion to distract from some more nefarious plans.
Now, with three weeks until Ontario's upcoming provincial election, Ford is out here playing Captain Canada - appearing on CNN talking about how much these tariffs would harm both Canadian and American workers. He urged Ontarians to buy Canadian products, threatened to tear up a $100-million contract from Elon Musk's Starlink and pull American liquor out of the LCBO. Strong countermeasures, no doubt.
It's also great stagecraft during an election campaign, keeping Ford on local, national and international news every night, looking like he's standing up for his constituents.
Why would Ford, who blamed immigrants for antisemitic violence, nearly triggered a general strike in protest of Bill 28, has a weird fixation with Ontarians accessing alcohol outside the LCBO or Beer Stores, and refused to act during the convoy blockade in Ottawa, have wanted Trump to win the 2024 U.S. election?
Birds of a feather and all that.
If you're surprised that Ford would prefer a Trump presidency over a Kamala Harris presidency, you haven't been paying attention.
Truthfully, I don't think that Ford is looking for world domination the way Trump is. Ford is trying to hold onto power and scrape together an election victory now that he's seemingly in our good graces. But, aside from $200 cheques that bounce and boondoggles that keep us all scratching our heads, I am befuddled as to what this premier has done that would make anyone give him another mandate.
The great, wise African American poet Maya Angelou said, "When someone shows you who they are, believe them the first time. People know themselves much better than you do. That's why it's important to stop expecting them to be something other than who they are."
Ford has shown us, over and over again, who he is. Why do we ever expect anything different?
Shellene Drakes-Tull
is a Toronto-based
storyteller, educator,
and the founder of
Sweet Lime Communications.
CAPTION:

Document 210 of 500

Trudeau planning summit amid threat of Trump tariffs: Trade, business and union leaders will meet to discuss economic strategies

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/trudeau-planning-summit-amid-threat-trump-tariffs/docview/3163706549/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Trudeau+planning+summit+amid+threat+of+Trump+tariffs%3A+Trade%2C+business+and+union+leaders+will+meet+to+discuss+economic+strategies&amp;title=The+Spectator&amp;issn=11899417&amp;date=2025-02-06&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.10&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Spectator&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Prime Minister Justin Trudeau will host a Canada-U.S. economic summit in Toronto on Friday, days after President Donald Trump said he would hold off on deploying his threatened tariffs against Canada for a month.
"We want businesses, investors and workers to choose Canada," Trudeau said in a news release.
The Prime Minister's Office said the pause on tariffs presents an important opportunity to build a long-term prosperity agenda for Canada.
The event will assemble Canadian trade and business leaders and representatives of organized labour to discuss strategies to expand the economy, break down internal trade barriers and diversify exports.
It will also feature members of the Council on Canada-U.S. Relations, formed earlier this year to advise Trudeau on bilateral relations and Trump's tariff threat.
Trudeau held a virtual meeting with the premiers Wednesday to discuss the federal government's response to the tariff threat. Ontario Progressive Conservative Leader Doug Ford said they talked about how to knock down interprovincial trade barriers, expedite permits for resource development and secure the border.
"I've asked the federal government, who is the czar when it comes to drugs overall, especially fentanyl?" Ford said at a campaign stop in Pickering.
Trump signed an executive order on Saturday to impose 25 per cent across-the-board tariffs on all Mexican and Canadian imports, with a lower 10 per cent tariff on Canadian energy. The president linked the duties to what he called the illegal flow of people and fentanyl across the borders.
The threat of a trade war - which economists have said would damage both economies and boost inflation - was resolved temporarily Monday for both countries after Trump spoke separately by phone with Trudeau and Mexican President Claudia Sheinbaum.
Trump agreed to freeze the levies until March 4 in response to border security commitments from Canada and Mexico. Trump said in a social media post that the tariffs will be delayed to see if the two countries can reach a "final economic deal."
White House Press Secretary Karoline Leavitt claimed victory for Trump in his negotiations with Canada and Mexico. When asked Wednesday whether Trump intends to use tariffs to cut taxes in America, Leavitt said he "believes tariffs are a great revenue raiser for this country."
Canadian officials have taken little solace from the delay. The president has made a wide range of complaints against Canada, citing trade deficits and Canada's modest defence spending. He has said repeatedly he wants to make the country the 51st state.
Experts have warned that ongoing trade uncertainty will make Canada a less desirable place to invest than the U.S.
Energy and Natural Resources Minister Jonathan Wilkinson, Industry Minister François-Philippe Champagne and Defence Minister Bill Blair were in the U.S. capital this week looking to arrange meetings with industry representatives, Republican lawmakers and key members of Trump's team.
A delegation of Canadian premiers will also make the case for Canada in Washington next week. Ford, who is chair of the Council of the Federation, said it's critical to communicate directly with American lawmakers and business groups.
Ford, who used Trump's tariffs threat as justification for calling an early provincial election, said he will have 15 meetings on his first day in Washington, during which he'll discuss the benefits of the United States and Canada working together.
"I have yet to hear one elected official, no matter Republican or Democrat, (that) thinks this is a good idea to attack your closest neighbour and your cherished ally," Ford said.
"Let's build a stronger two countries."
The Canadian Press
CAPTION: A Royal Canadian Mounted Police officer looks over the force's new Black Hawk helicopter to be used to fulfil their border mandate in St-Antoine- Abbe, Que.Thursday.Christinne Muschi The Canadian Press
