Feb. 6: Trump looks to build empire, here's an alternative to booing and other letters to the editor

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/feb-6-trump-looks-build-empire-heres-alternative/docview/3163706436/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Feb.+6%3A+Trump+looks+to+build+empire%2C+here%27s+an+alternative+to+booing+and+other+letters+to+the+editor&amp;title=The+Hamilton+Spectator+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2025-02-06&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Hamilton+Spectator+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Trump looks to build empire
So now, after Panama, Greenland and Canada, Donald Trump demands Gaza should also be part of his New American Empire. Under his brilliant rule, of course. That's four countries in just two weeks since his inauguration. With 195 countries recognized by the United Nations, at this rate, Trump could rule the world by the American mid-term elections. A scary new world indeed.
Jim Young, Burlington
Ford's comments raise questions
Doug Ford is caught saying on a video with a group of firefighters, "Was I happy this guy won? One hundred per cent I was. Then the guy pulled out the knife."
Let's put this in context. During Donald Trump's election campaign, Trump made numerous negative comments about Canada; slanderous and sexual comments about women; racist remarks about racialized Americans and called undocumented immigrants rapists, murderous and dog eaters.
Ford was pleased that Trump was elected president. This says a lot about Doug Ford. He openly supported Trump until "the guy pulled out the knife."
Questions should be raised as to whether Ford has the "moral character" to be re-elected premier of Ontario.
Malcolm Buchanan, Hamilton
We're living in strange times
What's a Canadian to do? Donald Trump threatens tariffs while the premier mumbles his support into a hot microphone. The leader of the opposition scrambles for new catchphrases while the man they said was done buys us another 30 days. Sometimes it's best to just follow Glinda's advice to Dorothy: click your heels together three times while saying, "There's no place like home."
Phil Beard, Dundas
An alternative to booing
First, let me say that I totally understand the impulse to show our disgust with actions and comments of the current U.S. administration. However, our argument is not with our American friends. To maintain our dignity, out of consideration for the singer and respect for our historic relationship, let's remain silent during the singing of the U.S. anthem - but during "O Canada," let's all stand and sing loud and proud! Too few athletes and fans exercise this expression of national pride.
Rose Marie Woodley-Robinson
Drop anthems at games altogether
My initial response to hearing about this is: why are we even playing our two countries' national anthems at professional sporting events? These games have nothing to do with national pride anymore, it's now all about millionaire athletes and billionaire owners. Have you ever watched the athletes on the field, or on the ice, while the national anthems are being played? They all look to be bored silly and can't wait until it's over. Dispense with the national anthems altogether and get on with the games.
David Ward, Hagersville
Canada needs to stay strong
No one really knows what His Orangeness is thinking or planning. It is safe to assume that virtually everything pouring from his mouth is a lie.
Canada's best position is to remain tough. Donald Trump does entertain, if not respect, those who will stand up to him. Kudos to the Liberal government for standing up for Canada. That alone should earn the respect of the voters, even before considering the content of our response to the proposed tariffs.
Pierre Poilievre's response, to lower taxes (that is, cutting income just as more cash flow is needed to lessen the impact on business and individuals) and to recall Parliament so he can attempt to bring down the government, is not in the national interest. Now is the time for solidarity, not a divisive election campaign (are you listening, Doug?).
It's time to show the world it is wrong to conflate niceness and politeness for weakness. It's the perfect moment to show the world how tough and resilient Canadians are when the moment calls for it. We have done it before, and we will do it again to protect this greatest of nations.
Paul W. Bognar, Hamilton
Fly your flag with pride
Every Canadian who feels we are better off on our own rather than being a 51st state should fly the flag prominently. Go buy a flag if you don't have one. In fact, buy as many as you can! Clearly, we don't want to experience tariff pain, but if we stand proud and resolute and unyielding to President Donald Trump, he will soon give up, especially as the U.S. inflation rate starts to creep up. Bullies only fight easy prey.
Martin Sarabura, Ancaster
It's time to secure our future
During the worst of COVID-19, we had four years that demonstrated our inability to live without the help of others. We needed the world's drug supply for vaccines. We needed the world's supply of protective gear and gloves.
Now, with the threat of tariffs in a month, we are hearing that the auto sector could have plants close within one week, that food costs will rise, etc. Whether we actually face tariffs or not, it is the time for our governments at the municipal, provincial and federal levels to work toward a secure future.
Let's expand our markets and stop this reliance on our greatest partner. Let's spend the billions on our northern - yes, northern borders and seas. Let's buy Canadian. Let's rebuild our manufacturing plants and make them flexible in order that they can pivot as needs arise. Let's unite and be strong.
Suki Garson, Dundas
CAPTION: President Donald Trump welcomes Prime Minister Benjamin Netanyahu of Israel to the White House on Tuesday. With the addition of Gaza, Trump has now set his sights on four foreign territories in just a couple of weeks, a reader notes.

Liberal Leader Bonnie Crombie campaigns in Hamilton

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/liberal-leader-bonnie-crombie-campaigns-hamilton/docview/3163933202/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Liberal+Leader+Bonnie+Crombie+campaigns+in+Hamilton&amp;title=The+Hamilton+Spectator+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2025-02-05&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Hamilton+Spectator+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Liberal Leader Bonnie Crombie made her first provincial election visit to the city Wednesday on Hamilton Mountain, the only riding with an open race in the city.
Longtime New Democrat incumbent Monique Taylor announced plans to run federally for that party, meaning a new MPP is guaranteed on the Mountain.
Crombie campaigned with Mountain candidate Dawn Danko before gathering with citywide Liberal candidates at the Neighbour to Neighbour Centre to announce the party's campaign vow to double Ontario Disability Support benefits over two years.
CAPTION: Liberal Leader Bonnie Crombie made her first campaign visit to the city on Hamilton Mountain.Liberal Leader Bonnie Crombie made her first campaign visit to the city on Hamilton Mountain.

Document 213 of 500

Ford defends saying he was '100% happy' Trump won U.S. election: 'Lost his reason for election': Crombie

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/ford-defends-saying-he-was-100-happy-trump-won-u/docview/3163368345/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Ford+defends+saying+he+was+%27100%25+happy%27+Trump+won+U.S.+election%3A+%27Lost+his+reason+for+election%27%3A+Crombie&amp;title=The+Spectator&amp;issn=11899417&amp;date=2025-02-05&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.6&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Spectator&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Ontario's Liberals and NDP are using a pause in the tariff threat from the United States to begin a new chapter in the provincial election, focusing on issues such as health care and education, but Doug Ford doesn't appear ready to turn the page.
The Progressive Conservative leader's campaign announcement Tuesday was ostensibly about a provincial-specific issue, a promise to take on billions of dollars in costs for Ottawa's light-rail transit system, but he spent much of his time hammering home his tariff warnings.
"We've averted an absolute disaster, at least for now," Ford said in Ottawa.
"But make no mistake, Canada and Ontario continue to stare down the threat of tariffs. Whether it's tomorrow, in a month, or a year from now, President Trump will continue to use the threat of tariffs to get what he wants."
U.S. President Donald Trump announced Monday, hours before threatened 25 per cent tariffs on Canadian goods were set to take effect, that he would put them on hold for at least 30 days.
Ford had cited the threat of tariffs as the reason he called the snap, $189-million election for Feb. 27, saying he needed the "largest mandate in Ontario's history" in order to advocate for the province's interests in the face of a punishing trade war, and to spend tens of billions of dollars on provincial stimulus.
The other parties have said an election well over a year before the June 2026 fixed date is not necessary because they would support stimulus spending, and Ford already has a majority mandate to protect Ontario's interests.
Liberal Leader Bonnie Crombie, who has suggested the election is an attempt by Ford to "cling to power," said the 30-day tariff reprieve is very revealing.
"Doug Ford lost his reason for an election," she said at a campaign stop in Brampton.
"So I'd like to understand, Doug, why did you call this election? What is your motivation behind it? The people of Ontario want to know why you called an unnecessary, expensive election in the middle of the winter, when you should be at your desk working to protect the people of Ontario, working to provide the basics for them."
The Liberals have been trying to make health care the central campaign issue, touting their promise to guarantee every Ontarian a family doctor within four years, and splashing a message about 2.5 million people having no family doctor on the side of their bus travelling the province.
Still, the Liberals took the opportunity to make hay with a caught-on-camera moment, with Ford telling supporters on Monday he was "100 per cent" happy that Trump won the American election, before he betrayed Canada on tariffs.
The Liberals quickly turned the video of Ford into an attack ad, questioning what else Ford says when he thinks members of the public are not listening.
Ford defended his comments Tuesday, saying he meant that he "thought things might be a little different," and that Canada and the U.S. were going to move forward with Trump back in the White House as the economy was strong, and he believes in those who lower taxes on people and businesses.
NDP Leader Marit Stiles was also in Ottawa, and promised to spend more money in the education system, hiring more staff, supporting students with disabilities and adding $830 million per year for school repairs.
Ford is trying to "con" voters into thinking the snap election is all about tariffs, but it should be about Ford's record since he was first elected in 2018, Stiles said.
The Canadian Press
CAPTION: Ontario NDP Leader Marit Stiles speaks during a campaign event in Ottawa Tuesday. She promised to spend more money in the education system.Adrian Wyld The Canadian Press

CREDIT: Allison Jones The Canadian Press

Subject: Provincial elections; Political campaigns; Light rail transportation; Tariffs

Document 214 of 500

Hamilton councillors reject pitch for fast-tracked urban expansion

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/hamilton-councillors-reject-pitch-fast-tracked/docview/3163368225/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Hamilton+councillors+reject+pitch+for+fast-tracked+urban+expansion&amp;title=The+Hamilton+Spectator+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2025-02-05&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Hamilton+Spectator+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: A development consortium's pitch to advance plans to expand Hamilton's frozen urban boundary to build thousands of homes on rural land through a fast-tracked provincial zoning order has fallen flat at city hall.
The Upper West Side Landowners Group have failed to convince councillors to endorse a request to the province for a minister's zoning order (MZO) to open up parcels off Twenty Road West in Glanbrook for development.
An MZO, if approved, would override the municipal process to evaluate the application for roughly 5,000 homes and up to five million square feet of employment space on the land near the airport.
That provincial detour is not the right route, Coun. John-Paul Danko contended before a planning committee vote Tuesday registering opposition to the proposal.
"We should see that out and not consider an end-around or an alternative."
The committee's decision, which awaits a final vote at council next week, also directs staff to inform Municipal Affairs and Housing Minister Paul Calandra of the city's position.
As minister, Calandra can consider MZO requests that accord with "provincial policy" or have municipal backing, planning staff noted in a report.
But the minister can approve a request without municipal endorsement, and for zoning that doesn't necessarily dovetail with overarching land-use policies.
In December, the Upper West Side group detailed its MZO proposal in a letter to Mayor Andrea Horwath, who, on Tuesday, said she wouldn't back the measure.
"The fact is there are serious concerns with this proposed development that cannot be ignored," Horwath told The Spectator via email.
The Upper West Side group's proposed subdivision calls for an urban expansion of about 220 acres and the conversion of 245 acres of airport employment lands to residential and other uses, staff note.
Staff warn an MZO-approved project could eat up employment land, strain servicing capacity, hinder airport expansion plans, leave natural features unprotected, sideline public input, squander orderly development and undermine a strategy to focus growth inward.
And although the Twenty Road West-area lands aren't within a "prime agricultural area," their soils are of prime agricultural calibre, city planner Charlie Toman told the committee, addressing a key point of contention for the developers.
A raft of letters to the city from residents in response to the MZO request raised similar issues, echoing a pitched public debate in recent years over whether to build homes on tracts of outlying farmland to increase housing stock.
The Upper West Side consortium is also challenging the city's fixed urban boundary at the Ontario Land Tribunal (OLT), with a case that got underway this past fall and hearings scheduled for 2026.
The partners say their "complete infill community" will result in an estimated $327 million in development charges and $21.7 million in "annual net fiscal revenues."
The consortium acknowledges concerns over the OLT appeal, but "it wouldn't make sense" to drop the challenge before an MZO can be issued and eliminate the need for the litigation, Marcus Gagliardi of Cachet Homes told councillors Tuesday.
The lands in question are "surrounded" by the urban uses "on all four sides," added Gagliardi, recounting how a past tribunal settlement involving the city's airport employment growth district (AEGD) in 2015 placed them into the rural area.
The proposed development features parks space, 32 kilometres of natural networks or trails that will be "preserved or enhanced," stormwater management ponds, two school sites and retail offerings, he said.
Also in the equation is the potential for 500 affordable units - or 10 per cent of the overall mix - through an arrangement with Habitat for Humanity Hamilton.
Such an affordability component is "unprecedented" in a private-developer-led project, Sean Ferris, CEO of Habitat for Humanity Hamilton, told the committee.
"Habitat wants to be part of this because we can ensure that Hamilton families who couldn't otherwise access home ownership would move into these homes."
Danko, however, later argued the proposal was "not a serious ask" and "more of a PR exercise" as the consortium makes it case to the OLT.
In an effort to prevent sprawl, back-to-back city councils have approved a frozen urban boundary, a strategy that has won public support but also sparked pushback from the developers and owners of rural land.
In 2022, Premier Doug Ford's government, citing a goal to build 1.5 million homes by 2031 to ease Ontario's housing crunch, imposed an urban expansion on Hamilton and opened up tracts of the Greenbelt for development.
About a year later, the Progressive Conservatives walked back both measures amid public backlash and scathing provincial watchdog reports.
Despite the boundary U-turn, new legislation has since introduced changes that facilitate expansions, compromising once again the city's fixed urban area.
Bill 185 allows developers to appeal council "nondecisions" or rejections of urban expansion applications to the OLT.
Also, a newly drafted provincial planning statement axes the requirement of comprehensive municipal land-use studies before boundary-expanding bids can be made.
Expecting a series of applications, city planners have drafted a framework to evaluate boundary bids with requirements for a range of technical studies.
A team of staff has been created to respond to the applications, while $1.5 million is earmarked to fight OLT challenges.
Teviah Moro is a reporter at The Hamilton Spectator. tmoro@thespec.com
CAPTION: The Upper West Side Landowners Group aims to build roughly 5,000 homes on lands off Twenty Road West near the airport in Glanbrook.The Upper West Side Landowners Group aims to build roughly 5,000 homes on lands off Twenty Road West near the airport in Glanbrook.The Upper West Side Landowners Group aims to build roughly 5,000 homes on lands off Twenty Road West near the airport in Glanbrook.

Subject: Committees; Consortia; Landowners; Rural areas; Airport expansion; Stormwater management; Planning; Housing developments; Zoning

Pitch for fast-tracked urban expansion rejected by councillors: Consortium aims for provincial zoning approval to advance 5,000-home development

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/pitch-fast-tracked-urban-expansion-rejected/docview/3163368190/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Pitch+for+fast-tracked+urban+expansion+rejected+by+councillors%3A+Consortium+aims+for+provincial+zoning+approval+to+advance+5%2C000-home+development&amp;title=The+Spectator&amp;issn=11899417&amp;date=2025-02-05&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.1&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Spectator&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Document 216 of 500

Post-secondary system is in trouble

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/post-secondary-system-is-trouble/docview/3163367966/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=unknown&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Post-secondary+system+is+in+trouble&amp;title=The+Spectator&amp;issn=11899417&amp;date=2025-02-05&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.10&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Spectator&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Thanks to robust investing in the 1960s, Ontario established an enviable world-class education system, spanning elementary to post-secondary.
For the first time in history, public post-secondary education was affordable. But, oh, how times have changed.
Ontario has a ratio of 34 students to one professor. Contrast that with the rest of Canada, which averages 23:1. The Ford government spends $9,890 per domestic full-time student annually (2021) despite the national average being $15,807. That $5,917 deficit manufactured a crisis impacting students, faculty and the future of Ontario.
Since 2019, cuts to grants greatly increased student debt. Add increased domestic tuition fees and it's easy to understand why many post-secondary graduates have to make the painful decision of not pursuing a second degree or graduate studies.
To make up for provincial funding cuts, universities have increasingly exploited international students. A 2022 Ontario Auditor General's report noted international students made up 17 per cent of university and college enrolments (2021) while accounting for 45 per cent of tuition revenue. Just under 20 per cent of the student population is providing almost 50 per cent of the revenue.
Limits on international students put in place by the federal Liberals target private universities that often overpromise what they can deliver. The only public university impacted by this policy change is Algoma University, with campuses in Sault Ste. Marie and Brampton.
Algoma entered into a public-private partnership, or P3, with Yorkville University, a private, for-profit university. A 2022 report by Ontario's Auditor General revealed Algoma was "becoming overburdened by debt in 2016-17." To solve the funding crisis, Algoma began admitting more international students to its Brampton campus. The student population increased from 540 students to 5,372.
The impact is also being felt by faculty who are represented by the Ontario Public Service Employees Union (OPSEU).
As course design and delivery is transferred from Algoma to Yorkville, non-unionized faculty will be paid much less than faculty represented by OPSEU Local 685.
Historically, P3s have proven disastrous whether they involve hospitals or municipally owned water and waste water systems. These same outcomes have played out around the world leading some European countries to ban the use of P3s because ultimately the public commons shoulders all of the risks and unforeseen costs, while the private, for-profit portion of the arrangement ensures share holders receive their expected returns.
International students make up 18 per cent of medical students, yet Doug Ford declared all spots in Ontario's medical schools will be reserved for students from Ontario.
York University Medical School will add hundreds of family physician spots despite 108 reserved spots going unfilled in 2024. Those vacancies had nothing to do with international students, but everything to do with intersecting manufactured financial crises, including the cost of a basic post-secondary degree, medical degree, rent, food and transportation, plus the added expense of opening and operating a family practice.
And, I haven't even touched on the impacts this manufactured financial crisis has on professors who bear the brunt of not only larger classes, but travelling between universities piecing together multiple short-term contracts often bereft of benefits or job security.
Ford is destroying post-secondary education while perpetuating the doctor shortage. Bottom line: Ford's just too expensive.
Doreen Nicoll is a
journalist and podcaster
in Burlington. Find her work at doreenn.substack.com.
CAPTION:

CREDIT: Doreen Nicoll Doreen Nicoll is a journalist and podcaster in Burlington. Find her work at doreenn.substack.com.;

Document 217 of 500

Feb. 5: A mind-blowing display at RBG, don't spend a dime in the U.S. and other letters to the editor

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/feb-5-mind-blowing-display-at-rbg-dont-spend-dime/docview/3163367963/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Feb.+5%3A+A+mind-blowing+display+at+RBG%2C+don%27t+spend+a+dime+in+the+U.S.+and+other+letters+to+the+editor&amp;title=The+Hamilton+Spectator+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2025-02-05&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Hamilton+Spectator+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Mind-blowing display at RBG
Re: 'Alice in Bloomland' blossoms at Royal Botanical Gardens, Feb. 3
Kudos to the Royal Botanical Gardens, its new administration, designers and volunteers. Their creativity in keeping nature alive at this time of year is particularly vital for our well-being. As a teacher and judge in the late 1970s and '80s, this has blown my mind! Leslie and Barbara Laking were my mentors in horticulture and floral design. Leslie's vision for the future has now been expanded and become even more exciting, indoors and out and for all ages. As the late Queen Elizabeth remarked, "without beauty there is no hope."
Joan Lindley, Ancaster
We need to act against hate
Re: Charges laid against Hamilton man after online posts threaten Jewish community, Feb. 1
Saturday's paper had an article about the police charging a man with antisemitism and advocating genocide. The familiar "Hate has no place in Hamilton" punchline is again proclaimed. Yet all I read about is hate, be it from white supremacist neo-Nazis or mobs of thinly disguised antisemitic pro-Hamas yobs. And every time I read about it, that familiar line is proclaimed yet again.
I grew up in Toronto in the 1960s. It was a melting pot of cultures and ideals. I had friends of every colour, denomination and gender and I firmly believed my generation kicked racism and bigotry in the ass. It seems it's back. Maybe it's time to act on that punchline and do something about this, starting with educating young people to show respect for, and have a good relationship with, everyone and cracking down on those fringe groups that promote this poison. Yes, hate has no place in Hamilton, or Canada for that matter. Let's start acting like we mean it.
Paul E. Casey, Hamilton
Let public decide what's needed
Re: 'What we need, not what we want': Hamilton police chief on 2025 budget pitch, Jan. 29
It's cool to see that our police chief is a fan of the Rolling Stones' "You can't always get what you want, but if you try sometime, you get what you need." We're always talking about police budget increases after the fact. First, the money is spent on wants, then when the bill comes in they become needs. Maybe the police board or whoever negotiates police salaries, which make up 90 per cent of the police budget, should consult with us about the increases before they approve them, when they are stills wants, since they go on the city's credit card. Once they're approved and the bill comes in, what choice do taxpayers have? And that's how you turn wants into needs.
Stanley Klimowicz, Stoney Creek
Vote for health care, not highways
Vote with your head, not your wallet. A $200 cheque in the mail will not lessen your wait time for an MRI, nor will it provide help for your autistic child. If $200 buys your vote, it will help a politician spend billions to build a highway rather than lessen your wait time for an MRI or help your autistic child learn how to function in society.
Bill Sullivan, Hamilton
No excuse needed to read
Re: What a winter it's been. For me, the load of snow has rekindled a particular pleasure, Feb. 1
A true book lover and avid reader does not need an excuse to read. Reading is a part of each and every day. The writer gives herself too much credit for reading. She is not unique; indeed, most of my friends and family are also ardent readers. We share our love for reading in conversation discussing and recommending books to each other. Most of us prefer a physical book rather than a tablet, however they also have a place for book lovers.
Devoted readers take books everywhere we go, hoping for even a few minutes to read before an appointment. We even have protective covers to carry our books around! We are passionate about reading, we are not a minority, and we never need an excuse to read!
Jan Peirson-Reid, Carlisle
Don't let tariffs threat distract you
Doug Ford is taking every opportunity to talk about possible tariffs to be imposed on Canadian exports to the United States. He and his party are hoping this will distract voters from the reality that despite their majority in the legislature, they have not fulfilled any of their previous campaign promises. Every Canadian will have a role in responding to the tariffs. Voters, please remember: hallway medicine continues, long-term care is still inadequate, the promised homes have not been built, and school boards are still underfunded, to highlight just a few.
Theresa Flynn-Purchase, Hamilton
Dear Doug Ford ...
Sorry "Captain Canada," too little too late. You've shown us who and what you are, and we've learned well. Your character and priorities have been shown in your betrayal; i.e. the Greenbelt, your determination to fill corner stores' shelves with alcohol, building a spa for the elite at Ontario Place, and now, an unneeded and unjustified election. Each one, all of them, carrying multi-million-dollar costs borne by we the taxpayers.
Except our priorities are for all our neighbours desperately needing attention and dollars for health care, housing, education, etc. So no, my "X" will not be marked next to your name this time.
Lynda Pierce, Mount Hope
This is a 'vast amount of money'
Re: 'I'll pray for that tonight': Hamilton man hopes to trade tent for mini-cabin, Feb. 1
The article indicates an expected allocation of $192 million by city staff for housing and homelessness this coming year. A vast amount of money. Based on figures from local real estate for sale, the city could purchase roughly 300 new townhouses or 450 condos or 300 resale single-family dwellings. Per year!
I think one local MPP was certainly correct in requesting a complete audit of how these funds are spent, especially in light of the travesty the mini-shelter project has become.
Peter Demik, Hannon
Trudeau does Canadians proud
In his news conference announcing Canada's response to Trump's tariffs, Prime Minister Justin Trudeau showed us again that he is articulate, rational, and classy: everything that Trump is not. He conducted himself as a true leader should and made me proud to be Canadian.
Thomas Mulligan, Grimsby
Don't spend a dime in the U.S.
As a dual U.S./Canadian citizen permanently residing in the U.S., I am disgusted that President Donald Trump would impose these vindictive tariffs on an ally and neighbouring country. As such, I encourage all Canadians to avoid visiting the U.S. in response to the Trump tariffs until they are rescinded.
A significant portion of state tax revenue, especially for southern states such as Florida, Texas and Arizona, comes from Canadian visitors and vacationers and the loss of or reduction of this revenue will be painful and likely have a significant impact on these states. This would result in pressure on the American federal government to end the tariffs.
For those Canadians seeking sunshine, there are many alternatives in Mexico, Cuba, the Caribbean and around the world to explore. So please pass on the U.S. this year and send a message to the Trump administration that the U.S. tariff policy is unacceptable to Canadians.
David Sells, Sarasota, Fla.
With Trudeau gone, why vote for Poilievre?
Pierre Poilievre is quick to criticize the policies of the Trudeau government but is reluctant to let Canadians know where he stands on many important issues. Out of frustration, I emailed a letter to Pierre Poilievre on Jan. 10. Not surprisingly I have heard nothing back.
He is running on one issue only; the carbon tax. Now that the two major candidates for leadership of the Liberal party have both said they would "axe the tax," Poilievre has no issue to champion.
This negative and angry man has nothing good to say about Canada. I believe he would be a danger to Canada and would not represent us well on the world stage. Many people would vote for Poilievre, not because they like him, but because they are tired of Trudeau. Trudeau will be gone before election day. Think carefully before you cast your vote.
Al Knapp, Hamilton
It's easy to bash western civilization
RE: We live in a world where history is being weaponized, Feb. 1
It might be useful to know where this world is that Paul Racher writes about.
Mr. Racher would do well to read Thomas Sowell's book "Discrimination and Disparities" to learn why the civilization of Western Europe presently dominates the world. He would also do well to watch at least the first two of the four episodes put on YouTube by historian and classicist Victor Davis Hanson on the origin and rise of western civilization, beginning around 850 B.C. in Greece. What makes western civilization unique and special today is its absence of chauvinism, its willingness to absorb and assimilate what is good about foreign cultures, but most especially the fact that anyone regardless of race can be "western." This is not true of the Chinese, Japanese, Indian and many other cultures.
It's easy to bash western civilization because it's one of the few that will tolerate it.
Vincent Curtis, Calgary, Alta.
Put local resources to work
The recent tariff joint response by our prime minister and ministers brings opportunities and problems to our local entrepreneurial engineers and tradespeople.
A woman on the radio informed us that her local company made machines and about one third were exported to the U.S. Also, they used hydraulic parts made in the U.S. Because of tariffs, they would have to lay off staff and may move to the U.S.
As a long-retired consulting engineer, also an indentured tradesman, who did design equipment made for export, I make the following suggestion to younger entrepreneurs.
First, contact Mayor Andrea Horwath, and your local councillor to request their help. Next, obtain their lists of local probable importers and exporters of American machine-related items. Visit a couple of these companies and ask about any tariff problems.
Contact McMaster University which has teams of graduating engineers who help local business develop marketing plans.
Identify some simple American-manufactured components and visit local trade schools to make prototypes. Match sellers and buyers and integrate other resources as needed.
The above is a win-win for all involved with big potential.
Thomas C. Ferguson, Hamilton
CAPTION: The Alice in Bloomland show at the Royal Botanical Gardens, running now until March 30, is mind blowing, a reader says.

Full text: Mind-blowing
display at RBG
Re: 'Alice in Bloomland' blossoms at Royal Botanical Gardens, Feb. 3
Kudos to the Royal Botanical Gardens, its new administration, designers and volunteers. Their creativity in keeping nature alive at this time of year is particularly vital for our well-being. As a teacher and judge in the late 1970s and '80s, this has blown my mind! Leslie and Barbara Laking were my mentors in horticulture and floral design. Leslie's vision for the future has now been expanded and become even more exciting, indoors and out and for all ages. As the late Queen Elizabeth remarked, "without beauty there is no hope."
Joan Lindley, Ancaster

Document 219 of 500

Ontario's post-secondary education funding model needs to be fixed

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/ontarios-post-secondary-education-funding-model/docview/3163367652/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Ontario%27s+post-secondary+education+funding+model+needs+to+be+fixed&amp;title=The+Hamilton+Spectator+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2025-02-05&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Hamilton+Spectator+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Thanks to robust investing in the 1960s, Ontario established an enviable world-class education system, spanning elementary to post-secondary.
For the first time in history, public post-secondary education was affordable. But, oh, how times have changed.
Ontario has a ratio of 34 students to one professor. Contrast that with the rest of Canada, which averages 23:1.
The Ford government spends $9,890 per domestic full-time student annually (2021) despite the national average being $15,807. That $5,917 deficit manufactured a crisis impacting students, faculty and the future of Ontario.
Since 2019, cuts to grants greatly increased student debt. Add increased domestic tuition fees and it's easy to understand why many post-secondary graduates have to make the painful decision of not pursuing a second degree or graduate studies.
The scarcity of university funding not only affects the arts, but niche programs like diversifying Indigenous sciences and programs targeting underserviced communities.
To make up for provincial funding cuts, universities have increasingly exploited international students. A 2022 Ontario Auditor General's report noted international students made up 17 per cent of university and college enrolments (2021) while accounting for 45 per cent of tuition revenue. Just under 20 per cent of the student population is providing almost 50 per cent of the revenue.
Limits on international students put in place by the federal Liberals target private universities that often overpromise what they can deliver. The only public university impacted by this policy change is Algoma University, with campuses in Sault Ste. Marie and Brampton.
Algoma entered into a public-private partnership, or P3, with Yorkville University, a private, for-profit university. A 2022 report by Ontario's Auditor General revealed Algoma was "becoming overburdened by debt in 2016-17." To solve the funding crisis, Algoma began admitting more international students to its Brampton campus. The student population increased from 540 students to 5,372.
The impact is also being felt by faculty who are represented by the Ontario Public Service Employees Union (OPSEU). As course design and delivery is transferred from Algoma to Yorkville, non-unionized faculty will be paid much less than faculty represented by OPSEU Local 685.
Historically, P3s have proven disastrous whether they involve hospitals or municipally owned water and waste water systems.
These same outcomes have played out around the world leading some European countries to ban the use of P3s because ultimately the public commons shoulders all of the risks and unforeseen costs, while the private, for-profit portion of the arrangement ensures share holders receive their expected returns. Buying your way out of a P3 project gone wrong can keep taxpayers indebted to a corporation for decades.
International students make up 18 per cent of medical students, yet Doug Ford recently declared all spots in Ontario's medical schools will be reserved for students from Ontario.
York University Medical School will add hundreds of family physician spots despite 108 reserved spots going unfilled in 2024. Those vacancies had absolutely nothing to do with international students, but everything to do with intersecting manufactured financial crises, including the cost of a basic post-secondary degree, medical degree, rent, food and transportation, plus the added expense of opening and operating a family practice.
And, I haven't even touched on the impacts this manufactured financial crisis has on professors who bear the brunt of not only larger classes, but travelling between universities piecing together multiple short-term contracts often bereft of benefits or job security with precarious pay and no guarantee of tenure.
Ford is destroying post-secondary education while perpetuating the doctor shortage. Bottom line: Ford's just too expensive.
CAPTION: The Ford government spends $9,890 per domestic full-time student annually (2021) despite the national average being $15,807. That $5,917 deficit manufactured a crisis impacting students, faculty and the future of Ontario, Doreen Nicoll writes.
CREDIT: Doreen Nicoll

Subject: Tuition; Foreign students; Economic crisis; Colleges & universities; Funding; Education

Business indexing term: Subject: Economic crisis Colleges & universities

Document 220 of 500

Is Doug Ford's hot mic mistake the turning point in the Ontario election campaign?

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/is-doug-fords-hot-mic-mistake-turning-point/docview/3163570729/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Is+Doug+Ford%27s+hot+mic+mistake+the+turning+point+in+the+Ontario+election+campaign%3F&amp;title=The+Hamilton+Spectator+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2025-02-04&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Hamilton+Spectator+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Bonnie Crombie wants to make the Ontario election about Ontario again.
The rookie Liberal leader may now get her way, thanks to Doug Ford himself.
Hard as Ford has tried to make the campaign about just Donald Trump alone, Crombie is now confident that his re-election play has been exposed as a cynical ploy to retain power as premier. Right out of the gate this week, Ford dropped the ball when he was caught on an open mic telling his Progressive Conservative supporters the precise opposite, in private, of what he tells Ontarians in public.
Ford confessed he "100 per cent" rooted for Trump to win last Nov. 5 - the very politician who promised to impose ruinous tariffs on Ontario. In one blunder, Ford exposed his secret love-hate relationship with the American president.
That accidental moment of truth reveals the depths of his duplicity, Crombie told the Democracy Forum that I hosted Tuesday at Toronto Metropolitan University. And she believes it's a game-changer in an election she now wants to turn into a referendum on Ford - not Trump.
"I think the referendum will be on Doug Ford and his performance as premier, but he wants to make it about tariffs - even though the election is not about tariffs," she told me. "We know he used that as an excuse to call an election - he said so basically himself."
Crombie is convinced Ford's unintentional confessional will dog him until voting day on Feb. 27.
"I don't think it's a one-day wonder - there are always these moments in a campaign that are kind of pivot points, turning points, that are revealing about a person's character," she told me.
"So what does it say about a man who is a leader in this province, who would stand with the president, knowing that he was going to be hurtful to our economy?"
Stunning as Ford's mistake might seem, the bigger question is whether Crombie can truly capitalize on it. He has blundered before - on the Greenbelt scandal and the beer retailing boondoggle, with billions of dollars at stake - yet voters have forgiven and forgotten, leaving him far ahead in first place according to opinion polls.
Who can defeat Ford, as he eyes a third consecutive majority government? Can Crombie, who is also under attack from NDP Leader Marit Stiles?
The Liberal leader insists she can do it by shifting the "ballot question" from Trump to Ford, from America to Ontario, and from tariff tactics to the problems facing Ontarians at home. That means sidestepping Ford's traps as he tries to drive political wedges on divisive issues.
"I will, not sorry to say, be talking about booze, bike lanes, big spas, or my rich buddies. I will be speaking about health care, affordability, education to some degree, and building homes and tax cuts."
The Liberal leader has made health care the centrepiece of her campaign, focusing on a $3-billion plan to fix the shortage of family doctors that affects more than two million Ontarians. But she also spoke passionately - and personally - about the housing crisis that affects middle class people and afflicts homeless people.
Crombie has promised major investment to increase security on public transit, where many of the unhoused can be found riding the subway or streetcars - but she condemned Ford's plan to remove encampments in public parks if people have nowhere else to go.
Her opposition to Ford's tactics would compel Crombie to make common cause with other opposition parties - notably the New Democrats and Greens - if the Tories were to fall short of a governing majority in this election. Should the Liberals hold the balance of power, there is no circumstance under which she would prop up a Ford government.
"Would not, no. No, I would not keep him in power. I think he's doing ... immense damage to this province and the people of this province, and I think he has to go. He has to be sent packing," Crombie told the Democracy Forum.
She criticized the Tories for leaving colleges and universities underfunded after they cut tuition by 10 per cent in 2019, leaving it frozen ever since. Crombie pledged to make the post-secondary sector whole again with fresh infusions of cash flow, but stressed that she still wouldn't increase tuition.
She also cast herself as a tax cutter who'd still find more money for health and housing. How to reconcile all these seemingly opposed promises? Crombie claims she'd do it by eliminating the giveaways favoured by Ford - like the $3 billion cost of mailing out $200 rebate cheques to every Ontarian in the middle of an election campaign, billions more wasted on the beer boondoggle and overspending on Ontario Place.
That's money out the door, of course. But Crombie promised more precision in a fully-costed campaign platform that's coming soon.
* * *
Crombie's appearance at the Democracy Forum can be viewed online. I'll be hosting NDP Leader Marit Stiles at Toronto Metropolitan University on Wednesday at a noon event livestreamed here on thestar.com. Disclosure: I'm a senior fellow at the Dais at TMU, which sponsors the events.
CAPTION: Star columnist Martin Regg Cohn speaks with Ontario Liberal Leader Bonnie Crombie at Toronto Metropolitan University'sDemocracy Forum on Feb. 4, 2025.

Subject: Political campaigns; Referendums; Democracy; Homeless people; Tariffs

Business indexing term: Subject: Tariffs

Company / organization: Name: Toronto Metropolitan University; NAICS: 611310

Title: Is Doug Ford's hot mic mistake the turning point in the Ontario election campaign?

Publication title: The Hamilton Spectator (Online); Hamilton

Publication year: 2025

Publication date: Feb 4, 2025

Section: politics/political-opinion

Publisher: Torstar Syndication Services, a Division of Toronto Star Newspapers Limited

Place of publication: Hamilton

Country of publication: Canada

Publication subject: General Interest Periodicals--Canada

Source type: Blog, Podcast, or Website

Language of publication: English

Document 221 of 500

We didn't want this, but now we must fight

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/we-didnt-want-this-now-must-fight/docview/3163150295/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=unknown&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=We+didn%27t+want+this%2C+but+now+we+must+fight&amp;title=The+Spectator&amp;issn=11899417&amp;date=2025-02-04&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.8&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Spectator&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: The trade war we are now fighting with the United States - a war we never wanted - is a momentous test for our country.
The cost of living, already at a crisis level for many, will rise. Canadian businesses, already struggling amid a weak economy, will be hurt. The dollar will fall. Jobs will be lost.
As we start to feel this pain, the temptation will be to point fingers at one another. The political reflex of the moment is to divide at every opportunity, to use any crisis as a wedge to separate not only one party from its rivals, but also its supporters from everyone else.
This is what Donald Trump wants. He wants us to buy that his tariffs are the inevitable consequence of our border policies, never mind that the flow of fentanyl from our country is a trickle while the flow of guns from his is a flood. He wants us to be distracted by our anger and distrust, to believe that our standing in the world has been so weakened that we have no choice but to accept his terms. He wants us to blame ourselves and each other and acquiesce.
It's true that we have failed to adjust our posture relative to our most important trading partner, even as it has become capricious and belligerent. We have failed to diversify our trading relationships despite the evident trouble on the horizon. We have failed to ease arbitrary interprovincial trade barriers, update our antiquated industrial strategy or take other measures to make ourselves more self-sufficient. But he is dead wrong if he thinks we have been weakened to the point where we no longer believe that our sovereignty is real or worth protecting.
There's been much speculation about Trump's motives. Since the border excuse is obviously bogus, what's he actually after?
If this is, as he claims, also about saving American jobs, it's a funny way of going about it. Surely part of the actual reason is simply revenue, particularly to offset the costs of the tax cuts he has promised to impose. But key, too, is what he might call the art of the deal.
He appears to be after leverage, not just for renegotiating the United States-Mexico-Canada Agreement, but also to pursue whatever his regime determines is in his country's interests, whatever they might be.
In other words, he's trying to bully us - and how we and other like-minded, similarly threatened countries respond will shape the direction of the world in the years to come.
Power has always mattered. But Trump represents power unconstrained by legal or moral guardrails. He believes there is no place for a country such as ours, a middle power that has often been a leader and beneficiary of the rules-based liberal international order, wielding our soft power to change the world, or at least nudge it in a better direction. He sees no value in the peace and order our constitution guarantees.
Now the question is whether our commitment to these values and to our shared citizenship eclipses the differences we have become so focused on.
In the days since Trump confirmed his tariffs, we have seen glimmers of hope, an outpouring of something like patriotism, a decidedly un-Canadian sentiment. We have seen leaders from across the political spectrum - Jean Chrétien and Stephen Harper alike - call for unity. We have seen businesses support retaliatory measures and call on governments to support furloughed workers, knowing these will come at a cost. Many citizens have been quick to take matters into their own hands, signing petitions, vowing to boycott American goods and to change travel plans. Prime Minister Justin Trudeau and Premier Doug Ford have both projected resolve and strength, taking strategic and targeted countermeasures, as they should.
These Canadians understand what all of us must now grasp: No one has ever won by appeasing a bully. No one has ever won by negotiating with a knife to their throat. But again and again, battles have been won by those who were counted out, who had no right to survive, never mind thrive, but did because they found strength in each other and a shared commitment to ideals and together did the hard work necessary to overcome.
It has never been harder to band together despite our differences, and never more important.
CAPTION:

Document 222 of 500

Wrong time to be holding an election, Ford critics say

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/wrong-time-be-holding-election-ford-critics-say/docview/3163150040/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Wrong+time+to+be+holding+an+election%2C+Ford+critics+say&amp;title=The+Spectator&amp;issn=11899417&amp;date=2025-02-04&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.5&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Spectator&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Ontario Progressive Conservative Leader Doug Ford is defending simultaneously wearing two hats - campaigning in a snap election he called and taking anti-tariff actions as premier.
This is a bad time for Ontario to find itself in an election, the other political party leaders say, amid the chaos of U.S. President Donald Trump's tariff threats.
Tariffs of 25 per cent on Canadian goods were expected on Feb. 1, then on Feb. 4 and now not for at least another 30 days, Prime Minister Justin Trudeau said Monday.
Despite the turmoil, Ford steadfastly maintains he can respond to tariffs as premier while also campaigning for re-election.
He announced a few retaliatory measures as premier in recent days, including removing American products from Liquor Control Board of Ontario shelves and banning American companies from provincial contracts. He said Monday morning he would rip up a $100-million contract with Elon Musk's SpaceX to deliver high-speed internet to remote areas via Starlink satellite technology.
Those moves - including the Starlink cancellation he had touted hours earlier at a campaign event - were put on hold Monday afternoon after Trump spoke to Trudeau and agreed to a month-long reprieve.
As part of its re-election campaign, Ford's party also responded to the tariff threat by announcing big-ticket stimulus spending measures worth tens of billions of dollars that they would not be able to implement until after election day on Feb. 27.
At an election announcement earlier on Monday, Ford bristled at a suggestion that an early election call was reckless because he wouldn't be able to effectively respond to any tariffs implemented during the campaign.
"We're going to do everything we can as we're standing here," he said. "If any emergencies come, I'll reconvene cabinet. That's not an unusual request."
The election is "an attempt to cling to power," Liberal Leader Bonnie Crombie said in Kitchener.
"Why, when you know there's a threat coming with an incoming president rattling the sabre of punitive economic measures including tariffs, would you choose this moment to go to an election call?" she said prior to the tariff reversal on Monday afternoon.
Green Party Leader Mike Schreiner said Ford is "playing politics" with tariffs and it's wrong.
"We shouldn't even be in an election right now because we should all be working together to have a united front against Trump," he said.
"Then for the premier to use essentially government announcements to advantage him in the campaign is not good for democracy."
NDP Leader Marit Stiles noted that the government should technically be in caretaker mode.
Stiles later called the 30-day tariff reprieve a "relief," but said that doesn't change the fact that Ford has thrown the province into an election.
She said the next month should be used to "regroup as Team Canada" and work on making Ontario more "tariff-proof."
"We need to defend every single job and be ready to fight back," Stiles said in a statement.
Speaking in Oshawa, Stiles said Ford can't be trusted to protect workers because he "gave up" in 2018 when General Motors announced it would stop producing cars and trucks in the city east of Toronto.
The Canadian Press
CAPTION: Ontario NDP Leader Marit StilesOntario Liberal party Leader Bonnie Crombie

Feds allocate Hamilton $62M in transit funding starting in 2026

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/feds-allocate-hamilton-62m-transit-funding/docview/3163149618/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Feds+allocate+Hamilton+%2462M+in+transit+funding+starting+in+2026&amp;title=The+Hamilton+Spectator+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2025-02-04&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Hamilton+Spectator+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Hamilton is to receive more than $62 million in federal funding to help the city improve its transit network over the next decade.
But whether the new Liberal program, set to start allocating dollars in 2026, gets off the ground could hinge on the outcome of an upcoming federal election.
Public transit should be the focus of any "serious government," Housing, Infrastructure and Communities Minister Nathaniel Erskine-Smith said at the HSR's Mountain storage and maintenance building Monday.
And announcing Hamilton's share of the roughly $3-billion-a-year Canada Public Transit Fund ought not be about politics, Erskine-Smith suggested.
But "elections are about choices," he added, saying the federal Liberals have "invested more in public transit" than any over government.
The federal commitment comes as Hamilton embarks on Year 9 of its 10-year transit strategy with staff aiming to unveil a fresh transit plan later this year.
Ridership has rebounded since the onset of the COVID-19 pandemic to just under 22 million rides logged in 2024. This year, staff's goal is to add 53,000 hours of service.
"We all know that a thriving transit system is the heart, the beating heart of a city," Mayor Andrea Horwath said during Monday's announcement.
Starting in 2026, the federal government's commitment of about $62.12 million over 10 years offers a "stable line of sight" for the city as it aims to improve and maintain its transit service, Horwath said.
Amid city budget talks, the HSR is pitching a 6.2 per cent increase in 2025, making for a $108.6-million operation, as it looks to boost the transit network.
Transit's gross capital spending plan is $110 million, of which 42 per cent is to be funded through the municipal tax levy, staff noted in a recent budget presentation.
Part of that is $75 million toward a roughly $400-million lower-city transit maintenance and storage building under construction on Birch Avenue
The $6.2 million Hamilton stands to receive each year under the Canada Public Transit Fund is enough to buy about six buses a year, noted city transit director Maureen Cosyn Heath.
That will "give us a bit of a budget reprieve" on capital expenditures, said Cosyn Heath, noting the federal Liberals have forged a "strong partnership" with the city on transit.
"And we are very optimistic that whoever is in power will maintain that commitment to communities across Canada to move people."
Hamilton's federal transit funding in 2025 is $11 million through the Canada Infrastructure Program and $4.6 million via the Canada Community Building Fund.
The subtext of Erskine-Smith's transit announcement is political instability.
In recent weeks, Prime Minister Justin Trudeau's Liberal government has been rattled by a series of cabinet resignations, including the housing and infrastructure portfolio that Erskine-Smith took over in December.
Trudeau's decision to resign as leader and prime minister has sparked a leadership race and prorogued Parliament until March 24, when opposition leaders could bring down the government, leading to a potential spring election.
The federal political drama overlaps with a quickly emerging economic crisis as Canada faces the spectre of tariffs imposed by new U.S. President Donald Trump.
The presents "many fallouts" for the housing sector, Erskine-Smith said, noting his government aims to be "as proactive as we can" to quickly advance "shovel-ready" projects.
Demand for federal support remains high, he said, pointing out a number of funding envelopes under the Liberals' housing strategy are "oversubscribed."
The Residential Construction Council of Ontario (RESCON) has called Trump's tariffs "reckless," warning they will have a "detrimental effect on the homebuilding industry" in Canada and the United States alike.
"Such levies will only increase costs and lead to a further slowdown in residential construction activity which will exacerbate an already dire housing affordability crisis," RESCON president Richard Lyall said in a news release.
Teviah Moro is a reporter at The Hamilton Spectator. tmoro@thespec.com

Mayor Andrea Horwath, second from left, watches alongside MP Filomena Tassi, left, and MP Lisa Hepfner, right.

Document 224 of 500

Protecting what and from whom?

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/protecting-what-whom/docview/3163148769/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=unknown&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Protecting+what+and+from+whom%3F&amp;title=The+Spectator&amp;issn=11899417&amp;date=2025-02-04&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.8&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Spectator&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Doug Ford, who was already premier and whose Progressive Conservatives occupy 79 of the 124 seats at Queen's Park, has repeatedly stated he needs a "strong mandate" to deal with U.S. President Donald Trump's tariffs. Last Tuesday, having triggered a multimillion-dollar election one-and-a-half years early and in the dead of winter ostensibly to secure such a mandate, Ford revealed his party's new campaign slogan: "Protect Ontario."
Protect Ontario what? Hospitals? Schools? Place? Can't be. Ontario hospitals are overcrowded, Ontario schools are underfunded and Ontario Place has been leased to foreign interests. No, "from America" is what's implied. And Ford has done everything in his power to make you infer it: If you can't give the voters something to rally around, give them something to rally against.
After almost seven years of Ford rule in Ontario, things have gone more or less as you'd expect.
You've got your program cuts (e.g., supervised-consumption sites), your creeping privatization (e.g., the health-care system) and your vibes-based policy-making (e.g., removing bike lanes). You've got your insistent claims of fiscal prudence and your biggest, most expensive cabinet in history. And you've got your millionaire man of the people professing to shield you from threats foreign (Trump, explicitly) and domestic (cyclists, presumably).
It's all very predictable stuff, and if, as predicted, Ford wins on Feb. 27, there's a good chance you'll get more of the same. But after the bike lanes are removed and the consumption sites are eradicated, after the Ontario Science Centre meets whatever grim fate awaits it and Ontario Place becomes a parking garage, after Ford realizes his vision for this province, what will be left of it to protect?
See, this is one of the many reasons cultural and educational institutions matter: they give us a sense of our shared heritage, of what Ontario has been and could be and of why that's worth protecting. I thought conservatives loved that sort of thing, but politicians like Ford keep proving me wrong. No one's patriotic feelings are stirred by a privately operated luxury spa. No one's sense of self is located in corner-store beer.
This is not a liberal-versus-conservative thing. It was a conservative who established Ontario Place. It was a conservative who established the Ontario Science Centre. It was a conservative who established our system of community colleges and our public broadcaster. In fact, it was all the same guy: the late Bill Davis, premier from 1971 to 1985 and, one can only assume, sworn enemy of Doug Ford, who sold off the first thing, let rot the second thing, hobbled the third thing, and mostly ignored the fourth.
Meanwhile, what has Ford contributed to the cultural life of this province? What has he contributed to our sense of pride in Ontario? If he wants to protect us from America, he could start by showing us a future that's worth protecting. His sloganeering might briefly rouse the jingoist inside us, but it's leaders like Davis who manage to instil things that are more enduring, things that can't be expressed via baseball cap.
Alas, also things that don't send voters to the polls. And that's the point, really. Ontarians may be united against Trump (although even that's questionable), but that doesn't mean they're united. A substantial number of Ontarians - a substantial number of whom won't vote - don't want to give Ford a stronger mandate, because they saw what he did with the first two. Saving the economy from Trump's tariffs is obviously important, but so is being able to afford a house, see a family doctor, and bicycle to work without getting killed.
"Protect Ontario," sure. Only give us something to protect.
Graeme Bayliss is an opinion editor at the Toronto Star.
CAPTION:

CREDIT: Graeme Bayliss Graeme Bayliss is an opinion editor at the Toronto Star.;

Document 225 of 500

Trump's tariffs could devastate Ontario's job market. Here are 5 trades where workers can boost their chances of landing a job

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/trumps-tariffs-could-devastate-ontarios-job/docview/3163272579/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Trump%27s+tariffs+could+devastate+Ontario%27s+job+market.+Here+are+5+trades+where+workers+can+boost+their+chances+of+landing+a+job&amp;title=The+Hamilton+Spectator+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2025-02-03&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Hamilton+Spectator+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Fears of a North American recession are intensifying amid tariff threats against Canadian imports by U.S. President Donald Trump.
While the tariffs will be paused for at least 30 days, according to Prime Minister Justin Trudeau - who spoke with Trump on Monday - they would devastate Canada's job market if they do come into effect, according to economists.
A report by RBC economics notes the tariffs would represent the largest trade shock against Canada since 1930, with grave implications for the manufacturing sector and auto industry.
Ontario Premier Doug Ford has indicated the tariffs could cost Ontario up to 500,000 jobs.
As Canada stares down the economic tsunami of Trump's threatened tariffs, some may wonder how to succeed in an increasingly tight job market.
Ian Howcroft, CEO of Skills Ontario, promotes skilled trades and technology careers to young people through a variety of outreach initiatives, said workers can increase their chances of employment by considering the trades.
Provincial government figures show 1.3 million Ontario resident work in skilled trades-related occupations. At least one in three workers in Ontario with an apprenticeship or trade certificate is aged 55 or over and nearing retirement.
While Ontario recognizes 144 trades with apprenticeship programs, the following trades may experience acute shortages as workers retire.
Here are five trade sectors where Howcroft expects to see job openings, despite the threat of a tariff-induced recession.
Electricians
Ontario is facing a shortage of electricians. Statistics Canada notes in the third quarter of 2022, there were 5,275 job vacancies for electricians, an increase of nearly 2,000 positions compared to the same quarter in 2021.
"With work that's going to continue on in infrastructure, home construction and manufacturing, that's a skill in high demand," said Howcroft.
Plumbers
Ontario is also facing a shortage of plumbers due to an aging workforce, with fewer young workers stepping in to fill vacancies.
"Everybody at some point is going to need a plumber to fix, repair or install something with regards to plumbing in the home," said Howcroft. "That's where there's an expected shortage."
Carpenters
Last year, the provincial government cited more than 1,200 carpentry vacancies across Ontario when it announced new training programs to fill acute labour shortages.
While you might think about residential construction, Howcroft said carpenters are also needed for forming and framing work for the refurbishment of nuclear reactors at the Bruce, Darlington and Pickering power sites, for example.
Welders
Welders are needed in the construction and manufacturing industries, Howcroft said. They are crucial to Canada's economy. The federal government's online job bank notes the employment outlook for welders is moderate, with employment growth expected to lead to new positions through 2026.
Technology careers
Artificial intelligence, cloud computing and software development have the potential to drive job growth in Ontario.
Howcroft said the technology sector may experience worker shortages as advanced technologies begin to play a wider role in everyday life.
"We know that whatever happens, there will be a need for skilled tradespeople and technologists. So we're just trying to do all we can to make sure young people and their parents are aware of the opportunities," said Howcroft.
How Skills Ontario can help
Skills Ontario promotes skilled trades careers through presentations and demonstrations for students and parents. Last year the organization connected with 130,000 students in 2,000 schools.
"We host the largest skills competition in the country the first full week in May," Howcroft added.
Last year, 2,700 competitors and 48,000 people took part in the skills competition. Skills Ontario also promotes trade careers for young women and Indigenous communities and offers conferences for guidance counsellors.
"What we're trying to do is make sure people's eyes are open, that their minds are open and that opportunities are open for young people to explore and move forward (in trades) if that's what they would like to do," Howcroft added.
Skills Ontario has a mobile skills unit that offers experiential activities in skilled trades.
"It allows students and parents to come on and see what it's like to drive a bulldozer, or operate a crane. We have a welding simulator, we have an auto station, an electric station, a hydraulic station and a safety station," Howcroft added. "We're trying to be creative and innovative to get these opportunities out to young people."
For more details, visit skillsontario.com.
CAPTION: Homebuilding requires skilled trade workers, many of whom will be retiring in the coming years. Electricians, plumbers and welders will be needed to fill employment vacancies, even if Ontario's economy sinks into a recession, says Ian Howcroft of Skills Ontario.

Document 226 of 500

Doug Ford defends snap election call despite Trump trade war

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/doug-ford-defends-snap-election-call-despite/docview/3163272521/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Doug+Ford+defends+snap+election+call+despite+Trump+trade+war&amp;title=The+Hamilton+Spectator+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2025-02-03&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Hamilton+Spectator+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: A defensive Doug Ford insists it was not reckless to call a provincial election against the backdrop of a trade war that could devastate Ontario's economy.
The Progressive Conservative leader, who last week triggered the Feb. 27 vote 15 months early, maintained his hands are not tied during the campaign and he can still govern while electioneering.
"I totally disagree," Ford said Monday as he accepted the endorsement of the Ontario Professional Fire Fighters Association in Etobicoke.
He noted he unilaterally scrapped a $100 million contract with Starlink, which is owned by Elon Musk, a key ally of U.S. President Donald Trump.
That change of heart came after Ford had initially defended the deal with the satellite internet provider on Jan. 24 in Brampton.
"We went out for a transparent bid, they came out top," he said that day after journalists asked him if he would follow Liberal Leader Bonnie Crombie's call to scrap the Starlink agreement.
On Monday, Ford noted he can do his day job and campaign at the same time.
"I just pulled off a billion dollars from LCBO shelves of U.S. products. This is 25 days left (in the campaign). This gives us a strong four year mandate to outlive and outlast the Trump administration. People want certainty," said the Tory leader.
"I'm out in the international media every single day. I was on Fox and going on CNN, going back on Fox. What I'm doing, I'm getting the message out to the American people," he said.
Ford added that "if any emergencies come, I'll reconvene cabinet."
To ease the strain from tariffs, his Conservatives promised $3 billion in breaks on small-business payroll taxes, a six-month deferral of business taxes, a hike in the LCBO's wholesale discount for bars and restaurants to 15 per cent from 10 per cent, a $300 million expansion of tax credits for manufacturers investing in buildings, machinery and equipment, and $40 million for municipalities facing "severe economic disruption."
But Progressive Conservative MPP Peter Bethlenfalvy, who served as Ford's finance minister, acknowledged none of that aid can be provided until after Election Day, presuming his party wins a third term.
"We need a mandate from the people of Ontario to spend their money," Bethlenfalvy told reporters in Woodbridge.
NDP Leader Marit Stiles said the Tories not done enough to "tariff-proof Ontario."
"With Doug Ford, all you have to do is look at his record. His record is side deals, bad deals, deals that sell off and sell out Ontario companies every time - and we've seen it again and again (with) Staples, Walmart, Starlink," Stiles said at Oshawa's Canadian Automotive Museum, standing in front of an NDP orange Bricklin SV1 sports car, which was made in New Brunswick.
"Doug Ford didn't create this crisis, Donald Trump did. But Ford's failures and bad deals have left Ontario's economy weaker, and the families who live here vulnerable," she said.
"We need to make Ontario stronger and more resilient so we can withstand this crisis and move beyond it."
In Kitchener-Waterloo, Crombie conceded that on tariff response "there is no daylight between my approach right now and Doug Ford," but said it was irresponsible for him to call an "unnecessary" and "opportunistic" $189 million snap election during a trade crisis.
"I agree with him on most of the measures, obviously the procurement measures and buying local and buying Ontario and buying Canadian but there are lots of other things that we can be doing," she said after a speech to the Greater Kitchener Waterloo Chamber of Commerce.
Crombie pledged to fund $150,000 bonuses to "patriotic nurses and doctors who want to come back home to Canada from the U.S. and help solve Doug Ford's health-care crisis."
Green Leader Mike Schreiner expressed concern that the Tories are focused on the hustings instead of housing.
Schreiner promised a "first-time homebuyers plan that would remove fees and taxes on the purchase of new starter homes built within urban boundaries."
"Young people are leaving the province because they've given up hope of ever being able to afford a home in Ontario. Meanwhile, the only kind of housing Doug Ford wants to build is mansions in the Greenbelt that no one can afford," he said.
That's a reference to $8.28-billion Greenbelt land swap scandal that is the subject of an ongoing RCMP criminal investigation.
Ford, who has denied any wrongdoing in the cancelled land rezoning debacle, called the snap election in part to get in front of any potential charges by the Mounties who have been on the case since October 2023.
The Tory chief also wanted to take advantage of the fact he leads in public-opinion polls. As well, he wanted to face voters before a federal election expected in the spring.
CAPTION: Progressive Conservative Leader Doug Ford speaks from the podium during a visit to Walker Construction in St. Catharines, Ont. on Jan. 31, 2025.
