Donald Trump delays threatened tariffs on Canada for 30 days

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/donald-trump-delays-threatened-tariffs-on-canada/docview/3163272458/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Donald+Trump+delays+threatened+tariffs+on+Canada+for+30+days&amp;title=The+Hamilton+Spectator+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2025-02-03&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Hamilton+Spectator+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: OTTAWA-Canada won an 11th-hour reprieve from Donald Trump's threatened U.S. tariffs on Monday, just as Mexico received, after pledging to inject millions more into border security and committing to actions that may not stave off American duties economists say could be devastating to the Canadian economy.
Hours before a midnight deadline, Prime Minister Justin Trudeau said threatened U.S. tariffs against Canada will be paused for 30 days after his second call with the U.S. president Monday, a conversation that lasted 45 minutes and set in motion four more weeks of talks with the U.S. president's team.
Trump posted on Truth Social that he was "very pleased with this initial outcome" and the tariffs would be paused "to see whether or not a final economic deal with Canada can be structured."
The prime minister pledged another $200 million to fight organized crime and fentanyl smuggling, saying Canada would appoint a "fentanyl czar," add Mexican cartels to the list of terrorist entities, and take other moves to show it was serious about addressing Trump's concerns.
Trudeau posted on X that he laid out Canada's previous $1.3-billion plan promised in November and said, "Nearly 10,000 front-line personnel are and will be working on protecting the border."
That's the same number of additional soldiers Mexico earlier pledged to send to its border with the U.S., and Trump echoed that language later.
The prime minister said Ottawa will ensure "24/7 eyes on the border, launch a Canada-U.S. Joint Strike Force to combat organized crime, fentanyl and money laundering."
"I have also signed a new intelligence directive on organized crime and fentanyl and we will be backing it with $200 million. Proposed tariffs will be paused for at least 30 days while we work together," the prime minister posted.
The second high-stakes call between Trudeau and Trump ended with Canada achieving the same break Mexico scored earlier in the day when Trump spoke to Mexican President Claudia Sheinbaum.
As Canadians awaited the results of the Trump-Trudeau call, tensions were running high.
On the Ontario election campaign trail Monday, Progressive Conservative Leader Doug Ford was caught on a hot mic expressing frustration with the president.
"On election day, was I happy this guy won? One hundred per cent I was. Then the guy pulled out the knife and f---ing yanked it in me," said Ford.
Stock markets had slumped, reflecting concern about the midnight deadline for Trump's 25 per cent tariffs on most Canadian exports, with a slightly lower 10 per cent levy on oil shipments. Canada had pledged matching counter-tariffs on $30-billion worth of American imports.
After word spread Canada had avoided the midnight onset of a trade war, Ford called it "good news," reversing the province's ban on U.S. booze and his earlier decision to cancel Ontario's $100-million contract with Elon Musk's satellite internet provider Starlink.
"We have temporarily averted tariffs that would have severely damaged our economy, giving time for more negotiation and time for cooler heads to prevail. Thank you to the countless workers, union leaders, businesses and everyday proud Canadians who rallied together to make this happen," Ford said.
Ontario will pause its retaliatory measures but if Trump goes ahead, Ford said, "we won't hesitate to remove American products off LCBO shelves or ban American companies from provincial procurement."
"No one should let their guard down or breath a sign of relief at this 30-day pause on the tariffs," said Lana Payne, head of Unifor, Canada's largest private sector union. "As a country, we must use the days ahead to continue to bring Canadians together, to continue to plan for a potential trade war, and to use every single lever we have to build a strong, resilient and diverse economy."
Trump told American reporters Monday before his second call with Trudeau that Canada should become his country's 51st state and then there would be "no tariffs."
"I'd love to see that, but some people say that would be a long shot. If people wanted to play the game right, it would be 100 per cent certain that they'd become a state," he said.
"But a lot of people don't like to play the game because they don't have a threshold of pain. And there would be some pain, but not a lot. The pain would be really theirs."
A Canadian federal official, speaking on condition they not be identified, said Trump did not raise his desire to make Canada a 51st state in the call. And while Trump raised banking as an irritant in the morning call, it did not come up in their afternoon conversation. Nor did Trump raise water as a concern, as he has publicly in the past. The Trudeau team says it is clear that the president has a long list of irritants with Canada on the trade side, but the two leaders focused on the border in the afternoon call.
Public Safety Minister David McGuinty said Canada needs to take Trump's comments about becoming a 51st state "seriously," but he said in a CBC interview that "I think that comment is just not serious."
While McGuinty said some of the latest changes were in the works already, it's not yet clear what a "fentanyl czar" will do.
Mexican and Canadian officials have had several exchanges over the past weeks, but the official said Trudeau did not consult or co-ordinate with Sheinbaum on Monday.
Trump had earlier held out little hope for a deal to avoid tariffs on Canada.
Before he spoke Trudeau on Monday afternoon, Trump welcomed Mexico's pledge to deploy an additional 10,000 soldiers at the border but said, "Canada is very tough. They're very, very tough to do business with, and we can't let them take advantage of the U.S."
Trump raised a number of complaints including his latest - that American banks "cannot" do business in Canada - a declaration which is not accurate.
The president reiterated a statement that the U.S. does not need anything Canada makes, from cars to lumber, as he underlined that he had a "very good relationship" with Sheinbaum.
Trump said his final decision on Mexico would be made following a 30-day period of negotiations led by Secretary of State Marco Rubio.
I
The Canadian Bankers Association rebuffed Trump's claim about banking in a statement and said that "Canada has a strong and competitive banking sector comprised of both domestic and foreign banks."
It said there are 16 U.S.-based bank subsidiaries and branches with around $113 billion (CDN) in assets currently operating in Canada.
"These banks specialize in a range of financial services, including corporate and commercial lending, treasury services, credit card products, investment banking and mortgage financing. They serve not only customers with cross-border business activities, but also Canada's domestic retail market. U.S. banks now make up half of all foreign bank assets in Canada."
Conservative Leader Pierre Poilievre said Monday that Canada should deploy soldiers to the border too, posting on X what he called a plan to "take back control of the border and save" Canada-U.S. trade. "Send Canadian Forces troops, helicopters & surveillance to the border now."
Ontario's threat to cancel its Starlink contract had directly targeted Starlink owner Musk, a key Trump adviser.
Musk briefly responded on X, the social media platform he owns, with a shrug: "Oh well."
The Ontario government signed a $100-million deal in November for Starlink to deliver high-speed internet to remote residents in rural and northern Ontario.
Want to buy Canadian during a trade war? Here is a list of brands to support
Trudeau had responded to Trump's tariff threat with a plan to impose 25 per cent tariffs on a range of American products worth up to $30 billion, with another layer of counter-duties on $125 billion worth of American imports to come in three weeks. All that is now on hold.
Ontario, Manitoba, Nova Scotia and British Columbia said they would ban American liquor from store shelves as political leaders pushed a "Buy Canadian" response to Trump's weekend announcement he would impose tariffs, even as two western premiers urged Ottawa to "de-escalate" the rhetoric around tariffs.
Here are some ways Trump's tariffs might hurt Canadians, from food to housing to small businesses

Developer's Greenbelt urban expansion pitch along Ancaster's Sulphur Springs Road raises eyebrows

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/developers-greenbelt-urban-expansion-pitch-along/docview/3162834083/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Developer%27s+Greenbelt+urban+expansion+pitch+along+Ancaster%27s+Sulphur+Springs+Road+raises+eyebrows&amp;title=The+Hamilton+Spectator+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2025-02-03&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Hamilton+Spectator+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: A Toronto developer's pitch to expand Hamilton's fixed urban boundary to build homes in a swath of local Greenbelt has left city officials perplexed.
The Greenbelt is off-limits to development, despite the province's recent flip-flopping on Ontario's two-million-acre belt of protected natural and farmland.
Mizrahi Developments' plan for 14 single-detached and 61 townhouse units on Sulphur Springs Road in Ancaster also runs afoul of the Niagara Escarpment Plan.
The policy clashes make the proposal puzzling, local councillor Craig Cassar says.
"Why they put the application in and put in resources is very curious."
City planning staff, meanwhile, say they've asked the developer to withdraw the application due to its conflicts with provincial policies.
Mizrahi didn't immediately respond to The Spectator's request for comment.
In filing its materials, the developer skipped formal consultation with planning staff, an early step designed to work out issues with development proposals.
Whatever the case, the proposal is a non-starter for Cassar.
"I am not supportive of expanding into the Greenbelt."
In 2023, Premier Doug Ford's Tory government walked back Greenbelt carve-outs amid a pair of scathing watchdog reports and an RCMP probe.
Around the same time, the province reversed course on an urban expansion into non-Greenbelt rural lands it had imposed on Hamilton.
That U-turn, however, has been overtaken by recent legislative changes that facilitate expansions, compromising the city's strategy to freeze Hamilton's urban boundary and channel growth inward.
Bill 185, which came into effect in June 2024, allows developers to appeal council "nondecisions" or rejections of urban expansion applications to the Ontario Land Tribunal.
Also, a newly drafted provincial planning statement axes the requirement of comprehensive municipal land-use studies before boundary-expanding bids can be made.
That clears the way for urban expansion applications "at any time and with no limit on the size," Hamilton city planners have pointed out.
The appeal rights for boundary expansions - excluding the Greenbelt - allow "development applications to be considered in a balanced manner," Municipal Affairs and Housing Minister Paul Calandra's office previously told The Spectator.
The provincial planning statement, which came into effect in October, is meant to bolster Ontario's housing supply and protect the environment, prime agricultural areas and public health and safety, Calandra's office said.
In response to the changes, the city has drafted a framework to evaluate urban expansion applications and created a new team of staff to respond to them. In addition, $1.5 million is earmarked to fight OLT appeals.
"We didn't create these problems, but we have to deal with them," Coun. Brad Clark said during city budget deliberations Friday.
In a cover letter to the city, planning consultants for Mizrahi Developments note the proposal for 163 and 159 Sulphur Springs Rd. is focused on nearly 25 acres of land between Lovers Lane and Wilson Street East. Two homes are on the site.
"The design will optimize development based on the site's topography and preserve natural features, including enhancing the existing trail system and stormwater management," the Biglieri Group Ltd. wrote.
The urban expansion proposal is among a flurry of applications the city has received amid the provincial legislative changes:
The Whitechurch Landowners Group is proposing an 805-acre urban expansion for roughly 4,800 homes on land just east of Mount Hope in Glanbrook;The Elfrida Community Builders Group wants to fold about 3,000 acres to the south and east of upper Stoney Creek into the urban area for a mix of housing types.
The Upper West Side Landowners Group has taken its proposed urban expansion for parcels of Twenty Road West near the airport to the OLT, with hearings scheduled for 2026.
The consortium has also asked for Mayor Andrea Horwath's help to fast-track land-use changes to advance its plan for roughly 5,000 residential units and up to five million square feet of employment uses via a minister's zoning order (MZO).
Through an arrangement with Habitat for Humanity, the developers have noted as many as 500 of the homes, or 10 per cent, would be affordable.
If granted, the MZO would result in an urban expansion of about 220 acres and the conversion of 245 acres of airport employment lands to residential and other uses, notes a city report before councillors Tuesday.
Raising concerns, including preservation of Hamilton's firm urban boundary and employment lands, staff recommend opposing the consortium's MZO pitch.
Teviah Moro is a reporter at The Hamilton Spectator. tmoro@thespec.com
CAPTION: Mizrahi Developments is proposing 14 single-detached and 61 townhouse units on Sulphur Springs Road in Ancaster."I am not supportive of expanding into the Greenbelt," said Coun. Craig Cassar.

Trump tariffs: Trudeau and Trump speak this morning; Ontario banning American companies; Loonie falls to lowest level since 2003

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/trump-tariffs-trudeau-speak-this-morning-ontario/docview/3162833783/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Trump+tariffs%3A+Trudeau+and+Trump+speak+this+morning%3B+Ontario+banning+American+companies%3B+Loonie+falls+to+lowest+level+since+2003&amp;title=The+Hamilton+Spectator+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2025-02-03&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Hamilton+Spectator+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: The latest news as Donald Trump's administration imposes tariffs on Canada. Web links to longer stories if available. Follow the Star's live coverage on Monday.
10:05 a.m. Trump announced he'd spoken to Prime Minister Justin Trudeau Monday morning
They are expected to speak again this afternoon.
The Prime Minister's Office confirmed the back-to-back calls that come after Trudeau told Canadians he'd been unable to reach Trump since the president's Jan. 20 inauguration as the prime minister rolled out Canada's retaliation plan on the weekend. -Tonda MacCharles, Robert Benzie
10 a.m. Trump posts to social media account
U.S. President Donald Trump added more reasons for his looming tariff war with Canada, complaining on social media that American banks can't "open" or do business in Canada, as he announced he'd spoken to Prime Minister Justin Trudeau Monday morning.
In addition to banking barriers, Trump cited what he calls an influx of undocumented migrants and illegal drugs across Canada's and Mexico's borders as justification for a U.S.-led tariff war with Canada, Mexico and China, and said he expected to speak to Trudeau again Monday afternoon. - Tonda MacCharles, Robert Benzie
9:10 a.m. Here are some ways Trump's tariffs might hurt Canadians, from food to housing to small businesses
As Canadians brace themselves for the wallop of the White House's punishing tariffs and Canada's retaliation, the impacts are widely expected to be devastating - putting hundreds of jobs in major industries like automotive manufacturing on the line, depressing the Canadian dollar, and increasing the sticker price of countless everyday items.
Read the full story from the Star's Victoria Gibson
8:50 a.m. The onset of a U.S.-led tariff war with Canada, Mexico and China triggered turmoil in global markets and prompted Ontario to cancel its contract with Elon Musk's Starlink satellite company and to ban American companies from provincial contracts.
"We'll be ripping up the province's contract with Starlink. Ontario won't do business with people hellbent on destroying our economy," said PC Leader Doug Ford in a written statement Monday.
Starting today and "until U.S. tariffs are removed," Ford said Ontario would ban American companies from provincial contracts, noting that each year the provincial government and its agencies "spend $30 billion on procurement, alongside our $200 billion plan to build Ontario."
Read the full story from the Star's Tonda MacCharles and Robert Benzie
8:30 a.m. Trump and Trudeau to talk today
Justin Trudeau is expected to speak with President Donald Trump today as Canada's leaders scramble to prepare for a trade war with the United States.
Trump mentioned the planned conversation after returning to Washington from Florida on Sunday night and Trudeau's office confirmed a call is scheduled.
Speaking with reporters at the Joint Base Andrews Sunday evening, the Republican president said, "Canada has been abusive to the United States for many years," as he described the trade relationship between the two countries as a "one-way street."
Read the full story from the Canadian Press
8:15 a.m. PC Leader Doug Ford issued a release on tariffs on Monday, saying Ontario is banning American companies from provincial contracts.
"Starting today and until U.S. tariffs are removed, Ontario is banning American companies from provincial contracts.
Every year, the Ontario government and its agencies spend $30 billion on procurement, alongside our $200 billion plan to build Ontario. U.S.-based businesses will now lose out on tens of billions of dollars in new revenues. They only have President Trump to blame.
We're going one step further. We'll be ripping up the province's contract with Starlink. Ontario won't do business with people hellbent on destroying our economy.
8 a.m. The Canadian dollar fell to its lowest level in more than two decades after U.S. President Donald Trump announced sweeping tariffs to be enacted Tuesday.
The loonie was trading for 68.13 cents US, its lowest level against the U.S. dollar since 2003, compared with 69.04 cents US on Friday.
The futures market also pointed to large drops for U.S. stock markets when they begin trading this morning.
What do Trudeau's tariffs on the U.S. mean for Canadians?
As Ottawa slaps retaliatory tariffs on the U.S., life in Canada - for everyone from the average consumer to some of the country's largest manufacturer sectors - stands to get more expensive.
Effective Tuesday, the Canadian government is imposing 25 per cent counter-tariffs on $30 billion in goods imported from the United States in response to Trump's executive order imposing a 25 per cent surcharge on Canadian goods, as well as a 10 per cent one on energy imports.
The measures will hike the costs of everyday American imports, including meat, dairy, produce, coffee, booze, clothing, jewelry, footwear, and cosmetics, amongst dozens of others. It will also make U.S. building materials - metal, rubber, plastic, fabric, lumber, and glass - more expensive.
Read the full story from the Star's Abby O'Brien
Sunday: Ontario yanking U.S. alcohol from LCBO shelves 'indefinitely'
The provincial government is ordering the Liquor Control Board of Ontario to remove thousands of American products from its shelves and catalogue in response to U.S. President Donald Trump's order to impose tariffs on Canadian goods Tuesday.
"Every year, LCBO sells nearly $1 billion worth of American wine, beer, spirits and seltzers," Progressive Conservative party Leader Doug Ford wrote in an online statement. "Not anymore"
The removal will roll out Tuesday and will also prohibit Ontario-based restaurants and businesses from restocking and ordering American beer, wine and spirits since the LCBO is the only alcohol wholesaler in the province.
Read Sunday's tariff live coverage

Document 230 of 500

Province to pull U.S. booze from shelves: Ontario, Quebec, B.C. and others take aim at American liquor products as tariff war intensifies

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/province-pull-u-s-booze-shelves/docview/3162729359/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Province+to+pull+U.S.+booze+from+shelves%3A+Ontario%2C+Quebec%2C+B.C.+and+others+take+aim+at+American+liquor+products+as+tariff+war+intensifies&amp;title=The+Spectator&amp;issn=11899417&amp;date=2025-02-03&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.6&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Spectator&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Canadian alcohol producers are cheering decisions from several provinces to yank U.S. booze from provincial liquor stores.
They say the move will deliver a boost to homegrown business while adding ammunition to a federal plan aimed at getting the U.S. to back down from tariffs.
"No doubt, it's a big opportunity ... to showcase true nationalism, and it's supporting Canadian jobs, local manufacturing," said Bromlyn Bethune, the president of Toronto-based Steam Whistle Brewing.
Her comments came on the heels of Ontario Premier Doug Ford's Sunday announcement revealing that he would take aim at the nearly $1 billion worth of U.S. wine, beer, spirits and seltzers sold in the LCBO every year.
The more than 3,600 products from 35 U.S. states are due to leave the liquor store on Tuesday, when U.S. President Donald Trump's promised tariffs on Canadian goods come into effect.
"There's never been a better time to choose an amazing Ontario-made or Canadian-made product," Ford said in a statement.
British Columbia, Quebec, Manitoba, Nova Scotia and Newfoundland and Labrador all made the same move as Ford over the weekend.
The moves position alcohol as a key battleground in the trade feud that intensified Saturday when Trump signed an executive order applying 25 per cent tariffs on Canada goods starting Tuesday. He carved out an exception for Canadian energy, which will see a lower 10 per cent duty.
Trump has framed the tariffs as his way of tackling his concerns about security at American borders, including the flow of fentanyl.
U.S Customs and Border Protection statistics show less than one per cent of all fentanyl seized comes from the northern border.
Canada fired back against Trump's assertions and the tariffs with its own retaliatory package announced by Prime Minister Justin Trudeau on Saturday night.
The package begins with Canada targeting $30 billion in U.S goods on Tuesday, followed by $125 billion in duties on American products in 21 days. The list of goods making up the package includes beer made from malt, many wines produced with fresh grapes and vermouth, along with whiskies, tequilas, vodkas, gins and rums.
The Canadian Press

Document 231 of 500

Canada slaps retaliatory tariffs on hundreds of American goods: Government taking steps to ease the effects of countermeasures

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/canada-slaps-retaliatory-tariffs-on-hundreds/docview/3162729357/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Canada+slaps+retaliatory+tariffs+on+hundreds+of+American+goods%3A+Government+taking+steps+to+ease+the+effects+of+countermeasures&amp;title=The+Spectator&amp;issn=11899417&amp;date=2025-02-03&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.1&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Spectator&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Ottawa is imposing 25 per cent tariffs on hundreds of goods originating in the United States - from meat and milk to carpets and curtains - in response to steep new American levies against Canada.
A federal official also signalled that Canada will pursue available legal remedies in response to the U.S. breaching its international trade commitments.
The federal government released a detailed list of the retaliatory tariffs on $30 billion in goods Sunday, less than 24 hours after U.S. President Donald Trump forcibly dragged Canada into an unprecedented trade war. Trump signed executive orders Saturday evening to hit America's closest neighbour with damaging duties of 10 per cent on energy and 25 per cent on everything else.
Canada's initial response takes effect Tuesday, when the U.S. tariffs are applied. In three weeks, Ottawa plans to add another $125 billion in tariffs on many other U.S. goods after consulting with industry.
The federal government says the second list will include items such as cars, trucks, buses, steel and aluminum, aerospace products, and fruits and vegetables.
The government is also taking steps to ease the effects of the countermeasures on Canadians by launching a process to allow businesses to request exceptional relief from the tariffs.
Many Canadians weren't waiting for the government to retaliate. Some took to social media to announce they are cancelling subscriptions to U.S. streaming services, pledging not to travel south, and sharing lists and videos of Canadian-made items to help avoid buying American brands.
Politicians are pushing the buy-Canadian plan as well.
"Now is the time to choose products made right here in Canada," Prime Minister Justin Trudeau posted Sunday on X. "Check the labels. Let's do our part. Wherever we can, choose Canada."
Liberal leadership candidate and former finance minister Chrystia Freeland also issued a statement urging Canadians to "try your best not to buy products made in the United States."
Freeland joined Mark Carney and the three others running for the party leadership in a statement of solidarity vowing to "stand up against these unjust tariffs."
"The Liberal party was built on the promise of Canada, and it is what unites us today," the statement said.
Several provinces confirmed Sunday that U.S. products will be removed from their provincial liquor stores on Tuesday.
The LCBO, Ontario's alcohol wholesaler, will also scrub American products from its catalogue so that Ontario-based restaurants and sellers can't order or restock them.
After announcing the provincial liquor corporation would stop selling American products, Manitoba Premier Wab Kinew said the tariff war poses a threat to Canadian sovereignty.
"This is a question about national identity. This is a question about who we are as Canadians and Manitobans," Kinew said. "If you have to switch to Crown Royal as part of that patriotism, I think that's a pretty good deal."
Trudeau said Saturday that additional non-tariff measures were being discussed by the federal and provincial governments "including some relating to critical minerals, energy, procurement and other partnerships."
He did not disclose whether those measures would include cutting energy supplies to the U.S. Trudeau said that any measures that involve one region of the country more than others will be approached "carefully" and with the full partnership of regional leaders.
Alberta Premier Danielle Smith has been staunchly opposed to any retaliation that touches her province's oil and gas industry.
The prime minister said the better option for both countries is to avoid tariffs altogether.
On Sunday, Conservative Leader Pierre Poilievre urged the Liberal government to recall Parliament to put in place a "Canada first" plan to handle economic repercussions from the tariffs.
Poilievre proposed retaliating "dollar for dollar" by targeting industries that will have a maximum impact on the United States while harming Canadian consumers as little as possible.
At a technical briefing Sunday, a federal official who spoke on condition of anonymity said Canada clearly considers the U.S. tariffs to be a violation of trade commitments the United States has made under both the Canada-U.S.-Mexico agreement and the World Trade Organization.
"We will obviously pursue the legal recourse that we believe we have through the agreements that we share with the United States," the official said. "If other legal avenues are available to us, they will be considered as well."
Trump took to social media Sunday to repeat his false claim that the United States subsidizes Canada. He pushed his plan for Canada to become "our cherished 51st state" to avoid tariffs.
"We don't need anything they have," Trump posted. "We have unlimited energy, should make our own cars, and have more lumber than we can ever use. Without this massive subsidy, Canada ceases to exist as a viable country."
The president linked the levies to fentanyl crossing the border and included a retaliation clause that says if Canada responds with duties on American products, the levies could be increased.
The Canadian Press
CAPTION: Prime Minister Justin Trudeau, left, addresses the media following the imposition of a raft of tariffs by U.S. President Donald Trump against Canada, Mexico and China, in Ottawa, Saturday.Justin Tang The Canadian Press

Document 232 of 500

Ford is betting his opponents will stay home on election day. Again

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/ford-is-betting-his-opponents-will-stay-home-on/docview/3162729284/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Ford+is+betting+his+opponents+will+stay+home+on+election+day.+Again&amp;title=The+Hamilton+Spectator+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2025-02-03&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Hamilton+Spectator+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Doug Ford says he needs a new "mandate" to deal with Donald Trump. But Ford still has almost two more years remaining in a safe, majority government.
He has already abused that majority with unmandated spending of tax dollars to, among many things: break a contract unnecessarily because he wanted beer in corner stores sooner; close the Ontario Science Centre; remove cycling lanes; force the development of big highways when six-plus lanes of Highway 407 are near empty every day; and send us each $200 which most of us would have gladly redirected to health care and education if given the choice. And then there are his actions like shortchanging our health sector by $28 billion while, for example, moving seniors in long-term care out of their communities.
Ford's election call is really about grabbing power by adding two more years to his existing, safe mandate. He is betting that a cold February election in just four weeks will keep most people away from the polling booths. That is how he won the last election when only 43 per cent of eligible voters showed up. His share of those votes was 41 per cent. He knows his base of voters will turn out again, he has the money to make sure that happens and he is betting that most of the remaining people won't show up to vote again.
He has been planning this election for some time because, thanks to our tax dollars, we had to watch those expensive ads touting his spending during our hockey games. In step with his election plans, government ministers were quick to announce big spending just before the official election call.
He knows Ontarians are growing weary of his government's scandals like his Greenbelt fiasco that was about to shuffle $8 billion to his developer friends. Out on the playing field of politics, he can't bash Justin Trudeau anymore and he faces the possibility of a Conservative federal government this year (historically, this makes Ontarians vote for provincial Liberals) or worse, a federal government run by a prime minister who is a former governor of the Bank of Canada and Bank of England. These are political scenarios that don't bode well for his re-election in two years.
We each need to invest in understanding the politicians wooing us and vote regardless of the choices before us. Our political party system is terrible and forces most politicians to forgo any allegiance to their constituents. Regardless of the smell of today's politics, this election, and our choices on voting day, we at least know the source of the biggest smell which is something you can choose against in the polling booth.
Any party can oversee the government; it is the civil service that ensures the province successfully carries on despite the politics.
Don't be fooled by the political rhetoric and social media trolls. We still have reputable media sources you can trust and learn from. Most importantly, your vote secures a democracy that was hard won by the generations who came before us. If you are uncertain about where or how to vote, contact Elections Ontario, or any political party and they will gladly help you.
CAPTION: Progressive Conservative Leader Doug Ford speaks Friday during a visit in St. Catharines. His election call is really about grabbing power by adding two more years to his existing, safe mandate, Allen Curry writes.
CREDIT: Allen Curry

Document 233 of 500

Greenbelt urban expansion pitch raises eyebrows: Councillor calls developer's proposal for off-limits zone 'very curious'

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/greenbelt-urban-expansion-pitch-raises-eyebrows/docview/3162729260/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Greenbelt+urban+expansion+pitch+raises+eyebrows%3A+Councillor+calls+developer%27s+proposal+for+off-limits+zone+%27very+curious%27&amp;title=The+Spectator&amp;issn=11899417&amp;date=2025-02-03&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.1&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Spectator&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: A Toronto developer's pitch to expand Hamilton's fixed urban boundary to build homes in a swath of local Greenbelt has left city officials perplexed.
The Greenbelt is off-limits to development, despite the province's recent flip-flopping on Ontario's two-million-acre belt of protected natural and farmland.
Mizrahi Developments' plan for 14 single-detached and 61 townhouse units on Sulphur Springs Road in Ancaster also runs afoul of the Niagara Escarpment Plan.
The policy clashes make the proposal puzzling, local councillor Craig Cassar says.
"Why they put the application in and put in resources is very curious."
City planning staff, meanwhile, say they've asked the developer to withdraw the application due to its conflicts with the provincial policies.
Mizrahi didn't immediately respond to The Spectator's request for comment.
In filing its materials, the developer skipped formal consultation with planning staff, an early step designed to work out issues with development proposals.
Whatever the case, the proposal is a non-starter for Cassar.
"I am not supportive of expanding into the Greenbelt."
In 2023, Premier Doug Ford's Tory government walked back Greenbelt carve-outs amid a pair of scathing watchdog reports and an RCMP probe.
Around the same time, the province reversed course on an urban expansion into non-Greenbelt rural lands it had imposed on Hamilton.
That U-turn, however, has been overtaken by recent legislative changes that facilitate expansions, compromising the city's strategy to freeze Hamilton's urban boundary and channel growth inward.
Bill 185, which came into effect in June 2024, allows developers to appeal council "nondecisions" or rejections of urban expansion applications to the Ontario Land Tribunal.
Also, a newly drafted provincial planning statement axes the requirement of comprehensive municipal land-use studies before boundary-expanding bids can be made.
That clears the way for urban expansion applications "at any time and with no limit on the size," Hamilton city planners have pointed out.
The appeal rights for boundary expansions - excluding the Greenbelt - allow "development applications to be considered in a balanced manner," Municipal Affairs and Housing Minister Paul Calandra's office previously told The Spectator.
The provincial planning statement, which came into effect in October, is meant to bolster Ontario's housing supply and protect the environment, prime agricultural areas and public health and safety, Calandra's office said.
In response to the changes, the city has drafted a framework to evaluate urban expansion applications and created a new team of staff to respond to them.
In addition, $1.5 million is earmarked to fight OLT appeals.
"We didn't create these problems, but we have to deal with them," Coun. Brad Clark said during city budget deliberations Friday.
In a cover letter to the city, planning consultants for Mizrahi Developments note the proposal for 163 and 159 Sulphur Springs Rd. is focused on nearly 25 acres of land between Lovers Lane and Wilson Street East. Two homes are on the site.
"The design will optimize development based on the site's topography and preserve natural features, including enhancing the existing trail system and stormwater management," the Biglieri Group Ltd. wrote.
The urban expansion proposal is among a flurry of applications the city has received amid the provincial legislative changes:
The Whitechurch Landowners Group is proposing an 805-acre urban expansion for roughly 4,800 homes on land just east of Mount Hope in Glanbrook;
The Elfrida Community Builders Group wants to fold about 3,000 acres to the south and east of upper Stoney Creek into the urban area for a mix of housing types.
The Upper West Side Landowners Group has taken its proposed urban expansion for parcels of Twenty Road West near the airport to the OLT, with hearings scheduled for 2026.
The consortium has also asked for Mayor Andrea Horwath's help to fast-track land-use changes to advance its plan for roughly 5,000 residential units and up to five million square feet of employment uses via a minister's zoning order (MZO).
Through an arrangement with Habitat for Humanity, the developers have noted as many as 500 of the homes, or 10 per cent, would be affordable.
If granted, the MZO would result in an urban expansion of about 220 acres and the conversion of 245 acres of airport employment lands to residential and other uses, notes a city report before councillors Tuesday.
Raising concerns, including preservation of Hamilton's firm urban boundary and employment lands, staff recommend opposing the consortium's MZO pitch.
Teviah Moro is a reporter
at The Hamilton Spectator. tmoro@thespec.com
CAPTION: "I am not supportive of expanding into the Greenbelt," said Ancaster Coun. Craig Cassar.Mizrahi Developments is proposing 14 single-detached and 61 townhouse units on Sulphur Springs Road in Ancaster.hamilton Spectator file photoCity of Hamilton image

Document 234 of 500

Crombie accuses Ford of leaving Ontario in limbo amid trade war: Liberal leader worries not enough done to diversify province's economy

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/crombie-accuses-ford-leaving-ontario-limbo-amid/docview/3162728977/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Crombie+accuses+Ford+of+leaving+Ontario+in+limbo+amid+trade+war%3A+Liberal+leader+worries+not+enough+done+to+diversify+province%27s+economy&amp;title=The+Spectator&amp;issn=11899417&amp;date=2025-02-03&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.7&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Spectator&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Ontario Liberal Leader Bonnie Crombie is accusing Progressive Conservative Leader Doug Ford of not having a plan as Canada gears up for a trade war with the United States.
Crombie said she supports Ford's decision to remove American products from LCBO shelves on Tuesday, when U.S. President Donald Trump's promised 25 per cent tariffs on Canadian goods are slated to come into effect.
"He's going to remove American brands off the shelf of the LCBO. OK, fine, great. I support that. Where's the rest of the plan? Where's the stimulus for our industries?" Crombie said at a campaign stop in Ottawa.
"People are afraid. People are angry. They're worried about paying rent and putting food on their table today, and now they're worried about losing their job too. He knew this threat was coming."
The Liberal leader said Ford hasn't done enough to diversify Ontario's economy and accused him of abandoning the province during a major crisis by triggering the snap Feb. 27 vote and focusing on his re-election campaign.
She offered few specifics, however, on how she would handle the tariff response differently.
Like Ford, Crombie said she would work closely with the federal government. She added that it's time to knock down interprovincial trade barriers - something the incumbent premier has also advocated for.
"There's been so many obstacles, and that's an easy one," said Crombie.
"I bet it's going to be fixed pretty quick now that we're in this tariff war with the United States, but that's something that's long overdue and really the premiers need to sit down and hammer that out."
She also echoed her previous call for Ontario to rip up its $100-million deal with Starlink, a company owned by Trump ally Elon Musk.
NDP Leader Marit Stiles, who did not have any public events on her schedule on Sunday, issued her own statement criticizing Ford's government for inking the deal, which would add high-speed internet access in northern Ontario.
"At a time with Donald Trump and his billionaire friends are threatening Canadian livelihoods, Doug Ford handed over a multimillion-dollar deal to Elon Musk's Starlink," she said. "It's time to find a Canadian-made solution."
She added in a subsequent statement that she supports removing American products from the LCBO and that a "Buy Ontario procurement strategy" is needed.
The news that Trump is making good on his tariff threat has stymied opposition attempts to put other issues - like access to family doctors, which Crombie raised in front of the Montfort Hospital on Sunday - at the forefront of the election campaign.
Ford justified calling the election, which is happening nearly a year and a half ahead of schedule, by saying he needs a strong mandate from Ontarians to spend tens of billions of dollars on economic stimulus in response to tariffs.
Crombie and Stiles have both said his majority government already had the mandate it needed to steer the province, and they would have supported stimulus measures. They have raised concerns about what moves he can make during an election campaign.
Ford insisted on Saturday that he can still act as sitting premier while campaigning.
The Canadian Press
CAPTION: Ontario Liberal Party Leader Bonnie Crombie speaks during a campaign stop at Montfort Hospital in Ottawa on Sunday.Spencer Colby The Canadian Press

CREDIT: Maan Alhmidi The Canadian Press

Trump tariffs: Trump says 'pain' will be 'worth the price'; Canada says it won't back down; LCBO removing U.S. products

Full text: The latest news as Donald Trump's administration imposes tariffs on Canada. Web links to longer stories if available. More from the Canadian Press. Follow the Star for live updates on Sunday.
11:30 a.m. Read Trudeau's full speech to Canadians - and Americans from Saturday
Prime Minister Justin Trudeau has announced 25 per cent retaliatory tariffs beginning as early as Tuesday in response to U.S. President Donald Trump's surcharges on Canadian goods.
Trudeau addressed Canadians - and Americans - in a press conference Saturday evening. Here are his full remarks in English (French remarks not included).
Read the full speech
11:11 a.m. Conservative Leader Pierre Poilievre speaking with reporters in Vancouver. Watch live here.
11:05 a.m. Here are some goods in the crosshairs of Trump's tariffs on Mexico, Canada and China
Canada is by far America's biggest foreign supplier of crude oil. From January through November last year, Canada shipped $90 billion (U.S.) worth of crude, well ahead of No. 2 Mexico at $11 billion (U.S.).
For many U.S. refineries, there's not much choice.
Canada produces the "type of crude oil that American refineries are geared to process,'' said Scott Lincicome, a trade analyst at the libertarian Cato Institute.
"It's a heavier crude. All the fracking and all the oil and gas we make here in the United States - or most of it - is a lighter crude that a lot of American refineries don't process, particularly in the Midwest.''
Read the full story from the Associated Press
10:22 a.m. (updated) Canada will not back down, finance minister says
Canada will scale up its retaliation to Donald Trump's tariffs as needed and is not backing down in spite of the U.S. president's threat to escalate his own penalties against Canada, Finance Minister Dominic LeBlanc said.
In an interview with the Star Sunday, LeBlanc said what was announced is only "the initial response of the Government of Canada."
"But if these (U.S.) tariffs are still around weeks and months from now, and if it's an ongoing economic turbulence, then we'll look at other additional measures, including non-tariff measures, but it's a proportionate response that will be scaled up, as we see where the Canadian economy is over the next number of weeks."
Read the full story from the Star's Tonda MacCharles
9:50 a.m. U.S. President Donald Trump posted on his Truth Social account Sunday morning, leaning harder into his suggestion that Canada become the 51st state.
"We pay hundreds of Billions of Dollars to SUBSIDIZE Canada. Why? There is no reason. We don't need anything they have. We have unlimited Energy, should make our own Cars, and have more Lumber than we can ever use," Trump said.
"Without this massive subsidy, Canada ceases to exist as a viable Country. Harsh but true! Therefore, Canada should become our Cherished 51st State. Much lower taxes, and far better military protection for the people of Canada - AND NO TARIFFS!."
In an earlier post on Sunday, Trump suggested that Canada and other countries have been "ripping off" the U.S. for years and that the pain of tariffs would be worth it.
"THIS WILL BE THE GOLDEN AGE OF AMERICA! WILL THERE BE SOME PAIN? YES, MAYBE (AND MAYBE NOT!). BUT WE WILL MAKE AMERICA GREAT AGAIN, AND IT WILL ALL BE WORTH THE PRICE THAT MUST BE PAID," Trump said.
9:35 a.m. Here's a look at key imports from the three counties that could become more costly
Trump's upcoming 25 per cent tariffs on goods from Canada and Mexico and 10 per cent duties on products from China are set to ricochet through households, raising prices for a wide range of items including vegetables, fruits and cars.
Read the full story from Bloomberg
9:25 a.m. U.S. businesses brace for Trump's tariffs on Canada, Mexico and China to drive up costs
From an ice cream parlor in California to a medical supply business in North Carolina to a T-shirt vendor outside Detroit, U.S. businesses are bracing to take a hit from the taxes President Donald Trump imposed Saturday on imports from Canada, Mexico and China - America's three biggest trading partners.
The levies of 25 per cent on Canadian and Mexican and 10 per cent on Chinese goods will take effect Tuesday. Canadian energy, including oil, natural gas and electricity, will be taxed at a lower 10 per cent rate.
Mexico's president immediately ordered retaliatory tariffs and Canada's prime minister said the country would put matching 25 per cent tariffs on up to $155 billion in U.S. imports.
Read the full story from the Associated Press
9:05 a.m. Will the housing crisis worsen in Ontario because of the trade war?
Ontario's residential construction council fears the fledgling trade war between Canada and the U.S. will only worsen the province's housing progress, at a time when builders have already been challenged by higher interest rates and increasing materials and labour costs.
"The move is reckless," president Richard Lyall wrote in a press release Sunday, suggesting the decision out of the White House "will cause economic hardship in both the U.S. and Canada, affecting tens of billions of dollars of trade in construction materials alone."
Lyall noted the homebuilding industry in Canada relies "heavily" on imported material from the U.S., as the U.S. relies on Canada for most of its imported lumber as well as other supplies like cement and gypsum used for drywall.
"Builders in Canada will likely look to alternative sources, such as domestic Canadian producers or suppliers from other countries, but the disruption - along with increased costs - could lead to delayed or canceled projects or slowdowns in new home construction as supply chains adjust," Lyall warned.
"Such a scenario could exacerbate existing housing shortages and drive-up prices." - Victoria Gibson
9 a.m. (update) Ontario removing U.S. products from shelves
The LCBO will be removing American products from its shelves and catalogue in response to U.S. President Donald Trump's tariffs being imposed on Canadian goods Tuesday.
"Every year, LCBO sells nearly $1 billion worth of American wine, beer, spirits and seltzers," Progressive Party Leader Doug Ford wrote in an online statement. "Not anymore"
The removal will roll out Tuesday and will also prohibit Ontario-based restaurants and businesses from restocking and ordering American beer, wine and spirits since the LCBO is the only alcohol wholesaler in the province.
Read the full story from the Star's Anastasia Blosser
7:55 a.m. Minnesota Governor Tim Walz was vocal on X Saturday night, saying, "Trump's tariffs are a tax increase for Minnesota families, plain and simple."
"While Trump raises prices, I'm proposing the first sales tax rate cut in our state's history to help Minnesotans weather the storm," he said in post.
Trump's second week in office delivers jolts and chaotic orders with a mix of politics and tragedy
7:45 a.m. U.S. Senate minority leader Chuck Schumer has been vocal on X, posting about how the tariffs will raise the prices of goods in the country.
"You're watching the Super Bowl next week. Wait till Trump's tariffs raise your pizza prices," Schumer said in a post Saturday evening on X.
How will this affect Canada?
Sunday 7:30 a.m. Business associations on both sides of the border are sounding the alarm over the impact of U.S. President Donald Trump's incoming tariffs on the North American economy.
Trump is putting a 25-per-cent tariff on Canadian goods and a 10-per-cent tariff on Canadian energy starting Tuesday.
Prime Minister Justin Trudeau says Canada will hit back with counter-tariffs on $30-billion worth of goods starting the same day.
Read the full story from the Canadian Press
11 p.m. If the only way to stop a bully is to punch them in the nose, who is balling up their fists and putting Donald Trump in their sights?
In the less than two weeks since he returned to the White House, Trump has fired federal prosecutors who targeted him in criminal cases, stripped critical former top officials with threats on their lives of security details, and ordered that the Guantanamo Bay military base-home since 2002 to detained enemy combatants-be turned into a migrant detention camp.
That's on top of his threats to take Greenland and the Panama Canal by force, make Canada the 51st American state, and slap tariffs on any country that doesn't submit to his will.
Read the full story from the Star's Allan Woods
What did Trudeau say?
11 p.m. Hours after Donald Trump slapped strict tariffs on Canadian imports, Prime Minister Justin Trudeau warned the U.S. that the better path is to partner with Canada, "not punish it."
"Canada has the ingredients necessary to build a booming and secure partnership for the North American economy, and we stand at-the-ready to work together," Trudeau said, speaking at a Saturday evening press conference in Ottawa. "Unfortunately, the actions taken today by the White House split us apart."
The comments came hours after Trump signed an executive order imposing a 25 per cent tariff on almost all Canadian goods and a 10 per cent tariff on energy, marking an era of economic uncertainty on both sides of the border. Both China and Mexico face steep tariffs as well.
Read the full story from the Star's Abby O'Brien
Sunday 11 p.m. Despite weeks of cross-border meetings, the creation of expert councils, and the swift implementation of a $1.3-billion Canadian border security plan, U.S. President Donald Trump made good on his punishing tariff threats on Saturday, with Canada following suit just hours later.
"We don't want to be here. We didn't ask for this. But we will not back down," Prime Minister Justin Trudeau told reporters on Parliament Hill late in the evening.
And with that, Canada's relationship with its largest trading partner entered a new - and decidedly uncertain - chapter.
Read the full story from the Star's Ottawa Bureau
Sunday 10:00 p.m.: Catherine Cobden, President and CEO of the Canadian Steel Producers Association (CSPA), called Trump's announcement about tariffs on Canadian products "deeply disappointing."
"The North American steel industry is highly integrated across the Canadian and U.S. border. With $20B in annual steel trade between our nations, these tariffs will cause significant disruption and economic hardship in both Canada and the United States. Disruptions that will have negative effects on businesses, workers and their families," Cobden said in a statement.

Document 236 of 500

No one is listening to the left anymore. This is why

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/no-one-is-listening-left-anymore-this-why/docview/3162497157/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=No+one+is+listening+to+the+left+anymore.+This+is+why&amp;title=The+Hamilton+Spectator+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2025-02-02&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Hamilton+Spectator+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Last November, about a week after I returned from covering the second election of Donald Trump, I went to a performance of the National Ballet of Canada, at the Four Seasons Centre in Toronto. I was having a difficult time that week. Like the rest of the world, I had spent much of the past nine years thinking about Trump - and in my case following him, watching him and writing about him, too. I had almost convinced myself, despite all the evidence, that I wouldn't have to do that, any of it, anymore.
There were three separate performances that night. For reasons that are more complicated than complimentary, I still can't get the first one - a solo by Guillaume Côté, a retiring principal dancer in the company - out of my head. It was such a strange, brief thing. Somewhere between a warm-up and a swan song, it lasted less than six minutes end to end. The audience had barely settled in before the lights were up again and everyone was being prodded to applaud.
It's not that "body of work" - which Côté first performed in 2014 - is a bad piece of dancing. It's fine for what it is. It just didn't seem like it was being put on that night for the benefit of anyone in the crowd. The dance was billed in the program less as a performance than a kickoff to Côté's retirement tour, and that's what it felt like: A valedictory, or an instruction. It was more an opportunity for the audience to applaud Côté than it was a chance for him to awe.
I've been thinking a lot about the idea of performance since the election. Trump has cemented himself now as one of the most consequential, and perhaps the single greatest, political performer in American history. The parts of the Kamala Harris campaign I saw in person, on the other hand, featured some of the strangest political performances I have ever seen. They seemed, again and again, like Côté's solo, to have been planned without an audience in mind. You already like us, they screamed, all we have to do is arrive.
In one particularly striking example, the night before the election, Harris put on a baffling and endless rally in Philadelphia. Long before she finally made it on stage, near midnight, thousands of her most ardent supporters were openly booing her warm-up acts.
I wrote at the time that one self-defeating rally probably didn't mean that much in the grander scheme of a presidential election. Two months on, I'm less and less sure that's the case. I tend to see that night now as a sign of a much larger malaise, not just in the Harris campaign, but in progressivism more broadly. Put simply, progressives don't seem to have any idea how to perform politics in our current, socially mangled age.
On the Saturday before Trump's inauguration, I spent several hours following a protest march through the streets of Washington, D.C. The event was supposed to be a repeat of the Women's March that took over the U.S. capital the day after Trump was first inaugurated. It is largely lost to time now, but that demonstration was one of the largest acts of protest of its kind in American history. It was a fierce statement of dissent and a genuine mass movement.
The event last Saturday, rebranded as the People's March, was none of that. Instead, it was small and impossibly splintered. As I walked along one branch, shuffling past the Washington Monument, it seemed like a different faction had control every 10 metres. There were different bullhorns amplifying different messages, all melting into a monotonous buzz.
The day after the march, I met the artist Isabelle Brourman at the Phillips Collection, a gallery northwest of the U.S. Capitol. Brourman spent much of 2024 drawing and painting Trump, first in court, in New York, then later on the campaign. She was in Washington to paint Trump's first dance at one of his presidential balls.
Brourman's work perfectly conveys the non-stop sensation dump of watching Trump live. It's never flattering. Her final pieces often look chaotic and menacing (much like Trump himself). But Trump's people had nonetheless given her extraordinary access to his rallies, and even to his home. When I asked her why she thought that was the case, Brourman told me about a meeting she had with Susie Wiles, who is now Trump's chief of staff, at Trump Tower during the campaign. Brourman was trying to negotiate access to Trump's events around the country, so she showed Wiles some of her work.
"I said, obviously this isn't traditional sketch art," Brourman told me. "And she said, 'We like different at Trump.' "
That same sense of difference, Brourman believes, was totally lacking from the Harris campaign. Brourman had tried throughout the summer and fall to paint Harris, but the vice-president's camp never let her get close. To Brourman, that was one indication of a more significant failing.
"Trump understands his crowd and engages with them and is fuelled by them," she said. "I think that her team was not engaged on the ground in the same way at all. And there's a lot of frustration about that from people that are surprised by the results, that she wasn't planning things, that she seemed disconnected. She seemed almost untouchable."
To walk around Washington the week of the inauguration was to be surrounded by the ecstatic followers of a coherent movement. You can oppose that movement, and I do. I think Trump's second coming will mean almost incalculable misery for migrants, Trans Americans and many others. But progressives are never going to beat that movement unless they get over the idea that all they need to do is show up and be themselves. "We are here and we're not him" is not enough anymore. They need to speak to people in a new way.
The second act of the ballet that night in Toronto was a solid but unremarkable performance of a late work by the English choreographer Frederick Ashton. It was the third and final piece that shook me. "Silent Screen," by Sol Leon and Paul Lightfoot undid my expectations of dance. The movement, the sets, the sounds all combined to create something visceral and explicitly new. I wasn't watching the performance so much as feeling it. That's why it broke through.
Trumpism won't die in four years just because Donald Trump can't run again. If progressives actually want to beat the movement Trump has built, they need to forge something new and visceral for themselves, something coherent and different that engages people where they are in 2025.
As I walked around the People's March that Saturday, chatting to participants and listening to chants, I was struck by two contradictory thoughts. On the one hand, I believe with deep certainty that the people who came out that miserable day are on the right side of history. They will be proud in 20 years to be able to say that they were there, that they stood up.
At the same time, as I looked down at the marchers from the steps of the Lincoln Memorial, I knew their form of protest - splintered, hectoring and uninterested in anything broad - would never be enough. Not against Trump.
(This is not just an American phenomenon. Canadian progressives seem, if anything, even more lost than their U.S. counterparts. Few acts have ever worn more thin than Prime Minister Justin Trudeau's increasingly glassy-eyed cheer. NDP leader Jagmeet Singh is still pumping out his delusionally bad TikTok's to no return. Ontario Liberal Leader Bonnie Crombie showed up to her campaign launch last week wearing a hat that ripped off Premier Doug Ford ripping off Donald Trump. It made me wonder if she had advisers who are being paid to make her lose.)
Politics have changed. The old performances don't work anymore. Trump knows that, perhaps more than any politician alive. I wonder if there's a single progressive leader, in Canada or the U.S., who knows it too.
CAPTION: Donald Trump is pictured at an election night watch party on Nov. 6, 2024, in West Palm Beach, Fla.Supporters watch returns at a campaign election night watch party for Republican presidential nominee former President Donald Trump at the Palm Beach Convention Center on Nov. 6, 2024, in West Palm Beach, Fla.

Subject: American history; Political campaigns; Election results; Audiences; Presidential elections

Location: United States--US

People: Trump, Donald J

Title: No one is listening to the left anymore. This is why

Publication title: The Hamilton Spectator (Online); Hamilton

Publication year: 2025

Document 237 of 500

Four key issues for Hamilton voters in Ontario's snap election

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/four-key-issues-hamilton-voters-ontarios-snap/docview/3162207318/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Four+key+issues+for+Hamilton+voters+in+Ontario%27s+snap+election&amp;title=The+Hamilton+Spectator+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2025-02-01&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Hamilton+Spectator+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: This might be the first provincial election campaign ever waged against a politician who isn't even on the ballot.
Progressive Conservative leader Doug Ford has invoked U.S. president Donald Trump - and his love of tariffs - as the major reason to trigger an election more than a year ahead of schedule.
The strategy might pay off for a Tory leader clearly looking to sell himself as a "Captain Canada" figure willing to "stare down Trump," said York University political scientist Zachary Spicer, who noted inflation and affordability were already big worries for voters even before the now seemingly unavoidable trade war.
But it's also a "risk politically" if his effort doesn't pan out, or if his periodic absences from the campaign trail allow eager political leaders like the NDP's Marit Stiles, Liberal's Bonnie Crombie or Green's Mike Schreiner to win the attention of voters.
"They could very quickly turn this into an election about health care," suggested Spicer, who expects to see recent images of hundreds of people lined up to try to get a family doctor to make their way into political advertising.
"For sure, the price of groceries is still top of mind for a lot of people, but so is the frustration of not being able to find a family doctor."
In Hamilton, a number of issues could conceivably grab hold of local voters during the campaign. Here are four notable possibilities:
Trumpian tariffs
There is no way to ignore the impact a tariff war would have on Hamilton and communities across Ontario, election or no.
But it might worry local voters even more, given Hamilton's large manufacturing and steel footprint. A high-level city estimate suggests a trade war could take a $1-billion bite out of Hamilton's economy.
Ford has announced he will be visiting Washington to talk tariffs during the campaign - and he has publicly disparaged the ability of opposition party leaders to negotiate with U.S. leaders.
Stiles, Schreiner and Crombie, by contrast, argue holding a $189-million election campaign in the middle of a trade crisis is the opposite of leadership. They've also loudly questioned the Tory leader's own bargaining skills, pointing to a recent watchdog revelation that the past Ford government paid a whopping $612 million to rush beer into cornerstones.
Housing and homelessness
Residents in Hamilton and across Ontario were mad about skyrocketing rents and unaffordable homes during the 2022 election - and since then, the housing crisis has not disappeared.
The number of vulnerable residents losing shelter altogether has become much more prominent in the public eye, with tent encampments growing in parks.
The Tories were elected in 2022 promising to build 1.5 million new homes over the next 10 years, but so far, the province is behind target. The past government earned both praise and loud criticism late last year with a contentious bill meant to crack down on homeless tenters in parks - but the election call killed that prospective legislation.
Homelessness and the lack of affordable housing are always front and centre in Hamilton, where the city is experimenting with setting up tiny cabins for homeless residents, stickhandling legal appeals over encampments in parks and eyeing a worrisome spike in tent fires in public places.
Worth noting: Ontario is the only province in Canada to have downloaded the responsibility for social housing to cities - an ongoing budget challenge for local property taxpayers.
There are plenty of housing pitches to explore from other parties. For example, an NDP call for a massive building program for affordable housing and a Liberal proposal to spur construction by strategically cutting development charges. All three opposition parties, including the Greens, are calling for provincewide permissions for "missing middle" buildings like fourplexes.
Health and long-term care
Always an election issue, in Hamilton the numbers make it easy for voters to pay attention.
For example, there are 75,000 city residents without a family doctor. There were also nearly 2,000 seniors on a wait list for long-term care beds in December.
The Tories have promised nearly 60,000 new or upgraded LTC beds by 2028, but the city has actually lost 321 beds since the COVID-19 pandemic. Last year, The Spectator exclusively reported on a still-unannounced sale of provincial Mountain brow land that is meant to host a massive, private long-term care and retirement project.
All parties have pitched new plans to get residents connected with family doctors or health teams. NDP has argued for more publicly run long-term care facilities, while the Liberals have emphasized better access to home care among their pitches. On the campaign trail, the Greens recently called for better rural health care.
Greenbelt and sprawl
The Ford government was forced to apologetically backtrack in 2023 on a contentious decision to open up Greenbelt land to development after scathing watchdog reports and ongoing media reporting suggested undue influence by developers. Soon after, the province also flip-flopped on a huge opening of farmland to building beyond Hamilton's urban boundary.
Activists and many local politicians are still frustrated with the province because it has since changed the rules to allow developers to seek urban boundary expansions for individual projects - and if council says no, appeal to a tribunal.
But is the Greenbelt scandal already too far in the rear-view mirror to impact voters in Hamilton or anywhere else?
That might depend on the timing of any updates from the RCMP - which continues a criminal investigation into the Greenbelt land swap, even though it was reversed.
Matthew Van Dongen is a reporter at The Spectator. mvandongen@thespec.com
CAPTION: U.S. President Donald Trump - and his tariffs - loom over Ontario's election.Homeless encampments are a big issue for city council. Will they also move voters in the provincial election?An estimated 75,000 city residents do not have a family doctor.Will the Greenbelt controversy of 2023 still motivate voters this year?

Subject: Local elections; Long term health care; Political activism; Political leadership; Homeless people; Tariffs; Trade disputes; Provincial elections; Political campaigns; Voters; Affordable housing

Document 238 of 500

Letters

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/letters/docview/3162111855/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=unknown&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Letters&amp;title=The+Spectator&amp;issn=11899417&amp;date=2025-02-01&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.15&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Spectator&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Rebates are a
win-win for all
I'm not one of those hypocrites that complain about the cost of living and then whine when someone sends you some relief dollars to help. All the "everything is Doug Ford's fault" people that claim to donate their cheque to some charity should realize that that's also a good thing.
If you need the cheque, it helps; if you don't and donate it, that's also a good thing. Seems like a win-win to me.
I used the money for exactly what it was meant for. I filled the tank in the car and spent the rest on groceries. It was perfect timing, too, as the utility bills came with their latest fake increases.
I wonder whether everyone would be whining if Andrea Horwath sent the cheque.
Red Taylor, Hamilton
Look at Ford's track record
How are we to rationalize the premier's commitment to protecting Ontario jobs with his historical record of favouring gigantic American retailers over independently owned and operated Canadian businesses, as evidenced by his COVID-19 responses?
He ruthlessly supports foreign investment over Ontario enterprise (e.g., Ontario Place).
If there are Canadian interests at play, he always supports the retail monopolies and the land development cartel over the independents. So which jobs is he talking about?
David Braden, Puslinch
Aren't we past this kind of talk yet?
Re: Marianne Faithfull, singer and pop icon, dies at 78, Jan. 31
An article in Friday's newspaper (albeit The Associated Press) covering Marianne Faithfull's death described her as blond and voluptuous. How many times has a man been described as handsome and well endowed to denote his impact on our world? When will society accept that looks do not define a person?
Linda Ploen, Burlington
Reduce the role
of Canada Post
Re: Fixing Canada Post can help protect us against Donald Trump. Let me explain, Jan. 30
Canada Post can not compete with today's diverse mail and package delivery companies. The government-run business should only service the outlying regions that today's delivery companies can't afford to service.
You can't service areas that are not profitable as a business or you go bankrupt. Let the government subsidize the outlying areas only.
Canada Post employees who work hard would probably get hired by the fast-growing, profitable private delivery companies. Stop the government make-work project and save billions of taxpayers' dollars.
Tares Bobiak, Hamilton
Bring home
manufacturing
With the tariffs coming, I would suggest it is time for Canada to grow up, cease the globe-trotting begging tours for jobs and start thinking of homegrown manufacturing.
Roger Lambert, Hagersville
Demand action on smuggled guns
With all this talk about exaggerated amounts of migrants and fentanyl crossing into the U.S. from Canada, why is there no pushback about the illegal guns coming in from the gun capital of the world - the U.S.A. - into Canada?
Aldo Castelli, Winona
