Feb. 1: Rebate cheques a win-win for all, demand action on smuggled guns and other letters to the editor

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/feb-1-rebate-cheques-win-all-demand-action-on/docview/3162110879/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Feb.+1%3A+Rebate+cheques+a+win-win+for+all%2C+demand+action+on+smuggled+guns+and+other+letters+to+the+editor&amp;title=The+Hamilton+Spectator+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2025-02-01&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Hamilton+Spectator+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Rebates a win-win for all
I'm not one of those hypocrites that complain about the cost of living and then whine when someone sends you some relief dollars to help. All the "everything is Doug Ford's fault" people that claim to donate their cheque to some charity should realize that that's also a good thing. If you need the cheque, it helps; if you don't and donate it, that's also a good thing. Seems like a win-win to me. I used the money for exactly what it was meant for. I filled the tank in the car and spent the rest on groceries. It was perfect timing, too, as the utility bills came with their latest fake increases. I wonder whether everyone would be whining if Andrea Horwath sent the cheque.
Red Taylor, Hamilton
Look at Ford's track record
How are we to rationalize the premier's commitment to protecting Ontario jobs with his historical record of favouring gigantic American retailers over independently owned and operated Canadian businesses, as evidenced by his COVID-19 responses?
He ruthlessly supports foreign investment over Ontario enterprise (e.g., Ontario Place). If there are Canadian interests at play, he always supports the retail monopolies and the land development cartel over the independents. So which jobs is he talking about?
David Braden, Puslinch
Aren't we past this yet?
Re: Marianne Faithfull, singer and pop icon, dies at 78, Jan. 31
An article in Friday's newspaper (albeit The Associated Press) covering Marianne Faithfull's death described her as blond and voluptuous. How many times has a man been described as handsome and well endowed to denote his impact on our world? When will society accept that looks do not define a person?
Linda Ploen, Burlington
Reduce Canada Post's role
Re: Fixing Canada Post can help protect us against Donald Trump. Let me explain, Jan. 30
Canada Post can not compete with today's diverse mail and package delivery companies. The government-run business should only service the outlying regions that today's delivery companies can't afford to service. You can't service areas that are not profitable as a business or you go bankrupt. Let the government subsidize the outlying areas only.
Canada Post employees who work hard would probably get hired by the fast-growing, profitable private delivery companies. Stop the government make-work project and save billions of taxpayers' dollars.
Tares Bobiak, Hamilton
Bring manufacturing home
With the tariffs coming, I would suggest it is time for Canada to grow up, cease the globe-trotting begging tours for jobs and start thinking of homegrown manufacturing.
Roger Lambert, Hagersville
Demand action on smuggled guns
With all this talk about exaggerated amounts of migrants and fentanyl crossing into the U.S. from Canada, why is there no pushback about the illegal guns coming in from the gun capital of the world - the U.S.A. - into Canada?
Aldo Castelli, Winona
Trump doesn't get it
I don't think President Donald Trump quite understands what trade entails. You cannot obtain the products of a mind except on the owner's terms, by trade and voluntary consent. A trader does not treat men as masters or slaves, but as independent equals. He deals with men by means of free, voluntary, unforced exchange - an exchange which benefits both parties by their independent judgment. Apparently the president thinks we Canadians have somehow outwitted our American trading partners in some unfair fashion. By imposing tariffs on Canada, he is in fact admitting that American businesspeople are somehow too stupid to run their own affairs.
Dan Riga, Burlington
CAPTION: You can't complain about the high cost of living and whine when Doug Ford's government sends you some relief, a reader argues.

Document 240 of 500

Doug Ford is not the crisis leader he pretends to be

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/doug-ford-is-not-crisis-leader-he-pretends-be/docview/3162110844/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Doug+Ford+is+not+the+crisis+leader+he+pretends+to+be&amp;title=The+Hamilton+Spectator+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2025-02-01&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Hamilton+Spectator+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Premier Doug Ford claims he needs nothing less than the largest mandate in Ontario's history to address the looming tariff war with the Trump administration.
But all available evidence suggests the premier is not an effective political leader in times of crisis.
True, Ford's successful move to fill the current power vacuum in Ottawa gave rise to his image as Captain Canada. Navigating the monumental challenges ahead, however, requires much more than folksy rhetoric.
The COVID-19 pandemic was the litmus test for crisis leadership. For political scientists like me, the pandemic offered a unique opportunity to analyze in real time how political leaders respond to an unprecedented emergency.
I tracked the leadership performance in Canadian federalism between fall 2020 and summer 2021 by applying three established benchmarks: The ability to understand the policy challenge (sense-making); the ability to enact effective decisions (decision-making); and the ability to communicate with the public and other stakeholders to maintain support (meaning-making).
Ontario's crisis management was among the worst. Premier Ford failed continuously to demonstrate all three leadership skills.
First, the premier never seems to have fully grasped the nature of the public health crisis. Wave after wave, there was a profound disconnect between evidence-informed advice and the premier's own perception of the situation.
"We see the curve going down," he said on Oct. 28, 2020, while in fact case numbers were increasing exponentially.
In February 2021, when infections slowly came down, experts univocally warned the government about its intention to lift restrictions prematurely.
In a memorable news conference on Feb. 11, TVO's Jon Michael McGrath asked Adalsteinn Brown from Ontario's COVID-19 Science Advisory Table: "Am I missing something here? Or is this presentation actually predicting a disaster?" Brown responded after a short break: "No. I don't think you're missing anything."
Not surprisingly, the pandemic spiralled out of control again. The province experienced its most serious wave in March and April.
Ford insisted no one could have seen this coming.
But most public health experts, like Dr. Andrew Morris from the University of Toronto, were clear: "To suggest that any of this was unpredictable, surprising and occurred any faster than predicted is a fiction."
Second, Ford's poor sense-making translated into poor decision-making. The basic pattern of his crisis leadership remained the same: Too little, too late.
Internal documents revealed how the government quietly weakened its own emergency response scheme and school safety plan in the fall 2020. The government also never lived up to Doug Ford's promise to build an "iron ring" around long-term care (LTC) homes.
The consequences for Ontarians were dire: A second LTC home crisis unfolded in early 2021, hospital staff described units as war zones, schools remained closed for the rest of the school year, and the government was forced to impose prolonged lockdowns to get the situation under control.
Third, the premier was all but a successful communicator and lost support due to his poor crisis management.
When things turned bad, he was quick to deflect and shifted the blame on the federal government's border policy. At some point, the Ford government even targeted doctors who voiced criticism of the provincial response to COVID-19, most notably Dr. David Fisman and Dr. Brooks Fallis.
At other times, the premier disappeared entirely from the public radar. #WhereIsDougFord emerged as a trending hashtag on Twitter in April 2021.
Early in the pandemic, many Ontarians (me included) had a short Doug Ford moment. His approval ratings jumped from 31 per cent to more than 60 per cent between March and June 2020. Less than a year later, only 32 per cent had a positive impression of his crisis management.
An early election call has been in the air for months. If Ford was serious about his ambition to be Captain Canada, he would have scrapped this misguided idea.
CAPTION: During the worst of the COVID-19 pandemic, Ontario's crisis management was among the worst as Doug Ford failed to demonstrate key leadership skills, Jörg Broschek says.
CREDIT: Jörg Broschek

Subject: Management of crises; Public health; Political leadership; Pandemics; Decision making; COVID-19

Business indexing term: Subject: Management of crises

Location: Canada

Title: Doug Ford is not the crisis leader he pretends to be

Publication title: The Hamilton Spectator (Online); Hamilton

Publication year: 2025

Publication date: Feb 1, 2025

Section: opinion/contributors

Publisher: Torstar Syndication Services, a Division of Toronto Star Newspapers Limited

Place of publication: Hamilton

Country of publication: Canada

Publication subject: General Interest Periodicals--Canada

Document 241 of 500

Where does Donald Trump's assault on the climate leave Canada?

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/where-does-donald-trumps-assault-on-climate-leave/docview/3162110768/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Where+does+Donald+Trump%27s+assault+on+the+climate+leave+Canada%3F&amp;title=The+Hamilton+Spectator+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2025-02-01&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Hamilton+Spectator+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: For Canadian clean-energy advocates, the sight of U.S. President Donald Trump rescinding many of his predecessor's clean-energy initiatives on his first day in office on Jan. 20 was discouraging, but not alarming.
The global clean-energy transition has gained too much momentum for anyone to stop it. And its progress is especially evident in Canada.
The past 12 months have seen new clean-energy projects announced or going onstream in almost every province.
The projects include the first electricity generated by the $16 billion Site C hydro-electric dam on B.C.'s Peace River and completion in November of the refurbishment of a reactor at Ontario's Darlington nuclear station, extending its life by 30 years.
In a global survey of corporate CEOs released in January, consulting firm PricewaterhouseCoopers (PwC) found that 72 per cent of Canadian CEOs said they had made climate-related investments in their businesses in the past 12 months.
And in the U.S., about 90 per cent of increased capacity at American power utilities in the first half of 2024 came from renewable sources, including solar (59 per cent of the total) and wind (12 per cent).
Solar and wind installations are cost competitive with fossil fuels and often cheaper as the technology improves. And so, global investment in clean energy amounted to about $2.9 trillion last year, almost twice the investment in fossil fuels.
"This limits the degree to which the (Trump) administration can mount a more significant role for fossil fuels in the domestic energy system," says economist Francis Fong of TD Economics.
Severe weather events caused or made worse by climate change have become commonplace.
Those events have "permanently moved climate change out of an 'environmental policy' pigeonhole into a far more potent public policy space occupied by such issues as health care and crime," says Rick Smith, president of the Canadian Climate Institute.
Global sales of electric vehicles (EVs) jumped by 24 per cent last year, despite slowing EV sales growth in North America. And global sales of electric heat pumps are forecast to increase by more than 60 per cent from the current level to $205 billion by 2028.
Canada's clean-tech sector, which makes everything from EV charging equipment to LED lighting for greenhouses, exported $7.6 billion worth of goods to the U.S. in 2022.
The sector is bracing for the tariffs on Canadian and Mexican goods that Trump has said he will impose on Feb. 1.
Economists expect tariffs to be lifted once Canada and the U.S. negotiate an end to their differences, though that can take a year judging from the tariffs Trump imposed in his first presidency.
Another sustaining factor for clean energy is that governments have such a large influence in the transition, though private-sector utilities and tech firms also play major roles.
Somewhat insulated from the vagaries of the private sector, governments can stay the course on big-ticket energy projects that can take decades to plan and build.
And decisions on projects are shifting from national capitals to subgovernments, which increases the number of project sponsors and creates diversity in decision-making.
Provincial and state governments in North America are closer to the impact of severe climate events from which they are expected to protect their citizens.
Jurisdictions like Ontario, Quebec and B.C. are bigger players in planning future energy supplies than Ottawa.
And in the U.S., the more than 20 member states of the U.S. Climate Alliance are determined to continue promoting clean energy and have the Constitutional authority to do so.
Ontario is forecast to spend about $400 billion upgrading its power capacity by 2050 to reinforce its status as one of the world's biggest clean-power producers.
Expansions and overhauls are underway at the province's Bruce Power, Darlington and Pickering nuclear plants, and this month Ontario Premier Doug Ford announced plans for a new nuclear plant near Darlington.
Ontario is developing small modular reactor (SMR) technology that it hopes to export. And it seeks to double its energy production from wind, solar and other clean-energy sources.
Ford's response to the Trump tariff threat includes a proposal to more thoroughly integrate the energy systems of Canada and the U.S.
Ford says the project, which also calls for integrated critical mineral production, would help the U.S. "decouple" from China.
The idea might seem fanciful. It would eclipse the size of a St. Lawrence Seaway jointly built by Canada and the U.S.
Then again, "The U.S. president has declared an 'energy emergency' to build new critical infrastructure," wrote Yudullah Hussain of RBC Economics in a client note last week.
"Could some of it be built in Canada given the integrated nature of the two country's energy sectors?"
Ford's binational strategy might be worth pursuing just for its potential tech advances and cost efficiencies. And it would be ready for a Trump successor to embrace if Trump himself doesn't see its merit.
CAPTION: An aerial view of the Bruce Power nuclear generating station. Expansions and overhauls are underway at the province's Bruce Power, Darlington and Pickering nuclear plants, and this month Ontario Premier Doug Ford announced plans for a new nuclear plant near Darlington.

Subject: Energy; Nuclear reactors; Fossil fuels; Clean technology; Climate change; Tariffs

Business indexing term: Subject: Tariffs

Location: United States--US; Canada; North America

People: Trump, Donald J

Company / organization: Name: Bruce Power; NAICS: 221113

Document type: News

ProQuest document ID: 3162110768

Document URL: https://libproxy.wlu.ca/logi n?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/where-does-donald-trumps-assault-on-climate-leave/docview/3162110768/se-2?accountid=15090

Document 242 of 500

Ford pledges $22B in stimulus package if re-elected as premier: Vows to widen the QEW between Burlington and St. Catharines

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/ford-pledges-22b-stimulus-package-if-re-elected/docview/3162110755/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Ford+pledges+%2422B+in+stimulus+package+if+re-elected+as+premier%3A+Vows+to+widen+the+QEW+between+Burlington+and+St.+Catharines&amp;title=The+Spectator&amp;issn=11899417&amp;date=2025-02-01&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.18&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Spectator&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Two of Ontario's main party leaders focused their attention south of the border Friday with American tariffs possibly hours away, while a third set her sights on shoring up local votes with transit pledges.
Liberal Leader Bonnie Crombie pledged to install platform barriers at all Toronto subway stations, increase crisis intervention teams and hire more constables for transit services across the province.
Meanwhile, Progressive Conservative Leader Doug Ford said if re-elected he will spend $22 billion to build infrastructure as part of a stimulus package in the face of a possible trade war with the United States.
Ford said a re-elected Progressive Conservative government will widen the Queen Elizabeth Way between Burlington and St. Catharines.
Outside a busy subway station in Toronto's east end Friday, Crombie detailed part of her plan to get more people on transit with the confidence they'll be safe.
"We want people to ride public transit and I want to make it as accessible and safe as possible," Crombie said.
If her party wins the snap election, Crombie said she will hire 300 special constables for transit operations in Ottawa and the Greater Toronto Area.
A Crombie government would also invest in more security cameras and safety equipment.
Experts say platform barriers in cities around the world have reduced the vast majority of injuries and death on the tracks. They are particularly helpful for reducing suicides.
There have been 816 suicides on Toronto's subway system since 1954, the TTC said, and another 915 people have attempted suicide over that time. In 2023, the last full year of data available, 11 people died by suicide and another 33 people attempted suicide, the TTC said.
Ford did not commit to building platform barriers, but defended his government's transit investments over his past two terms.
"We're going to continue spending money on building transit, getting people out of the cars, getting them into transit," Ford said in Niagara Falls.
In Windsor, NDP Leader Marit Stiles stopped at a union shop to show support to autoworkers.
"The NDP will protect workers from these painful tariffs, and we will fight back against Trump's threats," she said.
The Canadian Press

in the department of political science and North American studies at Wilfrid Laurier
University.

Document 244 of 500

What will drive your ballot on Feb. 27?: From Trump's tariffs to housing, homelessness and long-term care, key issues loom large for voters

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/what-will-drive-your-ballot-on-feb-27/docview/3162110655/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=What+will+drive+your+ballot+on+Feb.+27%3F%3A+From+Trump%27s+tariffs+to+housing%2C+homelessness+and+long-term+care%2C+key+issues+loom+large+for+voters&amp;title=The+Spectator&amp;issn=11899417&amp;date=2025-02-01&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.1&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Spectator&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: This might be the first provincial election campaign ever waged against a politician who isn't even on the ballot.
Progressive Conservative Leader Doug Ford has invoked U.S. president Donald Trump - and his love of tariffs - as the major reason to trigger an election more than a year ahead of schedule.
The strategy might pay off for a Tory leader clearly looking to sell himself as a "Captain Canada" figure willing to "stare down Trump," said York University political scientist Zachary Spicer, who noted inflation and affordability were already big worries for voters even before the now seemingly unavoidable trade war.
But it's also a "risk politically" if his effort doesn't pan out, or if his periodic absences from the campaign trail allow eager political leaders like the NDP's Marit Stiles, Liberal's Bonnie Crombie or Green's Mike Schreiner to win the attention of voters.
"They could very quickly turn this into an election about health care," suggested Spicer, who expects to see recent images of hundreds of people lined up to try to get a family doctor to make their way into political advertising. "For sure, the price of groceries is still top of mind for a lot of people. But so is the frustration of not being able to find a family doctor."
In Hamilton, a number of issues could conceivably grab hold of local voters during the campaign. Here are four notable possibilities:
Trumpian tariffs
There is no way to ignore the impact a tariff war would have on Hamilton and communities across Ontario, election or no.
But it might worry local voters even more, given Hamilton's large manufacturing and steel footprint. A high-level city estimate suggests a trade war could take a $1-billion bite out of Hamilton's economy.
Ford has announced he will be visiting Washington to talk tariffs during the campaign - and he has publicly disparaged the ability of opposition party leaders to negotiate with U.S. leaders. Stiles, Schreiner and Crombie, by contrast, argue holding a $189-million election campaign in the middle of a trade crisis is the opposite of leadership. They've also loudly questioned the Tory leader's own bargaining skills, pointing to a recent watchdog revelation that the past Ford government paid a whopping $612 million to rush beer into cornerstones.
Housing and homelessness
Residents in Hamilton and across Ontario were angry about skyrocketing rents and unaffordable homes during the 2022 election - and since then, the housing crisis has not disappeared.
The number of vulnerable residents losing shelter altogether has become much more prominent in the public eye, with tent encampments growing in parks.
The Tories were elected in 2022 promising to build 1.5 million new homes over the next 10 years, but so far, the province is behind target. The past government earned both praise and loud criticism late last year with contentious bill meant to crack down on homeless tenters in parks - but the election call killed that prospective legislation.
Homelessness and the lack of affordable housing are always front and centre in Hamilton, where the city is experimenting with setting up tiny cabins for homeless residents, stickhandling legal appeals over encampments in parks and eyeing a worrisome spike in tent fires in public places.
Worth noting: Ontario is the only province in Canada to have downloaded the responsibility for social housing to cities - which is an ongoing budget challenge for local property taxpayers.
There are plenty of housing pitches to explore from other parties. For example, an NDP call for a massive building program for affordable housing and a Liberal proposal to spur construction by strategically cutting development charges. All three opposition parties, including the Greens, are calling for provincewide permissions for "missing middle" buildings like fourplexes.
Health and long-term care
Always an election issue, in Hamilton, the numbers make it easy for voters to pay attention.
For example, there are 75,000 city residents without a family doctor. There were also nearly 2,000 seniors on a wait-list for long-term-care beds in December.
The Tories have promised nearly 60,000 new or upgraded LTC beds by 2028, but city has actually lost 321 beds since the pandemic. Last year, The Spectator exclusively reported on a still-unannounced sale of provincial Mountain brow land that is meant to host a massive, private long-term care and retirement project.
All parties have pitched new plans to get residents connected with family doctors or health teams. NDP has argued for more publicly run long-term-care facilities, while the Liberals have emphasized better access to home care among their pitches.
On the campaign trail, the Greens recently called for better rural health care.
Greenbelt and sprawl
The Ford government was forced to apologetically backtrack in 2023 on a contentious decision to open up Greenbelt land to development after scathing watchdog reports and ongoing media reporting suggested undue influence by developers. Soon after, the province also flip-flopped on a huge opening of farmland to building beyond Hamilton's urban boundary.
Activists and many local politicians are still frustrated with the province because it has since changed the rules to allow developers to seek urban boundary expansions for individual projects - and if council says no, appeal to a tribunal.
But is the Greenbelt scandal already too far in the rear-view mirror to impact voters in Hamilton or anywhere else?
That might depend on the timing of any updates from the RCMP - which continues a criminal investigation into the Greenbelt land swap, even though it was reversed.
Matthew Van Dongen is a reporter at The Spectator. mvandongen@thespec.com
CAPTION: An estimated 75,000 city residents do not have a family doctor currently.Homeless encampments are a big issue for city council. Will they also move voters in the provincial election?Will the Greenbelt controversy of 2023 still motivate voters this year?U.S. President Donald Trump - and his tariffs - loom over Ontario's election.Dreamstime photothe hamilton Spectator file photoSpectator file photoassociated press file photo

Document 245 of 500

Bonnie Crombie says Doug Ford has put transit users at risk: 'You just don't feel safe'

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/bonnie-crombie-says-doug-ford-has-put-transit/docview/3161901542/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Bonnie+Crombie+says+Doug+Ford+has+put+transit+users+at+risk%3A+%27You+just+don%27t+feel+safe%27&amp;title=The+Hamilton+Spectator+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2025-01-31&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Hamilton+Spectator+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: It is more dangerous to ride the TTC after seven years of Doug Ford's Progressive Conservative government, charges Liberal Leader Bonnie Crombie.
"He'd rather spend $189 million on an unnecessary and expensive early election - when he has a majority government - rather than investing in things that matter to people on our public transit system," Crombie said Friday outside the Kennedy subway station in Scarborough.
"We know that over a million people ride the TTC each and every day, and for myself and for others, there are situations where you just don't feel safe," Crombie said, flanked by Scarborough Liberal candidates.
There are people who need help with mental health crises on transit as well as growing numbers of homeless people under Ford's watch, she added at the east-end transit hub as morning commuters streamed past.
"You have people using the TTC as a shelter from somewhere to come in off the cold and there are times on our subway cars where, quite frankly, you just feel unsafe."
There are more than 81,000 homeless people in the province, according to the Association of Municipalities of Ontario, and as many as 1,000 stay in tent encampments in Toronto parks and ride transit to keep warm.
Some can be seen sleeping on long benches on subways and at the back of streetcars.
Crombie said if the Liberals are elected Feb. 27, they would give the TTC and other municipal transit services like OC Transpo in Ottawa funds to hire an additional 300 transit constables, double funding for mobile crisis intervention teams, and add new cameras and other security measures, including the installation of costly "protective barriers" on subway station platforms.
Queen's Park would foot the tab, which she said she would disclose when the Liberals unveil their "fully costed" election platform in the days ahead.
Ford defended his record on transit Friday during a campaign event at a Niagara Falls construction company, but would not commit to building barriers in subway stations - a $4 billion idea that has been considered by the TTC and never implemented.
"We're building the largest transit system in North America," he said, referring the new Ontario Line subway, a Scarborough subway line, light rail transit lines in Hamilton and Brampton, along with a new "one fare" system saving money for riders on different GTA transit systems.
"We're going to continue expanding GO."
But the Tory leader ducked a question on the much-delayed Eglinton Crosstown light rail transit line, a project launched more than a decade ago by a previous Liberal government that is years overdue and remains unopened.
In December, Ford promised to earmark an additional $75.5 million for shelters and affordable housing and toughen trespassing laws to quell the explosion in tent encampments.
"We want to help people, we don't want to punish people," he said at the time.
"These encampments are taking over public spaces with illegal drug use happening out in the open, creating huge safety risks."
Critics have said the province needs to do far more to tackle the problems and that the extra funding is not enough to make a difference. Of the $75.5 million, $20 million is to expand shelters with most of the rest for "ready-to-build" affordable housing projects.
At a campaign stop in Windsor, NDP Leader Marit Stiles said there is increasing violence on public transit and against transit workers.
"We want to make it safe for people," she said with area candidates at a union hall, including Lisa Gretzky (Windsor West), the only New Democrat incumbent west of London after the party lost two local ridings to Ford's PCs in the 2022 provincial election.
The problems in transit go beyond safety, Stiles told reporters before cancelling an afternoon appearance in Sarnia.
"A lot of people right now are struggling with the cost of using public transit, and we have a government under Doug Ford and the Conservatives that have promised transit projects, bungled them and never opened many of them."
CAPTION: Ontario Liberal Party Leader Bonnie Crombie speaks to media at Kennedy subway station in Scarborough on Jan. 31, 2025.

Document 246 of 500

Donald Trump's tariffs on imports from Canada will begin Saturday, White House says

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/donald-trumps-tariffs-on-imports-canada-will/docview/3161901300/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Donald+Trump%27s+tariffs+on+imports+from+Canada+will+begin+Saturday%2C+White+House+says&amp;title=The+Hamilton+Spectator+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2025-01-31&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Hamilton+Spectator+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: OTTAWA - For Donald Trump's doubters, or Canadians living in hope, who thought he wouldn't act on his tariff threat, the White House says forget that.
The U.S. president will act Saturday to fulfil his vow to impose 25 per cent surcharges on Canadian and Mexican imports intended to force changes at America's northern and southern borders, White House press secretary Karoline Leavitt said Friday.
Leavitt threw cold water on a Reuters report that suggested Trump would delay the application of tariffs against Canada and Mexico until March 1, calling the story "false."
Within an hour of the Reuters story, Leavitt said she had just spoken to Trump in the Oval Office, and made clear Trump will proceed with the 25 per cent tariffs against Canada and Mexico and levy an additional 10 per cent tariff on Chinese imports.
"These are promises made and promises kept by the president," said Leavitt, repeating that Trump's objective is to halt "the illegal fentanyl that they have sourced and allowed to distribute into our country."
Asked if Trump would exempt oil imports from Canada, Leavitt didn't rule it out, but dodged a direct answer.
"I don't have an update or readout for you on the exemptions, but those tariffs will be for public consumption in about 24 hours, tomorrow, so you can read them then," she said.
Prime Minister Justin Trudeau, in remarks before meeting with an advisory council on Canada-U.S. relations, said he would not "sugar-coat" the impact that tariffs could be expected to have on Canadian exports and the overall economy.
Some economists say Trump's threatened 25-percent "across the board" tariffs would put Canada into a recession, with estimates that U.S. tariffs could cause up to a three-per-cent drop in economic activity here.
"Our nation could be facing difficult times in the coming weeks," said Trudeau.
The Canadian government will retaliate with countermeasures against any U.S. tariffs imposed by Trump, Trudeau said, and would support Canadian workers and industries who may be hard hit, especially if tariffs are sustained.
"If the president does choose to implement any tariffs against Canada, we're ready with a response, a purposeful, forceful but reasonable, immediate response," he said. "It's not what we want, but if he moves forward, we will also act."
Canada has planned an escalating series of measures that could potentially ramp up to impose counter-tariffs on $150 billion worth of American imports, and could include limits or export tariffs on Canadian energy shipments to the U.S.
The federal government says Canada provides more of the crude oil the U.S. imports than the rest of the world combined, a trading relationship that it says allows for "reliable supply and lower pump prices for Americans."
Trump and his threats have shaped a lot of the political rhetoric in a Liberal leadership race to replace Trudeau on March 9.
Earlier Friday, the two front-runners Mark Carney and Chrystia Freeland repeated their arguments, with Carney saying he stands "foursquare" behind efforts by Trudeau's cabinet team in Washington trying prevent the tariffs.
"President Trump thinks Canada will cave in. We will never, ever bow down to a bully," said Carney, who has backed dollar-for-dollar countermeasures.
Freeland, on the other hand, took a tougher line. She too backs a dollar-for-dollar response, but one that is more forceful and immediate. On Friday she said Trudeau should target up to $200 billion in American imports and take direct aim at Trump's adviser Elon Musk, owner of Tesla.
"Canada must threaten to impose a 100 per cent tariff on all Tesla vehicles and a 100 per cent tariff on U.S. wine, beer, and spirits if unfair tariffs are imposed on Canadians tomorrow," Freeland said in a release.
Trudeau has not tipped his government's hand on what American products would be hit with countermeasures, but federal officials have confirmed the first tranche would be targeted at politically sensitive American products, and actions would ramp up over time.
Trudeau said an advisory group of former premiers, business and union leaders are helping to make Ottawa's argument in the U.S. that Trump's tariffs against Canada "would have disastrous consequences for the United States, putting American jobs at risk, raising prices on Americans and undermining our collective security."
Trudeau's remarks prompted questions during a White House briefing, with reporters asking Leavitt if the president could guarantee that tariffs won't stoke inflation, or increase prices at gas pumps and in grocery stores for Americans. One asked if Canada's vow to retaliate showed the U.S. is on the brink of a trade war.
Leavitt bristled and said, "I don't think so."
Trump's purpose, she said, is to halt fentanyl and migrants from "pouring into" America, adding "the president is intent on doing this, and I think Justin Trudeau would be wise to talk to President Trump directly before pushing outlandish comments like that to the media."
Reuters had also reported that the Trump administration would provide a process for the countries to seek exemptions for certain imports. Leavitt did not directly answer when asked if the tariffs would be in place for the long term or whether Trump would remove them if Canada, Mexico or China offered concessions.
"If the president at any time decides to roll back those tariffs, I'll leave it to him to make that decision, but starting tomorrow, those tariffs will be in place," she said.
In Niagara Falls, Ontario Progressive Conservative Leader Doug Ford fully backed Trudeau's retaliation plan.
"Dollar for dollar, tariff for tariff, the federal government has my full support to hit back and hit back hard," Ford said as he campaigned for a third term as premier in a Feb. 27 vote.
"If President Trump proceeds with these tariffs, Canada can and should make him regret them," Ford told reporters, adding he is prepared to take "enormous action" to protect Ontario workers, businesses and families.
Ford called an early election this week seeking a mandate to spend "tens of billions" if necessary to shield Ontario's economy from tariff fallout with up to 500,000 jobs on the line.
But he is yet to lay out the full details of that economic support plan.
"We can't determine who needs the money until we see the tariffs," Ford said. "Then we'll allocate those funds to protect people, protect their businesses and the communities."
With files from Rob Ferguson, Queen's Park bureau
CAPTION: President Donald Trump speaks about a collision involving an American Airlines passenger jet and a military helicopter during a press briefing at the White House in Washington on Jan. 30, 2025.

Document 247 of 500

Ford to honour EV commitment, Crombie and Stiles less definitive: Trump has said the United States does not want or need Canadian cars

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/ford-honour-ev-commitment-crombie-stiles-less/docview/3161732576/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Ford+to+honour+EV+commitment%2C+Crombie+and+Stiles+less+definitive%3A+Trump+has+said+the+United+States+does+not+want+or+need+Canadian+cars&amp;title=The+Spectator&amp;issn=11899417&amp;date=2025-01-31&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.9&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Spectator&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Progressive Conservative Leader Doug Ford said Thursday he will honour Ontario's commitment to the burgeoning electric vehicle sector if re-elected, while his main political rivals were less definitive.
U.S. President Donald Trump has said his country does not want or need Canadian cars despite signing a free trade deal with Canada and Mexico in his first term. He has threatened 25 per cent across-the-board tariffs on Canadian imports unless border security is improved.
Trump has also made it clear that he's going after Ontario's automotive industry, hoping to increase vehicle production at home.
Ford's government agreed to an auto pact with the federal government in 2023 to pay one-third of production incentives in deals with Volkswagen, as well as Stellantis and LG Energy Solution, for their EV battery plants. The two governments ponied up the money in order to compete with former U.S. president Joe Biden's Inflation Reduction Act, which included a tax credit for clean vehicle purchases.
Trump has frozen funding under the act and has threatened to end it altogether.
"Even if President Trump tears up America's commitment to the auto industry, we will not," Ford said at a campaign stop in London, Ont.
"I want to make it clear to every partner we have in Ontario's electric vehicle and battery supply chain: a re-elected PC government will honour our commitment to invest in the sector."
NDP Leader Marit Stiles said she'll protect the auto industry, though she said she wanted to have a look at the deals with Stellantis and Volkswagen.
"I believe in the EV sector, I think this is important for the future of our own economy," Stiles said at a campaign event in Toronto.
But she is not convinced the Stellantis and Volkswagen deals are good for Ontarians.
"Doug Ford does not make good deals for Ontario generally, and I'm going to be looking very carefully at the details of the deals that he's struck with those folks," she said.
Liberal Leader Bonnie Crombie said she wanted to diversify Ontario's economy when asked whether she'd support Ontario's EV manufacturing and supply chain market.
"I'm very concerned that the premier has put all our eggs in the EV basket, has not tried to diversify our economy, insulate us in any way, and brought different kinds of investment into Ontario and create jobs," Crombie said at a stop in Mississauga. She said she would bring back EV rebates as one way to help grow the sector.
Should Trump follow through on his threat to go after Ontario's auto sector through tariffs and cancelled subsidies, Ford said he would invest an additional $1 billion in a skills development fund for auto workers to transition to a different trade.
He also pledged $100 million for an employment program to help workers "in sectors that are vulnerable to trade disruptions" train and transition to "in-demand" jobs.
Ford has made a big bet on electric vehicles during his first two terms as premier. In addition to the deals with Stellantis and Volkswagen, Ford again worked with Prime Minister Justin Trudeau to help attract Honda, which plans to open its own battery-making facility next to its production plant in Alliston, Ont.
Ontario voters will head to the polls Feb. 27.
The Canadian Press
CAPTION: Ontario PC Leader Doug Ford arrives for a tour of Starlim North America Corporation Thursday during a campaign stop in London.Geoff Robins The Canadian Press

Document 248 of 500

Where is Team Ontario in election?

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/where-is-team-ontario-election/docview/3161732311/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=unknown&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Where+is+Team+Ontario+in+election%3F&amp;title=The+Spectator&amp;issn=11899417&amp;date=2025-01-31&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.12&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Spectator&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Doug Ford now has his Captain Canada election.
He almost had me on side with his Canada-is-Not-For-Sale hat and his blunt straightforward defence that "country comes first, Canada's the priority" in the face of the unhinged U.S. president's attack. That is until his political relapse, succumbing to craven partisanship and a pursuit of "the largest mandate in Ontario's history" to take decisive measures "to fight Donald Trump."
No, Ontario doesn't need a Captain Canada election.
Decisive measures are not Ford's strong suit. Ford tends to major in minor things. Among Ford's follies is, his lacklustre pandemic response, the Greenbelt land grab controversy, his secret industrial expropriation of farmland in Waterloo Region, the $2.2-billion Ontario Place fiasco, the $1.4-billion booze-in-corner-stores giveaway to American brewers not to mention the $200 rebate - some say vote buying - cheques that will cost $3 billion. These may be great for his friends, but few Ontarians were asking for them.
Ontario doesn't need Captain Canada. It needs a united Team Ontario. In the face of such a threat a decisive leader could have reached out to the other parties and key leaders to forge a strong Team Ontario strategic response and reimagined a future for Ontario in Trump world.
Instead, we get a Captain Canada election that will bring us even more Trump hype. Yes, Doug Ford will keep his "day job" going to Washington and speaking to U.S. leaders. Sadly, it will make this a superspreader campaign raising more fear and anxiety over Trump and increasing cynicism among the electorate. No doubt Trump will love the attention. Most Ontarians will dread it.
In the face of a missed opportunity, Ontario's opposition parties might step up with a Team Ontario strategic alliance. What if they run candidates in ways that don't split the opposition vote in key ridings? I can hear the howls of the Conservative pundit class already. But that's democracy, too. Polls shows most voters don't want the Conservatives.
Perhaps the best voters can hope for is widespread strategic voting - an "anybody-but-Ford" vote - that could lead to a minority government that might force all parties into a Team Ontario approach. As Headmaster Dumbledore wisely told Harry Potter and the students at Hogwarts, "We are only as strong as we are united; as weak as we are divided."
Ontario doesn't need a $150-million Captain Canada election that will only deepen divisions among us. Yet sadly, this is where we are. What Canadians truly need are political leaders who can lead, co-operate, and work together effectively. Ontarians require a strong Team Ontario to contribute to a strategic, co-ordinated, confident Team Canada response.
We really need a Team Ontario that reimagines a province and a country with a strong, self-reliant economy and safeguards the environment during the climate crisis. We need a Team Ontario that emphasizes reconciliation with Indigenous peoples and ensures everyone has access to essential health care, affordable housing, and income security.
We must also invest in the education of future generations by realistic funding for excellence in our schools, colleges, and universities. It's vital to address the Canadian opioid and mental health crisis, make sure our seniors can age gracefully and safely, and create a Canada that is respected in the world.
During the first Trump administration, as a joke, I was given a red "MAGA-style" hat with the words "Canada is Already Great" emblazoned on it. Let's hope for an election outcome where every Canadian can make that claim proudly.
David Pfrimmer is professor emeritus for Public Ethics
at Martin Luther University College at Wilfrid Laurier University.
CAPTION:

Document 249 of 500

If the LCBO pulls American wine off its shelves, there are hundreds of Ontario options to choose from

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/if-lcbo-pulls-american-wine-off-shelves-there-are/docview/3161713311/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=If+the+LCBO+pulls+American+wine+off+its+shelves%2C+there+are+hundreds+of+Ontario+options+to+choose+from&amp;title=The+Hamilton+Spectator+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2025-01-31&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Hamilton+Spectator+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: It's doubtful anyone would cry over spilled American beer; after all, it's just water in a tall bottle.
But tears may be shed if our access to American-made wines, as well as other alcoholic drinks, is cut off. And that could happen soon enough.
Ontario Premier Doug Ford has instructed the LCBO, a provincial Crown corporation, to remove all products made in the United States if U.S. President Donald Trump goes through with his threat of hitting Canadian products entering his country with tariffs of 25 per cent.
The threatened date for these tariffs is Saturday, Feb. 1.
Will Donald Trump announce 25% tariffs against Canada on Saturday? Here's what could happen
That means popular wines from California winemakers such as J. Lohr, Josh Cellars and Bread & Butter could be gone.
Luckily for Ontario's wine drinkers, this province produces a lot of wine.
According to Wine Growers Ontario, there were 69 million litres of Ontario wine sold in the province in 2023-24, while Wines of Ontario points out there are 194 VQA wineries in the province.
VQA (Vintners Quality Alliance) is the Ontario Wine Appellation Authority's certification program that certifies wine for meeting its origin and quality standards. There are also wineries operating in Ontario without VQA certification.
Should U.S. wine be removed off LCBO shelves, there will be no problem filling its void, said Wine Growers Ontario president and CEO Aaron Dobbin. U.S. wine has an approximate 20 per cent market share at the LCBO, he added.
Finding Ontario wineries
You also don't need the LCBO or wine retailers to buy Ontario wine. You can buy straight from the winery and it may be closer than you think.
Ontario has three areas with established wine regions: the Niagara Peninsula, Lake Erie North Shore and Prince Edward County. But there are also wineries throughout Ontario.
Wines of Ontario, which promotes VQA wine, points out emerging regions include the Ottawa area, around the GTA, Norfolk (which is near Niagara), Elgin County in southwestern Ontario, Georgian Bay and Huron Shores.
The organization also a guide with 150 winery listings on its website.
"The producers in emerging regions are pushing new boundaries. They're showcasing the diversity of Ontario's terroir and the passion of local winemakers," said Wines of Ontario spokesperson Andrea Peters via email.
People can use the organization's Travel Guide to learn about wineries and its Trip Planner to find them in their communities. The Trip Planner is also being updated, which, when complete, will allow users to enter their starting point and the planner will show them nearby wineries as well as attractions and markets.
"Ontario VQA wines offer outstanding quality at accessible price points, particularly in categories like Chardonnay, Pinot Noir and Sparkling, compared to imports such as Burgundy and Champagne. For example, you can enjoy a premium VQA Sparkling wine for around $30, while Champagne may retail for upwards of $80. Ontario's VQA wines provide excellent value without compromising on quality," Peters said.
Peters also said because VQA wines are 100 per cent locally produced, they have a lesser environmental footprint than imported wines, plus they support local jobs.
Wine Growers Ontario's 2019 economic impact report showed the grape and wine sector generates $5.49 billion annually and supports more than 22,000 jobs.
"By choosing Ontario VQA wine, you contribute to local economies, foster innovation and support the continued growth of the industry across the province," Peters said.
Ontario wine at LCBO
The LCBO said it carries 600 VQA wines as well as 88 Ontario-made (non-VQA) wines and 150 international-domestic blends, though numbers fluctuate by season.
All VQA wines are labelled with the VQA logo on the bottle, plus LCBO stores have VQA sections where these wines can be easily found. They're also labelled as "VQA Wines" and "Made in Ontario" on the LCBO website.
Ontario-made wines made entirely with Ontario fruit but don't qualify for the VQA certification are labelled as "Product of Canada."
Products that are manufactured in Ontario but contain a blend of Ontario fruit and fruit imported from another region or country can be found in the store in the International Domestic Blends section.
On the LCBO website, they are labelled as "Made in Canada." These products also have a label that says "International blend from imported and domestic wines."
Furthermore, the LCBO has 21 Ontario Wine Boutique Locations, which are located inside LCBO stores and offer an expanded selection of VQA wines, including hard-to-find, niche and ultrapremium products.
CAPTION: The LCBO carries hundreds of Ontario VQA wine varieties.

Document 250 of 500

Where is Team Ontario in Ontario's election?

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/where-is-team-ontario-ontarios-election/docview/3161713303/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Where+is+Team+Ontario+in+Ontario%27s+election%3F&amp;title=The+Hamilton+Spectator+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2025-01-31&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Hamilton+Spectator+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Doug Ford now has his Captain Canada election.
He almost had me on side with his Canada-is-Not-For-Sale hat and his blunt straightforward defence that "country comes first, Canada's the priority" in the face of the unhinged U.S. president's attack. That is until his political relapse, succumbing to craven partisanship and a pursuit of "the largest mandate in Ontario's history" to take decisive measures "to fight Donald Trump."
No, Ontario doesn't need a Captain Canada election.
Decisive measures are not Ford's strong suit. Ford tends to major in minor things. Among Ford's follies is, his lacklustre pandemic response, the Greenbelt land grab controversy, his secret industrial expropriation of farmland in Waterloo Region, the $2.2-billion Ontario Place fiasco, the $1.4-billion booze-in-corner-stores giveaway to American brewers not to mention the $200 rebate - some say vote buying - cheques that will cost $3 billion. These may be great for his friends, but few Ontarians were asking for them.
Ontario doesn't need Captain Canada. It needs a united Team Ontario. In the face of such a threat a decisive leader could have reached out to the other parties and key leaders to forge a strong Team Ontario strategic response and reimagined a future for Ontario in Trump world.
Instead, we get a Captain Canada election that will bring us even more Trump hype. Yes, Doug Ford will keep his "day job" going to Washington and speaking to U.S. leaders. Sadly, it will make this a superspreader campaign raising more fear and anxiety over Trump and increasing cynicism among the electorate. No doubt Trump will love the attention. Most Ontarians will dread it.
In the face of a missed opportunity, Ontario's opposition parties might step up with a Team Ontario strategic alliance. What if they run candidates in ways that don't split the opposition vote in key ridings? I can hear the howls of the Conservative pundit class already. But that's democracy, too. Polls shows most voters don't want the Conservatives.
Yes, many will suggest that I am naive. Perhaps the best voters can hope for is widespread strategic voting - an "anybody-but-Ford" vote - that could potentially lead to a minority government that might force all parties into a Team Ontario approach. As Headmaster Dumbledore wisely told Harry Potter and the students at Hogwarts, "We are only as strong as we are united; as weak as we are divided."
Ontario doesn't need a $150-million Captain Canada election that will only deepen divisions among us. Yet sadly, this is where we are. What Canadians truly need are political leaders who can lead, co-operate, and work together effectively. Ontarians require a strong Team Ontario to contribute to a strategic, co-ordinated, confident Team Canada response.
We really need a Team Ontario that reimagines a province and a country with a strong, self-reliant economy and safeguards the environment during the climate crisis. We need a Team Ontario that emphasizes reconciliation with Indigenous peoples and ensures everyone has access to essential health care, affordable housing, and income security.
We must also invest in the education of future generations by realistic funding for excellence in our schools, colleges, and universities. It's vital to address the Canadian opioid and mental health crisis, make sure our seniors can age gracefully and safely, and create a Canada that is respected in the world.
During the first Trump administration, as a joke, I was given a red "MAGA-style" hat with the words "Canada is Already Great" emblazoned on it. Let's hope for an election outcome where every Canadian can make that claim proudly.
CAPTION: Doug Ford almost had me on side with his Canada-is-Not-For-Sale hat and his blunt straightforward defence that "country comes first." That is until he succumbed to craven partisanship, David Pfrimmer writes.
CREDIT: David Pfrimmer

Document 251 of 500

Letters

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/letters/docview/3161713130/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=unknown&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Letters&amp;title=The+Spectator&amp;issn=11899417&amp;date=2025-01-31&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.13&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Spectator&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Roy Green certainly
did this city proud
Re: Hamilton broadcast legend Roy Green hangs up the mic to 'fight for my life,' Jan. 29
Hamiltonians remember Roy Green on CHML, with his constant focus to preserve and honour Canada and Hamiltonians on all the good things in life. Roy, you shared many historical moments with us and the city returned the favour by recognizing you into our gallery of distinction, honouring citizens who go above and beyond to make Hamilton proud. Add this award to the many you already received from Canada's broadcasting industry. And now, as you retire from broadcasting, Roy, on behalf of our city, I would like to turn the tables on you.
There are members of our community who improve our lives, the image of our city and the respect for Canada. You, Roy Green, are one of these Hamiltonians. Your determination and passion to honour others and, when necessary, persist in pursuing cases of injustice done to our citizens, more often than not, eventually brought justice and comfort to victims and their families. Your determination and passion is legendary, and while it can be said, with justification, that Canada at times has been abysmally represented by some of our prime ministers, even during some of our best friends' and neighbours' times of terror, crises and mourning, Roy Green, you did this city - and country - proud! This is why you will always have the respect and gratitude of Hamiltonians, Canadians and many citizens around the world - and mine as well. Thank you, Roy! Enjoy your trip to Alaska!
Frank Stechey, Ancaster
Perpetual growth
is unsustainable
Re: Why nature should be an
Ontario election priority, Jan. 30
Tony Morris makes an excellent case for the endangered species and loss of nature in Ontario, and, certainly, it's a problem the world over. Nevertheless, the biggest enemy of our natural world is our addiction to an economic system that relies on perpetual growth.
Economic growth means destruction of world resources - and natural habitat. It means polluting the atmosphere and warming the planet, which brings with it natural disasters and climate catastrophes. Why does no one, especially those who appear to decry destruction of nature and natural habitat, ever call for changes to our economic system? Isn't it a no-brainer that we humans must focus our attention on sustainability, not growth? And on a more equitable distribution of what we already have.
We could begin with demilitarization, whereby we gain two positives: no more rampant destruction of our built environment, and armament funds that would be redirected to help cope with natural disasters. Why are we not hearing more about this very important direction for change to save our planet?
Renate Manthei, Hamilton
No more 'Made in the U.S.A.' for me
Up until recently, when purchasing a product, I would go out of my way to purchase "Made in the U.S.A." rather than a foreign import. Not anymore. Why enrich a country with imperialist aspirations to conquer us both politically, geographically and economically?
I will now support more offshore goods because at least they are not nipping at our heels in their determination to make us a vassal country. "Uncle Sam" is not your friend.
John H. Miller, Hamilton
Cancel American fighter jet order
Canada, in conjunction with the RCAF, has placed an order with the U.S. for 88 F-35 jets to bolster our defence to the tune of $19 billion. Cancel the order to start. I'd like to add that Canada can buy state-of-the art jets from Europe or Australia.
Sinclair Tyios, Burlington
Police need to get priorities straight
Re: 'What we need, not what we want': Hamilton police chief on 2025 budget pitch, Jan. 29
Hamilton police are not able to bring a specialized intimate partner violence unit to fruition due to budget constraints. The city has declared a crisis, but we all know that is political speak.
I would love for police Chief Frank Bergen to see if there is any way to reallocate funds. Perhaps working with other city partners to make it happen.
I guess the sad reality is when you send officers to remove a few unhoused people in tents, there is no manpower left for intimate partner violence. Let's send bylaw on these assignments. Our city is on a hiring spree, so surely we can afford it. Perhaps then police will find funds to respond to crimes like intimate partner violence.
Robin Magder, Dundas
Ford's cheque
won't buy my vote
I received Doug Ford's "bribe" yesterday. Today, I donated it to the Canadian Women's Foundation. I'm quite certain they'll appreciate that; however, when I go to vote, I will not vote for Ford. I hope that my fellow Ontarians do the same and do not give him the votes he seeks. Send him packing. Enough is enough.
Lisa Johnston, Hamilton
Donate rebate to
a common good
We are a retired couple of modest means, living within a budget. What fun to receive $200. But when that $200 per individual is part of a government expenditure of $3 billion, we have to ask, could not that money, already collected by the government, be put to better use? Some say it is a bribe to buy our vote and it is difficult to argue otherwise.
We plan to put our money toward two causes: Hamilton Food Share and Ontario's official Opposition, the New Democratic Party of Ontario.
We invite those we are able to do so to protest by putting their rebate cheque toward the common good.
Barry and Betty Lambeck,
Millgrove
Fix issues,
then call election
Doug Ford's new slogan is "Protect Ontario." Seven years later, here are a few of the past Ontario failures/broken promises that Doug Ford didn't protect: Greenbelt controversy, Ontario licence plates, buck a beer, housing affordability, Ontario Science Centre, Ontario Place, health-care funding and education funding.
He is now involved in the Canada-U.S. tariff threat. This is his new distraction. Unfortunately, he did a terrible job handling his past Ontario promises, so Ford's personal agenda now is to call an early election and spend Ontario's money on it. He should have finished his present term by fixing his broken promises to the people of Ontario first! Shame on you, Doug Ford. We need to elect someone who will "protect" us! It's time for you to go.
Maureen MacKenzie,
Stoney Creek
Ford hasn't
earned our votes
Spending millions to sell beer outside of the Beer Store in a hurry, extremely underfunding health care and secondary and post secondary education, bike lanes, closing supervised consumption sites, placing ServiceOntario in Staples, shutting down wastewater surveillance, which was very informative for health problems, the greenbelt fiasco. This premier has done absolutely nothing good for this province, other than his friends. Please, everyone, think twice about who you vote for on Feb. 27.
Robert Coxe, Hamilton
Get yourself a
library card today
I recently visited the library and was very happy to discover how much the library offers. It's not just books anymore, but embroidery machines, button making and the opportunity to work with knowledgeable staff to access all that is available.
The library is good value for my tax dollars. Everyone in Hamilton should get a library card.
Kathleen Bixby, Hamilton
CAPTION: Roy Green, who is battling Stage 4 metastatic prostrate cancer, is planning a motorcycle trip on the Alaska Highway.Cathie Coward The Hamilton Spectator file photo

Document 252 of 500

Jan. 31: Roy Green did this city proud, police priorities are askew and other letters to the editor

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/jan-31-roy-green-did-this-city-proud-police/docview/3161713086/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Jan.+31%3A+Roy+Green+did+this+city+proud%2C+police+priorities+are+askew+and+other+letters+to+the+editor&amp;title=The+Hamilton+Spectator+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2025-01-31&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Hamilton+Spectator+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Roy Green did this city proud
Re: Hamilton broadcast legend Roy Green hangs up the mic to 'fight for my life,' Jan. 29
Hamiltonians remember Roy Green on CHML, with his constant focus to preserve and honour Canada and Hamiltonians on all the good things in life. Roy, you shared many historical moments with us and the city returned the favour by recognizing you into our gallery of distinction, honouring citizens who go above and beyond to make Hamilton proud. Add this award to the many you already received from Canada's broadcasting industry. And now, as you retire from broadcasting, Roy, on behalf of our city, I would like to turn the tables on you.

Your determination and passion to honour others and, when necessary, persist in pursuing cases of injustice done to our citizens, more often than not, eventually brought justice and comfort to victims and their families. Frank Stechey, Ancaster

Spending millions to sell beer outside of the Beer Store in a hurry, extremely underfunding health care and secondary and post secondary education, bike lanes, closing supervised consumption sites, placing ServiceOntario in Staples, shutting down wastewater surveillance, which was very informative for health problems, the greenbelt fiasco. This premier has done absolutely nothing good for this province, other than his friends. Please, everyone, think twice about who you vote for on Feb. 27.
Robert Coxe, Hamilton
Don't get fooled again
Doug Ford has been planning an election for months and it is clear to me that a tariff mandate is not the reason. Perhaps he is worried about a backlash to Pierre Poilievre or the publication of the inquiry into the Greenbelt?
We have paid for his multi-million-dollar ads that tell us how wonderful Ontario is, specifically health care and education, and I am not convinced.
Also, PC party attack ads against Bonnie Crombie that mention her association with Justin Trudeau and having raised municipal taxes four years in a row as mayor, saying "We can't afford her." Crombie, as mayor of a municipality, is not allowed to declare a deficit and must raise taxes to counter inflation and pay for downloaded provincial costs.
Although Ford promised to eliminate the deficit 10 years ago, he has not done so. In fact, in the most recent two years, they have a declared an unnecessary deficit. This is called a deficit of convenience not of necessity. The PCs' annual budgets have far exceeded the spending of Kathleen Wynne. Unnecessary expenses such as easier access to beer and wine; $200 gifts; elimination of licensing fees; an unneeded move of Ontario Science Centre and building of new toll highway 413: We cannot afford him! To paraphrase the Who: Don't get fooled again.
David Woods, Dundas
Responding to Vinay Menon column
Re: Donald Trump's mass deportations are like a cruel reality TV show - with Dr. Phil as the special guest star, Jan. 29
Menon: If there were real "emergencies," Donald Trump wouldn't be golfing already.
You must have missed that he interrupted his golf game to deal with Colombia refusing to take their criminals back. The premise of your opinion also misses the fact that every person who has entered the U.S. illegally has broken the law and is therefore a criminal. Additionally, they are pursuing the child traffickers, gang members, rapists and murderers first.
Menon: Using such an aircraft to "repatriate" migrants is about as fiscally sound as taking your child to school in a hot-air balloon.
The economy in using military aircraft is that it is immediately available when required and immediately removes individuals requiring housing, food and medical services, offsetting the expense. I wonder if the five million immigrants in Canada due to exit this year are taxing the hospitals and social services in this country?
Menon: Notice how eggs cost even more after Joe Biden left.
Come on! He's been president for a week and a half. I believe the statistics are from December when Biden was still president. Wouldn't have anything to do with the 100 million chickens killed during the Biden administration for fear of bird flu?
Menon: Notice how Trump just froze spending on programs they depend on for food and medicine.
Those programs were exempt from the freeze. Have a little honesty.
Barry Kirk, Hamilton
Police need to get priorities straight
Re: 'What we need, not what we want': Hamilton police chief on 2025 budget pitch, Jan. 29
Hamilton police are not able to bring a specialized intimate partner violence unit to fruition due to budget constraints. The city has declared a crisis, but we all know that is political speak.
I would love for police Chief Frank Bergen to see if there is any way to reallocate funds. Perhaps working with other city partners to make it happen.
I guess the sad reality is when you send officers to remove a few unhoused people in tents, there is no manpower left for intimate partner violence. Let's send bylaw on these assignments. Our city is on a hiring spree, so surely we can afford it. Perhaps then police will find funds to respond to crimes like intimate partner violence.
Robin Magder, Dundas
We should demand real solutions
I have witnessed the beautiful colours of Canada for over two decades, but sadly, they now seem to be fading. I have always introduced myself as a proud Canadian, but lately, I find myself questioning - what am I truly proud of?
Poverty, drug addiction, violence, depression and materialism are becoming more visible in our society. Where have we gone wrong? Is the problem in our policies or their implementation? I once read that if you want to destroy a nation, weaken its education system. Who today is truly satisfied with the state of our education system? What vision and goals are we setting for our youth?
A nation rises when poverty is replaced by prosperity, and quality education provides the foundation for that transformation. But which political party is truly committed to addressing these issues? Who has a concrete action plan to restore Canada's proud legacy?
It is time for us to ask these critical questions and demand real solutions.
Ayesha Khan, Hamilton
CAPTION: Roy Green, who is battling Stage 4 metastatic prostrate cancer, is planning a motorcycle trip on the Alaska Highway.
