Document 253 of 500

Mark Carney says he would immediately scrap Justin Trudeau's consumer carbon price. Here's what he'd replace it with

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/mark-carney-says-he-would-immediately-scrap/docview/3161713018/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Mark+Carney+says+he+would+immediately+scrap+Justin+Trudeau%27s+consumer+carbon+price.+Here%27s+what+he%27d+replace+it+with&amp;title=The+Hamilton+Spectator+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2025-01-31&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Hamilton+Spectator+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: OTTAWA-Former Bank of Canada governor Mark Carney says he will replace Prime Minister Justin Trudeau's consumer carbon levy-and-rebate scheme with an array of green incentives if he wins the Liberal leadership race.
Carney unveiled his climate policy vision on Friday in Nova Scotia, declaring that the Liberal government's fuel levy is too politically "divisive" and tainted by "lies" peddled by Conservative Leader Pierre Poilievre.
Carney said he would immediately lift the federal fuel levy from consumers and small businesses. In its place, he would create a system of incentives that would see the government sell emissions credits to heavy industries, and use the money to pay people unspecified amounts when they make green purchases like electric cars, energy-efficient appliances and improved home insulation.
He would also maintain the national requirement for provinces to have carbon pricing systems for heavy industries. And he would keep the Liberal government's incoming emissions cap for the oil and gas sector, a policy that Alberta strongly opposes and the Conservatives have promised to scrap if they take power.
While Carney did not specify how much his policies would reduce Canada's greenhouse gas emissions, he said his plan would ensure they keep going down.
"It's time for a new, more effective climate plan that everyone can get behind," Carney said Friday.
"You'll no longer have to pay more to fuel your car or heat your home."
The consumer carbon price has long been a political lightning rod for the Liberal government. Poilievre has lampooned it as a driver of inflation that has failed to significantly reduce emissions, while provincial governments from Alberta to Ontario have challenged and criticized the policy for years.
A December study from University of Calgary economists concluded that carbon pricing has had a "minimal" impact on inflation.
On Friday, Poilievre's office continued trying to link Carney with Trudeau's carbon levy, calling him "Carbon Tax Carney" and dismissing his plan as nothing more than a "rebrand" of the government's current policy.
"Canadians will continue to suffer under a brand new name!" Poilievre's spokesperson Sebastian Skamski said in a written statement Friday morning.
Poilievre later posted a video claiming Carney would only "pause" the consumer carbon price until after the next election.
The NDP criticized Carney for overseeing an asset management firm that invested in fossil fuels. "How can Canadians trust him with a climate plan now?" the party asked in a statement Friday.
Now that Trudeau is on his way out, Carney and other contenders to replace him as prime minister have distanced themselves from the policy. Chrystia Freeland, the former finance minister whose resignation in December helped push Trudeau towards the door, has promised to scrap the consumer carbon price. Leadership candidate Karina Gould, the former government House leader, says she would freeze it at its current level. Gould has also promised to roll out a series of green incentives for consumers.
But the consumer carbon price has also been a key part of the Trudeau government's plan to slash Canada's emissions that contribute to the global climate crisis. According to the Canadian Climate Institute, the consumer carbon levy is expected to cover eight to 14 per cent of the country's projected emissions reductions by 2030.
The institute also concluded that the bulk of emissions reductions are slated to come from industrial carbon pricing and the oil-and-gas cap - policies Carney confirmed Friday he intends to keep.
Many economists, along with Liberal environment ministers who have endorsed Carney for the Liberal leadership, have argued that carbon pricing is the least costly way to encourage businesses and consumers to reduce their emissions. And leading scientists have rung the alarm through the Intergovernmental Panel on Climate Change, publishing warnings that time is running out to avoid the damaging extremes of global warming.
In recent months, however, more politicians have turned against the policy. NDP Leader Jagmeet Singh suggested last year that his party would stress imposing costs on industrial emitters over consumer carbon pricing, while B.C.'s NDP premier said he would remove the province's long-standing carbon price if the national requirement to have one disappears.
That requirement was introduced in 2019, after the Liberal government passed a law that mandates all provinces and territories to have carbon pricing systems that meet a minimum level of stringency. If they don't, there is a federal system that has two prongs: an industrial pricing system for heavy emitters, and a consumer carbon levy.
The federal levy is in place in several provinces, including Ontario, and comes with rebates to all households four times a year. A household of four in Ontario was slated to get $1,120 in rebates in the current fiscal year.
The minimum carbon price is also scheduled to increase annually, along with the size of the rebates. This year it is set at $80 per tonne of emissions, which the federal government says adds 17.6 cents to the cost of a litre of gasoline. It is supposed to increase until it hits $170 per tonne in 2030, adding 37.4 cents per litre at the pump.
While Carney would ditch the consumer levy, he said he would keep the scheduled carbon price increases for the industrial systems.
Carney also said he would implement a "carbon border adjustment," which is a tariff on imports from other countries with weaker climate policies that is meant to ensure Canadian businesses aren't disadvantaged by domestic green initiatives.
"What that does is promote jobs here at home" and prevents emitting industries from moving to other jurisdictions, he said.
Skamski, Poilievre's spokesperson, said this amounts to a "new carbon tax at the border" and predicted it would increase the cost of gas, home heating and food.
Under the Trudeau government, Canada has pledged to slash emissions to at least 40 per cent below 2005 levels by 2030, and at least 45 per cent below 2005 levels by 2035.
According to a preliminary assessment the government published in December, Canada's emissions were 8.5 per cent below 2005 levels in 2023.
CAPTION: Liberal leadership candidate Mark Carney, seen at his campaign launch earlier this month, says in a policy statement he'd create incentives to "reward" people for green initiative purchases such as electric cars and energy-efficient appliances.

Subject: Rebates; Leadership; Emissions; Carbon; Prime ministers; Climate change; Electric vehicles; Environmental tax

Document 254 of 500

Ontario Election 2025: What you need to know about voting in Burlington riding on Feb. 27

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/ontario-election-2025-what-you-need-know-about/docview/3161994554/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Ontario+Election+2025%3A+What+you+need+to+know+about+voting+in+Burlington+riding+on+Feb.+27&amp;title=The+Hamilton+Spectator+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2025-01-30&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Hamilton+Spectator+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: What is my riding?
The riding, or electoral district, of Burlington stretches from Burloak Drive in the east, to Highway 6 in the west, south to Lake Ontario and as far north as Highway 5 in some areas - but parts of the City of Burlington are included in the ridings of Milton and Oakville North-Burlington.
You can visit the Elections Ontario website for a map of the riding, and search by your postal code to confirm which riding you are in.
Visit Elections Ontario for more information on the Burlington riding in the upcoming election, including registered candidates, different ways to vote, and how to work for Elections Ontario during the election.
Who is running in Burlington?
Megan Beauchemin - NDP
James (Chilli) Chillingworth - New Blue Party
Andrea Grebenc - Ontario Liberal Party
Kyle Hutton - Green Party of Ontario
Natalie Pierre - PC Party of Ontario
For updates on registered candidates visit Elections Ontario.
Candidates can be nominated until 2 p.m. on Feb. 13.
Where and how do I vote?
Confirm, update or add your voter information at the Elections Ontario website by Feb. 17 to receive a Voter Information Card in the mail with details on where to vote in person.
Voter information cards will be mailed between Feb. 17 and 22.
To vote by mail, apply at the Elections Ontario website before 6 p.m. on Feb. 21.
Vote in person at your local election office between Jan. 30 and 6 p.m. on Feb. 26.
Vote at any advance voting locations in your district between Jan. 30 and 6 p.m. on Feb. 26.
Vote at your assigned voting location on election day, between 9 a.m. and 9 p.m. on Feb. 27.
For details on the various methods of voting, visit the Elections Ontario website.
Remember to bring your Voter Information Card and identification when voting in person. Valid ID includes most documents issued by any level of government that include your name and address. Photo ID is not required.
This article will be updated as more information becomes available during the election campaign period and closer to polling day.
CAPTION: Burlington riding.A provincial election in Ontario is scheduled for Feb. 27.The Ontario Votes logo.

Jan. 30: We can't afford four more years, there are angels are among us and other letters to the editor

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/jan-30-we-cant-afford-four-more-years-there-are/docview/3161487737/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Jan.+30%3A+We+can%27t+afford+four+more+years%2C+there+are+angels+are+among+us+and+other+letters+to+the+editor&amp;title=The+Hamilton+Spectator+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2025-01-30&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Hamilton+Spectator+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: We can't afford four more years
I just received my $200 vote-buying cheque in the mail. It doesn't match the last vote-buying trick of cancelling licence plate charges, but it will likely still work for Dougie.
Some people will realize the loss of health care and lowering of educational levels isn't worth what he is offering, but he is betting most voters will not notice what they are losing for $200.
What scares me most is the fact that with another four years of the Ford government, the health care system will be close to unrecoverable and we will spend the rest of our lives paying more to survive, all for the sake of a $200 cheque. This may be our last chance to get someone in who will fix the systems that are basic to our futures: let's not blow it again.
Beer in corner stores will not keep you alive, or educate our children.
Terry Horne, Hamilton
Cheque going to charity
I just got my cheque from the province. My wife's was made out using her maiden name. We have been married for almost 50 years.
The funds will be going to Hamilton Health Sciences Foundation. If you are a senior and getting more from your federal carbon tax rebate than you pay out, I encourage you to make a similar donation to a noble cause of your choice if you are able.
Ron French, Hamilton
Give rebate to those in need
I received a gift in the mail this week! A $200 cheque from Doug Ford and friends! I am not going to say, "Thank you."
While I realize this gift will help some of his "folks" cover some of the cost for one trip to the grocery store, it will not solve the big problems. It is like putting a Band-Aid on a bleeding artery to staunch the flow of blood when we need a tourniquet.
If all the millions of dollars spent on this "gift" of our own money were put into our over burdened health-care system, our underfunded education system or any one of a number of our other underfunded social safety nets, some real solutions to our problems could begin.
I am giving my $200 to help fund the hot meal program at my downtown Hamilton church. I am urging everyone else to find a place of need and do likewise. There are plenty of options!
Ruth Simmons, Hamilton
The cheque is in the mail
I am writing in response to Rusty Escott's letter to the editor on Wednesday, where he writes that he will not receive the $200 rebate cheque because he "didn't work in 2024."
Perhaps Mr. Escott has confused this rebate with the $250 rebate provided by the federal government, but he is, nevertheless, incorrect. Firstly, to be eligible to receive this $200 rebate, Ontarians had to have filed their income tax return for 2023, not be incarcerated in 2024 and not be bankrupt. Those are the only requirements.
Although I have not yet received mine, I personally know several seniors and retirees who have. So hang tight, Mr. Escott. Canada Post is doing their best to deliver them as quickly as possible.
Deborah Gale, Hamilton
Ford doesn't need new mandate
Dear Premier Doug,
You have 79 seats in the legislature, the NDP have 28, Liberals nine, Greens two. You have a super majority and can pass whatever legislation you want. The next election is scheduled for June 2026.
Forcing an election on the citizens of Ontario now is, in one word, wrong.
Joe Benedetti, Hamilton
There are angels are among us
My car broke down on Rymal Road East on the coldest night of the year. I called the road assist number on my credit card and was told that they would text me with the ETA.
After an hour, I called again and was given the towing company number. When I called them they said up to 40 minutes or as soon as they can. After another hour, I called the credit card number again and they said they would try their best.
During my third call another hour later, I saw a tow truck going the other way. He saw me on the street and turned around. He asked if he could help. He towed us home in a warm truck at a very reasonable price. Thank you, Gabe of High Class Recovery. You saved our lives! (My husband is 92 - good thing we had a warm blanket in the car!)
Mary McCracken, Hamilton
A time for reflection
Jan. 27 marked the 80th anniversary of the liberation of Auschwitz-Birkenau, the notorious Nazi death camp. We need to reflect on a number of disturbing events in our present world: the rise of antisemitism, Vladimir Putin and the war in Ukraine, fighting and unrest in the Middle East and more.
Particularly disturbing to the few remaining survivors of these death camps and to historians: Elon Musk's use of what appeared to be a Nazi salute in support of Trump, and a survey showing many people believe the horrors of camp prisoners are exaggerated. The horrors not only in camps, but also to the resistance and the brave people who hid victims even though they faced death if discovered.
The experience of studying in Poland and visiting Auschwitz-Birkenau decades ago stays with me forever. Not everyone can travel to Europe and visit concentration camps - however, the ROM has an exhibit, "Auschwitz. Not long ago. Not far away." It opened Jan. 10 and runs until Sept. 1. I encourage individuals, families and schools to visit this exhibit and see the original artifacts from Auschwitz-Birkenau. The experience will stay with you forever.
Jan Peirson-Reid, Carlisle
Stop guns and drugs at the border
To help Doug Ford negotiate Donald Trump's proposed tariffs, I suggest that Ford admit that drugs are crossing the border. However, in this case, 853 kg (1,837 pounds) of cocaine crossed from Mexico, to the U.S.A. then into Canada, according to Toronto police who made the recent bust. This has to stop.
On top of that, hundreds of Canadians are being killed every year by guns smuggled into Canada from the U.S. (7,700 in 2024 alone). Ford can tell Trump that he will forgo tariffs once the drug and gun smuggling stops.
Grant Ranalli, Hamilton
A waste of taxpayer dollars
Re: Doug Ford's alcohol sales expansion deal cost taxpayers an additional $612 million, watchdog says, Jan. 28
So it is going to cost $600 million to speed up alcohol in corner stores in Ontario. Three times what Doug Ford said it would. That would have built a lot of affordable housing.
Sharon McKibbon, Hamilton

Kyle Hutton nominated as Green Party candidate for Burlington

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/kyle-hutton-nominated-as-green-party-candidate/docview/3161487501/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Kyle+Hutton+nominated+as+Green+Party+candidate+for+Burlington&amp;title=The+Hamilton+Spectator+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2025-01-30&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Hamilton+Spectator+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Kyle Hutton is running in Burlington as the Green Party nominee in the upcoming provincial election.
The party announced Jan. 14 that Hutton, who also represented the party in 2022, had been nominated again as its local candidate.
Premier Doug Ford called an early election, sending voters to the ballot box on Thursday, Feb. 27.
A press release states Hutton earned the highest level of voter support in the Green Party's Burlington history. With 3,515 votes (or 6.7 per cent of votes cast), Hutton finished fourth out of six Burlington candidates in 2022.
"He is a committed climate and social activist, working in the non-profit sector helping organizations improve their outreach and data organization tools," the release states.
Hutton said he is "humbled" to have the confidence of Burlington Greens again.
"In the face of multiple crises in housing, climate, and leadership, Doug Ford is threatening Ontario with an early election - all for his own profit," Hutton said. "In contrast, Ontario Greens are leading with real solutions, with our leader Mike Schreiner offering practical ways to reverse course on many of the Conservatives' negligent policies. I'm proud to be running again as part of Mike's team."
For more information on the Burlington riding, the upcoming election and local candidates visit the Elections Ontario website.
CAPTION: Kyle Hutton

Document 257 of 500

Nobody asked for this election

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/nobody-asked-this-election/docview/3161407186/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=unknown&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Nobody+asked+for+this+election&amp;title=The+Spectator&amp;issn=11899417&amp;date=2025-01-30&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.12&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Spectator&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: As much as it hurts me to say this, I appreciate the forcefulness of Doug Ford attacking U.S. President Donald Trump about these tariffs. Sometimes a blustering bully requires someone who isn't scared to bully them back.
Ford is scrappy. He's not debonair and that's fine - that's good, because Trump is an agent of chaos who thrives on mudslinging.
In normal circumstances, I'd prefer negotiations versus threats of removing American liquor from the LCBO or flipping the power switch and plunging some states into darkness. But, in this particular situation, I support Ford and the Team Canada approach that "nothing is off the table."
That didn't last long. My goodwill evaporated when Ford announced a snap election for Feb. 27?
With a year and a half left until the scheduled provincial election in June 2026, this is what we're doing?
Ontarians may support Ford's tough stance on Trump, but that doesn't mean we're ready to head to the polls. We're already gearing up for a federal election sometime this spring - why add provincial upheaval to the mix?
The reason is obvious: Ford is hoping that we'll forget the things he delivered that we never asked for - and the things he completely ignored that we really needed. The list is long, but here are some examples.
Take the Ontario Science Centre. Last June, the provincial government closed it abruptly due to structural concerns with the roof. While second engineering report said significant repairs were needed, they said the centre wasn't in such dire condition that it had to be closed permanently and without notice.
What it really came down to was Ford's desire to move the science centre to Ontario Place. Save Ontario's Science Centre, a grassroots group protested but ultimately, a smaller science centre will be relocated to Ontario Place, with the opening date now pushed to 2029.
Or let's talk about the Greenbelt scandal.
The Ford government opened more than 7,400 acres of protected land to housing development. Yes, there is a housing crisis, but building on environmentally sensitive land wasn't solving our housing problems. What was really happening is that developers, with ties to the government, bought parcels of land in the Greenbelt - they were ready for Ford to announce the land was open for development. That had me, and many Ontarians, side-eyeing Ford due to allegations of favouritism and corruption. Following public backlash, Ford apologized in September 2023 and restored the Greenbelt's protected status.
Let's not forget the move to a two-tier health care system. In 2023, our provincial government announced plans to expand private clinics to perform surgeries and diagnostic tests to reduce wait times. I won't lie, after being in a couple of emergency rooms last spring, I am not against shorter wait times. I don't believe that private clinics poaching the health care workers is going to make wait times for most people shorter.
For those in the province who can afford to skip the line and get taken care of at a private clinic, great. They will get the MRI or CT scan in minutes by paying top dollar. The rest of us? We will have to wait in ever longer lines.
But we all know, when money is the motivator, private, for-profit clinic will prioritize their profits over the well-being of patients. In no way is that a good thing - the tragedies at Ontario's for-profit long-term care homes during COVID-19 taught us that.
Premier Ford, while we stand behind you against Trump and his threat of tariffs, we see this election call for what it is: you trying to ride this wave of support to another victory. It could happen - we are a forgetful bunch.
Maybe send us another $200 cheque to seal the deal.
Shellene Drakes-Tull is a Toronto-based storyteller, educator and the founder of Sweet Lime Communications.
CAPTION:

Document 258 of 500

Early election an unnecessary financial burden on citizens

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/early-election-unnecessary-financial-burden-on/docview/3161407004/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Early+election+an+unnecessary+financial+burden+on+citizens&amp;title=The+Hamilton+Spectator+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2025-01-30&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Hamilton+Spectator+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: We'll vote again this year - twice.
The federal election will likely move up from its September four-year time frame, but the provincial one, Feb 27, is a totally unnecessary hit to taxpayers' pocketbooks. Some estimates put the cost at about $175 million.
Federally, six candidates are running to replace Justin Trudeau as Liberal leader, having filed nomination papers and the $50,000 refundable down payment on the $350,000 fee.
All have electoral experience except Mark Carney. Two are cabinet ministers (Chrystia Freeland and Karina Gould). The other contenders are MPs Jaime Battiste, Frank Baylis and former MP Ruby Dhalla.
The Ontario election is unnecessary because the last one was on June 2, 2022. That means Premier Doug Ford's term ends in June 2026. So why call an election now? Answer: to get elected before the release of the Auditor General's report on the Greenbelt scandal.
I recall a high school teacher who preached "strengthen the opposition." Smart idea now!
The polls look good for Ford, so why wait? It does not matter to him that it's an unnecessary financial burden on citizens. All 124 ridings need multiple staff in every poll in every riding, so elections aren't cheap. It's costly to mail voting cards, and do essential printing such as advertising, candidates material, etc.
His leadership has not been stellar, with hasty decisions that often backfired, requiring an "oops," followed by a retraction.
Does anyone believe the timing of $200 cheques (bribes) in the mail to "help people through difficult times" is coincidental? And this big spender's 2024-25 deficit could exceed $6 billion!
Beware fraudsters
As mentioned in a previous column, I received the grandparent scam call from a supposed granddaughter. Listening, I became suspicious, and said I'd call her at home. The caller hung up.
A bank teller told me about a senior, romantically involved with a man she'd met online, but never in person, because he said he worked mostly overseas. He borrowed thousands of dollars from her on several occasions. The teller cautioned her, then even asked bank management what they could do. Could they contact her daughter? No. Invasion of privacy.
Most of that lady's savings were depleted, as the teller watched helplessly.
A man I know got a call a couple of years ago that his computer was infected by a virus. That encounter ended very badly when the scammers gained access to his banking information. He never recovered financially from that fraud.
No doubt many computer users have seen a pop-up screen (as have I) warning that there's a problem with their software. "Do not to turn the computer off, but call Microsoft for a correction at this number." An acquaintance did in January, then worried she'd provided too much info to the rep, she hastily called her bank, which closed her account.
It took about 10 days to get a new access card, during which her daughter paid her bills.
Another recent scam involved an astute local lady, retired from banking, who custom handcrafts children's items. She had an inquiry through Facebook Messenger from a new "customer" and quoted $85 for an item, including shipping.
The purchaser said she lived in Texas, and would instantly forward the $85 via PayPal, whose logo appeared, stating that the funds would be kept "on hold" until the buyer paid $100 to upgrade the seller's account. After a couple more suspicious emails, the crafty lady ceased replying. The $100 PayPal request just didn't add up.
Some scams are small, some notoriously large, but inevitably result in financial and/or emotional trauma. It seems new ones appear daily so when in doubt, heed that gut instinct. Better safe than sorry!
CAPTION: We'll have two elections this year. At least one of them is completely unnecessary, Joan Little writes.

Subject: Political advertising; Elections; Banking

Business indexing term: Subject: Banking

Document 259 of 500

Tariff fight takes centre stage as election campaign kicks off: Party leaders hold first rallies as sprint begins to election day Feb. 27

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/tariff-fight-takes-centre-stage-as-election/docview/3161406896/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Tariff+fight+takes+centre+stage+as+election+campaign+kicks+off%3A+Party+leaders+hold+first+rallies+as+sprint+begins+to+election+day+Feb.+27&amp;title=The+Spectator&amp;issn=11899417&amp;date=2025-01-30&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.9&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Spectator&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Ontario Progressive Conservative Leader Doug Ford pitched himself Wednesday as the best steward of the economy in the face of looming tariffs, but the other party leaders say his record from the last seven years suggests otherwise.
Ford says he needs the strongest majority in Ontario history in order to effectively deal with the threatened 25 per cent tariffs from U.S. President Donald Trump, and has called a snap election for Feb. 27, nearly 1 1/2 years before the scheduled June 2026 vote.
This election will cost about $189 million to run, the chief electoral officer of Elections Ontario said Wednesday.
Ford held his first official campaign event in Windsor, with a backdrop of the Ambassador Bridge to the U.S. to highlight the tariffs issue.
"The bigger the mandate I receive from you, the better we'll be able to protect our province, because this is a game to the president," Ford said.
"He seeks to divide and conquer. Whether he imposes tariffs next week, next month, or waits another year or more, Trump's threats are not going away."
But as focused as Ford has been lately on the tariff fight, he has taken his eye off the ball on other pressing provincial issues, Liberal Leader Bonnie Crombie suggested.
"While the premier is running around the province pretending he's Captain Canada, he's not solving the basic issues that matter to Ontarians," she said at her campaign launch in Barrie.
"He said he'd fix hallway medicine. He didn't get it done. He said he'd cut your taxes, and he didn't get it done. He said he'd build 1.5 million homes. He didn't get it done. I've looked at the homelessness, mental health and addiction crisis that exists here in Barrie. He is not helping the situation here in Barrie on the ground, or fixing the doctors crisis."
Crombie was in Barrie alongside Liberal candidate in Barrie-Springwater-Oro-Medonte, Rose Zacharias, an emergency and family doctor as well as past president of the Ontario Medical Association. They highlighted a promise to ensure everyone in Ontario has a family doctor within four years.
Green Party Leader Mike Schreiner said the whole election is unnecessary. With potential tariffs on the horizon, which Trump has mused about implementing Feb. 1, Ontario would be better positioned to respond if they didn't come in the middle of an election campaign, he said.
"We should be here at Queen's Park working across party lines to show Donald Trump strength through unity, but instead, Doug Ford has called an election abandoning the people of Ontario when ... people need him the most," Schreiner said.
"But you know, this should come as no surprise, because over the past seven years, since the Ford government has taken office, housing starts are down, housing costs are up, 2.5 million Ontarians don't have access to a family doctor, (there are) unprecedented emergency room closures and hallway health care out of control, overcrowded classrooms and the rich just seem to be getting richer while the rest of us struggle just to get by."
NDP Leader Marit Stiles touted her own bona fides in taking on the tariff fight, saying she has bargained and negotiated with American and multinational corporations to protect jobs. Meanwhile, she suggested that Ford called this snap election not over tariffs, but to protect his own job.
"He says it's because he wants us to hire him to be our negotiator with Trump," she said in a speech. "Doug Ford, our negotiator? Is he kidding? I mean, just look at Doug Ford's track record."
Stiles pointed to a 95-year lease for a spa on Toronto's waterfront, and that just Monday the province's financial accountability officer found that Ford's decision to speed up access to beer and wine in corner stores by one year will cost the province $612 million.
His government is also under criminal investigation by the RCMP, who are looking into Ford's decision to open up the protected Greenbelt lands to housing development. It is a now-reversed decision that the auditor general said stood to benefit certain developers to the tune of more than $8 billion.
Elections Ontario got little more notice than the public that an election campaign would begin Wednesday, Chief Electoral Officer Greg Essensa said, but had started preparations in May, when Ford first sparked early election speculation by refusing to rule out a 2025 campaign.
Nonetheless, Elections Ontario will be ready to run the election, he said, with all 124 returning offices set to open Wednesday. Due to the snap vote, advance voting days will be limited to three instead of the usual 10.
The Canadian Press
CAPTION: Ontario Liberal Leader Bonnie Crombie, right, and Barrie Liberal candidate Dr. Rose Zacharias speak to the media during a campaign stop outside the Barrie Primary Care Campusin Barrie on Wednesday. Ontario NDP Leader Marit Stiles suggested that Doug Ford called this snap election not over tariffs, but to protect his own job.Ontario Conservative Leader Doug Ford speaks during his campaign launch in Windsor.Green Party Leader Mike Schreiner said the whole election is unnecessary.Nathan Denette The Canadian Press Chris Young The Canadian PressDax Melmer The Canadian PressThe Canadian Press

Document 260 of 500

Early provincial election costs us all

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/early-provincial-election-costs-us-all/docview/3161406888/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=unknown&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Early+provincial+election+costs+us+all&amp;title=The+Spectator&amp;issn=11899417&amp;date=2025-01-30&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.13&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Spectator&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Freelance columnist
Joan Little is a former

Document 261 of 500

Will the dream of high speed rail become a casualty of Canada's current political turmoil?

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/will-dream-high-speed-rail-become-casualty/docview/3161406452/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Will+the+dream+of+high+speed+rail+become+a+casualty+of+Canada%27s+current+political+turmoil%3F&amp;title=The+Hamilton+Spectator+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2025-01-30&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Hamilton+Spectator+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: OTTAWA - Can the elusive dream of high speed rail through Canada's most populous region survive the political shifts of the coming months?
At least one Liberal leadership contender - Karina Gould - is backing the idea, but questions remain around how much the project would cost, and whether a Conservative government under Pierre Poilievre would proceed with the idea.
Expectations of a decision on a proposed superproject to build faster - possibly much faster - train service from Toronto to Quebec City ramped up last fall, and although further details are still under wraps, Transportation Minister Anita Anand said this week that the government maintains plans to reduce rail travel times between Toronto and Montreal to under four hours.
But events have barrelled down the track in recent weeks. Prime Minister Justin Trudeau, pushed out by caucus unrest and the shock resignation of his finance minister and longtime political partner, shut down Parliament so his party could run a leadership race to replace him. All three main opposition parties now say they want an election, and intend to topple the minority Liberal administration after Parliament resumes March 24.
At the same time, the political and business classes are transfixed by U.S. President Donald Trump's threat to impose trade tariffs on all Canadian imports that groups like the Canadian Chamber of Commerce warn could trigger a recession and jeopardize hundreds of thousands of jobs.
So where does that leave the proposal for the possible high speed rail (HSR) line?
The Crown corporation charged with managing the project, VIA HFR, says it's ready to proceed. After accepting bids to develop a new dedicated rail line to beat the travel times currently available from Via Rail, the government was expected to choose a private consortium to develop it at some point last fall.
In October, Radio-Canada reported the Trudeau government would announce plans for a high speed rail line from Toronto to Quebec City in the coming weeks.
Three months later, the government still hasn't made an announcement, after it extended the time frame for the bids from the private consortia that were submitted in July 2024. Anand's office said the extension is standard but further details are secret because they're commercially sensitive.
"We're working to revolutionize passenger rail with a vision for faster, more reliable service that could connect Montreal and Toronto in under four hours," Anand said in a statement from her office that took aim at Poilievre.
She accused him of planning spending cuts while the Liberals are "focused on what we're building to transform Canada for future generations."
A key question is how fast trains on a proposed new line would travel. Martin Imbleau, the head of VIA HFR, has likened the vision to the high speed trains in France. Last year, he floated the prospect of travelling by rail from Toronto to Montreal in little more than three hours, down from the more-than five hours it takes now. The private groups that have made bids to build the new line have each submitted two proposals: one for trains that go slower than 200 km/h, and one for high speed rail that exceeds 200 km/h.
Some Liberals, including at least one cabinet minister, are keen to see high speed rail from Toronto to Quebec City. Procurement Minister Jean-Yves Duclos is calling on the party's leadership contenders to endorse the vision in their drives to replace Trudeau as prime minister.
"This project would be of great, great value for all sorts of obvious reasons," Duclos told the Star this week, arguing it would spur economic growth and help reduce greenhouse gas emissions that cause climate change.
"My personal point of view as a member of the Liberal party is that this is a very important project, and I would encourage leader the candidates in the leadership race to consider that project in their policy proposals," he said.
Gould, the Burlington MP running for the Liberal leadership, would "absolutely" strive to build high speed rail from Toronto to Quebec City if she becomes prime minister, her campaign told the Star.
"The work being done right now on the proposed corridor will be vital to grow the economy in Ontario and Quebec," it said in a statement.
"We need to support projects like this around the country, which is why she is also supportive of Edmonton-Calgary high speed rail."
Former Bank of Canada and Bank of England governor Mark Carney, who is also running for the Liberal leadership, stopped short in his own statement of endorsing construction of HSR from Toronto to Quebec City. But his campaign said Carney - who has spoken about the world being in an "investment era" for reducing greenhouse gas emissions and advancing technology - wants to "build the fastest growing economy in the G7, including with the right plan to strengthen our transportation infrastructure from coast to coast to coast."
His campaign promised more details in the coming weeks.
Chrystia Freeland, the former finance minister who resigned in December and is now running for leader, will have "more to say" very soon as she rolls out her policy vision, according to campaign spokesperson Katherine Cuplinskas.
Other leadership campaigns did not respond this week when asked about high speed rail.
The Conservatives also did not respond when asked if a Poilievre government would keep the Crown corporation and pursue a high speed rail line through that corridor. Previously, the party's transport critic has cast doubt on the project, comparing the proposal to a "photo op" and arguing the public can't trust the Liberal government to get it built.
Meanwhile, the Bloc Québécois has expressed support for the project. The New Democrats want to see high speed rail from Toronto to Quebec City, but want any project to be exclude private corporations that the party argue will make it more expensive to build, NDP spokesperson Erin Morrison said.
Neither the Crown corporation nor the government has said how much the project could cost, given how critical aspects - like the speed of the trains - remain undecided.
In 2021, then-transport minister Omar Alghabra suggested it might cost up to $12 billion to build. But a government source told the Star last fall the cost would likely be higher, depending on factors like inflation over the past four years, and how much of the new line would be built for high speeds.
Paul Langan, a long-time advocate for high speed rail in Canada, said Wednesday that several possible projects have been derailed by political changes in the past. That includes a proposed high speed train that was abandoned in the early 2000s when Paul Martin took over from Jean Chrétien as prime minister, and how Doug Ford's Progressive Conservative government shelved plans created under Liberal premier Kathleen Wynne for a high-speed train from London to Toronto, Langan said.
Langan fears the shape of government that emerges from the current political churn will be one that does not favour an expensive new - and, in his view, beneficial and long overdue - project like this. Until then, he looks with envy to countries in Europe and Asia which have had high speed rail for decades.
"Politics at any level has a direct effect of us joining the rest of the modern world," he said.
CAPTION: Karina Gould speaks to reporters after filing her paperwork to run for the Liberal leadership in Ottawa on Jan. 23, 2025.

Subject: Greenhouse gases; Emissions; Political leadership; High speed; Prime ministers

Location: Montreal Quebec Canada; Quebec City Quebec Canada; Canada

People: Gould, Karina

Title: Will the dream of high speed rail become a casualty of Canada's current political turmoil?

Document 262 of 500

Letters

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/letters/docview/3161406262/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=unknown&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Letters&amp;title=The+Spectator&amp;issn=11899417&amp;date=2025-01-30&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.13&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Spectator&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: We can't afford
four more years
I just received my $200 vote-buying cheque in the mail. It doesn't match the last vote-buying trick of cancelling licence plate charges, but it will likely still work for Dougie.
Some people will realize the loss of health care and lowering of educational levels isn't worth what he is offering, but he is betting most voters will not notice what they are losing for $200.
What scares me most is the fact that with another four years of the Ford government, the health care system will be close to unrecoverable and we will spend the rest of our lives paying more to survive, all for the sake of a $200 cheque.
This may be our last chance to get someone in who will fix the systems that are basic to our futures: let's not blow it again.
Beer in corner stores will not keep you alive, or educate our children.
Terry Horne, Hamilton
Give your rebate
to those in need
I received a gift in the mail this week! A $200 cheque from Doug Ford and friends! I am not going to say, "Thank you."
While I realize this gift will help some of his "folks" cover some of the cost for one trip to the grocery store, it will not solve the big problems. It is like putting a Band-Aid on a bleeding artery to staunch the flow of blood when we need a tourniquet.
If all the millions of dollars spent on this "gift" of our own money were put into our over burdened health-care system, our underfunded education system or any one of a number of our other underfunded social safety nets, some real solutions to our problems could begin.
I am giving my $200 to help fund the hot meal program at my downtown Hamilton church. I am urging everyone else to find a place of need and do likewise. There are plenty of options!
Ruth Simmons, Hamilton
My cheque is going
to health sciences
I just got my cheque from the province. My wife's was made out using her maiden name. We have been married for almost 50 years.
The funds will be going to Hamilton Health Sciences Foundation. If you are a senior and getting more from your federal carbon tax rebate than you pay out, I encourage you to make a similar donation to a noble cause of your choice if you are able.
Ron French, Hamilton
The cheque
is in the mail
I am writing in response to Rusty Escott's letter to the editor on Wednesday, where he writes that he will not receive the $200 rebate cheque because he "didn't work in 2024."
Perhaps Mr. Escott has confused this rebate with the $250 rebate provided by the federal government, but he is, nevertheless, incorrect. Firstly, to be eligible to receive this $200 rebate, Ontarians had to have filed their income tax return for 2023, not be incarcerated in 2024 and not be bankrupt. Those are the only requirements.
Although I have not yet received mine, I personally know several seniors and retirees who have. So hang tight, Mr. Escott. Canada Post is doing their best to deliver them as quickly as possible.
Deborah Gale, Hamilton
Ford doesn't need
a new mandate
Dear Premier Ford,
You have 79 seats in the legislature, the NDP have 28, Liberals nine, Greens two. You have a super majority and can pass whatever legislation you want. The next election is scheduled for June 2026.
Forcing an election on the citizens of Ontario now is, in one word, wrong.
Joe Benedetti, Hamilton
There are angels
are among us
My car broke down on Rymal Road East on the coldest night of the year. I called the road assist number on my credit card and was told that they would text me with the ETA.
After an hour, I called again and was given the towing company number. When I called them they said up to 40 minutes or as soon as they can. After another hour, I called the credit card number again and they said they would try their best.
During my third call another hour later, I saw a tow truck going the other way. He saw me on the street and turned around. He asked if he could help. He towed us home in a warm truck at a very reasonable price.
Thank you, Gabe of High Class Recovery. You saved our lives! (My husband is 92 - good thing we had a warm blanket in the car!)
Mary McCracken, Hamilton

Andrea Grebenc nominated as Liberal candidate for Burlington

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/andrea-grebenc-nominated-as-liberal-candidate/docview/3161573808/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Andrea+Grebenc+nominated+as+Liberal+candidate+for+Burlington&amp;title=The+Hamilton+Spectator+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2025-01-29&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Hamilton+Spectator+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Andrea Grebenc is running as the Liberal party's nominee for Burlington in the upcoming provincial election.
The former school board trustee and former chair of Halton District School Board was nominated as the party's local candidate on Dec. 15.
This week, Premier Doug Ford called a snap election, sending voters to the ballot box on Thursday, Feb. 27.
According to a news release from the Ontario Liberals, Grebenc ran to be Halton's regional chair in 2022, finishing third with 20,276 votes. The release further states Grebenc serves on Burlington's Integrated Transportation Advisory Committee and is a member of the steering committee for the Appleby Line Street Festival.
"I deeply understand the concerns of our community. I worry about my young adult children and the shrinking post-secondary opportunities, the lack of affordable housing and rising unemployment in Ontario that will affect them directly," Grebenc stated in the release.
She added families are struggling with rising costs of essentials, all while health care workers are overburdened and education funding gaps are driving workers out of the system.
"The challenges are clear, and we need strong, proven leadership to address them," Grebenc said. "That's why I'm an Ontario Liberal and proud to be part of Bonnie Crombie's team."
For more information on the Burlington riding, the upcoming election and local candidates visit the Elections Ontario website.
CAPTION: Andrea Grebenc

Document 264 of 500

Doug Ford has stripped himself of credibility with a Trumped-up snap election

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/doug-ford-has-stripped-himself-credibility-with/docview/3161573765/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Doug+Ford+has+stripped+himself+of+credibility+with+a+Trumped-up+snap+election&amp;title=The+Hamilton+Spectator+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2025-01-29&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Hamilton+Spectator+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: On a mid-winter Wednesday morning in Windsor, with a chill wind blowing across the Detroit River, Doug Ford posed for a phoney photo-op.
Against the backdrop of the Ambassador Bridge that ties us inextricably and unpredictably to Donald Trump's America, Ford cast himself as our reliable protector against a volatile U.S. president.
He did it - he called an early election no one wants, at a time no one imagined.
So much for "Get It Done" - his 2022 campaign slogan. Back then, the Progressive Conservative leader earnestly sought a full four-year mandate to get it done if re-elected as premier - on housing, on hallway health care, on the economy.
Ford solemnly sought a contract with voters, expiring only in June 2026, to do all those things. All undone this week, 15 months ahead of schedule.
Promises unkept? Never mind, for there are new promises to keep us safe.
The politician who first won power in 2018 - posing near the American border to proclaim earnestly that "Ontario is open for business" - now says business in Ontario is so vulnerable that he needs a new mandate. The mother of all mandates, crushing the opposition for four more years, all justified by his latest campaign slogan:
"Protect Ontario."
Protect? Or a pretext to protect Ford himself - after seven years as premier - from a police probe, from a housing shortfall, from a doctor shortage?
For the next four weeks we'll be watching a scripted campaign without a point for a politician without a purpose - other than perpetuating his time in power. Riding high in the polls, with the province at its lowest point, Ford succumbed to the exhortations of the PC campaign machine.
He called a snap election in hopes of a no-surprises campaign. And yet, despite the urgent call to arms, the truth is that this early election was a long time coming.
Long before Trump regained power, well before anyone imagined a tariff war, Ford's Tories had been urgently planning an early election since early last year. Mindful of the fiscal, political and criminal risks that might get in the way of a third term as premier, they contrived to find a new pretext to pre-empt any bad news.
First they imagined that digging a tunnel under Highway 401 would clear the way to the campaign trail. Then they tried to drive a wedge by bulldozing bike lanes, but that too failed to gain traction on the trail. Belatedly they brought beer to corner stores, until it became a billion-dollar boondoggle.
And so the PC brain trust cooked up a fresh casus belli: Trump's threat to wage economic warfare with 25 per cent tariffs was just the livelihood-threatening crisis that Ford's spin doctors ordered.
Now the legislature has been dissolved so that the Tories can be deployed into battle with all the precision of a military campaign.
Stumbling through snow banks, trudging through slush and salt, bracing against the winter wind, New Democrats, Greens and Liberals will try to recruit supporters and rouse the anti-Ford vote on the doorstep. It will be an uphill climb, which is precisely why the governing PCs have chosen February for this exercise in democracy, hoping that winter will discourage even the most diehard opponents from rising up against them.
But in politics, the best-laid plans and the best-looking polls have a way of melting away when the winds change. His high standing in the polls belies the big risk he is taking.
Ford claims with a straight face that he needs a bigger mandate to spend "tens of billions" bailing out Ontario's economy and protecting its workforce, yet he conveniently ignores the public pledges from his opposition rivals to offer a united front in the legislature. That would be a true show of strength, but the Tories don't want unity, they crave adversity - notwithstanding Ford's eloquent pan-Canadian orations.
True, his big lead appears to be baked into the polls. But the foundational premises upon which this election is based are lodged in quicksand.
Ford faces three wild cards:
What if Trump doesn't impose 25 per cent tariffs this Saturday as threatened? His nominee for commerce secretary, Howard Lutnick, suggested on Wednesday in Washington that they weren't coming as long as Canada acted on its border, while Ford was cheerfully fear-mongering in Windsor.
We will see by late February just how successful Ford is in wooing Washington under the media spotlight after he makes two separate trips. The presidential audience he pined for a few weeks ago still isn't on the schedule; what if Mr. Ford goes to Washington and nobody notices - other than a few Ontario reporters tagging along to see what a campaigning politician sounds like on the stump, stateside?
We will know before voting day whether the president has absorbed Canada through military, economic or political coercion. Assuming we remain an independent country, Ford's favourite pose with a baseball cap - proclaiming "Canada is not for sale" - will wear thin on his head.
If all these political pillars sink, Ford will have a lot of explaining to do - not just about his early election call over a short-term emergency that wasn't so urgent, but about the long-neglected problems closer to home. Not just on hallway health care and housing shortages, but on spending boondoggles that have beset his government on the Greenbelt and beer in corner stores, adding up to billions of dollars.
The more Ford's credibility dissolves over a trumped-up election campaign against Trump, the more his believability will be doubted on election issues closer to home. And the more his major opponents - the NDP's Marit Stiles and Bonnie Crombie of the Liberals - will have the opportunity to call him out.
CAPTION: Progressive Conservative Leader Doug Ford meets with supporters during his campaign launch in Windsor on Jan. 29, 2025.

Natalie Pierre acclaimed as PC candidate for Burlington

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/natalie-pierre-acclaimed-as-pc-candidate/docview/3161573600/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Natalie+Pierre+acclaimed+as+PC+candidate+for+Burlington&amp;title=The+Hamilton+Spectator+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2025-01-29&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Hamilton+Spectator+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Natalie Pierre, incumbent Member of Provincial Parliament, is running in Burlington again as the Progressive Conservative Party nominee in the upcoming provincial election.
The party announced Jan. 13 that Pierre, originally elected Burlington MPP in June 2022, had been acclaimed as the party's local candidate.
Premier Doug Ford called an early election, sending voters to the ballot box on Thursday, Feb. 27.
Pierre won the Burlington provincial seat in 2022 with 22,326 votes (42 per cent of the vote). She topped her closest competitor by 6,891 votes.
A party press release states Pierre helped secure investments in local health care, infrastructure, and workforce development, including installation of a new MRI machine and creation of a future mental health unit at Joseph Brant Hospital.
Pierre said representing Burlington is a privilege and she's grateful for the trust the community placed in her.
"Every day, I'm motivated by the people I meet, and I'm focused on delivering the solutions that matter to them, from improving health care and creating good-paying jobs to keeping costs down for families," Pierre stated in the press release. "There's so much more to do, and I'm ready to continue working hard for the people of Burlington to build a brighter, stronger future."
For more information on the Burlington riding, the upcoming election, and local candidates visit the Elections Ontario website.

Document 266 of 500

Roy Green signs off after six decades on the air: Hamilton broadcast legend facing 'the fight for my life'

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/roy-green-signs-off-after-six-decades-on-air/docview/3160911607/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Roy+Green+signs+off+after+six+decades+on+the+air%3A+Hamilton+broadcast+legend+facing+%27the+fight+for+my+life%27&amp;title=The+Spectator&amp;issn=11899417&amp;date=2025-01-29&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.1&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Spectator&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: When he woke up on Monday morning, he started doing what he always does. Still lying in bed, he grabbed both of his mobile phones and began scouring news sites for possible segment ideas.
He'd made it through a couple stories and jotted a few mental notes when ... "The light bulb at the back of my head flashed and said, 'No hurry here,' " Roy Green chuckles.
Nope. After signing off from his national radio show on Sunday afternoon, his 62-year broadcast career - most of it spent here in Hamilton - was done. No need for any more topics.
He has retired before. But this time he means it. For a huge reason.
There have been plenty of broadcasting legends who've called this town home. Too many to list here and still have space to tell our tale. Yet you can't tell the story of this city's radio history without a chapter on Green.
He was 16 and living in Montreal when his path was unexpectedly laid in front of him. The general manager of a local rock station was a family friend and, while everyone was eating dinner together one night, he asked young Green what his plans were for the summer. Deliver groceries on a bicycle with a giant bucket in front, was the response.
"How would you like to work for the radio station?"
Uh, sure. Sounds great.
Three weeks later, the late-night disc jockey showed up for his shift a little, shall we say, wobbly. Green was just a high school kid, but he recognized this guy couldn't go on the air in his condition. So he called the boss at home to find out what he should do.
"Well, tag, you're it," was the answer.
A career was born.
He made it through that shift. Which became a thing. He stayed on the air there for a while, eventually did some time as program director, and moved to Calgary, where "at that time the station played music worse than Muzak," he laughs. His time there ended when he was chided for playing a song by that notorious rocker (sarcasm alert) Neil Diamond - and headed back to Montreal.
Hamilton came into his life at the start of 1973 when he was hired by 900CHML. This city was a mystery to him back then. But he got to know it doing nights, then covering swing shifts, then spending some time at local FM station CKDS, followed by a return to CHML to host "ML Magazine," a three-hour interview show.
By 1990, he was hosting in the afternoon slot. Then a year later, he took over the morning program from John Hardy where he really made his mark doing hard news and current events.
Exchanges and interviews were tough but generally civil. Though there was the one time two guests nearly came to blows on the air. And there was that day NDP leader Jack Layton stormed out of the studio when Green wouldn't relent with his questioning.
Oh, and a personal favourite of his was the show he spent two hours eviscerating a federal justice minister whose position on a particular issue enraged him.
He became must-listen. If you were a newsmaker who mattered in this city, this province or this country, you appeared on Green's show. Which lasted until 2007.
"At which time I tried to retire," he says.
Except the morning after his final shift he got a call pitching him on the idea of a national weekend show. Interview the biggest voices. Discuss the pressing issues of the day. Talk to prime ministers and leaders and others driving the conversation.
Most anyway. He never could get Justin Trudeau to come on. And once Doug Ford became premier, Green says his previously regular guest was no longer available.
Still, every Saturday and Sunday he'd take his place at the CHML studio on Main Street West and broadcast from coast to coast. Including to Hamilton. At least until August when CHML was shuttered.
"That hurt," he says. "That's where I had spent so much of my life. Not just my professional life. My life."
It became difficult for some around here to pick up his show that he was now doing in front of a microphone at home. Which, as he heard again and again, led some to believe he'd stopped altogether.
Yet that's not why he has decided to wrap things up now.
It isn't boredom, either. He's still engaged in the events of the day. It's certainly not that he has done it all, including winning countless broadcasting awards and being inducted into the Hamilton Gallery of Distinction. Or even that he'll turn 78 in a month.
"I'm literally in the fight for my life," he says.
A couple years ago, when his boss at the station hadn't heard from him for a week, police were called and asked to do a wellness check. When officers got to the house, they found Green incoherent and in rough shape. He was rushed to hospital where doctors weren't sure he'd make it through the day.
What few knew was that not long before, he'd been diagnosed with Stage 4 metastatic prostate cancer. Now he was in deep trouble with acute kidney failure.
While he doesn't remember anything about that day or several before, he pulled through. And kept doing the show. But the gig takes time. A lot of it. Not just the actual broadcasting. He's always worked 12 to 13 hours a day doing the research and work that comes before hitting the "on air" button.
"I've done over 100,000 interviews," he says. "I can honestly say I never did one I didn't prepare for."
Since he's spending more time with doctors and nurses these days, it just feels like the right moment to step away from the mic. Sixty-two years seems like a pretty good run. Plus, he has some stuff he'd still like to do.
One bucket-list item in particular.
"It's to ride a Harley up the Alaska Highway," Green says.
It's something he has thought about for a long time. Take a month or so and do the full picturesque, 2,500-kilometre route from Dawson Creek, B.C., through the Yukon to Delta Junction, Alaska.
The plan is to get a bike and head there this summer.
Some recent medical tests that were better than anticipated have really put some oomph behind the planning.
Yeah, he has pretty intense pain some days but more days are pretty good. And while the cancer is now in his bones, he has already beaten the initial prediction that he might only have a couple months left.
So he was out motorcycle shopping earlier this week. Looking for the perfect ride for the perfect trip. Where he won't have to think about segments or stories or prep time. Just the open road.
"I better do it while I can."
Scott Radley is a columnist with the Hamilton Spectator focusing on sports and politics. Reach him at
sradley@thespec.com.
CAPTION: Local broadcasting legend Roy Green hosted his final show on the weekend and is now planning his bucket-list motorcycle trip up the Alaska Highway. Roy Green on his old Suzuki bike during a trip to Nova Scotia a few years ago. He's planning on riding a Harley the length of the Alaska Highway this summer.Roy Green wraps up his final show on 900CHML to begin his first retirement in 2007. It didn't last long.Cathie Coward The Hamilton SpectatorCathie Coward Hamilton Spectator file photoKaz Novak Hamilton Spectator file photo
