Document 267 of 500

Letters

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/letters/docview/3160911368/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=unknown&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Letters&amp;title=The+Spectator&amp;issn=11899417&amp;date=2025-01-29&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.9&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Spectator&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: We need these stories that inspire
Re: 'A life of purpose': This Hamilton woman cooks through the night every Friday to feed more than a thousand in need, Jan. 27
The Spectator has featured many articles lately that restore faith in humanity in these times of despair for many. I refer specifically to today's feature on the front page about a wonderful lady who cooks food every week for so many people who come to Gore Park every Saturday. She is Anne Thompson and is soon to receive the Order of Hamilton for her selfless work to help those in need. Last week, there was an article about the Cathedral Cafe on James Street North run by St. Matthews House out of Christ Church Cathedral. Guests can receive breakfast and lunch six days a week and stay out of the cold for the day, 50 people at a time. Most of the staff are volunteers who help with food preparation, serving meals, etc. Please keep telling these stories about all these grassroots volunteers and organizations in our city that really care about our community. In dark days, these stories are the light that inspire us to keep going and caring about each other.
Colleen Dawson, Hamilton
Other cities
need to pitch in
Re: Hamilton can't shoulder this weight alone, Jan. 25
Bravo to Pete Wiesner! My wife and I have been saying this for years. Why does Hamilton have to take more than its fair share when it comes to social assistance throughout Ontario? What is Burlington, Oakville, Milton, etc. doing? It's time other communities share some of this burden. Whatever party manages to win the upcoming provincial election needs to make a point of forcing more services to communities outside of Hamilton.
Of course, I would say it should also be up to Hamilton's city council to be pushing this agenda, but this may be too much to ask from this mayor and council.
Phil Van Impe, Hamilton
Students should vote for education
Re: Why doesn't Doug Ford care about funding colleges and universities? Jan. 27
The answer to the question is in the article: "Students, surely worry, but they don't vote in large enough numbers to count." Maybe more of them should vote. After all, it is in their best interests.
John Valeri, Waterdown
School board has crossed the line
Re: Grand Erie school board sends warning letters to members of public, Jan. 25
So the Grand Erie District School Board (GEDSB) not only feels that it can operate without any public scrutiny, it now feels free to try and shut down any criticism by members of the public as evidenced by recent letters to Messrs. Barrett and Harris. The quote, "Each of us has the privilege of free speech, but not without limits" is particularly concerning coming from an organization responsible for education.
Whether the claims and actions regarding trustee Carol Ann Sloat are real or warranted is not the issue. Rather, the real concern is GEDSB's feeling that it, an elected body, can act in complete secrecy with no responsibility for transparency to the public, including the media, all by simply claiming confidentiality.
It's time for the provincial government to step in, review and take action, including removal of the entire board if warranted. I eagerly await the board's letter to me.
Wayne Maddever, Burlington
Survey results
unconscionable
Re: Survey says more young Canadians believe the history of the Holocaust is exaggerated, Jan. 26
I've not read a sadder headline, article, nor statistics in decades. That fully 18 per cent of Canadians between 18 and 24 "think the Holocaust has been exaggerated" is unconscionable. What a failure of an education system to have produced such a "result."
My Grade 10 civics teacher, Mrs. Legge (in my hometown, Sarnia), taught us of the atrocities. Until her class, I had only heard the term "concentration camps." My childish misunderstanding of the term (foolishly mistaking it as somehow related to orange juice, which also came from "concentrates") was soon shattered when she used the more accurate term: "extermination camps."
She opened our eyes to the harsh realities that today's high school graduates belittle as "exaggerated." Shame on them. And on their teachers. And shame on any who forget, ignore or belittle actual history. Let us not go down the same slippery slope as our neighbours to the south by rejecting recorded, verifiable facts.
George Olds, Hamilton
Jewish children know the truth
A survey says eight per cent of young Canadians between ages 25 to 34 believe the Holocaust has been exaggerated. Jewish children know this not to be true. We have seen numbered tattoos on arms, we have known relatives who watched as their family members were shot in front of their eyes.
Many Jews that suffered through the Holocaust don't discuss it as it's too triggering and upsetting.
It's now 2025 and it seems the most hate crimes are targeting Jewish people. What have we learned? Steven Spielberg directed "Schindler's List" for his children and future generations so they would not forget the atrocities. Perhaps it should be mandatory learning.
Robin Magder, Dundas
No rebate cheque for retirees
Re: Waiting for your $200
Ontario 'tax rebate'? Here's what to do if it hasn't arrived, Jan. 25
I just read an article about what to do if you're still waiting for your $200 Ontario tax credit. Hmm, John Doe over there, who has been flipping burgers for the last two years, (not that there's anything wrong with flipping burgers, at least it's a job) got his $200 because he was working last year. I've worked for north of 43 years paying my taxes every year, been retired now for some 10 years, still paying income taxes even though I no longer work, yet I don't qualify for the $200 tax rebate because I didn't "work" in 2024.
Now, the $200 won't make me rich, but it might get me a small bag of groceries. There is a point to be made here somewhere I think. So, while Doug Ford tries to figure it out, I'll just wait for my cheque. And wait. And wait. And wait.
Rusty Escott, Dunnville
Nothing beneath this premier
Re: Snap Ontario election call kicks parties' planning into high gear, Jan. 26
Doug Ford is adopting the dog-whistle approach embodied by the likes of Donald Trump and Pierre Poilievre when he asks whether voters can imagine Bonnie Crombie or Marit Stiles negotiating with Trump - a thinly veiled allusion to gender, perpetuating the tired and false notion that women are weaker and less effective negotiators. While it's tempting to suggest that this is beneath him, Ford's performance while in office has made it plain that nothing is beneath him.
His transparent effort to buy support by sending cheques to Ontario voters at a cost of $3 billion, his political opportunism calling an unnecessary election, his failure to meet his own housing targets, his persistent indifference to homelessness, his failure to restore health care and adequately fund education, his blithe reversal of his commitment to preserve the Greenbelt - clearly there is no bottom.
Dayna Firth, Hamilton
Coalition needed to defeat Ford
Dear Marit, Mike and Bonnie, please do something meaningful this election.
May I suggest a coalition, even temporarily, of your (our) three parties to beat the PCs or, at the very least, hold them to a minority.
The framework might be simple, in fact Ford has assured inadequate time for anything beyond simple. You, our three leaders, get your seats uncontested, by the other two coalition members. Non-PC incumbents retain their seats, again, uncontested. In every other riding, support only the coalition member with the best chance of winning. My guess is all three of you know the polling to the nearest one percentage point in all Ontario ridings.
I think the coalition would be embraced by even our most ardent party faithful; would they rather concede to the PCs by splitting the vote? Only Deceitful Doug would be angry, incensed in fact. Coalitions are common in Europe. You (we) could choose our leader after the election; you all seem to have closely aligned views.
Again, please do something different, bold and meaningful for the people this election.
Mark Hagar, Hamilton
The premier
just doesn't get it
Praise to the Spec for the continuous exposures of the totally dysfunctional premier, one Doug Ford. Without restating the list of his never ending mismanagement of this province and taxpayers' money, the only remedy is to vote him out once and for all. He just doesn't get it and will not give Ontario what is needs and deserves.
Get rid of him, now, and let's get someone who works for the people and not him/her self.
Also, Vinay Menon is brilliant and deserves an award.
Robert Panchyson,
Burlington
Early election
is unnecessary
Like many others, I'm shaking my head over why Doug Ford is calling an election. His party has a clear majority, so anything they want to get passed, could be passed. It's also a huge waste of taxpayers' money, some $15 million that, frankly, we do not have to throw around. It should be spent on new doctors, nurses, houses, infrastructure, etc.
Now Queen's Park will be shut down, nothing gets done for the next month or so. It'd be better if you stayed open for business and do what you are supposed to do: work for the citizens of Ontario!
Hopefully, this is going to go very, very badly for Ford if the voters remember the Greenbelt incident; the Ontario Place incident and this unnecessary election.
Jay Miller, Greensville
Wrong stance
taken on tariffs
Premier Ford made a serious mistake by calling an election, and responding incorrectly to the tariff threat situation. He said that he needed a majority mandate (which he already has) to combat U.S. President Donald Trump's tariffs.
He suggested tariffs would cost thousands of jobs, but we don't even know this is true since Ford's only saying so. But what if Trump withdraws his tariff threat? Ford's retaliatory approach, such as curbing electricity to the U.S. for instance, has already caused tension with the U.S.
Trump responded with saying we don't need anything they have. Because of Canada's lax border security, illegal fentanyl is entering the U.S., which is why Trump is threatening tariffs against Canada. The premier does not see this. Ford should have responded, simply assuring Trump that he was doing everything he could to secure the border, thereby appeasing both the situation and Trump.
If anything, the premier should cancel the election while he can.
Tina Maini Hamilton
Trump threats unite Canadians
Re: A wake-up call for Canada - and the case for optimism, Jan. 27
Congratulations to Daniel Tisch for his spot-on column. He is right - threats are making Canadians more united.
It is pointless to show weakness to or try to negotiate with a bully. Canada needs to be in full combat mode with this Trumptious knave on the doorstep. Doug Ford is not fighting an election: he is getting ready for war. All Canadians should be pulling the same wagon in this perilous time. Politics and oil are secondary to a show of unity, Canadian autonomy and our national interests.
Have we forgotten history and Hawaii? An independent, sovereign country until U.S. and European business interests deposed a reigning monarch in 1893 (who conceded to avoid bloodshed). There was little resistance when U.S. militia annexed the island in 1898. The U.S. also managed to change the name of the island from a republic to a state. Then there is Guam, Puerto Rico, Samoa, the Virgin Islands, all annexed by the U.S.
Trumps's rhetoric is timely: "Canada (and Greenland and Panama) needs to stand back and stand by. If we don't fight like hell for our country, we won't have a country anymore."
Suzanne Swan, Burlington
Muscle cars
and machismo
Re: Last hurrah for Dodge's V8 muscle trucks? Try this one on for size! Jan. 24
It's great that The Spec continues to publish reviews of supercharged gas vehicles.
I know that some people will complain that they're often noisy, overpowered and highly polluting - but these vehicles are important for males who feel, er, deficient in their own manliness. They help their owners maintain the level of machismo they crave.
Sure, the vehicles contribute excessively to climate change, but what's that compared to being able to disturb unfriendly neighbours at all hours and smothering them in exhaust fumes?
Sadly, in a few years, we will all be buying quiet, emission-free electric vehicles. But there is a silver lining: with EVs, it's all torque and plenty of action.
Harry Shannon, Dundas

Budget bump represents needs, chief says
Police are calling for a 5.7 per cent budget increase, which represents $12.25 million more from last year for a combined net operating and capital budget of $227 million.

Voters officially heading to polls early: Ford wants large mandate but opposition parties insist election is not necessary this year

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/voters-officially-heading-polls-early/docview/3160911231/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Voters+officially+heading+to+polls+early%3A+Ford+wants+large+mandate+but+opposition+parties+insist+election+is+not+necessary+this+year&amp;title=The+Spectator&amp;issn=11899417&amp;date=2025-01-29&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.6&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Spectator&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Ontario Premier Doug Ford officially set a provincial election in motion Tuesday for Feb. 27, more than a year before the next fixed election date.
The election had been set for June 2026, but Ford said he needs a new mandate to deal with four years of a Donald Trump presidency in the United States.
Ford has said Trump's threat to impose 25 per cent tariffs on Canadian goods would hit Ontario and its auto sector hard, and the provincial government would need to spend tens of billions of dollars to protect jobs and the economy.
Cabinet has discussed an "economic action plan" to respond to tariffs from the United States, the premier said earlier Tuesday.
Officials say the economic action plan has not yet been approved by cabinet and therefore won't be implemented during the campaign - and Ford instead suggested it will be rolled out as announcements.
"We want to move forward and make sure that we give certainty," Ford said.
"Right now, President Trump ... has put uncertainty to every single Canadian, a lot of other countries around the world, and this isn't going to happen overnight. It may not happen Feb. 1, I'm sure something's coming, but this is going to be a battle for the next four years, and I want to make sure that I have a strong mandate to outlast President Trump."
Green Party Leader Mike Schreiner said merely announcing such a plan once tariffs are in place doesn't make sense.
"If the premier has an economic action plan ready to go, why not just implement it?" Schreiner wrote in a statement. "Why not work across party lines to protect Ontario workers, Ontario jobs and Ontario companies?"
NDP Leader Marit Stiles said the threat of tariffs is precisely why Ontario should not be plunged into an election campaign right now.
"While the people of Ontario are anxious about the grave threat of tariffs, Doug Ford is pursuing his own political gain," she wrote in a statement. "People need a premier who will fight like hell for every single job that's at risk, not run to the polls over a year early."
Ford has said he needs the "largest mandate in Ontario's history."
The Canadian Press
CAPTION: Premier Doug Ford dons a tuque following a press conference with Chief Sonny Gagnon of Aroland First Nation at the Queens Park Legislature in Toronto Tuesday.Chris Young The Canadian Press

CREDIT: Liam Casey and Allison Jones The Canadian Press

Document 270 of 500

Hamilton broadcast legend Roy Green hangs up the mic to 'fight for my life'

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/hamilton-broadcast-legend-roy-green-hangs-up-mic/docview/3160911065/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Hamilton+broadcast+legend+Roy+Green+hangs+up+the+mic+to+%27fight+for+my+life%27&amp;title=The+Hamilton+Spectator+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2025-01-29&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Hamilton+Spectator+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: When he woke up on Monday morning, he started doing what he always does. Still lying in bed, he grabbed both of his mobile phones and began scouring news sites for possible segment ideas.
He'd made it through a couple stories and jotted a few mental notes when ... "The light bulb at the back of my head flashed and said, 'No hurry here,'" Roy Green chuckles.
Nope. After signing off from his national radio show on Sunday afternoon, his 62-year broadcast career - most of it spent here in Hamilton - was done. No need for any more topics.
He has retired before. But this time he means it. For a huge reason.
There have been plenty of broadcasting legends who've called this town home. Too many to list here and still have space to tell our tale. Yet you can't tell the story of this city's radio history without a chapter on Green.
He was 16 and living in Montreal when his path was unexpectedly laid in front of him. The general manager of a local rock station was a family friend and, while everyone was eating dinner together one night, he asked young Green what his plans were for the summer. Deliver groceries on a bicycle with a giant bucket in front, was the response.
"How would you like to work for the radio station?"
Uh, sure. Sounds great.
Three weeks later, the late-night disc jockey showed up for his shift a little, shall we say, wobbly. Green was just a high school kid, but he recognized this guy couldn't go on the air in his condition. So he called the boss at home to find out what he should do.
"Well, tag, you're it," was the answer.
A career was born.
He made it through that shift. Which became a thing. He stayed on the air there for a while, eventually did some time as program director, and moved to Calgary, where "at that time the station played music worse than Muzak," he laughs. His time there ended when he was chided for playing a song by that notorious rocker (sarcasm alert) Neil Diamond - and headed back to Montreal.
Hamilton came into his life at the start of 1973 when he was hired by 900CHML. This city was a mystery to him back then. But he got to know it doing nights, then covering swing shifts, then spending some time at local FM station CKDS, followed by a return to CHML to host ML Magazine, a three-hour interview show.
By 1990, he was hosting in the afternoon slot. Then a year later, he took over the morning program from John Hardy where he really made his mark doing hard news and current events.
Exchanges and interviews were tough but generally civil. Though there was the one time two guests nearly came to blows on the air. And there was that day NDP leader Jack Layton stormed out of the studio when Green wouldn't relent with his questioning.
Oh, and a personal favourite of his was the show he spent two hours eviscerating a federal justice minister whose position on a particular issue enraged him.
He became must-listen. If you were a newsmaker who mattered in this city, this province or this country, you appeared on Green's show. Which lasted until 2007.
"At which time I tried to retire," he says.
Except the morning after his final shift he got a call pitching him on the idea of a national weekend show. Interview the biggest voices. Discuss the pressing issues of the day. Talk to prime ministers and leaders and others driving the conversation.
Most anyway. He never could get Justin Trudeau to come on. And once Doug Ford became premier, Green says his previously regular guest was no longer available.
Still, every Saturday and Sunday he'd take his place at the CHML studio on Main Street West and broadcast from coast to coast. Including to Hamilton. At least until August when CHML was shuttered.
"That hurt," he says. "That's where I had spent so much of my life. Not just my professional life. My life."
It became difficult for some around here to pick up his show that he was now doing in front of a microphone at home. Which, as he heard again and again, led some to believe he'd stopped altogether.
Yet that's not why he has decided to wrap things up now.
It isn't boredom, either. He's still engaged in the events of the day. It's certainly not that he has done it all, including winning countless broadcasting awards and being inducted into the Hamilton Gallery of Distinction. Or even that he'll turn 78 in a month.
"I'm literally in the fight for my life," he says.
A couple years ago, when his boss at the station hadn't heard from him for a week, police were called and asked to do a wellness check. When officers got to the house, they found Green incoherent and in rough shape. He was rushed to hospital where doctors weren't sure he'd make it through the day.
What few knew was that not long before, he'd been diagnosed with Stage 4 metastatic prostate cancer. Now he was in deep trouble with acute kidney failure.
While he doesn't remember anything about that day or several before, he pulled through. And kept doing the show. But the gig takes time. A lot of it. Not just the actual broadcasting. He's always worked 12 to 13 hours a day doing the research and work that comes before hitting the "on air" button.
"I've done over 100,000 interviews," he says. "I can honestly say I never did one I didn't prepare for."
Since he's spending more time with doctors and nurses these days, it just feels like the right moment to step away from the mic. Sixty-two years seems like a pretty good run. Plus, he has some stuff he'd still like to do.
One bucket-list item in particular.
"It's to ride a Harley up the Alaska Highway," Green says.
It's something he has thought about for a long time. Take a month or so and do the full picturesque, 2,500-kilometre route from Dawson Creek, B.C., through the Yukon to Delta Junction, Alaska.
The plan is to get a bike and head there this summer.
Some recent medical tests that were better than anticipated have really put some oomph behind the planning.
Yeah, he has pretty intense pain some days but more days are pretty good. And while the cancer is now in his bones, he has already beaten the initial prediction that he might only have a couple months left.
So he was out motorcycle shopping earlier this week. Looking for the perfect ride for the perfect trip. Where he won't have to think about segments or stories or prep time. Just the open road.
"I better do it while I can."
CAPTION: Roy Green wraps up his final show on 900CHML to begin his first retirement in 2007. It didn't last long.Ontario Premier Mike Harris answers a caller's question while appearing on Roy Green's show at CHML in 1999.Roy Green on his old Suzuki bike during a trip to Nova Scotia a few years ago. He's planning on riding a Harley the length of the Alaska Highway this summer.Local broadcasting legend Roy Green hosted his final show on the weekend and is now planning his bucket-list motorcycle trip up the Alaska Highway on his new Harley, even as he battles Stage 4 metastatic prostate cancer.

Document 271 of 500

Sarah Jama to run as independent in Hamilton Centre after NDP rejection: Some party activists warn New Democrats could lose long-held riding to vote splitting

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/sarah-jama-run-as-independent-hamilton-centre/docview/3160911062/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Sarah+Jama+to+run+as+independent+in+Hamilton+Centre+after+NDP+rejection%3A+Some+party+activists+warn+New+Democrats+could+lose+long-held+riding+to+vote+splitting&amp;title=The+Spectator&amp;issn=11899417&amp;date=2025-01-29&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.3&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Spectator&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Sarah Jama says she will run for re-election as an independent MPP in Hamilton Centre now that the New Democrats have decided against welcoming back the activist ousted from the party over controversial comments on the Israel-Hamas war.
If the party runs a candidate against Jama in the snap election campaign beginning Wednesday - which appears to be the plan - it could open the door to vote splitting and a rare multi-party battle for a Hamilton riding long considered a "lock" for the New Democratic Party, said political scientist Chris Erl.
The New Democrats booted the rookie MPP from caucus in late 2023 in the aftermath of a statement Jama posted online about the Israel-Hamas war, but the party signalled last year it was studying whether she could return to the fold.
In an interview, Jama said she tried to do everything possible to "repair the relationship" and rejoin the party - but on Monday, she received an official letter rejecting her NDP nomination package.
That's unfortunate, she said - but it won't stop her from running for re-election.
"My campaign is fuelled by grassroots people looking for a political home," said Jama, who noted she has often voted with the NDP in the legislature but isn't willing to bow to "top-down" party politics. "Anyone else who puts their name forward risks splitting the vote."
In a statement, NDP provincial director Kevin Beaulieu called the vetting process for candidates "confidential," although a letter sent to the local riding association cited reasons for the rejection that included Jama's declaration that she would run as an independent if not welcomed back.
Members of the executive of the local NDP provincial riding association held a news conference Tuesday condemning the decision to deny Jama a shot - and warning about the potential fallout.
"This failure of the party to reconcile (with Jama) has weakened the party throughout the province," said riding association president Tom Baker, who warned the party brass is alienating "diverse perspectives of new activist members" and is risking a loss of support among both volunteers and voters.
Beaulieu said two prospective candidates will vie for the Hamilton Centre NDP nomination at a yet-be-announced meeting.
One of those prospective candidates is Dr. Robin Lennox, a family physician and well-known advocate for harm reduction services.
"My motivation to put my name forward really came from seeing, over the last seven years, how much harm that the ... Conservative government and their policies have been having on my patients," she said. "This is just another way to try to advocate."
Lennox declined to comment on the controversy around Jama's ouster or her rejected effort to rejoin the party. "It's (the voters') job to determine who is their best candidate."
The other prospective candidate is Aisha Jahangir, a mental health nurse at the Hamilton-Wentworth detention centre who previously ran for the party provincially in Etobicoke and federally in Guelph.
Jama, a well-known housing and disability activist who won a byelection in the riding for the NDP in 2023, was booted from caucus just months later in the aftermath of public comments on the Israel-Gaza war.
NDP leader Marit Stiles ultimately blamed Jama's ouster on "a number of unilateral actions that have undermined our collective work and broken the trust of her colleagues."
But since then, prominent party members and some local politicians have publicly called for Jama to be welcomed back under the orange banner, including NDP Hamilton Centre MP Matthew Green and city councillor Cameron Kroetsch. The internal party rift and potential split in voters, fundraisers and volunteers could open the door to another party's candidate "sneaking up the middle," Erl said, in a riding that hasn't been won by a party other than the NDP since the 1980s.
Other declared candidates in the riding include former justice of the peace Eileen Walker for the Liberals, community organizer and environmental advocate Lucia Iannantuono for the Greens, and political scientist and 2022 candidate Sarah Bokhari for the Progressive Conservatives.
Matthew Van Dongen is a reporter at The Spectator.
mvandongen@thespec.com
CAPTION: Sarah Jama says she will run as an independent candidate in Hamilton Centre for the snap provincial election.Cathie Coward The Hamilton Spectator

Who gets your vote in this provincial election?: We go to polls Feb. 27 to cast ballots originally scheduled for 2026

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/who-gets-your-vote-this-provincial-election/docview/3160911038/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Who+gets+your+vote+in+this+provincial+election%3F%3A+We+go+to+polls+Feb.+27+to+cast+ballots+originally+scheduled+for+2026&amp;title=The+Spectator&amp;issn=11899417&amp;date=2025-01-29&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.1&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Spectator&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Surprise, Hamilton - it's suddenly time to think about who deserves your vote in a snap provincial election triggered by the Tory government more than a year ahead of schedule.
Progressive Conservative Leader Doug Ford justified the early election call by saying the government needs a "clear mandate" from voters to spend big in confronting an across-the-board tariff threat from newly elected U.S. President Donald Trump.
Opposition parties like Marit Stiles's NDP, Bonnie Crombie's Liberals and Mike Schreiner's Greens, by contrast, argue spending between $150 million and $175 million on an early election campaign is unnecessary and irresponsible.
Some critics argue Ford wants get ahead of a looming federal vote or potential findings in an ongoing RCMP probe into the government's now-reversed Greenbelt land swap.
There's a chance the early election call could irritate the electorate and backfire on the government - and an even bigger likelihood it will dampen voter turnout, said local political scientist Chris Erl.
"People don't like having to go to the polls early; they don't like the idea of a February election," he said.
But there's no denying the impact that threatened tariffs would have on the economy and - if they are implemented as early as this weekend - on the "election narrative," he added, noting Ford's efforts to portray himself as a "Captain Canada" figure.
Either way, the election scramble is on for a vote now slated for Feb. 27, with some parties still working to nominate candidates in local ridings expected to feature competitive races, including Hamilton Centre and Hamilton Mountain.
In the downtown riding, a seat long held by the NDP is suddenly in play now that the NDP has decided not to welcome independent MPP Sarah Jama back to the party after her ouster in 2023 over contentious statements on the Israel-Hamas conflict.
The independent candidate will now face off against a new, yet-to-be-chosen NDP standard-bearer - a battle local New Democrat activists warn could lead to vote splitting in the once-safe orange riding held for years by former party leader Andrea Horwath.
Longtime New Democrat MPP Monique Taylor, meanwhile, has announced she will run as a candidate in the next federal election - meaning there is an open battle for the Hamilton Mountain seat. Taylor has held the seat since 2011, but since the late 1990s, the riding has also elected both Liberal and Tory MPPs.
Aside from the shadow of Trumpian tariffs, there are plenty of issues that could drive voters in this election.
Erl argued the economy, unattainable housing and "general affordability" are top of mind for many voters.
Locally and across the province, hot-button issues, including provincial policy on safe-injection sites, homelessness and tent encampments, as well as a doctor shortage that has left 75,000 city residents with no family physician.
Municipalities, meanwhile, are appealing for provincial help to deal with failing infrastructure, the cost of development and the fact Ontario is the only province in Canada that has downloaded responsibility for social housing on cities.
Those high-level needs are more likely to rile up local voters once the city approves its latest property tax hike, estimated at the moment to be north of five per cent. Last year, the city publicly blamed the province for nearly half of the 5.79 per cent tax hike right on the tax bill.
There are also uniquely local questions that an election might answer - like whether the next government will upload responsibility for the Red Hill Valley Parkway or commit to setting aside leftover LRT lands for affordable housing.
The Tories - after memorably killing Hamilton's light rail transit project in 2019, only to resurrect it in 2021 with federal Liberal help - recently announced procurement for the long-delayed project is going ahead.
But questions remain about whether parts of about 90 full properties purchased by provincial transit agency Metrolinx along the planned 14-kilometre line will be dedicated to affordable housing - especially since dozens of tenants have been forced to move out of the way of the train.
Matthew Van Dongen is a reporter at The Spectator. mvandongen@thespec.com
CAPTION: Ontario voters head to the polls earlier than expected.Spectator file photo

CREDIT: Matthew Van Dongen Reporter Matthew Van Dongen is a reporter at The Spectator. mvandongen@thespec.com

Jan. 29: Holocaust survey results unconscionable, coalition need to defeat Ford and other letters to the editor

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/jan-29-holocaust-survey-results-unconscionable/docview/3160911018/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Jan.+29%3A+Holocaust+survey+results+unconscionable%2C+coalition+need+to+defeat+Ford+and+other+letters+to+the+editor&amp;title=The+Hamilton+Spectator+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2025-01-29&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Hamilton+Spectator+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Holocaust survey results unconscionable
Re: Survey says more young Canadians believe the history of the Holocaust is exaggerated, Jan. 26
I've not read a sadder headline, article, nor statistics in decades. That fully 18 per cent of Canadians between 18 and 24 "think the Holocaust has been exaggerated" is unconscionable. What a failure of an education system to have produced such a "result."
My Grade 10 civics teacher, Mrs. Legge (in my hometown, Sarnia), taught us of the atrocities. Until her class, I had only heard the term "concentration camps." My childish misunderstanding of the term (foolishly mistaking it as somehow related to orange juice, which also came from "concentrates") was soon shattered when she used the more accurate term: "extermination camps."
She opened our eyes to the harsh realities that today's high-school graduates belittle as "exaggerated." Shame on them. And on their teachers. And shame on any who forget, ignore or belittle actual history. Let us not go down the same slippery slope as our neighbours to the south by rejecting recorded, verifiable facts.
George Olds, Hamilton
Jewish children know the truth
A survey says eight per cent young Canadians between ages 25 to 34 believe the Holocaust has been exaggerated. Jewish children know this not to be true. We have seen numbered tattoos on arms, we have known relatives who watched as their family members were shot in front of their eyes.
Many Jews that suffered through the Holocaust don't discuss it as it's too triggering and upsetting.
It's now 2025 and it seems the most hate crimes are targeting Jewish people. What have we learned? Steven Spielberg directed "Schindler's List" for his children and future generations so they would not forget the atrocities. Perhaps it should be mandatory learning.
Robin Magder, Dundas
We need these stories that inspire
Re: 'A life of purpose': This Hamilton woman cooks through the night every Friday to feed more than a thousand in need, Jan. 27
The Spectator has featured many articles lately that restore faith in humanity in these times of despair for many. I refer specifically to today's feature on the front page about a wonderful lady who cooks food every week for so many people who come to Gore Park every Saturday. She is Anne Thompson and is soon to receive the Order of Hamilton for her selfless work to help those in need.
Last week, there was an article about the Cathedral Cafe on James Street North run by St. Matthews House out of Christ Church Cathedral. Guests can receive breakfast and lunch six days a week and stay out of the cold for the day, 50 people at a time. Most of the staff are volunteers who help with food preparation, serving meals, etc.
Please keep telling these stories about all these grassroots volunteers and organizations in our city that really care about our community. In dark days, these stories are the light that inspire us to keep going and caring about each other.
Colleen Dawson, Hamilton
Students should vote for education
Re: Why doesn't Doug Ford care about funding colleges and universities? Jan. 27
The answer to the question is in the article: "Students, surely worry, but they don't vote in large enough numbers to count."
Maybe more of them should vote. After all, it is in their best interests.
John Valeri, Waterdown
School board has crossed the line
Re: Grand Erie school board sends warning letters to members of public, Jan. 25
So the Grand Erie District School Board (GEDSB) not only feels that it can operate without any public scrutiny, it now feels free to try and shut down any criticism by members of the public as evidenced by recent letters to Messrs. Barrett and Harris. The quote, "Each of us has the privilege of free speech, but not without limits" is particularly concerning coming from an organization responsible for education.
Whether the claims and actions regarding Trustee Carol Ann Sloat are real or warranted is not the issue. Rather, the real concern is GEDSB's feeling that it, an elected body, can act in complete secrecy with no responsibility for transparency to the public, including the media, all by simply claiming confidentiality.
It's time for the provincial government to step in, review and take action, including removal of the entire board if warranted. I eagerly await the board's letter to me.
Wayne Maddever, Burlington
Muscle cars and machismo
Re: Last hurrah for Dodge's V8 muscle trucks? Try this one on for size! Jan. 24
It's great that The Spec continues to publish reviews of supercharged gas vehicles.
I know that some people will complain that they're often noisy, overpowered and highly polluting - but these vehicles are important for males who feel, er, deficient in their own manliness. They help their owners maintain the level of machismo they crave.
Sure, the vehicles contribute excessively to climate change, but what's that compared to being able to disturb unfriendly neighbours at all hours and smothering them in exhaust fumes?
Sadly, in a few years, we will all be buying quiet, emission-free electric vehicles. But there is a silver lining: with EVs, it's all torque and plenty of action.
Harry Shannon, Dundas
Other cities need to pitch in
Re: Hamilton can't shoulder this weight alone, Jan. 25
Bravo to Pete Wiesner! My wife and I have been saying this for years. Why does Hamilton have to take more than its fair share when it comes to social assistance throughout Ontario? What is Burlington, Oakville, Milton, etc. doing? It's time other communities share some of this burden. Whatever party manages to win the upcoming provincial election needs to make a point of forcing more services to communities outside of Hamilton.
Of course, I would say it should also be up to Hamilton's city council to be pushing this agenda, but this may be too much to ask from this mayor and council.
Phil Van Impe, Hamilton
No rebate cheque for retirees
Re: Waiting for your $200 Ontario 'tax rebate'? Here's what to do if it hasn't arrived, Jan. 25
I just read an article about what to do if you're still waiting for your $200 Ontario tax credit. Hmm, John Doe over there, who has been flipping burgers for the last two years, (not that there's anything wrong with flipping burgers, at least it's a job) got his $200 because he was working last year. I've worked for north of 43 years paying my taxes every year, been retired now for some 10 years, still paying income taxes even though I no longer work, yet I don't qualify for the $200 tax rebate because I didn't "work" in 2024.
Now, the $200 won't make me rich, but it might get me a small bag of groceries. There is a point to be made here somewhere I think. So, while Doug Ford tries to figure it out, I'll just wait for my cheque. And wait. And wait. And wait.
Rusty Escott, Dunnville
Nothing is beneath this premier
Re: Snap Ontario election call kicks parties' planning into high gear, Jan. 26
Doug Ford is adopting the dog-whistle approach embodied by the likes of Donald Trump and Pierre Poilievre when he asks whether voters can imagine Bonnie Crombie or Marit Stiles negotiating with Trump - a thinly veiled allusion to gender, perpetuating the tired and false notion that women are weaker and less effective negotiators. While it's tempting to suggest that this is beneath him, Ford's performance while in office has made it plain that nothing is beneath him.
His transparent effort to buy support by sending cheques to Ontario voters at a cost of $3 billion, his political opportunism calling an unnecessary election, his failure to meet his own housing targets, his persistent indifference to homelessness, his failure to restore health care and adequately fund education, his blithe reversal of his commitment to preserve the Greenbelt - clearly there is no bottom.
Dayna Firth, Hamilton
Coalition needed to defeat Ford
Dear Marit, Mike and Bonnie, please do something meaningful this election.
May I suggest a coalition, even temporarily, of your (our) three parties to beat the PCs or, at the very least, hold them to a minority.
The framework might be simple, in fact Ford has assured inadequate time for anything beyond simple. You, our three leaders, get your seats uncontested, by the other two coalition members. Non-PC incumbents retain their seats, again, uncontested. In every other riding, support only the coalition member with the best chance of winning. My guess is all three of you know the polling to the nearest one percentage point in all Ontario ridings.
I think the coalition would be embraced by even our most ardent party faithful; would they rather concede to the PCs by splitting the vote? Only Deceitful Doug would be angry, incensed in fact. coalitions are common in Europe. You (we) could choose our leader after the election; you all seem to have closely aligned views.
Again, please do something different, bold and meaningful for the people this election.
Mark Hagar, Hamilton
He just doesn't get it
Praise to the Spec for the continuous exposures of the totally dysfunctional premier, one Doug Ford. Without restating the list of his never ending mismanagement of this province and taxpayers' money, the only remedy is to vote him out once and for all. He just doesn't get it and will not give Ontario what is needs and deserves.
Get rid of him, now, and let's get someone who works for the people and not him/her self.
Also, Vinay Menon is brilliant and deserves an award.
Robert Panchyson, Burlington
Early election is unnecessary
Like many others, I'm shaking my head over why Doug Ford is calling an election. His party has a clear majority, so anything they want to get passed, could be passed. It's also a huge waste of taxpayers' money, some $15 million that, frankly, we do not have to throw around. It should be spent on new doctors, nurses, houses, infrastructure, etc.
Now Queen's Park will be shut down, nothing gets done for the next month or so. It'd be better if you stayed open for business and do what you are supposed to do: work for the citizens of Ontario!
Hopefully this is going to go very, very badly for Ford if the voters remember the Greenbelt incident; the Ontario Place incident and this unnecessary election.
Jay Miller, Greensville
Wrong stance taken on tariffs
Premier Ford made a serious mistake by calling an election, and responding incorrectly to the tariff threat situation. He said that he needed a majority mandate (which he already has) to combat U.S. President Donald Trump's tariffs.
He suggested tariffs would cost thousands of jobs, but we don't even know this is true since Ford's only saying so. But what if Trump withdraws his tariff threat? Ford's retaliatory approach, such as curbing electricity to the U.S. for instance, has already caused tension with the U.S.
Trump responded with saying we don't need anything they have. Because of Canada's lax border security, illegal fentanyl is entering the U.S., which is why Trump is threatening tariffs against Canada. The premier does not see this. Ford should have responded, simply assuring Trump that he was doing everything he could to secure the border, thereby appeasing both the situation and Trump.
If anything, the premier should cancel the election while he can.
Tina Maini Hamilton
Trump threats unite Canadians
Re: A wake-up call for Canada - and the case for optimism, Jan. 27
Congratulations to Daniel Tisch for his spot-on column. He is right - threats are making Canadians more united.
It is pointless to show weakness to or try to negotiate with a bully. Canada needs to be in full combat mode with this Trumptious knave on the doorstep. Doug Ford is not fighting an election: he is getting ready for war. All Canadians should be pulling the same wagon in this perilous time. Politics and oil are secondary to a show of unity, Canadian autonomy and our national interests.
Have we forgotten history and Hawaii? An independent, sovereign country until U.S. and European business interests deposed a reigning monarch in 1893 (who conceded to avoid bloodshed). There was little resistance when U.S. militia annexed the island in 1898. The U.S. also managed to change the name of the island from a republic to a state. Then there is Guam, Puerto Rico, Samoa, the Virgin Islands, all annexed by the U.S.
Trumps's rhetoric is timely: "Canada (and Greenland and Panama) needs to stand back and stand by. If we don't fight like hell for our country, we won't have a country anymore."
Suzanne Swan, Burlington
Reconsider your U.S. vacation plans
Canada, time to change your travel plans. Why would you go to a country where you are branded an alien and their leader wants to punish us by crushing our economy? If you want some sun, maybe it is time to look at the Caribbean where you can enjoy great beaches, good food and you will be greeted as a friend.
It is time for Americans that appreciate our long-standing friendship to stand up and let their twice impeached, convicted felon, serial liar and con artist of all time know that they don't appreciate his bully tactics.
In the 1930s, the people of Germany and world leaders stood by while another bully almost destroyed our world. Are we going to do that again? If we back down from a bully, they view that as a weakness and they will do you more harm.
As a start, every mayor where we are twinned with a U.S. city should be writing to their counterpart to express the dismay of all Canadians that they would allow this excuse for a human being to destroy all the good work they have done to foster and promote a good relationship with Canadians over many years. Seriously, Canadians, have a look at any plans you have to travel to the U.S.
Robert Gordon Thomas, Waterdown
You can't give in to bullies
Where is Winston Churchill when the world needs him? Churchill knew that capitulating to a bully only feeds their inflated egos and makes they feel invincible. Donald Trump is a coward and a bully who attacks the weak and grows more emboldened with each concession given to him.
China just laughs at Trump because as a dictatorship they too have a powerful military and a huge arsenal of nuclear warheads and, like Russia, are willing to sacrifice their citizens to win at all cost.
The British government needs to warn Trump that Canada is part of the Commonwealth and any form of invasion will be considered an act of war. If the British government doesn't have the courage to tell a want-to-be dictator in no uncertain terms that they will not allow him to, then why does Canada pledge allegiance to King Charles and have taxpayers pay for royal visits?
Jared Harper, Hamilton
Some conditions to become a state
If America wants Canada to even consider becoming the 51st state, we should have conditions that the United States must meet first, before we even consider their request:
Americans have to start singing the American anthem - "The Star-Spangling Banner" - in both French and English.All Americans must start putting vinegar on all French fries immediately.All Americans living in Florida and Arizona must become "snowbirds" and spend at least one entire winter in Inuvik.All Americans must learn to finish this sentence: "The good old hockey game is the best game you can play and ... !"All Americans must learn to spell "Saskatchewan."All Americans must start calling "napkins" by their real name - "serviettes."All Americans must move their Thanksgiving to October as we will not wait till November to eat turkey (this is an absolute deal breaker).
Once Americans have met these prerequisites, we can start talking about our next set of conditions. One is going to be changing the name of Lake Michigan also into Lake Ontario, like North and South Dakota. Until then, Donald Trump can wait at Mar-a-Lago. (Did I mention that we also want the name of Mar-a-Lago changed to "Marmalade"?)
Chris Robertson, Stony Plain, Alta.
A renewed sense of patriotism
I was shocked, stressed and terrified after hearing the threat of tariffs from the American president. I am an Ahmadi Muslim, and love for my country is a part of my faith. I have always loved my country Canada, but I never truly realized the depth of that love until when I heard that Trump wishes for Canada to become the 51st state of America.
As a Canadian, I would never want this to happen. We are willing to make sacrifices for the sake of our country. We can work hard and live with less if it means protecting the dignity of our country. I would like to share the translation of the couplet of an Asian poet: "O bird, death is far more desirable than the food that comes at the cost of freedom"
Khalida Iffat, Hamilton
Bigger isn't always better
I believe the real reason behind President Donald Trump's threat of imposing tariffs on Canada is that our business community and economy are simply more efficient than the bloated, incompetent boards of directors who simply rubber-stamp the decisions of their CEOs.
Bigger is not necessarily better and many American corporations copy the Canadian model by splitting off subsidiaries into independent units. Smaller companies have the advantage of better returns and faster decision-making by decentralized management.
Dan Riga, Burlington
CAPTION: Anne Thompson often uses an office chair when she gets tired while cooking late into the night for the less fortunate. Telling stories like hersinspire us to keep going and caring about each other, a reader says.It is time for Americans that appreciate our longstanding friendship to stand up and let their president know they don't appreciate his bully tactics, a reader says.A Holocaust survivor gets a hug Monday after placing a candle at the Auschwitz-Birkenau former Nazi German concentration camp, during a ceremony marking the 80th anniversary of its liberation, in Oswiecim, Poland. Readers have responded to a story saying many young Canadians"think the Holocaust has been exaggerated."

Document 274 of 500

I get why youth envy the U.S.

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/i-get-why-youth-envy-u-s/docview/3160910873/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=unknown&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=I+get+why+youth+envy+the+U.S.&amp;title=The+Spectator&amp;issn=11899417&amp;date=2025-01-29&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.8&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Spectator&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: No way, no how. That's the message from Canadians to Donald Trump's taunts about becoming the 51st state. At least that's what the opinion polls show, isn't it?
Yes, if you look at the overall findings. Support for joining the U.S. of A. is down around 13 per cent (Leger Marketing) or 10 per cent (Angus Reid Institute). Those are the kind of numbers that support Prime Minister Justin Trudeau's confident statement that "there's not a snowball's chance in hell" of Canada becoming an American state.
Trudeau is right about that, but the top lines in those polls don't show the whole picture.
Ipsos tested the waters just before Trump's inauguration and found a surprisingly large number of younger Canadians (those aged 18 to 34) are prepared to support the notion of becoming the 51st state if a couple of basic conditions are met. More than four in 10 (43 per cent) said they'd vote to join the States if Canadians were offered full American citizenship and had their Canadian assets converted into U.S. dollars. (By comparison, only 17 per cent 55 and older would go along with the idea.) Almost a third of young Canadians think "it's only a matter of time" before Canada and the U.S. merge into one country.
Those are big numbers. I'm not suggesting they show a merger will happen. But the Ipsos data does show a worrisome lack of faith among younger Canadians in the value of this country and its long-term future as an independent concern.
The pollsters didn't ask why they feel this way, but here are a couple of guesses. First, younger Canadians haven't been dealt a good hand, certainly not as good a hand as those older people, who are much more likely to tell Trump to get lost.
Last year I wrote about something called the World Happiness Report, which ranked Canada a respectable 15th out of 143 nations when it comes to overall happiness. But those results concealed an enormous generation gap: older Canadians ranked eighth, while those under 30 were way down at 58th in the world.
Young people trying to get established in this country are grappling with huge issues their parents didn't face - the cost of housing, most obviously, but also the collapse of trust in almost every institution, the toxic impact of social media and the aftershocks of the pandemic. No wonder their faith in the country has been shaken.
Last year this disaffection showed up in a big swing among younger Canadians toward supporting the Conservatives, a sharp break from their traditional pattern of favouring progressive parties. Now it seems to manifest as loss of belief in Canada itself.
The other factor at play is surely the devaluation of the country over the past decade or so by political elites themselves. Many have noted the string of apologies Trudeau offered over the years for historic sins ranging from residential schools to the century-old Komagata Maru incident, when a shipload of Punjabi migrants was turned away.
It's tough finding the balance between recognizing past wrongs and writing off our common history as a shameful litany of wrongdoing. But the academic and intellectual classes have tipped way over toward condemnation in recent years, just when those young Canadians were forming their image of the country.
It's easier for older people with longer memories to put all that in a larger context, where there's also space for pride in a country that overall is a very decent place to live. But for younger people, I expect it's harder to believe in something when it's been publicly rubbished throughout your adult life.
No wonder so many are prepared to give up on the country - if the price is right. That's a problem, even if Trump's threats never become reality.
ANDREW PHILLIPS is a staff columnist with Torstar.

Doug Ford triggers snap provincial election: Who gets your vote, Hamilton?

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/doug-ford-triggers-snap-provincial-election-who/docview/3160910854/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Doug+Ford+triggers+snap+provincial+election%3A+Who+gets+your+vote%2C+Hamilton%3F&amp;title=The+Hamilton+Spectator+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2025-01-29&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Hamilton+Spectator+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Surprise, Hamilton - it's suddenly time to think about who deserves your vote in a snap provincial election triggered by the Tory government more than a year ahead of schedule.
Progressive Conservative leader Doug Ford justified the early election call by saying the government needs a "clear mandate" from voters to spend big in confronting an across-the-board tariff threat from newly elected U.S. President Donald Trump.
Opposition parties like Marit Stiles's NDP, Bonnie Crombie's Liberals and Mike Schreiner's Greens, by contrast, argue spending between $150 million and $175 million on an early election campaign is unnecessary and irresponsible.
Some critics argue Ford wants get ahead of a looming federal vote or potential findings in an ongoing RCMP probe into the government's now-reversed Greenbelt land swap.
There's a chance the early election call could irritate the electorate and backfire on the government - and an even bigger likelihood it will dampen voter turnout, said local political scientist Chris Erl. "People don't like having to go to the polls early; they don't like the idea of a February election," he said.
But there's no denying the impact that threatened tariffs would have on the economy and - if they are implemented as early as this weekend - on the "election narrative," he added, noting Ford's efforts to portray himself as a "Captain Canada" figure.
Either way, the election scramble is on for a vote now slated for Feb. 27, with some parties still working to nominate candidates in local ridings expected to feature competitive races, including Hamilton Centre and Hamilton Mountain.
In the downtown riding, a seat long held by the NDP is suddenly in play now that the NDP has decided not to welcome independent MPP Sarah Jama back to the party after her ouster in 2023 over contentious statements on the Israel-Hamas conflict.
The independent candidate will now face off against a new, yet-to-be-chosen NDP standard-bearer - a battle local New Democrat activists warn could lead to vote splitting in the once-safe orange riding held for years by former party leader Andrea Horwath.
Longtime New Democrat MPP Monique Taylor, meanwhile, has announced she will run as a candidate in the next federal election - meaning there is an open battle for the Hamilton Mountain seat. Taylor has held the seat since 2011, but since the late 1990s, the riding has also elected both Liberal and Tory MPPs.
Aside from the shadow of Trumpian tariffs, there are plenty of issues that could drive voters in this election.
Erl argued the economy, unattainable housing and "general affordability" are top of mind for many voters.
Locally and across the province, hot-button issues, including provincial policy on safe-injection sites, homelessness and tent encampments, as well as a doctor shortage that has left 75,000 city residents with no family physician.
Municipalities, meanwhile, are appealing for provincial help to deal with failing infrastructure, the cost of development and the fact Ontario is the only province in Canada that has downloaded responsibility for social housing on cities.
Those high-level needs are more likely to rile up local voters once the city approves its latest property tax hike, estimated at the moment to be north of five per cent. Last year, the city publicly blamed the province for nearly half of the 5.79 per cent tax hike right on the tax bill.
There are also uniquely local questions that an election might answer - like whether the next government will upload responsibility for the Red Hill Valley Parkway or commit to setting aside leftover LRT lands for affordable housing.
The Tories - after memorably killing Hamilton's light rail transit project in 2019, only to resurrect it in 2021 with federal Liberal help - recently announced procurement for the long-delayed project is going ahead.
But questions remain about whether parts of about 90 full properties purchased by provincial transit agency Metrolinx along the planned 14-kilometre line will be dedicated to affordable housing - especially since dozens of tenants have been forced to move out of the way of the train.
The Spectator will be updating its provincial election page with candidate information as it is made available, so stay tuned. If you have candidate or campaign information to share, please reach out to election editor Matthew Van Dongen (mvandongen@thespec.com).
Matthew Van Dongen is a reporter at The Spectator. mvandongen@thespec.com
CAPTION: Ontario voters will head to the polls more than a year earlier than expected.

Document 276 of 500

Sarah Jama to run for election as independent candidate after rejection by Ontario NDP

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/sarah-jama-run-election-as-independent-candidate/docview/3161369717/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Sarah+Jama+to+run+for+election+as+independent+candidate+after+rejection+by+Ontario+NDP&amp;title=The+Hamilton+Spectator+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2025-01-28&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Hamilton+Spectator+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Sarah Jama says she will run for re-election as an independent MPP in Hamilton Centre now that the New Democrats have decided against welcoming back the activist ousted from the party over controversial comments on the Israel-Hamas war.
If the party runs a candidate against Jama in the snap election campaign beginning Wednesday - which appears to be the plan - it could open the door to vote splitting and a rare multi-party battle for a Hamilton riding long considered a "lock" for the New Democratic Party, said political scientist Chris Erl.
The New Democrats booted the rookie MPP from caucus in late 2023 in the aftermath of a statement Jama posted online about the Israel-Hamas war, but the party signalled last year it was studying whether she could return to the fold.
In an interview, Jama said she tried to do everything possible to "repair the relationship" and rejoin the party - but on Monday, she received an official letter rejecting her NDP nomination package.
That's unfortunate, she said - but it won't stop her from running for re-election.
"My campaign is fuelled by grassroots people looking for a political home," said Jama, who noted she has often voted with the NDP in the legislature but isn't willing to bow to "top-down" party politics. "Anyone else who puts their name forward risks splitting the vote."
In a statement, NDP provincial director Kevin Beaulieu called the vetting process for candidates "confidential," although a letter sent to the local riding association cited reasons for the rejection that included Jama's declaration that she would run as an independent if not welcomed back.
Members of the executive of the local NDP provincial riding association held a news conference Tuesday condemning the decision to deny Jama a shot - and warning about the potential fallout.
"This failure of the party to reconcile (with Jama) has weakened the party throughout the province," said riding association president Tom Baker, who warned the party brass is alienating "diverse perspectives of new activist members" and is risking a loss of support among both volunteers and voters.
Beaulieu said two prospective candidates will vie for the Hamilton Centre NDP nomination at a yet-be-announced meeting.
One of those prospective candidates is Dr. Robin Lennox, a family physician and well-known advocate for harm reduction services.
"My motivation to put my name forward really came from seeing, over the last seven years, how much harm that the ... Conservative government and their policies have been having on my patients," she said. "This is just another way to try to advocate."
Lennox declined to comment on the controversy around Jama's ouster or her rejected effort to rejoin the party. "It's (the voters') job to determine who is their best candidate."
The other prospective candidate is Aisha Jahangir, a mental health nurse at the Hamilton-Wentworth detention centre who previously ran for the party provincially in Etobicoke and federally in Guelph.
Jama, a well-known housing and disability activist who won a byelection in the riding for the NDP in 2023, was booted from caucus just months later in the aftermath of public comments on the Israel-Gaza war.
NDP leader Marit Stiles ultimately blamed Jama's ouster on "a number of unilateral actions that have undermined our collective work and broken the trust of her colleagues."
But since then, prominent party members and some local politicians have publicly called for Jama to be welcomed back under the orange banner, including NDP Hamilton Centre MP Matthew Green and city councillor Cameron Kroetsch.
The internal party rift and potential split in voters, fundraisers and volunteers could open the door to another party's candidate "sneaking up the middle," Erl said, in a riding that hasn't been won by a party other than the NDP since the 1980s.
So far, the only other declared candidates in the riding include former justice of the peace Eileen Walker for the Liberals and community organizer and environmental advocate Lucia Iannantuono for the Greens.
Matthew Van Dongen is a reporter at The Spectator. mvandongen@thespec.com
CAPTION: Sarah Jama says she will run as an independent candidate in Hamilton Centre for the snap provincial election.

Document 277 of 500

Doug Ford triggers snap Ontario election

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/doug-ford-triggers-snap-ontario-election/docview/3161369705/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Doug+Ford+triggers+snap+Ontario+election&amp;title=The+Hamilton+Spectator+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2025-01-28&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Hamilton+Spectator+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: And they're off.
Doug Ford plunged Ontario into a Feb. 27 election on Tuesday, more than a year early in a bid for a third majority government.
Ford met with Lt.-Gov. Edith Dumont to request the dissolution of the legislature, setting the stage for a 29-day campaign officially beginning Wednesday. It will be the first winter provincial vote since 1981.
Ford is rushing Ontarians to the polls amid political instability in Ottawa after Prime Minister Justin Trudeau announced that he would resign and economic uncertainty over U.S. President Donald Trump's threat of 25 per cent tariffs on Canadian goods.
"This is going to be a battle for the next four years, and I want to make sure that I have an make sure I have a strong mandate to outlast President Trump," Ford told reporters at Queen's Park before his ceremonial visit to Dumont.
Doug Ford has strong support as election call looms, survey suggests
The Progressive Conservative leader will formally launch his campaign in Windsor on Wednesday against the backdrop of the Detroit skyline.
That's to underscore that his rationale for the snap vote is to get a renewed mandate to deal with hefty levies on Canadian exports that Trump is threatening to begin this Saturday.
But Ford, who is well ahead of his rivals in public-opinion surveys, has been scrambling for a reason to go early since last May.
NDP Leader Marit Stiles, Liberal Leader Bonnie Crombie and Green Leader Mike Schreiner argue he's opportunistically trying to get in front of potential fallout from the RCMP's ongoing criminal investigation into the $8.28-billion Greenbelt land swap scandal.
As first disclosed by the Star, the Mounties have interviewed past and current Ford staffers as part of their probe, and sources say the detectives' work is continuing during the writ period.
The opposition leaders would prefer Ford focus on the trade war with the U.S. and wait for the scheduled June 2026 vote instead of triggering an "unnecessary election" that will cost between $150 million and $175 million.
"While the people of Ontario are anxious about the grave threat of tariffs, Doug Ford is pursuing his own political gain," said Stiles.
"People need a premier who will fight like hell for every single job that's at risk, not run to the polls over a year early. Doug Ford thinks that he can call an early election to escape his record of failure on housing, health care, education and affordability. We won't let him," she said.
Crombie accused Ford of "wasting $175 million on an early election instead of using it to fix our broken health-care system and make your life more affordable.
"Doug Ford's Ontario has failed you. I'll deliver more for you by guaranteeing you'll have a family doctor, cutting taxes on your paycheque and home heating and building homes you can afford," she said.
Schreiner pointed to Ford's failure to deliver on housing.
"He's had seven years with a majority government to do something about it and the problem has only gotten worse," said the Green leader.
"It's time to kick out this NIMBY government and elect a government that will urgently build the homes we need to address the housing crisis."
Ford's rivals also contend his efforts should also be spent tackling Ontario's doctor shortage instead of electioneering.
But the Tory leader insisted he needs a new mandate because any Trump tariffs could necessitate tens of billions of dollars in stimulus spending to keep the economy afloat and people employed. Stiles, Crombie and Schreiner say they would back any such emergency expenditures.
As chair of the Council of the Federation, Ford has aligned himself with Trudeau, forcefully making the case that Canada must retaliate with counter-tariffs against the U.S.
Labour leaders urge Doug Ford to forgo an election and focus on Trump's tariff threat
His campaign maintains the election timing has to do with that and not the Greenbelt scandal stemming from him granting permission for developers to build on 7,400 acres of environmentally sensitive land. He reversed that decision after damning reports in August 2023 from the auditor general and the integrity commissioner revealed wealthy landowners with ties to the Tories had been "favoured."
Despite his September 2023 U-turn to thwart the development plans, the Mounties swept in the following month and about a dozen of his current and former aides have been interviewed.
Ford, maintaining his government has "nothing to hide" on the Greenbelt, set the table for a snap campaign with voter-friendly $200 "rebate" cheques that are now being mailed out to 15 million Ontario adults and children.
That $3-billion payout is designed to offset Ontarians' affordability concerns at a time of rising prices and unemployment rates creeping up.
He also spent an additional $612 million to expedite the liberalization of booze sales so beer, wine and premixed cocktails could be sold in all supermarkets, corner stores and big box outlets in time for the snap election.
In keeping with his populist bent and a penchant for intervening on matters traditionally the purview of municipalities, Ford announced portions of existing bike lanes on Bloor Street West in Etobicoke and University Avenue and Yonge Street in downtown Toronto would be removed to help alleviate the city's chronic gridlock.
Mindful that the $6.2-million Transportation Tomorrow Survey found 3.1 per cent of Toronto residents commute to work by bicycle each morning - and 3.6 per cent use their bikes "all day" in a city of three million people where 164,806 daily cycling trips are made - the Tories believe curbing some bike lanes could be popular with voters who drive.
Similarly, Ford is closing most supervised drug injection sites by forcing them to locate at least 200 metres away from schools and daycares while boosting provincial supports for addiction treatment as of March 31.
While that change was criticized by some health advocates, who predict more overdoses, it was hailed by some Toronto residents who are fed up with drug dealers.
Among the sites closing is one in Leslieville's South Riverdale Community Health Centre, near where mother-of-two Karolina Huebner-Makurat was killed by a stray bullet in July 2023, a brazen daylight crime that shocked the city.
As the legislature rose Dec. 12 for the winter break, Ford announced new measures to address the homeless tent encampments that have sprung up in parks across Toronto and throughout the province, promising an additional $75.5 million for shelters and affordable housing - and toughening up trespassing laws.
Since he took office almost seven years ago, the housing shortage has increased in fast-growing Ontario and there are now an estimated 81,000 homeless people in the province.
After his Tories won power in 2018, ending an almost 15-year Liberal dynasty under premiers Kathleen Wynne and Dalton McGuinty, Ford was re-elected in 2022 following a global COVID-19 pandemic in which Ontario fared better than any neighbouring province or American state during the health emergency.
Despite his current standing in the polls, not all PC operatives are happy about a snap election.
They are concerned about history repeating itself, recalling that in 1990, popular Liberal premier David Peterson held an election a year early and was punished by voters, losing to Bob Rae's New Democrats in one of the largest upsets in Canadian political history. Peterson is now chair of Torstar, the Star's parent company.
Along with that precedent, some Ford loyalists worry about the "Underhill balance theory," which posits that when Ontarians elect Liberals federally, they tend to choose Tories provincially. Conversely, when they elect federal Tory governments, the Liberals - and, once, in Rae's case, the NDP - usually win provincially at Queen's Park.
With polls suggesting federal Conservative Leader Pierre Poilievre will triumph in a national election expected later this year, the "balance" phenomenon was another consideration in going to provincial voters early.
At dissolution, there were 79 Tories, including Speaker Ted Arnott, who is not seeking re-election, 28 New Democrats, nine Liberals, six Independents and two Greens in the 124-member house.
CAPTION: Premier Doug Ford is joined by Greg Rickford, Minister of Indigenous Affairs and First Nations Economic Reconciliation, and Sonny Gagnon, Chief of Aroland First Nation, to provide remarks and hold a media availability.https://www.youtube.com/channel/UCKvajOhOW6H8Okrs8wSb-bQPremier Doug Ford, wearing a "Canada Is Not For Sale" hat, speaks as he arrives for a first ministers' meeting in Ottawa on Jan. 15, 2025.
