Document 278 of 500

Ontario is short 35,000 family physicians. Are you looking for a family doctor?

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/ontario-is-short-35-000-family-physicians-are-you/docview/3160453071/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Ontario+is+short+35%2C000+family+physicians.+Are+you+looking+for+a+family+doctor%3F&amp;title=The+Hamilton+Spectator+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2025-01-28&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Hamilton+Spectator+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: An estimated 2.5 million Ontario residents don't have a family doctor, according to the Ontario Medical Association (OMA).
The OMA says Ontario needs 3,500 more family doctors to meet current needs. Nearly 40 per cent of current family doctors are considering retirement in the next few years, making the problem even more acute.
On the eve of a snap provincial election expected on Feb. 27, parties are making announcements and promises to address the issue.
On Monday, Jan. 27 the Progressive Conservative government announced $1.4 billion in new funding and $400 million in previously approved primary care funding to connect every Ontario resident to a family doctor within four years.
The Tory government plans to create and expand 305 additional family health teams to connect two million people to primary care. Funding also includes more than $235 million in 2025-26 to establish and expand 80 additional primary care teams provincewide, connecting 300,000 more people to primary care this year.
While the OMA welcomed the announcement, the advocacy group warned the number of Ontarians who lack access to a family doctor could grow to 4.4 million by next year without urgent action.
If elected, Bonnie Crombie and the Ontario Liberal Party has pledged to guarantee a family doctor "close to home" for every Ontario resident by the end of the party's first term in office.
The Liberals have promised $3.1 billion to attract, recruit, retain, and integrate 3,100 family doctors by 2029.
The Liberals also intend to create two new medical schools and double the number of medical school spots and residency positions.
The party hopes to incentivize family doctors to serve in rural and northern communities, enhance virtual care and reduce the time medical professionals spend filing paperwork.
Ontario NDP leader Marit Stiles has vowed to "unlock" health care by building integrated teams around doctors to help free up their time.
The party has estimated family doctors are stuck spending 40 per cent of their time on paperwork instead of seeing patients.
In a news release earlier this month, Stiles said emergency rooms have been shuttered, and for-profit health care has exploded under the leadership of Doug Ford.
Are you struggling to find a family doctor? Have your say in our reader survey.
Disclaimer: Reader survey results are not scientific. As the informal findings of a survey presented to the readers of this site, they reflect the opinions of those who have chosen to participate. The survey is available online to anyone interested in taking it. This poll does not restrict the number of votes each person can cast.
CAPTION: Hundreds of people lined up outside the Walkerton Legion Hall on a cold and snowy day recently to sign up for a new family doctor.

Document 279 of 500

Ontario $200 taxpayer rebate: What are you doing with your cheque?

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/ontario-200-taxpayer-rebate-what-are-you-doing/docview/3160452566/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Ontario+%24200+taxpayer+rebate%3A+What+are+you+doing+with+your+cheque%3F&amp;title=The+Hamilton+Spectator+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2025-01-28&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Hamilton+Spectator+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: The Ontario taxpayer rebate has started to land in mailboxes across Ontario.
Cheques are being issued and they are expected to be delivered by late January or early February.
Track your Ontario taxpayer rebate
Ontarians can monitor the status of their cheque online. To check the status you'll need an account login, the last four digits of your Social Insurance Number, your date of birth and the postal code of the mailing address you used in your Canada Revenue Agency (CRA) tax return.
Check the status of your cheque on the government's website.
What will you do with your cheque?
The Ontario taxpayer $200 rebate cheques are in the mail. Are you spending it, donating or saving it? Let us know by taking our reader survey.
Who is eligible for the rebate and how much is it?
The cheques are being mailed to all Ontario residents who are eligible for the taxpayer rebate. To qualify for the rebate, the province says you must have been 18 years or older and a resident in Ontario on Dec. 31, 2023, not be bankrupt or incarcerated in 2024, and have filed a 2023 income tax return by Dec. 31, 2024.
Families who qualify for the Canada Child Benefit (CCB) payment will also receive a separate cheque totalling $200 for each eligible child under age 18. For example, an eligible family of five with two adults and three children will receive $1,000.
The province says for instances where a shared custody arrangement is in effect, payments will be split based on the most recent CCB information available.
What to do if you have issues with your rebate cheque
If you have issues after cheques go out, the province advises you call Service Ontario (1-800-267-8097). You can also visit Ontario.ca/TaxpayerRebate for more information.
CAPTION: Premier Doug Ford has delivered on his promise to send $200 cheques to eligible Ontarians.

Document 280 of 500

It's time to tell Ford the truth

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/time-tell-ford-truth/docview/3160304424/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=unknown&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=It%27s+time+to+tell+Ford+the+truth&amp;title=The+Spectator&amp;issn=11899417&amp;date=2025-01-28&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.9&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Spectator&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Premier Doug Ford has confirmed he will call an election Wednesday.
Ever since the rebate announcement, speculation has swirled on whether an election would soon follow. Why else gift the electorate with rebate cheques the treasury can't afford? Expect those cheques any day.
Or maybe it's to circumvent the results of the RCMP investigation that is taking far too long to complete? Ford's cosiness with the cops casts doubt on the whole inquiry - the delay in "finding their man" in whoever ends up taking the fall is troubling.
We just need to remember that in the Greenbelt scandal, the rot started at the top, not the bottom, with lax oversight and poor communications processes that could lead to plausible deniability of any wrongdoing. Surprise, surprise, surprise.
A snap election, in the dead of winter, when the governing party already has a clear majority to enact legislation is a blatant attempt to play the system, and the electorate, for four more years for no good reason except power. Not service, but power.
The threat to Canadian trade via Donald Trump's tariffs is a national issue and best left to the feds to take leadership on. The government is working, ministers are doing their jobs, the civil service is chugging along, and the provinces, well, most of them, are behind Team Canada. Trump is a threat to Canadian autonomy and our response is a national one. Canada-U.S. relations are tricky, and regional, and go to the roots of who we are.
Doug Ford leaping to be the face of Team Am-Can is his attempt to bolster support for his tough-on-tariffs messaging and doesn't hide the fact that Ford has declared support and admiration for Trump for years. That in itself is troubling enough, but now Ford is going to be tough on him? What's on the table and what can Ontarians expect to lose with Ford's love affair of all things American?
Ford has divided loyalties. He may be Team Am-Can when it comes to threats to Ontario's trade economy, but he's Team America in the bigger picture. I mean Am-Can? Come on. Can-Am rolls off the tongue far more easily. Where is our national self-esteem? We are an energy and resources rich country. Why are we buying anything off of anyone?
Oh yeah, I remember, we closed our manufacturing plants and sold out our workers by allowing corporations to move overseas or down south. That's a different piece for another time.
Rather than support a Tory Blue version of Ontario conservatism with ties to tradition, a touchstone of conservative ideology, the Ford government espouses a red, white and blue Tory version. Ford is happy to Americanize our institutions, privatize our health care, charter our public schools, commercialize our housing and recreation spaces and abandon founding ideals of Christian charity by legislating those in need to abject poverty. Canada, well, Ontario at least, is most certainly for sale under Ford.
The Ford government has created so many problems for families here in Ontario. So many problems. Families with special needs are still struggling, families on social assistance are still struggling, families in need of health care are still struggling. Instead, he's distracting us with nonsense about getting a mandate to fight Trump tariffs.
I don't know what makes me more frustrated. Ford's blatant attempt at gaming the system or the ignorance of the people falling for it.
When I hear stories of people living in areas underserved by our excellent health-care workers, I get mad at the Ford government and its Americanized ways. We are a country that educates the world, yet we don't have enough health-care workers to serve our population.
But we do have enough billions to spend on a mega spa and break legally binding contracts for more beer.
Don't sell out Ontarians for a cheap $200 bribe and accessible booze. Doug Ford and his government have been terrible for Ontario families. Ontarians need to tell him so.
Margaret Shkimba is a Hamilton writer. For more, visit margaretshkimba.Substack.com. You can follow her on X (@menrvasofia) or friend her on Facebook.
CAPTION: Doug Ford in Hamilton while on the 2022 campaign trail. A snap election, in the dead of winter, when the governing party already has a majority is a blatant attempt to play the system, and the electorate, Margaret Shkimba writes.Barry Gray The Hamilton Spectator file photo

CREDIT: Margaret Shkimba OPINION Margaret Shkimba is a Hamilton writer. For more, visit margaretshkimba.Substack.com. You can follow her on X (@menrvasofia) or friend her on Facebook.;

national briefs: Pedneault to co-lead Greens

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/national-briefs/docview/3160304270/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=national+briefs%3A+Pedneault+to+co-lead+Greens&amp;title=The+Spectator&amp;issn=11899417&amp;date=2025-01-28&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.5&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Spectator&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Canada
Freeland pitches tariff response
Liberal leadership candidate Chrystia Freeland said Monday that Canada can avoid massive U.S. tariffs on Canadian goods by scaring the Americans away from the idea. In a media statement, the former finance minister called on the federal government to release a list of $200 billion in retaliatory tariffs to prepare for U.S. President Donald Trump to make good on his threat to impose 25 per cent tariffs on Canadian imports. She said Ottawa must immediately consult with industry and put together a detailed, dollar-for-dollar retaliation plan.
Pedneault to
co-lead Greens
Jonathan Pedneault says he intends to return as co-leader of the Green Party of Canada. He says that the party has asked him to return to co-leadership with Elizabeth May after he stepped back from the role in July. Pedneault, who has worked for both Amnesty International and Human Rights Watch, says he chose to quit as co-leader after getting ''life altering news'' about his health. His situation has now improved, he says.
Ontario
Airport plan dropped
After more than a half century in limbo, the federal government has abandoned plans for an airport at a site in Pickering and will instead look to transfer land to Parks Canada.Transport Minister Anita Anand said a new airport was not the best use of the area and the government intends to transfer the "high conservation value lands" to the agency that manages national parklands. "We will soon begin public consultations to determine future uses, taking into account the region's greatest needs," she said.
Wire services

Ford government takes next step to remove Toronto bike lanes

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/ford-government-takes-next-step-remove-toronto/docview/3160304000/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Ford+government+takes+next+step+to+remove+Toronto+bike+lanes&amp;title=The+Hamilton+Spectator+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2025-01-28&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Hamilton+Spectator+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Premier Doug Ford is pedalling furiously toward removing bike lanes from "three of Toronto's most congested streets."
With an election campaign set to officially kick off Wednesday, Ford's Progressive Conservative government "has retained an engineering company to complete design work, as it prepares to reinstate vehicle lanes on Bloor Street West, Yonge Street and University Avenue," the province said.
"Our government has heard loud and clear from residents and businesses about the problems with gridlock. The failed approach of installing bike lanes without a second thought for drivers or local businesses is not working," said Transportation Minister Prabmeet Sarkaria.
"By freeing up some of Toronto's most important roads, we're bringing common sense back to municipal planning," Sarkaria said in one of his final acts before the government enters "caretaker mode" through the Feb. 27 election.
It is unclear when the bike lanes would actually be taken out.
Under Sarkaria's Bill 212, the Reducing Gridlock, Saving You Time Act, which received royal assent last month, Queen's Park now has the power to remove existing bike lanes and veto new ones the province feels would contribute to traffic jams.
The City of Toronto, which opposed the law, has estimated it would cost $48 million to dismantle $27 million in bike lane infrastructure, a claim Ford has dismissed as "hogwash."
"We're going to show them how to do it for a lot less and get traffic moving. We're going to keep bike riders safe, simple as that," he said in November.
Provincial taxpayers will foot the tab for taking out the lanes.
"One per cent of the population cannot take up 50 per cent of the roads, " Ford said in December when the bill passed, understating the number of Torontonians who cycle and overstating the amount of space bike lanes take up in the city.
The $6.2-million Transportation Tomorrow Survey found 3.1 per cent of Toronto residents commute to work by bicycle each morning, triple the number the premier often cites.
That jumps to 3.6 per cent of those who reported using their bikes "all day" in a city of three million people where 164,806 daily cycling trips were made.
However, that's far lower than some cycling advocates have argued in opposing the removal of bike lanes.
In comparison, 46 per cent of Torontonians drove their own cars or trucks in the morning with another 11 per cent being passengers in private vehicles and 25 per cent took public transit while 13 per cent walked.
The numbers change slightly when people were surveyed about "all day" modes of transportation: 50 per cent drove, 12 per cent were passengers, 22 per cent took transit, and 10 per cent walked.
Co-ordinated by the Ministry of Transportation in partnership with 24 municipalities and transit agencies, including the City of Toronto, the TTC and Metrolinx, the exhaustive survey is normally conducted every five years and involves thousands of interviews.
Sarkaria's bill indemnifies the province against any lawsuits from people or their families from suing if someone is killed or injured on a road where a bike lane is taken out.
While the new law affects cities across Ontario, Ford has acknowledged the most vexing bike lane to him is on the western stretch of Bloor West near his Etobicoke home.
NDP Leader Marit Stiles, Liberal Leader Bonnie Crombie and Green Leader Mike Schreiner opposed his bill.
CAPTION: A bike lane on University Avenue in Toronto is shown on Nov. 8, 2024.

Subject: Bicycling

Title: Ford government takes next step to remove Toronto bike lanes

Document 283 of 500

Jan. 28: Ford has lost our confidence, system too soft on car thieves and other letters to the editor

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/jan-28-ford-has-lost-our-confidence-system-too/docview/3160303953/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Jan.+28%3A+Ford+has+lost+our+confidence%2C+system+too+soft+on+car+thieves+and+other+letters+to+the+editor&amp;title=The+Hamilton+Spectator+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2025-01-28&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Hamilton+Spectator+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Ford has lost our confidence
So Premier Doug Ford says he needs a mandate to deal with Donald Trump. He still has close to 16 months to deal with Trump. He thinks that if he can get an election now and win, he will have four more years to waste Ontarians' money rather than just 16 months.
Wake up Ontario, especially the vast majority who didn't vote in the last election. Every eligible voter needs to get out and vote and give him and all the Conservative members of the legislature who don't serve their constituents the boot. Maybe another party - one that doesn't try to line their friends' pockets by trying to destroy the Greenbelt or build tunnels under the 401 - will spend the money on things we need, like heath, education and real affordable housing.
Garry Condon, Burlington
Take advantage of this opportunity
Premier Ford may inadvertently have the public interest at heart. He has proven how he favours the wealthy and powerful, like land developers. Think Greenbelt, Ontario Science Centre, Ontario Place, etc. He would cave to President Donald Trump.
An early election can free us from his weakness in the face of the powerful, and allow us to elect a strong, for-the-people leader. Either of our two strong female leadership alternatives would not only favour all of our province's people, but have Trump shaking in his boots. Trump does not seem to do well against a female whom he cannot dominate.
But, let's be careful in choosing our new provincial leader. We cannot split the vote and leave Ford in a minority government position.
Gary Caldwell, Lynden
We need a new electoral system
Doug Ford has found his excuse: Donald Trump's tariffs. He's using that excuse to force us to an early election and forcing us into giving him a bigger mandate. This, despite the leaders of the three opposition parties having offered to work with him to present a united front to Trump.
Why is Ford in such a rush? Maybe it has more to do with trying to outrun the RCMP investigation of the Greenbelt and get re-elected before animosity toward a Pierre Poilievre government spills over to the provincial Conservatives.
If we had proportional representation, where the number of seats reflects the proportion of the popular vote, Ford would currently have 41 per cent of the seats instead of 67 per cent of the seats and 100 per cent of the power. He would not be able to call an early election without the support of some of the parties that represent the other 59 per cent of Ontarians. He might even have to accept their offers to work together in a common front.
Which would be better for Ontario - an unnecessary early election or our leaders working together to stand up to Trump? We need proportional representation!
Heather Yoell, Dundas
Trump being used as a distraction
With Donald Trump acting like a god to all Americans and threatening to slap tariffs on Canada, Doug Ford has decided he is our saviour. The "two peas in the pod" are just that!
Why a snap election now? He has well over a year with a majority government to get the job done. He claims he needs a strong mandate to move forward with a plan to save our economy. Believe him or not, you do need to ask yourself, why now?
Our country is in a transition period and a soon to be prime minister will be appointed. Ford is taking advantage of this distraction and using Trump as a scare tactic, all to remain in power.
Marlene Girvan, Ancaster
Ulterior motive for election call?
Why does Doug Ford need to call a snap election when he already has his "mandate?" Is there perhaps a very big scandal looming that he needs us to distract from? How does he justify spending money we don't have?
Jackie Oke, Dundas
Council has lost its way
Re: Hamilton proposed budget hike lowered to 5.7 per cent as councillors look for more cuts, Jan. 24
There have been 746 new hires at city hall over the last two years, costing us $75 million a year. It seems we are living in a world of wants, not what we must have at city hall. We have an overall budget this year of $2.8 billion. And we can't find anything more to cut.
If this keeps up, by the time the next election rolls around in 2026, we will have hired another 400 or 500 full-time staff. And we will still be spending millions of dollars on the homeless without reducing the numbers. What a travesty.
We will have miles of bike lanes that are not being used. We will have two-way traffic on Main Street creating traffic chaos. We will have empty buses running around the city on some routes. We will still have many streets with potholes not fixed. We will still have hundreds of city homes not being lived in because they need extensive repair. We will be spending millions on climate change to do exactly what?
This council has lost its way.
Don Ross, Hamilton
Staff needed to improve services
Re: With city staffing costs poised to soar past $1 billion, are the taxpayers getting their money's worth? Jan. 22
I maintain that we are, finally, beginning to get our money's worth. At last we have a majority on council who don't simply sit back complaining about broken city services on one hand while voting to underfund and understaff those services on the other hand.
If city services are ever to improve, hiring competent individuals to deliver them is a no-brainer. Ever waited 30 minutes for a call centre agent to answer or lined up for hours at ServiceOntario? We all know what happens when an organization lacks staff and the Corporation of the City of Hamilton is no different.
As for the minority of councillors who continually vote against increasing staffing, who propose facile and simplistic "hiring freezes" and who pick a number from the ether and expect staff to build a budget around it, the word "lazy" comes to mind.
Jordan Jameson, Stoney Creek
Focus on the bigger picture
Re: Amid public scrutiny, council calls for review of city's microshelters purchase, Jan. 24
I agree that it's absolutely important to ask questions when it comes to public projects, and supporting our local businesses should be the first "go-to." However, these questions shouldn't overshadow the very real and urgent need for housing the homeless, as soon as possible. I feel like the focus on where the tiny shelters are coming from, or the issues surrounding suppliers, are distracting from the fact that these tiny shelters will provide a safe, warm space for people who are struggling to survive on the streets of our city.
It's easy for those of us who are housed to scrutinize the details, but the priority should be making sure these tiny shelters get to people who need them, as quickly as possible.
Natalie Marino, Hamilton
Shelter costs seem overly high
Once again the huts (or is that micro-shelters) are being discussed by our "un"cost conscious city council. The talks cover many areas of the situation, but one area seems to be glossed over by the council, media and concerned citizens. That is the annual operating cost for the 40 huts.
In today's Spectator a figure of around $4 million is mentioned. One doesn't need to be a mathematician to figure that is $100,000 per hut.
Now, I notice the hut residents are to be provided with air conditioning (that many hardworking taxpayers cannot afford), but I am at a loss to understand how it can cost $100,000 per unit to support these homeless residents.
Terry Butlin, Hamilton
Put vacant office space to use
There are lots of opportunities for sheltering the homeless in downtown Hamilton. The office building where I used to work has many empty floors whose offices could be converted to dormitories without too much trouble. Washrooms (and even kitchen facilities) are already in place. There need to be rules, of course, and services and volunteers need to be made available. It's really not that complicated if all parties join together to make such a solution happen. Furthermore, giving people something to do, helping them find a purpose, needs to be part of the equation.
Renate Manthei, Hamilton
Years later we're still waiting
Re: Jamesville tribunal showdown put off for settlement talks, Jan. 23
We should always be concerned when we read that a politician is "cautiously optimistic" about anything, but especially when they are talking about a development project and timelines.
In 2019, there was great fanfare about a plan to redevelop this property. Now we are asked to "wait a bit longer." I guess the good news is the talks continue and the lawyers keep getting paid on all sides.
Joe Benedetti, Hamilton
System too soft on car thieves
Re: Car thieves getting off easy? Believe me, we have bigger problems in our court system, Jan. 23
First of all, I don't believe auto thefts have recently "plummeted." I am constantly running into people who have been affected by their vehicles being stolen. And when I say "affected," I mean a serious disruption to their financial, mental and physical situations, not to mention insurance issues.
Car thefts have become increasingly violent, both by home break-ins and people being accosted in broad daylight with weapons. These criminals are being treated too lightly and if underage they don't even get identified.
The problem we have is that a criminal could be caught completely in the act of stealing or possessing a stolen car and even then, their defence lawyer would have them plead innocent. There is no downside to a guilty person pleading innocent.
Without video of a crime in action it is difficult to prove guilt, so the onus is on the prosecution.
Even when proven guilty, there are many "outs" for the criminal to get a light or no sentence, such as their upbringing, bad parents, stress from their youth, race, immigration issues, mental sickness, etc.
Defence attorneys are good at their job. They must be as far too many people nabbed by the police are back on the road doing the very same thing.
It is time to help the police put criminals away for a sentence that fits the crime. And give victims a break from this cycle of criminals out on leave doing what they do best, stealing.
The commentary ends with, "we cannot expect the public to retain confidence in our justice system for the sake of convicting a few more car thieves." Never mind retain, what makes you think we presently have confidence in the justice system?
Andy Price, Ancaster
Trump taking on the whole world
What the heck? Has Donald Trump just released the kraken? Has our neighbour, all twisted and curled, just unleashed a tentacular assault on the whole world?
So if it's not made in the U.S.A., we are going to punish you into submission through tariffs and unrealistic economic demands. Screw all of you, our country doesn't need anything from anyone, we have it all.
The richest country in the world threatening every other country with economic war.
Billionaires will flock to U.S. to reap the rewards Trump is offering: low or no taxes (depending on how closely related to him they are), lack of regulations on environmental or any aspect of the industry to increase the billionaires' salaries and highlight Trump's supposed business acumen.
Once Trump has all the major corporations and the world's billionaires invested, including those from China, Russia and other not-so-friendly countries, we will all be beholden to Trump, the "God endorsed" felon.
Maybe we had better get on the good side with China. It might be a whole lot easier to trade with them than trade with the U.S.
Paul McFarlane, Hamilton
Ask a woman for 'the truth of it'
Re: From dog whistles to blaring horns, Poilievre makes his case, Jan. 24
I cannot read Noah Fry's essay without commenting on his remarks regarding "barbaric cultural practices" that referred to comments made in the Harper government with the issues of female circumcision. If you want the truth of it, ask a woman who has endured it to explain it to you. There wouldn't be a feminist in the country who would argue for it, given that it is of benefit to men but is a pain-fuelled, lifelong condition for women.
Before we apply assumed knowledge to politics, we need to hear from those it affects most.
Judy Pollard Smith, Hamilton
CAPTION: "I am constantly running into people who have been affected by their vehicles being stolen. Document 284 of 500

CEO says Canada Post faces insolvency: Union says firm has made inaccurate projections in past

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/ceo-says-canada-post-faces-insolvency/docview/3160302957/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=CEO+says+Canada+Post+faces+insolvency%3A+Union+says+firm+has+made+inaccurate+projections+in+past&amp;title=The+Spectator&amp;issn=11899417&amp;date=2025-01-28&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.10&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Spectator&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Just days after Canada Post parted ways with their chief financial officer and two other senior executives, the company's leaders sounded the alarm on their financial situation in the first public hearing before the federal Industrial Inquiry Commission (IIC).
Doug Ettinger, the company's president and CEO, described the current situation as an "edge-of-the-cliff juncture." The Crown corporation is losing ground to its various competitors, he said, leaving them in financial dire straits in the aftermath of the five-week national postal strike.
"We are literally driving a 1967 Ford in a Formula One race in 2025," Ettinger said of the company's decline in letter and parcel volume. "This is simply - and I'll underline this - not sustainable and we are facing insolvency as a result."
The commission, appointed by Federal Labour Minister Steven MacKinnon, will have until May to probe potential ways to reach a new contract agreement between the country's national postal service and the 55,000-worker union.
Until then, the strike, which began on Nov. 15, will be on pause and workers and management will operate under the terms of the existing contract, which expired more than a year ago.
In remarks before the Ottawa-based commission, newly installed CFO Rindala El-Hage said that the company was projected to "run out of cash" by the end of July 2025 and that its losses are expected to increase, a projection that didn't include the strike. Last week, the federal government extended a $1.034 billion loan to Canada Post to "maintain its solvency and ensure it can continue its operations."
"The status quo is not an option," El-Hage said.
In its response, the Canadian Union of Postal Workers (CUPW) accused Canada Post of union-busting and said that the company is "trying to use its financial struggles as a pretext to implement drastic service cutbacks."
"In the past, the corporation has made inaccurate financial projections and misrepresented its financial condition to justify service cuts and demand concessions from the union in bargaining," CUPW president Jan Simpson said before the commission.
Ettinger said that the federal government's loan serves only as a stopgap.
"It is a temporary loan to ensure we have the money on hand to keep the lights on, continue operating and pay everyone's wages," Ettinger said. "This is clearly not a long-term fix. It is a liability we are expected to pay back. It just buys us time."
The commission, which is being led by mediator and arbitrator William Kaplan, will have subsequent hearings in February and March before presenting a report to the labour minister by May 15.
Throughout the five-hour hearing, Kaplan pressed both sides on their plans to resolve the labour dispute and secure longer term financial viability for the postal service.
With files from Josh Rubin and The Canadian Press
CAPTION:

CREDIT: Hayden Godfrey Toronto Star With files from Josh Rubin and The Canadian Press

Document 285 of 500

It's time to tell Doug Ford the truth

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/time-tell-doug-ford-truth/docview/3160302938/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=It%27s+time+to+tell+Doug+Ford+the+truth&amp;title=The+Hamilton+Spectator+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2025-01-28&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Hamilton+Spectator+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Premier Doug Ford has confirmed he will call an election Wednesday.
Ever since the rebate announcement, speculation has swirled on whether an election would soon follow. Why else gift the electorate with rebate cheques the treasury can't afford? Expect those cheques any day.
Or maybe it's to circumvent the results of the RCMP investigation that is taking far too long to complete? Ford's cosiness with the cops casts doubt on the whole inquiry - the delay in "finding their man" in whoever ends up taking the fall is troubling.
We just need to remember that in the Greenbelt scandal, the rot started at the top, not the bottom, with lax oversight and poor communications processes that could lead to plausible deniability of any wrongdoing. Surprise, surprise, surprise.
A snap election, in the dead of winter, when the governing party already has a clear majority to enact legislation is a blatant attempt to play the system, and the electorate, for four more years for no good reason except power. Not service, but power.
The threat to Canadian trade via Donald Trump's tariffs is a national issue and best left to the feds to take leadership on. The government is working, ministers are doing their jobs, the civil service is chugging along, and the provinces, well, most of them, are behind Team Canada. Trump is a threat to Canadian autonomy and our response is a national one. Canada-U.S. relations are tricky, and regional, and go to the roots of who we are.
Doug Ford leaping to be the face of Team Am-Can is his attempt to bolster support for his tough-on-tariffs messaging and doesn't hide the fact that Ford has declared support and admiration for Trump for years. That in itself is troubling enough, but now Ford is going to be tough on him? What's on the table and what can Ontarians expect to lose with Ford's love affair of all things American?
Ford has divided loyalties. He may be Team Am-Can when it comes to threats to Ontario's trade economy, but he's Team America in the bigger picture. I mean Am-Can? Come on. Can-Am rolls off the tongue far more easily. Where is our national self-esteem? We are an energy and resources rich country. Why are we buying anything off of anyone?
Oh yeah, I remember, we closed our manufacturing plants and sold out our workers by allowing corporations to move overseas or down south. That's a different piece for another time.
Rather than support a Tory Blue version of Ontario conservatism with ties to tradition, a touchstone of conservative ideology, the Ford government espouses a red, white and blue Tory version. Ford is happy to Americanize our institutions, privatize our health care, charter our public schools, commercialize our housing and recreation spaces and abandon founding ideals of Christian charity by legislating those in need to abject poverty. Canada, well, Ontario at least, is most certainly for sale under Ford.
The Ford government has created so many problems for families here in Ontario. So many problems. Families with special needs are still struggling, families on social assistance are still struggling, families in need of health care are still struggling. Instead, he's distracting us with nonsense about getting a mandate to fight Trump tariffs.
I don't know what makes me more frustrated. Ford's blatant attempt at gaming the system or the ignorance of the people falling for it.
When I hear stories of people living in areas underserved by our excellent health-care workers, I get mad at the Ford government and its Americanized ways. We are a country that educates the world, yet we don't have enough health-care workers to serve our population.
But we do have enough billions to spend on a mega spa and break legally binding contracts for more beer.
Don't sell out Ontarians for a cheap $200 bribe and accessible booze. Doug Ford and his government have been terrible for Ontario families. Ontarians need to tell him so.
CAPTION: Doug Ford at the Heddle Shipyards in Hamilton in 2022 while on the campaign trail. A snap election, in the dead of winter, when the governing party already has a majority is a blatant attempt to play the system, and the electorate, Margaret Shkimba writes.

Document 286 of 500

Ford's decision to expand alcohol sales will cost the province $612M

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/fords-decision-expand-alcohol-sales-will-cost/docview/3160302880/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Ford%27s+decision+to+expand+alcohol+sales+will+cost+the+province+%24612M&amp;title=The+Spectator&amp;issn=11899417&amp;date=2025-01-28&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.5&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Spectator&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Premier Doug Ford's decision to speed up the rollout of alcohol sales in corner stores - which first sparked early election speculation last spring - will cost the province more than $600 million, Ontario's budget watchdog said Monday.
That's nearly three times the amount the Progressive Conservative government said it would cost to accelerate the timeline.
The Financial Accountability Officer wrote in a report Monday that the expansion of beer, wine and coolers to convenience stores, big box stores and more grocery stores will cost $1.4 billion through to 2030, and $612 million of that is due to the sped-up timing.
However, that could be much higher or much lower depending on rates of alcohol consumption and consumer behaviour, Jeffrey Novak wrote in his report.
"The actual financial cost to the province will depend on how retailers and consumers respond to the expansion of the beverage alcohol marketplace," he wrote.
"After accounting for these and other factors, the FAO estimates that the financial cost to the province could range from $529 million to $1.9 billion through to Dec. 31, 2030."
Ford's previous plan was to expand sales of those alcoholic offerings by 2026, but in May he announced that would instead happen in 2024.
Liberal Leader Bonnie Crombie said it shows Ford has the wrong priorities.
"Doug Ford's priorities are clear, and they're not about serving you," she said. "The $1.9 billion, it could have been used to hire 1,400 family doctors -1,400 - and that would have been able to care for two million people."
The Canadian Press
CAPTION: Doug Ford's previous plan was to expand sales of alcohol by 2026, but in May he announced that would instead happen in 2024.The Canadian Press

Document 287 of 500

Poilievre facing stiff headwinds

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/poilievre-facing-stiff-headwinds/docview/3160302794/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=unknown&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Poilievre+facing+stiff+headwinds&amp;title=The+Spectator&amp;issn=11899417&amp;date=2025-01-28&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.8&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Spectator&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: It's starting to become clear why Pierre Poilievre was so keen to have an election last fall.
Not only is Donald Trump raining on the Conservative leader's parade, but now Premier Doug Ford will be beating Poilievre to the campaign trail with a Feb. 27 provincial election expected.
Poilievre is going to be hoping Ontarians are in such an anti-Liberal mood that they will be happy to vote conservative twice this year. A federal election campaign is widely expected to be launched not long after Parliament resumes at the end of March. Ford has long signalled he wanted a provincial election before that federal vote - and Poilievre is one of the reasons.
Back last June, when the Toronto Star first reported a 2025 election was on the horizon for Ontario, the reasons cited included the damage the federal Conservatives could do to Ford and their provincial counterparts. Ford and his team were worried a Poilievre victory would usher in slashed spending, reduced transfer payments to the provinces and a scrapping of the electric vehicle strategy Ford has been aggressively pursuing in concert with Justin Trudeau and his Liberals.
Trump is wreaking chaos on that last score with his own rollback of electric vehicle incentives in the U.S., but the more Ontarians absorb what that's doing to the huge auto industry in the province, the less they may like a federal Conservative leader headed down the same path.
There isn't a strict overlap between federal Conservatives and Ontario's Progressive Conservatives. Indeed, many of the same people who have voted for Ford have also voted for Trudeau's Liberals. The lines get a little more direct when it comes to volunteers and resources. Enthusiastic partisans, willing to sacrifice time and energy for a political cause, are precious to all political parties. Ford will be tapping into the same network that Poilievre is hoping to mobilize whenever Canadians do go to the polls.
Maybe it will be good practice for the conservative troops on the ground in Ontario. They'll get a chance to see what's working at the doorstep. The same will be true, too, for Ontario Liberals and New Democrats.
Ford has been asked whether he's trying to get around this quirk of the federation, in which Ontarians seem to be content with having one party in power federally and the opposite provincially. That phenomenon has been the reality for 70 of the past 81 years. The premier says that's not it; he's just looking for a mandate to take on Trump.
Trump, though, is another problem for Poilievre. It's early, but a new poll from Ekos suggests the Liberals may be benefiting from what is happening south of the border. In what Ekos is calling a "dramatic recovery," its latest polling shows the Liberals back in the realm of 30 per cent popularity, for the first time since 2022.
Abacus Data CEO David Coletto has also been thinking Poilievre may have a Trump problem. The U.S. president is not gaining any friends in Canada with all his belligerent talk and threats, but any Conservative leader here has to tread a fine line.
"He cannot appear too accommodating of Trump's threats, lest he alienate voters who view Trump as a destabilizing force, yet he cannot seem overly belligerent toward the new administration, given the need to safeguard cross-border economic ties and satisfy a sizable portion of his own base who likes Trump," Coletto wrote.
Meanwhile, the two campaigns that Poilievre intended to wage last fall, against the "carbon tax" and Justin Trudeau, are fizzling. The two leading contenders for the Liberal leadership, Chrystia Freeland and Mark Carney, have already said they are backing off on the consumer price on carbon.
The anti-Trudeau campaign now lacks a villain, given Trudeau's looming resignation. In politics, a week is a long time. By the time Poilievre goes to the polls, Trudeau will have been gone for many weeks.
Poilievre was definitely in majority territory if an election had been held last fall. Thanks to Trump, Trudeau and not least of all Ford, the fates of 2025 don't seem to be aligning on the Conservative leader's side.
Susan Delacourt covers national politics for Torstar.
CAPTION:

CREDIT: susan delacourt OPINION Susan Delacourt covers national politics for Torstar.;

Document 288 of 500

Letters

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=unknown&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Letters&amp;title=The+Spectator&amp;issn=11899417&amp;date=2025-01-28&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.9&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Spectator&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Ford has lost
our confidence
So Premier Doug Ford says he needs a mandate to deal with Donald Trump. He still has close to 16 months to deal with Trump. He thinks that if he can get an election now and win, he will have four more years to waste Ontarians' money rather than just 16 months.
Wake up Ontario, especially the vast majority who didn't vote in the last election. Every eligible voter needs to get out and vote and give him and all the Progressive Conservative members of the legislature who don't serve their constituents the boot.
Maybe another party - one that doesn't try to line their friends' pockets by trying to destroy the Greenbelt or build tunnels under the 401 - will spend the money on things we need, like heath, education and real affordable housing.
Garry Condon, Burlington
Take advantage
of this opportunity
Premier Ford may inadvertently have the public interest at heart. He has proven how he favours the wealthy and powerful, like land developers. Think Greenbelt, Ontario Science Centre, Ontario Place, etc. He would cave to President Donald Trump.
An early election can free us from his weakness in the face of the powerful, and allow us to elect a strong, for-the-people leader. Either of our two strong female leadership alternatives would not only favour all of our province's people, but have Trump shaking in his boots. Trump does not seem to do well against a female whom he cannot dominate.
But, let's be careful in choosing our new provincial leader. We cannot split the vote and leave Ford in a minority government position.
Gary Caldwell, Lynden
We need a new
electoral system
Doug Ford has found his excuse: Donald Trump's tariffs. He's using that excuse to force us to an early election and forcing us into giving him a bigger mandate. This, despite the leaders of the three opposition parties having offered to work with him to present a united front to Trump. Why is Ford in such a rush? Maybe it has more to do with trying to outrun the RCMP investigation of the Greenbelt and get re-elected before animosity toward a Pierre Poilievre government spills over to the provincial Progressive Conservatives.
If we had proportional representation, where the number of seats reflects the proportion of the popular vote, Ford would currently have 41 per cent of the seats instead of 67 per cent of the seats and 100 per cent of the power. He would not be able to call an early election without the support of some of the parties that represent the other 59 per cent of Ontarians. He might even have to accept their offers to work together in a common front.
Which would be better for Ontario - an unnecessary early election or our leaders working together to stand up to Trump? We need proportional representation!
Heather Yoell, Dundas
Trump being used
as a distraction
With Donald Trump acting like a god to all Americans and threatening to slap tariffs on Canada, Doug Ford has decided he is our saviour. The "two peas in the pod" are just that!
Why a snap election now? He has well over a year with a majority government to get the job done. He claims he needs a strong mandate to move forward with a plan to save our economy. Believe him or not, you do need to ask yourself, why now?
Our country is in a transition period and a soon to be prime minister will be appointed. Ford is taking advantage of this distraction and using Trump as a scare tactic, all to remain in power.
Marlene Girvan, Ancaster
Ulterior motive
for election call?
Why does Doug Ford need to call a snap election when he already has his "mandate?" Is there perhaps a very big scandal looming that he needs to distract us from? How does he justify spending money we don't have?
Jackie Oke, Dundas
Put vacant office space to use
There are lots of opportunities for sheltering the homeless in downtown Hamilton. The office building where I used to work has many empty floors whose offices could be converted to dormitories without too much trouble. Washrooms (and even kitchen facilities) are already in place. There need to be rules, of course, and services and volunteers need to be made available. It's really not that complicated if all parties join together to make such a solution happen. Furthermore, giving people something to do, helping them find a purpose, needs to be part of the equation.
Renate Manthei, Hamilton
City council
has lost its way
Re: Hamilton proposed budget hike lowered to 5.7 per cent as councillors look for more cuts,
Jan. 24
There have been 746 new hires at city hall over the last two years, costing us $75 million a year. It seems we are living in a world of wants, not what we must have at city hall. We have an overall budget this year of $2.8 billion. And we can't find anything more to cut.
If this keeps up, by the time the next election rolls around in 2026, we will have hired another 400 or 500 full-time staff. And we will still be spending millions of dollars on the homeless without reducing the numbers. What a travesty.
We will have miles of bike lanes that are not being used. We will have two-way traffic on Main Street creating traffic chaos. We will have empty buses running around the city on some routes. We will still have many streets with potholes not fixed. We will still have hundreds of city homes not being lived in because they need extensive repair. We will be spending millions on climate change to do exactly what?
This council has lost its way.
Don Ross, Hamilton
Staff needed to
improve services
Re: With city staffing costs poised to soar past $1 billion, are the taxpayers getting their money's worth? Jan. 22
I maintain that we are, finally, beginning to get our money's worth. At last we have a majority on council who don't simply sit back complaining about broken city services on one hand while voting to underfund and understaff those services on the other hand.
If city services are ever to improve, hiring competent individuals to deliver them is a no-brainer. Ever waited 30 minutes for a call centre agent to answer or lined up for hours at ServiceOntario? We all know what happens when an organization lacks staff and the Corporation of the City of Hamilton is no different.
As for the minority of councillors who continually vote against increasing staffing, who propose facile and simplistic "hiring freezes" and who pick a number from the ether and expect staff to build a budget around it, the word "lazy" comes to mind.
Jordan Jameson, Stoney Creek
Focus on the
bigger picture
Re: Amid public scrutiny,
council calls for review of city's microshelters purchase, Jan. 24
I agree that it's absolutely important to ask questions when it comes to public projects, and supporting our local businesses should be the first "go-to." However, these questions shouldn't overshadow the very real and urgent need for housing the homeless, as soon as possible. I feel like the focus on where the tiny shelters are coming from, or the issues surrounding suppliers, are distracting from the fact that these tiny shelters will provide a safe, warm space for people who are struggling to survive on the streets of our city.
It's easy for those of us who are housed to scrutinize the details, but the priority should be making sure these tiny shelters get to people who need them, as quickly as possible.
Natalie Marino, Hamilton

Document 289 of 500

Doug Ford's alcohol sales expansion deal cost taxpayers an additional $612M, watchdog says

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/doug-fords-alcohol-sales-expansion-deal-cost/docview/3160049487/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Doug+Ford%27s+alcohol+sales+expansion+deal+cost+taxpayers+an+additional+%24612M%2C+watchdog+says&amp;title=The+Hamilton+Spectator+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2025-01-27&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Hamilton+Spectator+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: It cost taxpayers an additional $612 million so Premier Doug Ford could expand booze sales in time for his early Feb. 27 election, a fiscal watchdog has found.
In a new report, the Financial Accountability Office of Ontario estimates the liberalization tab will jump to $1.4 billion by the end of 2030 due to lower retail tax revenue for the LCBO, industry subsidies and other market changes.
Of that, "$817 million relates to the planned expansion of the beverage alcohol marketplace starting on Jan. 1, 2026, and $612 million relates to the decision to accelerate that expansion to begin in 2024," the independent office said.
The report comes one day before Ford is to visit Lt.-Gov. Edith Dumont for the drawing up of the writs for a campaign that officially begins Wednesday.
It will cost between $150 million and $175 million to hold the early election, the first Ontario winter campaign since 1981.
The timing of the vote coincides with $200 "rebate" cheques being mailed out right now to 15 million Ontarians, which is costing the treasury $3 billion. That cash is flowing despite Ontario being saddled with a $6.6 billion budget deficit.
Finance Minister Peter Bethlenfalvy's office said the FAO "report confirms what we've been saying from the start, LCBO revenues will increase as it plays an important role as both a retailer and the exclusive wholesaler for all other retailers."
"Any potential drop in tax revenue is due to our government's deliberate decisions to reduce markups and remove fees, keeping the cost of beer and wine down for consumers," said Bethlenfalvy's spokesman Colin Blachar.
"We were elected twice on a promise to deliver people with more choice and convenience, and to end one of the worst deals in the province's history that shut out local businesses in favour of large foreign corporations," said Blachar, adding the opposition Liberals "would rather protect the status quo that favoured big businesses over local convenience stores, and raise taxes and fees on beer and wine."
While the Progressive Conservative leader first promised liberalization of alcohol sales during the 2018 election, he only managed to keep that pledge last September when beer, wine, cider and premixed cocktails were allowed to be sold in licensed convenience stores.
Most supermarkets and big box outlets were permitted to sell booze as of Oct. 31, although the provincially owned Liquor Control Board of Ontario retains the monopoly on the sale of spirits such as whisky, rum, vodka and gin.
Last May, when Ford first began musing about calling an election well before the scheduled June 2026 vote, the government paid the privately run Beer Store $225 million to break the 10-year "master framework agreement" former Liberal premier Kathleen Wynne signed in 2015, which allowed wine and six-packs of beer to be sold in 450 supermarkets.
Even though that deal would have ended on Dec. 31, 2025 with minimal cost to taxpayers, Ford scrambled to deliver the promise earlier to give himself the flexibility to call a snap election.
In 2019, he was forced to retreat from an initial bid to unilaterally scrap the Beer Store accord after the United States Chamber of Commerce, the world's largest business organization, accused his government of "sending a negative signal to U.S. and other international investors about the business and investment climate in Ontario."
Liberal Leader Bonnie Crombie dismissed Ford's pre-election move as a "$1-billion booze boondoggle," and has questioned the need for an early election with U.S. President Donald Trump threatening 25 per cent tariffs on Canadian goods as of this Saturday.
"Doug Ford has been caught red-handed," Crombie said at Queen's Park. "Doug Ford has been lying about the true costs of this ridiculous scheme."
She added that money would have been better used to ease the doctor shortage or improve schools and suggests Ford, who insists he's the best person to deal with Trump, is a poor dealmaker.
"Doug Ford has the wrong priorities. Doug Ford has been focused on booze, bike lanes, big spas and his rich buddies, all because he thinks he can distract you with beer and pre-election cheques," she said.
New Democratic MPP Catherine Fife (Waterloo) said "just like Ontario Place and ServiceOntario at Staples, Doug Ford has launched Ontario into another bad deal, which will compromise revenues and increase alcohol consumption."
"If he can't negotiate a fair deal for Ontario, how can we trust him to negotiate with Trump?" said Fife.
Ford has said Trump's levies are the main reason he is going to the polls early.
But Crombie, NDP Leader Marit Stiles and Green Leader Mike Schreiner say the Tory leader is pulling the plug early to get in front of the RCMP criminal probe of the $8.28-billion Greenbelt land swap scandal.
Ford also hopes to to take advantage of the fact Liberal activists may be too busy on the March 9 federal leadership race to help Crombie's provincial campaign.
Also, he wanted to go before a federal election expected this spring that public-opinion polls suggest Conservative Leader Pierre Poilievre could win.
Because Ontario voters traditionally elect different parties at Queen's Park and in Ottawa at the same time, a Poilievre victory might be bad for Ford.
CAPTION: Premier Doug Ford arrives for a first ministers meeting in Ottawa on Jan. 15.

Ontario is hiring thousands of election workers and you could earn up to $29.50 per hour

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/ontario-is-hiring-thousands-election-workers-you/docview/3160049322/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Ontario+is+hiring+thousands+of+election+workers+and+you+could+earn+up+to+%2429.50+per+hour&amp;title=The+Hamilton+Spectator+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2025-01-27&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Hamilton+Spectator+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Ontario Premier Doug Ford has confirmed he will seek a snap provincial election on Feb. 27 in response to threats of 25 per cent tariffs against Canadian imports by U.S. President Donald Trump.
Instead of its regularly scheduled June 2026 date, the accelerated vote forces Elections Ontario to ramp up the process of hiring thousands of poll workers.
On Jan. 24, Ford confirmed at a news conference: "We will be calling an election next Wednesday (Jan. 29) I will be seeing the lieutenant governor on Tuesday (Jan. 28). "We need a mandate from the people to fight against Donald Trump's tariffs ..."
Ford has suggested Ontario may need to distribute "tens of billions of dollars" in economic stimulus if Trump's tariffs are enacted.
Why Feb. 27?
According to the Ontario Election Act, as amended in 2005, the writ for an election "shall be dated on a Wednesday."
So while Ford has announced he will call an early election, it won't be official until Wednesday (Jan. 29).
The process of drawing up the writs launches a 28-day election period.
The Ontario Election Act also stipulates the day for the close of nominations and the grant of a poll. The act states, "Polling day shall be the fifth Thursday after the date of the writ."
That means voters will head to the polls on Feb. 27.
What positions are available?
Tabulator returning officers, information assistants and other election officials will be needed on short notice when voters return to the polls. Young people aged 16 or 17 can apply for "youth at the booth" positions, for example.
Some jobs will be paper-based while others will require the use of technology.
All jobs are paid positions and training is included.
To work as an information assistant, applicants must be at least 16 years old with a social insurance number.
"Election officials working at the technology-based polls will be using electronic poll books and vote tabulators," Elections Ontario explains online. "Election officials working at the non-technology-based polls will be using paper-based products."
Area managers, who oversee several paper-based voting locations and supervise poll officials on election day, can expect to earn $29.50 per hour working up to 13 hours on election day only.
A deputy returning officer can earn $22.42 per hour. Job duties include processing electors, revising voter information and issuing ballots.
Poll clerks can expect to earn $21.25 hourly on election day for up to 13 hours. A poll clerk assists the deputy returning officer with processing electors.
A polling day revision assistant also earns $21.25 per hour. In this role, workers verify whether an elector appears accurately on the list of electors.
Information assistants can earn $20 per hour. As an assistant, you will greet, direct, and assist electors at a voting location.
Information assistants are also required at advance polls.
What are the qualifications?
To work as an election official, you must be at least 18 years old (or at least 16 years old for the position of information assistant and tabulator deputy returning officer). You are also required to be a Canadian citizen and Ontario resident, except for the position of information assistant, for which Canadian citizenship is not required.
Elections Ontario says it is seeking individuals who are personable, have problem-solving skills, are able to act as a leader and can communicate in English and French in districts designated as bilingual.
You should also prepare yourself for long working hours on election day, with shifts that could exceed 14 hours.
Some positions may require a valid driver's licence and vehicle.
Previous election experience is considered an asset.
"Please note that positions (both tech and non-tech) and voting locations are assigned by the Returning Officer, and that travel may be required for training," Elections Ontario adds.
How to apply
Applicants are invited to visit Elections Ontario online to get started. The six-step online application process begins by providing information about you. You can then access job details and provide personal information collected as part of Election Ontario's privacy policy.
CAPTION: The returning office sign for Election Ontario is seen in St. Catharines in 2022. Premier Doug Ford is set to call a snap election Wednesday, sending voters to the polls on Feb. 27.

Police board acclaims new chair, vice-chair: Ontario Shipyards president CEO Shaun Padulo is provincial appointee for oversight body

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/police-board-acclaims-new-chair-vice/docview/3159971395/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Police+board+acclaims+new+chair%2C+vice-chair%3A+Ontario+Shipyards+president+CEO+Shaun+Padulo+is+provincial+appointee+for+oversight+body&amp;title=The+Spectator&amp;issn=11899417&amp;date=2025-01-27&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.3&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Spectator&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Hamilton's police board has a new face at the helm.
Don Robertson will chair the board through 2025 after being acclaimed to the position at the oversight body's first meeting of the year on Friday. He was the only nominee in the running.
The local realtor takes over for Geordie Elms, who assumed the role of chair last summer after the abrupt departure of Pat Mandy. Elms is set to depart the board in March after six years as a provincial appointee.
"Don, I have no doubt you're ready to take it on," Elms said.
Robertson began a three-year provincial term on the board last April. A lifelong Hamiltonian, he currently serves as owner and general manager of the Allan Cup-winning Dundas Real McCoys hockey team.
Joining him at the head of the governance body next year will be Coun. Esther Pauls, who was acclaimed as vice-chair. She was also the only nominee in the running.
The Mountain councillor was one of three council appointees to the board in 2022. With her son being a police staff sergeant, she said at the time she consulted with the city's integrity commissioner on potential conflicts of interest.
Meanwhile, a local businessperson is the latest addition to Hamilton's police board.
Shaun Padulo, president and CEO of Ontario Shipyards, was announced as the oversight body's new provincial appointee in a release Thursday. He will begin his two-year term Feb. 20.
"Shaun brings extensive leadership and governance experience to the board," an announcement from the board said. "As president and chief executive officer of Ontario Shipyards ... Shaun has successfully guided the organization as president since 2018, officially assuming the CEO role in 2022."
Padulo takes over the role from Frank Bennink, who left the board last spring amid new legislation that prohibits former auxiliary officers from sitting on police boards in jurisdictions where they once volunteered.
Padulo's decorated business career dates to 2005, when he navigated the Great Lakes aboard tugboats as a deckhand with McKeil Marine, a Burlington-based marine service provider. Later, Padulo took on commercial roles with renowned Dutch firms Dockwise Shipping B.V. and Boskalis Offshore B.V., sharpening his skills in logistics management, offshore, subsea inspection, repair and maintenance projects.
Padulo, who got his bachelor's degree at McMaster University, also sits on the board for Mohawk College and has been involved with community organizations such as ProAction Cops and Kids, the board said.
"With strong ties to Hamilton, Shaun is honoured to contribute to his community as a member of the Hamilton police service board."
Sebastian Bron is a reporter
at The Hamilton Spectator. sbron@thespec.com
CAPTION: Don Robertson, far left, has been acclaimed as the chair of Hamilton's police board.Coun. Esther Pauls was acclaimed as vice-chair of the police board.Shaun Padulo, Ontario Shipyards president and CEO, at a July 2023 funding announcement with Premier Doug Ford, was named as the police board's newest provincial appointee Thursday.the hamilton Spectator file photos

Document 292 of 500

Trump has brought Canada together

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/trump-has-brought-canada-together/docview/3159971364/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Trump+has+brought+Canada+together&amp;title=The+Spectator&amp;issn=11899417&amp;date=2025-01-27&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=G.4&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Spectator&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Donald Trump is the U.S. president once more, but Canada remains strong and free.
While the tariff threat still looms, it feels like Canada is slightly stronger than we were a few months ago.
In a bizarre way, we have Trump to thank for that.
The reality-TV star turned politician sees the world like a conflict drama: Allies are adversaries, and negotiation is a game of winners and losers.
In Canada, he saw weakness: a sluggish economy. Falling productivity. The rapid-fire resignations of both the finance minister and the prime minister. As an incredulous CNN anchor said to Premier Doug Ford, "the entire government has gone into chaos."
In November, Trump put the loaded gun of tariffs on the table, rooted in the falsehood that the U.S. trade deficit with Canada - driven by American consumption of Canadian oil - was a "subsidy." In January, he pressed his advantage, threatening to use "economic force" to make Canada the 51st state.
Was he serious? Probably not. More likely, he said it to keep Canada off balance. In doing so, he united Canada in a way Justin Trudeau hasn't in many years.
It was Ford who captured the national mood - without saying a word - when he emulated Trump's tactic of putting a message on a baseball cap: "CANADA IS NOT FOR SALE." Within a day, the Ottawa-based small business that made the cap sold 50,000.
The premier understands the best strategy to make peace with an aggressive opponent: Show strength - and a willingness to use it. Reduce your vulnerabilities. And offer a better alternative.
Yes, a trade war would hurt Canada badly. But the best path to averting or at least mitigating it is to show that it would cause economic devastation on both sides of the border. A trade war has no winners.
Canada's strength comes from its many assets - the things American businesses and consumers need. We represent 56 per cent of their imported oil, almost half of their nickel and potash, and more than a quarter of their uranium and germanium. Ontario alone provides electricity to almost 1.3 million homes in New York, Michigan and Minnesota.
Reduce your vulnerabilities
If it's going to get rough, Canada has to get buff.
In the past decade, Canada has done little to arrest its declining productivity. Faced with an existential threat, we must replace complacency with competitiveness.
For example, the Ontario Chamber of Commerce has called on provincial and federal leaders to dismantle interprovincial trade barriers (which alone could neutralize the economic impact of any Trump tariffs); lower the cost of doing business; accelerate the development of strategic industries such as mining; invest in infrastructure, post-secondary education and community health; and help businesses diversify their trade relationships.
Just as governments acted decisively to protect businesses, workers and communities during the COVID-19 pandemic, the same level of focus is required today - this time less through broad-based spending, and more through targeted investment, policy and regulatory reform.
Finally, our political leaders - federal and provincial - must stand together. Those who seek to use the Trump threat to benefit their party, or to prioritize local interests over the national interest, must be made to pay a heavy price.
Offer a better alternative
Unwittingly, Trump himself pointed toward the better path. "Canada and the United States: that would really be something," he said. This is what we already have: an economic partnership that delivers massive - and equal - benefit to both countries.
While the president's comments about annexing Canada were disrespectful and disappointing, they were also distracting to both Americans and Canadians, overshadowing a much greater risk and opportunity. In the next four years, America's single greatest geopolitical imperative is to strengthen its hand relative to China, and Canada is an essential partner in achieving that goal. Doing business with us is a far smarter alternative.
The case for optimism
The silver lining in all this comes from both Canada's new-found unity and Trump's new-found reality. Now that Trump has been sworn in, his words no longer come unburdened with consequences.
He now has many interests to satisfy, and many partners essential to anything he wants to do: cabinet members, congressional leaders, governors, senior staff and business leaders. Most of them acutely understand what's at stake, and see little benefit to a trade war with Canada.
Canada is stronger than we were in November because we're more united. But unity alone isn't enough. It's time to show strength; to reduce our vulnerabilities; and to offer our American partners a better alternative. This may involve short-term sacrifice, but the long-term returns can be handsome, with 2025 being the Year of Living Courageously.
Canada has assets the world needs, and the talent to turn these assets into an enduring competitive advantage. Today's currents of political and economic change are a wake-up call to government - and a call to action for business. Together, we must answer it.
Daniel Tisch is the president and CEO of the Ontario Chamber of Commerce.
CAPTION: U.S. President Donald Trump's tariff threats and 51st state bluster united Canada in a way Prime Minister Justin Trudeau hasn't in years, writes Daniel Tisch. Now it's time to show strength.Saul LoebGetty Images

CREDIT: Daniel Tisch toronto star Daniel Tisch is the president and CEO of the Ontario Chamber of Commerce.
