Document 307 of 500

Ford to call snap election next week: Premier says he needs 'big mandate' from Feb. 27 vote as he vows to fight Trump tariffs

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/ford-call-snap-election-next-week/docview/3159622931/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Ford+to+call+snap+election+next+week%3A+Premier+says+he+needs+%27big+mandate%27+from+Feb.+27+vote+as+he+vows+to+fight+Trump+tariffs&amp;title=The+Spectator&amp;issn=11899417&amp;date=2025-01-25&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.1&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Spectator&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Doug Ford insists he's not rushing into a snap Feb. 27 Ontario election to get in front of a federal vote expected this spring.
Ontarians traditionally prefer different parties in power at Queen's Park and in Ottawa at the same time, so some Progressive Conservative strategists have privately expressed concern about their provincial electoral prospects if, as polls suggest, Conservative Leader Pierre Poilievre is triumphant.
Asked Friday if he were "scared" of a Poilievre victory, Ford said: "No, not whatsoever."
Ford plans to call a snap election Wednesday. The Feb. 27 election date is more than a year before the June 2026 fixed election date.
Pressed on whether he was scrambling to get ahead of potential federal spending cuts should the Conservatives succeed Prime Minister Justin Trudeau's Liberals, the Ontario Tory leader shook his head.
"My job - when we hopefully get a big mandate - is going to be standing up to any federal government that wants to cut health care from Ontario, cut jobs from Ontario, to tax people from Ontario," Ford said at a news conference in Brampton.
Last weekend in Mississauga, Poilievre bumped into provincial Liberal Leader Bonnie Crombie at a community event where they had a constructive conversation after the federal Tory chief warmly greeted her.
"Pierre mentioned to Bonnie that he is a fan of her housing plan," said a senior Liberal strategist, speaking confidentially in order to recount details of the impromptu chat.
That's a reference to Crombie's plan to make housing more affordable by scrapping the land-transfer tax for first-time homebuyers and seniors who are downsizing, eliminating development charges on new homes, and interest-free loans for renters facing financial emergencies that could result in evictions.
"Look, these are two politicians who are focusing their plans on what people need - they couldn't be more different than Ford and Trudeau. Makes sense that they would be talking about it," said the provincial Liberal.
The federal Tory leader's office would not comment on the discussion. There is no love lost between the national Conservatives and the PCs, who work closely with Trudeau's Liberals.
Poilievre's "axe the tax" mantra is one reason he is leading in polls against Trudeau.
Crombie was one of the first prominent Liberals to distance herself from the consumer price on carbon, a stance now adopted by would-be federal Grit leaders such as Mark Carney and Chrystia Freeland.
Ford stressed the main rationale for the early vote is U.S. President Donald Trump's threatened 25 per cent levies on Canadian products as of Feb. 1.
"I'll be seeing the lieutenant governor on Tuesday (for the issuing of the writs Wednesday). We need a mandate from the people to fight against Donald Trump's tariffs, the attack, he's coming against our families, our businesses, our communities," he said.
The Tories already have a majority at Queen's Park and an election, which will cost between $150 million and $175 million, was not supposed to be held until June 2026.
"With a strong mandate, we will be able to fight with Donald Trump to make sure we stop the tariffs and make sure that we give certainty to the people of Ontario," said Ford, adding it may take "tens of billions of dollars" in stimulus funding to keep the province's economy afloat.
Aside from the Trump tariffs and a Poilievre victory, the provincial Tories are worried about the ongoing RCMP investigation into the $8.28-billion Greenbelt scandal and would like to strike the Liberals when they may be too busy with the March 9 federal leadership race to help Crombie.
Even though he was at a government announcement in Brampton, Ford struck a fiercely partisan tone, repeatedly attacking Crombie and NDP Leader Marit Stiles. "Imagine Bonnie Crombie or Marit Stiles sitting across from President Trump, negotiating a deal, it would be an absolute disaster, and God help us if that ever happens," he said dismissively, noting he will go to Washington twice during the campaign on previously planned trips to lobby U.S. officials.
No meeting between Ford and Trump is expected and the PC campaign will have to foot the tab for his February jaunts stateside.
Rival leaders accused him of being a political opportunist by triggering an election at a time of uncertainty.
"He thinks he can con Ontarians with an early election to escape his record - but we won't let him," said Stiles.
"Doug Ford has been on the side of wealthy insiders while life for working people has gotten harder and more expensive. How can we trust him to stand up against Donald Trump and his billionaires when he can't deliver on making life more affordable, getting people a family doctor, or building more homes?" said the New Democratic leader.
Crombie, who has yet to disclose which Mississauga riding she will run in, said "with the looming threat of tariffs and the challenges facing our province, Ford has chosen to prioritize his own political survival over the people he was elected to serve."
"He's not a leader - he's a coward," said the Liberal leader, a former Mississauga mayor.
"Doug Ford can attack me all he wants. But as premier, I wouldn't run from my responsibilities. This snap election isn't about Trump or tariffs, it's about Doug Ford scrambling to outrun the RCMP investigation into his Greenbelt scandal," she added.
Green Leader Mike Schreiner said "Doug Ford is putting his job before your job" when Trump tariffs could throw 500,000 Ontarians out of work.
"All the things that a provincial government should do - build affordable homes, make sure we have doctors and nurses, safe schools - Doug Ford has walked away from," said Schreiner.
Upon dissolution next Wednesday, there are 79 Tories, including Speaker Ted Arnott, 28 New Democrats, nine Liberals, six Independents and two Greens in the 124-member house.
CAPTION: Rival leaders accused Premier Doug Ford of being a political opportunist by triggering an election at a time of uncertainty.Chris Young the canadian press

Document 308 of 500

Canada is cracking under threats

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/canada-is-cracking-under-threats/docview/3159622891/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=unknown&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Canada+is+cracking+under+threats&amp;title=The+Spectator&amp;issn=11899417&amp;date=2025-01-25&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.17&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Spectator&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: "You never know how strong you are," said the late, great Bob Marley, "until being strong is the only choice you have." In the face of the unprecedented threat from Donald Trump, this is Canada's moment to be strong. It's not yet clear how we're going to measure up, but at this point the news frankly isn't great.
You only have to look at how Canada's first ministers are meeting the challenge - or failing to meet it. They got together virtually again this week a couple of days after Trump's latest threat to impose punishing tariffs as soon as Feb. 1 and the headline in the Toronto Star summed it up: "Leaders split on response to tariffs."
There was plenty of talk about unity and Team Canada and standing strong. But the reality is they're getting more divided, not less, as Trump ratchets up the pressure. Alberta's Danielle Smith keeps insisting Canada should not even talk about possible retaliation, as Prime Minister Justin Trudeau and Ontario's Doug Ford are doing. Saskatchewan Premier Scott Moe seemed to be lining up with the Trudeau-Ford majority last week, but now he's switched and is singing from Smith's songbook.
Maybe they'll eventually get on the same page, or at least pretend to in public. But what a sad display.
If just the threat from Trump has them running off in different directions, imagine how bad it may get once tariffs actually hit and hard decisions must be made.
But don't worry - now the premiers are talking again about tearing down those internal barriers to trade you may have heard about. We've really got to get on that, offered Nova Scotia's Tim Houston. And Anita Anand, who's been our minister of internal trade for just over a month and can expect maybe three more months in office, says it's her "top priority."
This is unquestionably a good idea but it's almost embarrassing to have to talk about it at this point. On the good idea front, consider this: a 25 per cent tariff on Canadian imports to the U.S., as threatened by Trump, would cut Canada's GDP by an estimated 2.6 per cent - enough to drive this country into recession.
But fully tearing down those internal barriers to trade, according to Scotiabank, would increase Canada's GDP by between three and seven per cent. So even at the lowest end of that range, the benefits from removing the obstacles we put up between provinces would be greater than the Trump threat that we're treating as an approaching economic apocalypse.
This could have been addressed years ago. In fact, it was - on paper. In 1994, three decades ago, all the first ministers signed onto an Agreement on Internal Trade that pledged to remove the barriers. As recently as 2017 they signed the grandly named Canadian Free Trade Agreement, aimed at doing the same thing.
The result in the real world: not much. The problem is that like all such agreements, the benefits would be widely spread but the pain would be felt by specific groups like B.C. wineries and Quebec construction workers, who would lose local protection - and would squeal loudly. It's much easier for the premiers to pay lip service to internal free trade while guarding the status quo and the votes that come with it.
Maybe the Trump tariffs will bite deeply and force change. But there's no guarantee. The worrisome truth is that the threat from the south has shone a bright light on Canada's weaknesses, both economic and political.
This country isn't as strong as it should be or united as it could be. We're being forced to take a hard look at ourselves, and we're discovering that we've let a lot of things slide over the past decade.
The good news is there's an upsurge of patriotism among ordinary Canadians angry at the disrespect shown to this country by the new president.
Our political leaders should honour that by showing a truly united front against Trump in the short run and making sure that in the longer run we aren't left as vulnerable to outside threats as we are now.
Andrew Phillips is a staff columnist with Torstar.
CAPTION:

CREDIT: Andrew Phillips OPINION;

Subject: Gross Domestic Product--GDP; Free trade; Tariffs

Doug Ford insists he's not rushing into snap Feb. 27 Ontario election

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/doug-ford-insists-hes-not-rushing-into-snap-feb/docview/3159702642/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Doug+Ford+insists+he%27s+not+rushing+into+snap+Feb.+27+Ontario+election&amp;title=The+Hamilton+Spectator+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2025-01-24&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Hamilton+Spectator+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Doug Ford insists he's not rushing into a snap Feb. 27 Ontario election to get in front of a federal vote expected this spring.
Ontarians traditionally prefer different parties in power at Queen's Park and in Ottawa at the same time, so some Progressive Conservative strategists have privately expressed concern about their provincial electoral prospects if, as polls suggest, Conservative Leader Pierre Poilievre is triumphant.
Asked Friday if he were "scared" of a Poilievre victory, Ford said: "No, not whatsoever."
Pressed on whether he was scrambling to get ahead of potential federal spending cuts should the Conservatives succeed Prime Minister Justin Trudeau's Liberals, the Ontario Tory leader shook his head.
"My job - when we hopefully get a big mandate - is going to be standing up to any federal government that wants to cut health care from Ontario, cut jobs from Ontario, to tax people from Ontario," Ford said in Brampton where he announced the downtown section of the still-unfinished Hazel McCallion Line LRT would be tunnelled at an undisclosed cost.
Last weekend in Mississauga, Poilievre bumped into provincial Liberal Leader Bonnie Crombie at a community event where they had a constructive conversation after the federal Tory chief warmly greeted her.
"Pierre mentioned to Bonnie that he is a fan of her housing plan," said a senior Liberal strategist, speaking confidentially in order to recount details of the impromptu chat.
That's a reference to Crombie's plan to make housing more affordable by scrapping the land-transfer tax for first-time homebuyers and seniors who are downsizing, eliminating development charges on new homes, and interest-free loans for renters facing financial emergencies that could result in evictions.
"Look, these are two politicians who are focusing their plans on what people need - they couldn't be more different than Ford and Trudeau. Makes sense that they would be talking about it," said the provincial Liberal.
The federal Tory leader's office would not comment on the discussion. There is no love lost between the national Conservatives and the PCs, who work closely with Trudeau's Liberals.
Poilievre's "axe the tax" mantra is one reason he is leading in polls against Trudeau.
Crombie was one of the first prominent Liberals to distance herself from the consumer price on carbon, a stance now adopted by would-be federal Grit leaders like Mark Carney and Chrystia Freeland.
Ford stressed the main rationale for the early vote is U.S. President Donald Trump's threatened 25 per cent levies on Canadian products as of Feb. 1.
"I'll be seeing the lieutenant governor on Tuesday (for the issuing of the writs Wednesday). We need a mandate from the people to fight against Donald Trump's tariffs, the attack he's coming against our families, our businesses, our communities," he said.
The Tories already have a majority at Queen's Park and an election, which will cost between $150 million and $175 million, was not supposed to be held until June 2026.
"With a strong mandate, we will be able to fight with Donald Trump to make sure we stop the tariffs and make sure that we give certainty to the people of Ontario," said Ford, adding it may take "tens of billions of dollars" in stimulus funding to keep the province's economy afloat.
Aside from the Trump tariffs and a Poilievre victory, the Tories are worried about the ongoing RCMP investigation into the $8.28-billion Greenbelt scandal and would like to strike the Liberals when they may be too busy with the March 9 federal leadership race to help Crombie.
Even though he was at a government announcement in Brampton, Ford struck a fiercely partisan tone, repeatedly attacking Crombie and NDP Leader Marit Stiles.
"Imagine Bonnie Crombie or Marit Stiles sitting across from President Trump, negotiating a deal, it would be an absolute disaster, and God help us if that ever happens," he said dismissively, noting he will go to Washington twice during the campaign on previously planned trips to lobby U.S. officials.
No meeting between Ford and Trump is expected and the PC campaign will have to foot the tab for his February jaunts stateside.
Rival leaders accused him of being a political opportunist by triggering an election at a time of uncertainty.
"He thinks he can con Ontarians with an early election to escape his record - but we won't let him," said Stiles.
"Doug Ford has been on the side of wealthy insiders while life for working people has gotten harder and more expensive. How can we trust him to stand up against Donald Trump and his billionaires when he can't deliver on making life more affordable, getting people a family doctor, or building more homes?" said the New Democratic leader.
Crombie, who has yet to disclose which Mississauga riding she will run in, said "with the looming threat of tariffs and the challenges facing our province, Ford has chosen to prioritize his own political survival over the people he was elected to serve."
"He's not a leader - he's a coward," said the Liberal leader, a former Mississauga mayor.
"Doug Ford can attack me all he wants. But as premier, I wouldn't run from my responsibilities. This snap election isn't about Trump or tariffs, it's about Doug Ford scrambling to outrun the RCMP investigation into his Greenbelt scandal," she added.
Green Leader Mike Schreiner said "Doug Ford is putting his job before your job" when Trump tariffs could throw 500,000 Ontarians out of work.
"All the things that a provincial government should do - build affordable homes, make sure we have doctors and nurses, safe schools - Doug Ford has walked away from," said Schreiner.
Ford bristled when reporters raised parallels to former Liberal premier David Peterson's ill-advised 1990 snap election that led to a surprise NDP victory at a time of angst over national unity due to the collapse of the Meech Lake Accord that, if passed, would have seen Quebec sign the Constitution.
"I love you media. You guys are classic. But 35 years ago, he woke up and decided to call election. He wasn't facing the largest attack in the history of our country, attack against our families, against what they do."
Peterson is currently chair of Torstar, the Star's parent company.
But there is some other Ontario political history Ford may be abiding by.
According to the "Underhill balance theory," Ontarians tend to opt for different parties in office at the same time provincially and federally. The phenomenon has held true for about 71 of the past 82 years.
Upon dissolution next Wednesday, there are 79 Tories, including Speaker Ted Arnott, 28 New Democrats, nine Liberals, six Independents and two Greens in the 124-member house.

Document 310 of 500

Letters

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/letters/docview/3159093755/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=unknown&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Letters&amp;title=The+Spectator&amp;issn=11899417&amp;date=2025-01-24&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.11&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Spectator&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Let the PC party foot the bill
Premier Doug Ford can call the election. I really don't care as long as the PC Party of Ontario pays the full cost of somewhere between $150 million to $200 million. Brought to you by the taxpayers of Ontario.
Terry Middlemiss, Hamilton
Ford doesn't need a new mandate
The last thing Ontarians need right now is an election. Hospitals are overflowing, homeless people are dying on the streets, colleges and universities are laying off thousands of staff. But hey, we can buy beer at the grocery store, even if we can't afford the food. Oh and soon we'll all get a $200 cheque to pay for it. Not matter that "gift" will cost our province $3.2 billion.
Doug Ford doesn't need a new mandate. He already has a strong one. The next election is set for June 2026. Instead, people will be forced to vote in the depths of winter - and fork out about $150 million for the privilege. Another government will be AWOL, out on the hustings. But I'm sure "Captain Canada" Doug will take the time out to join the other premiers in Washington.
No wonder we get cynical. It's crass political opportunism and nothing else.
Carol Town, Hamilton
Better uses for our money
Doug Ford says he needs a clear mandate to provide stimulus and is planning to call a snap provincial election that will cost at least $150 million. Mr. Ford, you have a majority government, that means you already have a clear mandate. You still have two years in your mandate; do your job! There are Ontarians with no family doctors. There are lengthy ER wait times. There is increasing homelessness. There are tent encampments. There is the Greenbelt land swap scandal that still needs to be dealt with. There are better ways to spend our money.
Alice Marie Phillips,
Hamilton
Election is unwarranted
Already bored with the federal government's instability, a lame duck prime minister, the Liberal leadership race, unpredictable Donald Trump in Washington? Yeah, me too! I think we'd all welcome the political adrenalin rush that only comes from a provincial election. Call it a much needed anxiety top up!
It's not as if we aren't already bombarded daily about the precarious nature of the financial system, incipient recession, resurgent inflation, income inequality, health services crises, Russia's invasion of Ukraine, the plight of the homeless and climate catastrophes that we don't have the mental health headroom to spare.
Premier Doug Ford claims he needs a mandate so he can talk to the Americans. Doesn't he already have one of those? He does but he wants to buy a new one with our election money. What else does he need to talk to Big Orange? You'd think he could be satisfied with the Captain Canada cape he wears among the premiers, but apparently not.
Is there an appetite for spending the money we don't have for housing and health care to buy Ford an unnecessary brand new electoral mandate?
For me, I'd rather not have more levels of government blowing in the wind at one time. Maybe Ford could just make do with the perfectly serviceable mandate we already gave him. It's not like it's worn out. And, after all, don't many of us just have to make do with what we already have in most areas of life these days?
Gary Mahoney, Hamilton
Trump's border
complaints empty
Re: Emerging Mexican cartel involved in historic $83-million cocaine bust, Toronto police say, Jan. 21
In the news recently, the Toronto Police Service seized $83-million worth of cocaine and other various drugs coming in from the United States. And the U.S. president has the audacity to blame Canada for anything?
Bruce Bochek, Stoney Creek
Speak up for the environment
The dryness and hot temperatures being caused by climate change are creating forest fires like nothing we have experienced before. They move quickly, ferociously and don't just burn but vaporize everything they hit. We've seen the horrendous photos of the L.A. fires. These 21st-century fires are having the same effect all over the world. Burnt forests can't create oxygen and sequester carbon dioxide. Oceans, forests, tundra and grasslands are nature's way to offset CO2 and methane gases, but the huge increase of these gases, promoted and financed by for-profit fossil fuel companies and certain governments, have overpowered the ability of nature to cope with this greenhouse effect.
The forest fires and other natural disasters are a clear signal that we must act on climate change for it will affect everyone especially the young, innocent and yet to be born.
We can still do something. Let's lobby for green energy which makes life on our planet possible and wonderful, encourage banks, governments and pension funds to stop investing in fossil fuels and vote for the party that has a viable plan to stop the destruction of this jewel of a planet.
Theresa Cardey, Hamilton
Fitness advice
appreciated
Thanks Ernie Schramayr for your column and great advice. Thanks for helping me with my physical recovery. Your patience and encouragement has been a great help through my medical issues. The extra you do for me is very much appreciated. At 81, you have kept me mobile, and will be a great help on our extended European tour.
Ed Tymkow, Hamilton
Book is a
fitting tribute
Re: HNIC broadcaster Scott Oake tells story of addiction, loss and hope in new book,
Jan. 22
An excellent article by Joshua Clipperton about "Hockey Night in Canada" broadcaster Scott Oake's heartfelt memoir, "For the Love of a Son." Oake not only honours the memory of his son Bruce who died from an accidental drug overdose, but also educates others in the hope of preventing a similar tragedy to another family.
I had just watched Oake on CTV's "Your Morning" openly and honestly talking about his son and emphasizing that drug addiction is a disease. It does not discriminate, for example, actor Matthew Perry (Oct. 25).
Clipperton's article further describes the Oake family's proactive efforts to inform, help and support others struggling with the disease of drug addiction. A fitting tribute to his son, Bruce.
Jan Peirson-Reid, Carlisle
City spending
is out of control
Re: With city staffing costs poised to soar past $1 billion, are the taxpayers getting their money's worth? Jan. 22
This council's spending has got to be the worst in years. They spend, they waste funds for consultants when employees should do their job that you hired them for and now 746 more jobs in three years? Where does it stop?
Pensioners don't get raises. Hamiltonians don't get raises to keep up with tax increases.
Our taxes are so high above everyone else and really what do we have to show for it? Where is the priority spending that is actually good for the city? People are not going to be able to afford to live in Hamilton.
It has to stop somewhere. It has gone beyond acceptable!
Debbie Escott, Hamilton

Subject: Cocaine; Provincial elections; Drug overdose; Forest & brush fires; Addictions; Homeless people; Climate change

Document 311 of 500

Jan. 24: Ford doesn't need new mandate, we need to kick our salt habit and other letters to the editor

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/jan-24-ford-doesnt-need-new-mandate-we-kick-our/docview/3159093405/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Jan.+24%3A+Ford+doesn%27t+need+new+mandate%2C+we+need+to+kick+our+salt+habit+and+other+letters+to+the+editor&amp;title=The+Hamilton+Spectator+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2025-01-24&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Hamilton+Spectator+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Ford doesn't need new mandate
The last thing Ontarians need right now is an election. Hospitals are overflowing, homeless people are dying on the streets, colleges and universities are laying off thousands of staff. But hey, we can buy beer at the grocery store, even if we can't afford the food. Oh and soon we'll all get a $200 cheque to pay for it. Not matter that "gift" will cost our province $3.2 billion.
Doug Ford doesn't need a new mandate. He already has a strong one. The next election is set for June 2026. Instead, people will be forced to vote in the depths of winter - and fork out about $150 million for the privilege. Another government will be AWOL, out on the hustings. But I'm sure "Captain Canada" Doug will take the time out to join the other premiers in Washington.
No wonder we get cynical. It's crass political opportunism and nothing else.
Carol Town, Hamilton
Let PC Party pay the bill
Premier Doug Ford can call the election. I really don't care as long as the PC Party of Ontario pays the full cost of somewhere between $150 million to $200 million. Brought to you by the taxpayers of Ontario.
Terry Middlemiss, Hamilton
Election is unwanted, unwarranted
Already bored with the federal government's instability, a lame duck prime minister, the Liberal leadership race, unpredictable Donald Trump in Washington? Yeah, me too! I think we'd all welcome the political adrenalin rush that only comes from a provincial election. Call it a much needed anxiety top up!
It's not as if we aren't already bombarded daily about the precarious nature of the financial system, incipient recession, resurgent inflation, income inequality, health services crises, Russia's invasion of Ukraine, the plight of the homeless and climate catastrophes that we don't have the mental health headroom to spare.
Premier Doug Ford claims he needs a mandate so he can talk to the Americans. Doesn't he already have one of those? He does but he wants to buy a new one with our election money. What else does he need to talk to Big Orange? You'd think he could be satisfied with the Captain Canada cape he wears among the premiers, but apparently not.
Is there an appetite for spending the money we don't have for housing and health care to buy Ford an unnecessary brand new electoral mandate?
For me, I'd rather not have more levels of government blowing in the wind at one time. Maybe Ford could just make do with the perfectly serviceable mandate we already gave him. It's not like it's worn out. And, after all, don't many of us just have to make do with what we already have in most areas of life these days?
Gary Mahoney, Hamilton
Better uses for our money
Doug Ford says he needs a clear mandate to provide stimulus and is planning to call a snap provincial election that will cost at least $150 million. Mr. Ford, you have a majority government, that means you already have a clear mandate. You still have two years in your mandate; do your job! There are Ontarians with no family doctors. There are lengthy ER wait times. There is increasing homelessness. There are tent encampments. There is the Greenbelt land swap scandal that still needs to be dealt with. There are better ways to spend our money.
Alice Marie Phillips, Hamilton
Trump's border complaints are empty
Re: Emerging Mexican cartel involved in historic $83-million cocaine bust, Toronto police say, Jan. 21
In the news tonight, the Toronto Police Service seized $83-million worth of cocaine and other various drugs coming in from the United States. And the U.S. president has the audacity to blame Canada for anything?
Bruce Bochek, Stoney Creek
Fitness advice appreciated
Thanks Ernie Schramayr for your column and great advice. Thanks for helping me with my physical recovery. Your patience and encouragement has been a great help through my medical issues. The extra you do for me is very much appreciated. At 81 you have kept me mobile, and will be a great help on our extended European tour.
Ed Tymkow, Hamilton
Book is a fitting tribute
Re: HNIC broadcaster Scott Oake tells story of addiction, loss and hope in new book, Jan. 22
An excellent article by Joshua Clipperton about "Hockey Night in Canada" broadcaster Scott Oake's heartfelt memoir, "For the Love of a Son." Oake not only honours the memory of his son Bruce who died from an accidental drug overdose, but also educates others in the hope of preventing a similar tragedy to another family.
I had just watched Oake on CTV's "Your Morning" openly and honestly talking about his son and emphasizing that drug addiction is a disease. It does not discriminate, for example, actor Matthew Perry (Oct. 25).
Clipperton's article further describes the Oake family's proactive efforts to inform, help and support others struggling with the disease of drug addiction. A fitting tribute to his son, Bruce.
Jan Peirson-Reid, Carlisle
Speak up for the environment
The dryness and hot temperatures being caused by climate change are creating forest fires like nothing we have experienced before. They move quickly, ferociously and don't just burn but vaporize everything they hit. We've seen the horrendous photos of the L.A. fires. These 21st-century fires are having the same effect all over the world. Burnt forests can't create oxygen and sequester carbon dioxide. Oceans, forests, tundra and grasslands are nature's way to offset CO2 and methane gases, but the huge increase of these gases, promoted and financed by for-profit fossil fuel companies and certain governments, have overpowered the ability of nature to cope with this greenhouse effect.
The forest fires and other natural disasters are a clear signal that we must act on climate change for it will affect everyone especially the young, innocent and yet to be born.
We can still do something. Let's lobby for green energy which makes life on our planet possible and wonderful, encourage banks, governments and pension funds to stop investing in fossil fuels and vote for the party that has a viable plan to stop the destruction of this jewel of a planet.
Theresa Cardey, Hamilton
Wasteful spending must stop
Re: With city staffing costs poised to soar past $1 billion, are the taxpayers getting their money's worth? Jan. 22
This council's spending has got to be the worst in years. They spend, they waste funds for consultants when employees should do their job that you hired them for and now 746 more jobs in three years? Where does it stop?
Pensioners don't get raises. Hamiltonians don't get raises to keep up with tax increases. Our taxes are so high above everyone else and really what do we have to show for it? Where is the priority spending that is actually good for the city? People are not going to be able to afford to live in Hamilton.
It has to stop somewhere. It has gone beyond acceptable!
Debbie Escott, Hamilton
Hamas must be defeated
Re: Don't expect the peace in Gaza to last for long, Jan. 21
Gwynne Dyer, in his Tuesday column, writes that if the Hamas-Israel ceasefire fails, it will be because of Israel. Of course. How simplistic.
For some pundits, Israel is seemingly always the culprit, and Hamas is not much more than a minor nuisance. But Hamas is more than just a nuisance; it started the war on Oct. 7, 2023, by invading Israel and conducting a campaign of rape, murder and kidnapping. And every single day that Hamas refused to free the hostages, it decided it wanted the war to continue.
Hamas has yelled from the mountaintops that it will stop at nothing short of Israel's annihilation, but for some reason some commentators don't seem to care. But nothing will change until Hamas, and their radical ideology, is defeated once and for all.
Cheri Elson, Dundas
We need to kick our salt habit
Re: We need to talk about all the shoe-destroying, dog-stinging salt that is plaguing our sidewalks, Jan. 20
The Hamilton Spectator recently published an opinion piece on excessive road salt use in Ontario. The writer listed concerns ranging from dog discomfort to soil pollution. I can think of a few more concerns, like harm to animals that consume road salt and impacts on the growth of fish and frogs in affected waterways.
Hamilton is home to legally protected at-risk species, including fish and amphibians that are incredibly sensitive to salt pollution. There are less harmful alternatives, like sand and urea, and Canadian cities should be seriously looking into strategies to reduce their salt use.
Liability is a valid concern, but that can go both ways. In 2015, the Ontario Superior Court ruled in favour of a couple in Lambton whose farm had been affected by excessive road salting. Whether it's about crop damage or conservation concerns, another case like this could go to court if current salting practices continue.
The Hamilton Biodiversity Action Plan lists salt management as one of its key priorities, but this problem isn't limited to municipalities. For example, McMaster University is diligent in salting its roads and walkways. However, McMaster's main campus borders Cootes Paradise, a provincially significant wetland. All that salt has to go somewhere, and Cootes is directly downhill. It's yet another problem for an already beleaguered wetland.
The amount of salt on our streets isn't working for people, pets or the environment. It's time to find ways to keep the winter safe the environment, too.
Jonathan Scholtens, Hamilton
Cut America off completely
Not long ago Donald Trump stated "Canada has nothing America needs." Accordingly, Canada should fully respect his assessment that America knows best what America's needs are, and not attempt to place the burden of Canadian exports upon America. Canada should turn its attention to the Canadian development and use of oil, natural gas, water, minerals, electricity, waterways, etc. solely for the benefit of Canadians and other trading partners as determined by Canada.
If after Canada takes such action, and for some reason the Trump administration on behalf of the American public upon whom this action negatively impacts, changes its mind, and decides they would welcome the import of Canadian goods and services mentioned above, my 30 years of experience in both negotiations and arbitrations with employers and governments both provincial and federal, lead me to believe that Canada would then have excellent bargaining chips that if applied would cause America to withdraw any unwanted or unjustified 25 per cent tariffs.
So I respectfully request that Canadian governments consider this strategy rather than some specific tariff responses to American tariffs now under consideration as being a more effective strategy in obtaining tariff freedom from America.
Gord Wilson, Port Rowan

Premier Ford to call snap election next week for Feb. 27

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/premier-ford-call-snap-election-next-week-feb-27/docview/3159093310/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Premier+Ford+to+call+snap+election+next+week+for+Feb.+27&amp;title=The+Spectator&amp;issn=11899417&amp;date=2025-01-24&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.7&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Spectator&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Premier Doug Ford plans to call a snap election next Wednesday and send Ontarians to the polls on Feb. 27, The Canadian Press has learned.
Two senior government sources say Ford recently made the decision for the rare winter election after waffling for months.
The Canadian Press is not naming them so they can speak candidly about internal government deliberations.
The election had been set for June 2026, but Ford has said he needs a new mandate in order to deal with four years of a Donald Trump presidency in the United States.
Ford has said he expects Trump tariffs to hit Ontario particularly hard, specifically the auto sector, and he believes the province would need to spend tens of billions of dollars in order to keep the economy and hundreds of thousands of jobs afloat.
Opposition parties have said an early election is not necessary because they would support stimulus spending, and Ford - a premier with a majority government - already has a mandate to protect Ontario's interests.
The opposition, led by NDP Leader Marit Stiles, newly minted Ontario Liberal Party Leader Bonnie Crombie and Green Party Leader Mike Schreiner, also accused Ford of trying to get an election in before the conclusion of an RCMP probe into the government's decision to open up parts of the protected Greenbelt to development.
Ford had walked back the Greenbelt plan, but it caused turmoil internally, leading to the resignations of then-housing minister Steve Clark and his chief of staff.
The Canadian Press
CAPTION:

CREDIT: Liam Casey and Allison Jones The Canadian Press

Document 313 of 500

No mandate to call an early election

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/no-mandate-call-early-election/docview/3159093274/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=unknown&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=No+mandate+to+call+an+early+election&amp;title=The+Spectator&amp;issn=11899417&amp;date=2025-01-24&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.10&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Spectator&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: With the looming threat of extreme tariffs levied against our country, some have stepped up to promote a strong, unified Team Canada approach to the new Trump administration.
First among them is Premier Doug Ford, who has cloaked himself in the mantle of Captain Canada and done an admirable job in the sabre-rattling phase of this trade war.
Even Ford's toughest critics are impressed by his clarity and conviction as chair of the Council of the Federation, putting country over politics and trying to save Canada from a hostile U.S. government. But while the country looks to him for more tough talk against U.S. President Donald Trump, Ford is trying to parlay this profile boost into a justification for calling an early election.
Ford is making his argument for a snap election on two fronts: that Ontario needs a loud voice and that his government needs a mandate to spend tens of billions of dollars to protect us from the impact of tariffs, which could claim half a million jobs. In 2018, when other tariffs were threatened, Ford did not seek a new mandate. Now, he says the aggressive tone of Trump 2.0 is the reason for the urgency.
While it is true that the new Trump regime's posture and "Golden Age of America" rhetoric echo the 19th-century doctrine of Manifest Destiny as they eye our land mass, call our prime minister a "governor" and talk of erasing our border, Ford's argument for the need to throw Ontario into an expensive election doesn't make sense.
It is, in fact, at this very moment when our federal government is prorogued and the country hangs in suspended animation, that we need Ford to focus on his role as a leader among the premiers. Like Ford or not, the country needs a steady hand on the wheel while the Liberal party selects its new leader.
A campaigning Ford under daily assault for his policy record and a litany of scandals is not as loud a voice as a premier with almost 500 days to go in his second majority.
With the tariff war's Sword of Damocles hanging over our province, the people of Ontario may not reflect on Ford's record of overpaying for private nurses, failing to meet his own housing targets, underfunding ODSP adding to 81,000 people suffering from homelessness and his government's inability to complete infrastructure projects on time. The spectre of the RCMP report on the Greenbelt scandal may also be overlooked in a snap election called under the auspices of saving our economy.
The second part of Ford's argument is about needing a new mandate to spend billions of tax dollars.
Why does that suddenly matter to a premier who made the unpopular decision to retrofit Ontario Place, which could now cost us as much as $2.2 billion?
Why does our permission to spend money matter to a premier who spent almost a quarter of a billion to prematurely back out of a deal with the Beer Store to rush alcohol into corner stores?
Why does the careful allocation of tax dollars matter now when recently he mused about an unnecessary tunnel under Highway 401?
For a premier who spent $103 million in the past year on advertisements telling us how he's "getting it done for the people" and who has all but dismissed scathing reports from the auditor general about his overspending, the argument that he suddenly needs our permission to spend our money is pretty rich.
No other premiers have seized on this trade war crisis to make their own political opportunities.
Ford risks losing his Captain Canada power if he puts his ambition over the country.
Laura Babcock
is president of POWERGROUP Communications and the host of "The OShow"
podcast.
CAPTION:

Document 314 of 500

Trudeau's last chance to shine

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/trudeaus-last-chance-shine/docview/3159091389/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=unknown&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Trudeau%27s+last+chance+to+shine&amp;title=The+Spectator&amp;issn=11899417&amp;date=2025-01-24&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.10&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Spectator&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Justin Trudeau is facing down one of the most powerful threats to Canada at a moment when his own power is waning.
It may turn out to be one of the defining chapters for Trudeau himself and Canada as a whole.
In an ideal world, Trudeau would be in the same position he was when Donald Trump first became president in 2017 - leader of a majority government, with premiers and political parties united behind Team Canada.
But that was never going to be the case. Conservative Leader Pierre Poilievre was never going to give the prime minister the endorsement of solidarity. Premiers, though mostly united now (Alberta's Danielle Smith excepted) had already been talking of going their own way with Trump and not waiting for Trudeau to act.
In short, Trudeau and Trump find themselves as usual in opposite spots. Trump has never been more powerful; Trudeau, arguably, is at his weakest.
But a case can be made that maybe this is the time when Canada needs a prime minister with just one job - staring down the threat that this even more erratic, unpredictable president presents to this country.
It's no doubt a disappointment for Trudeau that he won't be acting as host when Canada holds the G7 summit in June at Kananaskis, Alta. But here again is a burden lifted. Trump made the last G7 meeting in Canada a little too interesting in 2018, departing in a flood of angry tweets at the prime minister over Trudeau's audacity in condemning tariffs.
Trudeau is now free to trash talk those tariffs to his heart's content, and he can be reasonably confident Canadians are united behind him.
It's been interesting to watch Canada's premiers - again, Smith excepted - since Trudeau resigned. The usual shots at Ottawa are conspicuously absent, even from premiers such as Saskatchewan's Scott Moe, who can usually be relied upon to use his podium to say why Trudeau has to go.
Trudeau is going. Point made, taken. As heard among the flood of commentary over the past week, it's a bit like watching what happens when the dog catches the car it's been chasing: now what?
Of course, Trudeau has prime ministerial duties to manage beyond Trump in the weeks leading up to when Liberals choose his replacement on March 9.
There are the usual affairs of state, as well as whatever is within his limited power to do to seal his legacy - how much he can Poilievre-proof some signature achievements, whether that's on child care or the yet-unfinished business of pharmacare and dental care.
A wave of Liberal ads, prepared when Trudeau was still planning on fighting the next election, are very much organized around this theme - all that's at risk if Poilievre becomes the next prime minister.
Liberals will still hammer away at this idea, no matter who is the next leader, but with Trudeau leaving, this looks less like securing a personal legacy than a progressive one in the face of a conservative revival.
Trump, with his shock-and-awe assault on progressive policies just this week, may turn out to be the Liberals' best example of all that's imperilled by a re-energized right. Conservatives may applaud some of what Trump is doing to beat back the "woke" politics of progressivism, but they're going to be a little more careful as the full extent of Trump's rights rollbacks become evident.
What must be a relief for Trudeau is that he really doesn't need to give Poilievre a second thought right now. He is free to do what he has always seemed to do with the Conservative leader: ignore him. Poilievre didn't defeat Trudeau; that loss was suffered because of disunity in Liberal ranks.
Maybe that's why Trudeau has seemed to look somewhat liberated in his recent public appearances. He has one main job now - handling Trump, and how well he pulls it off may well define his career.
Susan Delacourt covers national politics for Torstar.
CAPTION:

CREDIT: Susan Delacourt OPINION;

Document 315 of 500

Donald Trump is exploiting Canada's reliance on trade with America. Why don't we trade with more countries?

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/donald-trump-is-exploiting-canadas-reliance-on/docview/3159091363/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Donald+Trump+is+exploiting+Canada%27s+reliance+on+trade+with+America.+Why+don%27t+we+trade+with+more+countries%3F&amp;title=The+Hamilton+Spectator+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2025-01-24&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Hamilton+Spectator+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: OTTAWA-Canada is a laggard when it comes to trade diversity despite all the years of political pledges to do better, and all the free trade agreements signed.
At a quick glance, the facts are promising.
Canada is the only G7 nation with a free-trade agreement with every other member. And with 15 trade deals covering 49 countries and more on the way, we can boast to ourselves about our preferential access to a world eager for the True North's rich resources.
But behind that rosy picture, our track record of reliance on the U.S. for nearly 80 per cent of our exports means that if U.S. President Donald Trump moves forward on his tariff threat, it will pummel our economy.
Canadian political and business leaders are now grappling with the fact that despite decades of signing deals in an attempt to diversify our trade, the Canadian economy remains deeply dependent on trade with America. That's forced Canadians to make an existential pitch to their American counterparts: Our economies are so connected that our fates are intertwined - basically, hurt me and you'll feel it too.
International Development Minister Ahmed Hussen, who has long advocated for Canada to do more business with emerging markets in Africa, said it's "understandable" Canadian businesses turn to American customers.
"There's two things at play. One is that we have access to the largest market in the world. The U.S. market is very attractive - it's very easy to work in. We have the same language. We have similar ways of doing business. It's easier to work and grow in the U.S.," he said in an interview.
"The second part of the equation is Canadian businesses have been extremely risk averse when it comes to emerging markets," he said.
"However, that reliance (on American markets) comes with risk," he said.
He believes new trade agreements and government supports like trade commissioner and consular offices have helped. "We're heading in the right direction, just not fast enough. I think the challenge for governments is that ... you can build a bridge, but unless people walk over the bridge or drive across the bridge, it's not very useful."
Meredith Lilly, a professor at Carleton University and a former international trade adviser to prime minister Stephen Harper, underlined the significance of a diversified export market: "It sends an important signal to the United States, when we have other partners, that we have other options."
But right now, she said, "the statistics just don't support that."
"We have actually become more dependent on the United States," Lilly said in an interview with the Star.
It's a tough geopolitical situation that is forcing Canadians to reckon with why we haven't positioned ourselves to be less reliant on the U.S., and has reignited decades-old calls for a transformation in how we do business abroad.
Hussen points to the example of Turkey, a country of 85 million and a "gateway" for Canadian businesses not just to that market but into western Asia, as well as to Africa. He spoke last week to his counterpart in Turkey, where the government has aggressively moved to build embassies in 51 of 54 African countries, and has ramped up trade on that continent to the point where it has aided Canadian contractors who are partnering with Turkish businesses to do work in places like Senegal and Tanzania on high-speed rail. "Canada benefitted from that hustle by Turkey," he said.
Hussen gave another example: Nigeria, where he said 70 per cent of the population doesn't have adequate electricity. "That's a huge inroad for Canadian power companies to go in there and build all forms of energy-producing projects. The opportunities are there, whether it's food security, whether it's health care, transport, logistics, education. Again, these middle powers are going all in," he said. So are big powers like China and India.
In the last week, Ontario Premier Doug Ford, whose province does about $500 billion in two-way trade annually with the U.S., acknowledged "we need to move forward as quickly as possible" with diversifying our trade.
So, too, did Liberal leadership contender Mark Carney, who declared that "if we can no longer rely on American neighbours, we must diversify our trading relationships and build new sources of jobs and growth."
But for now, the stats are grim. Over the last two decades at least, between 75 and 80 per cent of Canadian exports have gone to our neighbours south of the border. No other country has surpassed five per cent in that same time period.
Meanwhile, less than 20 per cent of U.S. exports go to Canada, even though we're America's biggest trading partner.
"We've been talking about it forever," said Catherine Fortin LeFaivre, the vice president of International Policy and Global Partnerships at the Canadian Chamber of Commerce. "There's nothing like an emergency, though, or urgency like the type of crisis that we're facing now, to really force people to really innovate ... perhaps this will be the catalyst for that."
The task ahead is much easier said than done. Part of it is complacency. But despite booming efforts from successive governments to diversify our trade with new agreements, Canadian companies face regulatory burdens and the country lacks the infrastructure required to expand its overseas exports.
While trade experts who spoke to the Star say there is no magic number to aim for in a quest to reduce our reliance on the United States, moving the needle by just a few percentage points would come at a steep cost.
That leads to a situation where it may always be easiest and most natural to trade with the U.S. Lilly said that can also be attributed to a trade theory called the "gravity model," which says that "the closer a country is to you, and the larger it is in GDP, the more likely you'll trade with it."
But there are other challenges, too. The Canadian economy has a high concentration of small- to medium-sized businesses that don't have the capacity to trade at all. Of the fraction who are able to export their products, 65 per cent only export to the U.S.
"It's not like you can just flip a switch and suddenly be exporting to a very different market on the other side of the world," Lilly said.
Canada is set to have several pipelines opening in the coming years, including B.C.'s Coastal Gaslink, and in the face of the Trump threats, there is renewed talk of breaking down interprovincial trade barriers.
Indeed, on Wednesday, Ford said it "it's unacceptable that we don't have (the) Energy East pipeline coming out to Ontario and out to New Brunswick, and again, the Northern Gateway going out through B.C. (so) we can ship our crude oil around the world."
Duncan Munn, the CEO of Elevate Finance, a company that supports Canadian exporters, said his phone has been buzzing with businesses now ready to explore trading beyond the U.S..
"We've always taken the assumption that we're going to have, you know, largely free and unfettered access to the American market. I do think that continues, but it may not be as free, and it may come with new costs," Munn told the Star. "I think this is an inflection point for Canadian business, and the risk posed is very real."
Fortin LeFaivre said that while businesses are starting to think more about the long-term risks due to the challenge with the U.S. right now, there is still uncertainty about what happens next.
She said the conversation a few months ago "was all about doubling down on working with the U.S., and decoupling from China and such states."
"The context is shifting quickly, and it's not to say that if we diversify the other markets, there aren't other risks," she said.
With files from Robert Benzie
CAPTION: A truck carries a cargo container at the Port of Vancouver Centerm container terminal as others are stacked under gantry cranes, in Vancouver, in 2022.

Document 316 of 500

Why doesn't Doug Ford care about funding colleges and universities? Because you don't care either

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/why-doesnt-doug-ford-care-about-funding-colleges/docview/3159091359/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Why+doesn%27t+Doug+Ford+care+about+funding+colleges+and+universities%3F+Because+you+don%27t+care+either&amp;title=The+Hamilton+Spectator+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2025-01-24&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Hamilton+Spectator+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Here's one issue you'll hardly hear about on the campaign trail ahead:
The crisis in post-secondary education.
Colleges and universities are core to the knowledge economy of tomorrow, but you wouldn't know it today. Most days, you'll hear Doug Ford talk about our highly skilled workforce as Ontario's biggest draw, yet nothing ranks lower than higher education in our political discourse.
There's a reason campuses across Ontario are forever underfunded by the premier: Ford's government sees little political payoff from investing more funds and, more importantly, senses little electoral risk from putting less money down.
So why don't political parties care more about Ontario's 24 colleges and 23 universities?
Here's a better question: Why don't more voters care more about the crisis on campus?
Yes, students surely worry, but they don't vote in large enough numbers to count. True, parents certainly care, but you'll never see them rise up and rally for their grown children as they do for younger kids in kindergarten or high school.
On the eve of an election, the results are already in for higher education: It doesn't rate as a vote-determining issue, which is why politicians don't put it on the agenda.
And so when colleges lay off staff, as they did again this month, it barely makes news. When professors threaten to strike, as they did again the other day, it's old news.
When universities cut their courses, as they did this semester, it's more of the same retrenchment. When our institutions of higher learning go deeper in debt, as they have in the last year, there's no one to dig them out.
How did we get here? Perennial funding shortfalls are nothing new, but Ford's Progressive Conservatives did what no other government had done before.
Shortly after taking power in 2018, with colleges and universities starved for money, the premier further reduced their cash flow by ordering every campus to cut tuition by 10 per cent. It was a classic populist gesture from a premier who plays pocketbook politics, knowing that affordability matters most to voters.
But those tuition dollars weren't his to cut - the money was remitted by students. More to the point, his government didn't consider making up the difference to keep universities and colleges whole, leaving them in a deeper fiscal hole.
It only got worse. In the six years since, the premier has compelled post-secondary institutions to keep tuition fees frozen at that same reduced level, while inflation rose steadily year after year.
That's no way to run an institution, as even the minister of post-secondary institutions, Nolan Quinn, surely understands. After all, Quinn is a college graduate and the proud owner of a Dairy Queen in Eastern Ontario.
If you'd told Quinn that headquarters had just dictated a 10 per cent price decrease in chocolate dipped cones from afar and expected local Dairy Queen outlets to make up the difference, he'd swallow hard. If you told Quinn that DQ HQ had also demanded a perpetual price freeze for the Misty Freeze slushy, he'd wonder how his own franchise would stay solvent.
Yet Quinn is just following orders from head office at Queen's Park. He is the fourth minister in six years, unable to push back against a premier determined to cut higher education down to size.
Even a government-appointed panel of experts, hand-picked to avoid embarrassing the Tories, dissented from the party line: A blue-ribbon panel recommended a major tuition increase but the government balked last year, offering only a short-term bailout that ignored warnings of a long-term flame-out.
Instead, Ford put forward what amounts to a perverse Ponzi scheme: Underfunding (and undercharging) for domestic students, cross-subsidized by overcharging (and overcrowding) for a seemingly unlimited number of foreign students whose endless pool of revenues would keep campuses afloat.
It was never sustainable, nor desirable. As the money rolled in, colleges recruited so many international students that they emerged as the majority on many campuses - shifting the demographic balance in the classroom.
Colleges (unlike universities) are on the provincial balance sheet, so red ink on campuses dragged down the overall Ontario deficit. By turning public colleges into profit centres, the Tories buffed up their own annual budget.
But a sudden surge in foreign students overwhelmed inner city housing supplies. Recurring visa abuses sparked a public clamour about a distorted agenda.
When the federal government belatedly cracked down on a student visa program out of control, reducing the allocations for each province, the fiscal fallout was inevitable. The sudden declines in foreign enrolments amounted to shock treatment for campuses that were already financially stressed, and are now doubly distressed.
Ultimately, it all came crashing down - leaving colleges and universities in the lurch. And yet the premier has never paid a political price .
Today, with a campaign perhaps just days away, Ford's Tories are riding high in public opinion polls - not least because they have kept tuition fees unsustainably low.
(Disclosure: I'm a senior fellow at the Dais at Toronto Metropolitan University and also at the University of Toronto's Munk School of Global Affairs and Public Policy).
CAPTION: Premier Doug Ford attends an announcement in Toronto on Jan. 13 2025.

Worried about Doug Ford pulling your favourite American liquor off LCBO shelves? Here's some Ontario brands to try

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/worried-about-doug-ford-pulling-your-favourite/docview/3159510721/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Worried+about+Doug+Ford+pulling+your+favourite+American+liquor+off+LCBO+shelves%3F+Here%27s+some+Ontario+brands+to+try&amp;title=The+Hamilton+Spectator+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2025-01-23&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Hamilton+Spectator+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: With all this talk of United States President Donald Trump putting tariffs on some Canadian goods, Ontario Premier Doug Ford is ready to retaliate.
In January, Ford threatened to retaliate against the proposed U.S. tariffs by removing American liquor from LCBO shelves.
For consumers, it would mean finding a replacement for their Jack Daniels Tennessee Whiskey or SKYY Vodka.
We contacted the LCBO to inquire about a list of Ontario-made products customers can try if their favourite product is removed from the shelf, or just to support a locally made liquor.
Here are their recommendations, but store availability varies across the province:
Gin
Dixon's Wicked Blueberry Gin, $41.95
Anticipate light notes of juniper and a hint of blueberry. It's medium-bodied and fruity with a long-lasting finish. It's best served with blueberry pie.
Dillon's Dry Gin $39.95
Expect moderately intense aromas and flavours of juniper, balsam, crisp pear and star anise on a dry, cleansing palate that finishes balanced and smooth.
Georgian Bay Gin $35.95
The nose is fragrant with aromas of juniper and citrus while the palate is dry and flavourful with pine and fresh lemon layered over notes of cinnamon and cardamon.
Whisky
Kavi Reserve Coffee Blended Whisky $32.45
Going beyond your standard flavoured whisky, this is a nuanced flavour experience that will complement a variety of cocktail recipes or serve neat with chocolate-based desserts.
Wolfhead Cinnamon Whisky $36.95
Wolfhead's Cinnamon Whisky is pale gold, with spicy cinnamon on the nose, followed by caramelized sweet flavours and a warm finish.
Dunrobin Canadian Whisky $44.95
A clear, pale amber whisky is a blend of wheat, corn, and rye, with clean and light aromas of butterscotch, caramel, burnt sugar and an undercurrent of cereal.
Wayne Gretzky Double Barrel Oaked Whisky $37.95
Initial aromas of vanilla, clove, toffee and coconut are complemented by notes of charred oak, caramel and rye spice.
Collingwood Whisky $33.95
Expect aromas of maple syrup, toasted cereal grain and caramel with complex flavours of roasted nuts and caramel balanced by citrus and a touch of spice followed by a warm mellow finish.
JP Wiser's Deluxe $32.45
An award-winning golden Canadian rye with aromas of sweet caramel, dried fruit and vanilla. Flavours of toasted grain, sweet cornmeal and vanilla are followed by a smooth, creamy finish.
Forty Creek Barrel Select $32.85
The original pours a medium amber and offers complex aromas and flavours of apricots, caramel, spice, vanilla, ginger and toasted oak, ending in a long smooth finish.
Canadian Club Premium $31.15
Pouring a medium gold you will find aromas of roasted almond, vanilla and pepper; with spicy vanilla, cornflakes and a faint nutty flavour on a medium weight palate with a long finish.
Vodka
Flyte Vodka $31.15
Crystal clear and clean, with notes of citrus and vanilla on the nose. The palate is smooth, with a sweet note and anise and black pepper tones on the finish.
Dillon's Vodka $34.95
Look for aromas and flavours of flower, banana, white pepper, grain and spice. It is medium-bodied and spicy/sweet, with a viscous mouthfeel and a long, warming finish.
Junction 56 Distillery Vodka $32.95
Using both corn and wheat for its base, the vodka is distilled to a high level of purity. The spirit shows aromas and flavours of citrus, pear and pepper with a silky mouthfeel and a hint of spice on the finish.
Polar Ice Vodka $31.15
Crafted from the purest water and grains, quadruple distilled and triple filtered for a clean, smooth taste.
Prince Igor Vodka $31.15
This premium vodka showcases subtle aromas and flavours while delivering a smooth and clean taste.
Tag No. 5 Vodka $32.15
Craft vodka made from locally grown Ontario sweet corn giving it a distinct hint of sweetness followed by subtle notes of lemon zest, caramel, and black licorice.
Top Shelf Vodka $34.15
It is light and fresh with a dry finish and rounded smoothness on the palate that makes it versatile for cocktails.
There are also several independent craft distilleries throughout Ontario that make their own liquor. Take a day trip to visit a local distillery to inquire about their process and any limited edition products they may have available.
For more details about what's available at an LCBO location near you, visit LCBO.com.

Head of Ontario Shipyards named as new Hamilton police board member

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/head-ontario-shipyards-named-as-new-hamilton/docview/3159510446/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Head+of+Ontario+Shipyards+named+as+new+Hamilton+police+board+member&amp;title=The+Hamilton+Spectator+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2025-01-23&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Hamilton+Spectator+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: A local businessperson is the latest addition to Hamilton's police board.
Shaun Padulo, president and CEO of Ontario Shipyards, was announced as the oversight body's new provincial appointee in a release Thursday. He will begin his two-year term Feb. 20.
"Shaun brings extensive leadership and governance experience to the board," an announcement from the board said. "As president and chief executive officer of Ontario Shipyards ... Shaun has successfully guided the organization as president since 2018, officially assuming the CEO role in 2022."
Padulo takes over the role from Frank Bennink, who left the board last spring amid new legislation that prohibits former auxiliary officers from sitting on police boards in jurisdictions where they once volunteered.
Padulo's decorated business career dates to 2005, when he navigated the Great Lakes aboard tugboats as a deckhand with McKeil Marine, a Burlington-based marine service provider. Later, Padulo took on commercial roles with renowned Dutch firms Dockwise Shipping B.V. and Boskalis Offshore B.V., sharpening his skills in logistics management, offshore, subsea inspection, repair and maintenance projects.
Padulo, who got his bachelor's degree at McMaster University, also sits on the board for Mohawk College and has been involved with community organization such as ProAction Cops and Kids, the board said.
"With strong ties to Hamilton, Shaun is honoured to contribute to his community as a member of the Hamilton police service board."
Sebastian Bron is a reporter with The Hamilton Spectator. sbron@thespec.com.
CAPTION: Ontario Shipyards President and CEO Shaun Padulo, pictured here in July 2023 at a funding announcement with Doug Ford, was named as the Hamilton police board's newest provincial appointee Thursday.

Jan. 23: Fans lose a musical hero, careful what you wish for and other letters to the editor

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/jan-23-fans-lose-musical-hero-careful-what-you/docview/3158722452/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Jan.+23%3A+Fans+lose+a+musical+hero%2C+careful+what+you+wish+for+and+other+letters+to+the+editor&amp;title=The+Hamilton+Spectator+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2025-01-23&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Hamilton+Spectator+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Losing a musical hero
Re: Garth Hudson, the last living member of The Band, dead at 87, Jan. 21
Sad to hear of the passing of Garth Hudson. As a huge Bob Dylan fan in the mid-1960s, the arrival of the band in 1967 was like a second coming for me. I was too young to get into The Golden Rail or The Grange when they cut their musical teeth in those Hamilton bars as The Hawks, but I made sure to catch them several times when they later toured as The Band. One night at the McMaster gym in 1970 was especially memorable. The boys took a well-deserved break between sets and I was standing in the main aisle awaiting their return when I felt a hand on my shoulder and heard a voice say "Excuse me." I turned and found myself face to face with Garth Hudson, one of my musical heroes. I stutteringly asked, "can I help you?" He smiled and said, "Well you can get out of the way. I have to get to the stage." RIP, Garth. Thanks for the memories.
Doug Foley, Hamilton
Hats off to columnist
Re: We all need Melania Trump's inauguration hat, Jan. 21
Vinay Menon deserves the Pulitzer Prize for humour with his article on Melania's hat! I am left so weak from laughing that I can't do the vacuuming - so many incredible comments wrapped up with a monumental finish. Describing Donald Trump as "strident and sclerotic" was simply brilliant. Congratulations, Vinay!
Jim Warren, Hamilton
No need for early election
Re: Doug Ford says a 'clear mandate' from Ontario voters is the best way to deal with Donald Trump, Jan. 20
It just blows me away how Doug Ford can dream up ways to waste our hard-earned tax dollars. Doug has a year and a half left in his term with a clear majority. Why would he call an expensive early election? He has a clear mandate and a year and a half to do something with it. I wish these politicians would stop politicizing every opportunity and just do their job.
Frank Jalsevac, Stoney Creek
Careful what you wish for
Wouldn't it be hilarious if Dougie pushed through this entirely unnecessary election and people - finally having had enough of his bull - did not vote him back in? Think about it.
Catherine Werner, Caledonia
Musk 'salute' is troubling
So Elon Musk is so excited about Trump's inauguration that he feels compelled to emphatically display his Nazi-like salute. Twice. So it clearly is not an accident. (I have also heard this salute referred to as a "Roman" salute, not coincidentally adopted by Mussolini and the Italian fascists, then later by the Nazis.)
When the next federal election rolls around later this year, I urge everyone to remember that Musk has given his stamp of approval to Pierre Poilievre and the Conservative Party. If the Conservatives should win, I wonder if Musk will take the time to come to Canada and extend his right arm to Pierre.
We've been warned.
Paul W. Bognar, Hamilton
Troubling implications
Elon Musk has not explicitly denied that his gesture during the Donald Trump inauguration was a Nazi salute, but anyone who witnessed it recognizes the troubling implications. It seems only a matter of time before this kind of display becomes a regular feature at Trump rallies. Once again, Musk has enabled and amplified the worst kinds of behaviour, likely to excuse it under the guise of "free speech" or dismiss it as a joke we shouldn't take seriously.
Such actions deserve unambiguous condemnation, not indifference disguised as humour or liberty.
Martin Sarabura, Ancaster
Trump wasting no time
Donald Trump is now in power and Americans are already experiencing Russian-style purges of government and military officials to accompany the massing of police at southern borders. And soon the president will be declaring economic warfare on Canada, an ally and wonderful neighbour. Let's hope that the water bombers sent to California from various provinces don't get shot down along with all the environmental protections that will be history in the great United States.
Jim Warren, Hamilton
More of the same please
Hamilton graciously hosted "Pretty Woman - The Musical" on Jan. 10 at FirstOntario Concert Hall. What a fabulous show! The entire production was outstanding. We are all familiar with the storyline from the original movie, but it really came to life with wonderful music, singing and dancing. It was completely comparable to any musical hosted by our Toronto neighbours. Please bring more entertainment with this calibre to Hamilton!
Kathy Kislinsky, Hamilton
Budgets are about trade-offs
Re: A wish list for Doug Ford, Jan. 20
The letter writer that advocates for the advancement of women, elegantly listed social programs that should be given provincial funding priority, but it truly is a wish list. There is a cost to everything and there are many groups pushing and pulling for the same dollars.
The tax burden placed on all Ontarians is also a high priority. How much more taxes are you willing to pay, what other programs should have their funding cut to support all this wish list? Health care is 39.6 per cent, education 23.2 per cent and children and social services 9.3 per cent of total compensated expenses in the 2024-25 budget.
Public sector employment in Ontario is 34.4 per cent on average higher than the private sector (StatCan 2021 data). So, perhaps we could look at public sector wage and compensation for the extra money? Right, I didn't think that would go over well.
All budget proposals are a balance in trade-offs; infrastructure programs require maintenance and the longer they are delayed the more expensive they get. Large scale multi-year projects have been neglected or delayed for years.
Your wish list is noted, but until Ontario can get public sector compensated payouts under control and we get more private sector investment and partnerships, we can stop blaming the Ford government for everything. If someone can come up with workable solution that mitigates huge tax increases, balances social, medical, education and infrastructure programs and the "wish list," I'm 100 per cent in.
Troy Smillie, Hannon
CAPTION: Musician Garth Hudson, the Windsor, Ont.-born multi-instrumentalist who was one of the original members of influential '60s and '70s rock group The Band, has died at age 87. A reader recalls a brief encounter with Hudson at a local concert.
