Document 320 of 500

Canada leaning toward matching tariffs: Alberta's Smith hopes premiers will focus on 'constructive, proactive diplomacy' with U.S.

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/canada-leaning-toward-matching-tariffs/docview/3158397153/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Canada+leaning+toward+matching+tariffs%3A+Alberta%27s+Smith+hopes+premiers+will+focus+on+%27constructive%2C+proactive+diplomacy%27+with+U.S.&amp;title=The+Spectator&amp;issn=11899417&amp;date=2025-01-23&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.13&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Spectator&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Canada appears to be leaning toward hitting the United States with matching tariffs if President Donald Trump makes good on his threats, but not all of the country's premiers support that approach.
The premiers met virtually with Prime Minister Justin Trudeau and other federal officials on Wednesday morning to talk about Trump's early moves as president and the looming threat of tariffs, which could come as soon as next week.
Ontario Premier Doug Ford, who currently chairs the Council of the Federation, said Wednesday that premiers want to ensure Canada has a "solid plan."
"We know these tariffs are coming Feb. 1. We need to match those tariffs dollar-for-dollar, tariff-for-tariff, and make sure that it hurts the Americans as much as it hurts Canadians," he told reporters.
The federal Liberal cabinet spent two days at a retreat this week, waiting to see what Trump might do and hammering out the details of its response.
The shifts in Trump's rhetoric and timelines are par for the course, Trudeau told reporters on Tuesday. He said his government has plans for a variety of scenarios and is ready to retaliate, and that he supports "the principle of dollar-for-dollar matching tariffs."
Saskatchewan Premier Scott Moe said Wednesday that while he is on board with the Team Canada approach, "broad-based tariffs, dollar-for-dollar tariffs with the U.S. is not in Canadians' best interest, nor should export tariffs ever be considered by our government."
Export taxes on energy were the key sticking point for Alberta Premier Danielle Smith after last week's meeting. She refused to sign a joint statement coming out of that meeting because the federal government would not exempt energy exports from its list of possible retaliatory actions.
Smith posted on social media on Wednesday that the meeting was "more positive this week" and that there's a growing consensus that Canada needs to commit to improving security at the border and boosting defence spending.
Smith said there was also "general agreement on the need to focus more on constructive, proactive diplomacy with U.S. lawmakers rather than escalating rhetoric" at Wednesday's meeting.
The Canadian Press
CAPTION:

CREDIT: Sarah Ritchie The Canadian Press

Document 321 of 500

Ford looking out for himself

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/ford-looking-out-himself/docview/3158397007/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=unknown&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Ford+looking+out+for+himself&amp;title=The+Spectator&amp;issn=11899417&amp;date=2025-01-23&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.16&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Spectator&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Canada is not yet the 51st state, and Ontarians never voted for the U.S. president. Why should Donald Trump's musings now determine when we next vote in a provincial election?
Our premier well understands the power of Ontario's antipathy to America's bullying over tariffs. Doug Ford wants to harness the visceral anger over Trump's provocations, the better to harvest the angry votes here at home.
Ever since Trump won the last U.S. election, the premier has been promising to save the day. Now it seems his plan all along was to save the date for the next Ontario election, that he might win the day for himself.
The premier's rhetorical juggling act is pulling him in two directions.
On the one hand, he bangs the war drums for the battle ahead. In the next breath, he decries the vacuum of power in Ottawa. Then, with a straight face, the premier proposes to dissolve the legislature, triggering more uncertainty at Queen's Park. How that strengthens Canada's hand he has yet to explain.
The premier governs with a massive majority that runs until June 2026 under Ontario's fixed election date law. Yet he contrives to seek a newer, bigger mandate 16 months ahead of schedule, without a credible pitch.
Oh, Ford keeps repeating his "mandate" mantra, as if he needs a vote of confidence from the people. But for what? To send a signal to Trump that Ontario stands united? While our rival politicians spend the next month on the campaign trail taking partisan potshots at one another?
The problem with Ford's premise is that the opposition is onside. The NDP's Marit Stiles and the Liberals' Bonnie Crombie have publicly pledged their co-operation in standing united against tariffs and any economic fallout.
Ford boasts about rising above party lines when he's conferring with his first ministers. Why seek contrived political combat with his provincial opposition rivals on the campaign trail at the very same time? Not to mention that setting tariffs is hardly a provincial prerogative. Or that fending off American attacks is properly in federal jurisdiction.
Justin Trudeau is now a lame duck prime minister, which has created more space for Ford to step in as a persuasive advocate on American TV making the case for Canada. Why convert Queen's Park into a caretaker government, requiring him as premier to step back from strategic decision-making while senior civil servants hold sway during the campaign period?
The premier has won plaudits across the spectrum for calling for national unity and hard-line strategy to combat the threat of 25 per cent tariffs. He has articulated Canada's case and Ontario's position with passion and persuasion, at home and abroad. Why let it unravel by being so suddenly unserious?
A truly temperate leader would resist the temptation to put his personal and political fortunes ahead of the province's fate, merely because he's riding high in public opinion polls at our lowest point. If we are really facing an unprecedented economic crisis that requires unity in our polity, an unnecessary election is the height of folly and hypocrisy.
We still don't know if the tariff talk is deadly serious or empty rhetoric. If Trump clarifies that Canada won't be targeted, the premier will have talked himself out of an election. And if the president continues to troll us on tariffs with studied ambiguity, how will Ford keep whipping up anti-Trump fever for the duration of a sustained 29-day campaign?
Either way, voters will quickly notice there's no discernible discord among rival leaders about how to respond, turning the tariff question into a point of agreement in any debate. The campaign will inevitably shift to local issues that people refuse to forget about - from a housing emergency to hallway health care and from allegations of impropriety on the Greenbelt to frustrations over unfinished work on transit.
We need the Ontario government on the job, fighting to protect the jobs Ford says are imperilled. What we don't need is a premier campaigning for his own job.
Martin Regg Cohn covers provincial politics for Torstar.
CAPTION:

Letters

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=unknown&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Letters&amp;title=The+Spectator&amp;issn=11899417&amp;date=2025-01-23&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.17&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Spectator&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Losing a musical hero
Re: Garth Hudson, the last living member
of The Band, dead at 87, Jan. 21
Sad to hear of the passing of Garth Hudson. As a huge Bob Dylan fan in the mid-1960s, the arrival of the band in 1967 was like a second coming for me. I was too young to get into The Golden Rail or The Grange when they cut their musical teeth in those Hamilton bars as The Hawks, but I made sure to catch them several times when they later toured as The Band. One night at the McMaster gym in 1970 was especially memorable. The boys took a well-deserved break between sets and I was standing in the main aisle awaiting their return when I felt a hand on my shoulder and heard a voice say "Excuse me." I turned and found myself face to face with Garth Hudson, one of my musical heroes. I stutteringly asked, "can I help you?" He smiled and said, "Well you can get out of the way. I have to get to the stage." RIP, Garth. Thanks for the memories.
Doug Foley, Hamilton
Hats off to columnist Menon
Re: We all need Melania Trump's inauguration hat, Jan. 21
Vinay Menon deserves the Pulitzer Prize for humour with his article on Melania's hat! I am left so weak from laughing that I can't do the vacuuming - so many incredible comments wrapped up with a monumental finish. Describing Donald Trump as "strident and sclerotic" was simply brilliant. Congratulations, Vinay!
Jim Warren, Hamilton
No need for early election
Re: Ford says a 'clear mandate' from Ontario voters
is the best way to deal with Donald Trump, Jan. 20
It just blows me away how Doug Ford can dream up ways to waste our hard-earned tax dollars. Doug has a year and a half left in his term with a clear majority. Why would he call an expensive early election? He has a clear mandate and a year and a half to do something with it. I wish these politicians would stop politicizing every opportunity and just do their job.
Frank Jalsevac, Stoney Creek
Careful what you wish for
Wouldn't it be hilarious if Dougie pushed through this entirely unnecessary election and people - finally having had enough of his bull - did not vote him back in? Think about it.
Catherine Werner, Caledonia
Musk 'salute' is troubling
So Elon Musk is so excited about Trump's inauguration that he feels compelled to emphatically display his Nazi-like salute. Twice. So it clearly is not an accident.
When the next federal election rolls around later this year, I urge everyone to remember that Musk has given his stamp of approval to Pierre Poilievre and the Conservative Party. If the Conservatives should win, I wonder if Musk will take the time to come to Canada and extend his right arm to Pierre.
We've been warned.
Paul W. Bognar, Hamilton
Amplifying the worst behaviour
Elon Musk has not explicitly denied that his gesture during the Donald Trump inauguration was a Nazi salute, but anyone who witnessed it recognizes the troubling implications. It seems only a matter of time before this kind of display becomes a regular feature at Trump rallies.
Once again, Musk has enabled and amplified the worst kinds of behaviour, likely to excuse it under the guise of "free speech" or dismiss it as a joke we shouldn't take seriously. Such actions deserve unambiguous condemnation, not indifference disguised as humour or liberty.
Martin Sarabura, Ancaster
Trump wasting no time
Donald Trump is now in power and Americans are already experiencing Russian-style purges of government and military officials to accompany the massing of police at southern borders. And soon the president will be declaring economic warfare on Canada, an ally and wonderful neighbour.
Let's hope that the water bombers sent to California from various provinces don't get shot down along with all the environmental protections that will be history in the great United States.
Jim Warren, Hamilton
More of the same please
Hamilton graciously hosted "Pretty Woman - The Musical" on Jan. 10 at FirstOntario Concert Hall. What a fabulous show! The entire production was outstanding. We are all familiar with the storyline from the original movie, but it really came to life with wonderful music, singing and dancing. It was completely comparable to any musical hosted by our Toronto neighbours.
Please bring more entertainment with this calibre to Hamilton!
Kathy Kislinsky, Hamilton

Document 323 of 500

Canada could fight Trump's economic threats with counter tariffs. Here's what American-made products could cost more

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/canada-could-fight-trumps-economic-threats-with/docview/3158070095/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Canada+could+fight+Trump%27s+economic+threats+with+counter+tariffs.+Here%27s+what+American-made+products+could+cost+more&amp;title=The+Hamilton+Spectator+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2025-01-22&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Hamilton+Spectator+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: U.S. President Donald Trump has indicated "he thinks" he will impose 25 per cent tariffs on Canadian and Mexican goods starting Feb. 1.
Canadian officials, including Prime Minister Justin Trudeau, have indicated all options are on the table to fight impacts on Canada's economy, including counter tariffs against U.S. imports.
Canada could begin by compiling a list of American-made imports to apply tariffs.
Marvin Ryder, associate professor of marketing at McMaster University, noted prices won't rise immediately for Canadians if Trump imposes tariffs. Tariffs are paid by importers, he noted, so Americans would pay more for Canadian products as fees are passed down to consumers.
But Canada could respond to Trump's tariffs by singling out a list of American-made consumer products for counter tariffs.
Ryder said Canada could apply tariffs on so-called "luxury" items like U.S. alcohol and chocolate, without further harming Canadians. Canada would never slap a tariff on American oil, for example, because we need it.
Here are eight products imported from the U.S. that could cost more this year if Canada enacts counter tariffs.
Alcohol
Canada ranked second among the top export markets for U.S. spirits in 2023, according to the Distilled Spirits Council, accounting for US$262 million.
"If you are someone who really likes drinking Jim Beam whiskey, stock up now," Ryder suggested.
While Ontario Premier Doug Ford has said he could pull U.S. alcohol imports from Ontario store shelves, Ryder said a better strategy would include allowing it to be sold with steep tariffs, boosting revenue for the LCBO while encouraging consumers to purchase lower-priced domestic alternatives.
Fresh fruit and vegetables
While Ontario farmers have adapted to harsh winters by growing tomatoes, cucumbers and even strawberries indoors, our reliance on imports can be unavoidable.
"At this time of the year, it's winter and we bring in fresh fruits and vegetables," Ryder noted. "Some of those come from Mexico or farther down in Central and South America, but some of them come from Florida and California."
According to the U.S. Foreign Agricultural Service, Canada received more than US$1.9 billion in American fresh vegetables and US$1.7 billion in fresh fruit imports from south of the border in 2023.
Fruit juice
Canada imported US$596 million worth of fruit juice, including US$385 million from the U.S., in 2023.
Given its status as a staple item, Ryder said he would be "a little surprised" if Canada tariffed Florida orange juice, but it could be an option if Trump enacts blanket 25 per cent tariffs on Canadian imports.
Automobiles
As Ryder notes, Trump would be wise to exempt the automotive industry from any proposed tariffs, which would elevate prices, but it's not clear if the U.S. president will do so.
Ryder said the average North American automobile crosses the border eight or more times during production, as inputs are produced in both nations. A 25 per cent tariff would lead to higher vehicle prices in both Canada and the U.S.
Breakfast cereal
Go to the cupboard, pick up your favourite breakfast cereal and look at the fine print. There's a good chance you'll find "Product of USA" somewhere on the box. According to the U.S. Department of Agriculture, the top consumer-oriented export category to Canada in 2023 was bakery goods, cereals and pasta, with nearly US$2.8 billion in sales. That's an increase of 24 per cent since 2019.
Chocolate and sweets
Citing a trend from 2019 to 2023, the U.S. Department of Agriculture notes: "Canadian indulgence for sweets in the cold north remains strong, as chocolate and cocoa product exports have grown by 23 per cent from $713 million (USD) to $878 million spanning the last five years."
Confectionery exports were also up 41 per cent during that time, surpassing US$447 million.
Canadian counter tariffs could raise the prices of these imports for Canadians, unless, of course, Canadians satisfy their cravings with locally produced confectioneries.
Beauty products
Canada is ranked eighth in the world for imports of beauty products, taking in US$1.74 billion in 2022, according to the Observatory of Economic Complexity, (OEC). Of that figure, the United States was the fastest-growing region of origin.
Canada imports more beauty products than we export, according to 2022 figures. Canada ranks 11th in the world in exports, at US$1.64 billion in 2022.
Seafood
In 2023, the United States was by far the top exporter of fish and seafood to Canada, according to Fisheries and Oceans Canada, with a total value of $1.41 billion. The top species imported were salmon ($949 million), shrimp and prawn ($702 million), tuna ($290 million) and lobster ($283 million), accounting for 49 per cent of Canada's fish and seafood imports by value.
CAPTION: Go to the cupboard, pick up your favourite breakfast cereal and look at the fine print. There's a good chance you'll find "Product of USA" somewhere on the box. According to the U.S. Department of Agriculture, the top consumer-oriented export category to Canada in 2023 was bakery goods, cereals, and pasta, with nearly $2.8 billion USD in sales. If the product category is chosen for counter tariffs, Canadians should expect to pay more.

Document 324 of 500

Making a case for becoming the 51st state

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/making-case-becoming-51st-state/docview/3158069973/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Making+a+case+for+becoming+the+51st+state&amp;title=The+Hamilton+Spectator+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2025-01-22&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Hamilton+Spectator+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: "Not a snowball's chance in hell" of becoming the 51st state? Think of the advantages.
You would decrease your life expectancy while doubling your cost for health care and tripling it for prescription drugs. You would lose 3.4 years of life, if female, but males win out with a loss of 4.3 years. You could have the uniquely personal experience of bankruptcy arising from catastrophic illness or injury in the face of denied coverage.
You would increase the availability of weapons for personal use to kill yourself, your neighbours, your co-workers or, for a lark, a random collection of people gathered for social, religious or business reasons or just out for a walk on a Sunday afternoon. Our gun ownership, relatively high by international standards, is about one third the size of our southern neighbours and needs boosting.
You could eliminate concerns about the importation of handguns from the U.S. If we now manufactured the estimated 85 per cent of handguns imported illegally from the U.S. and contributing to crimes in Ontario, the challenge of gun running would disappear. Think of the investment opportunities for producing threatening products and nudging our trade imbalances in the right direction by illegally exporting to Mexico.
You would enjoy seeing our incarceration rate tick upwards. At 127 people per 100,000, we lag far behind our neighbour (541 per 100,000). We should be able catch-up by some basic tweaks to the justice system. Let's take to heart the idea that "more is better" (death sentences, jail time) and get behind the appointment of judges lusting to punish the poor, marginalized and destitute - people not like us. Think too of the investment opportunities in prison construction that will address our housing crisis.
You would have the opportunity to invade countries that piqued your interest because of something we wanted from them or didn't like. In the extreme, this might mean killing and displacing hundreds of thousands of people. The upside: none of our leaders would be tagged for war crimes. They might utter a silent "Oops," but get over it with lucrative speaking engagements and award ceremonies.
If you were lucky enough to be among the richest one per cent, you would bear the heavy responsibility of controlling 35 per cent of the country's wealth and welcome a whopping tax cut. There would be no concern about living in a place with the highest level of income inequality among post-industrial nations - it's just business and capital finding a proper balance.
You could welcome an electoral system that grasps the idea of democracy - one person, one vote - then delights in disenfranchising citizens, using "science" applied by the party in power to configure electoral boundaries which clump voters in a way that makes it possible to "win" elections with an overall minority. Then there is the powerful senate with two members from each state. Based on population, one vote in Wyoming is equivalent to 67 votes in California.
You could label any attempt to improve the health and life quality of citizens as communism while making friendly with bullies and kleptocrats. You may now claim that human agency is the wellspring of your future as a whimsical billionaire inhabiting 30,000 square feet with an ocean view or a rickety pauper living in a garbage can
There is great hope: becoming the 51st state would make us No. 1 in population size with a land mass full of treasure and much larger than all the other states combined. Swearing fealty to Donald Trump, you would be insulated from tariffs, no longer dissed by friends, well positioned to increase green-house gas emissions and, under presidential negligence, able to double our mortality rate in the next pandemic.
What's not to like?
CAPTION: By becoming the 51st state, Canadians could gain the ability to invade countries that piqued their interest because of something we wanted from them or didn't like, Michael H. Boyle writes.
CREDIT: Michael H. Boyle

Document 325 of 500

Ford already has a mandate to invest in Ontario: Province's attention and dollars have been poured into populist nonsense nobody asked for

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/ford-already-has-mandate-invest-ontario/docview/3157827728/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=unknown&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Ford+already+has+a+mandate+to+invest+in+Ontario%3A+Province%27s+attention+and+dollars+have+been+poured+into+populist+nonsense+nobody+asked+for&amp;title=The+Spectator&amp;issn=11899417&amp;date=2025-01-22&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.10&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Spectator&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: In response to Donald Trump's threat to impose high tariffs on Canadian products, Premier Doug Ford said he might have to call an early election to secure a mandate to protect Ontario's economy.
That's a bad excuse to call an early election.
It's like firefighters saying they must ring the doorbell and ask permission to put out a fire and save the people trapped inside the house.
The current government already has a mandate to invest in Ontario's economy and people. Given the province's stable finances, it also had the means to do so.
It simply chooses to stash money away and spend it on vain populist measures.
Take the first years of the COVID-19 pandemic, for example.
Ottawa picked up the tab for 92 per cent of emergency measures, giving provinces the fiscal room to invest in public services people needed more than ever.
Instead, at the start of 2021, amid high death counts and economic turmoil, Ontario was sitting on $6.4 billion of unspent COVID emergency funds. The job was obvious. The money was there. But Ford chose not to spend it on Ontarians.
Fast forward to today, and we will see the same pattern.
The provincial government's revenue is anticipated to rise from $205.7 billion in 2024 to $217.4 billion in 2025 - a solid 5.7 per cent increase.
Spending is set to rise by 2.6 per cent, or less than half the revenue increase.
Does the current government have the mandate to expand child-care provision, tackle the colossal school repair backlog, reduce emergency room waiting times and assist the more than 100,000 Torontonians relying on food banks and 80,000 Ontarians experiencing homelessness?
It does.
Yet, that's not the focus.
Year in and year out, the Ontario government's attention and dollars have been poured into populist and nonsense measures nobody asked for.
It's a long list.
Let's look only at the three most recent ones.
Last May, the Ontario government announced it would spend $225 million to breach a contract with the Beer Store and speed up the expansion of alcohol sales in the province. The measure is expected to result in higher alcohol consumption and lower sales tax revenues.
Last September, Ford vowed to build a traffic tunnel under Highway 401, even though Highway 407 is highly underutilized.
The project is already consuming tonnes of planning hours and political attention that could have been invested in things Ontarians actually need.
Last October, the Ford government announced it would send a $200 cheque to every Ontarian, rich and poor, for a total cost of $3.2 billion.
Targeting the cash transfers to low-income families and investing the rest in universal public services would have been easy, but less popular.
If the house is on fire, people expect firefighters and majority governments to go in and deal with it.
They don't need to ask for a mandate to do what they are supposed to do.
Ricardo Tranjan is a senior researcher on housing
and social policy with the
Canadian Centre for Policy Alternatives' Ontario office.
CAPTION:

CREDIT: Ricardo Tranjan Ricardo Tranjan is a senior researcher on housing and social policy with the Canadian Centre for Policy Alternatives' Ontario office.;

Document 326 of 500

Ford may call Ontario election as soon as next week

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/ford-may-call-ontario-election-as-soon-next-week/docview/3157827314/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Ford+may+call+Ontario+election+as+soon+as+next+week&amp;title=The+Spectator&amp;issn=11899417&amp;date=2025-01-22&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.6&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Spectator&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Doug Ford's governing Progressive Conservatives are eyeing a snap provincial election call as early as next Wednesday, sources told the Toronto Star.
The premier, who signalled he needs a "clear mandate" to spend the tens of billions of dollars in stimulus funds to keep the province's economy afloat after U.S. President Donald Trump's tariffs hit next month, is looking at sending voters to the polls on Feb. 27 or March 6.
That would entail him visiting Lt. Gov. Edith Dumont on either Jan. 29 or Feb. 5 to dissolve the legislature and issue the writs for an election that will cost between $150 million and $175 million to run.
Even though an Ontario vote is not scheduled until June 2026, and Ford already has a stable majority government, the premier huddled with his top aides Tuesday to determine next moves.
There's added urgency because the window for a provincial election is narrow - and not just because Trump tariffs could cripple Ontario's economy.
The Tories hope Liberal activists will be too busy in the coming weeks helping on the March 9 federal leadership race to volunteer on the provincial campaign.
They would also prefer to face voters before a national election expected this spring that polls suggest federal Conservative Leader Pierre Poilievre is poised to win. That's because Ford's PCs are aware that Ontario voters have historically opted for different parties in power at Queen's Park and in Ottawa at the same time, which means a Poilievre victory could be bad for them.
CAPTION: Ontario Premier Doug Ford

CREDIT: Robert Benzie and Rob Ferguson Toronto Star

Document 327 of 500

The 'perks' of being America's 51st state

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/perks-being-americas-51st-state/docview/3157827194/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=unknown&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=The+%27perks%27+of+being+America%27s+51st+state&amp;title=The+Spectator&amp;issn=11899417&amp;date=2025-01-22&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.11&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Spectator&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: "Not a snowball's chance in hell" of becoming the 51st state? Think of the advantages.
You would decrease your life expectancy while doubling your cost for health care and tripling it for prescription drugs. You would lose 3.4 years of life, if female, but males win out with a loss of 4.3 years. You could have the uniquely personal experience of bankruptcy arising from catastrophic illness or injury in the face of denied coverage.
You would increase the availability of weapons for personal use to kill yourself, your neighbours, your co-workers or, for a lark, a random collection of people gathered for social, religious or business reasons or just out for a walk on a Sunday afternoon. Our gun ownership, relatively high by international standards, is about one third the size of our southern neighbours and needs boosting.
You could eliminate concerns about the importation of handguns from the U.S. If we now manufactured the estimated 85 per cent of handguns imported illegally from the U.S. and contributing to crimes in Ontario, the challenge of gun running would disappear. Think of the investment opportunities for producing threatening products and nudging our trade imbalances in the right direction by illegally exporting to Mexico.
You would enjoy seeing our incarceration rate tick upwards. At 127 people per 100,000, we lag far behind our neighbour (541 per 100,000). We should be able catch-up by some basic tweaks to the justice system. Let's take to heart the idea that "more is better" (death sentences, jail time) and get behind the appointment of judges lusting to punish the poor, marginalized and destitute - people not like us. Think too of the investment opportunities in prison construction that will address our housing crisis.
You would have the opportunity to invade countries that piqued your interest because of something we wanted from them or didn't like. In the extreme, this might mean killing and displacing hundreds of thousands of people. The upside: none of our leaders would be tagged for war crimes. They might utter a silent "Oops," but get over it with lucrative speaking engagements and award ceremonies.
If you were lucky enough to be among the richest one per cent, you would bear the heavy responsibility of controlling 35 per cent of the country's wealth and welcome a whopping tax cut. There would be no concern about living in a place with the highest level of income inequality among post-industrial nations - it's just business and capital finding a proper balance.
You could welcome an electoral system that grasps the idea of democracy - one person, one vote - then delights in disenfranchising citizens, using "science" applied by the party in power to configure electoral boundaries which clump voters in a way that makes it possible to "win" elections with an overall minority. Then there is the powerful senate with two members from each state. Based on population, one vote in Wyoming is equivalent to 67 votes in California.
You could label any attempt to improve the health and life quality of citizens as communism while making friendly with bullies and kleptocrats. You may now claim human agency is the wellspring of your future as a whimsical billionaire inhabiting 30,000 square feet with an ocean view or a rickety pauper living in a garbage can.
There is great hope: becoming the 51st state would make us No. 1 in population size with a land mass full of treasure and much larger than all the other states combined. Swearing fealty to Donald Trump, you would be insulated from tariffs, no longer dissed by friends, well positioned to increase green-house gas emissions and, under presidential negligence, able to double our mortality rate in the next pandemic.
What's not to like?
Professor emeritus
Michael H. Boyle is a retired McMaster University
psychiatric epidemiologist.
CAPTION: By becoming the 51st state, Canadians could gain the ability to invade countries that piqued their interest because of something we wanted from them or didn't like, Michael H. Boyle writes.Peter Lee Waterloo Region Record file photo

CREDIT: Michael H. Boyle Professor emeritus Michael H. Boyle is a retired McMaster University psychiatric epidemiologist.;

Subject: Capital punishment

Location: United States--US

Document 328 of 500

Letters

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/letters/docview/3157827182/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=unknown&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Letters&amp;title=The+Spectator&amp;issn=11899417&amp;date=2025-01-22&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.11&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Spectator&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Not interested in changing citizenship
Re: We still like Americans -
as neighbours, Jan. 20
A very well thought out article. I love going to the U.S.A. and visit historic sites along with some shopping. That being said, I do not want ever to be an American. I am a true Canadian who loves this country with all of its faults. Thank you Vallance Patrick for saying exactly what I was thinking.
Brenda Lee Kurpe, Hamilton
Proud to remain
loyal to Canada
Thank you, Vallance Patrick for your insightful letter explaining to our American friends our values and history which makes us different from you. And thank you for mentioning the Empire Loyalists and why they came to what was to be Canada. This group of refugees provided the values on which the nation of Canada was to become.
They planted the seed of what was to become our form of government: constitutional monarchy and the Parliamentary form of government, i.e. meaning law and order and good government.
I am the descendant of two Loyalist families both arriving in 1787. The Dennisses settled around Thorold and the Lymburners around Caistor. Both families fought in the War of 1812 with the Lincoln Militia at Queenston Heights and Lundy's Lane.
As Patrick noted, "We absolutely want no part of being a territory of your empire."
Edward Early, Hamilton
Reading the signs
on inauguration day
For those of us who believe there are no coincidences, just signs to be interpreted, I noted the following on inauguration day. Monday was one of the coldest inauguration days ever, the American flag was at half mast, and for good measure, on Sunday my cable music stream played Barry McGuire's "Eve of Destruction." It does not augur well for the coming months.
Paul Tavares, Hamilton
What happened
to common sense?
I promised myself that I would not watch the inauguration of Donald Trump, but as I passed the TV, I was rivetted to the recitation of his speech. It was not only the speech of a person suffering from some sort of a chemical imbalance, but someone who sees himself as the ruler of the world. This my folks is a dictator. Do you actually think the nations that he wishes to control are going to take his threats lying down? This happened decades ago, and we learned a very hard lesson from that.
You have to wonder whatever happened to common sense and decency in this crazy world we live in. Well, the American people have spoken - let's see where that takes them.
Linda Ferrie, Waterdown
Countries don't have 'friends'
Re: Washington no longer a friend, Jan. 21
Although I appreciate the sentiment expressed in the letter, it must be remembered by all that countries do not have "friends." They have "interests." When those interests are at odds with other countries, no amount of goodwill or historic ties matter. You can be certain that the Trump administration's interests in Canada are not based on friendship. Besides, we know that Donald Trump truly has no friends.
Andy Blackwood, Simcoe
We don't need
an early election
Doug Ford wants to call an election. Elections cost money. He does not have to waste money on an election, he has a majority for another year. But wasting money is what he likes to do: a quarter of a billion dollars to the Beer Store; $200 to each person in Ontario, billions spent to buy your vote. What about spending the money on health care, education and homes for the homeless? We don't need Ford wasting money on an unnecessary election.
Ian Martin, Hamilton

People: Trump, Donald J

Jan. 22: Reading the inauguration day signs, we don't need an early election and other letters to the editor

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/jan-22-reading-inauguration-day-signs-we-dont/docview/3157827180/se-2?accountid=15090

Full text: Reading the signs
For those of us who believe there are no coincidences, just signs to be interpreted, I noted the following on inauguration day. Monday was one of the coldest inauguration days ever, the American flag was at half mast, and for good measure, on Sunday my cable music stream played Barry McGuire's "Eve of Destruction." Ian Martin, Hamilton
What's the housing holdup?
At the Sept. 18, 2024, Hamilton City council meeting, the city committed to working with HATS to develop a tiny home village with a goal of completion by the end of 2024. HATS volunteers have constructed and are storing 15 of the planned 25 units and have raised donations of $800,000 to cover costs related to the tiny homes village and running costs through 2025. St. Matthew's House has agreed to run the tiny village and provide services there. When will the city provide a site for the tiny village and make use of this opportunity to house at least 25 people who are currently homeless? Would this not be a more positive way for the city to reduce tents in parks?
Nigel Holmes, Hamilton
CAPTION: U.S. President Donald Trump takes the oath of office on Monday. Countries don't have "friends," they have "interests," a reader points out. And you can be certain the Trump administration's interests in Canada are not based on friendship.

Document 330 of 500

Doug Ford may call Ontario election as early as next week: sources

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/doug-ford-may-call-ontario-election-as-early-next/docview/3158201952/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Doug+Ford+may+call+Ontario+election+as+early+as+next+week%3A+sources&amp;title=The+Hamilton+Spectator+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2025-01-21&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Hamilton+Spectator+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Doug Ford's governing Progressive Conservatives are eyeing a snap provincial election call as early as next Wednesday, sources told the Star.
The premier, who signalled he needs a "clear mandate" to spend the tens of billions of dollars in stimulus funds to keep the province's economy afloat after President Donald Trump's tariffs hit next month, is looking at sending voters to the polls on Feb. 27 or March 6.
That would entail him visiting Lieut. Gov. Edith Dumont on either Jan. 29 or Feb. 5 to dissolve the legislature and issue the writs for an election that will cost between $150 million and $175 million to run.
Even though an Ontario vote is not scheduled until June 2026, and Ford already has a stable majority government, the premier huddled with his top aides Tuesday to determine next moves.
Minutes after Trump said Monday night he would likely slap tariffs on Canadian and Mexican goods within days, the premier's chief of staff, Patrick Sackville, emailed Tory MPPs and ministerial chiefs of staff and told them to be prepared.
"Moments ago, on live television, the president openly commented about his plan to bring 25 per cent tariffs against Canada as of Feb. 1," wrote Sackville in separate internal missives obtained by the Star.
"Make no mistake: this is only the beginning of what will be a long, hard fight. Ontario's workers and businesses will look to us to step up with the financial aid and leadership they need to support them in outlasting and overcoming these existential threats," he wrote.
"As we enter a period of unparalleled economic risk and critical negotiations, our government will need a strong mandate from the people to stand up for Ontario with President Trump, Canada's federal government, and other provinces to ensure we can effectively respond to, outlast and, ultimately, win this fight," continued Sackville.
"The stronger the mandate the better."
That echoed what Ford had told reporters hours earlier - before Trump dropped the Feb. 1 bombshell - that he needs the backing of voters before launching a stimulus program to offset the potential loss of 500,000 jobs.
The premier's main rivals warned an early provincial election would play right into Trump's hands, given the political uncertainty in Ottawa in the wake of Prime Minister Justin Trudeau's pending resignation.
"A bully like Donald Trump, they sense weakness, and creating this insecurity in this moment is going to be a sign of weakness, not of strength," NDP Leader Marit Stiles told reporters Tuesday at Queen's Park.
"This is about (the premier) protecting his own job. That's why he sees this as an opportunity and not ... the threat that it really is," said Stiles, predicting "this won't just be an election about tariffs, this will be an election on Doug Ford's government and his record."
That's a reference to the ongoing RCMP investigation into the Tories' $8.28-billion Greenbelt land swap scandal, criticism that 2.5 million Ontarians don't have a family doctor, and lengthy emergency room wait times.
Ford also faces challenges over rising unemployment, a shortage of affordable housing and increasing homelessness, with tent encampments springing up in parks and vacant lots across Ontario.
In a speech Tuesday to the Rural Ontario Municipal Association, Liberal Leader Bonnie Crombie said "the answer to chaos is not more chaos," and urged Ford to serve his full four-year term instead of launching an election.
"Calling an early election is unnecessary, expensive and creates instability and uncertainty when we need a calm and firm resolve," said Crombie.
"It's a ridiculous argument that the premier needs special permission and a new mandate to do his job - the one he was elected to do."
One reason Ford wants to face voters soon is his party believes Liberals are distracted by their federal leadership race, which will pick a successor for Trudeau on March 9.
Because many Liberal activists volunteer to help both the federal and provincial parties, the Tories feel the Grits might be too preoccupied by that contest to help Crombie provincially.
Finally, the provincial Tories would like to head to the polls before a national election expected this spring that federal Conservative Leader Pierre Poilievre appears poised to win.
That's because Ford's PCs are aware that Ontario voters have historically opted for different parties in power at Queen's Park and in Ottawa at the same time, which means a Poilievre victory could be bad for them.
CAPTION: Prime Minister Justin Trudeau and Premier Doug Ford are shown at a new conference following a first ministers' meeting in Ottawa on Jan. 15, 2025.

Subject: Provincial elections; Leadership; Scandals; Voters; Prime ministers; Tariffs

2025 inauguration: Donald Trump signs first round of executive orders, issues pardons for Jan. 6 rioters
