Document 337 of 500

'They will feel the pain': Doug Ford threatens to pull U.S. booze off LCBO shelves if Trump's tariffs are enacted

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/they-will-feel-pain-doug-ford-threatens-pull-u-s/docview/3157221592/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=%27They+will+feel+the+pain%27%3A+Doug+Ford+threatens+to+pull+U.S.+booze+off+LCBO+shelves+if+Trump%27s+tariffs+are+enacted&amp;title=The+Hamilton+Spectator+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2025-01-20&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Hamilton+Spectator+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Premier Doug Ford made one final appeal Monday to U.S. President Donald Trump to reconsider his threats to impose 25 per cent tariffs on all Canadian imports, promising to yank U.S. alcohol from LCBO stores across Ontario.
Trump, who is being inaugurated at noon on Jan. 20, has promised to implement dozens of executive orders on his first day of office on key policy areas like border security and immigration. He previously stated on social media his intention to impose tariffs against Mexico and Canada, citing illegal immigration and the drug trade.
If tariffs enacted, Ford vies to pull U.S. booze from LCBO
Speaking at the Rural Ontario Municipal Association's (ROMA) annual conference in Toronto Monday morning, Ford said he will send a directive to the LCBO to "clear off every bit" of U.S. alcohol from the shelves if tariffs are enacted.
"Let's start promoting more Ontario-made wines and the vodkas and spirits," Ford said. "That's what we need to do."
Ford said the LCBO is the largest purchaser of alcohol in the world and cutting off the sale of U.S. products will hurt American producers.
"They will feel the pain," Ford added. "I will make sure I will communicate this to our other premiers. They will follow suit."
Ford reiterated his position that tariffs "would be an absolute disaster for Canadian and American workers," including in rural Ontario.
"Our economies are linked together and unnecessary U.S. tariffs will hurt workers on both sides of the border," Ford noted.
Nine million Americans wake up every morning to produce products for Ontario alone, Ford added.
Ford said 47 per cent of Ontario's high-grade nickel is shipped to the U.S. and Ontario-generated electricity powers 1.5 million homes in Michigan, New York and Minnesota.
Fortress Am-Can alliance
Ford said he hopes to ship more energy and goods to the U.S. through his Fortress Am-Can alliance, but only if Trump's tariff threats are averted.
In an earlier statement, Ford said the only thing standing in the way of a renewed strategic alliance between Canada and the U.S. is an unnecessary trade and tariff war.
Ford said the conflict will only benefit China and Chinese-backed companies by creating economic uncertainty.
The province is ready to support federal tariffs as part of the country's first line of retaliation, he said.
"If necessary, we are ready with additional retaliatory measures within provincial jurisdiction that can support escalation over time," said Ford. "We will make decisions about when and how to implement provincial retaliatory measures in collaboration with the federal government as they lead Canada's response."
ROMA address
During Monday's address at ROMA, Ford also spoke about investments in rural high-speed internet, a $50-billion provincewide infrastructure investment plan and Practice Ready Ontario, an initiative to bring 100 foreign-trained doctors into rural regions.

Document 338 of 500

Doug Ford warns Trump tariffs would be an 'absolute disaster' for Canadians and Americans

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/doug-ford-warns-trump-tariffs-would-be-absolute/docview/3157221498/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Doug+Ford+warns+Trump+tariffs+would+be+an+%27absolute+disaster%27+for+Canadians+and+Americans&amp;title=The+Hamilton+Spectator+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2025-01-20&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Hamilton+Spectator+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Premier Doug Ford is warning Ontarians to brace for the impact of Donald Trump's tariffs - and signalling Canada will hit back hard, including by removing American booze from LCBO stores.
In a campaign-style speech Monday to delegates at the Rural Ontario Municipal Association conference, Ford emphasized that tumultuous times are looming.
"We can't ignore the uncertain times we are facing right now," the premier said at the Sheraton Centre conference.
"In a matter of hours, President Trump takes office in Washington, D.C and we have all heard his threats to impose major tariffs on Ontario exports," he said of the incoming American president's proposed 25 per cent levies on all Canadian and Mexican goods.
"This would be an absolute disaster for Canadian and American workers. In our agri-food sector alone in 2023, more than $45 billion in two-way trade was carried out between the U.S. and Ontario," said Ford.
"Our economies our linked together and unnecessary U.S. tariffs will hurt workers on both sides of the border."
In sharp contrast to Alberta Premier Danielle Smith, who has sought a "carve out" from tariffs for her province's oil and gas exports and opposes retaliatory tariffs, Ford said Canada must stand up to the new U.S. administration.
"I promise you as premier of Ontario that if President Trump's tariffs make retaliation necessary I will stand shoulder to shoulder with the rest of Team Canada to fight back using every tool in our toolkit."
That's a message shared by Prime Minister Justin Trudeau and all the other premiers except Smith.
In a surprise announcement Monday, Ford said the U.S. "will feel the pain" because he will ask the LCBO to "clear off every bit of U.S. alcohol off the shelves" of the Crown liquor monopoly, which is one of the world's largest purchasers of American booze.
The premier said he spoke recently with Kentucky Governor Andy Bashear, whose state produces bourbon that is exported around the world.
"I said, 'Governor, that's a serious business down there ... you've got to talk to your president because the first thing we're doing is we're getting the bourbon,'" he said.
It is unclear if Bashear, a Democrat who campaigned against Trump in last November's U.S. election, will be able to persuade the new president to hold off on tariffs - even though the Republican won Kentucky handily.
But Ford emphasized "it shouldn't have to come to this and I hope it doesn't."
Most of the premier's speech to ROMA delegates focused on highlighting his Progressive Conservative government's record on rural issues.
It comes against the backdrop of a possible snap election call as early as next week - even though the next provincial vote is scheduled for June 2026.
CAPTION: Premier Doug Ford, wearing a "Canada Is Not For Sale" hat, speaks as he arrives for a first ministers meeting in Ottawa on Wednesday.

Live 2025 inauguration: Trump considering pardons for Jan. 6 convicts; Musk under fire for salute

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/live-2025-inauguration-trump-considering-pardons/docview/3157113773/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Live+2025+inauguration%3A+Trump+considering+pardons+for+Jan.+6+convicts%3B+Musk+under+fire+for+salute&amp;title=The+Hamilton+Spectator+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2025-01-20&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Hamilton+Spectator+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: President-elect Donald Trump's inauguration for his second presidential term took place in Washington, D.C. on Monday. Trump has officially become the 47th president of the United States and Vice-President-elect JD Vance has also take the oath of office. Follow the Star's live updates all day.
4:40 p.m. Environment minister says Donald Trump pulling out of Paris pact is "deplorable"
Environment Minister Steven Guilbeault says it's "deplorable" that President Donald Trump is again pulling the U.S. out of the 2015 Paris Accord.
He says it's "quite ironic" that President Trump is abandoning the global environmental pact while California is experiencing one of its worst forest fire seasons ever.
Guilbeault also points out it's not the first time Trump has taken the U.S. out of the accord - he did the same thing during his first term in the Oval Office.
The environment minister says the nearly 200 nations still in the pact will forge ahead in the fight against climate change without the U.S. on board.
Read the full story.
3:28 p.m. Elon Musk draws fire for salute
Elon Musk delivered a "odd-looking salute" on live television during his speech at Trump's inaugural parade, pounding his chest and shooting out his right arm in a familiar manner. He then turned around and repeated the gesture: "My heart goes out to you," he said.
"You saw him come out with that odd-looking salute," CNN anchor Erin Burnett noted during the broadcast.
The ensuing fervour on social media was more blunt: "Yes, that is very clearly a Nazi salute," one X user wrote.
3:05 p.m. Trump may pardon Jan. 6 convicts: reports
Trump is reportedly preparing to issue sweeping pardons for the defendants charged in the Jan. 6 attack on the U.S. Capitol, according to ABC News.
This would include commuting the sentences of hundreds of his followers convicted of assaulting law enforcement on that day, and full pardons to those not charged with engaging with violence, ABC reports, citing unnamed sources.
It's possible the plans could change or be scaled back before they're formally signed.
2:51 p.m. DOGE hit by lawsuits
The nascent Department of Government Efficiency (DOGE), to be headed by Trump ally Elon Musk, has reportedly been hit by a flurry of lawsuits mere minutes after Trump's inauguration.
Several of the suits accused the Trump administration of flouting transparency rules that required DOGE to disclose its hiring practices and interactions, and seek to bar the government from taking on any of the advisory group's recommendations until it complies with the law.
The suits come from public interest law firm National Security Counselors; Citizens for Responsibility and Ethics, representing a coalition of teachers' unions and veterans' organizations; and a trio of organizations including nonprofit Public Citizen, the American Federation of Government Employees and the State Democracy Defenders Fund.
2:43 p.m. Wayne Gretzky, Janet Gretzky among celebrities at Trump inauguration
Wayne and Janet Gretzky were among the many celebrities and high profile public figures who attended Trump's inauguration on Monday Morning.
"We are so very proud," Janet wrote under a photo alongside Wayne shared on Instagram. "We love America, We love Canada. God Bless everyone!"
The Great One's friendly relationship with Trump has been under scrutiny in recent weeks. In December, the president-elect told his followers on Truth Social that he had encouraged Gretzky to run for office: "I said, 'Wayne, why don't you run for prime minister of Canada, soon to be known as the governor of Canada - you would win easily, you wouldn't even have to campaign.' He had no interest," Trump wrote.
Doug Ford says a 'clear mandate' from Ontario voters is the best way to deal with Donald Trump
2:38 p.m. Danielle Smith urges Canadian officials to allay Trump concerns
Alberta Premier Danielle Smith issued a statement saying she was "pleased" but that "despite the promising news today, the threat of U.S. tariffs is still very real."
In a post on X, Smith urged Canadian federal and provincial leaders to stop talking about "retaliatory measures including export tariffs or cutting off energy to the U.S." and instead allay Trump's concerns through tighter border security, more defence spending, tougher immigration policies, and the repeal of "anti-energy" policies.
2:29 p.m. A wave of Trump-demanded departures hits senior leadership at the State Department
A large number of senior career diplomats who served in politically appointed top leadership positions at the State Department have left their posts at the demand of the incoming Trump administration, which plans to install its own people in those positions, according to current and outgoing U.S. officials.
Personnel changes in the senior ranks of the department, like those at all federal agencies, are not uncommon after a presidential election, and career officials serving in those roles are required, just as non-career political appointees, to submit letters of resignation before an incoming administration takes office.
Read the full story.
2:22 p.m. Biden reacts to Trump's speech
Biden reacted to Trump's inaugural address in his first speech as a former president at Joint Base Andrews just before leaving for California.
"We heard the inaugural address today - you know, we got a lot more to do," he said, before crossing himself to laughter from the crowd and his former staffers.
"No president gets to choose the moment they enter history. But they get to choose the team they enter history with. And we chose the best damn team in the world," he said. "We're leaving office. But we're not leaving the fight."
Biden exited to chants of "thank you, Joe."
2:06 p.m. "Nelk Boys" co-founder attends with Conor McGregor and Jake and Logan Paul
Internet personality Kyle Forgeard - the Mississauga-born co-founder of the absurdly popular YouTube channel "Nelk Boys" - shared a video on Instagram Monday en route to Trump's inauguration alongside influencers Jake and Logan Paul and Irish MMA fighter Conor McGregor.
The Nelk Boys started out as a prank show, but has in recent years become a platform known for promoting right-wing political causes. In 2022, Forbes named Forgeard as one of the top social media influencers under 30.
McGregor was recently sued for alleged sexual battery over an incident that took place at the 2023 NBA finals. In November of last year, a civil court jury in Ireland awarded 250,000 euros to a woman who said she was "brutally raped and battered" by the MMA fighter.
2 p.m. (Updated) Trump rebuffs Biden's pardons
Trump rebuffed Biden's pardons of his family members and others including Anthony Fauci, Liz Cheney and the committee investigating the January 6 insurrection in a rambling speech at Emancipation Hall, claiming that some of the people were "very, very guilty of very bad crimes."
These included the January 6th Unselect Committee, who he claimed "deleted all the information on Nancy Pelosi having turned down the offer of 10,000 soldiers" at the event. It's a repeated claim of Trump's that the committee found no evidence of ever happening.
Trump also claimed Biden pardoned "33 murderers" and denounced Cheney as a "crying lunatic."
Biden commuted the sentences of 37 people facing the death sentence. They must still serve life in prison without parole - they were not "pardoned" as Trump implied.
Earlier Monday, Biden also commuted the life sentence of Native American activist Leonard Peltier, who was convicted of murdering two FBI agents. Peltier will serve out the rest of his sentence in home confinement.
Read the full transcript of Trump's speech.
1:54 p.m. Senator John Fetterman keeps it casual
Trump's official swearing-in ceremony was moved inside the Capitol Rotunda due to forecasts of "intense cold weather," with temperatures dipping below zero around noon. But the chilly air did not seem to bother Democratic Senator John Fetterman, who arrived at the Capitol wearing a baggy pair of shorts and a black Carhartt hoodie. Though Fetterman is known for his casual attire, he was spotted wearing a suit for Israeli Prime Minister Benjamin Netanyahu's address to Congress last summer.
1:45 p.m. Donald Trump becomes U.S. president for a second time
Donald Trump, the populist disrupter who has vowed political revenge, the mass deportation of "animals" from other countries, and the use of tariffs to win influence and prosperity from abroad - including, perhaps, from Canada - is now the 47th president of the United States.
At a ceremony inside the rotunda of the domed U.S. Capitol Building, where his supporters rioted over the lost presidential election four years ago, Trump officially returned to power at noon on Monday. The moment capped a political restoration that saw Trump survive a criminal conviction, get past investigations into his alleged role in seeking to overturn the 2020 presidential election, and clinch expanded support from American voters in winning November's presidential vote.
"The golden age of America begins right now," said Trump, 78, in his second inaugural address.
Read the full story from the Star's Alex Ballingall
1:31 p.m. Trump is giving a speech to an inauguration crowd after being introduced by JD Vance.
"You're a younger, far more beautiful audience than the one I just spoke to," he joked. "I want to keep it off the record."
Trump also addressed Biden's pre-emptive pardons of his family members and others hours before the inauguration, noting they were "very, very guilty of very bad crimes."
1:30 p.m. A group of men clad in the garb of the Proud Boys marched through the streets of Washington shortly after Trump was sworn in. A handful of counter protesters yelled through megaphones that they were "losers."
1:25 p.m. Trump administration shuts down CBP One
The new Trump administration has already shut down CBP One, a government mobile app that previously helped migrants submit information and schedule appointments at ports of entry.
"Effective January 20, 2025, the functionalities of CBP One that previously allowed undocumented aliens to submit advance information and schedule appointments at eight southwest border ports of entry is no longer available, and existing appointments have been cancelled," an alert on the app's website reads.
Roughly 270,000 Mexican migrants were waiting to get an appointment through the app before it shuttered, CBS reports. As of Jan. 16, nearly 919,000 migrants were allowed into the States through the app.
1:16 p.m. Trudeau congratulates Trump
In a social media post, Prime Minister Justin Trudeau congratulated Trump on his inauguration. "We have the chance to work together again -- to create more jobs and prosperity for both our nations," Trudeau said.
In his speech, Trump did not mention Canada but reiterated his threat of tariffs on foreign nations. Trump had previously threatened 25 per cent tariffs against Canada.
FBI acting chief Paul Abbate says he's retiring
Paul Abbate, who became the FBI's acting director following the departure of Christopher Wray, is retiring from the bureau, according to an email he sent to senior executives that was obtained by The Associated Press on Monday.
Abbate had been expected to run the FBI while Senate confirmation proceedings get underway for Kash Patel, who is President Donald Trump's pick for director. But with Abbate's retirement effective Monday, when Trump took office, it was not immediately clear who would fill that role.
1:02 p.m. Government site gets makeover
The whitehouse.gov website got a makeover, welcoming visitors with a video glorifying Trump to soaring orchestral music before giving way to a landing page boasting that "America is back" in large lettering and an image of Trump.
"Every single day I will be fighting for you with every breath in my body," an accompanying caption reads. "I will not rest until we have delivered the strong, safe and prosperous America that our children deserve and that you deserve. This will truly be the golden age of America."
12:43 p.m. Carrie Underwood's performance beset by technical difficulties
Carrie Underwood's highly-anticipated performance at Trump's inauguration did not exactly go as planned.
After being introduced, the Grammy-winning country singer stood in awkward silence caused by technical difficulties as she waited for the musical track to begin. After about a minute, Underwood could be seen mouthing the words "I can just sing it," before requesting that the audience "help me out here." She then proceeded to perform the song a cappella without further issue.
An eight-time Grammy winner, Underwood, 41, is the highest profile artist slated to perform at Trump's inauguration. Her decision to perform at the inauguration was met with significant negative reactions from her fans, particularly her LGBTQ+ fans. Underwood has not publicly responded to those criticisms.
12:54 p.m. What did Melania Trump wear?
Incoming First Lady Melania Trump, who was mostly shunned by America's fashion establishment during her husband's last term in office, arrived at the church service ahead of her husband's second inauguration in a slim-fit navy silk-and-wool coat and skirt set by American designer Adam Lippes.
Notably, she accessorized it with a flat-brimmed, low-slung hat with white trim by Eric Javits, an American milliner since the 1980s with celebrity clients including Dua Lipa and Lady Gaga. Sitting low on her face Spy vs. Spy-style, the hat is destined to become an instant meme, while preventing the cameras from catching any accidental facial expressions-Melania has been captured seemingly giving side-eye at events in the past.
The choice of Adam Lippes for her outfit seems calculated to show support for a smaller, independent fashion designer. Lippes, who worked for Oscar de la Renta before starting his own label a decade ago, specializes in American sportswear; his dresses run in the $3,000 to $4,000 range. For Trump's first inauguration, Melanie wore a light blue dress by heritage brand Ralph Lauren (also a favourite designer of the Biden family) that drew comparisons to Jackie Kennedy's style.
"Melania's look is restrained, classic, uniform like. It looks like a dramatic governess outfit," said Tracy Richardson, a Canadian image consultant who has dressed clients for the White House, Buckingham Palace, the Oscars, TIFF and the Junos. "The hat is dramatic. But she looks neat. We aren't distracted by what she is wearing." Leanne Delap/Special to the Star
12:53 p.m. Trump stops short of immediately imposing Canadian tariffs
U.S. President Donald Trump has stopped short of immediately imposing his threatened 25-percent tariff on Canadian and Mexican goods.
But in his inaugural address minutes after taking office, Trump left no doubt he has foreign manufacturers and America's allies and competitors in his crosshairs.
He vowed he would set up a new agency to collect tariff revenues, and that he will fill American coffers with tariff and tax revenue from foreign countries, saying he was "saved by God to make America great again."
Trump declared a series of national emergencies to deploy soldiers to the Mexican border, ramp up oil drilling, end electric vehicle mandates, and began what he called "an overhaul" of U.S. trade relations.

"Many people thought it was impossible for me to stage such a historic political comeback. But as you see today, here I am. The American people have spoken," Trump said to a standing ovation.

Trump says it is the official policy of the U.S. government that there are only two genders.

12:26 p.m. (updated) Trump promises 'golden age of America'
In his inaugural address, Trump promised the "golden age of America begins right now," reiterating his vow to "put America first."
"From this day forward, our country will flourish and be respected again all over the world," he said. "We will be the envy of every nation, and we will not allow ourselves to be taken advantage of any longer."
"I was saved by God to make America great again," Trump says.
Trump says today is "liberation day" for Americans, and hopes his election will be viewed as among the most influential in American history.
Trump says he will declare a national emergency at the Mexican-U.S. border, a line that draws a standing ovation from the rotunda -- with Biden, Harris, the Clintons and the Bushs notably sitting.
Trump vowed to end the Green New Deal and revoke the "electric vehicle mandate."
"In other words, you'll be able to buy the car of your choice. We will build automobiles in America again at a rate that nobody could have dreamt possible just a few years ago."
How much is Trump's crypto coin worth?
On Monday, Donald Trump's newly created cryptocurrency, a "meme coin" known as $TRUMP, soared to more than $8 billion dollars in market value, as of writing. Launched on Sunday, the collectible token, which has no inherent value, currently ranks third among other "meme coins" behind digital assets Dogecoin and Shiba Inu.
First Lady Melania Trump also launched a crypto coin, called $MELANIA,, on Sunday, which is currently trading just under $1 billion, behind other popular coins like "Pepe" and "Fartcoin."
Some crypto enthusiasts hailed the Trump meme coin's release, saying it's symbolic of the incoming president's support for an industry that felt unfairly targeted by the Biden administration. Trump has promised to usher in crypto-friendly regulations and picked crypto cheerleaders for key government positions.
Critics said the Trump meme coin could be a dangerous way for special interests and foreign governments to try and buy influence with the president.
How tall are Barron Trump and Donald Trump?
Since Trump was first elected, much ink has been spilled about the president's "large adult sons," Donald Jr. and Eric. But Trump's youngest child, Barron has largely evaded the spotlight.
That may be about to change, given that Barron is now 18 and looks looks like he could an up-and-coming shooting guard for Washington Wizards. Indeed, standing behind the president's podium at Monday's inauguration, the baby-faced Trump towered over the other attendees.
But exactly how tall is Barron? Google has him listed at 2.06 metres, which is almost 6 foot 8. However, he was listed at 6 foot 7 during his graduation from Oxbridge Academy in West Palm Beach last May. That's at least four inches taller than his father, who is listed at 6 foot 3, or 1.9 metres.

11:47 a.m. Biden pardon family members
In the final minutes of his presidency, Joe Biden has pardoned the Biden family, his brothers James and Francis, sister-in-law Sara Jones, sister Valerie and brother-in-law John.
"My family has been subjected to unrelenting attacks and threats, motivated solely by a desire to hurt me-the worst kind of partisan politics," Biden said in a statement. "Unfortunately, I have no reason to believe these attacks will end."
Biden also commuted the life sentence of Leonard Peltier. The 80-year-old was convicted for the 1975 killings of two FBI agents during a standoff on a reservation in South Dakota.
"This commutation will enable Mr. Peltier to spend his remaining days in home confinement but will not pardon him for his underlying crimes," a White House statement said.

11:35 a.m. (updated) Guests arriving for the inauguration
Guests have arrived at the U.S. Capitol for the inauguration, which has been moved indoors due freezing temperatures brought by the Polar Vortex.
Former president Barack Obama has arrived without former first lady Michelle Obama, who announced last week she would forego Monday's event.
Met with significant boos by the crowd, former Vice President Mike Pence, Former President Bill Clinton, and former first lady Hillary Clinton are also amongst the guests in attendance.
Tech titan and major Trump donor Elon Musk, who will be heading the new Department of Government Efficiency, has also arrived, alongside Meta CEO Mark Zuckerberg and Apple CEO Tim Cook. Podcast host Joe Rogan has also been seen in attendance.
Argentine President Javier Milei and Italian Premier Giorgia Meloni have also been seen in the Rotunda, the first foreign leaders to attend the inauguration since 1874.
Trump's cabinet picks, including future secretary of state Marco Rubio and Pete Hegseth, the controversial pick for defence secretary, have also been seen at the capitol.

At 78 years and 220 days old, Trump is five months older than Joe Biden when he was inaugurated in 2021.
Meanwhile, at 40 years old, JD Vance will be the third-youngest vice president in U.S. history.

11:12 a.m. Trump does the 'Trump dance' during Village People performance
The Village People, a campy disco group, known for their goofy lyrics and problematic costumes, performed their hit song "Y.M.C.A." on stage with Trump on Sunday - assless chaps, Native American headdresses and all. The song has become a staple at Trump rallies - you've likely seen the President-elect's awkward, jerky dance moves, which first went viral during his 2020 election campaign. Since then, the "Trump" dance has been embraced by Trump supporters, frat boys and several pro athletes, including San Francisco 49ers star Nick Bosa and U.S. men's soccer team captain Christian Pulisic.
Over the decades, the 1978 hit has been embraced by the gay community. However, according to Victor Willis, the band's litigious lead singer (and only remaining original member) has insisted the song is not a "gay anthem." In fact, he recently stated that he will take legal action against anyone who suggests it is.

Canada bracing for Donald Trump's decision amid reports 25 per cent tariffs not immediately on the table
The Canadian government has not received any official confirmation that incoming U.S. President Donald Trump will not impose tariffs on Canadian products, as threatened.
Two senior Canadian officials said Monday the government was still waiting for official word that would confirm reports by the Wall Street Journal and New York Times that Trump is instead going to order a trade deficit review which would include a study of the North American free-trade pact.
One official said it is encouraging if true that Trump's threatened 25 per cent tariffs on all Canadian goods is not immediately on the table.

She announced her token Sunday evening in a post to X: "The official Melania Meme is live!", she wrote, adding that "You can buy $Melania now."
Her coin is currently trading at $9.08 as of time of writing, up over 24 per cent in the last day, according to CoinMarketCap. It has a market cap of $1.76 billion.
A disclaimer on the cryptocurrency's website cautions the tokens are "not intended to be, or to be the subject of, an investment opportunity, investment contract, or security of any type."
Instead, they're being marketed as "digital collectibles intended to function as an expression of support for and engagement with the values embodied by the symbol MELANIA. and the associated artwork."
Melania's announcement came days after the president-elect unveiled his own meme coin $Trump on Friday; as of time of writing, his coin is down more than 13 per cent at $53.91, according to CoinMarketCap.
"Official Trump" is currently the most-traded meme coin on the Solana platform, according to CoinGecko. "Melania Meme" is in sixth place, as of the time of writing.

Small businesses in Canada losing jobs as Trump's tariff threat looms
A new report has found that Canadian small business job growth has been "lagging" since last summer, particularly within manufacturing and professional services, signalling possible trouble ahead as president-elect Donald Trump's threat of U.S. tariffs looms.
The report by Intuit QuickBooks found that Canadian small businesses have lost more than 84,000 jobs since July, despite adding a cumulative 126,600 jobs between October 2023 and October 2024.
The year-over-year increase in small business jobs is in stark contrast to the second half losses in 2024 and reflects a tale of two trends, said Simon Worsfold, head of data communications at Intuit QuickBooks.

Trump swearing-in will move inside Capitol Rotunda because of intense cold
President-elect Donald Trump may take the oath of office from inside the Capitol Rotunda on Monday due to forecasts of intense cold weather.
"The weather forecast for Washington, D.C., with the windchill factor, could take temperatures into severe record lows," Trump posted on his Truth Social platform. "There is an Arctic blast sweeping the Country. I don't want to see people hurt, or injured, in any way."
The Rotunda is prepared as the inclement weather alternative for each inauguration in the event of inclement weather. The swearing-in was last moved indoors in 1985, when President Ronald Reagan began his second term. Monday's forecast calls for the lowest inauguration day temperatures since that day.
Read the full story from the Associated Press
Canada ready to respond should tariffs be imposed as soon as Monday
Canada should brace for U.S. tariffs to be imposed as soon as Monday, and if so, Ottawa will be ready to respond immediately, says Foreign Affairs Minister Melanie Joly who held out hope on Friday there's still time to avoid a trade war.
Joly said the federal government estimates the impact, if U.S. President Donald Trump follows through on his threatened 25-percent tariff on all Canadian goods, would be massive. It would increase the cost of living and mean job losses across Canada and in key U.S. states but she refused to provide federal estimates of what the national job losses or the hit to economic activity could be.
"Definitely we have numbers that are highlighting how much this could be devastating," Joly told reporters in a virtual news conference. "The Americans would be starting a trade war against us. And this would be the biggest trade war between Canada and U.S. in decades."

Canada is getting ready to strike back against Donald Trump's tariffs, even though there is dissension in the nation's ranks.

Canadian business leaders prepare for Trump tariff announcement as early as Monday - but there will be exceptions, they predict
With Donald Trump set to take the oath of office Monday, the North American economy stands on the verge of a trade war.
But will the U.S. president-elect actually follow through on repeated threats of across-the-board, 25 per cent tariffs on Canadian imports on Day One?
The answer, say business groups, trade experts and economists, is probably not.
Read the full story from the Star's Josh Rubin
Everything you need to know about Trump's inauguration
At 12 p.m. Trump and J.D. Vance will be sworn in as the 47th president and 50th vice-president outside of the U.S. Capitol as part of an inauguration ceremony that happens every four years for every incoming president. (This year's inauguration will be the 60th in the country's history.)
While many are familiar with the photos of presidents and vice-presidents bundled up in winter coats and scarves with their right hands raised to take their oaths of office, the swearing-in ceremony isn't the only thing that happens on Inauguration Day - and there are often events planned leading up to the day as well.
Here's everything you need to know about Monday's ceremony and festivities
Who is attending the inauguration?
With incoming U.S. president Donald Trump's inauguration days away, much may be gleaned about the upcoming administration by who's on the guest list, who isn't - and who declined to take part.
From what we know so far, the ceremony on Monday will be attended by former presidents, government officials, tech billionaires and a smattering of far-right leaders including Argentinian President Javier Milei and U.K. politician Nigel Farage.
Notably, former first lady Michelle Obama will be skipping the ceremony, according to her office. Her husband, former president Barack Obama, has confirmed his attendance.
Read the full story from the Star's Kevin Jiang
Is Donald Trump's inauguration on Martin Luther King Jr. Day?
Yes, this year, Inauguration Day falls on the same day as Martin Luther King Jr. Day.
What time is the inauguration?
Donald Trump's presidential inauguration is scheduled to begin at noon (EST).
Trump to rally in D.C. ahead of inauguration
Donald Trump is expected to hold a big Washington rally the day before he's sworn in as the nation's 47th president.
The victory rally, as it's being billed, will be held at the Capitol One Arena in the District of Columbia on Jan. 19. Trump has always preferred rally-style events where he can freewheel and joke with his supporters. He'll be inaugurated in a formal ceremony at the U.S. Capitol on Jan. 20th.
"This is President Trump's VICTORY RALLY the DAY before being sworn in as the 47th President - the crowd needs to be HUGE!" read an email to supporters advertising the event.
Read the full story from the Associated Press
U.S. Capitol flags raised to full height for Trump's inauguration
House Speaker Mike Johnson on Tuesday ordered that flags at the U.S. Capitol be raised to their full height on Inauguration Day, pausing a 30-day flag-lowering order following the death of former President Jimmy Carter.
The Republican leader's decision means that President-elect Donald Trump will not take the oath of office for his second term under a half-staff flag, a prospect that he had previously complained about.
It mirrors actions taken in recent days by some Republican governors who have announced that flags in their states would be raised on Inauguration Day to mark Trump's second term.
Read the full story from the Associated Press
Will Michelle Obama attend the inauguration?
Former first lady Michelle Obama will skip the inauguration of President-elect Donald Trump, the second time in two weeks that she is not attending a gathering of former U.S. leaders and their spouses, but former Presidents Barack Obama, George W. Bush and Bill Clinton will be there. Laura Bush and former Secretary of State Hillary Clinton will join their husbands for the Jan. 20 swearing-in ceremony at the Capitol, representatives said.
Read the full story from the Associated Press

Document 340 of 500

Unity or 'American carnage': What tone will Trump take in second inaugural address?

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/unity-american-carnage-what-tone-will-trump-take/docview/3157113712/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Unity+or+%27American+carnage%27%3A+What+tone+will+Trump+take+in+second+inaugural+address%3F&amp;title=The+Spectator&amp;issn=11899417&amp;date=2025-01-20&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.1&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Spectator&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: John F. Kennedy inspired Americans with the words, "Ask not what your country can do for you - ask what you can do for your country." Franklin D. Roosevelt assured them there's "nothing to fear but fear itself."
Abraham Lincoln sought to heal a nation divided by civil war by calling for "malice toward none" in a speech that would later be carved into the wall of the Lincoln Memorial.
These inaugural speeches have become part of the cultural fabric of America and are cited constantly in calls for patriotism, unity and peace.
President-elect Donald Trump's first speech as president in 2017 painted a bleak picture of what he called "American carnage." As Trump returns to the White House on Monday, the world is watching to see whether the Republican leader will take a different approach.
Not all inaugural speeches stand the test of time, said Aaron Kall, the director of debate for the University of Michigan. They do, however, set the tone for the four years of an administration.
Eight years ago, Trump's speech described a dwindling nation riddled with crime and drugs and fiercely criticized the policies of his predecessors. The approximately 16-minute speech was peppered with isolationist "America First" rhetoric.
At its conclusion, former president George W. Bush reportedly called it weird.
"It wasn't very well received just because it was very dark in tone, about crime and immigration," said Kall, an expert in speeches and politics. "And you want something a bit more uplifting and rallying."
Not much in Trump's messaging has changed in the years since. He campaigned on claims that violent migrants were crossing the border in droves, and on tackling crime and saving the economy. He repeatedly called the U.S. a nation in decline.
Since the election, he's pushed both isolationism and American imperialism. He threatened to impose 25 per cent tariffs on Canada and Mexico, initially saying the duties were linked to border security. He later connected the proposed levies on Canada to trade disparities and defence funding.
Trump mused about using "economic force" to make Canada the 51st state. He wouldn't rule out using the military to take over Greenland and the Panama Canal. Despite these comments, Kall said he believes a different kind of speech is in store when the Republican leader gives his second inaugural address on Monday.
"It doesn't mean everything will change, but there's some reasons to believe it could be a little different," he said.
Trump has a lot more confidence going into this speech, Kall said. He won the popular vote and Republicans now control both the House and the Senate. Trump has raised a lot more money for the celebration and his transition is rolling out more smoothly than in 2017.
More importantly, he's done this before.
"I think it could be more confident, forward-looking and optimistic because he's more confident," Kall said.
Kall said he's watching to see if Trump follows historical norms by thanking outgoing President Joe Biden and Vice-President Kamala Harris.
There are many reasons to be skeptical about the idea of Trump embracing unity as he returns to America's highest office, Kall added. But a lot has happened since Trump's first term, including the assassination attempt last summer.
Inauguration addresses are typically much shorter and less detailed than the State of the Union, another important speech usually given by presidents once a year, but this one should still provide some details about priorities in Trump's policy agenda.
Trump has pledged to sign an executive order Monday to put his tariff plan in place. Canadian officials are preparing a response but it remains unclear what the president-elect will ultimately do.
"This will kind of be the calm before the storm," Kall said of the speech.
"And once it's all over, then things will be back to a very rapid pace. So, this may be the last calming portion of the administration."
Here are some of the Canadians expected to be in Washington Monday:
Alberta Premier Danielle Smith: Smith has emerged as main Canadian voice amid the tariff threat, regularly appearing on U.S. news stations and recently travelling to Trump's Florida Mar-a-Lago resort to meet with the president-elect. But the premier caused controversy in Canada last week after refusing to sign off on a joint statement with other premiers and Prime Minister Justin Trudeau that said every countermeasure was on the table to push back any proposed duties.
Industry Minister François-Philippe Champagne: As co-lead of the federal government's Team Canada strategy, the cabinet minister has been connecting with American politicians and businesses since early 2024. Champagne declined to join the Liberal leadership race, saying he is focused on ensuring Ottawa is ready to protect Canadian industry from the trade threats posed by Trump.
Trade Minister Mary Ng: The other co-lead of Team Canada, Ng has also spent the past year connecting with Americans to promote the Canada-U.S. relationship. Last week, Ng was in New Jersey and New York where she met with business leaders and state government officials.
Newfoundland and Labrador Premier Andrew Furey: Furey is among the Canadian leaders who criticized Trump's comments about making Canada the 51st state and using "economic force." Furey, whose province is an oil producer, has said Canada should make sure the U.S. is aware of its critical reliance on Canadian energy but not pull back on those exports just yet.
Ontario Energy Minister Stephen Lecce and Trade Minister Vic Fedeli: Lecce and Fedeli will likely be headed to D.C. with Premier Doug Ford's "Fortress Am-Can" proposal in hand. Ontario's strategy to counter Trump's tariff threat has been pushing for a deeper relationship between Ontario and the United States, particularly around energy security. Ontario officials estimate the tariffs could cost half a million jobs.
Quebec International Relations Minister Martine Biron: Biron is headed south on what the Quebec government calls a "mission to Washington," during which she'll stress Quebec's shared interests with the U.S. She's due to attend an inauguration day reception hosted by Canada's ambassador to the U.S., Kirsten Hillman.
Liberal MP John McKay: McKay is co-chair Canada-United States Inter-Parliamentary Group and has met many American legislators over the years. McKay, who has been an MP since 1997, has said he won't run for office again.
With files from
The Associated Press
The Canadian Press
CAPTION: U.S. president-elect Donald Trump mused about using "economic force" to make Canada the 51st state.Alex Brandon The Canadian Press file photo

CREDIT: Kelly Geraldine Malone With files from The Associated Press The Canadian Press

Document 341 of 500

Vast majority of Canadians fear impact of Donald Trump's tariffs, survey shows

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/vast-majority-canadians-fear-impact-donald-trumps/docview/3156967407/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Vast+majority+of+Canadians+fear+impact+of+Donald+Trump%27s+tariffs%2C+survey+shows&amp;title=The+Hamilton+Spectator+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2025-01-19&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Hamilton+Spectator+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: More than four in five Canadians believe Donald Trump's 25-per-cent tariffs will hurt the economy, with two-thirds fearing his levies could impact them personally, a new poll suggests.
A staggering 84 per cent of those surveyed have a "negative" view of what the incoming U.S. president's measures could do to Canada with only three per cent being "positive" about them and eight per cent unsure.
At the same time, Pollara Strategic Insights found 65 per cent believe a trade war could affect their day-to-day lives in a negative way while five per cent held a positive view and 12 per cent didn't know.
Dan Arnold, chief strategy officer at Pollara, said that's significant.
"Generally, when we ask questions about a policy - either good or bad - people don't usually think it's going to impact them at the end of the day," said Arnold.
"They say, 'Well, this will hurt some people, but maybe it won't hurt me,' but you've got two-thirds of Canadians who actually feel like this could have a negative impact on their lives day-to-day," he said.
"They have a sense that it's going to be bad - whether or not that impacts their job or whether or not it means things just get more expensive at the grocery store because we're into a trade war."
Pollara surveyed 1,506 people across Canada from Dec. 4 until Dec. 13 - after Prime Minister Justin Trudeau visited Trump at the president-elect's Mara-a-Lago resort in Florida.
While online panel samples cannot be assigned a margin of error, for comparison purposes, a random sample of this size would have one of plus or minus 2.5 percentage points, 19 times out of 20.
With Trump threatening tariffs on all Canadian and Mexican goods immediately after his inauguration Monday, political leaders here have been readying a counterattack.
Trudeau and Ontario Premier Doug Ford, chair of the Council of the Federation, have been warning that all options are on the table if Canada is forced to retaliate.
"If the president-elect does choose to move forward on tariffs, Canada will, of course, respond," the prime minister said Friday in Toronto.
Ford, who has warned the U.S. tariffs could cost 500,000 job in Ontario, stressed "the retaliatory tariffs need to be hard."
"You can't let someone hit you over the head with a sledgehammer without hitting him back twice as hard," the premier said at the first minister's meeting Wednesday in Ottawa with Trudeau seated beside him nodding in agreement.
But Alberta Premier Danielle Smith, concerned about her province's flow of oil and natural gas to the U.S., strongly disagreed with the other first ministers.
"Federal government officials continue to publicly and privately float the idea of cutting off energy supply to the U.S. and imposing export tariffs on Alberta energy and other products to the United States," Smith, who attended Wednesday's meeting virtually as she was on vacation, said on social media.
"Until these threats cease, Alberta will not be able to fully support the federal government's plan in dealing with the threatened tariffs."
But Pollara found Albertans are just as worried about the effect of Trump's levies as other Canadians.
"It is pretty consistent across the board. In Ontario, 67 per cent think it's going to have an impact on their day-to-day life; it's 61 per cent in Alberta," said Arnold.
"So even though premiers are reacting differently to this issue, I think Canadians themselves everywhere are worried about tariffs and the impact this is going to have on them," he said.
Pollara also found that Canadians are souring on the U.S. in the wake of Trump's return to the White House.
Last month, 59 per cent of those surveyed had a negative view of Canada's closest neighbour and largest trading partner while only 33 per cent had positive feelings and eight per cent didn't know.
That compared to findings last June, before President Joe Biden announced he wouldn't seek re-election, when 40 per cent had a negative view of the U.S. and 53 per cent had positive feelings and seven per cent were unsure.
"That's because (Trump) is the United States in the eyes of most Canadians right now," said Arnold.
When respondents were asked to describe Trump in different ways, 70 per cent said he "wants to help the rich," 69 per cent said he's "corrupt," 54 per cent believe he's a "fascist" and 50 per cent felt he's "senile."
Only 36 per cent said his actions are because he "wants to help working-class Americans get ahead" while 31 per cent said he was "doing things I wish more Canadian politicians would do."
About one-quarter - 27 per cent - described Trump as "competent" with 22 per cent declaring he's "stable" and 21 per cent "rational."
CAPTION: President-elect Donald Trump speaks during a meeting with Republican governors at Mar-a-Lago, in Palm Beach, Fla., on Jan. 9, 2025.

Document 342 of 500

Jan. 18: Scrapping encampment protocol is no solution, Ford's patriotism is hypocritical and other letters to the editor

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/jan-18-scrapping-encampment-protocol-is-no/docview/3156796583/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Jan.+18%3A+Scrapping+encampment+protocol+is+no+solution%2C+Ford%27s+patriotism+is+hypocritical+and+other+letters+to+the+editor&amp;title=The+Hamilton+Spectator+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2025-01-18&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Hamilton+Spectator+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Ford's patriotism is hypocritical
Re: Ford government's ServiceOntario move to Staples will cost more than estimated, financial watchdog says, Jan. 15
You have to shake your head that our premier, while wearing a hat that says "Canada not for sale," has made a deal with the American company Staples to house ServiceOntario services. The best part is that it will cost the taxpayers $11.7 million by 2027.
This is not to be confused with the long-term lease of Ontario Place to an Austrian company. With thousands of Ontarians homeless, it seems ridiculous to take a beloved landmark and turn it into a spa for the wealthy.
I'm not buying what Captain Canada is selling.
Robin Magder, Dundas
Scrapping protocol no solution
Re: Council scraps Hamilton's encampment protocol, Jan. 16
I read with disappointment the City of Hamilton's decision to abolish the encampment protocol. Quite apart from being callous, it ignores the reality of homelessness and will likely lead to future legal risk for the city. In my opinion, city council is misinterpreting the recent Charter court decision where members of the homeless community sought damages arising from the enforcement of the previous protocol. The city was successful, in part because it was able to demonstrate to the court that it was, at least, trying to address the homeless problem. The next time the city goes to court, it will have nothing to show other than the "whack-a-mole" approach of shooing homeless people from park to park across the city. This is not constructive and will make it easier for a different court to reach a different and more costly conclusion against the city.
Ralph D'Angelo, Hamilton
City could've done better
Re: Half of Hamilton's microshelters arrive, Jan. 14
I am very glad to see the microshelters have finally arrived and will hopefully be occupied soon. However, I am not impressed that it has taken so long to help desperate people. I am also not impressed that they were purchased from China. Personally, I avoid buying "made in China" since the horrific treatment of the two Michaels. In future, I hope the city will use better judgment and purchase "made in Canada" shelters. They are available.
Sharon McKibbon, Hamilton
Water monitoring is worth the cost
Re: Should Hamilton bring back wastewater surveillance? Jan. 15
Monitoring Hamilton's raw sewage entering the wastewater treatment plant will be economical prudence for the community. When a pandemic hits, the economy suffers greatly. Not only will the residents suffer more debt challenges, the health-care system will also suffer. Increased hospital stays, overcrowded emergency centres and lost wages within the workforce are just a few examples of the cost of not being prepared. Financially assisting the monitoring of the raw sewage will give the city advanced warning of any potential pandemic. With the advanced knowledge, the city will not be caught off guard, allowing safety protocols to be implemented in a timely fashion. This will alleviate some of the financial burdens caused by a full-blown pandemic. I hope the city sees this as an investment rather than a financial burden to the taxpayers.
Roger Lambert, Hagersville
Congratulations to Brantford hockey fans
Re: A Hamilton hockey-era ends. Another Bulldogs era begins, Jan. 14
So-long, farewell, auf Wiedersehen, adieu!
Congratulations are in order to Brantford for being the permanent new home for the Bulldogs hockey team. Kudos to Michael Andlauer for making that stipulation a condition for the sale of the team to the group led by Zach Hyman.
Kicks to Hamilton's powers-that-be for their highly questionable treatment of Andlauer and the team, which was one of the best things Hamilton had going for it, by failing to give respect where respect was due to Andlauer and the Bulldogs.
Brenda Bianchi, Hamilton
We can and must do better
Re: Catering to business is what got us here, Jan. 15
Kudos to Noelle Allen for her column outlining and bemoaning how wealth inequality has become unconscionable in Canada. As she so rightly points out, "no one should be forced to sleep outside in January in Canada." How did it come to be in our great country that Canadians who have to rely on disability pensions often live in abject poverty?
Canada needs a political and economic reset in the direction of greater income and wealth equality. We can, and must, do better going forward.
Philip White, professor emeritus, McMaster University
Keep carbon pricing in place
The carbon tax for 2024 on my natural gas was $389.65. The carbon tax rebate for 2024 was $957.30. I did not keep track of gasoline purchases, but I as a retiree do not drive as much. I am over $500 ahead. Keep the tax and especially the rebate
Bob Reed, Simcoe
Poilievre should show us he's ready
Like "Where's Waldo," I've been wondering where Pierre Poilievre is during all of this talk about tariffs, unity, power vacuums and Donald Trump threatening to take Canada over through economic pressure. I thought this was the guy who wanted to be prime minister so bad he could taste the mahogany on the PM's chair that he is about to inherit by the mere formality of a spring election.
Justin Trudeau gave him the absolute best post Christmas gift a prime minister wannabe could get; his resignation. With Trump's recent rhetoric, this was Poilievre's big (and I mean huge) chance to step up and show Canadians what his leadership would be like in the face of Trump and all he has to say every time a reporter asks him the question about what's going on right now is "I'm not the prime minister"?
If this is the job you really want, Pierre, it's past time you show us Canadians and them Americans that you will be a staunchly proud and fierce Canadian leader who will protect our sovereignty and resources at all cost and that you are the leader in waiting and you won't stand for such bullying and intimidation of the strongest and most loyal ally the U.S. has ever had. But alas, you are nowhere to be found. No bluster, no bravado, no show of unity because perhaps all you want to portray is that "Canada is broken," which makes the U.S. president salivate every time he hears you say it. Or maybe you're too busy writing up the next batch of insults and one-liners for whomever takes over the Liberal leadership.
John Poole, Hamilton
Smith's actions 'absolutely disgraceful'
All of our first ministers pledged "Canada first" with regard to Donald Trump's tariff threats with the exception of Daniella Smith. She could not spare time to attend the first ministers meeting. However, she did find the time to sneak down to Mar-a-Lago to wine and dine with Trump and Kevin O'Leary! I dare say that she will also find the time from her travels to Panama to attend Trump's inauguration. The actions of this premier are absolutely disgraceful and could very well jeopardize Canada's efforts to avoid the very damaging tariffs.
Nelson Buffett, Hamilton
Criticism of Alberta is unfair
I'm disappointed in Canada and all the media-bashing of Alberta because Premier Danielle Smith did not sign on to the agreement to use every tool in the tool box to fight Donald Trump's tariffs. What hypocrisy!
Let's set the record straight. No other province is being asked or is volunteering to shut down their No. 1 industry to fight Trump. The Maritimes is not being asked to stop shipping all fishery or potato products to the United States. British Columbia has not volunteered to ban American tourists or cruise ships from entering B.C. ports. Now we have heard that Doug Ford has said he will cut off electricity from being sent to America. You know what we have not heard from Ford or Ontario? That Ontario will stop sending cars manufactured in Ontario to America to stop Trump's tariffs. So when Ford says we are using all tools in the tool box, he is lying. But, Ford and the rest of Canada wants Alberta to restrict oil exports; a commitment no other province is taking to stop Trump.
The truth is Ford knows Trump tariffs have the potential to end the auto pact, as the boardrooms in Detroit may decide to end auto manufacturing in Ontario forever. So, Ford is doing everything in his power to "save" Ontario's No. 1 industry: auto manufacturing. But please Premier Ford, don't ask Alberta to sacrifice our No. 1 industry of oil, only to protect your No. 1 industry of auto manufacturing. It's not fair. It's not even honest the way the situation is being portrayed in the national media.
Thank goodness Premier Smith has our backs and has made the right decision to absolutely unconditionally protect Alberta!
Chris Robertson, Stony Plain, Alta.
Remember how Ignatieff turned out?
Do these parties never learn? So Mark Carney is now running for the head of the Liberal Party, with what looks like a lot of senior Liberals backing him. This is a disaster waiting to happen. Do they not remember the Michael Ignatieff fiasco? I'll say it now so that I do not have to write in later: told you so!
David Eyles, Hamilton
CAPTION: The city's decision to abolish the encampment protocol, apart from being callous, ignores the reality of homelessness, a reader says.Premier Doug Ford wears a "Canada Is Not For Sale" hat. Where was his patriotism when he put ServiceOntario in Staples locations or when Ontario Place was closed for an Austrian spa, a reader asks.

Document 343 of 500

Letters

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/letters/docview/3156767649/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=unknown&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Letters&amp;title=The+Spectator&amp;issn=11899417&amp;date=2025-01-18&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.19&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Spectator&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Ford's patriotism is hypocritical
Re: Ford government's
ServiceOntario move to
Staples will cost more than
estimated, financial watchdog says, Jan. 15
You have to shake your head that our premier, while wearing a hat that says "Canada not for sale," has made a deal with the American company Staples to house ServiceOntario services. The best part is that it will cost the taxpayers $11.7 million by 2027.
This is not to be confused with the long-term lease of Ontario Place to an Austrian company. With thousands of Ontarians homeless, it seems ridiculous to take a beloved landmark and turn it into a spa for the wealthy.
I'm not buying what Captain Canada is selling.
Robin Magder, Dundas
Scrapping
protocol wrong
Re: Council scraps Hamilton's encampment protocol, Jan. 16
I read with disappointment the City of Hamilton's decision to abolish the encampment protocol. Quite apart from being callous, it ignores the reality of homelessness and will likely lead to future legal risk for the city.
In my opinion, city council is misinterpreting the recent Charter court decision where members of the homeless community sought damages arising from the enforcement of the previous protocol.
The city was successful, in part because it was able to demonstrate to the court that it was, at least, trying to address the homeless problem.
The next time the city goes to court, it will have nothing to show other than the "whack-a-mole" approach of shooing homeless people from park to park across the city.
This is not constructive and will make it easier for a different court to reach a different and more costly conclusion against the city.
Ralph D'Angelo, Hamilton
City could've done better
Re: Half of Hamilton's
microshelters arrive, Jan. 14
I am very glad to see the microshelters have finally arrived and will hopefully be occupied soon. However, I am not impressed that it has taken so long to help desperate people. I am also not impressed that they were purchased from China. Personally, I avoid buying "made in China" since the horrific treatment of the two Michaels.
In future, I hope the city will use better judgment and purchase "made in Canada" shelters. They are available.
Sharon McKibbon, Hamilton
Water monitoring is worth the cost
Re: Should Hamilton bring back wastewater surveillance? Jan. 15
Monitoring Hamilton's raw sewage entering the wastewater treatment plant will be economical prudence for the community. When a pandemic hits, the economy suffers greatly. Not only will the residents suffer more debt challenges, the health-care system will also suffer. Increased hospital stays, overcrowded emergency centres and lost wages within the workforce are just a few examples of the cost of not being prepared.
Financially assisting the monitoring of the raw sewage will give the city advanced warning of any potential pandemic.
With the advanced knowledge, the city will not be caught off guard, allowing safety protocols to be implemented in a timely fashion. This will alleviate some of the financial burdens caused by a full-blown pandemic. I hope the city sees this as an investment rather than a financial burden to the taxpayers.
Roger Lambert, Hagersville
Congratulations to Brantford
Re: A Hamilton hockey-era ends. Another Bulldogs era
begins, Jan. 14
So-long, farewell, auf Wiedersehen, adieu!
Congratulations are in order to Brantford for being the permanent new home for the Bulldogs hockey team. Kudos to Michael Andlauer for making that stipulation a condition for the sale of the team to the group led by Zach Hyman.
Kicks to Hamilton's powers-that-be for their highly questionable treatment of Andlauer and the team, which was one of the best things Hamilton had going for it, by failing to give respect where respect was due to Andlauer and the Bulldogs.
Brenda Bianchi, Hamilton
We can and
must do better
Re: Catering to business is what got us here, Jan. 15
Kudos to Noelle Allen for her column outlining and bemoaning how wealth inequality has become unconscionable in Canada.
As she so rightly points out, "no one should be forced to sleep outside in January in Canada."
How did it come to be in our great country that Canadians who have to rely on disability pensions often live in abject poverty?
Canada needs a political and economic reset in the direction of greater income and wealth equality. We can, and must, do better going forward.
Philip White, professor emeritus, McMaster University
Keep carbon
pricing in place
The carbon tax for 2024 on my natural gas was $389.65. The carbon tax rebate for 2024 was $957.30.
I did not keep track of gasoline purchases, but I as a retiree do not drive as much. I am over $500 ahead. Keep the tax and especially the rebate
Bob Reed, Simcoe
Remember how Ignatieff fared?
Do these parties never learn? So Mark Carney is now running for the head of the Liberal Party, with what looks like a lot of senior Liberals backing him. This is a disaster waiting to happen. Do they not remember the Michael Ignatieff fiasco?
I'll say it now so that I do not have to write in later: told you so!
David Eyles, Hamilton

Document 344 of 500

Who should pay for homelessness? Hamilton mayor challenges province to take bigger role

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/who-should-pay-homelessness-hamilton-mayor/docview/3156537894/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Who+should+pay+for+homelessness%3F+Hamilton+mayor+challenges+province+to+take+bigger+role&amp;title=The+Hamilton+Spectator+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2025-01-17&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Hamilton+Spectator+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: The city is doing its best to tackle Hamilton's homelessness crisis, but municipal taxpayers can only shoulder so much of the burden, Mayor Andrea Horwath says.
Last year, Hamilton put $186 million toward housing and homelessness initiatives, with the city picking up 67 per cent of the tab, Horwath told a provincial prebudget panel Thursday.
That's not sustainable, she said during her presentation to the standing committee on finance and economic affairs at the Sheraton Hamilton Hotel downtown.
"The sheer magnitude of the crisis that we're facing in this city and other communities across the province is not a thing that can be borne by the municipal taxpayers. It just can't."
The city's contribution shouldn't be 67 per cent, but "quite the opposite," with upper levels of government covering the lion's share, Horwath said.
The mayor's remarks follow the release of a new report which shows roughly 81,500 people were homeless in Ontario in 2024, which represented a more than 25 per cent increase from 2022.
By 2035, the number could spike to 128,122 under "optimistic economic conditions" and as high as 294,266 during an economic slowdown, the Association of Municipalities of Ontario (AMO) report predicted.
Between 2016 and 2024, funding for housing and homelessness programs in Ontario increased from $1.9 billion to $4.1 billion, with municipalities covering 51.5 per cent of that freight, the analysis found.
Asked about Hamilton's calls for more provincial dollars, Tory MPP Donna Skelly argued the city should demonstrate how it's spending the funds it already has.
"Show me where the money has gone before you ask for another dime, because it's an awful lot of money and the homelessness problem is only getting worse," said Skelly, who represents Flamborough-Glanbrook.
Local New Democrat MPPs Sandy Shaw and Monique Taylor seized upon Skelly's remarks during the prebudget session.
"There is still one taxpayer in this city and this country and, unfortunately, other levels of government have continued to download those issues onto our city, leaving us to hold the bag as taxpayers," Taylor said.
"The last thing we need right now is for an elected official to throw doubt, chaos and division at a time when we need to be coming together," Shaw added in an interview.
Asked about Skelly's challenge, Horwath, who led the Ontario NDP before becoming mayor in 2022, said the municipal budget is a public document.
"We regularly have our staff reporting to city council in terms of how the programs are working and what might need to be adjusted to make them work better," she told the panel.
Of the $185.9 million, $21.3 million is for affordable housing, $23.6 million for tenant supports, $93.6 million for social housing, $39.1 million for shelters and $8.3 million for encampments.
Despite municipal calls for more funding, Municipal Affairs and Housing Minister Paul Calandra says his government has "made the largest investment in affordable housing and anti-homelessness programs in Ontario's history."
Over the next three years, it's "investing a record $3 billion in affordable housing, anti-homelessness and emergency shelter supports," Calandra's spokesperson told The Spectator in an emailed statement.
In Hamilton, city officials have cited various figures to describe investment in housing and homelessness. Here's a breakdown:
In 2024, council approved $158 million through the budget, but then added $28 million in funds from the three levels of government, topping up the annual total to $186 million.
Overall, the federal government contributed $19.9 million (11 per cent), the province $41 million (22 per cent) and the city $125 million (67 per cent).
In Hamilton, roughly 1,600 people are homeless, with about 250 of them living outdoors as of December, the city says.
This past fall, council backed a plan to create 272 more temporary shelter beds this winter, including 40 modular cabins for up to 80 people with support services on vacant municipal land off Barton Street West. Staff expect all of those spaces to be available by the end of the month.
Housing and homelessness, as well as their intersection with health, figured prominently during Thursday's prebudget feedback session.
There's an "urgent need" to bolster income security through higher rates for Ontario Works (OW) and the Ontario Disability Support Program (ODSP), said Tom Cooper, director of the Hamilton Roundtable for Poverty Reduction.
The province should also do more to protect tenants from eviction to prevent homelessness and improve services for mental health and addictions, Cooper said.
"These interconnected issues demand bold action to address the root causes of poverty, housing insecurity, and the growing mental-health crisis."
Cooper said OW and ODSP rates "fall far below" the poverty line and don't come close to covering the cost of rent in Hamilton.
"Without adequate income supports, individuals are at a heightened risk of homelessness. Preventing homelessness is far more cost-effective than responding to its aftermath."
A bed in shelter runs $2,100 a month, $4,300 in a correctional facility and $13,500 in a hospital, Cooper noted.
"Yet, raising OW and ODSP rates to align with the cost of living could reduce reliance on these costly services while restoring dignity and stability to Ontarians."
Teviah Moro is a reporter at The Hamilton Spectator. tmoro@thespec.com
CAPTION: People have pitched tents in a small green space at the foot of the Claremont Access in Hamilton's lower city.Mayor Andrea Horwath, centre, addresses the province's standing committee on finance and economic affairs Thursday at the Sheraton Hamilton Hotel downtown. Theresa Malar, executive of the Hamilton Aquatic Club, right; and Brenda Wilson, executive director and CEO of the Stoney Creek Chamber of Commerce, look on.

People: Horwath, Andrea
