Document 345 of 500

Jan. 17: Fire highlights need for housing, Skelly has it all wrong and other letters to the editor

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/jan-17-fire-highlights-need-housing-skelly-has/docview/3156386056/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Jan.+17%3A+Fire+highlights+need+for+housing%2C+Skelly+has+it+all+wrong+and+other+letters+to+the+editor&amp;title=The+Hamilton+Spectator+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2025-01-17&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Hamilton+Spectator+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Fire highlights need for housing
Re: Zeke Fox meets man he rescued from burning tent in Bayfront Park, launches fundraiser, Jan. 15
Zeke Fox, the young guy who rescued Shawn Goodwin from a burning tent, certainly is a hero. But the story highlights once again how essential it is to have decent housing. This accident would not have happened if Mr. Goodwin had been appropriately housed. Now he is facing a long and painful hospital recovery, which will cost taxpayers thousands of dollars.
We need to invest in housing to prevent accidents like these and to provide every Hamiltonian with a place to call home.
Karen Trollope-Kumar, Hamilton
Politics shouldn't be boring
Re: We could do with a lot more boring, Jan. 15
Steve Buist's idea that politics should be boring is quite wrong. Politics are about how we decide what needs to be done collectively - that inherently involves clashes of values. Take even what he considers mundane matters, such as sewers and roads. Who should pay? New homeowners through development charges or existing taxpayers? Which roads and where? Do we need a Highway 413? Roads versus transit?
"Making people safe and healthy" involves clashes over individual autonomy versus group safety, and how to deal with crime. Health funding is always debated - how much and what is the priority?
And budgets? Every budget is, at its base, a values document. How much do we spend, on what, and how we pay for it? User fees or taxes? What types of taxes? Income? Consumption? Property? Capital gains? With what impact on which incomes groups?
There are fundamental debates over what governments should do versus markets, and within markets, how they should be regulated, if at all. We debate the place of public input in decisions. And in some countries, democratic politics itself is, or appears to be, at stake.
Like Buist, I hate that politics has been reduced to a sportslike battle and winning at any cost. But as long as vision and vales are at stake, politics can't be boring. What we need to insist on is that it be civil.
Bill Johnston, Dundas
Skelly has it all wrong
Re: Hamilton MPP Donna Skelly wants to see receipts for city's $158 million in spending, Jan. 14
How dare MPP Donna Skelly admonish the citizens of Hamilton for asking for more money to address homelessness! "Show me where the money has gone before you ask for another dime." You? The money is ours, not hers. Instead of advocating for our city, she treats us like children. Same with her boss, Doug Ford. He seems to think Ontario is his own company to do with as he wishes.
Hamilton is a poor city. Under the Mike Harris Progressive Conservative government, we had social service and housing costs dumped on us. The effects remain, and have been compounding over decades. We need real money - a portion of income taxes - not crumbs thrown our way if we ask nicely. And we need representatives who will stand up for Hamilton, no matter their party stripe.
Carol Town, Hamilton
Keep tax hike at inflation level
Our math-challenged mayor is proposing a tax hike that is nearly three times the current inflation rate. Perhaps she is selling this on the basis that Toronto is proposing a 6.9 per cent hike. But that is on a base that is one half of Hamilton's residential property tax rate. How about an increase at the inflation rate and tell the bureaucrats to live with it? Or find some who can, just like the world the rest of us live in.
Richard Ronchka, Carlisle
Jama acting like activist, not MPP
Re: New year, new chance for Jama, Jan. 14
Even after voting for Sarah Jama in the last election, I do not agree with reinstating her to the NDP caucus.
Jama's repeated anti-Israeli comments on social media, her speech at a pro-Palestinian rally during which she referred to Israel as an "apartheid state," as well as her insincere apology for these remarks, demonstrate a lack of awareness of the role of a civil servant and politician.
Jama was unable to transition from political activist and protester to a leader who represents all constituents in her riding. As a candidate who has proven in several instances that she is unable to do so, I question whether she should be given a chance to reclaim the NDP nomination.
Andrea Rado, Hamilton
Opinion or propaganda?
How could you possibly publish that "opinion" from Margaret Shkimba? It is like reading something out of Cuba's Granma (the official newspaper of the Communist Party of Cuba). My goodness, this is exactly what Justin Trudeau's ruination of Canada has brought us to. Sarah Jama and Shkimba are the Canada that I no longer recognize. This is craziness. This isn't Canada.
Jack Fernihough, Hamilton
A step back from sustainability
The provincial government has passed controversial legislation that will prevent municipal governments from adding bike lanes and, believe it or not, remove bike lanes to fit more cars on the roads. Municipalities will now need to ask permission from the provincial government.
Now this whole situation may backfire on the Progressive Conservative party as it may receive backlash from urban residents, cyclists and environmental groups. It is recognized that the party does not support climate change efforts, but that does not give it a reason to make it worse.
The Progressive Conservative party argues this decision is meant to provide consistency and prevent local municipalities from creating changes that will affect the flow of traffic and needs of drivers.
Municipalities are best positioned to understand specific needs of the community. The mayor and city council were all voted in by the public. By restricting their ability to make vital decisions, they become powerless.
Jake Peshko, Dundas Valley High School
Don't get caught in Trump's distractions
In today's information world, the name of the game is distraction. Donald Trump's main platform during the election was to fix immigration, and that was one of the main reasons he was elected. Along comes Elon Musk and his donations to the Trump campaign and now they say they need more immigration to fill the employment demand. Through the H-1B visa program, they want to bring in more workers, not because there's a shortage, but because they want to suppress wages.
Soon after they start talking about Canada becoming the 51st state, and there's your distraction, and everyone is having a hissy fit. It's never gonna happen. Headlines are never the important story.
Tony Fallucco, Hamilton
CAPTION: Zeke Fox, right, was able to meet recently with Shawn Goodwin, the man he rescued from a burning tent. The story highlights once again how essential it is to have decent housing, a reader says.

Subject: Municipal government; Taxes; Political campaigns; Tax rates; Local elections; Inflation rates; Accident prevention; Presidential elections; Immigration; Political parties; Tax increases

Who should pay for homelessness?: Hamilton mayor challenges province to take bigger role

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/who-should-pay-homelessness/docview/3156385645/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Who+should+pay+for+homelessness%3F%3A+Hamilton+mayor+challenges+province+to+take+bigger+role&amp;title=The+Spectator&amp;issn=11899417&amp;date=2025-01-17&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.1&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Spectator&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

"The last thing we need right now is for an elected official to throw doubt, chaos and division at a time when we need to be coming together," Shaw added in an interview.
Asked about Skelly's challenge, Horwath, who led the Ontario NDP before becoming mayor in 2022, said the municipal budget is a public document.
"We regularly have our staff reporting to city council in terms of how the programs are working and what might need to be adjusted to make them work better," she told the panel.
Of the $185.9 million, $21.3 million is for affordable housing, $23.6 million for tenant supports, $93.6 million for social housing, $39.1 million for shelters and $8.3 million for encampments.
Despite municipal calls for more funding, Municipal Affairs and Housing Minister Paul Calandra says his government has "made the largest investment in affordable housing and anti-homelessness programs in Ontario's history."
Over the next three years, it's "investing a record $3 billion in affordable housing, anti-homelessness and emergency shelter supports," Calandra's spokesperson told The Spectator in an emailed statement.
In Hamilton, city officials have cited various figures to describe investment in housing and homelessness. Here's a breakdown:
In 2024, council approved $158 million through the budget, but then added $28 million in funds from the three levels of government, topping up the annual total to $186 million.
Overall, the federal government contributed $19.9 million (11 per cent), the province $41 million (22 per cent) and the city $125 million (67 per cent).
In Hamilton, roughly 1,600 people are homeless, with about 250 of them living outdoors as of December, the city says.
This past fall, council backed a plan to create 272 more temporary shelter beds this winter, including 40 modular cabins for up to 80 people with support services on vacant municipal land off Barton Street West. Staff expect all of those spaces to be available by the end of the month.
Housing and homelessness, as well as their intersection with health, figured prominently during Thursday's prebudget feedback session.
There's an "urgent need" to bolster income security through higher rates for Ontario Works (OW) and the Ontario Disability Support Program (ODSP), said Tom Cooper, director of the Hamilton Roundtable for Poverty Reduction.
The province should also do more to protect tenants from eviction to prevent homelessness and improve services for mental health and addictions, Cooper said.
"These interconnected issues demand bold action to address the root causes of poverty, housing insecurity, and the growing mental-health crisis."
Cooper said OW and ODSP rates "fall far below" the poverty line and don't come close to covering the cost of rent in Hamilton.
"Without adequate income supports, individuals are at a heightened risk of homelessness. Preventing homelessness is far more cost-effective than responding to its aftermath."
A bed in shelter runs $2,100 a month, $4,300 in a correctional facility and $13,500 in a hospital, Cooper noted.
"Yet, raising OW and ODSP rates to align with the cost of living could reduce reliance on these costly services while restoring dignity and stability to Ontarians."
Teviah Moro is a reporter
at The Hamilton Spectator. tmoro@thespec.com

Brenda Wilson, executive director and CEO of the Stoney Creek Chamber of Commerce looks on.The Hamilton SpectatorTeviah Moro The Hamilton Spectator

Letters

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/letters/docview/3156385349/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=unknown&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Letters&amp;title=The+Spectator&amp;issn=11899417&amp;date=2025-01-17&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.11&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Spectator&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Tent fire highlights need for housing
Re: Zeke Fox meets man he rescued from burning tent in Bayfront Park, launches fundraiser, Jan. 15
Zeke Fox, the young guy who rescued Shawn Goodwin from a burning tent, certainly is a hero. But the story highlights once again how essential it is to have decent housing. This accident would not have happened if Mr. Goodwin had been appropriately housed. Now he is facing a long and painful hospital recovery, which will cost taxpayers thousands of dollars.
We need to invest in housing to prevent accidents like these and to provide every Hamiltonian with a place to call home.
Karen Trollope-Kumar,
Hamilton
Politics shouldn't be boring
Re: We could do with a lot more boring, Jan. 15
Steve Buist's idea that politics should be boring is quite wrong. Politics are about how we decide what needs to be done collectively - that inherently involves clashes of values. Take even what he considers mundane matters, such as sewers and roads. Who should pay? New homeowners through development charges or existing taxpayers? Which roads and where? Do we need a Highway 413? Roads versus transit?
"Making people safe and healthy" involves clashes over individual autonomy versus group safety, and how to deal with crime. Health funding is always debated - how much and what is the priority?
And budgets? Every budget is, at its base, a values document. How much do we spend, on what, and how we pay for it? User fees or taxes? What types of taxes? Income? Consumption? Property? Capital gains? With what impact on which incomes groups?
There are fundamental debates over what governments should do versus markets, and within markets, how they should be regulated, if at all. We debate the place of public input in decisions. And in some countries, democratic politics itself is, or appears to be, at stake.
Like Buist, I hate that politics has been reduced to a sportslike battle and winning at any cost. But as long as vision and vales are at stake, politics can't be boring. What we need to insist on is that it be civil.
Bill Johnston, Dundas
MPP Skelly has it all wrong
Re: Hamilton MPP Donna Skelly wants to see receipts for city's $158 million in spending, Jan. 14
How dare MPP Donna Skelly admonish the citizens of Hamilton for asking for more money to address homelessness! "Show me where the money has gone before you ask for another dime." You? The money is ours, not hers. Instead of advocating for our city, she treats us like children. Same with her boss, Doug Ford. He seems to think Ontario is his own company to do with as he wishes.
Hamilton is a poor city. Under the Mike Harris Progressive Conservative government, we had social service and housing costs dumped on us. The effects remain, and have been compounding over decades. We need real money - a portion of income taxes - not crumbs thrown our way if we ask nicely. And we need representatives who will stand up for Hamilton, no matter their party stripe.
Carol Town, Hamilton
Keep tax hike at inflation level
Our math-challenged mayor is proposing a tax hike that is nearly three times the current inflation rate. Perhaps she is selling this on the basis that Toronto is proposing a 6.9 per cent hike. But that is on a base that is one half of Hamilton's residential property tax rate.
How about an increase at the inflation rate and tell the bureaucrats to live with it? Or find some who can, just like the world the rest of us live in.
Richard Ronchka, Carlisle
Jama acting like activist, not MPP
Re: New year, new chance for
Jama, Jan. 14
Even after voting for Sarah Jama in the last election, I do not agree with reinstating her to the NDP caucus.
Jama's repeated anti-Israeli comments on social media, her speech at a pro-Palestinian rally during which she referred to Israel as an "apartheid state," as well as her insincere apology for these remarks, demonstrate a lack of awareness of the role of a civil servant and politician.
Jama was unable to transition from political activist and protester to a leader who represents all constituents in her riding. As a candidate who has proven in several instances that she is unable to do so, I question whether she should be given a chance to reclaim the NDP nomination.
Andrea Rado, Hamilton

Document 348 of 500

Will Trump's tariffs trigger a recession in Ontario? Here's what economists are saying

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/will-trumps-tariffs-trigger-recession-ontario/docview/3155986529/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Will+Trump%27s+tariffs+trigger+a+recession+in+Ontario%3F+Here%27s+what+economists+are+saying&amp;title=The+Hamilton+Spectator+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2025-01-16&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Hamilton+Spectator+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: U.S. president-elect Donald Trump has pledged to enact 25 per cent tariffs on all products from Canada and Mexico after taking office on Jan. 20.
Ontario Premier Doug Ford has said the tariffs would cripple Ontario's economy, disrupt supply chains and put up to 500,000 Ontarians out of work.
Economists have also issued dire predictions that tariffs could plunge Ontario into a deep recession.
What is a recession?
Beata Caranci, chief economist at TD, notes the basic definition of recession is two consecutive quarters where the economy contracts. That usually equates to a reduction in gross domestic product (GDP). A recession can vary in length, however, it typically ranges between two and four quarters.
What will tariffs do to Ontario's economy?
University of Calgary economist Trevor Tombe has estimated a 25 per cent tariff would cut Canada's economy by 2.6 per cent in 2025, sending the country into a recession.
Prior to the U.S. election, an analysis by TD economist Marc Ercolao found that a 10 per cent across-the-board tariff would cause real GDP to fall 2.4 percentage points over two years relative to baseline projections.
Could tariffs happen immediately?
Tuesday (Jan. 14) on social media, Canadian businessperson and media pundit Kevin O'Leary said he doesn't expect tariffs to be implemented immediately upon Trump's Jan. 20 inauguration.
O'Leary met the president-elect last weekend at his Mar-a-Lago home in Florida and posed for a photo with Trump along with Alberta Premier Danielle Smith.
"The number 1 thing on Trump's agenda isn't to tariff Canada," O'Leary said Tuesday on X. "Danielle Smith was wise enough to spend time with him and scored a lot of points with a lot of different people in the cabinet."
O'Leary noted he has also told Trump to "stop wasting his time" with the current Liberal government, given the opposition Conservatives have a big polling lead heading into this year's election.
Economic devastation
A December analysis of Trump's proposed tariffs by Ben Eisen, Fraser Institute senior fellow, predicted 25 per cent tariffs would devastate Ontario's economy.
In 2023, Ontario's exports totalled $226 billion and represented more than one-fifth of the provincial economy, Eisen noted. Trade with the U.S. is estimated to account for 39 per cent of Ontario's overall economy.
Increase the cost of doing business
Eisen noted tariffs may also increase costs for Ontario businesses by making machinery and inputs more expensive. That could lead to job losses and reduced economic opportunities for Ontario residents.
"For the same reasons that lowering trade barriers has benefitted consumers in Canada and Ontario, Trump's proposed trade tariffs would hurt his own constituents, as Americans will face higher prices for many goods," Eisen noted. "But the damage to Canada and particularly Ontario will be greater because we depend more on trade with the U.S. than Americans depend on trade with Canada."
Marvin Ryder, associate professor of marketing at McMaster University, said tariffs against Canada and Mexico would trigger an immediate North American recession.
"It wouldn't just be in Canada it would be in the United States," said Ryder.
The U.S. imports 4.3 million barrels of oil daily from Canada, Ryder noted. If energy exports are subject to tariffs, gas prices for Americans would instantly rise 25 per cent.
Ryder said blanket tariffs would also cripple the North American auto industry. The average North American automobile crosses the border eight times as it's made, said Ryder. A 25 per cent tariff would raise automobile prices on both sides of the border and lead to potential job losses, Ryder added.
CAPTION: This is a screen shot of a Nov. 25 social media post made by U.S. president-elect Donald Trump stating he will impose a 25 per cent tariff on all Canadian and Mexican imports the day he takes office, which is on Monday, unless the two countries curb the flow of drugs and migrants across their borders.This photo shows Niagara Falls from the Canadian side of the border, in May 2024. In 2023, Ontario's exports totalled $226 billion and represent more than one-fifth of the provincial economy. Economists have predicted 25 per cent tariffs on Canadian exports to the U.S. would plunge Ontario's economy into a recession.

Premiers urge unity in face of Trump's tariff threats: 'Alberta will simply not agree to export tariffs on our energy,' Smith says

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/premiers-urge-unity-face-trumps-tariff-threats/docview/3155986449/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Premiers+urge+unity+in+face+of+Trump%27s+tariff+threats%3A+%27Alberta+will+simply+not+agree+to+export+tariffs+on+our+energy%2C%27+Smith+says&amp;title=The+Spectator&amp;issn=11899417&amp;date=2025-01-16&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.11&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Spectator&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: The country's premiers emerged from a key meeting about Canada-U.S. relations in Ottawa on Wednesday with a message of unity and a pledge to stand together, with one exception: Alberta's Danielle Smith, who said she cannot support the federal government's plan if energy export tariffs could be a part of it.
Prime Minister Justin Trudeau, who chaired the first ministers' meeting, said it was "highly productive."
"We have a shared sense of purpose, a shared sense of understanding, and a commitment to stand together on a united path forward," he said.
Saskatchewan Premier Scott Moe, who has long been a vocal critic of Trudeau's Liberal government, began Wednesday by echoing Smith's concerns that blocking energy exports to the United States would trigger a national unity crisis.
After the meeting, he struck a diplomatic tone.
"Canadians can be thankful their 13 premiers, the prime minister and a few federal ministers are coming together not only to hash out what those differences might be, but to understand where we can come together," Moe said.
But as the premiers spoke to reporters in Ottawa, Smith, who attended the meeting remotely while on vacation and didn't take part in the news conference, took to social media to explain why she refused to sign a joint statement with her colleagues.
"Alberta will simply not agree to export tariffs on our energy or other products, nor do we support a ban on exports of these same products. We will take whatever actions are needed to protect the livelihoods of Albertans from such destructive federal policies," said Smith.
She added that Canada needs to "correct the misguided direction of this country" and focus on developing, upgrading and exporting oil and gas and other resources.
Ontario Premier Doug Ford, chair of the Council of the Federation, said he respects that Smith is concerned with protecting Alberta energy but he believes incoming U.S. president Donald Trump is trying to divide and conquer.
"He's coming full-tilt at Canadians as a whole, and that's one thing we have to understand. We need to be united," Ford said.
Trump has threatened to impose a 25 per cent tariff on all Canadian goods beginning on Monday, inauguration day.
While he initially said the tariffs were being imposed in response to drugs and illegal immigration crossing the shared border, he has since pivoted to citing the United States' trade deficit with Canada.
Senior government officials have previously said Ottawa is looking at imposing retaliatory tariffs on things like American steel, ceramics, plastics and orange juice.
Trudeau said Wednesday he supports the principle of a dollar-for-dollar response to the American tariffs, but said Canada must wait to see what Trump actually does next week before laying out a response.
"We'll make sure that it's fair across the country, but nothing can be off the table if the U.S. continues to choose to move forward with these punishing tariffs on both Canadians and Americans," Trudeau said.
He also opened the door to providing federal bailouts for industries or regions that are affected by tariffs.
"We've made the commitment to do everything we can, and I know everyone around the table has agreed to, if their region is less impacted, they'll do what they can to help out regions that are more impacted," he said.
Canada is the top supplier of foreign oil to the U.S. and accounts for about one-fifth of its oil supply.
The Canadian Press
CAPTION: Prime Minister Justin Trudeau speaks during a first ministers meeting in Ottawa Wednesday.Ontario Premier Doug Ford arrives for a first ministers meeting in Ottawa Wednesday.Justin Tang The Canadian Press the Canadian Press

Port Hope may get nuclear plant: Ford government's proposed project could power 10 million homes, energy minister says

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/port-hope-may-get-nuclear-plant/docview/3155986345/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Port+Hope+may+get+nuclear+plant%3A+Ford+government%27s+proposed+project+could+power+10+million+homes%2C+energy+minister+says&amp;title=The+Spectator&amp;issn=11899417&amp;date=2025-01-16&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.16&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Spectator&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Ontario is focusing on the Port Hope area - no stranger to the nuclear industry - as the best bet for a massive new nuclear power station to meet forecasts for soaring electricity needs in the next 25 years.
But there are many hurdles in terms of community consultation, studies and approvals that make it unlikely that a power plant producing up to 10,000 megawatts would be up and running until the 2040s.
"We have a lot of work ahead of us," Ontario Power Generation (OPG) president Nicole Butcher told a news conference Wednesday announcing the Lake Ontario town east of Oshawa has expressed interest in being the host.
The site in Wesleyville has long been owned by OPG and was destined to become an oil-fired power plant until a 1970s oil crisis sent fuel prices skyrocketing and scuttled that plan. It has been sitting idle but is zoned for a power plant and is close to transmission lines, as well as the existing Darlington nuclear plant.
Energy Minister Stephen Lecce said the new plant, if it is built following extensive consultations with Port Hope and the Williams Treaties First Nations, would be equivalent to "two Darlingtons," making it one of the biggest nuclear generating stations in the world and capable of powering 10 million homes.
While Darlington and the older Pickering nuclear plant east of it use Canada-designed CANDU nuclear technology, no decisions have been made on the type of reactors that could be built on the 1,300-acre Wesleyville site.
Officials at a background briefing on the plan said if all goes well, the first reactor could be operating in the 2040s, although Lecce spoke of a "10-to-15 year time frame."
Port Hope was the only one of three municipalities that expressed interest in a power plant after the province in late November named sites it owns in Wesleyville, Nanticoke on the north shore of Lake Erie and along the St. Clair River south of Sarnia, as possible locations.
The Nanticoke and Sarnia-area sites were home to massive coal-fired generation stations shut down years ago in the fight against climate change.
Green Leader Mike Schreiner has called for public hearings on a new energy plan the Ford government is developing in the wake of forecasts that the need for electricity will jump 75 per cent by 2050.
He wants to see a "competitive procurement process for the lowest-cost, emission-free electricity" instead of automatically choosing nuclear power.
Lecce has said the government is considering "all types of new energy generation," but nuclear is needed as emission-free baseload power.
CAPTION:

CREDIT: Rob Ferguson Toronto Star

Document 351 of 500

Local councillors getting work done while politicians in Ottawa, Toronto focus on themselves

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/local-councillors-getting-work-done-while/docview/3155986140/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Local+councillors+getting+work+done+while+politicians+in+Ottawa%2C+Toronto+focus+on+themselves&amp;title=The+Hamilton+Spectator+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2025-01-16&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Hamilton+Spectator+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Political leaders make promises during campaigns, and then what happens?
Canadian politicians are elected for four years. Four years is four years, with the next election date for a municipal level carved in stone. Federally and provincially, four years can be four years, or whenever internal polls tell incumbents they'd have a good chance of winning.
This is why Premier Doug Ford is handing out goodies to municipalities and the public, while doing what previous Conservative premiers shunned: building a huge deficit - the highest in my recollection, north of $6.5 billion (with a B)!
The game plan for politicians is to have and hold power as long as they can.
Federally, Justin Trudeau didn't heed the public or his own caucus, his timing leaving us in a mess, considering the U.S. situation, just before his own caucus reached total revolt mode. Pierre Poilievre, probably the most unlikeable politician in years, may succeed him and Trudeau's unpopularity could extend to Liberals at other levels.
In my opinion, leaders have a shelf life - a "best-before date" - seldom of 10 or more years.
An exception, probably because of his middle-of-the-road policies, was former premier Bill Davis. First elected in 1959, he was premier from 1971 until 1985. Prior to that, he was education minister for 12 years. Davis was probably Ontario's most popular premier.
Ford, on the other hand, bumbles from one gaffe to another: the Greenbelt, allowing a former housing minister to run amok, developer favouritism at the staff level, friendships with active developers, health care and long-term care issues, the Ontario Science Centre debacle and minister's zoning orders - their numbers exceeding those of all previous governments combined.
He amassed this large deficit as he heads for an early election. Disdain for municipalities is a trademark of his legislation - overruling councils on where and if they install bike lanes, and enacting planning laws that hog-tie them with excessively tight timelines that stifle public participation and provide the Ontario Land Tribunal (OLT) a way to overrule elected councils, like Burlington's and Hamilton's. The OLT's approval of developers' appeals has been 97 per cent!
Ford's changes weakened councils by bestowing "strong mayor" powers. If Liberal Leader Bonnie Crombie's platform includes rescinding those powers, it could resonate with tuned-in voters. That and curtailing the OLT's power, or outright scrapping it, would bolster democracy. In all of Canada, only Ontario has such a tribunal, and one lone provincial appointee can overrule an elected council's decision. It's no wonder councils abhor this system.
Burlington City Hall happenings
This was committee week in Burlington, and two major housing initiatives were approved. One deserving praise required short-term rental owners and operators to be licensed so they can be controlled. That will help ease the minds of neighbours who have suffered from the hassles of rowdy partygoers and trespassing.
What is needed is long-term rentals. Some developers are proposing purpose-built rentals (in various stages of approval), but to construct a highrise takes a long time.
The second item, too complex to detail in a short column, was allowing ARUs (additional residential units) in most of our urban area as of right, termed "gentle density intensification."
This means more units in the areas we always thought of as "single family." These could be in a new building, a house could be divided into more units, an outbuilding converted, etc. - conditional on several factors, such as lot size and shape, location and other parameters. This also applies to medium-density zones (townhouses, fourplexes, etc.) which can increase their density too.
The report is almost 100 pages long, and interested owners should contact the planning department for detailed requirements.
What will the overall impact be on Burlington? Will new units be affordable? Will the look and feel of our city change much? Let's just say the jury's out, and I'm a little nervous.
CAPTION: Premier Doug Ford is reportedly looking to call an early election, but he'll be carrying a lot baggage into that race, Joan Little writes.

Subject: Councils; Rentals; Politicians

Document 352 of 500

Jan. 16: Steep fee caters to the rich, who mails anything anymore and other letters to the editor

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/jan-16-steep-fee-caters-rich-who-mails-anything/docview/3155986124/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Jan.+16%3A+Steep+fee+caters+to+the+rich%2C+who+mails+anything+anymore+and+other+letters+to+the+editor&amp;title=The+Hamilton+Spectator+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2025-01-16&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Hamilton+Spectator+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Steep fee caters to the rich
Re: Liberals set tighter rules for coming leadership race amid foreign interference concerns, Jan. 10
The buy-in to run for Liberal Party leader is $350,000. I think we all need to take a moment to sit back and contemplate that amount. Over a third of a million dollars? That's winning the lottery money. If your concern is that the Liberal Party is too heavily in the pocket of monied interests to care about us common folk, they certainly are not sending the right message by guaranteeing that only the rich need apply for prime minister.
David La Rush, Hamilton
Who mails letters anymore?
OK, so, the price of a single stamp to mail a domestic letter is now $1.44, up from $1.15 (hands up if you remember two-cent stamps). Canada Post justifies the increase to better align stamp prices with the rising cost of providing better mail service to all Canadians. Hello? Who mails anything anymore?
I can't remember the last time l licked an envelope, put a stamp on it and dropped it in a mail box. The last thing I mailed was a customer return with the postage provided by the vendor. So why is the cost of mailing fewer items becoming more expensive? I suggest Canada Post have a good look in the mirror and come up with a plan - a realistic plan - to change how it services Canadians and how to approach the dwindling amount of actual "mail" being delivered without simply raising prices.
Rusty Escott, Dunnville
Express your frustration by voting
Every day I read the letters to the Spec showing citizens voicing their frustration with health care, lack of funding for schools and Ontario cities grappling with homelessness. Remember, these problems have only exacerbated under Doug Ford's leadership for the wealthy.
Ford cut funding to our cities for our social networks, thus forcing cities to raise taxes higher than ever. Ford has underfunded our schools creating a learning environment that is toxic to staff and students, and Ford has understaffed and underfunded our hospitals to subsidize private health care.
Remember to vote for the candidate who will promise to help you, the average citizens of Ontario.
David Intini, Stoney Creek
Build then expand GO service
The other day, NDP Leader Marit Stiles and MPP Wayne Gates staged a photo op in Grimsby, promising all-day service to Niagara when she becomes premier. She will not (be) premier. Let me mention Bob Rae brought GO to downtown Hamilton then said it would go to Niagara. Dalton McGuinty promised in 2010 all-day service to Hamilton and Niagara.
Didn't happen.
Doug Ford brought all-day service to Hamilton and Niagara now has six trains a day between Toronto and Niagara. What Stiles ignores is the soon-to-open Centennial (Stoney Creek) GO station and the $32-million work on the St. Catharines GO station. All started under Ford. A lot more infrastructure must be done to get all-day service to Niagara. What the NDP ignores is the basic facts: the numbers are not there to support all-day service. Build your riders then improve the service.
Kevin Argue, Jordan Station
Our priorities getting fumbled
Re: Discovery of new fungi compound leads to hope for potential dementia, Parkinson's and depression drug, Jan. 13
It was great to read the story of Dr. Timsy Bhando in today's Spectator. To have such talent as part of McMaster is amazing. Her hard work and dedication could one day decrease the effects or even cure Alzheimer's, dementia or Parkinson's.
It was, however, interesting that there was a picture below her article on Page A1 of Josh Allen of the Buffalo Bills. I would assume Dr. Bhando is making somewhere in the range of $100,000 to $250,000 per year. I looked up Josh Allen's 2024 salary and it is said to be $14 million (for about 30 games). It just brings to mind that, more and more, society has its priorities all screwed up.
Philip Watkins, Binbrook
Remembrance and reflection
Re: 'Education, remembrance, and reflection' aim of annual Hamilton event to commemorate Auschwitz liberation, Jan. 14
Education, remembrance and reflection are important aspects of social and cultural life, pasts, presents and futures. Bearing witness is important, too, for the sake of those past, present and to come, and I'm grateful that such an invitation exists for a community to reflect on who it was, is and can be.
In such a spirit, however, the truth of our days must also be told, particularly remembering present victims and the dangers of antisemitism. While we educate on and remember the Shoah and attempted genocide(s) of the past, let us reflect on, bear witness to, and, most importantly, stop the genocides of the present.
To do any less is to be utterly hypocritical. We do absolutely need to educate and to talk about the past with our children, so that we can learn from past mistakes, not repeat them in the present days, and refuse to end peoples' dignified futures.
Joshua Weresch, Hamilton
Ready to sing loud and proud
On inauguration day, I intend to stand under my neighbour's Canadian flag and sing "O Canada" loud and clear, and post on Facebook me playing ukulele singing "O Canada." I am a protester from the '60s when we sang "We shall overcome" and we will not be overcome by new president. I don't speak his name - a silent protest.
Suzanne Burchell, Hamilton
Thanks for the memories
Re: A Road to Blandness, Jan. 14
What a great read! As someone who has travelled Highway 6 to Guelph frequently over the years, both my family and I recall all these iconic landmarks. Definitely brought back memories of how much more fun and interesting a drive it used to be! Thanks for the memories!
Mary Lane, Stoney Creek
Stop the blame game
So our esteemed mayor says the province should step up its game on the homelessness issue. Well Andrea, like Donna Skelly says, please show us how the $158 million is being spent, because that's an awful lot of money for a problem that is only getting worse!
It has come to the point that we cannot enjoy our parks anymore (that are littered with garbage and discarded needles), stroll through the downtown area without being approached and threatened by the homeless, and in general, the expanse of a once lovely city being literally taken over by this escalating problem.
Maybe put your money where your mouth is and perhaps study the ways that some of the European countries dealt with their homelessness problems. We know it is not an easy fix, but please stop using the taxpayers' money for something that is definitely not improving and stop blaming the federal and provincial governments.
Linda Ferrie, Waterdown
CAPTION: With a buy-in fee of $350,000, the Liberals are guaranteeing that only the rich need apply to become leader, a reader writes.

Subject: Professional football; Leadership; Education; Dementia; Homeless people; Social networks

Document 354 of 500

Local politicians getting work done

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/local-politicians-getting-work-done/docview/3155986088/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=unknown&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Local+politicians+getting+work+done&amp;title=The+Spectator&amp;issn=11899417&amp;date=2025-01-16&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.15&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Spectator&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Political leaders make promises during campaigns, and then what happens?
Canadian politicians are elected for four years. Four years is four years, with the next election date for a municipal level carved in stone. Federally and provincially, four years can be four years, or whenever internal polls tell incumbents they'd have a good chance of winning.
This is why Premier Doug Ford is handing out goodies to municipalities and the public, while doing what previous Conservative premiers shunned: building a huge deficit - the highest in my recollection, north of $6.5 billion!
The game plan for politicians is to hold power as long as they can.
Federally, Justin Trudeau didn't heed the public or his own caucus, his timing leaving us in a mess, considering the U.S. situation, just before his own caucus reached total revolt mode. Pierre Poilievre, probably the most unlikeable politician in years, may succeed him and Trudeau's unpopularity could extend to Liberals at other levels.
In my opinion, leaders have a shelf life - a "best-before date" - seldom of 10 or more years.
An exception, probably because of his middle-of-the-road policies, was former premier Bill Davis. First elected in 1959, he was premier from 1971 until 1985. Prior to that, he was education minister for 12 years. Davis was probably Ontario's most popular premier.
Ford, on the other hand, bumbles from one gaffe to another: the Greenbelt, allowing a former housing minister to run amok, developer favouritism at the staff level, friendships with active developers, health care and long-term care issues, the Ontario Science Centre debacle and minister's zoning orders - their numbers exceeding those of all previous governments combined.
He amassed this large deficit as he heads for an early election. Disdain for municipalities is a trademark of his legislation - overruling councils on where and if they install bike lanes, and enacting planning laws that hog-tie them with excessively tight timelines that stifle public participation and provide the Ontario Land Tribunal (OLT) a way to overrule elected councils, like Burlington's and Hamilton's. The OLT's approval of developers' appeals has been 97 per cent!
Ford's changes weakened councils by bestowing "strong mayor" powers. If Liberal Leader Bonnie Crombie's platform includes rescinding those powers, it could resonate with tuned-in voters. That and curtailing the OLT's power, or outright scrapping it, would bolster democracy. In all of Canada, only Ontario has such a tribunal, and one lone provincial appointee can overrule an elected council's decision. It's no wonder councils abhor this system.
Burlington City Hall happenings
This was committee week in Burlington, and two major housing initiatives were approved. One deserving praise required short-term rental owners and operators to be licensed so they can be controlled. That will help ease the minds of neighbours who have suffered from the hassles of rowdy partygoers and trespassing.
What is needed is long-term rentals. Some developers are proposing purpose-built rentals (in various stages of approval), but to construct a highrise takes a long time.
The second item, too complex to detail in a short column, was allowing ARUs (additional residential units) in most of our urban area as of right, termed "gentle density intensification."
This means more units in the areas we always thought of as "single family." These could be in a new building, a house could be divided into more units, an outbuilding converted, etc. - conditional on several factors, such as lot size and shape, location and other parameters. This also applies to medium-density zones (townhouses, fourplexes, etc.) which can increase their density too.
The report is almost 100 pages long, and interested owners should contact the planning department for detailed requirements.
What will the overall impact be on Burlington? Will new units be affordable? Will the look and feel of our city change much? Let's just say the jury's out, and I'm a little nervous.
Freelance columnist
Joan Little is a former
Burlington alderperson and Halton councillor. Reach her
at specjoan@cogeco.ca.
CAPTION:

Hamilton-area Today: It'll feel like -20 C this morning + Convicted killer backs away from freedom bid + Residents with no heat speak out + City's encampment protocol

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/hamilton-area-today-itll-feel-like-20-c-this/docview/3155986067/se-2?accountid=15090

Full text: Good morning! It's Jan. 16, and here are your top stories today in the Hamilton area.
Today's traffic and weather
Flurries and wind gusts up to 50 km/h are expected throughout the Hamilton area this morning. Environment Canada says the daytime high of -5 C will feel like -20 in the morning and -12 in the afternoon when you add the wind chill. Cloudy and more flurries are expected overnight. There are no weather alerts.
Traffic and transit updates
Find the latest provincial road closures and traffic incidents via Ontario 511. Closures in Hamilton are available on the Hamilton police incident feed.
Taking transit today? Find the latest GO Transit service updates here. The latest information on local bus services are available here: Hamilton, Burlington and Oakville.

Council scraps Hamilton's encampment protocol
Policy that allows tents in parks to end March 6, city politicians decide

Two options can provide monthly cash or lump-sums.
Subscribe to our newsletters for the latest local content.

Document 356 of 500

Ottawa reveals new details of border security plan ahead of Donald Trump's inauguration

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/ottawa-reveals-new-details-border-security-plan/docview/3156204316/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Ottawa+reveals+new+details+of+border+security+plan+ahead+of+Donald+Trump%27s+inauguration&amp;title=The+Hamilton+Spectator+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2025-01-15&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Hamilton+Spectator+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: OTTAWA-The federal government will soon deploy two leased Blackhawk helicopters as part of its $1.3-billion border security plan that was made in the shadow of U.S. president-elect Donald Trump's threats to trigger a trade war with Canada.
Public Safety Minister David McGuinty and Immigration Minister Marc Miller detailed part of that plan on Wednesday, ahead of Prime Minister Justin Trudeau's gathering to discuss Trump's threatened tariffs with the premiers of the provinces and territories.
Alongside the two Blackhawks, which are being leased for $5.3 million, the government is also adding 60 new surveillance drones and is looking to acquire new "mobile surveillance towers" and other new technologies to beef up border security.
The plan was part of last fall's fiscal update, and came after Trump first threatened to impose punishing 25-per-cent tariffs on all Canadian imports into the United States. In laying out that threat, Trump cited the flow of illegal migrants and drugs like fentanyl from Canada and Mexico into the U.S.
On Wednesday, both cabinet ministers repeatedly made the case that apprehensions of asylum seekers and fentanyl at the northern U.S. border is a fraction of the levels at its frontier with Mexico. They also argued that Canada has been nonetheless taking steps to improve security at the border, and shared statistics like an 89-per-cent decrease in the number of apprehensions heading into the U.S. from June to December 2024.
Earlier this week, after being briefed on the plan, Ontario Premier Doug Ford called it "phenomenal."
Asked about how the border plan has not caused Trump to withdraw his threatened tariffs - which he said he would impose on Monday, after he is sworn-in for a second time as U.S. president - Miller said there is no "magic potion" to solve all issues with the incoming American administration.
McGuinty added that Canadian leaders need to work together and avoid "intemperate" pronouncements that shouldn't be taken seriously, and noted Trump's repeated taunts that Canada is a "51st state." Trump also said last week he would use "economic force" to annex Canada, and spoke of erasing the border.
"We're not interested in drama, we're interested in making progress," McGuinty said.
"We are hopeful, and remain hopeful, that the new incoming administration will understand just how incredibly serious we are."
Since Trump's threatened tariffs, Conservative Leader Pierre Poilievre has claimed the government has lost control of the U.S.-Canada border, and has demanded an immediate election so that his party can better deal with Trump.
At the same time, NDP Leader Jagmeet Singh has described Trump as a bully and insisted the government must retaliate against any tariffs the Americans impose.
The Liberal government - which, amid tensions with the Trump administration, is running a leadership race to replace Trudeau - has been working on a potential response to the tariffs, as the Star has reported. It has also not ruled out more extreme measures, such as slapping an export tax on Canadian fossil fuels that get sent to the U.S. - exports that Statistics Canada has said were worth almost $120 billion in 2023.
Premiers, meanwhile, have put forward proposals, too. In recent days, Ford has pitched cooperation with the U.S. on critical minerals and energy. He had also previously spoken of cutting off electricity that is sent to the U.S. if Trump follows through with his tariffs.
Danielle Smith, the Alberta premier, has pushed back against proposals to restrict energy exports, which are a boon to her province's economy. In December, Smith's United Conservative government announced a $29-million plan to increase security at its own border with the U.S. state of Montana.
On Wednesday, the federal public safety and immigration ministers insisted Canada is also preparing in case Trump's campaign pledge to start mass deportations of illegal immigrants in the U.S. prompts a surge of asylum seekers trying to claim refuge in Canada.
Noting no spike has happened yet, Miller said the U.S. needs to make sure no such surge happens, and that migrants who enter into Canada without stopping at a border crossing are "not welcome."
McGuinty added that he plans to invite incoming Trump "border czar" Tom Homan fishing, and that he wants to discuss the promised mass deportations when they first meet.
CAPTION: Public Safety Minister David McGuinty detailed the security plan on Wednesday.

Subject: Deportation; Public safety; Border security; Tariffs; Political campaigns; Leadership; Surveillance; Political asylum; Fentanyl; Immigration

Document 357 of 500

Canada would not immediately match Donald Trump tariffs with 'dollar for dollar' penalties: sources

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/canada-would-not-immediately-match-donald-trump/docview/3156204264/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Canada+would+not+immediately+match+Donald+Trump+tariffs+with+%27dollar+for+dollar%27+penalties%3A+sources&amp;title=The+Hamilton+Spectator+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2025-01-15&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Hamilton+Spectator+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: OTTAWA - Canada would not immediately match any U.S. tariffs with "dollar for dollar" penalties on American products coming into Canada - an approach that Ontario Premier Doug Ford views as not "proportional" or strong enough to counter Donald Trump's threats, the Star has learned.
Prime Minister Justin Trudeau told premiers "everything is on the table" as he met them behind closed doors Wednesday, sources said.
But Trudeau conveyed his government's reluctance to immediately levy dollar-for-dollar counter-tariffs because Ottawa does not yet know whether the incoming Trump administration would follow through on Trump's vow to slap a 25-per-cent surcharge on everything Canada and Mexico ship to the U.S.
If the Americans go ahead with that threat, it would represent an "absolutely massive" surcharge on Canadian exports to the U.S. with one government official saying a dollar-for-dollar counterpunch could cost $600 billion.
Instead the federal government discussed an initial list that amounts to more than $150 billion in counter-tariffs, said a senior federal official.
Trudeau told premiers it is not necessarily the end of what Canada would do, the official added.
Another senior federal official, speaking on a background-only basis to brief reporters, said that if Trump issues tariffs but exempts Canadian oil and gas, for example, that would show where the Americans feel they are vulnerable.
But the official said Trudeau has not ruled out any move to potentially make Americans feel the pain of higher priced Canadian oil exports - over the objection of Alberta. The second official said the point is to to take actions that would make the Americans feel pain and lift their tariffs, but not so much that it would hurt Canadians or lead to a decade-long tariff war. The official noted that America refines and sells fuel products back to Canada and the federal government is aware that export tariffs could also end up hurting Canadian households at the gas pump.
Trudeau also urged premiers that everyone has to be prepared for a period of pain.
The federal official said three premiers in the room also urged their colleagues not to voice their complaints about Canada's strategy publicly, but to express dissent with each other in private - remarks that were construed as aimed at Alberta Premier Danielle Smith.
Smith attended the meeting virtually, and a pool reporter said her screen was a tropical backdrop, prompting Québec Premier François Legault to ask if she was still at Trump's Mar-a-Lago resort - which she visited last weekend.
Canada's counter-tariff list would not take immediate effect but would be subject to a period of public consultation.
At the end of their meeting, first ministers tried to paper over cracks in Team Canada.
Premiers reversed a decision to hold a separate news conference from the prime minister.
Earlier, differences were on full display.
Leaders of Ontario, Quebec and Newfoundland insisted energy, critical minerals and hydroelectricity should be in play to counter Trump's tariff threats -
Premier Doug Ford, sporting a ball cap with a "Canada Is Not For Sale" logo, said the U.S. president-elect is going to "try to devastate our country," and provinces with different interests cannot get in the way Canada's national response.
Ford said key commodity exports are among Canada's "aces" in any trade war.
"You're in a card game, but you don't show your opposition your cards. You have some aces to hold on to, those aces, but it's all part of negotiation tactics," he said.
"I understand different jurisdictions around the country have their concerns," said Ford. "But nothing is more important than standing united.
"Nothing is more important than keeping every tool in our tool box, no matter if it's energy or other commodities - high-grade nickel from Ontario, great aluminum from Quebec ... potash from Saskatchewan. These are the tools that we have. You don't negotiate through weakness, you negotiate through strength."
Legault echoed that sentiment in French, saying while his top priority is to prevent Trump from executing his threat of a 25-per-cent levies on all Canadian products, "we have to prepare a retaliation plan."
Legault said the premiers will discuss "many scenarios" behind closed doors Wednesday for how Canada should react, but "we are excluding nothing" in terms of what actions the federal government should take.
Newfoundland and Labrador Premier Andrew Furey used a chess analogy to make clear that oil and hydroelectricity exports should be considered part of how Canada could apply pressure to the U.S.
"I see energy as Canada's queen in this game of chess. We don't need to expose our queen too early, and the opposition does need to know that the queen exist, but they don't need to know what we're going to do with the queen. So let's see what the tariffs actually are first, and have a proportional, but let me be very clear, a strong response to the United States," Furey said.
"But we need to in no way, shape or form, let the urgency of this economic and fiscal crisis jeopardize our national identity."
Smith is on record as opposing any federal move to weaponize oil and gas exports against the United States.
Saskatchewan Premier Scott Moe also warned that Canada should not levy tariffs on its own products, or restrict energy or other vital exports to the U.S. in other ways. He said the leaders' proposals should not escalate "any of the divisions that we have in this nation or on this continent."
Instead, Moe wants talks to focus on how integrated the economies of Canada and the U.S. are, and to "morph the discussion" with Trump back to what the countries can do together to ensure energy, food and manufacturing "security."
"Taxing Canadian-produced goods in the form of export tariffs ... would be not only counterproductive to this entire conversation," he said, but would hurt Canada. "Canada does not do well in that environment. I would say North Americans will not do well."
The premiers met against a backdrop of increasing national anxiety as Trump's inauguration looms, along with his vow to impose tariffs on Canadian products on his first day in office. That all increases political pressure on Trudeau - who took no questions before the meeting - and the premiers.
In a speech in Washington on Wednesday, Natural Resources Minister Jonathan Wilkinson pitched the benefits of free trade, including a proposal for an "alliance" between Canada and the U.S. on energy and minerals, an idea that Ontario's premier has also raised.
Wilkinson said there are chances for both countries to put money toward projects to produce more minerals like germanium and gallium in a way that displaces supplies currently provided by China.
He also said the countries can collaborate to increase uranium supplies to wean themselves off purchases from Russia, and to "enhance the flow" of crude oil from Alberta into the U.S. to help Trump achieve his goal of "energy dominance."
Trump, in a departure from President Joe Biden's emphasis on climate action, has promised to "drill, baby drill" and increase American fossil-fuel production.
"While a single, talented player can make a difference, a well-coordinated team is unstoppable. Such is the energy and minerals relationship between Canada and the United States," Wilkinson said, according to a draft of his speech that was provided to the Star.
The threat of tariffs has spooked Canadian businesses, with industry associations warning about the damaging fallout of a trade war with the country's biggest commercial partner.
In a letter to Trudeau and opposition leaders, the head of the Canadian Manufacturers and Exporters urged the federal government to respond to any American tariffs in a careful and targeted manner "to minimize collateral damage."
The letter also called for government relief to cover tariff-related costs that exporters might incur for at least three months, along with other aid like deferred taxes to help weather a potential trade spat.
The association said MPs should be recalled if any legislation is necessary to confront the situation, after Trudeau prorogued Parliament when he announced his intention to resign after the Liberal leadership race to replace him.
CAPTION: Left to right, Ontario Premier Doug Ford, Quebec Premier Francois Legault and New Brunswick Premier Susan Holt speak before a first ministers' meeting in Ottawa on Jan. 15, 2025.

Subject: Trade disputes; International trade; Federal government; Leadership; Energy; Exports; Prime ministers; Tariffs

Business indexing term: Subject: Trade disputes International trade Leadership Exports Tariffs

Location: Saskatchewan Canada; United States--US; Canada; Quebec Canada

People: Trump, Donald J

Ford government reveals proposed location for massive nuclear power plant

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/ford-government-reveals-proposed-location-massive/docview/3156204243/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Ford+government+reveals+proposed+location+for+massive+nuclear+power+plant&amp;title=The+Hamilton+Spectator+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2025-01-15&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Hamilton+Spectator+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Ontario is focusing on the Port Hope area - no stranger to the nuclear industry - as the best bet for a massive new nuclear power station to meet forecasts for soaring electricity needs in the next 25 years.
But there are many hurdles in terms of community consultation, studies and approvals that make it unlikely that a power plant producing up to 10,000 megawatts would be up and running until the 2040s.
"We have a lot of work ahead of us," Ontario Power Generation (OPG) president Nicole Butcher told a news conference Wednesday announcing the Lake Ontario town east of Oshawa has expressed interest in being the host.
The site in Wesleyville has long been owned by OPG and was destined to become an oil-fired power plant until a 1970s oil crisis sent fuel prices skyrocketing and scuttled that plan. It has been sitting idle but is zoned for a power plant and is close to transmission lines, as well as the existing Darlington nuclear plant.
Energy Minister Stephen Lecce said the new plant, if it is built following extensive consultations with Port Hope and the Williams Treaties First Nations, would be equivalent to "two Darlingtons," making it one of the biggest nuclear generating stations in the world and capable of powering 10 million homes.
While Darlington and the older Pickering nuclear plant east of it use Canada-designed CANDU nuclear technology, no decisions have been made on the type of reactors that could be built on the 1,300-acre Wesleyville site.
Officials at a background briefing on the plan said if all goes well, the first reactor could be operating in the 2040s, although Lecce spoke of a "10-to-15 year time frame."
Port Hope was the only one of three municipalities that expressed interest in a power plant after the province in late November named sites it owns in Wesleyville, Nanticoke on the north shore of Lake Erie and along the St. Clair River south of Sarnia, as possible locations.
The Nanticoke and Sarnia-area sites were home to massive coal-fired generation stations shut down years ago in the fight against climate change.
Green Leader Mike Schreiner has called for public hearings on a new energy plan the Ford government is developing in the wake of forecasts that the need for electricity will jump 75 per cent by 2050.
He wants to see a "competitive procurement process for the lowest-cost, emission-free electricity" instead of automatically choosing nuclear power.
Lecce has said the government is considering "all types of new energy generation" but nuclear is needed as emission-free baseload power.
CAPTION: The Darlington nuclear plant sits close to the site in Wesleyville where the Ontario government would like to build a massive power plant.

Subject: Electricity; Industrial plant emissions; Nuclear power plants

Secondary teachers' union slams funding with 'Ford High' ad campaign

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/secondary-teachers-union-slams-funding-with-ford/docview/3156204089/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Secondary+teachers%27+union+slams+funding+with+%27Ford+High%27+ad+campaign&amp;title=The+Hamilton+Spectator+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2025-01-15&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Hamilton+Spectator+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Ontario's public high school teachers' union has launched a new ad campaign, saying the education system is "at a breaking point" and calling on the provincial government to provide more funding.
"Our schools are falling apart, our teachers and education workers are burning out, and students are being left behind," said Karen Littlewood, president of the Ontario Secondary School Teachers' Federation.
The campaign, which features ads fashioned as a TV series trailer about the dismal conditions in "Ford High," is "set in a dystopian near-future where public education is crumbling due to defunding and neglect. Using dark humour and poignant storytelling, the campaign sheds light on the current trajectory of Ontario's education system under Ontario Premier Doug Ford's government," the union said in a news release.
The ads will be posted on social media and broadcast on Crave, CTV and CBC Gem, among others, and come as Ford continues to refuse to rule out calling a snap provincial election.
The union did not provide details of the cost of the ad campaign.
In late 2023, the high school teachers' union, school boards and the provincial government turned to binding arbitration to reach a tentative agreement and settle some issues, including salary increases. All other teacher unions followed suit, leading to labour peace in schools.
School boards have said provincial funding, while increasing by $745 million to $29 billion this school year, has not kept up with their actual costs. The Ontario Public School Boards' Association estimates that, when adjusted for inflation, the province is actually spending $776 less per student this year.
Ontario's Catholic teachers have launched their own online campaign at knowmore.ca, saying the government needs to address class sizes and staffing in schools.
CAPTION: On Jan. 15, 2025, the Ontario Secondary School Teachers' Federation launched an ad campaign that says the province's education system is "at a breaking point" and calls on the Ford government to provide more funding.Ontario Secondary School Teachers' Federation president Karen Littlewood speaks to union members at their annual general meeting in Toronto on Aug. 18, 2022.

Subject: Secondary schools; Funding; Education; Secondary school teachers; School boards; Advertising campaigns
