Document 360 of 500

Jan. 15: We could follow the EU model, Ford needs to help all Ontarians and other letters to the editor

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/jan-15-we-could-follow-eu-model-ford-needs-help/docview/3155546289/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Jan.+15%3A+We+could+follow+the+EU+model%2C+Ford+needs+to+help+all+Ontarians+and+other+letters+to+the+editor&amp;title=The+Hamilton+Spectator+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2025-01-15&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Hamilton+Spectator+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: We could follow the EU model
Because Canada and the U.S. have many common interests (other than those of Donald Trump) and have been peaceful, co-operative and friendly neighbours that help each other in times of need, why can't we become like the European Union countries, where we maintain our sovereignty and have a common currency and no border checks between Canada and the U.S.?
I definitely would not want to be an American and deal with their politics, but it seems pointless for border checks except for foreigners coming into Canada or the U.S. from abroad. This would cut expenses for border security and currency exchange.
Tom Hammill, Hamilton
Teach youth how to thrive
The latest statistics show one in two youth are struggling with mental health in Canada. When are we going to stop what we are teaching in middle school upwards and get with the times so we can reduce/reverse these trends?
It is 2025, we need to change our curriculum and begin to teach fundamental basics on mental health and finance so our youth (grades 6 or 7 upwards) can begin to understand how to help themselves through resources and tools.
Our school system needs to start teaching our youth "skills for life," otherwise we are going to continue to see homelessness and mental health issues skyrocket.
No one wants to live in a tent, no one wants to fail in life. Our governments and school system need to be part of the solution and not part of the problem.
Bill Matetich, Hamilton
Gage Park greenhouse priceless
Dear city managers, I wish to commend you for creating and continuing to fund the Gage Park greenhouse. It is a needed green space for all of us, to restore our minds and hearts, in these busy and fraught times.
My father and I enjoy it in every season. The spring and fall flower shows are a highlight for us. During the winter, we are most grateful for the bright, beautiful and accessible greenhouse. There, my 96-year-old father can savour the lush plants, nurturing his lifelong love of plants that began in Gage Park as a child.
The greenhouse gardeners, who willingly share their knowledge with us, provide a bonus each and every time we come.
Finally, we are most grateful for the Gage Park plants that beautify our streets. Please continue to fund the Gage Park greenhouse - its value to our city is priceless.
Christine Robinson, Dundas
Annexation backlash would be intense
Re: Attempting to annex Canada would spell disaster for the U.S. at home and abroad, Jan. 11
When I was a kid in the '50s, we practised air raid drills. We didn't understand why, but the ear-splitting sirens made the point loud and clear - this was serious stuff. "The bomb" was very new then and the Cold War was intense, leading up to a close call with the Cuban Missile Crisis of 1962.
Now another threat looms over us, this time coming from a supposedly friendly neighbour in the form of Donald Trump shaking a fist of economic violence under Canada's nose. It is difficult to understand what is real here since these are the ravings of an unstable megalomaniac.
It is helpful to have a knowledgeable person like George S. Rigakos shed light on the matter in his column. I am reassured that should the U.S. follow through with this "plan," the backlash would be immediate and intense, with the result being that country becoming a pariah and its standing in the world irrevocably smashed. There has to be a few smart folks in Washington who also know this and will prevent the worst from happening, but still, the future is uncertain and the direction we are heading is definitely downwards.
Not everyone in America voted to "release the kraken," but a shocking mass of them did. Consequently, I no longer feel welcome in that country. Besides, I will be very busy holding my breath for the next four years.
Emil Andrew Sekerak, Beamsville
We need to stand up to bullies
How do you deal with authoritarians with imperialistic ambitions? You could try appeasing them, as Neville Chamberlain did to Adolf Hitler. We know how that turned out.
We could just ignore them, but that's hard to do when they're constantly in your face, baiting you.
A third option is to cosy up to them, as per Alberta Premier Danielle Smith.
The remaining option is to stand up to them, and call them out for the bullies they are. I believe that to do otherwise simply encourages them to push harder with their thuggery and intimidation tactics. You know who I'm talking about.
Wayne Poole, Dundas
Take back control of housing
Re: More than 80,000 people in Ontario were homeless in 2024, new report finds, Jan. 9
With federal and provincial elections in the offing, we recognize the significance of the Association of Municipalities of Ontario's report on homelessness.
For decades, successive governments have treated housing as a for-profit commodity, instead of investing to help guarantee housing as a human right. The result? Large corporate landlords treat housing as their own cash cow, and housing costs have spiralled. At the same time, the Ford Conservatives slashed protections like rent controls and allowed corporate investors to dominate the rental market. Meanwhile, federal and provincial governments abandoned the construction of affordable social housing and both continue to scapegoat immigrants, refugees and the unhoused themselves for a crisis those groups played no part in creating.
With the right kind of investment, we can stop the financialization of housing and take back control of housing. As part of a suite of direct government action to deal with homelessness, we must invest and build not-for-profit, affordable, public housing and reinstitute strong rent controls.
Fred Hahn, president, CUPE Ontario, and Krista Laing, chair, Ontario Municipal Workers, CUPE Ontario
Ford needs to help all Ontarians
I guess Premier Doug Ford and the Progressive Conservatives don't care much about those Ontarians who are homeless, living in tents. Why would they care? These constituents don't vote because they don't have a permanent address. Ford has been generous about giving perks to those people who are most likely to vote for him - that's the way to get re-elected.
Mr. Ford, I would like to remind you that you are the premier for all Ontarians and you have a moral obligation to help those who are most vulnerable. It cannot be left to the municipalities alone to provide assistance to the homeless. The municipal level of government alone does not have the means to do so.
Hinda Levine, Hamilton
Firefighters deserve more praise
Despite the talk from a certain American politician of making Canada the 51st state and the threat of punishing tariffs, Canada and Canadians have brushed these aside. Our great nation has done so to assist our American friends and neighbours in California to help with the devastating Los Angeles wild fires.
Canadian firefighters and equipment from many parts of our nation have been sent to help with these fires, especially, our CL-415 Super Scooper water bombers, an all-Canadian aircraft designed to tackle fires from the air with precision.
These Canadian men and women presently in the Los Angeles area assisting in fighting these devastating fires with their American neighbours deserve thanks and praise from both sides of the border. They should also be thanked from the incoming American administration.
Canadians have positively demonstrated to our neighbours to the south and the world our willingness to help all in times of need! Kudos to Canada!
Mike LoSchiavo, Stoney Creek
A more pessimistic outlook
Re: Columnists ease my mind, Jan. 10
Re: Don't let Ford privatize health, Jan. 10
Despite public opinion to the contrary, one letter writer is convinced we are all better off than we were in 2015, even though our per capita income has fallen from 85 per cent of the standard of living in the U.S. to 60 per cent, personal debt is at an all-time high, our national debt has doubled, we are paying for a 40 per cent increase in the size of government and most young people can only dream of owning a home. That's hardly sounds like better off to me!
The second letter writer questions why Ontario would consider privatizing health care when we already have "a proven and effective health-care system?" A November report that compared 30 countries that also have universal systems (with varying degrees of private/public participation) concluded that while Canada ranked fourth-highest in health-care funding, it ranked close to bottom in performance, which indicates that despite adequate funding, Canada (and Ontario) does not have an effective health-care system.
Canada employs 10 times more health administrators than the successful German system which indicates a top heavy bureaucracy that is failing to manage efficiently and not using the substantial taxpayer funding effectively. A problem fiscally responsible privatization would correct by eliminating the bureaucracy and redirecting funding to the most pressing and effective areas of patient care to the benefit of all Canadians.
Les Farrington, Ancaster
CAPTION: Instead of annexation, a reader suggests Canada and U.S. join in a European Union model where each nation retains sovereignty, but do away with borders.

Title: Jan. 15: We could follow the EU model, Ford needs to help all Ontarians and other letters to the editor

Publication title: The Hamilton Spectator (Online); Hamilton

Publication year: 2025

Document 361 of 500

Letters

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/letters/docview/3155545307/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=unknown&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Letters&amp;title=The+Spectator&amp;issn=11899417&amp;date=2025-01-15&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.11&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Spectator&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: We could follow
the EU model
Because Canada and the U.S. have many common interests (other than those of Donald Trump) and have been peaceful, co-operative and friendly neighbours that help each other in times of need, why can't we become like the European Union countries, where we maintain our sovereignty and have a common currency and no border checks between Canada and the U.S.?
I definitely would not want to be an American and deal with their politics, but it seems pointless for border checks except for foreigners coming into Canada or the U.S. from abroad. This would cut expenses for border security and currency exchange.
Tom Hammill, Hamilton
Teach youth
how to thrive
The latest statistics show one in two youth are struggling with mental health in Canada. When are we going to stop what we are teaching in middle school upwards and get with the times so we can reduce/reverse these trends?
It is 2025, we need to change our curriculum and begin to teach fundamental basics on mental health and finance so our youth (grades 6 or 7 upwards) can begin to understand how to help themselves through resources and tools.
Our school system needs to start teaching our youth "skills for life," otherwise we are going to continue to see homelessness and mental health issues skyrocket.
No one wants to live in a tent, no one wants to fail in life. Our governments and school system need to be part of the solution and not part of the problem.
Bill Matetich, Hamilton
Greenhouse
remains priceless
Dear city managers, I wish to commend you for creating and continuing to fund the Gage Park greenhouse. It is a needed green space for all of us, to restore our minds and hearts, in these busy and fraught times.
My father and I enjoy it in every season. The spring and fall flower shows are a highlight for us. During the winter, we are most grateful for the bright, beautiful and accessible greenhouse. There, my 96-year-old father can savour the lush plants, nurturing his lifelong love of plants that began in Gage Park as a child.
The greenhouse gardeners, who willingly share their knowledge with us, provide a bonus each and every time we come.
Finally, we are most grateful for the Gage Park plants that beautify our streets. Please continue to fund the Gage Park greenhouse - its value to our city is priceless.
Christine Robinson, Dundas
Backlash would
be intense
Re: Attempting to annex Canada would spell disaster for the U.S. at home and abroad, Jan. 11
When I was a kid in the '50s, we practised air raid drills. We didn't understand why, but the ear-splitting sirens made the point loud and clear - this was serious stuff. "The bomb" was very new then and the Cold War was intense, leading up to a close call with the Cuban Missile Crisis of 1962.
Now another threat looms over us, this time coming from a supposedly friendly neighbour in the form of Donald Trump shaking a fist of economic violence under Canada's nose. It is difficult to understand what is real here since these are the ravings of an unstable megalomaniac.
It is helpful to have a knowledgeable person like George S. Rigakos shed light on the matter in his column. I am reassured that should the U.S. follow through with this "plan," the backlash would be immediate and intense, with the result being that country becoming a pariah and its standing in the world irrevocably smashed.
There has to be a few smart folks in Washington who also know this and will prevent the worst from happening, but still, the future is uncertain and the direction we are heading is definitely downwards.
Not everyone in America voted to "release the kraken," but a shocking mass of them did. Consequently, I no longer feel welcome in that country. Besides, I will be very busy holding my breath for the next four years.
Emil Andrew Sekerak, Beamsville
Canada needs to stand up to bullies
How do you deal with authoritarians with imperialistic ambitions? You could try appeasing them, as Neville Chamberlain did to Adolf Hitler. We know how that turned out.
We could just ignore them, but that's hard to do when they're constantly in your face, baiting you.
A third option is to cosy up to them, as per Alberta Premier Danielle Smith.
The remaining option is to stand up to them, and call them out for the bullies they are. I believe that to do otherwise simply encourages them to push harder with their thuggery and intimidation tactics. You know who I'm talking about.
Wayne Poole, Dundas
Take back control
of housing
Re: More than 80,000 people in Ontario were homeless in 2024, new report finds, Jan. 9
With federal and provincial elections in the offing, we recognize the significance of the Association of Municipalities of Ontario's report on homelessness.
For decades, successive governments have treated housing as a for-profit commodity, instead of investing to help guarantee housing as a human right. The result? Large corporate landlords treat housing as their own cash cow, and housing costs have spiralled.
At the same time, the Ford Conservatives slashed protections like rent controls and allowed corporate investors to dominate the rental market. Meanwhile, federal and provincial governments abandoned the construction of affordable social housing and both continue to scapegoat immigrants, refugees and the unhoused themselves for a crisis those groups played no part in creating.
With the right kind of investment, we can stop the financialization of housing and take back control of housing. As part of a suite of direct government action to deal with homelessness, we must invest and build not-for-profit, affordable, public housing and reinstitute strong rent controls.
Fred Hahn, president, CUPE
Ontario, and Krista Laing, chair, Ontario Municipal Workers, CUPE Ontario
Ford needs to help all Ontarians
I guess Premier Doug Ford and the Progressive Conservatives don't care much about those Ontarians who are homeless, living in tents. Why would they care? These constituents don't vote because they don't have a permanent address. Ford has been generous about giving perks to those people who are most likely to vote for him - that's the way to get re-elected.
Mr. Ford, I would like to remind you that you are the premier for all Ontarians and you have a moral obligation to help those who are most vulnerable. It cannot be left to the municipalities alone to provide assistance to the homeless. The municipal level of government alone does not have the means to do so.
Hinda Levine, Hamilton
Firefighters in L.A.
deserve more praise
Despite the talk from a certain American politician of making Canada the 51st state and the threat of punishing tariffs, Canada and Canadians have brushed these aside.
Our great nation has done so to assist our American friends and neighbours in California to help with the devastating Los Angeles wild fires.
Canadian firefighters and equipment from many parts of our nation have been sent to help with these fires, especially, our CL-415 Super Scooper water bombers, an all-Canadian aircraft designed to tackle fires from the air with precision.
These Canadian men and women presently in the Los Angeles area assisting in fighting these devastating fires with their American neighbours deserve thanks and praise from both sides of the border. They should also be thanked from the incoming American administration.
Canadians have positively demonstrated to our neighbours to the south and the world our willingness to help all in times of need! Kudos to Canada!
Mike LoSchiavo, Stoney Creek
CAPTION: Tbe bridge from Fort Erie to Buffalo. Instead of annexation, a reader suggests Canada and U.S. join in a European Union model where each nation retains sovereignty, but do away with borders.Bob Tymczyszyn St. Catharines Standard file photo

Premiers debate as Trump celebrates: Tariffs threatened by incoming president could cost up to 500,000 Ontario jobs, Doug Ford says

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/premiers-debate-as-trump-celebrates/docview/3155544840/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Premiers+debate+as+Trump+celebrates%3A+Tariffs+threatened+by+incoming+president+could+cost+up+to+500%2C000+Ontario+jobs%2C+Doug+Ford+says&amp;title=The+Spectator&amp;issn=11899417&amp;date=2025-01-15&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.6&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Spectator&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Ontario Premier Doug Ford said Tuesday he's now convinced the federal government has a "phenomenal" plan to secure the Canada-U.S. border in response to Donald Trump's tariff threat - although he didn't offer up any details of that plan.
Trump has threatened to impose a 25 per cent tariff on all imports from Canada. The U.S. president-elect initially said that the tariff would be in response to what he called Canada's inaction on drugs and migrants crossing the shared border.
Speaking to reporters after a meeting with Finance and Intergovernmental Affairs Minister Dominic LeBlanc in Toronto on Tuesday, Ford was full of praise for LeBlanc and for the "solid, solid plan." He urged federal Public Safety Minister David McGuinty to "get out there and put the plan in front of the Canadian people."
LeBlanc, who previously announced a $1.3-billion, six-year plan to secure the border, said he told Ford the money will allow the RCMP and the Canada Border Services Agency to use long-range drones and surveillance towers, create a joint air command, hire more officers and deploy 80 canine units to search for fentanyl. He did not unveil any new measures, timelines or specific costs.
LeBlanc said he also presented those measures to key members of the Trump team when they met in Florida - although that has not convinced Trump to back down.
Ford began the day by slamming the Liberal government on the border file.
"I'm going to be blunt: they need to get their act together. Simple as that," Ford said on Tuesday morning.
Ford said Ontario officials have estimated the proposed tariffs on Canadian goods could cost his province up to half a million jobs.
"This is serious," he said. "It's unprecedented."
LeBlanc said the federal government has done its own modelling on the national impact of the tariffs but would not share those figures.
"We're not going to speculate on what exactly are different scenarios. Next week, we think we'll know the precise details of what these tariffs will mean to the Canadian economy, and we'll be ready, of course, to respond from a position of strength and, we hope, as a unified country," he said.
LeBlanc said unity will be the message when he and Prime Minister Justin Trudeau meet with the country's premiers on Wednesday.
The federal cabinet is also set to gather for a two-day retreat starting Monday, which is inauguration day and the day Trump said his tariffs will begin.
In a social media post Tuesday, Trump pledged to create an "external revenue service" to start collecting "tariffs, duties and all revenue that come from foreign sources."
LeBlanc said he and Ford discussed the possibility of heading to Washington next week or visiting the border. He stressed the need for premiers and the federal government to make a common case for defending Canada's economy.
Alberta Premier Danielle Smith, who recently visited with Trump, has cautioned Ottawa against blocking energy exports to the U.S. in response to tariffs.
The Canadian Press
CAPTION: Ontario Premier Doug Ford

CREDIT: Allison Jones and Sarah Ritchie The Canadian Press

Subject: Tariffs

Document 363 of 500

Doug Ford warns 500,000 jobs in Ontario could be lost due to Donald Trump tariffs

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/doug-ford-warns-500-000-jobs-ontario-could-be/docview/3155813377/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Doug+Ford+warns+500%2C000+jobs+in+Ontario+could+be+lost+due+to+Donald+Trump+tariffs&amp;title=The+Hamilton+Spectator+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2025-01-14&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Hamilton+Spectator+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Half a million Ontario workers could lose their jobs if American tariffs hit full-force on a wide range of industries once Donald Trump is back in the White House, Premier Doug Ford warns.
Ford painted a stark unemployment picture for the manufacturing engine of Canada as premiers head to Ottawa to meet with departing Prime Minister Justin Trudeau on Wednesday.
It will be the first in-person leaders' meeting in two years and comes against the backdrop of concerns that Canada's response to Trump's 25-per-cent-tariff threat has been hamstrung by federal Liberal leadership tumult.
"This is serious, it's unprecedented, and let's hope it doesn't cost 500,000 jobs," Ford said Tuesday, noting that number is based on estimates provided by provincial ministries this week with the fear that factories could be shuttered.
"It all depends on the amount of tariffs, what sectors they're affecting," said the premier, who backs Canadian retaliatory levies if Trump doesn't back down.
Finance Minister Dominic LeBlanc, Trudeau's point person on the U.S. trade war threat, said "we're not going to speculate" on how many people could be thrown out of work across Canada if the U.S. imposes levies on Canadian products shipped to America. Nor would he say whether Canada would respond by putting an export surcharge on oil and gas shipments to U.S. customers - a move Alberta and Saskatchewan adamantly oppose.
"We'll be ready, of course, to respond from a position of strength, and, we hope, as a unified country," LeBlanc said at Queen's Park with Ford at his side.
Trump begins his second term as president on Monday.
Alberta Premier Danielle Smith, who visited the president-elect at his Mar-a-Lago resort over the weekend, has spoken out against retaliatory tariffs against the U.S. that would impact her province's lucrative oil export revenues.
But LeBlanc stressed "the only responsible thing for the national government is to acknowledge that all options are on the table."
"We need to be ready to deploy all of the measures necessary to defend the Canadian economy. But we're not going to publicly speculate what is or what isn't an option," he said as Ford nodded approvingly.
New Brunswick Premier Susan Holt said in an interview that despite political turmoil in Ottawa, the premiers have worked with the federal government on potential tariff lists, and have reached out with American counterparts to try and pre-empt any levies.
Holt wants to see how the federal government intends to deal with any influx of migrants to Canada should Trump also issue a mass deportation order.
"We'll need clear processes in terms of how we detain folks who have crossed over into Canada illegally and then where their final destination is, whether it's back to the U.S., where they're being deported from, or to a country of origin."
Newfoundland and Labrador Premier Andrew Furey said "a unified Team Canada approach" is essential after Wednesday's confab.
"I would like to see a cohesive strategy about a proportional response," said Furey, adding it could entail "fiscal tariffs" or more targeted "political" levies that would directly impact Trump and his sphere of influencers.
Ford praised LeBlanc for Ottawa's "solid" $1.3 billion border plan that includes long-range surveillance drones, new canine units, helicopters and more RCMP and Canada Border Services Agency patrols.
It is hoped that might appease Trump, who launched his tariff salvo against Canada and Mexico with a demand that America's neighbours tighten their borders to stem illegal immigration and fentanyl smuggling.
Details of the border security plan will be made public Wednesday morning at an Ottawa news conference by Public Safety Minister David McGuinty and Immigration, Refugees and Citizenship Minister Marc Miller.
With Ontario doing $500 billion in two-way trade annually with the U.S., Ford has repeatedly said his province would be hurt more than any other.
LeBlanc expressed gratitude to the premier for being "an effective advocate for the interests of the Canadian economy."
Ford said Trump's plan for slapping 25-per-cent tariffs on imports from Canada and Mexico could necessitate "tens of billions" of dollars in government supports for Ontario workers and industries.
Ottawa would be there to help just as the two governments worked in unison during the COVID-19 pandemic, added LeBlanc.
"The government of Canada will, of course, be there to support industries and workers in collaboration with Premier Ford and his government and (Wednesday's) conversation allows all of us to discuss how we can be ready next week," the finance minister said.
With speculation rampant about a snap provincial election within weeks, Ford argued the level of spending is so significant it may require him to seek a new mandate.
But he would not detail how any such government expenditures could break down.
According to the provincial government, there were 8.1 million people working in Ontario last month - 82 per cent in full-time jobs and the unemployment rate is 7.5 per cent. So the loss of 450,000 to 500,000 jobs would be a serious blow.
Ford, who will be in Washington twice next month to lobby U.S. lawmakers against the measures, said "these tariffs will affect every single person, even if they don't think there's a tariff in their sector - it's going to indirectly affect them, it's not going to be good."
Ford dismissed concerns that plunging Ontario into an election, instead of waiting until the next scheduled provincewide vote in June 2026, would add to the "uncertainty" he has been complaining about in Ottawa.
"I'll still be premier if that happens. We haven't decided. I'll still be working, answering the calls, speaking to U.S. governors and congresspeople, speaking to other premiers ... you never stop working."
New Democrat Leader Marit Stiles and Liberal Leader Bonnie Crombie have urged Ford not to call an early election and said they would work with his majority government on economic stimulus packages.
"You've got a mandate," Stiles told Ford on Monday. "You don't need to go to the polls."
Crombie said "Donald Trump and his tariffs should be treated as a threat not as an excuse for an illegitimate early election call" that would cost taxpayers $175 million.
CAPTION: Premier Doug Ford attends an announcement in Toronto on Monday.

Document 364 of 500

New year, new chance for MPP Sarah Jama

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/new-year-chance-mpp-sarah-jama/docview/3155344057/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=New+year%2C+new+chance+for+MPP+Sarah+Jama&amp;title=The+Hamilton+Spectator+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2025-01-14&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Hamilton+Spectator+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: The good news for a new year is that the Ontario NDP is giving independent MPP Sarah Jama the opportunity to win back her seat in Hamilton Centre. Funny how things work.
Nominations are needed because Jama was kicked out of the party not long after winning the byelection by a huge margin over her closest competitor and with a clear majority of votes.
The official NDP line is Jama was insubordinate and difficult to work with, but there's no evidence to support the claim. No evidence, because there's been no hearing of any kind, no process by which the decision was made in an open and transparent fashion. No opportunity for Jama to answer to her peers, defend herself.
For those who forgot, the NDP announced Jama's removal immediately after her refusal to read a prepared statement they wrote in an effort to force her apology for comments she made in support of the Palestinian people after the Oct. 7 Hamas attacks.
It happened in the blink of an eye, a draconian response by NDP leadership, followed by one even more brutal as the Conservatives jumped on the hate wagon and censured Jama in the legislature as antisemitic.
Whether you support Israel or not, what happened to Sarah Jama was fundamentally undemocratic at its core.
If the party can't be democratic in its internal processes and procedures, how democratic will it be when in power? Democracy is a muscle. It needs exercise to keep away the forces of authoritarianism. This should matter to every NDP supporter. It should matter to all of us.
As should the unending and relentless attempts to portray Jama as antisemitic for her pro-Palestinian, pro-peace position. Like so many people who have watched, unfiltered on social media, the devastating destruction unleashed by Israel, Jama is only one of the millions of people in Canada and around the world, who agree with the accusations of genocide.
For taking this position, she continues to be the target of pro-Israel propaganda that would paint her as "evil."
It's both uninformed and deliberate.
The NDP are not like Conservative and Liberals. As a party they are a bit rough at the edges, they're working people who tell it like they live it, plain-spoken. They don't have lots of money to donate to politics, but they give their time to improving the lives of others.
They will stand up and speak out at an injustice and they will demand more from government for the people as a whole, not just for special interest groups or deep-pocketed donors. Monied interests tend to be afraid of the NDP.
Hamilton Centre knew who Jama was and they supported her when they voted for her in a byelection. For the NDP leadership to silence the voices of Hamilton Centre unilaterally, without transparency or public process, is profoundly undemocratic.
Without process, this could happen to any duly elected politician, in any riding. That's not democracy. Neither was the censure vote immediately after, which history will read as the weaponization of the Ontario legislature by interests that have nothing to do with provincial jurisdiction.
What happened to Jama made international headlines, and not in a good way.
The discourse against her support for Palestine is drowning out the work Jama has been doing as a representative of the people of Hamilton Centre. This is intentional. It's obvious. And it's racist. I won't repeat things I've read online; I wasn't raised that way.
Even sitting as an independent member, Jama has submitted motions consistent with her campaign promises, she has been present and vocal for issues where others have stayed silent. She has done the job the people elected her to do, and she has conducted herself, despite being the object of so much vitriol and hate, with grace and dignity.
It's a sign of party maturity that the NDP have listened to Jama's supporters and are giving her a chance to reclaim the party nomination for Hamilton Centre. It's the least they could do.
CAPTION: Independent MPP Sarah Jama has begun the process to seek the NDP nomination for Hamilton Centre for the next provincial election.

Subject: Antisemitism; Nominations; Democracy

Location: Israel

Title: New year, new chance for MPP Sarah Jama

Document 365 of 500

Retaliating to Trump tariffs not the answer, Smith says: Alberta premier says escalating spat would spark a 'national unity crisis'

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/retaliating-trump-tariffs-not-answer-smith-says/docview/3155061691/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Retaliating+to+Trump+tariffs+not+the+answer%2C+Smith+says%3A+Alberta+premier+says+escalating+spat+would+spark+a+%27national+unity+crisis%27&amp;title=The+Spectator&amp;issn=11899417&amp;date=2025-01-14&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.5&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Spectator&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Alberta Premier Danielle Smith, fresh off a weekend visit with U.S. president-elect Donald Trump, cautioned Ottawa against blocking energy exports to the U.S. in response to Trump's tariff threat on Monday - but Ontario Premier Doug Ford said he wants to keep the option open.
"Oil and gas is owned by the provinces, principally Alberta, and we won't stand for that," Smith told reporters in a virtual news conference.
NDP Leader Jagmeet Singh, meanwhile, said Monday he wants to see Canada block exports to the United States of critical minerals like aluminum, lithium and potash in response to the tariff threat.
"So if (Trump) wants to pick a fight with Canada, we have to make sure it's clear that it's going to hurt Americans as well," Singh told a news conference in Ottawa.
Ford said that while an embargo on energy and critical mineral exports to the U.S. is the last thing he wants to see, Canada shouldn't rule out any means to inflict economic pain on the U.S. if the tariff dispute escalates.
Canada's critical minerals exports to the United States were valued at nearly $30 billion in 2023. That's nearly 60 per cent of the value of all Canadian critical mineral exports.
"I want to ship them more critical minerals. I want to ship them more energy," Ford said. "But make no mistake about it. If they're coming full-tilt at us, I won't hesitate to pull out every single tool we have until they can feel the pain. But that's the last thing I want to do."
Foreign Affairs Minister Melanie Joly hasn't ruled out an energy embargo in response to Trump's promise to impose 25 per cent tariffs on Canadian products.
Smith said the federal government shouldn't be making "empty threats," and it's not Joly's call to make. She said cutting off pipeline supplies through Michigan would choke key supplies to Ontario and Quebec as well.
If Ottawa moves to cut off energy exports, Smith said, "they will have a national unity crisis on their hands at the same time as having a crisis with our U.S. trade partners."
Alberta's premier said Canada needs to be prepared for the tariffs to come into effect on Jan. 20, Trump's first day in office. "I haven't seen any indication in any of the president's public commentary, or even in the comments that he had with me, that he's inclined to change his approach," she said.
Singh argued that the fact Trump still intends to implement broad-based tariffs after he and members of his administration met with Joly, Prime Minister Justin Trudeau and Finance Minister Dominic LeBlanc shows he can't be reasoned with.
"Donald Trump is not rational. You can't discuss a plan over a meal with him. He is a bully. And bullies understand one thing ... that is strength. They also understand pain," Singh said.
Canada is a primary exporter to the U.S. for several critical minerals, including graphite, potash and zinc.
The U.S. imports all of its graphite, which is used in manufacturing a wide range of products, from pencils to nuclear reactors. China, Mexico and Madagascar are the other major suppliers of graphite to the U.S. market.
Over the weekend, Joly said Canadians need to "be ready" for economic threats, adding all leaders need to show a united front.
"We have to be very realistic, very pragmatic, and we have to be ready, because something we can't do is not to take president-elect Trump at his word. Because when he says something, he usually does it," Joly said Sunday on CBC's Rosemary Barton Live.
Smith visited Trump at his Florida home over the weekend as a guest of Canadian businessman and television personality Kevin O'Leary.
The Canadian Press
CAPTION: Alberta Premier Danielle Smith posted these photos to X after meeting with U.S. president-elect Donald Trump at his Mar-a-Lago club in Florida. She was joined by Canadian businessman Kevin O'Leary, right.Justin Smirlies Danielle Smith/X

Document 366 of 500

'Homelessness problem is only getting worse': Hamilton MPP Donna Skelly wants to see receipts for city's $158 million in spending

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/homelessness-problem-is-only-getting-worse/docview/3155061685/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=%27Homelessness+problem+is+only+getting+worse%27%3A+Hamilton+MPP+Donna+Skelly+wants+to+see+receipts+for+city%27s+%24158+million+in+spending&amp;title=The+Hamilton+Spectator+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2025-01-14&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Hamilton+Spectator+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Mayor Andrea Horwath has renewed Hamilton's call for more upper-level government funding to tackle homelessness in light of a new report that shows the crisis in Ontario will only worsen without substantial action.
Horwath calls the roughly $75 million in additional funding that Premier Doug Ford's Tory government announced in December "a start," but not enough for municipalities across the province to respond to the crisis.
"The funding is welcome. The new programs are welcome. But the scale of the problem far outweighs the amount of resources being made available," she told The Spectator.
But if Hamilton wants more funding from her government, the city should demonstrate how it's spending $158 million it approved in 2024 to combat the crisis, local Tory MPP Donna Skelly argues.
"Show me where the money has gone before you ask for another dime, because it's an awful lot of money and the homelessness problem is only getting worse," said Skelly, who represents Flamborough-Glanbrook at Queen's Park.
The Association of Municipalities of Ontario (AMO)'s new report shows roughly 81,500 people were homeless in Ontario in 2024, which represented a more than 25 per cent spike from 2022.
"By 2035, the number of people experiencing homelessness in Ontario could range from 128,122 under optimistic economic conditions to as high as 294,266 in an economic downturn," the analysis found.
"These estimates underscore the urgent need for action to prevent higher rates of homelessness and to address the systemic factors driving homelessness."
Between 2016 and 2024, funding for housing and homelessness programs in Ontario increased from $1.9 billion to $4.1 billion, with municipalities covering 51.5 per cent of that freight.
An additional $11 billion over 10 years to create more than 75,000 affordable and supportive housing units would be needed to end chronic homelessness, the AMO report asserts.
And to immediately house those living in encampments, Ontario needs an additional $2 billion over eight years.
"The scope and scale of homelessness across Ontario's municipalities is truly staggering," AMO president Robin Jones said in a news release.
"Without real and meaningful provincial action, the quality of life and economic prosperity of Ontario's communities is at risk. We can solve this crisis, but we need to work together."
His government has "made the largest investment in affordable housing and anti-homelessness programs in Ontario's history and has taken decisive action to support municipalities in dealing with homeless encampments across the province," Municipal Affairs and Housing Minister Paul Calandra's office said in an emailed statement.
Over the next three years, the province is "investing a record $3 billion in affordable housing, anti-homelessness, and emergency shelter supports," Calandra's spokesperson wrote.
"While Ontario has stepped up to the table with the largest increased investment in the province's history, we continue to call on the federal government to step up, pay their fair share and take responsibility for the consequences of their policies that have escalated the number of individuals facing homelessness."
The office of the federal Liberal housing minister, Nathaniel Erskine-Smith, didn't immediately respond to The Spectator's request for comment.
In Hamilton, roughly 1,600 people are homeless, with about 250 of them living outdoors, the city says.
In line with the 2024 budget, $158 million "was invested to increase affordable housing and reduce homelessness," which was $33 million more than 2023, the city noted in a news release about the AMO paper.
And in September, council backed a staff plan to create 272 more temporary shelter spaces this winter, including 40 modular cabins for up to 80 people with support services on vacant municipal land off Barton Street West.
For years, provincial and federal governments have given short shrift to funding for affordable housing and homelessness, leaving municipalities to fill in the gaps, Horwath said.
"It's often that municipalities step up to solve the problems because the crises are unfolding at our doorstep," she said, adding "none of this actually, to be frank, belongs on the property tax base."
Skelly, who blamed federal immigration policies for contributing to Ontario's housing crunch, noted how in November city politicians supported Coun. Mike Spadafora's call for an audit of Hamilton's housing and homelessness initiatives.
"If it shows that there is waste, then they have to deal with that before they come to any level of government asking for more money."
Conservative MPP Neil Lumsden (Hamilton East-Stoney Creek) didn't respond to The Spectator's request for comment.
Teviah Moro is a reporter at The Hamilton Spectator. tmoro@thespec.com
CAPTION: MPP Donna Skelly says the city should demonstrate how it's spending funding on housing and homelessness before asking the province for more.Mayor Andrea Horwath says additional provincial funding to deal with homelessness is welcome but says the "scale of the problem far outweighs the amount of resources being made available."City workers and contractors clean up debris at an encampment at the Delta Parkette in east Hamilton last week.

Document 367 of 500

Freeland to enter Liberal leadership race: Seven prospective candidates to replace PM have forms posted on the Liberal party website

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/freeland-enter-liberal-leadership-race/docview/3155061301/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Freeland+to+enter+Liberal+leadership+race%3A+Seven+prospective+candidates+to+replace+PM+have+forms+posted+on+the+Liberal+party+website&amp;title=The+Spectator&amp;issn=11899417&amp;date=2025-01-14&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.5&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Spectator&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Former finance minister Chrystia Freeland will announce her intention to run for the Liberal party leadership just before the U.S. presidential inauguration, a source close to her campaign team said Monday.
The source also said Freeland's first policy promise will be to impose dollar-for-dollar retaliatory tariffs on U.S. imports to match the tariffs U.S. president-elect Donald Trump has vowed to impose on Canada.
Trump has promised to impose 25 per cent tariffs on all imports from Canada and Mexico on Jan. 20, the day he is inaugurated.
The federal and provincial governments are working together to set retaliatory tariffs to respond to that threat, trying to target U.S. exports that would wreak maximum damage on regions important to Republican leaders including Trump.
Freeland was a key player in responding to Trump tariffs during his first term in the White House. As the minister of foreign affairs she oversaw a dollar-for-dollar tariff response to Trump's imposition of import taxes on Canadian steel and aluminum in 2018.
Freeland initially led Canada's cabinet committee working to mount the response this time but has not been at that table since she resigned from cabinet Dec. 16.
That bombshell move - which came after Prime Minister Justin Trudeau told her he was replacing her as finance minister with former Bank of Canada governor Mark Carney - set in motion the final chapter of Trudeau's tenure at the top. It ignited a firestorm of criticism within his caucus that ultimately led him to say last week that he would step down as soon as a new leader is chosen.
The party has now set the date for that as March 9, and candidates have until Jan. 23 to declare their intention to run.
The Freeland campaign source said maintaining Canada's strong relationship with the U.S. is shaping her early policy decisions, and the dollar-for-dollar retaliatory tariffs would be developed in concert with the provinces.
Freeland is expected to be joined in the race by Carney, and former B.C. premier Christy Clark though neither have yet formally declared their candidacies.
Ontario MP Chandra Arya and former Montreal MP Frank Baylis are the only two to officially join the contest.
A source close to Industry Minister François-Philippe Champagne said he plans to reveal Tuesday whether he will run.
Freeland, Carney, Clark, Arya and Baylis are among the seven prospective leadership candidates with forms posted on the Liberal party website to collect the signatures they require to enter the race.
Cabinet ministers Karina Gould and Jonathan Wilkinson are also on that list.
The Canadian Press
CAPTION:

CREDIT: Kyle Duggan The Canadian Press

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Horwath+says+province%2C+feds+must+step+up+their+game+on+homelessness+issue%3A+Local+Tories+say+accountability+for+%24158+million+in+city+funding+is+needed+before+more+aid+is+given&amp;title=The+Spectator&amp;issn=11899417&amp;date=2025-01-14&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.1&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Spectator&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Document 369 of 500

Who will step up for Canada?

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/who-will-step-up-canada/docview/3155061062/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=unknown&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Who+will+step+up+for+Canada%3F&amp;title=The+Spectator&amp;issn=11899417&amp;date=2025-01-14&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.8&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Spectator&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: In the aftermath of a major historical event, the causative chain that produced it is often shrouded in mystery. The real drivers are almost always impossible to discover in real time. Clarity only emerges decades later, when long-classified documents are finally released from the archives, illuminating what truly transpired.
But sometimes, history doesn't make us wait. This is undeniably true of the recent announcement by our 23rd prime minister to resign his office this past week.
No sane human being on planet Earth is asking: "Why?" or "How?" They're asking, "What the hell took him so long?" And maybe that's the question only the historians can solve. But, at the moment, it doesn't matter in the least. What matters is that this country is staring down the barrel of existential threats. And to appreciate their magnitude, we only need to put two things side by side.
One: We are facing the greatest threat to our sovereignty and prosperity in generations.
Two: We are led by a lame-duck prime minister who the majority of Canadians have exactly zero confidence in to lead us.
Trudeau's resignation has left a vacuum for genuine, popularly supported leadership in this country. So, what happens next? Who steps up in this moment of historic challenge?
It's a painfully familiar feeling: Just when you need something the very most, you find it's hiding from you.
The simple fact is that in our hour of greatest need, we need our greatest people to lead us. And in the wake of the fallout to Justin Trudeau's resignation and Donald Trump's very real threat of economic war, this is what concerns me: that many of our best and brightest will succumb to the temptation to bury their heads in the sand and pretend this is all a dream.
It's not. It's anything but a dream. And nothing is a foregone conclusion.
Leadership is not a title; it's behaviour. It is how you act. It is what you do. And it is what you are prepared to sacrifice for the good of your country.
Fortunately, across jurisdictional and partisan lines we've seen examples of just that. Premier Doug Ford is going into the snake pit on Fox News. Finance Minister Dominic LeBlanc has decided it's better to focus his attention on the threat of U.S. tariffs than his personal ambitions to become Liberal leader. These are commendable actions, but we need far more - and not just from our politicians.
In a recent interview with Jordan Peterson, Conservative Leader Pierre Poilievre underlined that Canadians cannot assume, "by simply voting in an election that ... all the problems are going to reverse instantaneously." That's neither cynicism nor mere expectation management. It's an acknowledgment of reality. National challenges aren't solved at the ballot box alone - they're solved by extraordinary people stepping up, not just in politics, but in every corner of public life.
Trump himself said he does not care who wins our next federal election. What he does care about is pretty clear: beating us.
That kind of pressure can drive a country into despair, numbing it with a fatalistic sense of inevitability. Or it can do the opposite. It can light a fire. It can inspire resilience, defiance, and the will to fight back.
The most critical question we ought to be asking in this historic moment is not whether Trudeau deserved the slings and arrows or how his legacy will take shape. That will be a rigidly partisan, tiresome tug of war.
The real question is where do we go from here and who rises to the occasion?
"History never looks like history when you are living through it," so the saying goes. Except when it does. This week, many Canadians woke up to the realization that they are living through a turning point. The prime minister's resignation isn't the end of the story. It's the prologue to one of the most challenging chapters in our history.
Now comes the hard part.
Jaime Watt is the executive chair of Navigator Ltd.
and a Conservative strategist.
CAPTION:

CREDIT: JAIME WATT OPINION Jaime Watt is the executive chair of Navigator Ltd. and a Conservative strategist.;

Subject: Leadership; Prime ministers

Document 370 of 500

New year, new chance for Jama

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/new-year-chance-jama/docview/3155060830/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=unknown&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=New+year%2C+new+chance+for+Jama&amp;title=The+Spectator&amp;issn=11899417&amp;date=2025-01-14&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.9&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Spectator&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: The good news for a new year is that the Ontario NDP is giving independent MPP Sarah Jama the opportunity to win back her seat in Hamilton Centre.
Funny how things work.
Nominations are needed because Jama was kicked out of the party not long after winning the byelection by a huge margin over her closest competitor and with a clear majority of votes.
The official NDP line is Jama was insubordinate and difficult to work with, but there's no evidence to support the claim. No evidence, because there's been no hearing of any kind, no process by which the decision was made in an open and transparent fashion. No opportunity for Jama to answer to her peers, defend herself.
For those who forgot, the NDP announced Jama's removal immediately after her refusal to read a prepared statement they wrote in an effort to force her apology for comments she made in support of the Palestinian people after the Oct. 7 Hamas attacks.
It happened in the blink of an eye, a draconian response by NDP leadership, followed by one even more brutal as the Conservatives jumped on the hate wagon and censured Jama in the legislature as antisemitic. Whether you support Israel or not, what happened to Sarah Jama was fundamentally undemocratic at its core.
If the party can't be democratic in its internal processes and procedures, how democratic will it be when in power? Democracy is a muscle. It needs exercise to keep away the forces of authoritarianism. This should matter to every NDP supporter. It should matter to all of us.
As should the unending and relentless attempts to portray Jama as antisemitic for her pro-Palestinian, pro-peace position. Like so many people who have watched, unfiltered on social media, the devastating destruction unleashed by Israel, Jama is only one of the millions of people in Canada and around the world, who agree with the accusations of genocide.
For taking this position, she continues to be the target of pro-Israel propaganda that would paint her as "evil."
It's both uninformed and deliberate.
The NDP are not like Conservative and Liberals. As a party they are a bit rough at the edges, they're working people who tell it like they live it, plain-spoken. They don't have lots of money to donate to politics, but they give their time to improving the lives of others.
They will stand up and speak out at an injustice and they will demand more from government for the people as a whole, not just for special interest groups or deep-pocketed donors. Monied interests tend to be afraid of the NDP.
Hamilton Centre knew who Jama was and they supported her when they voted for her in a byelection. For the NDP leadership to silence the voices of Hamilton Centre unilaterally, without transparency or public process, is profoundly undemocratic.
Without process, this could happen to any duly elected politician, in any riding. That's not democracy. Neither was the censure vote immediately after, which history will read as the weaponization of the Ontario legislature by interests that have nothing to do with provincial jurisdiction.
What happened to Jama made international headlines, and not in a good way.
The discourse against her support for Palestine is drowning out the work Jama has been doing as a representative of the people of Hamilton Centre. This is intentional. It's obvious. And it's racist. I won't repeat things I've read online; I wasn't raised that way.
Even sitting as an independent member, Jama has submitted motions consistent with her campaign promises, she has been present and vocal for issues where others have stayed silent.
She has done the job the people elected her to do, and she has conducted herself, despite being the object of so much vitriol and hate, with grace and dignity.
It's a sign of party maturity that the NDP have listened to Jama's supporters and are giving her a chance to reclaim the party nomination for Hamilton Centre. It's the least they could do.
Margaret Shkimba is a
Hamilton writer. For more visit
margaretshkimba.Substack
.com. You can follow her on X (@menrvasofia) and friend her on Facebook.
CAPTION: Independent MPP Sarah Jama has begun the process toseek the NDP nominationfor Hamilton Centre for the next provincial election.Christopher Katsarov The Canadian Press file photo

Document 371 of 500

Who actually uses Toronto's bike lanes - and who really benefits from them?

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/who-actually-uses-torontos-bike-lanes-really/docview/3155060814/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Who+actually+uses+Toronto%27s+bike+lanes+-+and+who+really+benefits+from+them%3F&amp;title=The+Hamilton+Spectator+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2025-01-14&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Hamilton+Spectator+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: In the cold light of day, with snow on the streets and ice on the sidewalks, Toronto's bike lanes have never looked clearer.
Clear of snow most days. Clear of cyclists most times.
To be perfectly clear: by cyclists, I mean people who pedal - not the e-bikers with pretend pedals who don't.
We rarely stop to think about who really uses them and who doesn't. In the overheated debate about bike lanes, winter has a way of clearing the air.
These days, it's hard not to notice that the most steadfast, fastest bikers are e-bikers ferrying food for Uber Eats and competing delivery outfits. Commuters and recreational cyclists, not so much - not in the salty, slushy winter chill.
How did we come to the point where massive public funds are invested in building and maintaining a network of part-time trails used mostly by part-time gig workers? How do we justify the specialized winter crews that plow and salt these mostly empty bike trails while adjoining sidewalks are icy and slippery, and emergency shelters for the unhoused remain underfunded and overcapacity?
Set aside, for the moment, your feelings about Doug Ford. For the premier, all bike lanes lead to the campaign trail - he's clearly ready to ride the divide and drive a political wedge to win votes.
Park your thoughts, for the moment, about e-bike safety and utility. Let's focus on facts and stats.
I cheerfully make the most of bike lanes, but I'm a fair-weather rider - I cycle to work most days until the snow and salt set in. Yet even in the best of weather, I see bike lanes on University Avenue and Yonge Street relatively little-used while motor vehicles are stalled in traffic; in the worst of winter, it's typically e-bike delivery workers who own the trails, sailing past the congestion at high speed and high risk because they are so often unlit and unsafe (despite all the earnest rhetoric about how bike lanes put safety first, pedestrians beware).
Ask yourself this:
Why are taxpayers underwriting a major infrastructure network for a marginal economic activity that exploits the most marginalized workers? Nothing against those e-bikers eking out a living, but an in-depth, undercover investigation by my Star colleague Ghada Alsharif last month revealed the relentless uphill economic climb that workers face, leaving them stuck in dead-end jobs (her hourly waged worked out to a pittance).
Cheap delivery apps helped resuscitate the restaurant industry during the pandemic. But how does it benefit us to subsidize a delivery sector that relies on cheap labour from predominantly recent arrivals to Canada?
Consider the Eglinton Crosstown project that will one day - some day - deliver mass transit across the city. At great expense, we tunnelled the LRT below ground along major segments so as to preserve surface car lanes; Toronto has taken away one lane in each direction for segregated bike lanes.
Did we really spend hundreds of billions of dollars to dig a tunnel just to save space for a bike lane? If we didn't really need those car lanes, why didn't we just keep the LRT at grade?
By the time the snows melt, the political rhetoric is destined to heat up on the campaign trail. The premier has targeted bike lanes for removal on major roads like Yonge, University, and a stretch of Bloor Street West in Etobicoke because he believes it's a vote-winner.
He may be right. As much as I enjoy sailing past the gridlock on my Yonge Street bike lane, which keeps creeping north, it's painfully clear that city planners misjudged this one.
From what I've read, they believe as an article of faith that the inner city must be protected from suburbanites who dare to drive in, as if Toronto is an island in the GTA - and that commuters can glide into town on imaginary mass transit lines that don't yet exist. They believed that if you build it, they will bike.
Yet the cyclists have not come - not in numbers that would justify the resources in urban planning, municipal spending and provincial bandwidth. Instead, a bizarre statistical debate has broken out among proponents and opponents of bike lanes - which fizzled when the Star's Robert Benzie revealed that a major provincial-municipal survey ultimately showed only 3.1 per cent of Torontonians commute by bike to work.
The numbers edge higher in some wards, but still not the throngs that many imagine. A clear-eyed look at major arteries suggests that the expected cycling revolution hasn't happened.
We've created a new vector for e-bike delivery services so that hungry diners can order hot food on a cold night within minutes for a few dollars - thanks to the misallocation of municipal money better spent elsewhere. On Monday, we learned that Toronto taxes are going up yet again, this time by 6.9 per cent after a 9.5 per cent hike last year, and a seven per cent rise the year before.
I'm likely in the minority of residents who supports tax hikes in tough economic times to pay for essential services such as health care, education and shelter for those in need. But the cold reality is that subsidizing the marginal economics of e-bike apps for marginalized gig workers isn't smart spending.
CAPTION: A cyclist uses a bike lane on Richmond Street West in downtown Toronto on Nov. 19, 2024.

Subject: Mass transit; Electric bicycles; Bicycling

Document 372 of 500

What minerals does the U.S. get from Ontario and how can Canada fight back against Trump's tariff threats?

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/what-minerals-does-u-s-get-ontario-how-can-canada/docview/3155431250/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=What+minerals+does+the+U.S.+get+from+Ontario+and+how+can+Canada+fight+back+against+Trump%27s+tariff+threats%3F&amp;title=The+Hamilton+Spectator+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2025-01-13&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Hamilton+Spectator+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: The United States should look to Ontario and Canada for critical minerals like nickel, copper and platinum rather than China, says Ontario Premier Doug Ford.
Ford made the statement as part of Jan. 13 news conference during which he expanded upon plans for a North American economic alliance he calls "Fortress Am-Can."
Watch the news conference
Ford said he is proposing "bold new measures" to help extract, process and deliver the critical minerals North American supply chains need.
The move comes exactly one week before the inauguration of U.S. president Donald Trump, who has proposed crippling 25 per cent tariffs against Canadian exports.
What Fortress Am-Can means for mining
Building upon his "Fortress Am-Can" energy alliance with the U.S. announced last week, Ford pledged to speed up regulatory approval timelines for current and planned critical mineral projects, with priority given to projects that displace and replace Chinese supply.
The plan includes fast-tracking resource extraction in Ontario's Ring of Fire region.
"Ontario plans to start a new Am-Can critical mineral security alliance, to invest and then build out American and Canadian critical mineral supply chains, including by significantly expanding Am-Can processing capacity," said Ford.
"Forty-six per cent of the nickel the U.S. uses comes from Ontario," said Ford.
Ford said Canada should work with the U.S. Department of Defense to prioritize shared Am-Can national security objectives for Canadian government investments in critical mineral supply chains, including strategic investments in mining and processing projects.
Ontario has also proposed a cross-border working group on mineral price stability to identify Am-Can policy solutions that promote the long-term sustainability of critical mineral development projects.
Ford said the province also intends to enshrine a one project-one process permitting approach to all resource development in Canada with service standards and timeline guarantees for regulatory approvals, while promoting First Nation equity and economic participation and respecting and meeting duty to consult obligations.
Importance of Ontario minerals
George Pirie, Ontario minister of mines, said Ontario is a producer of more than 77 per cent of the platinum group elements in Canada, almost half of the country's nickel and one-third of its copper.
Pirie added Ontario nickel is critical to the American aerospace and defence industries.
Could Ontario block electricity and mineral exports?
Ford, who said he hasn't met face-to-face with the president-elect, said Ontario could be the hardest hit province if Trump's tariff threats are enacted. He refused to rule out cutting off electricity exports to the U.S. states of Minnesota, New York and Michigan if the tariffs are imposed.
"They're targeting Ontario. They're targeting good paying jobs in Ontario and I'm going to fight with every tool I have in our tool box to protect Ontario jobs," said Ford.
When asked whether Ontario might withhold exports of critical minerals to the United States, Ford said: "Let's see what they're going to do on Jan. 20 and then we'll retaliate."
"Let's try not to go down that road. It's just going to delay and hurt both economies."
CAPTION: Premier Ford holds a press conferenceOntario plans to start a new Am-Can critical mineral security alliance, to invest and then build out American and Canadian critical mineral supply chains, in response to tariff threats by U.S. president-elect Donald Trump.
