Jan. 13: Scrapping protocol the wrong move, indifference isn't an option and other letters to the editor

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/jan-13-scrapping-protocol-wrong-move-indifference/docview/3154873162/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Jan.+13%3A+Scrapping+protocol+the+wrong+move%2C+indifference+isn%27t+an+option+and+other+letters+to+the+editor&amp;title=The+Hamilton+Spectator+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2025-01-13&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Hamilton+Spectator+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Scrapping protocol the wrong move
Re: Councillors want to scrap Hamilton's encampment protocol, Jan. 10
This letter is directed to councillors Mike Spadafora and Tom Jackson and any councillors who might consider supporting their motion. I would like you to acknowledge that in introducing this motion, you are disregarding many facts.
 Removing encampments costs money. Those police don't come cheap, as we know. For councillors who loudly and frequently cite the wishes of the "hardworking taxpayers," it is imperative that you include for your constituents the information on how much the removal of encampments will add to taxes.
 Removing encampments is a temporary - and inhumane - measure. Given there is an increase in the number of homeless people across the province, folks will disappear from said park for awhile, then set up again when they are able to accumulate the necessary gear to do so.
 Despite the claim "it's time to give the parks back to residents," my observation is that parks are and always have been available to residents. I challenge you to visit any park, where you'll see dog walkers, children with caregivers, and residents. If there is a tent, I am betting the inhabitants mostly keep to themselves.
 The clearing of encampments does not improve public safety. Multiple studies have demonstrated this. Google it.
I know it is a fool's game to bring facts to a "feelings" fight, but here we are. By introducing an expensive, pointless and traumatizing motion you are wasting council's time and taxpayer funds. Instead, you should be working on solutions with your colleagues.
Kris Gadjanski, Dundas
Shelter project will be costly
The city's tiny homes project underway in the Barton and Hess area is set to install a 40-unit encampment of residential structures on a former industrial property. This will be the first of five such sites. Depending on the information referenced, the first site will entail an initial capital cost of about $3 million, and annual costs of about $4.2 million (partly including seven new staff for the overall shelter program). If these costs do not escalate from the current estimates - a highly optimistic outlook, given the city's recent track record - the first year's cost will be $180,000 per unit, with ongoing annual costs of $105,000 per unit per year. For the full picture, multiply these numbers by the five proposed sites and do your own math. Wow! Homeowners, get your chequebooks out!
Costs aside, in my experience, the city is strict and demanding with all development applications and the complex related protocols, so it would be of interest to verify if, for this project, the city is adhering to its own regulations and policies with respect to zoning (existing and/or revised zoning), advance public notification and consultation, site plan approvals, services design and provision, building permit designs and approvals. Or is there a double-standard?
Romeo Palombella, Hamilton
Indifference isn't an option
Re: Mayor speaks out on Islamic group's conference plans, Jan. 8
I am encouraged that Mayor Andrea Horwath has spoken out against the relocation of the annual Khilafah conference, scheduled for Jan. 18, to Hamilton. Organized by Hizb ut Tahrir Canada, an Islamic fundamentalist group, the event is not welcome in its original location of Mississauga.
Since the Israel-Hamas war broke out in October 2023, bomb threats and gunfire targeting Jewish institutions have increased, not only globally, but also in our own community. As a longtime Hamiltonian, I have recently heard antisemitic remarks from certain groups and elected officials. In addition, I have seen racist graffiti on walls around the city.
To prevent the occurrence of this event, Hamiltonians must take a proactive approach by actively opposing all forms of racism and public demonstrations of hate. It is only by doing so, that we can ensure that everyone feels safe and welcome in all public spaces in our community. In this case, indifference is not an option.
Andrea Rado, Hamilton
U.S. owes Canada gratitude
Since Donald Trump's victory in November, he has threatened a takeover of the Panama Canal, buying Greenland and annexing Canada. Except for Premier Doug Ford, no politician has seen the importance of sending him a strong message that Canada is not for sale and will never be under an American flag.
Threats of tariffs have seen federal politicians rush to Mar-a-Lago as lapdogs summoned by their master. This is not the Canadian way, never has and should never be. Nobody can deny that stronger measures are needed at our borders to stop the flow of illegal immigrants and drugs. What measures we take is our decision, not the U.S. If illegal immigrants and drugs are entering the U.S., it is their responsibility. It would be foreign interference to even suggest how to do their job.
What is more shocking is the number of Canadians on the sidelines, with no reaction, no self-respect. Canadians must send a strong message to Trump that he and he alone has a decision to make: tell Canadians clearly if he intends to treat Canada as a friendly country or not. No more statements of grandeur! No more threats!
In all his remarks, Trump has stated clearly that taking over Canada and Greenland would be for no other purpose than to protect the U.S., not Canada or Greenland, but the U.S. For years, the U.S. has had the advantages of an early warning system on Canadian soil; in other words, the U.S. found a way to use Canada as a shield against possible attacks from Russia.
Canada is not and will never be a superpower; the threat to Russia is not Canada, but the U.S. Early warning stations on Canadian soil were and are still more for the protection of the U.S. than of Canada. If, after 30years of using Canada as a shield the most gratitude Canada can expect from the U.S. are threats of tariffs, then Canadians can only conclude that America owes Canada big time for exploiting Canada at a very cheap price.
The time has come for all Canadians to take a stand. Trump has made it clear that he will stage an economic war against Canada. We cannot stand still, we must react. The only way to stop a bully is to take a stronger stand. Give Trump a choice, a simple one: treat Canada as a friendly country, no tariffs, no economic war or break all diplomatic ties with Canada, go your way, we will choose our own path and destiny. For now, your attitude and threats have made you a persona non grata. Stay home!
Pierre Drouin, Ancaster
Voters need to educate themselves
Now that the Liberal situation is somewhat cleared up, it's time for all Canadians to seriously reflect on their political future. It is the responsibility of every voting Canadian to educate themselves on the important issues, demand clear and honest answers from everyone running in the next election and most importantly get out and vote.
There is no time for any excuses for not voting. Many who do not vote use as their excuse, "all politicians are the same so it no use voting." That is not true!
The conservative leader has used the last two years to condemn everything the Liberals have done. Recently he used parliamentary rules to stop all work in the house and wasted days by his actions.
He recently remarked about the "dark chapter in our history" referring to the past nine years and Justin Trudeau breaking "the country over the last nine years" and of course his "axe the tax" campaign. He has created a false illusion about the state of Canada.
I urge every voter to read the opinion peace by David Olive in the Jan. 8 edition of the Spec; "Canadians are much better off today." It's a must read.
Some examples: During the Trudeau years, Canadian economy grew by 41 per cent. Under Stephen Harper it grew 18 per cent. Our per capita income grew by more than 23 per cent vs. 7.6 per cent under Harper.
On the basic measure of fiscal prudence, Canada ranks better than all G7 countries.
Trudeau invested billions to build the Trans Mountain pipeline that has tripled oil exports.
Voters need to understand the facts. I appeal to voters to use the next months before the election make an educated decision and not to listen to lame promises. But most of all, let's get at least 80 per cent of the voters to cast a ballot. There may be many things that Canadians cannot control, but each vote can make a difference.
Roman Caruk, Hamilton
Annexation anxiety is growing
Wake up Canada. Are we going to relax and sit back and do nothing and allow ourselves to be "frumped by Trump?" Annexation is the act of seizing or purchasing an area of land or country and making it a permanent part of the annexing nation. There are numerous historic examples. One of the earliest was by the Roman Emperor Caligula in AD 40 of Mauretania. Then of course, the purchase of Louisiana by the U.S. from France in 1803, followed by the U.S. purchase of Alaska from Russia in 1867. And how could we forget the annexation of Austria in 1938 by Nazi Germany. Yes, this 85-year-old Canuck is concerned, very concerned.
Glenn Simpson, Hamilton
Will new Liberal leader reach out to Albertans?
I love Alberta. I've lived here over a decade after moving here from the Hammer.
I've watched Justin Trudeau attack Alberta mercilessly with devastating consequences.Imagine if a Canadian prime minister did everything in his power to destroy the Ontario auto industry, wiping it off the map including the thousands jobs that people depend on to feed their families. Imagine the devastation to the Ontario economy, if there were no more autoworkers' salaries to support the communities like Windsor, Woodstock, Oakville, Brampton, Alliston and Oshawa. Unthinkable? Well Alberta has been living with unthinkable under Trudeau.
So I want to know what every Liberal leadership candidate's plan is to "welcome" Alberta back into Canada? We were never welcome in Trudeau's Canada, so I want to know what will change under their leadership as a prospective Liberal leader, if anything, when it comes to Alberta.
Thank goodness, we now have the Iron Lady Premier Danielle Smith. Alberta will no longer be a doormat for Liberals to wipe their feet on!
Chris Robertson, Stony Plain, Alta.
CAPTION: A tent encampment in Bayfront Park. Removing encampments is a temporary and inhumane measure, a reader writes.

Subject: Political campaigns; Canadians; Threats; Parks & recreation areas; Protocol; Costs; Voters; Homeless people; Tariffs

Business indexing term: Subject: Costs Tariffs

Document 374 of 500

Doug Ford is wrong to use Trump's tariffs to justify an early election, Bonnie Crombie says

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/doug-ford-is-wrong-use-trumps-tariffs-justify/docview/3154872436/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Doug+Ford+is+wrong+to+use+Trump%27s+tariffs+to+justify+an+early+election%2C+Bonnie+Crombie+says&amp;title=The+Hamilton+Spectator+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2025-01-13&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Hamilton+Spectator+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Ontario Liberal Leader Bonnie Crombie says Premier Doug Ford doesn't need an early election to counter the economic upheaval that would follow punitive American tariffs.
Crombie said her party would work with the governing Progressive Conservatives on a package of supports to mitigate damage to Ontario jobs from threatened 25-per-cent levies on goods imported from Canada to the U.S.
"Donald Trump and his tariffs should be treated as a threat, not as an excuse for an illegitimate early election call," Crombie told a news conference Monday.
"All he cares about is his own job," she said of Ford, who has repeatedly refused to rule out an early election this spring instead of waiting until the next scheduled provincewide vote in June 2026.
"He has a majority government, so he can pass any legislation he wants to right now," Crombie added.
"You've got the mandate," NDP Leader Marit Stiles said Monday in Windsor.
But the prospect of a relatively easy re-election to a third term - with the rationale to voters that he needs a fresh mandate to spend the tens of billions of dollars that could be required to keep the Ontario economy afloat if Trump's levies come to fruition - might be hard for Ford to resist given his strong lead in public opinion polls.
As recently as last week, the premier again refused to rule out a snap election after he raised concerns about uncertainty at the federal level following Prime Minister Justin Trudeau's resignation announcement.
"Right now what I'm focused on, right this second and moving forward, is making sure that we don't have tariffs," Ford said.
Trump has threatened the tariffs against Canada and Mexico after his inauguration next Monday.
Crombie said Ford is eager to hold an election to get ahead of an RCMP investigation into his government's $8.2 billion Greenbelt land swap scandal at a time when millions of Ontarians can't find a family doctor and unemployment is high amid a homelessness and affordability crisis.
"The premier didn't seek a mandate when he spent billions on pre-election goodies," she added in reference to Ford's plan to send $200 "tax rebate" cheques to Ontarians in the coming weeks at a cost of $3 billion.
CAPTION: Ontario Liberal Leader Bonnie Crombie at a party convention in London, Ont., on Sept. 21, 2024.

Document 375 of 500

Ford emerges as Canada's top defender

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/ford-emerges-as-canadas-top-defender/docview/3154671189/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=unknown&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Ford+emerges+as+Canada%27s+top+defender&amp;title=The+Spectator&amp;issn=11899417&amp;date=2025-01-13&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.9&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Spectator&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: With a single post on his X platform, Elon Musk made the power vacuum in Ottawa official around the world. And he didn't stand on ceremony: "Girl, you're not the governor of Canada anymore, so doesn't matter what you say."
He was responding to Prime Minister Justin Trudeau's post asserting that "There isn't a snowball's chance in hell that Canada would become part of the United States."
Even before the resignation of our finance minister followed by our prime minister, Ottawa wasn't walking the talk on Team Canada. And meetings in Florida have so far yielded very few reassuring signs that Donald Trump's promised tariffs won't be as severe as feared.
In the absence of a strong response from Ottawa, and with the distraction of a leadership race to choose another temporary prime minister, it's Premier Doug Ford - and to a lesser degree Alberta Premier Danielle Smith - who have emerged as Canada's most effective advocates.
They show up where it matters most: on Fox News. It's where not only U.S. lawmakers and business leaders get what is offered as news, but also where Americans who are tickled by the idea of annexing Canada are told it's a real possibility.
Ford is visibly at ease on Fox News. He rolled with the punches this week when host Jesse Watters tried to get a rise out of him with the faux outrage typical of the network by framing Canadians' refusal to be annexed as offensive: "I would consider it a privilege to be taken over by the United States of America. That's what everybody else in the world wants," adding that "for some reason, that's repellent to you Canadians. And I find that personally offensive, Premier."
Ford kept his cool in the face of Watters' sensationalism, repeating his message to a broader audience. He did so as the premier of Canada's largest province, but also the way any Canadian entrepreneur trading with the U.S. would - in a constructive, businesslike manner.
Perhaps inspired by his slogan that Ontario is "Open for Business," he comes across as a straight-shooter who calls a spade a spade: "I get it. President-elect Trump is a real estate tycoon. He's made billions. But that property is not for sale. It's as simple as that."
Instead, Ford speaks of a win-win Am-Can partnership between two neighbours - a message that Canada should forcefully and factually communicate directly to all Americans.
The man is emerging as Canada's strongest advocate for integrated commerce that benefits both countries, without erasing the border. He should be readily accessible to every booker and segment producer in the U.S. media.
It's also time for Canada to use paid advertising to speak directly to those who influence state governors and legislators in Washington: American voters. They should know that when Trump claims the U.S. doesn't need anything from Canada, they actually get 60 per cent of their energy imports from us.
Ontario's multimillion-dollar U.S. advertising campaign against tariffs should be quickly revisited. While the intention is right, its message is wrong: "For generations, this ally to the north has been here - and for generations more we'll still be here, right by your side."
That soft approach was crafted before the tariff-related Mar-a-Lago dinner joke morphed into talks about annexation. One of the dinner guests who initially laughed it off, now Finance Minister Dominic LeBlanc, says the joke is over. Instead of this Hallmark card, the ads should feature Ford speaking directly to Americans.
As we learned from Jimmy Carter, Americans see good, decent guys as weak. They like no-nonsense men who are willing to fight back.
Éric Blais is president of Headspace Marketing Inc.
CAPTION:

CREDIT: Éric Blais Éric Blais is president of Headspace Marketing Inc.;

Subject: Political campaigns; Prime ministers; Tariffs

Business indexing term: Subject: Tariffs

Document 376 of 500

Doug Ford has gone on an unprecedented American media blitz. Here's what is behind it

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/doug-ford-has-gone-on-unprecedented-american/docview/3154395382/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Doug+Ford+has+gone+on+an+unprecedented+American+media+blitz.+Here%27s+what+is+behind+it&amp;title=The+Hamilton+Spectator+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2025-01-12&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Hamilton+Spectator+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Jesse Watters, the bombastic Fox News host, was hectoring a prominent Canadian political leader the other day about president-elect Donald Trump's bizarre scheme to annex Canada.
"What's your problem with the United States absorbing Canada?" barked Watters as his guest waited patiently to respond.
The Canadian replied firmly but politely.
"I get it. President-elect Trump is a real-estate tycoon. He's made billions, but that property is not for sale, as simple as that," he said.
No, it wasn't departing Prime Minister Justin Trudeau or Finance Minister Dominic LeBlanc or even newly minted Public Safety Minister David McGuinty defending the sanctity of Canada's border on "Jesse Watters Primetime," one of the many Fox shows the TV-obsessed Trump is known to monitor.
It was Premier Doug Ford, who has been on an unprecedented U.S. media blitz in the weeks since the incoming president vowed to slap 25 per cent tariffs on Canadian and Mexican products after his Jan. 20 inauguration.
The trade war threat has escalated with Trump's social-media trolling about making Canada "the 51st state" and deriding Trudeau as "governor of the great state of Canada."
That gambit is juicy red meat to Trumpian TV personalities like Watters.
"If I were a citizen of another country and I was a neighbour of the United States, I would consider it a privilege to be taken over by the United States of America," the right-wing cable news stalwart intoned to Ford last Tuesday.
"That's what everybody else in the world wants: American citizenship. For some reason that's repellent to you Canadians - and I find that personally offensive."
But Ford, whose communication skills improved dramatically during the COVID-19 pandemic when his daily health briefings were broadcast live across the province to Ontarians locked down at home, didn't take the bait.
Maintaining message discipline, he pivoted to his central theme: averting the tariffs he fears could devastate Ontario's economy.
"You know something, Jesse, we're proud Canadians - just like there's proud Americans - and if we join together and take on the world with a great trade deal between us, I think that would be fabulous," emphasized the premier.
Along with repeated appearances on Fox, Ford has done interviews on CNN, NBC and CNBC, among other U.S. networks, to argue against tariffs that would lead to higher prices for consumers at home and stateside.
He will be in Washington twice next month, leading a Council of the Federation delegation on Feb. 12, and attending the National Governors Association conference on Feb. 20.
His government is spending millions of dollars on a slick advertising campaign in the U.S. to remind Americans of how important trade with Ontario is to their economic well-being.
And he is on the phone daily with any governor, senator or representative who will take his call to plead Canada's case.
While the premier may indeed be basking in the media attention, his high profile is helped by the fact that politics, like nature, abhors a vacuum.
"There's a lack of leadership right now in Ottawa," Ford complained to reporters at Queen's Park after Trudeau announced his resignation Monday.
With the federal Liberals distracted by a nascent leadership contest that concludes March 9 and a national election looming as early as this spring, the premier has stepped into the void.
The federal chaos comes at an opportune time for a provincial leader who is polling so well against Liberal Leader Bonnie Crombie and NDP Leader Marit Stiles that he is still considering an Ontario election this year, even though he doesn't have to face voters until June 2026.
Ford pointedly and repeatedly refused to rule out such a move last Monday when journalists asked him five times about the possibility of a snap provincial election.
"Right now what I'm focused on - right this second and moving forward - is making sure that we don't have tariffs. I can tell you, my eye hasn't been taken off the ball for a second here," he insisted.
But the prospect of a relatively easy re-election to a third term - with the rationale to voters that he needs a fresh mandate to spend the tens of billions of dollars that could be required to keep the Ontario economy afloat if Trump's levies come to fruition - might be hard for him to resist.
His political advisers believe his standing with the public is as high as it was at the peak of the pandemic, when he won praise for working closely with Trudeau and then intergovernmental affairs minister Chrystia Freeland, who remains a personal friend and is now a front-runner for the Liberal leadership.
The premier's campaign team, however, is mindful of the limitations of what he can actually do as the leader of a subnational jurisdiction when it comes to retaliating against Trump's tariffs - to say nothing of the potential backlash if he calls an Ontario election at an already uncertain time and is viewed as opportunistic.
That's one reason he has toned down his Dec. 11 threat to cut off the electricity Ontario exports to Michigan, New York and Wisconsin, which supplies power for 1.5 million Americans.
Instead, Ford is pitching a "Fortress Am-Can" energy alliance with the U.S. in order to ship more Ontario-generated power south of the border.
At the same time, he is signalling that American consumers would ultimately pay the price for any Trump tariffs.
"Someone in Texas buys a pickup truck that's produced here in Oshawa, not far away from here, they typically would pay $60,000," he said in Courtice, Ont. on Wednesday.
"Now, that same person in Texas is going to pay $75,000 if he puts tariffs on vehicles," said Ford.
"These tariffs would be devastating for both of our economies. They'd disrupt deeply integrated supply chains, create massive uncertainty for investors, raise costs for businesses and put countless jobs at risk on both sides of the border," he said.
"It doesn't have to be this way."
CAPTION: Premier Doug Ford is shown at Queen's Park in Toronto on Jan. 6, 2025.

Document 377 of 500

Justin Trudeau had thought about resigning for a long time. The inside story of what finally made him quit

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/justin-trudeau-had-thought-about-resigning-long/docview/3154140661/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Justin+Trudeau+had+thought+about+resigning+for+a+long+time.+The+inside+story+of+what+finally+made+him+quit&amp;title=The+Hamilton+Spectator+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2025-01-11&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Hamilton+Spectator+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: OTTAWA - Prime Minister Justin Trudeau once considered quitting politics long before Monday.
On the day he announced his coming resignation, Trudeau wrote his own first draft of history. He told the nation he is a fighter who never backs away from a fight - that he wouldn't be stepping down if it weren't for his internal caucus struggles that eclipsed his ability to stay on.
In fact, in early 2023, months before Trudeau publicly revealed his separation from his wife Sophie Grégoire Trudeau, the prime minister seriously weighed leaving office, the Star has learned.
Their 18-year marriage had been on the rocks for a while. His third-term Liberal government had emerged from the COVID-19 pandemic struggling to find its footing amid high interest rates and punishing inflation. An inquiry slammed a failure of federalism during his handling of the 2022 "Freedom Convoy" emergency. And national security leaks on foreign electoral interference pummelled the Trudeau government as incompetent and indifferent to a growing threat.
A visit to Canada by U.S. President Joe Biden in late March that year rallied Trudeau. By May's Liberal convention, he'd chosen to stay on - a fateful decision for him, his legacy, and his party's fortunes.
The prime minister believed then, and may still believe, that he alone can beat Conservative Leader Pierre Poilievre. His resolve only crumbled in the first days of this month.
The trigger for that decision is clear. The exact moment of it is not.
Multiple sources spoke to the Star for this story on condition their names would not be revealed.
One source said the prime minister came to his decision to leave over the holidays, that it evolved, and there was no one event or conversation that persuaded him, but "a combination of things," including calls for him to go from majorities of his Ontario, Atlantic and Quebec caucuses.
Trudeau talked about his future to very few trusted friends and advisers, including at least one outside politics, in the past several weeks.
Sources who spoke to the Star also agreed the catalyst for the prime minister's downfall was Chrystia Freeland's explosive exit as finance minister on Dec. 16, and Trudeau's failed effort to ensure Mark Carney, Canada's former central banker, would replace her.
Trudeau and his team didn't see it coming. They thought they had agreements that would see strong performers in two key roles - Carney as finance minister and Freeland on the critical Canada-U.S. challenge.
However, the seeds of the political disaster to come were sown long beforehand.
"This was the product of bruised egos over the course of six months," said one Liberal source who was close to the events as they unfolded.
Sources in both Trudeau's and Freeland's camps say Freeland was hurt by leaks in July suggesting Trudeau and the Prime Minister's Office viewed her as a poor communicator and had offered her job as finance minister to Carney.
Those leaks were wrong on a key point: Trudeau did not guarantee or even offer Carney the finance portfolio then or any time prior to December, sources say.
However, neither Trudeau nor his team - who viewed the leaks as "ridiculous" given how much Trudeau trusted Freeland - effectively dampened the speculation. In part, that was because some of them, and much of the Liberal caucus, did see Freeland as an ineffective communicator (for instance, when she urged Canadians who were worried about the cost of living to cancel their Disney Plus subscriptions) and wanted her shuffled, especially after the party's byelection loss in Toronto-St. Paul's rattled all of them.
But the leaked stories - which Freeland took to heart - were correct on one point: Trudeau and his advisers had been trying to persuade Carney, a godfather to Freeland's son, to join the team for a long time.
Carney always declined.
At least as far back as 2019, Trudeau's Liberals made failed efforts to recruit Carney to run in an Ottawa riding.
In September 2018, Carney had just agreed to extend his term as governor of the Bank of England to January 2020 to help navigate Brexit.
Although Carney chose not to run, he made clear to the Liberals that for family reasons, he would prefer an Ottawa riding if it ever came to that.
He did, however, deliver a keynote speech at the Liberals' virtual convention in 2021, held amid the pandemic, and promised to do "whatever I can to support the Liberal party."
Talks began anew last spring to lure Carney in and continued into the late summer, culminating with Carney agreeing in September to write an economic growth report for the Liberal Party of Canada that would be incorporated into a future platform.
"He's a serial fence-sitter," said one Liberal - a sentiment echoed by others.
In December, talks with Carney were again underway in earnest.
By that time, Freeland had had weeks of tense discussions with the prime minister over the fall economic statement. The government would need the support of New Democrats to pass any new measures, and they had publicly called for a GST cut as an affordability measure. The prime minister and his office hammered Freeland to deliver measures - like their proposed GST cut - that would fly with the New Democrats.
Two sources said she wanted to avoid ongoing structural commitments, and that it was Freeland who proposed the time-limited, $1.6-billion GST holiday on certain goods . But Freeland was cool to the idea of sending Canadians $250 cheques that the PMO wanted.
Several sources said Freeland was determined that the fall update would hew to the fiscal "anchor" or goal of a declining debt-to-GDP ratio, even though she had to report a whopping $60-billion deficit for the year just ended.
On Dec. 2, Trudeau and Freeland met in her office. Sources agree Trudeau signalled then that he wanted her to stay on as his finance minister, although Freeland was having doubts that he actually did.
Freeland had found Trudeau's decision to exclude her from a Nov. 29 trip to Florida to meet with U.S. president-elect Donald Trump "perplexing" and "confusing," despite publicly suggesting she understood it as the "right choice," since borders were on the agenda and that was the purview of then-public safety minister Dominic LeBlanc.
The prime minister and Freeland met again in person on Dec. 8, at his residence in Rideau Cottage, and signed off on the fall economic update. While it would include the temporary GST break, Trudeau agreed not to book the $4.7 billion for the promised $250 cheques for Canadians who had earned income in 2023 - which his team saw as a multibillion-dollar win for Freeland.
Still to come that week was a decision on Freeland's proposal to book $12 billion in cuts and launch a review of program spending.
But on a Sunday night, six days before she quit, sources close to Trudeau said the pair discussed the prime minister's desire for Freeland to take on a new role on the Canada-U.S. file. Two sources said the prime minister was clear that Freeland would remain deputy prime minister, and that she said she looked forward to seeing how the proposed new role would be defined.
Another insider said that mischaracterizes the conversation. They say Freeland made clear she believed Trump's threat to impose tariffs on Canadian imports needed to be strategically addressed, that she knew Scott Bessent, Trump's pick for Treasury secretary who would be her counterpart, and she expected to lead the response from her position as finance minister.
Over the course of the week, there were other conversations between their offices, the sources said. The insiders disagree about whether Freeland was told she would additionally take on the title of intergovernmental affairs minister because of the need to co-ordinate with the premiers.
On the prime minister's side, he and his team viewed the new role not as a demotion - as Freeland's letter later portrayed it - but rather as a position Freeland was most suited for, having led Canada's response to the NAFTA renegotiations and steel and aluminum tariffs during Trump's first administration. The fact that Trump doesn't like her was not seen as a drawback, they said.
Yet sources close to Freeland say she was angered by the offer of an undefined role with no staff, separate department, or statutory authority to levy tariffs against the U.S., which reside in law with the finance minister.
"That's bulls---," countered one of the sources familiar with their discussions. That source said Freeland would have directed Canada's response, including how to wield the authorities of the departments of Global Affairs and Finance, with all the resources of the Privy Council Office, a department and central agency that as deputy prime minister she would continue to have full access to.
On Wednesday, Dec. 11, the prime minister and his office put the kibosh on the $12 billion in cuts she proposed. A source close to Trudeau said she had pitched the package only about 10 days before, and it was not thoroughly thought through. A source close to Freeland said it had been "many weeks" in the offing, and she'd advocated it as part of Canada's readiness for the fallout of a future trade war.
By Friday, Dec. 13, it became clear during a video conference call - with Freeland in Toronto and Trudeau in Vancouver - that they were far apart on their understanding of what the other wanted.
According to Freeland's resignation letter, Trudeau told her then that Carney would become his finance minister after the economic statement she was to deliver the following Monday.
It was the first time, according to sources close to Freeland, that Trudeau explicitly told her she'd be out of that portfolio.
Freeland told Trudeau she did not want to go along with that, and the call ended badly, say both sides.
Freeland and Trudeau did not speak again. There were additional conversations between their senior staff members, but it was clear to some that Carney had over the weekend decided he, too, was out.
By Monday, Dec. 16, it was all over.
On the day she was to deliver the Liberal government's long-awaited fall fiscal update, Freeland phoned Trudeau just as he was arriving at Parliament Hill to lead a cabinet meeting.
Shortly after their call, Freeland posted her damning letter on X, accusing Trudeau of wanting to fire her because she had argued against blowing the budget on "costly political gimmicks."
She portrayed Trudeau as undermining Canada's fiscal capacity to respond to a looming tariff war with the Trump administration.
Several insiders say the prime minister was shocked and devastated. Trudeau immediately believed it was a blow that would make it impossible for him to stay on, said three sources.
One said the prime minister went into a dark place. Chaos reigned behind closed doors.
The media lock-up for what is essentially a mini-budget hadn't started, but the stock markets and the Canadian dollar dipped upon news of Freeland's resignation.
The PMO had already understood Carney was also no longer on board, and Trudeau named LeBlanc to replace Freeland. The prime minister was persuaded to take time to reflect, and to not make any sudden decisions.
The government determined the economic update - which revealed a $60-billion deficit - had to be released as scheduled. (Much of the eye-popping deficit was ascribed to liabilities for Indigenous legal cases, in part at Freeland's insistence.)
Carney's team says that in the 24 hours after Freeland's announcement, he made clear to the Trudeau government he was out.
A source close to Carney said he had originally agreed in December to be part of a bigger governing team that was going to help Canada navigate the economic peril posed by Trump's incoming administration. That source declined to say whether Carney had agreed specifically to take on the finance minister's job, which multiple sources confirmed Trudeau had asked him to fill.
In Carney's mind, Freeland had been an integral part of the team, and with that clearly no longer the case, he was not willing to sign on, the source said.
It was a colossal mess, one Trudeau could not recover from. He was now a fighter not just on the ropes, but down for the count.
CAPTION: Prime Minister Justin Trudeau makes an announcement of his pending resignation outside Rideau Cottage in Ottawa on Jan.6, 2025.

Subject: Scandals; Politics; Books; Prime ministers; Pandemics; Tariffs

Document 378 of 500

Is it good for Pierre Poilievre if Donald Trump likes him - or very, very bad?

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/is-good-pierre-poilievre-if-donald-trump-likes/docview/3154140617/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Is+it+good+for+Pierre+Poilievre+if+Donald+Trump+likes+him+-+or+very%2C+very+bad%3F&amp;title=The+Hamilton+Spectator+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2025-01-11&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Hamilton+Spectator+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: In a recurring feature, Susan Delacourt, a small-l liberal, and Matt Gurney, a small-c conservative, bring their different perspectives - and shared commitment to civil disagreement - to the political debates of the moment.
Susan Delacourt: Any other week, Matt, we probably would have been talking about Justin Trudeau's resignation last Monday. But Donald Trump has walked all over that conversation with his threats to force Canada into a merger with the United States. I actually can't believe I just wrote that last sentence. So let's talk about what he has done to throw even more chaos into a chaotic political time for Canada.
Matt Gurney: He's responded rationally to our weakness. I don't like what Trump's doing. I'd wish he'd stop. And I don't like what it tells us about the world that I'm not surprised at all by any of it. But that's my honest answer. He's sensed political weakness on our part, and since he's not the kind of guy who's bound by sentimentality or even just a traditional sense of how a U.S. president should behave, he's pouncing on us. It's awful. But it'll be our new reality until and unless he gets distracted.
SD: I'll tell you what leaped out at me - something that hasn't been discussed as widely as his threats. It was when Trump, in a radio interview, finally acknowledged Pierre Poilievre as the next likely prime minister of Canada. It wasn't exactly an endorsement; in fact, Trump first said that the Conservative leader might not win. But then, this: "It would be very good. Our views would be more aligned, certainly." Something tells me that's not great news for Poilievre.
MG: This will be a short reply, but ... why? I don't think it means anything for Poilievre.
SD: Oh, I disagree. On the list of endorsements you don't want in Canada, I'd say one from Trump tops the list.
MG: You know, I'm old enough to remember a time, and it wasn't that long ago, when a lot of otherwise smart Liberals pinned their hopes of some kind of polling turnaround on the idea that a Trump victory would scare Canadians back in line behind Trudeau. And then a funny thing happened - Trump won, and since then the Conservatives have gone up even more in the polls. Freeland bailed, and a tearful Trudeau has announced he'll bail, too - eventually. The poll aggregator 338Canada has the Conservatives with a majority so large that leftover Tories could themselves form the second-largest party in the House without dropping the CPC to a minority. I think we need to get to a national place of acceptance with the fact that an idle comment by Trump probably isn't going to knock 60 seats out of reach for Poilievre.
SD: Hey, I'm old enough to remember when Justin Trudeau was planning on sticking around for the next election. Seriously, and I would say this, but no matter what the polls say, Canada is by and large a progressive country. And if Trump is going to kick off his next term by beating up on Canada, I firmly believe Canadians are going to be really ticked with him, very quickly. I think they may get really curious about what Trump means about being aligned with Poilievre.
MG: I don't. I don't know if there is a single voter who is currently planning to vote for Poilievre who is going to change their mind because of something Trump says. And I especially don't think there are so many of those voters out there that they are going to form a critical mass in enough ridings to matter. Again, the only careers in Canadian politics that have been negatively affected by Trump's reelection thus far have been the Liberal now-outgoing prime minister and the Liberal former deputy prime minister.
SD: So, I don't think I've asked you this before, but how do you think Poilievre would fare against Trump, especially this belligerent one?
MG: I've spent a good long few minutes staring at that question, and I admit I have no idea how to answer it. I don't think it's possible to answer. Do we mean politically? I think he'll "fare" fine against Trump. When Poilievre eventually loses, it'll be because of domestic stuff, as ever. I think Trump will cause headaches and probably outright nightmares for a Poilievre government, as he has for the Trudeau one. I'm not trying to give a vague answer, I just don't even know how to begin to approach the question.
SD: You don't have to get used to that question, but Poilievre had better. I would really like to know what he would do when Trump starts goading and teasing him. Say what you like about Trudeau - and God knows, everyone is - he had remarkable self-discipline in dealing with the taunts, gained from a lifetime of experience. Poilievre has a tendency to be thin-skinned and to double down on the outrageous. These aren't good skills for dealing with Trump.
MG: Well, OK, but that's a different question. "What he'll do" is easier to answer than how he'll fare - I dunno how well any of us are going to fare! We already see signs of it. He'll ignore as much as he can and respond with a forceful statement when he has to. He'll probably do as Trudeau did and lean on members of his caucus or staff with ties to the U.S. to work the back channels. I don't know if that'll be enough - I don't know if it could ever be enough - but I think that's what'll happen.
SD: Agreed. But I think Poilievre is quickly going to realize he has a new, more formidable rival now, and that's not the person who succeeds Trudeau. It's Trump, and this is a whole different fight for the Conservatives. He can't keep rambling on about how Trudeau got us into this mess with Trump. Because I'm a loyal Canadian, I wish Poilievre well if he does end up being the man to handle this chaos. But I hope it makes the Conservative leader stop trash-talking Canada in the face of these Trump insults. That would be one tiny silver lining in what is looking like an even crazier time for Canadian politics in the coming weeks.
MG: I think Poilievre has been better at sensing both opportunity and danger than the prime minister has been in a long time. That'll wear off as he spends time in office. It always does. I hope it lasts long enough. Maybe until the midterms?
CAPTION: Conservative Leader Pierre Poilievre leaves a news conference on Parliament Hill onDec. 20, 2024.

Subject: Conservatism; Politics; Prime ministers

Location: United States--US; Canada

People: Trump, Donald J; Gurney, Matt; Poilievre, Pierre; Trudeau, Justin

Document 379 of 500

We can now stop blaming Trudeau

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/we-can-now-stop-blaming-trudeau/docview/3154083738/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=unknown&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=We+can+now+stop+blaming+Trudeau&amp;title=The+Spectator&amp;issn=11899417&amp;date=2025-01-11&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.16&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Spectator&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: What a relief that Justin Trudeau is finally heading off into the sunset.
Unlike his many critics, I don't curse his name. I'm just tired of the infantile name-calling and vile flags that will soon become redundant.
Countless Canadians blame Trudeau for a series of problems without looking at how his team kept groceries and masks readily available during a health crisis. In a recent Toronto Star commentary, David Olive also summarized how the Liberals reduced poverty and grew the economy.
Yet, it's so much easier to blame someone on Parliament Hill for all our ills.
Principally, people have been fixated on how housing is seemingly harder to secure than ever. Since things became more dire in recent years, it's simple to blame the most recent government.
As someone who has sounded the alert - and created affordable housing - for years, I assure you there are many players who should hang their heads. Yes, a national housing strategy would have helped but remember our prime minister didn't start one either.
Why do we see so many people unhoused?
Let's look at the main culprits:
The province which refuses to increase Ontario Works and Ontario Disability Support Program payments. What single person can live on $733 a month? A single parent receives up to $1,218 while a couple with two children gets $1,795. Do the math. It's impossible.
Ontario's Health Services Restructuring Commission recommendation to divest psychiatric care to public hospitals. When you see people having a mental health crisis on the street, that's the result of this decision. After couch surfing with family and friends, these individuals often end up unhoused without the supports they need.
Opioid manufacturers and suppliers who made the situation worse by adding a new layer of addiction for those dying to tame their demons or manage their pain.
NIMBY neighbours who oppose affordable housing projects drive up legal costs and building costs due to delays. In some cases, they force the reduction in the number of units offered as a compromise, leaving more families with nowhere to go.
Bad tenants make life as property owners unbearable. The damage and drama they cause led to countless property owners to cut and run when the real estate market was hot.
The province made it easier to drive up rents in new buildings in the past four years. In a bid to support these buildings' owners' bottom line, they pushed out tenants who had signed on to leases they could afford.
Inflationary forces and supply chains that make it harder to build housing and get your money back. These days, it costs $450 per square foot. Next time you think a new building should offer cheaper rent, work with that number in mind.
People living in homes with four bedrooms when they only need one. That's a lot of space to hang onto in case your adult kids come home for a visit.
People who bought overpriced houses and drove up costs. And those who build monster homes that eat up more space than needed.
Big corporations who seize the opportunity to buy up apartments and hike up rents, plus the laws that allow this to happen.
Finally, the public who oversimplify this issue and point fingers in one direction.
On a positive note, thank you to all the fair and reasonable landlords who do not take advantage of the current dynamics. You offer some stability at a time when we truly need it. Sorry if all this logic makes your next anti-Trudeau rant more complicated. Actually, I'm not sorry at all.
Lois Tuffin is a former
editor-in-chief of
Peterborough ThisWeek.
CAPTION:

CREDIT: Lois Tuffin;

Subject: Affordable housing

Business indexing term: Subject: Affordable housing

Quebec energy deal gets green light in N.L.: Tories walk out of the vote as demands for review denied

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/quebec-energy-deal-gets-green-light-n-l/docview/3154083646/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Quebec+energy+deal+gets+green+light+in+N.L.%3A+Tories+walk+out+of+the+vote+as+demands+for+review+denied&amp;title=The+Spectator&amp;issn=11899417&amp;date=2025-01-11&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.18&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Spectator&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: A dramatic vote ended with the Newfoundland and Labrador government deciding to move forward with a draft energy deal with Quebec heralded as a game-changer for the indebted Atlantic province.
The Opposition Progressive Conservatives walked out of the legislature in protest, without casting a vote. The party had repeatedly called for the tentative agreement to be reviewed before a decision, but their demands were denied.
"People are demanding more than just rhetoric that this agreement changes everything," Tory Leader Tony Wakeham said before his 13 fellow party members stood up and left Thursday evening.
Meanwhile, Liberal Premier Andrew Furey got a standing ovation from his caucus as he gave a speech before the vote. "We have waited over 50 years," he told the legislature. "Our position has never been stronger. We know it. Quebec knows it."
The government opened the House of Assembly on Monday for four days of debate about the memorandum of understanding unveiled Dec. 12 between Hydro-Québec and Newfoundland and Labrador Hydro. The agreement promises about $227 billion in revenue to the Newfoundland and Labrador treasury, much of it coming from 50 years of new rates Hydro-Québec will pay for power from the Churchill Falls plant in Labrador.
The tentative agreement doesn't just promise new revenue, it promises an end to a bitterness and sense of injustice stemming from a 1969 contract that heavily favoured Quebec. Under the old agreement, Hydro-Québec purchased about 15 per cent of its energy from the Churchill Falls plant at prices far below market value. Under the new scenario, Hydro-Québec will pay about 30 times more for power, netting Newfoundland and Labrador an average of $1 billion a year until 2041, and $4 billion a year after 2056.
Quebec's provincially owned hydro utility will also pay Newfoundland and Labrador Hydro a $3.5-billion fee for the right to co-develop two more energy projects on the Churchill River. Newfoundland and Labrador Hydro will be the majority owner of these developments, and Hydro-Québec will absorb any cost overruns.
Newfoundland and Labrador Hydro gets to keep more than one-third of the $3.5-billion fee, whether the projects go ahead or not.
The province's total budget this year is about $10.4 billion, and the province is carrying a net debt of about $17.7 billion.
Jennifer Williams, CEO of Newfoundland and Labrador Hydro, received a standing ovation after reading out a long list of benefits offered in the deal.
"Fairness. Which did not exist in the original contract," she said. "We are taking back control of that river. And this is the best deal."
Sean Cadigan, a history professor at Memorial University in St. John's, said he was "flabbergasted" when the two provinces announced they'd worked together to come to a new arrangement.
"The perceived sense of grievance over the 1969 Churchill Falls development has been profound in Newfoundland and Labrador," Cadigan said. "This is a historic occasion."
The provincial NDP threw their support behind the arrangement, alongside the province's two Independents. They made the decision after the Liberals announced an independent panel led by the province's consumer advocate will review the memorandum of understanding and subsequent negotiations. Firms including JP Morgan and Power Advisory have also provided advice, and their representatives testified on Wednesday.
The Progressive Conservatives asked several times throughout debate for the vote to be delayed until that scrutiny is complete. The party occupies 14 of the legislature's 40 seats.
The Tories brought up historical friction with Quebec. On Tuesday, Wakeham held up a map from the Quebec government showing the boundary between the provinces in Labrador was disputed. Quebec has never recognized the 1927 border ruling by the British Privy Council, which says the southern border of Labrador lies at the 52nd parallel north of the equator.
"That boundary is final, non-negotiable and it will be forever," Furey responded.
Compared with the cost of energy from other sources, the rates are indeed cheaper, Furey told reporters on Wednesday. "If it wasn't a good alternative for them, why would they do a deal with us?"
Jason Chee-Aloy, managing director with the Toronto-based Power Advisory, said the tentative deal is "very good" for Newfoundland and Labrador, and it reflects lessons learned from past wrongs.
Most people in the Canadian energy sector know the hydroelectric history of Newfoundland and Labrador and Quebec, he told reporters on Wednesday. "They understand how complex and how big this deal is," he said. "They're very impressed that it actually got to the point of a memorandum of understanding."
The Canadian Press
CAPTION:

CREDIT: Sarah Smellie The Canadian Press

Joly off to Washington to talk tariffs: 'Canada has a deep affection for the U.S. ... but also, we have leverage,' foreign affairs minister says

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/joly-off-washington-talk-tariffs/docview/3154083516/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Joly+off+to+Washington+to+talk+tariffs%3A+%27Canada+has+a+deep+affection+for+the+U.S.+...+but+also%2C+we+have+leverage%2C%27+foreign+affairs+minister+says&amp;title=The+Spectator&amp;issn=11899417&amp;date=2025-01-11&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.11&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Spectator&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Foreign Affairs Minister Mélanie Joly will head to Washington next week as part of her government's efforts to press the incoming Trump administration not to impose damaging tariffs on Canada.
President-elect Donald Trump has threatened to impose 25 per cent across-the-board tariffs on imports from Canada and Mexico when he is inaugurated later this month.
Joly's office did not say exactly when she's departing or whether other ministers will accompany her, and did not name the officials she'll be meeting with in Washington.
"We have to be ready," Joly told reporters Friday morning on Parliament Hill, ahead of a meeting of ministers on the Canada-U. S. cabinet committee to discuss the retaliatory measures Ottawa would deploy if those tariffs are applied.
"We need to take him very seriously and we need to show the American people, and folks and people around Trump, that there will be real consequences. That Canada has a deep affection for the U.S., has a strong relationship, is its closest ally - but also, we have leverage," she said.
Joly said she spoke Friday morning with U.S. Sen. Lindsey Graham, a key Republican. She said she speaks with him "pretty much every day" and met with him in Florida in December.
She said she also has met with Trump's likely cabinet members, including her probable future counterpart Marco Rubio, border chief nominee Tom Holman, commerce secretary nominee Howard Lutnick and Doug Burgum, likely to be named energy secretary. She said she's had "contacts with" Mike Waltz, likely to be named national security adviser.
"These folks are starting officially on Jan. 20, but the reality is, their action plan is already ready," Joly said, adding that Trump is "a seasoned decision-maker" who has a loyal team made up of officials who might restrain him less than those in his first administration.
She said Canada needs to be focused as it prepares to welcome Trump and other world leaders to the G7 summit in Alberta in June, adding Canada's allies are "concerned" about his administration.
"My job, along with the prime minister, is to make sure that these meetings go well and that we play a leadership role," she said.
Next week, Prime Minister Justin Trudeau and the premiers will meet in Ottawa to discuss Canada's plan for responding to tariffs, a plan which is expected to include retaliatory measures.
The premiers are also planning their own trip to Washington on Feb. 12, led by Ontario Premier Doug Ford. His office has been asked which premiers will be going.
Joly also announced Friday she will not run for the Liberal leadership because it would distract from her ministerial role during what she calls "a crucial time in the Canada-U. S. relationship." She said she had "good chances" of winning the leadership.
The Canadian Press
CAPTION:

NDP commits to Grimsby GO station, double track to Niagara Falls

Full text: The Ontario NDP wants the proposed GO station on Casablanca Boulevard in Grimsby up and running sooner than later.
"New Democrats are actually going to get that project moving," said party Leader Marit Stiles following a visit to the GO station site Friday morning.
"We know that's been announced multiple times by both Conservatives and Liberals, but nothing has been done; we're committed to getting that open and going in providing reliable transit, the GO service that is going to be absolutely essential for a region that is growing as fast as Niagara."
Stiles said the provincial NDP is also committed to adding a second track from Grimsby to Niagara Falls to boost two-way GO train service, but did not provide any further details.
"We are absolutely committed to that, we believe that's got to be a priority," Stiles said. "I (am) hoping to make another announcement about that in the future."
On Thursday night, former Pelham mayor Dave Augustyn was again selected the Ontario NDP candidate for Niagara West.

Letters

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/letters/docview/3153467141/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=unknown&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Letters&amp;title=The+Spectator&amp;issn=11899417&amp;date=2025-01-10&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.13&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Spectator&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: This project is for the birds
Re: Chimney swift habitat still standing as homes are demolished for Hamilton LRT, Jan. 7
I'll make this short and swift. Since when did birds become more important than people?
Pete Demik, Hannon
Constant thefts hard to endure
My mentally challenged son moved into assisted living 17 years ago. A great new building on Main Street West across from the Wendy's and Fortinos. I couldn't have been more pleased as the area was safe and well kept and most of all he was close to a Tim Hortons, his favourite place to go each morning. I could relax and know my son was safe and happy. Staff in the building always did their best for our guys.
Fast forward to 2025. Over this span of time, lets say in the last eight years things in society have changed. It is no longer the friendly, safe location it was. He has had four bikes (that he says he "drives" each day) stolen. I've given up on good bikes anymore and buy used.
Today he had his backpack stolen off a bench in Fortinos while he used the washroom. How do you explain to a special needs 45-year-old that he can't trust anyone? He loves talking to people and I've tried to explain not everyone who's friendly can be trusted.
What he lost was worthless to others. His old glasses, a toy truck he got for Christmas, a water bottle and two packs of gum; things he treasured. The bag is probably in a dumpster as it contained no money - just junk to a stranger but meant a lot to him.
It's a sad day when stealing is an everyday event in our lives now. Whether it's your car or a bike or a backpack, it is a sad day that we live in when you can't trust anyone or anything to be safe. Welcome to 2025.
Brenda Stricker, Jerseyville
Curbside wisdom appreciated
This morning being garbage day, the ritual is: garbage, plus bottles of water for those who do the collection jobs. Most times, the people who the water is meant for, get the water. There are times, though, when the water disappears, collected by others. This morning some of the water disappeared and when the blue box person was putting the box back, I was there grumbling about the missing water. Looking at me, eyeball to eyeball, he said, "It's all good!" Then, still face to face, he repeated, "It's all good!" Whoa! Yes, message received, lesson learned? I hope so. Today, for me, it's a double thank you to the blue box people!
Janet Ryding, Hamilton
Are road tolls
the end goal?
I have to take issue with our city's transportation managers' justification to spend $26 million to convert Main Street to two-way in response to a "spate" of pedestrian deaths in 2022. My research from the annual police accident reports shows two pedestrian deaths for the five years between 2017 to 2021. Unfortunately, the spate referred to were two in 2022 and l did not find any for the last two years. The idealogues on this council are hell bent with every decision to make driving in the lower city as difficult as possible. Maybe when the downtown gets so clogged up to get through with an added bike lane on Main Street and when King Street loses lanes to accommodate the unnecessary LRT they will get their wish and start charging a road toll a la London and New York City.
Tom Winning, Hamilton
A truly fowl scheme
With Donald Trump threatening to annex Canada, perhaps it's time the defence department implemented the nation's greatest resource; the Canada goose. Tens of thousands of trained geese with bulging bellies could fly over Florida, and in unison drop their payload on Mar-a-Lago. Talk about carpet bombing.
Ellie Chase, Hamilton
Will NATO
stand for Canada?
I'm just curious, if Donald Trump decides to invade Canada, does NATO declare war against the United States? And will they have to buy weapons from America to do so?
Buddy Winston, Hamilton
City's rain tax
is all wet
Re: Hamilton MPP Skelly seeks provincial storm fee
exemption for farmers, Jan. 2
The next foolish piece of theft is a tax on water falling from the sky. Why on God's green earth should a rural resident with no water or sewer service pay a tax on run off when almost 80 per cent of the little parcel we call home is grass and absorbs all run off?
I will never have these services and am getting rather sick of bailing out the poor decisions made by these left-wing lunatics spending millions on the likes of tiny homes!
Rob Montgomery, Hannon
Columnists
ease my mind
The three commentators on the Wednesday comment page have somewhat eased my mind, as far as the next elections in Canada are concerned. With a worthier contender in sight, perhaps Canadians will not follow the United States and choose a leader who will be "bellowing insults at his political rivals in Parliament," as pointed out by Craig Wallace. Perhaps Canadians will not vote for a leader that was chumming around with the MAGA supporters during the protests in Ottawa. As David Olive wrote, "the greatest damage to the Liberal brand was inflicted by their we-know-best arrogance." None of which changes the fact that, "on average, Canadians are better off than we were in 2015." And as Gwynne Dyer points out, "the ideological rants" of Poilievre are not so much the Canadian style. I do hope that he is right.
Gudrun Boehm-Johnson,
Ancaster
Seeking a 'just peace' for Ukraine
Re: Can Trump
swing a peace deal? Jan. 7
The challenge for Ukraine, and the real question for Volodymyr Zelenskyy, is how to guarantee future security for their country in the face of Russian aggression and fickle allies. The goal is a "just peace" not a "fair deal." No deal which concedes Ukrainian territory can be described as "fair." It may be unavoidable, it may be "better than nothing," it will certainly be made under the shadow of future violence, but it will definitely not be "fair."
Steve Dylag, Burlington
Don't let Ford privatize health
I have received a number of messages about the Ford government's determination to privatize Ontario health care. This is a serious and genuine concern to me.
Is there anything positive for the citizens of Ontario from privatizing our health care? Would we get better health care? Would privatized health care be equally available to all Ontarians? Is privatization the most effective way to deliver health care rather than supporting our proven (and paid for with taxes) existing health-care system?
What right does one person have to unilaterally and surreptitiously change a proven and effective health-care system? Has anyone seen a study or investigation that shows that a private-care system is better for all Ontarians? Perhaps we could ask our neighbours to the south.
What is the next target for privatization? Main Street, the fire department, side walks? Highway 407 was privatized, how much did we benefit from that change and how do we feel about it now?
The most effective way to avoid this selfish and ill thought-out move is at the ballot box. Vote the Progressive Conservatives out.
Roger Rose, Mount Hope

Document 384 of 500

Jan. 10: Constant thefts hard to take, city's rain tax is all wet and other letters to the editor

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/jan-10-constant-thefts-hard-take-citys-rain-tax/docview/3153464142/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Jan.+10%3A+Constant+thefts+hard+to+take%2C+city%27s+rain+tax+is+all+wet+and+other+letters+to+the+editor&amp;title=The+Hamilton+Spectator+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2025-01-10&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Hamilton+Spectator+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Constant thefts hard to take
My mentally challenged son moved into assisted living 17 years ago. A great new building on Main Street West across from the Wendy's and Fortinos. I couldn't have been more pleased as the area was safe and well kept and most of all he was close to a Tim Hortons, his favourite place to go each morning. I could relax and know my son was safe and happy. Staff in the building always did their best for our guys.
Fast forward to 2025. Over this span of time, lets say in the last eight years things in society have changed. It is no longer the friendly, safe location it was. He has had four bikes (that he says he "drives" each day) stolen. I've given up on good bikes anymore and buy used.
Today he had his backpack stolen off a bench in Fortinos while he used the washroom. How do you explain to a special needs 45-year-old that he can't trust anyone? He loves talking to people and I've tried to explain not everyone who's friendly can be trusted.
What he lost was worthless to others. His old glasses, a toy truck he got for Christmas, a water bottle and two packs of gum; things he treasured. The bag is probably in a dumpster as it contained no money - just junk to a stranger but meant a lot to him.
It's a sad day when stealing is an everyday event in our lives now. Whether it's your car or a bike or a backpack, it is a sad day that we live in when you can't trust anyone or anything to be safe. Welcome to 2025.
Brenda Stricker, Jerseyville
This project is for the birds
Re: Chimney swift habitat still standing as homes are demolished for Hamilton LRT, Jan. 7
I'll make this short and swift. Since when did birds become more important than people?
Pete Demik, Hannon
Are road tolls the end goal?
I have to take issue with our city's transportation managers' justification to spend $26 million to convert Main Street to two-way in response to a "spate" of pedestrian deaths in 2022. My research from the annual police accident reports shows two pedestrian deaths for the five years between 2017 to 2021. Unfortunately, the spate referred to were two in 2022 and l did not find any for the last two years. The idealogues on this council are hell bent with every decision to make driving in the lower city as difficult as possible. Maybe when the downtown gets so clogged up to get through with an added bike lane on Main Street and when King Street loses lanes to accommodate the unnecessary LRT they will get their wish and start charging a road toll a la London and New York City.
Tom Winning, Hamilton
A truly fowl scheme
With Donald Trump threatening to annex Canada, perhaps it's time the defence department implemented the nation's greatest resource; the Canada goose. Tens of thousands of trained geese with bulging bellies could fly over Florida, and in unison drop their payload on Mar-a-Lago. Talk about carpet bombing.
Ellie Chase, Hamilton
Will NATO stand for Canada?
I'm just curious, if Donald Trump decides to invade Canada, does NATO declare war against the United States? And will they have to buy weapons from America to do so?
Buddy Winston, Hamilton
Curbside wisdom appreciated
This morning being garbage day, the ritual is: garbage, plus bottles of water for those who do the collection jobs. Most times, the people who the water is meant for, get the water. There are times, though, when the water disappears, collected by others. This morning some of the water disappeared and when the blue box person was putting the box back, I was there grumbling about the missing water. Looking at me, eyeball to eyeball, he said, "It's all good!" Then, still face to face, he repeated, "It's all good!" Whoa! Yes, message received, lesson learned? I hope so. Today, for me, it's a double thank you to the blue box people!
Janet Ryding, Hamilton
City's rain tax is all wet
Re: Hamilton MPP Donna Skelly seeks provincial storm fee exemption for farmers, Jan. 2
The next foolish piece of theft is a tax on water falling from the sky. Why on God's green earth should a rural resident with no water or sewer service pay a tax on run off when almost 80 per cent of the little parcel we call home is grass and absorbs all run off?
I will never have these services and am getting rather sick of bailing out the poor decisions made by these left-wing lunatics spending millions on the likes of tiny homes!
Rob Montgomery, Hannon

Steve Dylag, Burlington

It's all just a distraction
The only reason Donald Distraction keeps talking about Panama, Canada and Greenland and renaming the Gulf of Mexico is because the president-elect has no idea on how he will lower grocery and gas prices after inauguration day.
For a guy who campaigned on ending global conflicts, it's odd that he's now talking about creating wars in North America.
Jim McEwen, Bowmanville

Document 385 of 500

Trump clearly hasn't done the math on Canadian annexation

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/trump-clearly-hasnt-done-math-on-canadian/docview/3153463079/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Trump+clearly+hasn%27t+done+the+math+on+Canadian+annexation&amp;title=The+Hamilton+Spectator+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2025-01-10&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Hamilton+Spectator+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: In the Dec. 28 Spectator, Eric Blais argued we need to take Donald Trump jokes seriously.
Their recent repetition seems to imply at least a strong negotiating ploy. Consequently, this column argues that, if we take his jokes seriously, Canada should form not only the 51st state but the 51st to 60th - 10 extra states! I hasten to add, that while there might be some benefits to such a merger (annexation?), I would strongly oppose such a move as would a huge majority of Canadians.
To mention only one reason, why would we want to suffer an outdated system of presidential election (electoral college) where only the votes of people in "swing states" appear to matter? Or why would we seek to be presided over by a president who has ambitions to take over Greenland and the Panama Canal and won't reject the use of the military to do that - à la Putin and Ukraine? In any case, if such a move were to be even considered, it should be recognized that we are, on the basis of population, entitled to an additional 10 states - not one. Let's forget all this talk of Canada as a "51st state."
Ranking by population shows Ontario - our most populous province - would rank fifth in size after New York. Three of our provinces (Ontario, Quebec and B.C.) would rank in top 25. All provinces except P.E.I. would exceed or be similar in population to Wyoming - the state with the smallest population. History would demand that P.E.I. would also deserve statehood but some combination of Maritime provinces could possibly be negotiated. Of course, that ignores our territories which would also have to be considered if Puerto Rico were to be granted statehood.
With 10 additional states, Canada would be entitled to 20 out of 120 senators - or 17 per cent. Moreover, we would rate 49 members of an expanded House of Representatives of 484 members or 10 per cent. If annexation were to occur with our present political expectations, the majority of those additional members would be conservatives. Whether they would operate as a separate Conservative party in Congress or form a moderating anti-MAGA bloc within the Republican party would have to be decided. At any rate, they would have a significant influence in already razor-thin balance in the U.S. Congress.
On the other hand, in the longer run, Canada's votes might produce a longtime "progressive" Democratic majority if Liberal, N.D.P. and Green voters go in that direction. While Donald Trump may not worry unduly about the time "after-Trump," fellow Republicans might be concerned about such an eventuality. And, how about those 11 Representatives from Quebec? How would Americans react if they had a strong separatist Bloc Québécois representation in their midst?
It is moreover obvious that within the 10 additional states, there will be no room for our now departing "governor" Trudeau, since there is no reason to assume that our current premiers would not vie for the job. However, after a successful term as Canada's prime minister, Pierre Poilievre might do very well in any eventual joint Republican presidential primary as a more moderate, non-MAGA candidate. Has Trump considered that? No Mr. Trump, it won't be simply erasing an artificial border line. Ten additional states - not one! You may not like what you wish for.
CAPTION: President-elect Donald Trump talks to reporters after a meeting at the Capitol on Wednesday in Washington. Annexing Canada would complicate the Republican Party's hold on power,John Boersema argues.
CREDIT: John Boersema

Subject: Provinces; Statehood; Political parties

Location: Canada; Quebec Canada

People: Trump, Donald J
