National briefs: Space projects get $17M boost

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/national-briefs/docview/3153462321/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=National+briefs%3A+Space+projects+get+%2417M+boost&amp;title=The+Spectator&amp;issn=11899417&amp;date=2025-01-10&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.7&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Spectator&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Canada
MP Arya joins leadership race
Ontario MP Chandra Arya is the second Liberal to announce he wants to replace Prime Minister Justin Trudeau as leader of the Liberal party. Arya posted on social media Thursday morning that he wants to campaign on running a "small, more efficient government" and on offering "bold political decisions" to solve the country's problems. Arya joins former Montreal MP and businessperson Frank Baylis as the only two declared candidates in the race so far.
Manitoba
Tributes pour in for police chief
A Manitoba police chief believed to have died in a house fire is being remembered as a dedicated leader and an advocate for the safety and well-being of First Nations communities.The Manitoba First Nations Police Service said in a statement Wednesday evening it is believed Chief Doug Palson died in a fire at his home earlier that day.RCMP were called to the home west of Winnipeg in the Rural Municipality of Portage la Prairie, which they confirmed belonged to Palson. RCMP have not confirmed the identity of the deceased.
Space projects get $17M boost
The Manitoba government is spending $17 million in a bid to create more aerospace jobs and training opportunities. The money includes an $8-million grant and a $9-million loan, to be repaid over 12 years, to Magellan Aerospace. Premier Wab Kinew says some of the money will be used to set up a state-of-the-art machining centre and a new testing environment, and 64 new jobs are expected overall.
News services

Trump hasn't done the math on annexation: Canada as 10 states would wield influence

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/trump-hasnt-done-math-on-annexation/docview/3153453652/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=unknown&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Trump+hasn%27t+done+the+math+on+annexation%3A+Canada+as+10+states+would+wield+influence&amp;title=The+Spectator&amp;issn=11899417&amp;date=2025-01-10&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.12&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Spectator&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Document 388 of 500

More than 80,000 people in Ontario were homeless in 2024, report finds: Numbers have jumped 25 per cent compared to two years ago

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/more-than-80-000-people-ontario-were-homeless/docview/3153453640/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=More+than+80%2C000+people+in+Ontario+were+homeless+in+2024%2C+report+finds%3A+Numbers+have+jumped+25+per+cent+compared+to+two+years+ago&amp;title=The+Spectator&amp;issn=11899417&amp;date=2025-01-10&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.7&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Spectator&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: More than 80,000 people in Ontario were homeless last year, a new report from the province's municipalities shows in what is the clearest picture of the issue to date.
And nearly half of those people have lived either in shelters or on the streets for more than six months, or experienced recurrent homelessness over the past three years, the Association of Municipalities of Ontario found in its report examining the human and financial cost of the province's homelessness crisis.
The association, which represents 444 municipalities across Ontario, said a fundamentally different approach is needed to tackle the crisis, one that prioritizes long-term housing solutions rather than temporary measures or policing solutions.
That would require an $11-billion investment over 10 years to create more than 75,000 affordable and supportive housing units. The municipalities also said some $2 billion over eight years is needed to ensure all people living in encampments are properly housed.
"Those numbers are shocking," said Robin Jones, president of the association and the mayor of Westport, north of Kingston.
The association pulled data from the province's 47 service managers who deal with social housing and homelessness.
There are now 25 per cent more people living in shelters or on the streets compared to two years ago, the report found.
AMO policy director Lindsay Jones said they knew the problem was widespread, but still found the total number of homeless people to be "staggering."
"To put the numbers in context, that's about the same size as the city of Peterborough," she said in an interview. "Imagine everyone in Peterborough being homeless or everybody in Sault Ste. Marie being homeless, that's the scale of what we're talking about."
Ontario Premier Doug Ford said he found the number of homeless people provincewide "concerning."
"I've never seen anything like it," he said. "But we need to support these people, get them back on their feet, get them a good paying job and we need to build more homes."
He also said the province is spending some $700 million on Toronto alone for homelessness prevention.
The Canadian Press
CAPTION: An $11-billion investment is needed over 10 years to create more than 75,000 affordable and supportive housing units, the Association of Municipalities of Ontario says.Chris Young The Canadian Press

CREDIT: Liam Casey The Canadian Press

Subject: Homeless people

Title: More than 80,000 people in Ontario were homeless in 2024, report finds:   Numbers have jumped 25 per cent compared to two years ago

Publication title: The Spectator; Hamilton, Ont.

First page: A.7

Document 389 of 500

Ford needs to hold off on early election

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/ford-needs-hold-off-on-early-election/docview/3152880775/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=unknown&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Ford+needs+to+hold+off+on+early+election&amp;title=The+Spectator&amp;issn=11899417&amp;date=2025-01-09&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.8&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Spectator&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Don't do it, Doug Ford. Don't give us double electoral trouble.
It's not that the province necessarily needs you to stick around. It's that you need not - dare not - stick it to Ontarians by saddling us with another political campaign at the provincial level, while federal leadership and election races are underway over the next few months.
At the very moment that our American neighbours are taking their best shots at us, in both tariffs and tirades, the federal government is now paralyzed by Justin Trudeau's resignation odyssey. An early election call in Ontario would compound the uncertainty amid the upheaval of a trade war.
A newly empowered U.S. President Donald Trump would be dealing not only with a disembowelled prime minister on Parliament Hill, but a disempowered government at Queen's Park. With America on the attack, we'd be defended by a lame duck prime minister in Ottawa and a caretaker premier in Ontario - leaving the country's biggest and second-biggest governments in limbo while Ford cavorts on the campaign trail.
Yet still, the premier refuses to rule out an early election.
Ford persists in ruminating about going to the polls prematurely so as to lock in his current lead in public opinion polls, lest he lose ground in any future economic downturn or face awkward questions from a police probe into his Greenbelt land swap fiasco. Since last spring, his Tories have obsessed over an early election footing that defies convention and, now, common sense.
Back then, when this early election notion first surfaced, a second Trump presidency seemed pure fantasy and the spectre of a tariff war seemed unfathomable. Today, more than a year ahead of the scheduled June 2026 vote that is prescribed by Ontario's fixed election date law, Ford is still putting his personal ambition ahead of the tradition of four-year mandates.
Less than three years after winning the June 2022 election, the premier keeps conflating campaigning with governing.
On the one hand, he is making the most of Trudeau's troubles by insinuating himself into the Canada-U.S. mix, describing his fellow premiers as critical players who demand a seat at the bilateral table. Yet in the same breath, Ford can't bring himself to declare, clearly, that this is no time to put his personal and political self-interest over the provincial and national interest.
Ontarians are about to receive $200 cheques in the mail as part of the premier's thinly veiled pre-election vote-buying scheme, at a cost of more than $3 billion to the deficit-ridden, heavily indebted provincial treasury. The premier had planned to cash in on the political dividends from that voter giveaway. And yet, a truly responsible and prudent premier would tell reporters that he has instructed his campaign team to stand down until further notice.
But Ford won't say what needs to be said. Given the chance by reporters, repeatedly, to clear the air this week after Trudeau's announcement, the premier left the door wide open to more electoral tumult when Canada least needs it.
"What I can tell you today is I'm 100 per cent focused on the tariffs," he began. "I can tell you, my eye hasn't been taken off the ball for a second here. We've been leading the way - all the premiers."
Ford likes to remind people that he holds the rotating chair of the premiers' association, implying this makes him indispensable, not to mention electable. But why add campaigning to the responsibilities of governing when Ottawa is already sidelined?
"This is when all Canadians step up, this is when our provincial and territorial leaders step up."
Step up - not step aside and onto the campaign trail. We don't need two of our governments in limbo while the U.S. government lashes out.
Time to clear the air, premier. Time to declare that you'll stick to the fixed election law - with a fixed focus on our economic challenges, not your political ambitions.
Martin Regg Cohn covers provincial politics for Torstar.
CAPTION:

CREDIT: Martin Regg cohn OPINION Martin Regg Cohn covers provincial politics for Torstar.;

national briefs

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/national-briefs/docview/3152878858/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=national+briefs&amp;title=The+Spectator&amp;issn=11899417&amp;date=2025-01-09&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.11&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Spectator&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Ontario
Ford pitches energy plan
Ontario Premier Doug Ford is pitching U.S. president-elect Donald Trump on a vision to supply the two nations with energy, as he positions himself as the country's protector amid a "lack of leadership" in Ottawa in the face of American tariff threats. Ford wants to see the province's grid expanded to allow more energy to be sent to the U.S. The premier is trumpeting Ontario's nuclear energy expertise to build more large and small modular nuclear reactors in partnership with the U.S. "We owe it to workers in Ontario, across Canada and the U.S. to act now," Ford said.
British Columbia
Mounties seize illicit cigarettes
Mounties in British Columbia say officers have seized more than $1.7 million worth of contraband tobacco after executing a search warrant at a property in Langford. West Shore RCMP say they seized about 17,100 cartons, containing more than 171,000 packs of contraband cigarettes. Todd Preston, superintendent in charge of West Shore RCMP, said it was the "largest seizure of illicit tobacco" by the department to date.
Manitoba
Man dies in Winnipeg ER
Health officials are investigating a death at a Winnipeg hospital. Officials at the Health Sciences Centre say a middle-aged man arrived shortly after midnight Tuesday morning at the hospital's emergency department, was triaged as a lower-acuity case and directed to the waiting room, where he later died. The hospital's chief operating officer, Dr. Shawn Young, said the emergency room was busy at the time, but not at an abnormal level.

Document 391 of 500

Ontario launches new rebates to make homes more efficient: Small businesses can also take advantage of Peak Perks plan

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/ontario-launches-new-rebates-make-homes-more/docview/3152878562/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Ontario+launches+new+rebates+to+make+homes+more+efficient%3A+Small+businesses+can+also+take+advantage+of+Peak+Perks+plan&amp;title=The+Spectator&amp;issn=11899417&amp;date=2025-01-09&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.14&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Spectator&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Ontario is introducing two new energy efficiency programs, including one offering rebates for certain home improvements.
The Home Renovation Savings Program, launching Jan. 28, will rebate homeowners up to 30 per cent of the cost of energy efficiency renovations and improvements, such as new windows, doors, insulation, air sealing, smart thermostats, heat pumps, and rooftop solar panels and battery storage systems.
The province is also expanding the residential Peak Perks program to small businesses, giving them a $75 virtual prepaid credit card for each eligible smart thermostat connected to a central air conditioning system or heat pump unit, and another $20 for each additional year in the program.
Energy Minister Stephen Lecce said the home renovation program will mean both upfront and long-term savings for Ontarians.
"A family looking to keep themselves warm over the winter by upgrading their insulation, their windows and doors, they'd receive a rebate of up to $8,900 for installation, and $100 for every door, for every window that they will replace," he said at a news conference.
"Take for example a homeowner that invests $15,000 in a new heat pump. They could see a $4,500 rebate up front, while benefiting from an ongoing savings of up to 50 per cent on their heating portion of the electricity bill."
The home renovation program is similar to one the Progressive Conservative government cancelled in 2018, though Lecce says it is different because more homeowners will qualify and the new program has a longer commitment.
The government says that under the new energy efficiency plan involving the two new and 12 continuing programs, spending will be about $900 million per year on average over 12 years, compared to an annual budget of $342 million on average over the past 13 years.
The new home renovation program and the new Peak Perks for small business program will cost about $60 million and $18 million per year, respectively, for the first few years, the government said.
In response to projections from the Independent Electricity System Operator showing electricity demand sharply rising over the next few decades, the province is looking to build new power generation, but Lecce also touted an electricity sector maxim that the cheapest source of generation is conservation - generation that doesn't need to get built at all.
"Not only are these programs going to help put more money in your pockets, it is also a crucial investment in the future of our grid," he said.
"With greater pressure being placed on the province's grid, energy efficiency programs are an opportunity to mitigate demand without compromising affordability."
Green Party Leader Mike Schreiner said energy efficiency programs like the ones announced this week are great, and they would have been even better if they hadn't been cancelled six years ago.
"It's unfortunate that people have had to wait six long years for this government to act because home retrofits like heat pumps, solar panels or even just new windows and doors help Ontarians save money every single day," Schreiner wrote in a statement.
The Canadian Press

Document 392 of 500

Is Donald Trump kidding?: Americans in Canada react to tariff, annexation threats

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/is-donald-trump-kidding/docview/3152878555/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Is+Donald+Trump+kidding%3F%3A+Americans+in+Canada+react+to+tariff%2C+annexation+threats&amp;title=The+Spectator&amp;issn=11899417&amp;date=2025-01-09&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.12&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Spectator&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Incoming U.S. president Donald Trump has been courting controversy in Canada since his election victory, with threats to impose whopping tariffs on Canadian goods and musings about the country becoming "the 51st state."
While Trump's comments have drawn anger and fear among Canadians, reaction from Americans who live, study or work in Canada has been mixed - and largely influenced by how they voted in the presidential election.
Jacob Wesoky, the executive vice chair of Democrats Abroad Canada who is studying political science at McGill University in Montreal, said he is "extremely" disappointed by the president-elect's approach to his country's "closest" ally.
"As an American living in Canada, it is really sad to watch," said Wesoky, who voted for Trump's rival Kamala Harris.
But Canadian American Georganne Burke, a staunch Trump supporter, said that while the incoming president may be "a bit of a troll," she doesn't see his actions as harmful to Canada.
"He's not asking for anything unreasonable," she said.
Trump's first shot at Canada came in November, when he said he would impose a 25 per cent tariff on all Canadian and Mexican imports as soon as he returns to the White House on Jan. 20 - unless the two countries improve security along their borders with the U.S.
After outgoing Prime Minister Justin Trudeau met with Trump in Florida to discuss the tariff threat, the president-elect started pitching the idea of Canada becoming the next U.S. state, trolling Trudeau by calling him "governor" of the "great state of Canada" in social media posts.
The prime minister's announcement Monday that he would step down after the Liberal party elects a new leader only seemed to further embolden Trump, who claimed on his Truth Social platform that "many people in Canada LOVE being the 51st state."
What started out as an apparent joke took another turn Tuesday when Trump threatened to use "economic force" to make Canada the next U.S. state and reiterated his tariff promise at a news conference in Florida. Trudeau responded by saying there "isn't a snowball's chance in hell" that Canada would become part of the United States.
Canada is home to more than a million Americans, including visitors on long-term visas and dual citizens, according to estimates from the Association of Americans Resident Overseas. Among them are many Republicans who voted for Trump, and who said in the days before Trump's latest declaration that Canadians shouldn't worry about his return to the White House.
Burke said Trump "isn't anti-Canada" but he has good reasons to be "very worried" about the threat of what she called "terrorism" along the border and Canada's failure to meet NATO's military spending benchmark.
"He's saying: you know what, time's up, the U.S. is not going be the ATM anymore for anybody, not Canada, not anybody else," the Ontario resident said.
"He's a bit of a troll, whether people like it or not, people may say, oh, that's not presidential or whatever, but that's just the way he is."
The federal government announced last month that it would invest $1.3 billion to strengthen border security. Burke said the results of that pledge might encourage Trump to delay the tariffs and wait to negotiate details with the next Canadian government after Trudeau's departure.
Renna Bassal, a longtime Trump supporter and an American who moved to Canada about five decades ago, said last week that Trump's remarks about annexing Canada had been a joke aimed at disrespecting and taunting Trudeau - not Canada as a nation.
The Montreal resident said she isn't sure Trump will make good on his tariff threats, and believes the heavy-handed approach he has adopted is a "bargaining chip" ahead of expected trade negotiations between the two countries.
She said she remains optimistic about U.S.-Canada relations.
"Sometimes, you know, he says things that perhaps he shouldn't say, and I'm hoping that this will be one of those instances where he's not going to follow through on (tariffs)," she said. "It would be extremely unfortunate, and I certainly hope that this doesn't happen."
Wesoky, the political science student involved with Democrats Abroad Canada, said Trump's tariffs would not only increase the cost of living in the United States, but also harm American consumers and businesses as much as they would hurt the Canadian economy.
"So this rhetoric that really goes against this relationship that is built on trust and respect ... I think is not only dangerous, but it'll be detrimental to the U.S economy," he said.
Trump has a habit of exaggerating problems so that he can take credit once they are resolved, Mark R. Brawley, an American Canadian professor of international relations at McGill University said, and his stance on security at the U.S.-Canada border could be one example of that.
"The whole idea that Canada would be a single state in the U.S. or that the U.S. wants Canada to be a state is kind of preposterous," he said. "It's just his style of doing things, so I wouldn't take it too seriously."
The Canadian Press
CAPTION: President-elect Donald Trump's first shot at Canada came in November, when he said he would impose a 25 per cent tariff on all Canadian imports as soon as he returns to the White House on Jan. 20.Evan Vucci THE ASSOCIATED PRESS

Document 393 of 500

Jan. 9: Need for change long overdue, conference not welcome here and other letters to the editor

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/jan-9-need-change-long-overdue-conference-not/docview/3152878245/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Jan.+9%3A+Need+for+change+long+overdue%2C+conference+not+welcome+here+and+other+letters+to+the+editor&amp;title=The+Hamilton+Spectator+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2025-01-09&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Hamilton+Spectator+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Need for change long overdue
Re: MP refuses to let go of racism complaint against police, Jan. 4
I am disappointed that more than eight years after Const. Andrew Pfeifer randomly stopped then councillor Matthew Green, who is now an MP, while he waited for the bus, the Human Rights Tribunal of Ontario (HRTO) has dismissed the racial profiling complaint filed by Green.
This demonstrates the HRTO's ongoing delayed and ineffective response to applicant complaints and further perpetuates the negative public perception of police forces, not only in Hamilton, but in many large cities across Canada.
Green's experience indicates the need for change is long overdue. In order to prevent such incidents from occurring in the future, the Hamilton Police Service must recognize Const. Pfeifer's actions as racial profiling and take proactive measures to eliminate this practice.
It is high time that anti-Black racism within policing is recognized. It is only by doing so that the Hamilton Police Service can gain the trust and respect of all Hamiltonians.
Andrea Rado, Hamilton
Body cameras may be the answer
The incident with Matthew Green is an unfortunate one to have to go through for himself and the Hamilton Police Service and the officer involved. I'm not taking sides here, as it has been long and protracted and turned into a finger pointing he said/she said situation.
Let this incident serve as a reminder to the city's police services board when allocating money for the budget, that individual body cameras could have helped solve this. They are for everyone's protection. They cost a lot, but the same debate was had over Tasers. At one point, only supervising officers on duty had Tasers, and front line officers had to wait for them to arrive, often too late before an outcome became fatal. Then all officers got Tasers, as one more tool in the box, to protect everyone. The same debate is now happening whether the cost of body cameras are worth it. Lets compare the human cost and legal costs of not having them. This protracted event could have been over long ago with body cam evidence.
Andrew Schroeder, Stoney Creek
Police were doing their job
Eight years ago, on a cold and rainy day, a member of the Hamilton Police Crisis Outreach and Support Team and his partner noticed a man standing under a bridge with no overcoat. They stopped to check on his well-being and possibly his mental health. The man was hostile and sarcastic. He resented the police talking to him. The police did not accost or harm him. They simply left.
It turned out the man under the bridge, standing in a puddle, was Matthew Green, then a city councillor.
Green states he has a duty because of his power and privilege to continue to hound these officers, even after the Human Rights Tribunal of Ontario has dismissed this as being a racial profiling case.
The police have a duty to check on the well-being of people that stand out for odd reasons.
If fewer people were hostile to the police, I believe events would not escalate to the breaking point.
Could it be that Green was more offended at not being recognized as being a man of "power and privilege" (to quote his own words)?
Andy Price, Ancaster
Conference not welcome here
Re: Mayor speaks out on Islamic group's conference plans, Jan. 8
I am very confused. Hizb ut Tahrir "is seen as starkly antisemitic, hawkish on eradicating Israel, rejecting international co-operation and intervention and approving of elements of Islamic militant jihad action," according to the National Post.
On the other hand, Mayor Andrea Horwath has said, "I have consistently spoken out against hate in all its forms and will continue to do so unequivocally ... there is no place in our city for messages of division or intolerance."
So please explain why, even with provisos, and at great cost to the taxpayers for all of the police presence, are we allowing this group to meet here in Hamilton?
Why can you not have the strength to follow your beliefs that Hamilton is no place for hate Madam Horwath to say no? You are not welcome. Follow the lead of the Mississauga Mayor Carolyn Parrish.
Suki Garson, Dundas
No more tip-toeing on the issue
Mayor Andrea Horwath has "significant concerns" regarding "the annual Khilafah conference, organized by Hizb ut Tahrir Canada" which may be relocating to Hamilton.
Well, I have "significant concerns" too. I am concerned that the city's mayor lacks the necessary leadership qualities to state publicly in no uncertain terms to this group that they are not welcome. Enough of tip-toeing around the issue with political bafflegab.
Neville Newman, Hamilton
We need to come together
Re: Trudeau had no choice but to resign, Jan. 7
Many thanks to Kathleen Wynne and The Spec for the thoughtful, fair, understanding and constructive reflection on Justin Trudeau's resignation as federal Liberal leader. The task of political leadership has never been heavier. So many factors that are beyond any party's control can force leaders into choices that were not necessarily foreseen, and divert efforts away from dealing with promised attention to other important issues. It is not enough for citizens to support a candidate's election, important though that is. In a democracy, citizens must continue to support those they have elected to provide the best possible service to our country so that all may thrive, not just a particular political party.
My hope is that all of us can do a better job of working together for the common good in 2025. Lets listen to one another and work together beyond party boundaries for a happier new year for all. Lets find the best win-win situations possible.
Joan Nathanson, Hamilton
Give Trudeau his due
What transpired between Justin Trudeau and former finance minister Chrystia Freeland was their business; it was Freeland who resigned.
Not to defend Trudeau, but when Premier Doug Ford shuffled former minister of education Stephen Lecce to another post, no one publicly fussed about that move, nor was Ford asked to retire. Now Trudeau shuffles Freeland's position around and he has stepped aside, with much criticism from the media.
Say what we will about Trudeau, but at least he admitted overestimating the number of immigrants he should have admitted to Canada and is trying to fix it, along with other things. We should also credit Trudeau for acting swiftly on the tariff threat by meeting with president-elect Donald Trump.
I may not agree with some of Trudeau's ways but, while he has held office, his diplomacy in dealing with other nations has maintained the peace. Which is essential in these troubling times with nations rising against each other.
Tina Maini, Hamilton
Prime minister lacks humility
Far from the fighter Justin Trudeau says he is, his inability to show humility and admit when he's wrong in the face of opposition from all sides is symptomatic of a narcissist who craves power and blames others for his own failures.
Chris Asimoudis, Ancaster
Not buying what Poilievre's selling
Warning! Beware of conservative politicians trying to sell "common sense." The two drawbacks to common sense are that it's usually not very sensible and is definitely not at all common.
Pierre Poilievre wants us to "bring it home." He needs to explain what "it" is and where it has been. I suspect "it" has been on extended leave in the United States. Canadians need to ask themselves if we really want to bring "it" home.
Paul Bognar, Hamilton
A 'fair deal' for Ukraine
Re: Can Trump swing a peace deal? Jan. 7
Ukraine is a crime scene, not a transaction. Describing it in casual business terms is an injustice in itself. What would a fair deal look like? How about an immediate ceasefire followed by a complete withdrawal of all aggressor troops from illegally occupied sovereign Ukrainian land (and Ukrainians out of Russia). This should be followed by reparations paid by the Russian state (or Vladimir Putin's own piggy bank - he can afford it) and ongoing apologies by the Russian state and Putin for a century. Finally, the self-serving autocrat, thug and criminal (Putin) finds himself before the International Court of Justice, is found guilty of war crimes and then spends the rest of his life in jail. One more thing; Russia doesn't get to dictate whether a sovereign nation can apply to an international defensive alliance. I present this in ideal terms and realize my proposal is pie-in-the-sky. But as global citizens our aim should be to uphold the international rule of law and not reduce it to deal-making.
Chris Carroll, Hamilton
Trump far from 'a man of peace'
Hamilton Spectator readers who believe Donald Trump is a man of peace and kept United States out of wars are delusional. President Trump in his first term bombed Syria, and had it not been for John Kelly and Mark Milley, was seriously considering invading Venezuela, Mexico, North Korea and China. Trump refused to denounce deadly white supremacists riots and was the driving force behind the Jan. 6 Capitol insurrectionists who wanted to hang Mike Pence and kill Nancy Pelosi. The only war Trump stayed out of was the Vietnam War. He didn't answer the call when asked to serve and now bullies others to prove how tough he is.
Ukraine, thanks to the United States pressuring them to give up its nuclear stockpile in 1994, now finds itself fighting a war 30 years later against Russia. President Volodymyr Zelenskyy talks positively of Trump because he has no other choice. Trump has threatened to cut off military supplies and force Ukraine to give up land to Russia so Vladimir Putin can declare victory and save face. The lives of hundreds of thousands of soldiers and innocent men, women and children will have meant nothing. Dictators like Putin, and North Korea's Kim Jong Un who sent troops in to be slaughtered in Ukraine, will go on with their lavish lives. Trump will boast of being a fearless leader and demand a Nobel Peace Prize for selling out another U.S. ally.
Frieda Huffman, Hamilton
Ultraprocessed foods have a major drawback
Re: In praise of ultraprocessed foods, Jan. 2
Wow, who knew it? All those extraordinary benefits of ultraprocessed foods! Apparently they even promote gender equality because, as we well know, it's a woman's job to do the cooking.
Not mentioned in this article is one teeny, tiny, practically insignificant drawback to these "foods." And that is, they do not provide the nourishment and essential health-giving nutrients that only real food can provide. But apart from that ...
Penny Dibben, Kitchener

Document 394 of 500

George Whyte, former Burlington alderman, called 'mayor of Kilbride': One-time history teacher known for scrutiny of city spending, sought 'common sense, frugality'

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/george-whyte-former-burlington-alderman-called/docview/3152878093/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=George+Whyte%2C+former+Burlington+alderman%2C+called+%27mayor+of+Kilbride%27%3A+One-time+history+teacher+known+for+scrutiny+of+city+spending%2C+sought+%27common+sense%2C+frugality%27&amp;title=The+Spectator&amp;issn=11899417&amp;date=2025-01-09&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.7&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Spectator&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Former Burlington alderman George Whyte was colourful, outspoken and a fiscal hawk who was not afraid to stand up for what he believed in despite facing overwhelming opposition.
Known as "the mayor of Kilbride," Whyte - who died Oct. 26 at age 81 - led efforts in 1984 to get council to reconsider spending between $7.6 million and $8.4 million on the expansion of Burlington City Hall. In 1988, he backed introducing market value assessment immediately, instead of phasing it in, and said he wouldn't support the proposed Burlington Performing Arts Centre if it looked to be a drain on taxpayers.
"I am not able to sleep at night with what we've done so far," he said in 1984, during the final debate and vote on the city hall expansion project. "I'm not trying to stop city hall. I just think we're spending too much money."
On the MVA debate, he told councillors, "It's time we chew our gum or spit it out."
Council overwhelmingly backed the city hall expansion. In one vote, a motion he put forward lost 15-2 (this was when Burlington had a 17-member council). The phasing in of MVA was approved and the arts centre moved forward.
Whyte also took on Burlington MP Bill Kempling for the PC federal nomination in 1984. Whyte was upset about the control of signing up new memberships by the riding executive. Kempling had been MP since 1972. Whyte lost the attempt to dislodge Kempling, who served until 1993.
Whyte, a history teacher and developer, represented Ward 4 in north Burlington. He was elected in 1982 and acclaimed in 1985. He moved his family to the Dominican Republic to build a hotel-condo project and did not seek re-election in 1988. For a few months, he was commuting back and forth between Burlington and Puerto Plata to finish his term.
"I just want some common sense and frugality at city council," Whyte told The Spectator in 1984. "Let them save the taxpayers some money."
Former Ward 2 alderman and Spectator columnist Joan Little said Whyte was popular with his fellow councillors. She said his relocating to the Caribbean left a "big hole" on council.
On social media, his former ward mate Barry Quinn said Whyte "enjoyed being the champion of rural Burlington. He tried so hard to have a covered bridge built over Bronte Creek at No. 2 Sideroad, near Appleby Line - it lost by one vote. It would have been a Burlington treasure."
George Herbert Whyte was born Aug. 5, 1943 to Herb and Violetta Whyte in Toronto. He was raised in Port Credit and attended Port Credit High School between 1957 and 1962. He attended Middlebury College in Vermont on a hockey scholarship from 1962-66 and then attended McGill University between 1966 and 1968, where he obtained a master's degree in Canadian and American history.
He and his first wife Helen travelled the world before he came back to Canada and taught on Manitoulin Island, Cambrian College in Sudbury and Hillfield Strathallan College in Hamilton. He and his family bought the former Kilbride School, built in 1876, and moved into the area in 1976. Before he ran for council he was involved in the restoration of two heritage homes and served on the Burlington Social Planning Council.
His family lived in a house on the Hillfield Strathallan campus before moving to Kilbride. He was fondly remembered at the school.
"He made history come alive for countless students," former colleague Michaele Robertson said on social media. "We were all richer for being in his orbit."
Whyte is survived by his partner Donna Haynes, children Andrew and Martin and two grandchildren. He is also survived by his first wife Helen.
Daniel Nolan
can be reached at
dannolanwrites@gmail.com
CAPTION: George Whyte was the colourful and outspoken alderman for Burlington's rural Ward 4 in the 1980s.The Hamilton Spectator File Photo

Letters

Full text: Need for change long overdue
Re: MP refuses to let go of racism complaint against police, Jan. 4
I am disappointed that more than eight years after Const. Andrew Pfeifer randomly stopped then councillor Matthew Green, who is now an MP, while he waited for the bus, the Human Rights Tribunal of Ontario (HRTO) has dismissed the racial profiling complaint filed by Green.
This demonstrates the HRTO's ongoing delayed and ineffective response to applicant complaints and further perpetuates the negative public perception of police forces, not only in Hamilton, but in many large cities across Canada.
Green's experience indicates the need for change is long overdue. In order to prevent such incidents from occurring in the future, the Hamilton Police Service must recognize Const. Pfeifer's actions as racial profiling and take proactive measures to eliminate this practice.
It is high time that anti-Black racism within policing is recognized. It is only by doing so that the Hamilton Police Service can gain the trust and respect of all Hamiltonians.
Andrea Rado, Hamilton
Police were
doing their job
Eight years ago, on a cold and rainy day, a member of the Hamilton Police Crisis Outreach and Support Team and his partner noticed a man standing under a bridge with no overcoat. They stopped to check on his well-being and possibly his mental health. The man was hostile and sarcastic. He resented the police talking to him. The police did not accost or harm him. They simply left. It turned out the man under the bridge, standing in a puddle, was Matthew Green, then a city councillor.
Green states he has a duty because of his power and privilege to continue to hound these officers, even after the Human Rights Tribunal of Ontario has dismissed this as being a racial profiling case.
The police have a duty to check on the well-being of people that stand out for odd reasons.
If fewer people were hostile to the police, I believe events would not escalate to the breaking point.
Could it be that Green was more offended at not being recognized as being a man of "power and privilege" (to quote his own words)?
Andy Price, Ancaster
Body cameras
may be the answer
The incident with Matthew Green is an unfortunate one to have to go through for himself and the Hamilton Police Service and the officer involved. I'm not taking sides here, as it has been long and protracted and turned into a finger pointing he said/she said situation.
Let this incident serve as a reminder to the city's police services board when allocating money for the budget, that individual body cameras could have helped solve this. They are for everyone's protection. They cost a lot, but the same debate was had over Tasers.
At one point, only supervising officers on duty had Tasers, and front line officers had to wait for them to arrive, often too late before an outcome became fatal. Then all officers got Tasers, as one more tool in the box, to protect everyone. The same debate is now happening whether the cost of body cameras are worth it. Lets compare the human cost and legal costs of not having them.
This protracted event could have been over long ago with body cam evidence.
Andrew Schroeder, Stoney Creek
Conference not
welcome here
Re: Mayor speaks out on Islamic group's conference plans, Jan. 8
I am very confused. Hizb ut Tahrir "is seen as starkly antisemitic, hawkish on eradicating Israel, rejecting international co-operation and intervention and approving of elements of Islamic militant jihad action," according to the National Post.
On the other hand, Mayor Andrea Horwath has said, "I have consistently spoken out against hate in all its forms and will continue to do so unequivocally ... there is no place in our city for messages of division or intolerance."
So please explain why, even with provisos, and at great cost to the taxpayers for all of the police presence, are we allowing this group to meet here in Hamilton?
Why can you not have the strength to follow your beliefs that Hamilton is no place for hate Madam Horwath to say no? You are not welcome. Follow the lead of the Mississauga Mayor Carolyn Parrish.
Suki Garson, Dundas
No more tiptoeing around the issue
Mayor Andrea Horwath has "significant concerns" regarding "the annual Khilafah conference, organized by Hizb ut Tahrir Canada" which may be relocating to Hamilton.
Well, I have "significant concerns" too. I am concerned that the city's mayor lacks the necessary leadership qualities to state publicly in no uncertain terms to this group that they are not welcome. Enough of tiptoeing around the issue with political bafflegab.
Neville Newman, Hamilton
We need to
come together
Re: Trudeau had no choice but to resign, Jan. 7
Many thanks to Kathleen Wynne and The Spec for the thoughtful, fair, understanding and constructive reflection on Justin Trudeau's resignation as federal Liberal leader. The task of political leadership has never been heavier.
So many factors that are beyond any party's control can force leaders into choices that were not necessarily foreseen, and divert efforts away from dealing with promised attention to other important issues.
It is not enough for citizens to support a candidate's election, important though that is. In a democracy, citizens must continue to support those they have elected to provide the best possible service to our country so that all may thrive, not just a particular political party.
My hope is that all of us can do a better job of working together for the common good in 2025. Lets listen to one another and work together beyond party boundaries for a happier new year for all. Lets find the best win-win situations possible.
Joan Nathanson, Hamilton
Prime minister lacks humility
Far from the fighter Justin Trudeau says he is, his inability to show humility and admit when he's wrong in the face of opposition from all sides is symptomatic of a narcissist who craves power and blames others for his own failures.
Chris Asimoudis, Ancaster
Not buying what Poilievre's selling
Warning! Beware of conservative politicians trying to sell "common sense." The two drawbacks to common sense are that it's usually not very sensible and is definitely not at all common. Pierre Poilievre wants us to "bring it home." He needs to explain what "it" is and where it has been. I suspect "it" has been on extended leave in the United States. Canadians need to ask themselves if we really want to bring "it" home.
Paul Bognar, Hamilton
Seeking a 'fair deal' for Ukraine?
Re: Can Trump swing a peace deal? Jan. 7
Ukraine is a crime scene, not a transaction. Describing it in casual business terms is an injustice in itself. What would a fair deal look like?
How about an immediate ceasefire followed by a complete withdrawal of all aggressor troops from illegally occupied sovereign Ukrainian land (and Ukrainians out of Russia). This should be followed by reparations paid by the Russian state (or Vladimir Putin's own piggy bank - he can afford it) and ongoing apologies by the Russian state and Putin for a century. Finally, the self-serving autocrat, thug and criminal (Putin) finds himself before the International Court of Justice, is found guilty of war crimes and then spends the rest of his life in jail.
One more thing; Russia doesn't get to dictate whether a sovereign nation can apply to an international defensive alliance. I present this in ideal terms and realize my proposal is pie-in-the-sky. But as global citizens our aim should be to uphold the international rule of law and not reduce it to deal-making.
Chris Carroll, Hamilton

Document 396 of 500

Canada close to retaliatory tariffs plan: International Trade Minister Ng said Trump 'not' joking about somehow absorbing Canada

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/canada-close-retaliatory-tariffs-plan/docview/3152877973/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Canada+close+to+retaliatory+tariffs+plan%3A+International+Trade+Minister+Ng+said+Trump+%27not%27+joking+about+somehow+absorbing+Canada&amp;title=The+Spectator&amp;issn=11899417&amp;date=2025-01-09&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.11&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Spectator&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Prime Minister Justin Trudeau and Canada's premiers will meet in Ottawa Jan. 15 to finalize a plan to respond to tariff threats from U.S. president-elect Donald Trump, including discussing what the best targets are for Canada to retaliate with tariffs of its own.
The meeting comes as the federal and provincial governments are seized with responding to Trump's promise to slap an across-the-board import tariff of 25 per cent on Canada and Mexico on Jan. 20 and his escalating rhetoric about turning Canada into the 51st state.
Trudeau's office said earlier this week they were looking to meet in-person after two virtual first ministers' meetings on Trump's tariffs took place in November and December.
Ontario Premier Doug Ford, after meeting virtually with other premiers Wednesday, confirmed that meeting will happen and said Ottawa has provided a "small list" of proposed retaliatory tariffs. He would not reveal what they are.
"I don't think it's strategic for Canadians or Ontarians to hand out the list," Ford said. The premiers have a strong plan, but Ford said they want to hear what strategy the federal government has come up with.
In 2018, after Trump imposed tariffs on Canadian steel and aluminum during his first term in the White House, Canada retaliated with tariffs on dozens of American exports tailored to cause maximum pain to Republican leaders in their home states, including whisky, chocolate, yogurt, and orange juice.
International Trade Minister Mary Ng said Wednesday that Trump is "not" joking about the U.S. somehow absorbing Canada.
"He's not and we are taking it seriously and we will respond seriously as well," she said. "But I have done a lot of work meeting Canadian businesses, American businesses, American legislators and they understand the importance of this relationship."
Two former U.S. ambassadors to Canada also weighed in on how Canada should respond to the annexation threat. Gordon Giffin, who served under former U.S. president Bill Clinton, said Trump has "gone too far."
"Unfortunately, the president-elect has become enamoured with the line and he is almost just reverting to it in an unconscious way," he said.
Giffin said Canada should be calm and firm in its response. "I think Canada and Canadian leaders ought to be dismissive of it," he said. "I wouldn't scowl and take offence. I would just be dismissive."
Bruce Heyman served as U.S. ambassador to Canada under former president Barack Obama from 2014 to 2017. He also called on Canadian officials to keep their cool.
"Don't overreact to every press conference and look at the opportunity you have ahead of you and work that diplomatically," Heyman said.
The Canadian Press

Document 397 of 500

Ottawa preparing response to threatened U.S. tariffs as Donald Trump ramps up attacks on Canada

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/ottawa-preparing-response-threatened-u-s-tariffs/docview/3153254111/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Ottawa+preparing+response+to+threatened+U.S.+tariffs+as+Donald+Trump+ramps+up+attacks+on+Canada&amp;title=The+Hamilton+Spectator+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2025-01-08&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Hamilton+Spectator+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: OTTAWA - Joke's over.
The federal government signalled Wednesday that it took U.S. president-elect Donald Trump seriously when he spoke this week of using "economic force" to annex Canada, after previously downplaying as a joke his repeated taunts about making this country the "51st state."
A senior government official, who spoke to the Star on condition of anonymity, said the latest and most extreme talk of erasing Canada's sovereignty has galvanized the government. Ottawa is now pushing to soon release details of a potential response to Trump's threat of a trade war, if he follows through on his plan to impose punishing 25-per-cent levies on all Canadian goods entering the United States, the official said.
Trump's comments came after Prime Minister Justin Trudeau announced he will step down, and suspended Parliament for almost three months while his Liberal party stages a leadership race to replace him.
On Wednesday, as Liberal MPs gathered on Parliament Hill to discuss that leadership contest, several cabinet ministers stressed the need to take Trump's belligerence seriously, and warned that the incoming American leader's threatened tariffs would jeopardize thousands of Canadian jobs.
The Canadian Chamber of Commerce released an analysis in November with University of Calgary economist Trevor Tombe that predicted Trump's tariffs could take a $78-billion chunk out of the Canadian economy - the equivalent of 2.6 per cent of national gross domestic product (GDP).
International Trade Minister Mary Ng said Wednesday it's no longer a joke to hear Trump talk about making Canada part of the United States.
"We are taking it seriously, and we will respond seriously as well," Ng said.
"We have to be concerned when tariffs and punishing tariffs are going to hurt the Canadian economy."
Dominic LeBlanc, who took over as finance minister after Chrystia Freeland resignation from cabinet in December, ruled out a run for Trudeau's job on Wednesday, in part to keep his focus on how to counter the danger of Trump's tariffs. LeBlanc, who earlier said he thought Trump's comment about Canada being the 51st state was a joke, told reporters Wednesday that that's no longer the case.
"I find he has far surpassed the idea of a joke," LeBlanc said in French. "It is becoming, frankly, completely counterproductive," he added, suggesting it was a tactic to sow confusion.
LeBlanc said he was set to meet with top government officials Wednesday evening to discuss the tariff threats.
After Trump made the comments about annexing Canada at a news conference at his luxury club in Mar-a-Lago, Florida on Tuesday, Trudeau posted on social media that there is not "a snowball's chance in hell" that Canada would become part of the U.S.
Opposition parties also pushed back against Trump, with Conservative Leader Pierre Poilievre stating online that "we are a great and independent country," and promising to expand the military, improve border control and protect the Arctic from Russia and China if his party wins the next election. NDP Leader Jagmeet Singh called Trump a "bully" and said Canada should impose retaliatory tariffs on the U.S. if Trump follows through with his threats, which he said would harm the economies of both countries.
"You come for Canadians' jobs, Americans will pay a price," Singh wrote on social media.
On Wednesday, Premier Doug Ford - who has been appearing on American news channels to argue Trump's threatened tariffs are misguided - proposed to better integrate energy systems in Canada and the U.S. to create a "Fortress Can-Am." Ford has also said Canada should ink a trade deal with the U.S. that excludes Mexico, a partner in North American trade deals since the 1990s.
The federal government committed $1.3 billion to beef up border security after Trump named illegal immigration and drug smuggling as justifications for the tariffs he threatened to impose.
During Trump's first term as president, from 2017 to 2021, he forced Canada and Mexico to renegotiate the North American Free Trade Agreement. In 2018, Trump slapped tariffs on Canadian steel and aluminum, arguing the action was necessary for U.S. national security. At the time, Trudeau called the tariffs "an affront to the long-standing security partnership between Canada and the United States" and responded with duties on more than $16 billion worth of American goods.
This time, as Trump prepares to return to the White House and impose his new tariffs on Jan. 20, the federal government has not ruled out any response as it prepares a new list of American goods to target if the incoming president follows through on his threats.
"We have to be extremely ready because the threat of tariffs is real," said Foreign Affairs Minister Mélanie Joly, who is contemplating a run for the Liberal party leadership.
"We need to take (Trump) very seriously. I never take his threats lightly," she added.
"At the same time, we can't take the bait, and we're extremely strong as a country," she said. "We will never back down."
For Roland Paris, a professor of international affairs at the University of Ottawa and a former foreign policy adviser to Trudeau, Trump's latest comments about using economic force to annex Canada smacks of American imperialism. The incoming U.S. president also refused this week to rule out using military force to take over the Panama Canal and Greenland, the latter a territory of Denmark, an American and Canadian ally in the North Atlantic Treaty Organization.
"Trump's language is really just an assertion of American dominance," Paris said, describing how it "upends" decades of Canadian foreign policy assumptions, as North America became more integrated and through economic partnerships like the 1960s Auto Pact, and military co-operation such that seen in the North American Aerospace Defence Command.
While noting that it's unclear whether Trump is sincere or merely blustering in an attempt to wrench trade concessions from Canada, Paris said the situation is nonetheless "unusual and disturbing."
Aaron Ettinger, a professor who specializes in U.S. foreign policy at Carleton University, agreed that Trump's posture echoes American "expansionism" in the 19th century. Some argued at the time that the U.S. was destined to control all of North America as part of a divinely inspired "manifest destiny."
But while Ettinger dismissed the idea that Trump could use economic tools to take over Canada, he said his statements mark a break with decades of co-operation that have defined the Canada-U.S. relationship.
They also highlight how the U.S. could seriously harm the Canadian economy, given that it is by far Canada's largest trading partner. According to Statistics Canada, 77 per cent of Canada's exports went to the U.S. and 50 per cent of its imports came from there in 2023.
"There's no escaping the economic pull of the United States," Ettinger said.
"(Trump) can do us harm."
CAPTION: U.S. president-elect Donald Trump speaks during a news conference at Mar-a-Lago on Jan. 7, 2025, in Palm Beach, Fla.

Subject: Trade agreements; Decades; Social networks; Tariffs; Press conferences; International trade; Leadership; Presidents; Gross Domestic Product--GDP; Economic growth; Foreign policy

Business indexing term: Subject: Trade agreements Social networks Tariffs International trade Leadership Gross Domestic Product--GDP Economic growth

Location: Mexico; United States--US; Canada; North America

People: Trump, Donald J

Title: Ottawa preparing response to threatened U.S. tariffs as Donald Trump ramps up attacks on Canada

Document 398 of 500

Doug Ford pitches energy alliance with the U.S. to avert trade war with Donald Trump

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/doug-ford-pitches-energy-alliance-with-u-s-avert/docview/3152685410/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Doug+Ford+pitches+energy+alliance+with+the+U.S.+to+avert+trade+war+with+Donald+Trump&amp;title=The+Hamilton+Spectator+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2025-01-08&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Hamilton+Spectator+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: After warning Ontario would cut off electricity exports stateside in retaliation for any Donald Trump tariffs, Premier Doug Ford wants to better integrate energy systems with the U.S. to avert a trade war.
With the president-elect threatening 25-per-cent levies on Canadian and Mexican goods later this month unless America's neighbours tighten borders, Ford on Wednesday pitched "Fortress Am-Can" fuelled by domestic energy production.
"With a new administration set to take office in the White House, our government has an ambitious plan to build up Fortress Am-Can to usher in a new American and Canadian century defined by unprecedented growth, job creation and prosperity," he said.
"We can only do so by working together and respecting each other," the premier said Wednesday at the Darlington energy complex in Courtice, Ont., 70 km east of Toronto.
"Fortress Am-Can should be powered by Am-Can energy of every type that's produced, consumed and creates jobs in every region of both countries," said Ford, touting Ontario existing nuclear fleet and its planned small modular reactors.
His comments come exactly four weeks after he warned the province would not sit idly by if Trump makes good on his threat to slap 25-per-cent tariffs on Canadian products after his Jan. 20 inauguration.
"We will go to the extent of cutting off their energy - going down to Michigan, going down to New York state and over to Wisconsin," Ford said on Dec. 18, noting Ontario supplies about 1.5 million Americans with electricity.
While that gambit got Trump's attention, the president-elect has ramped up his rhetoric, deriding America's largest trading partner as "the great state of Canada" and Prime Minister Justin Trudeau as a state "governor."
At a rambling 70-minute news conference at his Florida resort on Tuesday, he said he would use "economic force" to make Canada a "51st state."
"Canada and the United States, that would really be something," said Trump.
"You get rid of that artificially drawn line, and you take a look at what that looks like ... it would also be much better for national security," said the president-elect, who also mused about annexing Greenland, which is part of Denmark, as well as Panama and renaming the Gulf of Mexico the "Gulf of America."
In response, Trudeau, who announced his resignation as prime minister and Liberal leader Monday, said "there isn't a snowball's chance in hell" that Canada would ever join the U.S.
"Workers and communities in both our countries benefit from being each other's biggest trading and security partner," he said.
Conservative Leader Pierre Poilievre, who polls suggest would win the next federal election expected later this year, echoed Trudeau, saying "Canada will never be the 51st state."
On Wednesday in Courtice, Ford said that beyond further integration of the electricity grid, he wants Canadian and American lawmakers to forge a working group with energy and security experts "to ensure both countries adhere to best practices for power system security against foreign interference, cyber attacks, terrorism and extreme weather, among other threats."
As well, he would like to "align regulations and eliminate red-tape that slows building cross-border energy infrastructure, including for transmission lines, interties and pipelines."
But that does not mean Ontario isn't prepared to counter any Trump tariffs by withholding energy exports to the U.S.
Officials emphasized the province would "use every tool in our tool belt to defend Ontario and retaliate if we need to."
Later Wednesday, Ford, who is chair of the Council of the Federation, will meet virtually with the other provincial and territorial leaders to discuss the Trump threat and next moves after the prime minister's departure.
On Jan. 15, Trudeau will convene the premiers for an in-person first ministers' meeting in Ottawa on the tariff crisis.
CAPTION: U.S. president-elect Donald Trump (left) and Premier Doug Ford.

Document 399 of 500

Past failures weighing on Quebec energy deal in N.L.

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/past-failures-weighing-on-quebec-energy-deal-n-l/docview/3152478911/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Past+failures+weighing+on+Quebec+energy+deal+in+N.L.&amp;title=The+Spectator&amp;issn=11899417&amp;date=2025-01-08&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.10&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Spectator&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: ST. JOHN'S Newfoundland and Labrador Premier Andrew Furey promised Monday that an independent panel of experts will monitor negotiations ahead of a final energy agreement with Quebec's electric utility - but he wouldn't commit to a review of the deal as it stands now.
The Liberal government of Canada's easternmost province opened its legislature this week for four days of debate about a memorandum of understanding between Hydro-Québec and Newfoundland and Labrador promising thousands of jobs and billions of dollars in revenue.
Opposition politicians spent much of the first day repeating their calls for an arm's-length review of the tentative agreement, pointing to the province's past megaproject failures.
"I just don't understand the reluctance to take this (contract), which has such a significant impact on the opportunities for the people in Newfoundland and Labrador, and allow it to be reviewed," said Tony Wakeham, leader of the Opposition Progressive Conservatives.
"Will you commit to an independent review of this (memorandum of understanding)?" he asked Furey.
In response, Furey said there have already been independent firms advising during negotiations.
His government will provide "outside and independent advice to cabinet" at the final stages of the agreement.
The tentative deal would see Hydro-Québec pay significantly more than it currently does for electricity from the Churchill Falls hydroelectric plant in Labrador, and develop additional projects with Newfoundland and Labrador Hydro along the Churchill River.
Furey presented the agreement on Dec. 12 at a press conference in St. John's, N.L.
The provinces have had tense relations since 1969, when a contract was signed allowing Hydro-Québec to buy most of the power from Churchill Falls at prices far below market value.
Newfoundland and Labrador tried unsuccessfully to overturn the deal, in a fight that wound up in the Supreme Court of Canada in 2017. The arrangement was set to expire in 2041, but Hydro-Québec has agreed to end it now. Under the new agreement, the utility will pay about 30 times more for Churchill Falls power, resulting in an increase of about $17 billion to the Newfoundland and Labrador treasury between now and 2041.
CAPTION:

CREDIT: The Canadian Press

Document 400 of 500

Is Poilievre ready for the top job?

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/is-poilievre-ready-top-job/docview/3152478892/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=unknown&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Is+Poilievre+ready+for+the+top+job%3F&amp;title=The+Spectator&amp;issn=11899417&amp;date=2025-01-08&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.9&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Spectator&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: As we move into 2025, it is clear to all observers the Liberal government is running on fumes. And unless something truly unprecedented occurs Pierre Poilievre, leader of the Conservative Party of Canada (CPC), will become our next elected prime minister.
Will that be such a bad thing for Canada and Canadians? Before I attempt to answer this, let me go on the record by saying I have never voted for the federal Liberal party. I am also a former member of the federal Progressive Conservative Party of Canada, as well as the Reform Party of Canada. (These two parties merged to form the modern CPC.)
I say this to show I am not a political partisan attacking Poilievre.
As for Poilievre becoming prime minister, I have some deep misgivings.
My first concern is his judgment. In April, Poilievre while campaigning in the Maritimes came upon protesters who were flying flags, referring to Prime Minister Justin Trudeau in obscene ways and espousing some very extremist views (e.g., opposition to COVID-19 public health orders and some "odd" beliefs related to science among others). Poilievre appeared quite friendly with them. Why would he be seen with them? Most Canadians do not share these views and appearing with them might make it appear Poilievre condones their beliefs.
In late 2024 Poilievre was interviewed by Canadian psychologist Jordan Peterson. While being exceptionally academically accomplished, Peterson is also a controversial figure. He was investigated by the College of Psychologists of Ontario in regard to statements he made, which appeared to be inappropriate. Peterson was not found to be guilty of any wrongdoings, however the college expressed concern "the manner and tone in which Dr. Peterson espouses his public statements may reflect poorly on the profession of psychology."
Poilievre can speak with anyone he wishes, but consider this: Peterson is very friendly with the "political right." Poilievre spoke with him, but refuses to speak to much of the mainstream media. It appears Poilievre is only comfortable speaking with those whose views coincide with his own. Part of being a leader is having uncomfortable and even unpleasant conversations. Our possible next prime minister seems to lack the courage to do this outside of bellowing insults at his political rivals in Parliament. Is this a real leader?
My second reason is Poilievre is a career politician. He was elected to Parliament when he was 25. He has practically no private-sector background and no experience living the lives the majority of Canadian voters live. He has been in the "Ottawa bubble" for almost half his life. Is this what we want?
Finally, his political career is in itself quite underwhelming. Stephen Harper was prime minister for almost 10 years (from 2006 to 2015.) In that time Poilievre served as the "government attack dog" and defended the policies of the Harper government. As well, he spent seven years as a parliamentary secretary for various ministries. He wasn't promoted to a ministerial role until 2013, seven years into the administration. He ended up holding two cabinet portfolios: minister of state for democratic reform and minister of employment and social development. He never held a high-level ministerial role such as foreign affairs, finance or national defence.
I certainly don't know Harper, nor do I have any true insight in how he thinks. However, I do find it quite interesting he took his time (seven years!) before adding Poilievre to the cabinet. Perhaps he saw something in how Poilievre operates that made him cautious or even uncomfortable.
Poilievre may very well prove me wrong and make a fine prime minister. But so far I see no indications based on his past that he will be.
Craig Wallace
is a Hamilton resident
and author of five books.
CAPTION: Pierre Poilievre's political and private-sector career histories are quite underwhelming, Craig Wallace writes.Sean Kilpatrick The Canadian Press

CREDIT: Craig Wallace OPINION Craig Wallace is a Hamilton resident and author of five books.;

Subject: Careers; Prime ministers; Political parties

Business indexing term: Subject: Careers

Trudeau is stepping down, but is Poilievre ready for the top job?

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/trudeau-is-stepping-down-poilievre-ready-top-job/docview/3152478784/se-2?accountid=15090

Document 402 of 500

Doug Ford boosts Ontario's border security amid Donald Trump tariff threat

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/doug-ford-boosts-ontarios-border-security-amid/docview/3152189714/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Doug+Ford+boosts+Ontario%27s+border+security+amid+Donald+Trump+tariff+threat&amp;title=The+Hamilton+Spectator+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2025-01-07&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Hamilton+Spectator+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: As Donald Trump threatens 25 per cent tariffs on Canadian goods unless the border with the U.S. is strengthened, Premier Doug Ford is enhancing security on the frontier.
"Ontario has been calling on the federal government to step up and address safety and security concerns at the border. We need to see words turned into visible action," Ford said Tuesday.
The premier's comments come as the U.S. president-elect is vowing to slap levies on Canadian and Mexican products after his Jan. 20 inauguration unless America's neighbours curb illegal immigration and fentanyl smuggling.
"Ontario is stepping up with Operation Deterrence to crack down on illegal border crossings and illegal guns and drugs," said Ford, imploring Ottawa to also do more.
"A more co-ordinated Team Canada approach that includes more boots on the ground is the only way to detect, deter and disrupt illegal activity and ensure the safety and security of Canadian and American communities."
Ford will meet virtually with the other premiers Wednesday afternoon to further discuss steps to mitigate Trump's gambit.
Operation Deterrence includes 200 Ontario Provincial Police officers, who are working in partnership with the RCMP.
In the past five weeks, the OPP has already done more than 6,000 hours of "focused patrols to deter, detect and disrupt illegal border activity using fixed-wing aircraft, helicopters, remotely piloted aircraft, boats, off-road vehicles, patrol vehicles and foot patrol," the province said.
As well, the government said "Operation Deterrence activity will be enhanced through the use of the emergency response team, front-line officer and additional specialty resources, including canine units, commercial motor vehicle inspections and criminal investigators."
The initiative is above and beyond what is already being done at Ontario's 14 official U.S. border crossings by the Canada Border Services Agency.
In the wake of Prime Minister Justin Trudeau announcing his resignation Monday, Ford has been imploring Ottawa to remain focused on the Trump tariff threat.
Speaking to reporters after Trudeau told the nation he would prorogue Parliament and step down after nine years as prime minister, the premier said the federal uncertainty is troubling.
"We need all hands on deck. We need the prime minister to continue doing his job. Prorogation doesn't mean vacation," said Ford.
"God forbid president-elect Trump gets in there and puts tariffs. We have to retaliate. We have to retaliate hard against our friends south of the border, which is unfortunate."
Since the incoming president's Nov. 25 tariff threat against Canada and Mexico, Ford, who is chair of the premiers' Council of the Federation, has been more aggressive than Trudeau or any federal leader.
On Dec. 18, the premier warned Ontario could cut off electricity exports flowing stateside in retaliation for any tariffs on trade.
With Trump repeatedly trolling Canadians on social media about annexing this country as a "51st state" and referring to the prime minister as the "governor of the great state of Canada," Ford stressed that joke isn't funny.
"To the president, I'll make him a counter-offer. How about if we buy Alaska and we'll throw in Minnesota or Minneapolis at the same time," the premier said Monday.
"It's not realistic. I know he likes to make these comments, he likes joking around, (but) I take that seriously," he said.
"He may be joking, but under my watch, that will never, ever happen."
CAPTION: Premier Doug Ford speaks to the media outside of his office in the Queens Park Legislature, in Toronto on Dec. 11, 2024.
