Document 403 of 500

Six stories of political intrigue to follow in Haldimand-Norfolk in 2025

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/six-stories-political-intrigue-follow-haldimand/docview/3152189512/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Six+stories+of+political+intrigue+to+follow+in+Haldimand-Norfolk+in+2025&amp;title=The+Hamilton+Spectator+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2025-01-07&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Hamilton+Spectator+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: The political scene in Haldimand and Norfolk got a little wild during the COVID-19 pandemic, and the new year promises plenty of intrigue.
Here are six political situations along Lake Erie's northern shore to keep an eye on as the calendar flips to 2025.
Brady versus Martin for MPP
A political showdown is looming in Haldimand-Norfolk as the next provincial election will feature incumbent independent MPP Bobbi Ann Brady squaring off against Conservative challenger Amy Martin, currently the mayor of Norfolk County.
The two have been feuding for months in an increasingly public manner as Brady complained about being sidelined from meetings with Conservative ministers visiting the riding, and from the county's delegations with the province at the Association of Municipalities of Ontario conference.
Martin's announcement in November sparked backlash among some local Conservatives because there was no open nomination meeting held locally, creating the perception that the mayor was appointed by Premier Doug Ford in what Brady called "the demise of democracy."
Brady took out then-Haldimand Mayor Ken Hewitt when she won in 2022. Can she fend off another local mayor hand-picked by the Conservative party machine?
What's next for Nanticoke?
In late November, the province floated Nanticoke in Haldimand as a possible location for a future gas or nuclear power plant to meet Ontario's growing energy demand. The plant would be built on the site of the former coal-fired plant run by Ontario Power Generation.
Brady and Mayor Shelley Ann Bentley like the idea - and the new jobs they say the project would bring to the county.
The announcement adds a new wrinkle to a plan from Empire Communities to build 15,000 homes on land ringing Nanticoke's Stelco steelworks. The previous Haldimand council asked the province for a minister's zoning order (MZO) to rezone the land around the steelworks from industrial to residential in order to start reviewing the proposal. That application was denied this year when the province changed the rules.
Over objections from Bentley - she and Brady publicly oppose putting houses within a provincially designated industrial park - the current council gave its blessing to the developers to reapply for an MZO. Empire says it is still interested in applying, but will they follow through?
A challenger for Lewis?
Avid watchers of Question Period will have spotted Haldimand-Norfolk MP Leslyn Lewis occupying a prominent spot on the front bench near Conservative leader Pierre Poilievre.
Lewis has been less visible in the riding, where some voters are still smarting that a so-called "parachute candidate" was appointed by the party brass - sidelining at least one local would-be Conservative candidate.
Lewis has twice ran for the party leadership and continues to grab headlines nationwide, including last year by sponsoring a petition in the House of Commons calling on Canada to leave the United Nations and affiliated agencies like the World Health Organization.
With an election coming in 2025 - perhaps sooner than later - will a challenger push Lewis on her local bona fides and make it more of a race in this typically safe Conservative riding?
New councillors for Haldimand
Two seats are empty around Haldimand County's council table after the deaths of Ward 4 representative Marie Trainer and Stewart Patterson from Ward 1.
Trainer, Haldimand's former mayor, died on Oct. 31 due to complications from a car crash several weeks prior, while Patterson succumbed to illness in late November.
With just under two years left in the current term of council, the vacant seats must be filled. In December, council voted to hold byelections rather than appoint replacement councillors, meaning voters in the two wards will go to the polls in the spring.
Shot in the arm for health unit
At the invitation of the provincial Ministry of Health, the health units responsible for Haldimand-Norfolk and Brantford-Brant will merge in the new year. The leaders of the respective boards of health tout greater efficiency, closer collaboration and better patient care for residents. But some city officials in Brantford worry the new deal will mean expanded services for rural residents while city taxpayers get stuck with the bill.
Grand Erie Public Health, as the merged organization is known, will be overseen by a board consisting of representatives from Brant, Haldimand and Norfolk counties, the city of Brantford and several provincial appointees. This is a plus for Haldimand, which had no formal decision-making power on Haldimand-Norfolk's board of health despite contributing 40 per cent of the funding.
It remains to be seen who will run the merged health unit as medical officer of health, and whether the senior executive ranks of the participating health units will be thinned. Haldimand-Norfolk is in the midst of crafting a new strategy to address high rates of substance abuse, including among local youth, a challenge familiar to health officials in Brantford-Brant.
Norfolk thirsty for water money
Decision time looms for how Norfolk will improve its aging and leaky water system. Council has endorsed a $450-million plan to shutter the county's five existing water treatment plants and pipe water to Norfolk's urban areas from a large treatment plan in Nanticoke.
The interurban water system would also supply towns in Haldimand and even reach Six Nations of the Grand River. Political leaders in those communities are on board, but they are not awash with cash. So the province and Ottawa need to turn on the funding taps for the project to stay afloat.
Norfolk County Mayor Amy Martin argues the money will be spent anyway to shore up the current facilities, and the interurban plan would put more users on municipal water and ease water bills that are among Ontario's highest. That would be welcome news for a sparsely populated rural county already drowning in debt.
J.P. Antonacci's reporting is funded by the Canadian government through its Local Journalism Initiative. jpantonacci@thespec.com

Document 404 of 500

Jan. 7: Health care needs to change, connecting the education dots and other letters to the editor

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/jan-7-health-care-needs-change-connecting/docview/3152189511/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Jan.+7%3A+Health+care+needs+to+change%2C+connecting+the+education+dots+and+other+letters+to+the+editor&amp;title=The+Hamilton+Spectator+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2025-01-07&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Hamilton+Spectator+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Health care in need of change
Re: Delayed care leads to ICU, Jan. 3
Dr. Kwadwo Kyeremanteng wrote an excellent commentary on solutions for our health-care system. The solution of ramping up nurse practitioners and physician assistants makes sense as Canada is in short supply of primary care doctors.
Lets also revive preventative care so we can avoid the catastrophic ICU experience. And lets hope our Premier Doug Ford and Health Minister Sylvia Jones listen to the experts, so every Ontarian can access timely medical interventions.
Janet Connelly, Stoney Creek
Helping us to grieve gracefully
Re: 'You're not alone': Hamilton woman helps grievers worldwide fight holiday heartache, Dec. 31
Thank you to Kate McCullough for your article about Kelly Sammon's Grieving Gracefully website, which I plan to join. It allowed me to feel validated and reassured that I am not alone in my grief.
Andrea Rado, Hamilton
Connecting the dots in education
Re: In my next teacher-parent meeting, my focus will be the teacher, Dec. 10
Re: Six Hamilton-area education stories to watch in 2025, Jan. 3
Ricardo Trajan noted that since Doug Ford came to power in 2018, school board funding has dropped $1,500 per student. Increases in class size and special education underfunding (educational assistants). Fast forward to Kate McCullough's article about growing sick days among Hamilton's school board employees, I am able to see the connection.
Do not put an unwieldy number of children into a classroom with more children that require medical support with a disappearing number of educational assistants. Nobody learns, society suffers: disaster for all. Full disclosure, I am a retired nurse, I do not have children in the school system, I just care and I am worried.
Karen Woods, Hamilton
Change capital gains taxes
Re: Stop treating housing as an investment, Jan. 4
I'm in agreement with many of the points brought forward. Housing is the only "investment" that we treat as a tax free capital gain when it comes to a primary residence. But this just incentivizes people to put more and more of their life savings into what is a single asset on a single street in a single municipality. With the threats we face from climate change, why put your entire financial worth (i.e. all of your eggs) in one basket?
Instead of treating primary residences as tax free, why not just have a lifetime capital gains exemption up to a capped amount? This would eliminate the incentive to treat housing as an investment and even the playing field for renters who can be given the same ability to make capital gains (in other investments) tax free?
Matthew Freeland, Hamilton
Politicians need to do better
Canada is facing many serious challenges, internal and external. Most people want change, so we are moving toward an early election. But change is needed beyond political parties.
All elected officials, regardless of their (micro) political loyalties, need to come together and work hard to address our nation's challenges. In a democracy, debate is healthy only if it is not entirely motivated by partisan politics. Collaboration and unity are needed, now more than ever if we are to gift our children a country better than the one we have inherited.
In every other societal context - family, school, work, community - Canadians are encouraged and expected to work together for the collective good. Why not in the House of Commons? Dear members of Parliament, you can do better. You need to do better.
Stelios Georgiades, Ancaster
Can Trump swing a peace deal?
There is a complexity in trying to obtain a peace agreement with Russia and Ukraine.
For about two years now Donald Trump has been the only substantial world leader that has insisted that peace needs to happen and the killing needs to end. He boasted that he could end the war in 24 hours. Since his election both Volodymyr Zelenskyy and Vladimir Putin have expressed their desire to let him try.
The challenge for Ukraine will be just how much of their territory are they willing concede to obtain peace. The large Crimea region of Ukraine was annexed by Russia in 2014. An independent European poll indicated 82 per cent of Ukrainians in Crimea support Russia's annexation of this region. Since Russia's total invasion in 2022, it now controls a substantial amount of Ukraine (Donetsk, Mariupol, Melitopol).
The real question for Zelenskyy is just how much of Ukraine's territory is he willing to concede, and will he also concede Ukraine will not try to become part of NATO. The tough question for Trump is the same. How much pressure will he apply to Zelenskyy and to Putin to get a fair deal. After about 800,000 dead Ukrainians and 700,000 dead Russians, this war needs to end. Hopefully, the Trump administration will obtain what is perceived to be a "fair deal" for both countries.
David Gallo, Ancaster
Canada's Arctic sovereignty at risk
Spectator readers who suggest Donald Trump's taunts that he will make Canada the 51st state shouldn't be taken seriously, have forgotten his first term already. With snow in the Arctic rapidly melting, the United States along with Russia have disputed claims to Canada's sovereignty of the Northwest Passage.
Trump's Secretary of State Mike Pompeo at the time was salivating at the prospect of opening up the north to drilling for minerals once it becomes financially feasible.
With Trump even more powerful than in his first term, and surrounded by even more corrupt and greedy billionaire businesspeople, he will ignore Canada's claim of ownership and do as pleases, whether the Canadian government or the United Nations International Court of Justice rule otherwise.
Gary Johnson, Hamilton
Enough about Trump already
I usually love the letters to the editor and often nod my head in approval with a lot of the great ideas.
But why does this paper just want to post letters about Donald Trump? I'm not American and what is the sense of talking ad nauseam about something we have no control over?
I'm interested in Hamilton. I want action for the unhoused and hungry who line Gore Park in the hundreds every Saturday. I am interested in why we haven't used already built citizen-paid-for tiny homes? I'm interested in the deal about FirstOntario Place that saw us lose the Bulldogs. I'm interested in the bloated police budget that keeps a mounted unit while Dundas has no community police.
Start publishing letters about our city and our solutions. Enough about Trump.
Robin Magder, Dundas
Wishing for peace this year
Hearing about the attack on pedestrians in New Orleans, my heart sank. Fourteen people were caught in the roots of this terrorist act and 31 were injured. They will remember this pain at the beginning of every new year to come. I feel great pain when I think about the suffered families. As an Ahmadi Muslim, I strongly condemn this attack. Islam teaches peace and love to ... humanity. The perpetrator is a stain on the name of Islam as they commit terrorism in the name of Islam.
These are not the teachings of the founder of Islam. I hope the new year is the beginning of new thought in the world and hate, violence and crime vanish from the face of earth.
Khalida Iffat, Hamilton
CAPTION: We need to ramp up nurse practitioners and physician assistants and revive preventative care to improve our health-care system, a reader writes.

Document 405 of 500

Trudeau stepping down, prorogues Parliament: PM said he asked Liberal party president Sachit Mehra Sunday night to immediately launch a leadership race ahead of the next election

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/trudeau-stepping-down-prorogues-parliament/docview/3152068075/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Trudeau+stepping+down%2C+prorogues+Parliament%3A+PM+said+he+asked+Liberal+party+president+Sachit+Mehra+Sunday+night+to+immediately+launch+a+leadership+race+ahead+of+the+next+election&amp;title=The+Spectator&amp;issn=11899417&amp;date=2025-01-07&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.1&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Spectator&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: As a tearful Justin Trudeau outlined his plans to resign as Liberal leader Monday, he put the country on track for an early election featuring a new party flag-bearer for the first time in a decade.
Once seen as the Liberal saviour who lifted a battered party brand up from the ashes, Trudeau came up against a groundswell of pressure from party rank-and-file to step aside as the public soured on his government and grew hungry for change.
The questions now are when exactly the election will come, and who will vie to become Trudeau's successor and the next to attempt the herculean feat of raising the party back up from the depths.
After more than a year of plummeting poll numbers and surging pressure from within his own caucus to step aside, Trudeau informed Canadians Monday he will resign as leader and prime minister as soon as a new leader is chosen.
"This country deserves a real choice in the next election and it has become clear to me that if I'm having to fight internal battles, I cannot be the best option in that election," Trudeau said outside his official residence in Ottawa Monday morning.
Trudeau said he reflected on his political future over the holidays and told his three kids about his decision over dinner Sunday.
He also said he met with Gov. Gen. Mary Simon on Monday morning and that she has agreed to his request to prorogue Parliament until March 24.
Trudeau had consistently signalled over the past year he intended to remain at the helm, but the decisive blow came when Chrystia Freeland suddenly resigned as minister of finance and deputy prime minister on Dec. 16, days after Trudeau had informed her he was going to move her out of the finance portfolio. Her departure, hours before she was to table the fall economic statement in the House of Commons, sent shock waves through the governing party.
Questions about Trudeau's future have swirled since support for his party began to tumble in 2023. The Liberals have trailed the Conservatives by more than 20 points for more than a year.
Trudeau said he asked for Parliament to be prorogued because the House of Commons has been paralyzed for months through obstruction and needs a reset. This move will shutter the House for two months, wipe clear the current slate of legislation and delay any opportunities for non-confidence votes that could trigger an election until it resumes in spring.
"It's time for the temperature to come down, for people to have a fresh start in Parliament to be able to navigate through these complex times domestically and internationally," he said.
"Removing me from the equation as the leader who will fight the next election for the Liberal party should also decrease the level of polarization we're seeing right now in the House and in Canadian politics."
Trudeau said he asked Liberal party president Sachit Mehra Sunday night to immediately launch a leadership race ahead of the next election.
Mehra could not be reached for an interview, but said in a statement he will call a national board meeting this week to begin the "nation-wide democratic process of selecting a new leader of the party." A concluding date for that race has not yet been set.
The spotlight now will be cast on long-suspected leadership aspirants such as Freeland, Finance Minister Dominic LeBlanc, former central banker Mark Carney, Foreign Minister Mélanie Joly, Industry Minister François-Philippe Champagne and a cast of others.
Candidates will have to scramble to launch speedy campaigns as they jostle under tight time constraints to organize and claim the mantle as the best to take on popular firebrand Conservative leader Pierre Poilievre.
Liberal MP Wayne Long, who had agitated for Trudeau to leave, said the prime minister could have figured out that he had passed his political expiration date a lot sooner. But his decision to finally step aside marks day-one of the party's rebuild and will give the party a fighting chance in the next election.
"This shouldn't be a Pierre Poilievre coronation," he said.
While the next election must be held by this October, spring or early summer are much more likely given the precarious minority Parliament that has all three main opposition parties pronouncing they're ready to bring the government down in a confidence vote.
Poilievre sought to cast Trudeau's move as a desperate political play by a sputtering Liberal party, whose MPs stood by their leader right up until he cratered in the polls and was no longer a viable candidate.
"Their only objection is that he is no longer popular enough to win an election and keep them in power," Poilievre said in a statement.
Trudeau's decision comes two weeks before Donald Trump is sworn back into office as president of the United States and Trudeau will remain at the helm during what is expected to be a rocky start to Trump's second term in the Oval Office. Trump has threatened to impose steep import tariffs on all Canadian goods the day he is inaugurated.
The Canadian Press

Reaction to Justin Trudeau's decision to step down
"Canadians desperate to turn the page on this dark chapter in our history might be relieved today that Justin Trudeau is finally leaving. But what has really changed? Every Liberal MP in power today and every potential Liberal leadership contender fighting for the top job helped Justin Trudeau break the country over the last nine years."
Conservative Leader
Pierre Poilievre
"The problem is not just Justin Trudeau. It's every minister that's been calling the shots. It's every Liberal MP that looked down their nose at Canadians who are worried about high costs or crumbling health care. The Liberals do not deserve another chance, no matter who is the leader."
NDP Leader Jagmeet Singh
"There is no significant difference between the Liberal party that we know and the Liberal party that will be presented to us after there's a new leader."
Bloc Québécois Leader
Yves-François Blanchet
"President-elect Trump continues to make real threats about imposing sweeping tariffs that would devastate Canada's economy. In two weeks, the president-elect will be sworn in as America's next president and will have every opportunity to make his threats real. Between now and then, the federal government needs to do everything humanly possible to avoid these tariffs, including by doing more to secure our border and offering a credible plan to invest more in Canada's military to meet and exceed our NATO spending commitments."
Ontario Premier Doug Ford
"I thank Justin Trudeau for his years of service to Canada and Canadians. I wish him and his family the very best."
Chrystia Freeland, former
Liberal deputy prime minister
The Canadian Press
CAPTION: Prime Minister Justin Trudeau speaks with media outside Rideau Cottage Monday as he announced his resignation. Prime Minister Justin Trudeau announces his resignation as Liberal leader and prime minister outside Rideau Cottage in Ottawa on Monday.Adrian Wyld The Canadian PressSean KilpatrickThe Canadian Press

CREDIT: Kyle Duggan The Canadian Press

Document 407 of 500

Trudeau had no choice but to resign

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/trudeau-had-no-choice-resign/docview/3152068049/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=unknown&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Trudeau+had+no+choice+but+to+resign&amp;title=The+Spectator&amp;issn=11899417&amp;date=2025-01-07&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.10&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Spectator&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: No one is surprised today that Prime Minister Justin Trudeau has stepped down. Some are chagrined that it took so long. Some will be angry for the same reason. Some will gloat that their attacks have brought down the sitting prime minister. But no one should be surprised.
For a year there have been voices in the commentariat sounding the death knell of his term in office. Pollsters have been breathless in their assessments of who could carry the Liberal Party of Canada to something less than the devastating defeat that would be a sure thing with Trudeau at the helm.
Among Liberals there has been hand-wringing and confusion. Those of us who believe in what Trudeau and his government have accomplished have wanted to continue to support his vision. With all his flaws, and as with most of us human creatures, there are flaws, Trudeau's view of the world, his optimism and his belief in the possibility of a country that is inclusive and caring stands in stark contrast to the mean, fear-mongering often dishonest, if not delusional rhetoric of Pierre Poilievre. But that support for Trudeau's leadership has been harder and harder to sustain in the face of such overwhelming public antipathy.
When Chrystia Freeland broke with the government, it was over for his leadership. The one thing this prime minister, or any leader, cannot survive is the loss of support of his team.
I have really only known Trudeau personally since 2013 when we both became leaders of our parties. But I feel like I have known him much longer. I was 18 years old when Justin was born that Christmas Day in 1971. He belonged to the country as the newborn son of the prime minister and his birth was cause for happy celebration.
I believe Trudeau lived his entire life with a sense of obligation to do what he could to make the country better and to make the lives of Canadians healthier and happier. I believe he entered politics, not because he felt entitled to be prime minister, but because he really believed he could use his experience, his positive energy and yes, his name to do some good.
There is no shortage of cynicism about the man at this moment. But when I heard the clip of him saying to his colleagues and staff at their December holiday party that he loved them, I took it at face value. These are people he has worked closely with. They have sat with him in the House of Commons as he has been attacked and hammered by the Opposition every day for months. Every commentator across the country, including the Liberals in that category, has been relentlessly critical. These people have been his team and there is affection mingled with pain. Once he lost their support, he had no choice. He had to step down.
Do I wish for Justin Trudeau, the man, that he had left earlier before he had to face the public humiliation of losing his team? Sure, but he had to make this decision himself.
Trudeau changed forever the nature of the relationship between Indigenous and non-Indigenous people in Canada. He ended the debate about national child care. He moved boldly on climate action. He kept the vision of green investment real bringing recalcitrant provinces along. He lifted millions of children out of poverty. There is a long list of actions taken by his government that have had positive, life-changing impacts. None of them is perfect but they are life-affirming, optimistic endeavours.
His desire to fight the bleak vision of Canada offered by Poilievre was understandable. He knows better than anyone what is at risk.
My circumstance in 2018 when we lost so badly in Ontario was different, of course. I was at the end of only my first term but our government had been in office for 15 years. When I stepped down from leadership of the Party on the night of the election, there was no choice then, either. None of that softened the feeling on my part of having let my team down. Nor did it take away my concern about what was in store for Ontario.
Today is a rough day for Justin Trudeau, for his children and family and for his team. Those who have been cheering on his demise will have a good day, no doubt, but in my experience, it is wise to be measured in both the jubilance in victory and the despair in loss. Neither lasts forever.
Kathleen Wynne was Ontario's first woman premier and
was leader of the province's Liberal Party from 2013 to 2018.

Document 408 of 500

Six stories of political intrigue to follow in Haldimand-Norfolk: New councillors for Haldimand and a grudge match on the provincial ballot

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/six-stories-political-intrigue-follow-haldimand/docview/3152068015/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Six+stories+of+political+intrigue+to+follow+in+Haldimand-Norfolk%3A+New+councillors+for+Haldimand+and+a+grudge+match+on+the+provincial+ballot&amp;title=The+Spectator&amp;issn=11899417&amp;date=2025-01-07&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.4&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Spectator&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: The political scene in Haldimand and Norfolk got a little wild during the COVID-19 pandemic, and the new year promises plenty of intrigue.
Here are six political situations along Lake Erie's northern shore to keep an eye on as the calendar flips to 2025.
Brady versus Martin for MPP
A political showdown is looming in Haldimand-Norfolk as the next provincial election will feature incumbent independent MPP Bobbi Ann Brady squaring off against Conservative challenger Amy Martin, currently the mayor of Norfolk County.
The two have been feuding for months in an increasingly public manner as Brady complained about being sidelined from meetings with Conservative ministers visiting the riding, and from the county's delegations with the province at the Association of Municipalities of Ontario conference.
Martin's announcement in November sparked backlash among some local Conservatives because there was no open nomination meeting held locally, creating the perception that the mayor was appointed by Premier Doug Ford in what Brady called "the demise of democracy."
Brady took out then-Haldimand mayor Ken Hewitt when she won in 2022. Can she fend off another local mayor hand-picked by the Conservative party machine?
What's next for Nanticoke?
In late November, the province floated Nanticoke in Haldimand as a possible location for a future gas or nuclear power plant to meet Ontario's growing energy demand. The plant would be built on the site of the former coal-fired plant run by Ontario Power Generation.
Brady and Mayor Shelley Ann Bentley like the idea - and the new jobs they say the project would bring to the county.
The announcement adds a new wrinkle to Empire Communities' plan to build 15,000 homes on land ringing Nanticoke's Stelco steelworks. The previous Haldimand council asked the province for a minister's zoning order (MZO) to rezone the land around the steelworks from industrial to residential in order to start reviewing the proposal. That application was denied this year when the province changed the rules. Over objections from Bentley - she and Brady publicly oppose putting houses within a provincially designated industrial park - the current council gave its blessing to the developers to reapply for an MZO. Empire says it is still interested in applying, but will they follow through?
A challenger for Lewis?
Avid watchers of question period will have spotted Haldimand-Norfolk MP Leslyn Lewis occupying a prominent spot on the front bench near Conservative Leader Pierre Poilievre.
Lewis has been less visible in the riding, where some voters are still smarting that a so-called "parachute candidate" was appointed by the party brass - sidelining at least one local would-be Conservative candidate.
Lewis has twice ran for the party leadership and continues to grab headlines nationwide, including last year by sponsoring a petition in the House of Commons calling on Canada to leave the United Nations and affiliated agencies like the World Health Organization.
With an election coming in 2025 - perhaps sooner than later - will a challenger push Lewis on her local bona fides and make it more of a race in this typically safe Conservative riding?
New councillors for Haldimand
Two seats are empty around Haldimand County's council table after the deaths of Ward 4 representative Marie Trainer and Stewart Patterson from Ward 1.
Trainer, Haldimand's former mayor, died on Oct. 31 due to complications from a car crash several weeks prior, while Patterson succumbed to illness in late November.
With just under two years left in the current term of council, the vacant seats must be filled. In December, council voted to hold byelections rather than appoint replacement councillors, meaning voters in the two wards will go to the polls in the spring.
Shot in the arm for health unit
At the invitation of the provincial Ministry of Health, the health units responsible for Haldimand-Norfolk and Brantford-Brant will merge in the new year. The leaders of the respective boards of health tout greater efficiency, closer collaboration and better patient care for residents. But some city officials in Brantford worry the new deal will mean expanded services for rural residents while city taxpayers get stuck with the bill.
Grand Erie Public Health, as the merged organization is known, will be overseen by a board consisting of representatives from Brant, Haldimand and Norfolk counties, the city of Brantford and several provincial appointees. This is a plus for Haldimand, which had no formal decision-making power on Haldimand-Norfolk's board of health despite contributing 40 per cent of the funding.
It remains to be seen who will run the merged health unit as medical officer of health, and whether the senior executive ranks of the participating health units will be thinned. Haldimand-Norfolk is in the midst of crafting a new strategy to address high rates of substance abuse, including among local youth, a challenge familiar to health officials in Brantford-Brant.
Norfolk thirsty for water money
Decision time looms for how Norfolk will improve its aging and leaky water system. Council has endorsed a $450-million plan to shutter the county's five existing water treatment plants and pipe water to Norfolk's urban areas from a large treatment plan in Nanticoke.
The interurban water system would also supply towns in Haldimand and even reach Six Nations of the Grand River. Political leaders in those communities are on board, but they are not awash with cash. So the province and Ottawa need to turn on the funding taps for the project to stay afloat.
Norfolk County Mayor Amy Martin argues the money will be spent anyway to shore up the current facilities, and the interurban plan would put more users on municipal water and ease water bills that are among Ontario's highest. That would be welcome news for a sparsely populated rural county already drowning in debt.

What to expect from 2025's CES showcase: Just about every major exhibitor will be talking AI at annual Consumer Electronics Show in Las Vegas

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/what-expect-2025s-ces-showcase/docview/3152067970/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=What+to+expect+from+2025%27s+CES+showcase%3A+Just+about+every+major+exhibitor+will+be+talking+AI+at+annual+Consumer+Electronics+Show+in+Las+Vegas&amp;title=The+Spectator&amp;issn=11899417&amp;date=2025-01-07&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.12&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Spectator&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: NEW YORK CES, the annual trade show of all things tech, is upon us.
The multi-day event, organized by the Consumer Technology Association, kicks off this week in Las Vegas - where swaths of the latest gadgets and advances across industries like personal tech, transportation, health care, sustainability and more are set to be on display. And, like last year, artificial intelligence is everywhere you look.
CES 2024 saw more than 138,000 attendees, according to the CTA, and organizers expect to see at least that amount again for this year's show. More than 4,500 exhibitors, including 1,400 startups, are also anticipated across 2.5 million net square feet of floor space this week.
"These are the people getting together, focusing on solving some of the world's biggest problems - and magic occurs," Gary Shapiro, CEO and vice-chair of the CTA, told the Associated Press ahead of this week's show.
The AP spoke with Shapiro about what to expect for CES 2025:
CES 2025 is here. What are the main themes we can expect this week?
CES 2025 will be an exceptional leap forward in terms of innovation and technology, with a focus on a variety of themes important to the future of humanity. Over 4,000 companies will be here, on literally miles and miles of exhibit space. And they'll be showing solutions to fundamental human problems - like access to food, clean air, clean water and mobility, as well as health-care technology.
We also have newer categories. We're seeing more technology for the disability community, for example. Products that can serve people with disabilities had a large presence last year, and we expect that to be even bigger this year. Overall, the show is serving humanity and using technology and innovation to fill in gaps where we need a lot more work.
AI is everywhere again this year. Are there any specific trends we should keep an eye out for?
Generative AI is affecting virtually every area. Just about every major exhibitor will be talking about AI in one form or another at CES this week. For businesses, the focus is more on enterprise and productivity. But for individuals, it's about personalization.
We'll see exhibitors, such as PC companies, focusing on personalization in new products for the PC and laptop marketplace. And then there's AI being used in mobility - and not just in features for cars and self-driving. It's for all sorts of vehicles, including agricultural products from companies like Caterpillar and John Deere.
What kind of oversight is there for the gadgets and products we'll see at CES this week?
The industry representing the companies involved welcomes government regulation. The government's job is to say this is what is unsafe. And so the companies know what is legal and what is not. At the same time, a big challenge is if government stops innovation - so the question is for every country is finding that balance.
How could policies from the incoming Trump administration - like proposed tariffs on foreign goods - impact consumer products like those seen at CES?
We expect the Trump administration to take a much more pro-business approach that favours innovation, including a focus on all sorts of energy sources, which is very important. But tariffs are taxes. And we know that they are inflationary and paid for by consumers.
If we go forward with the proposals that the president-elect has indicated he may put in place, there would be severe impacts on the prosperity of the U.S. It depends on how exactly these tariffs are adopted but under some of the proposals, we estimate a range of a 30 to 50 per cent price increases for basic products like PCs, cellphones, laptops, tablets, smartwatches and all sorts of different products that consumers get a great value on today. That means that fewer people will be able to access these products. And, of course, other countries may then retaliate against U.S. exports. We don't want to go in that direction.
The Associated Press
CAPTION: Companies like John Deere and Caterpillar will be detailing new artificial intelligence features coming to their agricultural vehicles atthis year's Consumer Electronics Show.Abbie ParrThe Associated Press

Document 410 of 500

A timeline of Justin Trudeau's career: From a meteoric rise to prime minister to his resignation as the Liberals' unpopular leader

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/timeline-justin-trudeaus-career-meteoric-rise/docview/3151865650/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=A+timeline+of+Justin+Trudeau%27s+career%3A+From+a+meteoric+rise+to+prime+minister+to+his+resignation+as+the+Liberals%27+unpopular+leader&amp;title=The+Hamilton+Spectator+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2025-01-06&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Hamilton+Spectator+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: He brought the Liberal party back from the brink and now leaves it as it teeters on the edge.
Justin Trudeau's long political life spans nearly two decades - he's policies that change how we live, for better or for worse; overseen international and national unrest; and been wrapped in several scandals, some of which he has escaped, and others that have dragged him down.
As Trudeau closes this chapter in his political career, as Liberal party leader and prime minister, here's the legacy he's leaving behind, from the highs to the lows.
His early career and first electionSince birth, Trudeau's life has revolved around politics. He was born during his father's first tenure in office and was just a young boy as Pierre Trudeau oversaw key moments in Canadian history, including the 1980 Quebec referendum and the patriation of the Constitution.
The younger Trudeau was just 12 when his father took his fabled "walk in the snow," deciding to step down while facing a substantial deficit in the polls against future Progressive Conservative prime minister Brian Mulroney.
Trudeau faced a pair of family tragedies in quick succession as a young man - first the death of his younger brother Michel in 1998 and the death of his father in 2000.
He steadily became more involved in politics, until he finally entered the foray in 2007, successfully winning the Liberal nomination for Papineau.
"I am carrying the Trudeau name, yes. I am also carrying my own name," Trudeau told reporters after his nomination.
Trudeau won the seat in 2008, defeating a Bloc Québécois incumbent. Just three years into his time in Parliament, the 2011 election plummeted the Liberals to third-party status under the leadership of Michael Ignatieff, while Trudeau retained his seat.
2013 - Leader of the LiberalsIgnatieff resigned shortly thereafter, replaced on an interim basis by former Ontario premier Bob Rae. Rae had mulled a leadership bid of his own, but bowed out, paving the way for Trudeau to step into the spotlight.
He won the leadership race handily with more than 80 per cent of the vote, defeating Joyce Murray and Martha Hall Findlay.
"Let us be clear-eyed about what we have accomplished. We have worked hard and we have had a great campaign. We are united, hopeful and resolute in our purpose," Trudeau said after his victory.
"But know this: we have won nothing more and nothing less than the opportunity to work even harder."
2015-16 - The honeymoon phaseIn the 2015 election, the new Trudeau Liberals defeated Stephen Harper's Conservatives and kept Thomas Mulcair's New Democrats at bay. He reduced Harper's majority government by 67 seats, winning a majority, and was seen as part of a new generation of young, liberal leaders around the Western world, following Barack Obama in the U.S. and preceding Emmanuel Macron in France.
After he took office, Trudeau unveiled Canada's first gender-balanced cabinet, promising a transparent government built upon "evidence-based policy." He elevated mainstay MPs that have survived several years in Trudeau's cabinet, including Dominic LeBlanc, Mélanie Joly and, until recently, Chrystia Freeland.
In his first full year, Trudeau championed progressive policy - introducing a steeper tax bracket for the highest-income Canadians; remaking the Canada Child Benefit, which dramatically cut child poverty; and pouring hundreds of billions into infrastructure projects.
Also in 2016, Trudeau introduced his government's national carbon-pricing plan, and gave provinces two years to develop their own pricing scheme or face one imposed by Ottawa.
The Trudeau government also set into motion the controversial legalization of medical assistance in dying (MAID) and the legalization of cannabis.
Trudeau also ran on the promise of ending Canada's first-past-the-post voting system - a promise that never came to fruition.
2017 - ScandalJustin Trudeau made it two years into office before his first major scandal. He had accepted a vacation to the private island of Prince Shah Karim Al Hussaini, Aga Khan IV, in December 2016. Aga Khan, on top of being one of the world's richest royals, also presided over a network of charities, including one that received millions from the Canadian government.
A scathing report by the ethics commissioner found that Trudeau had violated multiple conflict laws when he accepted the Aga Khan vacation.
"(The report) makes it very clear I should have taken precautions and cleared my family vacation and dealings with the Aga Khan in advance," a contrite Trudeau said at a press conference after the report's release. "I'm sorry I didn't."
2018 - The first tariff warThe Trudeau government faced down a major spat with the U.S., when President Donald Trump launched a series of tariffs on Canadian goods after negotiations on the North American Free Trade Agreement stalled. Trudeau, during what he called a "turning point" for Canada-U. S. relations, retaliated with targeted tariffs of his own. At a stalemate, the two countries, as well as Mexico, renegotiated the North American Free Trade Agreement into the current US-Mexico-Canada Agreement.
2019 - The SNC-Lavalin scandalThe next scandal had much larger political consequences. In early 2019, the Globe and Mail reported that aides in Trudeau's office had leaned on then-attorney general Jody Wilson-Raybould for a deferred prosecution agreement against SNC-Lavalin amidst charges of corruption and fraud for business dealings in Libya. SNC-Lavalin had been a big political donor to the Liberals, and less so to other political parties.
Wilson-Raybould resigned from cabinet days after the story came out and was subsequently ousted from the Liberal caucus. Shortly after, Trudeau's principal secretary, Gerald Butts, resigned, though maintained that nobody in the prime minister's office pressured Wilson-Raybould. Finally, then Treasury Board president Jane Philpott resigned from her post in a shocking announcement, saying she had lost confidence in the way the Trudeau government had handled the SNC-Lavalin affair.
The federal ethics watchdog found Trudeau had broken the law, reporting that the PM and his staff had leaned on Wilson-Raybould with interventions that were "tantamount to political direction."
The report dropped just a month before the start of the 2019 federal election campaign; the Liberals remained in power, but were reduced to a minority government for the first time under Trudeau's leadership.
Another bombshell during the 2019 election - an image of Justin Trudeau dressed in blackface from 2001 - surfaced, drawing worldwide condemnation. Trudeau expressed deep regret and called his own actions "racist."
2020 - The pandemicAs the world shut down amidst the spread of the COVID-19 pandemic, the Trudeau government struggled to handle the virus' economic impact.
His government rolled out a series of benefits - including the Canada Emergency Response Benefit, the Canada Emergency Wage Subsidy and the Canada Emergency Student Benefit - to help steady the incomes of Canadians and waived student loan payments.
The federal government also oversaw increased funding for coronavirus research, international travel restrictions and self-isolation orders. And, in 2020, the Trudeau government negotiated deals with Pfizer and Moderna to secure a supply of COVID-19 vaccines.
In the background, another political scandal rocked the Trudeau government. In August 2020, then-finance minister Bill Morneau resigned during the WE Charity scandal after it was alleged that the charity had funded two Morneau family vacations, and had ties to Trudeau's family as well.
Trudeau was later cleared of any conflicts of interest, and Morneau was partially cleared.
2022 - The trucker convoyResentments against the Trudeau government over pandemic lockdowns manifested themselves in the 2022 "Freedom Convoy" protests - what initially began as protests against a vaccination mandate for cross-border truckers that resulted in a weeks-long illegal occupation of Ottawa by hundreds of semi-trucks that blocked key streets and access points. The border-crossing city of Windsor was also subject to blockades.
The protests saw about 3,000 big rigs and other trucks, alongside tens of thousands of other protesters, converge on Ottawa that January, and then remain, disrupting day-to-day life outside Parliament.
The city of Ottawa and the provincial government each declared states of emergency near the start of February. Then, after more than two weeks of occupation, Trudeau's government took drastic action, enacting the Emergencies Act to end the protests. It allowed the government to freeze the bank accounts of protesters and barred others from joining in.
Ottawa's police chief later resigned over criticism of how he handled the protests. Meanwhile, Trudeau's use of the Emergencies Act would trigger legal challenges and criticism for years onward.
2023 - Personal and global turmoilIn May, special rapporteur David Johnston released his first report on foreign interference in Canadian politics, following up on allegations that China meddled in the country's 2019 and 2021 elections. The former governor general, selected by Trudeau, ruled out a public inquiry but promised to hold his own public hearings instead - sparking backlash and skepticism.
Johnston would resign from his role a month later, citing the "highly partisan atmosphere" around his work.
In August, Trudeau and his wife Sophie Grégoire announced their separation after 18 years of marriage. She would open up about their separation in interviews the next year, telling Vogue: "It takes two to tango and I think we both acknowledge that."
Later that year, Hamas-led militants carried out the Oct. 7 attacks on Israel, killing nearly 1,200 and taking more than 250 hostages. Israel retaliated by launching an invasion of the Gaza Strip, initiating the ongoing Israel-Hamas war.
In response, Trudeau condemned Hamas and vowed to support Israel and its right to self-defence. His government also pledged millions in humanitarian aid to both Israel and Gaza, although he would abstain from a UN resolution calling for a ceasefire later that month.
In December, Canada alongside Australia and New Zealand would make its first call for a ceasefire. It also supported a UN resolution calling for a "humanitarian ceasefire" in the region.
As a year-end policy drop, the government also announced the rolling out of its dental care program, fulfilling a promise that was part of its governing agreement with the NDP.
2024 - A steady declineLast year started off rocky for Trudeau's Liberals after the Federal Court of Appeal ruled his use of the Emergencies Act to quell the so-called "Freedom Convoy" protests infringed on constitutional rights. The government vowed to appeal the decision.
Later in 2024, the Trudeau government unveiled a national pharmacare program, which currently covers diabetes medication and contraceptives.
Then, in September, the NDP ended their confidence and supply agreement with the Liberals, resulting in a volley of three failed no-confidence motions in September, October and December. Despite pulling out of the deal, the NDP toppled each of the motions.
While it supported the Liberals in the first two votes, the Bloc Québécois eventually vowed to topple Trudeau's party at the first opportunity after it failed to meet its demands to boost pension payments and protect supply management for dairy producers.
The latter months of 2024 saw a wave of high-profile resignations from Trudeau's cabinet, including Transport Minister Pablo Rodriguez on Sept. 19 to run for leadership of the Quebec Liberal party and Employment Minister Randy Boissonnault on Nov. 20, following revelations that he allegedly falsely claimed to be Indigenous.
At a tense October caucus meeting, Liberal MPs confronted Trudeau on his leadership as the party slumped in the polls.
Then, on Dec. 15, Housing Minister Sean Fraser announced he will not be running in the next federal election, citing a need to spend more time with his family.
The very next day, former finance minister and deputy Prime Minister Chrystia Freeland resigned from cabinet, hours before she was due to deliver the fall economic statement.
In a blunt letter posted to social media, Freeland addressed disagreements she had with Trudeau regarding costly "political gimmicks" and revealed the prime minister had planned to pull her from the finance portfolio.
Her departure opened the floodgates for further calls for Trudeau's resignation within his own caucus as public opinion polls showed the Grits trailing far behind the Tories, led by Pierre Poilievre.
2025 - Stepping downDays before Trudeau's resignation, the Star learned Freeland had been calling around to Liberal MPs, raising speculation from insiders that she was mulling a leadership run of her own. Former Bank of Canada governor Mark Carney also called dozens of Liberal MPs as he considers a leadership bid.
Around the same time, MP and former minister of public safety Marco Mendicino became the latest Liberal to announce they're not running again; he had represented Eglinton-Lawrence since 2015.
On Jan. 6, Trudeau received approval from Governor General Mary Simon to prorogue Parliament and announced he would be stepping down as prime minister and Liberal leader.
CAPTION: Then-newly elected MP for the Liberal Party, Justin Trudeau arrives victorious with his wife Sophie after winning in the riding of Papineau in an Oct. 14, 2008, file photo.

Document 411 of 500

Justin Trudeau promises to resign as prime minister and Liberal leader after leadership race

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/justin-trudeau-promises-resign-as-prime-minister/docview/3151865626/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Justin+Trudeau+promises+to+resign+as+prime+minister+and+Liberal+leader+after+leadership+race&amp;title=The+Hamilton+Spectator+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2025-01-06&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Hamilton+Spectator+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: OTTAWA- Embattled by internal party strife and under attack by political opponents, Prime Minister Justin Trudeau told Canadians Monday he will resign as prime minister and Liberal leader after the party chooses his successor sometime in the next few months.
Trudeau, in a nationally televised news conference, said that although his instinct was to stay on in the face of what he called a "critical moment" for the country, the internal battles in his own party that had flared into a crisis in the past three weeks made that impossible.
"My friends, as you all know, I am a fighter. Every bone in my body has always told me to fight because I care deeply about Canadians. I care deeply about this country," he said.
Yet he realized he could no longer continue, telling his family Sunday night he would step down, and announcing Monday in a sombre tone: "I intend to resign as party leader, as prime minister after the party selects its next leader through a robust nationwide competitive process."
For now, Trudeau will lead the government for another two-and-a-half months or more, asTrump assumes the U.S. presidency with threats of crippling tariffs on all Canadian products.
Trudeau met with Gov. Gen. Mary Simon early Monday morning and received her approval to prorogue - or suspend - Parliament until March 24, citing the inability to conduct normal parliamentary business because of Conservative filibustering tactics, he told reporters.
He rejected suggestions that it was anti-democratic or unfair not to call an immediate election, saying Parliament needs a "reset."
But he also acknowledged the obvious: so does the Liberal party.
Trudeau has faced a growing caucus mutiny since the Liberals lost key by-elections in the past six months, and his ill-fated decision to fire Chrystia Freeland as finance minister in December triggered a flood of new calls by dozens of Liberal MPs for him to step down.
"This country deserves a real choice in the next election, and it has become clear to me that if I am having to fight internal battles, I cannot be the best option in that election," he said.
In response to Trudeau's announcement, Trump took to his social media platform Truth Social to taunt the prime minister and Canadians, saying his 25 per cent tariff threat and vow to ensure Canada paid a price to the U.S. drove Trudeau to act.
U.S. president-elect Donald Trump reacts to Trudeau's plan to resign
"Justin Trudeau knew this, and resigned," Trump wrote, repeating his claim that Canada would be better off as a 51st state. "If Canada merged with the U.S., there would be no Tariffs, taxes would go way down, and they would be TOTALLY SECURE from the threat of the Russian and Chinese Ships that are constantly surrounding them."
Trudeau declined to explain why he had wanted to shift Freeland out of finance last month, saying only that she rejected his appeal to remain as deputy prime minister and take on a key role in charge of the Canada-U.S. file, and wouldn't dish on their "private" conversations.
Her dramatic departure deeply shook Trudeau to the point where he had wanted to step down that week but, according to two sources with knowledge of the discussions, was persuaded not to take any immediate decision about his future and take time to reflect over the holidays.
In the end, Trudeau came to the same conclusion: that his time was up.
He will not ask for an interim leader to be installed, saying prorogation allows "time for the temperature to come down, for people to have a fresh start in Parliament and to be able to navigate through these complex times both domestically and internationally."
It is not yet clear when a vote to replace him will take place, nor whether any of his current cabinet ministers on the front lines of the Canada-U.S. tensions will have to step aside if they want to vie for the leadership.
Liberal party sources say that a leadership election committee to be named this week by the party's board has up to 26 days to set rules for a race, including whether sitting cabinet ministers can compete. The prime minister also can have a say in that, but on Monday he deferred to the party.
The ultimate leadership selection date depends on several factors, including venue availability. The party has discretion to adapt other rules affecting timing, sources said.
Exiting more than 15 minutes late for his announcement in front of cameras - and several months too late in the eyes of his critics - Trudeau quipped that he'd "wing it" as his speech notes flew off a podium outside his home at Rideau Cottage. It was the same spot where he used to address the nation during the COVID-19 pandemic back when his approval ratings soared.
On this bitterly cold Ottawa day, a teleprompter rolled out his script for why he'd asked for the minority Parliament to be suspended until a new Liberal leader is picked.
The House of Commons has moved little legislation forward in months due to a privilege debate that ground almost all other business to a halt. Trudeau said it was time to break the logjam.
"Despite best efforts to work through it, Parliament has been paralyzed for months after what has been the longest session of a minority Parliament in Canadian history," he said.
Removing him from the equation should reduce the polarization in Canadian politics, Trudeau added. He insisted Canadians will see that the Conservative plan for Canada is a mistake.
"Pierre Poilievre's vision for this country is not the right one for Canadians," he said. "We need an ambitious, optimistic view of the future, and Pierre Poilievre is not offering that."
Would-be contenders for Trudeau's job including Freeland, former Bank of Canada governor Mark Carney and former B.C. premier Christy Clark took to social media to issue perfunctory thanks to Trudeau for his "service" to the country and Canadians. "I wish him and his family the very best," Freeland posted on X.
Trudeau's other political rivals reacted quickly.
Poilievre said in a recorded video and statement that there should be an immediate election, charging that "Liberals want to protect their pensions and pay cheques by sweeping their hated leader under the rug months before an election to trick you, and then do it all over again."
Bloc Québécois Leader Yves-François Blanchet said while it is not a bad idea to suspend Parliament during a Liberal leadership race, the country needs an election as soon as possible. NDP Leader Jagmeet Singh said the Liberals do not deserve another chance at governing, no matter who their leader is.
"They should get fired," Singh said.
Was Justin Trudeau the Liberals' only problem? We're about to find out
Only Green Party Leader Elizabeth May, who once sat with Trudeau on the opposition backbenches, spoke kindly and personally, saying Trudeau struggled with the decision to contest the party leadership in 2013 "weighing how it might impact his personal life. He was being recruited as someone who could rescue the Liberals" at a time when a majority win "was not a likelihood. If anyone knew what it would mean for his children if he were to become PM - as the child of a PM - Justin Trudeau knew."
Premier Doug Ford, responding to Trudeau's news, said in the next two weeks before Trump's tariffs turn into a reality, "the federal government needs to do everything humanly possible to avoid these tariffs, including by doing more to secure our border and offering a credible plan to invest more in Canada's military to meet and exceed our NATO spending commitments."
The Liberal government will continue to direct Canada's response to Trump.
For now, prorogation means Parliament will not return as scheduled at the end of January, and kills any legislation currently before the House of Commons. It will force the government to reopen March 24 with a throne speech when the members return, which will require a confidence vote in the days that follow in the Commons.
Assessing his own legacy, Trudeau said he regretted he had not brought in electoral reform, as promised in 2015. Yet he believes he leaves with a solid record of helping the middle class.
"We reduced their taxes. We increased the benefits to families. We made sure the economy was focused on working for everyone and not just a few," he said. "That has dropped poverty rates in Canada, that has brought more people into the workforce, that has moved us forward on reconciliation."
Politically, Trudeau is leaving his party with the Liberals more than 20 points behind the Conservatives in almost all opinion polls.
Who will replace Justin Trudeau as Liberal party leader? Here are some likely contenders
Pressed to say whether he thought another Liberal leader can succeed in defeating Poilievre in the next campaign, Trudeau said "absolutely," a new Liberal leader will have an opportunity to turn the party's fortunes around.
"A new prime minister and leader of the Liberal party will carry its values and ideals into that next election. I'm excited to see the process unfold in the months ahead.
CAPTION: Trudeau said his instinct was to stay on, but the internal battles in his own party made that impossible. For the time being, he said he intends to stay on as Liberal party leader and prime minister until a new leader is chosen.Prime Minister Justin Trudeau makes an announcement outside Rideau Cottage in Ottawa.Prime Minister Justin Trudeau makes his way through the foyer outside the House of Commons in December.Justin Trudeau is planning on speaking today from Rideau Cottage.Prime Minister Justin Trudeau announces his resignation as Liberal leader and prime minister outside Rideau Cottage.Justin Trudeau warns Canadians against Pierre Poilievre after announcing he plans to resign

Document 412 of 500

Former Burlington alderman George Whyte called 'the mayor of Kilbride'

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/former-burlington-alderman-george-whyte-called/docview/3151835932/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Former+Burlington+alderman+George+Whyte+called+%27the+mayor+of+Kilbride%27&amp;title=The+Hamilton+Spectator+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2025-01-06&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Hamilton+Spectator+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Former Burlington alderman George Whyte was colourful, outspoken and a fiscal hawk who was not afraid to stand up for what he believed in despite facing overwhelming opposition.
Known as "the mayor of Kilbride," Whyte - who died Oct. 26 at age 81 - led efforts in 1984 to get council to reconsider spending between $7.6 million and $8.4 million on the expansion of Burlington City Hall. In 1988, he backed introducing market value assessment immediately, instead of phasing it in, and said he wouldn't support the proposed Burlington Performing Arts Centre if it looked to be a drain on taxpayers.
"I am not able to sleep at night with what we've done so far," he said in 1984, during the final debate and vote on the city hall expansion project. "I'm not trying to stop city hall. I just think we're spending too much money."
On the MVA debate, he told councillors, "It's time we chew our gum or spit it out."
Council overwhelmingly backed the city hall expansion. In one vote, a motion he put forward lost 15-2 (this was when Burlington had a 17-member council). The phasing in of MVA was approved and the arts centre moved forward.
Whyte also took on Burlington MP Bill Kempling for the PC federal nomination in 1984. Whyte was upset about the control of signing up new memberships by the riding executive. Kempling had been MP since 1972. Whyte lost the attempt to dislodge Kempling, who served until 1993.
Whyte, a history teacher and developer, represented Ward 4 in north Burlington. He was elected in 1982 and acclaimed in 1985. He moved his family to the Dominican Republic to build a hotel-condo project and did not seek re-election in 1988. For a few months, he was commuting back and forth between Burlington and Puerto Plata to finish his term.
"I just want some common sense and frugality at city council," Whyte told The Spectator in 1984. "Let them save the taxpayers some money."
Former Ward 2 alderman and Spectator columnist Joan Little said Whyte was popular with his fellow councillors. She said his relocating to the Caribbean left a "big hole" on council.
"He was always upbeat and witty," Little recalled. "He was just a really good fellow. When he got off the tax stuff, he was just fun. He kept us in stitches all the time."
On social media, his former ward mate Barry Quinn said Whyte "enjoyed being the champion of rural Burlington. He tried so hard to have a covered bridge built over Bronte Creek at No. 2 Sideroad, near Appleby Line - it lost by one vote. It would have been a Burlington treasure."
George Herbert Whyte was born Aug. 5, 1943 to Herb and Violetta Whyte in Toronto. He was raised in Port Credit and attended Port Credit High School between 1957 and 1962. He attended Middlebury College in Vermont on a hockey scholarship from 1962-66 and then attended McGill University between 1966 and 1968, where he obtained a master's degree in Canadian and American history.
He and his first wife Helen travelled the world before he came back to Canada and taught on Manitoulin Island, Cambrian College in Sudbury and Hillfield Strathallan College in Hamilton. He and his family bought the former Kilbride School, built in 1876, and moved into the area in 1976. Before he ran for council he was involved in the restoration of two heritage homes and served on the Burlington Social Planning Council and was president of the North Burlington Tennis Club.
His family lived in a house on the Hillfield Strathallan campus before moving to Kilbride. He was fondly remembered at the school.
"He made history come alive for countless students," former colleague Michaele Robertson said on social media. "We were all richer for being in his orbit."
Whyte is survived by his partner Donna Haynes, children Andrew and Martin and two grandchildren. He is also survived by his first wife Helen.
Daniel Nolan can be reached at dannolanwrites@gmail.com
CAPTION: George Whyte was the colourful and outspoken alderman for Burlington's rural Ward 4 in the 1980s. He was often called "the mayor of Kilbride."George Whyte, in a 1983 photo, was the colourful alderman for Burlington's rural Ward 4 in the 1980s.

Document 413 of 500

Ontarians are hungry for an alternative to Doug Ford. Why isn't Bonnie Crombie providing?

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/ontarians-are-hungry-alternative-doug-ford-why/docview/3151553482/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Ontarians+are+hungry+for+an+alternative+to+Doug+Ford.+Why+isn%27t+Bonnie+Crombie+providing%3F&amp;title=The+Hamilton+Spectator+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2025-01-05&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Hamilton+Spectator+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Give Bonnie Crombie points for honesty.
In an interview with the Star's Rob Ferguson to mark the one-year anniversary of her victory in the Ontario Liberal leadership race, the former Mississauga mayor said she thought the new job "would be easier."
"It's been tough," she said.
To an outsider, confusingly so. At a time when incumbent governments across the democratic world are being tossed out on their collective ears, Crombie's Liberals have been unable to make a dent in Ontario Premier Doug Ford's popular support. The latest provincial poll gave the Progressive Conservatives a 16-point lead over the Liberals. Last December, it was a 13-point lead. This despite the fact that Ford himself has only a 34 per cent approval rating, lowest of any Canadian premier.
The PCs have kept that healthy cushion even as there have been controversies big and small: A greenbelt land swap that Ford later reversed, a police investigation into the same issue, the sudden closure of the Ontario Science Centre, and an out-of-nowhere plan to tear up bike-lane infrastructure, among others.
And while Prime Minister Justin Trudeau's federal government has gone to great lengths in recent weeks to prolong its life in fear of an electoral drubbing, the Ontario Premier is by all accounts keen to hold an election this coming spring, ahead of the planned 2026 date, so confident is he in another win.
Crombie, at the helm of a party that ruled the province for 15 straight years not that long ago, doesn't seem to scare him in the slightest.
It is, as mentioned earlier, a bit confusing. How did the Ontario Liberals get so bad at this?
There are some practical reasons for their struggles. With only nine MPPs in the legislature, the Liberals don't have official party status that would grant them additional staff and resources. And Crombie herself doesn't have a seat at Queen's Park, though she might if she had decided to run in any of the three provincial by-elections that were held this year. Choosing to sit on the sidelines while Ford sets the agenda undoubtedly makes Crombie's job harder.
It's also true that there are some branding issues. The federal Liberals are deeply unpopular, and have been for many months. People are furious at Trudeau over inflation, housing costs and other affordability issues, and the provincial Liberals are likely taking shrapnel from the grenades lobbed at the prime minster simply because they are also the red party.
But, if a provincial election will soon be in the offing, it's fair to wonder about Crombie's plan for it. Her first big policy plank was a "middle class" income-tax cut that would be combined with lopping the provincial portion of the HST off residential heating and hydro bills. Those sound like the kinds of things a Conservative government might do, and in fact Crombie promises to bring in the tax cuts that Ford has "failed to deliver." So, you want to do a better job of being Doug Ford? (As if to prove the point, she's also said she disapproves of the federal carbon tax.)
Crombie has more recently pledged to bring housing costs down by eliminating the land-transfer tax and development charges on certain home purchases and vowed to spend billions on recruiting and training more doctors to make up a provincial shortfall. As laudable as those initiatives might be, they came and went without much fanfare, underscoring the problem that Crombie has had in getting anyone to pay attention to her. Many, probably most, Ontarians are likely unaware of the job she holds. That seems like a significant problem if an election is only a couple of months away.
With the holiday season over, will Crombie try to seize the public's attention with some kind of bold promise or sweeping plan? The longer she waits, the closer she will get to an election in which she's forced to try to introduce herself to voters on the fly.
Being the rookie leader of the third-place party was never going to be easy. Crombie is correct on that count. But it also shouldn't have been quite this hard. With 2025 just around the corner, it's hard not to think that, for the once-mighty Ontario Liberals, 2024 was a wasted year.
CAPTION: Ontario Liberal leader Bonnie Crombie is seen at Queen's Park in November 2024.
CREDIT: Scott Stinson
