Document 414 of 500

MP refuses to let go of racism complaint against Hamilton police

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/mp-refuses-let-go-racism-complaint-against/docview/3151432614/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=MP+refuses+to+let+go+of+racism+complaint+against+Hamilton+police&amp;title=The+Hamilton+Spectator+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2025-01-04&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Hamilton+Spectator+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: More than eight years after police stopped to talk with Matthew Green - then a city councillor, now an MP - while he waited for a bus, the Human Rights Tribunal of Ontario (HRTO) has dismissed the racial profiling case the incident sparked.
"The Tribunal has been described as where human rights claims go to die," says Green, who is Black. "Ninety-three per cent of cases at this tribunal have been dismissed."
Tribunal Watch Ontario, a watchdog group, published a report in November that says an unprecedented 93 per cent of final decisions made by the HRTO in 2023 and 2024 were "jurisdictional or procedural" dismissals of applications.
The report pointed to years-long wait times for decisions, and says many cases were dismissed because the HRTO determined they were "abandoned" by applicants - after years of inertia.
Green says that nowhere in his complaints process has anyone sufficiently looked into the incident as being race-based.
The HRTO says it upholds the result of a Police Service Act hearing that found the officer who approached Green did nothing wrong and was merely concerned about his well-being. That disciplinary hearing did not directly examine if Green was targeted because of his race.
Green intends to appeal the HRTO's dismissal.
"I believe I have a responsibility, because of my power and privilege, to see this through," he says.
On April 26, 2016, a cold and rainy day, Green was a Hamilton city councillor.
He was standing at the edge of an underpass at the Claremont Access when police drove by.
Green says he was waiting for a bus, and was taking shelter until it arrived. He says police had no reason whatsoever to approach him.
Green says he was "arbitrarily and pointlessly stopped and questioned" because he is Black.
Hamilton police Const. Andrew Pfeifer says he and his partner saw Green partially concealed, standing in a mud puddle. He says he was concerned about Green's mental health because he wasn't wearing a coat.
At the time, Pfeifer was a member of the Crisis Outreach and Support Team (COAST).
When they spoke, Green was hostile and sarcastic, Pfeifer would later testify.
After asking Green if he was OK, Pfeifer says he told him to "have a good day" and left.
The entire exchange, according to the officer, took less than a minute. Another witness, however, says the exchange lasted about seven minutes.
Pfeifer says the interaction had nothing to do with race.
Neither officer knew they were speaking with Hamilton's first Black city councillor.
In April 2018, a Police Service Act hearing cleared Pfeifer of any wrongdoing.
Green says he felt humiliated by the encounter.
"I felt I was criminalized in my own community."
The following year, Green was elected as the NDP member of Parliament for Hamilton Centre.
Though Green applied to the HRTO in April 2017, alleging he was discriminated against by police because of his "race and colour," the matter was deferred until the end of the PSA hearing.
It took another six and a half years for the HRTO to resolve the matter - by dismissing it.
The HRTO held a one-day preliminary hearing by video conference last October.
On Nov. 27, it dismissed the case, saying it was properly dealt with at the police hearing and there is no need to "relitigate" it.
"The applicant (Green) is unhappy with the PSA ... decision, but it is not for the Tribunal to comment on whether the PSA hearing officer made the correct decision," HRTO vice-chair Joseph Tascona wrote. Conducting another hearing "risks inconsistent findings of fact or law bringing the administration of justice into disrepute to permit relitigation," he wrote.
Meanwhile, says Green, Hamilton police continue to face allegations of systemic racism. There have been several court decisions in which judges have tossed out cases because police violated the Charter rights of Black people, and an Indigenous man had his head stomped on by an officer during his arrest.
The Hamilton Police Service's own report last year indicated its officers disproportionately use force against Black people compared to other groups of people.
"There just seems to be an institutional refusal to take accountability for what is in fact a problem," says Green.
He points to the recent police fatal shooting of Erixon Kabera, a well-known member of Hamilton's Rwandan community.
That case is being investigated by the province's Special Investigations Unit and few details have been made public.
"Anti-Black racism within policing can be a death sentence for people," says Green.
Chief Frank Bergen, who was not yet chief on the day of the bus stop incident, did not hide his dismay that Green is publicly discussing the Kabera matter.
"I'm a little bit taken aback and I would caution anyone, including an elected official, not to speculate on the outcome in the middle of an SIU investigation."
He urged Green and others to wait until "the facts are accurately shared with the public, once the SIU investigation is complete."
Bergen says many improvements have been made since Green first filed his complaint about police.
"We are committed to equity, fairness and non-discriminatory policing and have recently established a community advisory panel to work alongside police on the development of our race identity strategy," he says. "We hire from the human race and therefore we also are making sure that we are supporting and training and educating our members to work alongside all members of the community."
Green, however, says his own complaint has never been fully aired, so he is left with no option but to appeal.
"I refuse to let go of this case."
CAPTION: Const. Andrew Pfeifer at his Police Act hearing in 2017. He was found not guilty.Councillor Matthew Green at the Police Act hearing concerning his complaint in 2017.

Subject: Racism; Court decisions; Race; Black people; Human rights; Racial profiling; Tribunals & commissions

Title: MP refuses to let go of racism complaint against Hamilton police

Publication title: The Hamilton Spectator (Online); Hamilton

Publication year: 2025

Publication date: Jan 4, 2025

Section: news/hamilton-region

Document 415 of 500

MP refuses to let go of racism complaint against police: Matthew Green says he was approached for being Black

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/mp-refuses-let-go-racism-complaint-against-police/docview/3151359040/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=MP+refuses+to+let+go+of+racism+complaint+against+police%3A+Matthew+Green+says+he+was+approached+for+being+Black&amp;title=The+Spectator&amp;issn=11899417&amp;date=2025-01-04&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.1&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Spectator&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: More than eight years after police stopped to talk with Matthew Green - then a city councillor, now an MP - while he waited for a bus, the Human Rights Tribunal of Ontario (HRTO) has dismissed the racial profiling case the incident sparked.
"The tribunal has been described as where human rights claims go to die," says Green, who is Black. "Ninety-three per cent of cases at this tribunal have been dismissed."
Tribunal Watch Ontario, a watchdog group, published a report in November that says an unprecedented 93 per cent of final decisions made by the HRTO in 2023 and 2024 were "jurisdictional or procedural" dismissals of applications.
The report pointed to years-long wait times for decisions, and says many cases were dismissed because the HRTO determined they were "abandoned" by applicants - after years of inertia.
Green says that nowhere in his complaints process has anyone sufficiently looked into the incident as being race-based.
The HRTO says it upholds the result of a Police Service Act hearing that found the officer who approached Green did nothing wrong and was merely concerned about his well-being. That disciplinary hearing did not directly examine if Green was targeted because of his race.
Green intends to appeal the HRTO's dismissal.
"I believe I have a responsibility, because of my power and privilege, to see this through," he says.
On April 26, 2016, a cold and rainy day, Green was a Hamilton city councillor.
He was standing at the edge of an underpass at the Claremont Access when police drove by.
Green says he was waiting for a bus, and was taking shelter until it arrived. He says police had no reason whatsoever to approach him.
Green says he was "arbitrarily and pointlessly stopped and questioned" because he is Black.
Hamilton police Const. Andrew Pfeifer says he and his partner saw Green partially concealed, standing in a mud puddle. He says he was concerned about Green's mental health because he wasn't wearing a coat.
At the time, Pfeifer was a member of the Crisis Outreach and Support Team (COAST).
When they spoke, Green was hostile and sarcastic, Pfeifer would later testify.
After asking Green if he was OK, Pfeifer says he told him to "have a good day" and left.
The entire exchange, according to the officer, took less than a minute. Another witness, however, says the exchange lasted about seven minutes.
Pfeifer says the interaction had nothing to do with race.
Neither officer knew they were speaking with Hamilton's first Black city councillor.
In April 2018, a Police Service Act hearing cleared Pfeifer of any wrongdoing.
Green says he felt humiliated by the encounter.
"I felt I was criminalized in my own community."
The following year, Green was elected as the NDP member of Parliament for Hamilton Centre. Though Green applied to the HRTO in April 2017, alleging he was discriminated against by police because of his "race and colour," the matter was deferred until the end of the PSA hearing.
It took another six and a half years for the HRTO to resolve the matter - by dismissing it.
The HRTO held a one-day preliminary hearing by video conference last October.
On Nov. 27, it dismissed the case, saying it was properly dealt with at the police hearing and there is no need to "relitigate" it.
"The applicant (Green) is unhappy with the PSA ... decision, but it is not for the Tribunal to comment on whether the PSA hearing officer made the correct decision," HRTO vice-chair Joseph Tascona wrote. Conducting another hearing "risks inconsistent findings of fact or law bringing the administration of justice into disrepute to permit relitigation," he wrote.
Meanwhile, says Green, Hamilton police continue to face allegations of systemic racism. There have been several court decisions in which judges have tossed out cases because police violated the Charter rights of Black people, and an Indigenous man had his head stomped on by an officer during his arrest.
The Hamilton Police Service's own report last year indicated its officers disproportionately use force against Black people compared to other groups of people.
"There just seems to be an institutional refusal to take accountability for what is in fact a problem," says Green.
He points to the recent police fatal shooting of Erixon Kabera, a well-known member of Hamilton's Rwandan community.
That case is being investigated by the province's Special Investigations Unit and few details have been made public.
"Anti-Black racism within policing can be a death sentence for people," says Green.
Chief Frank Bergen, who was not yet chief on the day of the bus stop incident, did not hide his dismay that Green is publicly discussing the Kabera matter.
"I'm a little bit taken aback and I would caution anyone, including an elected official, not to speculate on the outcome in the middle of an SIU investigation."
He urged Green and others to wait until "the facts are accurately shared with the public, once the SIU investigation is complete." Bergen says many improvements have been made since Green first filed his complaint about police.
"We are committed to equity, fairness and non-discriminatory policing and have recently established a community advisory panel to work alongside police on the development of our race identity strategy," he says. "We hire from the human race and therefore we also are making sure that we are supporting and training and educating our members to work alongside all members of the community."
Green, however, says his own complaint has never been fully aired, so he is left with no option but to appeal.
"I refuse to let go of this case."
Susan Clairmont is a
columnist and investigative reporter with
the Hamilton Spectator. sclairmont@thespec.com.
CAPTION: Const. Andrew Pfeifer attendshis Police Act hearing in 2017. Pfeifer was found not guilty.Spectator file photo

CREDIT: Susan Clairmont The Hamilton Spectator OPINION

Subject: Court decisions; Racism; Race; Black people; Racial profiling; Human rights; Tribunals & commissions

Title: MP refuses to let go of racism complaint against police:   Matthew Green says he was approached for being Black

Document 416 of 500

Letters

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/letters/docview/3151357933/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=unknown&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Letters&amp;title=The+Spectator&amp;issn=11899417&amp;date=2025-01-04&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.15&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Spectator&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: How did America get here?
Re: Former president Jimmy
Carter is dead at age 100, Dec. 29
How does a country go in the span of 35 years from electing a humble, religious, faithful family man who served his country in the military and won a Nobel Prize for his humanitarian acts and striving toward peaceful resolutions in the world to voting for Donald Trump?
How does a nation known for their generosity and being first to offer aid to other countries in times of disaster embrace a spiteful, vindicate cheating bully who spews hatred, racism, misogyny and vulgarity? How does a political party once lead by military men like Abraham Lincoln, Theodore Roosevelt, Ulysses S. Grant and Dwight D. Eisenhower who would lead their country to victory in some of the darkest times in history now embrace a man who avoided military service, insults veterans and embraces dictators? It's a sad reflection of United States that a leader who embraces the American flag also drags the Stars and Stripes through mud.
Walt Fisher, Hamilton
Trump taunting aimed at leaders
We've read and heard taunts from Donald Trump about Canada becoming the 51st state. But, I think us Canadians are a little thin skinned. It's possible these taunts are not against Canada or Canadians. Their purpose is to simply get under the skin of our fearless, but weak leader. Say what you will about Trump, love him or hate him. But, there's enough evidence to show that Justin Trudeau doesn't play well with other children.
Jerry Pryde, Stoney Creek
Many questions
to be answered
There's lots of talk about the tiny home issue. Where will they be placed? What facilities and services will be provided? When will the city's offshore homes arrive?
Look around the city. We have many of our citizens living in tents. Forty homes will not accommodate all the potential candidates. What are the selection criteria for the dwellers in these Chinese-built homes which are to be transited through the States? Who will decide who lives there? One can only imagine the disappointment and tension involved in making that selection.
Meanwhile, the long abandoned and deserted properties in Jamesville on James Street North remain empty with no sign of any progress. How long will we allow the Montreal-based CNR lawyers to tie up development on that site with specious legal wrangling?
Ray Varey, Dundas
Les Prince hasn't been forgotten
Re: Remembering Les Prince Field, Dec. 31
McMaster University has seen many changes since the years that Les Prince was dean of students. He was beloved by generations of students and colleagues, including myself. The original McMaster football stadium was named Les Prince Field in his honour. When the old wooden benches became unsafe and a natural grass field was no longer sustainable, it was time to replace the field with a full stadium like other major universities across Canada. As a member of the planning committee, I remember discussing at great length how we could respect the legacy of Les Prince in our vision. In the end it was the generosity of Ron Joyce that allowed us to build an exceptional facility. It would not exist otherwise. Ron Joyce Stadium is among the best in the country benefitting the entire Hamilton community.
The field within the stadium retains the name, Les Prince Field, and is recognized by a new generation of athletes. Also, McMaster acknowledged his contributions with the naming of Les Prince Hall, a 400-bed student residence on campus. His contributions have not been diminished and will never be forgotten.
Roger Trull, vice-president,
university advancement
McMaster University (retired)
There's more to life than 'efficiency'
I was very happy to see two letters disagree with Goran Calic's paean to the glories of Elon Musk. Together, both writers say all there is to be said about Musk's dangerous ambition to remake government through the narrow lens of efficiency. I want to add a critique of Calic's positing of effectiveness and efficiency as two yardsticks to measure human endeavour.
There have been many effective and efficient things that humans have done over the centuries that have resulted in the grotesque slaughtering of countless other human beings in the name of a shared goal. You know what I mean. Effectiveness and efficiency should be a consideration, because resources will be scarce, but to allow those concepts to be the primary way we frame any institutional evaluation is woefully, mind-bogglingly, misguided. How does one measure the effectiveness and efficiency of health care for an aging population? Of programs for people with disabilities? Of environmental catastrophes? Calic argues we should hive off the moral and ethical questions that are fundamental to governing and managing people. Doing so only leads one way, and it's a way we have already seen play out. And it should never happen again.
Peter Mountford, Hamilton
Lets march
on Washington
Wouldn't it be wonderful if on Jan. 20 the people of Canada marched on Washington D.C. and occupied it? It's fairly obvious many Americans want to be part of Canada anyway. We would end the evil regime of Donald Trump, make the Constitution null and void and declare the United States our newest province. We could even return Texas and California to Mexico to show our belief in free trade. Once achieved, we would then load Elon Musk, the Trump family and as many Republicans as we can catch on Elon's rocket to Mars with their citizenship revoked.
Dan Riga, Burlington
We're heading in
the wrong direction
A report by World Weather Attribution and Climate Central reviews a year of extreme weather and warns all countries need to adapt to rising climate risks to minimize deaths and damages in 2025 and beyond. It also highlights that a much faster transition away from fossil fuels is needed to "avoid a future of relentless heatwaves, drought, wildfires, storms and floods."
When I was born in 1962, carbon dioxide levels were 316 parts per million; a fair amount below the 350 ppm levels that climate scientists would determine to be unsafe to pass. As 2024 ends we're over 425 ppm; three to four ppm more than the same time last year. In short it seems we're doing everything we can to increase extreme weather events and increase the deaths and damages that come with them.
Richard S. MacKinnon, Hamilton
I'm saying no
to plastic bags
In my desire to reduce my use of plastics, I have made a request to my paper delivery person to not put my newspaper in a plastic bag, but just throw them on the porch near the door. My carrier Lesley has been wonderful in complying with my wishes and I have appreciated it immensely. We must all look for ways to reduce our plastic use. And the elastics that Lesley uses I expect cost less than plastic bag wrappers.
Thank you, Lesley, for your accommodation!
Renate Manthei, Hamilton
We should embrace renewable energies
Re: How agrivoltaics is marrying food production with green
energy, Dec. 25
The Spec article about mixing solar power and farming, known as agri-voltics, shows that even Alberta, the "oil province," is busy working on renewable energy. Ontario, under the Ford administration, has cancelled renewable projects and continued the call of gas and nuclear as the answer for future needs. Why, if even our "tarsands province" is busy with solar projects, is Ontario so resistant?
We have about 17,000 acres of vineyards in Ontario. There are projects in France, Italy and California exploring agri-voltics. They are finding improved vineyard harvest, protection from hail and other positive outcomes in addition to the power generated. I have sent emails and made other inquiries of the academic and vineyard resources in the province with no response. It seems to me that the potential should at least be investigated with a pilot project.
Phil Davis, Burlington
CAPTION: Supporters of Donald Trump rally at the U.S. Capitol in Washingtonon Jan. 6, 2021. Wouldn't it be wonderful, a reader suggests, that if on Jan. 20 the people of Canada marched on Washington D.C. and occupied it?Jose Luis Magana The Associated Press file photo

CREDIT: ;

Subject: American history; Efficiency; Wineries & vineyards

Business indexing term: Subject: Wineries & vineyards

Location: California; United States--US; Canada

People: Trump, Donald J; Musk, Elon

Company / organization: Name: McMaster University; NAICS: 611310

Document 417 of 500

Jan. 4: How did Americans go from Carter to Trump and other letters to the editor

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/jan-4-how-did-americans-go-carter-trump-other/docview/3151357884/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Jan.+4%3A+How+did+Americans+go+from+Carter+to+Trump+and+other+letters+to+the+editor&amp;title=The+Hamilton+Spectator+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2025-01-04&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Hamilton+Spectator+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Lets march on Washington
Wouldn't it be wonderful if on Jan. 20 the people of Canada marched on Washington D.C. and occupied it? It's fairly obvious many Americans want to be part of Canada anyway. Ontario should embrace renewables
Re: How agrivoltaics is marrying food production with green energy, Dec. 25
The Spec article about mixing solar power and farming, known as agri-voltics, shows that even Alberta, the "oil province," is busy working on renewable energy. Ontario, under the Ford administration, has cancelled renewable projects and continued the call of gas and nuclear as the answer for future needs. Why, if even our "tarsands province" is busy with solar projects, is Ontario so resistant? We have about 17,000 acres of vineyards in Ontario. There are projects in France, Italy and California exploring agri-voltics. They are finding improved vineyard harvest, protection from hail and other positive outcomes in addition to the power generated. I have sent emails and made other inquiries of the academic and vineyard resources in the province with no response. It seems to me that the potential should at least be investigated with a pilot project.
Phil Davis, Burlington
Shelters could've been built locally
The city of Hamilton decided to clear a spot for the purpose of erecting small shacks so some of the cities tent people will be a little better off with a wooden roof instead of the flimsy top of a tent. They are now in the California, just in from China. Can you believe this? From all these millions of immigrants that our wise government brought here in the last few years, not one is an experienced carpenter? So the smart councillors of Hamilton sent between $2.5 million and $4 million for shelters from China that should have been outsourced to our own community. This gave me a flashback to the Sheila Copps flag days when all Canadian flags came from China. When is our hard earned taxed money going to stay here?
Dennis Marten, Winona
Government needs to start building
How many times must the public be metaphorically "kicked in the face" by the failure of the free market to bring down the high cost of housing before governments solve the problem? The government has a responsibility to its citizens to provide affordable shelter. Since free enterprise has been incapable of doing this, it is time for governments to be bold and do their job.
Federal and provincial governments must acknowledge that the only way to bring down the expense of housing is by becoming directly involved in the building of new homes. They are the only ones who can provide people with not-for-profit new housing. This will reduce the overall cost of buying a home across the board.
As far as depending on the free market to bring down the price of buying a house, we are all familiar with that much misquoted but apt saying, "Insanity is doing the same thing over and over again and expecting different results."
Lou Masi, Hamilton
Cancel your cross-border trips
In a recent letter I suggested Canadians who, as a Trump tariff retaliatory measure, fail to stop vacations to the U.S., should be viewed as traitors to Canada.
On Dec. 14, the New York Times reported a lawyer for Robert Kennedy Jr. petitioned the U.S. government in 2022 to revoke its approval of the vaccine for polio, a virus that can cause paralysis or death. He also filed a petition to pause the distribution of 13 other vaccines. Extremely irresponsible!
Now that Kennedy is the proposed health secretary for the incoming Trump administration, his anti-vaccine agenda partnered with this lawyer may make the U.S. an alarming potential cesspool source of disease contagion. This could make Canadians' travel and vacation to the U.S. irresponsibly dangerous for both the individual and the Canadian community back home to whom they may transmit these diseases.
Canadians now have a second very good reason to avoid vacations and all other travel to the U.S.
Fred Leech, Dundas
CAPTION: Supporters of Donald Trump rally at the U.S. Capitol in Washington on Jan. 6, 2021. Wouldn't it be wonderful, a reader suggests, that if on Jan. 20 the people of Canada marched on Washington D.C. and occupied it?Egyptian president Anwar Sadat, left, U.S. president Jimmy Carter, centre, and Israeli prime minister Menachem Begin clasp hands at the White House after signing a peace treaty in 1979. How did Americans go from Carter to Donald Trump, a reader asks.

Subject: American history; Efficiency; Wineries & vineyards; Flags

Business indexing term: Subject: Wineries & vineyards

Title: Jan. 4: How did Americans go from Carter to Trump and other letters to the editor

Document 418 of 500

Tough choices, food banks, shelters: Here's what life is like on social assistance in Hamilton

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/tough-choices-food-banks-shelters-heres-what-life/docview/3151357837/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Tough+choices%2C+food+banks%2C+shelters%3A+Here%27s+what+life+is+like+on+social+assistance+in+Hamilton&amp;title=The+Hamilton+Spectator+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2025-01-04&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Hamilton+Spectator+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Chris Martinez holds a dim view of his prospects if he ever had to leave the modest apartment he has called home for about a decade.
"The consequences would be me and tent city," says Martinez, who relies on a disability benefit of roughly $1,300 a month to cover his expenses.
So, like others in the Main Street East building across from Gage Park, he has fought his landlord's attempt to evict him to do renovations.
In doing so, he has endured turmoil, notably, a nearly three-month stretch without running water during the winter of 2022-23.
When he looked for an escape, there was no obvious path.
"Every time I tried to look for apartments, it was more than I made in a month," says Martinez, citing rents of $1,500 to $1,800 in Hamilton's rental market.
That's well above the nearly $800 he pays for his bachelor-plus-den, an amount capped at Ontario's annual guideline for increases that landlords can charge existing tenants. Much lower, but it still makes for a tricky balancing act out of what Martinez, 33, receives from the Ontario Disability Support Program (ODSP).
After rent, bus fare and groceries, there's nothing left for frills. He visits the food banks at least once a month.
"When you're on ODSP, you have to pick and choose what you're going to pay for that month."
A new study of Ontario's social assistance rates by the Social Planning and Research Council of Hamilton (SPRC) underscores the tough choices Martinez and others face.
In her report, "Indecent Social Assistance in Hamilton and Ontario," SPRC social planner Sara Mayo puts it this way:
"Ontario's monthly social assistance rates ... are completely inadequate to cover even the basic necessities of life, and exclude any expenses for participating in society, such as internet access, going out with friends, or travelling to community events or to visit family."
The result is Ontario food banks serve "high numbers of social assistance recipients because there is no way a person can have a budget that allows them to buy food that provides enough nutrition to survive on social assistance alone, without additional help."
To cover all expenses, a single person on ODSP receives $1,368, while someone on Ontario Works receives $733 a month.
People living in residential care facilities or other care homes receive a $149 personal needs allowance to cover everyday items apart from meals and housing.
The personal needs allowance hasn't changed since 2019, while Ontario Works has been static since 2018. Having been flatlined for years, ODSP was pegged to inflation in 2022.
In particular, when measured against inflation, the $733-a-month Ontario Works rate is at the lowest point in 30 years, Mayo points out in her report.
That means it's valued at $207 less than it was even after then-Tory premier Mike Harris's "draconian cuts to social assistance" in 1995.
Premier Doug Ford's Progressive Conservatives indexed ODSP to inflation, but that "does not make up for the decades of declines," Mayo observes.
The Ministry of Children, Community and Social Services says the government is "making the social assistance system effective and efficient," with a focus on helping people "achieve sustainable employment and independence, where possible."
Ontario Works "has always been intended to be a short-term support for people who are temporarily out of work," the ministry told The Spectator via email.
"Recipients are supported by caseworkers focused on helping them return to full-time, meaningful employment and financial independence. In addition to amounts for basic needs and shelter, social assistance recipients may also qualify for various health and discretionary benefits."
But the positioning of Ontario Works as a short-term measure, Mayo counters in her report, "cruelly overlooks that the only way to survive even in the short term" is through food banks, loans and credit card debt, or resorting to emergency shelters.
"This 'short-term support' leaves people in much worse off situation than before accessing social assistance, and makes climbing out of poverty and leaving social assistance so much harder due to accumulated debt."
In addition to the individual hardship, the collective upshot is a cost to taxpayers, Mayo wrote, pointing out many social assistance recipients wind up in emergency shelters, where the average cost to keep someone is $2,100 a month.
A city staff report noted Hamilton's Ontario Works caseload increased by roughly 20 per cent between June 2023 and June 2024, which was sharper than the provincial hike of 16.4 per cent. As of January 2024, there were 11,291 cases, with a total of 20,416 beneficiaries.
In January 2024, 793 local Ontario Works cases involved people who were without housing.
"It is important to understand the complex needs of clients and how this may impact achieving goals," city staff wrote in their report. "One barrier for clients is a lack of services in the community and clients are often held up by lengthy wait lists."
In a recent presentation to city councillors, Sally Palmer, an advocate for reform of Ontario's social assistance programs, called the province's Ontario Works freeze "inhumane."
"We're seeing our poorest citizens suffering as the weather goes colder. Please let the government know they must treat them fairly," she said.
Palmer reflected on how she and others gathered signatures for a petition from those waiting in line for food during a regular weekend charitable event at Gore Park.
"And they're sitting in wheelchairs, not enough clothes on ...," she said. "The Gore Park outreach is really good, but it isn't going to solve the problem."
Palmer, Mayo of the SPRC and others are calling on the province to immediately double rates for social assistance to close the survival gap people face.
That rings true for Chris Martinez, the Main Street East tenant.
"I have other friends who are on ODSP, and we all say the same thing: It should be at least double to live in this day and age."
When family and friends ask him how he tolerates issues at his building, his answer is simple.
"Because I can't move. I can't pick up and take off ... Where am I going to go?"
To read the SPRC study, visit sprchamilton.ca.
Teviah Moro is a reporter at The Hamilton Spectator. tmoro@thespec.com
CAPTION: Chris Martinez, outside his Main Street East apartment in February 2023, during a period without running water. He says he can't afford to move out.Sara Mayo, a social planner with the Social Planning and Research Council of Hamilton.People line up for food at a regular charitable event at Gore Park in downtown Hamilton.Social assistance rates in Ontario are "completely inadequate to cover even the basic necessities of life," Sara Mayo, a social planner with the Social Planning and Research Council of Hamilton, writes in a new study.

Subject: Food programs; Councils; Shelters; Planning

Title: Tough choices, food banks, shelters: Here's what life is like on social assistance in Hamilton

Publication year: 2025

Publication date: Jan 4, 2025

Section: news/hamilton-region

Joan's 'Little List' looks back at 2024 and what lies ahead in 2025

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/joans-little-list-looks-back-at-2024-what-lies/docview/3150705853/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Joan%27s+%27Little+List%27+looks+back+at+2024+and+what+lies+ahead+in+2025&amp;title=The+Hamilton+Spectator+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2025-01-02&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Hamilton+Spectator+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: In the operetta "The Mikado," Ko-Ko, the Lord High Executioner of Titipu, creates a list of potential victims in need of execution. So with apologies to Gilbert and Sullivan, here's my 2024 looking-back "Little List."
As someday it may happen
That some victims must be found
I've got a Little list
These grace Joan Little's list
Of things we've seen in Burlington
And other places 'round
That never would be missed
They'd none of them be missed.
The biggest shock of '24?
Our neighbours to the south
Elected con-man Trump again
Despite his vulgar mouth.
How will life look in '28
Will all that bunch is gone
With climate change still unaddressed
And fire and floods rage on?
Trudeau will likely disappear
And Poilievre be in.
His shtick's unlikely to last long
So who will follow him?
But they're all on the list
Of leaders we'd not miss
It's just a partial list
Of those who'd ne'er be missed.
Will Doug Ford's Green Belt scams result
In Crombie getting in?
Though even a minority
For us would be a win.
The roadblock that's the OLT
Most surely has to go
Its backups just stall housing starts
As new homebuyers know.
And unaffordability's
The buzz word for today
There's no way now to buy new homes
In all the GTA.
But we have beer in corner stores
A spa will come along
And then in spring of '25
Will his bunch too be gone?
But now Trudeau and Ford combined
And doling out "free" cash
Bribing us with our tax bucks
What could be more crass!
These two are on our list
Of rulers we'd dismiss
If folks decide to switch
They'd neither one be missed.
Our council raised our taxes high
Which delegates opposed
But listening wasn't what they did
Folks think we're being hosed.
Our Lakeshore's still a parking lot
Congestion's bad out there
And patios close downtown lanes
Does council even care?
Will these improve in '25
Or stay like '24?
Without change some who run this show
May well be shown the door.
But let's enjoy our favourite things
Escarpment and the lake
Spencer Smith and Christmas lights
Are really, really great.
And let's all dream our favourite dreams
That things will end up bright
That '25 will bring world peace
And everything be right.
So let's make this year's list
A chance to get things fixed
And just be optimists
Our future will be bliss.
CAPTION: "Let's all dream our favourite dreams. That things will end up bright. That '25 will bring world peace. And everything be right," Joan Little writes in her annual poetic roundup.

Document 421 of 500

Canadians must stand up to Trump

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/canadians-must-stand-up-trump/docview/3150705206/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=unknown&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Canadians+must+stand+up+to+Trump&amp;title=The+Spectator&amp;issn=11899417&amp;date=2025-01-02&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.10&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Spectator&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: He isn't even president yet but Donald Trump has been mocking Canadians with ridiculous taunts; the demented ramblings of an old man.
I don't know about you but I would prefer if Canadians weren't so polite about all the insults coming our way from south of the border. How about we hurl a few insults back at Trump? Like: Most of us would have voted for Kamala Harris, not you. Or: Why don't you get a real socialized health care system instead of the pathetic one you have. Or: What's the matter? America running out of water?
First it was the joke about Canada becoming the 51st state and referring to Justin Trudeau as "governor." He even posted a photo of himself on his Truth Social platform beside the Canadian flag staring triumphantly at the supposed Rocky Mountains; except it was a Swiss mountain.
His son, Eric, then posted on X a doctored photograph of Trump smugly looking at maps of Canada, Greenland, and Panama (he also wants them as part of his empire) on the Amazon website. "We are so back" the junior Trump wrote above it. As if annexing Canada would be that simple.
Of course this taunting is all about the 25 per cent tariff on Canadian goods that Trump has threatened to impose the moment he takes office on Jan. 20 if Canada doesn't do more to stop the minuscule amounts of illegal immigrants and fentanyl from the crossing the U.S. border. Canada quickly came up with plans to spend $1 billion to beef up our side.
Will it be enough? Who knows? Trump refers to the fact that Canada sells more to the U.S. than we buy from them as a "subsidy." Does he not understand how international trade works? It appears not.
This all comes at a particularly bad time for Trudeau, who is hanging on to power by a frayed thread. He not only has the majority of Canadians, his caucus and the opposition hounding him to leave office, he has Trump on the sidelines gleefully launching sour lemons.
When Trudeau's most faithful minister Chrystia Freeland resigned from cabinet a couple of weeks ago Trump jumped right in with a blistering post on Truth Social. "The Great State of Canada is stunned as the Finance Minister resigns, or was fired, from her position by Gov. Justin Trudeau. Her behaviour was totally toxic, and not at all conducive to making deals which are good for the very unhappy citizens of Canada. She will not be missed!!!"
He was referring to the hard-nosed response Freeland gave his negotiators during the wrangling over the new NAFTA.
Premier Doug Ford has threatened to cut off the electricity the province sends to the U.S. if Trump goes ahead with the tariffs. But another premier, Alberta's Danielle Smith, is only too pleased to subjugate herself to Trump's demands.
She has already set up an Alberta border patrol and is planning on attending Trump's inauguration so she can talk to all his cronies and maybe even the man himself about exempting from the proposed tariff all the Alberta oil that is shipped to the U.S.
No Liberal - Trudeau, a member of his shrinking cabinet, or any Liberal MP - has taken a shot back. Conservative Leader Pierre Poilievre said Canada would never become the 51st state and has mumbled a few words about getting along with our neighbour. On the other hand, NDP MP Charlie Angus told Trump to "piss off" after he heard about the insults that he levelled at us on Christmas Day.
That's more like it. If you don't stand up to a bully he will keep on taunting until you are nothing more than a shrivelled piece of lettuce. And if it's like this now, it's only going to get worse after he assumes the presidency. Trump isn't backing down from his tariff threat, so it's time to start standing up for ourselves.
Gillian Steward is a Calgary writer
and journalist.
CAPTION:

CREDIT: Gillian Steward;

Subject: Canadians; Border patrol; Tariffs

Business indexing term: Subject: Tariffs

Location: United States--US; Canada

People: Trudeau, Justin

Company / organization: Name: Truth Social; NAICS: 516210

Title: Canadians must stand up to Trump

Publication title: The Spectator; Hamilton, Ont.

First page: A.10

Publication year: 2025

Publication date: Jan 2, 2025

Section: Editorial

Publisher: Torstar Syndication Services, a Division of Toronto Star Newspapers Limited

Language of publication: English

'Little List' looks back at the year that was: Donald Trump, Justin Trudeau and Doug Ford all make Joan Little's annual musical roundup

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/little-list-looks-back-at-year-that-was/docview/3150705197/se-2?accountid=15090

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=unknown&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=%27Little+List%27+looks+back+at+the+year+that+was%3A+Donald+Trump%2C+Justin+Trudeau+and+Doug+Ford+all+make+Joan+Little%27s+annual+musical+roundup&amp;title=The+Spectator&amp;issn=11899417&amp;date=2025-01-02&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.10&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Spectator&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Without change some who run this show
May well be shown the door.
But let's enjoy our favourite things
Escarpment and the lake
Spencer Smith and Christmas lights
Are really, really great.
And let's all dream our favourite dreams
That things will end up bright
That '25 will bring world peace
And everything be right.

So let's make this year's list
A chance to get things fixed
And just be optimists
Our future will be bliss.
Freelance columnist
Joan Little is a former
Burlington alderperson
and Halton councillor. Reach her at specjoan@cogeco.ca.
CAPTION:

CREDIT: Joan Little OPINION Freelance columnist;

Subject: Housing starts

Business indexing term: Subject: Housing starts

Document 423 of 500

If you haven't filed your 2023 tax return, time is running out to receive the $200 Ontario taxpayer rebate in 2025

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/if-you-havent-filed-your-2023-tax-return-time-is/docview/3150429657/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=If+you+haven%27t+filed+your+2023+tax+return%2C+time+is+running+out+to+receive+the+%24200+Ontario+taxpayer+rebate+in+2025&amp;title=The+Hamilton+Spectator+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2024-12-30&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Hamilton+Spectator+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: The clock is ticking for those who haven't filed their 2023 tax returns yet.
If you want to be eligible to receive the $200 rebate from the Ontario government coming in the new year, you have until the end of 2024 to file your tax return.
On Oct. 29, the province announced it was providing a $200 rebate to all eligible taxpayers and eligible children.
"This $200 taxpayer rebate will give Ontario taxpayers, especially families with children, some much-deserved help so they can make ends meet," Premier Doug Ford said in an earlier press release.
The rebate cheques should hit the mailbox in early 2025.
The government is able to provide taxpayers with this relief because of higher-than-expected returns as a result of the impact of inflation on provincial sales tax revenues and the impact of recent changes to the federal government's capital gains tax.
In order to be eligible for the rebate, people must be 18 years old, not incarcerated or bankrupt in 2024, and have filed their 2023 Income Tax and Benefit Return by Dec. 31.
Usually, Canadians have until April 30 to file their tax returns for the previous year. So if you did file your tax return by April 2024, you should be fine.
Eligible children are ones who receive a Canada Child Benefit, and must reside in Ontario. For parents who share custody, the payments are also split, based on the most recent Canada Child Benefit information.
For more details about the $200 rebate, visit news.ontario.ca.
CAPTION: Ontario residents have until Dec. 31 to file their 2023 Income Tax return in order to be eligible for the $200 rebate coming in 2025.

Document 424 of 500

Dec. 28: Ending the year on a positive note, Musk needs to stay in his lane and other letters to the editor

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/dec-28-ending-year-on-positive-note-musk-needs/docview/3149696720/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Dec.+28%3A+Ending+the+year+on+a+positive+note%2C+Musk+needs+to+stay+in+his+lane+and+other+letters+to+the+editor&amp;title=The+Hamilton+Spectator+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2024-12-28&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Hamilton+Spectator+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Ending the year on a positive note
Re: All is calm, Dec. 24
Kudos to Cathie Coward for a special photo at this time of year. Her work all year capturing the area has been excellent.
To the editors: This was your best cover all year, ending a tumultuous year on a positive note.
Great job to TEAD with the horse therapy program at Mount Hope.
Al Kruzins, Dundas
Doesn't my opinion matter?
A few days before Christmas I received a phone call from someone who said they represented my MPP and they wanted to know if that person and Doug Ford could count on my vote in the next provincial election. I did say no and they said thank you and hung up. It struck me as curious as to why they wouldn't want to know why? Why in either case, if I was a supporter or not. Perhaps my opinion doesn't matter.
Ron Wilson, Burlington
Musk needs to stay in his lane
Re: Why Elon Musk is the right choice to drive government efficiency, Dec. 24
I rolled my eyes through this whole column. Stop trying to normalize Elon Musk, the world's richest man, who very clearly wants to control the entire world. Like all of the world's billionaires, it's dangerous for people like Musk to have that much control, who buys elections, who acts like he's an elected official, influencing and dictating to elected senators what and how to vote on important decisions. Like Donald Trump, Musk has no business being in government, his disdain for average working people is well documented. He needs to stay in his lane and out of government.
Lisa Johnston, Stoney Creek
The wrong man for the job
I read with interest the article extolling the virtues of Elon Musk being put in charge of driving efficiencies in the U.S. federal government. One cannot dispute Musk's ingenuity and business savvy (Tesla and SpaceX). But when it comes to revamping an existing large venture, particularly one that touches ordinary people in so many ways as the government does, Musk has less than a stellar track record. Several years ago, he bought the social media behemoth then known as Twitter. He immediately laid off 50 per cent of its workforce and realized later that he may have cut too many and started a rehiring initiative. He gutted the content moderation department, which resulted in the proliferation of hate speech and misinformation and disinformation about everything from COVID-19 to Vladimir Putin's invasion of Ukraine.
One needs to bear in mind that the government does not provide discretionary luxuries like electric cars or space launch vehicles. It provides a vital safety net that millions of Americans rely on for everything from basic income to food and medicine. Given his missteps with Twitter, do Americans really want Musk, who seems to be accountable to no one, trolling for "efficiencies" in these programs?
Ralph D'Angelo, Hamilton
Task Musk with getting shelters built
Re: Mayor 'hopeful' Hamilton's micro-shelter site ready in January amid contractor questions, Dec. 25
Re: Why Elon Musk is the right choice to drive government efficiency, Dec. 24
According to this article, it would cost the U.S. government $10,900 (U.S.) to deliver an orange to the space station. Musk reduced the cost using private technology to $540 and possibly as low as $40.
This is indicative of Musk's drive for efficiency, but also indicates the bloated overspending in government.
We don't need an Elon Musk, but we do need people in government that can think like a privatized company to reduce costs.
Hamilton has ordered 40 "micro-shelter units" from a Chinese manufacturer (that's another letter). They are being shipped through the U.S. and have encountered delays, when we could have provided them locally and been installed already.
The cost of the Chinese units is $2.8 million (Canadian - $70,000 per unit). Operating cost yearly is $4 million. ($100,000 per unit).
Additionally, the city has had "unexpected delays," including "additional health and safety measures" and production delays affecting the delivery of shelters and washrooms.
The sole-sourced project was also delayed for about two weeks in late November while the city responded to a Ministry of Labour order that flagged a series of issues. Bottom line; the homeless issue has been going on for a long time.
The City of Hamilton "rushes" an order even though they have had lots of time to do their due diligence. And still no small homes for the cold weather. I'm not an Elon Musk fan, but I have a feeling he could have had small homes installed on the moon by now.
Andy Price, Ancaster
Thanks to all Spec carriers
Re: The 'paper girl' who delivers holiday cheer every morning, Dec. 21
Thanks so much for Anne Bokma's tribute article to paper carriers and in particular about Cindy Addley. We continue to get a delivered Spec and I am sure part of the reason is that I had a Mountain route 65 years ago. At the time I didn't realize it was a life-learning experience - responsibility, time management, money management, customer service, organization, commitment to the task, record keeping, banking and perseverance on those windy and cold winter afternoons. At 12 years old, I was more interested in having a few bucks in my pocket for pop, Oh Henry bars and the odd plastic car model. I did save and bought my first $500 Volkswagen at 17 out of my savings. A big thank you to the Spec, and of course my parents who insisted I saved a portion of my money each week.
It is also great to see the old picture of the carrier. I believe it is Doug Grant who had a route in Westdale. That would be in the 1950s. He was my brother-in-law and certainly had quite a rig for his route. Unfortunately, he passed away a number of years ago. His entrepreneurship lead him to found Grantek, which continues to be a thriving company in Burlington.
To all the carriers, a big thank you for your commitment and to the Spec for impacting a generation of young Hamiltonians.
Doug Booth, Hamilton
It's time to restore our parks
Re: Council to consider changes to Hamilton's response to encampments, Dec. 25
It is no secret that this city council has been divided on many issues, not the least of which has been the homeless setting up encampments in our city parks.
Nevertheless, one area where council has been united has been our eager anticipation to receive the Ontario Superior Court's ruling as to whether the City of Hamilton infringed on the rights of the homeless living at encampments by enforcing a bylaw prohibiting tents in parks. Ultimately, Justice Ramsay concluded there has not been any breach of Charter rights and no damages are warranted. Some of the more noteworthy elements of the ruling which stand out to me include:
 "I was reminded by counsel for the applicant that while the legislature represents the will of the people, the court holds the moral compass that is often the only protection for the most vulnerable. I observe that the most vulnerable includes not only the homeless but also the elderly person and the child who want to use a sidewalk or a city park without tiptoeing through used needles and human feces."
 "The life, liberty, and security of the applicants are not put at risk by enforcement of the bylaw. They are put at risk by homelessness. Encampments contribute to this risk. They are lawless, dangerous and unsanitary."
 "Encampments are a symptom, not a solution. The city is trying to find a solution to homelessness in consultation with numerous others. It has attempted to address the problem with the old protocol, the encampment process and the new protocol. It has limited resources and a duty to its housed constituency. I think I am well advised to leave them to it without interference. Micromanagement by judges will not be productive."
With this judgment in our rear-view mirror, I call on my council colleagues to unite and shift our focus toward rescinding the encampment protocol and restoring parks to be the inviting, safe, green recreational spaces that they once were. My motion will be brought forward at Jan. 15 general issues committee.
Mike Spadafora, Ward 14 councillor
We can't make everyone happy
Re: I'm a Canada Post worker, back at work. Here's why I'm angry and demoralized, Dec. 22
On your opinion page, a postal worker describes eloquently the grief they continue to harbour after being forced back to work.
To address her grief to their satisfaction will increase the financial burden on taxpayers. Who should be concerned about Canada's growing deficit and debt? How much time we have until our country hits a debt wall? What will happen then?
Postal workers are not the only ones with an extensive list of griefs. Health-care workers, education workers and an array of public employees: what is the total cost of addressing all the griefs in aggregate?
Obviously, we cannot afford to make every public employee happy. Realistically, what can governments do?
Andrew Chan, Vancouver
CAPTION: A newspaper carrier for The Hamilton Spectator goes door to door on his bicycle in the late 1960s. This photo, and a column by Anne Bokma, revived some memories for a Spec reader.Melissa Horvath gives Cody a brush down after a winter ride as five-month-old pup Callie watches from a nearby straw bale and Cody's brother, Diesel, watches from his stall. Cody is one of 15 horses at the Mount Hope stable where individuals with disabilities are empowered through the transformative power of equine-assisted therapy.

Subject: Workers; Local elections; Councils; Cost control; Homeless people; Efficiency

Business indexing term: Subject: Workers Cost control

Location: United States--US; Canada

People: Musk, Elon

Document 425 of 500

Poilievre needs to show he's ready

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/poilievre-needs-show-hes-ready/docview/3149696696/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=unknown&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Poilievre+needs+to+show+he%27s+ready&amp;title=The+Spectator&amp;issn=11899417&amp;date=2024-12-28&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.15&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Spectator&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: As we see 2024 coming to a close, it is clear that barring an event of unprecedented magnitude, the governing Liberal party will be defeated within months and the Conservative Party of Canada (CPC), led by Pierre Poilievre, will form a majority government.
Poilievre became Conservative leader in September 2022, and since then he and the CPC have risen steadily in the polls. Currently, the Conservatives are almost 20 points ahead of Prime Minister Justin Trudeau's Liberals. Poilievre has done this through being probably the most aggressive and nastiest opposition leader Canada has ever seen. There is nothing that Trudeau says or does that won't draw Poilievre's fury.
As we move into the final days of Trudeau's government, Canadians rightly expect to see less of Poilievre's fury and some signs of being "prime ministerial," and leadership from both Poilievre and his party. And to date we are not. All Canadians are getting from Poilievre and his party is anger, vacuous talking points and rhetoric.
In October, I wrote about being handed a CPC flyer while I was standing on my driveway. I asked the young man who gave it to me for some details. He was unable to answer any of my questions, directing me instead to the CPC website. I checked the website and found no details as to how they would "axe the tax" and what would replace it. How would they "build the homes," fix the budget" and "stop the crime." Again no details were there.
I live in the riding of Hamilton East-Stoney Creek so I called the CPC candidates' office and left a message that I would like details of their plans. I received no call back. I followed up with a detailed email spelling out exactly what I was looking for (i.e., their climate change plan, their crime reduction plan). I followed with another email, as I had received no response. And to date the Conservative candidate for my riding will not or cannot answer my questions.
Various journalists have also asked Poilievre those same questions and have received no detailed answers. It makes one wonder if perhaps the intellectual depth of the CPC's plan to govern is about that of a rain puddle.
Donald Trump has been threatening Canada with 25 per cent tariffs if we don't strengthen our border security. What was Poilievre's response? In a news conference he referred to our border as "Trudeau's broken border." He didn't defend Canada, nor did he remind Trump that American border security is the responsibility of the American government, not Canada. He in fact doubled down on Trump's claims about our border. To say I was disappointed in his response would be a gross understatement.
Adding to my disappointment is the fact Poilievre belonged to the Reform Party, which eventually merged with the Progressive Conservative Party of Canada to eventually form the CPC. One of the beliefs of the Reform Party was that opposition politicians should not only criticize the government, but they should also put forth detailed alternative ideas. Poilievre has turned his back on his roots in favour of a nasty, attack-dog persona with no intellectual depth.
The government of Justin Trudeau is scandal-ridden, stale and out of ideas. It does not deserve to remain in power. However, Poilievre and his Conservative party have provided Canadians with absolutely no examples that indicate they can behave like statesmen/women, nor have they presented any credible policy ideas to Canadians.
I believe our future is looking quite bleak.
Craig Wallace is a resident of Hamilton and author
of five books.
CAPTION:

CREDIT: Craig Wallace OPINION;

Subject: Canadians; Scandals; Political leadership; Border security; Political parties

Location: Canada

People: Trudeau, Justin

Company / organization: Name: Conservative Party-Canada; NAICS: 813940

Section: Editorial

Publisher: Torstar Syndication Services, a Division of Toronto Star Newspapers Limited

Place of publication: Hamilton, Ont.

Country of publication: Canada

Publication subject: General Interest Periodicals--Canada

ISSN: 11899417

Re: Council to consider changes to Hamilton's response to encampments, Dec. 25
It is no secret that this city council has been divided on many issues, not the least of which has been the homeless setting up encampments in our city parks.
Nevertheless, one area where council has been united has been our eager anticipation to receive the Ontario Superior Court's ruling as to whether the City of Hamilton infringed on the rights of the homeless living at encampments by enforcing a bylaw prohibiting tents in parks. Ultimately, Justice Ramsay concluded there has not been any breach of Charter rights and no damages are warranted. Some of the more noteworthy elements of the ruling which stand out to me include:
"I was reminded by counsel for the applicant that while the legislature represents the will of the people, the court holds the moral compass that is often the only protection for the most vulnerable. I observe that the most vulnerable includes not only the homeless but also the elderly person and the child who want to use a sidewalk or a city park without tiptoeing through used needles and human feces."

"Encampments are a symptom, not a solution. The city is trying to find a solution to homelessness in consultation with numerous others. It has attempted to address the problem with the old protocol, the encampment process and the new protocol. It has limited resources and a duty to its housed constituency. I think I am well advised to leave them to it without interference. Micromanagement by judges will not be productive."
With this judgment in our rear-view mirror, I call on my council colleagues to unite and shift our focus toward rescinding the encampment protocol and restoring parks to be the inviting, safe, green recreational spaces that they once were. My motion will be brought forward at Jan. 15 general issues committee.
Mike Spadafora,
Ward 14 councillor

everyone happy
Re: I'm a Canada Post worker, back at work. Here's why I'm angry and demoralized,
Dec. 22
On your opinion page, a postal worker describes eloquently the grief they continue to harbour after being forced back to work.
To address her grief to their satisfaction will increase the financial burden on taxpayers. Who should be concerned about Canada's growing deficit and debt? How much time we have until our country hits a debt wall? What will happen then?
Postal workers are not the only ones with an extensive list of griefs. Health-care workers, education workers and an array of public employees: what is the total cost of addressing all the griefs in aggregate?
Obviously, we cannot afford to make every public employee happy. Realistically, what can governments do?
Andrew Chan, Vancouver

Document 427 of 500

Bloc as official Opposition? Leader says voters 'don't have to fear us'

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/bloc-as-official-opposition-leader-says-voters/docview/3149695830/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Bloc+as+official+Opposition%3F+Leader+says+voters+%27don%27t+have+to+fear+us%27&amp;title=The+Spectator&amp;issn=11899417&amp;date=2024-12-28&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.11&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Spectator&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Bloc Québécois Leader Yves-François Blanchet says he's staying modest about the prospect of his party forming official Opposition in the next federal election, though it would be a "spectacular" result.
"I refuse to see the election in terms of pride or a trophy, or to claim to be part of history," he told The Canadian Press in a year-end interview.
As Justin Trudeau's minority government teeters and polls point to a possible collapse of the Liberal vote, Blanchet said he's staying focused on earning the confidence of Quebecers. "Quebecers will give us a mandate, and we'll carry it out responsibly by always repeating to ourselves, I hope, that we have to live with modesty," he said.
The most recent projections from poll aggregator Canada338.com show that if an election were held today, the Conservatives led by Pierre Poilievre would win a crushing majority of 232 seats. The site suggests the Bloc is running a distant second with 45 seats - but ahead of the Liberals with 39, the NDP with 25, and the Greens with two.
If such a scenario were to come to pass, the anti-monarchist, Quebec-focused sovereigntist party would become His Majesty's Loyal Opposition, and Blanchet would get the keys to Stornoway, the Opposition leader's official residence.
"If that happens, I can only say that we respect institutions ... while having already said that the Senate should be abolished, that the monarchy in Canada should be abolished," he said, adding, "but we are not there to break the toys."
Blanchet said his party would carry itself with a "positive and constructive attitude," while continuing to advocate for Quebec sovereignty.
"We can expect that, when an issue affects all of Canada, including Quebec, we will do our job," he said. "When an issue affects just Quebec, or almost just Quebec, we're right in our business." The party may be "more discreet," however, on issues that impact the rest of English Canada, he said.
Blanchet said his party would continue to speak only French in the House of Commons. But when asked if he had a message to English Canada, he said "people don't have to fear us."
He said the party's position on many issues are well-known, "and we're not going to pull the ideological rabbit out of the hat." What's good for Quebec is also, often, good for Canada, he added. As examples, he cited his party's recent battles to raise Old Age Security for younger seniors, to ensure supply management is protected in trade agreements, and to remove an exemption for religious speech in the section of the Criminal Code dealing with hate speech.
Blanchet laughed when asked if he'd prefer a medium-sized Bloc contingent that holds the balance of power or a larger number of MPs in a majority parliament. "An interesting trap question," he said. Either scenario can be worked to his party's advantage, he said, adding that he is focused on getting as many MPs elected as possible.
"A very strong Bloc Québécois delegation forces a government, any government, to respect what Quebec wants," he said. "And the proof is in front of us: Justin Trudeau's government didn't respect Quebec and it's possible they'll find themselves with a historically low number of seats in Quebec in the next election." Quebec, he said, "is making them pay the price."
The Bloc Québécois has formed the Official Opposition only once before, in 1993, after Lucien Bouchard left Brian Mulroney's Progressive Conservatives to found the party.
Trudeau's minority government seems all but certain to fall now that the NDP has announced that it will vote to bring down the government. Blanchet, who has voted against the government since the fall, said he would not support the Liberals under any circumstances and confirmed he would also vote to head for an election at the first opportunity.
That means he's resisting the "clear temptation" to help the government survive long enough to allow his bill C-282, which protects supply management on dairy, poultry and eggs, to get adopted in the Senate.
There's a lot more at stake in an election than just supply management, he said. "It's the whole of trade, the whole of the economy, the whole of international relations, the whole of the treatment of the most disadvantaged, housing, homelessness."
The Canadian Press
CAPTION: Bloc Québécois Leader Yves-Francois Blanchet said his party would carry itself with a "positive and constructive attitude," while continuing to advocate for Quebec sovereignty.Adrian Wyld The Canadian Press

Document 428 of 500

Pierre Poilievre needs to show he's ready for top job

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/pierre-poilievre-needs-show-hes-ready-top-job/docview/3149695778/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Pierre+Poilievre+needs+to+show+he%27s+ready+for+top+job&amp;title=The+Hamilton+Spectator+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2024-12-28&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Hamilton+Spectator+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: As we see 2024 coming to a close, it is clear that barring an event of unprecedented magnitude, the governing Liberal party will be defeated within months and the Conservative Party of Canada (CPC), led by Pierre Poilievre, will form a majority government.
Poilievre became Conservative leader in September 2022, and since then he and the CPC have risen steadily in the polls. Currently, the Conservatives are almost 20 points ahead of Prime Minister Justin Trudeau's Liberals. Poilievre has done this through being probably the most aggressive and nastiest opposition leader Canada has ever seen. There is nothing that Trudeau says or does that won't draw Poilievre's fury.
As we move into the final days of Trudeau's government, Canadians rightly expect to see less of Poilievre's fury and some signs of being "prime ministerial," and leadership from both Poilievre and his party. And to date we are not. All Canadians are getting from Poilievre and his party is anger, vacuous talking points and rhetoric.
In October, I wrote about being handed a CPC flyer while I was standing on my driveway. I asked the young man who gave it to me for some details. He was unable to answer any of my questions, directing me instead to the CPC website. I checked the website and found no details as to how they would "axe the tax" and what would replace it. How would they "build the homes," fix the budget" and "stop the crime." Again no details were there.
I live in the riding of Hamilton East-Stoney Creek so I called the CPC candidates' office and left a message that I would like details of their plans. I received no call back. I followed up with a detailed email spelling out exactly what I was looking for (i.e., their climate change plan, their crime reduction plan). I followed with another email, as I had received no response. And to date the Conservative candidate for my riding will not or cannot answer my questions. That certainly does not make me confident in his ability be an effective member of Parliament.
Various journalists have also asked Poilievre those same questions and have received no detailed answers. It makes one wonder if perhaps the intellectual depth of the CPC's plan to govern is about that of a rain puddle.
President-elect Donald Trump has been threatening Canada with 25 per cent tariffs if we don't strengthen our border security to prevent migrants and illegal narcotics from crossing into the U.S. What was Poilievre's response? In a news conference he referred to our border as "Trudeau's broken border." He didn't defend Canada, nor did he remind Trump that American border security is the responsibility of the American government, not Canada. He in fact doubled down on Trump's claims about our border. To say I was disappointed in his response would be a gross understatement.
Adding to my disappointment is the fact Poilievre belonged to the Reform Party, which eventually merged with the Progressive Conservative Party of Canada to eventually form the CPC. One of the beliefs of the Reform Party was that opposition politicians should not only criticize the government, but they should also put forth detailed alternative ideas. Poilievre has turned his back on his roots in favour of a nasty, attack-dog persona with no intellectual depth.
The government of Justin Trudeau is scandal-ridden, stale and out of ideas. It does not deserve to remain in power. However, Poilievre and his Conservative party have provided Canadians with absolutely no examples that indicate they can behave like statesmen/women, nor have they presented any credible policy ideas to Canadians.
I believe our future is looking quite bleak.
CAPTION: Craig Wallace has had no luck in learning how the Conservative Party will implement its "common sense" plan.Conservative Leader Pierre Poilievre speaks during a press conference in Mississauga, Dec. 17, 2024.
CREDIT: Craig Wallace
