Document 429 of 500

Ford sees an opportunity in Trump

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/ford-sees-opportunity-trump/docview/3149442590/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=unknown&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Ford+sees+an+opportunity+in+Trump&amp;title=The+Spectator&amp;issn=11899417&amp;date=2024-12-27&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.10&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Spectator&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Never mind Donald Trump and Justin Trudeau.
Forget the Toronto's mayor and council.
Doug Ford can do it all and get it done. He is one-stop shopping and one-stop voting.
Our once-upon-a-time premier has been Toronto's acting mayor since winning his first provincial election six years ago. Now he's acting like prime minister until further notice, or the next federal election - whichever comes first.
Perched at Queen's Park, ensconced in his Etobicoke hideaway, he lords it over city hall and presides over Parliament Hill. Grabbing the gavel as rotating chair of the Council of the Federation this month, he now speaks, purportedly, for the premiers. Not to mention his inherited leadership of the family firm, Ford Nation. From coast to Etobicoke to coast.
Think of him as premier of premiers, prime minister of the 51st state, mayor of Toronto and ruler (or reeve) of Ontario's 443 other municipalities. All in one.
Pasha, for short.
In short, Ford now stands tall. He casts a long shadow, broadcasting and recasting his image on every possible platform.
See him on CP24's all-news channel cutting a ribbon or slashing a program. Watch him on CPAC's all-politics channel convening his fellow premiers.
Look now - he's on CNN appealing to America's president-elect. Look again - he's on Fox News, trying again to get Trump's attention. He's all over X, ready to dance a jig with Elon Musk.
In politics, appearing on every channel is a good way to change the channel.
Why worry about homeless people when you can adjourn the legislature for nearly three months during the worst of the winter chill and spend your days saving the country from America's predatory depredations?
He's already booked a flight to Washington in late February, leading a delegation of his fellow premiers to meet congressional leaders and U.S. governors. Ford has announced he will go to the White House to plead Canada's case, if Trump will have him.
"I just look forward to sitting down with him eye to eye, businessman to businessman, and saying, 'You know, we need to cut a deal that's going to be beneficial for Americans and Canadians,' " Ford said recently, after boasting about go-betweens trying to arrange a meeting between the leader of the free world and the governor of Ford Nation.
Let us not be churlish about Ford's multi-tasking, multi-asking, multimedia act. There's something to be said for Ford saying whatever comes into his head these days.
Trudeau is absent without love or leave, as is his parliamentary opposition. Amid the opportunism, the premier sees an opportunity.
After six years in power at Queen's Park meddling municipally, he can't help fantasizing federally and dabbling diplomatically. He sees himself as Canada's communicator-in-chief, no matter his mangled messaging.
The disputatious among us might question his judgment about threatening to cut off Ontario's energy exports to our dear neighbours in Dearborn, Detroit and beyond. Certainly the premiers of Canada's energy-abundant provinces - Newfoundland, Quebec and Alberta - were quick to dissociate themselves from Ford's musings, lest his threats introduce uncertainty into the lucrative, long-term contracts they have signed and seek with other neighbouring states.
Their jitters were unsurprising. If you are trying to lock Vermont into an eternal relationship, complete with expensive power lines, you don't want to put it on the chopping block every time Trump creates a crisis that may turn into an empty threat from one year to the next.
That said, as much as Alberta's Danielle Smith shuddered over Ford's faux pas, her predecessors were willing to let us shiver: "Let those eastern bastards freeze in the dark," Albertans were once fond of saying.
On balance, however, there is method to the premier's madness. The president-elect doesn't think twice when uttering the equivalent of economic death threats to Canada, so why not fight mad with mad, madness with madness, Trump with Ford?
No matter how unpredictable Ford can be, he deserves credit for trying to break through the noise, as federal Finance Minister Dominic LeBlanc mused the other day.
His rhetorical approach and latest reproach provoked a riposte from the president-elect, who publicly dismissed the idea of Ontario cutting the cord on electricity exports.
Turns out we got a rise out of Trump. And raised a few raised eyebrows among electricity consumers in Michigan (home of American's next ambassador to Ottawa, Pete Hoekstra, lest we forget).
We won't know until next year whether Ford's crusade helps Canada. The question of the hour, however, is will it help him win another term as premier?
Martin Regg Cohn covers provincial
politics for Torstar.
CAPTION:

Document 430 of 500

Dismiss Trump taunts, expert says after online posts about Canada

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/dismiss-trump-taunts-expert-says-after-online/docview/3149197697/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Dismiss+Trump+taunts%2C+expert+says+after+online+posts+about+Canada&amp;title=The+Spectator&amp;issn=11899417&amp;date=2024-12-26&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.11&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Spectator&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: U.S. president-elect Donald Trump and those in his corner continue to muse about annexing Canada, though Canadian officials have largely sidestepped those comments.
In a post on the social media platform X, Eric Trump shared a doctored photo of his father purchasing Canada, Greenland and the Panama Canal on Amazon, with the phrase "We are so back!!!"
Since winning the presidential election in November, Trump has repeatedly referred to Canada as the "51st state."
On Trump's Truth Social platform, he's also repeatedly referred to Prime Minister Justin Trudeau as the "governor" of Canada.
Trump has also threatened 25 per cent tariffs against Canada, which has prompted discussions at both the federal and provincial levels on the best way to deal with the incoming Trump government.
Carleton University professor Aaron Ettinger said federal officials have rightly been dismissing the social-media posts and maintained a "focus like a laser beam" on the real and "existential threat" of tariffs.
"This strikes me as being profoundly unserious," said Ettinger, who has studied Canada-U.S. relations during the first Trump presidency. "These are taunts; these are churlish provocations that are not mature, and do not reflect just how serious the coming trade war is," he said.
Ettinger said Trump is likely continually posting about Canada because it plays well to his supporters, without being seen as a real threat of annexation. "We know his moves. He makes fun of, he belittles, he mocks, because he can," he said. But he said that a vacuum of leadership in Ottawa from embattled Prime Minister Justin Trudeau is spurring "the freelancing of some of the provincial leaders" in response to Trump's comments.
For example, Ontario Premier Doug Ford weighed in on Trump's postings on Dec. 18, telling media that "we'll never be the 51st state. We're Canada; we're proud to be Canadians. We'll always fight for that."
Ettinger said civil society is similarly better to focus on convincing Americans to not impose damaging tariffs on Canada, instead of amping up anti-Trump rhetoric.
"Canadians should worry first about what Canada's actual national interests are," he said. "We're not going to out-trash talk Donald Trump, so don't even bother trying and focus instead on the core stuff that really matters."
Tyler Meredith, a former economic advisor to the Trudeau government, said countries like Canada are grappling with what Trump's second administration will look like. He noted the recent rhetoric echoes that of Trump's first term, but he has a team that feels more emboldened and has less ties to Canada. "They are much more mercantilist and real politic in the way that they want to fight and extract value," said Meredith.
He said it's likely Ottawa and the premiers have a sequenced strategy on dealing with Trump, where Ottawa will not escalate or provoke the president-elect through to his inauguration on Jan. 20.
The Canadian Press
With files from
The Associated Press

Document 431 of 500

'I may end up in tears telling my story': How a former MPP and Toronto city councillor found himself living in a homeless shelter

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/i-may-end-up-tears-telling-my-story-how-former/docview/3149197338/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=%27I+may+end+up+in+tears+telling+my+story%27%3A+How+a+former+MPP+and+Toronto+city+councillor+found+himself+living+in+a+homeless+shelter&amp;title=The+Hamilton+Spectator+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2024-12-26&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Hamilton+Spectator+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Twenty-six years ago, Lorenzo Berardinetti was a leader at the dawn of a new civic era, the head of the Scarborough delegation in the first Toronto megacity council.
Six years ago, he was at Queen's Park, helping steer the province from the helm of its justice policy standing committee after winning four elections as Liberal MPP.
Tonight, he will have his bag searched. He will take his shoes off and have them inspected for weapons and drugs. He will sleep in a room with 23 other people.
"I may end up in tears telling my story," Berardinetti said recently from the public library he uses as an office.
"It's been really hard. Really hard."
Most people Berardinetti used to govern Ontario with - or sit on council beside, or practise law with - don't know he became homeless last year. He said he agreed to an interview with the Star to share his tale of how he wound up where he is to remind them that luck is the only real insulation from hardship.
"People will start laughing at me, saying, 'What an idiot, he should have saved his money,'" he said. "But anyone can end up homeless. Unfortunately, I got sick, and it's taken a long time to recover."
Marla Walters, executive director of the Ajax shelter Berardinetti lives in, knows he's right. A veteran politician showing up needing a bed isn't remarkable to someone who works to help homeless people: Berardinetti sleeps next to doctors, nurses and other lawyers some nights.
"Life can change suddenly for people," she said. "People from all walks of life end up in shelters. We're not as far away from their situation as we think we are."
A political touchBerardinetti won his first elections early. As student council president in high school, he used to meet with kids individually to "make sure they were happy."
He took that micro-level method to adult politics. A longtime supporter of his, Doris Ward, 85, was quoted in a 2011 Toronto Observer article characterizing him as "a person who listens to people." He, in return, was recorded eulogizing her in the online guest book attached to her 2021 obituary as "one of the most beautiful people that I have ever met in my life."
At 26 years old, Berardinetti both passed the bar and got voted onto Scarborough council. As he tells it, his aim then and throughout his career, was to "be honest, to serve with good intentions and to help people."
Politics as family affairBerardinetti's life had always hewed close to that of his older cousin, politician Mike Colle. They grew up together in the same home in downtown Toronto.
"He was really a very sweet little boy," said Colle, who is 16 years older. "It's hard to believe that from one family, one house, we both ended up where we did. I was in city politics, then he got into city politics. We were in the legislature together. Our paths have been intertwined."
Both cousins were voted out in the provincial election in 2018. Colle says there is a depressive "black hole" at the end of a political career - he doesn't think Berardinetti ever made it out.
"People have no idea the psychological impact it has, especially if you lose your seat," he said. "How do you adjust? There's no one to help you. And once you're out, nobody wants anything to do with you. They won't hire you."
Colle wasn't in the abyss long. Just weeks later, he won the Eglinton-Lawrence seat on council after the front-runner - his son, Josh - quit the race and retired. Last year, Colle was elevated to deputy mayor. Berardinetti hasn't found work since.
"Lorenzo was a lawyer, he certainly had the credentials," said Colle. "But if you're a defeated politician, no one wants to give you an opportunity. There's always an excuse. I've seen it happen with so many people over the years."
A life-changing lossBerardinetti tried running in the 2022 municipal election but was badly beaten.
"That was a huge mistake," he said. "I wasn't getting donations, I wasn't getting volunteers. But I needed a job. I was running out of money. I had to sell my car to pay my rent."
Only four years removed from his three-decade career in politics, Berardinetti was already destitute. He says now the membrane separating a secure life from one on the street is thinner than anyone with means is comfortable imagining. As Berardinetti tells his story, rips formed when he lost his job, but it tore open when he had his first seizure.
He said it happened when he was nearly 60 years old. "I just got divorced and I was all alone," he said. "I don't remember calling my brother. But he told me I did. I said, 'I don't feel well, something weird is going on.' He called the ambulance."
Berardinetti woke up a month later. The nurse changing his IV bag told him he had been seizing uncontrollably. He had to be ferried from Scarborough to a specialist at Toronto Western Hospital to be put in a coma.
The cause, he was told, was an over-prescription of Trileptal, a medication for epilepsy sometimes used to treat bipolar disorder.
Berardinetti said his psychiatrist prescribed him the drug in combination with a sleeping pill and a tranquilizer. He said he is neither bipolar nor epileptic, but instead believed he had been given Trileptal to manage side effects from his other prescriptions.
"I couldn't do anything for a while after the coma," said Berardinetti. "The doctors told me I needed to rest for a couple years to get my brain back."
A plea for helpMeanwhile, his savings were ebbing away. After Berardinetti's failed return to city council, he couldn't make rent, and his landlord in Scarborough kicked him out. He moved to Ajax to live with his brother but ended up leaving, with nowhere else to go, after what he said was a dispute over finances.
Berardinetti's sister called Colle for help shortly after.
"He called me back and said, 'I can't help you, there are no shelter beds in Toronto,'" said Berardinetti. "I said, 'I'm OK, I'm going to be starting my law practice soon.'"
Colle said it was regrettable, not being able to find his cousin a bed in Toronto. He said it's been months since the two last spoke.
"He was in a coma, so there's no income coming in, but you've still got to pay your bills, you've still got to pay your rent," said Colle. "You know, it gets to a point where your own relatives and friends, they don't have the wherewithal to help you get out of the financial hole."
The hard financial truthLeaving Queen's Park in 2018, Berardinetti had about a year's salary saved up. Base pay for an MPP has been frozen at $116,000 since 2009 - $12,000 less per year than a city councillor. He used to own a home with his wife, former councillor Michelle Holland, but moved out when they divorced.
After Berardinetti transitioned to provincial politics, he says he cashed out his municipal employee pension, worth about $200,000, to pay off a chunk of his and Holland's mortgage.
"I should have kept the money in the account, I guess," he said. Holland declined an interview request.
Life would be easier, Berardinetti said, if it wasn't for the Mike Harris government, which got rid of the lifetime pension plan for MPPs in 1995 in an effort to win back public trust in government. Councillors and MPs still get pensions, and Berardinetti said that his lack of one has forced him, at 63 to try to reignite his legal career so he can afford to move out of the shelter.
"I'm not saying I should be getting more than other people," said Berardinetti. "But I could have had a successful law career. I put it on hold to serve the community. Now I'm surviving on $830 a month from my Canada pension."
Berardinetti said he wished the two Liberal leaders after Harris weren't "too afraid" of blowback from the media and public to reinstate the pension.
Kathleen Wynne said she was "tormented" by the pension issue when she was premier. She said she knew some MPPs who only stayed in office because they couldn't afford to retire, but there was no "public permission" to help them out, she said.
"It's very hard, once you've taken something away from politicians, to give it back," Wynne told the Star. "It looks like you're feeding fat cats. People think politicians are at the trough. They think that we're all crooks."
A spokesperson for Premier Doug Ford's office told the Star his government will not be reinstating the pension plan either.
"That's easy for Doug Ford to say," said Berardinetti. "He's from a rich family. It's not the same for him. My father worked at a lumber mill."
Money can create emotional distance - even Berardinetti had trouble empathizing once. As a rookie councillor, he fiercely opposed a new youth shelter in his ward.
"Having been through the system, my perspective is totally different," he said. "People see me, see homeless people as dangerous. I walk into the shops around here and get asked to leave. But we just need compassion."
Finding a new purposeBerardinetti has taken a professional interest in filling that void of compassion.
He said he wants to work for Legal Aid Ontario to represent low-income newcomers and refugees. There must be a purpose behind all his misfortune, he said - maybe it was meeting other vulnerable people he could help.
He's also been processing his pain into literature. Last year, he self-published a book called "The Owl and the Dove." Chapter one opens with a meditation on his crisis of faith in the wake of his coma.
"How could God allow me to go through this terrible experience?" he wrote. "Why does God allow terrible things to happen?"
The birds, which represent rationality and spirituality, respectively, talk him through his anguish. He feels relief when the dove tells him he, like everyone, is tethered to God. The owl reminds him he is not the only person to have suffered seizures. In that moment, he writes, they make him feel as though a life of "joy, abundance and love" is still possible.
Over the summer, around the third anniversary of the coma, Berardinetti said his neurologist gave him the green light to work again.
By early December, he'd regained the licence to practise law he let lapse when he was elected to the legislature.
For now, Lorenzo Berardinetti Barristers & Solicitors only exists on paper, registered in the same building as the shelter he lives in.
But he's working to bring it to life. Just like he did with the megacity all those years ago. As administration committee chair, he helped appoint this city's inaugural treasurer and fire chief, weighed in on its flag and coat of arms, too. He knows how to build a place up.
"It's hard to start a law practice at this age," he admitted. "I have friends my age who are retired. But I have to do it. I have no pension. I can't survive."
CAPTION: Lorenzo Berardinetti, right, presents actor Mike Myers with a street sign in Toronto in June 2003. Myers was also honoured by the city of Toronto with the key to the city.Lorenzo Berardinetti, seen here at his election win in 2014 as MPP for Scarborough Southwest.Lorenzo Berardinetti was elected four times as the MPP for Scarborough Southwest."Having been through the system, my perspective is totally different," said Lorenzo Berardinetti.Lorenzo Berardinetti, a former Toronto city councillor and MPP, has been living in a homeless shelter. "Anyone can end up homeless," he said.

Ontario asks highest court to hear youth-led climate case

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/ontario-asks-highest-court-hear-youth-led-climate/docview/3148781406/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Ontario+asks+highest+court+to+hear+youth-led+climate+case&amp;title=The+Spectator&amp;issn=11899417&amp;date=2024-12-24&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.6&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Spectator&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Ontario has asked the Supreme Court to weigh in on a historic youth-led challenge of the province's climate plan, moving the case a step closer to a possible hearing before Canada's top court.
While the court only hears a fraction of the cases it's asked to review, lawyers for Ontario say this case strikes at an unresolved issue of national concern.
"This proposed appeal would ask this Court to determine, for the first time, whether and to what extent the Canadian Charter of Rights and Freedoms imposes obligations on Canadian governments to combat climate change," read Ontario's application for leave to appeal.
The case was brought by seven young people who argue Ontario's weakened emissions target violated the Charter.
They allege the target violated their right to life in part by committing Ontario to dangerously high levels of planet-warming emissions and discriminated against them as youth who will bear the brunt of the impacts.
While their case was initially dismissed at trial, the young people secured a victory on appeal in October when Ontario's highest court sent the case back to a lower court for a new hearing and left open the possibility the constitutional challenge may prevail.
Fraser Thomson, a lawyer representing the young people, says Ontario's application "opens the door to a generation-defining hearing before Canada's highest court."
"The climate crisis isn't going away, and neither are we," said Thomson, environmental law charity Ecojustice's climate director.
The case dates back to when Premier Doug Ford's then-newly elected Progressive Conservative government repealed the law underpinning Ontario's cap-and-trade system for lowering emissions.
The Canadian Press
CAPTION:

Document 433 of 500

Encampments are 'necessary,' advocates for homeless argue: 'The only way to solve homelessness is with homes,' outreach workers say

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/encampments-are-necessary-advocates-homeless/docview/3148781025/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Encampments+are+%27necessary%2C%27+advocates+for+homeless+argue%3A+%27The+only+way+to+solve+homelessness+is+with+homes%2C%27+outreach+workers+say&amp;title=The+Spectator&amp;issn=11899417&amp;date=2024-12-24&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.6&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Spectator&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Sean Horrell walked into the woods holding a basket filled with hot meals, water bottles, juice boxes and snacks for a group of people living in a small London, Ont., homeless encampment.
When he approached the camp, he was greeted by a brown dog that barked and wagged its tail. As Horrell called out names, four people left their makeshift shelters to grab the lunch he'd brought.
It was a chilly day in late November, with thick clouds and a light drizzle making it feel even colder.
"Do you have any winter clothes?" asked a woman in a black hoodie with "Good Vibes" written on it.
Horrell responded with a smile, "Yes, you have to walk with me."
Two men and a woman followed as he strolled through muddy leaves to a minivan in a nearby parking lot, where they tried on jackets, boots and socks.
"On days like today, where it's raining or as the weather gets worse, it means so much more that we show up and we come to them," Horrell said.
"Not only does it make them feel very valued but often if you're sick out here, that's when you need energy and a warm meal the most."
As the province seeks to end homeless encampments in public spaces with tough new legislation, many Ontarians are divided over how the issue should be handled amid ongoing housing, addictions and mental-health crises.
But Horrell and his team at 519Pursuit, a non-profit organization that helps homeless people in London, are determined to continue delivering hot meals and supplies five days a week to people living in tents.
Horrell's routine is almost the same every day.
In the morning, he drives to a storage building where 519Pursuit keeps donated items including sleeping bags, drinks, snacks and pet food.
Then, he and a group of volunteers pack food and drinks in plastic bags before heading off in different directions to distribute help across the city.
Premier Doug Ford's government recently introduced legislation to give more powers to police and municipalities to clear encampments out of public parks, a move critics say would further marginalize an already marginalized group.
The bill aims to strengthen penalties for those who repeatedly break trespass laws and use illegal drugs in public, but it's unclear when that will take effect since the legislature won't return from its winter break until March 3.
The Canadian Alliance to End Homelessness has called the pending legislation "ineffective, costly and cruel."
"The only way to solve homelessness is with homes," it said in a news release.
Horrell said encampments should continue to exist as a "harm reduction" measure for now because there is no better solution available.
"Encampments are necessary right now because the system itself is inundated, there aren't enough (shelter) beds," he said. "If it is a choice between having no space for people who are experiencing homelessness and having allocated spaces, I think it is an obvious choice."
The premier also announced an additional $75.5 million funding toward homelessness prevention programs, including $50 million for affordable housing and $20 million to expand shelter capacity.
Horrell said the issue has always been a "double-edged sword." While encampments provide homeless people with support and a sense of community, they also pose challenges for area residents and businesses, he said.
As he spoke with The Canadian Press, Horrell was approached by a man walking his dog close to an encampment who said he enjoyed talking to a couple in one of the tents when dropping them food and supplies, calling both "good" people.
But he also heard from an elderly woman who complained about her shoes being stolen from her property and quickly pointed finger at people in a nearby camp - who denied the accusation.
That divide is reflected in a recent survey of opinions on the issue across the province.
The Abacus Data survey, commissioned by the Canadian Alliance to End Homelessness, asked 1,500 adults about encampments and homelessness. It found that while an overwhelming majority in Ontario expressed some level of concern over encampments, a relatively low number of respondents favoured a heavy-handed approach to clear them.
Although 65 per cent of respondents said they were concerned about encampments in their community, only 12 per cent supported stronger law enforcement measures.
The Association of Municipalities of Ontario has estimated that there were at least 1,400 encampments in cities and towns across the province in 2023.
The City of London said around 200 people currently live in 105 tents and another 100 individuals are completely unsheltered.
Spokesperson Andrea Rosebrugh said the city's approach to encampments is reactive, meaning any removals are based on complaints and subsequent evaluation of the sites.
She also said the city's 396 shelter spaces are always at capacity.
"We continue to approach encampments in our city with compassion and a desire to support those who are living unsheltered," Rosebrugh said in a statement.
In Canada's most populous city, there are around 450 tents across more than 100 parks, according to City of Toronto officials.
Toronto Deputy Mayor Amber Morley said any efforts aimed at preventing the growth of encampments in public spaces are welcomed, but expressed concerns about the proposed provincial legislation.
"There is the obvious question when we talk about clearing encampments ... where are we clearing the individuals within those encampments to go?" Morley said at a recent news conference about the city's shelter plans.
"These are human beings and clearing people that are residents of our city for me is not a reasonable or respectful thing for us to be talking about."
That question was also raised by Michelle Boissonneault, another outreach worker at 519Pursuit in London, who has struggled with addiction and homelessness in the past.
Boissonneault said she started using drugs as a teenager and lived on the streets for years.
"I never felt the need to take care of myself," she said. "I just didn't care, like my humanity had been absolutely ripped from me. I was an empty shell of a human being."
Boissonneault visits local encampments three times a week to help those going through what she has personally experienced.
Removing the camps will simply push people onto the streets, she said, which might make the situation even worse.
The Canadian Press
CAPTION: As the province seeks to end homeless encampments in public spaces with tough new legislation, many Ontarians are divided over how the issue should be handled amid ongoing housing, addictions and mental-health crises.Chris Young The Canadian Press

Document 434 of 500

From beloved pets to political differences, MPPs' holiday cards are a peek at family life

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/beloved-pets-political-differences-mpps-holiday/docview/3148491592/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=From+beloved+pets+to+political+differences%2C+MPPs%27+holiday+cards+are+a+peek+at+family+life&amp;title=The+Hamilton+Spectator+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2024-12-23&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Hamilton+Spectator+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Neither snow nor rain nor even a month-long Canada Post strike could stop politicians' Christmas cards from getting through.
While the postal work stoppage did dramatically reduce the number of cards received at the Star's Queen's Park bureau this holiday season, there were enough to liven up our office on the third floor of the Ontario legislature.
Premier Doug Ford's card, which is normally mailed out to 20,000 friends and supporters, wishes a "Merry Christmas & Happy Holidays" from "the Ford Family."
The front of the card features the premier and wife Karla with three of their four adult daughters, Kayla, Kyla and Kara. The family's dog, a mixed breed named Kobe, is shown inside the card, sporting a dashing beret.
As the Star first reported in 2022, the Fords' other daughter, Krista, has declined to appear in family portraits for public consumption due to political differences with her father over his support for vaccines and lockdowns during the COVID-19 pandemic.
While the family remains close away from the cameras, the premier has acknowledged in the past how the health emergency "polarized us in a way that we could have never imagined."
NDP Leader Marit Stiles's card boasts a picture of her and spouse, Jordan Berger, and their daughters, Lila and Mahala, along with their dogs, Stevie Licks and Hobbes, in front of a fireplace in their Toronto home.
Stiles is sending out 2,981 cards this year.
Liberal Leader Bonnie Crombie's card is her on a lakeside dock with her adult children and their partners.
"Wishing You and Your Family a Happy Holiday, Merry Christmas and Happy New Year! Bonnie, Alex & Katherine, Jonathan, Natasha & Brian," reads the inscription.
Crombie will be mailing out several hundred cards and emailing thousands more to Liberals across the province.
Green Leader Mike Schreiner's card is him and wife, Sandy Welsh, and their daughters, Isabelle and Beata, against an appropriately bucolic backdrop above the words: "Happy Holidays, Joyeuses fêtes."
Inside, also in both official languages, the card says: "Wishing you health, peace, prosperity and great happiness this holiday season and always."
Schreiner is mailing out 3,000 cards this holiday now that the postal strike is over.
The first seasonal greeting delivered to the Star bureau this yuletide was from Infrastructure Minister Kinga Surma, whose card showcases her Siberian husky, Ayana.
Beloved pets also figured prominently on Progressive Conservative MPP Laurie Scott's offering.
Scott (Haliburton-Kawartha Lakes-Brock), who always has fun with her Christmas card, is shown seated in a harness racing sulky being pulled by her horse Mini Prince Hardy while her terrier Phineas jealously says in a cartoon thought bubble: "I've been replaced again...! What if I painted my nose red, do you think I could lead the sleigh?"
NDP MPP Wayne Gates (Niagara Falls), who also puts a lot of effort into his cards, had one drawn by his 18-year-old grandson Tanner Bidal.
It depicts Gates, with his trademark bushy moustache, sporting a Spider-Man Christmas sweater and a flowing scarf, throwing snowballs at a Grinch-like monster.
Another piece of original artwork was on Liberal Mary-Margaret McMahon's card.
The Beaches-East York MPP's holiday greeting is a wintery city scene with snowmen, sledders, tobogganers, skaters and Christmas trees that was drawn by Crescent Town Elementary School students with special thanks to their teachers.
She delivered it by hand to the Star and it was one of our favourites this year.

Document 435 of 500

Dec. 23: Don't let Premier Ford know about our libraries, get locals to build shelters and other letters to the editor

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/dec-23-dont-let-premier-ford-know-about-our/docview/3148337491/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Dec.+23%3A+Don%27t+let+Premier+Ford+know+about+our+libraries%2C+get+locals+to+build+shelters+and+other+letters+to+the+editor&amp;title=The+Hamilton+Spectator+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2024-12-23&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Hamilton+Spectator+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Don't let Premier Ford know
Re: Thankful for our libraries, Dec. 19
I too think that libraries are wonderful, helpful institutions for people of all ages and cultures. Allyn Walsh has expressed the feelings of many who avail themselves of all the services offered in our libraries.
I think it could be a little dangerous, though, to say too many good things about libraries. If Doug Ford hears about this, chances are good he would want to close them or privatize them or do some other ridiculous thing for "the people!"
Roger Rose, Mount Hope
Get locals to build shelters
Re: 'Unexpected delays' push opening of Barton temporary shelters to mid-January, Dec. 19
Why does Hamilton have to buy "tiny homes" for their homeless that are manufactured overseas? Now there is a "delay" in delivery. Surely there must be a company in Ontario or elsewhere in Canada that could build them. Or why not commission a high school or college to build them as a project?
The city has had plenty of notice. Why overseas?
Rusty Escott, Dunnville
Musk buys his way into power
First billionaires bought Supreme Court justices. Then the richest man in the world did what even "La Cosa Nostra" couldn't do: buy a president.
Even after being repeatedly warned, again, the American people still voted for this. I am old enough to remember when we had a decent neighbour.
Doak MacPherson, Brantford
Work to create a better world
This holiday season, as many prepare for festive shopping and celebrations, I find myself reflecting on the words of Gisele Pelicot: "Shame must change sides."
Recent headlines about government agencies spending millions to contest children's advocacy efforts for Indigenous communities or cities disbanding encampments of unhoused individuals and fighting them through the legal system deeply troubles me. Are we prioritizing systems of power over our shared responsibility to uplift the most vulnerable among us?
Having spent more than 25 years in the greater Hamilton area volunteering with local organizations, I've realized that while acts of charity matter, they cannot address systemic inequities. Managing homelessness is not the same as ending it - providing warm meals cannot ensure food security, and knitting mittens for those living rough does not address the fundamental need for a stable home.
This season, as many celebrate the first Christmas of a young couple in desperate need of shelter, I ask: can we stop victimizing those already battling poverty, trauma, discrimination, disabilities or addiction - often without the social scaffolding needed to recover? Instead of holiday rituals, let's reflect on building a society where equity and compassion are cornerstones of action.
Perhaps the greatest gift we can give our children this holiday season is a commitment to use our power, position and privilege to create a better and equitable world! And those rendered voiceless and faceless among us - it is not their burden to carry the shame - the onus is on us.
Myrtle Wong, Kilbride
CAPTION: Don't say too many good things about our libraries. If Premier Doug Ford hears about this, he'll want to close or privatize them, a reader says.

Subject: Privatization; Libraries; Homeless people

Business indexing term: Subject: Privatization

Title: Dec. 23: Don't let Premier Ford know about our libraries, get locals to build shelters and other letters to the editor

Publication title: The Hamilton Spectator (Online); Hamilton

Publication year: 2024

Publication date: Dec 23, 2024

Section: opinion/letters-to-the-editor

Publisher: Torstar Syndication Services, a Division of Toronto Star Newspapers Limited

Place of publication: Hamilton

Document 436 of 500

Letters

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/letters/docview/3148337463/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=unknown&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Letters&amp;title=The+Spectator&amp;issn=11899417&amp;date=2024-12-23&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.10&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Spectator&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Work to create
a better world
This holiday season, as many prepare for festive shopping and celebrations, I find myself reflecting on the words of Gisele Pelicot: "Shame must change sides."
Recent headlines about government agencies spending millions to contest children's advocacy efforts for Indigenous communities or cities disbanding encampments of unhoused individuals and fighting them through the legal system deeply troubles me. Are we prioritizing systems of power over our shared responsibility to uplift the most vulnerable among us?
Having spent more than 25 years in the greater Hamilton area volunteering with local organizations, I've realized that while acts of charity matter, they cannot address systemic inequities. Managing homelessness is not the same as ending it - providing warm meals cannot ensure food security, and knitting mittens for those living rough does not address the fundamental need for a stable home.
This season, as many celebrate the first Christmas of a young couple in desperate need of shelter, I ask: can we stop victimizing those already battling poverty, trauma, discrimination, disabilities or addiction - often without the social scaffolding needed to recover? Instead of holiday rituals, let's reflect on building a society where equity and compassion are cornerstones of action.
Perhaps the greatest gift we can give our children this holiday season is a commitment to use our power, position and privilege to create a better and equitable world! And those rendered voiceless and faceless among us - it is not their burden to carry the shame - the onus is on us.
Myrtle Wong, Kilbride
Get locals
to build shelters
Re: 'Unexpected delays' push opening of Barton temporary shelters to mid-January,
Dec. 19
Why does Hamilton have to buy "tiny homes" for their homeless that are manufactured overseas? Now there is a "delay" in delivery. Surely there must be a company in Ontario or elsewhere in Canada that could build them. Or why not commission a high school or college to build them as a project? The city has had plenty of notice. Why overseas?
Rusty Escott, Dunnville
Don't let Premier Ford know
Re: Thankful for our libraries, Dec. 19
I too think that libraries are wonderful, helpful institutions for people of all ages and cultures. Allyn Walsh has expressed the feelings of many who avail themselves of all the services offered in our libraries.
I think it could be a little dangerous, though, to say too many good things about libraries. If Doug Ford hears about this, chances are good he would want to close them or privatize them or do some other ridiculous thing for "the people!"
Roger Rose, Mount Hope
Freeland
not blameless
Chrystia Freeland had every right to do what she did. She had enough from a prime minister who was out of control. To have Trudeau sucking his thumb in a corner while the country is in crisis proves what a lot of Canadians already knew; he's immature. However, how does Freeland get a free pass? She did what she did not only for her own benefit (leadership wise), she dropped the bomb on Monday having to know what harm it might do to Canada. In my opinion these two have helped put this country into a tailspin. So, Freeland is at fault here (even though she seems to be getting a pass), but certainly not as much as Trudeau.
Robert Savelli, Dunnville

Document 437 of 500

Liberal push to oust PM gains steam: Dozens of Grit MPs from Ontario form 'consensus' that Trudeau must step down, CBC reports

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/liberal-push-oust-pm-gains-steam/docview/3148337104/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Liberal+push+to+oust+PM+gains+steam%3A+Dozens+of+Grit+MPs+from+Ontario+form+%27consensus%27+that+Trudeau+must+step+down%2C+CBC+reports&amp;title=The+Spectator&amp;issn=11899417&amp;date=2024-12-23&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.7&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Spectator&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: More than 50 Liberal party members of parliament from Ontario came to a "consensus" during a conference call Saturday that Justin Trudeau must step down as prime minister, the Canadian Broadcasting Corp. reported.
That would represent about a third of the Liberal caucus, which has 153 seats in Canada's House of Commons. However, there's no formal mechanism for Liberal lawmakers to eject their leader or trigger a contest.
While the Liberals comprise the largest group in parliament, they lack a majority and have relied on votes from members of other parties - primarily the New Democratic Party - to pass legislation and stay in power.
Trudeau's government has been in turmoil since Finance Minister Chrystia Freeland resigned on Dec. 16, saying she and the prime minister were at odds over the direction of the government. Then NDP Leader Jagmeet Singh said on Friday that his 25 members will join two other opposition parties in voting to bring down the government early next year.
Trudeau isn't planning to step down over the winter holiday - he's spending Christmas in Ottawa before a ski vacation in British Columbia - but will continue to reflect on what to do, according to a separate report in the Globe and Mail newspaper on Sunday, which didn't identify where it got the information.
"The die is cast, with the Conservatives, Bloc Québécois and the NDP all saying they want to vote down the current prime minister," Chandra Arya, an Ottawa-area Liberal lawmaker who participated in the weekend meeting, told CBC. "So there's no alternative but to have the leadership change now."
Arya has publicly backed Freeland as an alternative to Trudeau. Others are privately offering her support, the Globe report added, citing an anonymous person.
"We're in an impossible situation if he stays because he will be the ballot issue," Anthony Housefather, a lawmaker from the Montreal area, told the broadcaster in a television interview aired Sunday. Arya and Housefather are two of more than 20 Liberal members of parliament to have come out publicly calling on the prime minister to resign after nine years in power.
If Trudeau tries to run in the next election, "we won't be looking at Liberal programs, we won't be looking at anything else," Housefather said. "It'll all be a question for voters, do they want Justin Trudeau to stay prime minister? I think they've clearly come to a conclusion on that."
Bloomberg News
CAPTION: Prime Minister Justin Trudeau departs after a ceremony at Rideau Hall in Ottawa on Friday.The Canadian Press

CREDIT: Thomas Seal and Derek Decloet Bloomberg News

Document 438 of 500

Is there a Liberal alive who can actually deal with Donald Trump?

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/is-there-liberal-alive-who-can-actually-deal-with/docview/3147862039/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Is+there+a+Liberal+alive+who+can+actually+deal+with+Donald+Trump%3F&amp;title=The+Hamilton+Spectator+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2024-12-22&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Hamilton+Spectator+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Justin Trudeau, abandoned by allies, besieged on all sides, is still, as of this writing, both the leader of the Liberal Party and the prime minister of Canada. But when pundits and political insiders gather in the smoky backrooms (ed note: do vapes emit smoke?) of Ottawa and Toronto this Christmas season they will all be asking each other one question: Who is going to take his job?
In the long term, the answer is still likely Pierre Poilievre, the Conservative leader who holds a commanding lead in national polls. But if Trudeau does step down, a Liberal will have to take his place, at least temporarily.
To get ahead of the speculation, the Star asked some of Canada's keenest political minds to rank the contenders on one criterium: Who is best equipped to deal with Donald Trump? There was a clear winner among the living. But it may say something about Liberal prospects that two of the four pundits put a man who has been dead for 212 years at the top of their lists.
Each contender was allotted points based on the experts voting: nine points for a firs- place vote, eight for second, etc. Our expert panel was made up of Justin Ling, freelance journalist and Star contributing columnist, Ashley Csanady, vice-president, McMillan Vantage, politics writer Matt Gurney and Amanda Galbraith, co-founder and partner at the Oyster Group.
1) Dominic LeBlanc: Finance Minister, Trudeau confidant, man often thrown at things the boss would rather not deal with himself
34 points
Ashley Csanady: 1
LeBlanc sits atop for two reasons: he is the only one on the list to have eaten recently at Mar-a-Lago and is the only one who has the personality to gel with a president who eschews elites, their vernacular and global norms. Word is Trump and LeBlanc developed a bit of a vibe already during the Florida hop, and with Trump that's half the battle.
Justin Ling: 1
He's already the one dealing with Trump. I'm told he fared very well over dinner at Mar-a-Lago.
Amanda Galbraith: 2
A skilled political operator who has the respect of the premiers and opposition parties and could get them to fall in line with a Team Canada approach. One of the few liberals who could speak Trump, and I mean that as a compliment.
Matt Gurney: 2
The PM brought him to Mar-a-Lago for a reason. He's good at the schmooze, by every account. He's also a man, which I hate to admit probably carries more weight than it should with Trump. He would probably do no harm and might be able to charm a few concessions out of the Americans.
2. The ghost of Major-General Sir Isaac Brock: Native of Guernsey, captured Detroit in battle, Britain's military leader during the future Canada's 1812 war with the United States.
26 Points
Gurney: 1
I think my editor included this as a joke, but it's a brilliant idea. At a time when Canada's military spending has become a sore spot with allies, who better than a general with a proven combat record to lead the Liberals? Also, Brock is clearly willing to stand up to the Americans - he literally died in battle against them. Further, given the state of the party today, there's a natural synergy: the Liberals need someone with a proven ability to come back from the dead. Maj.-Gen. Brock all the way.
Galbraith: 1
Experienced at thwarting American ambitions since 1802 and would bring the BDE (can I say that in the Toronto Star?) we need to these negotiations. Was not so secretly contemptuous of Canada so he and Trump could bond over that.
Csanady: 4
The odds are about even that Canada will need Major-General Brock's very specific set of skills (defending the Niagara, saving Windsor, etc.) soon. That's why I'm putting him smack in the middle.
Ling: 8
Canada's archaic Challenger jets aren't retrofitted to carry ghosts. Brock would have to walk back-and-forth to Mar-a-Lago, wasting precious time.
3. Anita Anand: Minister of Transport and Internal Trade, ex-procurement, defence and Treasury Board, famous brooker of no nonsense
23 Points
Gurney: 3
Anand is smart and has a reputation for not tolerating BS. That's going to be an advantage. She has experience at defence and with critical financial files. She'd be understood to be serious and wouldn't be pushed around. That ain't nothing, and "ain't nothing" counts for more than it used to.
Ling: 3
Just as Joe Biden hates malarkey, Anita Anand hates nonsense. Given how much nonsense is coming from the White House, her work would be cut out for her.
Csanady: 5
Has the CV of someone who should be able to negotiate a trade deal with the U.S. She's also a Fulbright-award-winning lawyer and professor who guest lectured at Yale. But her academic pedigree and tone is exactly what would make it a challenge for her to build the personal rapport that Trump values.
Galbraith: 6
Smart and hard working but has never stood out as someone with the communications chops to go toe-to-toe with Trump.
4. François-Philippe Champagne: Minister of Innovation, Science and Industry, ex-international businessman, Shawnigan's second most famous political son
22 Points
Gurney: 4
Honestly, my list gets a bit grim starting here. I don't think Champagne would be an aggressively bad idea to lead Canada in dealing with Trump. That's enough to secure him the fourth spot.
Galbraith: 4
Brings energy and ambition Trump might like, but is too closely tied to Trudeau.
Csanady: 5
Could use his deep network to pull together top CEOs to drive a true Team Canada push from Bay Street. He's indefatigable. But I don't see Trump kicking back over a burger with him the same way he would with LeBlanc or Clark.
Ling: 5
Charming and business-minded, but Trump's people will have no idea what he's saying.
5. Mark Carney: ex governor, banks of Canada and England. The political equivalent of the guy who always says he's going to come out next time but never, ever comes out.
21 points
Ling: 2
The former England (and Canadian) central banker has played big boy geopolitics for ages. He's got the chops.
Galbraith: 3
Has star power Trump might like. Savvy diplomat that stick-handled Brexit and dealt with Boris Johnson while at the Bank of England. Experienced in chaos but his support for the carbon tax will hurt him.
Csanady: 6
I just can't see him jibing with Trump. He's a central banker twice over and Trump hates the global financial order. The one argument to put Carney higher on this list is his global network might allow him to tap into others who could bend Trump's ear. Maybe Boris Johnson could broker trade talks?
Gurney: 8
I can't really imagine any possible qualification for Carney here. I know he's a qualified guy in general. But he's ideologically wrong for Trump and seems to have alienated the only political party in Canada that has shown any interest in him. He's never been a worse choice for the job. And even he seems to get that.
6. Christy Clark: Former premier of British Columbia. Once tried to run for mayor of Vancouver while not living in Vancouver.
19 points
Csanady: 3
Has personal charm and Western connections, especially to natural resources, that would likely appeal to Trump. Plus, she knows how to play the media stunt game required to take on a gameshow host turned president.
Galbraith: 3
Gets both business and politics. Charismatic and has the unique ability to appeal to conservatives and liberals. Only person on this list who has openly criticized the PM - which would play in her favour with Trump. Is, however, a woman, which is a disadvantage when dealing with Trump, unless you are Angela Merkel or a contestant for Miss Universe. (This also applies to Freeland, Anand and Joly.)
Gurney: 6
This would be such a baffling choice - most Canadians don't even know who Clark is - that maybe it would throw the Americans off? Worth a shot?
Ling: 9
Who cares? She will never be leader.
7. Chrystia Freeland: Negotiated Canada's last trade deal with the United States. Last seen chucking her boss over the side of her boat.
16 points
Csanady: 2
Freeland already lives rent-free in Trump's head. So, for better or worse, he knows who she is and with Trump, that's a start. The gumption Freeland showed in her letter of resignation is the same force of will that brought back the Canada-EU Free Trade deal from the brink and saved free trade with the U.S. the first time around.
Ling: 6
She is, if nothing else, very good at pissing off Donald Trump.
Galbraith: 8
I mean, do I even have to explain it anymore?
Gurney: 9
If we are deliberately looking to insult Trump, Freeland is our best chance. He clearly can't stand her.
We're not trying to insult him, are we?
Are we?
8. Nathaniel Erskine-Smith: Newly crowned housing minister. Famous maverick. Known to sometimes change his mind about quitting.
14 points
Ling: 4
He's young, wonky, ambitious, optimistic, charismatic, and critical of all the ways in which Trudeau has floundered. He's like an alternate reality JD Vance.
Gurney: 5
Too young, too inexperienced, no particular heft or skillset that the Trump team would find valuable, unless they're really into podcasting and granular urban policy plans.
Galbraith: 9
A nice person who lacks the real-world experience to manage a crisis of this scale. But Godspeed if by some miracle he's the last man standing and has to try.
Csanady: 9
Really?
9. Melanie Joly: Foreign Affairs Minister. Possibly more popular in New York than Ottawa.
11 points
Gurney: 7
Basically the ideological opposite of what the U.S. is going to want from us. They're demanding we up our military, she's extolling the virtue of the UN. Pass.
Galbraith: 7
Way too progressive and far too closely tied to the PM to make it work. Her past lectures on progressive values will land like a lead balloon.
Ling: 7
Foreign affairs are not Joly's strong suit.
Csanady: 8
Has a lot of strengths as a leadership contender: charisma, Quebec connections and a breadth of experience in cabinet. But negotiating with Trump means negotiating in public, via the media, with clean, crisp messaging, something Joly has struggled with on the Middle East file.
CAPTION: The death of Brock at Queeston Heights.Dominic LeBlanc, Minister of Finance, Public Safety and Intergovernmental Affairs, participates in a news conference after a swearing in ceremony at Rideau Hall in Ottawa, on Monday, Dec. 16, 2024.

Document 439 of 500

It seems everyone but Justin Trudeau knows it's time for him to go. Why is it so hard to quit?

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/seems-everyone-justin-trudeau-knows-time-him-go/docview/3147692480/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=It+seems+everyone+but+Justin+Trudeau+knows+it%27s+time+for+him+to+go.+Why+is+it+so+hard+to+quit%3F&amp;title=The+Hamilton+Spectator+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2024-12-21&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Hamilton+Spectator+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Prime Minister Justin Trudeau appears to be teetering on the brink.
He's dealt with scandal before. A family trip to a private Bahamas island yielded a damning 2017 ethics report. The SNC-Lavalin affair threatened his government. Photos of Trudeau wearing brownface and a turban rocked his 2019 election campaign.
But right now, things are grim. The latest polls indicate Trudeau's Liberal party is trailing the Conservatives by 20 percentage points. The number of Liberal MPs calling for Trudeau's resignation climbed to 21 this week, with as many as 45 signing a letter behind closed doors calling for him to step down, sources have told the Star. And, also this week, the biggest blow of all: deputy prime minister and minister of finance Chrystia Freeland resigned from Trudeau's cabinet in a stunning public rebuke.
Sources not authorized to speak to the media said Trudeau is reflecting on his political future. And yet, publicly, he has not committed to leaving. Perhaps it is because, according to experts in politics, history and psychology, quitting is simply hard. It is a tortuous internal process playing out in an extraordinarily public way. The weighing of identity against failure, humiliation against graceful defeat, public discontent against legacy. All the while, the sharks circle.
"It was very hard," said Kathleen Wynne of her experience in the 2018 Ontario election, when as premier she suffered a stunning loss that reduced her Liberal party to seven seats before she resigned as party leader. "The whole thing was brutal because it did feel like, as the leader, I was letting people down and that's a horrible feeling."
Knowing when to quit is part of the dilemma.
There are signs. According to Raymond Blake, a professor at the University of Regina who specializes in 20th century Canadian politics, two of the biggest are public and party discontent. A lapse of judgment is another. Failure to meet expectations, too.
But even with the signs, it can be hard to quit. History is littered with examples of those who have hung on past their expiration date.
"It's really, really tough for anyone who's at the top of their game to give it up," Blake said. "Walking away is tough."
But why?
Psychologically speaking, our careers become deeply ingrained in who we are, according to Marie-Hélène Budworth, the director of York University's school of human resource management who has a PhD in organizational behaviour. At a dinner party, the answer to the question "Who are you?" is often occupation.
Quitting leaves the quitter searching for a new answer.
"It can be a real struggle to make the decision to leave it behind," Budworth said, "because you're leaving part of your identity."
This dilemma, of course, is only exaggerated for a role as public-facing as the prime minister of Canada.
Budworth also suggests social circle could play a role in Trudeau's decision. Often, the people around us agree with our views. It can create the notion of a false consensus - that most people are on my side because this small group is.
And there's also the public consequences of quitting.
"We live in a world where quitting is often seen as failure," Budworth explained. "Someone who quits couldn't hack it. They couldn't make it work."
It doesn't help people aren't good at remembering accomplishments, Budworth said. Instead, our nature is to simply remember how something ends. Thus, leaving in a cloud of controversy "does leave a lasting scar," Blake said.
It impacts legacy. Rehabilitating public opinion is a natural process for any public leader once they've left office. Trudeau's legacy will be damaged if he leaves now, but it will suffer even more if he sticks around and is crushed in the next election, Blake predicted.
Wynne has wrestled with these questions. She polled as low as 12 per cent the year prior to the 2018 election. She said she knew she would never ride to victory in the election, but as the campaign progressed, she learned just how bad it was. Door-knocking Liberal candidates were told: Love you, hate your leader.
By early June, the writing was on the wall. Five days before the election, Wynne admitted defeat.
"I don't know who voters will choose, but I am pretty sure that it won't be me," she told reporters. "After Thursday, I will no longer be Ontario's premier."
Her goal, she told the Star this week, was to get out of the way. She wanted voters to support her party with full knowledge she would no longer hold her office. Reaching that decision was brutal, she said, but she knew she had to go.
"I had become a lightning rod for anger among people in the province," Wynne said. "People associated me with something they didn't want anymore ... I was the past and we needed the future to take over."
What Trudeau faces is different. Every situation is. It is different from his father, Pierre, who in 1984 decided to resign after a walk in the snow. That decision came amidst a period of unpopularity for the Liberals, with a struggling economy and divided country weighing the prime minister down. And it was certainly not a surprise, with Trudeau having committed to stepping down as leader in 1979 before backtracking.
For the elder Trudeau, his decision in 1984 seemed to come with breezing ease. "I had a good day yesterday," he said on making his decision, "and it seemed like a good day to have a last day."
For Justin Trudeau, this decision appears to be anything but easy.
CAPTION: Liberal Leader Justin Trudeau speaks during a Liberal Party fundraising event in Richmond, B.C., on Dec. 12, 2024.

Title: It seems everyone but Justin Trudeau knows it's time for him to go. Why is it so hard to quit?

Document 440 of 500

When Doug Ford looks at Donald Trump, he sees an opportunity

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/when-doug-ford-looks-at-donald-trump-he-sees/docview/3147640927/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=When+Doug+Ford+looks+at+Donald+Trump%2C+he+sees+an+opportunity&amp;title=The+Hamilton+Spectator+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2024-12-21&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Hamilton+Spectator+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Never mind Donald Trump and Justin Trudeau.
Forget the mayor and municipal council.
Doug Ford can do it all and get it done. He is one-stop shopping and one-stop voting.
Our once-upon-a-time premier has been our acting mayor since winning his first provincial election six years ago. Now he's acting like prime minister until further notice, or the next federal election - whichever comes first.
Perched at Queen's Park, ensconced in his Etobicoke hideaway, he lords it over city hall and presides over Parliament Hill. Grabbing the gavel as rotating chair of the Council of the Federation this month, he now speaks, purportedly, for the premiers.
Not to mention his inherited leadership of the family firm, Ford Nation. From coast to Etobicoke to coast.
Think of him as premier of premiers, prime minister of the 51st state, mayor of Toronto and ruler (or reeve) of Ontario's 443 other municipalities. All in one.
Pasha, for short.
In short, Ford now stands tall. He casts a long shadow, broadcasting and recasting his image on every possible platform.
See him on CP24's all-news channel cutting a ribbon or slashing a program. Watch him on CPAC's all-politics channel convening his fellow premiers.
Look now - he's on CNN appealing to America's president-elect. Look again - he's on Fox News, trying again to get Trump's attention. He's all over X, ready to dance a jig with Elon Musk.
In politics, appearing on every channel is a good way to change the channel. Why worry about homeless people when you can adjourn the legislature for nearly three months during the worst of the winter chill and spend your days saving the country from America's predatory depredations?
He's already booked a flight to Washington in late February, leading a delegation of his fellow premiers to meet congressional leaders and U.S. governors. Ford has announced he will go to the White House to plead Canada's case, if Trump will have him.
"I just look forward to sitting down with him eye to eye, businessman to businessman, and saying, 'You know, we need to cut a deal that's going to be beneficial for Americans and Canadians,'" Ford said this week, after boasting about go-betweens trying to arrange a meeting between the leader of the free world and the governor of Ford Nation.
Let us not be churlish, in this holiday season, about Ford's multi-tasking, multi-asking, multimedia act. There's something to be said for Ford saying whatever comes into his head these days.
Trudeau is absent without love or leave, as is his parliamentary opposition. Amid the opportunism, the premier sees an opportunity.
After six years in power at Queen's Park meddling municipally, he can't help fantasizing federally and dabbling diplomatically. He sees himself as Canada's communicator-in-chief, no matter his mangled messaging.
The disputatious among us might question his judgment about threatening to cut off Ontario's energy exports to our dear neighbours in Dearborn, Detroit and beyond. Certainly the premiers of Canada's energy-abundant provinces - Newfoundland, Quebec and Alberta - were quick to dissociate themselves from Ford's musings, lest his threats introduce uncertainty into the lucrative, long-term contracts they have signed and seek with other neighbouring states.
Their jitters were unsurprising. If you are trying to lock Vermont into an eternal relationship, complete with expensive power lines, you don't want to put it on the chopping block every time Trump creates a crisis that may turn into an empty threat from one year to the next.
That said, as much as Alberta's Danielle Smith shuddered over Ford's faux pas, her predecessors were willing to let us shiver: "Let those eastern bastards freeze in the dark," Albertans were once fond of saying.
On balance, however, there is method to the premier's madness. The president-elect doesn't think twice when uttering the equivalent of economic death threats to Canada, so why not fight mad with mad, madness with madness, Trump with Ford?
No matter how unpredictable Ford can be, he deserves credit for trying to break through the noise, as federal Finance Minister Dominic LeBlanc mused the other day. His rhetorical approach and latest reproach provoked a riposte from the president-elect, who publicly dismissed the idea of Ontario cutting the cord on electricity exports.
Turns out we got a rise out of Trump. And raised a few raised eyebrows among electricity consumers in Michigan (home of American's next ambassador to Ottawa, Pete Hoekstra, lest we forget).
We won't know until next year whether Ford's crusade helps Canada. The question of the hour, however, is will it help him win another term as premier?
CAPTION: Premier Doug Ford speaks to reporters during the fall meetings of Canada's premiers hosted by Ontario in Toronto on Dec. 16, 2024.

Hamilton home that was site of deadly fire being rebuilt

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/hamilton-home-that-was-site-deadly-fire-being/docview/3147378733/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Hamilton+home+that+was+site+of+deadly+fire+being+rebuilt&amp;title=The+Hamilton+Spectator+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2024-12-20&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Hamilton+Spectator+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: A fire-damaged home that drew safety concerns from residents is getting a major facelift.
The city says it has issued a building permit to reconstruct the derelict property at 8 Dalkeith Ave., which staff deemed "structurally inadequate" after a blaze March 19 left one person dead and another critically hurt.
Remnants of the two-storey century home - one neighbours previously described as a makeshift rooming house - were limited to its concrete front porch Tuesday, with construction workers pictured standing atop a half-built first floor of plywood. The property currently has no second floor or attic.
Three days after the deadly fire, the city issued an order to the homeowner, requiring them to safely secure the crumbling structure and hire an engineer to assess its structural integrity.
Neither of those happened for months.
In late May, a fence was erected around the property after multiple Spectator queries to the city about the state of the dwelling and the lack of action from the owner.
Residents had expressed safety concerns about the home - soot-covered debris still hung from blown-out windows - and people allegedly continued to filter in and out despite the bylaw order and a plank of wood covering its front door. Tents, propane tanks and piles of garbage could be seen in the backyard at the time. Coun. Nrinder Nann said she'd heard from residents who were "concerned further fires may occur."
Neighbourhood worries about the property were nothing new.
At the time of the fire, police said the home was running without water, heat or hydro. The Ontario Fire Marshal - who's yet to determine a cause in the blaze - said there were no working smoke alarms inside. Neighbours, meanwhile, described the dwelling as an unsanctioned rooming house with people constantly shuffling in and out. Police and bylaw were frequently called there for noise and garbage complaints, according to residents.
The city said last summer it was consulting with a third-party engineer to conduct a structural review of the property given its owner's neglect of the bylaw order.
Property records show the home was first purchased in February 1975.
After its owner died on May 23, the property was transferred to a family member under the terms of a will, records show. The home was then sold for $202,500 on July 16 to a numbered corporation whose director is a Toronto realtor, according to federal corporate filings.
The Spectator was unable to reach the realtor.
It's unclear what they plan to do with the property after it's built. The city said it's given the new owner the green light to "return the dwelling to pre-loss conditions in accordance with the engineered plans/report."
Sebastian Bron is a reporter with The Hamilton Spectator.
CAPTION: A fence was erected around 8 Dalkeith Ave. in late May after neighbours expressed concerns about its structural integrity.Construction has begun at 8 Dalkeith Ave., where one person was killed and another critically injured after a blaze March 19.

Document 442 of 500

Ontario auditor makes serious allegations against the Doug Ford's government. Here's what columnist Peter Watson thinks of the controversial report

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/ontario-auditor-makes-serious-allegations-against/docview/3147378361/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Ontario+auditor+makes+serious+allegations+against+the+Doug+Ford%27s+government.+Here%27s+what+columnist+Peter+Watson+thinks+of+the+controversial+report&amp;title=The+Hamilton+Spectator+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2024-12-20&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Hamilton+Spectator+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: There is an allegation that the Ontario Conservative government used public funds to advertise and promote their political party versus for the good of the public. Ontario's auditor reported that Ontario spent $103 million on government advertising, which is three times more money than it spent the previous year. This information was reported by CBC.
The auditor said the majority of advertising campaigns were more partisan, supporting the Conservative party rather than informing Ontario residents of provincial issues.
Politicians sometimes make decisions that, in my opinion, are in their best interest and not in the best interests of taxpayers.
Financially, Ontario is a powerhouse. We have a lot going for us, and for that, we can be proud. But the reality is our provincial government spends more money than it takes in taxes, and our debt continues to climb.
My recent article criticized the federal Liberal government for offering a brief GST holiday that was announced as a generous gift to acknowledge many struggling to keep up with the cost of living.
The federal gift was not an actual gift from the Liberal party, but this generous holiday season gift was paid for by adding to our increasingly high federal debt.
The way that public money is spent by politicians is vastly different than how money is spent by business.
If senior business leaders overspent their company's money during times that they have significant negative cash outflows and mounting debt, there would be serious consequences. Most likely they would be fired - likely immediately.
Canada and many of the provinces are not dealing from a position of financial strength. Strong fiscal management is necessary.
This allegation from Ontario's auditor is a serious pushback on how our province appears to be mismanaging its finances.
CAPTION: Peter Watson, of Watson Investments, writes a weekly column Dollars & Sense.
