Document 443 of 500

Justin Trudeau avoids questions about his future after cabinet overhaul

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/justin-trudeau-avoids-questions-about-his-future/docview/3147378334/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Justin+Trudeau+avoids+questions+about+his+future+after+cabinet+overhaul&amp;title=The+Hamilton+Spectator+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2024-12-20&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Hamilton+Spectator+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: OTTAWA-An embattled Prime Minister Justin Trudeau made a significant overhaul to his cabinet Friday, while his leadership continues to be in question.
However, Trudeau has still not answered questions about his future, following Chrystia Freeland's surprise resignation as finance minister on Monday and calls from Liberal MPs to step down.
After the first meeting of his new cabinet, Trudeau briefly spoke with reporters, but did not address questions about whether he will stay or whether he will prorogue Parliament.
He said the first cabinet meeting was focused on the incoming Donald Trump administration and the president-elect's threat to impose 25-per-cent tariffs on Canadian exports to the U.S.
"We know how important it is for Canadians that we be there to protect not just the Canadian economy, but their jobs. The cost of living challenges they're facing, they could get worse. We have a lot of work to do," he said.
Trudeau brought in eight new people in the cabinet shuffle and moved several others.
Nate Erskine-Smith, a Toronto MP known for being outspoken and sometimes voting against his own party, will join cabinet as housing minister, replacing Sean Fraser who announced he would not run again on Monday.
Ottawa MP David McGuinty will be the new public safety minister, taking over the role from Dominic LeBlanc, who became finance minister on Monday after Chrystia Freeland's shocking resignation.
Also among the new faces, Montreal MP Rachel Bendayan will become the official languages minister and associate minister of public safety.
Nova Scotia MP Darren Fisher will be the veterans' affairs minister and associate minister of defence. Winnipeg MP Terry Duguid will be the minister for sport and responsible for Prairies economic development.
Newfoundland MP Joanne Thompson will take on the seniors file, Quebec MP Élisabeth Brière will take on the post of national revenue and Ruby Sahota will be responsible for southern Ontario economic development and democratic institutions.
There are also changes for current cabinet ministers. Oakville MP Anita Anand who has been doing double duty as transport minister and Treasury Board president, will stay on as transport minister and pick up a new portfolio focused on internal trade.
New Brunswick MP Ginette Petitpas Taylor will take on the Treasury Board post, dropping her position as veterans' affairs and employment minister. The employment portfolio will be taken on by Labour Minister Steven MacKinnon.
Gary Anandasangaree, the minister of Indigenous Crown Relations, will have northern affairs and northern economic development added to his responsibilities.
Just before the shuffle, NDP Leader Jagmeet Singh made clear he will vote to bring down the government at the first opportunity when the House of Commons returns in late January, this could mean the new ministers will only serve in their portfolio for a matter of weeks.
Erskine-Smith who had indicated he would not be running for reelection said he will now stand in the next campaign. He said ultimately, however long he has the portfolio, he believes he has a chance to make real change.
"I understand there's gonna be a short runway. I'm not blind to that. But if I can make a small difference, if I can make a big difference, I will make the biggest difference that I can. That's why I joined politics in the first place," he said.
Bendayan said the prime minister is still reflecting on his next steps, but she took him up on the offer because she felt called to serve.
"The prime minister was quite clear that he has a choice that he's reflecting on. I think each one of us, and certainly I'll speak for myself. I made a choice today," she said. "The decision that I made is based on the intention that I always had and that was to enter politics not worrying about the next election or the partisan nature of this job, but in order to serve Canadians."
Ahead of his first cabinet meeting, McGuinty said he believes the government can still find a way to negotiate with the other parties, despite Singh's promise to bring down the government.
"Everything is negotiable in the House of Commons. That has always been the case. There is always a pathway forward," he said.
Trudeau has continued to face questions about his leadership since Freeland announced her resignation as finance minister and deputy prime minister on Monday. There are currently 20 Liberal MPs calling on the prime minister to resign, with Toronto MP Rob Oliphant the latest to add his name to this list.
Freeland quit just hours before she was set to deliver the fall fiscal update, and was critical of the government's spending plans in her resignation letter, arguing the prime minister was not taking U.S. president-elect Donald Trump's trade threats seriously.
CAPTION: Prime Minister Justin Trudeau delivers remarks during a national caucus holiday event in Ottawa on Dec. 17, 2024.Trudeau shuffles his cabinet after chaotic week for Liberals | LIVECPAC Special: Cabinet shuffle in Ottawa - December 20th, 2024

Document 444 of 500

How Bonnie Crombie fits into Chrystia Freeland's critique

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/how-bonnie-crombie-fits-into-chrystia-freelands/docview/3147191743/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=How+Bonnie+Crombie+fits+into+Chrystia+Freeland%27s+critique&amp;title=The+Hamilton+Spectator+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2024-12-18&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Hamilton+Spectator+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Bonnie Crombie, meet Chrystia Freeland.
You have a common frenemy. But you each have a very different political strategy and personal reality.
Crombie, of course, is the Ontario Liberal leader who aspires to be the province's next premier. Freeland, before we forget, is the former federal finance minister who dreams of being Canada's next Liberal prime minister.
Both politicians are feverishly distancing themselves from Justin Trudeau's political dead weight: Crombie doesn't want an albatross around her neck; Freeland didn't want Trudeau chopping her political neck.
But they are wary of the PM in very different ways.
This week, Freeland offered a furious denunciation of Trudeau's latest tax cuts and rebates as ill-timed "gimmicks." Her headline-grabbing critique on Parliament Hill was economically instructive, if perhaps politically destructive and personally self-serving.
In recent weeks, Crombie has proffered her own public objections to federal government tax policies. In search of her own headlines (harder to garner at Queen's Park), Crombie dissociated herself from the shared Freeland-Trudeau legacy, notably the carbon levy.
In a flurry of pre-election platform announcements, Crombie has promised to cut income taxes and slash government fees for eligible first-time homebuyers, as she remakes herself into a tax-fighter for the times. For we live in increasingly anti-tax times.
The timing and tactics of these two public figures, Crombie and Freeland, serve as reminders of how hard it is, in today's increasingly volatile climate, to discern serious policy from self-serving politicking.
In her belated cri de coeur - made public, conveniently, only after she lost her job - Freeland decried Trudeau's Christmas tax cuts as pointless politics. Better to "keep our powder dry," she argued, in case all those billions are needed to put out fires in any future trade war with Donald Trump's America.
Fair enough. But what if the president-elect ultimately forgoes those threatened 25-per-cent tariffs, would Freeland still condemn the Liberal government's tax breaks down the road?
One suspects that if she were still in cabinet, keen to save the collective political skin of her fellow Liberals, she'd be fully on board for a pre-campaign tax giveaway - not today, just later in the day, before election day. Which makes Freeland's clear-eyed critique less thoughtful and more situational.
The point is that Freeland, too, is a product of our political times, even if she purports to be different. Tax cuts are now the order of the day - the only way for any politician to cut through the noise and collect votes.
Tories aren't the only tax-fighters. New Democrats are also leading the way, from federal NDP Leader Jagmeet Singh's latest demands to Manitoba Premier Wab Kinew's early tax cuts when his NDP government took power.
The difference, perhaps, is that Freeland felt more guilt than the others because she backed the expansion of child care, pharmacare and dental care programs. It's hard to call for more spending in one breath while calling for tax cuts in the next, although that hasn't stopped some politicians (Trudeau and Ford, for example) from sucking and blowing at the same time.
It's worth noting that various provincial premiers, led by Ontario's Doug Ford and Manitoba's Kinew, offered Freeland heartfelt praise when her resignation became public Monday. Never mind that her impassioned critique of gimmicky tax cuts applies just as much to the tax-cutting sprees of these two like-minded premiers from two opposing parties.
Which is where Crombie finds herself as she tries to stand out in a crowded political field. Personally, my heart sinks whenever I hear politicians play the anti-tax card, because I believe in the social programs that make government an essential force for good in increasingly bad times.
I hear people complain bitterly about "hallway health care" (as Ford has since before taking power six years ago); I listen to people worry about the impact of climate change on weather patterns (as they lambaste the refundable carbon levy); I watch families fret about the scourge of rising homelessness and drug addiction on our streets; and I heed the need for pharmacare and dental care as part of medicare.
You can't have it both ways, calling for more funding and fewer taxes in the same breath, because de-taxing ultimately leads to defunding. On the other hand, Crombie (and Freeland, for that matter) can't get elected today when they are flanked by new-found tax-fighters to the left and right.
Which is why Freeland's critique felt fatuous after the fact. Her gratuitous rhetoric about gimmicky tax cuts might have more credibility were she not guilty of her own inconstancy and inconsistency on fiscal policy.
Against that backdrop of political contradictions, what are we to make of Crombie's new-found tax-cutting persona as she tries to court anti-tax voters, which is to say most of the electorate? Should we reproach her for telling voters what they like to hear - and are hearing in full force from Tories and New Democrats as elections loom?
It turns out that Crombie's housing plan has won plaudits from some experts and advocates as good policy - a way to break down barriers for first-time buyers. Mostly, though, it may be good politics for first-time voters.
The challenge for any politician - friend, foe or frenemy - is to find a way.
CAPTION: Ontario Liberal Leader Bonnie Crombie is shown at Queen's Park in Toronto on Dec. 12, 2024.

Subject: Dental care; Tax cuts; Voters; Politicians; Political parties

Business indexing term: Subject: Tax cuts

People: Freeland, Chrystia

Title: How Bonnie Crombie fits into Chrystia Freeland's critique

Document 445 of 500

Province's Taro petition response touts 'progress' on dump odours - but residents disagree

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/provinces-taro-petition-response-touts-progress/docview/3146162513/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Province%27s+Taro+petition+response+touts+%27progress%27+on+dump+odours+-+but+residents+disagree&amp;title=The+Hamilton+Spectator+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2024-12-18&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Hamilton+Spectator+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: It took more than a year, but the 4,150 people who signed a petition demanding the province order GFL to cease any odour-causing operations at upper Stoney Creek's Taro industrial dump finally have an answer.
"GFL has made significant progress in addressing odour emissions from the landfill, resulting in a notable reduction in community complaints," environment minister Andrea Khanjin said in a Dec. 5 formal written response.
"While some complaints persist, their frequency has significantly decreased, indicating the effectiveness of the measures taken to date."
Khanjin credits the "significant progress" on odours to her ministry's enforcement actions, including orders requiring GFL to speed up the removal the dump's excessive leachate levels and lower waste piles exceeding approved height limits.
"GFL plans to install a new, fully enclosed leachate treatment system, which will be equipped with pollution control systems to manage odours," she stated, citing a target completion date of mid-2025.
"It will replace the current outdoor pond, which was a significant source of odours experienced by residents in the summer of 2023."
Victoria Moral, a petition organizer and member of citizens group Stand for Stoney Creek, said Khanjin's assessment "disregards the ongoing disruption to daily life, property enjoyment, and community well-being" created by the dump odours.
The ministry received 898 odour complaints in 2024 as of Dec. 13, most of which were "from the roads around the perimeter of the landfill and from residents who live in very close proximity to the site," a spokesperson said. GFL had also received 427 complaints as of Dec. 4.
That compares to 1,132 complaints to the ministry in 2023, when odours became a problem that April. Figures for complaints to GFL that year aren't publicly available.
"While the odours are less severe than the peak during the summer of 2023, they remain a recurring nuisance," Moral said in an email on behalf of her group. "The ministry appears to have shifted expectations, suggesting the community should tolerate 'intermittent' odours."
Moral said Khanjin rebuffed relentless efforts to meet with her and even getting the petition tabled at Queen's Park proved to be an ordeal.
Her group dropped the 508 pages of signatures off at area MPP Donna Skelly's office on Dec. 1, 2023, only to be told the petition didn't meet a requirement that it address the Legislative Assembly of Ontario, she said.
It was ultimately returned at the end of April, she said, and organizers added a cover sheet to meet the legislature's rules, with Toronto NDP MPP Mary-Margaret McMahon tabling it on June 6.
That required Khanjin to file a response to the legislature within 24 sessional days - a period prolonged by MPPs' summer break - and she complied on the 24th day.
Moral said despite Khanjin's assurances, the ministry, GFL and the city's public health department have yet to explain why the dump suddenly became a problem in 2023 after decades of only getting periodic odour complaints.
The planned leachate treatment plant and two years the ministry has given GFL to lower waste stockpiles lack the "urgency needed to restore the community's quality of life," she said.
"In the meantime, odorous stockpiles remain visible and continue to contribute to the problem," she said. "How is this acceptable?"
In a recent interview, Brad Clark, the area's councillor, said he isn't convinced the site's leachate is the only odour source and believes GFL and the ministry are underestimating how long it will take to get the treatment plant in place.
A GFL consultant initially said the plant would be running by the end of 2024, but it still needs city and ministry approvals, including on the site plan and to connect to a city trunk sewer by Centennial Parkway, Clark said.
The ministry's Hamilton district manager, Stephen Burt, also told a Dec. 2 meeting of the site's community liaison committee the treatment plant will need an amendment to the dump's approvals that will first require a 45-day public commenting period on the province's environmental registry.
CAPTION: It took more than a year, but the 4,150 people who signed a petition demanding the province to order GFL to cease any odour-causing operations at upper Stoney Creek's Taro industrial dump finally have an answer.

National briefs: Net-zero target pushed to 2050

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/national-briefs/docview/3146138194/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=National+briefs%3A+Net-zero+target+pushed+to+2050&amp;title=The+Spectator&amp;issn=11899417&amp;date=2024-12-18&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.10&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Spectator&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Canada
Liberals lose
B.C. byelection
Federal Conservative Leader Pierre Poilievre is hailing a decisive British Columbia byelection victory in Cloverdale-Langley City, where Tamara Jansen has won with about two-thirds of the vote. Monday's byelection in the Fraser Valley seat, which had been held by the Liberals, came on the day Prime Minister Justin Trudeau's ruling party was thrown into turmoil by finance minister Chrystia Freeland's resignation.
Net-zero target pushed to 2050
The federal government has pushed its target to achieve a net-zero electricity grid to 2050. The government had aimed to fully decarbonize electricity grids by 2035. But some provinces, namely Alberta and Saskatchewan, said that was not doable. The final clean electricity regulations published Tuesday also projected a lower reduction of greenhouse gas emissions than had previously been forecast.
Ontario
Terror suspect charged with war crimes
A man facing terror charges for allegedly plotting an attack in Toronto with his son is now also facing multiple war crimes charges. The RCMP say 62-year-old Ahmed Eldidi - who was arrested along with his son earlier this year for alleged terrorism offences - faces four war crimes charges, including murder, mutilation and torture against a protected person in a "non-international armed conflict." Details on the alleged war crimes, including where they occurred, were not immediately available. The RCMP say it's the first national security investigation where war crimes charges have been laid in Canada.
News services

Province's Taro petition response touts 'progress' on odours: Organizer says minister's assessment disregards 'ongoing disruption to daily life'

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/provinces-taro-petition-response-touts-progress/docview/3146137443/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Province%27s+Taro+petition+response+touts+%27progress%27+on+odours%3A+Organizer+says+minister%27s+assessment+disregards+%27ongoing+disruption+to+daily+life%27&amp;title=The+Spectator&amp;issn=11899417&amp;date=2024-12-18&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.4&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Spectator&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: It took more than a year, but the 4,150 people who signed a petition demanding the province order GFL to cease any odour-causing operations at upper Stoney Creek's Taro industrial dump finally have an answer.
"GFL has made significant progress in addressing odour emissions from the landfill, resulting in a notable reduction in community complaints," environment minister Andrea Khanjin said in a Dec. 5 formal written response.
"While some complaints persist, their frequency has significantly decreased, indicating the effectiveness of the measures taken to date."
Khanjin credits the "significant progress" on odours to her ministry's enforcement actions, including orders requiring GFL to speed up the removal the dump's excessive leachate levels and lower waste piles exceeding approved height limits.
"GFL plans to install a new, fully enclosed leachate treatment system, which will be equipped with pollution control systems to manage odours," she stated, citing a target completion date of mid-2025.
"It will replace the current outdoor pond, which was a significant source of odours experienced by residents in the summer of 2023."
Victoria Moral, a petition organizer and member of citizens group Stand for Stoney Creek, said Khanjin's assessment "disregards the ongoing disruption to daily life, property enjoyment, and community well-being" created by the dump odours.
The ministry received 898 odour complaints in 2024 as of Friday, most of which were "from the roads around the perimeter of the landfill and from residents who live in very close proximity to the site," a spokesperson said. GFL had also received 427 complaints as of Dec. 4.
That compares to 1,132 complaints to the ministry in 2023, when odours became a problem that April. Figures for complaints to GFL that year aren't publicly available.
"While the odours are less severe than the peak during the summer of 2023, they remain a recurring nuisance," Moral said in an email on behalf of her group. "The ministry appears to have shifted expectations, suggesting the community should tolerate 'intermittent' odours."
Moral said Khanjin rebuffed relentless efforts to meet with her and even getting the petition tabled at Queen's Park proved to be an ordeal. Her group dropped the 508 pages of signatures off at area MPP Donna Skelly's office on Dec. 1, 2023, only to be told the petition didn't meet a requirement that it address the Legislative Assembly of Ontario, she said.
It was ultimately returned at the end of April, she said, and organizers added a cover sheet to meet the legislature's rules, with Toronto NDP MPP Mary-Margaret McMahon tabling it on June 6.
That required Khanjin to file a response to the legislature within 24 sessional days - a period prolonged by MPPs' summer break - and she complied on the 24th day.
Moral said despite Khanjin's assurances, the ministry, GFL and the city's public health department have yet to explain why the dump suddenly became a problem in 2023 after decades of only getting periodic odour complaints.
The planned leachate treatment plant and two years the ministry has given GFL to lower waste stockpiles lack the "urgency needed to restore the community's quality of life," she said.
"In the meantime, odorous stockpiles remain visible and continue to contribute to the problem," she said. "How is this acceptable?"
In a recent interview, Brad Clark, the area's councillor, said he isn't convinced the site's leachate is the only odour source and believes GFL and the ministry are underestimating how long it will take to get the treatment plant in place.

The ministry's Hamilton district manager, Stephen Burt, also told a Dec. 2 meeting of the site's community liaison committee the treatment plant will need an amendment to the dump's approvals that will first require a 45-day public commenting period on the province's environmental registry.

Useful tools make thoughtful gifts

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/useful-tools-make-thoughtful-gifts/docview/3146137392/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Useful+tools+make+thoughtful+gifts&amp;title=The+Hamilton+Spectator+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2024-12-18&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Hamilton+Spectator+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Looking for some last-minute gifts? Practical, quality tools - including these three - can make much appreciated presents!
DeWalt 14-PC cordless screwdriver set (item DWHT66719)
dewalt.ca
Now this is an awesome screwdriver set! The 14-piece cordless screwdriver set features a 2Ah integrated battery that can deliver hundreds of screws per charge. The tool can also recharge in approximately an hour and comes with a USB charging cable along with 12 two-inch single-ended bits (Phillips, slotted, TORQ, square). For increased visibility the tool has four built-in LED lights. An LED battery status indicator lets you know when the tool requires recharging. The screwdriver has a variable speed dial as well as a reverse switch allowing you to easily remove screws.
Stanley 20-PC ratcheting multi-bit screwdriver (item 62-574)
stanleytools.ca
If you are old-school and prefer your screwdrivers not to be battery-powered, this ratcheting multi-headed screwdriver is a convenient set that doesn't take up much space making it ideal for the car or boat as well as in the workshop.
The tool features an ergonomically designed handle, bit storage in the handle, a magnetic bit holder and it comes with 14 screwdriver bits (Phillips, slotted, TORQ, square) and six nut drivers. There is also a storage rack for the bits.
DeWalt 20V cordless 23-gauge pin nailer (tool only, item DCN623B)
dewalt.ca
There are always renovations that need doing in our 1895 house so any tools that can make the work easier and/or quicker are much appreciated - including this DeWalt 20V Cordless 23 Ga. Pin Nailer. Currently, we are installing trim in a mud room and working on a door frame with this tool and it works great.
This pin nailer (it is the tool only, it doesn't include a battery or charger) drives 23-gauge pins from 5/8 inch up to 1 1/2 inch in length. According to DeWalt, this pin nailer can drive 2,000 nails on a charge. Some of the features of this pin nailer include a tool-free jam release and a tool-free depth adjust, LED indicator indicating when it is time to reload, a belt hook, nose cover and a headlight.
Along with using this cordless nailer to renovate our house, it will also be useful for smaller projects such as building a bat box!

Document 449 of 500

Ford's homeless funding may be historic, but it's still not enough

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/fords-homeless-funding-may-be-historic-still-not/docview/3145730888/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Ford%27s+homeless+funding+may+be+historic%2C+but+it%27s+still+not+enough&amp;title=The+Hamilton+Spectator+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2024-12-17&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Hamilton+Spectator+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Last week, our fearless leader Doug Ford, on the eve of a two-and-a-half-month shutdown, held a news conference to announce more funding along with new tools to help municipalities dealing with the park encampments of homeless people.
There is no disagreement that encampments are all that MPP Paul Calandra says they are: "a public safety concern and not a solution to homelessness." Absolutely. It's in the details about how the Ford government is dealing with them where the government diverges from reality.
Ford saying that his government has invested more than any other government ignores the historic need we're at right now. While economic downturns have marked our past, the combined drug/mental health/housing challenge is new. The drugs are more deadly, the supply more sophisticated, while mental health care continues to flounder, as it always has, in addressing the need. I have to think back to the late 19th century to mimic the conditions we find ourselves in. Frankly, I think it's worse.
COVID-19 created a nightmare. Rents have never been higher. The gap between those that can participate in the housing market and those who can't has never been greater. To say it's the most funding ever allocated, and it's still not enough - both can be true.
And it's not enough. Just doing simple math as an average Ontarian, I can see that there's not enough to spread over the entire province, never mind to comprehensively and compassionately address the needs in Toronto, Ottawa and Hamilton. Municipalities have poured millions themselves into the challenge, much of it to temporary solutions.
During the news conference, Ford raised the possibility of an audit to see where and how the billions have been spent, suggesting nefarious actors are involved via the vague "I've heard stories" accusation.
Let's see an audit. Maybe then Ford will see just how underwhelming his government's response is. Just how much money is being spent on emergency responses when what's needed are permanent solutions. Like more money to social assistance. Like more funding for deeply affordable housing. Without one or the other, change will be difficult.
What an insult to suggest that people who are actually doing something to alleviate the suffering of those on the streets are malfeasant with their funding, while the Ford government itself is under a RCMP investigation. You have to laugh, or you'll lose your mind.
When Ford answered a question, saying there are "too many people taking in this province, not enough people going out there and giving and working hard," he followed it with a vow, channeling fake sincerity, to take care of people with addictions, mental health and disabilities for the rest of their lives. That last part made me snort my tea. Ouch.
The social assistance rates for people on ODSP are abysmal. There are people with disabilities living in poverty staring at the prospect of medical assistance in dying as their only way out of the pain and indignity in which they live. There are people with disabilities living in encampments, forced out of the rental market because of an inadequate housing allowance, unable to afford ambulatory devices or other aids that would improve their quality of life. They are often taken advantage of by the private rental market with horrific living conditions. Is this the level of care on offer? It's beyond pathetic.
Come on, Scrooge, it's Christmas! One can only hope Ford meets his Marley.
All the right buzzwords are being used to massage the message into one we can all support. How can we not feel the pain and anguish felt from so many families who have lost or are losing a loved one to the uncertainty and instability of their addiction or illness? But I'm as wary of buzzwords such as compassionate intervention as I was about tough love.
I mean, really, how compassionate can a $10,000 fine be on someone who has nothing?
CAPTION: Premier Doug Ford speaks to members of the media at Queen's Park on Thursday, where he announced $75.5 million for shelters and affordable housing and a pledge to toughen up trespassing laws.

Subject: Press conferences; Mental health; People with disabilities; Affordable housing; Funding; Homeless people

Document 450 of 500

Hamilton encampment residents' Charter rights violated, lawyers argue

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/hamilton-encampment-residents-charter-rights/docview/3145730532/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Hamilton+encampment+residents%27+Charter+rights+violated%2C+lawyers+argue&amp;title=The+Hamilton+Spectator+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2024-12-17&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Hamilton+Spectator+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: People who lived in tents were thrust into even more desperate circumstances due to Hamilton's approach to encampments amid inadequate shelter and housing options, lawyers behind a human-rights challenge contended on Monday.
The highly anticipated case on behalf of 14 applicants, some of whom now have housing, launched into its first day of arguments at John Sopinka Courthouse before Justice James Ramsay in a small but packed courtroom.
They argue city bylaw enforcement that bars tents in parks and other public spaces amid the shelter and housing crunch amounted to a violation of their rights under the Canadian Charter of Rights and Freedoms.
The focus of the litigation is a two-year period leading up to the city's adoption of a new encampment protocol last year that allows people to pitch tents in public spaces subject to certain spatial restrictions and rules.
His clients don't "live in encampments by choice," Wade Poziomka, co-counsel, said at the outset of Monday's proceedings.
"They want stable, secure and affordable housing. Unfortunately, you will hear extensive evidence today that there is a dramatic shortage of housing."
As a result, the applicants have "had to make hard choices in imperfect circumstances" as demand for shelter beds often outstrips supply.
But "even when shelter spaces are available, there are barriers imposed that render those spaces inaccessible to some individuals without homes," Poziomka continued.
"If there aren't enough housing options, and insufficient supply of shelter beds, what are the most vulnerable members of our society to do?"
All told, the applicants seek $455,000 in damages under the Charter, a "declaration that the encampment ban and evictions were unconstitutional" and court-related costs.
In response, the city has rejected their Charter rights were violated and contended "no relief is warranted," arguing the challenge "should be dismissed in its entirety."
"There is no dispute that homelessness is a complex and terrible social ill, and that the state of being homeless can result in serious harm to those experiencing it," the city's legal team wrote in its factum.
"Indeed, this is why the city marshals and expends vast amounts of money and resources annually to support homeless individuals in obtaining housing and in mitigating the harms of homelessness."
In its factum, the city contends it has "gone well above and beyond permitting" people to "raise temporary overnight shelter in its public parks at all relevant times ..."
The court case unfolds as Hamilton grapples with an estimated 300 people living outside, a subset of the roughly 1,600 people the city says are homeless overall.
As stopgaps, while it angles for more funding for affordable and supportive housing, the city and agency partners are creating an additional 192 temporary shelter beds and situating 40 micro-shelters for up to 80 people on municipal land off Barton Street West this winter.
And last week, Premier Doug Ford announced an additional $75.5 million for municipalities across Ontario to respond to homelessness while introducing a bill that aims to give more powers to police and cities to deal with trespassing and public drug use.
In Hamilton, city officials have periodically flagged that Hamilton doesn't have enough shelter beds to meet demands, nor the kind that all are able to access - information that the applicants' lawyers emphasized during Monday's proceedings.
"The numbers of maximum beds are lower than the homelessness counts throughout this entire period of time," co-counsel Sujit Choudhry told the court.
It was under these circumstances, and with the ban on encampments, that his clients saw their Charter rights violated, Choudhry contended.
"The applicants were forced to sleep rough," he said, drawing a distinction between living in a tent and being completely exposed to the elements without one.
In the latter scenario, they faced greater health risks "that would have been mitigated" had they been able to stay in tents.
Eight of the applicants are women, eight are Indigenous, two are Black and one is a transgender woman.
The encampment bans and evictions discriminated on the basis of sex, race and disabilities, Choudhry said, pointing to systemic inequities such as the overrepresentation of Indigenous people without housing, lack of accommodation for those with disabilities and fewer shelter beds for women.
The pitfalls of displacement were myriad, suggested colleague Sharon Crowe in reviewing her clients' experiences: assaults, sexual assaults, thefts, sleep deprivation, frostbite, an amputated leg, a dislocated shoulder, a gunshot wound.
Shelters were either full or not available to them for a range of reasons for the applicants, including the fact that some were unwilling to separate from partners to stay in gender-specific programs or leave their pets behind, court heard.
Descriptions of "wandering" the streets to find a place to sleep and frequently calling shelters for available beds before giving up formed a common thread in their submissions as shared by Crowe.
Her clients, meanwhile, have struggled with mental health, including post-traumatic stress disorder and drug dependencies, as well as physical ailments, such as degenerative disc disease and diabetes.
The applicants' legal team and that of the city have sparred over the admissibility of their respective expert witnesses and the inclusion of city documents.
The city's lawyers are expected to present their arguments when the case resumes Tuesday.
Teviah Moro is a reporter at the Hamilton Spectator and can be reached at tmoro@thespec.com.
CAPTION: Police and city staff clear an encampment at Peace Park, at the corner of Hunter Street West and Bay Street South, last week.The city cleared a large encampment on Ferguson Avenue North outside the then-Wesley Day Centre in October 2020.City workers clean up garbage in the encampment at the Delta Parkette in east Hamilton this past summer.

Subject: Public spaces; Charter of Rights-Canada; Tents; Attorneys; Homeless people

Business indexing term: Subject: Attorneys

Title: Hamilton encampment residents' Charter rights violated, lawyers argue

Publication title: The Hamilton Spectator (Online); Hamilton

Publication year: 2024

Publication date: Dec 17, 2024

Section: news/hamilton-region

Publisher: Torstar Syndication Services, a Division of Toronto Star Newspapers Limited

Place of publication: Hamilton

Country of publication: Canada

Publication subject: General Interest Periodicals--Canada

Document 451 of 500

Homeless funding is historic, but still not enough

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/homeless-funding-is-historic-still-not-enough/docview/3145482296/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=unknown&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Homeless+funding+is+historic%2C+but+still+not+enough&amp;title=The+Spectator&amp;issn=11899417&amp;date=2024-12-17&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.7&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Spectator&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Last week, our fearless leader Doug Ford, on the eve of a two-and-a-half-month shutdown, held a news conference to announce more funding along with new tools to help municipalities dealing with the park encampments of homeless people.
There is no disagreement that encampments are all that MPP Paul Calandra says they are: "a public safety concern and not a solution to homelessness." Absolutely. It's in the details about how the Ford government is dealing with them where the government diverges from reality.
Ford saying that his government has invested more than any other government ignores the historic need we're at right now. While economic downturns have marked our past, the combined drug/mental health/housing challenge is new. The drugs are more deadly, the supply more sophisticated, while mental health care continues to flounder, as it always has, in addressing the need. I have to think back to the late 19th century to mimic the conditions we find ourselves in. Frankly, I think it's worse.
COVID-19 created a nightmare. Rents have never been higher. The gap between those that can participate in the housing market and those who can't has never been greater. To say it's the most funding ever allocated, and it's still not enough - both can be true.
And it's not enough. Just doing simple math as an average Ontarian, I can see that there's not enough to spread over the entire province, never mind to comprehensively and compassionately address the needs in Toronto, Ottawa and Hamilton. Municipalities have poured millions themselves into the challenge, much of it to temporary solutions.
During the news conference, Ford raised the possibility of an audit to see where and how the billions have been spent, suggesting nefarious actors are involved via the vague "I've heard stories" accusation.
Let's see an audit. Maybe then Ford will see just how underwhelming his government's response is. Just how much money is being spent on emergency responses when what's needed are permanent solutions. Like more money to social assistance. Like more funding for deeply affordable housing. Without one or the other, change will be difficult.
What an insult to suggest that people who are actually doing something to alleviate the suffering of those on the streets are malfeasant with their funding, while the Ford government itself is under a RCMP investigation. You have to laugh, or you'll lose your mind.
When Ford answered a question, saying there are "too many people taking in this province, not enough people going out there and giving and working hard," he followed it with a vow, channeling fake sincerity, to take care of people with addictions, mental health and disabilities for the rest of their lives. That last part made me snort my tea. Ouch.
The social assistance rates for people on ODSP are abysmal. There are people with disabilities living in poverty staring at the prospect of medical assistance in dying as their only way out of the pain and indignity in which they live. There are people with disabilities living in encampments, forced out of the rental market because of an inadequate housing allowance, unable to afford ambulatory devices or other aids that would improve their quality of life. They are often taken advantage of by the private rental market with horrific living conditions. Is this the level of care on offer? It's beyond pathetic.
Come on, Scrooge, it's Christmas! One can only hope Ford meets his Marley.
All the right buzzwords are being used to massage the message into one we can all support. How can we not feel the pain and anguish felt from so many families who have lost or are losing a loved one to the uncertainty and instability of their addiction or illness? But I'm as wary of buzzwords such as compassionate intervention as I was about tough love.
I mean, really, how compassionate can a $10,000 fine be on someone who has nothing?
Margaret Shkimba is a Hamilton writer.
For more visit margaretshkimba.Substack.com.
CAPTION: Premier Doug Ford speaks to members of the media at Queen's Park on Thursday, where he announced $75.5 million for shelters and affordable housing and a pledge to toughen up trespassing laws.Chris Young The Canadian Press

CREDIT: Margaret Shkimba OPINION Margaret Shkimba is a Hamilton writer. For more visit margaretshkimba.Substack.com.;

Document 452 of 500

Encampment residents' rights violated, lawyers argue: Applicants seek damages for displacement amid lack of shelter, housing options

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/encampment-residents-rights-violated-lawyers/docview/3145482269/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Encampment+residents%27+rights+violated%2C+lawyers+argue%3A+Applicants+seek+damages+for+displacement+amid+lack+of+shelter%2C+housing+options&amp;title=The+Spectator&amp;issn=11899417&amp;date=2024-12-17&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.1&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Spectator&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: People who lived in tents were thrust into even more desperate circumstances due to Hamilton's approach to encampments amid inadequate shelter and housing options, lawyers behind a human-rights challenge contended on Monday.
The highly anticipated case on behalf of 14 applicants, some of whom now have housing, launched into its first day of arguments at John Sopinka Courthouse before Justice James Ramsay in a small but packed courtroom.
They argue city bylaw enforcement that bars tents in parks and other public spaces amid the shelter and housing crunch amounted to a violation of their rights under the Canadian Charter of Rights and Freedoms.
The focus of the litigation is a two-year period leading up to the city's adoption of a new encampment protocol last year that allows people to pitch tents in public spaces subject to certain spatial restrictions and rules.
His clients don't "live in encampments by choice," Wade Poziomka, co-counsel, said at the outset of Monday's proceedings.
"They want stable, secure and affordable housing. Unfortunately, you will hear extensive evidence today that there is a dramatic shortage of housing."
As a result, the applicants have "had to make hard choices in imperfect circumstances" as demand for shelter beds often outstrips supply.
But "even when shelter spaces are available, there are barriers imposed that render those spaces inaccessible to some individuals without homes," Poziomka continued.
"If there aren't enough housing options, and insufficient supply of shelter beds, what are the most vulnerable members of our society to do?"
All told, the applicants seek $455,000 in damages under the Charter, a "declaration that the encampment ban and evictions were unconstitutional" and court-related costs.
In response, the city has rejected their Charter rights were violated and contended "no relief is warranted," arguing the challenge "should be dismissed in its entirety."
"There is no dispute that homelessness is a complex and terrible social ill, and that the state of being homeless can result in serious harm to those experiencing it," the city's legal team wrote in its factum.
"Indeed, this is why the city marshals and expends vast amounts of money and resources annually to support homeless individuals in obtaining housing and in mitigating the harms of homelessness."
In its factum, the city contends it has "gone well above and beyond permitting" people to "raise temporary overnight shelter in its public parks at all relevant times ..."
The court case unfolds as Hamilton grapples with an estimated 300 people living outside, a subset of the roughly 1,600 people the city says are homeless overall.
As stopgaps, while it angles for more funding for affordable and supportive housing, the city and agency partners are creating an additional 192 temporary shelter beds and situating 40 micro-shelters for up to 80 people on municipal land off Barton Street West this winter.
And last week, Premier Doug Ford announced an additional $75.5 million for municipalities across Ontario to respond to homelessness while introducing a bill that aims to give more powers to police and cities to deal with trespassing and public drug use.
In Hamilton, city officials have periodically flagged that the city doesn't have enough shelter beds to meet demands, nor the kind that all are able to access - information that the applicants' lawyers emphasized during Monday's proceedings.
"The numbers of maximum beds are lower than the homelessness counts throughout this entire period of time," co-counsel Sujit Choudhry told the court.
It was under these circumstances, and with the ban on encampments, that his clients saw their Charter rights violated, Choudhry contended.
"The applicants were forced to sleep rough," he said, drawing a distinction between living in a tent and being completely exposed to the elements without one.
In the latter scenario, they faced greater health risks "that would have been mitigated" had they been able to stay in tents.
Eight of the applicants are women, eight are Indigenous, two are Black and one is a transgender woman.
The encampment bans and evictions discriminated on the basis of sex, race and disabilities, Choudhry said, pointing to systemic inequities such as the overrepresentation of Indigenous people without housing, lack of accommodation for those with disabilities and fewer shelter beds for women.
The pitfalls of displacement were myriad, suggested colleague Sharon Crowe in reviewing her clients' experiences: assaults, sexual assaults, thefts, sleep deprivation, frostbite, an amputated leg, a dislocated shoulder, a gunshot wound.
Shelters were either full or not available to them for a range of reasons for the applicants, including the fact that some were unwilling to separate from partners to stay in gender-specific programs or leave their pets behind, court heard.
Descriptions of "wandering" the streets to find a place to sleep and frequently calling shelters for available beds before giving up formed a common thread in their submissions as shared by Crowe.
Her clients, meanwhile, have struggled with mental health, including post-traumatic stress disorder and drug dependencies, as well as physical ailments, such as degenerative disc disease and diabetes.
The applicants' legal team and that of the city have sparred over the admissibility of their respective expert witnesses and the inclusion of city documents.
The city's lawyers are expected to present their arguments when the case resumes Tuesday.
Teviah Moro is a reporter
at the Hamilton Spectator and can be reached at
tmoro@thespec.com.
CAPTION: City workers clean up garbage in the Delta Parkette encampment in east Hamilton last summer. The city cleared a large encampment on Ferguson Avenue North outside the then-Wesley Day Centre in October 2020.Police and city staff clear an encampment at Peace Park, at the corner of Hunter Street West and Bay Street South, last week.Hamilton Spectator file photos

CREDIT: Teviah Moro Reporter Teviah Moro is a reporter at the Hamilton Spectator and can be reached at tmoro@thespec.com.

Document 453 of 500

Ford says premiers united in fight against tariff threat despite 'chaos' in Ottawa

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/ford-says-premiers-united-fight-against-tariff/docview/3145482199/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Ford+says+premiers+united+in+fight+against+tariff+threat+despite+%27chaos%27+in+Ottawa&amp;title=The+Spectator&amp;issn=11899417&amp;date=2024-12-17&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.8&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Spectator&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: All 13 of the country's premiers will remain united as "Team Canada" in the face of a tariff threat from the United States despite "chaos" in Ottawa, Premier Doug Ford said hours after the federal finance minister's resignation.
Ford chaired a meeting Monday of the provincial and territorial leaders in Toronto, and one of the top agenda items was to discuss a warning from U.S. president-elect Donald Trump that he will impose a 25 per cent import tariff on goods coming from Canada and Mexico when he takes office.
But the discussions took on a different tone, as Chrystia Freeland announced her resignation from cabinet around the time the premiers began their meeting.
At the Council of the Federation meeting closing news conference, Ford painted the premiers as having a steady set of hands on the tiller amid far more turbulent federal waters. "It's chaos right now up in Ottawa and it's time that what we do every single day, the premiers: we step up and we'll make sure that we tell the world that there is stability here," Ford said. "There is certainty here in Canada and by all means it's a great place to invest in any of our provinces or territories."
Canada's efforts to push back on the tariff threat won't be derailed by the resignation of one person, said Prince Edward Island Premier Dennis King.
"Everything seems chaotic, but if you look to the south of the border, chaos seems to rule the day down there every day of the week," he said.
"We have a really good case to put forward. And there are a lot of people working on this, from the premiers' tables, from all of our staffs, or all of the individual businesses we deal with across the country... There's a lot more going on than just what might be captivating our attention in Ottawa today."
The premiers, through Ford, said Monday the federal government needs to do a better job of engaging them on a strategy to fight back against the threatened tariffs. The provincial and territorial leaders are planning a premiers' mission to Washington, D.C., in February.
They can't afford to lose momentum, said Alberta Premier Danielle Smith.
"If our collective goal is to avoid tariffs, we're only a month out from that, or thereabouts," she said.
"I would say that the premiers at this table offer the continuity in being able to maintain that pressure, and maintain that push with our American counterparts, and I think we're all going to be committed to doing that."
Ford has come out strongly in favour of retaliatory tariffs and has threatened to in turn cut off the electricity the province supplies to several states.
But other premiers have spoken in favour of a different approach, with Smith saying she prefers the diplomatic route and doesn't support retaliatory tariffs or cutting off Alberta's oil and gas exports.
The premiers also agreed Monday on a new proposal to speed up approvals for life-saving medications.
The Canadian Press
CAPTION: At a Council of the Federation news conference on Monday, Premier Doug Ford painted the premiers as having a steady set of hands on the tiller amid far more turbulent federal waters.Nathan Denette The Canadian Press

Dec. 17: Colleges can't follow Sears model, time to end Bernardo coverage and other letters to the editor

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/dec-17-colleges-cant-follow-sears-model-time-end/docview/3145482173/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Dec.+17%3A+Colleges+can%27t+follow+Sears+model%2C+time+to+end+Bernardo+coverage+and+other+letters+to+the+editor&amp;title=The+Hamilton+Spectator+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2024-12-17&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Hamilton+Spectator+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Colleges can't follow Sears model
Re: Canada's international student crisis was predicted - and ignored, Dec. 10
Mark Bulgutch's column struck a sour note in an otherwise good piece. Ontario colleges and universities rely far more than they should on tuition from international students and when student numbers decrease, institutions look up and find that they have dug themselves into a hole. It's astounding to think that digging has gone on for years and those institutions understood perfectly well that they were getting in deeper and deeper with decreasing prospects of being able to climb out. So far so good, but then the dissonance with Mr. Bulgutch claiming taxpayers aren't giving them enough money.
A cursory glance at their course offerings tells another story. A lick of common sense shows their money problems come less because they are poorly funded than because they follow the Sears model. Offer the customer everything from soup to nuts and persuade them to buy. It didn't work for Sears and it won't work for institutions of higher learning. Downsize, right size, whatever. Get rid of half the merchandise is what they have to do. The investors - the taxpayer, in the case of colleges and universities - might then consider that they're making a good investment. Right now, we're forced into buying junk bonds.
Jim Sebastian, Simcoe
Time to end Bernardo coverage
Re: Paul Bernardo sheds new light on Karla Homolka's role in crimes, Dec. 11
What compels the Spectator to continue to report on Paul Bernardo? Haven't the families suffered enough without having to see article after article (with pictures) every time he's up for parole? News flash: no parole board would ever dare release him!
I think I speak for the majority of Hamiltonians when I say I don't want a rehashing of what he did every few years. And I don't care what he thinks or where he is. I just want him to rot away in prison until he dies with absolutely no publicity.
In the future, may I suggest a one-sentence blurb buried in the national news section stating: "Convicted killer Paul Bernardo was denied parole on (insert date)."
Paul Kelly, Hamilton
The politics of cruelty on display
In order to give municipal leaders the power to dismantle homeless encampments, Premier Doug Ford has indicated he will trample on the Charter of Rights and Freedoms, using the notwithstanding clause, to override any interference from the courts. This does nothing to improve our crisis of homelessness. Where does anyone who supports this idea think that these people are going to go?
To make matters worse, Ford also has announced the intention to close supervised consumption sites, defying evidence-based experience that these sites save lives.
It seems clear there is a fatigue among the general public around issues of homelessness, drug use and mental illness. Fair enough. The petty crime, violence, public drug consumption and the depressing unsightliness of all this playing out on our streets are real problems. But Ford's actions will lead to an increased death rate in our communities. Of that there can be no doubt. So instead of this vulnerable population having the support of supervised consumption sites, they now will be driven to use in alleyways, public washrooms and so on.
Ford, however, has no empathy here. He likely feels that these people are subhuman, and if their lifestyle kills them, so be it. Like Scrooge, Ford might say, "If they would rather die, they had better do it and decrease the surplus population." A premier playing to the baser feelings of a frustrated populace: the politics of cruelty are on full display here.
Ken Durkacz, Hamilton
Get to know your neighbours
I don't pretend to have the answer to Hamilton's homelessness problem, but I believe there is more than one answer and many steps that need to be taken to find those answers. I also believe it will take our whole community, from politicians and professionals to everyday citizens, to take those steps to find those answers. I have recently learned that a meaningful step all of us can take is that if you have a home, get to know someone who is homeless. Let the step-taking and learning begin all across Hamilton. The answers are out there waiting to be found.
Pam Byers, Hamilton
Some ER visits go smoothly
Last Wednesday, at 11:45 a.m., I presented at the Juravinski ER (not my first time presenting to ER). Within five minutes I was triaged, IV inserted, blood and urinalysis samples taken and a shot for the pain. Another five-minute wait to get a hospital arm band and 10 minutes later, I was taken to the back and made as comfortable as they could in a reclining chair (no beds available). ER staff made sure I was comfortable despite there being a steady stream of paramedics bringing in new patients. Once my test results were back, the doctor came to talk to me and after a course of IV antibiotics, I was allowed to go home with a script for antibiotics and instructions to return if my symptoms persisted. I was discharged at 3:30 p.m.
I have heard many complaints over the years about long waits in the ER and I have been there longer than four hours in the past, but I am always amazed at the efficiency and friendly care the staff gives, no matter how busy they are, and I would like to take this opportunity to say a huge thank you to them all.
Sandra Carpenter, Ancaster
We can't all afford Aston Martins
Every Friday I look forward to the Wheels section with "Answers to your car questions" by Dennis O'Sullivan.
I wish I could say the same about the other half of the page, where there are vehicle reviews. More often than not, the reviews are of high-end vehicles that you never see. This week's review was for an Aston Martin that costs $400,000! Any chance you could have reviews of cars that average people might consider buying?
Bob Berberick, Hamilton
New provinces on the way?
Re: Donald Trump calls Justin Trudeau 'Governor' of the 'Great State of Canada,' Dec. 10
Considering the number of Americans who have inquired about moving to Canada since the U.S. election, perhaps it should be Canada that suggests a number of U.S. states become new Canadian provinces!
Phil Van Impe, Hamilton
A job is better than jail
Re: Hamilton police unearth another hole used to power Gage Park encampment, Dec. 12
So we have an ambitious soul under arrest that displays ability to use a tool (shovel) and fiddle some wires together. Would a contractor out there be interested at least in the shovel work energy to provide employment for the individual? Possibly even an electrician apprenticeship, if qualified? Save jail time and become productive.
On another note, where did all the excavated soil get hidden from the crater so as not to be discovered much sooner? Magic, eh!
Garry Young, Canfield
Kudos for heartwarming ad
I absolutely was touched by the new Tim Hortons commercial about "Frank" in the nursing/retirement home. It is beautiful. Heartwarming for sure. Congratulations. Great sentiment.
Jann Hanson, Hamilton
Wishing you a merry Christmas
There is no ideal Christmas, only the one Christmas you decide to make as a reflection of our values, desires and affections or traditions. Blessed, though, is the season that engages the whole world in a conspiracy of love.
I hope that you all have the opportunity at this time of the year to indulge in some quality time over the holidays and are able to bask in the company of our family, friends and soulmates.
Let us eat drink and be merry, for you are the joy-bearing agents of the world and if you can attain repose and calm with your loved ones, believe then that you have seized happiness.
And maybe too - if you have a chance - get down on the floor and play with the child on Christmas Day, for it is the most remarkable thing.
Ross Prince, Hamilton
Shooting a symptom of bigger problem
Re: Murder suspects aren't sexy folk heroes, Dec. 10
Brian Thompson was a bigger American problem than Luigi Mangione. Mangione isn't the answer, but he is what happens when you refuse to take the problem seriously
David La Rush, Hamilton
Registration system is flawed
Recently, the city brought in a new registration program. There was a write-up in the Spec saying that all registered participants received an email notification. I know at least 10 people that did not receive a notification.
When you go to the new website, it is very hard to navigate to programs. Also, there were two videos to show how to change a password and how to register. When you need to do this, you know it will not be user-friendly. The registration was for Dec. 11. At the Ancaster Seniors Achievement Centre, there were still classes going the week of Dec. 9. What were participants to do that were enrolled in a class at 9 a.m. on Wednesday morning? Either miss their class or come to class with a laptop and try to register during class? When the registration date was set, would not a supervisor been aware that classes were still running that day?
At the same time, there was registration for other recreation programs. In most families both parents work, or are a single-parent household. They would either have to take time off work or go to work and hope their supervisor would allow them to register. It was a real mess, with people being locked out and not being able to register and/or not even able to get on.
Maryann Knight, Ancaster
CAPTION: Minister Paul Calandra simulates a wellness check last year with Humber College Faculty of Health Sciences and Wellness students in a classroom. The prognosis for the college system isn't good if it continues to try to offer everything to everyone, a reader writes.

Document 455 of 500

National briefs: Byelection new test for Trudeau

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/national-briefs/docview/3145482095/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Full text: British Columbia
Person missing after mudslide
A search is ongoing for a person believed to have been caught in a mudslide that hit their home and closed British Columbia's Sea to Sky Highway. RCMP said emergency crews recovered a body of one of the two residents of the Sea to Sky area home on Sunday, and were searching for the second person. The recovery of the body means at least two people died because of a weekend storm after a woman was killed by a falling tree branch in Surrey. The mudslide near Lions Bay, about 40 kilometres north of Vancouver, came down during a severe windstorm that hit B.C.'s south coast on Saturday.
Byelection new test for Trudeau
Voters in the Liberal-held British Columbia riding of Cloverdale-Langley City went to the polls Monday to pick a new member of Parliament, in another test for Prime Minister Justin Trudeau. Liberal candidate Madison Fleischer, a local business owner, will face Conservative Tamara Jansen who previously held the seat. The byelection comes after the Liberals have already lost two long-held seats in the last several months.
Ontario
DNA leads to murder suspect
Ottawa police said they've identified a suspect in an unsolved 1996 stabbing death on the Portage Bridge using DNA evidence to track down the man who was arrested in Vancouver last week. Police said 73-year-old Lawrence Diehl was arrested by Vancouver police on Dec. 10, and he's accused of second-degree murder in the death of Christopher Smith on April 12, 1996. Police said it's the first time they've used "investigative genetic genealogy" to identify a suspect using genetic databases to track down "potential family lineages."
News services

Plan addresses some hard truths

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/plan-addresses-some-hard-truths/docview/3145481980/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=unknown&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Plan+addresses+some+hard+truths&amp;title=The+Spectator&amp;issn=11899417&amp;date=2024-12-17&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.6&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Spectator&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: I've spent years advocating for bold reforms to tackle Ontario's housing affordability crisis at the volunteer organization More Neighbours Toronto.
We've called for modernizing outdated planning rules, loosening restrictive land-use policies, and streamlining sluggish permitting - long-term measures that will help stabilize prices and boost supply. But these deeper changes take years to bear fruit, and right now Ontario's housing market is on the brink. Housing starts are collapsing, construction workers face mounting job losses and families are watching their dreams of owning a home slip away.
One major reason housing is so expensive is that governments have quietly piled massive taxes and fees onto new homes. Consider this: in 2000, a benchmark two-bedroom Toronto condo cost about $145,000 (around $244,000 in today's dollars). Now, the taxes and fees slapped onto a similar new unit exceed $240,000 - essentially more than the condo once cost.
More Neighbours Toronto is a non-partisan group committed to working with any government, at any level, that takes the affordability crisis seriously. We've consulted with every major party at the provincial and federal levels, and we were a cited stakeholder in the Ford government's Housing Affordability Task Force. We've maintained open lines with the Ministry of Municipal Affairs and Housing (MMAH) and also engaged with the Ontario NDP on its Homes Ontario plan.
So, when Bonnie Crombie's team asked me to join their housing platform task force to provide input to their plans, it signalled they weren't just paying lip service - they were serious. Now, seeing their commitment in action, I believe the Ontario Liberals have nailed a key piece of housing policy that Doug Ford should have adopted long ago - and still could.
At the heart of the Ontario Liberal's new proposal - More Homes You Can Afford - is a direct assault on these runaway fees. They plan to scrap development charges - hidden levies that can add six figures to a home's cost - for all new homes under 3,000 square feet, large enough for a four-bedroom family home.
This represents a radical break from the old "growth pays for growth" model that burdened one generation with staggering debts to fund citywide infrastructure just to keep property taxes low for another. Crombie's plan suggests the province would step in with a "better communities fund," which pledges to make municipalities whole and provide stable, long-term infrastructure funding they can count on.
Some might dismiss this as a developer giveaway, but that misunderstands how markets work. Since all developers pay these fees, they must pass them on to buyers, pushing prices even higher and ironically encouraging speculative demand.
Cutting development charges and other housing-related taxes lowers the price floor, making it easier to launch projects, get housing built, and easing price pressures market-wide.
The Ontario Liberals proposal doesn't stop at development charges. It would also eliminate the provincial Land Transfer Tax for first-time buyers and seniors, instantly saving young families about $9,500 and downsizing seniors about $13,500. The rental market gets attention, too. The Ontario Liberal's plan suggests implementing phased-in rent control that protects tenants without deterring new construction.
As an advocate of housing abundance as a solution to our housing crisis, I consider this proposal among the boldest I've seen proposed by a political leader in years. No single measure will fix everything overnight - we still need modernized building codes, faster approvals and more flexible land use - but More Homes You Can Afford signals that our politics is finally willing to confront the hard truths at the root of our problem.
Eric Lombardi is the founder and president of More
Neighbours Toronto, a
volunteer organization committed to ending the housing crisis.
CAPTION:

CREDIT: Eric Lombardi Eric Lombardi is the founder and president of More Neighbours Toronto, a volunteer organization committed to ending the housing crisis.;
