Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=unknown&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Letters&amp;title=The+Spectator&amp;issn=11899417&amp;date=2024-12-17&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.7&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Spectator&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Schools can't follow the Sears model
Re: Canada's international
student crisis was predicted
- and ignored, Dec. 10
Mark Bulgutch's column struck a sour note in an otherwise good piece. Ontario colleges and universities rely far more than they should on tuition from international students and when student numbers decrease, institutions look up and find that they have dug themselves into a hole.
It's astounding to think that digging has gone on for years and those institutions understood perfectly well that they were getting in deeper and deeper with decreasing prospects of being able to climb out.
So far so good, but then the dissonance with Mr. Bulgutch claiming taxpayers aren't giving them enough money.
A cursory glance at their course offerings tells another story. A lick of common sense shows their money problems come less because they are poorly funded than because they follow the Sears model. Offer the customer everything from soup to nuts and persuade them to buy. It didn't work for Sears and it won't work for institutions of higher learning. Downsize, right size, whatever. Get rid of half the merchandise is what they have to do. The investors - the taxpayer, in the case of colleges and universities - might then consider that they're making a good investment. Right now, we're forced into buying junk bonds.
Jim Sebastian, Simcoe
Time to end the
Bernardo coverage
Re: Paul Bernardo sheds new light on Karla Homolka's role in crimes, Dec. 11
What compels the Spectator to continue to report on Paul Bernardo? Haven't the families suffered enough without having to see article after article (with pictures) every time he's up for parole? News flash: no parole board would ever dare release him!
I think I speak for the majority of Hamiltonians when I say I don't want a rehashing of what he did every few years. And I don't care what he thinks or where he is. I just want him to rot away in prison until he dies with absolutely no publicity.
In the future, may I suggest a one-sentence blurb buried in the national news section stating: "Convicted killer Paul Bernardo was denied parole on (insert date)."
Paul Kelly, Hamilton
The politics of
cruelty on display
In order to give municipal leaders the power to dismantle homeless encampments, Premier Doug Ford has indicated he will trample on the Charter of Rights and Freedoms, using the notwithstanding clause, to override any interference from the courts. This does nothing to improve our crisis of homelessness. Where does anyone who supports this idea think that these people are going to go?
To make matters worse, Ford also has announced the intention to close supervised consumption sites, defying evidence-based experience that these sites save lives.
It seems clear there is a fatigue among the general public around issues of homelessness, drug use and mental illness. Fair enough. The petty crime, violence, public drug consumption and the depressing unsightliness of all this playing out on our streets are real problems. But Ford's actions will lead to an increased death rate in our communities. Of that there can be no doubt. So instead of this vulnerable population having the support of supervised consumption sites, they now will be driven to use in alleyways, public washrooms and so on.
Ford, however, has no empathy here. He likely feels that these people are subhuman, and if their lifestyle kills them, so be it. Like Scrooge, Ford might say, "If they would rather die, they had better do it and decrease the surplus population." A premier playing to the baser feelings of a frustrated populace: the politics of cruelty are on full display here.
Ken Durkacz, Hamilton
Get to know
your neighbours
I don't pretend to have the answer to Hamilton's homelessness problem, but I believe there is more than one answer and many steps that need to be taken to find those answers. I also believe it will take our whole community, from politicians and professionals to everyday citizens, to take those steps to find those answers. I have recently learned that a meaningful step all of us can take is that if you have a home, get to know someone who is homeless. Let the step-taking and learning begin all across Hamilton. The answers are out there waiting to be found.
Pam Byers, Hamilton

Some ER visits
go smoothly
Last Wednesday, at 11:45 a.m., I presented at the Juravinski ER (not my first time presenting to ER). Within five minutes I was triaged, IV inserted, blood and urinalysis samples taken and a shot for the pain. Another five-minute wait to get a hospital arm band and 10 minutes later, I was taken to the back and made as comfortable as they could in a reclining chair (no beds available). ER staff made sure I was comfortable despite there being a steady stream of paramedics bringing in new patients. Once my test results were back, the doctor came to talk to me and after a course of IV antibiotics, I was allowed to go home with a script for antibiotics and instructions to return if my symptoms persisted. I was discharged at 3:30 p.m.
I have heard many complaints over the years about long waits in the ER and I have been there longer than four hours in the past, but I am always amazed at the efficiency and friendly care the staff gives, no matter how busy they are, and I would like to take this opportunity to say a huge thank you to them all.
Sandra Carpenter, Ancaster
We can't all afford Aston Martins
Every Friday I look forward to the Wheels section with "Answers to your car questions" by Dennis O'Sullivan.
I wish I could say the same about the other half of the page, where there are vehicle reviews. More often than not, the reviews are of high-end vehicles that you never see. This week's review was for an Aston Martin that costs $400,000! Any chance you could have reviews of cars that average people might consider buying?
Bob Berberick, Hamilton
New provinces
on the way?
Re: Donald Trump calls Justin
Trudeau 'Governor' of the
'Great State of Canada,' Dec. 10
Considering the number of Americans who have inquired about moving to Canada since the U.S. election, perhaps it should be Canada that suggests a number of U.S. states become new Canadian provinces!
Phil Van Impe, Hamilton
Offering a job better than sending to jail
Re: Hamilton police unearth
another hole used to power Gage Park encampment, Dec. 12
So we have an ambitious soul under arrest that displays ability to use a tool (shovel) and fiddle some wires together. Would a contractor out there be interested at least in the shovel work energy to provide employment for the individual? Possibly even an electrician apprenticeship, if qualified? Save jail time and become productive.
On another note, where did all the excavated soil get hidden from the crater so as not to be discovered much sooner? Magic, eh!
Garry Young, Canfield
Wishing you a
merry Christmas
There is no ideal Christmas, only the one Christmas you decide to make as a reflection of our values, desires and affections or traditions. Blessed, though, is the season that engages the whole world in a conspiracy of love.
I hope that you all have the opportunity at this time of the year to indulge in some quality time over the holidays and are able to bask in the company of our family, friends and soulmates.
Let us eat drink and be merry, for you are the joy-bearing agents of the world and if you can attain repose and calm with your loved ones, believe then that you have seized happiness.
And maybe too - if you have a chance - get down on the floor and play with the child on Christmas Day, for it is the most remarkable thing.
Ross Prince, Hamilton

Document 458 of 500

Chrystia Freeland's resignation triggers chaos and Liberal calls for Justin Trudeau to step down

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/chrystia-freelands-resignation-triggers-chaos/docview/3145189052/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Chrystia+Freeland%27s+resignation+triggers+chaos+and+Liberal+calls+for+Justin+Trudeau+to+step+down&amp;title=The+Hamilton+Spectator+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2024-12-16&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Hamilton+Spectator+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: The sudden resignation of Justin Trudeau's finance minister threw his leadership into doubt and his minority government into chaos Monday after Chrystia Freeland quit just hours before she was set to deliver a key fiscal update in the House of Commons.
Instead, Freeland's stunning resignation, her revelations that Trudeau wanted to yank her from the finance portfolio, that she and Trudeau had argued for weeks over "political gimmicks" in the fall economic update, and clashed over how best to ready Canada for the Trump administration's threatened tariff war set the stage for a day of intense political drama on Parliament Hill.
It stunned her cabinet and caucus colleagues and caused chaos among the Liberal ranks, with some like Liberal MP Chad Collins publicly urging Trudeau to resign for the first time.
"It's obvious that Canadians across the country feel the same and want him to leave," he said.
Freeland dropped the announcement on "X" - formerly Twitter - via a missive just as Housing Minister Sean Fraser announced he was quitting for "family reasons," he said. It took him, too, by surprise.
Finance officials had almost no notice of their minister's plans which immediately threw the launch of the fall fiscal economic statement into disarray.
The prime minister huddled with his top officials and cabinet ministers all morning. By midday, it was not clear whether the prime minister would address Canadians, or would have some other minister step in to deliver the speech about the fall statement after markets close at 4 p.m. In the end, House Leader Karina Gould presented the document in the Commons, as Public Safety Minister Dominic LeBlanc was being sworn in as the new finance minister at Rideau Hall.
Immediately upon Freeland's news, the Toronto stock exchange and the Canadian dollar dipped and hadn't recovered by end of day before the fiscal update landed.
When the document was finally tabled, it revealed a whopping $61.9 billion deficit for last year and an unfunded promise to deliver $250 cheques to Canadians.
Freeland and Fraser's departures from Justin Trudeau's cabinet are a blow to the Liberal government and sowed more confusion in an unstable minority Parliament and reignited questions about the prime minister's political future.
Several of his most loyal ministers left cabinet without taking any questions. An emergency Liberal caucus meeting was to be held at 5 p.m.
Freeland's statement said Trudeau told her Friday he no longer wants her to serve as finance minister and offered her another unspecified position in cabinet, signalling she no longer had his confidence and leading to her resignation. Freeland, who was in Ottawa on Monday, said she intends to remain sitting as an MP and to contest her seat in the next election.
The prospect of a snap election - which the Conservatives called for - could not be ruled out, given the explosive reaction to Freeland's exit. The NDP, which has propped up the Trudeau minority government, did not pronounce on what comes next, only issuing a statement that accused the Liberals of being obsessed with infighting.
"For the past number of weeks, you and I have found ourselves at odds about the best path forward for Canada," Freeland said, adding that Canadians "know when we are working for them," and "when we are focused on ourselves."
Citing the looming threat of U.S. president-elect Donald Trump's promised tariffs, she said Canada faces a "grave challenge" from his policy of "economic nationalism" that requires fiscal discipline instead of "political gimmicks."
"That means keeping our fiscal powder dry today, so we have the reserves we may need for a coming tariff war. That means eschewing costly political gimmicks we can ill afford and which make Canadians doubt that we recognize the gravity of the moment."
The Trudeau government's fall fiscal update - which was expected to unveil more than $1 billion in border security measures - comes amid heavy scrutiny over the federal government's spending and its inability to stick to its own fiscal "guardrails" - the self-imposed spending guidelines Freeland had once set.
The Trudeau government has been scrambling for three weeks to allay fears of tariff threats from the incoming Trump administration, and has promised to spend more on helicopters, drones, surveillance technology and manpower at the border. Freeland was offered a ministerial role to deal with Canada-U.S. relations, but the position would have given her none of the tools she had when she renegotiated NAFTA with the previous Trump administration, according to a senior Liberal source.
Ministers emerged grim-faced from the cabinet meeting with most offering no comment. Some gamely asserted they continue to have confidence in the government and said Canadians should too.
Industry Minister François-Philippe Champagne told reporters "it is a serious moment" for the government.
Asked if he still has confidence in the prime minister, Champagne said "the moment now is for us to focus on the best interest of Canadians, that's what we're doing."
Only a few others offered kind words about Freeland, with Fraser, who said he intends to step down on Monday due to family reasons, and Labour Minister Steve MacKinnon describing her as a respected friend.
Before the meeting, just minutes after Freeland's letter was put out, Treasury Board President and Transport Minister Anita Anand appeared on the verge of tears when asked to comment.
"I'll just say that Chrystia Freeland is a good friend, someone I work with very closely," Anand said. "This news has hit me really hard, and I'll reserve further comment until I have time to process it."
But among the Liberal ranks, calls for Trudeau to resign resurfaced.
Wayne Long, one of the MPs who has called on Trudeau to resign before, told the Star he hopes the resignation "opens the door to some cabinet ministers speaking up."
"How much more can the PM endure before doing the right thing and stepping down?" he said.
Hamilton MP Chad Collins, who publicly called for the government's cheque plan to be expanded this fall, also revealed for the first time on Monday that he was among 24 MPs who signed a letter to the prime minister calling on him to step down.
Several Liberal MPs, speaking to the Star on the condition they not be named, expressed surprise and disappointment at the news, with one characterizing Freeland's departure as a "devastating blow" for Trudeau, who earlier this fall survived a challenge from dozens of MPs who called for his resignation.
That MP said conversations will be renewed within caucus about the prime minister's future, and that there will be "reflecting" during the holiday period over next steps. The MP added that they have become increasingly "disillusioned" with running again under the Liberal banner, a feeling they said was prompted by the government's GST holiday and $250 rebate proposal for working Canadians, a multibillion dollar affordability plan that threatens Ottawa's fiscal guardrails promised by Freeland.
"We're going to spend between the two (measures) in excess of $6 billion to what, save Justin Trudeau's career?" The MP said, noting that Freeland's reference to "political gimmicks" in her resignation letter was particularly damning.
Two other MPs who have not previously called on Trudeau to resign, said now is the time for him to go. One told the Star the best course for the Liberals now is for Trudeau to step down and then table a new throne speech under a new prime minister for a fresh start ahead of the next federal election. The other said it was understandable that Freeland would walk away, in light of how the Liberals are struggling and that their predicament is mainly because of Trudeau.
The Star reported this summer that some Liberal MPs wanted Freeland out of cabinet. Freeland has long been rumoured to be a potential successor to Trudeau.
Meanwhile, Trudeau's political opponents in Parliament pounced on the shock resignation, with Conservative Leader Pierre Poilievre saying it is evidence that the "government of Canada itself is spiralling out of control."
He said it is now up to NDP Leader Jagmeet Singh to decide whether or not the government falls - and when. While Singh exited his parliamentary accord with the minority Liberals in September, his party has yet to declare a loss of confidence in the Trudeau government.
Singh, for the first time, demanded Trudeau resign on Monday, but refused to say if he was prepared to help bring down the government.
"All tools, all options are on the table. People are hurting, people are struggling, and so all options are on the table," the NDP leader said.
In Montreal, Bloc Québécois Leader Yves-François Blanchet declared the days of the Trudeau government were "over" and called for the prime minister to ask the Governor General to dissolve Parliament by the start of the new year.
The chaos extended into question period in the House of Commons, where the Conservatives taunted the Liberals and Poilievre repeatedly asked "who is the Finance Minister?"
In Toronto, asked if the premiers were concerned about Freeland's stunning resignation Monday, Ontario Premier Doug Ford said: "We all are."
Ford, chair of the Council of the Federation, emphasized "this is a time for the premiers to step up, which we're going to, and project unity across the country."
Fraser, in his own resignation press conference, said he only learned of Freeland stepping down following a reporter's question.
"This is news for me," he said in French.
The Nova Scotia MP, who held the immigration file before moving to housing, said he was not seeking re-election due to family reasons, but described his time in government as "the best job I can imagine having." He said he made his decision earlier this fall following a surgery.
"Our path to creating a family was a challenging one. We've experienced the highest of highs and the lowest of lows when it comes to our kids. We have had micro-preemies, lengthy hospitalizations, medical interventions, and know the pain of losing our one-day-old daughter, Ruth, who I miss every day and who I love very much," he said.
Freeland and Fraser's resignations come as Trudeau prepares to shake up his cabinet after several ministers announced they won't be seeking re-election this fall. Their resignations make it nine ministers in Trudeau's cabinet who have stepped down or announced their intention to step down in recent months.
The Trudeau government is also facing a byelection in B.C. Monday night where polls suggest they will lose the seat they previously held.
With files from Robert Benzie.

Document 459 of 500

Having Americans freeze in the dark might get Trump's respect

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/having-americans-freeze-dark-might-get-trumps/docview/3145189049/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Having+Americans+freeze+in+the+dark+might+get+Trump%27s+respect&amp;title=The+Hamilton+Spectator+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2024-12-16&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Hamilton+Spectator+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Throughout history, Canadians have rarely been concerned about the security of their nation.
From the earliest days of European settlement in what became Canada, we were protected by the mighty British Empire. That began to change on Aug. 18, 1938, when American president Franklin Roosevelt spoke at Queen's University in Kingston, Ont., and there he declared: "I give to you assurance that the people of the United States will not stand idly by if domination of Canadian soil is threatened by any other empire."
In 1940 Canada and the United States signed the "Ogdensburg Agreement" which outlined a plan for mutual defence between our two nations.
After the Second World War, Canada and the U.S. helped create NATO and then Norad which further cemented our two nations together in the cause of mutual defence. As Canadians we knew that in the very slim chance we were threatened militarily, the U.S. would come to our aid.
This changed when Donald Trump won the 2016 American presidential election. For the first time since before Confederation, Canada had to deal with a president that was openly hostile to our well-being.
Such actions as personal insults of Prime Minister Justin Trudeau, imposing illegal tariffs on our steel and aluminum, etc. clearly indicated we did not have a friend in the White House. Luckily for Canada, there were still some moderate and reasonable Republicans in that administration who probably spared Canada a great deal more damage.
Most Canadians had a deep sigh of relief when Joe Biden defeated Trump in 2020. When Trump left office many Canadians, I am sure, thought of the words that former American president Gerald Ford said in 1974 when he replaced Richard Nixon in the White House: "Our long, national nightmare is over."
But alas the nightmare has returned, and Canadians face a terrible threat to our well-being. President-elect Trump ran on a campaign promise of imposing 25 per cent tariffs on imported Canadian goods (as well as products from other nations.) These tariffs will be paid by companies importing our products and will raise the cost of our products by that amount and perhaps more. Trudeau has vowed that Canada will retaliate with tariffs of our own on imported American goods.
But perhaps Premier Doug Ford has come up with the best solution. On Wednesday he announced that he would cut exports of Ontario energy (mainly electricity) to American states. Ontario electricity is used in more than 1.5 million American homes in such states as New York, Michigan and Minnesota.
I agree with Ford, but suggest if these tariffs are imposed, we must go further. By imposing them, Trump will make it clear he wishes to cause great harm to Canadians. We need to "take the gloves off" and cut off all energy exports to the U.S. until Trump backs down. This means oil, natural gas and electricity.
Sixty-five per cent of all American oil imports are from Canada.
We supply electricity to border states and down the American eastern seaboard. Our natural gas heats many American homes and businesses. It will take gasoline prices going "into the stratosphere" due to no Canadian oil, and unheated homes and blacked out cities due to no Canadian electricity and natural gas for Trump to wake up. (If that is even possible.)
Trump also badly needs a history lesson. He needs to remember who hid and later smuggled American diplomats out of Tehran in 1979-80.
Who put up stranded American travellers on 9/11 who were forced to land in Canada? Who went to war with the U.S. in Afghanistan? The answer to all of these is Canada. Trump needs to understand that he can't treat friends like this.
Trumps' tariff threats are an existential threat to Canada. We must respond by hitting back so hard, he will never threaten us again.
CAPTION: It will take gasoline prices going "into the stratosphere" due to no Canadian oil, and unheated homes and blacked out cities due to no Canadian electricity and natural gas for Trump to wake up, Craig Wallace writes.
CREDIT: Craig Wallace

Subject: Gasoline prices; Political campaigns; Canadians; Presidential elections; Natural gas; Tariffs

Document 460 of 500

Prioritize timely access to cancer drugs

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/prioritize-timely-access-cancer-drugs/docview/3144879539/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=unknown&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Prioritize+timely+access+to+cancer+drugs&amp;title=The+Spectator&amp;issn=11899417&amp;date=2024-12-16&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.9&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Spectator&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: As Canada's 13 provincial and territorial premiers gather in Toronto for a meeting of the Council of the Federation, accelerating patient access to new, effective cancer treatments to improve health outcomes should be at the top of their agenda.
Colorectal Cancer Canada and the CanCertainty Coalition are calling out the long delays patients face when they need new cancer treatments. These delays are taking a toll and can be measured in the number of lives lost.
There will be almost a quarter of a million people newly diagnosed with cancer in Canada this year. For some, new medications are their only hope to prolong their lives. Despite this, accessing them may take years - time many won't have. Patients and families ask groups like ours, why does it take so long?
Canada ranks a dismal last place of the G7 countries for the length of time it takes to approve and provide patients new cancer drugs. This takes about two years of waiting in Canada, while other countries manage their regulatory and health technology assessment process in half the time. We must do much better for Canadians already living with the burden of a cancer diagnosis.
Since 2022, our organizations have been examining this issue. We have convened policy roundtable sessions and summits. We have sought expert insights from a range of health-care professionals, industry leaders, health policy consultants, patient groups, technology assessment agency representatives and provincial drug program managers to explore new thinking and update frameworks to enhance patient access to new cancer medicines.
We have studied international best practices and their relevance in the Canadian context. We have also examined the intricate and protracted drug negotiation process, led by the pan-Canadian Pharmaceutical Alliance (pCPA). And, we have scrutinized the often-lengthy time it takes provinces to list these drugs on their respective formularies and are finally made available to patients. For example, in Ontario it takes almost six months once a price has been negotiated at pCPA before the new drug is available.
Provinces can and must move much faster.
We applaud the announcement by Ontario's Premier Doug Ford that he will focus on accelerating treatment access for patients in Ontario, and that as chair of the Council of the Federation, he will encourage other premiers to make this issue a priority, as this is a national crisis.
Patient organizations support Premier Ford's intention to see innovative treatments for life-threatening or severely debilitating conditions, such as cancer, funded immediately after Canada's Drug Agency (CDA) issues a funding recommendation. This means patients would not have to endure the lengthy bureaucratic price negotiations before accessing the treatment they need.
Premiers can take immediate action to help cancer patients by ensuring new drugs are made available immediately following the reviews conducted by CDA or L'Institut national d'excellence en santé et en services sociaux by incentivizing rapid price negotiations at the pCPA. This is not an unsurmountable task.
We call on Ford and all of the premiers to make timely access to new cancer drugs a priority now with the unified goal of dramatically reducing wait times for access to new cancer drugs, because cancer patients cannot wait.
Robert Bick is a charter board member of Kidney Cancer Canada, and the co-lead and co-founder of the CanCertainty Coalition. Barry Stein is the president and CEO of Colorectal
Cancer Canada and is a
survivor of metastatic
colorectal cancer.

Document 461 of 500

'Institutional circuit' is not the answer

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/institutional-circuit-is-not-answer/docview/3144878852/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=unknown&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=%27Institutional+circuit%27+is+not+the+answer&amp;title=The+Spectator&amp;issn=11899417&amp;date=2024-12-16&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.9&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Spectator&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Homeless encampments have become a regular fixture in cities across Canada. Unsheltered homelessness, including people staying in encampments, rose 88 per cent across the country from 2018 to 2022, according to the last national point-in-time count.
In Ontario, advocates, clinicians, researchers, mayors and city councillors, in addition to people experiencing homelessness, have been sounding the alarm, calling on the provincial government to act. Premier Doug Ford responded to those calls by signalling he would introduce legislation to provide municipalities with "enhanced legislative powers" to dismantle encampments.
Then on Thursday, the government introduced new legislation giving police more power to punish drug use in public parks, and penalties for those who continually "trespass" in parks and other properties. These changes are all part of the Ford government's plans to address encampments.
The premier also pledged $75.5 million for shelters and affordable housing across the province. While this is a much-needed investment, building sufficient affordable housing will take time and additional resources. Where will encampment residents go in the meantime?
The government's approach to dismantling encampments in effect puts forward three potential paths for residents: shelter, jail or hospital. These options, known for decades as the institutional circuit, do not resolve the primary unmet need of encampment residents: rapid access to housing with supports. Moreover, shelter, jail and hospitals represent the most expensive types of services where you can find people experiencing homelessness.
Dismantling encampments may also cause further harm to those residing there. A U.S. simulation study found involuntary displacement, such as encampment dismantlement, produces additional overdose deaths. Involuntary displacement also results in more hospitalizations as well as reduced initiation of medications for opioid use disorder, the first-line treatment for this condition. This research strongly suggests dismantling encampments in Ontario would have a similar result: increased overdose fatalities and reduced access to addiction treatment.
As researchers and clinicians working with people who are homeless, we know there is a better and more efficient way to reduce the number of encampments in Ontario: a housing-first intervention. That involves the provision of a rent supplement along with community-based mental health and addiction supports to help those with serious mental illness, substance use disorders, and chronic medical conditions immediately access permanent housing. Research from the Canadian At Home/Chez Soi demonstration project found the intervention is effective in housing 80-90 per cent of people experiencing long-term homelessness. Followup research conducted in Toronto showed that these outcomes were sustained as many as six years later.
Housing-first intervention is smart and cost-effective. The cost is partially offset by a reduction in the use of health care, shelter, and criminal justice services. Many hospital visits by people experiencing homelessness are preventable with housing and the right supports, giving the housing-first approach a role to play in reducing hallway medicine in the province.
It is not just in research where we see housing-first's effectiveness. The City of Houston has become a leader in the U.S. when it comes to ending homelessness. In 2011, the city had about 8,500 people who were homeless. A decade later, it had reduced that by 61 per cent. Unsheltered homelessness is also down 33 per cent in the last four years. Houston's approach? Housing first.
We agree with the premier that enough is enough: It's time to end unsheltered homelessness in Ontario. But, let's not waste more money shuffling people experiencing homelessness around the institutional circuit. Instead, let's leverage a housing-first intervention, a proven way to get people the housing and support they need. This approach is not only more fiscally prudent for taxpayers, it is a more sustainable solution for improving access and safety in our parks and other public spaces.
Nick Kerman is a psychologist and scientific associate
at the Centre for Addiction and Mental Health. Vicky
Stergiopoulos is a psychiatrist and senior scientist
at the centre.
CAPTION:

CREDIT: Nick Kerman and Vicky Stergiopoulos Nick Kerman is a psychologist and scientific associate at the Centre for Addiction and Mental Health. Vicky Stergiopoulos is a psychiatrist and senior scientist at the centre.;

Document 462 of 500

Premiers express alarm at political 'chaos' in Ottawa amid looming Trump trade threat

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/premiers-express-alarm-at-political-chaos-ottawa/docview/3144878847/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Premiers+express+alarm+at+political+%27chaos%27+in+Ottawa+amid+looming+Trump+trade+threat&amp;title=The+Hamilton+Spectator+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2024-12-16&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Hamilton+Spectator+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Canada's provincial and territorial leaders are expressing alarm at the political chaos in Ottawa as they gird for U.S. president-elect Donald Trump's threatened 25-per-cent tariffs against Canadian products.
The premiers, meeting in the GTA Monday, said the stunning resignation of Chrystia Freeland from Prime Minister Justin Trudeau's cabinet could not have come at a worst time - and they vowed to provide stability for the nation.
"It's chaos right now up in Ottawa," Premier Doug Ford, chair of the Council of the Federation, said after a conference at the Hilton Hotel near Pearson International Airport.
Ford, a close personal friend and political ally of Freeland, put her on speakerphone so she could address the other premiers when she called him during their meeting.
"We have a good relationship with Chrystia over the years. I just want to wish her all the best," he said, emphasizing the uncertainty is not helpful.
"As a country, we have to project strength and unity."
Manitoba Premier Wab Kinew praised Freeland for being "very positive and collaborative" and said the provinces and territories are focused on dealing with Trump.
Alberta Premier Danielle Smith pointed out that she and her colleagues "have a secure mandate" from voters.
"This is a stable table and I think it's up to us to continue offering that continuity if there is that uncertainty happening at the federal level," said Smith.
Amid the unpredictability in federal politics, the premiers will travel to Washington in February on a Team Canada mission to promote trade to senators, congresspeople, governors and other American officials.
Behind closed doors, the premiers heard for two hours from former Canadian ambassadors to Washington David McNaughton, Gary Doer and Frank McKenna, who shared strategies for coping with Trump.
"That was ... a very engaging discussion and some very astute advice provided to each of us here," said Saskatchewan Premier Scott Moe.
The premiers were so impressed with the presentation that their federation council may retain one of the former ambassadors as an emissary in Washington in the coming months.
Freeland's departure as finance minister and deputy prime minister came hours before she was supposed to deliver a fall economic statement outlining $1 billion in new spending on border security measures, including increased personnel and new equipment such as helicopters and drones.
The premiers' in-person confab was held after two recent virtual first ministers' meetings with Trudeau in the wake of Trump's plan to impose levies on Canadian and Mexican goods unless America's neighbours tighten their borders to stem illegal immigration and fentanyl smuggling.
Ford made headlines stateside last Wednesday with his threat to "cut off" Ontario electricity exports to the U.S. in retaliation for any tariffs.
"Americans are going to feel the pain as well and isn't that unfortunate," he warned.
The premier's gambit forced Trump to respond the next day.
"That's OK if he that does that. That's fine," the president-elect said Thursday, complaining the U.S. "is subsidizing Canada" by spending so much on defence.
"We shouldn't have to do that. And we have a great relationship. I have so many friends in Canada, but we shouldn't have to subsidize a country. We're subsidizing more than $100 billion a year," maintained Trump.
Ford was encouraged that the incoming president took notice, and Freeland and Public Safety Minister Dominic LeBlanc appeared to endorse his hawkish stance even though some other premiers from energy-exporting provinces disagree.
"We have to be candid about the reality of the incoming U.S. administration. This is an administration that believes in America first. This is an administration which is proudly an economic nationalist administration," Freeland said Friday in her last public event as finance minister.
LeBlanc, for his part, said pointedly, "I've gotten to know Doug Ford and like him over the last number of years.
"He's certainly able to speak for himself," he said, conceding "other premiers have offered views on what might be the appropriate response."
Indeed, Alberta's Smith, whose province supplies 60 per cent of oil used in the U.S., and Quebec Premier François Legault, whose province is a major exporter of hydro electricity, have rejected Ford's musings about curbing energy exports.
Ontario sells electricity to Michigan, New York and Minnesota, powering the homes of 1.5 million Americans.
Ford said Monday the premiers will continue to pressure Ottawa to boost Canada's military spending, a push being led by Manitoba's Kinew.
"The reason why we have that unity on these priorities is because if we as Canadians come together to invest in the Canadian Armed Forces, to protect our border, to rid the scourge of toxic drugs in our communities, what's the downside we take action on those things?" said the Manitoban.
"We're unified in investing in the military, we're unified in cracking down on drug trafficking, we're unified on having a secure border," said Kinew.
So far, Canada is falling short of NATO's demand that its 32 member nations spend at least two per cent of gross domestic product on defence.
NATO estimates the Trudeau government will spend 1.37 per cent of GDP on the military this year.
The prime minister has previously pledged to meet the two-per-cent target by 2032, including increased spending on defence infrastructure totalling $8.1 billion over the next five years.
CAPTION: Ontario Premier Doug Ford steps out of an office to speak to the media at the Ontario Legislature in Toronto on Dec. 11.Premier Doug Ford speaks to the media during the fall meetings of Canada's premiers hosted by Ontario on Monday.

Subject: International trade; Presidents; Prime ministers; Tariffs

Canada's premiers must prioritize timely access to cancer drugs

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/canadas-premiers-must-prioritize-timely-access/docview/3144878823/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Canada%27s+premiers+must+prioritize+timely+access+to+cancer+drugs&amp;title=The+Hamilton+Spectator+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2024-12-16&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Hamilton+Spectator+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: When Canada's 13 provincial and territorial premiers gather in Toronto Dec. 15 and 16 for a meeting of the Council of the Federation, accelerating patient access to new, effective cancer treatments to improve health outcomes should be at the top of their agenda.
As organizations representing cancer patients for many years, Colorectal Cancer Canada and the CanCertainty Coalition are calling out the long delays patients face when they need new cancer treatments. These delays are taking a toll and can be measured in the number of lives lost.
There will be almost a quarter of a million people newly diagnosed with cancer in Canada this year. For some, new medications are their only hope to prolong their lives. Despite this, accessing them may take years - time many won't have.

We call on Ford and all of the premiers to make timely access to new cancer drugs a priority now with the unified goal of dramatically reducing wait times for access to new cancer drugs, because cancer patients cannot wait.

Document 464 of 500

Blackouts might get some respect

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/blackouts-might-get-some-respect/docview/3144878770/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=unknown&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Blackouts+might+get+some+respect&amp;title=The+Spectator&amp;issn=11899417&amp;date=2024-12-16&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.9&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Spectator&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Throughout history, Canadians have rarely been concerned about the security of their nation.
From the earliest days of European settlement in what became Canada, we were protected by the mighty British Empire. That began to change on Aug. 18, 1938, when American president Franklin Roosevelt spoke at Queen's University in Kingston, Ont., and there he declared: "I give to you assurance that the people of the United States will not stand idly by if domination of Canadian soil is threatened by any other empire."
In 1940 Canada and the United States signed the "Ogdensburg Agreement" which outlined a plan for mutual defence between our two nations.
After the Second World War, Canada and the U.S. cemented our two nations together in the cause of mutual defence. As Canadians, we knew that in the very slim chance we were threatened militarily, the U.S. would come to our aid.
This changed when Donald Trump won the 2016 American presidential election. For the first time since before Confederation, Canada had to deal with a president that was openly hostile to our well-being.
Such actions as personal insults of Prime Minister Justin Trudeau, imposing illegal tariffs on our steel and aluminum, etc. clearly indicated we did not have a friend in the White House.
Most Canadians had a deep sigh of relief when Joe Biden defeated Trump in 2020. When Trump left office many Canadians, I am sure, thought of the words that former American president Gerald Ford said in 1974 when he replaced Richard Nixon in the White House: "Our long, national nightmare is over."
But alas the nightmare has returned, and Canadians face a terrible threat to our well-being. President-elect Trump ran on a campaign promise of imposing 25 per cent tariffs on imported Canadian goods (as well as products from other nations.) These tariffs will be paid by companies importing our products and will raise the cost of our products by that amount and perhaps more. Trudeau has vowed that Canada will retaliate with tariffs of our own on imported American goods.
But perhaps Premier Doug Ford has come up with the best solution. On Wednesday he announced that he would cut exports of Ontario energy (mainly electricity) to American states. Ontario electricity is used in more than 1.5 million American homes in such states as New York, Michigan and Minnesota.
I agree with Ford, but suggest if these tariffs are imposed, we must go further. By imposing them, Trump will make it clear he wishes to cause great harm to Canadians. We need to "take the gloves off" and cut off all energy exports to the U.S. until Trump backs down. This means oil, natural gas and electricity.
Sixty-five per cent of all American oil imports are from Canada.
We supply electricity to border states and down the American eastern seaboard. Our natural gas heats many American homes and businesses. It will take gasoline prices going "into the stratosphere" due to no Canadian oil, and unheated homes and blacked out cities due to no Canadian electricity and natural gas for Trump to wake up. (If that is even possible.)
Trump also badly needs a history lesson. He needs to remember who hid and later smuggled American diplomats out of Tehran in 1979-80.
Who put up stranded American travellers on 9/11 who were forced to land in Canada? Who went to war with the U.S. in Afghanistan? The answer to all of these is Canada. Trump needs to understand that he can't treat friends like this.
Trumps' tariff threats are an existential threat to Canada. We must respond by hitting back so hard, he will never threaten us again.
Craig Wallace
is a resident of Hamilton
and author of five books.
CAPTION:

Document 465 of 500

Team Canada approach is a total mess

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/team-canada-approach-is-total-mess/docview/3144297066/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=unknown&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Team+Canada+approach+is+a+total+mess&amp;title=The+Spectator&amp;issn=11899417&amp;date=2024-12-14&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.15&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Spectator&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: I hope there's a method to the oddly disjointed response that the Trudeau government and the premiers are displaying to the Trump tariff threat.
Consider Wednesday evening, after the prime minister and some of his key cabinet members met (virtually) with the premiers for the second time in a week. Everyone was talking about a "Team Canada" approach, but what we saw was a strange version of that.
First out was Doug Ford, premier of Ontario and, as chair of the Council of the Federation, leader for now of the premiers. Ford looked sombre and talked tough. Tariffs, he warned, are coming for sure and "we need to be ready to fight."
Then he dropped a bombshell: "We will go to the extent of cutting off their energy, going down to Michigan, going down to New York state and over to Wisconsin." Ford later said such a move would be only "a last resort." But even as a suggestion it's big stuff, far beyond anything the federal government has mentioned.
Then came Deputy Prime Minister Chrystia Freeland and Public Safety Minister Dominic LeBlanc. With Ford having set the tone, they were left to react to what he had said. There will be a plan for the border, they said. What is it? Won't say. When will it come? Can't say. What about Ford's threat about energy? Canada will respond, mumble, mumble.
Where was the prime minister at this existential moment for Canada? Why wasn't he front and centre after convening Team Canada once again?
I don't know why he sent out his deputies instead of facing the cameras himself. I'm guessing it might have had something to do with the speech he gave Tuesday in which he lamented the fact that Kamala Harris lost to Donald Trump in November. He went to an event hosted by Equal Voice, which promotes women in politics, and noted the U.S. has just "voted for a second time not to elect its first woman president."
Nice one. There was Trudeau, playing to type, burnishing his feminist credentials with a poke at Trump. Ford made clear the premiers didn't like that; they told the PM his comments "were not helpful at all." That's diplomatic language for "what on earth were you thinking?" I expect Team Trudeau decided it wasn't a good idea for him to go out and be grilled about that.
Some see in all this a cunning Canadian plan to push back against Trump. In this scenario, Trudeau plays the good cop by jetting to Mar-a-Lago and paying homage to the great man. Meanwhile Ford plays bad cop by dangling the threat of an energy boycott if we do get hit with tariffs. See? There is a method to it all.
I'd like to believe our side is that strategic, but frankly I have my doubts. The Mar-a-Lago foray is looking worse every day. It's not just that Trump sent Trudeau away with no assurances on tariffs. He's using the PM's eagerness to meet as stick to beat him with, boasting that "Justin" turned up "about 15 seconds" after they spoke.
It's clear Trump has no respect for Trudeau, or for Canada if it comes to that. You could dismiss the "51st state" jibe as a joke if was just a one-off. But instead it's become part of Trump's regular patter. At an event last week hosted by Fox News he used it again and the conservative audience responded by chanting "51! 51!" We're not a real country in their eyes.
So far, at least, it's been premiers who have had the most effective response. Ontario had an ad campaign ready to go and is promoting the province's importance to the U.S. economy in places where Republicans are likely to see it (such as Fox News).
Alberta's Danielle Smith talked up Canadian oil and gas with western state governors in Las Vegas this week (she ruled out a Ford-style energy boycott). She's also joined a group of U.S. governors that focuses on energy security and put forward her own border security plan.
Maybe there is a Team Canada after all. Except this time it's premiers taking the lead.
ANDREW PHILLIPS is a staff columnist with Torstar.
CAPTION:

Document 466 of 500

Pierre Poilievre's love affair with labour hits a fork in the road

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/pierre-poilievres-love-affair-with-labour-hits/docview/3144296724/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Pierre+Poilievre%27s+love+affair+with+labour+hits+a+fork+in+the+road&amp;title=The+Hamilton+Spectator+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2024-12-14&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Hamilton+Spectator+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Like teenage infatuation, it burned bright and fizzled quickly, with one party barely aware the love affair had begun. This week, the Conservatives appeared to mark the end of their outward courtship of organized labour.
For a while, it seemed Conservative Leader Pierre Poilievre was serious about standing up for workers' rights. He'd picked up the ball from former leader Erin O'Toole, reaching out to blue-collar workers, especially those in private-sector unions, hoping to mimic Ontario Progressive Conservative Premier Doug Ford's early success.
In May, the Conservative party helped unanimously pass the Liberal government's anti-scab legislation, Bill C-58. "Knock me over with a feather!" Unifor National President Lana Payne said in expressing her surprise. Poilievre's office pledged to keep the measures - which kick in next year - if the Tories form government.
This fall, in the lead up to a potentially disruptive Air Canada strike, Poilievre sided with the pilots, saying they were just looking for the same compensation as their American counterparts. "We are not going to support pre-empting those negotiations," he told reporters. It was a clear reversal from his 2012 position, when he supported then-prime minister Stephen Harper's pre-emptive use of back-to-work legislation for those same pilots.
In the summer, Poilievre declined to weigh in on the lockouts affecting employees of the National Railway Company (CN) and Canadian Pacific Kansas City Limited (CPKC), even after Labour Minister Steve MacKinnon asked the Canada Industrial Relations Board (CIRB) to impose binding arbitration and NDP Leader Jagmeet Singh attacked the Liberal government for caving to "corporate greed." Conservative MPs were told to keep their mouths shut.
Then in September, Poilievre tried to distance himself from his anti-union record. In a statement sent to the National Post, he promised not to introduce legislation he'd supported while in Harper's cabinet. "Hundreds of visits to workshops, factories, union halls and training centres have taught me a lot and helped me evolve my thinking," Poilievre is quoted telling the paper. "A common sense Conservative government will not introduce or pass bills C-377, C-525 or right to work laws. Period. This commitment will be written in my election platform."
Those bills were sponsored by Conservative MPs and were an attack against organized labour. Bill C-377 placed onerous, overarching, expensive reporting requirements on unions, drowning them in red tape, forcing not just transparency disclosures on union leadership but on union suppliers, too. Bill C-525 made it more difficult for workers to unionize by replacing the card-check system with a secret-ballot vote. A move that sounds good but studies show opens the door to more threats and intimidation from employers, and leads to declines in union membership. When the Liberals were elected in 2015, they repealed the laws - and Poilievre spoke against it in the Commons.
The Liberals, the NDP, the Bloc Québécois, the Greens, and many labour groups never believed Poilievre's support for workers was anything more than an electoral ploy. They frequently suggested the Conservative leader wasn't as committed to workers' rights as he portrays himself to be.
This week, Poilievre and his Conservative MP Blaine Calkins showed the skepticism is valid.
It wasn't just that on Wednesday Poilievre called on the prime minister to end the mail strike by forcing Canada Post and the Canadian Union of Postal Workers to sit down and "get a deal." (The Liberals initially described the move as "hostile" to workers but on Friday, MacKinnon declared the parties were at an impasse and asked the Canada Industrial Relations Board to order workers back to work until May 22, during which time a commission would be established to help resolve the labour dispute.)
It wasn't just that Poilievre decried the large number of strikes this year, portraying worker action in a negative light. Despite the tension, Payne counts 98 disputes since August 2022, Unifor's president said she's tried to stress to "every politician who wants to listen" that strikes have "a very positive impact overall in terms of progress for working people.
"These companies don't come to the table wanting to hand over things," she said. Workers took advantage of the moment before them - with high inflation and low unemployment, she said, and helped push the envelope on pensions, wages, and benefits.
The next telling moment will be how Poilievre reacts to a quip by Calkins in the House of Commons.
As MacKinnon slammed Poilievre on Monday for supporting C-525 and C-377, and erroneously accusing him of being the "author" of the most oppressive pieces of anti-labour legislation, Calkins piped up from his seat on the far south corner of the chamber to proudly claim ownership and defend the bill's content. "That was my bill," he yelled at MacKinnon, about C-525. "Wanting more democracy?" he said, smiling. "How draconian," he mocked.
Far from distancing himself, here was a Poilievre shadow minister praising the controversial legislation. Calkins's thinking on labour, and those of the Conservative MPs laughing around him, doesn't appear to have "evolved."
Poilievre takes great pains to muzzle his MPs. They are told to avoid commenting, ignore media requests, and rat on colleagues who speak to reporters. Whether he now responds to Calkins's outburst by remaining silent or disciplining him will be a strong indicator of whether the Conservative leader still has any love for organized labour, or if the honeymoon is truly over.
CAPTION: Conservative Leader Pierre Poilievre rises during question period in the House of Commons on Dec. 11, 2024.

Document 467 of 500

Put this 'clause' to better use

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/put-this-clause-better-use/docview/3144295927/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=unknown&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Put+this+%27clause%27+to+better+use&amp;title=The+Spectator&amp;issn=11899417&amp;date=2024-12-14&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.15&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Spectator&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Is there a more obscure, confounding word in the English language that deserves to be relegated to the confines of academia than "notwithstanding?"
When I hear the word, I envision a tweedy professor pacing a lecture hall using it while belabouring a minuscule point. Never did I imagine it would be the subject of such widespread debate or use. I seems I can't stand in line at Starbucks, or attend a social function without overhearing someone drop it. Not only drop it, but do so with such passion.
Even more surprising, "notwithstanding" is showing up regularly in Premier Doug Ford's parlance, a man not noted for his eloquence .
Last month, a movement emerged calling on Ontario mayors to petition the province to use Section 33 of the Charter of Rights and Freedoms, more commonly known as the notwithstanding clause, as a tool for clearing homeless encampments from city parks. The provincial government can obviously use this extraordinary power without lobbying from big city mayors, but Ford gave himself political cover when he floated the suggestion that they write to him and urge him to act in this draconian fashion.
This is not the first time Ford has mused about the notwithstanding clause. His government has invoked it three times since taking office in 2018.
Since 2018, governments in Saskatchewan have reached for it three times, Quebec twice and New Brunswick once. Alberta has never used it, but Premier Danielle Smith frequently cites it in her sabre rattling with the federal government.
It's not my imagination then, that I'm hearing the word more and more often.
The notwithstanding clause is often metaphorically referred to as the nuclear option, given the power it holds in overriding parts of the Charter. Its founders likely thought it would be used sparingly and certainly not frivolously. But 40 years later, along came the likes of Ford, Smith and Saskatchewan's Scott Moe.
It's notable that in its 42-year history, the notwithstanding clause has never been used by the federal government. With the recent propensity for various premiers to invoke it for arguably insubstantial reasons, maybe it's time the federal government used it to drag recalcitrant provinces toward solutions to two significant problems facing Canada today: housing and wealth and income inequality.
Housing and income security are provincial responsibilities. One could make a strong case that the provinces have failed in these two areas, given the fact we are facing simultaneous crises in both.
The proof is in the proliferation of homeless encampments across the country. And for this reason, the federal government needs to consider stepping into this void with the power of the notwithstanding clause behind it.
It's become de rigueur for Prime Minister Justin Trudeau to call the first ministers together to plan strategy on how Canada is going to deal with a second Trump administration.
I suggest he convene two separate first ministers meetings where he presents impending federal legislation.
The first one deals with federal legislation and money to support the provinces in the development and implementation of comprehensive public housing infrastructure.
The second involves legislation and money to support provincially administered guaranteed basic income programs.
With both initiatives, the federal government reserves the right to invoke the notwithstanding clause to bypass any provinces that signal they might opt out.
Federal leadership where the provinces have failed just might help mitigate these two nagging national problems.
Other than these two specific federal forays into this intricate section of the Constitution, the only time I want to hear the word "clause" used with such frequency, is not when it follows "notwithstanding," but when it follows Santa.
Tyler Firth lives in Hamilton.
CAPTION:

CREDIT: Tyler Firth;

Subject: Homeless encampments; Provinces; Federal government; Legislation

Location: Saskatchewan Canada; Canada

The only 'clause' I'm interested in is the Santa kind

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/only-clause-im-interested-is-santa-kind/docview/3144285128/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=The+only+%27clause%27+I%27m+interested+in+is+the+Santa+kind&amp;title=The+Hamilton+Spectator+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2024-12-14&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Hamilton+Spectator+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Housing and income security are provincial responsibilities. One could make a strong case that the provinces have failed in these two areas, given the fact we are facing simultaneous crises in both. I suggest he convene two separate first ministers meetings where he presents impending federal legislation. The first one deals with federal legislation and money to support the provinces in the development and implementation of comprehensive public housing infrastructure. The second involves legislation and money to support provincially administered guaranteed basic income programs.
With both initiatives, the federal government reserves the right to invoke the notwithstanding clause to bypass any provinces that signal they might opt out.
