Document 469 of 500

Bending the rules How Canada opened its doors to Nazi war criminals: 'It would be rash to assume that significant numbers of war criminals and Nazi collaborators did not enter Canada'

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/bending-rules-how-canada-opened-doors-nazi-war/docview/3144285066/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Bending+the+rules+How+Canada+opened+its+doors+to+Nazi+war+criminals%3A+%27It+would+be+rash+to+assume+that+significant+numbers+of+war+criminals+and+Nazi+collaborators+did+not+enter+Canada%27&amp;title=The+Spectator&amp;issn=11899417&amp;date=2024-12-14&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.1&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Spectator&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: An unknown number of Nazi war criminals and collaborators lived out their lives in peace in Canada, in places such as the Mennonite farms and villages of Waterloo Region.
They were among thousands of ethnic Germans brought into Canada after the Second World War - many of them members of the most reprehensible units of the Nazi war machine - by a group called the Canadian Christian Council for the Resettlement of Refugees (CCCRR), which included the Mennonite Central Committee (MCC).
How this happened is detailed in a Government of Canada report, "Nazi War Criminals in Canada: The Historical And Policy Setting From the 1940s to the Present." Known as The Rodal Report, it is a 600-page study prepared by Oxford-trained historian Alti Rodal for the Deschênes Commission on Nazi War Criminals in Canada, which released its findings in 1986.
For decades, Ottawa refused to release the entirety of the Rodal report. The Waterloo Region Record filed an Access to Information request for a complete copy six years ago.
The Deschênes Commission said there were 29 cases of suspected Nazi war criminals in Canada that warranted special consideration because of the seriousness of the allegations and the availability of evidence against the men.
Among those priority cases was Waterloo resident Helmut Oberlander. The Record filed an Access to information Act request for the files on the other 28 cases, but Library and Archives Canada has refused to release any of the documents.
Before he died in September 2021 Oberlander was front page news in Waterloo Region, fighting a deportation order on and off since 1995. That's when Ottawa announced it was going to strip Oberlander of his citizenship and deport him for failing to disclose his voluntary service with Einsatzgruppe D, one of the Nazi's mobile death squads that operated in Eastern Europe, Soviet Ukraine and the Caucuses.
Oberlander and his wife came to Canada in May 1954, sponsored by a German relative in Waterloo. He was 'Volksdeutsche' - a 'racial' term coined by the Nazis for ethnic Germans who didn't hold German citizenship - from one of the oldest Mennonite communities in southern Russia. Oberlander was not brought to Waterloo Region by the MCC, but others were.
The Rodal report makes it clear there were many more - in fact it was easier for Nazi war criminals to come to Canada after the Second World War than Jews who wanted to rebuild their lives in Canada following the Holocaust.
Refugee aid groups and immigration officials preferred Germans and Baltic people, deemed to be highly attractive and desirable immigrants, "who were likely to have included - and indeed have been shown to have included in their midst persons who were Nazi collaborators and alleged war criminals," says Rodal.
During 1947-1949, the MCC and the CCCRR helped bring 6,005 Mennonites to Canada, and 15,000 Volksdeutsche - primarily from the western and southwestern areas of the former Soviet Union that became Ukraine in 1991.
About 807 of them settled in Ontario, primarily Kitchener-Waterloo, St. Catharines and Leamington. Many who first settled in other parts of Canada later moved to this region, say Mennonite historians.
Some Mennonites who were former SS and Nazi collaborators came to this region, but not many, said Troy Osborne, a history professor and dean of Mennonite studies at Conrad Grebel University in Waterloo.
"I don't think there were a lot in this area," said Osborne.
But researchers are reluctant to dig into this chapter of Mennonite history in the region.
"Part of the reason I think it is frosted over around here, you are talking about people's grandparents, it is not just about the MCC, it is about people's own family histories too," said Osborne. "That's what I've discovered."
Library and Archives Canada released the most complete version of the Rodal report in February 2024 following an international scandal six months earlier. The House of Commons gave two standing ovations to a former member of the Ukrainian-SS in September 2023.
That's when Ukraine president Volodymyr Zelenskyy was a guest of honour in Ottawa and spoke in Parliament to thank Canada for help in the war against Russia.
In the audience was 98-year-old Yaroslav Hunka, a former member of the Ukrainian SS - part of the Waffen-SS largely made up of non-German volunteers.
Federal politicians and their guests gave Hunka two standing ovations after he was introduced as a true hero of Ukraine by the Speaker of the House, Anthony Rota, a Liberal MP from North Bay.
When Hunka's background became public, Prime Minister Justin Trudeau apologized, and Rota resigned as Speaker of the House. Immigration Minister Marc Miller acknowledged Canada has a dark history when it comes to Nazis, and said requests to release more classified information about it would be revisited.
In February 2024, Library and Archives Canada released almost everything from the Rodal Report, minus one page and a handful of redactions.
The report lays out in detail a seeming indifference to war crimes by officials, openly discriminatory immigration policies, antisemitism, and security screening that was lax to non-existent.
"It would be rash to assume that significant numbers of war criminals and Nazi collaborators did not enter Canada," Rodal says in her report.
The original SS were early, enthusiastic supporters of Adolf Hitler, pledging loyalty to him alone. Later, the Nazis easily found enough volunteers in the Baltics, Eastern Europe, the Balkans and Soviet Ukraine to form divisions called the Waffen-SS.
The SS and Waffen-SS ran the concentration camps and death camps. They were largely responsible for carrying out the Holocaust.
The Nuremberg War Crimes Tribunal ruled the SS as a whole, including Waffen-SS divisions, a criminal organization. From 1945-1950 they were officially banned from coming to Canada, along with members of the SD (the brutal SS intelligence agency that operated in tandem with the SS), the Brown Shirts, or Storm Troopers, known as the SA, and members of the Einsatzgruppen mobile death squads that killed 1.5 million people, mostly Jews, in 1941-1943.
After the war, the Allies suspected many men from Eastern Europe and Soviet Ukraine had volunteered for some of the Nazi's worst units, says Rodal. Among that group were the Volksdeutsche living in Eastern Europe and Soviet Ukraine.
By 1950 Canada had increased its immigration quotas and was relaxing its policies on the banned groups and favouring 'desirable' ethnic German immigrants.
Rodal ties the CCCRR - an umbrella organization that represented Baptist, Lutheran, Mennonite and Catholic church groups mainly of German origin - with the changes in government policy.
The group lobbied Ottawa tirelessly to drop the immigration bans on former SS members, and senior bureaucrats did, without telling House of Commons or the Canadian public, says Rodal, setting the stage for Germany to become the biggest source of immigrants to Canada in the 1950s.
The church group "is closely linked to changes in government policy with regard to admitting enemy aliens and former members of the Nazi party and the Waffen SS, and other Nazi organizations," says Rodal.
In fact, Helmut Rauca, a former member of Einsatzgruppe A, one of the Nazis' mobile death squads responsible for killing thousands of Jews in Latvia, came to Canada in the late 1940s as a CCCRR sponsored refugee, under his own name, settling in Toronto.
West Germany issued an arrest warrant for Rauca in 1963, and Canada deported him in 1983 to face charges of crimes against humanity. He died in a West German jail awaiting trial.
By an order-in-council, Ottawa provided funds to the CCCRR, says Rodal, and it enjoyed direct access to ministers of immigration, deputy-ministers of immigration, and the prime minister.
It also had access to a powerful group of senior bureaucrats that screened certain visa applications, the civil service and the military looking for communists, fellow travellers and gays. It was called the Security Panel, and it always met behind closed doors.
In 1950 the Security Panel ranked communists as the most dangerous security threat facing Canada, and former members of the SS, the Nazi Party or Nazi collaborators were ranked lower, says Rodal.
At the same time, Jews faced widespread discrimination when applying for visas to Canada and were often rejected by screening officials as communists or communist sympathizers, perpetuating Third Reich propaganda that equated Jews with communism, says Rodal.
The Rodal report is supported by many Mennonite historians.
The Mennonite Central Committee has released reports by 12 historians with access to MCC archives. Their research was summarized in a special, 68-page special edition of the MCC newsletter "Intersections" in the fall of 2021.
To help Mennonites get out of Europe after the war, the MCC publicly and systemically downplayed the collaboration of tens of thousands of Mennonites with the Nazis, says that special edition. Many of them were coached on what to say during interviews with immigration and security officers: You are not German, but Mennonite, maybe Dutch; sorry but all my ID was lost; and most importantly you were forced to join any Nazi military unit, you never volunteered.
Mennonites had enjoyed a privileged status under German occupation, notes the Intersections special edition.
"MCC partnered with or directly hired multiple former Nazi officials and even SS agents because of the expertise they had built promoting fascist schemes to resettle Mennonites during WWII," says the publication.
One of the former Nazi officials hired by MCC to help Mennonite refugees was Heinrich Wiebe. The Nazis had installed Wiebe as mayor of the Ukrainian city of Zaporizhzhia. He implemented Nazi racial policies, confiscated the property of Jews and moved them into a ghetto. Wiebe fled to the west when the Soviet Red Army began pushing the German military out of Russian territory.
With MCC help Wiebe and his wife Olga came to Canada, and settled in Vancouver after the war.
MCC Canada director Rick Cober Bauman supported the decision to bring in independent historians to review the archives. It was not a unanimous decision, and a minority was opposed to digging into old relationships with Nazis, he said.
"All things considered I am pleased that we did it," said Bauman, who grew up in a rural part of Waterloo Region, the son of a preacher. "I think it was a good decision."
Aileen Friesen is among the new generation of scholars asking questions about what Mennonites did before, during and after the Second World War. She wrote about Wiebe in the special edition of Intersections.
She is a University of Winnipeg historian, executive director of the Plett Foundation, which researches the history of Mennonites who came from Russia in the 1870s and settled in Western Canada, and co-director of the centre for transnational Mennonite studies. For years she's researched the Holocaust, Soviet Ukraine and Mennonites.
Friesen stresses it was a complicated time for millions of desperate and traumatized people on the move after the war, with thousands trying to hide their Nazi ties.
The MCC had about 100 people on the ground in postwar Europe to sort through everything and decide who to help.
The MCC was founded in 1920 to help Mennonites get out of the newly formed Soviet Union and come to Canada. In the early 1930s Soviet leader Josef Stalin engineered a famine in Ukraine that killed millions, including many Mennonites. Some Mennonite farmers were killed by the Soviets during anti-Kulak campaign that targeted prosperous peasants in Soviet Ukraine. Then Stalin had 750,000 people executed during a bloody purge of political opponents called "the Great Terror."
"You look at the 1930s in Ukraine, just indescribable, but then you look at what happened next in the Holocaust, also indescribable," said Friesen.
The MCC helped some Mennonites and Volksdeutsche come to Canada who were otherwise ineligible to come because of their war records, she said, but it is difficult to know how many. Some of the answers may be in archives in Ukraine that are now threatened by Russian drones and missiles.
"It is heartbreaking," said Friesen. "We are finally at the stage to tell the story more fully and these archives might not be there to do it."
In 1948 the MCC helped Jacob Loitjens emigrate to Paraguay from the Netherlands. In 1950 a Dutch court sentenced Loitjens to life in prison for war crimes. In 1962, Loitjens, his wife and three children came to Canada from Paraguay with MCC help and settled in Vancouver where he taught biology at UBC. He joined the First Mennonite Church in Vancouver and in 1972 he became a Canadian citizen.
In the early 1990s he was stripped of his citizenship and deported, but not before the MCC and the Mennonite church publicly defended Loitjens and spoke against his deportation.
This is not just Mennonite history but Canadian history and everyone should care about it because many different immigrant groups are implicated in the very difficult story of the Holocaust, said Friesen in an interview from Winnipeg.
"The antisemitism in the Christian churches within the broader society still needs to be addressed, we can't ignore that element of it," said Friesen. "Because it is important to be very honest about ways in which prejudice has encouraged people to take violent actions."
After the publication of the special newsletter, MCC leadership went through training and workshops on antisemitism.
"We take a firm and clear stand against antisemitism as an organization," said Bauman. "We have ongoing anti-racism work both internationally and, in the U.S., and Canada."
In the summer of 1949, the UN's International Refugee Organization ordered a pause in the MCC's immigration work, says Rodal. American intelligence had screened some Mennonite refugees preparing to board a ship in Germany bound for the U.S. and Canada.
The Americans found many former members of the Ukrainian-SS and former members of the Nazi's mobile death squads - the Einsatzgruppen - and German citizens among the Mennonite refugees selected by the MCC, says Rodal. The UN refugee agency passed on the warning to Ottawa that many Mennonites had volunteered for the Nazi's "most reprehensible units," says Rodal.
Cornelius Klassen, who played a key role as the European director of refugee and resettlement services for the MCC from 1945-1954, communicated directly with A.L. Jolliffe, the Director of the Immigration Branch and the federal Department of Mines and Resources, to explain.
"He presented the argument the Mennonites were tricked into German citizenship by being asked to sign blank forms when they were in the camps in Germany, forms which turned out to be applications for German citizenship," says Rodal.
Jolliffe was skeptical, but there was pressure to bring more immigrants to Canada as labour shortages were holding back economic growth, says Rodal.
The International Refugee Organization came up with a compromise - allow this ship load of Mennonites to sail, and those who were now barred from the U.S. could go to either Canada or Paraguay. The names of future immigrants would be screened with name checks at the Berlin Document Centre - a huge repository of all German military records.
They were allowed to come to Canada, and about a year later in April 1950, Prime Minister Louis St. Laurent, the Security Panel and the cabinet decided to drop the blanket ban on German nationals and Volksdeutsche, says Rodal.
The change was made quietly, by orders-in-council, says Rodal. They did that knowing it was impossible to get reliable information on war records for those from Eastern Europe, says Rodal. They were allowed to come if they had relatives of friends in Canada willing to sponsor them, and if they passed the medical examination, says Rodal.
When a Toronto couple sponsored their two sons, both former members of the Waffen-SS, the Security Panel cleared them because the parents were "reliable and respectable people," and the West German government had asked Canadian Immigration officials in Germany to approve the visa requests and reunite the family in Toronto, says Rodal.
The director of immigration in Ottawa cabled the head of the Canadian Immigration Mission in Germany about the approval of visas for the two brothers - "strictly secret information."
In the spring of 1950, the British wanted Canada to take in 8,500 Ukrainians as immigrants. British intelligence claimed the men were from the regular German army - the Wehrmacht. But they were in fact Ukrainian SS troops who fled west to escape the Soviet Red Army, says Rodal.
The plan was delayed when Samuel Bronfman, head of the Canadian Jewish Congress, warned Ottawa it was about to allow a division of Waffen-SS into the country.
The story of the Ukrainian-SS division is not well known in Canada, which is why a former member received two standing ovations in Parliament. Rodal is clear in her report - the SS did not have to force people in Soviet Ukraine to take up arms against their Jewish neighbours or the Soviet Red Army.
"That members joined the division voluntarily is an established fact," says Rodal. "The response to the call for volunteers was overwhelming."
According to German records cited by Rodal, 80,000 Ukrainian men volunteered for the SS, and 50,000 were accepted. "The quota set for the unit was over-filled many times over," says the Rodal report, "and tens of thousands of volunteers had to be rejected."
By the summer of 1943, the Nazi's formed a new Ukrainian Division with 13,000 men. Many of the other volunteers went into Nazi-organized police formations.
The Germans were astonished, says Rodal, even after so many Ukrainians helped the mobile Nazi death squads - the Einsatzgruppen units that massacred 1.5 million people, mostly Jews in 1941 and 1942 in Soviet Ukraine, Eastern Europe, and the Baltics.
The Ukrainian-SS Division fought against the Red Army at the Battle of Brody in July 1943 and 10,000 Waffen-SS were killed or captured, says Rodal. The Germans had no trouble in re-filling the ranks with Nazi police formations and concentration camp guards, says Rodal.
"It is likely that at least some persons who had served with Nazi sponsored Ukrainian police/militia units used in killing actions in 1941-1942 found their way into the ranks of the division either before or after the Battle of Brody," says the Rodal report.
The Nazis called it the 14th Galician Waffen Grenadier Division. It is sometimes called the Galician SS, the Ukrainian SS, the First Galician or the 14th Grenadiers. Different names for the same unit. Ukrainian-SS members massacred people in Delatyn, Nadvirna, Lawoczne, Obertyn and Kossów.
After the Battle of Brody, the Ukrainian-SS Division headed west and surrendered to the British. Less than two weeks before the end of the war, the Ukrainian-SS was rebranded as the Ukrainian Army of Liberation, or the OUN. The British were suspicious and kept 8,500 of them in prisoner of war camps for years, first in Italy and then in the U.K.
When they were finally allowed to come to Canada, the RCMP was supposed to screen them, but the Mounties relied on the British, who didn't look too deeply into the backgrounds of the disciplined troops with no badges on their uniforms and no identity papers in their pockets.
The outrage of the Canadian Jewish Congress slowed, but did not stop the immigration of the former Ukrainian-SS members to Canada, says Rodal.
The international embarrassment following the standing ovations for Hunka in the House of Commons is not the only time members of the Ukrainian-SS have given Canada an international black eye.
There is a cenotaph in an Oakville cemetery topped with the symbol of the Ukrainian-SS, and the 200 or so grave monuments around it also have the symbol - a stylized silhouette of a lion, standing on its hind legs and surround by three crowns. It is a Nazi symbol banned in Germany.
St. Volodymyr's Ukrainian Cemetery at 1280 Dundas St. W. in Oakville is on land owned by St. Volodymyr's Ukrainian Orthodox Cathedral on Bathurst Street in Toronto.
In Edmonton there is a bust of Roman Shukhevych in front of a Ukrainian youth centre. Shukhevych was the commander of the Nachtigall Battalion, made up of 650 Ukrainian volunteers who perpetrated massacres of ethnic Poles in Galicia-Volhynia and Jews in Lviv.
In the spring of 1951, again by order-in-council, the blanket immigration ban on former SS and Waffen-SS members was relaxed and replaced with a case-by-case approach which focused on voluntary service versus forced conscription, says Rodal.
RCMP war crimes investigators found 15 members of the Ukrainian-SS had entered Canada before the immigration rules were relaxed, says Rodal.
In October 1955, the immigration restrictions put in place after the war were completely removed. Former members of the SS, the Waffen-SS and SA became admissible to Canada, says Rodal.
That decision was made by a subgroup of the Security Panel, and it did not seek the approval of the full Security Panel or the federal cabinet, says Rodal. It was treated as an administrative procedure within the discretionary authority of the responsible minister, says Rodal.
At that time the federal Minister of Immigration and Citizenship was Jack Pickersgill. As the immigration restrictions for former members of the SS were relaxed, he gave the opposite impression to a reporter.
In 1955 Pickersgill said in interview he said that German immigrants were carefully screened for communist sympathies, or membership in the Nazi Party and the SS.
"The best that can be said for this public stance is that it is untruthful," says Rodal, adding the government continued to imply that mere membership in the SS or collaboration with the enemy was an absolute bar to entering Canada.
The changes were kept quiet.
Millions of Europeans had fresh memories of SS atrocities, and Canada did not want its lax immigration policies for former SS members to become a public scandal among European allies, says Rodal.
Terry Pender is a Waterloo Region Record reporter. Reach him via email at
tpender@therecord.com

The Beaverbrae was a former German navy supply ship given to the Canadian government as war reparations. Refitted in 1947, it made 52 crossings every 28 days between Germany and Canada, from 1948 to 1954. The Canadian Christian Council for the Resettlement of Refugees used this ship to bring thousands of Mennonites and Volksdeutsche to Canada after the Second World War.Heinrich Hamm, right, office manager of the MCC refugee camp at Gronau, Germany, greets former Mennonite soldiers released from internment in Allied camps after the Second World War. During the occupation of the Soviet Ukraine, Hamm collaborated with the Nazis, and after evacuating to Stutthof, Poland, in 1944 he was an administrator at a Mennonite-owned munitions factory that used slave labour from the Stutthof concentration camp. After the war, he organized a list of all known Mennonite refugees for the MCC and led efforts to free Mennonite POWs interned in Allied camps. He immigrated to Canada in 1948.St. Volodymyr Ukrainian Cemetery in Oakville is on land owned by St. Volodymyr's Ukrainian Orthodox Cathedral on Bathurst Street in Toronto. Historian Alti Rodal wrote the background study for the Deschênes Commission on Nazi War Criminals in Canada. The 600-page document, known as The Rodal Report, details how many former members of the SS and other units of the Nazi war machine came to Canada.Helmut Oberlander in a uniform of the German Sicherheitsdienst (SD), the internal security service of the SS. Oberlander volunteered for the SS, and worked as an interpreter for a mobile death squad that was responsible for the murder of thousands of Jewish men, women and children. He was from one of the oldest Mennonite communities in what is now Ukraine.This monument in the St. Volodymyr Ukrainian Cemetery in Oakville was removed in February after Library and Archives Canada released a near-complete version of a government study on Nazi war criminals in Canada. Please don't be a Scrooge this Christmas

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/please-dont-be-scrooge-this-christmas/docview/3144285060/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Please+don%27t+be+a+Scrooge+this+Christmas&amp;title=The+Hamilton+Spectator+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2024-12-14&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Hamilton+Spectator+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: December public readings of "A Christmas Carol" are enjoying a revival. This is the famous tale about Ebenezer Scrooge, the glowering and self-absorbed Victorian merchant who steadfastly refuses charity to everyone.
We certainly have all kinds of miserly and dangerous Scrooges these days:
The richest one per cent of Canadians - according to 350.org, it would take 1,500 years for an average Canadian to match the carbon emissions that these entitled individuals emit in one year. Why aren't these people paying a lot more in tax?The oil and gas industry which, despite its enormous profits, annually receives a record $7 trillion in government subsidies on a global basis. This money is used for more fossil fuel exploration (the very last thing we need), infrastructure (e.g., more pipelines) and the decidedly unproven technology of carbon capture and storage (CCS). CCS may, at some distant point in the future, be a partial answer, but our current and very pressing emergency dictates that we quickly shed our status as a major fossil fuel exporter and become instead a clean energy superpower.The oil and gas industry deserves additional censure for its frequent and flagrant lobbying. In 2023, there was a record of 1,225 meetings held by professional fossil fuel lobbyists with the federal government. Not only that, but these lobbyists also attend international conferences on climate change in large numbers - talk about foxes guarding the henhouse!Canadian banks are also in egregious collusion with fossil fuel companies as the former continue to profit from large development loans to the latter. The banks like to offer us wall calendars with beautiful Canadian scenes adorning each month - they should instead feature earth-ravaged scenes of the Canadian tarsands.The federal Conservatives have undermined progressive national carbon pricing (which the vast majority of Canadian economists support) with their simplistic and very misleading "Axe the tax" mantra. They conveniently overlook the Bank of Canada's finding that 80 per cent of Canadians receive a greater rebate than they pay for carbon. They also fail to grasp the deep irony of their summer 2023 cancellation of an "Axe the tax" rally in Yellowknife - due to fossil-fuel fanned wildfires. I am also deeply disappointed the federal NDP has now abandoned support for carbon pricing.
So, who plays the roles of the three ghosts (our collective conscience) in our current version of this Dickens classic? This falls to you, dear reader. You must awaken yourself from your bills, the multiple political distractions that dominate the news and the busyness of modern life in order to rediscover what really matters in our lives - past, present and future.
I remember a New Yorker cartoon where a dying old man ruefully notes that "I should have bought more crap." We smile uncomfortably at this. As if.
"To be is to be in relation," notes author Parker Palmer, and this relationship is not with stuff - it's with the people we care about.
The darlings of "A Christmas Carol" are, of course, Tiny Tim and his Cratchit clan - you know, the family that values relationships above all else. For those of us who are faring better than the Cratchits, I suggest that in addition to normal gift giving to children, grandchildren, etc., you help them to appreciate the very real needs of others globally by donating to charities in their name.
Through UNICEF, for example, I have gifted a fresh water well for an African community. Through Oxfam, I have given goats to families in need on behalf of young people I know. Open their eyes to these wonderful opportunities. Open their eyes to those who are really struggling - and who desperately need our help - in the name of both climate and social justice.
"God bless us, everyone," declares Tiny Tim, to which I add a hearty "Amen."
CAPTION: A scene with Mervyn Johns (Bob Cratchit), left, and Alastair Sim (Scrooge) in the 1954 version of "A Christmas Carol." We certainly have all kinds of miserly and dangerous Scrooges these days, Grant Linney writes.
CREDIT: Grant Linney

Document 471 of 500

Minister touts credit rating: S&P Global upgrades the province's status for the first time since 2001

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/minister-touts-credit-rating/docview/3144284927/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Minister+touts+credit+rating%3A+S%26amp%3BP+Global+upgrades+the+province%27s+status+for+the+first+time+since+2001&amp;title=The+Spectator&amp;issn=11899417&amp;date=2024-12-14&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=GA.4&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Spectator&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Even in a time of economic uncertainty, you have to give credit where credit is due.
Despite the threat of a trade war with the U.S. and rising unemployment, Ontario got some very positive fiscal news this year. The province's credit rating was upgraded to "AA-" from "A+(P)" by S&P Global last week, the first such improvement from that agency since 2001.
That followed a credit upgrade in June to "AA" from "AA (low) (P)" by Morningstar DBRS, which had last boosted Ontario's ranking in 2006.
For Finance Minister Peter Bethlenfalvy that means much more than just an alphabet soup of ratings decipherable only to those on Bay Street and Wall Street.
"Bond holders are looking at who is managing their credit most effectively. It leads to lower interest costs," Bethlenfalvy said in an interview with the Star this week.
"Ultimately, what that means is that your borrowing costs ... are lower," said the treasurer.
According to the most recent public accounts, Ontario's interest expenditure dropped from $12.4 billion in 2022-23 to $11.4 billion last fiscal year - a savings of $1 billion.
"That's $1 billion you have to pay down debt, you can invest in health care, you've got options for your priorities," he said.
"When you have to borrow in the bond markets - which we continue to do, primarily for infrastructure because we've got an ambitious plan - investors have choices," said Bethlenfalvy, referring to the billions the province is borrowing to fund subways, highways, hospitals and other projects.
"So how do you stand out amongst all those that are issuing bonds and get the lowest rate? They look at your fiscal track record, they look at your credit ratings, they look at a host of factors. This is a vote of confidence in Ontario."
That's especially important to Bethlenfalvy, who was co-president of DBRS when the rating agency downgraded Ontario to "AA (low)" from "AA" in 2009 after the global financial crash.
"I was there, of course, and now, whoops, the shoe is on the other foot," the treasurer mused.
"Having worked in the capital markets and at a credit-rating agency - working both in New York and Toronto - I've seen capital markets, I've seen sovereigns and how they manage their debt. And I think it still matters," he said.
"It matters because you don't know what's around the corner, whether it's a pandemic or ... a financial crisis (like) 2008. We're dealing with economic uncertainty all the time so I think it's wise to be prudent on the fiscal side and to have targets - and that's what rating agencies like."
Credit raters also like fiscal capacity so Premier Doug Ford's frequent boast that he has "never raised a tax" is not unhelpful because it suggests the province has untapped revenue streams if need be.
Bethlenfalvy, who has no plans to raise any taxes, counters that by noting rating agencies "like it when you grow your economy, because that increases your tax revenues."
He emphasized that S&P upgraded Ontario's rating after he announced the province would spend $3 billion to send $200 "rebate" cheques to 15 million Ontarians in the new year despite running a $6.6-billion budget deficit.
"If I can point out, under the previous government - 15 years - taxes were raised and credit ratings fell," said Bethlenfalvy, referring to the Liberals of premiers Dalton McGuinty and Kathleen Wynne.
CAPTION:

CREDIT: Robert Benzie Queen's Park Bureau Chief

Subject: Uncertainty; Rating services; Borrowing; Ratings & rankings; Cost control; Credit ratings; Capital markets

Document 472 of 500

Don't be a Scrooge this Christmas

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/dont-be-scrooge-this-christmas/docview/3144284911/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=unknown&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Don%27t+be+a+Scrooge+this+Christmas&amp;title=The+Spectator&amp;issn=11899417&amp;date=2024-12-14&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.16&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Spectator&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: December public readings of "A Christmas Carol" are enjoying a revival. This is the famous tale about Ebenezer Scrooge, the glowering and self-absorbed Victorian merchant who steadfastly refuses charity to everyone.
We certainly have all kinds of miserly and dangerous Scrooges these days:
The richest one per cent of Canadians - according to 350.org, it would take 1,500 years for an average Canadian to match the carbon emissions that these entitled individuals emit in one year. Why aren't these people paying a lot more in tax?
The oil and gas industry which, despite its enormous profits, annually receives a record $7 trillion in government subsidies on a global basis. This money is used for more fossil fuel exploration (the very last thing we need), infrastructure (e.g., more pipelines) and the decidedly unproven technology of carbon capture and storage (CCS). CCS may, at some distant point in the future, be a partial answer, but our current and very pressing emergency dictates that we quickly shed our status as a major fossil fuel exporter and become instead a clean energy superpower.
The oil and gas industry deserves additional censure for its frequent and flagrant lobbying. In 2023, there was a record of 1,225 meetings held by professional fossil fuel lobbyists with the federal government. Not only that, but these lobbyists also attend international conferences on climate change in large numbers - talk about foxes guarding the henhouse!
Canadian banks are also in egregious collusion with fossil fuel companies as the former continue to profit from large development loans to the latter. The banks like to offer us wall calendars with beautiful Canadian scenes adorning each month - they should instead feature earth-ravaged scenes of the Canadian tarsands.
The federal Conservatives have undermined progressive national carbon pricing (which the vast majority of Canadian economists support) with their simplistic and very misleading "Axe the tax" mantra. They conveniently overlook the Bank of Canada's finding that 80 per cent of Canadians receive a greater rebate than they pay for carbon. They also fail to grasp the deep irony of their summer 2023 cancellation of an "Axe the tax" rally in Yellowknife - due to fossil-fuel fanned wildfires. I am also deeply disappointed the federal NDP has now abandoned support for carbon pricing.
So, who plays the roles of the three ghosts (our collective conscience) in our current version of this Dickens classic? This falls to you, dear reader. You must awaken yourself from your bills, the multiple political distractions that dominate the news and the busyness of modern life in order to rediscover what really matters in our lives - past, present and future.
I remember a New Yorker cartoon where a dying old man ruefully notes that "I should have bought more crap." We smile uncomfortably at this. As if.
"To be is to be in relation," notes author Parker Palmer, and this relationship is not with stuff - it's with the people we care about.
The darlings of "A Christmas Carol" are, of course, Tiny Tim and his Cratchit clan - you know, the family that values relationships above all else. For those of us who are faring better than the Cratchits, I suggest that in addition to normal gift giving to children, grandchildren, etc., you help them to appreciate the very real needs of others globally by donating to charities in their name.
Through UNICEF, for example, I have gifted a fresh water well for an African community. Through Oxfam, I have given goats to families in need on behalf of young people I know. Open their eyes to these wonderful opportunities. Open their eyes to those who are really struggling - and who desperately need our help - in the name of both climate and social justice.
"God bless us, everyone," declares Tiny Tim, to which I add a hearty "Amen."
Grant Linney lives in Dundas.
He will receive a King Charles III Coronation Medal next month for his climate activism.
CAPTION:

'A start' but not nearly enough, Hamilton says of Ford's $75.5M boost to tackle homelessness

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/start-not-nearly-enough-hamilton-says-fords-75-5m/docview/3143997793/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=%27A+start%27+but+not+nearly+enough%2C+Hamilton+says+of+Ford%27s+%2475.5M+boost+to+tackle+homelessness&amp;title=The+Hamilton+Spectator+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2024-12-13&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Hamilton+Spectator+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Hamilton city officials say Premier Doug Ford's new plan to put an additional $75.5 million toward affordable housing and shelter spaces falls well short of what's needed to tackle Ontario's deepening homelessness crisis.
"This is a start, but it's certainly not going to have the kind of impact that we need because the resources are not robust enough to really solve the crisis across the entire province," Mayor Andrea Horwath said Thursday.
"The city's putting in a whole lot of money when it comes to homelessness and when it comes to housing, and we need to see our partners step up to help us bear that cost."
Likewise, Coun. Cameron Kroetsch said the additional funding Ford announced Thursday amounts to a "drop in the bucket" compared to the scale of the problem in Hamilton alone. "It's not going to do anything."
Coun. John-Paul Danko said while any extra dollars are welcome, the provincial measures come "maybe too little too late" as the city continues to pour more money into the pressing file than upper levels of government.
Ford announced the additional $50 million for affordable housing, $20 million for shelters and $5.5 million for rental supplements Thursday as his Progressive Conservatives introduced proposed legislation that aims to enshrine more powers for police and municipalities to deal with trespassing and public drug use.
"Enough is enough. This has to stop and it will stop. Families deserve to play in and enjoy their parks without fearing for the safety of their kids," he told reporters at Queen's Park with a clutch of mayors from Ontario municipalities who have called for more action on encampments standing behind him.
"These encampments are taking over public spaces with illegal drug use happening out in the open, creating huge safety risks for people and communities," Ford said.
The $75.5 million is in addition to the $700 million the province is spending on homelessness prevention and the $378 million for 19 "homelessness and addiction recovery treatment" (HART) hubs that the government aims to open as it closes 10 supervised drug-use sites by March.
As part of Ford's plan, municipalities will have to keep track of how many homeless people are moved from encampments into new accommodations and report that to the province to justify how the funds are being spent.
"We want to help people, we don't want to punish people," the premier said in announcing the amendments to the Trespass to Property Act.
Under the trespass changes aimed at encampment dwellers, there would be a new offence of "continuous trespass" that judges could consider as an "aggravating factor" along with gauging the "likelihood to reoffend," said Attorney General Doug Downey.
"That will give greater tools to the judiciary and for others enforcing the law."
The new laws also focus on drug use in parks, empowering police and provincial offences officers to order people in encampments to stop using illegal drugs or face fines up to $10,000, arrest or a maximum of six months in prison.
But stepped-up enforcement doesn't "address in any meaningful way" that "there's nowhere for people to go," Brad Evoy, executive director of the Disability Justice Network of Ontario, told The Spectator.
"Ultimately, this just leads to people being more likely to be criminalized and incarcerated."
The focus on drug use and trespassing seem "very reactionary," said Andrew Matthews, a manager with St. Matthew's House, a social-service agency in Hamilton that provides services for people who live outside. "I just don't think it will actually be effective."
The Ford government's response to growing municipal frustration over encampments comes as Hamilton grapples with an estimated 300 people living outside, a subset of the roughly 1,600 the city says are homeless overall.
City officials have regularly pointed to how Hamilton is outspending upper orders of government on housing and homelessness.
The city expects 2024's spending on the massive files across various divisions - and including in-year council approvals - to be $186 million, which is $39 million more than in 2023. Of that, $41 million is from the provincial government and $20 million from Ottawa, with the city covering about $125 million - or 67 per cent.
As stopgaps, while it angles for more funding for affordable and supportive housing, the city and its agency partners are creating an additional 192 temporary shelters beds and situating 40 micro shelters for up to 80 people on municipal land off Barton Street West this winter.
Meanwhile, the city is preparing to respond to a human-rights-based legal challenge over how the municipality has approached people living outside. Hearings for that case are scheduled to start Monday.
The litigation has influenced how the city responds to issues with encampments, Danko said.
"We are under a microscope in the City of Hamilton, in particular, because every municipality is following along with this legal challenge."
Danko plans to introduce a motion next month that calls on staff to stick with the provisions of the city's encampment protocol, which stipulates spatial restrictions and other rules for tents in public spaces.
Staff are doing their best with the resources they have, but residents are increasingly irked by such issues as debris left at abandoned encampment sites, he said.
"It's really, really frustrating for residents that they don't see action from the municipality."
-With files from the Toronto Star
Teviah Moro is a reporter at The Hamilton Spectator. tmoro@thespec.com

That's a portion of the roughly 1,600 the city says don't have housing.

Document 474 of 500

Bending the rules: How Canada opened its doors to Nazi war criminals

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/bending-rules-how-canada-opened-doors-nazi-war/docview/3143996601/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Bending+the+rules%3A+How+Canada+opened+its+doors+to+Nazi+war+criminals&amp;title=The+Hamilton+Spectator+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2024-12-13&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Hamilton+Spectator+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: An unknown number of Nazi war criminals and collaborators lived out their lives in peace in Canada, in places such as the Mennonite farms and villages of Waterloo Region.
They were among thousands of ethnic Germans brought into Canada after the Second World War - many of them members of the most reprehensible units of the Nazi war machine - by a group called the Canadian Christian Council for the Resettlement of Refugees (CCCRR), which included the Mennonite Central Committee (MCC).
How this happened is detailed in a Government of Canada report, "Nazi War Criminals in Canada: The Historical And Policy Setting From the 1940s to the Present." Known as The Rodal Report, it is a 600-page study prepared by Oxford-trained historian Alti Rodal for the Deschênes Commission on Nazi War Criminals in Canada, which released its findings in 1986.
For decades, Ottawa refused to release the entirety of the Rodal report. The Waterloo Region Record filed an Access to Information request for a complete copy six years ago.
The Deschênes Commission said there were 29 cases of suspected Nazi war criminals in Canada that warranted special consideration because of the seriousness of the allegations and the availability of evidence against the men.
Among those priority cases was Waterloo resident Helmut Oberlander. The Record filed an Access to information Act request for the files on the other 28 cases, but Library and Archives Canada has refused to release any of the documents.
Before he died in September 2021 Oberlander was front page news in Waterloo Region, fighting a deportation order on and off since 1995. That's when Ottawa announced it was going to strip Oberlander of his citizenship and deport him for failing to disclose his voluntary service with Einsatzgruppe D, one of the Nazi's mobile death squads that operated in Eastern Europe, Soviet Ukraine and the Caucuses.
Oberlander and his wife came to Canada in May 1954, sponsored by a German relative in Waterloo. He was 'Volksdeutsche' - a 'racial' term coined by the Nazis for ethnic Germans who didn't hold German citizenship - from one of the oldest Mennonite communities in southern Russia. Oberlander was not brought to Waterloo Region by the MCC, but others were.
The Rodal report makes it clear there were many more - in fact it was easier for Nazi war criminals to come to Canada after the Second World War than Jews who wanted to rebuild their lives in Canada following the Holocaust.
Refugee aid groups and immigration officials preferred Germans and Baltic people, deemed to be highly attractive and desirable immigrants, "who were likely to have included - and indeed have been shown to have included in their midst persons who were Nazi collaborators and alleged war criminals," says Rodal.
During 1947-1949, the MCC and the CCCRR helped bring 6,005 Mennonites to Canada, and 15,000 Volksdeutsche - primarily from the Western and Southwestern areas of the former Soviet Union that became Ukraine in 1991.
About 807 of them settled in Ontario, primarily Kitchener-Waterloo, St. Catharines and Leamington. Many who first settled in other parts of Canada later moved to this region, say Mennonite historians.
Some Mennonites who were former SS and Nazi collaborators came to this region, but not many, said Troy Osborne, a history professor and dean of Mennonite studies at Conrad Grebel University in Waterloo.
"I don't think there were a lot in this area," said Osborne.
But researchers are reluctant to dig into this chapter of Mennonite history in the region.
"Part of the reason I think it is frosted over around here, you are talking about people's grandparents, it is not just about the MCC, it is about people's own family histories too," said Osborne. "That's what I've discovered."
Library and Archives Canada released the most complete version of the Rodal report in February 2024 following an international scandal six months earlier. The House of Commons gave two standing ovations to a former member of the Ukrainian-SS in September 2023.
That's when Ukraine president Volodymyr Zelenskyy was a guest of honour in Ottawa and spoke in Parliament to thank Canada for help in the war against Russia.
In the audience was 98-year-old Yaroslav Hunka, a former member of the Ukrainian SS - part of the Waffen-SS largely made up of non-German volunteers.
Federal politicians and their guests gave Hunka two standing ovations after he was introduced as a true hero of Ukraine by the Speaker of the House, Anthony Rota, a Liberal MP from North Bay.
When Hunka's background became public, Prime Minister Trudeau apologized, and Rota resigned as Speaker of the House. Immigration Minister Marc Miller acknowledged Canada has a dark history when it comes to Nazis, and said requests to release more classified information about it would be revisited.
In February 2024, Library and Archives Canada released almost everything from the Rodal Report, minus one page and a handful of redactions.
The report lays out in detail a seeming indifference to war crimes by officials, openly discriminatory immigration policies, antisemitism, and security screening that was lax to non-existent.
"It would be rash to assume that significant numbers of war criminals and Nazi collaborators did not enter Canada," Rodal says in her report.
The original SS were early, enthusiastic supporters of Adolf Hitler, pledging loyalty to him alone. Later, the Nazis easily found enough volunteers in the Baltics, Eastern Europe, the Balkans and Soviet Ukraine to form divisions called the Waffen-SS.
The SS and Waffen-SS ran the concentration camps and death camps. They were largely responsible for carrying out the Holocaust.
The Nuremberg War Crimes Tribunal ruled the SS as a whole, including Waffen-SS divisions, a criminal organization. From 1945-1950 they were officially banned from coming to Canada, along with members of the SD (the brutal SS intelligence agency that operated in tandem with the SS), the Brown Shirts, or Storm Troopers, known as the SA, and members of the Einsatzgruppen mobile death squads that killed 1.5 million people, mostly Jews, in 1941-1943.
After the war, the Allies suspected many men from Eastern Europe and Soviet Ukraine had volunteered for some of the Nazi's worst units, says Rodal. Among that group were the Volksdeutsche living in Eastern Europe and Soviet Ukraine.
By 1950 Canada had increased its immigration quotas and was relaxing its policies on the banned groups and favouring 'desirable' ethnic German immigrants.
Rodal ties the CCCRR - an umbrella organization that represented Baptist, Lutheran, Mennonite and Catholic church groups mainly of German origin - with the changes in government policy.
The group lobbied Ottawa tirelessly to drop the immigration bans on former SS members, and senior bureaucrats did, without telling House of Commons or the Canadian public, says Rodal, setting the stage for Germany to become the biggest source of immigrants to Canada in the 1950s.
The church group "is closely linked to changes in government policy with regard to admitting enemy aliens and former members of the Nazi party and the Waffen SS, and other Nazi organizations," says Rodal.
In fact, Helmut Rauca, a former member of Einsatzgruppe A, one of the Nazis' mobile death squads responsible for killing thousands of Jews in Latvia, came to Canada in the late 1940s as a CCCRR sponsored refugee, under his own name, settling in Toronto.
West Germany issued an arrest warrant for Rauca in 1963, and Canada deported him in 1983 to face charges of crimes against humanity. He died in a West German jail awaiting trial.
By an order-in-council, Ottawa provided funds to the CCCRR, says Rodal, and it enjoyed direct access to ministers of immigration, deputy-ministers of immigration, and the prime minister.
It also had access to a powerful group of senior bureaucrats that screened certain visa applications, the civil service and the military looking for communists, fellow travellers and gays. It was called the Security Panel, and it always met behind closed doors.
In 1950 the Security Panel ranked communists as the most dangerous security threat facing Canada, and former members of the SS, the Nazi Party or Nazi collaborators were ranked lower, says Rodal.
At the same time, Jews faced widespread discrimination when applying for visas to Canada and were often rejected by screening officials as communists or communist sympathizers, perpetuating Third Reich propaganda that equated Jews with communism, says Rodal.
The Rodal report is supported by many Mennonite historians.
The Mennonite Central Committee has released reports by 12 historians with access to MCC archives. Their research was summarized in a special, 68-page special edition of the MCC newsletter "Intersections" in the fall of 2021.
To help Mennonites get out of Europe after the war, the MCC publicly and systemically downplayed the collaboration of tens of thousands of Mennonites with the Nazis, says that special edition. Many of them were coached on what to say during interviews with immigration and security officers: You are not German, but Mennonite, maybe Dutch; sorry but all my ID was lost; and most importantly you were forced to join any Nazi military unit, you never volunteered.
Mennonites had enjoyed a privileged status under German occupation, notes the Intersections special edition.
"MCC partnered with or directly hired multiple former Nazi officials and even SS agents because of the expertise they had built promoting fascist schemes to resettle Mennonites during WWII," says the publication.
One of the former Nazi officials hired by MCC to help Mennonite refugees was Heinrich Wiebe. The Nazis had installed Wiebe as mayor of the Ukrainian city of Zaporizhzhia. He implemented Nazi racial policies, confiscated the property of Jews and moved them into a ghetto. Wiebe fled to the west when the Soviet Red Army began pushing the German military out of Russian territory.
With MCC help Wiebe and his wife Olga came to Canada, and settled in Vancouver after the war.
MCC Canada director Rick Cober Bauman supported the decision to bring in independent historians to review the archives. It was not a unanimous decision, and a minority was opposed to digging into old relationships with Nazis, he said.
"All things considered I am pleased that we did it," said Bauman, who grew up in a rural part of Waterloo Region, the son of a preacher. "I think it was a good decision."
Aileen Friesen is among the new generation of scholars asking questions about what Mennonites did before, during and after the Second World War. She wrote about Wiebe in the special edition of Intersections.
She is a University of Winnipeg historian, executive director of the Plett Foundation, which researches the history of Mennonites who came from Russia in the 1870s and settled in Western Canada, and co-director of the centre for transnational Mennonite studies. For years she's researched the Holocaust, Soviet Ukraine and Mennonites.
Friesen stresses it was a complicated time for millions of desperate and traumatized people on the move after the war, with thousands trying to hide their Nazi ties.
The MCC had about 100 people on the ground in postwar Europe to sort through everything and decide who to help.
The MCC was founded in 1920 to help Mennonites get out of the newly formed Soviet Union and come to Canada. In the early 1930s Soviet leader Josef Stalin engineered a famine in Ukraine that killed millions, including many Mennonites. Some Mennonite farmers were killed by the Soviets during anti-Kulak campaign that targeted prosperous peasants in Soviet Ukraine. Then Stalin had 750,000 people executed during a bloody purge of political opponents called "the Great Terror."
"You look at the 1930s in Ukraine, just indescribable, but then you look at what happened next in the Holocaust, also indescribable," said Friesen.
The MCC helped some Mennonites and Volksdeutsche come to Canada who were otherwise ineligible to come because of their war records, she said, but it is difficult to know how many. Some of the answers may be in archives in Ukraine that are now threatened by Russian drones and missiles.
"It is heartbreaking," said Friesen. "We are finally at the stage to tell the story more fully and these archives might not be there to do it."
In 1948 the MCC helped Jacob Loitjens emigrate to Paraguay from the Netherlands. In 1950 a Dutch court sentenced Loitjens to life in prison for war crimes. In 1962, Loitjens, his wife and three children came to Canada from Paraguay with MCC help and settled in Vancouver where he taught biology at UBC. He joined the First Mennonite Church in Vancouver and in 1972 he became a Canadian citizen.
In the early 1990s he was stripped of his citizenship and deported, but not before the MCC and the Mennonite church publicly defended Loitjens and spoke against his deportation.
This is not just Mennonite history but Canadian history and everyone should care about it because many different immigrant groups are implicated in the very difficult story of the Holocaust, said Friesen in an interview from Winnipeg.
"The antisemitism in the Christian churches within the broader society still needs to be addressed, we can't ignore that element of it," said Friesen. "Because it is important to be very honest about ways in which prejudice has encouraged people to take violent actions."
After the publication of the special newsletter, MCC leadership went through training and workshops on antisemitism.
"We take a firm and clear stand against antisemitism as an organization," said Bauman. "We have ongoing anti-racism work both internationally and, in the U.S., and Canada."
In the summer of 1949, the UN's International Refugee Organization ordered a pause in the MCC's immigration work, says Rodal. American intelligence had screened some Mennonite refugees preparing to board a ship in Germany bound for the U.S. and Canada.
The Americans found many former members of the Ukrainian-SS and former members of the Nazi's mobile death squads - the Einsatzgruppen - and German citizens among the Mennonite refugees selected by the MCC, says Rodal. The UN refugee agency passed on the warning to Ottawa that many Mennonites had volunteered for the Nazi's "most reprehensible units," says Rodal.
Cornelius Klassen, who played a key role as the European director of refugee and resettlement services for the MCC from 1945-1954, communicated directly with A.L. Jolliffe, the Director of the Immigration Branch and the federal Department of Mines and Resources, to explain.
"He presented the argument the Mennonites were tricked into German citizenship by being asked to sign blank forms when they were in the camps in Germany, forms which turned out to be applications for German citizenship," says Rodal.
Jolliffe was skeptical, but there was pressure to bring more immigrants to Canada as labour shortages were holding back economic growth, says Rodal.
The International Refugee Organization came up with a compromise - allow this ship load of Mennonites to sail, and those who were now barred from the U.S. could go to either Canada or Paraguay. The names of future immigrants would be screened with name checks at the Berlin Document Centre - a huge repository of all German military records.
They were allowed to come to Canada, and about a year later in April 1950, Prime Minister Louis St. Laurent, the Security Panel and the cabinet decided to drop the blanket ban on German nationals and Volksdeutsche, says Rodal.
The change was made quietly, by orders-in-council, says Rodal. They did that knowing it was impossible to get reliable information on war records for those from Eastern Europe, says Rodal. They were allowed to come if they had relatives of friends in Canada willing to sponsor them, and if they passed the medical examination, says Rodal.
When a Toronto couple sponsored their two sons, both former members of the Waffen-SS, the Security Panel cleared them because the parents were "reliable and respectable people," and the West German government had asked Canadian Immigration officials in Germany to approve the visa requests and reunite the family in Toronto, says Rodal.
The director of immigration in Ottawa cabled the head of the Canadian Immigration Mission in Germany about the approval of visas for the two brothers - "strictly secret information."
In the spring of 1950, the British wanted Canada to take in 8,500 Ukrainians as immigrants. British intelligence claimed the men were from the regular German army - the Wehrmacht. But they were in fact Ukrainian SS troops who fled west to escape the Soviet Red Army, says Rodal.
The plan was delayed when Samuel Bronfman, head of the Canadian Jewish Congress, warned Ottawa it was about to allow a division of Waffen-SS into the country.
The story of the Ukrainian-SS division is not well known in Canada, which is why a former member received two standing ovations in Parliament. Rodal is clear in her report - the SS did not have to force people in Soviet Ukraine to take up arms against their Jewish neighbours or the Soviet Red Army.
"That members joined the division voluntarily is an established fact," says Rodal. "The response to the call for volunteers was overwhelming."
According to German records cited by Rodal, 80,000 Ukrainian men volunteered for the SS, and 50,000 were accepted. "The quota set for the unit was over-filled many times over," says the Rodal report, "and tens of thousands of volunteers had to be rejected."
By the summer of 1943, the Nazi's formed a new Ukrainian Division with 13,000 men. Many of the other volunteers went into Nazi-organized police formations.
The Germans were astonished, says Rodal, even after so many Ukrainians helped the mobile Nazi death squads - the Einsatzgruppen units that massacred 1.5 million people, mostly Jews in 1941 and 1942 in Soviet Ukraine, Eastern Europe, and the Baltics.
The Ukrainian-SS Division fought against the Red Army at the Battle of Brody in July 1943 and 10,000 Waffen-SS were killed or captured, says Rodal. The Germans had no trouble in re-filling the ranks with Nazi police formations and concentration camp guards, says Rodal.
"It is likely that at least some persons who had served with Nazi sponsored Ukrainian police/militia units used in killing actions in 1941-1942 found their way into the ranks of the division either before or after the Battle of Brody," says the Rodal report.
The Nazis called it the 14th Galician Waffen Grenadier Division. It is sometimes called the Galician SS, the Ukrainian SS, the First Galician or the 14th Grenadiers. Different names for the same unit. Ukrainian-SS members massacred people in Delatyn, Nadvirna, Lawoczne, Obertyn and Kossów.
After the Battle of Brody, the Ukrainian-SS Division headed west and surrendered to the British. Less than two weeks before the end of the war, the Ukrainian-SS was rebranded as the Ukrainian Army of Liberation, or the OUN. The British were suspicious and kept 8,500 of them in prisoner of war camps for years, first in Italy and then in the U.K.
When they were finally allowed to come to Canada, the RCMP was supposed to screen them, but the Mounties relied on the British, who didn't look too deeply into the backgrounds of the disciplined troops with no badges on their uniforms and no identity papers in their pockets.
The outrage of the Canadian Jewish Congress slowed, but did not stop the immigration of the former Ukrainian-SS members to Canada, says Rodal.
The international embarrassment following the standing ovations for Hunka in the House of Commons is not the only time members of the Ukrainian-SS have given Canada an international black eye.
There is a cenotaph in an Oakville cemetery topped with the symbol of the Ukrainian-SS, and the 200 or so grave monuments around it also have the symbol - a stylized silhouette of a lion, standing on its hind legs and surround by three crowns. It is a Nazi symbol banned in Germany.
St. Volodymyr's Ukrainian Cemetery at 1280 Dundas St. W. in Oakville is on land owned by St. Volodymyr's Ukrainian Orthodox Cathedral on Bathurst Street in Toronto.
In Edmonton there is a bust of Roman Shukhevych in front of a Ukrainian youth centre. Shukhevych was the commander of the Nachtigall Battalion, made up of 650 Ukrainian volunteers who perpetrated massacres of ethnic Poles in Galicia-Volhynia and Jews in Lviv.
In the spring of 1951, again by order-in-council, the blanket immigration ban on former SS and Waffen-SS members was relaxed and replaced with a case-by-case approach which focused on voluntary service versus forced conscription, says Rodal.
RCMP war crimes investigators found 15 members of the Ukrainian-SS had entered Canada before the immigration rules were relaxed, says Rodal.
In October 1955, the immigration restrictions put in place after the war were completely removed. Former members of the SS, the Waffen-SS and SA became admissible to Canada, says Rodal.
That decision was made by a subgroup of the Security Panel, and it did not seek the approval of the full Security Panel or the federal cabinet, says Rodal. It was treated as an administrative procedure within the discretionary authority of the responsible minister, says Rodal.
At that time the federal Minister of Immigration and Citizenship was Jack Pickersgill. As the immigration restrictions for former members of the SS were relaxed, he gave the opposite impression to a reporter.
In 1955 Pickersgill said in interview he said that German immigrants were carefully screened for communist sympathies, or membership in the Nazi Party and the SS.
"The best that can be said for this public stance is that it is untruthful," says Rodal, adding the government continued to imply that mere membership in the SS or collaboration with the enemy was an absolute bar to entering Canada.
The changes were kept quiet.
Millions of Europeans had fresh memories of SS atrocities, and Canada did not want its lax immigration policies for former SS members to become a public scandal among European allies, says Rodal.
Terry Pender is a Waterloo Region Record reporter. Reach him via: tpender@therecord.com
CAPTION: Helmut Oberlander in a uniform of the German Sicherheitsdienst (SD), the internal security service of the SS. Oberlander volunteered for the SS, and worked as an interpreter for a mobile death squad that was responsible for the murder of thousands of Jewish men, women and children. He was from one of the oldest Mennonite communities in what is now Ukraine.Historian Alti Rodal wrote the background study for the Deschenes Commission on Nazi War Criminals in Canada. The 600-page document, known as The Rodal Report, details how many former members of the SS and other units of the Nazi war machine came to Canada.Heinrich Hamm (right), office manager of the MCC refugee camp at Gronau, Germany, greets former Mennonite soldiers released from internment in Allied camps after the Second World War. During the occupation of the Soviet Ukraine,Hamm collaborated with the Nazis, and after evacuating to Stutthof, Poland in 1944 he was an administrator at a Mennonite-owned munitions factory that used slave labor from the Stutthof concentration camp. After the war, he organized a list of all known Mennonite refugees for the MCC and directed efforts to free Mennonite POWs interned in Allied camps. He immigrated to Canada in 1948.Rodal page 212Rodal page 213This monument in the St. Volodymyr Ukrainian Cemetery in Oakville was removed in February 2024 after Library and Archives Canada released a near-complete version of a government study on Nazi war criminals in Canada. The symbol at the top of the monument, a lion surrounded by three crowns, was the official emblem of the Ukrainian SS.Where a war monument was removed at St. Volodymyr Ukrainian Cemetery in Oakville.A war monument at St. Volodymyr Ukrainian Cemetery in Oakville.Immigrants crowd the deck of the S.S. Beaverbrae, as the moment comes to disembark at the port of Quebec. The Beaverbrae was a former German navy supply ship given to the Canadian government as war reparations. Re-fitted in 1947, it made 52 crossings every 28 days between Germany and Canada from 1948 to 1954. The Canadian Christian Council for the Resettlement of Refugees used this ship to bring thousands of Mennonites and Volksdeutsche to Canada after the war.

Document 475 of 500

Dec. 13: LRT will make matters worse downtown, RBG does a wonderful job and other letters to the editor

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/dec-13-lrt-will-make-matters-worse-downtown-rbg/docview/3143996212/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Dec.+13%3A+LRT+will+make+matters+worse+downtown%2C+RBG+does+a+wonderful+job+and+other+letters+to+the+editor&amp;title=The+Hamilton+Spectator+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2024-12-13&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Hamilton+Spectator+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Downtown still to face LRT
Re: Hamilton council backs plan for 10-year strategy to tackle downtown problems, Dec. 6
This article makes it very clear that Hamilton's downtown has fallen from its heyday, but the optimism for return to those days is unfounded. The article points out 18.2 per cent vacancy in office space and 29 per cent vacancy in storefronts in two core business improvement areas. Why would anyone want to invest in occupying space in the downtown when council is so set on shutting off the area for four years while the white elephant known as the LRT tears up the roads and sidewalks?
If they think the downtown has been devastated by what is going on now, just wait. The old saying "you ain't seen nothing yet" couldn't be more true.
Dennis Martin, Binbrook
RBG does a wonderful job
Re: Royal Botanical Gardens will transform into a winter wonderland starting this week, Dec. 2
Looking for a little magic this holiday season without leaving town? Find it at the Royal Botanical Gardens' "Winter Wonders."
The beautiful Hendrie Park and all its pathways have been adorned with a spectacular display of festive lights, synchronized music, cosy fire pit seating and charming, scenic domes. Take a family photo on your stroll framed by a favourite snowman. Hug a tree and watch it glow! Warm up with hot coffee or cocoa. Inside, enjoy Christmas favourites by the Saphire Quartet, a two-storey model railway and the lovely Mediterranean Garden, dressed to impress.
Bravo to the RBG team! You don't have to travel to New York's Rockefeller Centre, or London's Hyde Park, for an enchanting evening. But you may think you did!
Robin McGinlay, Dundas
We don't need to take rights away
Re: Ford on the right track, Dec. 7
To say I almost choked on my morning tea when reading the Waterdown resident's letter would be an understatement. Calling Ford's move to override the Charter a smart move and basically the only move is beyond ridiculous - it is downright false.
The notwithstanding clause should be used in only the most extreme circumstances and this is not one of them. It would never cross Ford's mind to use the notwithstanding clause to build homes for the homeless, many of whom are homeless as a direct result of his government's policies - namely, removing rent controls on buildings constructed after 2018 and making it easier for wealthy landowners to use renovictions to increase rents on units built before 2018.
In another time, known as the Great Depression, here in Canada the homeless were also criminalized and actually put in camps and paid two dollars a day for their labour. Many of them, as history records, went on to do Canada proud in fighting against the Nazis. Here we are repeating the same mistakes.
The only thing that stops us, and particularly the Ford government, from solving the homeless crisis is the will to do it. Numerous reports have been written on how to address the homeless problem. Experts knowledgeable in the field have offered politicians the way forward to solving the drug dependency crisis. Ford, as is usual for him, does not want to be confused by facts.
Edward Seymour, Hamilton
Liberals need to return to the centre
The irony is that Donald Trump, a man born into wealth, is now the voice of so many in the world who are not privileged like him, but who believe he shares their views regarding immigration, huge deficits, separating us into victim groups, inflation, lock downs, parental rights, denigrating our country's history, etc. Enough Canadians supported moderation on these issues to keep the Liberal party in power for much of the last 100 years.
Recently, the left in many countries moved so far to radicalism that they have abandoned centrist voters. Electing leaders like Trump is occurring all over the democratic world.
Small "l" liberals must go back to supporting their previous principles of fiscal responsibility and social cohesion. They must ditch being beholden to the most "progressive" element of their followers to regain control of the centre of the political spectrum and again speak to voters like me.
Reg McGuire, Mount Hope
Wealthy will flee tax increases
I believe many wealthy individuals have left Canada to live in the U.K., U.S., Mexico and elsewhere abroad. These expats were fed up with ridiculously high taxes. As if it's their sole responsibility to fund health care, education, housing initiatives, Ontario Works, ODSP programs, immigrant settlement, etc.
There's been discussions about taxing the wealthy even more through excess profits taxes and a wealth tax. Be careful about taxing wealthy Canadians too much - if they continue leaving, there will be no one left to shoulder the burden of funding social programs.
Blanche Baldwin, Hamilton
Strike will bring change to Canada Post
Canada Post reported it delivered three billion fewer letters and one million fewer parcels in 2023 than it did in 2006. It also reported a loss of $748 million in 2023, which is likely to worsen after the current strike because many folks will stick with the easier and more reliable methods that they have being forced to find, thereby further reducing the corporation's business. Canadian taxpayers can reasonably ask if Canada Post operations have become a financial liability that is no longer fit-for-purpose.
The apportionment of management-versus-union blame does not negate the fact that in order to have any semblance of sustainability, significant changes to Canada Post mandate and operations must be made. Perhaps it is time for a reduction in operations to become alternating weeks of once-weekly followed by twice-weekly deliveries. This would reduce the overhead costs associated with Canada Post's reduced business.
Although the reduced number of staff required would improve the corporation's financial ability to meet the pay expectations of the postal union, the potential impact on our highly regarded neighbourhood letter carrier would be most unfortunate.
Fred Leech, Dundas
CAPTION: Residential development has taken a nosedive due to a market downturn and office and storefront vacancies are an issue. Matters will only worsen when LRT construction commences, a reader warns.

Robert Panchyson,
Burlington
