Document 477 of 500

'A start' but not nearly enough, city says of Ford's $75.5M boost to tackle homelessness

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/start-not-nearly-enough-city-says-fords-75-5m/docview/3143664489/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=%27A+start%27+but+not+nearly+enough%2C+city+says+of+Ford%27s+%2475.5M+boost+to+tackle+homelessness&amp;title=The+Spectator&amp;issn=11899417&amp;date=2024-12-13&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.1&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Spectator&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Hamilton city officials say Premier Doug Ford's new plan to put an additional $75.5 million toward affordable housing and shelter spaces falls well short of what's needed to tackle Ontario's deepening homelessness crisis.
"This is a start, but it's certainly not going to have the kind of impact that we need because the resources are not robust enough to really solve the crisis across the entire province," Mayor Andrea Horwath said Thursday.
"The city's putting in a whole lot of money when it comes to homelessness and when it comes to housing, and we need to see our partners step up to help us bear that cost."
Likewise, Coun. Cameron Kroetsch said the additional funding Ford announced Thursday amounts to a "drop in the bucket" compared to the scale of the problem in Hamilton alone. "It's not going to do anything."
Coun. John-Paul Danko said while any extra dollars are welcome, the provincial measures come "maybe too little too late" as the city continues to pour more money into the pressing file than upper levels of government.
Ford announced the additional $50 million for affordable housing, $20 million for shelters and $5.5 million for rental supplements Thursday as his Progressive Conservatives introduced proposed legislation that aims to enshrine more powers for police and municipalities to deal with trespassing and public drug use.
"Enough is enough. This has to stop and it will stop. Families deserve to play in and enjoy their parks without fearing for the safety of their kids," he told reporters at Queen's Park with a clutch of mayors from Ontario municipalities who have called for more action on encampments standing behind him.
"These encampments are taking over public spaces with illegal drug use happening out in the open, creating huge safety risks for people and communities," Ford said.
The $75.5 million is in addition to the $700 million the province is spending on homelessness prevention and the $378 million for 19 "homelessness and addiction recovery treatment" (HART) hubs that the government aims to open as it closes 10 supervised drug-use sites by March.
As part of Ford's plan, municipalities will have to keep track of how many homeless people are moved from encampments into new accommodations and report that to the province to justify how the funds are being spent.
"We want to help people, we don't want to punish people," the premier said in announcing the amendments to the Trespass to Property Act.
Under the trespass changes aimed at encampment dwellers, there would be a new offence of "continuous trespass" that judges could consider as an "aggravating factor" along with gauging the "likelihood to reoffend," said Attorney General Doug Downey.
"That will give greater tools to the judiciary and for others enforcing the law."
The new laws also focus on drug use in parks, empowering police and provincial offences officers to order people in encampments to stop using illegal drugs or face fines up to $10,000, arrest or a maximum of six months in prison.
But stepped-up enforcement doesn't "address in any meaningful way" that "there's nowhere for people to go," Brad Evoy, executive director of the Disability Justice Network of Ontario, told The Spectator.
"Ultimately, this just leads to people being more likely to be criminalized and incarcerated."
The focus on drug use and trespassing seem "very reactionary," said Andrew Matthews, a manager with St. Matthew's House, a social-service agency in Hamilton that provides services for people who live outside. "I just don't think it will actually be effective."
The Ford government's response to growing municipal frustration over encampments comes as Hamilton grapples with an estimated 300 people living outside, a subset of the roughly 1,600 the city says are homeless overall.
City officials have regularly pointed to how Hamilton is outspending upper orders of government on housing and homelessness.
The city expects 2024's spending on the massive files across various divisions - and including in-year council approvals - to be $186 million, which is $39 million more than in 2023. Of that, $41 million is from the provincial government and $20 million from Ottawa, with the city covering about $125 million - or 67 per cent.
As stopgaps, while it angles for more funding for affordable and supportive housing, the city and its agency partners are creating an additional 192 temporary shelters beds and situating 40 micro shelters for up to 80 people on municipal land off Barton Street West this winter.
Meanwhile, the city is preparing to respond to a human-rights-based legal challenge over how the municipality has approached people living outside. Hearings for that case are scheduled to start Monday.
The litigation has influenced how the city responds to issues with encampments, Danko said.
"We are under a microscope in the City of Hamilton, in particular, because every municipality is following along with this legal challenge."
Danko plans to introduce a motion next month that calls on staff to stick with the provisions of the city's encampment protocol, which stipulates spatial restrictions and other rules for tents in public spaces. Staff are doing their best with the resources they have, but residents are increasingly irked by such issues as debris left at abandoned encampment sites, he said.
"It's really, really frustrating for residents that they don't see action from the municipality."
-With files from the Toronto Star
Teviah Moro is a reporter
at The Hamilton Spectator.
tmoro@thespec.com

Mayor Andrea Horwath speaks during a visit by Premier Doug Ford to Hamilton in March 2023. "The city's putting in a whole lot of money when it comes to homelessness and when it comes to housing, and we need to see our partners step up to help us bear that cost," Horwath said Thursday.Chris Young Canadian PressCathie Coward The Hamilton Spectator Hamilton Spectator file photo

Document 478 of 500

Key premiers won't back threat to cut energy to U.S.

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/key-premiers-wont-back-threat-cut-energy-u-s/docview/3143663940/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Key+premiers+won%27t+back+threat+to+cut+energy+to+U.S.&amp;title=The+Spectator&amp;issn=11899417&amp;date=2024-12-13&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.9&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Spectator&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Three key premiers whose provinces generate energy exports dismissed Premier Doug Ford's suggestion that Canada should slap punitive tariffs on America or shut off electricity, gas and oil shipments to the U.S. in retaliation for Donald Trump's threatened tariffs on Canadian products, as Trump shrugged off Ford's warning.
Alberta Premier Danielle Smith, Quebec Premier François Legault, and Newfoundland and Labrador's Andrew Furey said bluntly they do not support Ford's threat to "cut off their energy" which he said would make Americans "feel the pain."
Ford made the threat after all 13 premiers met the night before with Prime Minister Justin Trudeau on Canada's response, and he reiterated it Thursday.
However Trump, the incoming U.S. president appeared unmoved by Ford's tough talk when speaking Thursday on the floor of the New York Stock Exchange.
"That's OK if he does that. That's fine," Trump told CNBC, repeating his view about the Canada-U.S. trade deficit.
"The United States is subsidizing Canada and we shouldn't have to do that," Trump said.
"And we have a great relationship. I have so many friends in Canada, but we shouldn't have to subsidize a country. We're subsidizing more than a $100 billion a year. We shouldn't have to be doing that."
Alberta's Smith dismissed throttling Canadian energy shipments as she unveiled Alberta's own plan to beef up border security with new provincial sheriff patrol teams to allay Trump's border concerns.
"Under no circumstances will Alberta agree to cut off oil and gas exports," Smith said. "We don't support tariffs. I don't support tariffs on Canadian goods, and I don't support tariffs on U.S. goods, because all it does is make life more expensive for everyday Canadians and everyday Americans," Smith said.
"Instead, we're taking a diplomatic approach, and we're meeting with our allies in the U.S. We're making the case for Alberta oil and gas to be part of the solution to energy affordability, to energy security and to, generally speaking, North American defence security as well," she said.
Ford reiterated the notion of cutting off Canadian energy exports to the U.S. right before Quebec and Newfoundland and Labrador unveiled a blockbuster new $225-billion hydroelectricity deal, one those two provinces touted as key to Canada's renewable energy commitments.
The Alberta premier said her new measures, which include sheriff patrol teams, and a "red zone" within two kilometres of the Alberta-Montana border, had been in the works since 2023 and would have been implemented even without Trump's tariff threat.
Steve Verheul, Canada's former top trade negotiator, has suggested that Canada could put export levies on key Canadian goods like oil and agricultural commodities, saying it would quickly drive up the cost of fuel and food to American consumers, and could be used as leverage to negotiate a "broader exemption across all the sectors" that may be hit by Trump's tariffs.
Smith dismissed that, too, as a "terrible idea."
Manitoba Premier Wab Kinew said Thursday he also intends to beef up border security using provincial conservation officers and spend more on overtime for RCMP officers at the border.
Kinew did not directly answer whether he would restrict electricity exports, but Kinew said Manitoba is drafting a list of potential retaliatory tariffs in order to protect Canadian jobs at risk from Trump's tariffs.
As Canada looks to beef up its border security after Trump threatened tariffs while raising concerns about illicit fentanyl pouring into his country, border officials also pointed out Thursday there's barely any illegal drugs coming from Canada.
Though, none of them wanted to say the name Trump when they said so.
"Canada is not a significant source of fentanyl in the United States," said Aaron McCrorie, vice-president of intelligence and enforcement at the Canada Border Services Agency.
CBSA President Erin O'Gorman also said the U.S. Drug Enforcement Administration has characterized the amount coming from Canada as "slippage" - small amounts sent over for personal use, mostly by post.
CAPTION: Ontario Premier Doug Ford has threatened to "cut off their energy" which he said would make Americans "feel the pain."Chris YoungThe Canadian Press

CREDIT: Tonda MacCharles and Robert Benzie Toronto Star

Document 479 of 500

Key premiers dismiss Doug Ford's threat to stop Donald Trump's tariffs by cutting off energy to Americans

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/key-premiers-dismiss-doug-fords-threat-stop/docview/3144167457/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Key+premiers+dismiss+Doug+Ford%27s+threat+to+stop+Donald+Trump%27s+tariffs+by+cutting+off+energy+to+Americans&amp;title=The+Hamilton+Spectator+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2024-12-12&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Hamilton+Spectator+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: OTTAWA-Three key premiers whose provinces generate energy exports dismissed Premier Doug Ford's suggestion that Canada should slap punitive tariffs on America or shut off electricity, gas and oil shipments to the U.S. in retaliation for Donald Trump's threatened tariffs on Canadian products.
Alberta Premier Danielle Smith, Quebec Premier François Legault, and Newfoundland and Labrador's Andrew Furey said bluntly they do not support Ford's threat to "cut off their energy" and make Americans "feel the pain," made Wednesday night after all 13 premiers met with Prime Minister Justin Trudeau on Canada's response to Trump, and reiterated by Ontario's leader Thursday.
Smith dismissed throttling Canadian energy shipments as she unveiled Alberta's own plan to beef up border security with new provincial sheriff patrol teams to allay Trump's border concerns.
"Under no circumstances will Alberta agree to cut off oil and gas exports," Smith said. "We don't support tariffs. I don't support tariffs on Canadian goods, and I don't support tariffs on U.S. goods, because all it does is make life more expensive for everyday Canadians and everyday Americans," Smith said.
"Instead, we're taking a diplomatic approach, and we're meeting with our allies in the U.S. We're making the case for Alberta oil and gas to be part of the solution to energy affordability, to energy security and to, generally speaking, North American defence security as well," she said.
Ford floated the idea of cutting off Canadian energy exports to the U.S. and reiterated that call right before Quebec and Newfoundland and Labrador unveiled a blockbuster new $225-billion hydroelectricity deal, one that those provinces touted as key to Canada's renewable energy commitments.
The deal replaces a scorned decades-old contract due to expire in 2041, and requires Quebec to pay 30 times more for thousands of kilowatt hours of power generated from the Churchill Falls plant. It will also see the two provinces develop new hydro power projects along the Churchill River, and generate 2,400 more megawatts for Quebec.
Legault called it an "extraordinary" deal for Quebec that means Labrador hydroelectricity will ramp up from a 17 per cent share of Quebec's supply to 50 per cent. Furey said the agreement "changes everything."
"It is a fair deal for both parties. It's a good commercial deal that recognizes the ills of the past and creates a new path forward for Newfoundland and Labrador, while allowing Quebec some certainty moving forward. So Premier Legault did show us the money," Furey quipped.
So it was no surprise when both premiers immediately dismissed any call to shut off energy exports to U.S. markets.
Flanked by Legault, Furey said they both "share significant concerns when it comes to the suggestion of tariffs."
"These tariffs will have significant impact on families and macro economies on both sides of the border. We hope it is just bluster. We're preparing as if it is not. There will be no winners in a trade war," Furey said, echoing Smith's pledge.
"Certainly from Newfoundland and Labrador's perspective, we have no interest in stopping the flow of oil and gas, our incredibly valuable and now well-sought-after-world-class oil and gas to the United States. Nor do we now have any interest in stopping export of any electrons that could be produced in Labrador to the Northeastern seaboard."
Legault said he met Trump in Paris last weekend, "and he told me very clearly that we can avoid those tariffs if we do what needs to be done with the borders."
"He doesn't want to see any more illegal immigrants coming from Canada to U.S. So I think the best choice right now for Mr. Trudeau is to very fast table a plan with money, with the number of people, to better secure the border. I think we have to do that. It's a lot better than getting 25-per-cent tariffs starting on Jan. 21. So I prefer that than starting a war and stopping sending energy to (the) United States."
Furey said he held a call with New England governors earlier this week and "there is a significant degree of concern on both sides of the border."
"I think we need to bring it really down to a local level, so that the president and decision-makers on both sides understand that this could be incredibly punishing to, not just the economy, but to the tables and the gas tanks and the electricity switches of every household across the northeastern seaboard."
Alberta's Smith said her new measures, which include sheriff patrol teams, and a "red zone" within two kilometres of the Alberta-Montana border, had been in the works since 2023 and would have been implemented even without Trump's threat. However, she said "we will work with our counterparts in Montana in a shared border arrangement so that we can improve public safety crackdown on illegal migrants and illegal drug trafficking."
Steve Verheul, Canada's former top trade negotiator, has suggested that Canada could also put export levies on key goods like oil and agricultural commodities, telling Bloomberg it would quickly drive up the cost of fuel and food to American consumers, and could be used as leverage to negotiate a "broader exemption across all the sectors" that may be hit by Trump's tariffs.
Smith dismissed that, too, as a "terrible idea."
But at Queen's Park, Ford did not walk anything back.
He said he's "sending a message to the U.S." not to impose tariffs on Canadian goods or else - as a "last resort" - Ontario will strike back.
"We power 1.5 million homes," Ford said Thursday, referring to the electricity Ontario supplies Michigan, New York and Minnesota.
"If they put on tariffs, it's going to be unaffordable for Americans to buy electricity," the premier said, noting his province alone does about $500 billion in annual two-way trade with the U.S. and nine million American jobs depend on trading with Ontario.
"Just like if they put tariffs on the 4.3 million barrels of oil that Alberta is shipping down to the U.S. - if you put 25 per cent increases, every barrel of oil, every gallon of gasoline (goes up) by $1," he said at Queen's Park.
Ford noted that "along with the federal government, all the premiers are putting a list together" of American goods that could be targeted with counter-tariffs.
"We can't just roll over as we're being under attack and hurting our families and our jobs."
Trudeau on Monday said Canada would respond to Trump's threat to impose tariffs on all Canadian and Mexican products, as of his first day in office, to force the two border countries to "stop" illegal immigration and fentanyl from entering the U.S.
But it is far from clear what products the Liberal government is looking at using, with Chrystia Freeland, the deputy prime minister, saying only that several premiers suggested critical minerals and metals that the U.S. needs should be included on any "robust" retaliatory tariff list Canada draws up.
CAPTION: Alberta Premier Danielle Smith, seen here in Edmonton in July, joined withQuebec Premier François Legault, and Newfoundland and Labrador's Andrew Furey to dismiss Premier Doug Ford's threat to "cut off their energy" and make Americans "feel the pain."

Premier Doug Ford expected to explain plan to deal with homeless encampments

Joining the premier for the live YouTube media conference are Sylvia Jones, deputy premier and minister of health; Paul Calandra, minister of municipal affairs and housing; Attorney General Doug Downey, Solicitor General Michael Kerzner and Michael Tibollo, associate minister of mental health and addictions.

Document 481 of 500

Ford threatens to cut off energy to U.S. over tariff threat

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/ford-threatens-cut-off-energy-u-s-over-tariff/docview/3143190516/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Ford+threatens+to+cut+off+energy+to+U.S.+over+tariff+threat&amp;title=The+Spectator&amp;issn=11899417&amp;date=2024-12-12&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.19&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Spectator&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Canada is preparing retaliatory tariffs in response to U.S. president-elect Donald Trump's threat to levy a 25 per cent import tax on all Canadian goods, and Ontario Premier Doug Ford threatened to withhold the province's energy, which it exports to five states.
"We'll use every tool in our toolbox, including cutting them off," Ford said immediately after a meeting of the country's premiers with Prime Minister Justin Trudeau and some members of the federal cabinet.
It was the second such meeting since Trump made the tariff threat, and the first since Trudeau flew to Florida to have dinner with the president-elect at his Mar-a-Lago estate.
In a Nov. 25 social media post, Trump said he would impose tariffs on all goods coming from Canada and Mexico unless both countries stop the flow of migrants and illegal drugs into the U.S.
Ford told reporters in Toronto on Wednesday that the federal government is preparing retaliatory tariffs.
"We need to be ready to fight, this fight is 100 per cent coming on Jan. 20 or Jan. 21," he said, referencing to the date of Trump's inauguration.
In Ottawa, Finance Minister Chrystia Freeland told reporters "a number of premiers spoke out strongly in favour of a robust Canadian response to unjustified tariffs" during the virtual meeting late Wednesday afternoon.
"Some premiers proactively identified products that their provinces produce and export to the United States and which the U.S. relies on, and which should be considered as part of the Canadian response. This included some critical minerals and metals."
Public Safety Minister Dominic LeBlanc said the government shared details of its border plan with the premiers, who offered to contribute provincial resources to the effort.
"We're going to incorporate many of the positive suggestions that the premiers made into finalizing our border plan," he said. "And then obviously a priority will be to share details of this plan with the incoming Trump administration and with Canadians in the coming days."
Ford said the premiers are on board with the plan to deal with Trump's concerns about the border, but it needs a bit of "polish."
The premiers also asked for more "boots on the ground" at the border, including increasing the number of officers with the Canada Border Services Agency and the RCMP.
The first ministers' meeting came a day after Trump launched more jibes at Trudeau on social media, calling him governor of "the great state of Canada" - a nod to his ribbing that he might just have Canada join the U.S. as its 51st state.

This Christmas tradition has been going on for 125 years. Here's how you can help inform bird conservation across Ontario and North America

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/this-christmas-tradition-has-been-going-on-125/docview/3143189722/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=This+Christmas+tradition+has+been+going+on+for+125+years.+Here%27s+how+you+can+help+inform+bird+conservation+across+Ontario+and+North+America&amp;title=The+Hamilton+Spectator+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2024-12-12&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Hamilton+Spectator+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Are you looking for something to do outdoors during the holidays?
North America's longest-running wildlife census returns to Ontario this weekend (Dec. 14).
First held in 1900, the Christmas Bird Count is turning 125.
At least 67 nature groups across the province are leading Christmas Bird Counts from Dec. 14 to Jan. 5.
Bird lovers of all ages and skill levels are invited to participate.
Christmas Bird Counts unite community scientists around the western hemisphere to count as many different species - and individuals of those species - as possible over the course of a single day, within a 24-kilometre diameter circle.
Bird counts welcome nature enthusiasts to enjoy the great outdoors, spend time with other nature lovers and collect data that informs bird conservation.
Ontario Nature, a charity that advocates for wild species and spaces, offers a comprehensive list of bird counts happening across the province.
All events are free to attend, but participants are asked to email the organizer to confirm their attendance, as well as exact time and location.

Trudeau government's carbon price has had 'minimal' effect on inflation and food costs, study concludes

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/trudeau-governments-carbon-price-has-had-minimal/docview/3143188304/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Trudeau+government%27s+carbon+price+has+had+%27minimal%27+effect+on+inflation+and+food+costs%2C+study+concludes&amp;title=The+Hamilton+Spectator+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2024-12-12&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Hamilton+Spectator+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: OTTAWA - Carbon pricing - including the Liberal government's carbon levy-and-rebate scheme - had a small impact on the rate of inflation over the past five years, when the climate policy was responsible for a "small fraction" of the surge in consumer prices, according to a new study.
Published Thursday, the report from University of Calgary economists Trevor Tombe and Jennifer Winter concluded emissions pricing had a "minimal impact" on inflation since 2019. During that time, emissions pricing has contributed to pushing up overall consumer prices by 0.5 per cent, the report found.
That's roughly 1/38th of the overall 19-per-cent increase in consumer prices over the past five years, with the effect on food prices being "even smaller," the report said.
Rising costs grew into a front-line political concern as inflation surged around the globe during the COVID-19 pandemic, peaking in Canada at 8.1 per cent in June 2022.
"Most of the price increases were driven by global factors, such as surging energy prices and disruptions in supply chains, rather than domestic climate policies," the authors wrote in their report, which was published by the Institute for Research on Public Policy and used Statistics Canada's data on household expenditures and modelling tools to measure the effects of tax policies on goods and services.
It also used government figures on energy consumption and emissions intensity, among other things.
"While emissions pricing does influence costs, its role in driving inflation is relatively small compared to other economic pressures," the study concluded.
The findings come as the opposition Conservatives continue to demand an immediate "carbon tax election" to unseat Prime Minister Justin Trudeau's governing Liberals. Conservative Leader Pierre Poilievre has regularly blamed the federal carbon levy-and-rebate scheme for juicing inflation and promises to "axe the tax" if he takes power in the next federal election.
In recent months, Poilievre has claimed that planned increases in the federal carbon price will cause an economic "nuclear winter" by 2030, when "inflation would run rampant and people would not be able to leave their homes or drive anywhere."
Experts use the term "nuclear winter" to describe the global devastation that would follow a major nuclear war, with some projections predicting billions of people would die.
Poilievre also regularly cites the federal carbon price during parliamentary debates when lamenting the increase in food prices, accusing the government of starving Canadians with the policy.
"Before he starves more people, why can we not have a carbon-tax election and axe the tax?" Poilievre said in Parliament on Oct. 30.
Tombe and Winter's report included figures from Statistics Canada that show how much carbon pricing increased the average cost of a host of goods in 2023, including a 0.42 per cent increase for food and non-alcoholic drinks.
The federal fuel charge also adds almost 18 cents per litre to the cost of gasoline, according to government figures.
Asked about the debate around the carbon price, Tombe told the Star that "exaggerated claims by politicians are not new," and that voters should be mindful of rhetorical stretches.
But he said both the Liberals and Conservatives are guilty of exaggerating, with the Tories inflating the costs of carbon pricing, and the government downplaying impacts on affordability.
"The costs of carbon pricing are measurable. They're real, but they're small," Tombe said, noting the Bank of Canada has also pegged the policy's contribution to annual inflation at 0.15 percentage points.
"We shouldn't be under the illusion that if we eliminate the carbon tax that the affordability challenges that we're facing will disappear. That's simply not the case," he said.
Since 2019, the federal government has required all provinces and territories to have pricing systems to discourage greenhouse gas emissions that cause climate change, and to reward businesses and consumers for reducing them. Each jurisdiction can design its own system or adopt a made-in-Ottawa scheme that includes a system for industrial carbon pricing and a "fuel charge" on consumer fuels like gasoline. This federal fuel charge, which applies in Ontario, comes with quarterly rebates that are set to deliver $1,120 to a household of four in the province in the current fiscal year.
The federal government also sets a minimum level for the carbon price in all jurisdictions, which sits at $80 per tonne this year and is set to increase annually until it hits $170 per tonne in 2030.
The rebates for the federal fuel charge are also set to increase each year.
Tombe and Winter said these rebates offset the cost increases from carbon pricing for most households.
"This means that many families, particularly those with lower incomes, are shielded from the negative financial impact of emissions pricing and some may end up with a net financial gain," their report said.
The Liberals and Conservatives have clashed over this claim, which the government has made for years. The argument intensified after the independent Parliamentary Budget Officer concluded most households ultimately lose money because of national carbon pricing when the broader economic impacts of the policy on employment and income are taken into account.
In their study, Tombe and Winter said their analysis does not consider "broader economic burdens or environmental benefits" of climate policies, noting that action to reduce emissions inevitably comes with costs.
"That said, considering only the costs of climate action presents an incomplete picture," they wrote. "There are tangible benefits to climate action that should not be overlooked," including lowering damages and cost of adaptation to a changing world.
The Canadian Climate Institute has estimated that national carbon pricing is set to drive much of the country's projected emissions reductions over the next five years, although the consumer "fuel charge" is expected to be responsible for a smaller chunk of those cuts - eight to 14 per cent - compared with industrial pricing systems.
CAPTION: Prime Minister Justin Trudeau responds to a question in the House of Commons on Dec. 11, 2024.

Doug Ford to crack down on homeless encampments with fines for drug use and more funding

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/doug-ford-crack-down-on-homeless-encampments-with/docview/3143188126/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Doug+Ford+to+crack+down+on+homeless+encampments+with+fines+for+drug+use+and+more+funding&amp;title=The+Hamilton+Spectator+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2024-12-12&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Hamilton+Spectator+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Amid rising homelessness and an explosion of tent encampments in parks, Premier Doug Ford will earmark an additional $75.5 million for shelters and affordable housing - and toughen up trespassing laws.
"Enough is enough. This has to stop and it will stop. Families deserve to play in and enjoy their parks without fearing for the safety of their kids," Ford said Thursday as critics warned he's criminalizing homelessness, which has grown worse under his watch.
"These encampments are taking over public spaces with illegal drug use happening out in the open, creating huge safety risks for people and communities," said the premier.
His comments came on the final day of the legislative session as the Progressive Conservatives introduced changes that will immediately give municipalities more money for shelter and services for homeless people.
But the legislation to give police more power to punish scofflaws would not be in force until next the spring at the earliest.
Pressure has been mounting on Ford to act because encampments have spread throughout the province and many mayors have appealed to him for help.
Municipalities will have to start tracking how many homeless people are moved from encampments into new accommodations and report that to the province to justify how the funds are being spent.
"We want to help people, we don't want to punish people," the premier said at a Queen's Park press conference where he announced amendments to the Trespass to Property Act.
With the government extending the legislature's winter break by two weeks - meaning MPPs won't return until March 3 - the proposed legislation on new powers would not go to a final vote for weeks after that.
Liberal Leader Bonnie dismissed the plan as "political grandstanding."
"When we come back, we will likely be going to an election," said Crombie.
Andrew Boozary, a physician and professor at the University of Toronto's Dalla Lana School of Public Health, noted incarcerating someone costs triple the monthly amount of operating a single supportive housing unit.
"Housing is a matter of human dignity and sound health economics," said Boozary.
Harini Sivalingam, director of the equality program at the Canadian Civil Liberties Association, expressed concern the new measures "will criminalize unhoused people living in encampments."
Under the trespass changes aimed at encampment dwellers, there would be a new offence of "continuous trespass" that judges could consider as an "aggravating factor" along with gauging the "likelihood to reoffend," said Attorney General Doug Downey.
"That will give greater tools to the judiciary and for others enforcing the law. We're quite confident this will be effective," said Downey.
The new laws also take aim at narcotics use in parks, empowering police and provincial offences officers to order people in encampments to stop using illegal drugs or face fines up to $10,000 or arrest and a maximum of six months in prison.
Opposition parties questioned the rationale and whether the extra funding is enough to make a difference, blaming Ford for the skyrocketing number of encampments since he took office in 2018.
"Look, if someone has $10,000 to pay a fine, I doubt they're living in a park," said New Democrat MPP Jessica Bell (University-Rosedale).
"It is cheaper to find a permanent home for someone than it is to send them to jail, and it's a lot more effective in getting them to rebuild their lives. This government's priorities are all wrong," said Bell.
Crombie added that Ford needs to show a detailed plan on "where we will house them other than in our jails, and give them the treatment they need."
Green Leader Mike Schreiner predicted the measures are "not going to work" because there is a chronic shortage of affordable housing.
"The Ford government has failed to build homes and now they want to throw people in jail," said Schreiner.
Included in the immediate $75.5 million cash infusion is $50 million for "ready-to-build affordable housing projects" across Ontario, $20 million to expand shelter capacity and a $5.5 million to the Canada-Ontario Housing Benefit to free up shelter spaces for encampment dwellers.
That's atop the annual provincial $700 million for homelessness prevention programs and the $378 million on the 19 new Homelessness and Addiction Recovery Treatment (HART) hubs that are replacing the 10 supervised drug injection sites the government is closing in March.
Municipal Affairs and Housing Minister Paul Calandra said local authorities would be held more accountable for the tent clusters in their cities and towns.
"Encampments are a public safety concern and not a solution to homelessness," said Calandra.
"We do not believe that somebody suffering from mental health and addiction is best treated in an encampment," he said.
In the wake of court rulings in Waterloo and Kingston, Ford threatened to use the Constitution's "notwithstanding clause" to override any legal hurdles that would prevent the removal of tents from parks, though he believes the legislative changes could withstand a Charter challenge.
There are about 1,000 people living in Toronto parks despite existing city bylaws that prohibit camping or lighting fires and a flood of daily calls from concerned residents to the 311 customer service line.
Toronto Mayor Olivia Chow, who was not among the municipal leaders with Ford at the announcement, said while the city welcomed the extra provincial funding, "we cannot arrest or jail our way out of the homelessness crisis."
Chow said she would like to see thousands more supportive housing units, increased rent supplements, a rise in social assistance rates, and the reinstatement of rent controls.
The mayor said those measures would help homeless people "get back on their feet, rather than forcing them to move from space to space outside when they have nowhere to go."
Ford's political adversaries insist his approach is not enough to deal with something as complex as homelessness, which involves addiction, mental health as well as the soaring cost of rent.
His bill comes after a dozen mayors urged him to enact tougher laws to help them cope with encampments that have sprouted up across Ontario since the COVID-19 pandemic.
Brampton Mayor Patrick Brown, who was at Queen's Park for the announcement with more than a dozen other mayors, applauded the measures to clamp down on drug use in encampments because authorities have been "turning a blind eye" to it.
"This is helpful. We don't want to see fentanyl left behind in parks," said Brown, noting Brampton generally has enough space in its shelters but some homeless people don't want to stay in them because of a no-drugs policy.
With files from Ben Spurr and Victoria Gibson
CAPTION: Municipal Affairs and Housing Minister Paul Calandra speaks to reporters at the Ontario Legislature in Toronto on March 7, 2024.Doug Ford, the premier of Ontario, holds a news conference at Queen's Park alongside provincial ministers Sylvia Jones (health), Paul Calandra (municipal affairs and housing), Doug Downey (attorney general), Michael Kerzner (solicitor general), and Michael Tibollo (associate minister of mental health and addictions).Premier Doug Ford speaks to the media outside of his office in the Queen's Park Legislature on Wednesday.

Bonnie Crombie's housing plan would axe land-transfer tax for first-time home buyers

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/bonnie-crombies-housing-plan-would-axe-land/docview/3142958754/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Bonnie+Crombie%27s+housing+plan+would+axe+land-transfer+tax+for+first-time+home+buyers&amp;title=The+Hamilton+Spectator+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2024-12-11&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Hamilton+Spectator+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: First-time homebuyers and seniors downsizing would not pay the land-transfer tax under a Liberal plan to make housing more affordable.
Non-profit homebuilders would also be exempt, party leader Bonnie Crombie said Wednesday as politicians at Queen's Park prepare for a possible early election next year.
The savings would amount to an average of $13,500 for new home buyers and seniors, she added, taking aim at what she called a housing crisis "disaster" under Premier Doug Ford that has seen prices and rents skyrocket out of the reach of many Ontarians and encampments sprouting up in parks.
"Doug Ford has failed to build the new homes Ontario needs," Crombie said in a statement.
The Liberal plan would also scrap development charges on new housing, cutting costs by up to $170,000 on family-sized homes and provide funding to municipalities to cover infrastructure costs.
Crombie also promised a "phased-in" rent control plan, resources to clear a backlog of disputes before the Landlord Tenant Board and a "rental emergency support for tenants" fund to provide short-term, interest-free loans for renters facing financial emergencies that could otherwise result in evictions.
The former Mississauga mayor said the plan would cost $3.6 billion.
This is a developing story.
CAPTION: Ontario Liberal leader Bonnie Crombie launched her housing plan on Wednesday.

Dec. 11: Fix HSR phone system, don't blame courts for homelessness and other letters to the editor

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/dec-11-fix-hsr-phone-system-dont-blame-courts/docview/3142836325/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Dec.+11%3A+Fix+HSR+phone+system%2C+don%27t+blame+courts+for+homelessness+and+other+letters+to+the+editor&amp;title=The+Hamilton+Spectator+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2024-12-11&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Hamilton+Spectator+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Still waiting in the cold
On Saturday morning we waited 25 minutes for a bus on King Street. It was cold. Ever since the city cyberattack, nobody can call HSR. Those who depend on buses cannot ask about schedules, or call to find out when the next bus will come. It's puzzling why this service has still not been fixed. It doesn't encourage bus riding. Shouldn't being able to call HSR be a priority?
Rose Janson, Hamilton
Courses don't match labour market
We are now seeing that Ontario's university and college system has been operated as a government-supported Ponzi scheme. It turns out that a business model that relied on stuffing schools with international students paying much-higher fees in order to subsidize Ontario students wasn't the best idea. But that's not the only problem.
Does anyone in the Ontario government pay any attention to what these schools are actually teaching? Does anyone match up the programs with what we need? Take my profession, for example. In Ontario alone, Carleton, Toronto Metropolitan, Western and (until recently) Laurier all offer journalism programs at the university level. Sheridan, Seneca, Humber, Centennial, Fanshawe, Conestoga, Algonquin and (until a few weeks ago) Mohawk all offer journalism programs at the college level. (I may have even missed some.) Why? For a profession whose employment levels have been shrinking for two decades? Where are these hundreds of graduating students going every year? PR? Communications? Blogs? The dreaded "influencers?" Because they sure aren't going into newsrooms. We might as well be training them to be telegraph operators.
Does anyone at the government level look at the situation and question the craziness of it? And don't expect the colleges and universities to necessarily do it themselves. Why? Because they need the bums in the seats to keep attracting the government funding necessary to keep the Ponzi scheme rolling along.
Steve Buist, Burlington
Don't blame courts for homelessness
To begin with, the use of the notwithstanding clause, in most cases, has been used to make changes in Canada that should be done in a more measured and timely manner. Our "impatient" politicians were gifted with Section 33 of the Canadian Charter of Rights and Freedoms by other impatient politicians.
To imply that our courts have allowed our parks to be dumping grounds for drug paraphernalia and violence, wrong. The courts have applied the Constitution of this country properly. There are other ways for our lazy politicians to solve these situations. We as a community of tolerant Canadians should stand and be counted when these sloths use the notwithstanding bullfeathers to get around the law.
This is not the first time some of our neighbours have found themselves in some kind of a jam. We worked together before and can do so to solve this. By the way, Andrea Horwath stands out with other mayors as someone with empathy.
One final word on Section 33, someday it could come for you, like when you become old and a Ford-like guy has the power.
Bill Brush, Hamilton
Is it just my imagination?
Re: Nurses and other health-care professionals are first responders - except in the Ford government's view, Dec. 3
Is it my imagination, or has our esteemed premier singled out professions dominated by women for special treatment? Perhaps it would be more accurate to say that they have been singled out to exclude them from special treatment.
Doug Ford announced a new mental health program for first responders to provide them with access to a 24-hour call line and web portal for therapy and peer support. However, nurses and other health-care professionals were not included - the professions that are predominantly women.
This continues the government's draconian spirit of Bill 124. Public sector workers' wage increases were capped at one per cent until the Ford government lifted the cap for police officers, correctional workers and firefighters, but not for nurses, other health-care workers and teachers - the professions that are predominantly women.
This action flies in the face of the declaration by Ford during the COVID-19 pandemic - which heralded our nurses as "our heroes." Is this the way "our heroes" are appreciated? Please, Premier Ford, you can do better.
Hinda Levine, Hamilton
Spend on housing, not rebates
The province is giving $200 and the federal government $250 as rebates adding up to billions of dollars. Meanwhile, thousands are living in tents or overcrowded shelters. Lets put an end to this issue and spend those billions on resolving this ongoing and growing problem.
In addition, our police and courts need to focus on apprehending the multitude of drug dealers in an effort to reduce newly addicted people from entering the system. I wish I had a remedy to offer those currently addicted. The world is so cruel to so many in society.
Bob Graham, Waterdown
Seeking transparency and accessibility
At Hamilton Anti-Racism Resource Centre (HARRC) we regularly hear from individuals who face discrimination in schools, workplaces and public spaces. Their experiences paint a clear picture: Racism is not an abstract issue, it is a daily reality that undermines trust in our institutions.
This distrust is compounded when people feel excluded from decision-making processes or when the mechanisms for addressing their concerns are vague and inaccessible.
One of the most pressing challenges is the lack of accessible avenues for racialized residents to voice their concerns safely and effectively. How can we expect individuals to report inequities if they fear their voices are ignored? How can we rebuild trust when communities feel that their concerns are met with silence or inaction?
When communities are left in the dark about how decisions are made about who gets resources, whose voices are prioritized, and how accountability is ensured, the gap between governance and the people it serves grows wider. A perfect example is just this past week - members of the city's own committee against racism were left seeking answers as to why their volunteer advisory committee was recommended to be disbanded without being connected to a staff liaison.
The Mayor's Task Force has a unique and powerful opportunity to set a new standard for governance in Hamilton. We can begin to dismantle systemic barriers with communities that have felt excluded for far too long.
Another example is advocating for greater transparency regarding how the Hamilton Police Service selects its community advisory panels is essential to fostering trust. The City of Hamilton should urge HPS to follow the city's new citizen selection process for the police service board and allow applicants who have concerns with sharing sensitive personal information with the police to submit applications through the City of Hamilton and not HPS. Racialized and marginalized communities, who often experience over-policing or systemic inequities, need assurance these panels genuinely represent their voices and concerns.
Without transparency, skepticism arises about whether the panels are diverse, impartial, or adequately engaged with the broader community's needs.
Lyndon George, HARRC executive director
Article helped raise awareness
Re: Ancaster artist opens new chapter at 93 with published novel, Dec. 8
Many thanks to the Spectator for reporter Richard Leitner's engaging and insightful feature article: a superb, detailed and thoroughly enjoyable read on the multi-talented Denise McKay, sculptor, artist and author of "Sweetly." She was one of several outstanding local novelists (also including Heather Rath and Bess Georgakakos) featured at the Manor House Authors New Releases event this past Sunday at the Coach & Lantern in Ancaster. This informative and engaging article helped raise awareness of a popular annual event of major interest to your readers - the release of new works by local Manor House authors - and also helped raise attendance at this event to record levels. It's a reminder of the Spectator's important, powerful and influential role in supporting the arts in our community. Thank you for a truly terrific article. Well done!
Michael Davie, Ancaster
CAPTION: A reader is frustrated they were unable to reach anyone over the phone to ask about a delayed bus recently. If this is related to the cyberattack, shouldn't it be fixed by now, they wonder.

Document 487 of 500

Letters

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/letters/docview/3142825615/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=unknown&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Letters&amp;title=The+Spectator&amp;issn=11899417&amp;date=2024-12-11&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.11&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Spectator&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Is it just my
imagination?
Re: Nurses and other health-care professionals are first responders - except in the Ford government's view, Dec. 3
Is it my imagination, or has our esteemed premier singled out professions dominated by women for special treatment? Perhaps it would be more accurate to say that they have been singled out to exclude them from special treatment.

Still waiting
out in the cold
On Saturday morning we waited 25 minutes for a bus on King Street. It was cold. Ever since the city cyberattack, nobody can call HSR. Those who depend on buses cannot ask about schedules, or call to find out when the next bus will come. It's puzzling why this service has still not been fixed. It doesn't encourage bus riding. Shouldn't being able to call HSR be a priority?
Rose Janson, Hamilton
Don't blame courts for homelessness
To begin with, the use of the notwithstanding clause, in most cases, has been used to make changes in Canada that should be done in a more measured and timely manner. Our "impatient" politicians were gifted with Section 33 of the Canadian Charter of Rights and Freedoms by other impatient politicians.
To imply that our courts have allowed our parks to be dumping grounds for drug paraphernalia and violence, wrong. The courts have applied the Constitution of this country properly.
There are other ways for our lazy politicians to solve these situations. We as a community of tolerant Canadians should stand and be counted when these sloths use the notwithstanding bull feathers to get around the law.
This is not the first time some of our neighbours have found themselves in some kind of a jam. We worked together before and can do so to solve this. By the way, Andrea Horwath stands out with other mayors as someone with empathy.
One final word on Section 33, someday it could come for you, like when you become old and a Ford-like guy has the power.
Bill Brush, Hamilton
Courses don't match the market
We are now seeing that Ontario's university and college system has been operated as a government-supported Ponzi scheme. It turns out that a business model that relied on stuffing schools with international students paying much-higher fees in order to subsidize Ontario students wasn't the best idea. But that's not the only problem.
Does anyone in the Ontario government pay any attention to what these schools are actually teaching? Does anyone match up the programs with what we need?
Take my profession, for example. In Ontario alone, Carleton, Toronto Metropolitan, Western and (until recently) Laurier all offer journalism programs at the university level. Sheridan, Seneca, Humber, Centennial, Fanshawe, Conestoga, Algonquin and (until a few weeks ago) Mohawk all offer journalism programs at the college level. (I may have even missed some.) Why? For a profession whose employment levels have been shrinking for two decades? Where are these hundreds of graduating students going every year? PR? Communications? Blogs? The dreaded "influencers?" Because they sure aren't going into newsrooms. We might as well be training them to be telegraph operators.
Does anyone at the government level look at the situation and question the craziness of it? And don't expect the colleges and universities to necessarily do it themselves. Why? Because they need the bums in the seats to keep attracting the government funding necessary to keep the Ponzi scheme rolling along.
Steve Buist, Burlington
Spend on housing, not rebates
The province is giving $200 and the federal government $250 as rebates adding up to billions of dollars. Meanwhile, thousands are living in tents or overcrowded shelters. Lets put an end to this issue and spend those billions on resolving this ongoing and growing problem.
Bob Graham, Waterdown
Transparency and accessibility sought
At Hamilton Anti-Racism Resource Centre (HARRC) we regularly hear from individuals who face discrimination in schools, workplaces and public spaces. Their experiences paint a clear picture: Racism is not an abstract issue, it is a daily reality that undermines trust in our institutions.
This distrust is compounded when people feel excluded from decision-making processes or when the mechanisms for addressing their concerns are vague and inaccessible.
One of the most pressing challenges is the lack of accessible avenues for racialized residents to voice their concerns safely and effectively. How can we expect individuals to report inequities if they fear their voices are ignored? How can we rebuild trust when communities feel that their concerns are met with silence or inaction?
When communities are left in the dark about how decisions are made about who gets resources, whose voices are prioritized, and how accountability is ensured, the gap between governance and the people it serves grows wider.
A perfect example is just this past week - members of the city's own committee against racism were left seeking answers as to why their volunteer advisory committee was recommended to be disbanded without being connected to a staff liaison.
The Mayor's Task Force has a unique and powerful opportunity to set a new standard for governance in Hamilton. We can begin to dismantle systemic barriers with communities that have felt excluded for far too long.
Another example is advocating for greater transparency regarding how the Hamilton Police Service selects its community advisory panels is essential to fostering trust.
The City of Hamilton should urge HPS to follow the city's new citizen selection process for the police service board and allow applicants who have concerns with sharing sensitive personal information with the police to submit applications through the City of Hamilton and not HPS. Racialized and marginalized communities, who often experience over-policing or systemic inequities, need assurance these panels genuinely represent their voices and concerns.
Without transparency, skepticism arises about whether the panels are diverse, impartial, or adequately engaged with the broader community's needs.
Lyndon George, HARRC
executive director
Article helped
raise awareness
Re: Ancaster artist opens new chapter at 93 with published novel, Dec. 8
Many thanks to the Spectator for reporter Richard Leitner's engaging and insightful feature article: a superb, detailed and thoroughly enjoyable read on the multitalented Denise McKay, sculptor, artist and author of "Sweetly." She was one of several outstanding local novelists (also including Heather Rath and Bess Georgakakos) featured at the Manor House Authors New Releases event this past Sunday at the Coach & Lantern in Ancaster.
This informative and engaging article helped raise awareness of a popular annual event of major interest to your readers - the release of new works by local Manor House authors - and also helped raise attendance at this event to record levels. It's a reminder of the Spectator's important, powerful and influential role in supporting the arts in our community. Thank you for a truly terrific article. Well done!
Michael Davie, Ancaster
CAPTION: A reader is frustrated they were unable to reach anyone over the phone to ask about a delayed bus recently. If this is related to the cyberattack, shouldn't it be fixed by now, they wonder.The Hamilton Spectator file photo

What items will be exempt from sales tax in Ontario during GST/HST holiday, starting Dec. 14?

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/what-items-will-be-exempt-sales-tax-ontario/docview/3142825546/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=What+items+will+be+exempt+from+sales+tax+in+Ontario+during+GST%2FHST+holiday%2C+starting+Dec.+14%3F&amp;title=The+Hamilton+Spectator+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2024-12-11&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Hamilton+Spectator+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Starting Saturday, Dec. 14, retailers and restaurants will have to skip charging both goods and services tax (GST) and provincial sales tax (PST) on certain items in Ontario.
In Ontario, the GST is five per cent, and the PST is eight per cent, making the blended rate a total of 13 per cent on most items.
This province blends the two taxes into a harmonized sales tax (HST), which is usually what appears on your bill.
The Canadian government has a handy tax calculator if you are wondering what the cost would normally be for an item you wish to purchase. Visit canada.ca to try it out.
Following the federal government's approval of the tax discount Nov. 28, the Ontario government agreed to match it.
Here's a list of the items that will be exempt from sales tax, starting Dec. 14:
Prepared foods, including vegetable trays, pre-made meals and salads, and sandwiches - some prepared food is already exempt from HSTRestaurant meals, whether dine-in, takeout, or deliverySnacks, including chips, candy, and granola barsBeer, wine, cider, and pre-mixed alcoholic beverages below seven per cent ABVChildren's clothing and footwear, car seats, and diapers - children's clothing is already exempt from HST if it's for a child under age 15Children's toys, such as board games, dolls, and video game consolesBooks, print newspapers, and puzzles for all agesChristmas trees - both real and fake
This sales tax break will last until Feb. 15, 2025.
"Removing the GST from these qualifying goods for two months will provide an estimated $1.6 billion in federal tax relief," the federal government said in an earlier media release.
"A family spending $2,000 on qualifying goods, such as children's clothing, shoes and toys, diapers, books, snacks for the house, or restaurant meals would realize GST savings of $100 over the two-month period."
On Dec. 9, the NDP tried to get the GST removed permanently from basic essential items like home heating, grocery meals, internet and mobile phone bills, diapers and kids' clothes.
But that motion failed in the House of Commons.
CAPTION: Various prepared foods, including vegetable trays, pre-made meals and salads, and sandwiches, along with takeout meals, will be tax-free starting Dec. 14.

Subject: Meals; Sales taxes; GST

Justin Trudeau and premiers to meet Wednesday on Trump threats

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/justin-trudeau-premiers-meet-wednesday-on-trump/docview/3142615032/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Justin+Trudeau+and+premiers+to+meet+Wednesday+on+Trump+threats&amp;title=The+Hamilton+Spectator+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2024-12-10&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Hamilton+Spectator+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Canada's first ministers will meet Wednesday so the premiers can hear Prime Minister Justin Trudeau's plan for addressing president-elect Donald Trump's border concerns.
"They said they were going to come back with a plan to put together a proposal ... to strengthen our borders and hopefully meet our two per cent NATO commitment and to come up with a plan for negotiations with the U.S. on the trade," Premier Doug Ford said Tuesday.
Ford downplayed Trump's pre-dawn social media post describing Trudeau as the "Governor" of the "Great State of Canada."
"That's the president's words. I can tell the president, I'm sure not thinking of Justin Trudeau at midnight. So if he's thinking of Justin at midnight, it's probably a good relationship," said the premier.
Trudeau is to outline beefed up border security measures and expand on any potential retaliatory levies on American products if Trump makes good on his threat for 25 per cent tariffs on Canadian goods.
The 4 p.m. virtual meeting is the second the first ministers have held since the president-elect's Nov. 25 attack on Canada and Mexico, vowing new tariffs on the day he is inaugurated Jan. 20, unless America's neighbours stop illegal immigration and fentanyl smuggling.
They last met virtually on Nov. 27.
On Monday, the premiers will gather in person in Toronto to discuss the crisis.
Public Safety Minister Dominic LeBlanc, who was with Trudeau at a Nov. 29 dinner with Trump at his Florida manse, Mar-a-Lago, has promised "additional investments to reassure Canadians that all of the measures necessary are in place and will continue to be in place" at the border.
Ford warned that "if it's appropriate, then we will retaliate" with stiff tariffs on American products.
"Hopefully we'll never go down that road. It's not worth it," he said, adding Trump "should be worrying about China, he should be worrying about North Korea and Iran and all the other countries out there - that's a much bigger threat than Canada."
CAPTION: Prime Minister Justin Trudeau, Premier Doug Ford and other first ministers will meet Wednesday to discuss how to deal with Donald Trump's tariff threats.

In my next parent meeting, I'll focus on the teacher

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/my-next-parent-meeting-ill-focus-on-teacher/docview/3142494805/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=unknown&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=In+my+next+parent+meeting%2C+I%27ll+focus+on+the+teacher&amp;title=The+Spectator&amp;issn=11899417&amp;date=2024-12-10&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.10&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Spectator&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Last week, at my parent-teacher meeting for the term, I flooded the teacher with questions about my kid - which is what parents do.
As I walked out of the building, past some peeling walls and water fountains with lead pipes, the public policy researcher in me came up with a different question: How is my kid's teacher coping with the repeated funding cuts, increased demands on schools, and public attacks from our own provincial government?
I didn't ask. But here's why we should.
Since Premier Doug Ford came to power in 2018, school board funding has dropped a stunning $1,500 per student, on average, when adjusted for inflation.
The increase in class sizes and the introduction of mandatory online courses have resulted in the province having 5,000 fewer teachers than it would otherwise.
Special education is grossly underfunded.
In 2024, 63 per cent of elementary school principals had to, at least once, call the parents of a child with special-education needs and tell them to stay at home for the day. Imagine having to make that call.
Imagine getting that call.
School buildings are also in bad shape.
The latest estimate puts the school repair backlog at $16.8 billion.
This amount doesn't include many things, like maintaining portable classrooms that were meant to be temporary but have been used for so long that they need fixing.
It also doesn't include monitoring air quality or replacing those lead pipes.
In the meantime, the pressure on the public education system continues to grow.
Artificial intelligence has thrown off all notions of where the good jobs will be in 10 years, heightening the need for high-quality, multidisciplinary education that prepares children for what the future holds.
Public health and education practitioners around the world, including in Ontario, are sounding the alarm about children's and youth's mental health.
Schools are our first line of defence - or at least they could be if adequately funded.
Statistics show growing wealth inequality across Canada, which translates into larger learning gaps among kids in the same classroom.
With social media and artificial intelligence blending lies and facts at breakneck speed, the role of schools in producing citizens who can make informed decisions about political issues and candidates is ever more critical.
Ontario educators must do more with less.
And to add insult to injury, Ford has now taken to attacking teachers publicly, accusing them of (among other things) trying to indoctrinate our kids.
A recent government analysis estimates that Ontario will have a sizable teacher shortage by 2027.
The analysis assumes that the number of teachers in the public education system will remain stable, aside from retirements, but not keep up with enrolment growth.
That's not a safe assumption.
With Ontario asking teachers to do more with less and blaming them for the shortfalls of underfunded schools, recruitment and retention will only become more challenging.
Teachers should at least feel that parents are on their side.
In my next teacher-parent meeting,
I will start with an earnest: How are you?
Ricardo Tranjan is a
senior researcher on
housing and social policy with the Canadian Centre for Policy Alternatives' Ontario office,
@ricardotranjan.
bsky.social
CAPTION:

Document 491 of 500

Bank of Canada expected to cut interest rates by 50 basis points Wednesday - not every economist is thrilled

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/bank-canada-expected-cut-interest-rates-50-basis/docview/3142493670/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Bank+of+Canada+expected+to+cut+interest+rates+by+50+basis+points+Wednesday+-+not+every+economist+is+thrilled&amp;title=The+Hamilton+Spectator+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2024-12-10&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Hamilton+Spectator+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Economists are largely anticipating the Bank of Canada to cut its policy interest rate by 50 basis points on Wednesday, but some are advising against the move.
According to a Bloomberg survey of economists, the central bank is expected to lower the policy rate to 3.25 per cent in its last meeting of 2024 from 3.75 in October. If that turns out to be the case, it will have slashed the rate by 175 basis points in six months.
While many experts support a larger cut saying interest rates are higher than they need to be, others argue that the bank should take a more restrictive approach and reduce the rate by 25 basis points as the threat of inflation and a weaker Canadian dollar looms in 2025.
There have been signs of weakness in the economy since the October announcement that justify a large cut.
Unemployment ticked up to 6.8 per cent in November, its highest level since January 2017, excluding the pandemic. Meanwhile, per-person gross domestic product declined for a sixth consecutive quarter.
And while inflation rose to two per cent in October from 1.6 per cent the month prior, it's still in line with the bank's target.
"Interest rate cuts so far have been the equivalent of the Bank of Canada easing off the economy's brakes rather than stepping on the gas," wrote RBC economists Nathan Janzen and Abbey Xu in a report.
But a creeping rebound in the housing market is fueling inflation concerns, which, some say, should not be overlooked.
Home sales have already picked up in recent months despite fewer new listings, while the federal government is relaxing mortgage rules in December, putting further pressure on prices.
"Arguably, housing has achieved a reasonably soft landing, and there's no need to risk sparking exuberance with more aggressive rate cuts," said BMO's Benjamin Reitzes.
In a recent interview with the Star, senior deputy governor Carolyn Rogers said the risk of increasing pressure on the housing market is "one we'll watch," but rate cuts will continue as long as the economy progresses as expected.
And U.S. president-elect Donald Trump's pledge to implement 25 per cent tariffs on Canadian goods might lead to retaliatory tariffs that would drive imported prices higher. Some Canadian politicians, including Ontario Premier Doug Ford, are already exploring that idea.
"I have to admit that I'm not entirely sold on the idea of cutting 50 basis points at this meeting," said Scotiabank's Derek Holt.
"While the Bank of Canada publicly says they don't act upon possibilities like tariffs until they become fact and incorporated into their outlook, they may wish to take out some insurance against the disinflationary effects," he said.
"If the Bank of Canada thinks Canada may retaliate, then they wouldn't want to do this because the higher tariffs would stoke more imported inflation at least for a time."
Another concern of an aggressive rate cut on Wednesday is the growing weakness of the loonie versus the dollar as the U.S. economic outlook has been stronger relative to Canada's. The higher the central bank interest rate, the more worthwhile it is to hold that country's currency.
"The sharp increase in the unemployment rate is likely enough to drive a second consecutive 50 basis point cut from the Bank of Canada," said BMO's Reitzes.
"Despite that expectation, the risks to the Canadian dollar and housing from such a move should be considered and point to a more cautious path in 2025."
CAPTION: Bank of Canada Governor Tiff Macklem is expected by most economists to announce a 50 basis points cut to the bank's key interest rate on Wednesday.

Subject: Gross Domestic Product--GDP; Interest rates; Central banks; Unemployment; American dollar; Economists; Economic growth; Canadian dollar; Tariffs
