Document 492 of 500

Compassion is taking a back seat

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/compassion-is-taking-back-seat/docview/3142493656/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=unknown&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Compassion+is+taking+a+back+seat&amp;title=The+Spectator&amp;issn=11899417&amp;date=2024-12-10&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.10&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Spectator&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: How grimly fitting that Premier Doug Ford has chosen the season of want to threaten the use of the notwithstanding clause in the Canadian Charter of Rights and Freedoms to clear the city's homeless encampments.
A Hollywood movie might track the evening snow settling on tents and tarps as the provincial government moves to introduce clampdown legislation on the eve of the legislature's jollily generous holiday break. Yes, it sounds borderline Dickensian.
To parse the facts more carefully, the premier penned a letter, not to Santa Claus, but to 12 mayors, scantily outlining legislation to be introduced next week that will "achieve the outcomes all of us agree are necessary in order to protect public safety and dismantle encampments."
Ford's main focus: "to put an end to public disorder, drug use and trafficking and loss of public space that have resulted from the widespread growth of the encampments."
A more thoughtful missive would have begun thusly: We have failed. We have collectively - municipalities and successive provincial governments alike - failed to provide long-promised solutions to chronic homelessness. We have exacerbated the tragedy of want. We have inflamed the tragedy of ignorance.
Let's be clear. These encampments are occupying too much of the already limited green space in this city, spaces meant for the leisure of all residents, not to provide inadequate shelter for the most vulnerable. And public disorder and threats to safety can, and have, emerged from these tent cities.
They must be dealt with. But they cannot be meaningfully addressed until there is somewhere for those being evicted to go.
The premier's precis of the pending legislation does include additional funding to create more capacity in the shelter system, and "new tools and authorities" to assist police enforcing the prohibition of illicit drugs in public.
There's additionally the promise of new, unspecified approaches to drug treatment and rehabilitation that prioritize recovery over incarceration.
These promises are directionally right, though absent specific details, we'll wait to pass judgment. But Ford did not put down his pen. Instead, he prophesied.
Should the courts "interfere," as they have in the past, by upholding the Charter rights of individuals deprived of housing, his government is, he wrote, "fully prepared" to use the notwithstanding clause, Section 33 of the Charter of Rights and Freedoms, to override the court's decision. That includes being an intervener "in any court case that restricts the ability of municipalities to regulate and prohibit encampments."
The courts have done precisely that in the past. An Ontario Supreme Court ruling last year rejected an application from the Region of Waterloo to be granted an injunction, under a local bylaw, to evict homeless people - about 70 shelters with an estimated 50 residents - from property owned by the municipality. The bylaw infringed Section 7 of the Charter, the court found. That is, that everyone has the right to "life, liberty and security of the person."
Keep in mind that Ford's notwithstanding threat, for that's what it is, comes on the heels of a letter addressed to the premier and signed by 450 members of Ontario's legal community urging him to stand down. "The notwithstanding clause was never intended to be used to deprive vulnerable groups of constitutional protection," the group wrote. Government policy should "promote not undermine the charter rights of disadvantaged groups."
Therein lies the heart of the matter.
Compassion takes a back seat in Ford's clear-'em-out strategy. The province should take its share of the responsibility for how we got here. As should municipalities. Have they, collectively, exhausted all reasonable means of assisting homeless people? No, they have not.
The encampments must be dealt with. But they are a blight of our collective making. And those living within them, as the courts have wisely concluded, have a right not to be moved until there is somewhere safe to move them to, not least as a cold and dreary winter descends upon us.
CAPTION:

CREDIT: ;

Subject: Charter of Rights-Canada; Legislation; Evictions; Homeless people; Municipalities

Title: Compassion is taking a back seat

Document 493 of 500

Dec. 10: RBG cuts a slap in the face, older adults will vote their disapproval and other letters to the editor

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/dec-10-rbg-cuts-slap-face-older-adults-will-vote/docview/3142493467/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Dec.+10%3A+RBG+cuts+a+slap+in+the+face%2C+older+adults+will+vote+their+disapproval+and+other+letters+to+the+editor&amp;title=The+Hamilton+Spectator+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2024-12-10&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Hamilton+Spectator+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: RBG cuts a slap in the face
Re: Royal Botanical Gardens says painful job cuts needed for 'long-term financial sustainability,' Dec. 7
As a former member of the Royal Botanical Gardens (RBG) biodiversity programs committee for eight years, I am deeply distressed with the ongoing job cuts announced by the RBG, a Hamilton and Burlington landmark.
CEO Nancy Rowland's assurance "these decisions were not made lightly and are not a reflection on the performance of those affected" rings very hollow when one takes into account the years of tireless service offered by dedicated and widely respected individuals such as the longtime directors of taxonomy (Jim Pringle) and education (Barb McKean). Regardless of severance packages, this is an egregious slap in the face.
It's really too bad the premier has cut down so many trees at Ontario Place. Otherwise, the RBG could join the Science Centre in these new corporate digs.
Grant Linney, Dundas
What are RBG's priorities?
The recent article about the Royal Botanical Gardens (RBG) is concerning and disappointing. What are botanical gardens without horticulturalists? The abrupt cuts to key horticultural staff seems particularly mean-spirited at this time of the year. According to CEO Nancy Rowland, the cuts "are necessary to reduce costs and focus our resources to meet our business priorities."
It would be helpful to know exactly, what are the RBG's "business priorities" and vision? To maintain the RBG as world-class gardens or Disneyfied "to grow visitation." What compromises will be made and at what cost? If it is the latter then I believe the RBG can expect to lose member support, including my own. Discouraged and disillusioned volunteers, the heart of the organization, may also head for the exits. Can the RBG really afford to lose members and volunteers?
Perhaps its ambitious master plan is too ambitious. This venerable institution is unique and we can only speculate on RBG's future, which is hopefully not some version of Busch Gardens. I am looking forward to the board enlightening the public about their plans.
Wayne Poole, Dundas
Are air filters working?
Winter is here and the rates of illness due to COVID-19, flu, colds, etc., are on the increase again and reflect that reality.
Does your child's classroom have a working air filter unit? A couple of years ago, boards were forced to invest in high-quality filter appliances, but how many of them are currently operating as the COVID panic peak has passed? I wonder how many have been maintained properly? How many of the filters themselves have been changed as required? They are expensive and individual schools and boards will be tempted to overlook this maintenance if they are not encouraged.
If we want our children to be safe, parents need to ask the question directly: is there a working air filter in my child's classroom?
Tony Kilgannon, Hamilton
Enough of this buffoonery
The Conservatives, under Pierre Poilievre, have stalled the work of Parliament for the last two months. In their mania to "dethrone" Justin Trudeau they put forward a motion of non-confidence in the government. They then blocked that motion so it could not go forward.
If, like me, you have lost confidence in Parliament, due to the infantile behaviour of our leaders, let your MP know. There is a very good chance you will be ignored, but you never know, there may be one or two of them who recognize they are acting like buffoons and want to change.
Bill Sullivan, Hamilton
Bike lanes are for safety
Although I am a cyclist (in the good weather), I do understand some of the complaints people have against bike lanes. There are good locations and bad locations. Personally, I find trails the best and safest. Automobile drivers are just in too much of a rush to look for cyclists (or pedestrians). Walking in the Meadowlands area is risky business.
I also like to walk around Ancaster but plan it so I don't cross at too many intersections. Cars simply do not stop before turning right on red lights and the driver looks left, but not right.
I walked on Stonehenge yesterday and found no less than three of the new bike lane plastic guards broken off, either by cars or plows.
Andy Price, Ancaster
Older adults will vote their disapproval
All but the very naive know Justin Trudeau's "generous rebate" is just our money to begin with. However his refusal to not extend payments to the unemployed and seniors ignores a substantial voting block. Us "old people" will not forget this snub at the ballot box.
John H. Miller, Hamilton
Ford as PM an expensive prospect
Re: Justin Trudeau, Pierre Poilievre and Doug Ford have one eye on Donald Trump and another on their own futures, Dec. 6
Martin Regg Cohn's opinion on Ford aspiring to be prime minister one day is hopefully not a precursor to the future.
Look what Ford has spent our tax dollars on and broadcast what he wants to spend them on in the future: A billion dollars to get beer into convenience stores one year early; $3 billion to give $200 to every taxpayer, no matter how wealthy they are; as of yet, an unknown amount for Hwy. 413, a completely unnecessary road outside the "gridlock corridor"; more than $1 billion for Ontario Place so a privately owned spa can have parking; and more than $1 billion for privately owned for profit medical practices.
Meanwhile, we have a large shortage of nurses, hospital hallway beds, long waiting times for surgeries and long waiting lists for long-term-care beds. If he can mess up Ontario so badly, imagine what he can do to Canada! Heaven help us taxpayers if this comes to pass.
Dennis Martin, Binbrook
CAPTION: In the wake of cuts at the Royal Botanical Gardens (RBG), a reader asks, what are its business priorities and vision - to maintain the RBG as world-class gardens or to be "Disneyfied" to grow visitation?

BoC rate cut won't thrill every economist: Weaker loonie and threat of inflation spur calls of caution

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/boc-rate-cut-wont-thrill-every-economist/docview/3142492026/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=BoC+rate+cut+won%27t+thrill+every+economist%3A+Weaker+loonie+and+threat+of+inflation+spur+calls+of+caution&amp;title=The+Spectator&amp;issn=11899417&amp;date=2024-12-10&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.9&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Spectator&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Economists are largely anticipating the Bank of Canada to cut its policy interest rate by 50 basis points on Wednesday, but some are advising against the move.
According to a Bloomberg survey of economists, the central bank is expected to lower the policy rate to 3.25 per cent in its last meeting of 2024 from 3.75 in October. If that turns out to be the case, it will have slashed the rate by 175 basis points in six months.
While many experts support a larger cut saying interest rates are higher than they need to be, others argue that the bank should take a more restrictive approach and reduce the rate by 25 basis points as the threat of inflation and a weaker Canadian dollar looms in 2025.
There have been signs of weakness in the economy since the October announcement that justify a large cut.
Unemployment ticked up to 6.8 per cent in November, its highest level since January 2017, excluding the pandemic. Meanwhile, per-person gross domestic product declined for a sixth consecutive quarter. And while inflation rose to two per cent in October from 1.6 per cent the month prior, it's still in line with the bank's target.
"Interest rate cuts so far have been the equivalent of the Bank of Canada easing off the economy's brakes rather than stepping on the gas," wrote RBC economists Nathan Janzen and Abbey Xu in a report.
But a creeping rebound in the housing market is fueling inflation concerns, which, some say, should not be overlooked.
Home sales have already picked up in recent months despite fewer new listings, while the federal government is relaxing mortgage rules in December, putting further pressure on prices.
"Arguably, housing has achieved a reasonably soft landing, and there's no need to risk sparking exuberance with more aggressive rate cuts," said BMO's Benjamin Reitzes.
In a recent interview with the Star, senior deputy governor Carolyn Rogers said the risk of increasing pressure on the housing market is "one we'll watch," but rate cuts will continue as long as the economy progresses as expected.
And U.S. president-elect Donald Trump's pledge to implement 25 per cent tariffs on Canadian goods might lead to retaliatory tariffs that would drive imported prices higher. Some Canadian politicians, including Premier Doug Ford, are already exploring that idea.
Another concern of an aggressive rate cut on Wednesday is the growing weakness of the loonie versus the dollar as the U.S. economic outlook has been stronger relative to Canada's. The higher the central bank interest rate, the more worthwhile it is to hold that country's currency.
"The sharp increase in the unemployment rate is likely enough to drive a second consecutive 50 basis point cut from the Bank of Canada," said BMO's Reitzes. "Despite that expectation, the risks to the Canadian dollar and housing from such a move should be considered and point to a more cautious path in 2025."
CAPTION:

CREDIT: Ana Pereira Toronto Star

'People are furious': Gun supporters disappointed with another firearm ban in Canada, as they appeal previous ban

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/people-are-furious-gun-supporters-disappointed/docview/3142684882/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=%27People+are+furious%27%3A+Gun+supporters+disappointed+with+another+firearm+ban+in+Canada%2C+as+they+appeal+previous+ban&amp;title=The+Hamilton+Spectator+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2024-12-09&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Hamilton+Spectator+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: People who support the right to own firearms in Canada are upset with yet another gun ban in the country, announced by the federal government Dec. 5.
The announcement - made on the eve of the 35th anniversary of the Ecole Polytechnique attack - prohibits more military-style assault-style firearms, encompassing 324 unique makes and models. The full list is available here.
For owners who have those now banned guns, they can continue to have them for the time being, but have until Oct. 30, 2025 to come into compliance with the law.
The National Association of Women and the Law said Canada's announcement to strengthen gun control measures and increase women's safety is commendable.
"(Our organization) commends the federal government for listening to the voices of survivors and women's organizations," said Suzanne Zaccour, the director of legal services, in a press release. "These measures will create a path forward toward a Canada that is free from gun violence."
But, for firearms aficionados, the announcement is another blow to those who are trying to abide by Canada's laws.
"What the Liberals came out with is absolutely devastating," said Rod Giltaca, director of the Canadian Coalition for Firearm Rights.
But he didn't have too much time to dwell on it, as he was prepping to make an appearance in the Supreme Court Dec. 9 and 10, appealing a previous firearm ban.
In the United States, the second amendment of the constitution is the right to bear arms, but in Ontario a 2010 legal decision says there is no constitutional right to bear arms in Canada.
People like Giltaca can't understand why the federal government is still chipping away at the legal guns people can own - especially when it's targeting those complying with the rules.
In 2014, Statistics Canada showed Canada was the safest it had ever been when it comes to gun violence, Giltaca said.
However, the years following brought further gun control amendments to existing legislation.
"Gun owners knew what the rules are. We don't like them - most of them - but we could go to the range, we could still hunt, and there was an equilibrium."
However, crackdowns have continued. In 2018, Bill C-71 was introduced to help law enforcement investigate firearms-related crimes.
In 2020, the federal government prohibited more than 1,500 models of assault-style firearms, including some M16, AR-10, AR-15 and M4 pattern firearms.
It banned guns with a bore diameter of 20 mm or more.
"It destroyed a lot of shooting sports, and rendered probably a billion dollars worth of property useless."
That ban took away approximately 600,000 individual firearms, Giltaca said, adding he's fighting the ban, currently before the Superior Court of Appeal.
Part of that 2020 ban - and the one announced Dec. 5 - includes a so-called buy back program, called the Assault-Style Firearms Compensation Program to provide fair compensation for eligible businesses and individuals who possess these prohibited firearms and devices.
So far, individuals are unable to apply.
With both bans, owners have until Oct. 30, 2025 to comply with the law, under an amnesty period, and should forfeit their firearm to have it destroyed.
Giltaca noted the government is still telling firearms enthusiasts there are plenty of legal firearms available to use in Canada.
"(After the first ban), there were people like me, who went out to replace the ones that were banned, so they could continue to do what they were doing. In my case, that was $9,000 to replace my firearms," Giltaca said.
He still hasn't been able to apply to get that purchase compensated.
"Then two years later (in 2024), they've banned everything that people replaced," he said. "It's like, LOL, the joke's on you. You should have never trusted (the government) in the first place."
There are now gun owners with thousands of dollars worth of legally obtained guns, which are properly authorized, licensed and stored, which are now essentially worthless, Giltaca said.
"There's a reason why people are absolutely livid. And in my opinion, it's destabilizing."
This latest ban will be devastating for gun clubs and fish and game clubs, where members won't be allowed to shoot banned firearms, he noted. And also for shops that sell legal firearms that are now on the list of banned items.
For residents who aren't into the sport, Giltaca was asked to explain why people need to own the now-banned assault-style firearms.
"It's a symbolic conversation. Why does anyone need a car that can go 270 km/h? There's posted speed limits, and we give them fines if they go over," he said. "What do you need that (fast) car for anyway?"
He said the firearms world is a passion for some, with people becoming part of a community around gun clubs, run by members, who volunteer their time with charity shoots, game dinners and events.
"These clubs and these sports, meaning target shooting and hunting and firearm collecting, people's entire lives are wrapped into this. This is just like people who are into dogs, so they go to dog shows in society."
Some travel the country to meet with other enthusiasts and collectors.
Some Canadians may agree with the firearms ban from a perspective of recent news of gun violence, but Giltaca said it's not a fair comparison.
He noted a rental vehicle was used in the deadly Toronto van attack in 2018 that killed 11 people, yet no one is out to ban vehicles.
The 2021 London truck attack, which killed four members of the same family, has now been labelled terrorism, and the perpetrator is serving a life in prison.
"Canadians have owned firearms since before we were a country," Giltaca said.
And he's not at all condoning violence - Giltaca sat on the Mass Casualty Commission, the inquiry into the Nova Scotia attack on April 18/19 2020.
"This guy secured every single firearm he used, he smuggled them in from the United States. He had two (legal) domestically sourced firearms, but he never used that in the spree shooting. He used an AR-15, and a handgun he took off an RCMP officer," he said. "So no regulation now or in the future would change that - this is why people are furious."
"If you look at statistics from 2017 to 2023, firearm violence has more than doubled. It's the most dangerous Canada has ever been."
This latest gun ban is going to shut down gun clubs - clubs that own acres of property where people come to use their legal firearms.
"Guns are so important to the people who own them. We store them responsibly, use them responsibly - once you get the card, you have a trusted responsibility."
This ban still won't stop illegally smuggled firearms from entering Canada, Giltaca said.
For now, he's awaiting the outcome of his appeal in the Supreme Court. If that 2020 ban is overturned, it could mean this latest ban is also not valid.
CAPTION: A 2018 photo of a Burlington Rifle and Revolver Club member, who opened a locked case at the range containing two firearms, including an AR-15.

Document 496 of 500

Ontarians divided over Doug Ford's get-tough approach on homeless encampments, survey suggests

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/ontarians-divided-over-doug-fords-get-tough/docview/3142290746/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Ontarians+divided+over+Doug+Ford%27s+get-tough+approach+on+homeless+encampments%2C+survey+suggests&amp;title=The+Hamilton+Spectator+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2024-12-09&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Hamilton+Spectator+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Ontarians are deeply divided over Premier Doug Ford's strategy for tackling homeless encampments, including his threatened use of the "notwithstanding clause," a new poll suggests.
With Ford bringing forward measures this week to give police and municipalities "enhanced legislative powers" to remove the tent encampments from parks, the Abacus Data survey found little backing for a heavy-handed approach.
Only 12 per cent favoured "stronger law enforcement measures to clear encampments."
About one-third - 34 per cent - backed "providing more affordable housing" with 16 per cent supporting "creating safe spaces and shelters," another 16 per cent said "increasing community-based support and outreach programs," and 14 per cent recommending expanded "access to mental health and addiction services." Seven per cent had no opinion.
Still, 65 per cent said they were "concerned about the impact of encampments in their community" with 13 per cent unconcerned while 22 were neutral or had no opinion.
Abacus regularly polls for the Star, but this particular survey was paid for by the Canadian Alliance to End Homelessness, an umbrella group that works with charities across the country.
"Politically, whether you're a mayor or the premier or an MPP, homelessness is now a visual representation of a crisis that ... people don't feel anybody's had a handle on," Abacus president David Coletto said Monday.
"It's a reflection of how the system isn't working," said Coletto.
While Ford said he is "fully prepared" to override the courts by using the Constitution's "notwithstanding clause" to secure his legislation and "dismantle" encampments, 40 per cent of respondents said they would oppose that.
Just 30 per cent support the government using the Section 33 of the Charter of Rights and Freedoms to ensure its efforts aren't overturned by the courts.
Another 29 per cent would neither support nor oppose that or had no opinion.
Of the 40 per cent that disagreed with using the "notwithstanding clause," about one-third - 32 per cent - said "police clearing encampments is not an effective solution to homelessness."
Another 26 per cent said "there are better, more compassionate solutions to the issue" with 14 per cent expressing concern "it could set a dangerous precedent for overriding the human rights of other groups" and 11 per cent fearing "it undermines individual rights and protections."
Six per cent were "worried it will hurt people" and five per cent said Ford's government "has been too quick to invoke the 'notwithstanding clause' in recent years" and two per cent didn't like that it would override the courts.
"This is an issue that creates some vulnerability for Ford in that it reminds them of the version of him that they didn't like, which was the pre-pandemic version," said Coletto.
"The pressure he's feeling is real. People right across the province ... believe something has to happen, but it's not a slam dunk that they are going to embrace using the notwithstanding clause on something like this," he added.
Abacus surveyed 1,500 Ontarians Nov. 29-Dec. 4 using online panels based on the Lucid exchange platform. While opt-in polls cannot be assigned a margin of error, for comparison purposes, a random sample of this size would have one of plus or minus 2.6 percentage points, 19 times out of 20.
Asked what they felt was the primary cause of encampments, which were far less prevalent before the COVID-19 pandemic in 2020, 55 per cent of respondents blamed "the housing affordability crisis" with 45 per cent citing "addiction, mental health issues or personal choices."
But the issue isn't hurting Ford yet - Abacus found his Progressive Conservatives 43 per cent, ahead of Bonnie Crombie's Liberals at 25 per cent, Marit Stiles' New Democrats at 21 per cent and Mike Schreiner's Greens at six per cent.
The premier is considering a spring election, one year ahead of the scheduled June 2026 vote.
CAPTION: A homeless tent encampment is seen in Dufferin Grove Park on Dec. 7, 2024.

Subject: Homeless encampments; Mental health; Affordable housing; Homeless people; Pandemics

Business indexing term: Subject: Affordable housing

Title: Ontarians divided over Doug Ford's get-tough approach on homeless encampments, survey suggests

Document 497 of 500

Dec. 9: Homeless people are just people, where will evicted campers go and other letters to the editor

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/dec-9-homeless-people-are-just-where-will-evicted/docview/3142182812/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Dec.+9%3A+Homeless+people+are+just+people%2C+where+will+evicted+campers+go+and+other+letters+to+the+editor&amp;title=The+Hamilton+Spectator+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2024-12-09&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Hamilton+Spectator+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: The words we choose matter
Re: Doug Ford preparing to override courts, give police and municipalities enhanced legislative powers to 'dismantle' homeless encampments, Dec. 5
The Premier of Ontario wants to suspend the individual human rights guaranteed by the Charter of Rights and Freedoms to enable the dismantling of "encampments." He wants to be able to remove the homeless from the view of the people of Ontario.
The words we choose to describe something or some people are very powerful. A choice of one word or another, the choice of one word or a phrase makes a lot of difference. For a long time when I introduced my father's second wife to people, I called her "my father's, wife, Mary." When I changed to calling her my stepmother, my father was very moved by the change. My dad was a man who didn't really like political correctness. If he were alive today, I don't think he would really appreciate "woke."
When we call some people "homeless," it is easier to think about doing terrible things to them. If we were just to call them people who don't have a home, I think it would be harder to talk about suspending their basic rights. If we were to call the people in our country, our fellow citizens, internally displaced persons; if we were to call encampments "displacement camps" I don't think we would be talking about dismantling them. We might be talking about how to remove the need for them. I think that would be a much more useful conversation.
Bruce Pilcher, Hamilton
How would MPPs like this treatment?
Dear Premier Ford and MPPs; Merry Christmas! Happy and blessed New Year! It is tough, but we are having your homes and garages dismantled as soon as possible. If people who must live in tents because we offer no housing or sufficient support for them do not need their possessions and homes/tents, then I am sure you and your colleagues will not be upset to lose everything you have on your property. I am sure many of the homes and properties would house those losing their tents and all their possessions. Please let me know when this can start ASAP. All the best as you look for a new place to live.
Gayle Cornell Schaming, Grimsby
Where will evicted people go?
I am totally disgusted with Doug Ford's being "fully prepared to use the notwithstanding clause" with regard to encampments. The notwithstanding clause simply allows a legislature to deny to the people certain rights laid out in our Charter of Rights and Freedoms.
Judges in both Waterloo Region and in Kingston have determined that, when there are not enough facilities to actually house the homeless, the municipality cannot forbid encampments, on the grounds that the Charter guarantees to everyone (even the homeless) "... the right to life, liberty and security of the person ..." And that is the Charter guarantee which Ford has said he will nullify by using the notwithstanding clause.
Here in Hamilton there certainly aren't enough places to house the otherwise homeless people. But Ford wants to, "make sure that we move the homeless along." To where I'd like to know.
Do I like having tents in our parks? Nope. But I despise the fact of people not being able to have even a tent over their heads for shelter if necessary.
What's necessary is that Hamilton and other municipalities build accommodation for the homeless. And the money for doing that could easily come from the province. Think for a minute: How much money did the province lose by doing away with licence plate renewals? Licence plate renewal "relief" that no one was even asking for! And how much money is it costing to give each of us a cheque for $200 "just because?" How about the province put that money to work, instead, to house those who are unable to house themselves?
The answer to the homeless question and to having parks without tents is to build housing, not to simply oust people from the only "homes" they can afford.
I am grateful that we live in a city in which the mayor, Andrea Horwath, did not sign a letter asking the province to use the notwithstanding clause to evict our homeless from our parks. But, especially now that winter is upon us, the mayor and council must find a way to build housing for all.
Suzie Scott, Hamilton
Were these truly 'heritage' buildings?
I noted the Dec. 2 front-page headline "History tumbles with collapse of King Street East heritage buildings." Perhaps it is time we simply called things what they are. These are not "heritage" buildings. They are derelicts. If they were heritage buildings, the money would have been spent to preserve them as soon as they received the designation.
Michael Blythe, Scotland, Brant County
Make a move that actually impacts gun crime
Bypassing our democratic process, an order-in-council was issued to ban yet another 324 models of firearms. I understand the emotional reasoning for this, but it will do nothing to stop the violence. Trudeau missed an opportunity to gain points when he met with Donald Trump who is threatening tariffs if Canada does not do more to secure the border from migrants and illegal drugs entering from Canada. Trudeau could have actually negotiated. Instead, he took a knee and acquiesced. Consider this: 87 per cent of all gun-related crimes in Canada are perpetrated using illegal guns smuggled into Canada from the U.S. Trudeau could have made demands that the U.S. also do whatever it takes to stem the flow. A move that would actually make a difference.
Jean Rigg-Brown, Binbrook
Litter is everybody's responsibility
It is a common misconception by Hamiltonians that so many of our issues are someone else's problem. It's time to take a bit of responsibility as citizens and work together to address at least one thing we have some control over: the cleanliness of our city.
Litter has become a huge issue and why people think it's the job of the city employees to take care of it is beyond me. The Women's Institute in Prince Edward Island started a roadside clean up program many years ago. They provide yellow bags to residents and the month of May is devoted to roadside cleanup. These or clear plastic bags can be used for collecting litter and left at the side of the road for pick up. This garbage includes appliances, tires, etc. dragged out of ditches.
Ireland has had a "Tidy Towns" competition running since 1958. It has become a prestigious event with towns taking great pride in winning. It now has funding and sponsors. Maybe our Wards could compete against each other. Perhaps we could implement both of these initiatives. People used to take pride in their property whether residential or industrial. That seems to have dwindled. We can't even pack our recycling bins properly.
The City of Hamilton makes it difficult to pick up litter. To get orange bags I have to go online and register, and host a cleanup event. Why can't I just pick up orange bags somewhere convenient and if I have a few hours, go pick up litter? Why do I have to leave it at the end of my driveway? Why can't I leave it where I picked it up? It also doesn't help that Hamilton removes park garbage cans in the winter. Or that the street sweepers go where it's clean. Litter is the responsibility of everyone so litter clean up should be, too. So many city improvements depend on some level of government financing; keeping our streets clean is something we can all do for very little to no expense. How about it Hamilton? From lunch bucket city to litter-free city?
Martha Howatt, Hamilton
CAPTION: When we call some people "homeless" it is easier to think about doing terrible things to them, a reader writes.

Our safety and democracy are at risk

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/our-safety-democracy-are-at-risk/docview/3142182692/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=unknown&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Our+safety+and+democracy+are+at+risk&amp;title=The+Spectator&amp;issn=11899417&amp;date=2024-12-09&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.11&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Spectator&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: The provincial auditor's report was very damning yet again. Particularly on the development of Ontario Place as "not transparent or fair."
The report also noted keeping the Ontario Science Centre where it is and making repairs was far less expensive than moving and building a new one.
The CEO of Infrastructure Ontario jumped to Ford's defence, claiming "the government's vision would have the best benefits to taxpayers." The AG's report shows that claim is complete bunk.
When Ford suggested a multi-billion-dollar tunnel under the 401, he was roundly criticized for it. The board of trade quickly jumped to Ford's defence, calling it "bold leadership," likely because its members would profit handsomely (showing again Ford's cosy relationship with big business).
Since first being elected in 2018, Ford has repeatedly shown he lets nothing stand in his way to benefit private corporations. Certainly not democratic principles or norms.
Ford has implemented ministerial zoning orders 114 times, overriding local democracy. Giving "strong-mayor" powers is a prime example of subverting democracy, where mayors now have the power to override decisions by democratically elected councillors.
The Mike Harris government repeatedly subverted democracy by eliminating referendum voting rights any time a public asset was to be sold. It seems that a Progressive Conservative government's normal operating procedure is to bypass and eliminate democracy to the benefit of the private sector.
Conservatives love claiming they are "cutting red tape," making it sound good, but are actually eliminating laws and regulations that require public input and approval. Regulations that were put there to protect people and the environment, but get in the way of profit making.
The prime example now is Ford's Building Highways Faster Act side-stepping environmental assessments.
The worst is whenever they pass new legislation, they give themselves and their ministers more power to rule by decree with no transparency and no public input whatsoever.
Harris did this repeatedly, particularly with his bills in 1998 on hydro deregulation. And now Ford has repeatedly done this. The prime example being the Ford government's Bill 165, Keeping Energy Costs Down Act, giving itself more power to rule by decree. Another example is Bill 218, Supporting Ontario's Recovery and Municipal Elections Act, which shielded private owners of long-term-care facilities from being sued for negligence, where so many died during the pandemic from neglect.
Now Ford has shielded his government from being sued for removing bike lanes where people could be injured or killed as a result of Bill 212. Ford has also moved to close supervised injection sites, which could result in more deaths.
The most serious threat to the public's health and safety is Ford's cancellation of the most effective low-cost way of detecting disease risk to the public: wastewater testing.
Ford saved a few bucks there, while spending more than $100 million on advertising telling us what a great job he is doing.
Ford has campaigned on two slogans, "For the people" and "Getting it done." He has gone to great effort showing he's "getting it done" for wealthy people. If only Ford put this much effort and money into solving the health care, housing, education and affordability crises.
The big questions now are, will $200 persuade you to ignore the dangers to you and your family? And, will you ignore the damage to our environment and trampling of your democratic rights?
The price we'll pay for voting for Ford is far too high.
Paul Kahnert is a
freelance writer in Ontario.
CAPTION:

CREDIT: Paul Kahnert Paul Kahnert is a freelance writer in Ontario.;

Document 499 of 500

Tory MP meets Vance amid tariff threats: U.S. Senator, Canadian politician have been close friends since the pair attended Yale University

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/tory-mp-meets-vance-amid-tariff-threats/docview/3142182683/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Tory+MP+meets+Vance+amid+tariff+threats%3A+U.S.+Senator%2C+Canadian+politician+have+been+close+friends+since+the+pair+attended+Yale+University&amp;title=The+Spectator&amp;issn=11899417&amp;date=2024-12-09&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.8&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Spectator&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: A Conservative member of Parliament met with Donald Trump's second-in-command over the weekend as Canada prepares for the U.S. president-elect's return to the White House next month amid threats of devastating tariffs.
Jamil Jivani said he had dinner with vice-president-elect JD Vance and British Conservative Opposition Leader Kemi Badenoch on Saturday in Arlington, Va., not far from Washington.
"The dinner was a good opportunity to catch up with my longtime friend, the vice president-elect, and also meet the new leader of the U.K. Conservative party," Jivani, the MP for Durham, said Sunday in a phone interview. "I think it's pretty clear right now that for the good of Canada we need to be able to build strong relationships with our allies. I felt like the dinner we had was good for that purpose."
A source with knowledge of the gathering said they discussed the importance of building strong relationships between all three countries. The source said Jivani invited the vice president-elect to come to Bowmanville, in his Ontario riding, in the new year.
Jivani expressed to Vance how "Canada is America's best friend and ally, and a reliable trade and security partner," the MP said in a followup emailed statement. "I've shared feedback from Canadian businesses and workers, including those in my constituency, about the impact tariffs would have on our economy," Jivani said.
"I have also expressed to him things that I have heard from constituents about the Canada-U.S. border and the need to work together to protect our collective security."
Jivani has been friends with the U.S. senator for Ohio since they both attended Yale University. Jivani has written about their close relationship and spoke at Vance's wedding.
Jivani shared a picture after Saturday'sdinner on social media, marking the first time he has publicly posted about meeting with his friend since the November election.
The display of cross-border camaraderie comes as Trump lobs tariff threats against America's closest neighbours and ahead of a review of the Canada-U.S.-Mexico Agreement. A Team Canada approach was quickly formed following Trump's first win in 2016, when the Republican promised to tear up the North American Free Trade Agreement and impose tariffs.
At that time, Trudeau reached out to former Tory prime minister Brian Mulroney to be an adviser. Former Conservative ministers Rona Ambrose and James Moore also joined the effort.
The Canadian approach has appeared less unified this time around. Provincial leaders, particularly Ontario Premier Doug Ford, have called on Ottawa to do more to avoid threatened duties.
Jivani confirmed that despite his close relationship with the incoming vice-president, he has not been contacted by anyone in the governing Liberals "in relation to things concerning the United States." He also has not been contacted by anyone in the Ontario government.
The Canadian Press
CAPTION: Jamil Jivani is a Conservative MP for Durham.

CREDIT: Kelly Geraldine Malone The Canadian Press

Document 500 of 500

Using PR against Trump's tariff love affair

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/using-pr-against-trumps-tariff-love-affair/docview/3142182481/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=unknown&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Using+PR+against+Trump%27s+tariff+love+affair&amp;title=The+Spectator&amp;issn=11899417&amp;date=2024-12-09&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.11&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Spectator&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: "To me, the most beautiful word in the dictionary is tariff and it's my favourite word," president-elect Trump told Bloomberg in October while speaking at the Economic Club of Chicago. "It needs a public relations firm."
Indeed, it does - but not for the reasons he imagines. Instead, tariffs need a communications campaign to deliver a blunt message to Americans.
Justin Trudeau's dinner at Mar-a-Lago may succeed at addressing Donald Trump's border issues, but it's a catch-22. Trump will view tariffs as a threat that has proven effective. Canada needs to respond to Trump by speaking directly to Americans with a simple message: "Tariffs hurt you more than us."
In trade wars, as in divorce, there is no winning, only degrees of losing. The key is to convince the other side that it is bound to lose more.
Premier Doug Ford's new ad targeting Washington decision-makers is rather timid but what he told Newstalk 1010 is spot on: "Will we get hurt if he puts a 25 per cent tariff - 100 per cent it's going to hurt us. Is it going to hurt the U.S. - 1,000 per cent."
Businesses in the U.S. know that tariffs will hurt them. But only a handful have made it part of the commercial message with a sense of urgency.
According to reporting in the Wall Street Journal, retailer Finally Home Furnishings of Dallas declared in Facebook post recently, "Pre-Tariff Sale! This is not a drill," urging customers to order now before prices "double."
Sporting goods retailer Tarptent offered up to 35 per cent off some tents, saying, "These are the best discounts we will likely offer until this time next year, and with potential tariffs looming, they might be the best prices for a lot longer than that."
And J&J Sports Service, posted "Buy now ... tariffs are coming not a joke. We will hold the line as long as we can. But we can say for certain this may be the last 'sale' you may see."
So here's a plan Trump would pay attention to - if he doesn't, American shoppers certainly will. It's a communications strategy modelled after Ford's gas pump stickers and Alberta's "Tell The Feds" and "Scrap the Tax" campaigns.
To deliver this message, a coalition funded by Canada's largest exporters to the U.S. could run a multimedia campaign in key U.S. markets, hammering home one fundamental truth: tariffs raise prices for American consumers. By highlighting the direct impact on household products, this campaign will make tariffs personal, shifting the debate from abstract trade policy to everyday costs.
Imagine seeing "tariff impact stickers" on everyday items, showing how Canadian exports contribute to their price.
For example:
Maple Syrup: "80 per cent of this product's cost is due to Canadian maple syrup." Canada produces over 70 per cent of the world's maple syrup. A tariff could double the cost of this breakfast staple.
Toilet Paper: "30 per cent of this product's cost comes from Canadian wood pulp." Canadian forests supply much of the pulp used in American-made toilet paper. A tariff would ripple through to this everyday necessity.
Not to mention what would be impacted by a tariff on oil.
The stickers would bring tariffs into sharp focus, tying them directly to higher grocery bills and everyday expenses. Americans, who often don't think about where their products come from, will quickly see the connection between tariffs and their wallets.
Beyond highlighting the financial burden, this campaign would position Canada as a practical, friendly neighbour trying to save Americans from an economic self-inflicted wound.
It's not about opposing "America First" policies - it's about helping Americans see the unintended consequences of those policies.
If tariffs truly need a PR firm, Canada should beat Trump to the punch. By putting the tariff's impact where it's felt most - on the shelves and in the wallets of everyday Americans - Canada can effectively counter the rhetoric and show that protectionism comes with a price.
As Trump himself might say, "It's just good PR" and take credit for the idea.
Éric Blais is president of Headspace Marketing Inc.
CAPTION:

CREDIT: Éric Blais Éric Blais is president of Headspace Marketing Inc.;
